text: Pre-trained Large Language Models (LLMs) are beginning to dominate the field of Natural language processing. While these models have shown impressive results in tasks such as question answering and sentiment analysis, they lack domain knowledge specific for a particular application or industry which hinders their ability perform well on real world problems like text summarization , classification etc., This problem can be solved by fine tuning LLMS with appropriate data sets from different domains . In this work we demonstrate how one might go about doing so using GPT 3 model trained over large amount generic datasets(like Wikipedia). We used our approach during NLP competition called "Text Summarizer" held at Colab AI Summit hosted jointly between Google Cloud Platform & IBM Watson Studio

Listing and counting triangles in graphs is a key algorithmic kernel for network analyses, especially when dealing with large-scale networks. In this paper we study the problem of listing all nonisomorphic triangle subgraphs (or motifs) within an input graph $G$ on one hand or to count them without duplication by using structural properties as identifiers instead ("structural enumeration"). For both problems our focus lies upon the class $\mathcal{C}$ consisting from directed multigraph data structures that support edge insertions/deletions efficiently ($\in \mathrm {AM}(2)$).

Reservoir computing (RC) offers a neuromorphic framework that is particularly effective for processing spatiotemporal data, and it has received much attention in recent years. However, existing RC-based models are mainly designed to deal with single-domain tasks. In real-world applications, various types of data often need to be integrated, such as textual and visual information. Therefore, how to effectively integrate heterogeneous multi-modal information is still a major challenge in the field of RC. To address this issue, we propose a novel unified model named Hybrid Reservoir Computing (HyRC). It first uses the same input layer to receive multiple modalities of data, which helps the model learn the common features of different inputs in advance. Then, the information from each modality

Neural models have shown impressive performance in natural language processing downstream tasks such as relation classification. However, they lack the explainability and transparency needed to understand how the model arrives at a certain prediction, which is crucial in domains such as biology, medicine, and law. On the other hand, symbolic approaches may lack the needed data efficiency and scalability to solve complex tasks. We propose a novel approach that incorporates the strengths of both worlds: a neuro-symbolic model that is generalizable and explainable, yet data efficient and scalable. Our model PlaNet incorporates graph neural networks and logical reasoning, and it outperforms relation classification baselines on both Snips and Wiki-ZSL. Moreover, it allows us to observe how the model uses its logical reasoning capabilities and verifies the correctness of inference steps.

This paper delves into the contrasting roles of data within academic and industrial spheres, highlighting how different conceptions drive divergent expectations. We investigate this through an analysis comparing a novel dataset designed by academics in academia with existing datasets used for similar purposes from industry; both are applied to predict student outcomes at university level across England using machine learning techniques such as Random Forest (RF) algorithms based on features extracted during application processing stages prior entry-point assessment scores being awarded or recorded elsewhere outside formal testing environments where applicants can opt out if they prefer not submit their work publicly available online repository websites like GitHub .

adaptNMT is an open-source application that offers a streamlined approach to the development and deployment of Recurrent Neural Networks and Transformer models. This application is built upon the widely-adopted OpenNMT ecosystem, and is particularly useful for new entrants to the field, as it simplifies the setup of the development environment and creation of train, validation, and test splits. The application offers a graphing feature that illustrates the progress of model training, and employs SentencePiece for creating subword segmentation models. Furthermore, the application provides an intuitive user interface that facilitates hyperparameter customization. Notably, a single-click model development approach has been implemented, and models developed by adaptNMT can be evaluated using a

Clustered Regularly Interspaced Short Palindromic Repeats (CRISPR) is a revolutionary bacterial defense mechanism against phage infections, which has been harnessed to develop CRISPR-Cas9 gene editing tools. This study explored the effects of a CRISPR-based antibody (mAb1208) on the viral life cycle and investigated the mechanisms underlying its anti-viral activity. We demonstrate that mAb1208 effectively binds to VLPs, blocks their release from cells, and impairs phage growth by targeting the capsid protein (VPg). Furthermore, the binding site for mAb1208 on VPg overlaps with the recognition site of Cas9, suggesting the possibility of using this antibody as a tool to control VLP production through CRISPR-mediated targeting of VPg.

We initiate a study of data collaboration analysis, a type of multi-party computation in which a number of data providers agree on certain properties of their combined data without disclosing their local data. Specifically, we assume that each data provider has a local data matrix, and they agree on the singular subspaces of the concatenated matrix. We focus on the question of how the data providers can learn about the singular subspaces of their combined data without disclosing their local data. To this end, we first formulate a mathematical problem of finding a low-dimensional subspace of the matrix manifold that preserves the singular subspaces of any given matrix. We solve the problem by proving that the optimal solution is given by the intersection of the singular subspaces of the matrix and a low-dimensional subspace that is orthogonal to the data manifold. We then propose a randomized algorithm for finding the optimal solution by projecting the matrix onto a high-dimensional subspace that contains the data manifold. We evaluate the proposed algorithm by using image data, and show that it outperforms a baseline algorithm that uses the SVD of the concatenated matrix.

The estimation of the 6DoF relative camera pose between a pair of images is a fundamental problem in computer vision. Recent approaches have shown that by using deep learning-based essential matrix estimation and subsequent refinement, we can achieve sub-pixel accurate 6DoF pose estimation. However, their robustness to noisy inputs is poor, and they lack flexibility for different application scenarios. In this work, we propose a novel flexible, accurate, and robust framework for 6DoF relative camera pose estimation, called FAR. We are the first to introduce the robustness-aware training paradigm, which can be applied to any essential matrix estimation method. Our method improves the state-of-the-art robustness by 20.1% in the presence of 1% pixel-level Gaussian noise. For flexibility, we are the first to introduce the trainable perspective projection model, which can be applied to any 6DoF pose estimation method. Our method can achieve 5.2% and 3.0% improvement in terms of testing accuracy over the state-of-the-art methods in the presence of 1% pixel-level Gaussian noise for the general and the challenging dataset, respectively. Our method can also be applied to other tasks, such as 3D point cloud registration, and achieves 4.3% and 3.7% improvement in terms of testing accuracy over the state-of-the-art methods in the presence of 1% pixel-level Gaussian noise for the general and the challenging dataset, respectively. Our code is available at https://github.com/luost28/FAR.

Brilla AI is a question answering system that participated in the National Science and Maths Quiz (NSMQ) in 2021 and 2022. This paper describes the system and its performance in the quiz. Brilla AI is an open-domain question answering system that uses a retrieval-based approach to find the most relevant document to an user's question and then extracts the answer to the question from that document using natural language processing techniques. In addition, Brilla AI is able to learn from its interactions with users and improve its answers over time. In the NSMQ, Brilla AI had to answer questions on various science and maths topics such as physics, chemistry, biology, and calculus. It competed against 11 other AI systems and 3 human teams. Brilla AI won the 2021 NSMQ with a score of 429.4 points, beating the second-place AI system by 20.6 points. In 2022, Brilla AI improved its performance further by winning the NSMQ with a score of 453.3 points, beating the second-place AI system by 29.7 points.

Over-the-air federated learning (OTAFL) is a promising paradigm for training a global model across multiple clients without sharing local data. However, OTAFL is vulnerable to adversarial attacks and lacks fairness among clients. In this paper, we investigate the coexistence of fairness and robustness in OTAFL. We propose a novel OTAFL framework with two phases: 1) a fair aggregation phase that ensures each client's contribution is fairly reflected in the global model, and 2) a robust defense phase that impedes adversarial attacks and noise injection from malicious clients. Specifically, in the fair aggregation phase, we propose a defense mechanism based on gradient compression to mitigate noise injection attacks, and a fair aggregation algorithm to ensure fairness under adversarial attacks; in the robust defense phase, we propose a robust aggregation algorithm to further robustify the model against adversarial attacks. Theoretical analysis shows that the proposed OTAFL framework achieves a provable fairness guarantee and robustness against noise injection attacks and adversarial attacks. Empirical evaluation on two real-world datasets demonstrates that the proposed OTAFL framework achieves superior performance compared with existing OTAFL algorithms.

Controllable decoding, in which a latent variable is used to manipulate the output of a decoder, has been used to exert some degree of control over the generation process. However, the complexity of natural language determines that in practice we can only roughly control the generation results, and it is difficult to precisely control the decoder's behavior by manipulating the latent variable. In contrast, human language use is characterized by the flexibility and precision with which expressions can be controlled according to the speaker's intentions. This raises the question of how to achieve precise control over machine generation. To address this issue, we draw inspiration from dual-system cognitive theory, which posits that human language production involves a lexical system for generating words and a syntactic system for combining them, and that these two systems are independently controlled by an abstract meaning representation. We propose a model named DECIDER, in which a meaning encoder converts the latent variable and context into a meaning representation, which is then provided to both a lexical decoder and a syntactic decoder. We show that DECIDER can achieve more precisely controlled generation results, as it can independently control the lexical and syntactic properties of the generation results. Compared to previous controllable decoding models, DECIDER is more transparent, as the meaning representation can be visualized directly. We evaluate our model on the SNLI-ENTAILMENT dataset, which consists of sentence pairs from the SNLI dataset that are manually labeled as entailed, contradiction, or neutral. Experimental results demonstrate that our model outperforms previous approaches.

Since Labov's (1964) foundational work on the social stratification of English vowel systems, sociolinguists have sought to uncover patterns of linguistic variation in relation to social categories like gender, age, or ethnicity. We argue that a key factor in this process has been the reliance on small-scale empirical investigations based on relatively homogeneous speaker populations. In this article, we describe a methodology for identifying and testing hypotheses about correlates of linguistic variation based on large-scale quantitative data from multiple speech communities. The approach can be used to identify significant correlations between variables and linguistic features using simple descriptive statistics and/or regression analysis. It also enables researchers to test hypotheses a

Cognitive training is beneficial for post-stroke individuals, but it is important to personalize the recommendations of cognitive training tasks to the specific situation of each individual. In this work, we focus on estimating the difficulty level of cognitive training tasks for post-stroke individuals by using causal trees. Causal trees are decision trees that split the data not only based on the observed features, but also take into account the causal effects of the features on the target variable. We use a publicly available dataset that contains information about cognitive training tasks performed by post-stroke individuals. Our results show that the estimated difficulty levels by the causal trees are in accordance with the actual difficulty levels of the tasks, as evaluated by a group of clinicians. Additionally, we show that the causal trees produce more homogeneous groups of post-stroke individuals based on their difficulty levels compared to other state-of-the-art methods.

G-Quadruplexes are the four-stranded non-canonical nucleobase structures formed by telomer sequence. They play an important role in regulating gene expression and maintaining genomic stability of cells, which has been proved to be associated with various cancers such as leukemia or colorectal cancer (CRC). G4 drug targeting therapy is considered a promising strategy for CRC treatment due its ability to inhibit proliferation without harming normal cell functions compared other conventional chemotherapy agents that cause severe side effects on healthy tissues/organs including heart muscle toxicity leading death from cardiac arrest among patients undergoing standard radiotherapy protocol during early stages when disease still treatable yet not curable unless surgery performed immediately upon

In the realm of big data and digital healthcare, Electronic Health Records (EHR) have become an invaluable source for medical research. However, existing EHR datasets are limited to single institutions and often fail to account for privacy concerns and regulatory restrictions. To address these challenges, we introduce DREAMER: The Digital REsearch AMple Medical EHR Dataset. DREAMER is a large-scale public dataset comprising 329,975 unique patient records from eight hospitals across four continents. These records span multiple specialties such as oncology, cardiology, neurology, and gastroenterology. Our goal is to foster the development of innovative AI solutions that can improve clinical decision-making while respecting patients' rights to confidentiality.

Due to the effectiveness of second-order algorithms in solving classical optimization problems, designing second-order methods for graph regularization is becoming a hot topic. Existing second-order algorithms for graph regularization usually rely on the assumption that the solution is within a certain region around the initial point, which can lead to suboptimal results if this assumption is violated. To address this issue, we propose a novel second-order method called S2GRL, which does not require any assumptions about the location of the global minimum. Our algorithm employs a quadratic approximation of the energy function using a finite difference scheme and performs backtracking line search to find the best step size. The key to the success of our approach is the introduction of a new

Underwater soft grippers exhibit potential for applications such as monitoring, research, and object retraction. However, most gripping systems are heavy and complicated, limiting their application in underwater environments. In this paper, we propose a novel design of a lightweight and modular underwater soft gripper that can be easily assembled by users without specialized training. The main body of our gripper is made of 3D printed material with low density, which reduces the weight to 20 g while maintaining the same strength as other materials. Moreover, the soft fingers and joints of our gripper are made of flexible materials, which enables them to adapt to various objects. The overall design is inspired by human hands, providing users with more intuitive control over the gripper's mo

Battery technology is increasingly important for global electrification efforts. However, batteries are limited in their ability to quickly charge and discharge, which can pose challenges for a range of applications. Now researchers from the University of Michigan have developed an alternative energy storage device that may be able to deliver energy at up to 10 times faster rates than existing batteries while also being more efficient. The study, published in Science Advances, outlines how the researchers' device is constructed using electrodes of alternating nano-scaled layers of nickel and carbon, which allows it to operate on a fast time scale. To test its performance, the researchers charged and discharged their prototype device by applying a voltage pulse that lasted just 50 microseco

In decision-making problem under uncertainty, predicting unknown parameters is often considered independent of the optimization part. In this work we propose a fully joint method which learns to optimize and predict simultaneously in an endtoend fashion by introducing explicit randomness into both parts via variational inference with latent variables (VIXL). The proposed framework offers three advantages: 1) it avoids manual tuning hyperparameters for each task;2 )it provides flexibility on different types or numberof predictors as wellas various loss functionsfor prediction tasks depending upon their requirements3); finally , V IX L can be easily extended towards multiobjective objectives without additional modifications thanks t o its inherent nature allowing simultaneous learning across

In the era of personalized education, the provision of comprehensible explanations for learning recommendations is becoming increasingly important. As a result, researchers have been developing models that provide explanations at different levels and incorporate various factors to tailor the explanation's content. However, there are still some challenges in the field. First, existing methods only focus on providing an overall explanation without considering individual students' needs or preferences. Second, they rely heavily on large-scale datasets with sufficient data points, which may not be feasible or ethical to collect from real users. Third, these methods often require additional human efforts such as manual annotation or post-processing steps to generate customizable explanations. T

When should a company shut down its operations? This question can be answered by comparing the company's revenue function to its cost function. In this paper, we fix a convex cost function and ask: for what revenue functions does the optimal shutdown time exist? We provide three theorems that answer this question. The first theorem provides a necessary and sufficient condition for the existence of a shutdown time. The second and third theorems give sufficient conditions for the existence of a shutdown time. These results are applied to the special cases of linear cost functions and concave revenue functions.

Most continual segmentation methods tackle the problem as a per-pixel classification task. However, existing work assumes that all pixels are equally important and does not take into account their spatial relations with neighboring labels which is essential for accurate semantic segmentations of complex scenes such as those in real world applications like autonomous driving or medical imaging (e..g., CT scans). To this end we propose Spatial Contextual Networks - SCNet , an architecture specifically designed to handle contextual information by using multiscale feature fusion combined together via dilated convolutions along side traditional convolutional blocks at each scale level within our backbone network .

In the realm of big data and digital healthcare, Electronic Health Records (EHR) have emerged as a central pillar. These electronic records are crucial for accurate patient diagnosis, treatment, and follow-up, and their integration into clinical decision support systems has significantly improved patient care. However, the potential risks associated with EHRs are often overlooked, particularly in terms of privacy violations, unauthorized access, and cybersecurity threats. The implementation of rigorous security measures and adherence to regulatory standards is paramount in mitigating these risks. This article delves into the intricacies of EHRs, highlighting their advantages and potential vulnerabilities. It explores the role of AI and machine learning in enhancing patient care through pre

Despite their performance, Resnets are difficult to train and prone towards local minima during optimization due the vanishing or exploding gradient problem caused by large number layers in deep networks [14]. To solve these problems several methods such as batch normalization[6], residual shortcut connections between two consecutive blocks were proposed with successful results on ImageNet dataset. Information retrieval models have witnessed a paradigm shift from unsupervised statistical approaches to feature-rich, supervised neural architectures. Recent works have explored the effectiveness of leveraging pretrained language models (PLMs) for indexing documents with the aim of improving search efficiency and effectiveness. However, existing PLM-based information retrieval systems suffer from two main limitations: 1) they fail to capture the localized context in document representations, and 2) their learned embeddings are not easily transferable to downstream tasks. To address these shortcomings, we introduce a novel method called SPIRAL (Self-supervision Pretrained Info Retrieval ALgoRithms). Our approach combines self-supervised learning with fine-tuning on a supervised task and ai

Recently, Mixture of Experts (MoE) Transformers have garnered increasing interest due to their potential for efficient inference. However, there are two main problems that need to be addressed: the sub-optimality of the expert weights and the inefficiency of training with a large number of experts. In this paper, we propose an efficient learning strategy to overcome these challenges. Specifically, we first derive a closed-form solution to the optimal weights of the mixture experts based on a mean squared error criterion. This is achieved by decomposing the problem into a series of smaller optimization tasks and employing a linear approximation technique. We then design an online algorithm to learn the optimal expert weights in an efficient manner without requiring the explicit calculation

Contention resolution is a fundamental problem in distributed computing. The task is as follows: There are $k$ processes, each with an initial value, and the goal is to compute the sum of all initial values, despite some processes possibly behaving adversarially and lying about their initial values. The processes communicate by sending messages along the communication network. A protocol solves the problem if it terminates with the correct sum with high probability, and the expected number of messages sent per round is bounded by a function of $k$ only (and independent of the problem size). In this survey, we overview the line of research on adversarial contention resolution protocols. We start by discussing the model and present the state-of-the-art lower bounds and protocols. We then discuss variations of the model, including allowing more powerful adversaries, studying the trade-off between the stretch (the ratio between the maximum and minimum initial values) and the number of rounds, and relaxing the termination guarantee. Finally, we conclude with a discussion of future directions and open problems.

A common way of assessing language learners' mastery of vocabulary is via multiple-choice tests. As a consequence, research has examined the conditions under which this method yields reliable results and provides valid scores (e., Schmitt & McNamara 1987; Shohamy et al,.204). This study extends previous work by examining the effects that test length - in terms of number items per subtest within an overall larger multisubtests exam -- can have on reliability estimates for different types or categories of words: general academic English vs domain specific medical terminology .

The recent success of Transformers has sparked a revolution in low-resource language processing. However, low-resource languages still suffer from a lack of linguistic annotation. In this work, we present a simple and effective solution to this problem by proposing to use Transformers for unsupervised multilingual learning, thus leveraging global linguistic patterns to boost performance on low-resource languages. We introduce a new model, Is F\'eidir Linn? (Is F\'eidir Linn?), that combines multilingual masked language modeling with a resource-friendly decoder-only architecture. The resulting model excels in low-resource settings, surpassing previous state-of-the-art performance on benchmark datasets for Irish and Welsh. Our approach not only demonstrates the potential of our model but also highlights the importance of linguistic annotation in improving the performance of low-resource languages.

To foster the neural encoding of Portuguese, this paper contributes foundation encoder models that represent an important stepping stone towards zero-shot translation and text-to-speech synthesis. We first propose a new model architecture for foundational encoders with a unique attention mechanism called Self-Attention with Gated Recurrent Units (SAGRU) to capture long-range contextual information. This is followed by training on a comprehensive benchmark dataset, comprising 132 million words from diverse genres and modalities. The resulting models achieve state-of-the-art results across various downstream tasks such as zero-shot text-to-speech synthesis and cross-modal retrieval. Moreover, our SAGRU-based encoders are particularly efficient, requiring only half the parameter count compare

Large language models (LLMs) have revolutionized the role of AI, yet also pose serious concerns regarding their potential for misuse. To address these issues and ensure responsible use in real-world applications such as digital assistants or chatbots with human interaction capabilities that respond to text input from users through conversation interfaces - Google has developed a new system called Language Model Discrimination Prevention Toolset(LMDPT). This groundbreaking tool is designed specifically around two core components: an evaluation framework based on three criteria; trustworthiness verification score calculation method using weighted average scores generated by each criterion's scoring function within LMFCT Framework Version 2019/3 which allows automated detection & prevention s

Point masses connected by springs, or mass-spring systems, are widely used in computer animation to generate physically plausible motion. However, the resulting simulations are often too slow for real-time applications and need to be sped up in order to run at interactive rates. One approach is to reduce the number of degrees of freedom (DOF) in the system using a reduction technique such as Eigensystem Reduction Algorithm (ERA). This method has the advantage of preserving the original dynamics of the system to some extent. A disadvantage is that it introduces numerical errors which can cause the reduced system to behave unexpectedly. In this paper we introduce the concept of a hybrid reduction system that consists of an ERA reduced system and a fast nonphysical simulator. We show that thi

Multiple access techniques are cornerstones of wireless communications. Their performance depends on the channel properties, which in turn depend on the physical properties of the environment and on how signals are transmitted (i.e., waveforms). In this paper we study the effect of waveform diversity on the performance of a cellular network employing multiple access with non-orthogonal waveforms. We consider two classes of waveforms: orthogonal frequency division multiplexing (OFDM) with equal time-bandwidth product (TBP) and OFDM with different TBPs. The latter waveforms allow for a reduction in power consumption and provide better protection against interference from other cells, but they also increase the number of pilot sequences needed to estimate the channels at the base station. For

A common subgraph of two graphs $G_1$ and $G_2$ is a connected induced subgraph that is contained in both $G_1$ and $G_2$. We study the problem of finding a maximum-size common subgraph of two given graphs $G_1$ and $G_2$. The problem, which we call Maximum Common Subgraph (MCS), was first studied by Karp et al. [SODA 2018], who gave an algorithm with running time $O(n^{\omega+1} + m^4)$ for MCS, where $\omega < 3$ is the exponent of matrix multiplication and $m$ is the number of edges in the input graphs.

In recent years, personalized recommendation technology has flourished and become one of the hot research directions in the field of artificial intelligence. As a widely used recommendation method, collaborative filtering (CF) has achieved good results in various recommendation scenarios. However, due to the sparsity problem, CF-based methods have limited performance when the data is sparse or cold start. To address this issue, we propose a novel method based on the combination of self-supervised learning (SSL) and collaborative filtering (CFFT). We leverage the SSL mechanism to mine the latent features of users and items through the pretext task, and then use the CFFT method to predict ratings by fusing the two types of feature representations. The experimental results show that our appro

We propose a methodology for modelling methane intensities of Oil and Gas upstream processes. This is the first study to combine a process-oriented approach with data-driven methods in order to capture the complex dynamics of methane emissions in oil and gas operations. We apply our model on the methane emission levels of different processes for a specific offshore field in Norway, using historical data from 2010 to 2019. The results show that the proposed model outperforms existing models in terms of accuracy and can provide valuable insights into the methane intensity of each process. This research contributes to the development of more accurate and reliable tools for monitoring and reducing methane emissions in oil and gas operations. It also highlights the importance of integrating dat

We create a dataset consisting of Japanese-English sentence translation exercises for college-level English as a foreign language (EFL) courses. The dataset is made available with reference translations provided by professional translators and gold standard machine translations from Google Translate. The dataset is intended to be used for research on automatic grading of EFL students' written work, in particular sentence translation exercises. We present a baseline system for the automatic grading of translation exercises and show that our dataset will be useful for research on this task.

The efficacy of machine learning has traditionally relied on the availability of increasingly larger datasets. However, in many practical applications such as recommender systems and self-driving cars, collecting data is difficult or impossible. In this work, we propose a novel approach to address this challenge by leveraging generative models that capture the underlying distribution of the data. Our key insight is that generative models can be used to create data samples that are more challenging for the model to fit, thereby improving generalization performance. We evaluate our method on three different classification tasks: MNIST, CIFAR10, and MiniImagenet. On all three tasks, we show that our proposed method consistently outperforms other baselines. Furthermore, we demonstrate that our

Existing Cross-Domain Few-Shot Learning (CDFSL) methods require access to labeled data from the target domain. Recently, some works have shown that only using unlabeled data for CDFSL is possible by leveraging a few-shot teacher-forcing learning strategy. However, the existing methods still suffer from three issues: 1) they are not effective enough in handling the cross-modal domain shift; 2) they cannot effectively handle the class imbalance problem between the source and target domains; 3) they have limited capabilities in generalizing to novel classes or scenarios. In this work, we propose to address these challenges through a novel framework named Joint Knowledge Distillation (JKD).

Language models are known to be sensitive to prompts, but the exact reason for this sensitivity is not well understood. In this work, we study the relationship between prompts and model outputs in terms of word importance. We measure word importance using three different methods: frequency-based, model-based, and human-based. Our main finding is that the importance of words in prompts correlates with the importance of words in model outputs. This correlation varies across models, prompts, and output words, but it is strong and statistically significant. We also find that prompts with more important words produce more important model outputs, and that the distribution of word importance in model outputs is predictive of model accuracy. These results provide insight into the black box of language models, showing that the sensitivity to prompts is driven by the prominence of certain words. They also have implications for applications of language models in real-world settings, as understanding and anticipating the effects of prompt engineering could improve communication with the model and lead to more accurate task performances.

Machine Translation is a mature technology for many high-resource language pairs. However in the context of low-resource languages, it has not yet been established as a reliable service. This paper presents an investigation into how machine translation can be used to help the development of multilingual content on Wikipedia, and specifically focusses on its use with two low-resource languages: Esperanto (EO) and Silesian (SIL). We examine both traditional machine translation systems and Neural Machine Translation models to determine which are best suited for these languages, investigating their performance on a number of metrics related to translation quality. We also investigate how human translators perceive the output of these tools when using them to translate texts from English into E

Facial Action Units (AU) is a vital concept in the realm of affective computing, providing a crucial framework for understanding and interpreting facial expressions. However, its application in computer vision has been hampered by several challenges, such as limited datasets, unbalanced AU distributions, and the lack of comprehensive evaluation metrics. To address these issues, we introduce an AU-centric dataset, AU-Lab, designed to facilitate research and development in affective computing. AU-Lab includes 20K high-quality videos with manual annotations of AUs and facial landmarks, offering a diverse range of emotions and extensive variability in pose, lighting, and background conditions. Additionally, we propose a novel benchmarking methodology that assesses facial expression recognition

Boosting meta-training with base class information is investigated for few-shot learning. Base class information is utilized to enhance few-shot meta-training via constraining the model predictions within the base classes. This idea is motivated by the reality that the few-shot classification task is often embedded in a specific domain, and the domain-related information can be extracted and utilized. Specifically, a base class set is provided for the few-shot learning task, and the base class information is incorporated into the model through the proposed base class attention (BCA) and base class constraint (BCC) mechanisms. BCA is a feature-level attention that emphasizes the base class features and de-emphasizes the non-base class features. BCC is a prediction-level constraint that forces the model to select the base class with the highest probability and eliminates the non-base classes from consideration. The model is trained in a meta-learning fashion to predict the labels of base classes and non-base classes, and the base class information is progressively learned. Extensive experiments demonstrate that the proposed BCA and BCC mechanisms are effective in improving the few-shot learning performance, and they can be integrated with other base classagnostic few-shot learning methods to further boost the performance.

With the growing popularity of global mobile networks, concerns over the security and privacy of mobile users have also increased. Many mobile users turn to mobile Virtual Private Network (mVPN) services to enjoy a higher degree of privacy and security while on the go. However, using a mVPN service incurs additional costs for the users. Hence, understanding what motivates users to switch their mVPN services is crucial for the survival of mVPN services in the wild. In this paper, we aim to data-driven understand the mVPN service switching preferences of 2.2K real users in the wild. For this purpose, we partner with a leading mVPN service and collect data on the usage patterns of 2.2K real users over 3G and 4G networks. We analyze the usage data to understand the switching behaviors and preferences of the users. Our findings show that (1) 44.6% of the users switch their mVPN services, (2) users are 50% more likely to switch from a free to a paid mVPN service, (3) users prefer switching to a different mVPN service every 24-48 hours, (4) users dislike advertisements and slow connection speeds, and (5) users are willing to pay \$2.5-\$5.5 per day for a good mVPN service. This study brings insights into the mVPN service switching behaviors of real users and can help mVPN service providers craft better pricing strategies and improve their services to retain users.

For learning with noisy labels, the transition matrix, which explicitly models the relation between noisy label and ground truth, is a crucial factor. Existing methods tend to underestimate or overestimate the noise rate, leading to suboptimal performance. In this paper, we propose a novel approach that considers both the softmax loss in training and the posterior distribution of classifier's outputs when estimating the transition matrix. With a principled derivation, our proposed model is proved to be able to learn the correct transition matrix regardless of the noise rate. Experiments on various datasets demonstrate the superiority of our method over several state-of-the-art methods. Code is available at https://github.com/yangcw7703/TransitionMatrixEstimation.git. Our method is also int

Relational graph neural networks (R-GNNs) have shown promising results on knowledge graph (KG) completion tasks. However, few-shot KG completion remains a challenge, as limited training data results in overfitting. In this paper, we propose an uncertainty-aware R-GNN model for few-shot KG completion. Our model introduces a novel way to estimate the uncertainty of each triple in the KG, which helps the model to better utilize the available training data. Specifically, we design an uncertainty propagation module (UPM) to estimate triple uncertainty based on the uncertainties of its constituent relations. We also propose a novel meta-learning-based uncertainty learning module (ULM) to learn the uncertainty of each relation. Our experimental results on the FewRel and Wiki-ZSL datasets demonstrate that our method can perform better than the current state-of-the-art methods.

Generative modeling of complex behaviors from labeled datasets has been a longstanding problem in decision-making and planning. This challenging task requires the generation of diverse sequences that accurately capture the target behavior while satisfying various constraints, such as consistency across time steps or task boundaries. To address this challenge, we propose Generative Behavior Model (GBM), a method that trains a generative model on a dataset of human demonstrations to produce realistic, diverse, and consistent behavior sequences. GBM is inspired by the idea that the human mind can generate highly diverse behaviors during execution, and leverages the power of large language models (LLMs) for generating text-based descriptions of behavioral sequences. Specifically, GBM uses a pr

Fine-grained monitoring is crucial for multiple data-driven tasks such as debugging, provisioning, and optimization. However, current solutions rely on expert knowledge and thus are costly to deploy at scale. In this work, we propose an end-to-end solution that combines the benefits of unsupervised learning and active learning techniques to provide scalable fine-grained monitoring capabilities. Our approach consists of three main components: 1) a pretraining phase leveraging self-supervised contrastive learning that enables us to generate latent representations for various data types; 2) a fine-tuning phase based on active learning which allows us to update these latent representations with respect to specific application needs (e.g., task performance or anomaly detection); and 3) an infer

Reinforcement learning (RL) is a powerful tool for solving complex control tasks, but applying RL in safety-critical domains (e.g., autonomous driving and robotic surgery) is challenging because of the potential high cost of exploration. Goal-conditioned RL (GCRL) reduces the exploration cost by leveraging a pretrained policy, which, however, may pose a threat to safety. To mitigate this risk, we propose a novel offline GCRL framework with a recovery policy (GCRER), which aims to exploit the safety knowledge from historical data and prevent the pretrained policy from deviating too far from the safe region. Specifically, we design a recovery policy based on the max-action Q-value to assist the agent in exploring safely in the early stage of training. We also propose a goal feasibility check to ensure that the generated trajectories are feasible before updating the recovery policy. In addition, we adopt a conservative update strategy to avoid overfitting the recovery policy to the historical data. Extensive experiments in the DMC suite and a real-world application demonstrate that our method can achieve superior performance compared with baselines.

This paper is concerned with the provenance and exploration of research outputs, in particular, the challenge of deriving meaning from the growing volume of data produced by computational models. We introduce the notion of conjugate operators to reason about the relationships between different representations of the same computational model, in terms of their respective provenance and exploration. Conjugate operators form the basis of a modular approach to building virtual research environments that support the complete lifecycle of research, from experiment setup and execution to result exploration and dissemination. In this first part, we focus on the provenance aspect and show how conjugate operators can be used to construct transparent pipelines for data processing and analysis, which make the relationships between different representations of the same model - as well as the transformations that relate them - explicitly clear.

Public bus transit systems provide critical transportation services for large sections of modern communities. On-time performance is one major factor in the success or failure a public transport system, and factors that influence on time are numerous ranging from mechanical issues with buses to traffic congestion along routes [1]. A number studies have looked at how weather conditions affect urban mobility through its impact upon road surface quality/traction as well by altering visibility levels within cities themselves; however these effects tend only apply when there's significant snow present (or other forms). This paper looks specifically into whether rainfall has an effect either positive OR negative towards late arrivals among commuters using data collected via mobile phone GPS trac

To support extremely high data rates, reconfigurable intelligent surface (RIS)-assisted terahertz communication systems are increasingly being investigated for their potential in both uplink and downlink applications. This paper presents a novel method to determine the optimal number of RIS elements required to achieve the maximum achievable sum rate through an optimization-based approach. In this approach, the impacts of various design parameters on the sum rate have been analyzed. The proposed algorithm is then utilized to optimize the sum rate under different scenarios such as the availability of different numbers of RIS elements or different channel conditions. Our results show that by using an appropriately selected number of RIS elements, it is possible to improve the efficiency of t

Fractional Differential Equations (FDEs) are essential tools for modelling complex dynamic systems. They have been extensively studied in the literature, but there is a notable lack of research on their practical applications in engineering and science. This paper aims to bridge this gap by presenting a comprehensive survey on FDEs from a practical perspective. We begin by introducing basic concepts related to FDEs, including fractional derivative operators, nonlocal terms, and boundary conditions. We then review existing methods for solving FDEs, focusing on numerical techniques and finite difference schemes. Additionally, we provide an overview of recent advancements in FDEs, such as the use of deep learning and artificial intelligence to solve them. Finally, we discuss potential future

The use of unsupervised learning to identify patient subgroups has emerged as a potentially promising strategy for personalized medicine. However, the effectiveness of this approach depends on the robustness and accuracy of the identified clusters, which can be influenced by various factors such as the number of patients in each cluster, cluster overlap, and cluster homogeneity. These factors are crucial for determining whether clustering results are reliable and meaningful. In this paper, we present a novel methodology that integrates data-driven and model-based approaches to evaluate the robustness and accuracy of patient clusters identified using unsupervised learning methods. Specifically, our method involves using an ensemble of self-organizing maps (SOMs) to generate multiple cluster

In this work, we present the first algorithm to compute expander decompositions in an $m$-edge graph with $n$ vertices, whose output is a set of subgraphs that are edge-disjoint and have average degree within $(1 \pm \varepsilon) m/n$. Our algorithm achieves a runtime of $O(n^2/\varepsilon^4\cdot\log^{3/2}(1/\delta))$ when $\varepsilon\le 1$, where $\delta\in[0,1]$ is an approximation parameter. When $\varepsilon>1$, our algorithm runs in time $O(mn\log(nm)/(\varepsilon-\log n)^2)$. This improves over the previous best known bound of $O(mn^2/(n^2-\varepsilon^2) + n^6/(\varepsilon^7\delta))$ due to Delling et al.~\cite{DVW:SODA20}.

A good evaluation framework should evaluate multimodal machine translation (MMT) models by measuring their performance for the full range of tasks they are capable of, while also providing fine-grained insights into model strengths and weaknesses. In this work, we present a comprehensive dataset and evaluation framework for MMT evaluation that addresses these needs. We introduce MultiModEval, which provides human judgments in 14 languages with two levels of granularity: translations and translation components (e.g., segments). The data is derived from the WMT20-Multimodal shared task, in particular from the English-German and Japanese-English subtasks, and the English-Spanish subtask for translation components.

We propose a new framework for robust optimization that computes robust solutions directly from data. Robust optimization is the task of finding solutions that are immune to unknown perturbations, and is commonly used in machine learning for risk control. However, existing methods require the perturbations to follow a known distribution, and are thus unsuitable when nothing is known about the perturbations. In this work, we instead make use of conditional distributions, which only require knowledge of the marginal distribution of the solutions. We introduce a new method for robust optimization that learns a conditional robust optimization model from data, and proves generalization guarantees. Our method is a natural fit for modern machine learning architectures, and allows for robust solutions that are tailored to the data distribution. We show experimentally that our method outperforms existing techniques on both synthetic and real-world datasets.

We study the problem of estimating the mean of an identity covariance Gaussian in the truncated isometry model. This is a natural extension to the non-truncated isometry model studied by Ding, Liu, and Sun (2017) for the same purpose, but with the additional restriction that only a subset of the coordinates can be measured, which we refer to as truncation. In particular, when $\ell$ coordinates are not observed, we show that $O(\sqrt{\ell})$ samples suffice to estimate the mean with error $\epsilon$, while $O(\log \ell)$ samples suffice to estimate the mean with constant accuracy. These results improve upon previous work in the literature that requires $O(\ell)$ or $O(\ell^2)$ samples in these settings. Our analysis uses a novel approach that allows us to analyze the mean estimation proble

Recent developments have underscored the critical role of \textit{differential privacy} (DP) in preserving data privacy. Recognizing that DP is an intricate topic, we introduce a novel taxonomy to delineate its various aspects and nuances. To this end, we employ a systematic approach, breaking down DP into five key dimensions: (1) Notion of Privacy, (2) Notion of Data, (3) Method of Calculus, (4) Type of Privacy, and (5) Type of Attacks. This categorization facilitates a deeper understanding of DP's multifaceted nature, thereby enhancing the comprehension and application of its principles across diverse domains. In particular, our taxonomy provides insights into emerging trends in DP research and highlights areas for future exploration.

Can we leverage the knowledge of multiple language models to improve the decoding of a target language model? To answer this question, we introduce a multi-model decoder that collates the knowledge of multiple language models to improve the decoding of a target language model in a black-box setting. Specifically, we design a novel collaborative decoding architecture that does not require the decoder to be trained from scratch. Our model is able to leverage the encoder-decoder framework of existing language models, while learning to decode collaboratively. We validate our approach on multiple language pairs and different model sizes, demonstrating an improvement over strong baselines.

Noise injection is a fundamental technique for differential privacy (DP), but it significantly affects model performance. To mitigate the impact, we propose DPAdapter, a novel pre-training technique that enhances DP model robustness against noise corruption. Specifically, we introduce a noise tolerance pre-training module that injects calibrated noise into the pre-training process. This noise-tolerant pre-training helps the model to mitigate the effects of noise corruption from two aspects: 1) The model parameters are adjusted to have a certain tolerance to noise, which reduces the harm of noise corruption to model performance. 2) The noise-tolerant pre-training enhances the representation learning capacity, thus mitigating the negative effects of noise corruption on downstream tasks. Extensive experiments demonstrate that DPAdapter effectively improves the performance of DP models without any additional post-training techniques.

The explosion in the size and the complexity of the available Knowledge Graphs on the web has made reasoning about them a challenging task, due to their inherent uncertainty. Given this situation, there is an increasing need for tools that can not only query and reason over these knowledge graphs, but also provide the means to learn from them by incorporating the information they contain into other tasks such as language modeling. In this work we present KG-BART2 (Knowledge Graph BERT), a novel approach based on a multi-task learning framework that combines the capabilities of large language models with those of knowledge graphs. Our main contribution is a new pretraining objective that learns from both the knowledge graph and the large language model. By exploiting the potential of knowle

We propose Masked Capsule Autoencoders (MCAE), the first CapsNet variant that leverages the masking mechanism to preserve the identity of data samples. We design a novel loss function to address the problem of identifying which capsules are responsible for producing masked vectors, in contrast to existing methods that only identify the correct capsules but do not determine whether they were identified correctly or incorrectly. Our experiments show that our method outperforms state-of-the-art approaches on three benchmark datasets: MNIST, Kaggle Handwritten Digits, and CIFAR-10. Moreover, it demonstrates superior performance compared to other masking techniques when training with limited data. The code is available at https://github.com/bhubel43/MCAE.git. This work can be found under arXiv:

Score-based diffusion models, while achieving remarkable empirical performance, often suffer from low sampling efficiency due to the need for expensive reverse time integration. In this work we address such inefficiency by introducing a novel formulation of score functions based on Riemannian geometry that allows us in combination with recent advances (evolving normal flows) and our own techniques (\emph{multivariate} normalizing flow), resulting into an efficient generative model called \textsc{\bf RIFT}.

Astronomy is present in the Colombian scientific production, although its development is not uniform across the country. In this work, we perform a bibliometric analysis of Colombian astronomy publications in the last two decades using data from the Web of Science database. We find a growth in the number of publications, with an acceleration in the last five years. We also see a strong growth in the number of authors and an increase in the collaboration both within the country and abroad. We analyze the productivity of Colombian universities and research centers, as well as the evolution of research topics over time. We find that most of the research is concentrated in a few institutions, which host the majority of authors. The most productive research centers are the Observatorio Astron\'omico de Colombia (OAC) and the Departamento de F\'isica of the Universidad Nacional de Colombia (UNAL). Regarding the research output, we find a strong growth of papers in planetary science and astrophysics, mainly driven by OAC. UNAL, on the other hand, has maintained a steady production of publications on variable stars and solar physics. We also analyze the citations of Colombian publications and identify the most cited papers. Finally, we present the results of a survey on the state of astronomy in Colombia, conducted to senior researchers in the field. The survey results confirm the bibliometric trends observed and provide additional information on the challenges and opportunities for the development of astronomy in the country.

Dynamic graphs, featuring continuously updated vertices and edges, have grown in importance for numerous real-world applications. However, the limited expressive power of existing graph neural networks (GNNs) has impeded their ability to handle dynamic graphs effectively. To address this challenge, we introduce Graph-Capsules-Transformer (GCT), a novel model that integrates capsule theory and self-attention to achieve powerful representation learning capabilities. GCT's capsule layer enhances the network's ability to learn local graph features, while its transformer layer captures global structure information. Notably, GCT employs a dynamic routing algorithm, which enables it to adaptively aggregate node features from various capsules, further improving the accuracy of local feature repres

In this study, the impact of turbulent diffusion on mixing of biochemical reaction models is investigated for a range of model parameters. The effect of the initial conditions in both reactant and solvent is examined using the CSTR (continuous stirred tank reactor) as a test case. The results are compared with the laminar counterpart and the effect of Reynolds number on mixing is highlighted. By applying a scaling approach for the turbulent dispersion term, the role of the molecular diffusivity in the reactive transport equation can be accounted for. It is shown that the relative importance of diffusion increases at high values of Péclet numbers. Furthermore, the influence of the initial condition on the average concentration level is found to diminish as the reaction rate constant increas

Real-time processing is crucial in autonomous driving systems due to the imperative of instantaneous decision making. In this paper, we present a lightweight multitask framework for endtoend real time object detection and tracking system with improved accuracy via using an efficient convolutional neural network (CNN) architecture as backbone model on different hardware platforms: Raspberry Pi 4 Model B equipped by NVIDIA Jetson Nano board; Intel OpenVINO toolkit running onboard CPU(Intel Core i7); Google Coral Edge TPU accelerator module connected through USB port interface or directly soldered onto PCB baseboard along side processor unit .

Context: Edge graphs are graphs whose edges are labelled with identifiers, and nodes can have multiple labels. These types of data structures appear in many practical applications such as social networks or the internet graph where each link is associated to an identifier (e. g., URL) which identifies a particular web page/user account on that connection [1]. However existing approaches for solving various algorithms over edge-labelled datasets either rely heavily upon preprocessing techniques leading into increased complexity; thus rendering them impractical when large amounts if information must be handled quickly by computers without sacrificing accuracy levels too much - leaving us stuck at square one again!. Supervised fine-tuning (SFT), supervised instruction tuning (SIT) and prompt learning have been shown to be effective in transferring knowledge from pretrained language models for downstream tasks. However, these methods rely on external supervision signals or additional training data which can not always readily available during deployment time of the model as an API service due to privacy concerns that may arise when collecting such information at scale across different domains/users etc., making it difficult if one wants only use unlabeled examples directly while still retaining performance gains provided by traditional SML approaches like self -supervised pretraining followed up with finetuning afterwards without requiring any extra labels beyond what was used initially(pretraining). This study marks a significant advancement by harnessing Large Language Models (LLMs) to improve the comprehension capabilities of code search engines. While leveraging LLMs as an auxiliary, we propose a novel approach that effectively utilizes their strength in textual processing and logical reasoning to enhance code search accuracy. Our solution involves a two-stage process wherein LLMs are initially used to generate relevant queries based on the search query and then subsequently deployed for enhanced code search results. To validate our model, we conducted extensive experiments using three representative code search datasets. These involved both supervised learning and fine-tuning approaches, demonstrating superior performance when compared with state-of-the-art baselines. Additionally

Thermal variations pose a paramount challenge to the reliability and performance of photonic tensor accelerators. These variations, arising from both spatially-static and dynamically-drifting hotspots, deteriorate the optical properties of the waveguide network and lead to faulty computations. Existing thermal compensation techniques are static in nature and, as such, are ineffective against spatially-dynamic thermal drifts. To this end, we propose a dynamic on-chip remediation scheme, dubbed DOCTOR, which proactively mitigates thermal drifts and maintains the computational integrity of tensor accelerators. DOCTOR employs a suite of sophisticated models that encompass the dynamic thermal drifts and uses gradient-based algorithms to compute the optimal remediation actions. We demonstrate that DOCTOR effectively compensates for thermal drifts with a high accuracy, thereby ensuring the reliability and performance of photonic tensor accelerators. Furthermore, we show that DOCTOR is computationally efficient and can be readily integrated with existing chip-scale architectures.

Recent years have witnessed a plethora of learning-based solutions for congestion control in computer networks. These algorithms, however are usually designed from scratch and do not take into account the underlying dynamics which govern network traffic (e.\,\@i., fluid flow or discrete event systems). This is problematic as such knowledge can often be leveraged to obtain better performance guarantees via more robust design methods like Lyapunov stability theory\,. In this work we attempt at bridging that gap by introducing \emph{fluid model assisted adaptive rate selection} (\FLAME), an algorithm based on recent results obtained using stochastic continuous time $\ell_1$ optimization techniques\,, coupled with insights regarding their applicability towards understanding packet loss phenome

The efficacy of large language models (LLMs) in understanding and generating natural language has arced upwards rapidly over the last few years, but it is yet to reach a level where LLMS can be considered truly human-like. While there are many reasons for this lackluster performance with respect to humans on certain tasks such as compositional generalization or commonsense reasoning that stem from fundamental problems inherent within these systems [1], one important reason stems simply due them being trained solely based upon text alone without any external sources like images/videos etc., which makes learning concepts more difficult than if we were also exposed those other modalities simultaneously during training time too! To tackle some aspects mentioned above by incorporating multimod

We study the problem of automatic theorem proving in the separation logic (SL) with pointer arithmetic and inductive definitions, a fragment of SL that can still express various pointer-based data structures. The main reason why this problem is challenging is that the standard definition of SL entails an infinite set of axioms, one for each formula. To overcome this issue, we propose a new approach based on a sequent calculus that uses as premises only a finite set of axioms, namely the axioms of a theory of non-linear arithmetic. Moreover, we introduce new proof rules to deal with the inductive definitions and the pointer arithmetic. With respect to the classical SL provability problem, our new approach entails the decidability of SL over a large and expressive fragment, that can still capture the essence of pointer-based data structures.

Recently, diffusion-based methods for monocular 3D human pose estimation have achieved state of the art performance. However such approaches suffer from long inference times and large model sizes due to heavy reliance on high resolution inputs (evolving across multiple spatial scales). In this paper we propose an efficient alternative: a novel multiscale transformer architecture which enables fast generation at low resolutions with minimal loss in accuracy compared to existing solutions that rely only upon higher fidelity data or more expensive processing steps during training/inference stages alike; thus enabling real world applications like gesture recognition among others without sacrificing efficiency nor compromising quality altogether! Our results demonstrate clear gains over competi

Participating in natural language processing (NLP) competitions is a common practice for evaluating and benchmarking newly developed methods against state-of-the-art approaches. However, the specific procedures and metrics used for evaluating the performance of competing systems are not always clearly defined. In addition, the characteristics of the datasets used for training and testing the systems may not be fully described. Assessing the real performance of NLP systems and understanding the results of competitions require a clear definition of the systems' capabilities and the environments in which they were tested. To this end, we propose a methodology for analyzing and interpreting the results of NLP competitions. Our approach aims to clarify the description of the tasks, the systems' performance, and the datasets used in such competitions. We demonstrate the application of our methodology in two case studies: the CoNLL 2014 and 2015 shared tasks on dependency parsing.

We present ZSL-RPPO, an improved zero-shot learning architecture that overcomes the limitations of existing approaches. By leveraging self-attention and a novel class-aware positional encoding scheme, we achieve superior performance on several benchmarks. Additionally, we introduce a novel technique to refine representations via multi-scale pooling, further enhancing our model's accuracy. Finally, we incorporate efficient dropout strategies into our architecture, resulting in significant memory savings without compromising accuracy. Our model outperforms other state-of-the-art ZSL architectures by 1.2% or more on five datasets with up to 10K classes. Code is available at: https://github.com/mzhang73/ZSL-RPPO . Project page: https://zsl-rppo.github.io/.

Advanced machine learning algorithms are increasingly utilized to provide data-based prediction and decision-making support. However, the complexity of such systems hinders their deployment in real-world scenarios. To address this challenge, we propose a framework for interpreting machine learning models through a novel perspective: that of a human observer. Our approach involves transforming the model into an interactive visualization tool, allowing users to explore its behavior by actively manipulating input variables. This method is based on two fundamental principles: (1) the representation of all possible inputs as a point cloud; and (2) the use of gradient-based optimization techniques to trace the path from any starting point to the desired goal state. Through empirical evaluation,

We study the computational complexity of several polynomial-time-solvable graph problems parameterized by vertex integrity, a measure of a graph's vulnerability to vertex removal in terms of connectivity. Vertex integrity is the smallest number $\iota$ such that there is a set $S$ of $\iota' \le \iota$ vertices such that every connected component of $G-S$ contains at most $\iota-\iota'$ vertices. It is known that the vertex integrity lies between the well-studied parameters vertex cover number and tree-depth. Alon and Yuster [ESA 2007] designed algorithms for graphs with small vertex cover number using fast matrix multiplications.

Large Language Models (LLMs) such as GPT-4 have recently demonstrated impressive results across a wide range of tasks. Unfortunately, they are prone to hallucinate facts, i.e., generate false statements that appear plausible. fact-checking models have been proposed to address this problem, but they are computationally expensive as they require evaluating the likelihood of alternative hypotheses. In this work, we propose a novel framework for fact-checking the output of LLMs using only token-level probabilities, which can be obtained from any standard LLM implementation. Our method, called CertLM, constructs a uncertainty score for each token in the LLM's response and uses these scores to estimate the overall uncertainty of the generated text. We evaluate CertLM on a dataset of math word problems and find that it outperforms existing fact-checking models while being more than 100 times faster to evaluate.

Binary decision diagrams (BDDs) are a well-studied representation that can compactly represent a boolean function by ordering the variables and using an efficient graph-based representation. Multiobjective BDDs (MOBDDs) extend BDDs to the multiobjective setting by tracking multiple objective functions during construction and can provide a more holistic view of the trade-off between the objectives. However, existing constructions of MOBDDs can lead to dense diagrams, resulting in poor query time. Therefore, we propose a new method for constructing sparse MOBDDs by leveraging machine learning. We introduce a new intermediate representation, sparse MOBDDs (S-MOBDDs), which are able to directly track the sparsity pattern of the intermediate nodes. This allows us to propose a novel learning-based method to construct S-MOBDDs by predicting the non-zero variables of the next node. We can then convert an S-MOBDD into a compacted MOBDD (C-MOBDD) by eliminating the zero variables of each node. Through an extensive set of experiments, we show that our method outperforms existing methods, providing sparse MOBDDs that better track the sparsity of the intermediate nodes, resulting in more compact and efficient diagrams.

Advanced language-processing and machine-learning techniques promise massive efficiency improvements in the previously widely manual field of semantic parsing. However, even these techniques require significant human effort for their initial training. We present a fully automated approach to semantic parsing that combines advanced language models with large-scale multi-lingual knowledge bases, allowing for completely unsupervised learning. Our results show that our model is able to perform on par with state-of-the-art supervised methods on three datasets: CELF2018, MENLO and NLG. For example, on the 7k-sentence MENLO dataset, our model achieved an F-score of 95.3% while requiring just 4 hours of training time (compared to over 3 weeks required by the best supervised method).

The ever-increasing sensor service, though opening a precious path and providing a deluge of data for the Internet of Things (IoT), is also confronted with challenges in terms of privacy. With the proliferation of IoT devices, the privacy issue in IoT has become more severe. In this paper, we propose an innovative approach named DDPS to address the privacy protection problem in IoT. We first introduce a novel distributed privacy-preserving model to solve the problem of privacy leakage during the process of data collection and transmission. Then, we present an efficient privacy preservation mechanism based on differential privacy. To verify the effectiveness of our solution, we conduct extensive experiments in various scenarios and compare it with state-of-the-art methods. Experimental resu

The Software-Defined Networks (SDNs) face persistent threats from various adversaries, including malicious insiders and external attackers. Traditional methods of network intrusion detection are based on signature-based anomaly detection or machine learning. However, they fail to identify novel attacks or anomalies in a timely manner, resulting in data breaches. Consequently, there is a pressing need for a robust solution that can proactively detect new network anomalies in real-time. This paper presents an innovative approach for detecting novel network anomalies in SDN environments using federated learning. The proposed method utilizes an autoencoder architecture to analyze the traffic patterns within the network, followed by a federated learning process to ensure privacy protection. To

Event causality identification (ECI) is the task of inferring whether an event $e_2$ in a target document $D_{target}$ is caused by another event $e_1$ in a source document $D_{source}$. Previous works mainly focus on the ECI task in a monolingual setting. However, with the increasing need of analyzing large-scale cross-lingual information, there is a pressing need to develop a zero-shot cross-lingual ECI model. In this paper, we propose a novel Heterogeneous Graph Contrastive Transfer Learning (HGCT) model to identify cross-lingual event causality. Specifically, we construct a heterogeneous graph structure to integrate cross-lingual event information and use contrastive learning to align the representations of causally related events. Furthermore, we introduce a self-supervised prediction mechanism to further boost the performance. Experimental results on two benchmark datasets for cross-lingual event causality identification demonstrate that our model outperforms previous state-of-the-art methods.

Sleep disorders are a significant public health issue associated with substantial economic costs and an enormous burden on healthcare systems. Various non-pharmacological interventions, including cognitive-behavioral therapy, are recommended as first-line treatments for sleep disorders. Social robots are novel tools that can support the delivery of these non-pharmacological interventions for sleep disorders. The aim of this scoping review is to explore the literature on social robots for sleep health and provide an overview of the current state of research in this field. We searched for studies on social robots for sleep health from inception to May 2021 in several electronic databases. We included studies that (1) involved a social robot as the intervention; (2) aimed to improve sleep duration, sleep quality, or both; and (3) included participants with sleep disorders or good sleepers. Two reviewers independently extracted data on study characteristics, robot characteristics, study design, participants, interventions, and outcomes. We included 38 studies in this review, which involved 18 different robots. Most studies (n = 30) were conducted with participants having sleep disorders, and most participants were adults (n = 35). Studies used sleep diaries (n = 34), actigraphy (n = 27), and polysomnography (n = 17) to measure sleep. The most common robot characteristics were human-like appearance (n = 34), followed by human-like behavior (n = 25). Most robots were interactive (n = 30), and verbal communication was the most common form of interaction (n = 27). Studies reported various theoretical frameworks and metrics to evaluate the effectiveness of social robots for sleep health. This review provides an overview of the current state of research on social robots for sleep health. Although there is growing interest in this area, more rigorous research is needed to determine the effectiveness of social robots for improving sleep health.

Continual Relation Extraction (CRE) aims to extract relation triples from sentences without relation labels during inference, which has achieved considerable attention. However, the performance drops off quickly when the model is faced with out-of-domain (OOD) texts during inference. This phenomenon is due to the fact that the conventional CRE models are trained and tested on the same dataset, i.e., they are not robust to OOD samples. Also, the existing robustness techniques in CRE are insufficient to handle the massive appearance of OOD samples in continual learning. To address the issue, we propose a novel decoupled contrastive learning and memory structure preservation approach, namely DP-CRE, for continual relation extraction. Specifically, we introduce a decoupled contrastive learning module to pull in-domain representations and push out-of-domain (OOD) representations away from each other. Furthermore, a memory structure preservation module is proposed to maintain the balance between the updates of the model and the memory. It prevents the model from being overwhelmed by OOD samples and enables the model to focus on extracting relations from OOD sentences. Experimental results on the benchmark dataset FewRel demonstrate that DP-CRE outperforms the state-of-the-art methods by a significant margin. The source code will be publicly released at https://github.com/2000ZRL/LC-Transformer.

In this paper, we introduce an entity-based answer rewriting approach in conversational search that aims to leverage question-answering techniques for generating self-contained answers. We propose a novel constrained decoding technique that forces the system to mention an entity if it is present in the context or the user has shown interest in it. We evaluate our approach on historical conversation data from Wikipedia and find that it helps in generating more relevant answers. Our experimental results also suggest that entity-based rewriting can help in disambiguating user intent and improving the coverage of conversational search.

The Software-Defined Networks (SDNs) face persistent threats from various adversaries, such as compromised switches and routers. In this paper, we focus on the trustworthiness of SDN applications, which play a critical role in protecting the network against malicious attacks. Traditional application verification techniques are challenged by two obstacles: 1) the intricate interplay between an SDN application's physical and logical components, and 2) the lack of formal methods to ensure the safety properties of SDN applications. To address these issues, we propose a novel verification framework for SDN applications based on the formal method Probabilistic Finite State Machine (PFSM). We demonstrate our approach using an example scenario, where a malicious attacker tries to disable the routi

Text data has become extremely valuable due to the emergence of machine learning algorithms that learn from it. However, most text is not well-structured and can be difficult for humans or machines alike (e..g., noisy).
