The goal in this thesis was therefore twofold: 1) provide a method which automatically enhances unstructured textual information by extracting relevant sentences; then using these enhanced texts as input into ML models with improved performance compared against baseline versions without any preprocessing steps taken beforehand during training time - leading ultimately towards more accurate predictions across various domains including healthcare/medicine research areas such s genomics where accuracy increases significantly when applying deep neural networks trained on large

This paper presents a novel application of explainable AI (XAI) for root-causing failure in complex systems. We introduce an explainability approach that integrates the systematic process of fault tree analysis with XAI techniques to identify the root cause of a system's failure and provide recommendations for preventing it from recurring. Our methodology first involves analyzing the system through fault tree analysis, which identifies critical paths and potential failure modes. The resulting model is then used as input to XAI models such as Bayesian networks and Differential Privacy Bayes Nets (DPBN), enabling the identification of causal relationships between nodes within the system. To further enhance the accuracy of our approach, we incorporate a self-adaptive strategy based on a dynam

In this paper we address the task of summarizing television shows, which touches key areas in A.I., namely multimodality and dialogue summarization. We first introduce a novel dataset, consisting of 250K TV show dialogues spanning across 67 different genres, collected from popular streaming platforms such as Hulu, Prime Video and Netflix. Our dataset is based on the idea of "summary-of-dialogue", i.e. that each episode can be effectively represented by its most salient parts (its summary). The dataset offers two levels of annotation: at the episode level, a textual summary for each show is provided, while at the scene level, a set of salient utterances are highlighted. To demonstrate the utility of our dataset, we propose several models for generating summaries, ranging from classical sequ

Abstract reasoning problems pose significant challenges to artificial intelligence algorithms, demanding cognitive capabilities beyond those required for basic symbolic manipulation. Recent work has shown that LLMs trained on a variety of text-based datasets exhibit impressive performance in solving abstract reasoning tasks, including the Mental Ability Test (MAT), a high-stakes admissions exam used by several top universities worldwide. In this paper, we analyze the role of dataset and training procedure variations in shaping the reasoning abilities of large language models (LLMs). We present a comprehensive evaluation framework, integrating a suite of benchmarks designed to evaluate abstract reasoning proficiency across different levels of complexity. Our results reveal that model traini

Recent advances in large language models (LLMs) demonstrate that their capabilities are comparable to those of humans for solving complex mathematical problems. In this work, we investigate whether these LLM-based agents can also be used as tools to support the mathematical problem-solving process of human learners. We design and conduct a series of experiments with students from 8th grade and beyond, comparing different LLM configurations against each other and against a control group. Our results show that LLM-based agents can effectively support student learning and are more effective than the control group in certain scenarios. However, we also found that LLM performance is highly dependent on the specific tasks, and there are some cases where LLM-based agents fail to provide sufficien

Recently, there has been an increasing focus on audio-text cross-modal learning. However, most existing works have focused mainly in the domain of speech recognition and dialogue generation or summarization tasks with limited success for other text understanding applications such as machine translation (MT). In this work we present a novel end to start data augmentation framework that enables effective training using large scale unlabeled datasets by augmenting them into labeled sets suitable from various downstream NLP problems including MT evaluation metrics like BLEU score improvement over baseline models trained without any additional supervision through supervised fine tuning only based off generated augmented samples produced via our proposed method which significantly improves perfo

In this letter, we present a novel bi-modal bi-copter robot called Skater, which consists of two independent rotorcrafts and a skateboard. The primary goal of Skater is to move through confined spaces without damaging the environment using the skateboard. By combining the advantages of both aircrafts (e.g., maneuverability, agility) with those of the skateboard (e.g., balance, speed), Skater can perform obstacle avoidance and rough terrain traversal in a more efficient manner than conventional quadrotors. To achieve this objective, the robot has been designed to meet specific requirements for small size and light weight, thereby allowing it to navigate tight corridors while remaining portable. We demonstrate that Skater can successfully traverse challenging environments such as narrow gaps

Reference-based phishing detection systems have seen significant improvements in recent years. However, they still suffer from low accuracy and are unable to keep up with the constantly evolving tactics used by phishing attackers. In this paper, we propose a novel approach to enhance reference-based phishing detection by integrating large language models (LLMs) and multimodal knowledge graphs (MKGs). We take advantage of the question-answering capabilities of LLMs to create a query vector, which, along with the multimodal evidence, is used to enhance the reference resolution process. This integration of LLMs and MKGs enables us to effectively identify potential phishing attacks and provides additional context to verify the authenticity of the referenced entities. We evaluate our proposal within a multi-modal setting, where the vectorized output of the LLM is combined with entity embeddings obtained from an MKG. We use a publicly available dataset that contains real-world phishing URLs, as well as synthetic data generated by GPT-3. We compare the performance of our approach against state-of-the-art baselines and show that our method achieves significant improvements in terms of detection rate and F-score.

In the realm of computer security, the importance of efficient and reliable user authentication methods has become increasingly critical. This paper examines the potential of mouse movement dynamics as a consistent metric for continuous authentication. By analyzing user mouse movement patterns in two contrasting gaming scenarios, "Team Fortress" and Poly Bridge we investigate the distinctive behavioral patterns inherent in high-intensity and low-intensity UI interactions. The study extends beyond conventional methodologies by employing a range of machine learning models. These models are carefully selected to assess their effectiveness in capturing and interpreting the subtleties of user behavior as reflected in their mouse movements. This multifaceted approach allows for a more nuanced an

Time Series Generation (TSG) has emerged as a pivotal technique in synthesizing new time-stamped data, encompassing both structured and unstructured domains. In recent years, advancements in Large Language Models (LLMs), such as GPT-4, have revolutionized the field of TSG by introducing the concept of textual prompts to facilitate this process. However, the majority of existing LLM-based methods employ predefined templates for TSG, which poses significant challenges when generating diverse and realistic sequences. To address these shortcomings, we introduce TimeCapsule, an innovative approach that leverages the powerful capabilities of LLMs to generate new time series without using any predetermined templates or templates from external datasets.

Current approaches to question answering rely on pre-trained language models (PLMs) like RoBERTa and BART, which are trained on massive amounts of text data but do not incorporate commonsense knowledge. In this paper, we propose a new approach for question answering that integrates PLMs with commonsense reasoning using an encoder-decoder architecture. Our approach incorporates a novel method for generating the decoder's inputs from PLM outputs, which ensures that the generated inputs accurately reflect the context in the original input. We evaluate our approach on the MCTest dataset and show that it outperforms previous approaches based on PLMs alone. Additionally, we demonstrate that our model is robust across different prompting strategies and that it is effective even when using PLMs tr

Few-Shot Class-Incremental Learning (FSCIL) has gained considerable attention due to its ability to accommodate new classes with a limited number of labeled samples. However, existing methods often encounter challenges in retaining knowledge from previous tasks while adapting to novel ones, which can result in degraded performance on both old and new tasks. To address this issue, we propose a novel strategy called Task-Consistent Knowledge Consolidation (TCKC), which incorporates task-consistency into the consolidation process, ensuring that the learned knowledge remains consistent across tasks. In addition, our approach also employs a novel mechanism for knowledge retrieval, which enables efficient utilization of previously acquired knowledge. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our me

The conjunction of edge intelligence and the ever-growing Internet-of-Things (IoT) creates an unprecedented opportunity for improving the quality of life in our society. In this paper, we introduce a novel framework called IoT-EdgeNet, which leverages deep learning techniques to enhance the efficiency of communication in the IoT ecosystem. Our approach employs a neural network architecture that is specifically designed to optimize data delivery within connected devices, enabling the transmission of essential information while minimizing resource consumption. By integrating intelligent decision-making capabilities into the communication process, IoT-EdgeNet ensures efficient utilization of resources such as energy and bandwidth. We conducted extensive experiments using real-world datasets t

Predictive-based neural architecture search (PBNAS) uses a search space of neural networks and aims to find the network architecture that minimizes the prediction error on a validation set. The search space is explored using a neural network called the controller, which predicts the weights of the candidate networks in the search space. The controller is trained using a loss function that minimizes the prediction error on the validation set. However, the controller tends to overfit to the validation set, resulting in poor generalization of the PBNAS method. In this paper, we propose encodings for PBNAS that allow the controller to focus on the task-irrelevant properties of the weights, such as their magnitude and sparsity, rather than their task-relevant properties, such as the feature maps they produce. We evaluate our method on image classification and super-resolution tasks and show that it improves the generalization of PBNAS. Our method is an important step towards finding a scalable and general solution for neural architecture search.

Boolean Satisfiability problems are vital components in Electronic Design Automation, particularly within the Logics Synthesis and Verification domains. The Boolean satisifiablity problem is given a boolean function f which takes zero or more variables as inputs that each can be true (1) of false(0), determine if there exists an input vector such so f returns TRUE for all its outputs? In other words: Can we find values to assign these binary truth-values where this formula evaluates True at every point?. If no solution exist then it’s called unsatisfiable; otherwise – satsifiable! This may seem like just another puzzle but has been proven NP complete by Cook&Levin .

Topic modelling is a popular unsupervised learning approach to reveal the latent topics in a collection of documents. Traditional approaches rely on the distributional properties of words, whereas recent approaches use pre-trained language models to capture semantic properties. However, they ignore the topic-specific usage of words and the dependency between words within a topic. In this paper, we propose a probabilistic topic modelling method that incorporates transformer representations into the topic modelling process. We introduce topic-conditioned masking tokens to break the dependency between words within a topic while preserving the semantic properties. Our experiments show that our approach outperforms the state-of-the-art topic modelling methods in terms of topic coherence and coverage.

Autonomous off-road navigation requires an accurate semantic understanding of the environment, often converted into a topological map. In this paper we describe an end-to-end framework for learning to generate topological maps from raw depth data. To do so, we introduce two novel concepts: (i) the use of point cloud augmentation to learn invariance to object scale and viewpoint and (ii) the use of graph convolutional networks to process point clouds. Our approach is based on a novel loss function that minimizes the distance between predicted and ground truth topologies by minimizing the number of edge deletions required to transform one into the other. We demonstrate our results on three different datasets collected with different sensors and show that our model can successfully predict th

The Transformer has achieved impressive success in natural language processing tasks, but its performance is limited for visual recognition tasks. This paper proposes a new Transformer variant for visual recognition tasks, namely Network in Network Transformer (NiNformer). Instead of using the standard multi-head self-attention (MSA) module, we propose a token mixing generated gating function (TMG) to select the tokens that need to be attended to in the current token's local neighborhood. The TMG function is generated by swish(token * token), which introduces non-linear activation and sparsity to the attention operation. To prove the superiority of our TMG function, we show that it can be interpreted as a sparse approximation of the MSA module. Experimental results show that NiNformer outperforms other visual Transformer models under the same parameter budget. We also pre-train our model using the masked image modeling (MIM) method and show that the pre-training scheme has a positive effect on the performance of our model. The source code is available at https://github.com/cunyu/NiNformer.

Federated learning (FL) is a new learning paradigm that decouples the training process from data collection and enables collaborators to jointly train a global model without sharing local data. Existing FL methods mainly focus on homogeneous settings with identical devices and simple topologies. However, in real-world applications, device heterogeneity in FL cannot be ignored, which is mainly due to the large variance in data distribution across devices. In this paper, we study the heterogeneous FL problem and aim to find an optimal customized architecture for FL. To this end, we propose a contrastive cloud-edge model decoupling (C2MD) framework, which performs FL on heterogeneous datasets with a two-level training strategy. Specifically, the global model is deployed on the cloud side and the local model is deployed on the edge side. The two models are decoupled with different parameters and independently updated by local and global updates. To prove the effectiveness of our proposed C2MD framework, we provide a theoretical analysis by using the convex-concave saddle point problem formulation. In particular, we propose an asynchronous variance reduced proximal (VRP) algorithm to solve the heterogeneous FL problem. The convergence of the VRP algorithm is analyzed with the condition number of the problem. Based on the theoretical analysis, we conduct experiments on the MNIST dataset and the Stackoverflow dataset. The experimental results show that our proposed C2MD framework achieves better performance than the conventional FL method in the heterogeneous setting. In addition, we discuss the privacy issue of FL and provide some potential directions to mitigate the privacy concern.

Pointer arithmetic is widely used in low-level programs, e.g. memory allocators. The specification of such programs usually requires using pointer arithmetic inside inductive definitions to define the common data structures, e.g. heap lists in memory allocators. In this work, we investigate decision problems for SLAH, a separation logic fragment that allows pointer arithmetic inside inductive definitions, thus enabling specification of properties for programs manipulating heap lists. Pointer arithmetic inside inductive definitions is challenging for automated reasoning. We tackle this challenge and achieve decision procedures for both satisfiability and entailment of SLAH formulas. The crux of our decision procedure for satisfiability is to compute summaries of inductive definitions. We sh

In this paper, we introduce a novel benchmark, dubbed VastTrack, towards facilitating the advancement of video-based object tracking. We encompass 10 challenging datasets covering 5 diverse categories: pedestrian, car, bicycle, motorcycle and person-with-object. The videos are collected from various scenarios, including city streets, highways and parking lots. To provide comprehensive assessment, we design a total of 24 trackers with different backbones, ranging from small to large scale models. Moreover, we propose two new evaluation metrics, namely Precision@k and F1@k, which can capture more subtle tracking behaviours in real world scenarios. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our proposed methods generally achieve superior performance compared to state-of-the-art algorithms on all

This paper presents an extensive empirical study on the integration of dimensionality reduction techniques with advanced unsuper- vised clustering algorithms to improve their performance. We investigate two major approaches: (i) preselection, where a subset is extracted from original data and then clustered; and(ii), postprocessing after which clusters are further merged by using selected features in order to achieve higher quality results compared against those obtained only via standard unsupervised clusterers without any preprocessing step or feature selection method applied beforehand .

Promptly discovering unknown network attacks is critical for reducing the risk of major loss imposed on enterprises and individuals. An effective approach to discovering such attacks is monitoring the behavior of all components in the network, including the traffic passing through it. While most of today's large-scale networks are monitored using passive sensors, active sensors are typically deployed only as a last resort due to their cost and complexity. Nevertheless, active sensors can enable monitoring at a much higher granularity than passive sensors, and thus offer more opportunities for attack detection. In this paper, we propose a new anomaly detection technique that combines knowledge from both types of sensor. Our method is based on an ensemble of classifiers, where each classifie

In recent years, large language models (LLMs) have become increasingly prevalent, with OpenAI's ChatGPT and Google's Bard being two examples of this technology. These models are characterized by their ability to generate natural-language responses to user queries. However, the rapid growth in LLM usage has raised concerns about its impact on society. This essay explores the positive and negative aspects of using LLMs in society and discusses how these concerns can be addressed. The use of LLMs for tasks such as automated writing and translation has the potential to improve efficiency and reduce errors. Additionally, they offer a more human-like interaction than other chatbots, making them more appealing to users. However, there are also significant drawbacks to using LLMs. For instance, th

This paper presents a novel multi-modal Multi-Object Tracking (MOT) algorithm for self-driving cars that combines camera and LiDAR data. Camera frames are processed with a state-of-the-art 3D object detector, whereas classical clustering techniques are used to process LiDAR observations. The proposed MOT algorithm comprises a three-step association process, an Extended Kalman filter for estimating the motion of each detected dynamic obstacle, and a track management phase. The EKF motion model requires the current measured relative position and orientation of the observed object and the longitudinal and angular velocities of the ego vehicle as inputs. Unlike most state-of-the-art multi-modal MOT approaches, the proposed algorithm does not rely on maps or knowledge of the ego global pose.

Handover (HO) is a key technology for providing continuous service to 5G users in a cost-efficient manner. A novel feature of 5G systems is the ability to perform conditional HO, where the target cell makes a decision on whether or not to accept the HO based on its load status. In this paper, we develop a novel mathematical framework to evaluate the performance of conditional HO in 5G systems under fading conditions. Specifically, we derive an analytical expression for the distribution of the signal-to-interference-plus-noise ratio (SINR) in the target cell after HO, given that the HO is performed. We use this result to obtain the analytical expressions for the average SINR and the call acceptance probability in the target cell. We also derive an expression for the probability that the SINR in the target cell exceeds a certain threshold, which can be used to analyze the quality of service provided to the HO user. We validate the accuracy of our analytical expressions through Monte Carlo simulations.

Recent work has shown that prompt optimization can help improve the zeroth-order (ZO) optimization performance for large language models (LLMs). However, existing approaches train a single prompt for all optimization tasks, which assumes that the LLM's response is uniformly distributed across the task spectrum. This uniformity assumption may not hold in reality, especially when the task distribution is wide and the LLM is prompted with diverse prompts. To address this limitation, we propose Localized Zeroth-Order Optimization (LZO), which selects an appropriate prompt for each task based on its response distribution. We evaluate LZO on a wide range of tasks, using both a single LLM (GPT-4) and a multi-LLM system (GPT-4 + GPT-3.5). Experimental results demonstrate that LZO consistently outperforms existing prompt optimization methods.

A novel hybrid active-passive reconfigurable intelligent surface (RIS) transmitter is presented, where a passive RIS is integrated with an active antenna array to enhance the coverage and reliability of wireless communication systems. In particular, this paper focuses on the design of an optimal phase shift profile for both the RIS and active antenna array at the transmitter to achieve the maximum sum rate of a two-user system, subject to the maximum transmit power constraint. To solve this nonconvex optimization problem, we first transform it into a convex one by using the semidefinite relaxation (SDR) technique. Then, we propose a novel algorithm to obtain a suboptimal solution by leveraging the block coordinate descent method (BCD). Furthermore, a low complexity scheme based on the succ

Recent LiDAR-based 3D Object Detection (3DOD) methods have achieved promising results on public datasets. However, these methods generally lack robustness due to the limited data availability, especially for real-world scenarios with diverse environments and conditions. In this paper, we propose an effective method to improve the robustness of LiDAR-based 3DOD through a self-supervised contrastive learning framework, which is trained by pseudo labels from weak supervision. We develop a novel weak supervision mechanism to generate pseudo labels based on the class-agnostic features extracted from raw LiDAR point clouds. The proposed contrastive learning framework is optimized through two contrastive objectives: Point Contrastive Loss and Bounding Box Contrastive Loss.

Real-time computational speed and a high degree of precision are requirements for computer-assisted intervent ion procedures in surgery. In order to achieve this, the use of parallel processing is inevitable because it provides higher performance on today's single processor computers than sequential computing does at equivalent cost or time [1]. The development of fast image analysis algorithms capable fo performing multiple tasks simultaneously has been one area where advances have occurred over recent years . These multitasked approaches require efficient parallelization techniques that maximize resource utilization while minimizing execution times[2].. One such method i s called "Task Graph Scheduling" (TGS) which describes how different subtasks can be arranged within their correspondi

The rise of generative models has driven significant advancements in recommender systems, leaving unique opportunistic spaces for their application to real-world problems. In this paper, we explore the potential of these models in recommendation tasks by introducing a novel framework called Generative Recommendation Systems (GRS). Our approach relies on a carefully designed objective function that optimizes for both diversity and similarity within generated recommendations. We also present an efficient training algorithm to solve the problem efficiently. Extensive experiments demonstrate the effectiveness of our method across various datasets and metrics, such as top-k recall. Moreover, our method achieves the state-of-the-art performance on large-scale datasets like Yelp and Amazon datase

We introduce Mars 2.0 for modeling, analysis, verification and code generation of Cyber-Physical Systems (CPS). In contrast to prior approaches in the literature, Mars 2.0 is a domain-independent formalism that encapsulates the essential features of CPS such as discrete time, continuous dynamics, hybrid systems, finite state machines, synchronous and asynchronous communication and distributed components. As a result, it can be applied to a wide range of applications including automotive, avionics, robotics, IoT, etc. Mars 2.0 also offers enhanced functionalities compared with other formal methods, e.g., by integrating timed automata into its specification language. This paper presents the underlying framework, which is based on a typed lambda calculus featuring untyped lambda terms for des

We investigate the empirical counterpart of group distributionally robust optimization (GDRO), which arose as an extension of the classical distributionally robust optimization to settings with group-based fairness constraints. In GDRO, the decision maker aims at optimizing the worst-case expected value over all distributions that satisfy a given group constraint on their marginal probabilities. Our main contribution is the introduction of a novel class of distributions for GDRO: group-constrained stochastic processes (GCSPs). In contrast to existing approaches, GCSPs allow for modeling dependencies between groups by means of a joint probability density function, while still enforcing the required group-level fairness constraints. Building upon this theoretical framework, we provide a new

Despite advancements in text-to-image generation (T2I), prior methods often face challenges when generating complex objects such as animals. Recent works have shown that prompt tuning can improve the performance of models, particularly with pre-trained language models (PLMs). However, these methods require either a large amount of training data or expensive computational resources to tune the model during inference. In this paper, we present the first attempt to integrate prompt tuning and self-training within an image generation framework. Our method is called Prompted Self-Training (PST). It allows the user to fine-tune the PLM's parameters without requiring additional training samples. We evaluate our approach on two challenging tasks: generating animal images from written descriptions

Unsupervised learning-based point cloud anomaly detection is a challenging task due to the presence of similar local structures in both normal and anomalous regions. Moreover, it is computationally expensive to compare large numbers of point clouds in an exhaustive manner. To overcome these problems, we propose an efficient unsupervised point cloud anomaly detector, PointCore. The proposed PointCore is based on the statistical difference between the local and global structures of a point cloud. Specifically, the local structure is represented by a deep feature, which is sensitive to local geometric changes, and the global structure is represented by a shallow feature, which is sensitive to the overall shape of the point cloud. We show that the statistical difference between the local-global features is effective in capturing both local and global anomalies. Furthermore, we propose a novel and efficient sampling method, called multi-scale voxel sampling, which enables our model to focus on both local and global structures without explicitly computing the global view for each point. To train our model, we use a self-supervised learning-based training scheme that does not require pairing normal and anomalous point clouds. To evaluate our method, we conduct extensive experiments using several benchmark datasets. We show that our method outperforms the state-of-the-art unsupervised learning-based methods in terms of both detection accuracy and computational efficiency.

Self-supervised contrastive learning strategy has attracted remarkable attention due to its exceptional ability in image representation, however the limited sample size and weak discriminative power of negative samples still pose challenges for further improvement. In this paper we propose a novel self supervised method named Multiple Image Contrastivity Augmented Learning (MICAL), which can effectively augment positive examples via multi view generation while suppress non informative features using multiple projection layers with different degrees as well enhance discrimmination capability by introducing hard negatives into training data set through negative sampling mechanism during pretraining process . Extensive experiments on standard benchmark datasets demonstrate that our p

Robot-assisted feeding can greatly enhance the lives of those with mobility limitations. The task is challenging due to its complex nature and high variation in human motion. To address this, we propose a novel approach that leverages deep learning for robot control, incorporating both visual and force data. Our method involves an offline training process with a large dataset comprising human feeding motions and associated forces recorded from our customized robotic system. This dataset serves as a reference for training the neural network controller, enabling it to emulate the human arm's natural feeding movements while ensuring proper interaction with food items. During online operation, the visual and force information collected by the robot is fed into the trained network, which then g

PCNet is a deep neural network architecture that excels in predicting continuous human pose from monocular RGB images. The network employs a unique triplet loss to learn a joint embedding space for pose, gender, and face, which are subsequently used to predict 3D face meshes. However, PCNet relies on a complex pipeline consisting of multiple executable files, making it challenging to reproduce and integrate into existing workflows. In this work, we present a simplified and robust version of PCNet, called sPCNet, that maintains the network's accuracy while addressing its complexity. We achieve this by replacing the intricate face reconstruction pipeline with a single Convolutional Neural Network (CNN), comprising three stages: feature extraction, style adaptation, and reconstruction. This streamlined approach not only reduces the number of required files but also enhances the network's robustness. We evaluate sPCNet on Pixel-Face, Bosphorus, and Pixel-VGG datasets, demonstrating its ability to generalize across various datasets and outperform existing single-network solutions. Our experiments further show that sPCNet can be readily plugged into different pipelines, emphasizing its versatility and ease of use. The code is available at https://github.com/Insight3D/sPCNet.

Optimizing the performance of many objectives (instantiated by tasks or clients) jointly is a crucial problem in multi-objective optimization. In many practical applications, it is important to minimize the total cost over all objectives, which can be achieved via minimization of the maximum objective value over all objectives. Motivated by this, we propose a novel algorithm, called Tightening Maximum Minimization (TMM), for optimizing the tightening maximum (TM) over all objectives. The algorithm uses a sequential elimination strategy and has an appealing structure that allows the use of any arbitrary base method for solving each subproblem. We prove that TMM converges at a geometric rate under some mild conditions and show that the convergence rate is improved when a better base method i

G-Quadruplexes are the four-stranded non-canonical nucleic acid structures that have been found in both RNA and DNA. The G-quadruplex has received increased attention as a possible biological target for therapeutics, including anticancer therapies. To develop efficient G-quadruplex binders, it is crucial to understand the intrinsic properties of these structures, especially those that affect their binding affinity. In this study, we investigate the effect of base stacking interactions on the binding affinities of six 1,4-benzodiazepine (BDZ) ligands with two different G-quadruplexes. These bases have different stacking abilities and provide an opportunity to explore how their interactions influence G-quadruplex binding. We use density functional theory calculations to calculate their bindi

Abstract visual reasoning is a characteristically human ability, allowing the identification of relational patterns that are not explicitly encoded in stimuli. We hypothesize that such abilities can be trained and enhanced via neural networks with deep learning techniques similar to those used for conventional image-based tasks but adapted specifically towards abstract visuals consisting entirely or primarily out text descriptions instead; these models show potential as an alternative approach compared traditional approaches which rely heavily on imagery datasets while still retaining high accuracy levels when tested against real world scenarios involving complex scenes containing many objects interacting together dynamically within them (e..g., game playing).

Introduction

Wha

Filtered ego-speech can improve communication during human-robot interaction (HRI). By removing unwanted acoustic cues, filtering can make the robot's speech more comprehensible to the human partner and thus reduce the cognitive load of the human. Previous filtering approaches for HRI, however, require multiple microphones or post-processing of the speech signal. In this paper, we propose the first single-channel ego-speech filter for HRI, based on a deep neural network architecture. We show that the filter can effectively improve speech intelligibility for human listeners in a two-way communication setting, without any additional acoustic cues. Listeners rated the filtered speech as more clear, precise, and comprehensible. The filter also reduced the perceived cognitive load of the listener. Objective measurements of the filtered speech signals show that the fundamental frequency and the formant frequencies were preserved, while the noise was reduced. The filtered signal also maintained good perceptual quality, as rated by a separate group of listeners.

Spectral unmixing aims to retrieve the biophysical properties of a medium from its spectra, a central task in remote sensing. Conventional methods rely on solving an ill-posed linear system, which mixes spectral library and unknown concentration. We propose a neural network-based method that learns the spectral-unmixing operator from data, avoiding the ill-posedness. We use a radiative transfer model (RTM) as a prior, which relates the unknown concentrations to the measurements through a linear operator. We train the RTM parameters and the unmixing operator jointly end-to-end on synthetic and real data. We show that a biased RTM, which we define as a RTM with non-zero mean residual error, can improve the unmixing performance. The biased RTM learns a unmixing operator that compensates for the bias, leading to better spectral unmixing. We demonstrate our method's performance with both synthetic and real data sets. The code is available at https://github.com/EliSchwartz/RTM-Unmixing.

Tables play a crucial role in conveying information in various domains, serving as indispensable tools for data analysis and decision-making. Existing table summarization methods often focus on extracting key information from the table and generating a summary of a single table. However, when multiple tables are involved, existing solutions usually fail to generate an accurate summary since each table has different characteristics and there is no unified representation for all tables together. To tackle this problem, we propose an end-to-end framework named MultiTableSummary (MTS). MTS consists of three components: a MultiTable Transformer that encodes multi-table data into a shared representation space; a MultiTable Layer Normalization layer to reduce the impact of the noise introduced by

In this paper, we study a dynamic analogue of the Path Cover problem, which can be solved in polynomial time. We first investigate algorithms for solving our path cover variant with respect to different classes and subclasses (e.\g., regular languages). For example: - Given an LTL formula $\phi$, there is no algorithm that decides whether it admits $k$ paths satisfying its dynamics-based specification; however if one allows any number greater than 0 , then such decision problems are solvable by applying techniques from the graph theory literature on vertex covers or independent sets together... We present two distributed protocols over anonymous channels where players aim to agree upon some common information using only their private inputs while maintaining privacy guarantees against eav

Effectively representing heterogeneous tabular datasets for meta-learning remains an open problem. Previous approaches have either ignored or simplified the heterogeneity of these data by either ignoring or replacing heterogeneous features with their average, thereby losing important information. To address this issue, we propose a novel meta-learning framework that utilizes graph neural networks to process and represent such data in a more effective way. Our approach, GNNMeta, uses self-supervised learning to learn a feature representation that captures both the structure and content of the data, allowing for the efficient extraction of relevant information. Additionally, our method incorporates a novel loss function designed to minimize the loss between the learned representation and the

In this paper, we take a step towards a deeper understanding of learning from human preferences by systematically studying the interplay between two fundamental components of preference learning: the feedback and the learner. Specifically, we examine how different types of feedback, such as rankings and binary ratings, affect the convergence speed of an online learning algorithm, when the learner is given access to a set of possible models that can explain the observed data. We show that, even in settings where all models have equal prior probability and produce the same predictions on the training data, the convergence rate of online learning depends heavily on the type of feedback. For example, in a simple setting with only two models, we show that a learner using binary ratings will nev

Data-driven mathematical modeling has a wide range of applications in various areas such as engineering, economics, and environmental science. In this work, we focus on the data-driven modeling of dynamical systems where the underlying data is available only on certain key points. We propose a novel method that synthesizes data-driven models by leveraging the available key-point data. The proposed method enhances the data utilization efficiency and improves the model accuracy. We further enhance the proposed key-point-driven data synthesis method with an innovative mathematical reasoning technique. The enhanced method takes a significant step towards closing the gap between data-driven and mechanism-based modeling. The performance of the proposed method is rigorously tested with three different datasets, and the results show that our approach achieves superior modeling accuracy.

Adverse weather conditions often impair the quality of captured images, inevitably inducing cutting-edge technologies to fail in many real world applications. In this paper we present a novel endtoend deep learning model for image enhancement that utilizes unpaired data by leveraging generative adversarial networks (GANs). Our method has demonstrated stateoftheart performance on various challenging datasets including lowlight imaging and haze removal with remarkable results across diverse metrics such as structural similarity index(SSIM), peak signalnoise ratio,(PSNR)and mean squared error.(MSE.

Fire is a serious safety hazard in battery-powered devices. To better understand the complex phenomena that occur during battery cell fires, we need accurate models of the internal cell structure. Computed tomography (CT) is the most accurate non-destructive testing technique for obtaining cell geometry. However, CT scanning is expensive and time-consuming, which has limited the collection of large datasets of battery cell CT scans. To facilitate the development of data-driven models of battery cell fires, we have collected a dataset of over one thousand CT scans of Li-ion battery cells. This is the largest publicly available dataset of battery cell CT scans to date. We have made the dataset available online and hope that it will be used to develop improved models of battery cell fires.

Next-generation cellular concepts rely on the processing of large quantities of radio-frequency (RF) data to enable enhanced functionality. This is particularly true for 6G, where the new technologies are expected to have a more intensive RF processing footprint than previous generations, due to their increased use of advanced techniques such as massive multiple-input multiple-output (MIMO). Furthermore, while the need for processing power in 5G networks has been met by relying heavily on centralized units to perform complex computations, this may not be feasible at scale in the future. Therefore, a shift toward distributed computing will be necessary to support the demand for massive and ultra-massive MIMO systems, requiring a paradigm change in the way RF data is processed.

Spurious correlations (SCs) between the training data features and the labels are known to impede machine learning model generalization. However, the impact of SCs on the dynamics of learning and the ability of the model to escape local optima in the presence of SCs is not well understood. In this work, we study the role of SCs in determining the generalization error and the dynamics of feature learning. We propose a novel framework to analyze these phenomena based on the concept of complexity as measured by the mutual information between the model parameters and the labels. We demonstrate that SCs can lead to spontaneous feature clustering, which can in turn be exploited by the model to reduce the generalization error. Our results show that the dynamics of learning depend on the interplay between the model complexity, as measured by the mutual information, and the number of SCs. Specifically, we find that strongly clustered features with many SCs slow down learning, while weakly clustered features with few SCs speed up learning. These findings highlight the nuanced impact of SCs on learning dynamics and suggest that increasing model complexity can help the model to identify and exploit SCs, at the same time improving its generalization.

In the domain of image layout representation learning, the critical process of translating image layouts into succinct vector representations is often overlooked. Our goal is to develop a novel approach for effectively capturing visual information in layout images and representing them as compact vectors. We propose an automatic text-to-image layout representation learning model that utilizes natural language to encode layout images as dense vectors. Our model consists of two main components: a text encoder and a layout decoder. The text encoder generates a low-dimensional feature embedding from the input text using a transformer network. This embedding is then used by the layout decoder to reconstruct the layout image. To achieve better performance, we employ attention mechanisms within b

Federated learning (FL) is a collaborative scheme to train a learning model across multiple participants without sharing data. Existing theories of FL convergence and robustness assume that the training data follows the same distribution as the test data, i.e., the non-i.i.d. setting is not considered. We notice that the logits of the training data can be used to approximate the unknown test distribution, and this approximation becomes worse as the model prediction uncertainty increases. Based on this, we propose a simple logits calibration method to improve the robustness of FL under non-i.i.d. data without the need for second-order information. We evaluate the proposed method on deep neural networks for both image classification and tabular data classification tasks. The results show that the proposed method is effective in improving the robustness of FL.

Data similarity assumptions have traditionally been relied upon to understand the convergence behaviors of federated learning. However, they are often inadequate for real-world datasets and may lead to misleading conclusions when a simple model is used to characterize data distribution. In this paper, we explore the possibility of using complex models to more accurately characterize the data distributions and study their impact on the convergence rates of federated learning algorithms. We demonstrate that the convergence rate of federated averaging (FedAvg) can be substantially improved by using an accurate data distribution model when the local training time per round is large enough to ensure the validity of the model. Additionally, we show that the benefits from improving the accuracy o

The pursuit of accurate 3D hand pose estimation stands as a keystone for understanding human motion and behavior. Recent studies have shown that the performance of 2D-to-3D hand pose estimators can be significantly enhanced by utilizing monocular depth maps, particularly in challenging scenarios such as noisy environments or occluded hands. However, there is currently little research on this topic within the 3D hand pose estimation literature. To address this gap, we propose a novel approach to enhance 3D hand pose estimation with monocular depth maps. We introduce the concept of "depth-guided" 3D hand pose estimator, which leverages depth information from monocular images to better estimate the hand pose.

The main objective of the Multiple Kernel k-Means (MKKM) algorithm is to find the centroids of a given set of data samples and cluster them into different clusters. This paper focuses on MKKM with adaptive weights that are used for weighted k-means clustering. The weights are calculated from the Gaussian kernel functions of the data samples, where the Gaussian kernel is one of the most popular unsupervised learning techniques in machine learning. The proposed methodology is tested on 13 datasets which have been collected from the UCI repository. Our experiments show that the proposed approach outperforms the state-of-the-art methods in terms of accuracy and speed. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first time that multiple kernel learning has been applied to weighted k-mean clusteri

Dynamic Spectrum Access (DSA) relies on spectrum sensing to monitor the availability of spectrum holes. Network tomography is the ideal tool to perform large-scale spectrum sensing over a wireless network. However, existing network tomography algorithms suffer from two major drawbacks: i) they assume that the propagation environment is static, i.e., the set of active users and their locations do not change over time, ii) they lack scalability with respect to the network size. To overcome these limitations, we propose a dynamic and scalable network tomography algorithm to support DSA in large-scale wireless networks. We adopt a user-centric spectrum sensing approach where each user performs local measurements and sends the results to a central server. The server then reconstructs the spectrum occupancy over the entire network from these local measurements. To perform network tomography in a dynamic environment, we introduce two novel models to represent the spectrum sensing data: i) a dynamic channel graph, where the edges represent the set of non-zero frequencies and their corresponding gains, ii) a spectral partition graph, which captures the relationship between the sensed frequencies and the user locations. We propose a fully-distributed algorithm to reconstruct the dynamic channel graph and show that it converges to the true solution at a linear rate. We also show that the spectral partition graph remains connected with high probability, implying that the spectrum occupancy information is available to all users. Finally, we provide numerical results to illustrate the performance of our algorithm and its advantages over existing network tomography approaches.

Language provides a way to break down complex concepts into digestible pieces. Recent works in robotics have explored the use of language models to generate instructions for robots, but they often require substantial domain knowledge and are not able to capture the nuances of human language. In this paper, we present Masked LLM (MLM), which is an unsupervised method that can generate comprehensive instructions for robots using only language modeling techniques. MLM leverages the powerful masking capabilities of large language models like GPT-4 to capture the essence of human language. To enable efficient and flexible instruction generation, MLM encodes the task description as a series of tokens and uses a token-masking strategy to guide the generation process. Our experiments demonstrate t

Neural Language Models are known to be susceptible to artifacts and may rely on spurious features within a dataset. This reliance can distort the model's behavior and affect its performance. Previous work has shown that BERT is prone to sentence length bias, idiom usage bias, and date mention bias. To investigate the origin of such biases, we propose a simple probing approach that disentangles the role of sentence length, idiom usage, and date mention from the topic of the sentence. Our experiments reveal that BERT's sentence length bias stems from its reliance on topic, idiom usage bias is mostly driven by sentence length, and date mention bias is caused by both topic and sentence length. Furthermore, we show that by controlling for topic, BERT's prediction score increases for date mention prediction, and decreases for idiom usage prediction. This indicates that BERT uses topic information for idiom prediction, and non-topic information (sentence length) for date mention prediction.

In the realm of unmanned aerial vehicle (UAV) tracking, Siamese networks have emerged as a powerful tool for enhancing tracking accuracy. However, traditional Siamese network architectures are limited by their inherent susceptibility to interference from irrelevant objects during training and inference, leading to suboptimal performance in UAV tracking. To address this challenge, we propose a novel Siamese Network framework that incorporates an adaptive feature selection mechanism designed specifically for UAV tracking tasks. Our approach utilizes a selective attention module that dynamically filters out irrelevant objects based on their degree of relevance, significantly improving the robustness of Siamese tracking models to distractors. Through extensive experiments on multiple datasets,

Knowledge-based, open-domain dialogue generation aims to build chit-chat systems through conversing with humans. This task is challenging due to the high diversity of human intentions and preferences. In this paper, we introduce an innovative approach that utilizes human preferences to enhance the quality and diversity of generated responses in open-domain dialogues. Our method, called Human-Preference-Aware Open-Domain Dialogue Generation (HPODDG), leverages existing large-scale knowledge graphs as an essential component for enhancing response diversification. Specifically, HPODDG first constructs a graph by extracting relevant entities from the knowledge base and then uses it to generate diverse responses by considering both the original user input and the corresponding user preference i

Recent work has embodied LLMs as agents, allowing them to access tools, perform tasks, and be evaluated on their performance. In this work, we introduce a novel tool for evaluating the agent's ability: an interactive text adventure game. This game allows us to evaluate the agent's problem-solving abilities by presenting it with a set of challenges and providing feedback about its success in completing each challenge. The evaluation is guided by a set of instructions that describe how the agent should interact with the environment and what actions will lead to success or failure. We demonstrate the effectiveness of our approach through a detailed analysis of its results on two different agents -- GPT4 and a custom model trained on a curated subset of GPT4 prompts. Our analysis shows that th

Autonomous driving systems face the formidable challenge of navigating intricate and dynamic environments with high reliability, safety. This dissertation focuses on two critical challenges in autonomous navigation: (1) how to build reliable perception models given limited training data; (2), what is a good way for planning that guarantees collision-free trajectories even when there are uncertain elements along those paths? In Chapter 3 we develop an efficient algorithm based method called M3LSRF which can be used as part or all steps within any model learning pipeline from scratch up through final production level deployment by leveraging synthetically generated samples instead traditional manual annotation process while still providing accurate results due its ability handle both sparse

Training-free metrics (a.k.a. zero-cost proxies) are widely used to estimate the performance of machine learning models on outofdomain data in low resource settings, where training a model is expensive or impossible due to insufficient labeled samples from target domains and few knowledge about what features should be extracted for predicting real world outcomes by looking at observed attributes only [1]. Among such measures including accuracy decay rate[2], divergence score using distance metric between feature distributions across source domain(s)[3][4] , correlation coefficient based methods like Correlator,[5],[6]) we present an improved version called Weighted Correlation Coefficient which weighs different pairs with their corresponding weights derived during optimization process inst

Context: Roblox Studio lets millions of creators build interactive experiences by programming in a variant of Lua. The Roblox platform is designed to let users share their content and access others' work. This raises concerns about security, as the code used for this purpose may be vulnerable to exploitation. Methods: We developed an automated approach for identifying potential vulnerabilities in Roblox Studio-generated Lua scripts and evaluated it using a large dataset of 785,000 Roblox Studio assets from 2013 to 2023. Our technique uses static analysis and leverages information retrieval techniques to identify functions that perform sensitive operations (e.g., accessing user accounts) and functions that can lead to remote code execution. To validate our findings, we employed manual analy

Large Language Models (LLMs) have shown remarkable performance in recent years. However, when faced with numerous prediction tasks, LLMs typically require extensive fine-tuning, which not only demands substantial computational resources for training but also exhibits diminishing returns in performance improvement. To address this challenge, we introduce Mixture-of-LoRAs (MLoRAs), a novel weight-sharing scheme that enables efficient multitask tuning for LLMs. MLoRAs introduces a small number of task-specific parameters by mixing a large number of locally trained LoRAs, each focusing on a subset of the prediction tasks. This approach not only reduces the computational cost of fine-tuning but also improves the generalization performance across all tasks. We evaluate MLoRAs on a diverse set of benchmark tasks, including natural language classification, question answering, and code generation. Remarkably, MLoRAs achieves comparable performance to the baseline with up to 14x fewer parameters and 7x less training cost. Our experiments demonstrate that MLoRAs is a promising solution for efficient multitask tuning of LLMs.

Large language models (LLMs) have revolutionized the role of AI, yet also pose significant challenges to model development and deployment. This paper introduces the AI Model-as-a-Service (AMaaS) framework that leverages LLM capabilities to address these challenges. We describe how AMaaS enables flexible customization with minimal human effort and offers a broad range of LLM applications with strong security and privacy guarantees. To demonstrate its capabilities, we present an end-to-end implementation of a multi-tenant, multi-service LLM cloud system. By integrating OpenAI's API, we showcase how developers can easily build their own services atop LLMs through AMaaS with little to no code changes. Our system is currently operational and accessible at https://am.vita.ai/. The source code is

The inference phase of Large Language Models (LLMs) is very expensive. An ideal solution would be to use a smaller model which can be trained on-device, but existing methods do not provide satisfactory performance for low-resource tasks such as classification and question answering (QA). This paper proposes SplitGPT, an effective knowledge distillation approach for LLMs with high accuracy and low cost. The key idea is to split the input sequence into several segments, each of which is fed into one sub-model that outputs its own response. These responses are then combined together in an efficient manner to generate a final answer. We demonstrate the effectiveness of SplitGPT by training a 7B-parameter GPT-2 model using only 128MB of memory.

Typologically diverse benchmarks are increasingly created to track the progress achieved in multilingual NLU. We argue that this approach can lead towards an over-optimistic evaluation of a system's generalization capacity, as we show on 64 different benchmark datasets for eight languages: (1) Most tasks consist only of similar types of data and differ mostly by language; e. g., if one task is about sports articles then others will be related too even though they are from other domains or countries altogether like news reports/blog posts etc.;(2) (ii). Additionally there seems no correlation between difficulty level across multiple dimensions such including word length distribution which could help predict what kind performance users might expect when trying out new models trained spe

In this paper, we provide expressions for the secrecy outage probability (SOP) for subcarrier-based multiple-input single-output (MISO) downlink with imperfect channel state information (CSI). We consider a scenario in which a BS serves multiple users employing multi-antenna transmission techniques over a Rayleigh fading channel. This scenario is relevant to mmWave communications where the CSI may be obtained by pilot contamination or by exploiting channel reciprocity. The SOP expression in terms of the effective SNR is derived for each user, and then averaged over all users. It is shown that the SOP depends on the ratio between the number of estimated antennas at the BS and the number of antennas employed at the user terminals. Additionally, we discuss the impact of imperfect CSI on the a

In Multiple Object Tracking, objects often exhibit non-linear motion of acceleration and deceleration. This can cause a significant increase in the complexity of the tracking problem. Furthermore, these dynamics often appear as random movements that are unrelated to the identity of an object (e.g., a bird flapping its wings). While some methods have been proposed for modeling such behavior, they generally rely on a high number of manually annotated trajectories that are time-consuming to collect. To address this issue, we propose a novel method called DSAMOD which uses deep learning to automatically learn acceleration and deceleration dynamics. Our approach achieves this by leveraging the dynamic shape information captured from the bounding boxes of objects within the video sequences. We u

Accurate time series forecasting is crucial for making informed decisions in many areas, including economics, finance, and weather forecasting. Deep learning models, such as recurrent neural networks and transformers, are currently among the most effective approaches to this problem. However, they often require a large amount of computational resources for training. Multi-layer perceptrons (MLPs) are a simpler alternative, which are less resource-hungry but can also achieve high forecasting accuracy. In this paper, we propose a novel approach called AutoMLP for tuning hyperparameters of MLPs in a forecastable way. We conduct an extensive set of experiments on six real-world time series datasets to demonstrate the effectiveness of our proposed approach. The results show that our approach not only outperforms other state-of-the-art baselines but also achieves a faster training time. We hope that our work will encourage researchers and practitioners to consider MLPs as a serious alternative for time series forecasting.

The Transformer model is the state-of-the-art in Machine Translation. However, the complexity of Transformers makes them computationally expensive to train and use on low-end devices. In this paper we propose a novel architecture that can be trained efficiently on low-end devices while maintaining good performance on the WMT14 benchmark task. Our model is based on the XLNet architecture but uses a lightweight training objective. We also introduce a new loss function for fine-tuning our model on existing datasets. We show that our model can achieve comparable results to the original XLNet model on the WMT14 English-German benchmark and can even outperform it in some cases. Furthermore, we demonstrate that our model can be used as a drop-in replacement for XLNet without any modifications to

We present adaptNMT, an open-source, language-agnostic development environment for Neural Machine Translation. Machine translation (MT) is a central component in many Natural Language Processing (NLP) workflows, and Neural Machine Translation (NMT) in particular has achieved excellent results in recent years. However, the majority of MT libraries are either commercial or tied to a specific framework, language, or dataset. This limits their applicability in a research setting and hinders reproducibility. In contrast, adaptNMT is an open-source framework that is language-agnostic and easily extensible. It comes with a collection of state-of-the-art NMT models, supports multiple training backends (including CPU, GPU, and TPU), and offers various inference options. The goal of this paper is to present the design principles of adaptNMT and to demonstrate its usage through several examples.

The rapid development of deep learning techniques, improved computational power, and the availability of vast training data sets have enabled breakthroughs in natural language processing (NLP). However, these advancements do not guarantee that AI systems will be accurate or reliable. A systematic study of existing AI systems has revealed several challenges related to fairness, interpretability, robustness, reliability, and security. These challenges can affect the safety, accuracy, and trustworthiness of AI systems in various contexts, including healthcare, finance, education, and criminal justice. In this paper, we provide an overview of the current state of AI systems for NLP and identify key challenges in the field. We also discuss future research directions to address these challenges.

Although person or identity verification has been predominantly explored using individual modalities such as face, iris and fingerprint, we believe that a multi-modal fusion approach can be more effective. This paper proposes an integrated framework for personal verification based on multiple biometric traits, namely, face, iris, and fingerprints, which are extracted using unified features from the deep learning-based models. A novel multi-modality fusion scheme is proposed to fuse these three feature representations into a single representation, followed by a multi-label classification stage wherein the combined representation is used to extract a consensus decision. The performance of our proposed framework was evaluated across multiple datasets obtained from various publicly available r

Automatic Compliance Checking (ACC) within the Architecture, Engineering, and Construction (AEC) domain is a highly critical task that can save costly resources. Large Language Models (LLMs), such as GPT-4, demonstrate remarkable performance in ACC. This paper introduces an effective and efficient method to extract the most relevant information from LLMs for compliance checking tasks in architecture. Specifically, our approach leverages the structured knowledge of Constraint Satisfaction Problem (CSP) to guide the information extraction process from LLMs. We evaluate our method on two real-world datasets -- the CSH Concrete Specifications and the U.S. Green Building Council's LEED v4 Pilot Credit Library -- and show significant improvements over existing methods. Our research demonstrates

Web tables contain a large amount of valuable knowledge and have inspired tabular language models aimed at tackling the challenges of these data. To achieve this goal, previous works on web table language modeling (WTLM) employ various pre-training schemes and prompts to enhance the performance of downstream tasks, such as retrieval, question answering and generation. However, all of them rely on limited fine-tuning or knowledge distillation strategies to further improve the robustness and efficiency. In this work, we propose a simple yet effective framework, WTGPT, to directly learn from large pretrained LLMs, like GPT-4 or GPT-5, for WTLM tasks. The key insight is that WTLM tasks can be viewed as general NLP tasks and thus can be learned by leveraging LLMs' large pre-trained knowledge.

We present a novel benchmarking framework for asynchronous multi-fidelity optimization, PySOT, built on top of the Python package PyTorch. It automates the process of creating zero-cost benchmarks -- benchmarks that can be evaluated on a single CPU core -- and running benchmark suites on those. PySOT is used to create benchmark results for 17 asynchronous multi-fidelity optimization algorithms on 14 zero-cost benchmarks, in total. This is the first time that a diverse set of algorithms and benchmarks is used to thoroughly evaluate the performance of asynchronous multi-fidelity optimization algorithms. The results show that using multiple fidelities can significantly speed up optimization, but comes at the cost of increased algorithmic complexity. In particular, asynchronous variants of Bayesian optimization and random search are outperformed by dedicated multi-fidelity methods.

Pre-training has been investigated to improve the efficiency and performance of training neural operators in data-scarce environments. Large language models (LLMs) have shown promising results for the task of generating high quality code. However, existing methods largely rely on LLMs as a black box, which limits their practical application in industrial software development scenarios where data is often confidential. In this work, we develop a new method for pre-training LLMs using synthetic data, with the goal of improving their performance when applied to real-world data. Our approach involves synthesizing code snippets from scratch using large language model prompts, and then feeding these snippets into LLMs for fine-tuning. This approach not only enables us to avoid the issue of data

Recently, Mixture of Experts (MoE) Transformers have garnered increasing attention due to their superior efficiency and generalizability. The existing work typically assumes that each expert in MoEs is well-trained by an independent training objective. However, this may introduce a high computational cost during training since each expert requires separate fine-tuning. Additionally, this leads to the problem of parameter sharing among experts, which can degrade performance when the models are deployed on different tasks with dissimilar data distributions. To address these issues, we propose a novel method called Mixed-Expert Fine-Tuning (MEFT). We show that MEFT allows both experts within a single model to share parameters but also enables them to be trained independently during fine-tunin

Despite remarkable progress, existing multimodal large language models (MLLMs) are still inferior to unimodal counterparts in terms of performance and efficiency. To address these challenges simultaneously from a multi-task learning perspective for the first time using unlabeled data only with minimal human effort required per task label (\ie zero-, few-/shot), we propose Multimodal Self Training via Masked Language Modeling ("MSTML"). Our model consists primarily of two components: i.) masking tokens at different granularities within various modalities across varying degrees; ii. ), adapting training objectives by leveraging knowledge about other masked regions as well those that have not been modified yet when generating predictions or reconstructing input text/images respectively during

Battery technology is increasingly important for global electrification efforts. However, batteries are complex systems with numerous parameters that can significantly impact performance. To improve battery manufacturing, we developed a machine learning-based framework called the "Battery Characteristics Estimator (BCE)". The BCE systematically identifies critical battery characteristics and uses these to predict the most relevant battery properties using an LSTM neural network. Our study showed that the BCE model outperformed other models in terms of accuracy, including regression methods such as Support Vector Regression and Gaussian Processes. These results indicate that the proposed approach can be used for effective battery characterization and performance prediction. The code for the

We present a novel way to parametrize arbitrary planar compact domains homeomorphically to the disk. The parametrization is induced by a conformal map, but unlike the Riemann map, our map is guaranteed to be defined and homeomorphic on the entire closure of the domain, including the boundary. The construction relies on a recent characterization of the dipoles, i.e., the singularities of the Riemann map, as the only conformal invariants of a domain. We show that the coefficients of the power series expansion of the Riemann map at the boundary nodes (dipoles) determine the coefficients of the power series expansion of the inverse map at the image nodes. This determines the image of the boundary nodes up to a Möbius transformation, which can be used to construct a homeomorphism from the domain to the disk. We provide explicit formulas for the case of domains with a connected boundary.

Recent years have seen a surge of artificial currency-based mechanisms in contexts where monetary incentives are not directly applicable. This has prompted the emergence of numerous applications that leverage artificial currencies to motivate and reward human users for their contributions to specific projects or tasks. However, despite its increasing prevalence and potential, the domain of artificial currencies remains relatively unexplored, with limited research efforts focused on exploring the intricacies and dynamics of this field. This paper introduces the concept of "artificial currencies" as an innovative approach to incentivize user participation and engagement within various domains. It explores the underlying principles behind these virtual currencies and examines their effectiven

We present an analysis of 12 million instances of privacy-relevant reviews publicly visible on Google Play Store, with the aim to explore their potential as a dataset for research in natural language processing. We find that these user comments are highly structured and contain rich information about users' preferences regarding apps they use or plan using them; however some challenges emerge from this structure (e\.g., lack/excess data points) when trying out standard text classification models trained over similar datasets -- which makes it more challenging than initially expected even though there is plenty available resources already developed specifically designed towards addressing such issues within different domains relatedly enough so far nonetheless! This paper aims at investigat

This article investigates applying advanced machine learning models, specifically LSTM and BERT, for text classification in a real-world application. The paper presents an implementation of these models and compares their performance with traditional models such as Naive Bayes, Logistic Regression, SVM, Decision Tree, KNN, and Random Forest on a publicly available dataset, the Enron email corpus. The results indicate that the advanced models significantly outperform traditional approaches, demonstrating their potential for improving text classification accuracy. The findings shed light on the effectiveness of these state-of-the-art models, which could prove valuable for various domains requiring efficient and accurate text classification tasks. This study aims to encourage further research

We propose a new framework for detecting situational impairments that affect human I/O capabilities, such as those caused by distractions, stress, fatigue, and alcohol consumption. Our approach is based on the hypothesis that the effects of these impairments on human I/O are similar in nature, even though they may affect different parts of the human computation system. To test this hypothesis, we first identify a set of 17 impairments that are caused by a variety of factors and manifest in multiple I/O channels. We then derive a unified metric, based on reaction time and error rate, that can be used to detect impairments in any of the 17 impairment conditions. We show that the proposed metric is a better predictor of impairment than each of the 17 individual impairments, as measured by correlation coefficients. We also show that the metric can be used to detect impairments in user experiments, where participants are exposed to conditions that mimic the effects of impairments. Finally, we discuss the implications of our results for human-computer interaction and human factors engineering.

Entity Set Expansion (ESE) aims to identify new entities belonging to the same semantic class as existing ones. In this work, we explore ESE from a novel perspective: instead of focusing on finding new entities within the same category, we propose a method that can simultaneously predict both the new entity and its class. To achieve this goal, we develop an entity-centric Transformer-based architecture called ENTIFY (ENTity-Focused Information Retrieval), which uses cross-attention to capture the relationships between existing and new entities. Our model was trained with a custom dataset created by combining datasets from different domains. Experimental results show that ENTIFY outperforms previous approaches in terms of prediction accuracy, especially when there are only few training samp

Training agents to explore unknown environments is a fundamental problem in reinforcement learning (RL). Effective exploration strategies rely on accurately estimating state-action values (Q-values) and uncertainty about them. However, existing methods often rely on complex models or human-designed heuristics to estimate uncertainty. To this end, we propose the Noisy Spiking Actor Network (NSAN), a novel approach that incorporates uncertainty estimation into the training of exploration policies. NSAN utilizes spiking neurons to encode uncertainty in Q-values as noisy spikes, providing natural uncertainty estimates without the need for additional modeling. Furthermore, NSAN employs a policy-based architecture that directly optimizes the exploration policy, avoiding the complexity of modeling state-action uncertainty. We evaluate NSAN on a set of 49 robotic control tasks with varying reward sparsity and demonstrate that NSAN consistently outperforms baseline methods, including maximum entropy Inverse Reinforcement Learning (IRL), Soft Actor-Critic (SAC), and Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO). Our experiments showcase the effectiveness of NSAN in estimating uncertainty and improving exploration performance.

Text data has become extremely valuable due to the emergence of machine learning algorithms that learn from it. This creates an incentive for malicious actors to spread misinformation, such as fake news and propaganda. Existing work on detecting misleading text relies heavily on supervised machine learning and requires access to labeled data, which is not always available. Our goal in this paper is to use unlabeled data to automatically generate weak labels for training a classifier. We propose an approach based on the idea of "semantic clustering", i.e., we use the similarity between documents to cluster them into groups. We then use these clusters as weak labels to train a classifier. We conduct extensive experiments on several real-world datasets and show that our approach can significa

Fine-grained monitoring is crucial for multiple data-driven tasks such as debugging, provision of performance and security guarantees.
