We present Pix2Gif, a motion-guided diffusion model for image-to-video synthesis. Our approach is based on the observation that video can be understood as an ordered sequence of images and thus treated similarly to static imagery by using existing techniques from computer vision (e..g., pose estimation) or generative models such VQGANs [10]. In contrast with most prior work in this area which use a series 3D object poses together along side text descriptions at training time only our method generates high quality videos without any explicit supervision during learning thanks its ability leverage both spatial information extracted via pixel representations learned alongside temporal dynamics captured through sequential sampling steps conducted within each epoch' s duration period when runni

In this paper, we perform a detailed analysis of what bands of the spectrogram are more relevant for speech enhancement. We focus on the enhanced speech reconstruction from the perspective of neural networks and investigate the role of different frequency bands. In particular, we design a novel model based on Sinc-convolution, a type of convolutional layer that is frequency-aware and can selectively listen to different frequency bands. We prove that Sinc-convolution is capable of enhancing speech by exploiting the mask estimation and the speech reconstruction. We perform a detailed analysis of the mask estimation and show that the proposed model can automatically select the relevant bands for mask estimation and speech reconstruction. We design a new dataset that allows the evaluation of the performance of the model in the case of time-varying masks. We show that the proposed method outperforms the state-of-the-art on the Libri-22k dataset, a standard benchmark for speech enhancement. We also show that the proposed method is able to exploit multi-band information for mask estimation and speech reconstruction.

In the contemporary digital landscape, the continuous generation of extensive streaming data across diverse domains has become pervassive. This phenomenon is known as Big Data and it raises new challenges for traditional systems to efficiently process large amounts in real-time with low latency while being cost effective at scale [1]. The need arises to develop a solution that can handle these types datasets effectively by leveraging novel technologies such AI/ML techniques or cloud computing resources available today within an environment where scalability demands are met without compromising on performance metrics like accuracy rates over time series prediction models deployed using deep neural networks (DNNs) — all this done quickly enough so businesses don't miss out opportunities due

Chain-of-Thought (CoT) serves as a critical emerging ability in LLMs, enabling them to generate text that is more aligned with human reasoning. This paper introduces a novel training method for CoT, based on the principle of self-supervised learning, where we leverage the LM's internal language modeling ability to automatically learn CoT. Specifically, we propose an approach called Self-Supervised CoT Training, which involves a two-step process: first, we utilize the prompted generation method to obtain CoT-enhanced contextualized representations; and second, we fine-tune these representations using a prompt-guided curriculum learning scheme. Our experiments demonstrate that this approach significantly improves CoT capabilities compared to existing methods.

Recently, because of the high-quality representations of contrastive learning methods, rehearsal-based continual learning methods have achieved remarkable results in various continual learning tasks. However, as a preliminary exploration of this method, there are still many problems that need to be solved. In particular, when training and testing different types of data simultaneously, existing works mainly focus on single-head encoders, while ignoring multi-head encoders and the associated challenges. To address these issues, we propose a novel framework called Heterogeneous Memory Retrieval (HetMemRet), which uses heterogeneous memory for online model parameter updates in multi-head encoder scenarios. Specifically, we introduce two types of memory: one is based on the current task and th

LiDAR odometry and mapping is a crucial component in autonomous robots. LiDAR odometry is typically performed in a perceptually rich environment with distinctive features for the sake of robustness. However, LiDAR odometry in perceptually poor environments, such as in large-scale flat areas or in featureless indoors, is still an challenging task. In this paper, we propose a deep LiDAR odometry and mapping algorithm DESIMON, which introduces two novel methods to improve the LiDAR odometry performance in perceptually poor environments. The first method extracts semantic features from the LiDAR point clouds and fuses them into the odometry calculation to provide global context. The second method detects novel outliers from the LiDAR point clouds and removes their influences on the odometry calculation. Without any additional sensor, our proposed method achieves a state-of-the-art LiDAR odometry performance on the KITTI odometry benchmark, and a published LiDAR mapping performance on the SemanticKITTI mapping benchmark.

Diffusion-weighted Magnetic Resonance Imaging (dMRI) is a technique that allows measuring the displacement of water molecules in biological tissues. While dMRI has been widely used for various applications such as brain functional connectivity, tumor identification and characterization, and joint motion analysis, its application to cardiac imaging remains scarce. This paper introduces a new technique called Cardiac Diffusion Tensor Mapping (cDTM), which leverages dMRI data to estimate tensor fields within the heart muscle. We present a pipeline to compute these tensor fields from dMRI data acquired with different b-values and diffusion times. Our experimental results on an in vitro setup show that cDTM enables accurate mapping of cardiac mechanical properties, including fiber orientation,

With the advancement of language models (LMs), their exposure to private data is increasingly concerning. For example, fine-tuning a large LM on sensitive information can lead it being used for malicious purposes such as phishing attacks or leaking personal preferences and interests from its outputs [1]. Furthermore recent research has shown that Lms trained using public datasets may be able infer user attributes with high accuracy even when no additional training occurs[2], which could potentially allow these systems to create personalized adverts based off users' social media activity without any further knowledge about them other than what was included in publicly available sources like Twitter feeds. [3] In this blog post we will discuss how researchers are trying address security is

Energy efficiency is a key performance indicator for edge-cloud computing, affecting both operational costs and carbon footprint. However, energy consumption data is often unavailable, impractical to measure, or too expensive to collect at the scale of millions of devices. To address this challenge, we propose GreenBytes, a machine learning-based energy estimation framework for edge-cloud environments. GreenBytes leverages readily available sensor data, namely CPU utilization and ambient temperature, to predict energy consumption at both the device and datacenter levels. We develop an intelligent monitoring agent that dynamically collects sensor data, performs energy accounting, and predicts future energy consumption using a deep learning model. We demonstrate the efficacy of GreenBytes through extensive experiments and open-source evaluations. Our results show that GreenBytes can accurately estimate energy consumption with an average error rate of 1.7% at the device level and 4.3% at the datacenter level. GreenBytes is lightweight, with a CPU overhead of only 2.8%, making it practical for real-world deployments. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first work to leverage readily available sensors to estimate energy consumption at scale.

In this paper, we introduce tiled graphs as models of learning and maturing processes. We show that for a given set of tiles, it is possible to construct a tiled graph representing the evolution from an initial state to the final state. It is also possible to find a minimal tiling, such that there are no other tiled graphs with fewer tiles describing this evolution. Furthermore, we establish connections between our model and the notion of an algorithmic program, and discuss how one can construct tiled graphs in a way similar to the approach taken by genetic programming. As a result, we propose two algorithms for solving various problems that are represented by tiled graphs. The first is based on standard genetic programming techniques and the second uses a modified version of the hill-clim

High dynamic range (HDR) imaging involves capturing a series of frames of the same scene with different exposures, then combining these frames to create an image that contains more detail in both bright and dark regions than traditional photography. Despite its potential for capturing more information about the visual world, HDR imaging has been limited by technical challenges such as motion blur, ghosting, and ghost removal artifacts in the final HDR images. In this paper, we introduce Ghost-Free HDR Imaging (GHOST), a new framework designed to address these issues. Unlike existing methods, GHOST employs a novel combination of ghost removal techniques tailored for HDR video sequences, resulting in sharper and artifact-free HDR videos. Experimental results on both synthetic and real-world

Real-world datasets commonly exhibit noisy labels and class imbalance, such as long-tail distributions. These two issues are important challenges in real life that have been extensively studied separately over the past decade; however there is currently no study to address them simultaneously with a single framework for both classification tasks (i) where classes appear imbalanced but their boundaries overlap significantly or when they do not clearly separate from each other -- so called overlapping data -or (ii). We propose an endtoend multitask learning approach which can deal jointly wellwith these problems by exploiting information obtained through auxiliary task training alongsida main target clasification problem .

Topic modeling seems to be almost synonymous with generating lists of top words to represent topics within a corpus. However, for several applications including content-based recommender systems and summarization, it is useful to generate topic representations in a low-dimensional space rather than simply as ranked lists of words. We demonstrate that, when compared to the widely-used Latent Dirichlet Allocation (LDA) approach, our alternative approach based on nonnegative matrix factorization (NMF) achieves superior performance in terms of both quality and efficiency, offering an attractive alternative to LDA for topic modeling tasks. Furthermore, we show that NMF performs particularly well for capturing textual coherence, a feature which is crucial for downstream text mining tasks. Our me

Legged robots are well-suited for broad exploration tasks in complex environments with yielding terrain. Understanding robotic foot-terrain interactions is critical for safe locomotion and walking efficiency for legged robots. This paper presents a reduced-order resistive-force model for robotic-foot/mud interactions. We focus on vertical robot locomotion on mud and propose a visco-elasto-plastic analog to model the foot/mud interaction forces. Dynamic behaviors such as mud visco-elasticity, withdrawing cohesive suction, and yielding are explicitly discussed with the proposed model. Besides comparing with dry/wet granular materials, mud intrusion experiments are conducted to validate the force model. The dependency of the model parameter on water content and foot velocity is also studied t

This paper develops a new filtering approach for state estimation in polynomial systems corrupted by arbitrary noise, which is based on the notion of minimal bases. The method is inspired from the well-known EKF and UKF approaches but differs in the way that the covariance matrix is computed: instead of computing it through a linearization of the system equations, we compute it using the linearization of the mapping between the minimal basis of the state space and its image under the noise model. The advantage of this approach is that it does not require to know explicit expressions of the Jacobian matrices associated with the minimal bases of interest and can therefore be used even when these expressions are unknown or difficult to compute. We illustrate our approach using a nonlinear ben

We propose a new data structure, called Sierpinski triangle data structure (STDS), which supports efficient array value update and prefix sum calculation. The STDS is based on a novel three-way partitioning scheme of the array elements, reminiscent of the Medial Axis Transform. The STDS can be viewed as a hierarchical clustering of the array elements, where the hierarchy depth is given by the base-2 logarithm of the array size. As such, the STDS provides a new level of array data management, which differs from the traditional row-major and column-major orders. We derive the prefix sum of any subarray in $\mathcal{O}(\log n)$ time, where $n$ is the array size. We also show that any update of an array value can be propagated through the STDS in $\mathcal{O}(\log n)$ time. We conducted experiments to validate the theoretical results and present a practical application of the STDS: computing the convex hull of a set of points in the plane.

Laboratory experiments have shown that communication plays an important role in solving social dilemmas. Here, we present a class of games where communication can be beneficial to players but not necessary for them to solve the game. We consider two-player games with a unique equilibrium strategy profile. In these games, if one player deviates from the unique Nash equilibrium and communicates her deviation to another player who follows it, they both reach a Pareto improvement over their original strategies. This is the case even when communication between players is costly. On the other hand, if players do not communicate, they may find themselves worse off than before. We then show that the same phenomenon occurs in games played by large populations. In particular, our results apply to tw

Fitting virtual clothes on 3D human models is an essential task for the realistic rendering of virtual try-on. Although previous works have made significant progress in this task by leveraging deep neural networks, they often lack control over the fitting process and rely on limited data. In this paper, we propose OOTDiffusion, a novel framework for controllable virtual try-on that fuses the strengths of deep latent diffusion models with a novel cross-modal conditioning approach. Our method not only improves the fitting accuracy by generating clothes and 3D models with consistent latent representations but also enables intuitive control through simple user interactions. Furthermore, we develop a unique training strategy that allows our model to learn from unpaired data, thus overcoming the limitations of data scarcity. Extensive experiments demonstrate that OOTDiffusion surpasses existing state-of-the-art methods in terms of fitting accuracy, control performance, and training stability. Our code and models are available at https://github.com/luost26/ootdiffusion.

We investigate the empirical counterpart of group distributionally robust optimization (GDRO), which a recent work has shown to be both theoretically and empirically superior in comparison with individual-level approaches. To this end, we consider two different types: GDROM1 where each sample is drawn from one common population for all groups; while M2N corresponds to $m$ populations ($n$-dimensional) shared across multiple subsets/groups $(k< m)$, i.~e., $\mathbb{R}^{d_i} \times \{0,\ldots , n^j\}$ such that every subset comprises exactly ${x_{t}^{(g)}}^\intercal {\mathbf 1}_{z= j}$, as proposed by Li et al.~\citeyearconference[Li+]{li}, hence forming an extension over their approach on multiclass classification problem via weighted margin loss function framework~\citep[][][]{shakernia}.

Industrial cyber-physical systems (ICPS) are characterized by their industrial control systems (ICS), which control the spatio-temporal state of physical processes. Monitoring the spatio-temporal state of ICPS is crucial for anomaly detection and diagnosis. However, ICPS data is typically heterogeneous, with missing values, and collected from large-scale industrial plants with hundreds of sensors. Consequently, traditional state estimation methods often fail to accurately estimate the spatio-temporal state of ICPS. To address these challenges, this paper proposes a novel unsupervised learning-based state estimation method that fuses temporal and spatial correlations to estimate the spatio-temporal state of ICPS. The proposed method first performs low-rank approximation on the spatial data matrix to obtain a spatially smooth state estimate. Then, a temporal refinement model is developed to further refine the spatial estimate by leveraging the temporal correlation. Finally, a clustering-based method is proposed to handle the heterogeneity of ICPS data and identify anomalous regions. The effectiveness of the proposed method is demonstrated using real-world ICPS data.

In the current machine translation (MT) landscape, the Transformer architecture stands out as the gold standard. It has been shown to outperform all other neural architectures on multiple MT benchmarks and is now widely used in industrial settings for many different languages pairs [1]. However one of its main limitations consists in being trained using a single large data set with parallel sentences across two or more language domains; this makes it difficult if not impossible to train custom models from scratch over smaller datasets without having access at least some amount thereof beforehand which can be challenging especially given that most public corpora tend only cover few popular ones such English-French/German etc.. To address these issues we propose an approach called "Transfor

Over recent years, news recommender systems have gained significant attention in both academia and industry, emphasising the importance of personalisation. Despite this popularity for personalised recommendations on online platforms like Google News or Facebook Feed; there remains a lack thereof when it comes to offline information consumption experiences such as newspapers/magazines which can also serve important roles within society by providing valuable insights into current events around us everyday lives without requiring internet access 24 hours per day every single minute throughout each hour across all days regardless if any one person has no access at that particular moment due limited connectivity issues (e g., rural areas).

Federated Learning (FL) is a popular algorithm to train machine learning models on user data constrained by privacy regulations. FL is based on the assumption that each client has access to an independent copy of training data and therefore training on the client's device does not violate the privacy of other clients. However, in several real-world applications, such as multi-robot systems or edge computing scenarios, the same robot/device processes data from multiple clients, which can lead to information leakage. This paper introduces the first formal definition of this scenario called semi-private FL. We present two different variants of semi-private FL, where either individual clients are protected against information leakage, or data owners protect their sensitive data. In addition, w

Lyndon words are extensively studied in combinatorics on words -- they play a crucial role in several problems, including linear orders on words and permutations. In this paper we give a new combinatorial proof of the following result, which is due to Rousseau (2008): if $w$ is a Lyndon word over an alphabet $\Sigma$, then for every $1\leq i<j\leq d(w)$, where $d(w)$ denotes the length of $w$, there exists a unique Lyndon word $u$ such that $uw$ is a prefix of $ww$. The main idea behind our proof is that we can construct a certain graph $G_i$ on the set of all Lyndon words with alphabet size $\Sigma$ and cardinality $|L|=d(w)$.

3D object detection accuracy can be improved by data augmentation. However, existing methods only focus on augmenting the training data distribution, which increases the risk of overfitting. In this paper, we propose a novel false positive sampling-based data augmentation method, FPSDA, to enhance 3D object detection accuracy. The FPSDA method introduces a new training sample construction scheme that allows the model to learn from both positive and negative samples simultaneously. This approach helps the model to better understand the characteristics of positive samples and improves the discrimination between positive and negative samples. We evaluate the FPSDA method on the KITTI and Waymo benchmarks, using the voxel-based detection framework. The FPSDA method significantly improves the 3D object detection accuracy of the baseline method on the KITTI benchmark, achieving 0.6% and 0.9% improvements in mAP on the moderate and difficult levels, respectively. The FPSDA method also improves the 3D object detection accuracy on the Waymo benchmark, achieving 1.7% and 0.5% improvements in mAP on the moderate and difficult levels, respectively.

An assurance case has become an integral component for the certification of safety-critical systems. The key objective is to establish that a system satisfies its specifications, and the case itself contains all evidence required by the customer or regulator for this purpose. However, in contrast to traditional software systems, many critical systems are not solely the result of software development but also stem from hardware design. This makes it difficult to guarantee that the hardware design fulfills the functional requirements expressed as specifications on the software side. In recent years, researchers have started exploring how these two worlds can be connected by applying formal methods such as model checking and theorem proving. However, the resulting results and the respective m

Charts, figures, and text derived from data play an important role in decision making, from data analysis to the interpretation of scientific results. A common practice for producing these visualizations is to create individual plots with a high degree of manual intervention. This process is time-consuming and error-prone. Therefore, we present PlotMaker: a framework that automates the creation of charts, figures, and text derived from data. PlotMaker integrates four key capabilities to achieve this goal: (1) a model based on the OpenAI API to understand users' requests; (2) an approach based on the concept of "plottable objects" to interpret the user's request; (3) a module to generate the plottable objects from data; and (4) an AI engine to produce the visualization of the requested info

The Mutual Reinforcement Effect (MRE) investigates the synergistic relationship between word-of-mouth (WOM) and online ratings, which can either reinforce or offset each other in the context of online reviews. Despite its importance to academia and industry, existing research has overlooked the effect of WOM on consumer attitudes towards products. To fill this gap, we introduce a novel approach that considers WOM as an additional information source in the form of textual reviews. We leverage the state-of-the-art Large Language Model (LLM), Llama2-7b, to generate new product reviews based on the original review corpus and conduct a comprehensive experimental study across two real-world datasets to investigate the MRE effect. Our results provide valuable insights into the role of WOM in shap

In the field of chemical structure recognition, the task of converting molecular images into graph structures and SMARTS codes is very important. The traditional method for solving this problem requires manual design features by domain experts which leads to a high workload burden on them in practice when dealing with complex molecules or multi-source data from different sources; therefore it's necessary that we explore automatic feature extraction methods instead as they will help reduce workloads significantly while still achieving comparable performance levels (if not better). In recent years there have been many advances made towards deep learning models specifically designed around these tasks - especially those utilizing generative adversarial networks(GAN) -- but due partly because

Aerial robots show significant potential for forest canopy research and environmental monitoring by providing data collection capabilities that can be difficult to obtain through ground-based methods. In this paper, we present a new framework called CANOPY (CANopy Oblique Photogrammetry) which enables the generation of high resolution digital surface models from oblique imagery taken with low cost consumer grade cameras onboard small aerial platforms such as multirotors or fixed wing aircraft in forests where traditional photogrametric approaches are not practical due their reliance on nadir viewing angles required when using dense matching techniques like Structure From Motion - Multi View Stereo [1].

Graph analytics is a key workloads in data-intensive applications. Dynamic graph analytics is more challenging than static graph analytics, since it requires frequent updates on the graph data. Existing dynamic graph analytics solutions on persistent memory focus on improving data access efficiency, neglecting the overheads of data update and analytics kernel execution. Moreover, they suffer from data redundancy, as the updated graph data is redundantly stored on both main memory and persistent memory. To address these challenges, we propose Dynamic Graph Analysis on Persistent memory (DGAP), which avoids data redundancy by storing the updated graph data only on persistent memory. To efficiently update and analyze the graph data in persistent memory, we propose a novel persistent memory-based data update method, called Put-Through-Update (PTU), and a two-level analytics kernel execution method, called Kernel-Through-Execution (KTE). PTU copies the updated data from main memory to persistent memory with hardware memory transaction, and KTE executes the analytics kernel on the updated data in an efficient manner. To evaluate DGAP, we design and implement a prototype system on top of Intel's Persistent Memory Technology. Experimental results show that DGAP improves the update and analysis performance by 1.97x and 2.22x, respectively, compared with the state-of-the-art dynamic graph analytics solution on persistent memory.

The Quadratic Assignment Problem (QAP) is a fundamental NP-hard optimization problem with many applications, and the existence of a polynomial time algorithm for QAP would imply P=NP. Many real-world applications, however, can be modeled as QAP instances with special structure, and these special cases have been extensively studied. In this paper, we investigate the complexity of QAP instances with a given number of distinct distances. We show that the worst-case complexity is determined by highly symmetric instances, and we introduce the QAP-SAT instances, i.e., QAP instances where the matrix entries are taken from a finite field of size at most 3. We show that the QAP-SAT instances are exactly the hardest QAP instances with respect to the worst-case complexity. Moreover, we show that there is a close connection between the QAP-SAT instances and the Permanent. As a byproduct, we are able to classify the complexity of the QAP instances with respect to the Permanent. Furthermore, we show that there is a close connection between the QAP-SAT instances and the Absolute Permanent. As a byproduct, we are able to classify the complexity of the QAP instances with respect to the Absolute Permanent.

Radio Frequency (RF) device fingerprinting has been recognized as a potential technology for enab ling security and privacy in Internet-ofThings. The RF fingerprint of an IoT de vice is formed by the unique physical characteristics, such that it can be used to identify or authenticate devices within its wireless coverage area without requiring any additional hardware modification on those target d evices . This paper first presents our survey work focusing primarily upon recent research efforts regarding radio frequency identification technologies based o n low power wideband signals at millimeter wave frequencies i nteractions between these two domains are still largely unexplored despite their growing importance across many application areas ranging from radar sensing through remote cont

Objective: To decrease the computational burden of weakly-supervised (WS) deep learning models for image classification. Methods: We propose to pre-train a Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) using a self-supervised learning (SSL) approach before fine-tuning it in a WS setting. Specifically, we use a pre-trained SSL model to extract features from image patches, and we fine-tune a light CNN using those features. We compare the performance of our proposed method against models trained using only WS labels. We also compare against the state-of-the-art WS method in terms of computational efficiency. Results: Our method achieves better classification performance than the state-of-the-art WS method and the WS-only models at both patch-level and slide-levels. It also achieves this performance using less computational resources, which is critical when deploying models on hospital pathology departments' infrastructure. Conclusions: Reducing the complexity of self-supervised learning approaches improves the performance of weakly-supervised classification models in a practical setting. Our method is, on average, 7.6x more computationally efficient than the state-of-the-art WS method.

In this paper, we provide expressions for the secrecy outage probability (SOP) for subcarrier-based orthogonal frequency division multiplexing (OFDM)-based systems in presence of a single eavesdropper and multiple legitimate users. We consider two different models for the eavesdropping channel: one is the general case where both the legitimate user and the eavesdropper channels are correlated to each other while the second model is an extreme case of the first one where the eavesdropper channel is completely independent of the legitimate user channel. For these two scenarios, we derive closed-form expressions for SOP as well as the average SOP. Our analysis shows that the SOP can be significantly reduced if the eavesdropper channel is independent of the legitimate user channel.

The voluminous nature of geospatial temporal data from physical monitors and simulation models poses challenges in terms of storage and processing. To address these issues, this study proposes the use of deep learning techniques for compressing and summarizing the information in time series data, thereby reducing the computational and storage overheads involved in storing large volumes of data while simultaneously retaining its essential characteristics. Specifically, this paper introduces a novel architecture called the LSTM-GAN model that utilizes both the Long Short Term Memory (LSTM) network and Generative Adversarial Network (GAN) to efficiently capture important features from time series data, thereby facilitating their compression and summarization.

We introduce a novel methodology for identifying adversarial attacks on deepfake detectors using eXplainable Artificial Intelligence (XAI). In an era characterized by digital advancement, deepfakes have emerged as a potent tool, creating a demand for efficient detection systems. However, these systems are frequently targeted by adversarial attacks that inhibit their performance. We address this gap, developing a defensible deepfake detector by leveraging the power of XAI. The proposed methodology uses XAI to generate interpretability maps for a given method, providing explicit visualizations of decision-making factors within the AI models. We subsequently employ a pretrained feature extractor that processes both the input image and its corresponding XAI image. The feature embeddings extrac

In this paper, we study a facility location problem within a competitive market context, where customer demand is modeled as a stochastic process. We consider a setting in which the service provider (SP) must open facilities that serve each customer once per week and may also be subject to some non-negligible operating costs at any given time during its lifetime. The SP can decide when to close a facility, and must make that decision with probability $1-\alpha$. We assume that the number of customers served by a facility follows a Poisson distribution, and that there exists an efficient allocation rule for these facilities. We focus on the case when the number of customers is large, and establish upper bounds for the cost of the optimal strategy and lower bounds for the expected revenue ob

Despite great advances in program synthesis techniques, they remain algorithmic black boxes. Although they guarantee to find a solution for any problem instance given the correct input, it is still unclear how they arrive at the final solution. In this paper, we investigate the process of program synthesis from a program semantics perspective. We establish a correspondence between the syntax and semantics of a program and its synthesized equivalent, thus making the synthesis process transparent. To illustrate our approach, we examine synthetic programs for the task of finding the product of two numbers. Our analysis reveals that the synthesis algorithms perform a series of transformations on the initial program until a final product is obtained. The transformations are based on common patt

Deep Learning (DL) models have become crucial in digital transformation, thus raising concerns about their intellectual property protection. This paper investigates the extent to which DL-based classification models trained on proprietary data can be protected by copyright law. The authors analyzed 198 patents from the USPTO database and 42 legal decisions regarding software copyrights to create a dataset used for training and testing five different machine learning algorithms: Logistic Regression, Decision Trees, Random Forest, XGBoost, and Neural Networks. Each model was evaluated based on its performance metrics, including accuracy, precision, recall, F1 score, and AUC, using 5-fold cross-validation. The findings suggest that Neural Networks outperformed other models, with an average ac

In manipulation planning with cables and ropes, the robot manipulates the cable by grasping and releasing it at different points, called grasp points. The set of all possible configurations of the cable with respect to the robot and the environment is called the cable configuration space. The topology of the configuration space determines the possible motions of the cable and, thus, the planar grasps that can be executed. In this paper, we propose a finite-state representation, called the grasp loop signature, of the configuration space of a cable with respect to a planar robot. The grasp loop signature is a directed graph that encodes the topology of the configuration space and the possible transitions between them. We show that two important problems, the reachability problem and the shortest path problem, can be solved efficiently using the grasp loop signature. The grasp loop signature can be easily adapted to new environments and can incorporate constraints on the cable motion, making it a powerful tool for the development of intelligent robotic systems. We demonstrate the use of the grasp loop signature in two ways: (1) as a standalone algorithm, and (2) as a tool for constructing a task-specific cable library for a manipulation planning system.

Typologically diverse benchmarks are increasingly created to track the progress achieved in multilingual NLP, yet their ability to identify gaps is limited by the languages they cover. We present a method for constructing large and diverse synthetic datasets that can be used as a proxy for real-world data from many more than the few dozen languages covered by existing benchmarks. By exploiting the diversity of Wikidata (1348 languages) we introduce a flexible data generation procedure that samples sentences from its multilingual knowledge base and combines them with a set of syntactic operations. The resulting dataset, Multilingual SynWiki, has 2.5 billion tokens in 296 languages with balanced coverage across language families. We show that models trained on this dataset outperform those t

News media has been utilized as a political tool to stray from facts, presenting biased information and misleading narratives. This phenomenon can have significant impacts on public perception of events and issues in politics, leading to the spread of fake news and further distortion of reality. In this paper, we propose an approach that leverages news textual content to automatically detect biases. Our framework first extracts features using BERT (a language model), followed by training a classifier with a multi-layered CNN architecture. We evaluate our methodology by conducting experiments on two datasets: The HuffPost and the Politifact news sources. Our results show that our framework performs well in terms of classification accuracy and recall rate, achieving a high F1 score of 0.84 a

The ongoing Moore's Law extension into the post-Moore's Law era has led to the widespread use of accelerators, resulting in a dramatic increase in the number of software components in modern software systems. This, in turn, exacerbates the already challenging software engineering tasks of testing and debugging, as the combinatorial explosion of the number of potential software configurations increases the likelihood of encountering bugs. To address this issue, this paper proposes a novel framework for automated testing and debugging of software systems that employ accelerators. This framework utilizes a generative software engineering approach, which involves the automatic creation of domain-specific software components. These components are then used to automatically generate test cases, which are executed on the target software system to detect bugs. Furthermore, the framework employs a novel multi-agent debugging approach, where each agent is responsible for one or more components. This approach enables the isolation of faulty components, the identification of interacting faults, and the determination of the correct component-level fix. The results of our case study show that the proposed framework can automatically create valid test cases that exercise the functionality of the target software system and detect bugs, including race conditions and deadlocks, that are caused by interactions between components.

Procedure planning of instructional videos is a promising application of artificial intelligence in education, with the potential to improve the quality of online learning resources. In this task, an AI system analyzes a given procedure and generates an instructional video that explains the procedure step by step. While previous approaches mainly focus on generating natural language instructions, visual instructions have been shown to better support learning complex procedures. However, instructional videos that combine text and visualizations are still challenging to generate automatically. In this paper, we propose a novel knowledge-enhanced procedure planning approach that generates both textual and visual instructions. We introduce VISUALIZE, a pre-trained model that leverages external knowledge, such as textbooks, to generate visual instructions. We also propose an attention-based text-to-text model that learns to select the most relevant text from external knowledge as contextualized textual instructions. Our experimental results show that the proposed model generates more accurate and detailed instructions than previous baselines, achieving 7.5% and 1.8% improvement on visual and textual instructions, respectively. We hope that our work will further the development of intelligent tutoring systems and help students learn complex procedures more effectively.

Metrics are the foundation for automatic evaluation in grammatical error correction (GEC), with their utility directly impacted by the quality of the datasets they are built upon. Therefore, a critical step towards improving GEC metrics is to ensure the quality and diversity of the data used for training them. However, the current state of affairs does not guarantee a systematic approach for collecting or creating high-quality datasets. The lack of a standardized methodology leads to inconsistent labeling practices, which can result in incorrect or incomplete annotations that may skew metric performance. This paper presents a comprehensive framework for ensuring the quality of GEC datasets. We identify four key aspects of dataset quality: relevance, precision, recall, and completeness. Fur

Existing 3D mesh shape evaluation metrics mainly focus on the overall shape but are usually less sensitive to local shape deformations. We present a novel metric, Local Shape Deformation Rate (LSDR), that evaluates the local shape variations between two mesh shapes by calculating the average rate of change in each voxel's position or orientation within a user-defined region. To avoid potential ambiguity caused by varying regions and voxels, we propose a data-driven approach for automatically determining these values. In our experiments, LSDR outperforms existing metrics at capturing the details of local shape differences. Additionally, it allows users to easily compare the local shape differences between different models or datasets with different numbers of vertices. Our code will be publ

The security of cloud field-programmable gate arrays (FPGAs) faces challenges from a lack of formal verification methods and the absence of open source FPGA design tools. This paper presents a novel method for developing a formal specification language for FPGAs, with applications in hardware security and trustworthy computing. Our approach is based on using a domain-specific language to represent the specific properties of FPGAs, such as their memory organization and data transfer mechanisms, which are not available in existing languages like SystemVerilog or Verilog. We demonstrate our method's effectiveness by implementing an example verification tool for a simple FPGA, and show how it can be used to detect potential vulnerabilities that could compromise the security of FPGA designs. Interpretable computer vision models can produce transparent predictions, where the features of an image are compared with a human-defined semantic hierarchy. This helps understand what the model "sees", and thus makes it possible to detect and correct bias in these models. However, existing approaches that leverage this property have not been explored in depth for their effectiveness at identifying and removing biases from visual recognition models. In this work we show that by using interpretability as a proxy for understanding how and why biases arise, we can identify previously unreported biases in state-of-the-art object classifiers. We also demonstrate that while current techniques can help reduce bias, they do not remove bias entirely. Finally, we show how the interpretability proxy

Learning to Optimize (LtO) is a problem setting in which a machine learning (ML) agent must learn how to optimize an unknown environment. To address this problem, we present a novel approach that leverages the strengths of reinforcement learning (RL) and self-supervised learning (SSL). Our method involves training an RL agent with a reward function derived from the environment's feedback on its actions. We further utilize SSL techniques to train a policy network to generate diverse action sequences during exploration. The key insight is that by combining these two approaches, we can exploit the benefits of both RL and SSL for LtO. The proposed method was evaluated using four challenging environments. Compared to state-of-the-art methods, our approach demonstrated superior performance in te

This paper focuses on the challenge of answering questions in scenarios that are composed of rich and complex dynamic interactions between elements. The primary motivation behind this work is to explore how to facilitate a more efficient and effective approach for answering such questions, which may require knowledge about not only current elements but also their past evolutionary histories. In addition to providing a comprehensive review of existing approaches and research directions for querying dynamic graphs, we introduce the Dynamic Graph Querying (DGQ) problem by proposing an initial set of formulation. We further discuss the challenges faced in this domain, highlighting some of the open problems that remain unsolved. Finally, we offer several potential future directions for research

Machine Learning (ML), addresses a multitude of complex issues in multiple disciplines, including social sciences. However, the use of ML models in these areas is hindered by the fact that most existing models are trained using data collected and annotated by people who share the same culture as their creators, making them biased to different degrees. In this paper, we aim at proposing a novel methodology for developing fair machine learning models specifically tailored for minority groups from data gathered through crowdsourcing techniques. Our approach leverages a hybrid paradigm based on semi-supervised Active Learning (SL) and active transfer learning (ATL). It allows us to selectively extract samples from the source domain, which are then used to train a model that transfers the knowl

Big models have achieved revolutionary breakthroughs in the field of AI, but they might also pose risks to humanity. However, it is not easy to directly evaluate the risks associated with big models since they can be complex and opaque, especially when the model is trained on a large corpus without any direct supervision. In this paper, we propose a novel methodology to quantify the risk posed by a specific model to society. Specifically, we first construct a dataset containing risky sentences from open-source social media data. Then, we employ this dataset to train a fine-tuned model called RiskGPT2, which can automatically generate risky text with diverse topics and styles. Finally, using the generated texts as prompts, we obtain the risk scores for different models by measuring the simi

Image segmentation plays a crucial role in computer vision applications like self-driving cars, medical imaging, and robotics. However, it is notoriously challenging due to the complexities of identifying and differentiating objects within images. Recently, large language models (LLMs) have emerged as promising tools for various natural language tasks, but their potential in image processing remains unexplored. In this paper, we introduce an innovative LLM-based approach called LVISegmentation that leverages LLMs to perform image segmentation. Unlike traditional methods that rely on predefined object categories, our method employs LLMs' ability to generate text descriptions from images, enabling more precise segmentation of complex scenes. We evaluate our method on five challenging dataset

The growing complexity of cloud based software systems has resulted in incident management becoming an integral part of the software development lifecycle. While there is a lot of research on automating incident management, very little work exists on applying machine learning to this problem. In this paper, we present a comprehensive study of machine learning techniques for automated incident handling. We compare five common machine learning algorithms (Logistic Regression, Random Forests, Gradient Boosting Classifier, Support Vector Machines, and Artificial Neural Network) for their accuracy in predicting incident resolutions within 60 minutes and identify the most effective algorithm for each stage of the incident resolution cycle: root cause analysis, resolution, and post-mortem. Real-world systems are often formulated as constrained optimization problems. Techniques to incorporate constraints in learning algorithms and develop novel constraint relaxation methods for solving such tasks have attracted a lot of attention recently, but most existing studies assume that the true underlying model is known or can be estimated beforehand; thus ignoring their uncertainty (or robustness) aspect which has been extensively studied by statistics community over decades yet rarely touched upon on this topic from machine learners' perspective until very recent works [1]. We argue it makes no sense not considering uncertainties when dealing with real world data streams because there always exist various sources leading them into being noisy/uncertain -- e g., measurement errors du

Despite great advances in program synthesis techniques, they remain algorithmic black boxes. Although they guarantee correctness of the synthesized code with respect to a given specification and input data range (in particular), their soundness guarantees are not accessible by end-users because it requires deep knowledge about formal verification tools like SMT solvers or theorem provers that synthesize programs from specifications using logic formulas as inputs for these systems [1]. On top of this technical challenge lies an even bigger one: How do we understand what is going on under our eyes when running such synthesizers? In other words — how can users interpret output results without having access directly into them internally?. In order tackle both challenges described above , two a

Industry is at the forefront of adopting new technologies, and the process followed by the industry to adopt a technology can be described in terms used in software development. While there has been much discussion about how companies develop strategies for deploying digital transformation initiatives within their organizations—and many successful examples exist that demonstrate how this works well when done right—, little attention has focused on what happens next once these programs are complete: How do we know if they worked? Are our goals being met or not yet achieved because something was missed along with way down-the line implementation plan phase 1 through phases n+20 (or whatever number you choose)?

Identifying errors in parallel MPI programs is a challenging task. Despite the growing number of verifiers for MPI codes, they are mostly limited to sequential parts of programs and do not scale to large-scale parallel applications. In this work, we propose a novel approach for identifying errors in MPI codes, called mpi_lgtm. It uses a lightweight instrumentation that allows it to detect more than 90% of common errors in MPI applications. The proposed technique can be used as an auxiliary tool to assist programmers and to validate other, more complex verifiers. We evaluate mpi_lgtm on 711 real-world MPI applications from the OpenMPI test suite. On average, we find that mpi_lgtm identifies over 28 errors per application. We show that the majority of detected errors are correctable and requ

In this paper, we study nonparametric estimation of instrumental variable (IV) regressions with heteroscedastic noise. We propose a novel estimator based on the idea of "inverse regression" that combines the smoothness and efficiency of the kernel smoother with an inverse-regression formulation to obtain a consistent estimator. Moreover, we derive a uniform convergence rate for our estimator in terms of $\ell_2$-norm, which matches the minimax lower bound under mild conditions. Finally, we also propose a plug-and-play approach that uses the empirical distribution function of the data as the bandwidth. Numerical experiments are conducted to showcase the performance of the proposed method. The code is available at https://github.com/michaelzhang0618/IV_bandwidth.git.

The first is MAV-to-(Ground Control Station)-(GCS)-link that can be used to send the control commands from GCS and receive sensor data/video streams back at it with high reliability but limited bandwidth; while for achieving higher rate communications between an onboard camera on board as well its corresponding display in front end device such devices are usually mounted closer together within distance range $d$ . Therefore , when there exists no direct lineofsight(LOS), these types have been widely adopted since they offer more flexibility compared other methods like satellite networks which require complex infrastructure setup process or expensive maintenance costs due t

Diffusion models benefit from instillation of task-specific information into the score function to stear generative process. In this work, we introduce a novel framework for injecting contextual features in diffusion model's latent space by leveraging contrastive learning with auxiliary labels generated via unsupervised clustering techniques such as KMeans and Spectral Clustering using image patches extracted at different time steps during training phase or sampling stage (i). We demonstrate that our approach significantly improves both unconditional generation quality on standard benchmarks while achieving superior results over existing methods across various tasks including text2image synthesis(ii), music composition & audio reconstruction/generation,(iii) object detection&segmentation.

Video stabilization is a longstanding computer vision problem, particularly pixel-level synthesis solutions for video sequences. Recent works have explored the application of diffusion models to the task of video stabilization, wherein multiple frames are used as inputs and outputs. In this work, we propose an approach that leverages only one frame as input and output and applies diffusion to it. Our model utilizes both generative and discriminative components to optimize the stability of a given frame. A latent variable model and a pre-trained language model (LLM) serve as our generative component while a convolutional network acts as our discriminative component. We demonstrate that our method outperforms state-of-the-art methods by 7% on CUHK03 dataset using BLIINDS metric. Code will be

Medical open-domain question answering demands substantial access to specialized knowledge. Recent efforts have sought to decouple knowledge from model parameters, counteracting architectural scaling and allowing for training on common low-resource hardware. The retrieve-then-read paradigm has become ubiquitous, with model predictions grounded on relevant knowledge pieces from external repositories such as PubMed, textbooks, and UMLS. An alternative path, still under-explored but made possible by the advent of domain-specific large language models, entails constructing artificial contexts through prompting. As a result, "to generate or to retrieve" is the modern equivalent of Hamlet's dilemma. This paper presents MedGENIE, the first generate-then-read framework for multiple-choice question

A subset $S$ of vertices in a graph $G=(V, E)$ is Dominating Set if for each vertex $v\in V \setminus S$, there exists a neighbor $u\in S$. The minimum cardinality of a Dominating Set is called the Domination Number. In this paper we study the problem of computing the Domination Number of a tree with a known set $S$ of vertices and a non-negative integer $k$ as input. This problem has been proposed by Chen et al.~\cite{Chen-2018}. We prove that the complexity of this problem depends on the type of the tree. For example, if the tree is a star or a path then this problem is polynomial time solvable whereas it is NP-hard in general trees.

Agent Based Models (ABMs) have emerged as a powerful tool for investigating complex systems, such those found in biology and economics. An ABM is composed of multiple interacting agents with individual decision-making capabilities that lead to emergent behavior at the macroscopic level when taken together; however this comes along side various challenges associated both mathematically/computationally due their inherent nonlinearities & stochastic nature respectively while also making it difficult understand what makes them so successful compared traditional methods like differential equations or Markov chains which lack these features entirely! In order address some concerns around using Agent based models without having proper understanding how they work - we will explore ways you can app

Live programming environments blend the capabilities of interactive debuggers and conventional IDEs, providing an advanced means of program development that is centered around immediate feedback and experimentation. However, the view of the program that they present to the programmer is rather limited, being mostly focused on the local area of the program state where the programmer is interested in. This limited view can hinder the ability of the programmer to understand the broader implications of their changes, potentially leading to overlooking important aspects of the program's run-time behavior. In this paper, we explore the idea of cross-cutting perspectives on live program execution. Our contribution is a new live programming environment that presents a broader view of the program, showing not only local state but also highlighting the connections between the current execution and the larger program context. We show that this context-aware perspective is valuable for programmers, as it helps them to understand the potential effects of their changes and to reason about the program's run-time behavior.

3D occupancy prediction is a fundamental problem in autonomous driving. Although the 2D bird's-eye view (BEV) representation is widely used, directly predicting 3D occupancy from the BEV view using a monocular camera results in missing depth information. In this paper, we propose a fast 3D occupancy prediction network, named FastOcc, which fuses the BEV view and the perspective view to predict 3D occupancy. Specifically, we introduce a novel cross-view transformation with a shared weight design that can efficiently fuse the BEV view and the perspective view. To improve the model's fusion performance further, we adopt a lightweight 3D convolutional backbone with a depth-wise convolutional operation. Moreover, we propose a novel implicit depth estimation method based on the fused feature. The estimated depth map can be directly concatenated with the fused feature to obtain the final 3D occupancy prediction. Extensive experiments on the KITTI and NuScenes datasets show that our method can achieve state-of-the-art performance. The experiments also show that our method can be accelerated by deploying the BEV view and the perspective view on two different GPUs.

This systematic literature review explores sustainability assessment frameworks (SAFs) across diverse industries, with a particular focus on the textile and fashion industry. It aims to identify key evaluation criteria for assessing the environmental, social, and economic dimensions of SAFs, and to provide insights into their use in this context. By systematically comparing existing SAFs and evaluating their effectiveness, we aim to inform future research and practice, ultimately contributing to more sustainable practices within the textile and fashion industry. We identified 269 SAFs through database searches and hand-searching of relevant journals. Following an initial screening process, 47 articles were included for further analysis. These studies cover a wide range of industries includ

We introduce a new notion of neighboring databases for coverage problems such as Max Cover and Set Cover under differential privacy. In contrast to the standard privacy notion for these problems, which is analogous to node-privacy in graphs, our new definition gives a more fine-grained privacy guarantee, which is analogous to edge-privacy. We illustrate several scenarios of Set Cover and Max Cover where our privacy notion is desired one for the application. Our main result is an $\epsilon$-edge differentially private algorithm for Max Cover which obtains an $(1-1/e-\eta,\tilde{O}(k/\epsilon))$-approximation with high probability. Furthermore, we show that this result is nearly tight: we give a lower bound show that an additive error of $\Omega(k/\epsilon)$ is necessary under edge-different

Large language models (LLMs) have demonstrated remarkable capabilities in problem-solving. However, they often require long training and are sensitive to model poisoning attacks. To address these limitations, we propose the MiniModel, a novel paradigm that leverages the advantages of small pretrained language models without compromising on performance. The MiniModel comprises a simple architecture with a multi-head self attention module as its core. We explore various ways to implement this module for enhanced efficiency, such as using only one multi-head self attention block or utilizing only 32 heads. Through extensive experiments, we find that the best performing MiniModel can outperform GPT-2.7B in the majority of benchmarks while being over three times faster, making it an ideal solut

Deep learning methods for perception are the cornerstone of many robotic systems. Despite their potential for performance and autonomy, they remain challenging due to limited access to training data, reliance on complex architectures, and the difficulty in collecting large-scale dataset. To address these limitations, this paper introduces a novel framework leveraging an active learning (AL) approach for robotic vision to improve the quality of training datasets and accelerate model development. Specifically, we introduce an AL algorithm that is tailored to the specifics of robotics: it optimizes the tradeoff between generating effective training samples for deep neural networks without overburdening robots with unnecessary tasks. Our results show that this approach achieves superior perfor

Disasters know no boundaries, and the utilization of Artificial Intelligence (AI) in disaster management has the potential to save lives by enabling faster and more informed decision-making. However, developing AI models without considering the human-computer interaction aspects may result in sub-optimal performance. Therefore, it is crucial to design user-friendly data annotation interfaces for AI-powered analytic systems that consider the cognitive load of the annotators. In this paper, we aim to close the knowledge gap in designing data annotation interfaces for AI-powered disaster management analytic systems by (1) identifying the key cognitive processes involved in the annotation task, (2) quantifying the cognitive load of the current interface, and (3) proposing and evaluating an improved interface design. We adopt a mixed-methods approach, combining quantitative cognitive load assessment using the NASA-TLX questionnaire and qualitative cognitive load assessment through think-aloud protocols. We find that the current interface design increases cognitive load by 24.5% compared to the baseline design. Our proposed design modifications, including re-arranging the layout, hiding and showing elements dynamically, and employing consistent labeling, could reduce the cognitive load by 17.6%, 14.3%, and 10.3%, respectively. These findings demonstrate that involving human-computer interaction considerations in the early stages of development can significantly improve the design of AI-powered analytic systems.

Identifying critical nodes in networks is a classical decision-making task, and many methods struggle to strike the right balance between robustness against targeted attacks (e.g., node deletion) or random failures of connections on one hand versus accuracy for subsequent reconstruction tasks such as link prediction using only information about deleted edges/nodes after an attack has occurred [1]. Here we propose that this dichotomy can be resolved by utilizing graph evolution via diffusion processes instead - namely how links are formed over time through pairwise interactions among connected neighbors within our network structure itself at each step rather than simply applying external constraints like traditional approaches often do which leads directly into why it's difficult eve

Deepfake technology has given rise to a spectrum of novel and compelling applications. Unfortunately, the same generative models that power these exciting new use cases also enable malicious content creation with little effort or expertise required from an adversary—raising serious concerns about privacy violations in online communities such as social media platforms where deepfakes are frequently encountered by users every day without their consent [1]. In this thesis project we propose DeepFake Check (DFC), which is built on top-of state -ofthe art Generative Adversarial Networks(GANs) for detecting both text based adversarial attacks against face recognition algorithms via manipulation detection strategies using Natural Language Processing techniques alongwith visual analysis tools like

To enable large-scale and efficient deployment of artificial intelligence (AI), the combination of AI and software engineering (SE) is essential. In this paper, we present our perspective on the challenges in this domain. We argue that AI and SE are not only intertwined but also symbiotic. The challenges in this domain can be divided into two categories: those related to AI models and those related to data. For AI models, we focus on explainability, fairness, interpretability, reusability, and trustworthiness. For data, we highlight its availability, annotation, curation, privacy, and security. We believe that AI and SE should collaborate closely to address these challenges. We envision an AI-enabled future where AI technologies will be seamlessly integrated with SE processes, enabling eff

Protein design requires a deep understanding of the inherent complexities of the protein universe. While many approaches to protein design have been developed, they are often constrained by the availability of experimental data and the choice of optimization function. Here we present an unconstrained sampling approach that allows for the exploration of large chemical spaces using a small training set. We demonstrate this method with two test cases: (1) a small-scale folding problem in which a random sequence is refolded into an alpha helix without a template; and (2) a real-world case in which a small-molecule binder can be optimized for enhanced binding affinity to the target protein. In both cases, our approach achieves good results compared to other methods, and shows promise as a tool

Maximum codes in MRD codes are MRD codes with parameters $[n,k,k+1]_{q^t}$, where $n=kq^t+1$. In this paper, we give necessary and sufficient conditions for the existence of MRD codes of type $\langle x^{q^t},x+\delta x^{q^{2t}},G(x) \rangle$ having a MRD subcode of type $\langle x^{q^t},x+\delta x^{q^{2t}},F(x) \rangle$ with $\deg F(t)=\deg G(t)$. Using these conditions, we determine the possible parameters of MRD codes of type $\langle x^{q^t},x+\delta x^{q^{2t}},G(x) \rangle$ whose MRD subcode has type $\langle x^{q^t},x+\delta x^{q^{2t}},F(x) \rangle$ for some polynomial $F(x)$ with $\deg F(t)=\deg G(t)$. Moreover, we give a way to construct MRD codes of type $\langle x^{q^t},x+\delta x^{q^{2t}},G(x) \rangle$ having a MRD subcode of type $\langle x^{q^t},x+\delta x^{q^{2t}},F(x) \rangle$ with $\deg F(t)=\deg G(t)$.

Large Language Models (LLMs) have demonstrated remarkable reasoning abilities, prompting interest in understanding their reasoning process. However, the underlying mechanisms remain largely unknown. In this paper, we introduce a novel method to quantify the influence of an input variable on an output prediction from LLMs. Our approach involves modifying LLM responses by adjusting the values of relevant variables and measuring how the output changes as a result. We demonstrate that our approach accurately measures the influence of specific variables on the model's predictions, enabling us to identify important influential factors. Additionally, our method provides insights into the causal relationships between variables and predictions, allowing us to gain a better understanding of the unde

Highlighting particularly relevant regions of an image can improve the performance of vision-language models (VLMs), enabling them to extract fine-grained information. In this work, we propose a novel method for highlighting relevant regions of images using prompt learning. We introduce an approach that utilizes both text and visual inputs during training by combining visual grounding with masked language modeling. Our prompt-learning strategy is designed to efficiently learn the association between textual descriptions and corresponding visual features. This process enables our model to learn the correlation between different types of input data while simultaneously improving their performance in downstream tasks such as classification and retrieval. Additionally, we demonstrate the effic

Despite the many advances of Large Language Models (LLMs) and their unprecendented ability to generate text from human-like input, they are still severely constrained in handling data outside a fixed size. This limitation is particularly relevant for scientific domains where large datasets can be challenging or even impossible due to lack access rights with LLMS not being able directly deal well this type information without extra effort on behalf user which makes them less effective than traditional models when dealing complex problems involving multiple sources inputs like those found within field science research today - an area that requires careful consideration given its importance especially during times crisis such as pandemics etc..

Recent advancements in drone technology have shown that commercial off-the-shelf Micro Aerial Vehicles (MAVs) can be used to collect high-quality, actionable data at a fraction of the cost and with less risk than traditional surveying methods. However, MAVs are often unable to operate over long distances due to battery constraints and limitations of current wireless communication technologies. Furthermore, in many applications it is desirable for the MAV to operate autonomously without the need for ground control stations. To enable these applications, we propose an approach that leverages the power of edge computing by using unmanned aerial vehicles as mobile edge computing nodes. This approach enables the use of MAVs to perform computational tasks such as image processing and machine lea

Maturity models are used in many fields to evaluate the development of a practice, such as software development methodologies, security practices, and others. Comparing two given practices, usually represented as nodes in a graph, is of great interest. In this paper, we present a new approach to construct a graph that represents the relations between a set of given practices, in order to compare them. We use notions of quantum graph drawing, a recently proposed generalization of classical graph drawing that allows the representation of relations between entities in terms of quantum superpositions of classical drawings. We show that quantum graph drawing is suitable for representing the relations between practices as quantum superpositions of comparisons obtained from maturity models. We present a quantum algorithm that constructs a quantum graph representing the relations between a set of practices, given their representations as maturity models. We show that our algorithm runs in polynomial time. We implement a prototype quantum graph drawing algorithm for maturity models represented as binary decision diagrams, a compact representation of Boolean functions that allows polynomial-time algorithm design. Our experiments show that the drawings obtained are meaningful for small sets of practices. We also show that two practices that are far apart in a quantum superposition drawing are likely to be close to each other in a classical drawing. This is important because mature practices are usually well understood, and it is precisely the comparison of mature practices that is meaningful for practitioners.

Recent work shows that the effectiveness of these methods is highly dependent on their initial parameters, which limits further improvement by optimization-based approaches with only limited capacity for parameter search and exploration during training due mainly to high computational costs or difficulty finding suitable solutions among a large number candidate models trained from scratch using different sets initialization values randomly drawn according some distribution as well its inherent nature leading towards unstable learning process where even small changes can lead significant impacts upon performance outcomes thus making it difficul

Large Language Models (LLMs) have shown impressive results across various tasks in recent years. Despite their success, no previous work has evaluated their capabilities on architectural design, a complex and creative task. This paper aims to fill this gap by examining LLMs' potential in generating architectural design decisions. We choose to work with LLMs that are readily available and do not require finetuning. We present an exploratory study with three objectives: (1) to trigger LLMs' architectural design capabilities by providing them with prompts that resemble common sense knowledge, (2) to compare the LLMs' outputs to real-world architectural designs, and (3) to investigate the extent to which the LLMs adhere to the prompts. We select three LLMs for evaluation, namely GPT-3.5, GPT-4, and Claude-2. We find that the LLMs produce varied responses, often deviating from the prompts. Their responses resemble architectural designs in terms of style, but not in terms of functionality. GPT-4 is the most compliant with the prompts, followed by Claude-2 and GPT-3.5. Our study is a first step towards understanding LLMs' capabilities and limitations for architectural design. We hope that our findings will inspire further research in this direction.

Existing research often posits spurious features as "easier" to learn than core features in neural network optimization, but the impact of their relative simplicity remains under-explored. Moreover they mainly focus on the end performance intead of the learning dynamics of feature learning. In this paper, we propose a theoretical framework and associated synthetic dataset grounded in boolean function analysis which allows for fine-grained control on the relative complexity (compared to core features) and correlation strength (with respect to the label) of spurious features to study the dynamics of feature learning under spurious correlation. Our setup uncovers several interesting phenomenon: (1) stronger spurious correlations or simpler spurious features slow down the rate of learning for

This paper presents the second ChatGPT4PCG competition at the 2024 ACM International Symposium on Model-Driven Engineering Languages and Systems (ISMDA-L&S'24). The competition will be held in a hybrid format, with an online qualifying round followed by two rounds of face-to-face competitions. Large language models (LLMs) are increasingly being used for program comprehension tasks as they show remarkable abilities to understand natural language inputs and provide answers based on this understanding. While their ability to perform such tasks is well established, there is less evidence about how these methods compare against existing approaches for program comprehension, particularly when it comes to more complex and open-ended questions.

We introduce CLEVR-POC, a reasoning-intensive visual question answering dataset in partially observable environments. Agents must reason about objects and their properties, as well as the relations between objects to answer questions. The dataset consists of 8,000 environments, each containing 6x6 grid-connected rooms with up to 30 objects. There are 8 object categories, 4 material categories, and 2 texture categories. Each environment comes with 500 training and 100 test questions, encompassing a wide range of cognitive tasks such as object location reasoning, object counting, shape reasoning, and more. We propose a novel agent architecture, CLEVR-POC Agent, that includes a world model, question encoder, and answer decoder. The world model is a 3D transformer that infers the layout of the environment and the properties of objects based on partial observations. The question encoder converts the natural language question into a sequence of tokens. The answer decoder predicts the final answer token by token. With extensive experiments, we demonstrate that our proposed agent outperforms other visual question answering models by a large margin. We also perform a variety of experiments to evaluate the representation learning capability, reasoning capability, and partial observability reasoning capability of our agent. The official leaderboard can be found at https://github.com/THUMNLab/CLEVR-POC.

Text-to-video diffusion models generate videos by gradually diffusing latent noise into a sequence of intermediate representations, which are then decoded into videos. To generate coherent videos, the diffusion process needs to be constrained such that the intermediate representations are consistent with the textual description. Existing diffusion models constrain the diffusion process by either (i) directly regularizing the intermediate representations, (ii) introducing an additional neural network to estimate the representation distribution, or (iii) using a large number of training samples to improve the spatiotemporal consistency. In this paper, we propose UniCtrl, a novel training-free method to improve the spatiotemporal consistency of text-to-video diffusion models. Specifically, we introduce a unified attention control mechanism that imposes consistency constraints at both the representation and pixel levels. To control the consistency at the representation level, we leverage the self-attention module to measure the similarity between intermediate representations and the textual description. To control the consistency at the pixel level, we propose a pixel-level attention module to compute the alignment score between the intermediate image and the textual description. We show that UniCtrl effectively improves the spatiotemporal consistency of existing diffusion models without additional training. On the three text-to-video benchmarks, UniCtrl achieves +1.6/ +0.7/ +1.1 dB FID improvement on average over the baseline models.

The rise of Machine Learning (ML) in recent years has led to an increasing need for optimizing ML models for specific hardware platforms to improve their performance and resource utilization.
