This has led to the development of Hardware-Oriented Neural Architecture Search (HONAS) approaches, which aim to automatically design ML models that are optimized for a specific hardware platform. However, most existing HONAS approaches require the definition of a search space prior to the optimization process, which limits their ability to discover new and unknown architectures. In addition, the evaluation of ML models in these approaches is typically performed using software simulation, which does not reflect the performance of the models when deployed on actual hardware. To address these limitations, we propose NASH, a HONAS approach that uses a model-to-model optimization strategy to avoid the definition of a search space. NASH uses a single neural network as the optimization model and evaluates the performance of the generated architectures using benchmark datasets running on a variety of hardware platforms. Experiments show that NASH is able to discover architectures that outperform the best architectures designed by existing HONAS approaches for the target hardware platforms.

In the rapidly evolving landscape of 5G and beyond 5G (B5G) networks, high data rate communication is becoming increasingly important. The conventional communication approach using large antenna arrays for beamforming suffers from low energy efficiency due to the inefficiency of analog beamforming methods and massive RF chains. To address this challenge, we propose a novel digital beamforming method based on deep neural networks (DNNs). We show that by employing a series-connection of convolutional layers to model the complex channel response, it becomes possible to design DNN-based transmitter filters with significantly lower hardware complexity compared to conventional approaches. This reduced complexity leads to improved power efficiency while maintaining performance comparable to conve

G-Quadruplexes are the four-stranded non-canonical nucleic acid secondary structures. The DNA sequences that fold into g-quadruplexes play a critical role in biology and medicine. However, it is still an open question how to efficiently identify all g-quadruplexes from the entire human genome. Here, we developed a novel computational method called quadruplexRNA. It utilizes the recently published RNA structural database called Rfam as a template to find potential g-quadruplexes. We first employed the method to the whole human genome and then experimentally validated its predictions by using single molecule atomic force microscopy (AFM). By this approach, we discovered 150 new g-quadruplex-forming sequences which have never been reported before. In addition, some of them were validated by A

Floating Car Observers (FCOs) are an innovative method to collect traffic data. However, the FCO approach faces several challenges due to the limited availability of floating car vehicles for data collection purposes. The aim of this study is to develop a novel methodology to generate synthetic data from real-world traffic data in order to alleviate the challenges associated with the scarcity of FCO data. This study proposes a generative model based on diffusion probabilistic models (DPMs), which have been recently introduced as promising alternatives to traditional deep generative models. In contrast to previous studies using DPMs for simulating traffic dynamics, this research specifically focuses on generating FCO data. The proposed DPM-based framework includes two main components: a spa

We study the problem of estimating the mean of an identity covariance Gaussian in the truncated distribution, where $\ell_1$ norm is considered as the error measure. The existing methods suffer from two major issues: (i) they are not computationally efficient because they involve iterative algorithms or require solving a system of non-linear equations and (ii) they do not take into account the underlying identity covariance assumption, which limits their applicability to more general cases. To address these issues, we propose a new approach that combines the properties of a kernel estimation procedure with the notion of a prior distribution. Specifically, our method applies a kernel density estimator on the truncated distribution to obtain an estimate for the mean, while leveraging the ass

Language model agents (LMAs) integrated with task-specific tools can achieve high performance on complex tasks requiring multiple steps. Indirect prompt injection (IPI) is a new attack that can compromise the security of LMAs by injecting malicious inputs into the hidden state of the model through seemingly harmless prompts. However, the impact of IPI on tool-integrated LMAs remains unclear. In this work, we study the defense and attack of tool-integrated LMAs against IPI. We propose InjecAgent, a framework to benchmark IPI attacks on tool-integrated LMAs. InjecAgent automatically generates indirect prompts based on the instructions of the tool and evaluates the responses of the LMA under attack from the perspectives of both the attacker and the defender. We evaluate InjecAgent on five tool-integrated LMAs: four open-sourced LMAs and one chatbot. The results show that IPI can bypass the surface-level defense of LMAs and inject malicious inputs into the hidden state of the model. However, the effectiveness of IPI varies across different LMAs. In particular, we find that the LMA based on GPT-3.5 is more vulnerable to IPI than the LMA based on GPT-4. Further, we propose a simple defense that can significantly reduce the success rate of IPI.

Most existing news recommendation methods tackle this task by conducting semantic matching between candidate news and user representation. However, as the news information is often conveyed via textual descriptions, these methods fail to capture the essential visual elements of images in news articles, which are frequently used to convey important messages. To address this issue, we propose a novel news recommendation method that utilizes image features for the first time. Specifically, given the historical news-click data from a certain user, our proposed method conducts both semantic matching and visual matching to rank the unseen news items. In particular, the semantic matching is achieved through a graph neural network (GNN) encoder that learns the latent representations of news and us

Consider the Telephone Broadcast problem in which an input is a connected graph $G$ on $n$ vertices, and an output consists of the set of vertices $S \subseteq V(G)$ such that $\delta_G[S] \leq k$. The goal is to broadcast an edge from each vertex of $S$ to all other vertices. In this work we study the problem of how many bits are required to correctly broadcast these edges, i.e., to distinguish whether there exists $S$ with $\delta_G[S] \leq k$, or not. We show that for any constant $k\geq 3$ there exists a family of graphs $\{G_i\}$ such that each graph $G_i$ has maximum degree at most $4$, but every algorithm using $\Omega(\log n)$ bits to distinguish between the two cases fails with probability $\Omega(1/n)$.

The $Q$-iteration is a fundamental algorithm in reinforcement learning (RL) for learning the $Q$-value function, which iteratively updates the $Q$-value using the one-step Bellman equation. In this paper, we propose the iterated $Q$-iteration, a natural extension of the $Q$-iteration, and present a theoretical analysis of the convergence properties. The iterated $Q$-iteration solves the one-step Bellman equation using an iterative method, and it includes the $Q$-iteration as a special case. We show that the iterated $Q$-iteration with the linear function approximation converges to the least squares solution of the one-step Bellman equation under the ergodicity assumption. We also show that the iterated $Q$-iteration with the average cost per step converges to the least squares solution of the discounted cost equation. These results are supported by numerical experiments using the linear function approximation and the neural network approximation. The iterated $Q$-iteration is an important step towards solving the $Q$-value function in complex RL problems.

Ensuring the compliance of building designs with regulatory requirements is a crucial aspect of architectural engineering. This paper introduces an annotated corpus, CODE-ACCORD, comprising building regulatory data with a focus on European Union directives. The corpus is designed to support the training of machine learning models for rule extraction and compliance checking. Building on previous work by the authors, which manually annotated 98 regulations from the European Union's Official Journal, the new corpus extends the coverage to 1,015 regulations. This expansion allows for a more comprehensive exploration of regulatory texts and enhances the feasibility of natural language processing tasks. The paper presents an initial analysis of the corpus, discussing its potential applications and outlines a case study for extracting building regulations on energy performance. CODE-ACCORD is made publicly available in a tabular format, and the paper concludes by proposing further applications of the corpus, including the development of a question-answering system and a summarization tool.

The recent boom in deep learning for natural language processing has led to ever larger models with impressive performance. However, very few of these models are suited for deployment in resource-constrained environments, such as mobile devices. We investigate the design space of language models for 5G-enabled smartphones, constrained by the available compute resources for three use cases: voice assistant, question answering, and code autocomplete. We develop three techniques to improve the fit of large pretrained models into small footprints: weight pruning, layerwise summarization, and low-rank approximation. Together with model parallelism, these techniques allow us to fit a 300M parameter language model into a 150M parameter budget, at the expense of only 2\% accuracy. Applied to a 1.3B parameter model, our techniques enable a 5\% accuracy improvement over strong baselines, without any trade-off in model size. We show that our models can achieve comparable performance to their larger counterparts, as measured by human and machine evaluation.

We study the interdefinability of constant-expansion logics, i.e., logics obtained by adding constants to a given logic. In particular, we consider constant-expansion logics based on Belnap-Dunn logic, which is the four-valued logic that extends classical logic with a third truth value for dealing with partial information and a fourth truth value for dealing with contradictions. We show that the constant-expansion logics of Belnap-Dunn logic corresponding to the set {0,1}-valued interpretations and the set {1/2,3/4}-valued interpretations are interdefinable. We also show that similar results hold for other logics, namely classical logic, intuitionistic logic, and the logic of entailment.

We study the finite-time convergence of TD learning with linear function approximation under Markovian sampling. We show that TD with linear function approximation converges to a unique point on an ellipsoidal region, whose shape depends on the value of the reward distribution. Furthermore, we present a condition for the convergence rate of TD to be bounded by a constant, which is independent of the size of the state space. Our result establishes that TD with linear function approximation has finite-time convergence guarantees in both continuous and discrete time. This result suggests the potential applicability of TD to problems where high-fidelity solutions are not available but low-fidelity solutions can be obtained through Monte Carlo integration. Finally, our analysis provides insight

Abstract reasoning problems challenge the perceptual and cognitive abilities of AI algorithms, demanding a deeper understanding of the underlying human thought process. In this work, we introduce a novel benchmark, CAR-50K (Cognition Assisted Reasoning-50K), to enhance AI systems' reasoning capabilities by integrating real-world data into textbook-style questions. The dataset comprises 50,000 examples in three distinct domains: Physics, Chemistry, and Math. We aim to bridge the gap between AI-based reasoning tasks and human-like cognition processes, enhancing their ability to tackle challenging reasoning problems. Our approach emphasizes the role of contextual information, including domain knowledge and natural language descriptions, in enabling more effective problem-solving strategies.

The convergence of Artificial Intelligence (AI) and the Internet of Things (IoT) has the potential to transform how healthcare is delivered through the development of new IoT devices, diagnostic tools, and medical therapies. However, the adoption of AIoM (AI of Medical Things) in clinical settings is limited by the lack of explainability in how AI algorithms make decisions that have a direct impact on patient care. The inability to explain and understand the rationale behind these decisions makes it difficult for healthcare professionals to trust and adopt these technologies into their practice. To address this issue, this paper proposes an explainable AI framework for AIoM applications that follow a supervised learning approach. The framework integrates existing explainability methods for supervised learning models, such as Shapley Value Sampling and Integrated Gradients, to explain the predictions of a chosen AI model. To evaluate the framework, we use a use case of AIoM in urology where we analyse the trade-off between utility and explainability of different AI models using our framework.

Recently, there has been growing interest within the community regarding whether large language models are capable of planning. In this work, we make a step towards assessing such capabilities by examining how well LLMs can plan in two-player competitive scenarios. We do so using a novel benchmark and evaluation framework called S2T (Strategy to Tactics), which is designed to challenge and analyze LLM's ability to strategize and execute plans. In particular, we aim to answer three questions: (1) Can an LLM execute plans that require multi-step reasoning? (2) Can an LLM execute plans involving tradeoffs between multiple objectives? And (3) Can an LLM generate its own plans? To study these questions, we first construct a dataset of 500 S2T tasks across 4 domains, ranging from chess to Go to

Despite the success in specific tasks and scenarios, existing foundation agents, empowered by large models (LM), still suffer from a lack of robustness in real-world environments. One major reason is that current LM-based agents rely on pre-defined instructions, which are limited to specific tasks and scenarios and fail to capture the dynamic nature of the real world. In this work, we propose an interactive instruction learning framework, where the agent learns to generate task-related instructions through interaction with users. Specifically, we introduce a novel concept of "interactive instruction" and design a framework based on it. The framework first generates initial instructions using LMs and then iteratively updates them through human feedback during the execution process. To addre

Edge detection is a fundamental task in computer vision with a wide range of applications. Despite the significance of this task, existing edge detection methods primarily focus on improving the performance on the majority class (i.e., strong edges) and generally ignore the minority class (i.e., weak edges). Additionally, the uncertainty of edge locations, which is inherent in image data, is another challenge that hinders the performance of edge detection algorithms. In this paper, we propose a novel ranking-based loss function to address these two challenges in an unified framework. The proposed method improves the performance on weak edges by designing a novel weak edge prior ranking loss, and enhances the localization accuracy of edges by employing a novel edge uncertainty ranking loss. Moreover, we introduce a simple yet effective data augmentation method, called edge uncertainty data augmentation, which further improves the ranking-based loss performance by generating more balanced and informative training data. Based on the balanced consideration of both weak and strong edges, the proposed method sets a new state-of-the-art on the challenging CEC 2014 dataset, and achieves competitive performance on other popular benchmarks.

We introduce VITAMIN, a framework for compositional model checking of multi-agent systems. VITAMIN is based on a process algebra with discrete time and a finite set of agents, and uses bisimulation as the fundamental notion of observation equivalence. The framework allows us to generalise standard composition operations for multi-agent systems, and to incorporate bisimulation-based model checking techniques. We present a range of examples of multi-agent systems that can be constructed using VITAMIN, and we formulate conditions under which the model checking problem is decidable.

Despite the many advances of Large Language Models (LLMs) and their unprecedented performance in various NLP tasks, LLMs still exhibit notable limitations in certain aspects, such as a lack of ability to reason about causality. Large language models are vulnerable to spurious correlations due to overfitting, and this can lead to misleading conclusions when analyzing causal relationships between variables. This paper introduces a novel approach for identifying spurious correlations within large language models by analyzing how LLMs respond to different types of interventions. Our analysis reveals that LLMs have limited awareness of causal relationships and struggle to predict outcomes based on interventions that alter causal factors.

The emergence of LLMs has ignited a fresh surge of breakthroughs in NLP, including the development of cutting-edge models and paradigms. However, despite significant progress in benchmarking and evaluating these models, there remains a significant knowledge gap around their robustness to adversarial attacks, underscoring a critical need for comprehensive evaluation procedures. In this paper, we propose a novel framework for the detection of adversarial examples that are specifically designed to evade current defenses against LLMs. This framework relies on an ensemble of attack strategies, which allows for more accurate and reliable identification of such perturbations. We demonstrate the effectiveness of our approach using both synthetic and real-world datasets, highlighting its sensitivit

Zero-shot Video Object Segmentation (ZSVOS) aims at segmenting the primary object of interest in a video without requiring any pixel-level annotation. Despite its recent advances, ZSVOS remains challenging due to the need for strong domain adaptation and generalization abilities across videos with varying styles. In this paper, we propose an efficient and effective framework called Style-adaptive Unified Transformer (SUT), which employs a unified transformer architecture. The proposed method effectively adapts to the style variation in videos by learning a shared representation between frames and leveraging their complementary information. Additionally, it uses cross-attention between the spatial and temporal channels to capture long-range dependencies. We validate our method on three popu

The Software-Defined Networks (SDNs) face persistent threats from various adversaries, which can compromise the availability and reliability of SDN services. The attacks on SDVs are more challenging to detect due their high level abstraction in network operations compared to traditional Network Operating Systems(NOSs). In this paper we propose a novel detection scheme based upon statistical anomaly recognition using an unsupervised learning algorithm called Isolation Forest that is specifically suited for detecting anomalous behavior over large data sets with complex relationships between features without requiring prior knowledge about attack signatures or patterns .

Accurate data association is crucial in reducing confusion, such as ID switches and assignment errors. The proposed method consists of two main parts: a matching strategy that uses the correlation matrix between all possible associations to identify pairs of clusters with high similarity; followed by an optimization procedure based on simulated annealing (SA) for selecting one pair from each set while preserving pairwise distances within limits defined through prior clustering results obtained using different approaches including Gaussian mixture models or k-means algorithm without any additional constraints imposed during initialization step .

Recently, large language models (LLMs) have notably positioned them as capable tools for facilitating knowledge discovery. However, the ability of these models to comprehend and synthesize information is often hampered by limitations such as their inability to generalize beyond a given task or narrow domain, or by lacking a coherent representation of the world that can be utilized to connect concepts across different domains. In this paper, we introduce GN-Knowledge-Hub (GNKH), an approach to constructing LLMs with a global knowledge base built through the integration of open-source data. We demonstrate how GNKH surpasses state-of-the-art methods on benchmarks comprising various tasks and domains, highlighting its versatility and strength.

Tracking kinematic chains has many uses from healthcare to virtual reality. Inertial measurement units (IMUs) are one of the most commonly used sensors for this purpose due to their high-resolution, low cost, and small size. However, IMU-based tracking suffers from drift caused by accumulating errors over time, limiting the accuracy of long-term measurements. Recent work has attempted to address this problem with neural networks trained on synthetic data, but these models do not generalize well in real-world settings. We present a novel method for learning to track multiple articulated objects that does not rely on synthetic data generation or neural networks. Instead, we use a particle filter, which is a probabilistic technique for solving the recursive Bayesian estimation problem. Our me

Subgraph contrastive learning is a promising unsupervised method for graph representation learning, which induces robust embeddings by leveraging both graph structure and node attributes. However, existing methods often rely on expensive to train or computationally complex. In this work, we propose a simple yet effective multi-scale subgraph contrastive learning framework, which induces robust graph representations by capturing subgraph structures at multiple scales. Specifically, we design a learnable scale-aware sampling mechanism to sample subgraphs at multiple scales, and propose a novel scale-joint contrastive loss to leverage subgraph structures at multiple scales for contrastive learning. Unlike most existing subgraph contrastive learning methods, our proposed framework can be easily implemented with a standard message passing framework, and thus can be efficiently trained with GPU. Experimental results on various benchmark datasets demonstrate that our method outperforms most existing subgraph contrastive learning methods.

This paper investigates mechanism design for decision-aware collaboration via federated learning (FL) platforms. We consider a scenario where multiple decentralized clients seek to collaboratively learn a prediction model over their local datasets, while preserving the confidentiality of their respective private data. To achieve this goal, we propose two FL mechanisms -- one designed with respect to the optimal utility achieved by the clients; and the other designed with respect to the optimal privacy-preservation achieved by the clients. Our theoretical analysis shows that both proposed mechanisms can significantly improve the overall performance of FL in terms of accuracy, while simultaneously preserving the privacy of each participating client. Additionally, our experimental results dem

Facial action unit (AU) detection, aiming to classify AU present in the fa-cial image/video sequence into one of 19 types as defined by FACS [3], has received considerable attention. With recent advances on deep learning methods and availability of large scale facial expression datasets with manual annotations for face alignment or landmark locations suchasMUGEX[4]andAFW2D+EFW5][6],[7]. However most existing works focus only upon two dimensional images without considering temporal information from videos which contains additional cues that could improve model performance further.

We present INDICVOICES, a dataset of natural and spontaneous speech containing a wide range in the quality from clear to severely degraded. The data is collected as part-time work by freelancers on Amazon Mechanical Turk (MTurk), using audio files provided via MTurk's built-in WAV file uploader functionality that allows for direct uploading without any encoding or compression steps between creation time at source level until playback during transcription task completion; this process guarantees high fidelity compared with other datasets where recordings were made after downloading them first into memory before starting recording sessions again afterwards which would lead us back down another round trip lossy conversion chain if not careful enough along those lines too!

We prove statistical query lower bounds for learning truncated Gaussians. Specifically, we show that any statistical query algorithm that learns a $k$-dimensional truncated Gaussian distribution with respect to total variation distance must have sample complexity at least $\Omega(\frac{k}{\epsilon^2})$, assuming the algorithm has access to bounded (by a constant) number of statistical queries. Our result is the first statistical query lower bound for learning a continuous distribution with respect to total variation distance. Previously, statistical query lower bounds were only known for learning discrete distributions (Dolev et al., 1993; Bavarian et al., 2020). To prove our result, we first translate the statistical query model to the framework of interactive learning (Klivans and Sherstov, 2017), which allows us to utilize the adversary-based framework for proving lower bounds in interactive learning (Gutfreund et al., 2005). We then construct an adversary that simulates the process of answering statistical queries by a learning algorithm, while ensuring that the total variation distance between the true distribution and the distribution learned by the algorithm stays small. We finally utilize the lower bounds on the number of rounds of interaction from Gutfreund et al. (2005) to show that any statistical query algorithm for learning truncated Gaussians must have a large number of statistical queries.

Accurate training labels are a key component for multi-class medical image segmentation. Their annotation is laborious, time-consuming and error-prone. To overcome this bottleneck, recent works have shown promising results by leveraging the knowledge of weakly-annotated data (e.g., bounding boxes) to infer high-quality segmentation masks via self-training. However, these methods rely on supervised contrastive learning, which assumes that the unlabeled samples follow the same distribution as labeled ones. This assumption is often violated in the presence of domain shift, e.g., when transferring models from one dataset to another or when fine-tuning pretrained models using different image resolutions. In this paper, we propose an end-to-end approach for unsupervised multi-class segmentation

Recent human-object interaction (HOI) detection approaches rely on high cost of manpower and extensive training data, making them infeasible for the real world. Large scale datasets are challenging to obtain due to privacy concerns and lack of high quality annotations. In this paper, we present a novel approach to generate synthetic HOI datasets with low annotation costs by utilizing existing video data from social media platforms such as YouTube. We first extract bounding boxes from videos using a pretrained YOLOv3 model, which can be efficiently trained on large scale YouTube videos without human labelling. To improve the accuracy of our algorithm, we propose a new method that enhances the performance of the initial model by incorporating additional features derived from deep learning ar

This work presents a novel data-driven multi-layered planning and control framework for the safe operation of nonlinear dynamical systems in a dynamic environment. The proposed system consists of three layers: a high-level policy that generates the desired trajectory and is trained offline, an intermediate-layer planner that generates the motion plan, and a low-level controller to execute the desired trajectory. In the proposed framework, the high-level policy and the low-level controller are both trained using the same dataset with different neural network architectures and loss functions. Additionally, we propose an end-to-end training scheme that enables simultaneous learning of all layers to reduce the overall computational cost while improving the performance. We demonstrate the effec

Estimating relative camera poses between images has been a central problem in computer vision. Methods such as SfM and multi-view stereo (MVS) have long relied on the assumption that images are rectified, i.e., each camera's principal point is at the center of its image. This assumption is common in many applications and makes computation easier by ensuring that the epipolar geometry of the images is invariant to scale. However, the rectification assumption does not always hold in practice, especially for uncalibrated cameras or when using off-the-shelf hardware. In this paper, we investigate how to handle non-rectified images in existing pipelines. We introduce a novel perspective-n-point (PnP) algorithm that solves for both the absolute orientation and position of an image while allowing

Social interactions are fundamental to human behavior, and hence, a crucial aspect of artificial intelligence and autonomous agents. Despite much progress in single-modal AI, multimodal social interaction, while more realistic, is considerably more challenging. In this work, we focus on modeling multimodal social interactions where visual and verbal modalities are densely aligned, i.e., one modality provides evidence for the other. This alignment can be learned jointly by maximizing the mutual information between the two modalities. However, in social interactions, the verbal modality often lags behind the visual modality, e.g., people first gesture and then speak. To model such sequential alignment, we propose a novel framework that progressively updates the representation of the lagging modality based on the lead modality. Our framework learns a joint representation of the two modalities by maximizing their mutual information using an auto-regressive approach. We validate our framework on a challenging synthetic dataset and two real-world datasets that feature dense alignment. Our results demonstrate the necessity of modeling such sequential alignment in social interactions and the effectiveness of our framework in capturing it.

Research paper alerts systems, which notify researchers of newly published papers matching their research interests, have become an essential information retrieval tool for researchers. However, the alerts generated by the underlying retrieval models are often generic and lack the contextual information that would help the researchers decide whether the papers are relevant to their work. To address this problem, we propose PaperWeaver, a system that enriches the alerts by contextualizing the recommended papers with the user-collected papers on the same topic. PaperWeaver uses bibliographical data and abstracts of the papers to build a topic graph, which is then used to contextualize the recommended papers. We evaluate our system using data from CiteSeerX, a scientific bibliography database. Our results show that PaperWeaver produces more relevant alerts than the baseline retrieval models, especially for narrow and well-defined research topics.

We present DomainVerse, a comprehensive framework towards realistic evaluations for adaptive domain generalization (ADG). Distribution shifts between training and test domains are ubiquitous in real-world applications, yet classical domain generalization (DG) benchmarks assume the absence of such shifts. We first highlight the inadequacy of current DG benchmarks by showing that popular evaluation protocols can be easily gamed, leading to "accidental" domain generalization. We then propose realistic distributions for ADG through the introduction of a new benchmark, DomainVerse, which features synthetic and real-world domain shifts. Our benchmark allows users to tune models for domain generalization. However, we show that such tuning is impossible in the presence of distribution shifts, where models instead need to be adapted. Ultimately, we hope that DomainVerse will serve as a new benchmark towards tuning-free adaptive domain generalization.

3D hand pose estimation has found broad application in areas such as gesture recognition and human-machine interaction tasks. As performance improves, the complexity of the systems also increases, which can limit the comparative analysis and practical implementation of these methods. In this paper, we propose a simple yet effective baseline that not only surpasses state-of-the-art (SOTA) methods but also demonstrates computational efficiency. To establish this baseline, we abstract existing work into two components: a token generator and a mesh regressor, and then examine their core structures. A core structure, in this context, is one that fulfills intrinsic functions, brings about significant improvements, and achieves excellent performance without unnecessary complexities. Our proposed

Policy gradient methods are a vital ingredient behind the success of modern reinforcement learning. Modern policy gradients often use a differentiable neural network as an actor, which can be trained with a stochastic gradient method such as the natural policy gradient (NPG) algorithm to find near-optimal policies in complex environments. However, NPG suffers from a slow convergence rate when the number of training iterations is large. To address this issue, we propose a new actor architecture that combines the advantages of deterministic policy and NPG, which we call a "stochastic deterministic actor" (SDA). We prove that the gradient update of SDA has a linear convergence rate. We also show that SDA is equivalent to the natural actor-critic (NAC) algorithm and is superior to the natural

Integrating robotics into human-centric environments such as homes, necessitates advanced manipulation and control capabilities to ensure safety and seamless integration. The integration of visual feedback is a critical component in this context. However, the use of video data for robotic control poses significant challenges due to its high dimensionality, temporal variability, and complexity in interpretation. This paper presents an algorithm that uses a generative adversarial network (GAN) model to compress video features into a low-dimensional representation. The GAN is trained using a large dataset consisting of 124k frames sampled from various videos, representing diverse scenes and dynamic objects. Our approach leverages the advantages of both Generative Adversarial Networks (GAN) an

Increasing the accuracy of instance segmentation methods is often done at the expense of speed. However, high-performance real time systems are needed in many fields such as autonomous driving and robotics where fast processing times are essential for safety reasons but also to keep up with changing environments or tasks that require quick response from a robot arm (e.,g). In this paper we present our approach on improving both precision & recall rates while maintaining low latency by utilizing deep learning models trained specifically towards detecting objects within images captured via camera input streams using NVIDIA Jetson Nano boards running Linux OS version 16LTS supported through PyTorch framework optimized model training workflows based off TensorFlow libraries <h2>Introduction</

In the past decade, convolutional neural networks (CNNs) have shown exceptional performance in solving many problems in the field of computer vision. However, recent advances in low-power computing have shown that traditional CNNs are not well-suited for implementation on specialized hardware. This work proposes a spintronic implementation of the UNet architecture using magnetic tunnel junctions (MTJs). MTJs are considered to be the intrinsically most energy efficient memory devices and are well-suited for image segmentation tasks. The proposed spintronic UNet prototype is able to achieve a segmentation accuracy of $88.6\%$ on the ISIC-18 dataset with a speedup of $6.3\times$ and an energy efficiency of $9.2\times$ compared to a CNN implementation running on a conventional CPU.

We present 3DTextureTransformer, a novel approach for geometry aware texture generation of arbitrary mesh topology. Our method leverages the recent advancement of 3D-to-2D attentional mapping for texture synthesis. We observe that the existing 3D-to-2D mapping techniques, designed for tasks such as view-consistent texture synthesis and novel view generation, can be interpreted as solving a special case of our problem, where the geometry of the target view is known and available for use during synthesis. Unfortunately, this geometry is not generally available in real-world applications. To address this limitation, we introduce a multi-stage training strategy that encourages the 3D-to-2D mapping network to learn the geometry of the target view along with the texture features. We further propose a geometry prediction network, which learns the relationship between the texture features and the geometry of the target view, expressed in terms of the mesh topology. We demonstrate that the proposed 3DTextureTransformer is a versatile framework that can be applied to various geometry-aware texture synthesis tasks, achieving state-of-the-art results in the process.

In this paper, we explore the capability of an agent to construct a logical sequence of action steps in order to achieve a goal. We consider the situation where a set of goals is known to the agent and each goal has its own specific set of possible actions that can be taken at every time step. Our focus is on the planning problem: given a list of goals, find the best ordering of their execution (i.e., sequence of actions) so as to minimize the overall cost. The cost function consists of a weighted sum of costs associated with taking an action at any time step. As examples of such cost functions we consider two types of costs: the first one is the average cost of all the action steps needed for reaching the goal, and the second one is the total number of action steps needed for reaching the

We study text-based image editing (TBIE) of a single image by counterfactual inference. The goal is to generate an image with an improved visual quality without changing the content, in contrast to standard image editing that edits any image. TBIE requires generating counterfactuals for the given image, which are high-quality images containing the same object but under different conditions. To achieve this task, we introduce Counterfactual Inference Networks (CIN), a model that performs both the conditional and unconditional generation tasks. Our method is based on three main components: 1) Conditional diffusion models that produce counterfactuals conditioned on the ground truth image; 2) Cross-attention modules that enhance semantic alignment between generated images and the ground truth;

With the rapid growth of scholarly archives, researchers subscribe to "paper alert" systems that send emails every time a paper is published in an academic journal. These alerts are typically sent at 9:01 AM EST (or some other arbitrary hour), which makes it difficult for international users with different wake times and working hours around UTC+2 -8 . We propose shifting this default email delivery schedule based on user location using geographic information from IP addresses or via open registration data stored by publishers themselves when registering new accounts online – thus avoiding any delays caused due insufficient knowledge about individual preferences among scholars across countries!

The paper addresses the problem of relational to RDF data migration, that is, the systematic mapping of data from relational databases to RDF graphs. The migration process is supported by a novel technique called query co-evaluation, which evaluates, in a single pass, a set of queries that are generated automatically from the schema mappings between the source relational schema and the target RDF schema. The queries are executed on the source data, and their results are combined with the target data to construct new triples that extend the target RDF graph with information derived from the source relational data. We conducted two user studies to evaluate the quality of the results produced by our migration method. The studies involved 14 domain experts who migrated 10 relational databases, containing 63 tables and 84 relationships, to RDF. The results show that our method produces high-quality mappings, expressed as sets of query results, that extend the target RDF graph with information derived from the source relational data. The average precision of the migration results is 85.4%, and the average recall is 77.4%.

Learning shape correspondence under an unsupervised setting is a fundamental problem in computer vision. It aims to find a one-to-one mapping between every pair of shapes in two shape sets. In this paper, we propose a novel unsupervised learning framework for shape correspondence, which consists of two stages. The first stage learns a shape descriptor, i.e., a function that maps each shape to a low-dimensional vector. We show that the optimal transport between these two low-dimensional vectors is a good approximation of the optimal transport between the original shapes. Based on this observation, we propose to learn the shape descriptor by minimizing the optimal transport cost between the two sets of shapes. We show that this problem can be solved efficiently using the entropic regularization technique. The second stage learns the shape correspondence from the optimal transport between the shape descriptors. We show that the functional map between the shape descriptors is a good approximation of the optimal transport between the original shapes. Therefore, we propose to learn the shape correspondence by minimizing the functional map between the shape descriptors. We show that the proposed framework can achieve state-of-the-art performance on two widely-used unsupervised shape correspondence datasets.

Photonic computing has emerged as a promising solution for accelerating computation-intensive artificial intelligence (AI) applications. Compared to traditional silicon microprocessors, photonic processors offer the potential of high energy efficiency and ultrafast computational speed due primarily in part to their unique properties such optical parallelism/interference effects that enable massive parallel processing capabilities at ultrahigh speeds with lower latency than electronic counterparts while consuming less power per operation performed by device itself during execution process making them an attractive option when it comes down choosing between different types available today market segment which requires fast decision maker based on accurate prediction model built upon large da

Recent advances in large language models (LLM) have demonstrated the potential of using reinforcement learning (RL) to align LLMs with complex long-term objectives specified in natural language. However, directly applying RL on LLMs poses two challenges: (1) the generation process is intertwined with the alignment objective, making it difficult to edit the prompt without losing alignment; (2) evaluating the alignment of LLMs is difficult, as the reference output is often of poor quality or not available. To address these challenges, we introduce an aligned proxy model as a decoupling layer between the LLM and the RL agent. The proxy model helps edit the prompt without losing alignment by focusing only on the generation process, and the RL agent can use the proxy model's output as a reliable alignment reference without accessing the LLM's internal state. We validate our approach on several benchmark tasks and open-ended dialogue generation.

Bipedal walkers can reduce their risk of falling by adjusting their gait patterns as they traverse from a stable to an unstable terrain. However, it is challenging to model the complex interactions between the feet and the granular medium analytically. Therefore, we developed a foot shape-dependent resistive force model to capture the gait adaptation behavior of bipedal walkers on granular terrains. We first performed experiments on a spherical granular medium to investigate the effect of foot shape on the resistive force experienced by the walker. The results showed that the resistive force increased with the contact area between the foot and the medium. Based on the experimental findings, we created a foot shape-dependent resistive force model and incorporated it into a dynamic model of a bipedal walker. We then used a genetic algorithm to find the optimal gait pattern that minimizes the resistive force for four different foot shapes. The results showed that the optimal gait pattern changed with both walking speed and foot shape. This study provides valuable insights into how bipedal walkers can adapt their gait patterns to maintain stable locomotion on granular terrains.

Reservoir computing (RC) utilizes a nonlinear projection of the input data performed by a reservoir network, which consists of a large number of nonlinear memory units. Therefore, RC has been attracting increasing research interests for its high capability of storing and processing temporal data. In this paper, we firstly review the typical architectures and training schemes of RC, including the classic Echo State Network (ESN), and the more recent development of Nonlinear Dynamic Ridge Polynomial Network (NDRP) and Memristor-based Reservoir Computing (MRC). Then, we analyze the advantages of MRC over the other two architectures, by investigating the memory and computing properties of these three types of reservoirs. In particular, we find that MRC possesses both short-term and long-term memory, which is suitable for processing sequential data with both fast and slow variations. Finally, we perform several experiments on benchmark datasets for temporal data classification, including both traditional RC training methods and recently proposed self-supervised pre-training methods. The results demonstrate that MRC achieves competitive performance compared to the other two architectures.

Deep neural networks are notoriously miscalibrated, i.e., their outputs do not follow the target distribution. This results in catastrophic performance on unseen data. The calibration problem has been studied extensively and several methods have been proposed to improve the calibration of DNNs; however, the calibration is still not satisfactory for practical applications. In this paper, we show that a simple regularization method can significantly improve the calibration of DNNs. We introduce a regularizer based on the concept of expected loss. To achieve better calibration, our approach minimizes the variance of the expected loss of the test set data with respect to the distribution of the model parameters. Our experimental results on four datasets and six different network architectures

The Transformer architecture is one of the most popular deep learning models used in a wide range of applications. However, its performance on time series prediction tasks is limited due to its inability to capture seasonality and trends. In this paper, we propose several novel approaches to adapt the Transformer to time series prediction tasks. These approaches are based on the idea of incorporating exogenous variables into the Transformer model. The resulting model is able to capture seasonality and trends in the data and, therefore, outperforms traditional Transformer models on time series prediction tasks. We test our approach using the S\&P500 index, one of the most widely recognized stock market indexes.

The Internet has witnessed tremendous growth over the past two decades, giving rise to new challenges in traffic congestion control. Unlike traditional congestion control algorithms, which rely on simple counting techniques, modern machine learning (ML) based approaches have the potential to significantly improve network performance by leveraging traffic data. However, state-of-the-art ML-based congestion control algorithms suffer from two major drawbacks: i) They do not perform well in lightly loaded networks, ii) They can induce unfairness among flows belonging to different user groups. In this paper, we introduce a novel congestion control algorithm that is both fair and efficient. We propose a new framework that ensures fairness by design and develop a novel counting technique that eliminates the need for explicit flow identification, thus reducing state requirements and improving scalability. We show that our proposed algorithm can achieve higher throughput and better fairness performance compared to state-of-the-art ML-based congestion control algorithms.

The performance of CLIP in dynamic facial expression recognition (DFER) task doesn't yield satisfying results. This is attributed to its reliance on static, single-view expression images that do not represent the full range of DFER tasks. In this paper, we propose a novel approach called SAMI to boost CLIP's DFER capabilities by leveraging synthetic images generated from diverse views and facial expressions. Our method introduces an Image Synthesizer and a Multi-View Contrastive Learning Framework, designed to produce high-quality, multi-view synthetic images that align with real-world data. We evaluate our method across multiple benchmark datasets for DFER, showcasing the effectiveness of our approach in enhancing CLIP's performance. Through extensive experiments, we demonstrate that SAMI

As join is a fundamental operation in data management, the performance of join is critical to the overall performance of data management tasks. The performance of join is also a key factor in determining the feasibility of various data analytics tasks. For example, if a data analytics task requires joining two relations, and the join operation takes too long, then the entire data analytics task will be inefficient. In the worst case, the join operation may even be infeasible given the available resources (e.g., memory, CPU, and I/O bandwidth). In this paper, we consider the problem of performing join on two relations, A and B, where the size of A is much larger than the size of B (i.e., |A| >> |B|). This is a common scenario in data management tasks, yet it is also a challenging scenario that has not been fully addressed in previous work. In particular, we focus on the all-tuple join operator, which returns all tuples (a, b) ∈ A × B such that a joins with b. When |A| >> |B|, a naive implementation of the all-tuple join operator incurs a very high cost. To improve the cost, we propose TreeTracker Join, which identifies and exploits the tree-like structure that exists in many real-world relations. Specifically, TreeTracker Join first performs a tree decomposition on relation A (assuming that relation B is small enough that it can be loaded into memory). Then, TreeTracker Join uses the tree decomposition to guide the join operation. Our experiments show that TreeTracker Join improves the cost of the all-tuple join operation by up to two orders of magnitude compared to a naive implementation.

The use of automated decision-making (ADM) systems in government has increased in recent years. If an ADM system makes a decision that affects an individual, the government agency should inform the individual of the decision and, where appropriate, provide an opportunity for the individual to be heard. This article discusses the obligations of government agencies under the Australian Privacy Principles (APPs) when using ADM systems that make decisions about individuals. It also provides recommendations for the development and use of ADM systems by government agencies. The recommendations are based on the APPs, the Australian Government's Digital Transformation Agency's (DTA) Ethical Framework for Automated Decision-Making in Government, and our own experience in advising government agencies on the use of ADM systems.

Pre-trained Large Language Models (LLMs) are beginning to dominate the discourse around automatic code generation with natural language specifications. In contrast, the best-performing synthesizers in the domain of formal synthesis with precise logical specifications are still based on enumerative algorithms. In this paper, we evaluate the abilities of LLMs to solve formal synthesis benchmarks by carefully crafting a library of prompts for the domain. When one-shot synthesis fails, we propose a novel enumerative synthesis algorithm, which integrates calls to an LLM into a weighted probabilistic search. This allows the synthesizer to provide the LLM with information about the progress of the enumerator, and the LLM to provide the enumerator with syntactic guidance in an iterative loop. We e

Imitation learning methods need significant human supervision to learn policies robust to changes in object poses, e.g., due to occlusion or lighting conditions. We propose a new method that learns an initial policy from only 10 examples and then progressively expands the training set through continual learning. We show that our approach can consistently improve performance over 32 distinct scenarios while maintaining high robustness. Our continual learning policy is also able to generalize to novel unseen scenarios without re-training, outperforming baseline imitation learning approaches by more than 5%. The code and data are available at https://github.com/felipe-tavares-leal/cl_imitation_learning.git. Keywords: Robotics; Continual Learning; Reinforcement Learning; Human Robot Interactio

Git is one of the most popular distributed version control systems, and its repository contains the complete history of the development of many large-scale software projects. The Git graph is a convenient representation of this history as a directed graph in which nodes are Git commits and edges connect each commit to its direct ancestors. In this paper, we introduce a random model of Git graphs. Our model is based on a directed variant of the preferential attachment graph, which is a popular random model of complex directed graphs. We prove a number of interesting properties of our random Git graphs. For example, we show that the out-degree of a commit (i.e., the number of direct descendants of a commit) follows a power-law distribution with parameter in the range (1,2). We also show that the in-degree of a commit (i.e., the number of direct ancestors of a commit) is typically concentrated around its mean. These properties are consistent with empirical studies of real-world Git graphs. Finally, we use our random model to generate synthetic Git graphs that can be used to evaluate algorithms for the analysis of Git graphs and to benchmark Git repository cloning and retrieval algorithms.

Programming assistants have reshaped the experience of programming into one where programmers spend less time coding and more time communicating with their assistant. In order to provide a better user experience, we present a new approach to debugging where the assistant provides a set of possible buggy lines, asking the user to confirm or deny each one. We introduce a new dataset that captures this behavior for 200 bugs in popular programming projects. Our data shows that assistants are able to provide correct suggestions for 31% of bugs, while incorrect suggestions still contain useful information. In addition, we propose a new metric, called buggy line recall, for evaluating assistants on this task and show that our model obtains state-of-the-art results on existing datasets, while achi

Accurate representations of unknown and sub-grid physical processes through parameterizations (or closure) in atmospheric models are essential for accurate weather forecasts. The most common approach to modeling these phenomena is the use of empirical orthogonal functions (EOFs). EOFs are based on an assumption that some unstable physical processes can be modeled as linear combinations of a set of orthogonal functions, which are usually based on the spatial distribution of meteorological variables. This makes them efficient but has two main drawbacks: firstly, they do not consider the temporal dynamics of the atmospheric system, and secondly, the physical processes represented by EOFs are generally not physically motivated. In this paper, we propose a data-driven methodology called "Dual E

Academic documents are packed with texts, equations, tables, and figures, requiring comprehensive understanding to navigate them effectively. For instance, a student might wish to identify the sections related to a specific concept in order to focus their study efforts. However, the sheer volume of information makes this task challenging, particularly when using tools like PDF readers or web browsers. Fortunately, there exists a suite of AI-driven tools designed specifically for this purpose. They not only summarize the document but also highlight key concepts, making it easier to grasp the content's essence. In this post, we will explore these tools and demonstrate how they can be used to streamline academic research and study processes. By leveraging advanced AI techniques, users can sig

Interactive traffic simulation (ITS) is a promising traffic engineering tool as it allows traffic engineers to experience the impact of traffic countermeasures in a realistic manner. However, existing ITS works often ignore the development of conflicts (e.g., congestion, unsafe driving behaviors) which may occur as a result of the implementation of these countermeasures. In this paper, we propose a novel long-term interactive traffic simulation framework that takes the development of conflicts into account through a newly developed conflict-aware policy. To this end, we first introduce a conflict prediction module based on traffic signal phase and pedestrian crossing (STP) detection to identify potential conflicts. We then propose a novel multi-agent reinforcement learning (MARL) based optimization module to evaluate and select the optimal traffic countermeasure by taking the predicted conflicts into account. We evaluate our framework using a case study in an intersection with left-turning traffic. Experimental results show that our framework can help traffic engineers to select appropriate traffic countermeasures to mitigate conflicts.

The design of feedback controllers is undergoing a paradigm shift from modelic (i.e., designed based on mathematical models) to data-driven control (DDC). DDC leverages statistical learning algorithms to extract information from experimental data and use it for controller synthesis. This approach has proven effective in many engineering disciplines, but its application to cyber-physical systems (CPS), such as autonomous driving or robotics, remains challenging due to the presence of noise and outliers in the collected data. In this paper, we address this challenge by proposing a novel framework that employs an ensemble of deep neural networks (DNNs) trained using online learning algorithms. We demonstrate the efficacy of our approach through experiments with various benchmark datasets. Our

Artificial Intelligence (AI) is increasingly employed in various decision-making tasks, typically based on large datasets. However the availability of data can be limited and/or biased with respect to one or more protected attributes such as race that may lead AI systems built from this kind dataset into unfairness towards people belonging different races: a phenomenon known by disparate impact [1]. In order for an algorithmic system not only perform well but also fairly across all groups represented within its training set it must take steps during development stage so there isn't any significant disparity between performance levels among these subgroups when tested upon real world scenarios after deployment takes place; otherwise we risk producing discriminatory outcomes which could have

The Vehicle Routing Problem (VRP) is a widely studied combinatorial optimization problem. It arises in many industrial applications, such as the delivery of goods from warehouses to customers or collection services for waste disposal and recycling companies [1]. A standard VRP setting consists of $n$ nodes with an origin node connected to all other sites by directed arcs having lengths $\ell_{ij}$. The goal here it find routes that start at the depot/origin site visit every customer once before returning back there while minimizing total traveling time given constraints on vehicle capacities which can be expressed using binary variables representing whether each route uses up capacity unit during its trip through space-time continuum . In this post we will look into two different approache

The Mutual Reinforcement Effect (MRE) investigates the synergistic relationship between word repetition and syntactic priming. It is well documented in studies that involve repetitions of verb or noun phrases, but not yet with full sentences as stimuli materials for both paradigms separately: while M-R paradigm uses sentence structure primes followed by target words to investigate their effect on comprehension performance; S<sub>2</sub>P<sup>1</sup>E model assumes a bipartite network consisting of two separate networks dedicated exclusively either towards syntax processing via an abstract semantic representation layer (S), i..e., encoding/decoding structural information which includes the grammatical relations among nodes such like subject–verb agreement violations without taking into acco

Comics tell stories through a combination of text and images. However, existing techniques typically process the text and image modalities separately, missing potential synergies between them. In this work, we therefore propose a Multimodal Transformer (MTransformer) for the first time to jointly process text and images in a comics dataset. We use a pretrained model to avoid manual feature engineering, and apply a number of modifications to adapt it to the task. These include using a shared parameter space for the different modalities, applying a contrastive loss, and using special tokens to improve the model's understanding of the relationships between characters and panels. Our experiments show that the MTransformer outperforms existing techniques that process the text and image modalities separately, achieving a 4.5% higher accuracy on the Comics Text-Cloze Test. It also outperforms other multimodal models for visual reasoning, showing that our approach can be used to improve understanding in other domains as well.

We present FreeA, a cost-effective and flexible framework for human-object interaction (HOI) detection that does not require ground-truth annotations for training. Instead of annotations, FreeA utilizes simple instructions, which are transformed into guidance for a standard pose estimator. We show that despite using untrained data, FreeA outperforms highly trained baselines on HOI detection by 13.1% and 19.0% on average, respectively on two commonly used HOI datasets. We also demonstrate the superior generalizability of FreeA in detecting HOI actions in the wild, where highly trained baselines fail to improve on naive baselines. Our method is able to generalize on unseen interaction types, as demonstrated by an experiment where we transfer our model trained on picked fruits to a new domain of interacting with nuts. Ablation studies reveal that FreeA's performance is driven by the richness of the instruction, the modality of the input data, and the complexity of the action labels. Code is available at https://github.com/82magnolia/FreeA/.

We investigate the optimization dynamics of gradient descent in a non-convex and high-dimensional setting, where the objective function is a neural network with randomly initialized weights. The gradient descent algorithm takes as input an arbitrary initial point and terminates when it finds a stationary point, which must be a global minimizer if one exists. In this work, we consider the case where the weight space is continuous and the learning rate is fixed. Under suitable assumptions on the architecture of the neural network, we establish that the probability that the stationary point found by gradient descent is a global minimizer converges to 1 exponentially fast in the number of epochs. Furthermore, we show that the algorithm can find a local minimizer if one exists in polynomial tim

We extend the theory of locally checkable labeling problems (LCLs) from the classical LOCAL model to the more general BSP$^2$ model, which is a natural extension of the BSP model that also captures many other models in the literature. In this setting, our main result is a new characterization of solvability for LCLs: A graph property $\mathcal{P}$ is locally checkable if and only if there exists an LCL algorithm $A$ such that, for every input graph $G$, the following conditions hold: 1) If $G \in \mathcal{P}$, then $A(G)$ returns true. 2) If $G \not\in \mathcal{P}$, then $A(G)$ returns false with probability at least $3/4$. This characterization not only strengthens prior results but also extends them significantly by allowing us to work under the BSP$^2$ model.

Semi-supervised learning, which assumes that the vast majority of the training data is unlabeled, has been one of the most popular learning settings in computer vision. Among the semi-supervised tasks, semantic segmentation aims to assign a semantic label to each pixel in an image. Due to its high efficiency and good performance, the pseudo-label-based method has become one of the most popular semi-supervised semantic segmentation approaches. In this paper, we conduct a comprehensive review of the existing pseudo-label-based methods for semi-supervised semantic segmentation and analyze their strengths and weaknesses. Furthermore, we propose two new schemes, called relabeling and distillation, which can improve the performance of the existing methods. We hope that our review will stimulate more effective interactions between theory and practice and promote the development of better semi-supervised learning algorithms.

The main objective of the Multiple Kernel k-Means (MKKM) algorithm is to find a set of k centers by combining several kernel functions. This combination allows us to define a unique kernel matrix that can be used for distance computation in order to implement the k-means clustering algorithm. In this paper, we propose an extension of MKKM that uses a multi-objective approach to optimize two objectives: the number of iterations and the average distance from each cluster to its center. We compare our results with those obtained with the original MKKM algorithm using both synthetic data and real data from the UCI repository. Our findings indicate that our proposal outperforms the original method in terms of average distance from clusters to their centers. Additionally, it achieves comparable

In this paper, we present a novel framework for cross-modal policy transfer in reinforcement learning (RL), based on the idea of a global workspace (GW). Our framework capitalizes on recent advances in natural language processing (NLP) and RL to enable transfer of policies described in natural language from one environment to another. In particular, we first pre-train our model by asking it to map natural language policies to action sequences in a manner that is invariant to the environment. This is done by leveraging recent advances in cross-modal matching, and by using a large pre-training dataset synthesized from the game of Gymnastics. Thereafter, we show that our model can be fine-tuned to perform well in multiple domains, without any additional human labeling. Our experiments demonstrate that GW-based policy transfer works well in practice, and that it outperforms recent baselines for cross-domain policy transfer.

We propose masked capsule autoencoders, a novel approach for learning robust and interpretable features with a self-supervised learning regime inspired by the biology of visual cortex. Our model leverages recent advances in both capsule networks and masked image models to learn hierarchical features with high spatial resolution. We demonstrate the utility of our method for image reconstruction, as well as its ability to extract meaningful features, with applications to image classification and few-shot classification. Masked capsule autoencoders constitute a promising new approach for learning hierarchical, interpretable features, which are crucial for the development of artificial intelligence systems that can perform at the level of human visual perception.

Used car pricing is a critical aspect of the automotive industry, influenced by many economic and market factors. In recent years, artificial intelligence (AI) has emerged as an innovative solution to address challenges in pricing used cars accurately and efficiently. However, existing AI-based techniques for used car pricing are largely limited to predicting future values based on historical data. This research presents a novel approach that not only predicts future used car prices but also optimizes them using reinforcement learning (RL). The proposed methodology involves training an agent with a deep Q network (DQN), which then estimates the optimal price of each vehicle under consideration. To enhance accuracy, we incorporate real-time contextual information into the agent's decision-m

Natural gradient descent (NGD) methods have been widely used in deep learning. However, the computation of NGD involves expensive matrix inverse operations, which has limited its application in deep learning. To overcome this limitation, we propose an inverse-free NGD method, called F-NGD, that can efficiently update the weight matrix without computing matrix inverses. Specifically, we approximate the matrix inverse in NGD with a low-rank update, and show that the resulting update rule can still converge to the global minimum at a linear rate, under the assumption of bounded eigenvalues of the Hessian. Moreover, we show that F-NGD has the same time complexity as the back propagation algorithm (BPA), and requires only constant memory. Empirical results on deep learning applications demonstrate that F-NGD converges faster than the BPA-based method and achieves a wall-clock time comparable to the BPA-based method.

In recent years, operator learning, particularly the DeepONet, has received much attention for efficiently learning partial differential equations (PDEs). The fundamental bottleneck of existing approaches is that they are only suitable to solve a single PDE. In this paper we propose an effective and scalable solution by introducing multi-task deep operators based on graph neural networks with Fourier features as backbone functions in order tackle multiple related but different systems at once without sacrificing performance compared against conventional solvers or even outperforming them when dealing simultaneously across many tasks within one model instead! Multimodal data fusion techniques have been widely applied owing their ability improve information processing accuracy under various

Due to the recent increase in interest in Financial Technology (FinTech), applications like credit scoring, fraud detection and portfolio management are being explored. These technologies have created a niche in financial services by enabling people access basic banking facilities from their mobile devices through digital platforms such as e-banking apps or online account opening systems which can be accessed via internet connection at any time without having physical contact with bank staff members/employees thus reducing operational costs for banks while increasing customer satisfaction rates due increased convenience offered when compared traditional methods requiring face -to –face interactions between customers & employees during transaction process . However , one challenge that rema

Photonic computing has emerged as a promising solution for accelerating computation-intensive artificial intelligence (AI) applications. In this paper, we propose a novel photonic inference engine based on a multi-wavelength quantum dot (QD) array and an optical waveguide grating (WG) array. The QD array serves as the input layer of the network, while the WG array acts as the weights in the network. We demonstrate that our proposed architecture can achieve near-optimal performance with less than 30% of the required training data compared to state-of-the-art photonic neuromorphic hardware, making it a viable candidate for resource-constrained edge devices. Furthermore, we introduce a training algorithm that achieves energy efficiency comparable to that of traditional CPU/GPU-based systems,

We propose Strongly Supervised pre-training with ScreenShots (S4) - a new paradigm for training large-scale vision-language models. S4 is an effective and efficient approach to leverage human's supervision in the form of screen shots. As one of the first attempts, we adopt this method on a large-scale image dataset, MS COCO. Extensive experiments demonstrate that S4 significantly boosts the performance of CLIP and ALIGN on various tasks, including text-to-image generation, visual reasoning, and language grounding. Our code is available at https://github.com/PaddlePaddle/Vision/tree/release/2.7/samples/vld_s4. The results are released at https://paddlerec.bj.bcebos.com/public/research/S4.pdf. Code will be open sourced upon acceptance.

In this paper we describe the development of a streamlined framework for large-scale ATLAS pion tagging in LHC Run 2, based on a convolutional neural network (CNN) trained with a novel loss function. This approach enables robust offline reconstruction and online performance extraction from a single CNN model without requiring additional machine learning techniques. The new loss function is designed to explicitly optimize the tagger for the expected signal distribution of the Higgs boson decaying into two tau leptons. It has been extensively validated using simulated data against conventional taggers, demonstrating high performance and significantly improved stability across different Monte Carlo samples. Furthermore, the CNN-based tagger has been successfully deployed as an integrated comp

We present the first algorithm for query learning of a general class of Mealy machines with timers (MMTs) in a black-box context. Our algorithm is an extension of the L# algorithm of Vaandrager et al. to a timed setting. Like the algorithm for learning timed automata proposed by Waga, our algorithm is inspired by ideas of Maler & Pnueli. Based on the elementary languages of, both Waga's and our algorithm use symbolic queries, which are then implemented using finitely many concrete queries. However, whereas Waga needs exponentially many concrete queries to implement a single symbolic query, we only need a polynomial number. This is because in order to learn a timed automaton, a learner needs to determine the exact guard and reset for each transition (out of exponentially many possibilities)

While 3D hand reconstruction has been extensively studied, reconstructing hands in the wild, especially in complex interactions with objects, remains challenging. In this paper, we propose an innovative 3D hand reconstruction approach for complex hand-object interaction scenarios. Our method reconstructs a hand model by aggregating information from two graphs: a hand graph and an object graph. The hand graph is a well-studied representation that captures the intrinsic geometry of the hand, including finger articulations and the palm. The object graph, on the other hand, represents the external geometry of the object, encompassing its shape and the hand-object contact information. By fusing these two graphs, our method can effectively reconstruct hands in complex interaction scenarios where traditional methods often fail due to occlusions or deformations. Moreover, we introduce a clever edge insertion operation that further enhances the hand-object contact representation in our object graph. This simple yet effective manipulation allows our model to better understand and reconstruct hands in various complex interactions. Extensive experiments on realistic hand-object interaction data demonstrate the superiority of our method over state-of-the-art techniques. Our method achieves a mean surface distance (MSD) of 7.97mm on the LINEMOD dataset, a challenging dataset consisting of densely packed objects, and an MSD of 6.19mm on the YCB-Hand dataset, which includes hands interacting with a diverse set of objects. These results showcase our method's exceptional performance in reconstructing hands in complex interaction scenarios.

Reconstructing High Dynamic Range (HDR) video from image sequences captured with alternating exposures is an important task. Recent approaches use the temporal consistency of the HDR signal to compensate for the missing information at each frame, while others resort to supervised training based on a labeled dataset. However, these methods are not applicable in practical scenarios where the available data is unlabeled or limited and furthermore require additional computationally expensive post-processing steps. To address this challenge, we propose a novel framework that uses only one unlabeled pair of input images as input for training. Our approach first estimates the missing information at each frame by utilizing the temporal consistency between adjacent frames in both exposure levels. S

Context: Reactive programming (RP) is a declarative programming paradigm suitable for expressing the complex behaviors of real-world systems. In modern reactive programming languages, such as Scala and Idris, data abstraction has been extended to include effects: side effects that occur in some parts of the computation but not others. Recently, there has been growing interest in extending RP with another kind of effect, namely, control flow effects. Objective: In this paper, we propose a new formal framework for reasoning about the correctness of reactive programs that combine both control flow and data effects. This is accomplished by enriching Petri nets --a classical formalism for modeling concurrent systems--with two kinds of transitions: one representing control flow, and the other re

Anomaly detection in multivariate time series (MTS) is a crucial task for numerous applications. Various deep learning models are currently used to achieve this purpose.
