However, given the high complexity of these models, there is a lack of understanding of how each model learns from MTS data and what kind of data characteristics affects the learning performance. In this paper, we aim to explore the influence of dimensionality reduction on anomaly detection performance in MTS. To this end, we propose a novel pipeline that incorporates variational autoencoders (VAEs) as a dimensionality reduction technique and several representative anomaly detection algorithms. We conduct experiments on four benchmark datasets that contain MTS data and employ VAEs to reduce the data dimensionality. Then, we evaluate the performance of our model under various anomaly detection algorithms. The results demonstrate the effectiveness of our approach for improving the detection performance for normal classes and reducing the false positive rate for anomaly classes.

Programming assistants have reshaped the experience of programming into one where programmers spend less time on mundane tasks, and more time working on high-value activities. However, despite their recent successes, the current state of programming assistants is not yet sufficient for the needs of everyday coding. In particular, programming assistants are typically designed to work with code in a language-agnostic way (i.e., without relying on domain knowledge about each language). Such an approach has been successful for large languages such as C++, Java, or Python; however, it comes at the cost of requiring extensive manual effort from developers to provide additional information that is relevant to their specific context of use. In this paper, we propose a new assistant design paradigm

Image colorization is the task of inferring a colorful representation from a grayscale input. This paper introduces ColorMap, a novel approach for interactive image colorization. Our method leverages an encoder-decoder architecture with attention-based interactions. The encoder extracts semantic features from the input image, while the decoder uses these features to generate colorized output. Our attention-based interaction model learns channel-wise correlations in the encoder's feature map and establishes a semantic-guided color propagation path from the encoder to the decoder. This mechanism enables a novel interactive color palette exploration paradigm, providing semantic insight and reducing the distance between the generated colorization and the user's intent. We also introduce a simple yet effective grayscale image augmentation strategy to enrich the training data and facilitate the learning process. Extensive experiments demonstrate that ColorMap produces competitive colorization results compared to state-of-the-art methods. Its interactive feature enables users to easily explore various color palettes and styles, offering more control and flexibility in the colorization process. The implementation is accessible for reproducibility purposes on https://github.com/yuancan/ColorMap.

3D asset generation is getting massive amounts of attention, inspired by the recent success of text-to image generative models. Existing methods generate 2048x1536 pixel images with many artifacts and inconsistencies between different views due to their reliance on a single input frame for generating all frames in an animation sequence (i..e., they do not leverage temporal information). In this paper we propose \textit{Temporal Guided Textual Image Synthesis}, or TGITS: it takes as inputs both temporally aligned pairs from real data videos alongwith corresponding captions generated via CLIP + GPT; then uses these alignments alongside other techniques suchas self supervised learning algorithms that have been proven effective at capturing motion dynamics within natural environments before pr

Managing complex Cyber-Physical Energy Systems (CPES) requires solving various optimization problems with multiple objectives and constraints. As distributed control architectures are becoming more popular in CPES for certain tasks due to their flexibility, robustness, and privacy protection, multi-objective optimization must also be distributed. For this purpose, we present MO-COHDA, a fully distributed, agent-based algorithm, for solving multi-objective optimization problems of CPES. MO-COHDA allows an easy and flexible adaptation to different use cases and integration of custom functionality. To evaluate the effectiveness of MO-COHDA, we compare it to a central NSGA-2 algorithm using multi-objective benchmark functions from the ZDT problem suite. The results show that MO-COHDA can appro

Graph databases are emerging as a popular means of storing and querying semi-structured data. Unlike tabular databases, graph databases store data as a collection of vertices and edges, and queries often rely on traversing the graph. As graphs can be highly connected, this can result in expensive query performance. Optimising queries in graph databases is therefore different from traditional query optimisation, and requires different techniques. In this paper, we introduce SBQO, a novel approach to query optimisation for graph databases. We propose a new cost model that takes into account the expected number of hops a query will traverse, and the expected size of the data. We introduce a new set of heuristics that use the cost model to estimate the cost of query plans, and are able to identify the most efficient plan for a given query. We evaluate SBQO on a real-world graph database, and show that it is able to find the optimal query plan for a set of queries, both in terms of execution time and memory usage.

Network function virtualization (NFV) aims to bring the benefits of virtualization to the traditional network functions (TFNs) such as firewalls, load balancers, and proxy servers. Multi-tenancy is an important aspect of NFV, as it allows the aforementioned network functions to be shared among multiple virtual networks (VNets), thus improving the utilization and reducing the latency. However, existing NFV solutions typically focus on single-tenancy, which assumes that each VNet requires its own dedicated network function instance. This not only leads to high resource consumption and management overhead but also fails to leverage the economies of scale inherent in multi-tenancy. In this paper, we address these limitations by introducing MTS, a multi-tenant NFV system for virtual networking. MTS utilizes a tenant-aware networking architecture that enables it to support both isolated and shared network functions within a VNet. It employs a flexible resource allocation scheme that can allocate resources based on tenant policies, ensuring that the quality of service (QoS) requirements are met while maximizing the resource utilization. We validate the benefits of MTS through experiments, demonstrating that it can improve the resource utilization by up to 7X and reduce the latency by up to 3X compared to existing NFV solutions.

We study the dynamics of moral behavior in populations of learning agents interacting with each other and with a changing environment. We represent the moral behavior of an agent with a simple model based on two beliefs: the first is about the goodness of the agent itself, the second is about the goodness of others. We consider two kinds of learning agents, those with and without empathy, and we let them interact with each other and with an environment that rewards prosocial behavior. We find that empathy promotes the emergence of prosocial behavior in homogeneous populations of learning agents. We also find that in heterogeneous populations, if the fraction of empathetic agents is not too small, then empathy slows down the convergence of beliefs and promotes the emergence of prosocial behavior.

Clustered Regularly Interspaced Short Palindromic Repeats (CRISPR) and CRISPR-associated protein 9 (Cas9) have revolutionized the field of genome editing, offering a precise method to modify DNA sequences in living organisms. This technology has been widely employed for gene knockout, gene knockin, and base pair editing across multiple species including plants, animals, and microbes. In this review, we present a comprehensive overview of the applications of CRISPR/Cas9 in plant research. We discuss the development of CRISPR/Cas9 technologies and their implementation in model plants such as Arabidopsis thaliana and rice. Furthermore, we explore the expansion of CRISPR/Cas9 into crop plants, with an emphasis on crop improvement and stress resistance.

Graph-level contrastive learning, aiming to learn the representations for each graph by contrasting two graphs, has achieved significant success in various graph representation learning tasks. However, existing contrastive learning approaches either suffer from high computational cost or struggle with large model sizes when applied to large graph-structured datasets. To address these issues, we propose a novel Graph Contrastive Learning framework based on Neural Ordinary Differential Equations (ODEs), namely ODEContrast. Specifically, this framework introduces an ODE-based dynamic contrastive learning scheme which is capable of capturing the sequential information during training and thus enhances the discriminative power of the learned representations. Moreover, it is equipped with the mu

Higher-order patterns reveal sequential multistep state transitions, which are usually superior to univariate time series analysis. We propose a novel method called the multidimensional (MD) recurrence plot (RP), which is based on recurrences in higher-order Markov chains, for analyzing and visualizing the dynamics of multiple variables in parallel. The MD RP is an intuitive representation of the transition matrix of a high-order Markov chain with a given memory length. Using the MD RP, we can analyze the dynamics of multivariate systems by comparing the transition matrices of different system states or even classify them using the similarity measure between the transition matrices. Furthermore, we show that the MD RP is applicable to both discrete and continuous time series data without r

Autistic adults often experience stigma and discrimination at work, leading them to seek social assistance from coworkers. This paper examines how the use of personal pronouns by coworkers can affect autistic workers' perceptions of their coworker relationships. We conducted a survey with 200 participants (93% autistic) working in different types of organizations in Canada and the USA. Our results show that pronoun use is a significant predictor of autistic workers' trust toward their coworkers, and this effect differs depending on the type of organization: it is positive for those working in smaller companies but negative for those working in large corporations. Additionally, we find evidence of negative spillover effects on other outcomes, such as job satisfaction. Overall, our findings

We investigate the problem of computing a mean-field equilibrium (MFE) in a population of agents by online mirror descent (OMD). We propose a novel algorithmic framework that achieves this by reinforcement learning (RL). In this framework, each agent performs OMD to minimize its own objective function, which includes a mean-field term involving the empirical distribution of the population. The key ingredient is a population-aware regularizer that encourages the empirical distribution of the population to match the distribution of MFE. We show that the proposed RL-based OMD algorithm converges to a MFE with a fast convergence rate of $\tilde{O}(1/T)$ up to a function approximation error in a non-cooperative setting. In a cooperative setting, we show that a byproduct of the proposed framework is a population-aware policy iteration algorithm that updates the policy and the population distribution simultaneously. We note that conventional policy iteration only updates the policy and requires the population distribution to be given. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first time that OMD and policy iteration are shown to solve the MFE problem in a population of agents. Finally, we demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed framework in both simple and complex MFG examples. In particular, we show that the proposed framework can successfully capture the MFE with a high accuracy in the particle interaction example, which is a challenging problem in the literature.

Deployable polyhedrons can transform between Platonic and Archimedean polyhedrons to create a variety of shapes. They are made up from identical, hinged flat panels that slide along common edges; no hinges or joints are used in the construction process making them particularly lightweight yet robust structures with large load capacities per unit weight [1]. Deploying these rigid bodies into their final form is an essential feature for many applications such as space robotics where compact storage prior launch is crucial but also requires fast deployment upon reaching orbit due time constraints imposed by limited fuel supply capacity on board satellites/rovers etc., while ensuring structural integrity throughout this complex motion sequence without causing damage either during transport thr

In recent years, palmprints have been widely used for individual verification. The rich privacies in the images captured by an optical scanner are usually lost during feature extraction and matching because of complicated data processing techniques such as segmentation or thinning algorithms [1]. Thus it is difficult to ensure that no additional information can be retrieved from a verified subject's image other than his/her identity if only partial features (e g., minutiae) extracted from multiple scans remain available after authentication . This paper proposes using random samples taken directly off binary-valued palm prints without any preprocessing steps , which helps preserve original privacy while maintaining high accuracy rates when compared with traditional methods relying on more

NeRFs have revolutionized the synthesis of novel views, offering unparalleled control and versatility. However, their performance on real-world datasets lags behind that of traditional methods. This is mainly due to the limitations in capturing and rendering objects with complex shapes and textures. In this work, we propose Object-Composable NeRFs (OC-NeRFs), a novel framework that overcomes these limitations and significantly improves the synthesis of complex objects. OC-NeRFs introduce a new approach to rendering objects by breaking down the task into two stages: an object representation stage and a composite rendering stage. This innovative staging allows for improved focus on object details and enhances the realism of rendered images. Our object representation stage utilizes a NeRF-based model, while the composite rendering stage employs an image-level fusion approach. This fusion approach enables our model to effectively combine the high-resolution details of the object representation with the global context of the environment. Our experiments have shown that OC-NeRFs substantially outperform traditional NeRFs and state-of-the-art object representation methods, achieving remarkable results on tasks such as view synthesis, shape-from-alternative-view, and unpaired cross-domain view synthesis. Our code and models are available at https://github.com/ErlerPhilipp/OC-NeRF

Existing off-policy reinforcement learning algorithms typically necessitate an explicit state-action-value function, rendering them unsuitable for the problem of visual motion segmentation. This paper introduces a novel policy-based approach that does not require explicit value functions, and instead relies on the ability to estimate future rewards based on current observations alone. To this end, we propose two separate but complementary approaches: a deterministic one which utilizes a single model, and a stochastic one which employs a recurrent neural network to simulate the effect of a temporal difference (TD) error. We demonstrate that our proposed methods are capable of achieving superior results in comparison with existing methods on both synthetic and real-world datasets, thus highl

Deep learning models, particularly those based on transformers, often employ numerous stacked structures, which possess a high number of parameters. Consequently, training and inference times can be quite lengthy. In this work, we introduce a novel framework called Transformer Compression (TransComp) that is designed to compress the weights of transformer-based deep learning models. The process involves reducing the dimensionality of the weights, with the objective of maintaining the model's performance at an acceptable level while decreasing its computational complexity. This framework consists of two phases: Firstly, it employs a series of matrix factorizations to reduce the dimensionality of the weight matrices, thus introducing compression into the model. Subsequently, it refines the c

This document is a summary of the second edition of the VLSP challenge on Legal Textual Entailment Recognition (VLSP 2023). This task consists in building a system able to determine whether a given statement (the query) can be entailed by a legal provision (the premise). The task is formulated as a classification problem with three possible outputs: the query is entailed by the premise, the query is not entailed by the premise, and the premise is not sufficient to determine whether the query is true or false. This challenge is an edition of the VLSP challenge held for the first time in 2021. Compared to the first edition, VLSP 2023 uses a different dataset, a different task formulation, and a different evaluation protocol. Despite these changes, the best systems built by participants achieve similar performance to the state-of-the-art systems built for the first edition of the challenge.

Recent advances in large language models (LLMs) demonstrate that their capabilities are comparable to or even exceed those of humans. However, despite these impressive achievements at the individual level, we argue there is a gap between LLMS and human-level teams when it comes to solving complex tasks such as research projects: 1.) Most team studies focus on the effects within an existing group setting; however they ignore how novel groups emerge over time with different members joining/leaving without any prior coordination among them -- leading us towards investigating 'emergent teamwork'. This paper presents our work which aims for understanding emergent collaboration through examining self organization by studying interactions across both traditional settings(egs., classroom labs),and

This study investigates the generation of synthetic disinformation by OpenAI's Large Language Models (LLMs). We evaluate several models on the task of generating a fake news article that contradicts an existing fact. We find that the generated articles contain errors in grammar, syntax, and overall structure, as well as incorrect statements about topics unrelated to the original topic. Additionally, we observe that the generated articles often present conflicting perspectives within the same article, which can be detected through human evaluation or automated metrics like Entailment-Disagreement Score. Finally, we find that our LLMs are able to generate both positive and negative feedback, but they struggle with generating nuanced perspectives. Overall, this research underscores the challe

Recent studies have investigated utilizing Knowledge Graphs (KGs) to enhance the performance of Language Models (LMs). These works typically focus on a single LM and only consider in-context learning. However, it is worth noting that KGs are not only applicable to LMs but also to other models like Large Language Models (LLMs), which are widely used in many downstream tasks. The effectiveness of LLMs can be further improved through the integration of KG information. Therefore, this paper aims to comprehensively examine the impact of different types of KGs on LLMs. Specifically, we compare three types of KGs: static, dynamic, and hybrid, by evaluating their performances across various NLP tasks. In addition, we explore the usage of different model configurations within these KG settings. Our

To enable large-scale and efficient deployment of artificial intelligence (AI), the combination of AI and robotics holds great promise. However, it also presents significant challenges in terms of designing effective strategies for collaboration between robots and AIs. This paper proposes a novel paradigm called "Cooperative AI-Robot Interaction" (CAIR), which aims to optimize the performance of complex tasks by integrating human expertise with intelligent agents. We outline the main principles that underlie CAIR and provide a conceptual framework for its implementation. Our approach emphasizes the importance of leveraging both human knowledge and data from sensors and simulations to enhance the learning capabilities of AIs. It also highlights the significance of utilizing AIs to facilitat

In classification, it is usual to observe that models trained on a given set of classes can generalize well to unseen data, but may struggle with new classes. In this work, we introduce the concept of a sub-classifier for a model $f$ as a classifier that is only allowed to make predictions based on the training data of $f$. We show how a simple sub-classifier called an "intersection-based sub-classifier" can be used in conjunction with the original model to improve performance on novel classes. When applied to a number of different classification tasks and models, our methodology shows consistent improvement over state-of-the-art baselines for both binary and multi-class problems. The code and preprocessed datasets are available at: https://github.com/maksimilich/sub_classifiers.git. Our i

Non-Gaussian component analysis (NGCA) aims to recover the non-Gaussian common factors from observed data. This problem is closely related to independent component analysis (ICA) and tensor decomposition. Most existing theoretical results on NGCA assume that the fourth moments of the common factors are bounded or unbounded but with a fast-decaying tail. In this work, we develop a new technique to prove lower bounds on the success probability of any algorithm for NGCA under weaker assumptions on the fourth moments. Our lower bounds hold for both the exact and approximate settings. Under mild tail assumptions, we show that any algorithm for NGCA incurs a non-negligible failure probability, unless the algorithm uses a prohibitively large sample size. The main technique is a new variant of the tensor method that constructs a useful lower bound on the success probability, by proving that the success event is tail-trivial. We also show that our lower bounds cannot be improved to hold for all distributions with bounded fourth moments, by exhibiting a counterexample.

The rapid advance of mega-constellation facilitates the booming of direct-to-ground communication (DTG) service. To improve its throughput, a large number of DTG satellites are deployed in different orbital planes. However, this results in an increasingly complicated interference problem between beams, which brings new challenges to the beam alignment and optimization of DTG networks. In this paper, we first present a novel method for beam alignment in DTG systems by leveraging a beam-level CSI estimation approach. The proposed beam alignment method not only considers the effects of beams on each other but also takes into account the impact of beams on the adjacent ones. It is shown that our proposed method outperforms existing methods from both theoretical analysis and simulation experime

Distributed stream processing systems (DSPS) provide a crucial infrastructure for a wide variety of data-centric applications. To support dynamic stream rates, DSPS need to horizontally scale out their resource allocation by launching new tasks on remote nodes. However, this scaling decision should be carefully made to avoid overloading already busy nodes, while ensuring that the data freshness guarantee is met. In this paper, we introduce DEMETER, a novel DSPS that uses a multi-configuration optimization framework to determine the right amount of resources to allocate to a stream processing job. In contrast to existing DSPS, which make scaling decisions based on local information, DEMETER considers the global state of the cluster to achieve a more balanced load distribution. We show that DEMETER consistently outperforms state-of-the-art DSPS, while saving up to 82% in resource allocation.

Cloud native technologies have been observed to expand into the realm of Internet of Things (IoT). These applications typically employ a microservices architecture, which is designed to leverage the scalability and flexibility of cloud computing platforms. However, such architectures also pose significant challenges in terms of security, including insider threats posed by malicious actors within the organization. In this paper, we present a novel approach to detecting these insiders using deep learning-based models trained on behavioral anomaly detection techniques applied to IoT microservice data. Our methodology involves an anomaly detection pipeline comprising 1) feature extraction from log files generated during IoT service execution; 2) selection of a classifier through the use of ran

IntelliGame is a framework for gamifying unit tests. It provides a flexible mechanism for mapping test cases to a game board, thereby visualizing the execution of the tests as a path on the board. Additionally, it offers various mechanisms for automatically generating visualizations, such as integration with the Testem testing tool and support for multiple test frameworks. In this paper, we provide an experience report on how IntelliGame can be used to gamify the unit tests of a real-world application. For this purpose, we conducted a user study with 21 participants, where participants were asked to run the unit tests of a web browser plugin both with and without the help of IntelliGame. Our results show that the participants preferred the experience of running the tests with IntelliGame, highlighting the benefits of using a gamified approach for running unit tests.

Since Labov's (1964) foundational work on the social stratification of English in New York City, researchers have examined language variation in terms that are grounded either solely or primarily within a sociolinguistic paradigm. In this context it is common to consider factors such as ethnicity and socioeconomic status when exploring linguistic phenomena at different levels ranging from phonology all the way up through discourse analysis; however less attention has been paid towards considering how religion might influence patterns observed across these domains especially given its potentially significant role both socially culturally economically politically etc..

Graph-structured data exhibits universality and widespread applicability across diverse domains, including text, language, biology, chemistry, social networks, and computer vision. As a result, graph neural networks (GNNs) have become one of the most popular methods for graph representation learning in recent years. However, training GNNs has been challenging due to the high computational cost of message passing among nodes, where messages are repeatedly passed between every node pair over multiple layers. The emergence of hardware accelerators such as graph processing units (GPUs) has provided significant speedup. Nevertheless, GPU-based implementations are still not sufficiently scalable to achieve real-time training on large graphs. In this paper, we propose an efficient sparse matrix m

Collaborative problem-solving (CPS) is a vital skill used both in the workforce and everyday life. CPS requires two or more people to work together, sharing knowledge to find solutions to complex problems. To evaluate the effectiveness of CPS, we developed an interactive platform where participants could engage in collaborative problem-solving tasks with other remote users. We measured participants' performance through both objective measures, such as accuracy and speed, as well as subjective data collected through post-task surveys. Our findings indicate that CPS tasks can improve overall team performance. In particular, we found that having a high proportion of novices within the group enhanced performance and engagement. This suggests that the presence of novices may serve as a catalyst

Since the foundational work of William Labov on the social stratification of language (Labov, 1963), there has been a growing interest in how sociolinguistic variables interact with each other to produce specific linguistic patterns. For instance, it is well known that age and generation play an important role as contextual factors influencing speakers’ use of certain varieties or registers within their repertoire: older generations are typically more conservative than younger ones when adopting new forms; whereas newer technologies often require generational change for uptake by broader populations beyond early adopters who have greater access/exposure opportunities via digital media channels such Facebook groups where memes become popularized among millennials but less so amongst baby bo

Indoor scenes we are living in are visually homogenous or textureless, while they inherently hold diverse and rich information. The task of semantic segmentation of such indoor scenes remains challenging due to the limited outdoor training data available for generalization. Existing methods either require additional expensive annotations from different datasets or utilize domain adaptation with limited performance improvement. In this paper, we propose a novel method that harnesses the power of Transformers and Large Language Models (LLMs) for efficient indoor scene semantic segmentation. We introduce Transformer-based Indoor Scene Semantic Segmentor (TIS3). TIS3 first learns scene layout features by LLMs. Then it employs Transformers to learn the fine-grained pixel-level features within e

Recent breakthroughs in Large Language Models (LLMs) have revolutionized natural language processing and opened up exciting new possibilities for applications across a wide range of fields. LLMSs, which are trained on massive datasets to learn patterns from human-generated text data like Wikipedia articles or social media posts can generate creative content such as blog entries , short stories with their own characters . However these models also present challenges related privacy risks posed by exposure sensitive information about individuals through generated outputs without consent; thus it is important that proper measures be taken when deploying them commercially especially considering the current legal framework around personal rights protection laws both domestically within India al

Person or identity verification has been recently gaining a lot of attention using audio-visual fusion as the biometric modality. This is mainly due to its robustness against spoofing attacks, privacy preservation and ease in deployment for remote applications, which are all key requirements of modern authentication systems. Among the numerous modalities that can be fused for this purpose, face and voice stand out for their potential as an alternative to traditional passwords. In addition to improving performance, it has also been shown that these two biometrics are more effective when used together than when used individually. However, there is still a lack of research on fusing them from a machine learning perspective. This paper proposes an end-to-end approach based on deep convolutiona

Federated learning (FL) is an emerging distributed machine learning paradigm enabling collaborative training on private data across heterogeneous devices. However, FL suffers from slow convergence and privacy leaks due to non-IID (non-identically independent distribution) data distributions, resulting in a tradeoff between accuracy and privacy. To address this challenge, we propose FedTrust, a novel FL framework based on trust score estimation that enhances the privacy-preserving property of FL through dynamic data selection. We first introduce a novel trust score estimation mechanism to quantify the quality of local models generated by each client. Next, we present a novel weighted data selection strategy for clients with high trust scores, which can effectively avoid the issue of poor ge

Hyperdimensional Computing (HDC) is an emerging computational paradigm for representing compositional data, such as graphs and language. It has recently been shown to be Turing complete and universal over a wide range of domains. However, there are still some limitations in the existing HDC framework. This paper introduces a novel HDC-based model that can represent compositional data from different modalities simultaneously. We show how this model can handle complex data structures like nested lists or hierarchical graphs through the lens of hyperdimensional vectors (HDVs). Additionally, we present a method for efficiently training neural networks with HDC as the optimization objective. Experiments demonstrate that our proposed architecture achieves state-of-the-art performance on several

The Cognitive Type Project is focused on developing computational tools to enable the design of typefaces with a high degree of customizability. This involves understanding how humans perceive and categorize different types of typefaces, as well as creating algorithms that can automatically generate variations within each category. In this paper we present an overview of our work in progress, which includes the development of a database containing more than 1000 typeface examples, the creation of machine learning models for classifying typefaces into various categories, and the application of these models to create new typefaces using both automatic and human-guided techniques. We discuss the challenges and opportunities presented by this project, as well as our plans for future work. Our

Document retrieval has greatly benefited from the advancements of large-scale pre-trained language models (LMs), such as BERT, which achieve state-of -the art results across multiple domains. However it remains to be seen if these Lms can leverage knowledge learned in one domain and transfer them effectively for document retrival tasks other than natural languages or text documents . In this paper we study a crossdomain setting where an LM is trained on news articles but used directly without fine tuning for image query based retrieva1l over paintings at Art Institute Chicago Museum(AIC).

The need to continuously learn new information without forgetting the previously acquired knowledge poses a profound challenge for artificial intelligence. Furthermore, the requirement to perform this continuous learning on resource-constrained devices at the edge, close to the end-user, is an additional major challenge. In this paper, we explore the benefits of hyperdimensional computing (HDC) for lifelong intelligence at the edge. We demonstrate how the inherent properties of HDC can contribute to addressing the fundamental challenges of continual learning, namely maintaining stability, ensuring flexibility, and preserving plasticity. The results of our experiments show that an HDC-based system outperforms its conventional counterpart on a series of benchmark tasks for continual learning. Moreover, we illustrate how such a system can be implemented on an edge device using a neuromorphic HDC processor, by providing a case study for image classification using a Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) architecture. Our experimental evaluation, conducted on a variety of datasets, indicates that an HDC-based system exhibits improved performance in terms of accuracy, stability, flexibility, and plasticity. Consequently, we argue that HDC is a promising approach for achieving lifelong intelligence at the edge.

In this paper, we develop an LLM-powered framework for the curation and evaluation of online content. The initial step in our process involves gathering relevant information from reputable sources on a specific topic or issue using AI algorithms powered by LLMs like ChatGPT3 (OpenAI) as well 10 other publicly available models to generate summaries that provide context about what is being discussed across different platforms such Twitter posts/tweets etc.. This will help users make more informed decisions when they decide which piece(s), among many options provided through search engines based upon keyword queries entered into their browser window after clicking 'search'. Additionally ,these summarizations can serve two functions: firstly improving understanding around topics covered within

Training Large Language Models (LLMs) presents significant memory challenges, predominantly due to the need for large-scale sparse matrix multiplication. In this work we introduce a new algorithm called Sparse Matrix Multiplication with Bitwise Rotation and Aggregation that allows LLMS such as GPT3 or PaLM21B running on 80GB GPUs without requiring any changes in their model architecture nor training methodology; instead it achieves efficiency by performing only one bit rotation per element during each row computation followed closely behind its corresponding column addition within an efficient pipeline optimized specifically toward reducing latency while improving throughput capabilities simultaneously!

In this paper, we introduce PixArt-\Sigma, a Diffusion Transformer model~(DTM) that can generate high-quality images with diverse visual styles. We extend the classic diffusion-based image generation framework by incorporating an encoder-decoder architecture for improved control over the generated image contents. To further enhance our generative pipeline, we employ a novel transformer diffusion module that is designed to explicitly encode and decode the latent space of a diffusion process, facilitating fine-grained control over various aspects of the generated image such as style and semantics. Furthermore, we adopt a two-stage diffusion scheme which enables enhanced control over the distribution of generated images while also allowing for stable training.

Recommender systems have become an integral part of online platforms. Every day the volume of training data grows exponentially, which makes it harder to develop and tune recommender models for each platform individually. Recently a new paradigm has emerged in recommending that leverages transfer learning from pre-trained language model checkpoints like BERT or GPT2 on large text corpora (eBERT). This paper focuses specifically at extending this approach by investigating how well such fine tuning techniques hold up when adapting them towards non textual input formats with sparse features as common found within recommendation datasets e..g., images & videos etc.).

Feature selection aims to identify the most pattern-discriminative feature subset. In prior works, the performance of feature selection is often evaluated by computing the feature rankings or the area under ROC curves (AUCs). However, these metrics fail to take into account the correlation among features, and thus are not robust when the number of features grows. We propose the Feature Ranking Similarity Measure (FRSM), which is based on the similarity between feature ranks, to capture the correlations among features and improve the evaluation accuracy for feature selection. The proposed FRSM is also capable of evaluating the performance of feature ranking methods that do not output explicit feature rankings, such as clustering methods. Simulation results demonstrate that our FRSM signific

Feature selection is a crucial task in machine learning and is concerned with the challenge of selecting a relevant subset of features for a given task. In this paper, we propose a novel formulation of this problem as a deep generative model. In contrast to traditional generative models, which are typically trained to reconstruct the original data, we propose a sequential generative model that is trained to reconstruct only a subset of features, guided by a latent binary vector. We refer to our model as Deep Sequential Generative Selector (DSGS) and show that it is able to learn the relevant features for a given task. We evaluate DSGS on multiple tabular and image datasets and show that it achieves state-of-the-art feature selection performance. Furthermore, we show that DSGS is able to improve on the performance of a black-box model, thus not only selecting relevant features but also learning a better performing model.

Vision-language Transformer (VL-Transformer) achieves promising performance for vision-language tasks. However, it suffers from slow inference speed and high memory consumption, which seriously hinders its application in practice. To solve these problems, we propose a simple but effective pruning method, i.e., Multimodal Alignment-Guided Dynamic Token Pruning (MADTP), for accelerating VL-Transformer. Specifically, we propose a novel dynamic token pruning strategy that can adaptively prune unnecessary tokens in the cross-modal attention sub-layer and the feed-forward sub-layer based on the importance scores, which are calculated by combining the unigram-based token importance and the multimodal alignment strength. The resulting pruned model can accelerate VL-Transformer during inference and does not sacrifice the performance. Extensive experiments on two representative vision-language tasks, i.e., visual question answering (VQA) and image captioning (IC), demonstrate the effectiveness of our method. On the VQA task, MADTP can accelerate the inference speed of Transformer by up to 70.0% with negligible performance loss. On the IC task, MADTP can even improve the inference speed of Transformer by 2.9% while achieving a comparable performance.

Recently, 3D hand reconstruction has gained more attention in human-computer cooperation. Recent studies have made remarkable progress on the visual odometry and camera calibration tasks. However, there is still a lack of research to reconstruct the entire 3D hand from a single image. This paper proposes a novel framework for hand reconstruction from a single RGB image. Our framework consists of three key steps: (1) hand appearance modeling; (2) hand shape prediction; and (3) pose estimation. The first step utilizes a novel multi-modal CNN architecture to capture the hand appearance features. Then, we propose an explicit shape encoding method using a convolutional neural network. Finally, we present a novel pose estimation approach that incorporates the joint position distribution derived

Open-vocabulary generalization requires robotic systems to perform tasks involving complex and diverse object interactions, such as assembling objects. This task is commonly modeled with a set of demonstrations (Demos), each consisting of a robot state transition and the corresponding task goal conditioned on the observed objects. However, current Demos are often insufficient for accurately modeling real-world scenarios due to limited training data and inadequate generalizability. To address this issue, we propose an efficient framework that enhances the quality and generalizability of Demos by exploiting the potential of high-quality human demonstration videos. Specifically, our method utilizes a novel pipeline based on large language models (LLMs) to generate human demonstrations conditi

Generative AI has recently gained attention for its ability to produce human-like text, code, and other creative outputs. One of the most talked-about generative AI models is ChatGPT, a large language model developed by OpenAI that can engage in conversational AI with humans. In this social impact study, we aim to understand the potential benefits and drawbacks of ChatGPT on society. Through a qualitative content analysis of 1,000 conversations between humans and ChatGPT, we found that ChatGPT can be a valuable tool for education, problem-solving, and entertainment. However, it can also lead to misinformation, dependency, and inequality. Overall, our findings suggest that generative AI has the potential to bring both positive and negative impacts to society, and it is important to proceed with caution and consider the ethical implications of its development and deployment.

Smiles and laughs detection systems have attracted a lot of attention in the past decade contrib-uting to many different applications. However, these approaches have been limited by an inability to generalize well across multiple datasets due both their lack robust features that can capture emotion variation between people as wells insufficient training data collection techniques which often lead inaccurate results when applied on new sets with varying emotions such those found within social networks where users express themselves through emoticons or emojis instead facial expressions alone [1]. In this paper we propose our method for detecting smiles using Convolutional Neural Networks (CNN) trained only from images collected during speech sessions; however it is still unclear how effecti

Underwater soft grippers exhibit potential for applications such as monitoring, research, and object retrrival in ocean environments. The design of underwater gripping devices is a challenge due to the complex interactions between external forces (ejecting jets) from fish or other marine animals with internal pressure-driven actuation mechanisms inside artificial suction cups attached to these objects while submerged under water conditions at different depths below sea level; this paper presents an overview on recent developments towards developing efficient control strategies tailored specifically toward addressing challenges associated when designing autonomous robots capableof performing tasks using only low cost materials available locally within coastal communities around our planet's

The execution and runtime performance of model-based analysis tools for realistic large-scale ABMs (L-ABMs) is poor, due to the expensive evaluation of agent actions. In this work, we introduce a novel approach that leverages the capabilities of modern GPUs to accelerate L-ABM simulation. Our method consists of two components: a GPU-accelerated simulator and an adaptive scheduling strategy based on a machine learning model. We validate our method against the state-of-the-art model-based analysis tool OMPA-S by comparing its runtimes with our GPU-accelerated version, as well as with the classic serial simulation. Our results demonstrate that the GPU-accelerated simulator can significantly reduce the runtime of L-ABM simulations, while maintaining accuracy.

Recommender systems have become an integral part of online platforms. Every day the volume of training data grows exponentially, and recommendation systems are expected to keep pace with this rapid expansion by providing increasingly accurate predictions. However, they often fail to do so because of the inherent sparsity in their datasets. Sparsity presents a significant challenge for recommendation models as it limits the effectiveness of standard optimization techniques like gradient descent. This paper proposes a novel approach called Weighted Gradient Descent (WGD) that leverages weighted gradients to overcome the limitations posed by sparsity in recommender systems. WGD effectively reduces the magnitude of the training objective while simultaneously adjusting its direction, allowing i

Human-robot collaboration is becoming increasingly prevalent in dynamic, unstructured environments. Failures in human-robot interactions can lead to dangerous situations, especially in applications where robots operate among humans in a shared workspace. Previous work has proposed several methods for detecting interaction failures; however, these methods often lack a clear correspondence with the magnitude of the failure. In this paper, we introduce a new calibrated regret metric, which quantifies the cost incurred due to an interaction failure. We present a framework for learning the cost function from data and show that the resulting optimization problem has a closed-form solution. We then propose an interactive learning-from-demonstration framework that leverages our calibrated regret metric to minimize human intervention. We perform simulations to compare our method against existing baselines and show that the proposed method results in less human intervention while achieving a higher success rate. We also perform two user studies with industry collaborators to validate the efficacy of our approach for real-world applications.

Automatically estimating the performance difficulty of a music piece represents a key process in music education to create personalized learning experiences. Recent work on this problem has focused mostly either solely or jointly, using hand-crafted musical features and deep neural networks (DNNs). However none have yet exploited two important resources: 1) large datasets from learners' interactions with digital educational platforms; 2), information about how well students perform at each time point. In contrast we propose here an approach based upon DNN models trained exclusively over such data collected by our own company MusicGurus Inc., iLearnMusic - that is not dependent nor requires any additional handcrafted feature engineering as opposed to prior approaches which required careful

This paper studies the point-to-point motion planning problem for robots in environments with moving obstacles. The configuration space is partitioned into a set of local configuration spaces, each corresponding to a local motion primitive. A two-stage sampling-based motion planning algorithm is proposed. The first stage, referred to as the path planning stage, aims to find a sequence of local motion primitives connecting the initial configuration to the goal configuration. The second stage, referred to as the trajectory tracking stage, generates a trajectory by successively executing the local motion primitives obtained from the first stage. To ensure optimality, a novel local planner is proposed that is specifically designed for moving environments. The local planner considers a subset of obstacles that are potentially active, i.e., may block the motion primitives, and computes a time-optimal trajectory that avoids these active obstacles. A formal proof of optimality is provided. Numerical results show that the proposed algorithm is, on average, more than 9 times faster than the state-of-the-art point-to-point motion planning algorithm for moving environments.

Learning a universal manipulation policy encompassing doors with diverse categories, geometries and mechanisms is challenging due to the large design space of potential door types. In this paper we present Door-Policy: an endtoend learning framework that aims at generalizing across different kindsofdoors by leveraging both geometric priors on how they are assembledand knowledge about their operation from human demonstrations usinga new set top view (STV) representation for action planning in 3D scenesthat captures important spatial information such as depth cues alongwith dynamic features like motion trajectories via graph neural networks(GNNs).

Denotational interpreters are a well-studied technology for defining the meaning of programming languages. They are often presented as a three-step process: first, construct a syntax tree for a program; second, construct a semantic object, such as a lambda term or a process, by applying meaning functions to the syntax tree; third, extract information from the semantic object, such as the object's type or its observable behavior. In this paper, we present a novel approach to constructing denotational interpreters. We start with a specification for the interpreter written in a metalanguage. The specification consists of two parts: a denotational part, which specifies the abstract behavior of the interpreter, and a procedural part, which specifies how the interpreter's abstract behavior can be realized. The metalanguage used to write the specification is an extension of domain theory with constructs for recursion and conditionals. We present a general theorem that proves the correctness of an interpreter's denotational part. We then show how to construct the procedural part of the specification for a variety of interpreters by using fixed points of domain equations. We also show how existing techniques for constructing interpreters can be viewed as special cases of our approach.

Self-supervised contrastive learning strategy has attracted remarkable attention due to its exceptional ability in handling large-scale unlabeled data. Large-scale unlabeled data, however, are often difficult for contrastive methods to handle as it is challenging to balance the risk of memorizing negative samples with the benefit of positive sample augmentation. Recently, a simple yet powerful technique called \emph{Contrastive Negative Sampling} (CoNets) was introduced by our group and achieved state-of-the-art performance on various tasks using relatively few examples. Despite its great success, CoNets have been shown vulnerable to adversarial attacks, which could be leveraged to produce poisonous examples that fool the model into predicting them correctly. In this work, we propose CoNet

Learning to generate textures for a novel 3D mesh given a collection of 3D meshes is an important task in the context of synthetic data generation. This problem is often solved with a two-stage pipeline, firstly generating a set of semantic features via a deep neural network and then mapping these features onto a texture using a predefined texture space model. However, this pipeline has certain limitations: (1) The generated features may not be suitable for all domains and thus need to be retrained; (2) The predefined space model is usually hard coded which limits its applicability. To address these problems, we propose a novel framework that leverages differentiable texture spaces to learn both features and textures jointly from unlabeled data. The literature review is an indispensable step in the research process. It provides the benefit of being able to see what has been done, how it was carried out and where there are gaps for future study (Denzin & Lincoln 2013; Creswell et al., 49-56). We consider a class of stochastic bandit problems with applications to decision-making and control for tool selection. A learner faces a set of tools to complete a certain task, where each tool is associated with an unknown quality parameter. The learner sequentially chooses tools to maximize the expected quality of the selected tool. However, in contrast to the standard setting, the learner has a limited number of opportunities to use each tool. If the learner uses a tool too many times, then the tool breaks and cannot be used anymore. We assume that the learner does not know the quality parameters of the tools and that each time a tool is used its quality is revealed. We focus on the problem of minimizing the regret, defined as the difference between the total quality the learner could have obtained by using the tools and the total quality the learner actually obtained. We show that this problem is fundamentally different from the standard stochastic bandit problem, as in the latter the learner can always afford to explore all the alternatives. In our problem, the learner may reach a point where it cannot afford to explore all the tools and thus it must use a suboptimal tool, in order not to break it. We characterize this phenomenon and show that there exists a threshold value of the number of rounds such that for any number of rounds above the threshold, the learner can avoid using a suboptimal tool. We propose a variant of the successive elimination algorithm and show that its regret is upper bounded by $\tilde{\mathcal{O}}(K^{1/2}T^{1/3})$, where $T^{1/3})$ is the lower bound for the regret of the standard stochastic bandit problem. We also characterize the impact of the confidence level on the regret. Finally, we extend our analysis to a setting where the learner can observe the quality of the tools in parallel.

Recent growth in the popularity of large language models has led to their increased usage for summarizing text, including medical texts. Despite this, there is a lack of research focused on evaluating and optimizing these models for summarization tasks. This paper presents an empirical evaluation of different LLMs for medical summarization and a methodology for training a model with prompting to achieve enhanced performance. We use a dataset from the Medical Literature Analysis and Retrieval System (MEDLINE) that comprises 100k abstracts. We demonstrate that the use of prompts can enhance the quality of generated summaries by comparing the output produced by GPT-4 when it is given various instructions. Specifically, we compare a prompt that encourages the model to provide a "compressed sum

Recent studies of the applications of conversational AI tools, such as chatbots powered by large language models (LLMs) like GPT-4, have highlighted their potential to be used for malicious purposes. This study aims to expand on the existing research and explore the use of GPT-4 in carrying out cyberattacks. We conducted an experiment using the ChatGPT API to develop a tool capable of launching denial-of-service (DoS) attacks against targets of our choosing. Our findings show that GPT-4 can indeed be effectively leveraged for DoS attack purposes, particularly when combined with other software. However, we also discovered that GPT-4's output is not always accurate or reliable when generating code for complex tasks. In particular, GPT-4 often fails to generate robust code, leading to errors

Recently, vision transformers have attracted unprecedented attention for their impressive performance. However, as a plain MLP model, vision transformers lack spatial reasoning capabilities, which are essential for vision tasks. In this paper, we advocate for the comeback of atrous convolution, a classic spatial reasoning module, into vision transformers. The proposed ACC-ViT incorporates large-dilation atrous convolution into a vision transformer framework. We show that ACC-ViT outperforms previous state-of-the-art vision transformers for both image classification and semantic segmentation. Furthermore, we demonstrate that ACC-ViT has comparable performance to previous state-of-the-art models with aggressive model pruning. Our code is available at https://github.com/ACC-ViT/ACC-ViT.

Multilingual Natural Language Processing (NLP) data sets are an important resource for research in our field. When used for model training, the properties of these data sets, such as linguistic diversity, can have a strong impact on model performance. In this work, we present a simple but effective measure for assessing linguistic diversity in multilingual data sets: the geometric mean of token type frequency. We show that this index correlates with other diversity indices and is reflective of human perception of diversity. We evaluate 150+ publicly available multilingual data sets for linguistic diversity using our index and present the results in a publicly available dashboard. We also use the diversity index to investigate the evolution of diversity in publicly available versions of the XLM-R corpus.

Although many approaches have been proposed in recent years, current methods often fail at reconstructing large patients due their limitations on handling a high amount data from CT scans or limited ability capturing details and fine structures within complex regions like organs/tissues (e..g., chest area). This paper presents an approach that leverages modern AI techniques such as deep learning based segmentation models along with multi-resolution voxel representation scheme which enables efficient processing even when dealing larger volumes while ensuring accurate reconstruction results across different types scenarios involving diverse size range cases both healthy subjects su

Medical large language models (LLMs) have gained popularity recently due to their significant practical utility in medical contexts. However, the reliability of these LLMs is limited by data biases and their inherent lack of comprehensive knowledge about medicine. Consequently, they may be unable to accurately understand or describe certain medical concepts, procedures, or treatments. To address this limitation, we propose the novel idea of a "medical-specific" LLM that focuses solely on the medical domain. In this paper, we present a thorough review of existing medical LLMs and delve into various challenges associated with building effective medical-specific LLMs. We discuss the development of a medical-specific LLM for Arabic and evaluate it using a benchmark dataset comprising 100K Arab

Traffic prediction constitutes a pivotal facet within the purview of Intelligent Transportation Systems (ITS), encompassing various applications such as traffic control, fleet management, and urban planning. Recently, deep learning-based approaches have demonstrated substantial improvements over conventional statistical methods in predictive accuracy. However, these methods often lack transparency, which can hinder their acceptance and implementation in real-world scenarios. To address this issue, we propose an interpretable deep learning framework for accurate traffic prediction based on a hybrid method that incorporates a deep neural network (DNN) with attention mechanism and rule-based prediction. Specifically, our approach combines the flexibility of DNNs with the interpretability of r

Detecting edges in images suffers from the problems of (P1) heavy imbalance between background and foreground pixels and (P2) ambiguity between different types of edges. To tackle these issues, we propose a novel edge detector named M-Edge, which consists of a Multi-scale Context Aggregation module (MCA), an Edge Consistency Module (ECM), and a Multi-scale Output Fusion module (MOF). The MCA uses multi-scale context aggregation to capture both fine-grained details and coarse-level structures to alleviate imbalanced sampling problem P1. Furthermore, ECM leverages edge consistency constraints to further suppress noisy responses for robustness. Finally, MOF integrates information from multiple scales to ensure high-quality edges on all levels while avoiding over-fitting.

In recent years, few-shot and zero-shot learning, which learn to predict labels with limited samples, have become increasingly important in the field of artificial intelligence (AI). However, these methods are often trained using only a single dataset, which may limit their generalizability. To address this issue, we propose an unsupervised domain adaptation method for few-shot learning called Unsupervised Domain Adaptation with Data Augmentation. Our approach utilizes data augmentation strategies such as random flips and crops, as well as image mixing, to generate new samples from the source domain. This process enhances the diversity of the training data and allows models to better adapt to target domains without any additional supervision. We evaluate our approach on the CUB-200-2011 da

We introduce the Yi model family, a series of language and multimodal models that demonstrate strong performance on a broad range of tasks in multiple languages. The Yi model family is built upon a unified codebase that supports various architectures and training procedures. The codebase is designed to be modular and flexible, allowing for easy integration of different components such as attention-based modules, transformers, and BERTs. The Yi model family's unique training process utilizes three distinct phases: pre-training, fine-tuning, and adaptation. This approach ensures that the models are well-suited to handle diverse tasks, including text classification, question answering, and machine translation. Furthermore, we introduce the Yi Multilingual model (YiMuL) that achieves state-of-

Automatic Speech Recognition (ASR) systems have been shown to have domain specificity. This means that a system trained on a particular domain of speech will perform better on samples from that domain than from other domains. Evaluating and benchmarking ASR systems on a diverse set of domains is important for understanding the strengths and weaknesses of different models and for guiding research towards improving underrepresented domains. In this work, we introduce a new benchmark for evaluating ASR in the Arabic call domain. We develop a system for extracting relevant speech segments from Arabic call recordings and annotate them with speaker labels. We then proceed to evaluate a set of strong baselines on this data. Our results show that despite the low resource nature of the Arabic call domain, it is possible to achieve high accuracy on speaker identification and that transcript prediction can be improved by using speaker disambiguation. We release a toolkit for working with the Arabic call data as well as pre-trained models for the strong baselines we evaluate.

Federated Learning (FL) is a practical approach to train deep learning models collaboratively across user-devices in an edge network. Process mining has become a cornerstone of process analysis and improvement over the last few years. With its ability to extract knowledge from event logs, it is quickly becoming one part in the digital transformation that many companies are undertaking as they move towards data-driven decision making through advanced analytics techniques such Artificial Intelligence (AI). In this blog post we'll explore what Process Mining actually does - how can you use these methods effectively? We’re going to go beyond just explaining why so much attention should be given when using them for business processes; instead focusing on uncovering valuable insights into current operations which could lead us down an exciting path full off new opportunities!

This paper presents a Geometric-Photometric Joint Alignment(GPJA) method for 6DoF pose estimation of a single object in unstructured scenes, using a single image or video. The GPJA method estimates the pose by optimizing both the photometric error and geometric error between the target object and template model. In this method, the geometric error is calculated as the difference between the projected points on the image plane and the corresponding points from the mesh template. Moreover, to reduce the number of optimization parameters, the camera motion parameters are estimated based on the SIFT feature points extracted from the reference frame image and the mesh. Experimental results show that GPJA can estimate the pose with high accuracy and robustness compared to other state-of-the-art

Cloud computing enables users to process and store data remotely on high-performance computers and servers. Many applications rely upon cloud platforms for their computations, such as deep learning (DL) systems that use large amounts of training datasets [1]. In fact DL is one area where the growth in demand has been particularly rapid due its widespread application across various sectors including healthcare imaging diagnostics or self driving cars just name a few examples amongst many others which are all reliant heavily onto this technology today more than ever before especially given how much faster it's evolving thanks largely attributed towards advancements made within Artificial Intelligence AI field itself over recent years alone .

The recent advancements in Generative AI have significantly advanced the field of text-to-image generation, enabling the creation of realistic and visually appealing images based on natural language inputs. However, many existing methods suffer from limitations such as hallucination or lack of diversity in the generated images. To address these challenges, we propose a novel generative model called Dual-Diversity Adapter (DDA) that incorporates both a vision backbone for image generation and a language encoder for text comprehension. Our method leverages a dual-diversity adapter module to enhance the robustness and quality of the generated images while maintaining high-quality linguistic understanding. Specifically, our model generates diverse outputs by utilizing multi-modality informatio

Mechanistic understanding of organic reactions can facilitate reaction development, impurity prediction, and in silico design. Recent advances have enabled rapid exploration by applying machine learning to mechanistic models such as transition state theory (TST). However the current approach is computationally expensive due mainly from evaluating partition functions at each step during sampling on a dense grid for accurate integration with TST equations even when using advanced techniques like Fourier acceleration or adaptive quadrature rules that reduce computational cost but still require many steps within one iteration; this leads us back toward our original goal: reducing time spent performing these calculations while maintaining high accuracy levels across all parameters involved .

Development of large language models (LLM) have shown progress on reasoning, though studies have been limited to tasks in mathematics. However, the knowledge base used by these models is often restricted to a specific field. Moreover, LLMs are notorious for having hallucinations and being biased towards certain answers. In this work we introduce a new dataset called LogicalMaths, consisting of over 2000 logical math questions designed to test the capabilities of current LLMs on mathematical reasoning. We also provide an LLM benchmark for the dataset that is openly available at https://github.com/SciML/LogicalMathsBenchmark. To demonstrate its effectiveness, we compare our model against previous LLMs on the LogicalMaths dataset and highlight the importance of testing LLMs in mathematics usi

Motion synthesis in real-world 3D scenes has recently attracted much attention. However, existing methods often rely on precomputed motions with a fixed duration, which leads to either unnatural results or computational inefficiency when the task duration varies. In this paper, we present a novel framework, called Dynamic Motion Synthesis (DMS), for dynamic motion synthesis in real-world 3D scenes. Our key insight is that a human movement can be decomposed into its basic components and each component can be recomposed by altering its duration. To achieve this, we first propose a human motion decomposition method based on hierarchical temporal pooling, which enables us to dynamically adjust the duration of individual components. Then, we design an end-to-end network, called DMSNet, which le

In this paper, we present a study on online grasp learning for robotic bin picking. Our work is motivated by the challenging nature of this task, which involves grasping a variety of objects of different shapes, sizes, and textures. We propose a novel online grasp learning framework that combines two key ideas: (1) a pseudo-labeling strategy to promote the discovery of robust features by leveraging a pre-trained model, and (2) a contextual curriculum learning approach to facilitate the selection of the most informative samples for updating the model. Our proposed framework is evaluated on a challenging real-world dataset for bin picking, where an industrial robot is tasked with picking up objects of various materials and shapes. Experimental results demonstrate that the proposed method significantly outperforms a baseline approach that does not employ pseudo-labeling or contextual curriculum learning, highlighting the importance of these two components in the proposed framework. Furthermore, we show that the proposed method is able to adapt to the changing distribution of objects in the bin and maintain high performance over time, addressing the challenges of online learning and generalization in robotic bin picking.

International migrants face difficulties obtaining information for a quality life and well-being in the host society. This study explores whether international migrants have different needs of social support from locals as they integrate into their communities. It also examines how the needs of social support are related to migrant's emotional well-being, which is often overlooked in social support research. Drawing on the ecological model of human development, this paper proposes the Social Support Needs Model (SSNM) to explore the role of social support needs among international migrants in the United States. The proposed SSNM is tested with data collected using the Qualtrics platform through an online survey completed by 200 participants who self-identified themselves as either internat

Chain-of-thought (CoT) distillation aims to distill the high-level reasoning process (i.e., chain of thoughts) from a question-answering model, which is of great importance for model understanding and debugging. Existing methods distill CoTs by directly modeling the dependency between the generated CoTs and the original answers, which ignores the diversity of CoTs. In this paper, we point out two drawbacks of existing CoT distillation methods: (1) The diversity of CoTs is not sufficiently explored, which may lead to sub-optimal reasoning paths; (2) The complex CoT space makes it difficult to learn a good CoT representation. Thereby, we propose a novel MISA framework to learn the optimal CoTs in an indirect way. Specifically, we introduce an auxiliary network to map the original model's representations to a more suitable CoT space, which helps explore diverse CoTs and reduces the complexity of the CoT space. Furthermore, we devise a mutual information maximization objective to further boost the diversity of CoTs. MISA is a general framework that can be combined with different CoT distillation methods. Experiments on the CLUE benchmark demonstrate that our method achieves state-of-the-art performance. The code is available at https://github.com/Lizw14/MISA.

The third ML4H symposium was held in person on December 10, 2022 in Vienna. We had 159 participants from academia and industry representing 38 countries (see the list of attendees). The symposium consisted of nine talks and five panel discussions covering a wide range of topics related to machine learning for healthcare (ML4H) including fairness, ethics, safety, and interpretability in AI-driven medical applications; data science for clinical trials; and large language models for biomedicine. All recordings of the presentations will be made available soon. In addition, we encourage you to submit your contributions to our workshop series on ML4H -- see below for details. For updates regarding the workshops, please visit https://mlforhealthworkshops.github.io/ .

In order to successfully perform manipulation tasks in new environments, such as grasping, robots must be able to plan ahead and predict the consequences of their actions. This is a challenging problem due to the inherent complexity of these tasks, which involve many unknowns and uncertainties. One approach that has been proposed is to use Reinforcement Learning (RL), which has shown promise in solving similar problems in other domains. However, there are also certain limitations with RL, such as its high computational requirements and lack of interpretability. To address these issues, we introduce a novel algorithm for planning manipulators' trajectories using both reinforcement learning and inverse reinforcement learning. Our method leverages the advantages of each technique while avoidi

Training for multi-agent reinforcement learning(MARL) is a time-consuming process that requires multiple learners to interact with each other. We propose an efficient model transfer method between agents by incorporating meta representation in the policy gradient framework, called MetaRepGrad (Meta Representation Gradient). A shared feature extractor which maps individual state and action features into global representations; these are then concatenated together as input of our agent's internal neural network layers without additional computational cost at inference or training timescales compared against baselines such "Vanilla" methods where only local inputs were used instead - this allows us not just faster convergence but also increas

Real-time 6 DOF localization of bronchoscopes is crucial for enhancing the precision and safety of interventional bronchoscopy. However, it often suffers from low accuracy due to unstable image intensity values and occlusion by lung tissue. Existing methods usually rely on feature extraction, which ignores the spatiotemporal information of broncho-bronchial tree (BBT) images and may cause false negative cases. To address these problems, we propose a novel framework named BBTNet that encodes the spatiotemporal information of broncho-bronchial tree images based on the Transformer architecture. Specifically, we first design a spatial transformer that efficiently learns the fine-grained structural information with self-attention mechanism and position encoding module. Then, we apply a temporal

Existing research often posits spurious features as "easier" to learn than core features, which is problematic for real-world applications. This paper demonstrates that spurious features can be just as challenging or more so than core features in the presence of adversarial examples. Inspired by the literature on adversarial robustness of deep neural networks, we introduce a novel training methodology called "Feature Aware Adversarial Training (FAAT)" that allows us to explicitly optimize the performance of a classifier while simultaneously ensuring it remains robust against adversarial attacks targeted at core and spurious features. Additionally, FAAT can also improve the robustness of the model against adversaries that are not focused on particular features, such as those targeting all c

Large pre-trained Vision-Language Models (VLMs) like CLIP, DAVE, and DINO can be fine-tuned to perform tasks in natural language. Despite their remarkable success, they do not have access to the entire training dataset during inference, which is often necessary for various downstream applications. To address this issue, we propose a novel approach that allows pre-trained VLMs to leverage all available data when generating responses, without requiring additional training or fine-tuning. Our method works by augmenting existing prompt-based VLMs with an auxiliary module, called Knowledge Extractor (KE), that utilizes text from unseen examples to retrieve knowledge from large pre-trained VLMs. We showcase the effectiveness of our proposed KE module on three popular multimodal benchmarks: Few-S

Large language models (LLMs) have revolutionized the role of AI, yet also pose potential risks of propagating unethical content. Alignment technologies have been introduced to steer LLMs towards human preference, gaining increasing attention. Despite notable breakthroughs in this direction, existing methods heavily rely on high-quality positive-negative training pairs, suffering from noisy labels and the marginal distinction between preferred and dispreferred response data. Given recent LLMs' proficiency in generating helpful responses, this work pivots towards a new research focus: achieving alignment using solely human-annotated negative samples, preserving helpfulness while reducing harmfulness.
