For this purpose, we propose Distributional Dispreference Optimization (D$^2$O), which maxim

Federated learning (FL) collaborates multiple clients to jointly train a global model without sharing their local data. Recent works have started to study the opposite problem, federated unlearning (FU), which aims to remove the influence of malicious clients on the global model without the participation of the malicious clients themselves. This paper provides a systematization of the current works on federated unlearning, analyzing the approaches taken by these works to address the problem. We identify the open challenges and research opportunities in this newly emerging research area, and provide our insights on how to tackle them.

Pre-trained Large Language Models (LLMs) are beginning to dominate the field of Natural Language Understanding, with their capability for generalizing knowledge across various domains. In this paper, we introduce a novel framework that utilizes LLMs for learning domain-specific knowledge in a semi-supervised manner. Our approach uses a set of domain-specific prompts and a small amount of labelled data, which is then used to fine-tune an LLM for improved performance on downstream tasks. We evaluate our method's effectiveness by testing it on various benchmark datasets such as QA4ML, CORD-19, and BioASQ-5. Our experiments demonstrate that our approach significantly outperforms baseline methods in terms of both accuracy and performance. We also conduct ablation studies to investigate the fact

Recent studies have focused on enhancing the performance of 3D object detection models. Among these, Pointpillars [1] has shown excellent results in terms accuracy and speed compared to other methods by using a two-stage structure consisting with an encoder module followed up prediction modules for point cloud processing stagewisely as well postprocessing procedure utilizing nonmaximum suppression (NMS) method after that each stages completed their task respectively; however its drawback lies within high computation cost due extensive use both deep neural networks(DNNs), which increases overall training time significantly while requiring more powerful hardware capabilities such GPUs .

Improving the safety of collaborative manipulators necessitates the reduction of inertia and friction. This paper proposes an innovative approach that combines the advantages of conventional methods, such as PD control and trajectory optimization, with the benefits of machine learning (ML), specifically reinforcement learning (RL). The proposed method involves training a deep neural network to optimize collision-free policies for a single robot using RL. The resulting policy is then applied to a fleet of robots through cooperative control techniques, where each robot is assigned a specific task based on its unique motion capabilities. This approach enables safe and efficient collaboration, enhancing operational flexibility and reducing downtime due to damage caused by collisions or malfunc

The realm of algorithms with predictions has led to the development of several new algorithms that leverage (latent) features in training data. While this approach offers promising results, it raises concerns about how well these algorithms generalize beyond the training set. To address this issue, we introduce the concept of algorithmic fairness, which requires an algorithm's predictions to be consistent across different feature spaces, even if they are generated by different generative models. In this paper, we present a framework for evaluating and improving the fairness of algorithms using a unified measure. Specifically, we propose a novel method called Fairness-Aware Uniformity (FAU), which leverages a generative adversarial network (GAN) to generate different feature spaces for a gi

Self-reflecting about our performance (e.g., how confident we are) before doing a task can help us to manage stress and improve the outcome of that activity by increasing activation in brain regions related with executive functions, according to an article published online ahead print in Psychological Science "It has been shown repeatedly that self reflection on one's own abilities helps people perform better," said lead author Christian Schlothauer from Ruhr University Bochum "However it is unclear whether such benefits extend beyond the scope where individuals have direct experience." This study addresses this question using fMRI neuroimaging techniques, as well making use for real world tasks: chess playing or singing karaoke songs; both activities that require high levels concentratio

Ensemble classifiers are often used in malicious URL detection, where the feature vectors are based on the web content. A common approach is to use a random forest, which is an ensemble of decision trees. In this paper, we discuss a tree-based ensemble approach to mitigate label flipping attacks, where an adversary attempts to misclassify a URL as malicious by flipping the labels of a set of training examples. We assume that the set of flipped labels is unknown to the defender, but the URLs corresponding to these labels are available. We demonstrate that the efficacy of label flipping attacks can be mitigated by using an ensemble of decision trees that have been trained on randomly selected subsets of the flipped data. We evaluate our approach against a range of decision tree ensembles on several publicly available datasets that contain flipped labels. We show that our approach often achieves higher detection rates and lower false positive rates than the decision tree ensembles.

Recent work has shown that the outputs of graph neural networks (GNNs) are asymptotically constant with respect to the spectral radius of the normalized adjacency matrix, if the GNN is stable and the input graphs are drawn from a distribution with bounded degrees. In this work, we show that the outputs of GNNs are almost surely asymptotically constant, without the need for a bounded degree assumption. In particular, we show that the spectral radius of the normalized adjacency matrix, which is a random variable, is with high probability close to a deterministic quantity, which we call the essential spectral radius. The essential spectral radius is the largest eigenvalue of a limiting object: the infinite graphon. The graphon is a function that captures the large-scale limit of the graph structure, and is uniquely characterized by the GNN's stability. We present three applications of our main result. First, we give a new perspective on the classical problem of spectral convergence of GNNs. Second, we show that the essentially constant behavior of GNNs can be extended to a large class of activation functions, including ReLU, maxpool, and their variants. Finally, we demonstrate that our result can be used to analyze the generalization error of GNNs.

Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) have demonstrated outstanding performance in computer vision tasks, yet the application of CNNs to audio processing has been limited by the lack of a suitable training dataset. Recent studies have focused on utilizing self-supervised learning methods to train models without explicit labels, but these methods are constrained by the availability of data and the generalization capabilities of the resulting models. In this paper, we propose the use of transfer learning to address these challenges by leveraging pre-trained visual models from image classification tasks and fine-tuning them for audio processing tasks. Our experiments demonstrate that this approach outperforms existing state-of-the-art techniques, achieving an accuracy of 96.1% on the Audioset-

Modern life sciences research is increasingly relying on artificial intelligence approaches to model biological systems, primarily through deep learning methods. The rapid development of these tools has been driven by the availability and accessibility provided via online cloud-based platforms such as Google Colab or Amazon SageMaker Studio Lab (formerly knownas AWS ML Studios). These solutions have enabled researchers with little prior experience in machinelearningto build sophisticated models without requiring access tounrestricted computing resources at their institutions' data centersor extensive knowledge about hardware architecture details like GPUs/TPUs used for training neural networks; however there are still some challenges associatedwith using them safely including security risk

Federated learning is a popular framework for collaborative machine learning where multiple clients only share gradient updates on their private data. The most common approach to federate models across heterogeneous devices and services, such as mobile phones or IoT sensors with varying compute capabilities in different environments (e. g., noisy versus clean), involves the use of regularization techniques like dropout that can be applied universally irrespective... We propose an iterative method based on stochastic gradients along smooth directions at each iteration step which allows us both fast convergence rates under mild conditions compared against state-of art methods while still being simple enough not requiring any special knowledge about how your problem looks/behaves before star

Traditional cross-domain tasks, including domain adaptation and domain generalization, rely heavily on training model parameters within the target distribution. This requires a large number of labeled examples from the target domain for effective learning. In recent years, there have been significant advancements in unsupervised pretexts, particularly in the field of vision. These unsupervised pretexts aim to improve the performance of pretrained models by providing additional information during pretraining without any labeling. However, their effectiveness is limited when it comes to domains that are far apart. In this paper, we propose a novel method called "Adaptive Unsupervised Pretext" (AUP) that adaptively selects the most effective pretexts across different domains. Our approach com

We present a novel method for efficiently producing semi-dense matches across images. Previous detectorless matching approaches rely on the assumption that a pair of images shares visual content, but this is not always true in real-world scenarios where similar images can exist due to shared concepts or environmental factors. To address this issue, we propose a two-stage model with an initial image descriptor and a subsequent similarity module. The initial stage uses a simple descriptor network to extract features from each image, which are then compared using a distance metric based on the cosine similarity between vectors. During training, the network is trained to learn the optimal weights for minimizing the distance between positive pairs while maximizing the distance between negative

Machine Learning (ML), addresses a multitude of complex issues in multiple disciplines, including social sciences and economics. However, the ML process is not transparent; it is challenging for humans to understand why an algorithm makes specific predictions, leading to mistrust and even legal challenges. In this paper, we propose a novel methodology that explains how the prediction from a trained ML model was generated by tracing back the original data points which were used to train the model. We also present a visualization tool to display the explanations graphically. The proposed approach can be applied to any kind of dataset regardless of whether it contains text or numeric values. Our results show that our proposed approach can be used as a powerful tool to gain insights into the t

This paper presents the second version of the OCEL (Object-Centric Event Log) specification, a provenance data model for event logs that emphasizes the objects involved in the events and their interrelations. Compared to the first version, OCEL 2.0 introduces (1) a more rigorous and formal presentation, (2) an extension to cover object-centered views on event logs, (3) a more general treatment of provenance metadata, and (4) additional examples. These improvements result in a more comprehensive and flexible specification that better supports the diverse use cases of provenance in the context of event logs.

Situationally Induced Impairments and Disabilities (SIIDs) can significantly hinder daily activities for individuals with disabilities. A number of assistive technologies have been developed to help mitigate the impact of SIIDs, but the challenge of designing effective assistive tools that can adapt to an individual's unique needs remains a significant obstacle. In this paper, we explore the potential of generative AI-based methods in enhancing the development process of assistive technology by utilizing existing data as the foundation for generating new designs. Our approach involves first leveraging a generative model to generate a diverse set of designs, followed by evaluating these designs using human subject studies. This process is then iteratively refined through the incorporation o

Contestability -- the ability to effectively challenge a decision -- is critical to the implementation of fairness in machine learning (ML). In this work, we propose and study a novel formulation of the contestability problem, which focuses on the question of how well ML models can be challenged by humans. We begin by developing a formal framework that characterizes the notions of contesters (i.e., human challengers) and ML models, as well as the contestability of an ML model given a specific set of contesters. We then propose an algorithm for obtaining high-quality contesters, which can be used for finding good challenges against any given ML model, with a guarantee on the quality of the contester. The proposed algorithm combines a nonlinear optimization procedure with techniques from con

With the escalating threats posed by cyberattacks on Industrial Control Systems (ICSs), there is a need for effective strategies to enhance their security. In recent years, there has been considerable progress in developing Machine Learning (ML) models that detect anomalous behavior within ICS networks. However, as these models become more sophisticated and complex, it becomes increasingly challenging to interpret and comprehend their decisions. To address this challenge, we propose an explainable model named XGAN, which uses Generative Adversarial Networks (GANs) to generate plausible explanations for the ML model's predictions.

We study text-based image editing (TBIE) of a single image by counterfactual inference. For example, we can modify the face of an individual from one person to another, or add new objects to the scene, simply by providing textual instructions. However, existing works either suffer from insufficient control over the edited images, or require many training examples and complex model architectures. To address these issues, we propose Text2ImageEditing (T2IE), a method that enables precise control over TBIE with fewer training examples and simpler model structures. The core idea behind T2IE is that we use the pretrained diffusion models as a template and then fine-tune them with few-shot examples to generate the desired results. Specifically, we define an auxiliary encoder network for generati

Distance-based time series anomaly detection methods are prevalent due to their relative non-parametric simplicity and good performance. However, they are not optimal because of a lack of information about the target distribution. In this paper, we propose an effective anomaly detection method that can be combined with any distance-based method to improve its accuracy. The proposed method, called Anomaly Score based on Distribution Information (ASDI), is based on a novel information theoretic score that quantifies the difference between the empirical probability density function of the target time series data and a uniform distribution. We show that the ASDI score has an interpretable meaning when the underlying time series data have a clear trend. Moreover, we demonstrate that ASDI outper

In this paper, we look at cross-domain few-shot classification which presents the challenging task of learning to classify new categories from a limited set of training examples when the test domain differs significantly from the training domain. We propose a novel solution that leverages a pretrained visual encoder and an unlabeled auxiliary dataset for domain adaptation. Our method, Domain Adaptive Few-Shot Learning (DAFS), involves aligning the feature distributions of source and target domains using the unlabeled auxiliary data, followed by fine-tuning on the labeled source data to learn the category-specific features. To further enhance performance, we introduce a novel approach called Category-Aware Feature Alignment (CFA) that incorporates class labels during domain alignment.

Continual Learning (CL) is essential for home service robots to gradually learn a wide range of household tasks over long-term interaction with humans. However, existing CL approaches mostly focus on improving the robots' performance on newly learned tasks, at the expense of deteriorated performance on previously learned tasks. To address this challenge, we propose a novel interactive CL architecture that enables long-term personalization of home service robots. The architecture consists of a personalization module, a skill module, and a human-robot interaction module. The personalization module stores the robot's personalized knowledge, which is continuously updated with new experiences. The skill module contains the robot's task-specific knowledge and is responsible for executing the tasks. The human-robot interaction module facilitates two-way interaction between the robot and the human, and collects human feedback to guide the update of the personalized knowledge. To evaluate the proposed architecture, we conduct a user study with three home service robots, including a kitchen assistant robot, a laundry assistant robot, and a general service robot. The results demonstrate that the proposed architecture enables robots to learn a diverse set of household tasks from limited human demonstrations, and maintain high performance on previously learned tasks even when learning new tasks. Moreover, the robot can be personalized by the human user to better adapt to the user's preferences and feedback.

This study addresses the challenge of real-time metaverse applications by proposing an in-network plaNing scheme for content delivery with Quality of Experience (QoE) guarantees. The proposed approach, named MCNP, exploits a multi-criteria network programming framework and is tailored to meet the strict QoE requirements of metaverse applications. We design a cost function that takes into account not only the number of hops but also other relevant constraints such as delay, bandwidth availability, and packet loss rate. We formulate a mixed integer linear program for joint content placement and routing to minimize the total cost while ensuring that all users receive the requested content within their specified latency threshold. Experimental results show that MCNP outperforms existing soluti

Simulation is pivotal in evaluating the performance of autonomous driving systems due to the advantages it provides over real-world testing. However, the simulation data may not always accurately replicate the actual road conditions and environment characteristics, thereby leading to inaccuracies in the evaluation results. In this work, we propose an approach that utilizes generative models to synthesize additional data samples from the limited available training data. Specifically, we employ a novel framework based on a conditional diffusion model that incorporates a conditional discriminator, designed to enhance the accuracy of synthetic images. We integrate this framework into our proposed method for autonomous vehicle simulation data augmentation. To validate the efficacy of our approa

Few-Shot Class-Incremental Learning (FSCIL) has gained considerable attention in recent years. Most existing works focus on the design of network architectures to tackle this task, while few research efforts are devoted towards designing appropriate loss functions for FSCICL tasks and most vanilla losses can only work well with specific model structures or datasets even though they have been widely used as basic building blocks across different models without any thorough investigation about their effectiveness within a broader context .

Learning ICU patient subgroups with unsupervised machine learning approaches may support the clinical decision-making process and enable tailored treatment. This study aims to identify reproducible subgroups of patients admitted to the ICU, characterise their clinical relevance, and validate the generalisability of the results. Clustering approaches were used to identify subgroups of patients based on their longitudinal Electronic Health Record (EHR) data. To assess the clinical relevance of the identified subgroups, we performed cluster-aware survival analysis and compared the results with the conventional survival analysis. Furthermore, we evaluated the generalisability of the findings by applying the trained models to independent datasets. We included 4776 ICU admissions from two Australian hospitals spanning 10 years. Using unsupervised learning approaches, we identified subgroups with different lengths of stay and mortality outcomes. The clinical relevance of these subgroups was supported by the cluster-aware survival analysis results, which showed different survival distributions and hazard ratios. Applying the clustering models to the independent datasets resulted in the identification of subgroups with similar characteristics, lengths of stay, and mortality outcomes, supporting the generalisability of the findings. This study demonstrates that unsupervised learning approaches can be used to identify clinically relevant subgroups of ICU patients that exhibit similar characteristics, lengths of stay, and mortality outcomes, which may support clinical decision-making.

The recent explosion in popularity of large language models (LLMs) has inspired learning engine designers to leverage them as a building block for conversational user interfaces. In this paper, we study the question of how LLMs can be integrated with learning engines in order to build more robust and powerful conversational agents. We propose a novel methodology for evaluating this integration. Our proposed evaluation pipeline is designed to identify and isolate the impact of different aspects of the integration, including factors such as the granularity of the LLM response, the quality of the LLM prompting, and the degree of interaction between the LLM and the learning engine. Our experiments show that integrating an LLM into a learning engine can improve its performance in several key di

In recent years, personalized recommendation technology has flourished and become one of the hot research directions in data mining. The main goal is to recommend items that meet users’ interests from a massive amount or even infinite number (eg., web pages) based on user preferences [1] . Personalized recommendations are widely used in e-commerce systems such as Amazon for product search engine optimization; social media platforms like Facebook provide targeted advertising services by analyzing their target audiences through personal information collected when logging into accounts via email addresses associated with each account holder so they can show ads relevant only towards those individuals who have expressed interest priorly without being intrusive while still pro

We propose a methodology for modelling methane intensities of Oil and Gas upstream operations. We use data from the most representative oil & gas companies in our sample, which covers 80% (by volume) global crude output production worldwide during period under analysis: January-December between years [2014–27]. Our results show that there is an inverse relationship among total volumes produced by company with their intensity values; this means more efficient producers have lower emissions per unit than less effective ones do because they can avoid unnecessary waste along pipeline networks or other facilities related to extraction process itself as well reduce costs associated therewith simultaneously allowing them achieve better returns on investment made earlier stages prior extracting ra

Large language models (LLMs) have demonstrated remarkable capabilities in problem-solving. However, their performance often degrades when presented with data distributions different from those used for training and fine-tuning. We present a novel approach to enhance LLM generalization through meta-learning. Our method, named META-LLM, leverages diverse in-context learning (ICL) datasets to train a single meta-learner that can quickly adapt to new tasks at test time. This adaptation process is achieved by transferring knowledge from the ICL datasets via gradient descent on a small number of task-specific parameters. Through experiments with GPT-2 and GPT-4, we demonstrate that our approach significantly improves the ability of LLMs to generalize across various domains and tasks without over

We study the inventory placement problem of splitting $Q$ units of a single item across warehouses under deterministic demand. The objective is to minimize the number of times each warehouse runs out of stock (called out-of-stock frequency, OOF). We consider a simple, yet realistic setting where the distribution of demand is known to be exponentially distributed and all warehouses share a common buffer capacity. In this scenario, we provide a closed-form expression for the optimal solution which can be solved in polynomial time. In addition, we show that our closed-form expression matches the lower bound on the optimal expected OOF provided by an algorithm based on dynamic programming. Finally, we propose an alternative algorithm which matches the lower bound almost exactly without resorti

For decades, researchers have been working on automating software development tasks, ranging from test generation to full-stack code synthesis. Meanwhile, modern web applications are becoming more complex, and designers play a increasingly important role in determining the user experience. As a result, the gap between design and code is growing, creating a need for automating the tasks that fill it. In this paper, we leverage existing research on design automation to predict how far we are from automating front-end engineering, identify research opportunities, and inform the development of tools that bridge the gap between design and code.

Reinforcement learning (RL) has achieved phenomenal success in various domains. However, its data-driven nature limits the generalizability of learned policies across diverse environments or domains. Furthermore, most RL algorithms fail to capture domain-specific knowledge such as the spatial structure of the environment, which is essential for robust and efficient exploration. To address these challenges, we propose an approach called Multi-Agent Path Planning (MAPP). Our main contribution is a novel agent architecture that encapsulates prior knowledge in the form of a graph. The graph captures the spatial structure of the environment, enabling agents to utilize prior information to perform faster and more effective explorations. We show that this method effectively enhances the performan

Large language models (LLMs) such as GPT-4 have recently demonstrated impressive results across a wide range of tasks. LLMs are able to produce high-quality text, code, and even generate entire stories and games. In parallel, there is a long-standing tradition of using natural language processing (NLP) to analyse and make sense of games, typically focusing on textual data such as game forums and reviews. However, the capabilities of LLMs have not yet been fully explored in the context of video games. This paper presents a scoping review of existing work that uses LLMs to analyse or generate game-related text. We focus on LLM applications to video games, a medium that is distinct from other forms of digital text in its multimodal nature. We identify four main areas of research: (1) LLM-assisted game text analysis, (2) LLM-generated game text, (3) LLMs as a tool for human-centred design, and (4) LLMs as a source of fictional worlds and narratives. We argue that LLMs have the potential to transform the way we research and design games, and we present a research agenda for LLMs in games.

Treatment effect estimation (TEE) is the task of determining the impact of various treatmenets on a given outcome. It is an important problem in many real-world settings, including healthcare and finance; however its computational complexity has been shown to be NP hard for general input distributions [3]. In this work we investigate whether TEE can still provide useful insights when restricted only by tractability constraints: that it should run quickly enough so as not interfere with production systems or cause delays during their operation even if they are offline learning agents such robots which require fast decisions due time sensitivity etc., but also provides accurate estimates about how well these different treatments perform over long periods without bias towards one particular t

Graph-level contrastive learning, aiming to learn the representations for each graph by contrasting two graphs of similar structure and different content, has achieved significant success in graph-structured data representation. Graph-level contrastive learning typically requires a large number of graphs as positive/negative pairs for supervision and is vulnerable to negative sampling errors. To alleviate these issues, we propose an unsupervised graph-level contrastive learning framework named GCL-UnSup that utilizes a pre-trained transformer network with self-attention on node features to extract global and local information from the input graphs. In addition, we introduce an edge-level contrastive loss to further enhance the representation ability of global and local information. Experim

In the realm of unmanned aerial vehicle (UAV) tracking, Siamese neural networks are predominantly employed for their inherent capability to compare images and distinguish between them. However, the utilization of Siamese networks in UAV tracking is limited by the requirement of an accurate ground truth and a considerable amount of training data. To address these limitations, we propose a novel framework that leverages weakly supervised learning techniques and deep learning methods for UAV tracking. The proposed approach employs a combination of deep learning and transfer learning methods, which enables us to effectively train on large-scale datasets without requiring exhaustive labeling or collecting sufficient training data. We demonstrate the effectiveness of our method through extensive

With the advancement of language models (LMs), their exposure to private data is increasingly important. However, existing studies focus on evaluating LM privacy risks from a legal and technical perspective but lack a systematic framework for analyzing these risks across different domains. To address this gap, we propose a novel framework, Privacy Risk Assessment for Language Models (PRALM), that systematically identifies potential privacy risks in LMs based on a comprehensive understanding of their internal structures and functionalities. PRALM encompasses three key modules: 1) Domain-specific Data Collection, which collects domain-specific data to train LMs, 2) Model Structural Analysis, which analyzes the model's internal structures, such as its tokenizer and embedding layer, and 3) Fun

In the highly competitive area of product marketing, the visibility of brand logos on packaging is paramount. But if you are to achieve this without compromising the design or quality standards expected by consumers and retailers alike – how do we go about it? With decades’ worth experience in graphic printing solutions for FMCG products like these (including both paper-based labels as well as plastic wrap), our team knows exactly what works best when creating customized label designs that will ensure your business gets noticed!

Despite recent advances in wearable technology, interfacing movement assistance devices with the human body remains a significant challenge. Most wearable exoskeletons are cumbersome and rigid, limiting their comfort and reducing the user's ability to move freely. This research proposes an innovative method for enhancing exoskeleton performance using soft robotic actuation. Soft robotics offers advantages such as lightweight construction, compliance, and adaptability to complex human motions. To achieve this, we propose a novel mechanism that uses soft tendons for actuating joints of wearable exoskeletons. The proposed mechanism integrates a custom-designed pneumatic tendon pump into the soft tendon-based exoskeleton design. We evaluate our approach by developing an experimental prototype

Recent advancements in text summarization, particularly with the advent of Large Language Models (LLMs), have revolutionized the field. However, a key limitation is the significant data size requirements for training and fine-tuning models. This paper introduces an efficient approach to fine-tune text summarizers on limited data using prompt engineering techniques. Specifically, we introduce the concept of Summarizer Prompt Engineering (SPE) and demonstrate its effectiveness through empirical analysis, comparing it against traditional methods. Our study reveals that SPE not only enhances model performance but also requires substantially less training data compared to conventional approaches. The findings underscore the importance of prompt engineering in optimizing LLM fine-tuning, especia

Novel-view synthesis with sparse input views is important for real-world applications like AR/VR. However, it remains a challenge due to the lack of information and severe viewpoint mismatch between images from different cameras (i. e., inconsistent illumination or background). To tackle this problem in an unsupervised manner while preserving high image quality at scale ($108\times 236$ pixels), we propose CrossNet: cross domain learning via adversarial training combined multiscale feature fusion network which consists three components -- view augmentation module(VM) that generates virtual reference frames based on target frame; discriminator component($D_T$) comprising two subnetworks $G_{R}$ & $\hat{V}_{RT}$, where former serves as style transfer model aiming towards achieving consistenc

Recent developments in prompt-based generative AI has given rise to discourse surrounding the need for a more comprehensive evaluation framework that accounts for a wider range of criteria. This paper introduces a multi-dimensional evaluation protocol for large language models (LLMs) that goes beyond existing approaches and considers multiple facets, including language fluency, consistency, semantic relevance, and contextual awareness. The results demonstrate that the proposed methodology yields more accurate assessments of LLM abilities, identifying both strengths and weaknesses with higher precision. We propose several refinements, highlighting their significance in improving the evaluation process's reliability and transparency. Our findings provide valuable insights into the current st

Markov Decision Processes (MDPs) are a popular framework for modeling decision-making agents in stochastic environments. Temporal-Difference (TD) learning algorithms are a class of popular methods for solving MDPs that update an estimate of the value function by using a single pass through the episodes. Experience Replay (ER) is a technique that improves the TD learning algorithms by storing the experiences in a buffer and using them to update the value function. Prior works have shown that ER-TD methods can outperform the TD methods on complex problems with sparse rewards. However, ER-TD methods can be unstable when the MDP has large state-action spaces or when the value function is parameterized by a neural network. We study a switching classes of ER-TD methods that use convex combinations of multiple learning rates. We show that switching classes can improve the performance of ER-TD methods and make them more robust. We also derive a novel method for computing the optimal switching policy for a given MDP, which we show empirically to be more effective than grid search.

Graph neural network (GNN) based recommender systems have become one of the mainstream trends in the recommendation field, which has demonstrated its superiority over traditional methods in many scenarios. However, it is still challenging to apply GNNs to large-scale recommendation tasks due to their high computational cost and memory consumption. To address these challenges, we propose a new GNN framework called SpGNN that incorporates a sparse-based sampling mechanism into existing GNNs. The proposed SpGNN can be easily applied to various GNNs by utilizing their output as the input for the next layer, while maintaining the same expressive power as the original GNNs. Experiments on two large-scale datasets demonstrate that the proposed SpGNN can achieve competitive performance compared wi

The network-based study of financial systems has received considerable attention in recent years but has seldom considered the effects on systemic risk arising from different market players. In this paper, we introduce a novel approach based upon Markov processes to model how investors' behavior can affect system stability and thereby shed light into which types are more likely to trigger crisis situations such as bank runs or crashes that lead markets astray by making them overly optimistic about future returns; thus leading towards increased volatility levels within these sectors with potentially devastating consequences for society at large if left unchecked over time periods long enough without intervention measures taken against it beforehand (e..g., regulatory interventions).

In recent years, palmprints have been widely used for individual verification. The rich privacy of palmprints makes them an ideal biometric modality in the field of personal authentication and identification. However, palmprints are prone to various types of attacks, including smudge, occlusion, and misalignment. To address these challenges, we propose a novel image restoration framework called PRIME (PRIvacy-enhanced MInimalist EMbedded) that can effectively handle these attacks. Our approach utilizes minimal information to restore the original features while ensuring the preservation of the privacy of the subject's identity. This paper introduces a detailed architecture diagram of our proposed method, which is comprised of three main modules: feature extraction, feature embedding, and fe

Graph neural networks (GNNs) have become increasingly popular in modeling graph-structured data. However, the scalability of GNNs is limited by their high memory footprint and computational complexity. To address these challenges, this paper introduces a novel and efficient graph encoding framework based on a multi-scale Fourier transform (MSFT). We propose a hierarchical convolution operator that decomposes the original graph into multiple frequency bands using MSFT. Each band captures different features, such as local, global, or structural information, which can be combined to reconstruct the original graph. Compared with existing graph encoders, our approach significantly reduces the number of operations and memory consumption while maintaining high accuracy. Experimental results demon

The quest for effective and efficient question-answer representations has been a driving force behind the advancement of various natural language processing tasks. This paper delves into the heart of the matter by investigating the joint optimization of question and answer representations in a black-box setting, where only question-answer pairs are accessible. We introduce EEE-QA, a novel approach that employs a contrastive loss to concurrently optimize question and answer representations. The innovative triple-negative maximum inner product loss used in EEE-QA acts as a catalyst for the success of our method, as it effectively improves the selectivity of the optimization process. Our method not only outperforms the state-of-the-art but also exhibits a remarkable ability to distill pre-trained models, making it a promising solution for question-answering tasks. We hope that our work will inspire further research into the optimization of question-answer representations and the development of more effective and efficient natural language processing systems.

Metrics are the foundation for automatic evaluation in grammatical error correction (GEC), with their use also becoming increasingly common in human-centered evaluations. In recent years, however – especially as we consider new forms of GECs such that correct beyond traditional sentence boundaries and across languages or domains -- researchers have argued against using these metrics to evaluate models’ performance because they do not fully capture what is important about a task like correcting text written by humans who make errors due its context: “correct” does depend on how well an answer fits into surrounding sentences; even if it's grammatically perfect but completely wrong otherwise! This paper examines current methods used within Automatic Machine Learning Systems (“AML”) which rely

Cedar is a new authorization policy language designed to be ergonomic, fast, safe, and expressive for modern systems. We identify a set of desirable properties that languages should possess to meet the needs of authorization in today's complex systems. For example, a language must allow for multiple authorization policies to be combined together into a single policy tree, such as with AND/OR operators. It also must offer support for advanced constructs like wildcard patterns to allow for efficient matching against complex roles or resources. The goal of our work is to design a language for authorizing access to resources that meets these requirements while also being intuitive and easy to use. To do so, we build on existing research in computer science, such as type theory and lambda calcu

Modern life sciences research is increasingly relying on artificial intelligence approaches to model biological systems, primarily through the use of neural networks. In many cases, this involves feeding large amounts of data into a neural network and hoping for the best outcome from its training. However, while this approach can be effective in some cases, it does not necessarily guarantee success. This paper discusses the potential pitfalls associated with over-reliance on such an approach, highlighting the need for more rigorous validation methods in this context. We also provide examples of when neural networks have been used effectively in the realm of computational biology. Overall, we argue that there needs to be greater emphasis placed on the evaluation and validation stages of dev

The implicit offensive language (IOL) dataset is a relatively new but rapidly growing dataset that includes offensive content but is less explicit than traditional offensive language. IOL can be found in online communities, and its presence can harm community members' well-being and safety. However, existing IOL datasets are limited in size and diversity. To address these limitations, we propose a new process to generate a large-scale IOL dataset (OffLanDat) using a pre-trained language model (PLM) through prompt engineering. We involve community members in the generation process to ensure the dataset's authenticity and diversity. Our dataset consists of 7,000 posts from 7 online communities, labeled as containing implicit offensive content or not. We also provide PLM-generated explanations for each post. We evaluate OffLanDat's utility for IOL research tasks, including classification and generation, and show that it can be used to improve the performance of PLMs. We also involve human evaluators in the study to assess the quality of the generated dataset.

Increasing the accuracy of instance segmentation methods is often done at the expense of speed. Using coarser representations, we can reduce the number of parameters and thus obtain real-time masks. In this paper, we take inspiration from the set cover problem to predict mask approximations. Given ground-truth binary masks of objects of interest as training input, our method learns to predict the approximate coverage of these objects by disks without supervision on their location or radius. Each object is represented by a fixed number of disks with different radii. In the learning phase, we consider the radius as proportional to a standard deviation in order to compute the error to propagate on a set of two-dimensional Gaussian functions rather than disks. We trained and tested our instanc

Deployable polyhedrons can transform between Platonic and Archimedean polyhedrons to fit a variety of tasks, as shown in the figure. However, most existing deployable structures are not reconfigurable, so their shapes cannot be changed once deployed. To address this, we present an approach for designing reconfigurable deployable polyhedra that can adapt to changing task requirements. We formulate a task-specific optimization problem that determines the optimal set of hinges to install on a given rigid base polyhedron. The solution gives a set of hinge locations, which we call a ``skeleton'' since it defines the backbone structure of the deployable polyhedron. To realize a skeleton, we introduce a method for automatically generating a deployable polyhedron that can conform to the desired sk

Fine classification of city-scale buildings from satellite remote sensing imagery is a crucial research area, where semantic segmentation plays a significant role. However, obtaining accurate and reliable building boundaries for large cities in a reasonable amount of time remains challenging. In this paper, we introduce the Scalable and Accurate Building Segmentation (SABS) approach to address this problem by using an effective combination of deep learning models, enhanced training techniques, and efficient data processing strategies. SABS comprises two stages: the initial stage includes an effective combination of state-of-the-art image-level and pixel-level semantic segmentation models, followed by a post-processing step that enhances the quality of the generated masks through iterative

Large Language Models (LLMs) have recently demonstrated impressive results across a wide range of tasks. However, reasoning and planning, which are fundamental for intelligence, have not been demonstrated at scale. In this work, we investigate the reasoning and planning capabilities of LLMs by evaluating a range of models, including GPT-3, GPT-4, and a newly developed LLM with access to a large planning library. We design a new benchmark for reasoning and planning, which involves answering yes-no questions about the contents of a story, with access to a planner that can generate partial plans and a reasoner that can generate natural language explanations. Our experiments show that LLMs can reason and plan, but their performance is limited by their ability to access and integrate external knowledge, and by their internal consistency.

Vision-language models (VLMs) are powerful tools for solving various downstream tasks. However, they can be vulnerable to adversarial attacks. We propose a new type of adversarial attack, called ImgTrojan, that aims to take control of the VLMs' output. Specifically, our attack adds a hidden image to the input image, which is invisible to human eyes but can be detected by the VLM. We show that ImgTrojan can effectively manipulate ten popular VLMs, including four pretrained models and six fine-tuned models, on three tasks: image classification, visual reasoning, and visual entailment. Remarkably, we show that a single image is sufficient to jailbreak these powerful VLMs. To defend against our attack, we propose a simple yet effective defense that imposes constraints on the hidden image.

Open-vocabulary generalization requires robotic systems to perform tasks involving complex and diverse objects not present in the training data. In this work, we introduce a novel open world vision language model (OWVLM) that combines an image encoder with object representations learned from self supervision on large scale Internet datasets into GPT3 style text models for visual reasoning over images of arbitrary domains at human level performance without any task or domain specific pretraining by using only small amounts (~20K tokens per class/task). We first show how our OWVLMs can be used as part 1: i.) A strong baseline system outperforming all previous approaches across various benchmarks including OpenAI's new MetaWorld Vision Language Challenge ii.).

Human comprehension of a video stream is naturally broad: in a few instants, we are able to understand the actions of people and objects that appear on screen. However, this is not easy for current state-of-the-art video understanding models. This paper presents an efficient approach for learning temporally robust representations from raw image frames by combining multi-frame context with single frame cues. We propose a multi-modal temporal pooling module that processes both single-frame features and temporal features learned from the entire sequence. This module is combined with a novel feature aggregation strategy, which takes into account the importance of temporal features for each query. This allows us to learn more effective and temporally robust representations, resulting in improve

The field of biomedical research has witnessed a significant increase in the accumulation of vast amounts of data. Large language models (LLMs) have been widely adopted for their ability to process and synthesize information from diverse sources. This paper introduces MEDICO, a large multimodal transformer-based model designed specifically for medical applications. Unlike previous LLMs, which were trained on general-purpose datasets or specialized but isolated ones, our method harnesses knowledge from multiple medical databases, including PubMed, OpenAI's Medical Dataset, WikiMD, and others. We also employ a multi-modal pretraining strategy that incorporates textual data from Pubmed and image data from Dermatology Atlas, enhancing its abilities to analyze visual data. To ensure that the mo

Despite great advances in program synthesis techniques, they remain algorithmic black boxes. Although they guarantee the correctness of generated programs, they provide no insight into why a particular program was chosen over others. Explaining the choices made by such techniques is important for assessing their validity and effectiveness. In this paper we study program synthesis from a human-in-the-loop perspective. We propose an interactive paradigm for program synthesis that can be used to explain program synthesizers' choices. The user can intervene during the search process to guide it towards a solution that fits her needs. Our approach does not require any changes to the underlying synthesizer; instead, it relies on two key features: (1) a modular implementation of the synthesizer a

Raman spectroscopy is a powerful chemical analysis tool that reveals the molecular composition of a sample without the need for destructive sampling. However, interpreting the Raman spectrum of a complex sample remains a challenge, especially when the sample is composed of unknown or novel materials. Hyperspectral unmixing aims to resolve mixed spectra into their pure components, a process also known as spectral unmixing. While hyperspectral unmixing has been widely studied in remote sensing and image processing, its application to Raman spectroscopy is not straightforward, as Raman spectra are composed of discrete spectral features, in contrast to the continuous spectra processed by image unmixing algorithms. Here we propose a physics-constrained autoencoder that exploits the discrete nature of Raman spectra and the physical processes underlying Raman scattering to unmix spectra mixture. The proposed method outperforms existing unmixing algorithms on both synthetic and real-world Raman spectra. We envision that our method will find applications in diverse areas, from art authentication to exoplanet exploration.

Recommender system (RS) models have shown remarkable performance in predicting the next item given the history of viewed items. However, the majority of existing models rely on simple Markovian assumptions or ignore the state of the recommendation process, which often leads to sub-optimal recommendations. In contrast, Sequential Recommendation (SR) models capture the state of the recommendation process by modeling the entire history of user interactions, improving the ability to predict relevant items. Nevertheless, modeling the entire state space of user interactions in SR models results in two major issues: (i) the state space can grow exponentially with the number of item categories, leading to high computational costs, and (ii) the model becomes overly complex, making it difficult to optimize. To address these issues, we propose Mamba4Rec, a novel SR model that employs a selective state space model based on a memory network. Mamba4Rec learns a representation of the state space by selectively visiting a subset of states using a value-based selection strategy. This approach not only reduces the computational burden but also helps the model focus on relevant states, improving optimization and generalization. Experimental results on three benchmark datasets demonstrate the effectiveness of Mamba4Rec in balancing performance, efficiency, and scalability.

Message Passing Interface (MPI) is a popular standard for writing portable, parallel programs on a variety of architectures. Errors in MPI programs are difficult to detect and diagnose due to their nondeterministic behavior and lack of useful failure information. Programmers usually rely on the debugger to find the root cause of a program failure, which is time-consuming and often ineffective. Thus, it is desirable to have a set of automated tools that can help locate the faults in MPI programs. Fault localization tools can potentially save researchers and engineers significant time and resources by reducing the need for manual inspection. In this paper, we propose an MPI fault localization tool based on graph neural networks (GNNs) and vector embedding techniques over the intermediate representation (IR) of MPI programs. We represent an MPI program as a graph data structure in the form of a call graph, which captures the control flow of the program. The graph is converted into a tensor representation using GNNs, and the vector representation of the IR is obtained using the LLVM toolchain. We combine these two representations to obtain a joint embedding of the program graph and its IR. We use this joint embedding to build a fault localization model that can predict the presence of faults in the program. We evaluate our approach on real-world MPI benchmark applications that exhibit various kinds of faults. Our experiments show that our GNN-based approach outperforms other state-of-the-art fault localization approaches in terms of accuracy.

In this work, we introduce Ducho 2.0, the latest stable version of our framework for modeling and solving linear optimization problems. Our software contains a collection of solvers that offer state-of-the-art performance in terms of scalability and robustness, enabling users to solve large-scale problems quickly and efficiently. We also highlight several significant advancements in our codebase, including improvements to parallelization techniques, the addition of new numerical solver components, and enhancements to existing components such as the preconditioner, which has been refined to increase its accuracy and efficiency. Lastly, we provide comprehensive benchmarks that compare the performance of our solvers against other existing methods, demonstrating their superior speed and accura

Vision-Language Transformers (VLTs) have shown great success recently, but are computationally expensive and require a large amount of training data to achieve good performance. The current practice is to use pretrained models for downstream tasks. However, the effectiveness of fine-tuning such models on new downstream tasks has not been explored in depth. In this paper, we propose to use the pretraining task as an auxiliary task during fine-tuning. Specifically, we propose a novel Pretraining Auxiliary Task (PAT) framework that incorporates the pretraining task as an additional loss function to be optimized during fine-tuning. We conduct extensive experiments on various vision-language tasks including visual question answering, object detection and segmentation, image captioning, and vide

Clique enumeration in networks under LDP (Local Differential Privacy) is a fundamental task for privacy-aware analytics. The goal is to find all cliques of size at least $q$ in a graph with $n$ nodes, while ensuring that the privacy of any user (node) is protected. In this work, we introduce K-stars LDP, a novel framework for clique enumeration under LDP. We prove that K-stars LDP achieves optimal privacy for the $(p, q)$-clique problem, for any choice of parameters $n$, $p$, and $q$. We also show that K-stars LDP outperforms the state-of-the-art method for $(p, q)$-clique enumeration under LDP on real-world graphs.

Natural Language Processing (NLP) has made tremendous progress in recent years, thanks to the success of deep learning approaches like Transformers. Unfortunately, this progress has primarily benefited certain social classes, while excluding others. In this work, we analyze the class bias in NLP datasets and the resulting impact on model performance. We find that state-of-the-art models reproduce and even exacerbate existing class disparities. Certain classes are underrepresented in the datasets, which leads to worse model performance for these classes. Moreover, the way we evaluate NLP models encourages the development of classist technology. We propose solutions to better incorporate social classes in NLP datasets, so that we can develop unbiased language technology for all members of society.

Self-attention computes a weighted sum of a set of vectors, where the weights are determined by computing similarities between all pairs of vectors. As the size of the set grows, the computation of self-attention becomes increasingly slower. To speed up the computation, existing proposals either assume the input vectors are fixed (e.g., ViT) or linearize the softmax function (e.g., Swin). In this work, we question the assumption that the input vectors are fixed and show that self-attention can be made linear in the size of the set by generalizing the softmax function to the Taylor-softmax function. The Taylor-softmax function computes a weighted sum of the input vectors, where the weights are determined by the Taylor expansion of the softmax function. We implement a novel computation kernel for Taylor-softmax and show that it has the same complexity as linear softmax (i.e., Swin) but can capture non-linear dependencies between the input vectors. We call our self-attention layer Taylor-softmax attention and show that it achieves higher performance than softmax attention for both image classification and natural language tasks. We also show that Taylor-softmax attention can be made to approximate softmax attention arbitrarily well.

Deep learning networks have been successfully applied to remote sensing image segmentation. However, it is challenging to obtain accurate ground truth labels for large-scale datasets. Human errors during the labeling process result in noisy labels, which propagate into the training process and affect the performance of the model. Most existing methods assume that the training data is fully annotated, and some researchers use a two-stage method to correct the noisy labels. However, this requires that the validation data be fully annotated, which is time-consuming and expensive in the real-world application. In this paper, we propose a one-stage method to correct the noisy labels during training. The proposed method assumes that the training data is completely or partially annotated and uses an auxiliary network to predict the confidence of each pixel. This confidence is used to correct the labels of pixels with low confidence. The experimental results on the ISPRS Vaihingen and Potsdam datasets show that the proposed method achieves better performance than the two-stage method.

Geospatial machine learning (GeoML) is increasingly being adopted to analyze spatial data. However, the lack of interpretability in GeoML models often hinders its application to real-world problems. Instead of relying on black-box models, domain experts prefer interpretable models that can be easily validated and trusted. In this paper, we propose an ensemble framework for explainable GeoML models. The framework, called XGeoEnsemble, incorporates state-of-the-art explainable machine learning (XML) models and ensemble learning techniques to improve the interpretability of GeoML models. We validate our approach under both controlled and realistic conditions using synthetic and real-world spatial data. Our results show that XGeoEnsemble produces more accurate and geographically meaningful explanations than existing XML models. XGeoEnsemble is available as an open-source Python package.

To use new robot hardware in a new environment, it is necessary to develop a control program tailored for the specific task. This can be tedious and time-consuming if not using methods that allow us to quickly adapt or learn controllers from examples of desired behavior (e. g., demonstrations). We propose an approach based on learning hierarchical policies with Bayesian optimization as well suited to this purpose because we can efficiently explore large spaces while optimizing several objectives simultaneously by utilizing multiple demonstrators at once during training instead just one single demonstration trajectory like most existing approaches do today .

The design of feedback controllers is undergoing a paradigm shift from modelic (i.e., model-based) to data-driven approaches, due to the former's inability to cope with the emerging complexities and nonlinearities of dynamical systems. In this context, reinforcement learning has shown great promise by achieving state-of-the-art performance in various benchmark tasks. However, its applicability to real-world scenarios is limited by the need for large amounts of training data and a substantial amount of computational time. To address these challenges, we propose a novel approach that leverages a combination of modelic and data-driven techniques called Modelic Reinforcement Learning (MoReL).

Large language models (LLMs) such as GPT-4 have recently demonstrated impressive capabilities across a wide range of tasks. There is ongoing debate as to whether LLMs have possessed these capabilities all along, or whether they have emerged recently as a result of model size. To address this debate, we developed a suite of 7B parameter LLMs spanning a wide range of math problem types. We show that these 7B models already possess strong math capabilities, outperforming GPT-4 and other large models. This demonstrates that the math capabilities of large models have not emerged recently as a result of model size, but have always been there. The 7B models are able to solve difficult algebra, calculus, and geometry problems, and are able to generate proofs. We also show that the 7B models are able to solve problems on the 2012 AMC 10 and 2014 AMC 12 math competitions, indicating strong problem-solving capabilities. Lastly, we pre-train a 7B model on a large math dataset and fine-tune it on a small science dataset. We find that the 7B models outperform the 30B GPT-4 on math problems from the 2012 AMC 10 and 2014 AMC 12, while also outperforming GPT-4 on a variety of other math problem types. This study demonstrates that smaller LLMs can possess strong math capabilities, and that these capabilities are not dependent on model size.

Large Language Models (LLMs) have demonstrated impressive results across a wide range of tasks. Many studies, however, have evaluated LLMs on English-only datasets. Is LLMs' performance limited to English? Or can they handle other languages as well? In this work, we develop a new framework to evaluate LLMs' multilingual capabilities, called MultiQ. MultiQ consists of two components: a multilingual dataset with questions in multiple languages, and a novel evaluation protocol that simulates the scenario where an LLM is queried in multiple languages. We use MultiQ to evaluate a range of LLMs, including GPT-3, GPT-4, BLOOM, and LLaMA. We also compare the performance of these LLMs on our challenging multilingual datasets with their performance on English-only datasets. Our experiments show that LLMs can achieve similar performance on our multilingual datasets as on English-only datasets, once we account for the length of the input questions. This suggests that LLMs have strong multilingual capabilities, and that their performance on English-only datasets is not due to English-specific biases.

Abstract reasoning problems challenge the perceptual and cognitive abilities of AI algorithms, demanding advanced problem-solving skills. We propose a novel approach to address such problems by harnessing the potential of Large Language Models (LLMs), in particular GPT4. Our method entails prompting LLM with a step-by-step solution to the problem, enabling it to identify key concepts, construct a solution plan, and eventually solve the abstract problem. This process is facilitated through our customized template and prompt generation strategies, specifically designed for enhancing LLM's ability in solving abstract problems. The proposed framework demonstrates its effectiveness on three challenging abstract reasoning benchmarks: Symbolic Reasoning Challenge (SRC), Abstract Reasoning Test (A

The increasing use of information technology has led to a significant share of energy consumption and carbon emissions from data centers. In this paper, we present the first large-scale study that comprehensively explores the energy efficiency of cloud computing systems by modeling them as a network of interconnected servers and clients. Specifically, we provide insights into how different system configurations impact the overall energy efficiency and discuss the trade-offs between cost savings and carbon emission reductions. Our study covers both greenfield deployments and migration scenarios where existing infrastructure is upgraded with more energy efficient hardware. We further explore potential mitigation strategies for reducing power consumption at the client end, focusing on adaptiv

Grading short answer questions automatically with interpretable reasoning behind the grading decision is a challenging goal. We introduce G-Net, which takes into account all aspects of human feedback for each student response when performing automatic scoring and provides an interpretation that justifies its decisions to improve learning outcomes at scale by enabling teachers/TAs who are overburdened in their work due lack access resources such as time or expertise required grade large amounts data efficiently without sacrificing quality assurance standards set forth during development process; this also allows them better understand how best practices should be implemented moving forward so they can continue making progress towards achieving these goals effectively while maintaining high

This study presents a systematic enumeration of spherical ($SO(3)$) type parallel robots and their generalized kinematic structures. The number of $SO(3)$ type parallel robots is derived from the number of different types of 2-dimensional parallel robotic systems, using the theory of generalized kinematics. An algorithm for generating all possible configurations of $SO(3)$ type parallel robots is provided with a discussion of its generality. In addition to the analysis of $SO(3)$-type parallel robots, the study also offers a new perspective on the concept of generalized kinematic structures by examining how the constraints imposed by the kinematic structure affect the performance of parallel robots. The results presented in this paper can be used as a reference for further research and dev

Coupled tensor decomposition (CTD) can extract joint features from multimodal data in a way that is invariant to modality-specific transformation. CTD has been extended for multidimensional tensors and its applications, such as the coupled low rank approximation of spatiotemporal images with multiple channels or signals at different frequencies within each channel [1]. In this work we extend it further by introducing an alternating iterative algorithm based on multiplicative update rules tailored specifically towards real world datasets consisting both spatial image frames over time together but also higher dimensional audio spectrograms across several frequency bands per frame pair which are all modeled using three separate components: global mean values plus two sets representing local v

Are malicious repositories hiding under the educational label in GitHub? Recent studies have identified collections of GitHub repositories (called "clusters") that consist of both legitimate and malicious projects. We hypothesize that these clusters are a result of a clustering bias, whereby legitimate and malicious projects group together due to the same labels they receive from GitHub's classification algorithms. However, little is known about whether the prevalence of malicious projects varies across these clusters. In this paper, we conduct an analysis on GitHub's Python ecosystem, focusing specifically on 735 clusters labeled as "educational" by GitHub. We find that malicious projects make up a significant percentage of all Python projects in the cluster: 69% of Python projects in edu

We study level set teleportation, an optimization sub-routine which seeks to accelerate the learning of complex dynamical systems. In this setting, we consider a dynamical system with unknown dynamics and initial state $x_0$. Our goal is to find the optimal input trajectory $\boldsymbol{u}^*$ that steers the system from an arbitrary initial state $x_0$ to a target state $\bar x$ in minimal time, i.e., $\min_{\boldsymbol{u}} \tau(\boldsymbol{u})$, where $\tau(\boldsymbol{u})$ is the length of the trajectory. When the dynamics are not known, we must learn them using some number of observations. The standard approach is to estimate the model parameters via gradient descent on the negative log likelihood of the data. This requires computing a loss function at each iteration, and the computatio

Orthogonal time frequency space (OTFS) modulation, a delay-Doppler (DD)-domain transmission method, has recently been proposed and has attracted considerable attention for future wireless communication systems. In this paper, we propose a new channel model for OTFS modulation that includes both DD information and spatial angle of arrival (AoA), which is the first channel model including AoA in orthogonal frequency division multiplexing (OFDM). This channel model considers multiple transmit antennas as well as spatial multipath components with arbitrary Doppler shifts. In addition, the effects of sub-Nyquist sampling and guard intervals are also considered. Based on this new channel model, we derive an analytical expression for the signal-to-interference plus noise ratio (SINR) of OFDM sign

Creating effective educational materials generally requires expensive and time-consuming studies of student learning outcomes. To address this challenge, we introduce a new paradigm for designing adaptive assessments: interactive machine teaching (IMT). IMT is the application to education domains of reinforcement learner selfplay [1]. We show how it can be used in three different ways as follows
Interactive Machine Teaching
Denote by $X$ some input space associated with an assessment question or problem instance such that $\mathbf{x} \in X$.

The exponential growth of scientific literature requires effective management and extraction of valuable insights. While existing scientific knowledge graphs (SKGs) have been proposed to address these challenges, they are restricted by the lack of rich data integration methods, limited coverage of disciplines, and inadequate semantic reasoning capabilities. To address these limitations, we propose the SCIKG-Fusion framework that integrates multiple SKGs through a unified graph neural network (U-GNN). Our framework enhances the representation learning ability of U-GNN through the introduction of a multi-layer context-aware fusion module (CA-FM), which leverages the hierarchical nature of science to enhance the cross-domain connections across SKGs. By incorporating cross-domain information,

We present Human to Humanoid (H2O), a reinforcement learning (RL) framework that enables an agent with humanoid body morphology and dynamics, such as humanoids or robots equipped for walking on two legs. We design the reward function of H-to O using imitation techniques via video demonstrations from humans; this makes it possible to learn effective behaviors in which our robot successfully executes tasks while maintaining stable balance during locomotion without additional training data other than those videos collected by us beforehand. This is especially important when implementing RL algorithms involving large amounts computation time required due their inherent exploration phase over multiple iterations before achieving satisfactory results even if given sufficient samples obtained thr

The advent of Large Language Models (LLMs) has advanced the benchmark in various Natural Language Processing tasks. However, as LLMs continue to demonstrate remarkable linguistic capabilities, their performance on the task of generating coherent and meaningful stories remains a challenging aspect. This work focuses on enhancing the storytelling capability of LLMs by incorporating the principle of "story flow" into the generation process. We introduce a framework, StoryFlow, that employs the concept of "story flow" to guide the generation of coherent and engaging stories from LLMs. Specifically, we employ an enhanced story flow-based prompt with predefined templates to generate an initial story draft. Subsequently, we utilize a novel approach based on language modeling for further refinemen

Three-dimensional Digital Subtraction Angiography (3D-DSA) is a well-established tool for the diagnosis and treatment planning of cerebrovascular disease. However, this technique is not widely used in clinical practice because it requires significant manual intervention and relies on a limited number of projections. In this study, we present an automatic approach to reconstruct 3D-DSA from standard 2D DSA images using a single-shot neural network, called S3DNet. Our method enables fast and accurate reconstruction without requiring additional input images or patient-specific models, making it suitable for various applications. To evaluate our approach, we conducted experiments on three public datasets containing 3D-DSA volumes with corresponding 2D DSA images.

We present the technical details of Breeze-7B, a 7B parameter causal language model trained by Alibaba Cloud. Breeze-7B is trained on a custom-built dataset called Breeze, which is a re-interpretation and extension of the widely-used ALBEF benchmark. The Breeze benchmark pushes the ALBEF card to its limit by requiring the model to generate 780M tokens per second on a single A100 GPU. This is equivalent to generating 102.9K words per second, or about 17.1 words per token on a 512 token prompt. Breeze-7B is the first model to pass the Breeze benchmark, achieving a score of 7.4K tokens/s/GPU. We also show that Breeze-7B outperforms Llama-7B in terms of F1 score on the Breeze benchmark.

Medical Visual Question Answering (MVQA) aims to answer clinical questions given visual evidence, and has gained increasing research attention because of its huge potential to facilitate clinical decision-making. However, existing MVQA models still suffer from poor generalization, especially on data with unfamiliar image appearances (e.g., artifacts). In this work, we propose a novel gradient-guided model perturbation method, dubbed GradMVQA, to enhance the generalization of existing MVQA models. Specifically, we first design a gradient-based feature extractor to capture the discriminative visual features for distinguishing data with artifacts from normal data. We then propose a data-augmentation-based model perturbation method to perturb the existing MVQA model and make it more robust against data with artifacts. Different from the existing model perturbation methods that randomly perturb the model parameters, we propose a gradient-guided parameter perturbation scheme to selectively perturb the parameters that contribute most to the model's wrong prediction. Extensive experiments on two widely-used MVQA datasets demonstrate that our GradMVQA method achieves substantial improvements over existing MVQA models.

Despite the success in specific tasks and scenarios, existing foundation agents, empowered by large models (e.g., LLaMA2-13B), suffer from a narrow and limited scope of generalization ability and flexibility. To address these challenges, we propose a novel paradigm called Flexible Foundation Agents (FFA) with a versatile architecture that can easily adapt to different downstream tasks via simple yet effective architectural modifications. This design allows for greater transferability across domains and applications, enabling the agent to quickly learn the nuances of new problems without compromising its robustness or efficiency. Additionally, the agent is trained on various real-world datasets and generalizes well on diverse tasks, demonstrating its capability as a universal foundation for

This study considers the linear contextual bandit problem with independent and identically distributed (i.i.d.) arms, which is a special case of the multi-armed bandit problem where the arm distributions are fixed for each round. We propose a dynamic lower bounding framework that adapts to the distribution of arms by selecting an appropriate subset of arms for each round, thereby ensuring that the regret does not increase too quickly from one round to the next. Specifically, we design a stochastic algorithm that uses a simple greedy selection rule and obtains a non-asymptotic upper bound on the expected regret in terms of the maximum value of the gap between the lower bound and the upper bound. Moreover, under certain conditions, we show that this bound matches the optimal rate of converge

We study the finite-time convergence of TD learning with linear function approximation under Markovian sampling. Existing proofs for this setting either assume a projection step in the algorithm to simplify the analysis, or require a fairly intricate argument to ensure stability of the iterates.
