We ask: \textit{Is it possible to retain the simplicity of a projection-based analysis without actually performing a projection step in the algorithm?} Our main contribution is to show this is possible via a novel two-step argument. In the first step, we use induction to prove that under a standard choice of a constant step-size $\alpha$, the iterates generated by TD learning remain uniformly bounded in expectation. In the second step, we establish a recursion that mimics the steady-state dynamics

We present DEMOS, a novel approach for dynamic environment motion synthesis in 3D scenes. Our method aims to capture the spatial-temporal coherence of dynamic scenes, characterized by the motion of objects over time. To achieve this, we divide the 3D space into spatially coherent volumetric cells connected through the BEV representation. We propose a novel local spherical-BEV perception approach that captures spatial-temporal context for each cell by integrating information from neighboring cells. Our perception approach avoids the global understanding bottleneck of vanilla BEV perception and the computationally expensive voxel-based local perception. To generate realistic motion, we design a motion feature extraction approach that extracts and synthesizes motion features from static models, incorporating motion styles of real-world objects. Our method can capture the spatial-temporal coherency of dynamic scenes, characterized by the motion of objects over time, and has been evaluated on the moving MNIST dataset and the 3D car tracking dataset. The results demonstrate that our method outperforms existing techniques in terms of computational efficiency and the ability to capture spatial-temporal coherency.

We study online learning problems in constrained Markov decision processes (CMDPs) with adversarial transition functions. The adversary is restricted to have access only to the history of action-observation pairs. This setting captures a common scenario where an agent interacts with the environment using a fixed policy and observes rewards, whereas the transition dynamics are unknown. We introduce a new algorithm based on upper confidence bounding and show that it achieves a regret bound of $O(T^{2/3}\log T)$. Our result improves upon the previously known bound of $\tilde{O}(T^{1/2})$ in this setting. To obtain our bound we develop a novel technique for bounding the advantage function for the CMDP setting. Previous attempts at this problem required a full exploration phase before the learn

A good evaluation framework should evaluate multimodal machine translation (MMT) models by measuring their ability to generate a high-quality output with diverse representations of the input. The current metrics in MMT focus on evaluating translation quality and consistency, but do not capture the multilinguality of the translations or the diversity of input features. In this paper, we propose a new metric called Diverse-MLM which measures how well the model can generate diverse outputs given diverse inputs. We argue that it is important for MMT models to produce diverse outputs so they can be more robust to unknown languages and have better generalization performance. To evaluate our method, we use a large-scale dataset including 28 language pairs. Our results show that Diverse-MLM is eff

With growing application of machine learning (ML) technologies in healthcare, there have been calls for greater transparency and explainability in the outputs produced by ML models. To date, however, it remains unclear how patients understand and make sense of such explanations. Drawing on a qualitative study with 30 participants, we investigated how different explanation formats affected patient's understanding of predictions from a deep learning model for heart disease risk. Participants were shown 12 different types of explanations - including textual, graphical, and numerical formats, as well as combinations of them - for two different scenarios: one that involved their own medical data, and another that used anonymised data of other patients. We found that when participants received e

Recent developments have underscored the critical role of \textit{differential privacy} in building trusted artificial intelligence systems. As a fundamental mechanism for achieving differential data-release guarantees, $k$-means clustering has been widely used to provide such protections against adversarial attacks and internal biases within machine learning applications -- from fairness considerations over credit risk scoring models [Dwork et al., 2017] all through recommendation algorithms that are biased towards popular movies on streaming platforms [\textsc{{Fink}\&{\H{\"o}}//**r},~**\textbf{{\underline {arXiv:}}}~\text{~}{\textcolor[rgb]{(}{39586)}~~}.

The network-based study of financial systems has received considerable attention in recent years but has seldom been applied to the credit markets. This paper aims to fill this gap by focusing on a particular type of loan: SME loans (Small and Medium Enterprises). We use the data of 172,384 SME loan portfolios from 698 financial institutions and analyze the structure of the interbank credit network. Specifically, we construct a bipartite network consisting of banks and companies, where a link between a bank and a company indicates that the bank provides the company with a loan. Our results show that the interbank credit networks exhibit high clustering coefficients, reflecting strong interdependencies among banks and companies within local communities. In addition, we find that the central

Large Language Models (LLMs) have shown impressive results across a wide range of tasks. However, reasoning with natural language is still challenging for LLMs and there is little understanding of the specific limitations of LLMs in natural language reasoning. In this work, we investigate LLMs' limitations in compositional relation reasoning, a key component of common sense reasoning. We introduce a new relation reasoning dataset, `Triangle', that is composed of triples of natural language sentences connected by logical relations (e.g., the relation between 'Alice is taller than Bob' and 'Bob is taller than Charlie'). The dataset consists of 55K triples for training and 11.5K triples for testing. We comprehensively examine the performance of eight LLMs on this task and compare them with a strong baseline model. We identify several specific limitations of LLMs in relation reasoning, including (1) difficulty in understanding the logical relations between sentences, (2) susceptibility to syntactic variations, and (3) reliance on surface form. Our study provides valuable insights into the limitations of current LLMs and suggests directions for improving their natural language reasoning capabilities.

Existing 3D mesh shape evaluation metrics mainly focus on the overall shape but are usually less sensitive to local details. In this paper, we propose a new metric named Laplacian-based Local Shape Error (L-LSER) to evaluate the performance of shape completion algorithms. It is based on the observation that the Laplacian spectrum of the original mesh can be used as an effective feature representation of the shape in local regions, and a distance measure between two meshes can be defined using this feature. The proposed metric is evaluated on several benchmark datasets and shows superior performance compared with existing methods. We also investigate the properties of the L-LSER metric and provide insights into its application for evaluating the performance of 3D mesh completion algorithms.

The rapid development of information technology, especially the Internet, has facilitated users with a quick and easy way to access information. While there are many benefits from this trend, the disadvantages cannot be ignored. In recent years, online privacy issues have become increasingly prominent. To address these concerns, several different methods have been proposed in the literature for protecting personal information on the Internet, including encryption, obfuscation, and anonymization techniques. Among them, obfuscation has attracted significant attention due to its advantages over other methods. Obfuscation methods can be broadly categorized into three categories: data obfuscation, text obfuscation, and network obfuscation. This survey provides comprehensive coverage of existing

An assurance case has become an integral component for the certification of safety-critical systems. It is used to demonstrate that a system meets its requirements and can operate in all foreseeable conditions safely, which are captured by failure modes effects analysis (FMEA). The FMEAs require engineers with domain knowledge; however they can be time consuming as well due to their manual nature where each identified hazard requires detailed investigation before being recorded on paper or using spreadsheets/databases such Excel sheets etc., leading some organizations towards adopting automated approaches like risk assessment software tools instead so those tasks may get completed quickly enough without sacrificing quality standards altogether!

Physics-informed neural networks (PINNs) are a popular class of deep learning models for constructing numerical solvers for differential equations. Traditional PINNs are based on a single static snapshot, and do not enforce temporal continuity. In this paper, we introduce a novel framework for PINNs that enforces temporal continuity through an explicit dependence on time. We construct a sequence of PINNs, each depending on the previous one through an intermediate variable, thereby enforcing a form of temporal continuity within the sequence. We then show that our method can be interpreted as a discretization of a continuous-time dynamical system, allowing us to use gradient descent to learn the dynamics. Our experiments show that our method is able to learn the dynamics of a nonlinear oscillator, a damped pendulum, and a Duffing oscillator, achieving better performance than a standard PINN for the same amount of training data.

We present SynCode a novel framework for efficient and general syntactical decoding of code with the ability to handle any syntactic form. In this work, we introduce a simple but powerful encoder-decoder architecture which can decode any syntactic string as long as it is known during training. This allows us to efficiently train our model on a diverse set of syntactic structures without needing to manually design the network to handle specific grammar structures. The key idea behind our approach is to represent the input string by its syntactic tree and then use the encoder to create the contextual embeddings that capture the dependencies between nodes in the tree. To further improve performance, we utilize a specialized attention mechanism called "syntactic attention" which is designed to

Large language models (LLMs) are prone to overload under high-traffic real-world deployments, resulting in degraded inference throughput and latency. While LLMs can be made robust to overload through traffic throttling, such a straightforward approach leads to a fixed throughput-latency tradeoff, which may not be optimal. In this paper, we present Sarathi-Serve, a novel framework that enables dynamic control over the throughput-latency tradeoff in LLM inference. Sarathi-Serve utilizes a queueing-based load balancer that dynamically routes incoming requests to active servers, each serving a unique LLM instance. By strategically placing request queues and employing a two-tier service mechanism, Sarathi-Serve balances the load among servers, minimizes idle time, and reduces the average response time. We derive a mean-field approximation for the steady-state queue lengths and server states in Sarathi-Serve, providing valuable insights for system design and parameter selection. Through trace-driven experiments, we demonstrate that Sarathi-Serve can improve throughput by 2.2X and reduce latency by 2.7X compared to a baseline system without load balancing.

Assessing action quality in a manifold-aligned manner is a preeminent yet underaddressed challenge in video analytics. Manifold alignment refers to the inherent spatial-temporal structure of actions, which can be captured by Riemannian manifolds. In this paper, we propose a novel deep learning framework for action quality assessment that leverages manifold alignment to improve the discrimination power of the model. Specifically, we introduce a Manifold-Aligned Graph Regularization (MAGR) method that encourages consistency between action representations and their manifold labels. MAGR is a graph-based regularization technique that constructs a graph structure on the manifold and aligns the action representations with the graph structure. This alignment process ensures that actions with similar manifold labels are mapped to nearby points in the representation space, enhancing the model's ability to capture subtle but meaningful differences in action quality. We validate our model on numerous benchmark datasets, demonstrating its superiority over existing methods in terms of performance and robustness. Results show an improvement of up to 9.7% in top-1 accuracy and 14.9% in mean accuracy compared to the state-of-the-art.

Recent advancements in video semantic segmentation have made substantial progress by exploiting temporal correlations to improve the accuracy of future frames. However, most existing methods adopt a static spatial representation for all frames and lack the ability to adaptively represent the temporal dynamics of the video sequence. This paper introduces a novel Transformer-based framework, named Spatial Dynamic Representation (SDR), to enhance video semantic segmentation. Specifically, SDR adopts a hierarchical structure with a coarse-to-fine approach that consists of a spatial-dynamic feature extraction network and a context aggregation network. The spatial-dynamic feature extraction network is designed to extract fine-grained features from each frame with the assistance of spatio-tempora

The use of deep learning methods in scientific computing represents a potential paradigm shift in engineering problem solving. Although these methods have shown promise in their ability to predict physical phenomena, there is still much work to be done before they can fully replace traditional numerical models. In this study, we present an approach to using the Neural Architecture Search (NAS) framework to automatically design a network architecture tailored for simulating fluid flows through porous media. We first create a dataset with labeled ground truth flow fields and then train our network using backpropagation-based optimization techniques. We find that our NAS-designed network outperforms state-of-the-art baselines by a significant margin. Furthermore, we demonstrate how our model

In this paper, we look at cross-domain few-shot classification which presents the challenging task of classifying new unseen classes in a novel domain by leveraging labeled samples from source domains. We propose a cross-domain pretrained model named Cross-Domain Pre-Training for Few-Shot Classification (CDP4FC) that utilizes a pretraining strategy to enhance the generalization ability on source and target domains. Specifically, our proposed CDP4FC introduces a cross-modality feature representation module and cross-domain knowledge distillation strategy during pretraining process. Then, we introduce a transfer learning method using our proposed cross-modal features that can be used to fine-tune the pretrained model in the target domain, while maintaining the learned knowledge on the source

Advanced Audio-Visual Speech Recognition (AVSR) systems have been observed to be sensitive to a speaker's voice and the surrounding environment. This study investigates the impact of various factors on AVSR performance, including gender, age, accent, background noise, and microphone positioning. The dataset used for evaluation is from the VoxCeleb2 dataset, which contains recordings from 1251 speakers in total. To evaluate the robustness of state-of-the-art AVSR systems against these factors, we apply a variety of pre-trained models to the data with varying levels of audio augmentation and speech enhancement. The results reveal that male voices are more accurately recognized than female voices, younger speakers are better understood than older ones, and American English accents are easier

Addressing the intricate challenge of modeling and re-rendering dynamic scenes, most recent approaches focus on modeling static environments and use camera motion to synthesize animations. However, such approaches suffer from artifacts due to the lack of attention to dynamics in both modeling and rendering phases, which causes degradation of visual quality for scene elements with complex motion patterns. In this paper, we propose a novel method called Dynamic Reasoned Rendering (DRR) for synthesizing high-fidelity animations of dynamic scenes. DRR introduces two core components: dynamic neural field (DNField) and dynamic shadow fields (DSF). The former represents dynamic geometry features using a sparse set of 3D keypoints, while the latter captures dynamic shadow features by learning the

Time series generation (TSG) models create artificial time series data from given observational data, enabling various applications. Existing TSG models can be roughly classified as autoregressive (AR) models and variational autoencoder (VAE) based models. While AR models achieve decent performance on synthetic data generation, they are limited to capturing linear or quadratic dependencies due to their simple design. On the other hand, VAE-based models have the capability to capture complex dependencies, but they lack adequate control over the generated time series, resulting in suboptimal performance. To address the limitations of existing TSG models, we propose a novel conditional time series generation model called Time Weaver. This model extends a basic VAE framework with a novel graph-based variational inference method and a conditional AR decoder. The graph-based variational inference helps the model learn the underlying structure of the time series data, while the conditional AR decoder allows for fine-grained control over the generation process. We evaluate Time Weaver on multiple real-world and synthetic datasets and show that it produces superior results in terms of both quantitative and qualitative performance metrics. We also show that Time Weaver can be used to generate data for any given date range, allowing for the creation of realistic time series data with specific characteristics.

Low-light image enhancement aims to enhance the details in dark regions of an image without introducing artifacts such as over-/under-shooting. To better preserve image details and avoid introducing artificial colors, previous works typically constrain the enhanced image to be non-negative. However, this ignores the fact that in low-light images, the pixel values are already close to zero, and thus the non-negative constraint is not optimal for preserving details in dark regions. In this work, we study the condition under which the non-negative constraint is beneficial, and show that for low-light images, the zero-reference constraint, which constrains the enhanced image to be larger than a certain reference image, is superior for detail preservation. Based on this, we propose a zero-reference lighting estimation diffusion model (ZLED) for low-light image enhancement. Specifically, we introduce a reference image generator to estimate the reference image without any human annotation. To enhance the low-light image, we use a diffusion model to learn the distribution of the reference image, and guide the enhanced image to be consistent with the learned distribution. To show the effectiveness of ZLED, we conduct extensive experiments using images from the Night Wenzhou dataset. We also conduct user studies to evaluate the visual quality of the enhanced images, and show that ZLED is preferred over previous state-of-the-art methods.

Knowledge distillation framework (KDF) is a promising graph representation learning method, which can extract useful knowledge from a large pre-trained model (i.e., teacher) and transfer it to a small fine-tuned model (i.e., student). Current KDFs mainly focus on distilling node-level knowledge from teachers to students, while the graph-level knowledge is often ignored. However, graph-level knowledge plays an important role in many graph-based tasks, such as graph classification and analogical reasoning. In this paper, we propose a novel teacher-free KDF, namely Dual Self-Distillation KDF (DSD-KDF), which can distill both node-level and graph-level knowledge from a large teacher model to a small student model. Specifically, we design a node-to-node distillation method to distill node-level knowledge, and a graph-to-graph distillation method to distill graph-level knowledge. Unlike traditional KDFs that rely on the existence of a teacher model, DSD-KDF can work in a teacher-free manner. We conduct extensive experiments on both node-level and graph-level tasks, showing that DSD-KDF outperforms the state-of-the-art teacher-based KDFs.

The study of continuous-time information diffusion has been an important area of research for many applications in recent years. In this work, we introduce a new class of models called the generalized random walk (GRW) and demonstrate its effectiveness at approximating and modeling real-world data. We show that GRW provides improved performance compared to classical random walks and Markov chains, especially when the graph is sparse or heterogeneous. Furthermore, our results suggest that GRW can be used as an alternative to Markov chain Monte Carlo sampling, which could have implications for various domains such as social networks and financial markets. Finally, we provide some theoretical justifications for the success of GRW and discuss potential future directions for the study of this n

In multicriteria decision-making, a user seeks a set of non-dominated solutions that are most preferred among all possible alternatives. The user usually prefers some criteria to others and desires the final solution set to be as diverse as possible. However, existing methods do not explicitly support such preferences. To address this issue, we introduce the concept of local diversity in addition to global diversity. We show that local diversity can effectively guide the search process to avoid premature convergence, especially when the problem has multiple equally good solutions or clusters. Furthermore, our method is flexible and easy to use with various optimization algorithms. We propose a novel approach for calculating local diversity and demonstrate its effectiveness through experime

Cyber threats continue to evolve in complexity, thereby traditional Cyber Threat Intelligence (CTI) methods and techniques are insufficient. The new CTI approach should focus on not only the external threat intelligence but also internal cyber data as a whole picture for proactive defense against sophisticated adversaries who can breach any security layer from outside or inside of an organization's network perimeter by exploiting vulnerabilities within applications used daily at work places such us email client software like Microsoft Outlook/Exchange Server 2013-onwards version numbers which contain known bugs that allow attackers with access rights over those systems execute malicious code remotely without having physical contact first - this is called 'Remote Code Execution'. This paper introduces BLaIR, a series of pretrained sentence embedding models specialized for the domain of biomedical literature. BLaIR is based on the widely used language model BERT and its variants (e.g., RoBERTa). To adapt the models to biomedical texts, we augment the pretraining datasets with PubMed-specific data, including abstracts from the most influential papers in the biomedical field and abstracts that have been manually annotated with their respective protein entities. Compared with the original BERT/RoBERTa models trained without biomedical data, our experiments show that BLaIR consistently outperforms these baselines in several downstream tasks such as text classification, named entity recognition, and relation extraction.

We consider competitive facility location as a two-stage multi-agent system with two types of clients, which are represented by a convex and concave polygon, respectively. Each agent selects a point in the plane to open a facility. A client is served if it is closer to an opened facility than to any other opened facility. We analyze the price of stability in two settings: (i) when agents take turns opening their facilities, and (ii) when they simultaneously open their facilities. In both cases we provide tight bounds for the price of stability. For the first setting, we present a 3-approximation algorithm that converts the problem into one with only linear demand. For the second setting, we show that there exists an instance where the price of stability is $\Theta(n)$, where $n$ denotes th

The emergence of large language models (LLMs) has revolutionized numerous applications across industries, including the healthcare sector. However, the prevalence and adoption of LLMs have also raised concerns regarding potential risks to patients and professionals. This survey aims to address these concerns by assessing the perceptions and experiences of healthcare professionals regarding the use of LLMs in their practice. It examines the impact on patient safety and security as well as potential legal implications for medical professionals. The survey was conducted among 130 healthcare professionals from various specialties worldwide, using both quantitative and qualitative methods. Results show that respondents are generally aware of LLM-related risks in their field, with most being fam

Vertical Federated Learning (VFL) is an emergent distributed machine learning paradigm where a single model needs to be trained on multiple vertical data sets. In this paper, we propose two novel frameworks for VRL: 1.) Vertical Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learners with Hierarchical Attention and Communication Networks - VMACN that enables training of multiagent RL learners in heterogeneous environments through hierarchical communication networks; b). Deep Actor Critic Reinforced Learners For Vertically Distributed Environments With Cooperative Strategies -- DARCSTRIVE which extends the actor critic framework by enabling cooperative strategies via reinforcement signals between agents across different devices/agents within vertically federated settings .

High-fidelity models of network telemetry time series can aid in the early detection of anomalies, faster root-cause analysis, and more effective mitigation of network-wide events. To build such models, network operators and researchers rely on a variety of statistical and machine learning techniques. Most methods struggle, however, with the mixed temporal resolutions of network data, which are typically collected at coarse intervals (e.g., 15 minutes) and lack sufficient density to capture short-term phenomena. In this paper, we present a novel approach to super-resolve network telemetry time series to a user-specified resolution (e.g., 1 minute) while preserving or even enhancing the underlying statistical properties of the signal. We super-resolve traffic data from a large research network using aggregated flow records, which we treat as a non-stationary signal with both deterministic and stochastic components. The deterministic part is modeled with a neural network, while the stochastic part is handled by a hidden Markov model. We show that our method can improve on state-of-the-art techniques, either by achieving comparable accuracy with significantly less parameter tuning or by outperforming existing methods that are manually tuned. We also demonstrate that super-resolution can be useful for anomaly detection, as it allows for the use of shorter-term models that capture seasonal and other local effects.

Complex networks are widely used to represent an abundance of real-world relations ranging from social networks and biological interaction maps, over communication systems in computer science. The study for the efficient analysis methods is a topic attracting much attention lately due especially to their potential role in applications such as clustering or classification tasks [1], which could be useful tools when dealing with large datasets found on modern platforms like Facebook (FB)2 or Twitter3; [4].

Large language models (LLMs) hold interesting potential for the design, development, and research of cybersecurity solutions. The primary objective of this paper is to examine the impact of LLMs on three critical domains of cybersecurity: threat intelligence, incident response, and vulnerability assessment. We conduct a comprehensive literature review using established systematic methods, identifying 27 studies published between 2018 and 2023, with 9 studies related to vulnerability assessment, 14 studies related to incident response, and 4 studies related to threat intelligence. Our analysis reveals that the majority of studies focus on the vulnerability assessment domain, followed by the incident response domain, while threat intelligence receives less attention. We identify key trends i

Imitation learning provides an efficient way to teach robots dexterous skills; however, learning complex tasks from demonstrations is challenging due to the sparsity of expert demonstrations. To address this challenge, we introduce a novel imitation-learning method that learns by exploring the environment and observing how the environment changes as it moves around. We employ a novel neural network architecture to learn from sparse demonstrations that are generated by a deep reinforcement learning agent. The proposed model can learn to perform diverse robot manipulation tasks with few samples, outperforming existing methods such as DDPG and PPO. Our experiments show that the proposed algorithm has lower training time and higher success rate than other algorithms in challenging environments

Despite remarkable progress, existing multimodal large language models (MLLMs) are still inferior to the human baseline for certain high-level reasoning tasks. In this paper, we focus on a specific problem called "multimodal question answering" where an input image is paired with a natural language question and the goal is to generate a coherent textual answer that contains both visual and linguistic information. We investigate the impact of various training and inference procedures on the performance of MLLMs in this domain. Our experiments show that pretraining the models using unsupervised methods such as Masked Language Models (MLM) can improve their ability to recognize patterns within images while using fine-tuning with pretrained language models (PLMs) can help them learn better lan

Many recent state-of-the-art results in language tasks were achieved using compound systems that integrate different models. In these systems, the components are trained independently with no explicit knowledge of their interconnections or shared parameters. While such compounds have shown promise for improved performance, they often require a substantial increase in parameter count to achieve competitive results across multiple tasks. One potential explanation is that many current methods lack sufficient training data, and thus rely on overfitting to compensate for this limitation. To address these issues, we introduce an approach called "Task-sharing via Training" (TST), where we share the parameters of two independent neural networks by training them simultaneously on common data, witho

Distributed Stream Processing (DSP) focuses on the near real-time processing of large volumes and high velocity data streams. The goal is to process, filter or aggregate these streaming events in order for them not only to be consumed by humans but also directly used as input into decision making engines such that they can generate output results faster than human intuition would allow without sacrificing accuracy at any given point along its path from source node through intermediate nodes until final destination where result will eventually show up once ready after all required steps have been completed successfully throughout entire system consisting multiple different components working together seamlessly with one another while staying independent enough so no single component failure

The privacy concerns associated with the use of Large Language Models (LLMs) have become increasingly apparent, especially in the context of open-domain question answering. While existing methods seek to alleviate these issues by relying on human annotations and supervision, this approach is costly and time consuming, limiting its practical applications. To address this challenge, we propose a novel framework that utilizes an LLM to enhance the performance of a traditional question answering system, thereby reducing reliance on annotation costs while maintaining accuracy. Our approach leverages the inherent capabilities of LLMs for language understanding and generation, allowing for the construction of a more robust and effective question answering system. This paper provides insights into

The transportation sector remains a major contributor to greenhouse gas emissions. The understanding of energy use and fuel consumption is important for planning the future development in this field, especially the introduction of new technologies such as electric vehicles (EVs). This study uses an agent-based model with detailed vehicle characteristics including type/model year mix by age class distribution within each segment category; activity profiles based on real travel patterns from different parts across Singapore’ s road network during peak hours between 2016–2035 time horizon period respectively ; route selection algorithm which simulates how drivers choose their routes while driving around town areas where there are multiple destinations available at once but only one destinatio

Generative modeling of complex behaviors from labeled datasets has been a longstanding problem in decision-making, and the ability to learn the underlying behavioral rules without explicit supervision is highly desirable. To address this challenge, we introduce the framework of unsupervised Markov chain learning, which aims to estimate the transition probabilities of a finite-state Markov model from a given dataset. We show that our algorithm can be viewed as a special case of maximum likelihood estimation with a carefully designed loss function that incorporates auxiliary information. Through extensive experiments on various benchmark tasks, including a synthetic task and three real-world tasks involving autonomous driving, we demonstrate that our method outperforms existing methods for u

Online bidding and auction are crucial aspects of the online advertising industry. Conventionally, the winning bid amount is determined by the value of the advertisement impression or click, which can be measured using various metrics such as click-through rate (CTR), cost per acquisition (CPA), and average revenue per user (ARPU). However, this approach may not always guarantee the optimal allocation of impressions and clicks to advertisers. To address this issue, we propose a new model that incorporates the concept of "advertising equity" into the bidding process. By considering both the advertiser's budget and its perceived value in the market, our model ensures that each advertiser receives an equitable share of the available inventory. The proposed algorithm adopts a game-theoretic fr

Lexicon-based constrained decoding approaches aim to control the meaning or style of the generated text by explicitly controlling certain words in a decoder's attention. However, these methods often suffer from lexical mismatch issues between controlled and unconstrained vocabularies at training time (e..g., "good" vs."well"), which degrades their performance significantly when applied on real world datasets with complex semantics that contain many such terms.\\ In this paper we propose an improved lexicon embedding method for alleviating \textit{mismatch loss} caused during learning process due semantic similarity among various English prepositions like 'in', `of`, etc.\ Our work is based upon our earlier findings where there exists large discrepancy within representation space while comp

We propose a formal model for intent-based markets, where buyers and sellers reveal their intentions, in the form of desired goods and available funds, respectively, and match based on compatibility. We focus on two simple notions of compatibility: (i) linear programming compatibility, where each buyer can obtain a subset of their desired goods, and (ii) threshold compatibility, where each buyer can obtain their most desired good, subject to a threshold on the required funds. We show that finding approximately optimal matches is computationally intractable, even in the linear programming setting. However, we provide an approximation algorithm for threshold compatibility based on submodularity. We then compare the performance of these two notions of compatibility in a game-theoretic setting, where buyers and sellers have strategic influence over their own intentions. We show that while both notions can lead to inefficient outcomes, threshold compatibility is "less bad" than linear programming compatibility. Finally, we discuss the implications of our results for digital marketplaces.

LiDAR point clouds are widely used for extracting semantically meaningful 3D models of buildings. The LiDAR-based building reconstruction task is typically formulated as a point cloud clustering problem. Existing methods first propagate labels of seed points to their neighboring points to generate a preliminary building model, and then refine the model by removing false positive clusters and merging broken clusters. However, LiDAR points of buildings are often unevenly distributed, which results in inaccurate clustering and affects the reconstruction performance. In this paper, we propose a LiDAR-based building reconstruction method that directly reconstructs the building geometry from raw LiDAR point clouds without the need of labeling seed points. The proposed method first performs a global shape descriptor learning on the input point clouds to obtain the building roof surface. Then, the learned roof surface is used to guide the reconstruction of the building envelope. Finally, the detailed building model is obtained by fusing the reconstructed envelope with the roof surface. Compared to existing methods, the proposed approach is more robust in the presence of LiDAR point clouds with uneven distribution. The proposed method has been extensively evaluated on both synthetic and real-world LiDAR datasets. The experimental results demonstrate that the proposed approach achieves state-of-the-art performance.

Multiple Object Tracking (MOT) is a crucial but challenging task in computer vision. The latest state-of-the-art in MOT is represented by tracking-by-detection approaches, which, however, are limited to linear object motion modeling. In this paper, we propose a novel diffusion-based object tracking framework that naturally incorporates non-linear motion modeling into the tracking process. The proposed DiffMOT tracker relies on a novel object representation, consisting of a location descriptor and a diffusion map, to capture object spatial relationships and non-linear motion simultaneously. Furthermore, we introduce an effective fusion strategy for the multi-object tracking process, by designing a novel non-linear prediction scheme. This scheme predicts the state of each target using a weighted sum of the states of all objects, where the weights are proportional to the affinities between objects. The affinity between objects is measured by a novel non-linear function, based on the object location descriptors. The proposed tracker is an open-loop system that updates the object states in a recursive manner. Experiments on public benchmarks show that our method outperforms existing state-of-the-art techniques, in terms of both effectiveness and efficiency.

The next-generation network (NGN) integrates the network function virtualization (NFV) and the software-defined network (SDN) technologies to realize the flexible and efficient resource allocation. For the virtual reality (VR) and the augmented reality (AR) services, the NGN can offload the computation tasks from the user equipment (UE) to the network side to reduce the latency. However, it is challenging to find the optimal task offloading decision for the massive and dynamic user requests. The computational complexity will also increase with the addition of more users and tasks. Thus, a heuristic algorithm based on the graph neural network (GNN) is proposed to place the real-time metaverse tasks on the optimal UE and base station (BS) pair. The simulation results show that the proposed algorithm can achieve a better performance compared with the other baseline algorithms in terms of latency and GNN parameters. Furthermore, the GNN algorithm can be deployed on the edge server to reduce the latency.

We study the finite-time convergence of TD learning with linear function approximation under Markovian sampling and non-linear function classes. Our main result is a generalization of the $O(1/T)$ regret bound for the case when the state space is infinite, and when the function class is not restricted to be linear. In particular, we show that the total variation distance between the learned stationary distribution and the target distribution is upper bounded by an $\tilde{O}(1/\sqrt{\eta})$ function of the number of iterations in the algorithm, where $\eta$ is the step size parameter. The main technical contribution of our paper is a novel analysis of the bias term in the Bellman operator arising from function approximation. This enables us to derive the first non-asymptotic results in thi

This paper studies how large language models (LLMs) can act as effective, high-level reasoning systems that perform well on a wide range of problems. We develop an LLM architecture called GopherL which is trained to solve math and logic questions by using the same question solving method across different tasks without any task specific fine tuning or pretraining data for each problem type in order reduce computational costs during training time while maintaining performance levels comparable with those achieved through traditional approaches such AI21's PAIL system -- making it ideal candidate model not only learning new skills but also serving diverse needs efficiently within single platform .

Multi-Label Text Classification (MLTC) is a fundamental task in the field of Natural Language Processing. Recent advancements have employed pre-trained language models as feature extractors for MLTC, leading to significant performance improvements. However, existing methods are prone to overfitting and lack robustness against noise. In this paper, we propose a novel self-supervised learning approach for MLTC based on contrastive learning. The proposed method involves two main components: (1) a cross-view encoder that jointly learns text and label representations by aligning text tokens across views; and (2) an instruction generator, which generates masked instructions based on the latent space representations of the encoder and labels. We demonstrate the effectiveness of our method through

Traditional approaches in mental health research apply General Linear Models (GLM) to describe the longitudinal dynamics of patients' behavioral symptoms. However, these models fail to capture the complex and non-linear patterns that characterize the trajectory of their symptoms, leading to suboptimal predictions. To overcome this limitation, we propose a novel approach based on Deep Neural Networks (DNN). Specifically, we utilize a Variational Autoencoder (VAE), which is a type of DNN, to learn a latent space representation of the data. This approach enables us to identify unique characteristics within the data, such as individualized symptom trajectories. By incorporating the learned VAE latent space into the GLM framework, we can enhance the predictive power of the model. We demonstrate

Digital twin (DT) technology has received immense attention over the years due to the promises it presents to various stakeholders in science and engineering. As a result, different thematic areas of DT have been explored. This is no different in specific fields such as manufacturing, automation, oil and gas, and civil engineering, leading to fragmented approaches for field-specific applications. The civil engineering industry is further disadvantaged in this regard as it relies on external techniques by other engineering fields for its DT adoption. A rising consequence of these extensions is a concentrated application of DT to the operations and maintenance phase. On another spectrum, Building Information Modeling (BIM) are pervasively utilized in the planning/design phase, and the transi

IoT and smart cities are current trends that have sparked the imagination of researchers and the general public alike. The latter is due, in part, to the possibility of improving the quality of life of city residents through the use of information and communication technologies, which is a primary driver for IoT development. In this paper, we provide an overview of the SmartSantander project, which aims to turn Santander (Spain) into a smart city. We focus, in particular, on the IoT experimentation carried out over the SmartSantander testbed. This testbed features a deployed infrastructure of more than 250 smart sensors of different types that monitor environmental parameters (e.g., noise, light, temperature, etc.), energy consumption, and water flow. We describe the experimental process, the analysis of the obtained results, and the lessons learned from this experience. We also provide an overview of the most relevant results and conclusions drawn by the different project work packages. The latter include the potential impact of the project on the city of Santander and other cities around the world.

While the volume of remote sensing data is increasing daily, deep learning in Earth Observation faces lack of labeled training data and the diversity of satellite images. To address these challenges, this work proposes a new self-supervised representation learning method for improving land cover classification by leveraging a large amount of unlabeled data. The proposed method jointly utilizes a contrastive loss for clustering representations with self-training and an information maximization loss to capture spatial contextual information from neighboring patches. We validate our method on the well-known IGBP dataset and show that it significantly outperforms existing methods for self-supervised representation learning for land cover classification. Furthermore, we demonstrate its generali

Federated Learning (FL) is a machine learning (ML) approach that enables multiple decentralized learners to collaboratively train a global model while maintaining the privacy of local data. This paradigm has gained immense traction in recent years due to its potential for real-world applications and the advancements made in this field. Despite these advancements, there still exists a significant challenge with FL in terms of optimizing the performance of local models without compromising on privacy. This paper proposes an innovative method for improving the performance of federated learning through the integration of a novel deep neural network architecture called a multi-head attention network. Specifically, we introduce a new type of attention mechanism that leverages a cascade of fully

With the rapid growth of scholarly archives, researchers subscribe to "paper alert" systems to keep up with the latest literature. However, due to the limited resources that can be allocated for these alerts, it is often difficult to discover relevant papers published by less-known authors or in journals not known to the researcher. In this paper we propose a new approach to help researchers overcome this limitation. Our method first identifies papers and authors that are highly related to the user's interest. It then proposes additional authors and papers that were not previously discovered by the user but share similar characteristics with the original set. These papers and authors are ranked based on their novelty and relevance. We evaluated our method using two real-world datasets cons

Multilingual semantic matching (MSM) aims to find semantic equivalents of a query concept expressed in a source language within a target language. It has been shown that parsing, i.e., sentence structure analysis, is beneficial for MSM because the structure information can be shared across languages. However, previous parsing-based MSM approaches are limited to bi-concept matching and cannot handle multi-concept queries. In this paper, we introduce a novel multi-concept parsing framework for MSM, called Multi-concept Parsing Network (MPN). The framework is designed to be general and flexible, allowing for different matching objectives and language pairs. It consists of a multi-concept dependency parser that analyzes the dependency structure of multi-concept queries, a crosslingual sentence encoder that captures crosslingual semantic information, and a matching module that computes the similarity between concepts and their representations in the target language. We evaluate our framework on three MSM datasets and achieve strong performances. It outperforms previous parsing-based approaches and the results also demonstrate the superiority of our framework over other state-of-the-art models. Our code is available at https://github.com/yuanpeng14/MPN.

Recently, text-to-image models have drawn a lot of attention for their impressive generation quality and efficiency. However, they still lack controllability and personalization. Although some works use classification models to achieve conditional generation, they suffer from a large drop in generation quality. In this work, we propose the implicit prompt mechanism, which can personalize the text-to-image model without hurting the generation quality. Specifically, we design the learnable token embedding and the adaptive normalization layer to capture the individual differences of style and semantics, respectively. We conduct a series of experiments to validate the effectiveness of our method. The results show that our approach can achieve better FIDs and/or mIoUs than the state-of-the-art methods.

Orthogonal time frequency space (OTFS) modulation, a delay-Doppler (DD) scheme for multi-carrier transmission in the time domain, is an attractive candidate for 6G wireless communication. We aim to design a new OTFS modulator with the capability of supporting large bandwidths and ultra-high spatial resolutions. To this end, we propose a novel DD modulator based on sparse non-binary constellations. By leveraging the sparse representation property of the constellation symbols, our proposed scheme can reduce the amount of required resources for signal processing significantly compared to existing methods that use conventional binary constellations. Specifically, we introduce a new class of symbol sequences called "constellation sequences", which allow us to construct constellations using only

Despite advancements in text-to-image generation (T2I), prior methods often face significant challenges when generating novel objects, especially those with fine details. In this paper we introduce the first conditional diffusion model to address such issues and demonstrate its effectiveness on a challenging task: generative image manipulation of real images using large scale datasets without requiring any manual annotations or editing tools.\\ To effectively manipulate an input reference photo into target style while preserving object consistency across multiple stages during training (\textit{i.~e.} from initial sketch stage through final rendering) requires careful balancing between global styles captured by high resolution latent codes ($L_1$) and local textures generated via low freq

We propose a method to teach multiple large language models (LLM) to collaborate by interleaving their outputs, which we name Collaborative Generator. This approach allows each LLM to learn from the others' responses and avoids the need for manual annotations of dialogue data. In our experiments with 24 different LLMs on the GPT-3.5-Turbo model card, we found that this method outperformed existing approaches in terms of both task performance and response quality. Additionally, it was shown to be more robust across different models, requiring only a single training run. Our results demonstrate that interleaved responses can improve the effectiveness of LLM collaboration, making it a promising direction for further research in this area. The source code is available at https://github.com/mic

In the literature, points and conics have been major features for camera geometric calibration. Although constraining their parameters in a proper manner leads to efficient solutions of this problem (e..g., using epipolar geometry), these calibrations are not always optimal because they do no take into account the relationship between all involved geometrical entities such as lines or points lying on planes at infinity etc.). In order improve our understanding about how different types if objects influence each other we propose an algorithm which takes advantage from three-dimensional constraints coming directly out off photogrammetry models that allow us achieve better results than classical approaches based only upon two dimensional data sets alone .

Large language models (LLMs) open up new horizons for sequential recommendations, owing to their remarkable language comprehension and generation capabilities. However, there are still numerous challenges that should be addressed to successfully implement sequential recommendations empowered by LLMs. Firstly, user behavior patterns are often complex, and relying solely on one-step reasoning from LLMs may lead to incorrect or task-irrelevant responses. Secondly, the prohibitively resource requirements of LLM (e.g., ChatGPT-175B) are overwhelmingly high and impractical for real sequential recommender systems.

Place recognition is crucial for tasks like loop-closure detection and re-localization. Single-view place recognizers can be trained to recognize views with high accuracy, but their generalization ability on unseen scenes remains unclear due the lack of sufficient training data in real world applications (e..g., 3D LIDAR scans). In this paper we investigate if single view networks have learned semantic information that could generalize across different environments by analyzing features extracted from a stateoftheart model named I2I .

Recommender systems (RS) are widely used by online platforms to facilitate user navigation and increase engagement. However, malicious users may abuse the RS to steer users towards items with the intention of causing harm, such as exposing users to violent content. Previously, RS vulnerability to malicious attacks has only been considered in the context of explicit user reviews. In this paper, we study the problem of target user attacks (TUAs) on RS, where a malicious user submits fake reviews to target a specific user rather than the entire user base. We aim to answer two key questions: (1) How vulnerable are modern RS to TUAs? (2) Can we mitigate the damage caused by TUAs? We model TUAs as a bilevel optimization problem, where the upper level represents the malicious user's decision-making process, and the lower level represents the RS's decision-making process. We show that TUAs can cause significant damage to modern RS, especially those that heavily rely on user-generated data, such as content-based RS and social RS. We propose a defense strategy by designing a novel uplift model that can effectively mitigate TUAs and preserve the utility for legitimate users.

Event cameras are novel bio-inspired sensors that measure per-pixel brightness differences asynchronously. Thanks to their sparse and digital nature, they are well-suited for motion estimation in dynamic scenes. Existing methods segment dynamic motion in the pixel plane, which simplifies the problem but limits the performance of the segmentation algorithm to the resolution of the sensor. In this paper, we propose a method that leverages the temporal dimension of events to segment motion at a higher level of abstraction. We introduce 3D event-based dynamic motion segmentation to segment motion not only in the pixel plane but also in 3D space. This approach exploits the fact that motion segmentation can be performed more easily in the absence of occlusions, and that 3D events provide depth information which helps in removing occlusions. The proposed algorithm first performs 2D motion segmentation in the event plane using a standard event camera representation. It then extends the segmentation to 3D space using depth information and finally refines the segmentation by removing small disconnected segments that are likely to be false positives. Quantitative and qualitative results show that our method outperforms existing techniques on event-based dynamic motion segmentation in terms of both accuracy and ability to generalize on complex scenes.

Biometric authentication is gradually becoming obsolete as it is susceptible to various spoofing attacks. Recognising the user's behaviour pattern can be a viable alternative for continuous authentication. This paper explores the possibility of identifying users based on their device usage pattern, collected as a by-product of the authentication process. We employ state-of-the-art machine learning techniques to recognise users from the smartphone usage data, collected over a long period of time and conducted a user study to evaluate the strength and weakness of our approach. The experimental results show that the proposed system can achieve a high rate of classification accuracy, above 97%, which is significantly better than the baseline models. The device data used for authentication can be easily collected by the operating system or a third-party application, without the user's active participation. Therefore, the proposed solution can be implemented on any device, anytime, anywhere to provide continuous authentication.

We address the task of long-horizon navigation in partially mapped environments for which active gathering of localization information is not possible due to a lack of sensors. To achieve this, we introduce a novel framework that leverages on-board cameras and off-board data collected by drones equipped with Lidar sensors. By enabling the autonomous operation of both vehicles, the system allows for efficient exploration and mapping of unknown environments while minimizing resource consumption. This paper focuses specifically on the design and evaluation of an integrated perception system comprising visual odometry (VO), monocular depth estimation (MDE) and dense mapping (DM). The proposed method has been evaluated using real-world datasets collected in the outdoors environment of rural Ire

In this paper, we propose a safe screening rule for $\nu$ support vector machine (SVM), which can efficiently reduce the size of the optimization problem. The proposed screening rule is based on a bi-level optimization problem that involves a nested minimization problem. We use a quadratic programming algorithm to solve the inner problem, and a subgradient method to solve the outer problem. We demonstrate that the proposed screening rule is safe, in the sense that the optimal solution of the original problem can be recovered from the optimal solution of the reduced problem with probability one. We also show that the proposed screening rule is more effective than the state-of-the-art screening rules for $\nu$ SVM on large-scale datasets.

In the rapidly advancing realm of visual generation, diffusion models have revolutionized the landscape, mark a significant milestone in this field. These state-of art algorithms are designed to generate high quality images from text descriptions or other image inputs using generative adversarial learning (GAN) techniques that involve two competing neural networks: one for generating and another discriminator network tasked with classifying generated vs real data samples accurately over several iterations until reaching convergence point where both parties agree on their respective roles within system architecture framework allowing accurate predictions without sacrificing computational efficiency due its simplicity compared more complex approaches often requiring greater resources making

Accurate training labels are a key component for multi-class medical image segmentation. Their annotation is time consuming and expensive, limiting the use of deep learning in clinical practice to well studied cases with abundant data or high monetary gain from sales such as brain tumor detection<sup>1</sup> . In contrast we believe that there could be substantial gains by using self supervised methods on understudied pathologies where little labeled examples exist but large amounts can still easily been generated via automatic patient screening algorithms (e. g., lung cancer). One approach towards reducing labeling cost would therefore involve applying active learner strategies which prioritize samples most likely correct class assignment based upon current knowledge state <sup>2–4 [show

We propose Strongly Supervised pre-training with ScreenShots (S4) - a simple yet effective framework for large language model (LLM) fine-tuning in domains where only a limited number of high-quality examples are available. Large language models trained on these data can exhibit catastrophic forgetting and struggle to generalize to novel tasks, even when finetuned using various prompting techniques. In contrast, our approach allows LLMs to leverage the collective knowledge of human annotators to train more robustly on smaller datasets. This is achieved by leveraging screen shots of instructional text from the target domain as additional training data, resulting in a strong initialization that enables fast adaptation to new tasks. Our framework not only improves performance on downstream tas

Webpage entity extraction is a fundamental natural language processing task in both research and applications. Nowadays, large language models (LLMs) have revolutionized this field. In recent years, many studies have explored how to effectively leverage LLMs to extract entities from webpages. However, they often involve expensive fine-tuning or demand substantial computational resources. This paper introduces a novel pre-trained entity extractor named WEA (Webpage Entity Extractor). It consists of two parts: an encoder and a decoder. The encoder part includes a BERT model that takes the webpage as input and generates hidden states for each token. Then, a transformer module converts these states into an entity representation. Furthermore, the decoder uses a Transformer-based architecture to

We propose a formalism for representing action hierarchies using natural language. Our approach extends classical planning graphs with linguistic expressions, which can be read as formal constraints over the graph. These constraints capture the hierarchical structure of the plan and allow us to compute subplans and generalizations of the original plan. We also show that our formalism can represent classical hierarchical planning formalisms, namely hierarchical task networks and planning with action templates. We then compare our approach to other related work, including hierarchical planning with linguistic templates, multi-agent planning, and planning under uncertainty.

With the development of deep learning, natural language processing technology has effectively improved the efficiency of various aspects of human life. But in reality, these technologies still cannot meet users' needs for personalized services due to the lack of understanding of user emotions and personality. For example, when a user submits an image to a social network platform to describe their emotion, the network will return suggestions based on the image's content and context, but it ignores the fact that different people have different preferences for images according to their own emotions and personality characteristics. To solve this problem, we propose a novel method called Emo-Per-CNN, which considers both emotional and personality factors when retrieving pictures from an image d

The Internet of Bio-Nano Things (IoBNT) is a novel concept that foresees a vast number of bio-nano devices connected to the Internet. These devices will be able to perform various monitoring tasks in medicine, biology, and environmental science. Given the small size of the devices, low power consumption, and weak processing capabilities, there is a need for efficient coding schemes for data compression and error correction. Arithmetic coding is a well-known data compression scheme that replaces a sequence of symbols with a single number, called the codeword. The length of the codeword is proportional to the entropy of the sequence, resulting in efficient compression. Moreover, arithmetic coding can be combined with error correction by, for example, using a Reed-Solomon (RS) code. The decoding process of an RS code is similar to the arithmetic decoding process, but in reverse. This means that the decoding process can be interrupted at any time, and the decoder can provide an approximation of the original sequence. In this paper, we introduce Molecular Arithmetic Coding (MAC), an adaptation of arithmetic coding for the IoBNT. The novelty of MAC is that it uses a molecular representation, which is more natural for nano devices, and it can be decoded using a simple algorithm that does not require much processing power. We show that MAC is, in principle, capable of encoding and decoding any sequence of symbols, including biological sequences, and that it can be combined with error correction using, for example, a Reed-Solomon code.

Large language models (LLMs) have demonstrated remarkable capabilities in problem-solving. However, their proficiency in solving mathematical problems remains inadequate. We propose MathScale, a simple and scalable method to create high-quality mathematical reasoning data using frontier LLMs (e.g., {\tt GPT-3.5}). Inspired by the cognitive mechanism in human mathematical learning, it first extracts topics and knowledge points from seed math questions and then build a concept graph, which is subsequently used to generate new math questions. MathScale exhibits effective scalability along the size axis of the math dataset that we generate. As a result, we create a mathematical reasoning dataset (MathScaleQA) containing two million math question-answer pairs.

3D hand pose estimation has found broad application in areas such as gesture recognition and human-machine interaction. This work proposes an effective approach to estimating the hand's 2D location and orientation. The proposed method incorporates a novel network architecture, which integrates a two-stage CNN backbone with a lightweight RNN for multi-resolution feature extraction. Moreover, the proposed method utilizes a simple yet powerful self-attention mechanism within the RNN, enabling it to capture long-range dependencies in the input sequence. To further enhance its performance, we introduce two types of attention, including cross-sequence and inter-image attention. We evaluate our model on five public datasets: Dexterous Hand Pose (DHP), HumanPose3D, Multi-view Hands (MVH), ALIBABA

DareFightingICE is a unique competition that brings together participants from the fields of sound design and artificial intelligence (AI) to create immersive audio-visual experiences. In this paper, we provide an overview of the first two iterations of the DareFightingICE competition, focusing on the challenges and opportunities that arise when combining these two disciplines. We present the competition's structure, the briefs that were given to the participants, and the feedback from the judges. We also discuss the technical aspects of creating an AI sound design competition and present a novel approach to scoring the participants' submissions. Finally, we reflect on the lessons learned from this experience and suggest potential directions for future iterations of the competition.

We study the limits and capability of public-data assisted differentially private (PA-DP) learning, where a machine learner can access both an internal training dataset via DP mechanism as well external data from open sources. Our key observation is that PA cannot be used to reduce privacy loss in general; however there are some settings for which it does significantly help with improved robustness against adversarial attacks or more efficient computations at lower computational cost than standard methods alone would allow even when using only one source instead two ones separately such cases include those involving large scale datasets like social media posts collected by non profit organizations who wish protect their users' personal information while still being able provide valuable in

The fusion models, which effectively combine information from different sources, are widely used in solving multimodality tasks. However，the existing methods usually only consider the linear combination of features or perform feature aggregation based on fixed structures without considering their specific differences and relationships between modalities (e.\g., spatial correlation), thereby neglecting to fully leverage multimodal interaction for enhancing model performance。In this paper we propose a novel Fusion Transformer with Multi-level Cross Attention（FTMCA）for cross modality attention learning across multiple levels、to achieve better data integration capability through hierarchical matching mechanism at both local level (\eg pixel)and global image scope(\ie semantic).

Prior work has found that pretrained language models (LMs) fine-tuned with only a few examples from the target domain exhibit limited generalization, while fine-tuning using more training data leads to overfitting. In this study, we aim to understand the role of the model's knowledge and data distribution in this phenomenon. We conduct a large-scale experiment on English Wikipedia articles to investigate whether fine-tuning a LM with many examples from the target domain can overcome these limitations. Our results suggest that fine-tuning with many examples is essential for effective adaptation, especially when the model lacks sufficient knowledge about the target domain. On the other hand, fine-tuning with fewer examples may help maintain the model's knowledge during adaptation. These find

In this paper, channel estimation problem for extremely large-scale multi-input multi-output (XSMIMO) system is studied. The XMSMIO communication systems are characterized by a massive number of antennas at the base station and small numbers on users’ side due to limited hardware resources in mobile terminals or drones [1]. In such scenario where many more transmitting than receiving elements exist with narrowband waveforms like orthogonal frequency division multiplexing signals employed as well known from existing 5G standard proposals[2], it becomes difficult/impossible task solving conventional least squares based approaches that require full rank matrix inversion operations which become infeasible when dealing huge amounts information simultaneously transmitted via wireless networks .

Speaker replication, the task of synthesizing speech from a target speaker given only an utterance-level identity vector, has been drawing broader attention owing to its compelling applications in voice cloning and speaker re-identification. Existing methods, while effective, typically rely on language-specific tokenization and struggle to generalize across different languages. In this work, we aim to tackle the language-agnostic speaker replication problem and propose a novel multi-level attention aggregation network. Specifically, we introduce token-level, word-level, and speaker-level attention mechanisms to capture the cross-lingual correlations within and across utterances. Furthermore, we employ a speaker identity disentanglement strategy to enhance the representation learning process. To evaluate the proposed model, we collect and utilize a multilingual speaker replication dataset comprising German, English, Spanish, and Swedish. Experimental results demonstrate that the proposed method outperforms existing state-of-the-art techniques for both within- and cross-lingual settings. We also conduct a variety of ablation studies to validate the effectiveness of different design choices.

Large Language Models (LLMs) are known to exhibit in-context translation capabilities. However, the underlying mechanisms and which neural layers are involved in this process remain unclear. In this work, we aim to characterize in-context translation in LLMs and investigate the involvement of neural layers in this phenomenon. To do so, we leverage a wide range of LLMs, from small-scale to large-scale models, and different pre-training objectives. First, we find that in-context translation is mainly driven by the pre-training objective, with masked language modeling (MLM) leading to better in-context translation performance than masked word embedding (MWE). Second, we show that in-context translation is mostly attributed to the encoder's middle layers, which learn to translate from the context. The decoder only participates in long-distance dependency resolution. Third, we observe a negative correlation between the number of decoder layers and in-context translation performance. This suggests that LLMs with a smaller decoder are more capable of performing in-context translation. Our study provides valuable insights into the factors that contribute to in-context translation in LLMs, highlighting the pre-training objective and the encoder's middle layers as crucial elements.
We prove that the generalized Atropos pattern matching problem with $k$ don't cares is PSPACE-complete for any fixed $k \geq 1$.

We present the first algorithm for query learning of a general class of Mealy machines with timers (MeT) using samples, even when no prior knowledge about such classes is available. The main result establishes sample complexity bounds on our learner that match those obtained in recent works dedicated to solving this problem by exploiting specialized machine-learning techniques and results from game theory [1]. The central idea behind all these approaches consists of encoding each finite state automata $M$ as an instance $\Phi( M)$ over some infinite domain ${}^{k}\!\Omega$, where every bitstring corresponds precisely one valid execution path through its states; i.~e., given input string $\{a_{0}, \ldots , \, k\}$ we look at which paths are taken along their respective transitions according

Conversational large language models (LLMs) have shown impressive performance in recent years. However, existing techniques for training conversational LLMs typically require expensive human annotation. In this work, we propose Direct RLHF, a novel method that trains a conversational LLM directly with reinforcement learning from human preferences, without the need for expensive manual annotation. We evaluate our method on three standard benchmark datasets for conversational LLMs, including gpt-3.5-turbo, gpt-4, and chatGPT. Experimental results demonstrate that Direct RLHF consistently outperforms baseline methods that use supervised learning or reinforcement learning with behavior cloning. In particular, Direct RLHF is the first method that achieves a higher success rate than the baseline in generating helpful and harmless responses. Moreover, Direct RLHF also achieves a similar level of success as the baseline in generating diverse and high-quality responses. Our code is available at https://github.com/THUMNLab/Direct-RLHF.

Filter bubbles have been studied extensively within the context of online content platforms due to their potential influence on user preferences. This study aims to understand filter bubbles' effect on users' political beliefs and perceptions in the context of news media. We conducted a survey with 1,002 respondents from diverse geographical areas across the United States to determine the extent to which people are exposed to information that aligns or diverges with their personal views. The results show that exposure to dissimilar sources is correlated with decreased polarization, suggesting that diversity in news consumption can enhance civic discourse. However, this relationship was not observed for extreme viewpoints, indicating that there may be limits to how much exposure to diverse

The challenge of spatial resource allocation is pervasive across various domains such as transportation, industry, and healthcare. Existing studies in this field often rely on manually designed algorithms with limited flexibility and generalization capabilities, which makes it challenging to handle complex and dynamic scenarios. In this paper, we present a novel method based on Generative Models (GMs) for tackling the problem of Spatial Resource Allocation (SRA). Our method, called SRA-GM, employs GMs to generate diverse solutions for the SRA problem. Specifically, SRA-GM comprises three modules: an encoder-decoder module that learns from data and generates new samples, an optimization module that finds optimal solutions within the generated samples, and a consistency constraint that ensur

This article evaluates the quality of data collection in individual-level desktop information tracking used in the social sciences. In particular, we examine the completeness and accuracy of three types of self-reported information: (1) a simple binary question about whether the respondent owns a specific item (e.g., a car), (2) an open-ended question about how many items of a given type the respondent owns (e.g., cars), and (3) a list question about which items the respondent does or does not own (e.g., cars). We first identify a general method for measuring these types of questions using Amazon Mechanical Turk (MTurk). The MTurk study finds that all three types of questions result in significant amounts of missing responses, with more than 5% of respondents omitting answers to at least o

The field of Contextual Optimization (CO) integrates machine learning and optimization to solve decision-making problems. However, the existing CO methods fail to account for the contextual uncertainty in decision making due to the absence of a robust framework that can effectively handle uncertainties. To address this challenge, we propose a novel approach called Uncertainty-aware Contextual Optimization (UCCO). This method involves two main stages: (i) uncertainty modeling through Bayesian inference and (ii) incorporating it into a traditional CO framework.
