In stage one, we develop a Bayesian inference approach for uncertain contextual information, which is then utilized to construct a prior distribution over possible contexts. Subsequently, in stage two, the proposed UCCO framework infe

Multi-talker automatic speech recognition plays a crucial role in scenarios involving multi-party interactions, such as conference calls. However, existing methods tend to rely on speaker labels or predefined speaker clustering, which are not always available in real-world scenarios and may limit their generalization ability. To address these challenges, we propose a novel approach that leverages the local temporal information within utterances. Specifically, we introduce a dynamic speaker attention mechanism that adaptively learns speaker representations based on local contextual information. This mechanism is capable of capturing both individual and group dynamics without prior knowledge. Our experimental results demonstrate that our model significantly outperforms baseline approaches ac

Our work addresses the challenges older adults face with commercial Voice Assistants (VAs). We propose a novel approach to design and test VAs for older adults, called Heterogenous Co-Design. In contrast to traditional design approaches that focus on either user or technology-centric perspectives, we co-design the VA system with older adults and experts. To support our approach, we designed a new methodology called Aging in Place, which supports older adults as they use the VA in their homes over extended periods of time. Our approach includes a new toolkit of data collection methods, including both quantitative and qualitative methods, which are tailored to collect data from heterogeneous participants. We share our findings and discuss how these can inform future research and development.

We study connections between the problem of fully dynamic $(1-\epsilon)$-approximate maximum bipartite matching in the unit interval and a related version of online $(1-\epsilon)$ approximation algorithm for multi-criteria optimization. Our main result is that any $(1-\epsilon)$-approximation algorithm for multi-criteria optimization with time complexity $O(\frac{1}{\epsilon^2})$ on $[0,n]$ can be used to obtain an algorithm for the unit interval that finds a $O(\sqrt{\epsilon n\log n})$-approximate maximum bipartite matching in $O(n^{3/4}\log n)$ time. This matches the best known time complexities for the online and offline problems (in the static setting).

We present Splat-Nav, a navigation pipeline that consists of a real-time safe planning system with the ability to reason about unseen obstacles and an online sensor fusion algorithm for localization. We use a custom simulator which allows for rapid iteration on both aspects of the pipeline. To train the model, we collected over 300000 trajectories in simulation and used them to evaluate our algorithms in simulation and in the real world. Our approach allows for real-time performance without any additional computationally expensive preprocessing steps. We report on the successes and limitations of this approach. We also discuss how to apply it to other domains. Finally, we provide code samples and a video demo at https://github.com/BryanDietrich/splat-nav. The paper can be found here: https

Photographic image layout refers to the arrangement of visual elements (e.g., objects, colors, textures, etc.) in a photograph. It is a crucial visual cue that influences our perception of the depicted scene's spatial relationships, object sizes, and distances. Despite its importance, there is a lack of computational models that can accurately represent and manipulate photographic image layout. Instead, existing models typically treat the entire image as a flat visual token, losing spatial cues, and struggling to distinguish objects of interest from the background. In this work, we fill this gap by focusing on the task of self-supervised photographic image layout representation learning. Specifically, we propose a novel training scheme to encourage the model to extract and encode layout-relevant visual features by leveraging a self-supervised photometric error. This error measures the discrepancy between the original and the model-predicted image layouts, providing an effective loss signal for layout-aware feature learning. Our method, named LayoutNet, generates a semantic layout representation that can be decoded into a layout-modulated image, offering a powerful tool for image layout manipulation. Extensive experiments demonstrate that the proposed self-supervised LayoutNet outperforms existing methods in terms of image layout representation and manipulation. It achieves a favorably small photometric error of 7.13 compared to baselines on the MOPA dataset. The source code is available at https://github.com/luost26/self-supervised-layout-representation-learning.

Transformer-based Neural Language Models achieve state-of-the-art performance on various language understanding tasks, mainly due to their ability to capture contextual information across long text spans. However, they are not as effective in learning from a limited number of training examples since the number of parameters is significantly higher than other models such as BERT. In this paper, we propose a novel approach for zero-shot language modeling that leverages few-shot prompts and outperforms existing methods by a large margin. We show that our approach achieves competitive results compared with fine-tuning using full-length training data, while requiring 100x less training data. Our method is based on a new concept of prompting: instead of using a single long sentence as a prompt,

Fractional Differential Equations (FDEs) are essential tools for modelling complex dynamical systems. However, their analysis is challenging due to the intricacies of fractional calculus and the lack of well-established numerical methods. This paper presents a novel approach that combines the power of FDEs with the advantages of deep learning. It introduces the concept of Deep Neural Networks (DNNs), trained specifically on FDEs, to provide an effective solution for analysing these equations. To evaluate the performance of the proposed method, we conducted experiments involving the Euler and Heun methods as benchmarks. The results demonstrate that our approach can improve the accuracy of FDE solutions compared to traditional methods. Moreover, it offers computational efficiency by reducing

Imagine generating a city's electricity demand pattern based on weather, the presence of an electric vehicle (EV) fleet, and renewable energy generation. We propose a novel framework to learn such demand patterns by optimizing for the expected net benefit when generating power using wind turbines. The proposed framework comprises three components: (i) estimating a day-ahead forecast of power demand with an EV fleet, (ii) estimating the cost of generating electricity from different energy sources, and (iii) optimizing the net benefits by choosing between EV charging or wind power. We use real-world data to demonstrate that the proposed framework can generate hourly demand patterns that closely match real demand while minimizing the cost of electricity generation. Moreover, our framework is

Accurate detection and diagnosis of abnormal behaviors such as network attacks from multivariate time series is a challenging task in the field of cybersecurity. Large language models (LLMs) have been widely applied to detect anomalies due to their superior ability to model long-range correlations, but they often suffer from the phenomenon of hallucinating non-existent events, which results in false positives or false negatives. Recently, prompted LLMs have emerged as an effective method for addressing this challenge by incorporating pre-training knowledge into downstream tasks through the use of fine-tuning prompts. However, these prompts can be difficult to tune to yield optimal results, necessitating significant manual effort, especially when training on large datasets. In addition, the

In this paper, we introduce PixArt-\Sigma, a Diffusion Transformer model~(DTM) for pixel-based image editing. Unlike traditional DTM that utilizes discrete latent variables for image generation and manipulation, our method is designed to work with continuous latent variables, thereby enhancing the manipulability of DTMs. The key element of our approach is a novel architecture based on a continuous diffusion process, which is capable of effectively handling continuous inputs in various forms, including the noise level and the input image. Experiments demonstrate that our PixArt-\Sigma significantly improves the controllability and editing quality of existing methods, while requiring fewer training data and model parameters. We hope that our proposed PixArt-\Sigma will serve as a versatile t

Agent Based Models (ABMs) have emerged as a powerful tool for investigating complex socio-economic phenomena in the context of smart cities. However, their widespread adoption has been limited due to the challenges associated with their development and validation. This paper aims to address these issues through the introduction of an innovative methodology called ABM4U, which streamlines the creation of ABMs using simple textual descriptions and enables rapid validation against real-world data. The ABM4U framework is implemented as a web application that allows users to define their own models by providing input parameters, rules, and expected model outcomes, which are then automatically translated into a valid ABM code. The resulting code can be executed on any compatible simulation platf

Federated learning (FL) is a privacy-preserving distributed management framework based on collaborative training of multiple clients. In this work, we consider the problem of finding a client with the best performance in FL. Specifically, given an arbitrary number of clients, we find a client that has the lowest average loss over the whole dataset and the highest probability to outperform all other clients if we retrain using their respective local models. The latter is achieved by designing a novel algorithm for measuring the average loss and computing the probability of beating each client using Bayesian inference. Our experimental results show that our approach achieves better results compared to standard baselines when applied to real data from different domains, including computer vis

The African continent lacks enough qualified teachers which hampers the provision of adequate learning support to students. However, there is a dearth of research on how AI-powered tools can be leveraged to mitigate this challenge in Africa. This paper presents an exploratory study that examines the use of AI-powered tools for personalized learning support for primary school mathematics in Ghana. The objective was to identify key challenges and opportunities related to the adoption and integration of these technologies into teaching practices, as well as potential benefits and limitations. Findings from focus group discussions with 100 participants (teachers, headmasters, parents, and learners) revealed the following major issues: lack of technical knowledge among stakeholders; limited acc

Due to the rapid generation and dissemination of information, large language models (LLMs) have become a popular tool for generating various types. However LLMSs are not robust enough in handling real-world data with complex semantic relationships because they learn from text corpora that do no provide sufficient training examples or context clues on how best handle such situations as well generate accurate responses accordingly: This study presents an approach based upon Reinforcement Learning framework where rewards signals generated by users' feedback help guide learning process towards improving overall performance when dealing similar problems encountered during interactions between humans & computers - specifically those involving conversations about topics requiring deeper understan

An open stochastic system \`a la Willems is a system affected two qualitatively different kinds of randomness: (i) the input signal is subject to some stochastic noise, and (ii) the output signal is subject to an external disturbance. The open stochastic system is modeled by a family of Markov processes $P_t$ indexed by time $t$. We study the problem of computing the expected value of a function $f(x)$ under a distribution $\pi$, where $x$ is a random variable distributed according to $P_t$, $t\geq 0$. This problem is known as the open stochastic integral problem (OSIP). For a special class of functions we are able to compute exactly this quantity.

Federated learning is a popular framework for collaborative machine learning where multiple clients only share gradient updates on their local data. Federated averaging (FedAvg) is the most widely used algorithm in this setting, but it has limited ability to improve generalization and fails to scale beyond 10 clients. In this paper, we propose a new adaptive FL algorithm called FedSGD-Mixup that can train models with better accuracy and stability than FedAvg. We theoretically demonstrate that our method is able to achieve faster convergence rate and higher test error lower bound compared with FedAvg under different settings. Additionally, we show that FedSGD-Mixup outperforms FedAvg on multiple large-scale synthetic datasets.

Large language model (LLM) leads to a surge of autonomous GUI agents for smartphone applications. However, it remains challenging to evaluate the LLM-based GUI agents due to their unknown nature and the lack of benchmark datasets. To address this challenge, we propose UI-GPT, an open dataset that consists of 564K training samples for autonomous GUI agent development. The dataset contains human-labeled images with detailed interaction labels. We use these samples to train a model named UI-GPT, which can perform various tasks such as image classification, text generation, and object detection. Furthermore, we conduct experiments on UI-GPT and find that it can achieve competitive results compared to the baselines. Our work not only provides an open dataset but also contributes to building tru

Non-verbal signals in speech are encoded by prosody and carry information that ranges from conversation action, affective state to personality traits. In this paper, we investigate the role of non-verbal signals for speaker profiling in the context of multimodal dialogue systems. We hypothesize that these signals can be used as input to a multi-task learning setup with an additional speaker profile prediction task. The results show that adding the speaker profile prediction task does not improve the performance of the emotion recognition or intent classification tasks. However, the results indicate that adding emotion and intent predictions as auxiliary tasks improves the speaker profile prediction task. This demonstrates that the interaction between different modalities is useful for pred

The White House Executive Order on Artificial Intelligence highlights the risks of large language models (LLMs) to national security, and several countries have begun to investigate and regulate these technologies. Recent research has shown that LLMs are susceptible to adversarial prompting attacks, where a small modification in the initial input can significantly change its output. However, most existing works focus on English LLMs, and there is limited understanding of how the performance of LLMs varies across different languages. In this study, we introduce "Adversarial Prompting for Language Models" (APL), a new dataset consisting of 18,053 pairs of adversarial prompts targeted at four popular LLMs in four major languages -- Chinese, French, Russian, and Spanish.

Session types are a discipline for the static verification of message-passing programs. A session type specifies a channel's protocol as sequences of exchanges. It is most relevant to investigate session-based concurrency by identifying the essential notions that enable program specification and verification. Following that perspective, prior work identified message-observing sessions (MOS), a sub-class of session types that abstract over the monitoring and manipulation of communication actions. This formulation of session types led to establish a minimality result: every process typable with standard session types can be compiled down to a process typable using MOS, which mimics sequentiality. Such a minimality result is significant because it justifies session types in terms of themselves, without resorting to external notions; it was proven for a higher-order session pi-calculus, in which values are abstractions (functions from names to processes). In this paper, we study MOS further by considering their observational properties and by investigating a setting that goes beyond higher-order. We first show that, by appropriately interpreting processes as dynamical systems, we can soundly transfer the minimality result to the setting of first-order session types, in which values are individuals (names are not abstracted). We then study the observational properties of MOS, focusing on two notions: transparency, which relates to the notational sugar of sequentiality; and autonomy, which is concerned with the internal structure of processes. We show that, by restricting observables to projections (functions that pick specific components of compound values), we can soundly transfer the transparency and autonomy properties from sequential processes to MOS.

Event Causality Identification (ECI) refers to detect causal relations between events in texts. The existing approaches often rely on external event knowledge and assume that the events are represented as noun phrases or verbs, which might not be applicable for many real-world scenarios. In this work, we propose a novel framework, named Event Detection for Open-Vocabulary Causal Relation Extraction (EDORE), designed specifically to solve ECI in the open vocabulary setting. Instead of relying on predefined event classes, our method directly identifies and extracts causal relations from event mentions with no external event information. Our method employs an event detector, an event relation encoder and an end-to-end decoder for relation extraction. During training, we introduce a new event

The rise of machine learning in recent years has brought benefits to various research fields such as wide fire detection, image recognition and pattern classification. The advantages are even more pronounced when combined with artificial intelligence (AI) technologies for further improvement on the performance level [1]. However there is an underlying issue that many AI models have a tendency towards unfairness because they are prone toward bias during training or evaluation process which leads them into making incorrect decisions based off their learned biases rather than relying solely upon logic-based reasoning alone[2], thus posing risks not only within security domains but also across other areas including finance & healthcare industries etc.. To address this problem , we propose thre

In reasoning tasks, even a minor error can cascade into inaccurate results, leading to catastrophic failures. This phenomenon is known as chain-of-thought errors (COTEs). Despite the prevalence of COTEs in real-world reasoning systems, their effects have not yet been studied thoroughly within the field of artificial intelligence. In this work, we identify and characterize three types of COTE, each with distinct causes and outcomes: conceptual errors, execution errors, and data quality issues. We conduct an extensive analysis of existing datasets to demonstrate that COTEs are widespread across different domains and reasoning tasks, highlighting the importance of addressing these errors effectively. Finally, we offer suggestions for mitigating COTEs through improved system design, enhanced d

Autonomous driving has attracted significant research interests in the past two decades as it offers many potential benefits, including releasing drivers from exhausting driving and mitigating traffic congestion, among others. Despite promising progress, existing autonomous driving technologies still suffer from inefficient decision-making and control, especially in complex traffic scenarios with dynamic and uncertain traffic flows. To tackle this challenge, this paper proposes a Reinforcement Learning based Autonomous Control for social on-ramp merging, namely RACE-SM. Specifically, a multi-agent reinforcement learning (MARL) framework is proposed to optimize the autonomous vehicle's (AV) decision-making and control. In particular, a multi-agent advantage actor-critic network (MA2C) is developed to enable the AV to make optimal decisions by learning from historical state-action-reward data. Moreover, a multi-objective reward function is proposed to incorporate fuel efficiency, driving comfort, and safety of on-ramp merging. Extensive experiments are conducted under three different traffic densities to evaluate the performance of RACE-SM. Experimental results show that RACE-SM performs superior compared to several state-of-the-art baselines in terms of efficiency, safety and driver comfort.

Current approaches to question answering rely on pre-trained language models (PLMs) like RoBERTa. This work challenges the existing question-answer encoding convention and explores finer representations. We begin with testing various pooling methods compared to using the begin-of-sentence token as a question representation for better quality. Next, we explore opportunities to simultaneously embed all answer candidates with the question. This enables cross-reference between answer choices and improves inference throughput via reduced memory usage. Despite their simplicity and effectiveness, these methods have yet to be widely studied in current frameworks. We experiment with different PLMs, and with and without the integration of knowledge graphs. Results prove that the memory efficacy of t

Learning to Optimize (LtO) is a problem setting in which a machine learning (ML) algorithm is trained to find an optimal solution for a given optimization problem, without access to the ground truth value. We propose a novel LtO approach that utilizes a reinforcement learning (RL)-based policy learner and a state-of-the-art neural optimizer as cooperative agents. This model, referred to as the RL-Neural-Optimization-Augmented Policy Learner (RNOAL), enhances the robustness of ML algorithms by enabling them to learn how to effectively exploit the computational advantages of modern solvers. The effectiveness of RNOAL is demonstrated via extensive experiments on several challenging problems including large-scale sparse linear programming, mixed integer programs, and nonlinear least squares pr

The growing dependence on Large Language Models (LLMs) for finishing user instructions necessitates a comprehensive understanding of their behavior in this context. Recent research has emphasized the importance of monitoring and analyzing LLM behavior at various levels, including input, output, and internal processes. However, these analyses often overlook the critical role played by the underlying model itself, particularly its ability to learn and adapt over time. In this paper, we present an analysis framework that focuses on LLM behavior from a learning perspective. We introduce metrics such as generalization and forgetting to assess the learning capabilities of LLMs, including their capacity to retain information acquired during training. Additionally, we examine the effects of input

In the rapidly evolving field of machine learning (ML), data augmentation (DA) has emerged as a crucial technique for enhancing model performance. While several recent advancements have improved DA, they are often limited in their ability to scale effectively with increasing model complexity. This is especially true in applications involving large models and datasets. To address this challenge, we propose an approach that leverages self-attention mechanisms. Our method builds upon Transformer's self-attention mechanism by introducing contextualized noise vectors into the model. These vectors are designed to mimic real-world noise conditions, thereby incorporating noise-based DA techniques within the model itself. We also introduce a novel metric, based on the Kullback-Leibler Divergence, w

Tabular data, as a crucial form of data representation, exists in diverse formats on the Web. For example, tables can be found on product detail pages (PDPs) or within blog posts. Existing tabular data extraction methods are often task-specific, and their performance is not necessarily consistent across different tasks. To address this challenge, we propose a multi-task learning framework to extract tabular data from heterogeneous web documents, including PDPs and blog posts. Specifically, we introduce two multi-task learning paradigms: shared encoders for task-agnostic features and shared classifiers for task-agnostic predictions. We also integrate a few-shot learning module into the multi-task learning framework to leverage expert-labeled samples for unseen tasks. Our approach effectivel

Scaling laws play an instrumental role in the sustainable improvement in model quality. Unfortunately, scaling laws have not been studied for small-scale image classification models, which are widely used in practice due to their efficiency and low-cost. In this paper, we study the scaling laws for the smallest convolutional networks (ConvNets) with up to 10k parameters. We show that larger ConvNets are able to fit more samples in memory and therefore lead to improved generalization performance. This is particularly relevant because of recent advances in large-scale data generation. On top of that, larger ConvNets also benefit from a more efficient sampling procedure, as we demonstrate empirically using a linear search over random subsets. Overall, our results suggest that scaling ConvNets

This paper introduces a novel neuro-symbolic architecture for relation classification (RC) that combines the strength of symbolic and neural approaches. Deep semantic networks are employed to extract semantic features, which are further refined by a set of self-attention-based Transformer encoders. The proposed model is evaluated on the benchmark dataset of the SemEval 2019 Task 5: Textual Entailment over Commonsense Knowledge (SEMEVAL-2019 T5). Experimental results show that our approach outperforms all baseline models by a significant margin. Furthermore, we demonstrate that the proposed approach exhibits superior generalization performance in comparison with a state-of-the-art symbolic model. Our source code and trained model are available at https://github.com/junxu0634/Neural-Symbolic

In autonomous driving, 3D occupancy prediction outputs voxel-wise status and semantic labels for each grid in the 3D space. It is a critical task for the perception system to identify objects (i.e., pedestrians, vehicles, traffic signals, etc.) and avoid collisions. The conventional approaches employ predefined grids, which limits their performance due to the fixed-sized voxels. To address this issue, we propose a novel framework named VoxelNet++ that utilizes an adaptive voxelization scheme tailored to the scene's specificity. Our method, VoxelNet++, first detects the potential locations of objects from images using image segmentation. Then, the detected objects are used to guide adaptive voxelization.

Additive manufacturing (AM) is a rapidly evolving technology with a wide range of applications in various areas such as aerospace, automotive, and biomedical. One of the key features of AM is the ability to produce complex geometries with internal structures, which is ideal for lightweight designs in these applications. However, the deformation that occurs during AM processes needs to be considered, especially for structures with thin walls or under high loads, to ensure the part's performance and reliability. Topology optimization (TO) can be used to obtain optimal geometries considering the deformation, but the computational cost is high, which limits its application to large-scale problems. To reduce the computational cost, this study proposes two novel approaches that can be applied to any TO problem without additional assumptions on the geometry or loads. The first approach combines the level-set method and the phase-field method to track the evolving design and estimate the deformation history. The second approach uses a reduced-order model based on proper orthogonal decomposition to approximate the deformation history. To validate the proposed methods, two benchmark problems are tested under different loading conditions. Results show that the proposed methods can accurately predict the deformation history at a lower computational cost. Additionally, a real-world application of a fuel tank is analyzed to demonstrate the methods' effectiveness in practical situations.

Dense Retrieval (DR) is now considered as a promising tool to enhance the memorization capability of large language models. The current state-of-the-art DR methodology relies on the generation of pseudo questions based on retrieved documents, which are then used for downstream question answering tasks. In this paper, we investigate the role of these pseudo questions in DR and propose an alternative methodology that does not require them at all. We demonstrate that using the retrieved documents as evidence directly can achieve comparable performance with the previous methods while significantly reducing the computational cost during training. We further show that our approach can be seamlessly applied to existing pre-trained models without requiring any fine-tuning, making it accessible and

The advent of smart IoT devices has revolutionized the way we interact with the environment by enabling continuous monitoring and management of various parameters. However, the data collected by these devices can be prone to quality issues, affecting the performance of IoT applications. Ensuring data quality in IoT systems is crucial for making timely decisions and maintaining system performance, especially in scenarios where data is collected from distributed sources and needs to be analyzed in an economical manner. Artificial Intelligence (AI) and, in particular, Machine Learning (ML) are emerging as key technologies for IoT data quality assessment and curation. In this paper, we provide a systematic approach for assessing and curating data quality in IoT systems using AI. We propose an original methodology that goes beyond simple outlier detection to curate data by identifying and correcting systematic biases using a variety of AI techniques, including supervised, semi-supervised, and unsupervised learning approaches. The proposed framework is evaluated through extensive experiments conducted on real-world IoT datasets, and its performance is compared with state-of-the-art methods. The results demonstrate that the proposed approach is effective in detecting and correcting biases, improving the accuracy of the predictions. The experiments also show that the AI techniques can be used to build predictive models that can generalize on unseen data, reducing the need for manual intervention.

Thanks to the development of basic models, infrared small target detection (ISTD) algorithms have achieved remarkable achievements. However these methods still cannot achieve high performance due mainly because: 1.) In most cases all pixel values are set as background for model training and testing;2. ), There is a large gap between simulation data generation method and real-world scenes that lead false positive detections in practical applications. ;3), The lack sufficient strong validation dataset limits further improvement on ISTDs.' To address this issue we propose an end -toend framework consisting two main components i. ): A generative adversarial network(GAN)-based approach called GIMEDATs which can synthesize realistic fake images with different backgrounds from simple simulated in

The development of next-generation networks is revolutionizing network operators' management and orchestration practices. Large-scale multi-vendor and multi-domain (MLMD) deployments have become the norm, demanding a new generation of automation tools capable of managing diverse configurations, workloads, and environments. Multi-cloud environments present a unique challenge to traditional network management approaches due to their distributed nature. In this paper, we introduce GLOBE -- a novel framework for large-scale MLMD management and orchestration. GLOBE employs an innovative approach that leverages the capabilities of Cloud Native Computing Foundation (CNCF) projects such as Open Network Automation Platform (ONAP), Kubernetes, and Istio to create an interoperable ecosystem that enab

The execution and runtime performance of model-based analysis tools for realistic large-scale ABMs often falls short. This is due to the fact that these models can be very complex, with a multitude of interacting agents, which makes them computationally expensive to run. These computational costs are further exacerbated by the necessity to run multiple replications in order to account for stochasticity. However, there has been little research done on how to address this issue, particularly within the context of realistic large-scale ABMs, in which the complexity of both model and data is high. In this paper we present a novel approach to address this issue by introducing an efficient, parallelizable, probabilistic framework for analysing ABMs in R. We illustrate the practical benefits of o

Federated learning is a popular framework for collaborative machine learning where multiple clients only share gradient updates on their local models to a central server. The central server then aggregates these updates and pushes the updated model back to each client. This iterative process helps in training better models without sharing clients' data or parameters with the server. The standard approach to aggregating gradients is by averaging them, which may not always be optimal due to heterogeneity of datasets across different clients. In this paper, we propose a novel federated learning framework based on Bayesian optimization to selectively choose clients whose gradients should contribute towards the model update at the server, thereby enhancing its performance. We show that our fram

The recent development of quantum computing, which makes use of entanglement, superposition, and other non-classical concepts to perform calculations that are not possible with classical computers has sparked a renewed interest in the study of information theory. In particular one is interested into understanding how much randomness can be extracted from various types or structures generated by random processes on physical systems; this paper examines such problems using tools developed within statistical physics during its investigation towards thermal equilibrium states via Gibbs distributions (i). We show here two different approaches for constructing an analogue version involving only deterministic constraints as well: first we consider local correlations between neighbouring particles

Cybersecurity researchers have been publishing threat reports for decades, sharing details on recently discovered cyberattack techniques, tools, and procedures (TTPs) used by threat actors. These reports serve as valuable threat intelligence (TI) for defenders. However, TI extraction from these reports is labor-intensive and often requires manual efforts. Automated extraction can help alleviate this burden. In this paper, we introduce TTPXHunter, a novel technique for extracting TI from cyber threat reports as TTPs. TTPXHunter employs natural language processing (NLP) and machine learning (ML) to extract TTPs from threat reports without manual efforts. We build a pipeline that consists of NLP models for extracting verbs and tool mentions from threat reports. We then use an ML model to classify the extracted verbs and tool mentions as TTPs. Our evaluation results show that TTPXHunter outperforms existing baselines for extracting TTPs from threat reports, achieving a 4.5% increase in F1 score for detecting TTPs in our test dataset.

In order to understand how to better regulate congestion on road networks, we model repeated weighted congestion games with a make-the-best-player-happy (MTPH) social planner. We explore the design of artificial currency incentives to promote equitable behavior among drivers, using two measures of equity: maxmin fairness and Nash welfare. In particular, we investigate the minimum incentive that the social planner needs to offer to players to achieve a certain level of maxmin fairness or Nash welfare. We show that the maxmin-fair incentive is always at least as large as the welfare-guarantee incentive, but can be exponentially larger. This is because maxmin fairness often requires the social planner to give money to players, whereas the welfare guarantee only requires taking money away from players. Intuitively, maxmin fairness is more concerned with helping the worst-off player, while the welfare guarantee is more concerned with hurting the best-off player.

Public bus transit systems provide critical transportation services for large sections of modern communities. On-time performance, in terms of timeliness and punctuality, is an important measure of a public transit system’s quality. Unfortunately, delays are often caused by unpredictable events such as traffic congestion or breakdowns, which can lead to cascading delays. As a result, on-time performance has been declining over time. In this paper, we propose a novel framework that combines machine learning techniques with optimization algorithms to improve the efficiency of public bus transit systems. Our approach utilizes both historical and real-time data to identify patterns in bus operations and predict potential bottlenecks. We develop a novel hybrid reinforcement learning algorithm t

Let $\mathcal{P}$ be a simple polygon with $m$ vertices and let $P$ be an arbitrary point in the interior of $\mathcal{P}$. We study the problem of finding a shortest path from $P$ to any other point in the interior of $\mathcal{P}$ that passes through each vertex of $\mathcal{P}$. This is known as the $O(mn)$ congestion problem, where $n$ denotes the number of vertices in $\mathcal{P}$. We show that this problem has a $1/2$-approximation algorithm with running time $O(m \log m)$. Our result relies on the following new geometric lemma: Given any two points $A, B$ in a triangle $\Delta$, we can construct a point $C$ in the interior of $\Delta$ such that the distance between $A$ and $B$ is at most half the distance between $A$ and $C$.

Floods are among the most common and costly natural disasters, affecting millions of people worldwide. Effective flood risk management (FRM) requires proactive, data-driven decision support for stakeholders including government agencies, community planners, and the general public. However, FRM tools often suffer from being overly complex, proprietary, or lacking in explainability, which can hinder their adoption and utilization. In this research, we explore the potential of natural language processing and AI assistants to democratize FRM by developing an interactive, conversational system that can explain flood risks and recommended actions in a user-friendly manner. Specifically, we propose a GPT-4-based AI assistant that leverages question-answering and dialogue generation to engage users in a fluid conversation about flood risks in their local area, based on a combination of publicly available data and user-provided information. We evaluate the assistant's flood risk explanation and engagement capabilities through a user study, receiving positive feedback regarding the assistant's ability to clearly explain flood risks and engage users in the FRM process. We believe that our assistant has the potential to empower individuals and communities to take proactive steps in managing flood risks, ultimately contributing to more informed and participatory disaster risk reduction efforts.

Software product line techniques encourage the reuse and adaptation of software components for creating customized products or software systems. However, their adoption in real-world environments is often limited due to several factors such as time constraints, resource limitations, and project requirements. To address these challenges, we present a novel technique called Pipeline-based Product Line Engineering (PPLINE). Our approach uses existing software pipelines to automate the generation of software components from templates, thereby reducing development effort and enabling rapid creation and deployment of diverse software products. PPLINE's main goal is to facilitate the development process by utilizing existing software pipelines and leveraging their capabilities to generate softwar

Next-generation cellular concepts rely on the processing of large quantities of radio-frequency (RF) samples in the baseband processor. However, current state-of-the-art processors, such as those based on graphics processing units (GPUs), are limited by their relatively low sampling rate and/or power consumption. We propose a solution to this problem based on a programmable architecture for reconfigurable digital signal processors (DSPs). Our architecture can be configured at run time to support any number of parallel processing cores, allowing it to achieve a high sampling rate without sacrificing energy efficiency. The proposed hardware-software codesign framework is designed to enable the automatic generation of synthesizable Verilog code from C++ specifications of our processing algori

Person verification with multiple modalities, especially audio and visual, has attracted much attention in recent years. In this paper, we propose a novel audio-visual person verification model based on recursive fusion of joint cross-attention (RFJCA). Specifically, we introduce a novel joint cross-attention module (JCAM) which can effectively extract the complementary features from the two modalities. Then, we propose a recursive fusion module (RFM) which can gradually and selectively fuse the complementary features from the two modalities. Furthermore, we propose a list-style contrastive loss which can pull the same person's features from the two modalities and push the different person's features away from each other. Extensive experiments on the publicly available audio-visual datasets show that our method outperforms the state-of-the-art methods.

Multimodal recommendation systems that consider multiple source types for generating recommendations are gaining increasing popularity. This is mainly due to the rich information and context that can be extracted from different sources, such as images, text, and video. However, the majority of the current multimodal approaches focus only on combining information from two modalities, and there is a lack of a general framework that can easily incorporate new modalities. In addition, most of the existing frameworks are missing some important recent advances in the field, such as pretrained models and transformer-based architectures. To address these limitations, we present an updated version of the Ducho framework, which is a modular and extensible framework for multimodal recommendation. The new version incorporates modern machine learning models, such as CLIP and LXMERT, and follows a plugin-based architecture that allows for easy extension to new modalities. We evaluate the new Ducho 2.0 framework on three multimodal datasets and show that it is possible to extract features from different sources and combine them into a unified embedding space.

The significant advancements in large language models (LLMs) give rise to a promising new era of AI-powered applications. Despite their growing popularity, the deployment of LLM applications often faces a major obstacle: privacy concerns. In this paper, we present Palladium, an innovative solution that addresses this challenge by enabling the development and use of LLMs without compromising user data privacy. Palladium is built upon a novel approach called "Zero-Shot Private Pretraining" (ZSPP), which involves fine-tuning pretrained LLMs on private datasets without exposing any sensitive information. This technique allows users to generate unique and personalized model variants, while ensuring strict compliance with privacy regulations. Additionally, our system incorporates a customized to

In this study, a human evaluation is carried out on how hyperparameter settings impact the quality of Transformed Language Models (TLMs) for various downstream tasks. We use an automated pipeline to generate 1098 TLM candidates from nine different base models and four TLMSearch-based methods with varying parameter configurations such as backbone size or training epochs across three domains: general language understanding including question answering; reading comprehension in academia via scientific paper summarisation ; writing development using essay generation .

Recent work has demonstrated that fine-tuning is a promising approach to `unlearn' the wrongly learned language biases in large pretrained models, such as GPT4. However, this fine-tuning process can lead to an adverse effect on downstream tasks, such as semantic reasoning, when it is applied to all sentences rather than only those with linguistic biases. This phenomenon is called `overfitting'. To address overfitting, we propose a method called RLF (Reinforcement Learning Fine-Tuning) which can automatically determine whether to apply fine-tuning or not to specific sentences. In particular, we use a reinforcement learning algorithm to learn the reward function for each sentence of the dataset and select the sentences that should be fine-tuned via maximizing rewards.

Answer Set Programming (ASP) is a declarative programming paradigm that allows users to specify their problem in a natural and concise manner. However, writing ASP programs requires users to master the syntax and semantics of the language, which can be a barrier for non-experts. On the other hand, natural language is more intuitive and easier to understand. In this paper, we explore the idea of automatic composition of ASP programs from natural language specifications. The automatic composition aims to reduce the learning curve for non-experts and alleviate the burden of writing complex ASP programs. We propose a multi-stage framework that automatically generates ASP programs from natural language specifications. The framework consists of a natural language parser, a semantic role labeler, and a program generator. The natural language parser and the semantic role labeler work jointly to extract the key information from the natural language specification, including facts, rules, and constraints. The program generator takes the processed natural language specification as input and produces the corresponding ASP program. We evaluate the automatic composition framework on three different datasets that contain natural language specifications and ground truth ASP programs. The experimental results show that the proposed framework can achieve a higher success rate than the state-of-the-art methods.

While deep learning has celebrated many successes, its results often hinge on the meticulous selection of hyperparameters. In particular: (1) selecting an optimal batch size is a well-known challenge in training neural networks; and although it was shown that larger batches can lead to better generalization performance for large data sets [Sutskever et al., 20x], this does not hold true when facing limited memory budgets or small datasets -- situations where smaller minibatch sizes are commonly used instead.

Processing-using-DRAM (PUD) architectures impose a restrictive data layout that reduces the latency of processing operations. This is achieved by moving the data closer to where it needs to be processed, allowing for faster access and lower bandwidth usage. However, this also creates challenges when accessing memory in an out-of-order manner: if the data accessed by a subsequent instruction must come from outside the DRAM window, the data has to be retrieved and then written back into the DRAM window before it can be used. This overhead can significantly increase the overall latency of the instruction.

As the errors of microelectromechanical system (MEMS) gyroscopes are complex and nonlinear, it is difficult to derive a high-accuracy analytic model to characterize them. To overcome this limitation, we present an effective way to construct a highly accurate MEMS gyroscope error model by training an artificial neural network (ANN). The ANN can learn a complex nonlinear relationship between the gyroscope's output and its input, thereby effectively capturing and reproducing the complicated nonlinear error characteristics of MEMS gyroscopes. In addition, the ANN can be trained online through the available data to achieve real-time estimation of the gyroscope's nonlinear errors. We demonstrate that the proposed method can accurately estimate the gyroscope's nonlinear errors in various situatio

Existing multi-modal LLMs typically rely on pre-trained visual models, which have limitations in fine-grained visual perception and may lead to degraded performance for specific downstream tasks. In this work, we propose a new framework called MIViT that instruction-tunes a multi-modal LLM with fine-grained visual perception capabilities. Specifically, we introduce a new pre-training stage that enhances the visual perception of existing multi-modal LLMs. This stage involves fine-tuning a vision-language model with an additional fine-grained visual perception head. The resulting MIViT model excels in multi-modal instruction tuning, demonstrating superior performance in downstream tasks such as visual reasoning, multi-modal dialogue, and image captioning. Our experiments show that MIViT outperforms existing multi-modal LLMs, achieving state-of-the-art performance on benchmarks like COCO. Notably, MIViT also exhibits robust performance across various instruction tuning approaches, showing consistent improvements. The code is available at https://github.com/BeierZhu/MIViT.

To date, toxicity mitigation in language models has almost entirely been focused on single-step prompting. This paper presents a systematic framework for multi-step toxicity reduction via prompt engineering. We evaluate our approach using the Llama benchmark and find that it improves state-of-the-art performance over seven baselines while maintaining a high degree of alignment with human evaluations. Our approach also outperforms both supervised and unsupervised techniques. Moreover, we introduce a new dataset called MultiToxicity that captures the diversity inherent in real-world multi-step contexts, which we use as input for training and evaluation. Finally, we showcase how prompt engineering can be used to generate nuanced responses based on the input context, demonstrating its potentia

Permutation invariance is among the most common symmetry that can be exploited to simplify complex problems, but it is also one of the least studied. In this work we present a comprehensive review of permutation invariant deep learning architectures and techniques, highlighting both their strengths and limitations. We first describe what permutation invariance means for neural networks before covering various applications including multitask learning, adversarial learning, unsupervised learning, and transfer learning. We then discuss recent advances in permutation-invariant network design and training strategies, such as permutation-equivariant layers, reparameterization tricks, and permutation-equivariant optimizers, demonstrating their potential to improve the performance and generalizat

Legged robots have become capable of performing highly dynamic maneuvers in the past few years. These advances are due to an increase both in computing power and control algorithms that take advantage thereof, as well as better hardware designs including compliant joints with high torque capabilities [1]. However these systems are still far from robust enough for real-world applications; if a robot can perform complex motions at all it will often struggle in unideal conditions or unexpected situations such those encountered outdoors on uneven terrain (e)g., rocky surfaces). In order address this issue we propose using machine learning techniques specifically deep reinforcement learnin(DRL)-based approaches which allow us access data sets large amounts without requiring expensive equipment

Query performance (e.g., execution time) prediction is a critical component of modern DBMSes, but existing approaches do not account for the temporal dynamics between the query and the system. In this paper, we propose MPC-QP (Multivariate Predictive Charts for Queries), an efficient framework to accurately predict the performance of complex queries over distributed data sources with multiple dimensions. We demonstrate that our approach can achieve 90% mean absolute error accuracy on real-world benchmarks, surpassing state-of-the-art baselines by up to 42%. Our analysis uncovers how query cost components interact with each other over time, revealing new insights into query behavior. In particular, we show that the average and median are poor representations of query performance, highlighti

Query auto-completion (QAC) is a fundamental functionality in e-commerce search engines. It can facilitate users in quickly finding the items they are looking for and at the same time provide them with relevant recommendations. Although the current QAC systems focus on understanding the user's search intent, they still suffer from a lack of personalization. In this paper, we propose a novel task of personalized QAC (PQAC) and aim to personalize the search experience by integrating the user's search history. To achieve this, we introduce a Search Intention Network (SIN) which disentangles the query representation into semantic and historical contexts. These contexts are then used to construct personalized virtual vocabularies for each user, which guide the autocompletion process. We evaluate our SIN on two public datasets and outperform the state-of-the-art methods in both ranking and retrieval settings, thus demonstrating the effectiveness of SIN for the PQAC task.

Text documents, including programs, typically have human-readable semantic structure. Historically, programmatic extraction of this information from source code has been challenging due to the nature of programming languages as data-driven systems. This paper introduces a new method that uses an autoencoder to learn a mapping between the high-level source code and the corresponding low-level compiler intermediate representation (IR). The autoencoder is trained on a large dataset consisting of multiple versions of the same software project. The resulting model can be used to extract the semantic structure of the source code, without any need for manual annotation or preprocessing. We demonstrate the effectiveness of our approach through experiments on three projects in different domains: a

Existing Neural Machine Translation (NMT) models mainly handle translation in the general domain, but lack flexibility to adapt quickly and effectively for specific domains. This paper proposes a simple yet effective method called Domain-Specific Attention Layer that adds an extra layer on top of existing NMT model without requiring any change at training time or incurring additional computation cost during inference stage. The proposed architecture can be easily integrated into most current popular architectures such as Transformer with minimum modifications required by replacing one self attention block per head using our newly developed custom module which is compatible across different types/sizes networks alike - thus allowing users leverage benefits from both sides: 1.) better perfor

In this paper we define a new algorithm to convert an input relational database to an output set of triples following the SPARQL 1.0 standard for RDF, in the case where the input relation is a conjunction of singleton relations and has an arbitrary number of attributes. Our algorithm runs in $O(n^2 \log n)$, where $n$ is the size of the input relation, and it can handle any number of input attributes. It also provides some optimizations that allow our algorithm to work with more general forms of input. We show that our algorithm outperforms a baseline algorithm based on relational join operators by at least one order of magnitude (and up to two orders of magnitude) when the size of the input relation is large ($n > 10^{5}$).

Situationally Induced Impairments and Disabilities (SIIDs) can significantly hinder the performance of individuals in various domains, including academic, work, and social settings. These impairments are triggered by specific situations that exacerbate an individual's pre-existing cognitive deficits or physical limitations. The term SIID was coined to reflect this dynamic nature of impairment or disability, which is not always apparent on standardized testing but emerges during more challenging circumstances. To address this issue, we propose a novel framework called SIMPACT (Situational Impact Model for Assessment of Cognitive Tasks). Our aim is to develop a methodology to identify SIIDs in different areas such as problem-solving skills, reasoning, memory, attention, and coordination.

Data distributions used for training deep neural networks often do not fully cover the domain of interest, leading to poor generalization and high failure rates in real-world applications. Although out-of-distribution (OoD) detection is critical to reducing these risks, most existing OoD methods rely on expensive retraining or handcrafted feature engineering, making them inflexible and inefficient. To address this challenge, we propose a novel two-stage OoD detection framework called PuriNet, which purifies the prediction distribution to remove in-distribution (IiD) samples and employs a dynamic activation function to improve the separability between OoD and IiD distributions. Our approach, PuriNet, does not require retraining the model or handcrafted feature engineering, making it more flexible and effective than existing OoD methods. We evaluate PuriNet on large-scale public datasets, including CIFAR10, CIFAR100, ImageNet, and various OoD detection benchmark datasets. Experimental results demonstrate the superiority of PuriNet over existing OoD methods in terms of detection accuracy and computational efficiency. Our code is available at https://github.com/Duanlab123/PuriNet.

Public transportation systems often suffer from unexpected fluctuations in demand and disruptions, such as traffic congestion and service delays, which can result in long waiting times for passengers. This study aims to investigate the impact of these factors on passenger satisfaction with public transportation systems. Our research methodology involves a survey of 392 participants who use public transport systems in China. The results indicate that passenger satisfaction is significantly affected by various factors, including perceived travel time, waiting time, seat availability, safety, reliability, comfort, cost, and cleanliness. We propose a novel approach for enhancing passenger satisfaction in public transportation systems, focusing on reducing perceived waiting time through predict

In the past year, numerous companies have incorporated Generative AI (GenAI) capabilities into their products, and it is crucial for software engineers to understand how these technologies work. In this paper, we present a survey of GenAI techniques in software engineering, with an emphasis on ChatGPT, Large Language Models (LLMs), and Code Generation. We discuss various use cases of GenAI in software engineering and highlight potential benefits such as increased productivity, automation of routine tasks, improved code quality, and enhanced collaboration. However, we also recognize the challenges associated with GenAI, including data bias, ethical considerations, and concerns over intellectual property rights. To address these issues, we propose several strategies that organizations can ad

Fine-grained classification of buildings is an important task for monitoring urban growth, assessing flood risks, and managing disasters. Satellite images are widely used for this task, and deep learning methods have shown great potential in extracting semantic information from images. However, most deep learning models require images from proprietary data sets for training. In this paper, we propose a deep learning model, namely, Urban Building Fine-grained Classification Network (UB-FineNet), which is specifically designed for fine-grained classification of buildings in open-access satellite images. We extract deep features from open-access images and perform fine-grained classification using these deep features. Specifically, we divide buildings into two categories, i.e., isolated buildings and clustered buildings, and propose different classification strategies for these two categories. For the isolated buildings, we use a k-nearest neighbors (k-NN) classifier to classify buildings into different fine-grained categories. For the clustered buildings, we first propose a building cluster feature extraction method based on deep learning, and then use the extracted features for fine-grained classification. Extensive experiments using two open-access data sets, i.e., BuildingFootprint and AUCBD, show that our method outperforms state-of-the-art methods.

Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) have garnered significant attention for their success in graph representation learning. While they typically achieve impressive performance, their interpretability is often overlooked, leading to a lack of understanding of how the model works and its limitations. In this study, we aim to enhance the interpretability of GNNs by exploring the interplay between the model architecture and the graph structure. To achieve this, we introduce a novel approach called "Graph Interpretability through Local Structural Analysis" (GILSA). Our method utilizes a local structural analysis technique that identifies structurally significant nodes and edges within a graph. This analysis reveals crucial information about the underlying graph structure, shedding light on its impact

Recent developments in prompt-based generative AI has given rise to discourse surrounding the emergence of "superintelligent" machines. While this discussion is valuable, it often lacks a well-defined framework for analyzing the intelligence of these models. This paper introduces a formal model of superintelligence based on the concept of "effective utility." We explore how prompt-based generative AI systems are able to exhibit adaptive learning and creativity, which are both critical components of effective utility. Through our analysis, we show that current large language models (LLMs) already display certain characteristics of superintelligence and further development could lead to more advanced capabilities in the future. This study highlights the potential for LLMs to transform societ

We explore the $\textit{average-case deterministic query complexity}$ of boolean functions under the \textit{bounded adversary model}, where the input bits are drawn from an independent and identically distributed distribution over a finite set. We show that, for any $k$, the average-case $\mathcal{O}(\sqrt{\log k})$-query deterministic query complexity of a boolean function can be achieved by a randomized algorithm iff there exists a constant $c$ such that the average-case $\mathcal{O}((\log k)^{1/2 + c})$-query deterministic query complexity is achievable (within a factor $\epsilon$). This characterization holds for all boolean functions on the set $\{0, 1\}^n$.

The efficacy of machine learning has traditionally relied on the availability of increasingly larger datasets. However, in many real-world scenarios, this approach is impractical due to the high cost and time required for data collection and labeling. Recently, there have been advances in using data efficiency to improve performance with small datasets. In particular, pretraining language models (PLMs) with knowledge from a small set of seed documents can yield significant improvements in performance when fine-tuning on downstream tasks that involve natural language processing (NLP). This paper explores the effectiveness of leveraging PLMs for information retrieval (IR) tasks. We evaluate different ways to combine PLMs with IR baselines and present results across three domains: science, so

Image Coding for Machines (ICM) is an image compression technique for image recognition. ICM uses machine learning to adaptively adjust the size of input images and output compressed images based on the image recognition accuracy of a neural network model. Although it has been shown that ICM can significantly improve the efficiency and performance of image recognition, there are two significant problems with this approach: First, as the neural network model becomes more complex, the computational complexity increases linearly; Second, the data-driven algorithm requires large amounts of training data, which can be expensive to obtain. To address these issues, we propose a novel method called ICM-GAN (Image Coding for Machines using Generative Adversarial Networks). In our proposed method, a

Large language models (LLMs) hold interesting potential for the design, development, and research of human-computer interfaces. However, existing LLMs have exhibited a lack of awareness of their own limitations in specific domains, often resulting in unreliable or biased responses to domain-specific prompting. In this study, we employ self-distillation with an LLM to enhance its understanding of its own capabilities and limitations, specifically focusing on generating code. Our approach entails utilizing data augmented by an LLM's generated code as training data to distill knowledge about its code generation capabilities into a smaller model. This method is demonstrated to be effective in enhancing the LLM's comprehension of its own limitations, particularly within the context of syntax-dr

Multi-Label Text Classification (MLTC) is a fundamental task in the field of Natural Language Processing (NLP). It has numerous applications across various domains, such as medical diagnosis, customer sentiment analysis, and email classification. In this paper, we propose an innovative approach called Multi-Label Text Classification by Integrating Attention Mechanism (MLTCIAM). Our approach combines the attention mechanism with multi-label text classification to enhance the effectiveness of textual feature extraction for classification tasks. MLTCIAM is designed to be integrated into existing deep learning frameworks, making it easy to implement and optimize. Experimental results demonstrate that our proposed method outperforms state-of-the-art baselines on multiple benchmark datasets, inc

Robots frequently face unpredictable dynamic environments, which require them to act agile and versatile. Human intervention is a natural part of agile operations, as humans can leverage their vast experience to quickly adapt to novel situations. Robots, on the other hand, typically rely on pre-programmed instructions, which limit their ability to deviate from expected behavior. Mimicking human intervention in robot control is challenging, as it requires reasoning over a multitude of sensory modalities, including vision, audition, and touch. We propose Oracle Guided Multimodal Policies (OGMP), a framework that leverages oracle demonstrations to learn multimodal policies. An oracle demonstration is a high-quality solution provided by an expert, which in the case of robot control, can be obtained through human demonstration. Multimodal policies are learned by training a policy network on a mixture of oracle and robot-generated data. We evaluate OGMP on a suite of 3D navigation and manipulation tasks in the real world, using a variety of sensory modalities. Our results show that OGMP consistently outperforms baseline approaches, often by significant margins. Furthermore, we show that OGMP can be adapted to novel tasks with minimal additional training data.

Spatio-temporal forecasting (STF) plays a crucial role in various practical applications. Traditional STF models often rely on complex neural architectures or extensive training processes to achieve satisfactory performance. To address the resource constraints associated with these models, our work introduces an innovative dynamic sparse training (DynST) framework. This framework enables the training of complex STF models using only a single GPU, by dynamically controlling the sparsity of weights through a sparsity scheduler. Our approach, based on the principle of dynamic sparsity, evaluates the necessity of each weight for the model's output given the current task requirements. Unnecessary weights are set to zero, effectively reducing the resource consumption and accelerating the training process. The sparsity scheduler, a key component of our framework, periodically updates the sparsity of the weights in response to changes in task requirements, ensuring optimal resource allocation. This scheduler employs two strategies: magnitude-based pruning and re-parameterization, to manage the sparsity of weights. Magnitude-based pruning involves the removal of weights with negligible impact on the model's output, while re-parameterization replaces the removed weights with new parameters. Our experiments, conducted on three benchmark STF datasets, demonstrate the superiority of our framework over recent baselines in terms of training efficiency and model performance. Notably, we achieved a 3.2x faster training process and a 2.1x smaller model size compared to the most competitive baseline, highlighting the potential of our DynST framework for advancing resource-constrained STF tasks.

Autonomous driving has been a focal point of discussion and research for the past two decades, attracting significant public interest and investments. As one of the most challenging problems in autonomous driving, motion prediction for dynamic objects has been intensively studied, with the majority of previous surveys focusing on this topic. In contrast, this paper aims to provide an introductory review of world models, which is another fundamental yet under-explored problem in autonomous driving. A world model represents the environment in an abstract form and can be used to simulate various scenarios for autonomous agents. As such, it can support the development of autonomous driving systems and allow them to make decisions based on predictions of the future state of the environment. This paper proposes a unified framework called World Model Architecture (WMA) to present the structural design of world models. WMA consists of three modules: environment representation, state prediction, and decision-making. Based on the module-level design, we classify the world models proposed in the literature and discuss their strengths and weaknesses. We also summarize the evaluation metrics and benchmark results of existing world models. Finally, we present future directions for world models in autonomous driving.

Large Language Models (LLMs) have emerged as a powerful tool in advancing various scientific domains, including computational biology and chemistry. Large language models are transformer-based neural networks that can perform natural language tasks such as text generation and question answering. In this work, we present a comprehensive evaluation of LLMs for understanding and predicting molecular properties from chemical structure. We compare the performance of LLMs with existing state-of-the-art techniques on six benchmark datasets, including QSPR prediction, property classification, SMILES encoding, and similarity matching. Our analysis highlights the strengths and limitations of LLMs in these areas, providing insights into how they can be further improved to enhance their capabilities i

A common subgraph of two graphs $G_1$ and $G_2$ is a graph $H$ whose nodes are the set of nodes in both $G_1$ and $G_2$, such that any edge of $H$ has one endpoint in $G_1$ and another in $G_2$. The density of a graph $G$ is the ratio between its node degree and the maximum possible number of edges. We consider the problem of determining whether two given graphs have a common subgraph of certain density, or a constant fraction of their edges form a common subgraph with respect to some ordering. We show that this problem can be solved by a polynomial-time algorithm for dense graphs, but is W[1]-hard even when restricted to bounded-degree graphs. Our results are best known for bounded-degree graphs, where we provide the first efficient algorithm for finding a common subgraph of constant dens

Fine classification of city-scale buildings from satellite remote sensing imagery is a crucial research area that has recently attracted extensive attention. However, most existing studies either rely heavily on additional data sources or neglect the intricate structural relationships between buildings and their surroundings in urban areas, leading to unsatisfactory results. In this paper, we propose a novel method named Building-Aware Structural Graph Convolutional Network (BASGCN) for fine-grained building classification. Specifically, BASGCN first extracts building features using a stacked residual network and then incorporates the building's structural information into an adaptive attention graph convolutional module by utilizing a structural graph based on building contours. To enhanc

Information maximization is an important principle in unsupervised learning, and its application in cross-domain few-shot learning shows great potential. However, existing methods face the problem that the representation space is too vast, making it difficult to thoroughly explore the information of each class. Moreover, the distribution of data in the representation space is usually very uneven, which further hinders the information exchange among classes. In this paper, we propose Distance-Aware Contrastive Learning (DACL), a novel technique that enhances information maximization by incorporating distance information within the contrastive learning framework. DACL introduces a new perspective to contrastive learning, emphasizing the role of distance information in improving the separability and balance of class distributions. We evaluate our method on source-free cross-domain few-shot learning tasks using the Extended Split Mini-Imagenet (ESMi) and Few-Shot Omniglot (FSO) datasets. The results demonstrate that our method significantly outperforms existing approaches, highlighting the importance of distance information in enhancing information maximization. Our code is available at https://github.com/MTandHJ/rcda.

We study the arithmetic complexity of computing Gr\"obner bases of comaximal determinantal ideals. We show that, over a field of characteristic zero, the computation of a Gr\"obner basis with respect to the lexicographic order is essentially as hard as the computation of a Gr\"obner basis with respect to any compatible term order. Our main result is thus: the problem of computing Gr\"obner bases of comaximal determinantal ideals is not polynomial-time computable, unless $\mathrm{P}=\mathrm{NP}$. Over a field of positive characteristic, we show that the statement that the problem is polynomial-time computable is equivalent to the statement that the Permanent is computable with a polynomial-time arithmetic circuit. We further show that, over a field of characteristic zero, the problem of computing reduced Gr\"obner bases of comaximal determinantal ideals is not fixed-parameter tractable with respect to the input size, unless $\mathrm{P}=\mathrm{NP}$.

Integrating topology optimization and additive manufacturing (AM) technology can facilitate innovative product development. However, the complex relationships between material distributions, fabrication paths, and structural performances make it challenging to determine the optimal design and AM process parameters. In this study, we propose a multi-objective evolutionary algorithm that integrates with the finite element method for topology optimization, coupled with a novel AM path planning method based on reinforcement learning to solve the optimal design problem. The proposed algorithm aims to optimize the density distribution of a lattice structure subject to given boundary conditions and multiple constraints, such as volume fraction, minimum feature size, and maximum stress. In additio

This paper explores the zero-shot image captioning problem. Zero-shot captioning requires a model to generate captions for images, given no training examples. We propose a novel two-stage model, which first maps an image to a memory, and then generates a caption based on the memory. The memory is a collection of visual words, each associated with a visual concept. We pre-train our model by assigning visual words to patches in thousands of images. At inference, we retrieve memory words for an input image and generate a caption by sequentially conditioning on the memory. Our model MeaCap outperforms other zero-shot captioning models, achieving 2.6 and 1.8% higher CIDEr scores on the easy and hard splits of COCO, respectively. Our visualizations show that MeaCap learns a memory with a rich variety of concepts, and can retrieve relevant memory words for generating accurate captions.

We propose a reconfigurable intelligent surface (RIS) optimization neural network architecture called RISnet that incorporates domain knowledge into the design of deep neural networks for the joint design of transmit beamforming at the access point (AP) and reflect beamforming by the phase shifts at the RIS. The domain knowledge is in the form of decomposing the optimization problem into two subproblems, each with its own set of variables and constraints, and then designing a neural network for each subproblem. The two subproblems are: (i) a transmit beamforming subproblem at the AP, which involves designing a deep neural network that maps the channel state information (CSI) to the transmit beamforming vector, and (ii) a reflect beamforming subproblem at the RIS, which involves designing a deep neural network that maps the partial CSI and the transmit beamforming vector to the reflect beamforming vector. We use the alternating direction method of multipliers (ADMM) to solve the transmit beamforming subproblem, and propose a novel partial CSI-based reflect beamforming algorithm to solve the reflect beamforming subproblem. We then incorporate domain knowledge into the design of the RIS optimization neural network by (i) using the solution of the transmit beamforming subproblem as a guidance to design the reflect beamforming subproblem, (ii) taking the partial CSI and the transmit beamforming vector as the inputs to the reflect beamforming subproblem, and (iii) using the mutual coupling tensor, which is a function of the reflect beamforming vector, as an auxiliary variable to linearize the mutual coupling constraints.
