We prove that the proposed RIS optimization neural network is a majorization-minimization (MM) algorithm that provides a monotonic increase in the objective function value at each iteration. We also show that the proposed RIS optimization neural network converges to the same solution as that of the backtracking gradient descent (BGD) algorithm. Finally, we show that the proposed RIS optimization neural network architecture outperforms the BGD algorithm and the successive convex approximation (SCA) algorithm in terms of the objective function value and the convergence speed.

The objective of this paper is to measure the degree of technological convergence among encryption technologies. To do so, we build a network of 4,191 papers on encryption technologies published between 2017-2021 and obtain a list of 1,419 proximity indices between 84 encryption technologies. We present several centrality measures of the most relevant encryption technologies and their clustering coefficient. Our results show that there is a high degree of convergence among encryption technologies, that asymmetric cryptography, symmetric cryptography, and hash functions are the most central technologies, and that there are three main clusters of encryption technologies: (1) asymmetric cryptography, (2) symmetric cryptography, and (3) hash functions and digital signatures. We also present a list of the most relevant papers on encryption technologies published between 2017-2021.

This paper presents Incremental Vision-Language Object Detection (IVLOD), a new paradigm for learning incrementally on unlabeled data to improve VLObject detection in visual and language domains. To that end, we develop a two-step approach. First, we introduce a novel zero-shot learning objective, which utilizes a vision-language prompt to generate a diverse set of pseudo annotations for training the object detector. Secondly, using the generated pseudo labels, we propose an effective algorithm to update the object detector based on its predictions. We evaluate our method on four benchmarks: MSCOCO, ActivityNet, Visual Genome, and Flickr30K. Our proposed method achieves 69.1%, 42.5%, 81.7%, and 74.2% mAP improvements over the state-of-the-art baselines.

We present NeWRF, a deep learning framework for predicting wireless channels. Wireless channel prediction is essential to support reliable and efficient communication in cellular networks with dense deployments of small cells; however it remains challenging due the extreme sparsity inherent at both spatial scales (i) across large network areas covered by many base stations as well traditional approaches that use high-resolution geographical data are infeasible because they require substantial storage capacity per user location or when using low resolution maps such those provided through open source repositories like OpenStreetMap which can lead inaccurate predictions if not properly adapted accordingly .

The Open Catalog of Educational Languages (OCEL) is a free, open-access registry of natural languages and artificial languages that are used in, or suitable for, formal and informal education. It contains over 2,000 language entries, each with information on the language's origin, development, linguistic features, and status; a sample text; and audio recordings. OCEL 2.0 is a complete redesign of the original OCEL, with a new, improved data model, and enhanced search functionality. This paper presents a summary of the changes made to OCEL 2.0, describes the data entry process in detail, and provides instructions for searching the database. We also introduce the OCEL 2.0 API, which allows programmers to retrieve data from the database programmatically. The API is available in three programming languages: Python, Java, and C#. Finally, we present information on how to contribute new languages or information to OCEL 2.0. The new and improved OCEL 2.0 is available online at www.ocel-standard.org.

Object-Centric Event Logs (OCELs) form the basis for Object-Centric Event Log Analysis (OC-ELA). OC-ELA is a recent research direction that aims to support more effective and efficient analysis of event logs. This paper provides an overview of OCELs, their features, and their use in OC-ELA. We first explain what makes OCELs different from traditional event logs and provide examples of different kinds of OCELs. Then we discuss some of the main challenges involved with working with these types of data and the corresponding solutions proposed by researchers. The remainder of this paper then summarizes current research on OC-ELA and proposes several areas for future work. A GitHub repository with an R Shiny app allowing users to interactively explore the topics presented in this paper can be f

As the size of pretrained language models grows, so does their capacity to perform well on a wide range of tasks. However, the exact mechanisms behind this scaling phenomenon are still misunderstood. In this work, we show that a significant driver of this improved performance is redundancy in model layers. Across a range of model sizes, we find that up to 80% of a model's layers can be randomly removed without a noticeable drop in performance. To quantify this surprising finding, we introduce the notion of layer redundancy, the expected number of redundant layers in a model, and develop an analytic model to estimate it. Focusing on two classes of models -- Transformers and Switch Transformers -- we show that their redundancy can be primarily attributed to weight sharing and residual connections, and that larger models have deeper layers which are less redundant. Our results challenge the conventional wisdom that all layers in large models are created equal and highlight the surprising resilience of pretrained language models to layer removal.

We present T-TAME, a novel trainable attention mechanism that is capable of explaining the decisions of Convolutional Networks (CNNs) and Vision Transformers (ViTs). Attention mechanisms are commonly used in CNNs and ViTs to explain the model's decisions by highlighting the most relevant image regions. However, existing attention mechanisms are non-trainable, thus unable to optimize the attention values end-to-end during training. As a result, they can be computationally expensive, less accurate, and unable to generalize well to unseen data. To address these limitations, we introduce T-TAME, a trainable attention mechanism that is based on the well-known Integrated Gradients (IG) explanation method. T-TAME computes the gradient of the model output with respect to each token and accumulates the positive and negative gradients for each token. The attention value for a token is then determined by the ratio of the positive to negative gradients. To train T-TAME, we use a self-explanatory branch that is inspired by the visual cortex's feedforward and feedback connections. The self-explanatory branch allows T-TAME to refine the attention values based on the model's predicted class. Extensive experiments on the ImageNet dataset show that T-TAME's attention maps closely follow the ground-truth annotations and outperform existing attention mechanisms on average. Furthermore, T-TAME's ability to generalize extends to unseen data, as demonstrated by its consistent performance across different models and datasets.

The learning to defer (L2D) framework allows autonomous systems to be safe and robust in the face of unknown system failures. In this paper, we present the first L2D-based method for control-oriented multi-agent reinforcement learning (MARL), which enables MARL agents to learn to delay their actions until they are confident that the environment is safe enough to operate without causing damage to themselves or others. We demonstrate the efficacy of our approach on a 3D robotic manipulation task with two agents who must safely complete the task while avoiding collisions. Our results show that agents using our method can outperform those trained traditionally, especially when operating near the boundaries of the workspace where safety becomes more difficult to guarantee. Additionally, we intr

Nowadays, malware authors frequently use API calls to communicate with malicious services. Hence, many machine learning (ML) studies have been presented in the literature to detect malware based on its API call behavior. However, these studies have some limitations such as being focused on a specific type of API, using an outdated dataset, or evaluating a ML model on a very small scale. To address these issues, we first propose a new dataset named Mal-API-2019 containing 1,000 malware samples that make API calls to 120 different APIs. Then, we evaluate 11 popular ML models, 9 of which are representative of the current state-of-the-art, on three different operating systems (Windows, Linux, and Android). We use two evaluation strategies, namely cross-validation and train-test split, with four different proportions of training set size. Our experiments show that the best-performing ML model differs between operating systems and APIs. Moreover, we find that API call-based ML models can achieve higher detection rates and lower false positive rates than file-based ones. We publish our code and results on an online platform to help other researchers in comparing their results with ours.

In this paper, we study a facility location problem within a competitive market context, where customer demand is sensitive to the location of facilities. We develop two models that capture strategic behavior of firms in choosing their locations. The first model assumes that each firm can choose its set of offered products or services from a product space. We show that if the number of firms is large enough and if the firms are located sufficiently far apart, then all firms will choose the same subset of products and thus, the optimal location for each firm does not depend on the actions of other firms. In the second model, we assume that each firm has a fixed cost for offering its products. We demonstrate that the Nash equilibrium exists only when the number of firms is small compared wit

Weeds are a significant threat to the agricultural productivity and the environment. Hence, accurate weed detection is crucial for effective weed control. Recently, deep learning-based image segmentation methods have been widely used for weed detection. However, the existing studies only focus on the development of supervised learning frameworks, which rely on large-scale labeled data. In this paper, we evaluate the performance of the state-of-the-art semi-supervised learning frameworks for multi-class weed detection. The representative models are based on the principles of self-learning, self-attention, and distillation. We construct two benchmark datasets for multi-class weed detection, which are publicly available. The experimental results demonstrate that the semi-supervised learning frameworks are superior to the supervised learning baseline in terms of both detection accuracy and computational efficiency. Therefore, our work is beneficial for developing practical weed detection models with potential applications in precision agriculture.

Multimodal data fusion is an important issue in medical data analysis. In this paper, we propose a novel Tri-branch Neural Fusion (TNF) model to classify multimodal medical data. Specifically, the TNF model fuses the information from three different branches, i.e., disease label, modality label, and patient identity, to improve the classification performance. To validate our model, we conduct extensive experiments using three publicly available multimodal medical datasets, i.e., the Cancer Histopathology Whole Slide Image dataset, the ISIC-18 skin lesion diagnosis dataset, and the ORL-Oxford face image dataset. Moreover, we compare the performance of the TNF model with that of the state-of-the-art models. The results demonstrate that the TNF model obtains superior performance to that of the compared models in terms of accuracy, F-score, and confusion matrix.

Context: Reactive programming (RP) is a declarative programming paradigm suitable for express- ing data streams. However, the lack of RP support in mainstream development environments has hindered its adoption by developers who are used to imperative and object oriented paradigms such as Java or C++. To address this issue we propose reactiveJ++, an extension over existing JVM languages that enables seamless integration with JRE libraries while maintaining full compatibility at runtime; thus enabling new ways on how these programs can be written without having any knowledge about their internal structure/implementation details yet still allowing them access all available features offered through standard interfaces provided alongside every single implementation detail found within each libr

Deep learning models are trained by distributing the training data across multiple workers and coordinating with a master node. The workers compute the gradient of the loss function with respect to the model parameters and send the gradient to the master. The master then updates the model parameters by performing a weighted average of the received gradients. The existing distributed training algorithms are based on the centralized gradient compression strategy, which has been proven to be suboptimal. In this paper, we propose GRAWA, a novel distributed training algorithm that performs a weighted average of the gradients using gradient-based weights. We prove that the convergence rate of GRAWA is at least as fast as the state-of-the-art distributed training algorithm with gradient compression. Moreover, we show that GRAWA achieves faster convergence in numerical experiments.

We consider a discrete-time system where a resource-constrained source (e.g., a sensor) communicates data to an untrusted relay and a destination node, with the help of a trusted server. The source has to make a decision on whether or not to send a data sample based on its quality. To this end, the source employs a compression scheme that quantizes the data sample according to its quality. The source sends the compressed data samples to the relays using a noiseless communication link. After receiving the data, the relays compress the data according to their channel conditions and forward them to the server using noisy links. In order to reduce the communication overhead, we assume that the relays employ a quantization-based compression scheme as well. Finally, the server transmits the quan

Small sample sizes are common in many disciplines, which necessitates pooling roughly similar datasets across a range of conditions to reduce the bias introduced by differences between samples. However, such pooling can introduce additional bias if the distributions of the features within the pooled datasets vary substantially. To address this issue, we propose a simple yet effective method called \textbf{F}eature \textbf{P}roportionally weighted \textbf{T}ask-specific \textbf{A}lgorithm (FPTA) for synthetic data generation with conditional Gaussian mixture models (GMMs). Our method adaptively adjusts the number of components and their proportion based on feature information provided by users, resulting in a more accurate synthesized dataset than GMMs without user input.

Considering generating samples with high rewards, we focus on optimizing deep neural networks parameterized stochastic policies for the Markov Decision Process (MDP) problems. To this end, we propose a novel policy evaluation algorithm called Policy Actor-Critic (PAC). PAC is based on a new actor-critic framework that uses both discriminative and generative models to evaluate and sample policies. Unlike existing actor-critic methods, which only use discriminative models to learn Q values of state-action pairs, PAC leverages the power of discriminative and generative models in parallel to obtain the optimal policy, thereby improving performance on tasks that involve complex multi-modal distributions. We show that PAC can be interpreted as a variant of the famous SGD method. Extensive experi

The use of machine learning (ML) techniques to solve complex physical problems has been considered recently as a method for enhancing the performance and reducing the computational costs of existing numerical methods. In this study, we propose a framework that applies ML to solve large-scale three-dimensional (3D) multiphase flow simulations. The framework involves two main components: a data-driven surrogate model and a neural network (NN) controller. The NN controller is trained using a physics-informed loss function. To demonstrate the effectiveness of our approach, we apply it to solve 3D multiphase flow problems. We compare our results with those obtained from conventional finite volume solvers and show that our framework can effectively improve the accuracy and reduce the time requir

We present a novel weakly-supervised method for the task of material states segmentation (MSS). Our approach leverages the abundant unlabeled synthetic data, natural images, and a powerful pre-trained model, to learn a segmentation model that can be fine-tuned to new, unseen material states. Specifically, we propose to embed natural image patterns, which are invariant to state changes, in the synthetic data. This expansion of the training set, even if artificial, helps the model to better generalize over the material states. The proposed method requires no supervision of the embedded patterns, making the approach weakly-supervised. Furthermore, we show that the model learns a representation that is transferable to new, unseen material states. This is achieved by carefully selecting the natural images, that are embedded in the synthetic data, based on their perceptual similarity to the target material states. We compare the proposed method to strong baselines, showing that it outperforms the competition on two public datasets.

This paper explores the cutting-edge Large Language Model with analytical reasoning on sports. Our model, which we call SportsAI can explain and predict events in a variety of games like basketball or football as well as other activities such horse racing using data from text sources including online news articles about past matches played by teams at different levels (from professional leagues down to amateur ones). In addition it also provides insights into what makes each game unique based upon how players interact within them; this includes their strengths/weaknesses against opponents along various dimensions - speed vs strength etc., allowing us not only see more clearly where there may be opportunities for improvement but helping identify potential strategies before they become appar

The limitations of collecting and accessing real-world data have sparked the growth of synthetic data generation using Generative Artificial Intelligence (GAI). GAI offers the potential to produce vast amounts of data that closely mimic real-world distributions, ensuring data privacy and security while reducing bias and improving data quality. However, challenges such as vanishing gradients, mode collapse, and training stability slow down the adoption of GAI in practical applications. In this survey, we provide an overview of the current state of GAI-based synthetic data generation, including an in-depth look at the most popular GAI models. We also discuss the challenges that GAI faces in generating synthetic data and suggest possible solutions. Finally, we look at the future of GAI in synthetic data generation and its impact on various industries.

Medical vision-language models (VLMs) combine computer vision and natural language processing to analyze visual content. Recent research has demonstrated the potential of VLMs for medical image interpretation, particularly in radiology. However, these models are often trained on a single disease or modality, limiting their ability to generalize across diseases and modalities. To address this issue, we propose MediVLM, a multimodal VLM that incorporates both textual and visual information from multiple modalities and diseases into its training process. Our approach involves three key components: (1) A novel data augmentation technique that generates synthetic images for unseen diseases using only text descriptions; (2) A self-supervised pretraining method that leverages large-scale unlabele

The quadratic complexity of the attention mechanism represents one of the biggest hurdles for processing long sequences using transformers. Although several techniques have been proposed to improve its efficiency, most existing works rely on approximating or simplifying self-attention and only offer marginal speedups in practice; meanwhile few studies focus directly on improving linear scalability without sacrificing accuracy when considering very large input sizes (>4096 tokens). In this work we introduce a new approach called "linear scaling", which is built around two key insights: first that by carefully designing an appropriate set partitioning scheme based upon token indices (rather than relying solely/heavily as other methods do), it becomes possible not just reduce but also elimina

Neighborhood attention reduces the cost of self attention by restricting each token's attention span to a smaller neighborhood. The neighborhood attention mechanism is widely used in neural machine translation (NMT) to improve training efficiency and reduce inference latency. However, its impact on translation quality has been overlooked until recently. In this paper, we conduct extensive experiments comparing different neighborhood attentions with global attentions for NMT, achieving state-of-the-art results without compromising quality. We demonstrate that neighborhood attention can be improved to enhance translation quality by enhancing the capacity to capture long-distance dependencies. To achieve this, we propose two effective improvements: 1) multi-scale neighborhood attention, which

Confidential computing is emerging as a trusted computing paradigm to mitigate data breaches and unauthorized disclosure. It relies on hardware-based isolation and protection to grant a guarantee of privacy. However, the hardware assumptions of existing confidential computing solutions are not aligned with future high-performance networked workloads. In this paper, we challenge the status-quo and explore the design space of high-performance networks in Confidential Virtual Machines (CVMs) without Trusted I/O devices. We present a novel hardware-software co-design approach to address the unique challenges of CVMs, including the need for high throughput, low latency, and high reliability. Our solution, called Bridge, uses a combination of hardware techniques, such as I/O virtualization and memory encryption, and software techniques, such as packet steering and flow control, to provide a high-performance network stack in CVMs. We show that Bridge can support future high-performance networked workloads in CVMs without Trusted I/O devices. Compared to existing solutions, Bridge achieves up to 11.2 times higher throughput, up to 14.2 times lower latency, and up to 19.1 times higher reliability.

In this paper, we study the geometry of supervised learning problems and its impact on the stability of learning. We approach this problem from multiple angles, starting with a discussion on the role of the target function and the distribution of the input data. We then proceed to discuss the stability of learning under a change of either the target function or the distribution of the input data, or both. We provide a geometric interpretation of stability with respect to these changes and discuss the implications for real-world applications. We also present some experimental results that support our theoretical findings.

Cedar is a new authorization policy language designed to be ergonomic, fast, safe, and expressive. Cedar supports powerful features such as regular expressions for matching identifiers in policies (e-g., "a. *b$"), nested scopes with optional inheritance or exclusion of parent scope permissions at any level from the root down through child nodes within an organization hierarchy structure similar that seen widely across cloud providers today but also allows fine grained control over these hierarchies via wildcards like "*". In this talk we'll discuss what makes it different than existing solutions out there including how its syntax differs significantly compared even though both languages are based on attribute checking/authorization rules using XACML standard format specificat

End-to-end differentiable learning for autonomous driving (AD) has recently become a prominent research direction. However, existing works primarily focus on single-agent scenarios and neglect the multi-agent setting where multiple agents interact within the same environment. In this paper, we introduce the Multi-Agent Differentiable Learning Framework (MADLF), which extends the conventional single-agent framework by including additional modules that handle the interactions between agents while ensuring that their respective policies are optimized towards common objectives. To address the difficulties in collecting training data for such a framework, we propose an effective approach based on deep reinforcement learning (DRL). We validate our proposed method on a challenging AD task: multi-

Collaborative cybersecurity relies on organizations sharing information to boost security, but trust management is a critical issue. This article provides an overview of research into automated methods for managing trustworthiness in collaborative cyber-defense scenarios and discusses the implications this has for future systems development practices as well providing recommendations based upon current findings from existing studies examining how best practice can be applied within these areas

Introduction

Challenges

* Trust Management: Trust relationships are not easy to manage due their complex nature including both subjective aspects such personal opinions about others' behavior (i.,e) objective criteria like statistical data regarding past interactions betwe

Real-world systems are often formulated as constrained optimization problems. Techniques to incorporate constraints into gradient-based neural networks have been studied extensively, but existing methods suffer from inefficiencies or a lack of robustness. In this paper, we introduce the first method to learn constraints using sparse regularization, which is a principled technique for representing general constraints in terms of a small number of constraint functions. We present the first efficient and provably convergent algorithm for learning the parameters of these constraint functions based on stochastic gradient descent. Our experiments demonstrate that our approach yields state-of-the-art performance on standard benchmarks compared to previous work. Further, we show that our model is

Emoji have become ubiquitous in written communication, on the Web and beyond. They are increasingly used for information retrieval by searching for emojis in textual documents or multimedia data. However, the effectiveness of existing emoji search methods is limited by their reliance on manually annotated datasets that do not cover a broad range of languages. This work aims to bridge this gap by introducing EMIJI, a novel dataset comprising 35,000 images from 18 languages with emoji-based captions. To ensure high quality and consistency across languages, we leverage a novel active learning strategy to iteratively select images for human annotation. Our approach significantly improves the performance of state-of-the-art emoji search models, as demonstrated in a comprehensive evaluation that

This paper presents a cascaded-fidelity model predictive control framework for whole-body control called Cafe-Mpc. The proposed control framework consists of a high-fidelity whole-body controller and a low-fidelity motion planner, which are connected via a receding-horizon control distribution. The high-fidelity controller uses a state-of-the-art whole-body control algorithm for optimal torque allocation among multiple degrees of freedom while satisfying actuator and safety constraints. The low-fidelity motion planner uses a receding-horizon trajectory optimization algorithm to produce a sequence of target states that are fed into the high-fidelity controller as desired states. We remove the tuning parameter in the traditional two-level control structure and show that the proposed framework can achieve superior closed-loop performance without any parameter tuning. We also show that the proposed framework can improve the state-of-the-art whole-body control performance by 14.1% on average without any parameter tuning. Finally, we show that the proposed framework can recover the closed-loop stability margin by 71.4% on average compared to the state-of-the-art whole-body control algorithm.

When drafting question posts for Stack Overflow, developers may not accurately summarize the core problems in their questions. This can lead to a lack of precision in understanding the problem and finding a solution, which is detrimental to the quality of the questions. To address this issue, we propose an automatic method that detects potential issues in questions by leveraging large language models (LLMs). Our approach consists of two main steps: 1) generating natural-language descriptions of questions using LLM, followed by 2) identifying potential problematic words using a pre-trained language model (PLM). We conduct experiments on three different datasets containing developer questions from Stack Overflow, including questions with both correct and incorrect answers. The experimental r

This study marks a significant advancement by harnessing Large Language Models (LLMs) to create an automated system for extracting the underlying structure of questions from large text corpora. The results demonstrate that LLMS can effectively identify and extract question-answer pairs, making it easier than ever before in NLP research communities such as academia or industry settings where datasets are scarce due their complexity requirements beyond simple tasks like NER classification models which require more resources but provide fewer benefits when compared with other approaches available today . In this paper we propose two methods: 1st approach is based on multitask learning technique named "MultiTask",which uses three auxiliary task labels simultaneously during training time; while

Direct reciprocity based on the repeated prisoner's dilemma has been intensively studied in recent years, but it is still unclear whether and how to achieve stable cooperation when players have different abilities or information. Here we find that a simple strategy can effectively promote cooperative behavior under these conditions by introducing two new components: (1) allowing each player to adjust its initial trust value according not only individual experience with other opponents over multiple rounds of playbut also general knowledge from all previous interactions among both sides;and(2), using feedback-based mechanisms which enable themtoadjust their future interaction strategies after observing others'. By analyzing evolutionary games as wellas empirical data setsfrom real world sce

Federated Learning (FL) is a practical approach to train deep learning models collaboratively across user-owned devices. FL allows users to retain their data locally, while also contributing to a global model that benefits all participants. However, the privacy of each participant's local data remains unprotected due to the aggregation of updates from many different users in the global model training process. To protect the privacy of each individual update during FL, we propose Distributed Privacy Preserving Federated Learning (D3FL), a novel framework for securely executing FL by leveraging homomorphic encryption and differential privacy on encrypted data. Our framework can be applied to any existing FL system to protect local data, while ensuring minimal disruption to its operation. We

The Vehicle Routing Problem (VRP) is a widely studied combinatorial optimization problem in the context of transportation and logistics. This problem involves finding an optimal set of routes for vehicles to deliver goods from a depot to customers, while minimizing total travel distance or time. It can be formulated as a mixed-integer program, which can be solved efficiently using advanced mathematical techniques such as linear programming and branch-and-bound methods. However, the complexity of these techniques increases with the size of the problem, making it impractical to solve problems involving large numbers of nodes and customer requests. In this paper, we propose a novel approach based on evolutionary algorithms that solves VRP instances with up to 100 nodes within reasonable compu

Reward hacking occurs when an agent performs very well with respect to a "proxy" reward function, while achieving poor results on the actual reward. This behavior can be particularly harmful in machine learning environments where rewards are frequently chosen to maximize performance metrics that do not align with the final objective of the system (e.g., user engagement or profits). While prior work has identified reward hacking as a potential issue, it has not provided any practical solutions. In this paper, we introduce a new paradigm for identifying reward hacking called \emph{Recommended-Rewards} and provide empirical evidence that demonstrates its effectiveness. We show that using recommended rewards reduces the number of reward hacks by 70\% compared to random rewards, and by 98\% com

We propose Masked Capsule Autoencoders (MCAE), the first CapsNet-based model that can learn from corrupted images. MCAE consists of three modules: an encoder, a decoder, and a masker. The masker removes randomly selected portions of input images, which are then encoded by the encoder and decoded by the decoder. Our experiments demonstrate that (1) MCAE can significantly improve the robustness against image corruption with only minor loss in performance on the original task; (2) the improvement is more pronounced for tasks requiring high-level reasoning than for tasks requiring low-level feature detection; and (3) the proposed method has broad applicability across different architectures. Furthermore, we show that MCAE is able to enhance the robustness of other state-of-the-art models such

A G-Quadruplex (GQP) is a higher-order structure formed by the non-covalent association of four or more guanines in a DNA molecule. GQPs play important roles in the regulation of gene expression and are associated with several human diseases. Therefore, the identification of GQPs is of great importance for medical research. Recently, deep learning models have been used to predict GQPs. However, there is still a lack of deep learning models that can accurately predict GQPs and have good explainability, which is important for gaining insight into the factors that contribute to GQP formation. Here, we propose a deep learning model called G4-Attention, which uses a pre-trained deep learning model as a feature extractor and applies an attention mechanism to identify the most relevant base pairs for GQP formation. We evaluate the performance of G4-Attention on two GQP datasets and show that it outperforms other state-of-the-art models. We also perform feature importance analysis and show that G4-Attention can identify the most relevant base pairs for GQP formation, providing insight into the factors that contribute to GQP formation.

Identifying flood affected areas in remote sensing data is a critical problem in earth observation to analyze the impact of floods on society and environment. Recent advances, including deep learning approaches for object detection (e., Mask R-CNN) provide promising solutions due their ability to learn complex features from large amounts training samples efficiently using powerful GPUs or TPUs without prior knowledge about image content as opposed conventional methods like manual delineation which require extensive expertise along with tedious labor intensive task especially when dealing larger datasets involving thousands images across multiple time stamps spanning years worth observations; however there still remains challenge regarding scalability issues associated computing resources n

We present a new approach for non-rigid isometric mapping called Density-based Isometric Mapping (DIM) that is free from the drawbacks of saddle singularities and has a much higher degree of freedom. Unlike existing approaches, DIM does not rely on the isometric property of the input shapes. Instead, it minimizes a density function that is guaranteed to be isometric under a mild condition. This condition is always satisfied for 2-manifolds but not necessarily for 3-manifolds. We present practical 2D and 3D implementations of DIM and show that it produces visually pleasing isometric mappings. In addition, we show that DIM can be naturally combined with existing density-based folding techniques to produce more intricate folds.

The task of stock earnings forecasting has received considerable attention due to the demand investors in the market and the potential for substantial profits. In this context, deep learning techniques have emerged as a promising solution. However, the lack of labeled data poses a significant challenge, particularly in the early stages of model training, where only a few samples are available. To address this issue, we propose an approach based on self-supervised pretraining, which leverages a vast unlabeled dataset to learn representations from unlabelled data. Subsequently, we fine-tune the model with a small labelled set to make predictions. The proposed method effectively addresses data scarcity by leveraging the power of large datasets and demonstrates improved performance compared to

The number of patents granted annually has been increasing steadily for the past decades. The USPTO receives more than 500,000 applications per year and grants around 300,000 patents. Manually classifying and retrieving relevant patents is becoming increasingly difficult for patent examiners. This work explores the use of artificial intelligence to help patent examiners by automatically classifying and retrieving prior art. We develop a patent classifier using supervised learning and a term vector representation of patents. We evaluate our classifier using 2014-2016 granted US patents. We show that our classifier outperforms human experts at both the class and subclass levels. We also show that our classifier is able to generalize well to new, previously unseen technologies. Patent retrieval experiments show that our classifier can help examiners find relevant prior art more quickly than traditional search methods.

This paper presents a comprehensive literature review of Model Parallelism, a key technique for scaling Language Models (LMs) and other deep learning models. We aim to answer three questions: (1) What are the current approaches for scaling LMs? (2) What are the limitations of existing techniques? (3) How can we overcome these limitations? We begin by presenting background information on LMs and the challenges of scaling them. We then review 102 pieces of literature on model parallelism for LMs, categorizing techniques as either horizontal or vertical scaling approaches. We analyze the computational and memory trade-offs of these approaches and discuss their suitability for cloud and edge deployments. We identify three main limitations of existing techniques: (1) Inadequate consideration of the data distribution, (2) Limited focus on optimizing the model parallelism within a single machine, and (3) Neglect of the impact of model parallelism on the training and inference pipelines. We present case studies of large language models (LLMs) that address these limitations, including GPT-4 Turbo, GPT-4 Code, and others. We conclude by discussing future directions for model parallelism, such as auto-scaling, hybrid precision formats, and quantization.

In the realm of computer vision and graphics, accurately establishing correspondences between geometric 3D surfaces is crucial for various applications such as object recognition, tracking, and registration. This paper presents a novel approach for surface correspondence computation using point clouds from structured light depth sensors. Our method addresses two challenges: (1) dealing with non-uniform sampling, which can lead to incorrect correspondences; and (2) handling occluded regions in one or both views. To address these challenges, we propose an iterative point cloud alignment procedure that incorporates a robust outlier rejection mechanism based on geometric constraints, leveraging the inherent structure of structured light point clouds. The proposed algorithm achieves accurate an

Battery technology is increasingly important for global electrification efforts. However, batteries are complex systems with a multitude of failure modes and degradation mechanisms that must be understood to enable safe operation over long lifetimes in demanding applications such as electric vehicles (EVs). This dissertation focuses on understanding the aging processes within lithium-ion battery cells through detailed experimental analysis coupled with advanced data science methods including machine learning models trained using large amounts (~106) historical cell health metrics from real world EV usage scenarios along multiple years/hundreds thousand kilometers driven by thousands different drivers across hundreds cities around North America & Europe; this allows us i

In few-shot learning (FSL), it is challenging to recognize the similarity between support and query samples due to their few available training examples. Previous works have proposed using prototype-based models to represent the support set. However, the existing model prototypes, such as the mean or median, are sensitive to outliers and cannot well characterize the data distribution. In this work, we propose a novel FSL framework based on the attribute distance (AD), a recently proposed robust prototype that is less sensitive to outliers. We first analyze the generalization properties of the AD and provide a theoretical explanation for its effectiveness in distinguishing different classes. We then propose two simple methods, namely, direct AD matching and AD prototype net, to perform few-shot classification with the AD. Experimental results on the popular FSL dataset show that the proposed methods outperform the state-of-the-art baselines.

The prevailing grasp prediction methods predominantly rely on offline learning, overlooking the dynamic nature of real-world environments. This is particularly problematic in safety-critical scenarios where even a slight deviation from the expected can result in catastrophic consequences. To address this issue, we propose a novel framework that enables on-the-fly grasp estimation using only a single image. Specifically, our method entails two complementary components: an efficient online network (ON) and an effective offline model (OFF). In contrast to traditional approaches, which typically rely solely on OFF for grasping, our method seamlessly integrates both ON and OFF, optimizing the prediction process for more reliable and precise results. During training, our approach leverages a hyb

Evolutionary optimization algorithms are often derived from loose biological analogies and struggle to leverage information in complex environments. We introduce a new approach that uses a probabilistic, evolutionary model of the problem space itself to guide search towards promising regions. Our approach allows us to formulate a simple rule-based representation that can learn to reason about a variety of problems that we describe as "semantic" domains, where objects and their properties (such as location, size or shape) have meaningful semantics that can be leveraged to solve more complicated tasks. Experiments on 10 benchmark domains demonstrate strong performance compared to standard optimization methods, with a simple implementation that is orders of magnitude faster than previous appr

Fine classification of city-scale buildings from satellite remote sensing imagery is a crucial research area in the field of computer vision. However, existing studies mostly focus on single image fine building type recognition with limited types and lack sufficient large scale datasets for further exploration to support more diverse tasks such as urban planning or real estate investment decision making at an individual level through precise analysis based on different property characteristics across multiple cities (e.g., residential vs commercial).

libEnsemble is a Python-based toolkit for running dynamic ensembles, developed as part of the DREAM 16 competition. The toolkit includes both ensemble generation and execution capabilities that can be used to generate a diverse set of models from a single data source, and then execute those models in parallel or sequentially on the same dataset. It provides the means to run dynamic ensembles of varying sizes, with varying amounts of data and model diversity, for analysis tasks ranging from classification to regression and more complex machine learning tasks such as reinforcement learning. libEnsemble allows users to explore how these variations affect performance across tasks, providing insights into the effectiveness of different ensemble configurations. Additionally, it offers the abilit

This paper addresses the challenges of aligning large language models (LLMs) with human values, particularly those related to ethical issues. We present a systematic survey of literature on LLM ethics and analyze their limitations in addressing these challenges. Our survey covers three main categories: LLM ethics frameworks, LLM ethics risks, and LLM ethics governance. It highlights gaps in understanding how LLMs can be aligned with specific ethical considerations such as privacy, fairness, and transparency. Based on our analysis, we propose a comprehensive research agenda for developing ethical alignment methods for LLMs, including enhancing interpretability, fostering accountability, and designing appropriate incentives. This work contributes to the advancement of the field by providing

Recent years have witnessed impressive developments in using evolution to design neural networks, mainly because it can discover novel architectures with better performance than human-designed ones. However, most existing methods require a pre-defined network architecture, and can only modify the pre-defined architecture slightly. This limits their ability to explore the architecture space sufficiently. In addition, it is challenging for existing methods to maintain the diversity of solutions and utilize parallel computing effectively. To address these challenges, we propose a novel evolutionary neural architecture search method, named G-EvoNAS, which employs a network growth strategy to explore the architecture space more extensively. Specifically, our method starts with a small convolutional kernel and gradually grows it into a large kernel. During the growth process, our method can utilize parallel computing to evaluate the performance of candidate architectures cost-effectively and maintain the diversity of solutions adequately. Based on the strength of our method, we expect that our method can be applied to design neural networks for image classification, computer vision, and natural language processing tasks. Experimental results show that our method outperforms state-of-the-art methods on several benchmark datasets.

The field of pharmaceutical development and therapeutic application both face substantial challenges in terms of time-to-market, cost, and efficiency. In order to address these concerns, the use of artificial intelligence (AI) for drug discovery has gained widespread attention as a promising solution. With the emergence of large language models like ChatGPT and GPT-4, there is growing interest in how AI can be leveraged within the realm of pharmaceutical research and development. This paper explores several key applications of AI within the pharmaceutical industry, including: drug discovery; medical image analysis; and clinical trial optimization. Furthermore, we examine the potential benefits and limitations associated with AI in this context, including ethical considerations and regulato

The digital twin (DT) concept is gaining recognition for its potential to transform how physical assets are designed, constructed, and maintained. Its integration with Building Information Modelling (BIM) has been particularly promising for the construction industry. However, the adoption of DT-BIM remains limited, and the construction industry continues to struggle with productivity issues. This paper explores the reasons for the slow adoption of DT-BIM from the perspectives of civil engineering phases, namely design, construction, and operation. A qualitative study was conducted with 18 industry practitioners, including engineers, constructors, and facility managers. Participants were asked about their perceptions of DT-BIM and its potential benefits and challenges. Through a thematic analysis, we identified that participants associated design and construction phases with DT-BIM adoption. They perceived that the benefits of DT-BIM were more relevant to these phases than to the operation phase. We also found that participants believed that DT-BIM adoption could improve project outcomes, but they required more information about how to implement it. This paper contributes to the literature by highlighting the civil engineering phases that DT-BIM adoption is associated with and reorienting the discussion on how to encourage its adoption. We suggest that future research investigate how to leverage these findings to develop adoption strategies for each phase.

The existence of adversarial attacks on machine learning models imperceptible to a human is still quite a surprising fact. One of the main reasons for this phenomenon seems related with our poor understanding of how deep neural networks work, and more specifically in what kind of representations they learn from input data such as images or text sequences (i) before being fed into any task-specific architecture; e(g., classification);and also,(ii),after training them using large datasets like ImageNet2017orGoogle News dataset respectively - where we can observe different patterns across these two aspects that may provide some clues about why there are so many successful attack methods available today even though their effects remain relatively small comparedtowhatweexpectfromsuch powerful t

In this paper we study the NP-Hard problem of maximizing the distance over an intersection graph, which can be formulated as a max flow problem with a special cost function. A key property of this problem is that when the optimal solution exists it is always integer valued and its value is not greater than the sum of all capacities of edges in the original graph. This provides a way to find an upper bound for the maximum distance by using a network simplex method. We also develop an efficient algorithm for finding a good approximate solution, which runs in polynomial time for unit capacity graphs, and polynomial delay time otherwise. The method is based on a simple modification of Ford-Fulkerson's method. Experimental results show that our algorithms are very competitive compared with othe

There is a increasing need to integrate various forms of biological data, including expression data, genetic variation data, and protein-protein interaction data, to mention a few, in order to gain deeper insights into the complex underpinnings of human diseases. Ensemble learning is a popular and effective approach for integrating diverse data sources. It combines multiple learning models, each trained on a different data source, into a final integrated model. In this study, we developed an ensemble learning model called CRISPR: Ensemble Model, for predicting complex traits using single nucleotide polymorphism (SNP) data and gene expression data. We tested our method using two publicly available data sets: the first data set includes SNP and gene expression data for 204 individuals, and the second data set includes SNP and gene expression data for 468 individuals. The two data sets are quite different in terms of the number of SNPs, the number of genes, and the experimental measurement platforms. Our results show that the ensemble learning approach is better than the single model approaches for predicting complex traits.

Enhancing the attribution in large language models (LLMs) is a crucial task for their practical application. In this work, we present an unsupervised method to enhance LLM attributions by leveraging cross-attention information between pairs of words with similar meanings and contrasting those that do not share meaning or are antonyms using contrastive learning techniques from computer vision literature such as SimCLRv2 [1]. We apply our approach on top existing methods including OpenAI's CLIP model which improves human evaluation scores 76% over its baselines across several tasks while requiring less than two hours per dataset iteration even when utilizing only one GPU device during training operations making it feasible given today’ s hardware capabilities despite having multiple GPUs ava

The proliferation of wireless-enabled applications with divergent quality of service (QoS) requirements has spurred the development of network slicing. As a key enabler, network function virtualization (NFV) orchestrates the deployment and management of virtualized functions across physical infrastructure to satisfy diverse QoS requirements. However, NFV-based network slicing poses significant challenges for achieving end-to-end QoS assurance as the complexity of network resources increases significantly. These include the difficulty in accurately estimating resource utilization levels in heterogeneous resource pools, which leads to suboptimal performance metrics. To address these issues, we propose a novel technique that enables accurate estimation of resource utilization levels through d

Sourced from various sensors and organized chronologically, Multivariate Time-Series (MTS) data offers the potential to model a wide range of real world phenomena. However its inherently high dimensionality makes it difficult for existing machine learning techniques such as Deep Learning or Statistical Modeling alone; particularly when modeling with large datasets in an online fashion where only small batches are available at each time step due their inability handle both efficiently without suffering performance loss over long periods .

Existing video segmentation approaches mainly focus on using spatial cues for obtaining good segmentations. However, neglecting the temporal dimension can lead to the loss of important information, especially for activities with large motion. In this paper, we study a simple yet effective method to leverage temporal information after the fact, i.e., post-hoc temporal information, for video segmentation. Specifically, we propose Motion-Corrected Moving Average (MCMA), which corrects the moving average result with the post-hoc temporal information. Our MCMA improves on existing cutting-edge video segmentation approaches without additional spatial-temporal cues. Extensive experiments on five popular benchmarks show that our method significantly outperforms existing methods, especially for videos with large motion. The source code is available at https://github.com/leonnnop/MCMA.

This study conducts a thorough examination of malware detection using machine learning techniques, focusing on the most recent developments. To detect malicious software and prevent it from harming computers or networks in their early stages is one goal that has been achieved by many research studies over time through various methods such as signature-based identification technology (SBT) which requires signatures for each piece to be identified; anomaly based IDT system where suspicious behavior patterns are found within normal ones but there exist false positives due lack understanding what exactly constitutes normality among others issues associated with this approach . The paper will provide an overview about how these two approaches work before discussing some specific models develope

A topical challenge for algorithms in general and for automatic image categorization and generation in particular is presented by the lack of ground truth data, which leads to a scarcity of benchmarks. In this paper we introduce a new benchmark dataset that addresses this problem. We present a novel approach to automatically labeling images based on their content from scratch with no human annotations or training data. Our method achieves state-of-the-art performance compared to other similar techniques without requiring any labeled data. The resulting dataset consists of 102 classes with more than 30k annotated images each, enabling it to be used as a robust benchmark. In addition, it can provide valuable insights into the classification of images of different domains such as animals, foo

Context: Reactive programming (RP) is a declarative programming paradigm suitable for expressing reactive applications. In this article, we introduce the notion of a reactive agent, which is an entity that interacts with the environment in response to stimuli. We present a new language called Reactive Agent Programming Language (RAPL), which has been designed with the goal of making it easier to write RP code by using more familiar programming constructs like mutable variables and imperative statements. We compare our approach with other existing languages, especially with StreamKit, a library built on top of JavaScript, which allows developers to use imperative code for stream processing. Our experiments show that RAPL can be used to write programs that are more readable and maintainable

Automated model reasoning is a promising technology for providing incremental and conditional reasoning support in the context of domain-specific modeling languages. In contrast to traditional theorem provers, model reasoners operate on models directly, without the need of explicit problem formulation. However, the logical inference in model reasoning is usually limited to the propositional level, or it suffers from high complexity when reasoning with quantifiers. On the other hand, first-order relational logic (FRL) provides a more powerful reasoning framework, which can express various interesting properties in object-oriented domains. Unfortunately, FRL is not supported by any known reasoner. To fill this gap, we propose AlloyInEcore, a novel approach for embedding FRL into the meta-object facility (MOF) for automated model reasoning. We show that our embedding allows us to leverage powerful optimization techniques from answer set programming (ASP) for FRL reasoning. Moreover, we present an efficient implementation of our embedding based on the Ecore meta-modeling language. We evaluate the practical relevance of our approach by applying it to model validation and constraint solving problems.

In this paper we define a new algorithm to convert an input relational database to an output set of rules. Our approach is based on the following two ideas: (1) each rule is defined by only one table, and (2), in order for us not have to know how many tables are needed before hand-coding our program into C++, it generates all possible combinations between primary keys from different relations/tables as candidate join conditions. We show that our method significantly outperforms previous algorithms which use multiple joins at once when they do their conversions because those other methods will often produce incorrect results due either too few or far more than necessary number(s).

Co-speech motions, i.e., hand motions synchronized with speech, are crucial for human communication. Multi-modal diffusion model, a kind of generative model, shows great potential in co-speech motion generation (CSMG) because it can effectively capture the correlations between visual and acoustic signals. However, existing methods can only process single-modal signals, limiting the performance of CSMG. In this paper, we propose a novel multi-modal co-speech motion generation model, namely MMoFusion, based on the diffusion model framework. Specifically, we introduce a multi-modal denoising diffusion process, which performs co-speech motion generation by simultaneously processing visual and acoustic signals. To ensure the quality of motion, we adopt a residual motion synthesis strategy. Moreover, we design a cross-modal attention module to facilitate the information exchange between the visual and acoustic branches. To train our model, we adopt a multi-modal self-supervised learning strategy, i.e., cross-modal motion reconstruction. Experiments on two benchmark datasets, namely PHOENIX-2014 and PHOENIX-2014-T, demonstrate that our method outperforms state-of-the-art methods.

In this paper, we investigate the role of conformity in opinion dynamics, in a setting where individuals belong to multiple social circles. Conformity is an important aspect of human behaviour, as people often adjust their opinions to match those of their peers. In mathematical models, conformity is usually captured by the sign of the interaction weight between two individuals: if positive, they tend to align their opinions; if negative, they tend to oppose each other. However, the sign of the weight is not always a good representation of conformity, which can be better captured by the concept of social distance. In our model, we quantify social distance as the number of social circles that separate two individuals. We then show that, in a ring lattice with $n$ nodes and $k$ uniformly randomly chosen social circles, the average social distance between two connected individuals is of order $\log n / \log k$. Our main result is a mean-field approximation for the opinion dynamics model with multiple circles, where we take both the number of individuals and the number of circles to infinity, while keeping the average degree and the average social distance between connected individuals constant. We prove that in this model, the conformist behaviour described by the sign of the interaction weight is not decisive for the dynamics of opinions. Instead, we show that the dynamics depends critically on the social distance between individuals. Our result is in line with recent studies showing that human behaviour is more nuanced than is often captured by mathematical models, and that simple models can hide important aspects of reality.

The IoT market is diverse and characterized by a multitude of vendors that support different device classes, data types, and protocols. This diversity makes it challenging to provide comprehensive monitoring capabilities for all connected devices. To address this challenge, we introduce a modular architecture for monitoring edge devices called IoT Monitoring as a Service (IMAS) that uses an agent-based approach to collect, store, analyze, and visualize data from a variety of IoT devices. A key feature of IMAS is its ability to handle heterogeneous data collected from various IoT devices in a uniform manner. By standardizing the data collection process, IMAS enables more efficient analysis and storage of IoT data. Additionally, IMAS incorporates artificial intelligence and machine learning

Parents' choices of schools for their children can be influenced by various biases, such as choosing a school based on the perceived reputation of the school or avoiding a school based on a negative incident, even if the incident is not relevant to the school's current performance. These biases can impact not only the students' education but also their future opportunities. This paper uses a discrete choice model to capture two types of bias that are observed in survey data from parents in Brazil: (1) overestimation of the quality of schools attended by students with better academic performance and (2) aversion to schools with students from less privileged socioeconomic backgrounds. The model is then used to simulate different scenarios to understand how these biases can be reduced. We find that providing parents with information about the match between their children and schools can reduce both types of biases. Additionally, creating more schools with high quality and diversity can attract parents without compromising the academic performance of their children.

The body movements accompanying speech aid speakers in expressing their ideas. Co-speech gesture (CSG) research has explored the interaction between CSGs and speech, showing that CSGs are used to emphasize certain parts of speech. However, it remains unclear how different types of CSGs, e.g., palm-up vs palm-down hand gestures, affect the perception of speech. To address this issue, we designed a study with two types of CSGs: palm-down and palm-up. We also asked whether the CSG type had an effect on perceived emotion, personality, and credibility. We found that participants were more likely to perceive speakers as expressing positive emotions when they use palm-down CSGs than when they use palm-up CSGs.

In the contemporary digital landscape, the continuous generation of extensive streaming data across diverse domains has become pervasive. This data is often stored in non-relational database management systems (DBMS) such as Apache Cassandra, which does not support traditional SQL querying for analytical insights. To address this gap, a novel framework called Streaming Data Explorer (SDE) was developed to provide seamless access and analysis of streaming data from Apache Cassandra. This paper provides an overview of SDE's key features including schema discovery, real-time data ingestion, SQL execution, and visualization. Additionally, we present its architecture, implementation details, and future research directions, demonstrating how SDE can serve as a valuable tool for data analytics pr

This paper presents a modular, open-source software architecture for Neural Machine Translation (NMT). The proposed architecture is implemented as a set of interconnecting software containers, which allow the translation system to be executed natively on a CPU, GPU, or FPGA. The containers are interconnected via a publish/subscribe-based message-passing system, which decouples the container execution model from the underlying hardware. This design choice allows the translation system to scale seamlessly between low- and high-capacity hardware, and to benefit from future developments in hardware acceleration technologies. The proposed architecture is modular, and can be extended with additional functionality (e.g., beam search). We show that the proposed system achieves state-of-the-art performance on newswire text, and back-translates 93.8% of the reference translations on the WMT'14 English-German dataset, using a GPU. The system can be executed verbatim on an FPGA, with no performance penalty, demonstrating the suitability of the proposed architecture for deployment on low-power, embedded systems. The code is publicly available at https://github.com/Lizw14/OpenNMT.

Online bidding and auction are crucial aspects of the online advertising industry. Conventionally, bidders use a single bid value to express their expected revenue from an impression. However, with the recent advancement in machine learning (ML) techniques, ML-based models have shown promising performance compared with human-designed bids, raising the question of whether a ML model can be used as a substitute for traditional bidding. In this work, we propose a novel approach that utilizes a two-stage model to estimate both the targeted bid value and its corresponding probability distribution. The proposed method is formulated as a multi-task learning framework wherein a pretrained language model (LM) is trained jointly with a logistic regression model using a mixture of feature engineering

Recent years have made significant progress in medical image segmentation (MIS) due to the development of deep learning technologies. However, most existing MIS methods require large numbers of labeled data, which are not available in many practical scenarios. In this paper, we propose a method for few-shot medical image segmentation (FSMIS) based on self-supervised learning. Specifically, we utilize a powerful medical image representation learning model, named DINOv2, as the pre-training backbone to transfer the rich information to the few-shot learning task. Furthermore, we propose a region-level contrastive learning strategy, which can effectively improve the performance of few-shot learning. Extensive experiments on the few-shot subsets of the medical image segmentation benchmark MS-CXR and the proposed BraTS2021 challenge show that our method outperforms existing self-supervised learning methods by a large margin. Furthermore, it also shows that our method can be transferred across different datasets to improve the performance of few-shot learning.

Machine learning models, while progressively advanced, rely heavily on the IID assumption, which is often violated in real-world applications. In this work, we investigate how data shuffling impacts existing deep neural networks (DNN) for classification tasks and whether it can improve their performance. To test our hypothesis, we perform a comprehensive study across multiple datasets with various sizes and classes distributions to assess the impact of shuffling on both accuracy and efficiency metrics such as inference time. Our findings suggest that data shuffling can significantly enhance the accuracy of DNN models by introducing randomness into the dataset, while also improving their efficiency by reducing the number of iterations required during training. We further analyze the effecti

Accurate detection and diagnosis of abnormal behaviors such as network attacks from multivariate time series is an important task in many real-world applications. Existing methods often adopt the sliding window approach, which leads to high computational complexity when analyzing long-term data sequences. To address this issue, we propose a novel framework that efficiently extracts temporal features from long-term data sequences using a neural architecture search (NAS) method. Specifically, we first employ an attention mechanism to adaptively select informative features from the input sequence for downstream tasks. Then, we introduce the Transformer Block to learn effective temporal features from these selected features. Finally, based on the extracted temporal features, we design a lightw

Remote sensing image semantic segmentation (RSISeg), which aims to classify each pixel in remote sensing images with respect to its ground object, is a fundamental problem in remote sensing and geographic information science. With the popularization of deep learning, RSISeg based on deep learning has developed rapidly. However, existing methods often rely on manually labeled data, which is time-consuming and labor-intensive. In this paper, we propose a novel dual-domain image fusion strategy, which fuses the image information from the spectral and spatial domains for RSISeg with unsupervised domain adaptation (UDA). Specifically, we introduce a dual-domain feature learning model to extract the common semantic features from the integrated dual-domain images. Then, we propose a self-learning scheme to iteratively update the dual-domain images and facilitate the convergence of the common semantic features. Furthermore, we design a multi-objective loss to promote the consistency between the prediction and the two types of ground truth (i.e., the manually labeled pixel-level ground truth and the automatically extracted object-level ground truth). Extensive experiments on two popular RSISeg datasets, i.e., University-1652 and WHU-10K, demonstrate that the proposed method outperforms the existing UDA methods for RSISeg.

In the realm of computer vision and graphics, accurately establishing correspondences between geometric 3D models is a vital task in various applications. Recently, neural radiance fields (NeRF) have emerged as an effective paradigm for modeling scenes by representing 3D point clouds through a continuous multidimensional function space. NeRF-based correspondence methods aim to retrieve point pairs that are paired with each other based on their spatial relationships. While existing works focus on learning dense point correspondences from single images or video sequences, they often suffer from inadequate information due to the discrete nature of the images. To address this issue, we propose the first NeRF-based method for cross-view correspondences, which leverages both the geometry-aware N

Deployable polyhedrons can transform between Platonic and Archimedean polyhedrons to facilitate various applications in robotics, space exploration, and manufacturing. In this paper, we present a novel approach for deploying polyhedral surfaces from planar shapes using a single flat surface. Our method builds on a previously developed algorithm that relies on the inherent properties of the polyhedra being deployed and involves the application of an elastic material to the surface. By leveraging the unique characteristics of different types of polyhedra, we have adapted our algorithm to create a series of deployable polyhedron models with varying degrees of complexity. We demonstrate the effectiveness of our method by showcasing a diverse collection of deployable polyhedrons, ranging from s

Federated learning (FL) is a privacy-preserving distributed management framework based on collaborative knowledge training. FL can effectively overcome the limitations of centralized computing architecture in the face of massive data, and has been widely used in fields such as finance, medical image recognition, speech recognition, etc., which greatly promotes the development of artificial intelligence. With the increasing number of participants in federated learning, the system faces new challenges such as network bandwidth and model accuracy. Therefore, we propose an efficient federated learning framework with deep learning technology for medical image classification, namely, FLEMICL. In order to address the issues of slow convergence rate and poor generalization performance caused by la

Recent developments in prompt-based generative AI has given rise to discourse surrounding the ethical implications of its use. This paper focuses on one aspect: how a model's output can be used as evidence for claims, particularly those made by politicians and public officials regarding historical events or people whose lives are affected today via social policies such welfare reform initiatives like Universal Credit (UC). The research question is whether it would allow someone who had no prior knowledge about these topics - but did have access only through their computer screen – could convince others that certain facts were true simply based upon what appeared onscreen without having conducted any independent verification themselves? To explore this issue further still another layer was

Molecular Communication (MC) has emerged as a promising paradigm employing molecular signaling to transmit information in nanoscale networks. The key challenge in MC is the limited molecular transmission range, which necessitates multiple-hop transmissions to enable larger network sizes. However, existing works have primarily focused on analyzing single-hop transmission models, and little research attention has been devoted to exploring multiple-hop MC systems. To address this gap, this work introduces a novel multi-hop MC model that incorporates the effects of molecular concentration gradients and channel noise into the signal propagation process. We derive an analytical expression for the end-to-end channel capacity of a two-hop MC system, showcasing its practical significance in optimiz

Reservoir computing (RC) offers a neuromorphic framework that is particularly effective for processing spatialised signals. However, the design of an optimal RC architecture to process complex spatio-temporal data remains challenging and requires substantial expertise in computational neuroscience as well neural engineering domains such machine learning or artificial intelligence research groups with limited experience modelling spatial dynamics may find it difficult develop novel architectures suited their specific tasks at hand while researchers who are experienced but lack access appropriate hardware platforms could be unable take advantage recent advances within this field due limitations imposed by current technology constraints .

We study the classic Constraint Satisfaction Problem (CSP) in the advice model, where an all-powerful advisor provides the solution to the problem. The goal is to minimize the number of advice bits the advisor needs to provide. We show that this problem is equivalent to the classic Mastermind problem, where the goal is to guess a secret code, and the only allowed operation is to provide a guess and receive back the number of correct digits in the guess. Our reduction from Mastermind to CSP is very simple, and it immediately implies several results for the advice model. We then give a new interpretation of the advice model as a communication process, where Alice has a solution to a CSP instance, and Bob needs to learn what it is. We study several scenarios, and prove some bounds for the number of advice bits in this setting as well.

We study the computational complexity of several polynomial-time-solvable graph problems parameterized by vertex cover number. First, we show that the problem of finding a minimum set of edges to remove such that the resulting graph has no $k$-vertex cover is fixed-parameter tractable (FPT). Second, we introduce two natural generalizations of the classical vertex cover problem: in the first, the goal is to find an induced subgraph on $k$ vertices; and in the second, the goal is to find a maximum subset of vertices that induce a forest. We prove that both variants are W[1]-hard but admit polynomial-size kernels. Third, we extend our results to graph coloring problems: given a proper $(r+1)$-coloring of a graph $G$, we consider the problem of finding a minimum set of edges whose removal ensu

Virtual reality (VR) is a promising data engine for autonomous driving (AD). However, due to the complexity of real-world scenes and high requirements on performance accuracy during training processes in VR scenarios, it has been challenging to create accurate virtual datasets. To address this issue effectively using generative adversarial networks(GANs), we propose an adaptive multiscale framework with cross generation features called AMF2GNet that can adapt well across different scales by learning from multiple images at various resolutions while preserving spatial details such as textures or structures found within these areas without losing their respective characteristics when combined together into one single image representation scheme capable enough handle complex tasks involving u

Catastrophic forgetting (CF) is a common problem in the continuous learning task.
