The key idea of the existing effort to solve CF is to preserve the gradient correlation (GC) of the parameters for the old tasks. However, the GC of the parameters for the new task will be damaged if the model is not parameterized properly. To address this issue, we propose a novel Gradient Correlation Subspace (GCS) learning method to learn the proper parameterization for the new task. Specifically, we decompose the parameter update into the GC subspace update and the orthogonal subspace update. We show that the GC subspace update has the function of preserving the GC of the parameters for the old tasks, and the orthogonal subspace update has the function of reducing the interference from the old tasks. To prove it, we analyze the update rule of the GCS method under the linear network approximation and show that the GCS method can approximate the ideal forgetting curve with a straight line. Moreover, we show that the learning rate of the GCS method is not sensitive to the task order. Based on the above analysis, we provide two perspectives to understand the GCS method. One is that the GCS method can be viewed as the gradient descent algorithm on the parameterized space. The other is that the GCS method can be viewed as the learning method on the feature space. The GCS method has the function of preserving the feature correlation of the parameters for the old tasks. We conduct extensive experiments on the typical continuous learning tasks with a deep neural network and show that the GCS method performs well against CF.

Menzerath's Law is a linguistic universal according to which the complexity of a linguistic unit decreases with the length of the unit. This law was formalized by Altmann (1979) for Germanic and Romance languages. We extend the formalization of this law to the case of syntactic structures in Ukrainian. In particular, we consider non-projective dependency trees and parse trees of different grammars for Ukrainian: a) a hand-crafted grammar, b) a treebank-based grammar, and c) a neural network-based grammar. The empirical analysis is conducted on the basis of 1,000,000 tokens of the Kyiv-Moscow corpus of modern Ukrainian. We have found that Menzerath-Altmann Law holds for syntactic structures of Ukrainian, with some grammatical peculiarities. Namely, the law is observed for the non-projective dependency trees of adnominal clauses, but not observed for the parse trees of the aforementioned grammars. We discuss these results from the perspective of syntactic complexity.

AI approaches are progressing besting humans at game-related tasks (e.g. chess). However, the performance of these approaches is not robust across different datasets and environments. This issue is exacerbated in adversarial settings where AI agents can be deceived by adversaries. In this paper we introduce a novel adversary for the StarCraft II domain called "The Trickster". Our approach allows us to study how AIs perform when they have to deal with adversarial strategies on a StarCraft II environment. We show that our agent, which employs simple trickery techniques such as cloaking units or manipulating vision, is able to outperform state-of-the-art GPT-4 models in adversarial scenarios. Additionally, we provide an analysis of the limitations of AI in adversarial settings, identifying po

Generative Artificial Intelligence (GAI) shows remarkable productivity and creativity in Mobile App Development, outperforming human developers. However, the effectiveness of GAI in improving app quality remains a significant concern. To address this issue, we propose a novel approach for enhancing app quality through GAI's generation of customized testing data. The proposed framework comprises three key components: Testing Data Generation with GPT-4, Quality Improvement via Automated App Testing, and Feedback Guidance for Human Editing. We demonstrate the effectiveness of our method on several real-world apps across different domains using various testing approaches. In addition, our approach significantly improves the efficiency of human editors during the app development process. Our st

Fine-grained visual recognition has attracted much attention in recent years. The discriminability between similar categories is weak, which makes it difficult to learn a discriminative representation space. Most existing methods use the class-agnostic backbone followed by a discriminative head for discrimination learning. However, we find that the feature distribution of different categories is actually quite different, which can provide additional discriminative information. In this paper, we propose a novel LoDisc model to exploit the global-local feature distribution information to learn discriminative features for fine-grained visual recognition. Specifically, a global feature extractor is employed to capture the overall discriminative information for class discrimination, while a local feature extractor is utilized to learn the subtle discriminative details for fine-grained separation. To improve the model's generalization ability, we adopt a self-supervised learning strategy to learn global-local discriminative features without the category label. Furthermore, a feature distillation strategy is employed to guide the feature learning direction and improve the model's discriminability. Extensive experiments on two widely-used fine-grained visual recognition datasets show that our method achieves a competitive performance. The code will be released at https://github.com/littleprince1121/LoDisc.

Land cover classification and change detection are two important applications of remote sensing and Earth observation (EO) data. This study presents a novel framework for improving the performance of land cover classification models by integrating change detection information into the training process, leveraging a newly developed 2D-3D CNN (Convolutional Neural Network) architecture that incorporates both spectral and spatial characteristics. We introduce the concept of "change-aware learning", which emphasizes the importance of capturing the change in spectral characteristics over time to improve classification accuracy. Our approach is validated using high resolution global dataset from Sentinel-2 satellite imagery from 1972 to 2021, with 24 classes of land cover. The results demonstrat

Pointer arithmetic is widely used in low-level programs, e.g. memory allocators. These programs are often written in high-level languages such as C or Rust and compiled to assembly code. However, it can be difficult for the compiler to optimize such programs due to implicit pointer arithmetic. We show that it is possible to significantly improve compilers by adding a specialized frontend for explicit pointer arithmetic (EPA). EPA can transform complex and error-prone pointer arithmetic expressions into simpler ones, making them easier for optimizers to analyze and handle. For example, it can replace an expression like `x + y` with one like `2*(y - x)`. Our evaluation shows that EPA can make a significant difference: on average, our optimizations reduce the number of instructions in 1000 li

Collaborative competitions have gained popularity in the scientific and technological fields. These competitions are designed to bring together multiple research groups or organizations with complementary expertise, allowing them effectively solve complex challenges that cannot be solved by a single team alone (e.,g.,"grand challenge" of DARPA). Despite their widespread use across various disciplines such as engineering ("DARPANet"), medical sciences("H20-COVID19") , social science(e."ARCTIC Challenge", "Global Pandemic Modeling Initiative), collaborative competition has been understudied from both theoretical perspectives on fairness issues within this setting . This study provides an overview into how competitive environments can affect individuals' behavior when they collaborate towards

Sequential recommendation aims to estimate the dynamic user preferences and sequential dependencies among historical user behavior. The success of large language models (LLMs) in various NLP tasks has inspired researchers to incorporate LLM-based methods into sequential recommendation systems for their superior performance. However, most existing studies neglect the impact of LLM's model size on sequential recommendation. In this paper, we provide a comprehensive analysis of the influence of LLM's model size on sequential recommendation, particularly in the context of recommender systems. Our findings reveal that smaller LLMs often perform better than larger ones. Additionally, we introduce two novel metrics, namely Recall@n and Average Top-$k$ Score@n, which are tailored specifically for

Recommender systems (RS) are one of the most extensively researched and widely deployed AI applications today. However, the majority of research has focused on evaluating the efficacy of RS in terms of the recommendations they produce. This paper aims to broaden the focus of RS research by investigating how the role an AI plays in the decision-making process influences the eventual decision. In doing so, this work hopes to shed light on the black box of AI-assisted decision making and provide insights into how AI systems can be designed to better support human decision making. We present a taxonomy of AI roles in decision making and categorize existing RS research from a historical perspective. We then report a qualitative study of 11 experts in the fields of AI and psychology, followed by a think-aloud study with 18 participants who made decisions in the role of a buyer, seller, and neutral third party. Our findings reveal that participants preferred certain AI roles over others and that these preferences were influenced by the participants' locus of control, their need for closure, and the level of detail the AI provided. We discuss the implications of our findings for designers of AI systems and suggest directions for future research.

Social recommendation systems face the problem of social influence bias, which can lead to an overemphasis on recommending items that friends have interacted with. Addressing this problem is crucial, and existing methods often rely on techniques such as weight adjustment or leveraging unbiased data to eliminate this bias. However, we argue that not all biases are detrimental, i.e., some items recommended by friends may align with the user's interests. Blindly eliminating such biases could undermine these positive effects, potentially diminishing recommendation accuracy. In this paper, we propose a Causal Disentanglement-based framework for Regulating Social influence Bias in social recommendation, named CDRSB, to improve recommendation performance. From the perspective of causal inference,

Existing causal discovery methods based on combinatorial optimization or search are slow, prohibiting their application to real-world datasets. In this paper, we propose a novel causal discovery algorithm that relies solely on a fixed set of candidate models for the underlying causal graph. Our method is a model selection approach: given a candidate set and an observation set, it selects the most likely model from the candidate set that fits the data well. The core idea is to use a score function to estimate the likelihood of each model under the observed data. By introducing a new graphical model called the {\em causal-Bayesian network} (CBN) to capture both causality and uncertainty, our method can be used without additional assumptions about the data.

In this work, we present GenML, a Python library to generate the Mittag-Leffler correlated noise which is a generalization of the Gaussian correlated noise and the $\alpha$-stable correlated noise. The library is based on a new approach to compute the Mittag-Leffler function with arbitrary precision, using a differential equation. The GenML library can generate Mittag-Leffler noise with various parameters, and it can be used in many applications, such as image processing and fractional calculus. We also present the results of some experiments on image processing, comparing the GenML library with other existing libraries.

The advent of the post-human era has blurred the boundary between the body and artifacts. This work presents an investigation into the potential for a new class of objects known as "Body-Artifact." In this study, we examine the characteristics of Body-Artifacts and their role in enhancing human capabilities and experiences. We propose a design methodology that incorporates the integration of human physiological data and digital fabrication techniques to create these innovative devices. Our approach utilizes machine learning algorithms to identify patterns in human physiological data, which are then used to generate customized designs for each user. This paper provides a comprehensive overview of our research efforts, including the development of a computational framework and its applicatio

Ship detection needs to identify ship locations from remote sensing (RS) scenes. However, due to the complex and changeable environment, ship detection is easily affected by the large number of similar objects in RS scenes. Besides, there are few labels on RS scenes. To address these problems, we propose a novel method for ship detection, named VSR-CNN. In our model, we first extract ship features from visible spectrum images by a convolutional neural network, then use a multi-scale transformer to enhance the semantic information of extracted features. The feature fusion module utilizes a channel attention mechanism and a spatial attention mechanism to further fuse features, thus improving feature extraction. Finally, the ship detection task is performed based on the refined feature map. E

Dense retrieval methods have demonstrated promising performance in multilingual information retrieval, where queries and documents are represented as dense vectors. However existing approaches often assume a single language model for both query-document matching (crosslingual) or crossdomain similarity estimation (\emph{e.\ g.} across news articles to Wikipedia). In this work we propose CrossMoD: \textbf{\underline C}ross\textsubscript{-}$\mathbf{~}$LMs with Dense Retrieval $\text {for Multi-$}\sigma$ Information Density Estimation using Transformer Language Models -- an approach that uses different models depending on the type of text being processed ($t$: document/query), domain ($\delta$) ,and target application $(\theta)$. We show how multiple such domains can be efficiently combined i

Training Large Language Models (LLMs) presents significant memory challenges, predominantly due to the need to store large amounts of data. Large LLMs often exhibit long-term forgetting of previously learned information over time as they are exposed to a continually changing set of input data. This paper explores methods for efficiently storing and retrieving knowledge from LLMs using an offline memory architecture with low memory overhead. We evaluate the effectiveness of two approaches: (i) using an associative memory model where we store all past examples in the memory at once and (ii) using a hybrid approach that combines our proposed method with a GPT-4 model. The results show that while our proposed method is effective at reducing memory overhead, it suffers from limited scalability.

Classification of EEG signals is important for diagnosis in clinical applications. It is also useful for understanding the brain's cognitive functions. Many researchers have developed various methods for classifying EEG signals. However, there is still a need for methods that are accurate, robust, and can handle large datasets. Convolutional neural networks (CNNs) are currently among the most popular methods for classifying EEG signals. The spatial-temporal characteristics of EEG data are suitable for CNNs. Typically, CNNs are applied directly to one-dimensional (1D) EEG signals. However, applying CNNs directly to 1D signals may result in the loss of some spatial-temporal information. Therefore, in this paper, we propose a method for classifying EEG signals by first converting them into two-dimensional (2D) images that preserve spatial-temporal information and then applying CNNs. Specifically, we design appropriate topological maps to represent EEG signals as 2D images. The topological maps are generated by using a sphere or a torus because these shapes can preserve the spatial-temporal characteristics of EEG data obtained from the scalp surface. We then perform spatiotemporal pooling on the resulting 2D images to extract important features. Finally, we apply a CNN to classify the resulting features. Our method outperforms state-of-the-art methods in terms of classification accuracy. It also achieves a high area under the receiver operating characteristic curve (AUC) of more than 0.99 for the Wisconsin Sleep EEG dataset. The AUC is a commonly used metric for evaluating the performance of sleep stage classification methods. Therefore, the proposed method is suitable for clinical applications.

Audio-visual person verification (AVPV) aims to verify the identity of a person based on audio and visual information. Previous works mainly adopt the multi-modal fusion strategy of adding vectorized audio features and visual features together. However, the fusion of vectorized features ignores the structural information within features, and the information loss results in sub-optimal verification performance. In this work, we study a novel dynamic cross attention network (DCAN) to perform cross-modal feature alignment and extraction of cross-modal features simultaneously. The proposed DCAN learns the dynamic cross attention weights by comparing the cross-modal local features and global features. The dynamic cross attention weights are then used to guide the subsequent cross-modal feature alignment and extraction. Extensive experiments on audio-visual datasets show that the proposed DCAN achieves state-of-the-art verification performance.

The transferability of deep neural networks (DNNs) has made significant progress in image and language modeling. However, their ability to deal with time series data is limited, especially for long sequences. This paper presents a new framework that leverages LLMs for temporal prediction tasks. Our main contribution lies in two aspects: firstly, we propose an effective approach that combines LLMs and transformers to enhance their capabilities in processing dynamic data. Specifically, we introduce a novel method called T-Transformer, which integrates the capabilities of LLMs and transformers by embedding each sample into a latent space. Secondly, we present a novel training strategy to further improve the performance of our proposed framework. Through extensive experiments on diverse benchm

Knowledge-grounded dialogue systems aim to generate coherent and engaging responses based on the dialogue contexts and selected external knowledge. Previous knowledge selection methods tend to rely too heavily on the dialogue contexts or over-emphasize the new information in the selected knowledge, resulting in the selection of repetitious or incongruous knowledge and further generating repetitive or incoherent responses, as the generation of the response depends on the chosen knowledge. To address these shortcomings, we introduce a Coherent and Engaging Topic Transition (CET2) framework to model topic transitions for selecting knowledge that is coherent to the context of the conversations while providing adequate knowledge diversity for topic development. Our CET2 framework considers mult

This paper considers a movable antenna (MA)-aided secure multiple-input multiple-output (MIMO) communication system. The MA is capable of moving along the user's trajectory in the time-varying channel, thereby providing an additional degree of freedom for improving the MIMO security performance and minimizing the power consumption. To capture this benefit, we first propose a new optimization problem to determine the MA location that minimizes the secrecy outage probability (SOP). Based on this optimization problem, we then develop two novel receiver processing schemes: the first is an optimal receiver scheme; the second is a practical receiver scheme based on sequential hypothesis testing (SHT). We show that both of these schemes achieve the minimum SOP at the cost of some tradeoff between

Actionable warning identification is a sub-task of disaster information extraction. It aims to identify warnings in disaster information that specify actions to be taken by individuals to mitigate the impact of the disaster. In this paper, we propose a pre-trained model-based actionable warning identification system and evaluate its feasibility. We extract features from disaster information using pre-trained models, including pre-trained language models (e.g., BART), pre-trained information extraction models (e.g., BIE), and pre-trained question-answering models (e.g., BQA). We then fine-tune a logistic regression model on top of these features. In addition, we propose a pre-trained model-based evaluation approach to evaluate the feasibility of pre-trained model-based actionable warning identification. We apply the approach to a collection of 4,101 disaster tweets annotated with actionable warnings manually. Experimental results suggest that our pre-trained model-based actionable warning identification system is feasible. It outperforms the state-of-the-art disaster information extraction system on actionable warning identification by 1.7% in terms of F-score.

The use of generative models for recommendation has been an increasingly popular research topic over the past years. The second workshop on Recommendation with Generative Models (RecGen 2022) brought together researchers and practitioners interested in this topic for a virtual two-day event. In addition to 17 outstanding research papers, the workshop featured an introductory tutorial on variational autoencoders for recommendation and a panel discussion on the topic of trustworthiness in recommendation with generative models.

The rapidly increasing capabilities of large language models (LLMs) raise an urgent need to align these powerful systems with human preferences. Existing methods, however, often fail to effectively incorporate fine-grained human feedback and lead to the emergence of new biases in LLM outputs. To address this problem, we propose a novel method called Actor-Critic Training for Aligning Large Language Models with Human Preferences (ACT), which leverages actor-critic reinforcement learning techniques. In our approach, we view model alignment as a goal-seeking process where the objective is to maximize the probability of generating outputs that are aligned with human preferences. Our method is designed to iteratively adjust the model's behavior based on actor-critic optimization, ensuring that

Recently, anomaly detection on few-shot data has attracted growing research interest. As anomalies often exhibit novel or rare frequency distributions, frequency discriminators (FDs) have been proposed to detect anomalies based on the principle of maximum likelihood. However, existing FDs usually suffer from two drawbacks. (1) The estimation of background distribution is inaccurate due to the limitation of sample size. (2) The impact of each sample on the estimation of background distribution is uniform, which may lead to overfitting on few-shot data. To address these problems, we propose dual-path frequency discriminators (DPFDs) to leverage the differences of sample impact on the estimation of background distribution. Specifically, we introduce a dual-path mechanism to FDs, which can not only capture the overall distribution information like the original FDs but also model the local distribution information. Furthermore, we propose a sample weighting strategy to DPFDs to highlight the impact of normal samples on the estimation of background distribution. To show the effectiveness of our method, we conduct extensive experiments on few-shot anomaly detection tasks with four public datasets. The results demonstrate that DPFDs achieve better performance than the state-of-the-art methods.

Accurate training labels are a key component for multi-class medical image segmentation. Their annotation is often resource-intensive and time-consuming, resulting in only limited data availability. Labeling can be improved by leveraging auxiliary information that is readily available during the training process. In this work, we propose a novel framework called \emph{Auxiliary Label Augmentation} (ALA). It consists of two steps: 1) training an auxiliary classifier on the labeled images, which provides additional information about the label space; and 2) using the generated auxiliary labels to augment the training set. Our methodology is evaluated across a diverse range of medical datasets including CT colonography, MR brain tumor, breast MRI, liver US, and chest X-rays. Experiments show t

The 5G core network (5GC) aims to provide flexible, customizable, and intent-based network slicing to fulfill the requirements of various new services. An intent-based network management system can interpret the service intent and convert it into network configurations automatically, saving operators' efforts and reducing service provision time. Large language models (LLMs) show great potential in natural language processing and can be fine-tuned to extract service intent from user requests. In this paper, we investigate the feasibility of using LLMs for intent extraction in the 5GC. We propose a pipeline that leverages existing 5GC data and open-source LLMs to extract intents automatically. We evaluate our pipeline on two publicly available datasets and show that it can achieve a 98% F1 score on intent classification. We also demonstrate that our pipeline can adapt to new datasets with minimal fine-tuning. Our work is a step towards intent-based network management in the 5GC.

Runtime Verification (RV) refers to a family of techniques in which system executions are observed and verified against expected properties. We present the first compilation-based RV framework for concurrent programs, in which we synthesize RV code from a specification by combining a type checker with an RV compiler. Our framework is based on the notion of RV annotations, which encode expected behaviour, and can be used to generate correctness proofs or verify program executions. We demonstrate our framework for verifying several types of concurrency errors using both a standard programming language and a domain-specific language. By providing a combination of type checking and RV synthesis, our framework allows us to leverage type systems to automate RV. We also compare the cost of our me

Background: Bronchoscopy is a life-saving procedure for diagnosing and treating diseases in the airways. Visually guided bronchoscopy (VGB) using endoscopic cameras is less invasive and more accurate than fluoroscopy. However, it is time-consuming and prone to errors due to the complexity of the procedure and the lack of a suitable navigation system. Therefore, we propose a real-time depth-based dual-loop framework for VGB, which aims to guide the physician to the target lesion while avoiding obstacles. Methods: A depth image-based registration method is proposed to track the motion of the bronchoscope tip with respect to the lung lesion. A novel dual-loop path planning algorithm is also proposed to generate collision-free trajectories in the bronchial tree. In vitro experiments are conducted on a human-sized lung phantom to evaluate the tracking accuracy and the computational complexity of the proposed method. Results: The experimental results show that the proposed framework can track the bronchoscope motion with a mean accuracy of 2.9 mm at a frame rate of 6fps on a standard CPU. The computational complexity analysis shows that our method can run in real-time on a standard GPU. Conclusions: The proposed framework is a good assistant for physicians during VGB procedures. It can provide real-time visual feedback and improve the accuracy and safety of the procedure.

With growing popularity, deep learning (DL) models are becoming larger-scale, and only the top few examples in the training data can be used to train these large DL models. However, due to the presence of outliers, the quality of the model trained on a small number of samples is not guaranteed to be good enough for practical applications. To address this problem, we propose the Sparse Gradient Refinement (SGR) algorithm, which refines the gradient information of a DL model using only the top few samples. The proposed method leverages an ensemble of linear regression models with different sparsity levels as approximators for the original loss function, thus effectively reducing the complexity of the original loss function. This approach enables us to construct more accurate loss functions a

Big models have achieved revolutionary breakthroughs in the field of AI, but they might also be a sign that we are approaching an inflection point. It is clear that large language and vision transformer (LLT) architectures can produce impressive results on many tasks — from generating text to producing images or even videos based off prompts alone; however as these systems continue scaling up their size it becomes more expensive both financially due costs associated with training them along side human labor required for curation/annotation processes which will only grow larger over time unless alternative solutions exist outside traditional approaches taken by deep learning researchers today who rely heavily upon GPUs & CPUs alike . To address this issue some new innovations such federated

In this article, we study the relationship between notions of depth for sequences, namely, Bennett's notion of conditional depth and the more recently introduced notion of bounded depth. We prove that both of these notions are equivalent in the context of finite-state machine (FSM) automata and regular languages over a two-letter alphabet. Our proof is based on an adaptation of the so-called "two-part" method proposed by Hemaspaandra and Shankar [10] to show the equivalence of the two notions of depth for FSM automata and regular languages over a three-letter alphabet. The main technical challenge is to adapt the techniques used in [10] to handle the case where the language under consideration has a two-letter alphabet instead of a three-letter one.

Large Language Models (LLMs) such as GPT-4 have recently demonstrated impressive results across a wide range of tasks. However, it has been shown that they are vulnerable to adversarial attacks in the preference optimization setting, where an adversary provides a set of preferences and seeks to elicit a desired response from the LLM. To align LLMs with human values, it is crucial to robustly align the LLM with these values, even in the presence of adversarial attacks. To this end, we introduce CURATRON, a comprehensive and robust preference dataset, and a new robust alignment method. The dataset consists of 7,000 preferences collected from 177 humans, designed to be diverse and representative of human values. The preferences in CURATRON are complex, comprising 128 unique concepts, 384 unique words, and 177 unique syntactic structures. Our robust alignment method is based on constrained optimization and can incorporate prior knowledge, such as alignment constraints and preference relations. Experimental results demonstrate that our method produces aligned LLM outputs across various preference optimization attacks, indicating its robustness and reliability.

Federated learning (FL) aims to collaboratively train a global model across multiple participants without sharing local data. The convergence of FL algorithms has been studied extensively under the assumption of data similarity across participants, typically measured by the Earth Mover's Distance (EMD). This paper focuses on the convergence of FL algorithms beyond the data similarity regime. We introduce a novel FL algorithm, called momentum-based FL (MFL), and establish its convergence guarantee under diverse client distributions, using a modified EMD called batch EMD (bEMD). We also introduce a new parameter-free variant of MFL, called self-adjusting MFL (SMFL), which automatically adjusts the momentum based on the problem dimension. SMFL exhibits superior performance compared to existing FL algorithms in non-i.i.d. settings. The convergence guarantees of both MFL and SMFL are supported by novel technical tools, including a new tail inequality for bEMD and a refined analysis of the excess risk. These tools are of independent interest.

Methods for medical image registration infer geometric transformations that align pairs/groups of images by maximising an objective function. This paper presents a new framework, called registration-based learning (ReL), to learn the parameters and update rules in such methods from data alone using deep neural networks as surrogates - without explicit knowledge or access about these underlying functions; ReLs are trained on both aligned source–target(s) paired datasets along with their corresponding ground truth alignments generated via conventional optimisation schemes [1]. A primary contribution is formulating this problem within modern machine translation frameworks: we show how standard sequence models can be leveraged directly when processing discrete input sequences representi

The network-based study of financial systems has received considerable attention in recent years but has seldom focused on cryptocurrencies, which are a promising new approach to digital finance. Despite their increased popularity and growing significance in the global economy, cryptocurrencies still remain understudied from a network perspective. In this work, we conduct extensive analysis of 10 representative cryptocurrency networks using complex network methods and demonstrate that they exhibit distinctive features compared with conventional financial networks, such as reduced centralization and higher resilience against attacks. Our findings offer insights into the structure and dynamics of cryptocurrency networks and highlight their potential for facilitating secure and efficient tran

Air quality inference, the prediction of past, present, and future air quality based on historical data, is a critical application area for environmental informatics. It is a typical spatio-temporal prediction problem, which requires a spatio-temporal model to capture the underlying spatio-temporal patterns in the data. Traditional machine learning models, while effective, are limited in their ability to capture these patterns. Neural networks, particularly those designed for spatio-temporal data, show great promise in addressing this problem. However, the majority of existing spatio-temporal neural network models are limited to scalar field data (e.g., temperature, rainfall) and cannot directly process vector field data (e.g., wind speed and direction). Vector field data is common in air quality inference, and its proper integration into neural network models is crucial for accurate predictions. To address this gap, we propose a novel Spatio-Temporal Field Neural Network (STF-Net) model that can effectively process vector field data. The model is based on a graph neural network framework, where the graph structure is designed to represent the spatio-temporal relationships in the data. We validate our STF-Net model using real-world air quality data from the State of California. The results demonstrate that our model outperforms other state-of-the-art baselines for the air quality inference task.

We consider the problem of online learning where the sequence of actions played by the learner must adhere to an unknown safety constraint at every round. The goal is to minimize regret with respect to the best safe action in hindsight while simultaneously satisfying the safety constraint with high probability on each round. We provide a general meta-algorithm that leverages an online regression oracle to estimate the unknown safety constraint, and converts the predictions of an online learning oracle to predictions that adhere to the unknown safety constraint. On the theoretical side, our algorithm's regret can be bounded by the regret of the online regression and online learning oracles, the eluder dimension of the model class containing the unknown safety constraint, and a novel complex

Reconstructing High Dynamic Range (HDR) video from image sequences captured with alternating exposures is a challenging task. Current state-of-the-art methods rely on per-frame supervision to estimate the exposure parameters, which limits their generalization ability. In this paper, we propose an efficient and generalizable framework for HDR video reconstruction using only one single ground truth frame in each sequence. Our method consists of two key components: a novel exposure estimation module that learns to predict the exposure parameters via a latent variable model and a spatio-temporal fusion module that leverages both global and local information to produce high quality HDR video reconstructions. Experimental results show that our approach outperforms existing methods in terms of bo

The existence of adversarial attacks on machine learning models imperceptible to a human is still quite a mystery, despite numerous studies on the topic. This research aims to analyze the relationship between model performance and adversarial robustness in an attempt to understand what makes a deep neural network susceptible or resistant to such attacks. In this paper, we examine how well a model performs at predicting the class labels of its training dataset and compare it against its performance in detecting adversarial examples crafted using several different attack algorithms. We also use several image augmentation techniques to increase the robustness of our models to adversarial samples. Furthermore, we explore whether there are any correlations between the number of parameters in a

Large language models (LLMs) adapted to follow user instructions are now widely deployed as conversational agents. We assess the alignment of LLMs with the user's goals and preferences, which is critical for their effective use in practical applications. For this purpose, we introduce a novel dataset, called Dexterous-Instructions (DeX-In), consisting of 315,000 instruction sequences collected from online communities, and annotated by multiple human judges on whether each sequence follows the user's intent or not. DeX-In also includes the corresponding reference answers provided by humans. To evaluate LLMs, we developed two benchmarks based on DeX-In: DeX-In-Follower that requires LLMs to generate the most coherent answer given an input; and DeX-In-Follower-Extended that additionally requi

We study the complexity of Non-Gaussian Component Analysis (NGCA) in the Statistical Query Model, where given i.i.d samples from a distribution over a non-Gaussian component and noise we want to recover the distribution. We prove that NGCA with $k$ components is $\mathsf{NP}$-complete for all $k$, and show an upper bound of $\widetilde{\mathcal{O}}(2^{1/4+\epsilon})$ on the number of queries needed to solve NGCA when $k=2$. Our results improve the previously known hardness result of NGCA with $3$ components. The lower bound improves upon existing results, which only hold for distributions with at most $2$ components. To obtain our results we introduce a new technique, called the "sandwich trick", which has applications beyond NGCA.

It is by now well established that low precision training of Deep Neural Networks (DNNs) is a useful tool to reduce the memory footprint and latency of DNNs at training time. However, low precision training is sensitive to the choice of precision format and training schedule. In this paper, we propose a new low precision training schedule that is based on dynamically changing the precision format of the weights and activations of the DNN. We show that our proposed schedule consistently improves the test accuracy of low precision trained DNNs across different datasets and networks. We also show that our proposed schedule can help reduce the memory consumption of DNNs by up to 8X compared to fixed precision formats.

Mars 2.0 is a toolchain for modeling, analysis, verification, and code generation of cyber-physical systems (CPS). It is composed of a modeling tool based on a customizable block diagram interface, a backend for model analysis and verification, and a code generator. As a block diagram tool, Mars 2.0 allows the user to conveniently model CPS containing discrete, continuous, and hybrid dynamics. The modeling environment is based on a multi-core processor model, which allows the user to model computation and communication delays, as well as data-dependent and nondeterministic behaviors. The backend performs various analyses on the model, including type checking, simulation, and formal verification. The verified properties include functional correctness, safety, liveness, and data confidentiality. The code generator produces reference implementations in C/C++ and HDL. The generated code can be imported into a variety of simulators, including Gazebo, VSim, and CoSSim. We demonstrate the usage of Mars 2.0 on two case studies: a networked control system and a cyber-physical voting machine.

In the standard model of fair allocation of resources to agents, every agent has some utility for every resource. This makes it possible that an efficient scheme will be unfair in a sense that no matter what is given out there are always two pairs (one pair gets more and one less). A natural extension here is when we give up on efficiency but require fairness: each group can have at most as much or only slightly different from another within its "group". We consider this problem in various contexts such single item allocations where all items are identical except their labels; multi-item auctions with $n$ bidders ($m$-items) whose bid sets consist entirely binary vectors $(0\text{s} \, \mathrm{~and}\ 1{\rm s})$, etc., which involve finding optimal solutions based upon min/max operations pe

Many machine learning tasks, including classification and retrieval, are naturally formulated as tasks of aligning (or misaligning) distributions. Alignment is often easier to specify than to obtain, however, especially when positive examples are difficult to acquire. Focusing on the case where only unlabeled data are available, we propose a novel distribution-to-distribution alignment method that does not require positive samples. The key ingredient is an optimization criterion that measures distributional misalignment by way of a novel distributional dispreference operator, which we show to have appealing theoretical properties. We present an empirical pipeline that enables distributional alignment in practice, and which can be applied to both unsupervised and weakly supervised learning settings. Our method achieves state-of-the-art performance on a variety of benchmark tasks, including cross-modal retrieval.

Time series forecasting attempts to predict future events by analyzing past trends and patterns. Although most time-series data is inherently nonstationary, many existing methods assume stationarity in order for the model structure or parameters not change over a period of observation (i) which requires additional work on pre/post processing; furthermore it can lead models towards erroneous conclusions when dealing with real world situations where they may be exposed only once before making predictions about them later downline such as predicting whether someone will buy an item based off their previous purchases instead relying solely upon historical prices alone without taking into account current stock levels etc., potentially resulting inaccurate forecasts due lack proper understanding

The task of SQL query equivalence checking is important in various real-world applications (including query rewriting, query optimization, and security audit). This task can be solved by comparing the relational algebra expressions (RAEs) underlying queries. As a first step, this paper proposes a novel way to represent RAEs using binary strings, which we call BRAE for Binary Relational Algebra Expression. In addition, we present a new algorithm for query equivalence checking that leverages BRAE representation. This approach significantly improves the efficiency of query equivalence checking by eliminating the need to explicitly construct the corresponding relation from the input RAEs. We experimentally demonstrate the effectiveness of our proposed approach on benchmarks designed specifical

End-to-end differentiable learning for autonomous driving (AD) has recently become a prominent research topic in the AD community. End to end, as opposed traditional model based approaches or perception plus planning approachs used today by many industry players and startups working on selfdriving cars; which require human input at some stage during their development process - such that they can be improved upon later down line without having been reprogrammed from scratch again after each iteration cycle ends .

The dominance of vision and language in modern AI is due in large part to the natural correspondence between the two modalities. Multimodal learning not only takes advantage of this natural correspondence, but also enhances the understanding of concepts through the process of comparing and contrasting different representations. However, existing multimodal contrastive learning methods primarily focus on the technical challenge of cross-modal representation alignment, and the educational benefit of contrastive learning is therefore not fully realized. To address this issue, we introduce a simple yet effective method to enhance conceptual understanding in multimodal contrastive learning, by actively mining hard negative samples that provide maximal contrastive information. We propose a new strategy for selecting hard negative samples, based on the intuition that samples with large representation gaps yield greater conceptual learning opportunities. To efficiently find these hard negative samples, we introduce a new technique that exploits the inverse of the attention map, which we show has a negative correlation with the contrastive loss. This simple technique significantly improves the performance of existing contrastive learning algorithms, without any additional model training. We conduct extensive experiments in both cross-modal retrieval and generation tasks, using diverse multimodal datasets. Our results demonstrate an average improvement of 7.4% in NDCG@10 and 10.1% in F1-score across all experiments.

The proliferation of wireless-enabled applications with divergent quality of service (QoS) requirements in a 5G system is expected to place unprecedented demands on the underlying physical layer, especially on its radio frequency front-end (RF). To achieve this, we propose a novel dynamic power allocation scheme for RF-based intelligent reflecting surfaces (IRSs), which can be dynamically configured by an RF-IRS to support multiple users with different QoS requirements. We consider two scenarios: 1) An IRS without transmit amplifiers; and 2) An IRS with transmit amplifiers. The proposed schemes are evaluated under practical conditions such as finite channel state information (CSI) and imperfect power estimation at the IRS.

This paper introduces BLaIR, a series of pretrained sentence embedding models specialized for biomedical applications. We focus on three distinct tasks: 1) identifying the core biological meaning of sentences; 2) predicting protein-protein interactions (PPIs); and 3) finding the nearest neighbor proteins based on sequence similarity. To accomplish these goals, we use biomedical data that spans multiple domains including biomolecules, diseases, drugs, genes, and pathways. For each task, we create a separate model using a different architecture. Moreover, to better capture domain-specific knowledge, we introduce a technique called BLAIR-Fusion, which is designed to incorporate both textual and structural information into our models.

Knowledge graph completion (KGC) is a widely used method to tackle incompleteness and noise in knowledge graphs. Existing KGC models either focus on learning representations from the neighborhood nodes of target entities, or exploit relations between candidate pairs by employing neural network architectures such as convolution networks for capturing structural information at diverse scales with limited capacity constraints due their inherent architecture limitations i..e., inability model long-range dependencies/interactions efficiently across multiple layers . This paper proposes GATEDConv -- an advanced version that incorporates gated mechanisms into conventional convolutions allowing it be applied directly over input matrices without requiring additional processing steps like reshaping

The proliferation of software vulnerabilities poses a significant challenge for security databases and analysts in maintaining up-to-date information. This study presents a novel framework, called FLARE, that combines AI techniques to improve the quality and timeliness of security database information. Our approach leverages Reinforcement Learning (RL) to optimize vulnerability extraction from source code repositories, enabling more accurate and efficient analysis. Additionally, we integrate Vision Transformer (ViT), a state-of-the-art transformer-based architecture, into the vulnerability description generation process, ensuring greater accuracy and robustness. With its ability to quickly and accurately generate vulnerability descriptions, FLARE provides a valuable tool for security resea

We consider the problem of predicting an unknown target concept, which is assumed to be consistent with a set of observed concepts, using a machine learning approach. We propose a novel algorithm that identifies the extent to which the target concept differs from the observed concepts and defers to a human expert when the difference is significant. The algorithm is based on a meta-learning approach that jointly learns a prediction model and a novelty detection model from data. We analyze the theoretical properties of the algorithm and demonstrate its benefits empirically on synthetic and real-world data sets.

Human-centric 3D scene understanding has recently drawn increasing attention, driven by its critical impact on the real world. In this paper, we focus on the problem of object detection and tracking in human-centric 3D scenes. Specifically, we introduce a human-centric 3D object detection framework that employs the novel 3D instance-level self-attention module to learn multi-scale context features for accurate detection. To further improve the performance of 3D object tracking, our approach also introduces an adaptive feature fusion module and an innovative two-stage tracker design based on sparse anchor boxes. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our method outperforms existing state-of-the-art methods in terms of both quantitative metrics and qualitative results.

The concept of vertex integrity was introduced by Carleton et al. in 2020. The integrity of a vertex $v$ in a graph $G$ is the minimum number of vertices that need to be removed from $G$ to obtain a subgraph with a trivial automorphism group, in other words, a subgraph that consists of a disjoint union of edges and isolated vertices. The computation of the integrity of a vertex is NP-hard, but it is fixed-parameter tractable with respect to the vertex integrity. In this paper, we first design a randomized Las Vegas time algorithm for the computation of the integrity of a vertex in a graph $G$ that runs in $O(m^{1+\delta} + n^{1+\delta/2})$ time with high probability, where $n$ is the number of vertices and $m$ is the number of edges of $G$, and $\delta \in (0,1)$ is a constant. This algorithm is based on a randomized Las Vegas time algorithm for the computation of the minimum vertex cover of a graph $G$ that runs in $O(m^{1+\delta} + n^{1+\delta/2})$ time with high probability, which might be of independent interest. We then show that the computation of the integrity of a vertex is in FPTAS. We improve this result by showing that the integrity of a vertex $v$ in a graph $G$ can be computed in $O(m^{1+\delta} + n^{1+\delta/2})$ time with high probability, where $n$ is the number of vertices and $m$ is the number of edges of $G$, and $\delta \in (0,1)$ is a constant. This algorithm is based on a randomized Las Vegas time algorithm for fast matrix multiplication that runs in $O(n^{1+\delta/2})$ time with high probability, where $n$ is the size of the matrices and $\delta \in (0,1)$ is a constant.

Deep Text-to-Image Synthesis (TIS) models such as Stable Diffusion have become increasingly popular and are used for various purposes. One of these is generating images of text that is not part of the training data. However, the quality of this generation heavily depends on the availability of high-quality image samples containing this text in the training dataset. In many cases, especially when dealing with less common texts, it can be challenging to find enough samples. We address this problem by introducing a novel approach to generate high-fidelity images based on low-resolution input. Our method leverages the knowledge learned from the original model to adapt to new textual inputs. Furthermore, we introduce an efficient sampling strategy that significantly improves the final image qua

Live programming provides feedback on run-time behavior by visualizing concrete values of expressions close to the source code. This information can be used for debugging purposes, but also to identify potential errors or optimization opportunities. Existing systems offer limited support for this kind of analysis due to their lack of a program representation which allows them to describe complex expression evaluation paths. In this paper, we introduce a novel system called DiffRank that extends existing live programming systems with a new approach for graph-based program representations and dynamic program slicing that allows to extract concrete value traces from expressions in programs. We show how DiffRank supports program analysis tasks such as identifying program branches responsible f

An efficient customer service management system hinges on precise forecasting of service volume. In this paper, we propose a deep learning model with the capability to predict the service demand of an entire service day in advance based solely on historical data. We first apply a time-series decomposition technique known as the EMD method to transform the time series into a low-dimensional space with better feature representation ability. Next, we employ a two-stage architecture to identify patterns from the input data and classify them according to their respective categories. The first stage employs a ResNet50 encoder to extract the representative features of each sequence, while the second stage leverages a multilayer perceptron (MLP) network for classification. This approach is evaluat

Tables in Wikipedia articles are extensively annotated by human editors. This annotation is, however, sparse in coverage and lacks consistency. To assist with the creation of a high-quality annotated dataset, we develop a system that automatically extracts tables and their column types, and then leverages Named Entity Recognition (NER) to annotate table cells. Our system, Wiki-TabNER, achieves state-of-the-art performance on the task of table column type prediction, and improves the state-of-the-art performance on the task of table row prediction. Our evaluation consists of a two-stage process. First, we conduct a human study to evaluate the system's output and its potential impact on the two downstream tasks of table question answering and table-to-text generation. Second, we release a new dataset that contains tables from Wikipedia articles on the topics of American colleges and universities. This dataset, named Wiki-Tab, consists of 7,000 tables annotated with column types and 1,000 tables annotated with both column types and row spans.

Downstream ecosystems are preserved by maintaining sufficient environmental flows. The environmental flow is determined by the flow regulation of the entire basin, which is mainly affected by hydropower operations. Therefore, hydropower plants are key actors for downstream ecosystem preservation. In this paper, we develop an adaptive hydropower management approach to preserve downstream ecosystems under uncertainty. We consider a reservoir-controlled hydropower plant with a downstream ecosystem that is represented by a water demand. The objective is to satisfy the water demand at minimum cost while preserving the downstream ecosystem. The cost is composed of a production cost, which is a function of the power production, and an environmental cost, which is a function of the environmental flow. The environmental flow is determined endogenously by the reservoir level, which affects the power production. The proposed optimization approach is a nonlinear mixed-integer program that is solved by a heuristic search algorithm. The search algorithm uses adaptive penalties to guide the search toward feasible regions with high environmental benefit. The approach is applied to a real-world case study, and we demonstrate that the proposed approach provides a satisfactory solution with respect to both economic and environmental objectives. The approach can be applied to other reservoirs in the basin to preserve downstream ecosystems under hydropower production.

The integration of learning and reasoning is high on the research agenda in AI. Nevertheless, there are few frameworks that combine both aspects adequately while offering a reasonable level abstraction for implementing applications quickly (e. g., knowledge graphs). In this paper we introduce Learning Reasoning Graphs – LRGs which offer an integrated framework to implement hybrid intelligent systems combining neural networks with rule-based logic programs as well known from Prolog programming languages or RuleML representations but now also including deep net components such e. as convolutional nets. A key feature compared to existing approaches like Pytorch/PyTorch Lightning or Tensorflow Keras APIs based only solely either one paradigm i.~a.)

We propose a framework for the generation of differentially private synthetic text data. We utilize the foundation model APIs to learn the underlying distribution of the original data and then sample from it to create synthetic data. We evaluate the utility and privacy of the synthetic data both quantitatively and qualitatively.

Federated learning (FL) is a collaborative scheme to train a learning model across multiple participants without sharing data. As FL is gaining popularity, it is essential to understand the security and privacy implications of this new trend in centralized learning. In this paper, we analyze the security of FL under data poisoning attacks in computer networks. Data poisoning attacks, in which a malicious participant injects poisoned data into the training process, have not been fully explored in the context of FL. We examine different poisoning strategies and their effectiveness. We also discuss how the poisoned data affects the convergence of the learning algorithm and the ability of the model to generalize. Through both theoretical analysis and empirical evaluations, we show that FL is not robust against data poisoning attacks and that poisoned data can significantly degrade the performance of the learning model. We propose a novel defense based on detecting the presence of poisoned data and show that it can effectively mitigate poisoning attacks.

When users access shared resources in a selfish manner, the resulting societal cost and perceived users' utility can be significantly reduced. Using a simple model, we study how sharing rates can be optimized to maximize social welfare. The key idea is that social welfare can be improved by increasing the sharing rate at times when resource utilization is low; however, this implies an increase in total resource usage, which reduces utility for individual users. We show that the optimal sharing rate increases with the level of resource utilization and is proportional to the inverse of the reciprocal of the marginal user utility. This suggests that an optimal policy has a non-monotonic dependence on the underlying resource usage pattern. Additionally, we establish the existence of a stationa

Version Control Systems, such as Git and Mercurial, manage the history of a project as a Directed Acyclic Graph (DAG). This DAG allows for tracking changes in a project while maintaining version control. While this graph is useful for managing version control, it does not provide a complete picture of how changes are made to a project. In this paper, we introduce Graphical Histories, which provide a visualization of the history of a project, allowing users to understand the evolution of the project over time. We demonstrate that Graphical Histories can be used effectively by developers, leading to increased collaboration and productivity. Our study shows that Graphical Histories significantly outperform existing DAG representations, with 82% of participants being more confident in their un

Generative artificial intelligence (GenAI) has ushered in a new era for storytellers, enabling them to harness the power of deep learning to create stories that are more engaging and diverse. We aim to develop a high-quality AI-assisted storytelling tool by leveraging large language models (LLMs) like GPT-3.5 Turbo, which excels at generating coherent narratives. To achieve this goal, we propose a novel framework called "Scribe", integrating LLMs with a graphical user interface (GUI), resulting in an intuitive and efficient authoring experience. The GUI enables users to seamlessly interact with their personalized Scribe, allowing them to edit and generate stories on-the-fly. In this paper, we introduce our GUI design and discuss how it facilitates effective interactions between humans and

Video motion magnification (VMM) aims at amplifying and thus enhancing the motion in videos, e.g., to make the motion of objects more evident or to fill in missing motion. VMM has attracted considerable research interests, especially in the past two decades. Recently, deep learning-based methods have pushed the development of VMM from a new perspective and achieved state-of-the-art performance. However, most of the existing learning-based VMM methods design the objective functions and algorithms to minimize the reconstruction error between the magnified motion and the ground truth, e.g., using the mean squared error (MSE) between them. Such an error metric, though effective in VMM, is computationally expensive for real-time processing. To address this issue, we revisit the learning-based VMM framework and propose a novel real-time VMM approach, termed fast motion magnification network (FMMNet). Specifically, we design a motion magnification transformer (MMT) to explicitly magnify the motion in the frequency domain. To make FMMNet more efficient, we further propose two lightweight models, namely FMMNet-light and FMMNet-ultra. To demonstrate the superiority of our approach, we compare our results with those of the state-of-the-art VMM methods in terms of objective qualities, e.g., using the peak signal-to-noise ratio (PSNR) and the structural similarity index (SSIM). Experiments show that our approach achieves competitive performance with respect to existing VMM methods at both full and lightweight versions. Our code is available at https://github.com/2000ZRL/Learning_based_VMM_realtime.

Robotic manipulation aims to extend a robot's dexterous capabilities to perform a wide range of tasks in various environments. However, the majority of robotic manipulation frameworks rely on manually crafted vocabularies, limiting their ability to generalize and adapt to novel situations. In contrast, humans excel in robotic manipulation due to their vast visual experience and the ability to learn from it. In this work, we aim to bridge the gap between human and robotic manipulation by proposing a open-vocabulary manipulation framework through the introduction of a novel Mark-based Visual Prompting (MVP) scheme. Our framework leverages the robot's visual capabilities to identify and manipulate objects, even when dealing with novel or unseen items. The MVP scheme serves as a bridge, facilitating communication between the robot and the user, and enabling the user to instruct the robot on how to manipulate the object based on the visual information. We evaluate our framework's performance in three different scenarios, including general object manipulation, food preparation, and drug sorting. Our experiments demonstrate that our open-vocabulary manipulation framework can significantly improve robotic manipulation performance in various real-world scenarios, showing great potential for home assistance and other similar applications.

Transformers have revolutionized computer vision and natural language processing, but their high computational complexity limits their application to a single task. This paper proposes a novel approach that leverages multitask learning to enable low-resource transformer models to simultaneously perform multiple tasks with minimal overhead, making them more practical for real-world applications. Our approach introduces the notion of "task-aware tokenization", where tokens are associated with specific tasks and only activated during relevant task execution. We demonstrate the effectiveness of our method through comprehensive experiments on multiple datasets and tasks, achieving state-of-the-art performance in many cases while requiring up to 50% fewer parameters than comparable models. Our c

In this work, we investigate the scaling properties of compound inference systems, i.e., systems that obtain a final decision through a sequence of inference steps. Our study is motivated by the widespread adoption of compound inference in AI applications, yet a lack of theoretical understanding on how these systems scale with respect to the number of inference steps. We consider a prototypical compound inference system: a sequence of $n$ conditionally independent binary classifiers, each with different resources (e.g., training data, compute). We model the classification problem using a hierarchical Bayesian model, where the top-level distribution represents the prior over the ground-truth labels, and the bottom-level distributions represent the conditional distributions of the labels given the resources. We then derive an asymptotic expression for the expected risk of the majority vote classifier, and identify two regimes where the relative advantage of the majority vote over a single classifier, in terms of expected risk, grows or shrinks with $n$. Interestingly, we find that if the top-level prior is uniform, the majority vote is risk-averse, in the sense that its expected risk is no more than the expected risk of any other classifier in the system, regardless of $n$. Our results provide insights on the design of scalable compound inference systems. In particular, we show that increasing the number of inference steps can lead to better performance, under certain conditions on the resources.

In the contemporary digital landscape, the continuous generation of extensive streaming data across diverse domains has become pervasive. Such vast streams of heterogeneous data present significant challenges in terms of processing and analysis, which can impede their timely utilization for informed decision-making. This is where Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) come into play. As a powerful tool in graph mining, GNNs have garnered attention in addressing the problem of stream graph analytics. However, the current methods in this field have limitations such as excessive model complexity, suboptimal performance on temporal and node features, and restricted scalability. To address these issues, we propose a novel method called Stream Graph Adaptation for Time-Aware Node Feature Evolution (STREAM

The human brain is an energy-efficient device that can perform complex tasks using a sparse network of neurons. Inspired by this, researchers have proposed sparse spiking neural networks (SNN), which use binary activity (spiking) and sparse connectivity to reduce energy consumption and increase computational efficiency. However, existing SNN pruning methods mainly focus on weight sparsity and do not exploit the potential of heterogeneous timescales. In this paper, we propose a novel pruning technique for recurrent SNN, the heterogeneous timescale pruning (HTP), based on the observation that spikes in biological neurons occur on multiple timescales. HTP identifies and removes unnecessary connections for computation by exploiting the natural heterogeneity in timescales. We apply HTP to a state-of-the-art recurrent SNN for the MNIST classification task and show that the accuracy can be improved by exploiting heterogeneity in timescales for pruning.

People have to remember an ever-expanding volume of information. Wearables that use information capture and retrieval systems could help with this task by helping people better integrate digital content into their lives, according the a new study from researchers at Carnegie Mellon University (CMU) in Pittsburgh The research team developed two prototypes for wearable devices: one is called “Wearable Memory” which uses image recognition software on Android wear smartwatches paired with Google Lens; another prototype was created using Amazon Echo Frames as well speech transcription capabilities via Alexa’s built–in microphone system.” Both platforms were used successfully during experiments conducted over three weeks where participants wore either device while completing tasks involving rea

Layout-aware text-to-image generation is a task to generate multi-object images that reflect the given layout structure. To achieve this, we propose an approach that jointly solves the image synthesis and layout optimization problems. Our approach leverages the power of diffusion models in learning latent representations and utilizes a graphical model to optimize for the layout of objects within an image. The resulting model learns to align the latent code with the ground truth layout, leading to more accurate generated images while also preserving the original layout structure. We compare our method against state-of-the-art methods on two benchmarks (Stanford Dress2MNIST and DFABS) and showcase its ability to produce high-quality results. Additionally, we demonstrate how our method can be

Runtime Verification (RV) refers to a family of techniques in which system executions are observed at runtime and their properties verified. This area has been growing steadily over the past two decades, with applications ranging from embedded systems such as avionics software or industrial control networks up through cloud-scale distributed data processing platforms like Apache Kafka®. In this talk I will focus on my own work that aims for scalability by leveraging RV tools developed primarily within computer science academia together into an end user ecosystem accessible via web browser -- no code required! In recent years there have been tremendous advances in AI/ML technologies across all sectors including healthcare - however these advancements come handinhandwithsignificant challenge

Video motion magnification is a technique to capture and amplify subtle motion in a video that is otherwise not visible. This paper introduces a novel approach for video motion magnification, based on local spatio-temporal attention mechanisms. We propose an end-to-end learning framework for both the encoder and decoder of our system, which enables effective modeling of different types of motion present in the video. The proposed method outperforms state-of-the-art methods while also being able to generalize well across diverse videos. Additionally, we showcase the versatility of this approach by exploring its potential applications in various tasks such as object detection, video classification, and action recognition. We hope that our work will inspire further research in this exciting f

Large Language Models (LLMs) are versatile tools that can complete tasks in natural language. However, task-specific inputs can be challenging for LLMs, such as table-based inputs in spreadsheet applications. To benchmark LLMs' robustness under such conditions, we introduce PPTC-R, a new framework for evaluating the robustness of LLMs in the context of PowerPoint presentations. We create a new dataset by extracting table-based inputs from existing PowerPoint presentations and introduce a robustness benchmark based on this dataset. The benchmark consists of two robustness tasks: Table Question Answering (TableQA) and Table-based Instruction Following (TableIF). We provide TableQA and TableIF results for seven LLMs. Based on our results, we recommend using LLMs with TableQA and TableIF capabilities for PowerPoint task completion.

Multidimensional arrays are a fundamental data structure, supported by all modern programming languages. arrays are used in a wide range of applications, and their performance characteristics are therefore of great practical relevance. This paper presents an empirical study of array access patterns on the Java Virtual Machine (JVM), one of the most popular run-time environments. We analyze arrays accessed through iterating over their indices (e.g., `for` loops), and arrays accessed through the `java.util.Arrays` class (e.g., `Arrays.sort`). We measure array access patterns on a set of 215 open-source Java programs, containing a total of 874 array access statements. Our experiments show that, counter to common intuition, not all array access patterns are created equal: some patterns are more prevalent than others, and some are more amenable to optimization by the JVM. We find that iterating over array indices with a `for` loop generates the most diverse access patterns, while `java.util.Arrays` methods generate the least diverse access patterns. We also find that array access patterns are fairly stable across different program executions, exhibiting little variation. Finally, we propose practical recommendations for array access optimization on the JVM.

We present a multi-time-step penalty-based coupling method for peridynamics (PD) and classical continuum mechanics (CCM). Our coupling method is based on the variational formulation of PD proposed by Chen et al. (2010) which uses a regularized non-local potential energy. This formulation of PD allows a seamless integration with CCM since the resulting energy functional has the same form as the classical energy functional for elasticity. We perform dynamic brittle fracture analyses of two-dimensional (2D) and three-dimensional (3D) structures using our coupling method. Specifically, we study the dynamic fracture of a 2D plate with a through-crack under various impact loads and the dynamic brittle failure of a 3D structure subjected to a central impact load. Our numerical results show that our coupling method produces accurate solutions for dynamic brittle fracture analyses.

Visual instruction tuning is a key training stage of large multimodal models (LMMs) in robotics, but remains challenging due to the limited availability of annotated visual instructions. Large-scale datasets are required for LMMs that can perform various tasks using only visual instructions, such as grasping and moving objects. However, creating such datasets from scratch is time consuming and costly. To address this challenge, we present a novel approach based on generative modeling to automatically generate high-quality large-scale datasets without human annotation. Our method leverages a fine-tuned GPT-4 model trained on a diverse set of human instructions and a language-vision transformer (LV2), which encodes the visual features of an object into text, to generate novel instructions.

Developing Large Language Models (LLMs) with robust long-context capabilities has been a challenging task. Existing methods mainly address this problem by enhancing the model's capacity, but these approaches often lead to diminishing returns and even harm the performance. In this paper, we propose an efficient method called CoNLLM, which leverages the connection between context and neural language models to enhance their performance. Specifically, given a sequence of tokens, we split them into smaller subsequences that better fit the LLM's input size limit, and then use a simple neural network to connect these sub-sequences together. Our experiments demonstrate that this approach can significantly improve the performance of LLMs with limited context capabilities, without increasing their c

Knowledge distillation, the technique of transferring knowledge from large, complex models to smaller ones with fewer parameters and lower computational costs for inference. The proposed work focuses on developing a novel method that utilizes two-stage Knowledge Distillation (KD) techniques combined using an ensemble approach based upon a Bayesian framework in order reduce generalization error during training process as well enhance performance metrics such accuracy rate or F1 score across multiple datasets consisting diverse types images including those related medical diagnosis applications .

Kronos is a novel Byzantine fault tolerant sharding blockchain consensus protocol that achieves optimal communication overhead, leveraging a new block dissemination technique called layer-based dissemination. In Kronos, each participating node maintains a unique state machine for each shard, and a global state machine that executes transactions in a sequential order across all shards. The global state machine ensures linearizability of cross-shard transactions. In contrast, existing sharding protocols either suffer from suboptimal communication overhead or violate the linearizability property. We provide a formal specification of Kronos and prove that the protocol is Byzantine fault tolerant and achieves optimal communication overhead. We implement Kronos and compare the performance of our prototype with existing state-of-the-art protocols.

Isabelle is a popular interactive theorem prover (ITP) with a large user community. Despite its widespread use, there is no comprehensive benchmarking of the most popular ITPs available, nor any systematic evaluation of the impact of the underlying proof assistant architecture on interactive proof construction. We fill this gap by providing a new framework to systematically benchmark ITPs and evaluate the impact of proof assistant architectures on interactive proof construction. We introduce BAIT, a new benchmarking framework for ITPs, based on a new formalization of the notion of proof assistant architecture. BAIT can be used to compare existing ITPs and explore new architectures for ITPs. We use BAIT to compare six existing ITPs: lean, mathlib, Isabelle, Isabelle/HOL, Mizar, and HOL4. We also use BAIT to explore two new architectures for ITPs.
