Our results show that BAIT is a useful framework for benchmarking ITPs and that it can be used to make informed decisions when choosing an ITP. Our results also show that BAIT can be used to explore new ITP architectures; e.g., we show that a lean backend can improve the performance of Isabelle.

In decision-making problem under uncertainty, predicting unknown parameters is often considered independent of the optimization part. However, this assumption may not hold in many practical scenarios where both are intertwined. In such cases, it is necessary to simultaneously learn the model parameters and optimize the objective function under uncertainty, a task known as Bayesian optimization (BO). Despite their importance, existing BO methods either focus on parameter estimation or are computationally expensive. To address these challenges, we introduce a new BO method called Ensemble Regret Optimization (ERO), which leverages a novel ensemble regret strategy that balances the trade-off between exploration and exploitation in BO. Theoretically, our approach ensures the convergence of the

The use of unsupervised learning to identify patient subgroups has emerged as a potentially promising method in the identification and characterizationof disease phenotypes. We present an overview, application examplesand discussion on recent developmentsin this field with particular emphasison approaches that are based upon clustering techniquesfor identifying homogeneous clusters (or "subgroups") within large datasets containing multiple types or modalities(e..g., clinical data) without requiring any pre-specified hypothesis about how these features relateto one another; we also highlight some limitations/challenges associatedwith using such methods for real world applications including bias concerns related tothe selection process itself alongside potential issues around interpretabilit

We study distributed training of deep learning models in time-constrained environments. We propose a new algorithm, called \textit{DynScaffolding}, to optimize the training schedule. Our approach dynamically adjusts the frequency of model updates based on the progress made by each worker. DynScaffolding uses a novel progress measure that incorporates both model and data dynamics. This measure allows our algorithm to adapt to varying update frequencies. We show that with dynamic scheduling we can train deep networks faster than the best static schedules on 10 datasets from various domains. Specifically, DynScaffolding achieves an average speedup of $30\%$ over state-of-the-art static methods when using an exponential scheduling policy, without compromising on test accuracy. This means that

Robotic manipulation of cloth objects is a challenging task. The lack of a standardized representation of cloth objects hinders the generalization of cloth manipulation skills to novel situations. In this paper, we propose a standardized representation of cloth objects based on their inherent physical properties. We argue that such a standardized representation is necessary for a robotic system to reason about cloth objects in an abstract manner. In particular, we focus on the standardization of cloth shape, which is essential for predicting the outcome of a manipulation action. We introduce a novel shape descriptor, called cloth shape tensor, which encodes the geometric and topological information of a cloth object in a task-agnostic manner. We evaluate the efficacy of our proposed representation in two ways. First, we conduct a user study to verify the perceptual discriminability of the proposed cloth shape tensor. Second, we develop a cloth manipulation model based on the proposed representation and demonstrate that it can outperform a baseline model that does not use the proposed representation. We also show that our model can be easily extended to handle a large variety of cloth objects.

The Internet of Things (IoT) has witnessed tremendous growth over the past two decades, leading to a pressing need for a self-organizing and self-managing network. The RPL protocol, being the de-facto multipoint communication protocol for IoT, needs to be enhanced to provide reliable multihop transmission with guaranteed end-to-end (E2E) delays. To this end, we propose an intelligent and trusted RPL protocol (iTRPL) leveraging multi-agent reinforcement learning (MARL). We design an actor-critic-based MARL algorithm, where each agent maintains a local view of the network state and makes independent decisions. We introduce a trusted node selection scheme to enhance the reliability of message transmission and employ a delayed reward mechanism to guarantee E2E delay. We prove that iTRPL converges to the global optimum with a guaranteed E2E delay. Simulation results validate that iTRPL outperforms state-of-the-art RPL protocols in terms of network performance, energy efficiency, and convergence time. Furthermore, iTRPL is shown to possess robustness against malicious attacks.

In this work, we present the first algorithm to compute expander decompositions in an $m$-dimensional hyperbolic space. We do so by adapting the notion of expanders and their decomposition from Euclidean spaces to hyperbolic spaces. Our main result is that there exists a polynomial time algorithm that computes the $\ell_p$ expander decomposition of any $n$-vertex graph $G \in H^{\ell_p}$ with $\Delta(G) < n^{1-\epsilon}$, for any constant $\epsilon > 0$. To prove our results, we introduce new techniques to handle certain properties of hyperbolic spaces which are not well understood yet, such as the lack of a clear notion of geodesics or hyperbolicity of subspaces.

The vast accumulation of neuroscience knowledge presents a challenge for researchers to timely and accurately utilize the existing data. However, current approaches largely rely on manual annotation or automated methods that are resource-intensive and time-consuming. To address this problem, we propose an end-to-end methodology called Evolve-Fuse-Augment (EFA). We first develop a novel multi-modal pretrained model, AUDIO-ViT, for audio-visual fusing. This model is further enhanced by incorporating evolutionary strategies based on reinforcement learning. Our approach not only improves performance but also reduces computational costs and increases scalability. Experiments conducted on various benchmarks show that our method outperforms state-of-the-art models.

In Ethereum, the gas fee is paid by the sender of a transaction and is used to secure the execution of smart contracts. High gas fees can deter users from interacting with Dapps, and thus, developers are encouraged to write gas-efficient smart contracts. However, gas inefficiency is not always apparent from reading the source code of a smart contract, as it also depends on the state of the blockchain at the time of execution. In this paper, we investigate gas issues in 3,096 smart contracts from 1,271 Dapps. We measure the gas consumption of these smart contracts under different conditions using three real-world testnets. We find that 17.8% of the smart contracts have gas issues, but only 14.5% of these gas-inefficient smart contracts are detectable by existing tools. Users who interact with gas-inefficient smart contracts pay 2X - 4X more gas fees than necessary. Unfortunately, most gas-inefficient smart contracts are not documented as such, and there are no readily available tools to help users identify them.

The effectiveness of machine learning (ML) algorithms is deeply intertwined with the quality and diverseness of training data. However, in many real-world scenarios it can be challenging to obtain highquality labeled examples that are representative for all target populations due either financial or ethical reasons related e g., privacy concerns such as sensitive information leakage during model building processes via biased samples collection approaches like manual labeling campaigns which could lead toward unfair decisions made by automated decision systems trained on those datasets containing skewed distributions among different groups within society leading towards discrimination against certain communities based solely off their demographics alone without any consideration given whats

Big models have achieved revolutionary breakthroughs in the field of AI, but they might also be a ticking time bomb for AI safety. Large language models (LLMs) like GPT-4 are capable of passing various tests that humans use to test the reasoning ability of an AI system. But the latest research suggests that these LLMs don't actually understand why their answers are correct. Researchers at MIT and Stanford University found that LLMs are relying on memorization instead of deep understanding when answering questions. This is problematic because it means that if you feed a model with biased data, it will reproduce those biases in its responses.

Virtual Private Networks (VPNs) are a crucial Privacy-Enhancing Technology (PET) that can protect users from malicious actors and surveillance. However, VPN providers often lack transparency, making it challenging for consumers to assess the trustworthiness of their services. In this study, we evaluate five popular VPN services - NordVPN, Surfshark, ExpressVPN, ProtonVPN, and Mullvad - based on their trustworthiness using an open framework called Trustworthy VPN Evaluation Framework (TVPEF). We collected data through multiple channels and analyzed them using machine learning techniques. Our findings reveal that Mullvad has the highest overall trust score among these services, followed by NordVPN, Surfshark, ProtonVPN, and ExpressVPN.

Abstract visual reasoning is a characteristically human ability, allowing the identification of relational patterns that are typically difficult to express in language. Our current understanding of how we understand such relationships is limited by the inherent challenges involved in eliciting and measuring human judgments on complex naturalistic stimuli. We introduce an alternative approach, which leverages computational image processing methods for the identification of salient objects in images, alongside large-scale natural language models for pattern recognition. We then employ these computational tools to identify patterns within real-world datasets, including animal social behavior, ecological interactions, and the structure of space. Through this process, we highlight the unique ch

The digital age introduced the Digital Ecological Niche (DEN), revolutionizing human interactions. The advent of Digital History (DHy) has marked a methodological shift in historical studies, tracing its roots to Babbage and Lovelace's 19th-century work on "coding" as a foundational communication process, fostering a new interaction paradigm between humans and machines, termed "person2persons2machines." This evolution, through digitization and informatization, builds upon ancient coding practices but was significantly advanced by Babbage and Lovelace's contributions to mathematical linguistic systems, laying the groundwork for Computer Science. This field, central to 20th-century mainframe interaction through programming languages and formalization, situates Digital History within a broade

We present a new extension for Neural Optimal Transport (NOT) training procedure, capable of accuratly handling discontinuities in the cost function. Our method is based on an improved formulation of NOT which handles discontinuous cost functions in a more efficient way than state-of-the-art methods. This approach also has advantages over standard discretization techniques, as it does not require any additional assumptions or modifications to the existing theory. To validate our findings, we conduct experiments with real-world datasets and show that our method outperforms previous approaches in terms of both accuracy and computational efficiency. We hope this work will inspire further research into using NOT for solving other complex optimization problems involving discontinuous costs. Fur

Diffusion models have emerged as a powerful new family of probabilistic models with connections to generative neural networks and cellular automata. Despite their recent popularity, their theoretical properties are not well understood, particularly in the regime where the model parameters are large. In this paper, we develop a non-asymptotic theory for a class of diffusion models that includes the popular Langevin diffusion and the Hamiltonian diffusion as special cases. Our main result is a concentration inequality for the density of states of diffusion models that we use to quantify feature emergence. A feature is a subset of the parameter space that the diffusion model fails to explore, and the density of states is a function that encodes information about the long-time behavior of the diffusion. Our concentration inequality is stated in terms of a certain divergence between two diffusion models, and we use this inequality to prove that the Hamiltonian diffusion exhibits feature emergence when the number of particles is large. Along the way, we also prove a concentration inequality for the eigenvalues of a random matrix, as well as new bounds for the eigenvalues of the Hessian of the potential in terms of the density of states, which we believe are of independent interest.

Pointer arithmetic is widely used in low-level programs, e.g. memory allocators. It allows a programmer to access a memory location relative to a given pointer by adding or subtracting an integer offset to it. This is often done implicitly via operators like \(\oplus\) and \(\ominus\). While pointer arithmetic is commonly used in C/C++, Java (and other languages) do not provide any direct support for pointer arithmetic. However, some JVMs do implement a pointer arithmetic API as part of their garbage collection system. In this paper we present the first type-safe implementation of a pointer arithmetic API in Java. Our approach relies on a novel combination of higher-kinded types with a specialised type class to achieve static safety for the arithmetic operations.

Fuzzy Datalog is an important query language for retrieving information from fuzzy databases. In this language, variables can take values from a complete residuated lattice, and rules can involve arbitrary t-norms for computing with these lattice values. So far, only a semantic approach to Fuzzy Datalog has been considered in the literature, in which the focus has been on the case where the lattice is the unit interval with the usual minimum as t-norm. In this paper, we take a syntactic approach and consider Fuzzy Datalog$^\exists$, a variant of Fuzzy Datalog where only existentially quantified variables are allowed. We study the expressive power of Fuzzy Datalog$^\exists$ and its complexity properties, in relation to the classical Datalog, and in relation to Fuzzy Datalog. We show that, for arbitrary t-norms, Fuzzy Datalog$^\exists$ equals classical Datalog, and that, for t-norms which are either continuous or have the sequential product as residuum, Fuzzy Datalog$^\exists$ equals Fuzzy Datalog. These results hold for arbitrary lattices. As a by-product of our study, we get some new characterizations of well-known fuzzy sets, and of the operations on fuzzy sets that preserve these properties.

Processing-using-DRAM (PUD) architectures impose a restrictive data layout, which requires the entirety of the input data to be allocated in contiguous regions within DRAM. This layout constraint presents significant challenges for modern deep learning models, particularly those with complex architectures and variable-length inputs. In this work, we propose Variable-Length PUD (VLPUD), a novel architecture that alleviates the limitations imposed by the restrictive data layout. VLPUD employs a flexible data organization strategy that enables the processing of variable-length inputs without the need for explicit padding or truncation operations. Experimental results show that our method achieves comparable or even superior performance compared to existing methods on several challenging datas

A fundamental component in the theoretical school choice literature is the problem a student faces in deciding which schools to apply for. This paper analyzes this decision using a model of student and family behavior that is based on the notion of school quality. The model assumes that students and families are risk averse, have heterogeneous beliefs about their academic performance and probability of acceptance at each school, and are aware of the correlations between these variables. We find that students with higher ability (as measured by their SAT scores) and students who expect to be accepted at more schools tend to apply to fewer schools than other students. Moreover, students applying to more schools tend to choose schools that are farther away from their home but closer to their

Tool learning empowers large language models (LLMs) as agents to use external tools to solve tasks. In this paper, we present the first study on tool learning from LLMs using a novel tool-learning methodology that builds on the concept of task decomposition and model selection. We introduce the TL4LLM tool-learning benchmark for LLMs, which contains 120 tool-learning tasks across five categories: arithmetic, programming, data processing, natural language processing, and vision processing. Our evaluation reveals that LLMs can effectively learn tools when given a set of predefined tool sets. Additionally, our findings showcase how LLMs are capable of utilizing these tools in various ways, including sequential usage, combination, and even multi-step tool chaining. We also identify potential l

In this paper, we introduce a novel benchmark, dubbed VastTrack, towards facilitating the development of advanced visual localization methods. To create the dataset, we annotate dense tracks using multiple LiDARs and cameras in the KITTI and nuScenes datasets. Our annotations are diverse, with over 10K tracks that span various types, including ground vehicles, pedestrians, cyclists, and motorcycles, providing a comprehensive representation of urban environments. We also provide ground truth trajectories for each track, enabling precise evaluation of localization accuracy. To further enhance the utility of our benchmark, we introduce a challenging new task: multi-camera monocular visual localization. This task involves determining the position and orientation of a monocular camera within a

We present GPTopic, a visual analytics system that enables dynamic and interactive exploration of topics in large document collections. Our system leverages natural language processing techniques, such as named entity recognition and dependency parsing, to dynamically highlight the most relevant terms and phrases in a topic, providing users with a rich, interactive, and contextualized topic representation. In addition, GPTopic visualizes the relationships between topics, allowing users to discover related topics and explore the connections between them. We evaluated the utility and effectiveness of GPTopic through a user study, and demonstrate how the system can be used to analyze and understand the contents of large document collections, as well as the relationships between topics.

Federated learning (FL) is a promising approach for learning a model from data distributed on massive mobile devices. In this work, we consider the setting of FL with linear models and differentially private training phases in which each user locally updates their local weight vector $w$ using noisy gradient descent steps by adding Gaussian noise to its gradients while maintaining differential privacy guarantees regarding $\epsilon$-DP over time-averaged weights across all users up until that point ($(\Delta,\alpha)$-TDP).

Recent studies have investigated utilizing Knowledge Graphs (KGs) to enhance the performance of Large Language Models (LLMs). However, these methods often fail to capture the intricate interactions among entities in KGs. These interactions are crucial for understanding contextualized text and generating human-like responses. To address this limitation, we introduce a novel framework called Interactive LLM that leverages KG embeddings to capture entity relationships. Specifically, we develop a multi-modal interaction module that extracts both structural information from KGs and linguistic cues from text. The resulting representation is then integrated into an existing LLM model through a pretraining paradigm. Our approach significantly improves the quality of generated responses compared to

-policy learning in a multi-task setup is a critical challenge in robotics. The key challenge is to learn policies that are adaptable to multiple tasks without expensive retraining. Guided by the observation that training tasks share a latent policy space, we propose a diffusion guided multi-task policy learning framework that leverages the pre-training latent policy space to learn multi-task policies. Specifically, we introduce a multi-task policy network that consists of a diffusion policy guidance network and a task-specific policy network. The diffusion policy guidance network is responsible for learning the latent policy space, which is shared by all tasks. The task-specific policy network is responsible for learning the task-specific policies. We show that our proposed framework can learn high-performing multi-task policies that outperform the baselines in terms of adaptation speed and generalization. We evaluate our method on a suite of 3D robotic control tasks involving different domains, structures, and observations. For each task, we train a multi-task policy that adapts to multiple training tasks and demonstrate that our method can learn policies that outperform the baselines in terms of adaptation speed and generalization.

Recently, 3D hand reconstruction has gained more attention in human-computer cooperation. Although there are many works on 3D hand tracking and pose estimation, the problem of occlusion still exists. The hand may be occluded by other objects or hands during interaction with virtual reality devices, which will lead to tracking failure. To address this issue, we propose a novel method called Occluded Hand Recovery (OHR), which utilizes the temporal and spatial consistency of hand motion to recover the missing parts of the hand in an occluded scene. Our approach is based on the observation that the motion of the occluded hand is highly correlated with that of the visible hand. Therefore, we first leverage optical flow to establish the correspondence between the two hands, and then use a pretr

In recent months, the social impact of Artificial Intelligence (AI) has gained considerable public attention. As a result, governments are increasingly looking to regulate the use and development of AI in order to better protect citizens from its potentially harmful effects. In this context, the European Commission released the European Strategy for Data on 19th February 2020, which outlines how data should be used in a way that is ethical, trustworthy and beneficial for society. This strategy also highlights the importance of ensuring that AI applications are fair, transparent and accountable. The proposed regulation will help ensure that data is used responsibly and ethically, while promoting innovation and economic growth. It is hoped that this new legislation will help safeguard the ri

In this paper, we abstract the process of people hearing speech, extracting meaningful cues, and making decisions from their actions. We model human decision-making as a two-stage process: (1) information processing and (2) action selection. The first stage is modeled as a sequence-to-sequence (Seq2Seq) model that takes speech segments as input and produces a representation for each segment. The second stage uses a Seq2Seq model to select an action based on the generated representations. Our experiments suggest that our proposed approach outperforms other state-of-the-art methods in both accuracy and efficiency. Our code is available at https://github.com/DongWang-NUS/HumanSpeechDecoding.git.

Bilevel optimization refers to scenarios whereby the optimal solution of a lower-level energy function serves as constraints for the upper-level energy function. In this paper, we propose a novel bilevel learning framework by optimizing a lower-level neural network that aims to approximate the underlying distribution of the lower-level energy function through minimizing a surrogate loss, while constraining the upper-level neural network from violating the lower-level constraint. We demonstrate our approach by applying it to two problems in reinforcement learning: value iteration and actor-critic methods. In both settings, we use an autoencoder to approximate the lower-level value function, which is then used to train a policy in the upper level. Our experiments show that this approach can

BusyBox, an open-source software bundling over 300 essential Linux commands into a single executable program, is celebrating its 25th anniversary this year. The project has been around for two and a half decades and continues to provide users with a convenient way to access various command line tools without having to install a full Linux distribution. In this article, we will take a look at the history of BusyBox, its features, and how it can be used in today's world. ## A Brief History of BusyBox BusyBox was first developed by Bruce Perens in 1996, shortly after the release of Linux kernel version 2.0. It was designed as a replacement for the Bourne Shell (sh), which was the default shell on most Unix systems at that time. Since then, it has evolved into a powerful tool that offers a wi

The pretrain-finetune paradigm represents a transformative approach in natural language processing (NLP) by enabling the transfer of knowledge between models, yet this process remains challenging due to the difficulty in optimizing large language models (LLMs). We present PretrainFiner, an efficient algorithm designed to optimize LLMs under a finetuning setting. We employ a novel hyperparameter tuning strategy to minimize hyperparameter tuning time and improve model performance. Our method offers several advantages over existing algorithms, including simplicity, scalability, and robustness to hyperparameters. Additionally, we introduce the concept of Hyperparameter Tuner Fitting (HTF), which ensures that the HTs are well-trained before using them for tuning. Experimental results on a diver

The rapid advance of mega-constellation facilitates the booming of direct-to-user (DTU) services. However, since DTUs typically suffer from limited transmit power and a single-antenna, they face the challenge of achieving reliable communication in the presence of severe channel fading. To cope with this challenge, the existing works primarily rely on the use of an orbital diversity technique where multiple satellites are involved to relay the same user's data. Nevertheless, such approach leads to a tremendous growth in the number of required satellites, which poses significant challenges for practical implementation. Motivated by the above observation, we propose a novel satellite networking protocol based on the concept of "tandem relays". Specifically, we introduce a new satellite role t

Radio advertising remains an integral part of modern marketing strategies, with its appeal and potential for effective brand exposure and engagement. However, in a highly competitive landscape, it's crucial to maximize the impact of radio ads and ensure they resonate with the intended audience. This is where voice-over artists come into play, providing the all-important human element that captivates listeners and drives desired outcomes. In this article, we explore why voice-overs are vital in radio advertising and how choosing the right voice-over talent can elevate your campaign's success. ## 1. The Human Connection: Voice-Over Artists as Brand Ambassadors Voice-over artists serve as the face of your brand when you cannot be there in person. They help establish a personal connection wit

In lung radiotherapy, infrared cameras can record the location of reflective objects on a patient’s skin. This allows for automatic tracking and real-time monitoring with respect to certain reference frames defined in preclinical treatment planning or simulation studies [1]. In this study we present an approach based upon image processing algorithms that automatically detects tumor positions from recorded images using intensity features extracted within user delineated regions surrounding each lesion site; such information is used as input data into our model which predicts where future locations will be located given knowledge about previous behavior patterns observed throughout prior sessions conducted under similar conditions (i .e., same radiation dose).

It has long been established that performance on natural language processing (NLP) tasks correlates with demographic factors such as gender and ethnicity. However, the impact of social class, a factor closely related to both gender and ethnicity, has not been thoroughly examined in NLP. To fill this gap, we introduce Classist Tools, a framework for examining the role of social class in NLP. We use educational attainment as a proxy for social class and explore its correlation with performance on various NLP tasks. We find that educational attainment correlates with performance on both binary classification and generation tasks. Furthermore, we show that the impact of class on performance is mediated by the degree of class bias in the training data. Our study highlights the importance of considering social class in NLP, as ignoring this factor can lead to an incomplete understanding of NLP model performance.

Peridynamics (PD), as a nonlocal theory, is well-suited for solving complex problems involving heterogeneous materials with irregular geometries. To accurately compute PD forces on such materials, efficient discretization schemes are needed. However, existing methods suffer from high computational cost and limited accuracy when applied to large systems. In this work, we propose an adaptive finite element method that allows us to efficiently solve the underlying peridynamic equations. Specifically, our approach involves adaptively refining the mesh in regions where significant deformation occurs. This results in improved accuracy while reducing computational complexity by up to 70%. Our numerical experiments demonstrate the effectiveness of our approach across different types of heterogeneo

Neural architecture search (NAS) is a challenging problem that aims to find the best neural network architecture for a given task. Recently, reinforcement learning (RL) based methods have shown a strong performance in NAS. However, RL methods require many evaluations to find good architectures and struggle with exploring the large search space. In this paper, we propose two novel NAS methods inspired by natural phenomena, particle swarm optimization (PSO) and ant colony optimization (ACO). These methods are meta-heuristic optimization techniques that do not require many evaluations to find good architectures. We first validate our methods on a simple benchmark problem, the one-dimensional function optimization problem, and a two-dimensional function optimization problem. The results show that our methods outperform the genetic algorithm (GA), one of the most popular NAS methods. We then evaluate our methods on the CIFAR-10 dataset. The results show that our methods also outperform the GA. Furthermore, we compare our methods with RL-based NAS methods for the first time. The results show that our methods perform competitively well with the RL-based methods. Our code is available at https://github.com/sohailakbari/PSO-ACO-NAS.

Image retrieval enables an efficient search through vast amounts of satellite imagery and returns similar images to a user. The challenge is that different users may require slightly differing features in their image, so the system must be flexible enough for each individual request while still maintaining accuracy across all requests at scale. We propose using Reinforcement Learning (RL) with Image Retrieval as its application domain; we call it RLIRR(Reinforcement learning applied on IR). Our solution comprises three components: 1.) A novel neural network model called Similarity Transformer Neural Network or STNN which learns similarity between two given objects by taking into account contextual information from neighboring pixels around those regions where similarities exist among them

Medical open-domain question answering demands substantial access to specialized knowledge. Recent efforts have sought to achieve this by integrating domain-specific knowledge into general language models (GLMs) via prompting. However, most existing methods employ a fixed prompt template during inference. To address this limitation, we present an adaptive prompting method that dynamically generates customized prompts for each input instance. Specifically, our approach leverages the model's internal representations as a proxy for domain-specific knowledge and employs reinforcement learning to optimize the prompt template generation process. Experiments on two medical QA benchmarks demonstrate the superiority of our approach over previous approaches. In particular, the best model achieves 47

Large language model (LLM) leads to a surge of autonomous GUI agents for smart devices. However, the existing LLM-based approaches suffer from high latency in real applications due that they need an extra step called tokenization before feeding text into LLMs and then parsing output results after querying them with texts containing tokens generated by human users or other bots through chat sessions between two parties on screen sharing platforms like Zoom/Microsoft Teams etc., which can cause serious problems if one party starts using abusive words during conversation because it will be difficult enough just identifying such cases without having any knowledge about what kindof keywords should trigger alerts within these systems beforehand so as not miss anything important while still keepi

Live programming provides feedback on run-time behavior by visualizing concrete values of expressions close to the source code. A common use case is live debugging, where developers can explore an execution trace of a program without stopping it and in the context that created this state (e. g., running environment). However current tools require manual effort from users such as explicitly configuring what should be visualized or selecting specific variables/expressions within each frame at runtime for visualization purposes only when there are more than two frames available; they also do not allow interactive exploration across multiple runs while still providing real time updates between iterations due limited memory capacity requirements imposed upon them via caching mechanism implement

Human brain and behavior provide a rich venue that can inspire novel control and learning methods for robot systems. The human-inspired algorithms are based on the neuroscience evidence of how humans perform complex tasks, such as cognitive task planning or social interaction with other agents (eukaryotic organisms). This thesis aims to develop new computational models inspired by biological mechanisms at different levels in order improve performance over traditional approaches while reducing energy consumption during execution time span from millisecond up into minutes depending upon application requirements which would help us understand better ways forward when designing future robotic platforms capable handling more sophisticated challenges found today world around them The developmen

Hyperproperties relate multiple executions of a program and are commonly used to specify security and information-flow properties. An important class of hyperproperties that can be expressed using hypergraphs is the interleaving property, which relates the executions of two programs. The interleaved program is the least restrictive hyperproperty with respect to the interleavability of the two programs. In this paper we present a new algorithm for computing all interleavings between two programs. Our algorithm is based on an extension of the existing method for computing the interleaving of a program and a constant. Our extension provides an easy way to extend the method to compute interleavings of more than two programs. As a consequence, our algorithm is also applicable to computing inter

Source-free model intellectual property (IP) protection aims to protect the intellectual property rights of model providers and prevent malicious attackers from obtaining the ownership information of the model. However, existing methods are vulnerable to advanced attacks. In this paper, we propose a novel MAsk-Pruning based source-free IP protection method, namely MAP, to provide robust protection for convolutional neural networks. Specifically, we introduce a mask-pruning mechanism to generate the pruned sub-networks from the original network and use these sub-networks to represent the original network. Since the mask-pruning mechanism introduces randomness, the generated sub-networks have high diversity. To further increase the difficulty for attackers, we use a hash-based scheme to map the diverse sub-networks to the same hash value. Thus, even if the attacker obtains the ownership information of some sub-networks, he cannot determine whether he has obtained the ownership of the original network. Extensive experiments show that MAP provides robust protection for models against strong attacks.

Ensuring Conditional Independence (CI) constraints is pivotal for the development of fair and trustworthy machine learning models. However, existing approaches often lack scalability, flexibility, or interpretability, which hinder their practical applications. In this paper, we propose a novel framework that leverages the power of continuous relaxations to achieve efficient and interpretable CI-guided model selection and tuning. Specifically, our method involves solving linear programming problems that are designed to ensure CI constraints while optimizing an objective function reflecting the performance of the machine learning model. The formulation provides a flexible approach for incorporating various CI constraints and allows for seamless integration with state-of-the-art neural networ

Federated learning (FL) is a popular distributed learning paradigm. Existing approaches, which often rely on gradient-based algorithms, have shown limitations in dealing with heterogeneous data across clients. Client-level aggregation in FL can significantly affect the model performance. To address this issue, we propose Solution Simplex Clustering (SSC), a novel federated learning method designed to improve model accuracy by effectively aggregating updates from clients with similar solutions. The proposed method first performs a clustering step to group clients with similar solutions into the same cluster. It then aggregates the gradients from clients within each cluster, rather than from all clients, to update the model. Each client maintains its local solution, allowing the model to benefit from diverse solutions across clients. The method, implemented on top of the FedAvg algorithm, is called FedSSC. Experimental results on Stackoverflow, a popular question-and-answer website for programmers, demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed method in improving model accuracy.

Optimizing the performance of many objectives (instantiated by tasks or clients) jointly is a common goal in multi-task learning, federate machine leaning and online/offline reinforcement. In this work we propose to approach it from an optimization perspective: given $T$ independent copies $\mathcal{D}_1,\dotsc\dot{\rm D}_{t}$, each consisting on two datasets with labels sampled independently according to some distribution function ($X_i^l \sim f(x)$), find weights that minimize average loss for all datapoints while maximizing accuracy across classes; i..e., maximize class diversity among losses using these samples only as inputs without any additional information about their label distributions (\textit{$f$-diversity}).

We study the finite-time convergence of TD learning with linear function approximation under Markovian sampling. For this setting, we prove that a regret bound is achievable without any assumptions on (a) number or cardinality in feature space; and it suffices to have $\mathcal{O}(n^{1/2}\tau^3)$ samples for an $L$-Lipschitz target value if there are at most ${\cal O}(\log n / \sqrt{\delta})$ linearly independent features among them ($\epsilon=\frac\gamma L$). Our analysis relies upon exploiting properties from convex geometry such as Farkas' Lemma together using techniques based around Lyapunov stability theory -- both novelties when applied towards proving nonasymptotic bounds via stochastic differential equations approach used here specifically tailored toward TDAF framework --- which a

Neighborhood attention is an essential component of computer vision models. It computes the attention between all pairs of elements within a local neighborhood, specified by a kernel size. Neighborhood attention has a quadratic time complexity with the number of elements in the neighborhood, which limits its application to large models. This paper proposes a novel algorithm that reduces the time complexity of neighborhood attention from O(n^2) to O(n), by eliminating the need of computing unnecessary pairwise interactions. The proposed algorithm, called Fast Neighborhood Attention (FNA), achieves this by sorting query and key values in each threadblock, and then only interacting with potentially matching elements. FNA is able to recover the performance of the baseline algorithm when the kernel size is small, while significantly outperforming the baseline when the kernel size is large. We evaluate FNA on masked image modeling and semantic segmentation tasks. FNA enables the use of large models for these tasks, without any loss in accuracy.

The third ML4H symposium was held in person on December 10, 29 and at the University of Basel. The event brought together more than fifty researchers from various fields to discuss recent advances related with machine learning for healthcare (MLHC) applications as well their potential challenges facing future research directions within this domain specifically targeting biomedical data sets including both structured/unstructured information sources such electronic health records or genomic sequencing datasets respectively This year's theme focused primarily around topics like personalized medicine - which combines different types into one treatment plan tailored towards each individual patient based upon his genetic makeup rather instead following standard protocol across board regardless

Diffusion-weighted Magnetic Resonance Imaging (dMRI) is a powerful imaging technique used to investigate brain white matter microstructure. However, its application has been limited by the time required for acquisition and processing of such data sets in order obtain high spatial resolution images that are suitable as input into computational models describing microstructural properties within tissue at various scales; this limitation can be overcome using accelerated methods which allow fast image reconstruction without compromising quality or accuracy when compared with conventional approaches based on full size datasets .

Fifth generation (5G) mobile networks have begun to be deployed around the world. 5G networks are expected to bring significant improvements compared to previous generations, including much higher data throughput for mobile users, support for a much larger number of connected devices, and improved location services. 5G networks incorporate a number of new technologies, many of which are aimed at facilitating the deployment of new services. One such technology is the 5G Core (5GC) network, which provides the backhaul infrastructure required to support new services. The 5GC network incorporates a number of web-based technologies, including the use of the HTTP/2 protocol and the adoption of digital certificates to secure network communications. In this paper we report the results of our penetration testing of 5GC network components running on a live 5G network. We were able to identify a number of vulnerabilities, including improper handling of user input, insufficient input validation, and insecure cryptographic practices. We also report on the configuration weaknesses that we identified, including default admin passwords, open Telnet ports, and misconfigured firewalls. We have worked with the operators of the 5G network to help them mitigate the risks associated with the vulnerabilities that we discovered.

We propose an approach to scaling up abstract visual reasoning to larger problems. We observe that the application of attention mechanisms to visual reasoning tasks yields a sparse interaction between the objects in the scene and the slots in the question. We leverage this observation to propose a novel two-stage architecture for visual reasoning. The first stage, the slot abstractor, observes the scene and the question, and proposes a small number of slots that are potentially relevant to the question. The second stage, the reasoning module, reasons over the slots produced by the slot abstractor and answers the question. We show that this approach is more efficient in terms of computation and memory usage, while achieving comparable performance to the baseline model on Visual Reasoning (VR) and Visual Entailment (VE) tasks. We further show that the slot abstractor can be repurposed for different questions, reducing the inference time per question. Our two-stage model achieves a 4x faster inference on VR and a 7x faster inference on VE while achieving comparable performance to the baseline model.

Continual Learning (CL) aims to learn continuously from a sequence of tasks without forgetting previous knowledge. Few Shot Continual Learning (FSCL) focuses on the scenario where each task only provides few training examples. Previous memory-based FSCL methods usually store the representations of training data in memory and perform poorly when the data similarity between memory and current task is low. To address this challenge, we propose a Contrastive Augmented Graph2Graph Memory Interaction (CAGM) model, which enhances the memory interaction by introducing a contrastive learning mechanism that compares the representations of data from the current task and the previous tasks. Specifically, we design a graph-based memory network that consists of a main memory and an auxiliary memory. The main memory stores the representations of training data, while the auxiliary memory stores the updated representations of data from the current task and the previous tasks. To improve the model's generalizability, we introduce a contrastive augmentation module that generates augmented representations by randomly erasing and replacing subgraphs in the graph-based memory. To facilitate the comparison between the representations of data from different tasks, we employ a dynamic soft graph view that dynamically highlights the important subgraph views for comparison. Experimental results demonstrate that our proposed CAGM model achieves state-of-the-art performance on few shot continual learning tasks.

Deep Learning models have been successfully utilized to extract clinically actionable insights from routinely collected healthcare data. While these models provide accurate predictions, they are often black boxes in terms of explainability, making it difficult for clinicians to interpret their results and use them effectively in decision-making processes. This paper introduces a novel approach that combines the predictive power of Deep Learning with the explainability of Symbolic Models, specifically Decision Trees, to generate explanations for complex predictions made by Deep Learning models. We utilize the symbolic model to find the most important features contributing to a prediction, and then use this information to create a visual representation of how the Deep Learning model makes it

The Mutual Reinforcement Effect (MRE) investigates the synergistic relationship between word embeddings and graph convolutions in text classification. Recent studies have shown that MRE can be improved by increasing the number of trainable parameters, but the effectiveness of this approach remains unclear. In addition, most previous work on MRE has focused on using a single model with two different sets of parameters, while the potential for combining multiple models to enhance MRE is overlooked. To address these issues, we propose three novel approaches: 1) Enhanced MRE, which includes fine-tuning the pre-trained BERT models with additional task-specific parameters; 2) Combined MRE, which involves applying multiple MRE-enhancing methods simultaneously; 3) Cross-Model MRE, which explores h

The security of cloud field-programmable gate arrays (FPGAs) faces challenges from both the FPGA hardware and software. In this paper, we propose a novel attack model called side-channel cache attacks against FPGA accelerators in cloud. This model assumes that an adversary can read data from caches but cannot modify them. We first demonstrate that such a side-channel cache attack is feasible and practical by attacking a benchmark deep learning model accelerated on FPGAs. Our proposed attacks achieve a success rate of over 90% with low overhead. To the best of our knowledge, this work is the first to apply cache attack techniques to the FPGA domain. Based on this, we design and implement CACHETHIEF, a side-channel cache attack framework for FPGA accelerators.

Hyperspectral images (HSI) clustering is an important but challenging task. The goal of this task is to cluster HSIs into different groups, called classes, in order to facilitate the subsequent data analysis and interpretation. This paper presents a novel method for the HSIs clustering task based on graph convolutional networks. The proposed method uses an efficient architecture that can process large HSIs with low computational complexity. It utilizes the spectral information provided by HSIs to construct a node-level graph and adopts a graph convolutional network to learn representations from the graph structure. To enhance the performance of our model, we propose two new techniques: 1) Spectral Graph Convolution; and 2) Graph Attention Network. These techniques enable our model to extra

The safety and efficiency of modern air traffic management (ATM) heavily rely on the accurate prediction of aircraft trajectories. The traditional approach of trajectory prediction is to use the state-of-the-art machine learning models, which, however, may suffer from a lack of diversity and completeness of the available flight trajectory datasets. In this work, we present a multi-modal aircraft trajectory dataset, TartanAviation, which includes 1) 4077 high-resolution images of aircraft taken at Glasgow Airport, UK, 2) 1175 audio recordings of aircraft taking off and landing at Glasgow Airport, and 3) 10777 aircraft trajectories obtained from the Automatic Dependent Surveillance-Broadcast (ADS-B) system. The data are provided in the form of pre-extracted features, which include radar observations, metadata, and signal processing results. We evaluate the usefulness of the proposed dataset for the task of trajectory prediction using four state-of-the-art machine learning models, namely Long-Short-Term Memory (LSTM), Multivariate Linear Regression (MLR), Feedforward Neural Network (FNN), and XGBoost. The results demonstrate the superiority of LSTM and XGBoost over the other models, with LSTM achieving a Percentage of Correct Trajectories (PCT) of 82.6% against 77.1% for XGBoost, 59.7% for FNN, and 47.9% for MLR. We also investigate the effect of training a trajectory prediction model using data from a single source (i.e., image, speech, or ADS-B) and from a combination of sources. Our findings show that 1) using data from a single source results in a PCT lower than 60%, 2) combining image and speech data improves the PCT by up to 17.8 percentage points, and 3) combining image, speech, and ADS-B data results in the highest PCT of 87.4%.

Distance metric learning (DML) plays a crucial role in many machine learning tasks. The goal of DML is to learn a distance metric that can effectively capture the similarity or dissimilarity between data samples. In this paper, we propose a novel DML model based on variational information bottleneck (VIB). The VIB-DML model learns a distance metric by maximizing the mutual information between the latent representation and the distance between data samples. We show that our proposed VIB-DML model achieves state-of-the-art performance on standard DML benchmark tasks. Furthermore, we also show that the VIB-DML model can be applied to semi-supervised clustering. The experimental results demonstrate that the VIB-DML model outperforms the existing DML models for the clustering task.

Although person or identity verification has been predominantly explored using individual modalities such as face, voice, gait and keystroke dynamics, multi-modal biometrics have become an increasingly important area of research. The purpose of this work is to compare a wide variety of state-of-the-art multi-modal biometric authentication systems that leverage facial images along with other modalities such as textual passwords, voice recordings, handwritten signatures, touchscreen patterns, and electrocardiograms (ECG). To do so, we conduct a thorough study on the performance of these methods across 13 publicly available datasets. We provide extensive analyses of these results in order to shed light on their strengths and weaknesses. Specifically, we consider the effects of various experim

In recent years, Cross-Domain Recommendation (CDR) has drawn significant attention, and its applications in online advertising have become increasingly prominent. The main challenge in CDR lies in the disparities between user behavior patterns across domains, which hinder existing recommendation methods from producing accurate recommendations. This paper introduces a novel approach that leverages the strengths of Large Language Models (LLMs), effectively addressing this issue by seamlessly fusing domain-specific information into the user behavior model. Our method first transforms the raw user interaction data into natural language descriptions, thereby enabling LLM to acquire knowledge regarding domain characteristics and extract salient features, such as user preferences and domain prope

This paper investigates the cybersecurity competence of older adult users of mobile devices, a population that is increasingly at risk due to its growing use of mobile devices for communication and finance. A questionnaire was developed and distributed to older adults attending a University of California extension course for non-tech-savvy individuals. The questionnaire was designed to assess participants' knowledge and attitudes towards cybersecurity, their device security practices, and their experiences with cybercrime. A total of 142 participants completed the questionnaire; 57% were female, and 43% were male, with an average age of 73.0 years (SD = 14.8). Results indicate that participants displayed low levels of cybersecurity knowledge and adopted few secure practices. They also reported experiencing higher levels of cybercrime victimisation, particularly from scammers posing as grandsons or granddaughters. Participants' attitudes towards cybersecurity were generally positive, and they reported a high level of concern about their personal safety. However, they displayed a low level of concern about their financial security. Findings highlight the need for targeted education programmes to improve older adults' cybersecurity competence and secure device practices.

QRscript is a domain-specific language for the specification of decision trees. In its original formulation, QRscript is a concise and compact language, but this results in a considerable distance between the specification and the algorithm. This paper presents QRtree, a new formulation of QRscript based on a more traditional dialect of decision trees, where the structural elements of the tree are explicitly shown. We present an automatic translation from QRscript to QRtree and vice-versa. We also show that, although QRtree is a richer language than QRscript, the two languages define the same set of trees. QRtree has been implemented as an extension of the Q programming language. An extensive set of experiments is conducted to evaluate the new formulation and the implementation.

Compliant grippers enable robots to work with humans in unstructured environments. In this paper, we introduce a new class of compliant parallel jaws that are capable of safely grasping and manipulating objects without the need for explicit shape-based detection or control strategies. We leverage the inherent friction of the gripper in combination with a compliant design, which enables us to perform reliable object grasping while avoiding excessive contact forces. To validate our approach, we conduct two experiments: a benchmarking experiment that evaluates the performance of the gripper on a diverse set of objects, and a real-world experiment involving the safe pick-and-place of fragile objects by human operators. Our results show that the proposed gripper is capable of reliably grasping

This paper explores the detection of children's age groups based on data collected from a human-computer interaction experiment. The data consists of timestamps of interactions between children and a tablet computer, as well as accelerometric data from a wearable device. The goal is to infer the age group of the child using this data. The detection of age groups is important for the personalization of educational content and adaptive interfaces. We propose a methodology that combines interaction data and accelerometric data to achieve better performance in the detection of age groups. The interaction data is processed to obtain time series of the distance between the child and the tablet, as well as the activity level. The accelerometric data is processed to obtain the activity level. The time series are analyzed using statistical features, and machine learning classifiers are applied to distinguish between the age groups. The results show that the classifiers perform better using the interaction data than using the accelerometric data. Additionally, the classifiers achieve higher accuracy when using both interaction and accelerometric data. The methodology can be improved by collecting data from more children and by using more sophisticated methods for analyzing the data.

3D object detection based on LiDAR point cloud and prior anchor boxes is a critical technology in autonomous driving. The challenge lies in the limited resolution of LiDAR sensors, which leads to a trade-off between precision and recall when performing object detection. In this paper, we propose a novel method called DETector by integrating the point cloud with 2D images for enhancing 3D object detection performance. Specifically, we first construct a point cloud supervision network to improve the point cloud feature representation ability. Then, a point cloud-image fusion module is proposed to integrate point clouds and images for better localization. Additionally, we introduce a point cloud-based anchoring approach that utilizes point cloud features to generate anchors with precise bound

We present a comprehensive statistical analysis of the novel "Perekhresni stezhky" ("The Cross-Paths") by the prominent Ukrainian writer Ivan Franko. The analysis is performed on the word level, and it reveals interesting information about the text, such as the most frequent words, the word length distribution, the most frequent n-grams, the trigram that appears most frequently in the novel, and many other characteristics. Our study is an example of how computational methods can be used to analyze literary works, and it provides valuable insights into the language and style of Ivan Franko.

We examine the security of a multi-input multi-output (MIMO) wireless network that employs movable antennas. We assume that the spatial location of each antenna is kept secret and that an eavesdropper tries to determine these locations by observing the phase shifts of the received signals. We show that the eavesdropper cannot determine the locations of the antennas if the number of antennas is at least as large as the number of eavesdropping sensors. We also show that the maximum likelihood estimator of the antenna locations is given by the solution of a convex optimization problem. Finally, we examine the problem of securing a MIMO communication link against a deterministic eavesdropper that knows the channel state information (CSI) of the legitimate user. We show that the legitimate user can secure the MIMO communication link by moving its antennas in a way that makes the CSI unavailable to the eavesdropper.

Mathematical capabilities were previously believed to emerge in common language models only at a very large scale or in fine-tuning scenarios. Despite recent studies demonstrating that simple and efficient mathematical models can be developed with a few thousand parameters, the exact computational bottleneck for these capabilities remains unclear. In this work, we uncover a fundamental limitation of current mathematics modules: they are unable to perform algebraic operations on high-dimensional vectors. We show that existing modules can perform complex vector arithmetic when restricted to a low-dimensional space. To address this, we propose a simple yet effective method that leverages the intrinsic geometry of vectors to transform complex vector arithmetic into simpler scalar multiplicatio

Zero-shot image captioning (IC) without well-paired image-text data can effectively improve the model's generalization ability and alleviate the labor costs of annotating image-text pairs. However, it is often not easy to directly apply the mainstream pre-trained language models (PLMs) to zero-shot IC due to their low image-text alignment capability. In this paper, we propose a novel method, namely ICToPLM, to align image-text modality in PLMs by a multi-modal contrastive learning (MC) strategy. Specifically, we first generate pseudo-image-text data through text-to-image generation, then use CLIP as the initial alignment for these data, and finally employ MC strategy to learn high-quality representations from these data. Finally, we apply these representations to the base PLM using knowled

Currently, infrared imaging technology enjoys widespread usage, with infrared cameras being an important part of this technology. However, the current infrared camera systems are not capable of capturing 360-degree images of large-area scenes, which limits their applications in autonomous driving and robotic vision. This paper introduces a novel method for capturing full-field infrared images by integrating a customized panoramic lens into existing infrared cameras. The proposed design addresses two critical challenges associated with full-field infrared imaging: vignetting due to the limited view angle of conventional lenses and distortion caused by the uneven distribution of infrared light across different angles.

Distributed stream processing frameworks help building scalable and reliable applications that perform transformations and aggregations on continuous data streams. In the context of edge computing, processing large-scale data generated by IoT devices is crucial to gain insights into their behaviour and enhance their performance. However, processing such a large amount of data within the limited resources available in distributed edge environments poses significant challenges. This paper introduces KODIL, an open source framework for developing scalable and fault-tolerant streaming applications. It proposes a novel data partitioning approach based on the similarity between data points. The experimental results show a reduction of 20% in the number of partitions compared to the traditional a

This study investigates the application of Nash equilibrium strategies in optimizing forest harvesting decisions. In our analysis, we assume that a forest owner has to decide whether to harvest or not a certain segment in a given period under specific forest conditions and constraints. We develop an optimization model to identify the optimal harvesting strategy based on the expected profit for each harvested segment. The model takes into account the different possible forest states at the end of the harvest season, as well as the impact of other forest owners' actions. To determine the optimal strategy for each forest owner, we employ the concept of Nash equilibrium strategies, which allows us to consider multiple forest owners interacting with each other through their harvesting decisions

The Attention mechanism is the main component of the Transformer architecture, and since its introduction, it has been applied to many language-related tasks. In this article, we present a novel attention mechanism called "Cuboid" that can handle multiple contexts (i.e., queries) in parallel at a constant complexity. While existing attention mechanisms are inherently sequential in nature and scale linearly with the number of contexts, our proposed Cuboid attention enables the processing of all contexts simultaneously, leading to significant computational efficiency gains. We also introduce two variants of the Cuboid attention: "Sparse Cuboid" and "Dense Cuboid". These two variants significantly reduce the memory footprint while maintaining high accuracy. Experimental results on several ben

The vast accumulation of neuroscience knowledge presents a challenge for researchers to timely and accuratly extract relevant information from scientific literature. This work introduces NEUROSUMM, a novel large-scale dataset of neuroscience literature that provides summaries of the content of individual papers in the form of simple yes/no questions. With 5 million question-answer pairs across 100 thousand papers, it is one order of magnitude larger than previous datasets with similar goals. We use the data to demonstrate state-of-the-art summarization models that can effectively answer neuroscience questions about both content and methods. We also show how NEUROSUMM can be used for training summarizers on specific research domains or subtopics. We release all data and code at https://gith

Estimating gaze direction from facial images is a common task in human-computer interaction. Recent advances in this field have been driven by deep neural networks, which have achieved suprathreshold performance in gaze estimation. However, they require illumination information, are sensitive to occlusions, and perform poorly in poorly lit environments. To address these limitations, we turn to event cameras, which measure per-pixel illumination changes and are invariant to global illumination changes. Thanks to their asynchronous nature and low consumption, they are increasingly used in wearable devices. However, their sparse and temporally coded nature poses challenges for standard neural networks, which have poor performance and stability. To overcome these issues, we propose a novel event-driven network that leverages the temporal encoding of changes in illumination to estimate gaze direction. We show that our method outperforms recent state-of-the-art methods in terms of accuracy, stability, and convergence speed. Our code is publicly available.

We study the limits and capability of public-data assisted differentially private (PA-DP) learning. Our first result shows that PA-DP is essentially optimal in its ability to learn a function $f$ over an input distribution $\mathcal{D}$: it requires $\Omega(\frac{\sqrt{n}}{\varepsilon}\cdot \operatorname{dist}(f,\mathcal{D}))$ samples to recover $f$. This is tight up to logarithmic factors, given that DP requires at least $\Omega(1/\varepsilon)$ samples for any function. However, this leaves open the possibility that we can do better by using more informative data, or exploiting additional assumptions on $f$.

Current state-of-the-art (SOTA) 3D object detection methods often face the challenge of producing overly complex and uninterpretable models. In this paper, we propose a novel model pruning framework for 3D detection, dubbed ProPriNAS. Our approach leverages the recent advancements in neuromorphic computing to realize a more efficient and effective 3D detector. We first introduce the concept of neuromorphic priors into neural architecture search by incorporating neuromorphic characteristics into the network structure. Through extensive experiments, we demonstrate that our method significantly outperforms the baseline model by an average improvement of 1.2% mAP on KITTI benchmarks while reducing the number of parameters by 75%. Additionally, we show that the proposed model has better general

We introduce Mars 2.0 for modeling, analysis, verification and code generation of Cylic Timed Automata (CTA) as well a variant with priority constraints called Priority-Aware Cyclic Timers automata(PACTAs). We extend the traditional CTA model in two ways: by providing support to models whose time is periodic but not necessarily constant;and second we enrich our timing language so that it includes an additional class which enables usto capture timed transitions triggered when specific conditions are met on multiple inputs or outputs at once within certain bounds instead just one input/output pair like most existing versions do today .

Large Language Models (LLMs) have shown impressive results on various tasks. Unfortunately, LLMs can produce incorrect results without any warning. Moreover, it is not practical to manually verify the results of LLMs on code frequently. We propose CatCode, a comprehensive evaluation framework for LLMs on the mixture of code and text. Suppose an LLM is used in a code autocomplete scenario. In that case, CatCode can automatically generate test cases for the completed code snippets and verify the correctness of the snippets in a unified manner without any manual intervention. To the best of our knowledge, no existing work simultaneously addresses the correctness and coverage of LLMs' results on the mixture of code and text. We evaluate CatCode on a large-scale real-world dataset containing millions of code snippets from GitHub. Moreover, we benchmark a wide range of LLMs, including GPT-3, GPT-4, and various code autocomplete models. We detect numerous bugs in the LLMs' results on the mixture of code and text. We also find that the LLMs often fail to generate correct code snippets for complex functions. We hope our framework can help the LLM developers to better understand their models' strengths and weaknesses on the mixture of code and text.

The complexity of visual stimuli plays an important role in many cognitive phenomena, including attention, engagement, memorability, time perception and aesthetic evaluation. Despite its importance, complexity is poorly understood and ironically, previous models of image complexity have been quite \textit{complex}. There have been many attempts to find handcrafted features that explain complexity, but these features are usually dataset specific, and hence fail to generalise. On the other hand, more recent work has employed deep neural networks to predict complexity, but these models remain difficult to interpret, and do not guide a theoretical understanding of the problem. Here we propose to model complexity using segment-based representations of images. We use state-of-the-art segmentatio

Large Language Models (LLMs) like ChatGPT, Google Bard, and Microsoft’s Bing are all the rage. LLMs can be used for a variety of tasks such as answering questions or generating code in Python—but they also have some well-known limitations: * They often make up answers or hallucinate them; and/OR • Their outputs may contain harmful content that'd rather not see proliferate on our platforms by means other than their own API access controls but it is important to understand how these systems work so we can know where things go wrong when using AI tools!

Real-world systems are often formulated as constrained optimization problems. Techniques to incorporate constraints into Neural Networks (NN), such as Neural Ordinary Differential Equations (Neural ODEs), have been used. However, these introduce hyperparameters that require manual tuning through trial and error, raising doubts about the successful incorporation of constraints into the generated model. This paper describes in detail the two-stage training method for Neural ODEs, a simple, effective, and penalty parameter-free approach to model constrained systems. In this approach the constrained optimization problem is rewritten as two unconstrained sub-problems that are solved in two stages. The first stage aims at finding feasible NN parameters by minimizing a measure of constraints viol

Patient-specific modeling is an emerging paradigm in medical image analysis with vast potential in supporting clinical decision-making. This task, however, is confronted with the challenge of limited availability of annotated data. In this work, we propose a novel self-supervised learning framework towards 3D patient modeling by harnessing multi-modal attentive fusion. Specifically, we introduce a cross-modal attention guidance strategy to facilitate information exchange and knowledge sharing between different modalities. Further, a mask-guided volume-to-volume learning approach is proposed to leverage the complementary strengths of different representations and suppress the potential interference. Based on the unified multi-modal learning paradigm, a modality-specific feature distillation module is designed to promote knowledge sharing while preserving the modality-specific information. Extensive experiments on synthetic and real-world datasets demonstrate that the proposed method outperforms state-of-the-art self-supervised learning approaches for 3D patient modeling. Furthermore, the experiments on large-scale real-world datasets showcase that the proposed method can be scaled up to handle big datasets efficiently.

This paper studies how large language models (LLMs) can act as effective, high-level data generators for the production of synthetic data sets. We find that LLMs are effective at generating synthetic data that preserves statistical properties of the real-world data set. For example, we show that LLMs trained on a standard tabular data set can be used to generate synthetic versions of each column in the original table. The resulting generated tables are highly accurate with respect to the original data set and retain many of its statistical properties. Our results highlight that LLMs have considerable potential to play an important role in the production of high-quality synthetic data. We additionally provide insights into how this process can be improved further through the use of prompts

Stance detection of social media text is a key component of downstream tasks involving the identification of groups with different opinions. Large pre-trained language models (PLMs) have become popular in recent years due to their ability to quickly process vast amounts of data and generate high-quality outputs. However, many PLMs struggle to generalize across domains due to their lack of fine-tuning capabilities. In this study, we introduce a novel approach for stance detection that leverages fine-tuned PLMs trained on large datasets. Our method consists of two stages: 1) fine-tuning the PLM using a set of domain-specific stance detection datasets; and 2) predicting stances by averaging scores from multiple PLMs finetuned on each dataset. We evaluate our method on three different datasets

Suppose we are given a text $T [1..n]$, a straight-line program with $N$ instructions, and an instruction numbering. We would like to compute the value of $T$ by evaluating $P$. Unfortunately, for certain programs it is not possible to evaluate $P$ in polynomial time. This motivates the study of efficient computation of the value of $T$. We give several efficient algorithms for this problem. The first one takes advantage of the fact that there is an efficient algorithm for computing the sum of two numbers on which we can efficiently apply the program $P$. Our second algorithm uses the notion of linear homomorphism to build a solution of $P$ from a solution of its dual. Finally, we give an algorithm for computing the value of $T$ using the same data structure as the algorithm by Grohe and T

Linking unseen (i.e., unknown, novel) security entities is a crucial task in various security applications. For example, linking unknown malware samples to known malware families can facilitate threat intelligence sharing and improve the effectiveness of existing defense mechanisms. Due to their unknown nature, however, unseen entities cannot be linked using conventional methods that rely on known attributes. To address this novel setting, we propose a novel Linking Unseen Entities (LUE) problem and present a solution named MUSE. MUSE learns a representation space that enables unseen entities to be linked based on their inherent properties, which are derived from self-supervised entity embeddings. We evaluate MUSE on three security datasets (i.e., malware, phishing URLs, and malicious domains) that contain both seen and unseen entities. Experimental results demonstrate the effectiveness of MUSE for the LUE problem. In particular, MUSE achieves a 4.5% improvement in linking unseen malware samples to known malware families compared to state-of-the-art baselines.

Vision-and-Language Navigation (VLN) has gained significant research interest in recent years, and many new methods have been proposed to tackle this challenging task. However, there remains a lack of unified benchmark for the existing methods, which leads to difficulty in comparing their performance. In this paper, we present VLNBench, a comprehensive benchmark for evaluating all the published VLN approaches on a common platform. VLNBench consists of 15 VLN tasks from various datasets, including 3D navigation, image navigation, and real-world navigation. For each task, we provide an evaluation toolkit that allows for automatic evaluation of the model performance based on different metrics. We also provide additional data augmentation strategies to help improve the generalization ability o

Parametric language models (LMs), which are trained on vast amounts of web data, exhibited a tendency to generate factually incorrect content and biased responses. The recent emergence of domain-specific LMs has demonstrated the potential for more accurate factual knowledge generation. However, due to the lack of high-quality data, most domain-specific LMs are limited to small domains, such as e-commerce descriptions, and thus have limited applications. In this paper, we introduce KG-LM, a general-purpose large LM with knowledge graph (KG) pretraining that generates facts with high accuracy and balanced bias.
