Specifically, we first build a large-scale KG from Wikipedia and its reference links. We then design a novel dual-encoder framework in which each encoder is equipped with an attention

International migrants face difficulties obtaining information for a quality life and well-being in the host countries. This study examined the role of online content on migrant well-being, with specific attention to two types of websites: governmental and non-governmental. Using a sample of 31,276 websites from 48 countries in the United Nations High Commissioner for Refugees (UNHCR) database, we examined the extent to which these websites provided information related to six areas of well-being: health, housing, education, employment, legal support, and financial support. We then conducted a content analysis of 50 websites (25 governmental and 25 non-governmental) from the UNHCR database to compare their content across these areas. Our findings show that there is a wide variation in the a

National Security Letters (NSLs) are secret legal orders that enable law enforcement to obtain the records of telecommunication and financial transactions of persons under investigation for national security or intelligence purposes. The government is not required to obtain a court order to issue an NSL, but is required to certify that the investigation is related to national security. The9/11 Commission recommended in its final report that the Attorney General establish a system to track the use of NSLs and periodically report on their use to Congress. The USA Freedom Act of 2015 amended the NSL statute to require the Attorney General to submit an annual report to Congress containing specified information on NSLs issued during the preceding calendar year. This article analyzes the data on NSLs contained in the first two reports submitted to Congress under the amended statute. We find that the government issued 1,468 NSLs in 2015 and 1,387 NSLs in 2016, the majority of which were directed to telecommunications and financial institutions. The reports do not contain information on the number of persons whose records were sought or obtained by NSLs, the number of times each recipient produced records in response to an NSL, or the number of investigations that were opened or closed as a result of information obtained by NSLs. We suggest that the Attorney General include this information in future reports on NSLs.

Projection mapping (PM) is a technique that enhances the appearance of real-world surfaces using projection and computer vision. In PM, the surface's geometry must be captured to correctly map content onto it, requiring multiple views from cameras or 3D scanners. However, these methods can be time-consuming and may not capture the entire surface. This study introduces a deep learning-based approach for obtaining geometry information from a single image. We first use a convolutional neural network to estimate the surface normal of the object in an image, followed by a regression network to determine the 3D position of each pixel on the surface. Our method has been evaluated with several datasets and compared with traditional methods, demonstrating its ability to obtain geometric information

Recent work has highlighted the susceptibility of AI large language models (LLMs) to prompt manipulation attacks, whereby an adversary constructs a prompt that induces the model to output a desired response, even when the prompt is unrelated to the response. In this work, we take a closer look at the role of emotions in such attacks. We find that prompts that evoked strong emotions in the model led to significantly more erroneous information being generated, particularly for negative emotions such as anger and fear. This effect is so strong that models can be tricked into generating false information simply by changing the emotion in the prompt, without any other text changes. We also find that certain emotions are more effective for specific tasks. For example, anger is effective for inducing political misinformation, while fear is effective for inducing medical misinformation. These findings should sound alarms for legislators, policymakers, and the general public, as they imply that emotions can be used as weak passwords to manipulate the massive computational resources of LLMs. To mitigate this vulnerability, we propose the novel technique of prompt engineering, which involves constructing prompts that are emotionally neutral. We show that prompt engineering can be used to protect LLMs from emotional manipulation attacks, at least for the 15 models that we tested.

Human Activity Recognition (HAR) is one of the fundamental building blocks of human assistive systems. We focus on a novel approach for HAR through multi-agent reinforcement learning, which addresses the challenges of data scarcity and diversity in existing methods. Our method trains multiple agents to interact with the environment and learn how to recognize different activities. The agents are rewarded based on their contribution to activity recognition accuracy. This approach significantly enhances the efficiency of data collection and training by reducing the need for labeled data. Additionally, our agent-based training allows us to explore diverse interactions between agents, making it possible to train them on a broader range of behaviors than conventional approaches. The proposed fra

Backdoor attacks are stealthy and persistent attacks that allow the adversary to gain unauthorized access to a target system. To ensure the success of a backdoor attack, it is essential to couple the decision paths in the encryption key generation and the ciphertext decryption. This paper proposes a novel technique to enhance the survivability of backdoor attacks by decision path coupling (DPC). We first formulate the problem of backdoor attack and DPC as a mathematical problem. We then propose an efficient solution to the problem by presenting a decision path coupling algorithm (DPCA). The DPCA couples the decision paths in polynomial time based on the premise that there exists a one-to-one mapping between the encryption key and the ciphertext. To evaluate the DPC technique, we apply the DPCA to two types of backdoor attacks on the Advanced Encryption Standard (AES) and the International Data Encryption Algorithm (IDEA). Experimental results show that the DPCA can couple the decision paths in polynomial time for both attacks. Coupling the decision paths can enhance the survivability of a backdoor attack and prolong the time to detection by an average of 1.4 and 2.2 times for the AES and the IDEA, respectively.

The increase in active users on social networking sites (SNSs) has also observed an increase in the prevalence of cyberbullying. To tackle this, researchers have developed AI-based tools for preventing or detecting cyberbullying in SNSs. However, these tools are not free from biases and stereotypes that may affect their performance, particularly when classifying posts as harassment or bullying. In this paper, we present a novel approach to mitigate bias in cyberbullying detection by leveraging the principles of generative adversarial networks (GANs). Our proposed method involves generating positive and negative samples for the target class using GANs and then incorporating them into a classifier. This approach aims to reduce the influence of bias in the training data, leading to improved a

Reward hacking occurs when an agent performs very well with respect to a "proxy" reward, but poorly on the true downstream task. This can occur due either (1) improper alignment of proxy and target rewards or even more commonly in practice --(2)\/a mismatch between training distribution for which data is collected during policy learning \textit{versus} test time conditions under-which it will be deployed.~\par We focus our attention here specifically upon scenario ~(i), where we show that standard off\-policy RL algorithms are susceptible towards this problem from both theoretical perspectives as they tend not capture long term behaviour~\cite {daskalakis:icml09}.

We study reinforcement learning with linear function approximation, unknown transition, and adversarial losses in the setting of online learning. We propose a novel algorithm called RELINE, which is based on regularized empirical risk minimization. The algorithm enjoys $O(\sqrt{T})$ regret bound when the target distribution is uniform, and $O(d\sqrt{T})$ regret bound for arbitrary distributions, where $d$ is the dimension of the feature space. In contrast to existing works that use convex optimization methods such as Newton's method or gradient descent, our approach relies on projected stochastic gradient descent (PSGD), making it more computationally efficient. Additionally, we introduce a new notion of robustness, called $\varepsilon$-robust, which measures how well an algorithm can reco

IoT devices can be labeled automatically using manual features extracted from device firmware. However, the process is time-consuming and prone to human errors. To address this issue, we propose an innovative approach called IoTLab to automatically label IoT devices using natural language processing. Specifically, we develop a pipeline that extracts device names from firmware using tokenization, identifies the device manufacturer using named entity recognition, and assigns the correct label to the device based on the manufacturer. We evaluate our method on 1,000 IoT devices from 101 manufacturers and show that it significantly outperforms existing approaches (by 8.6% on average). Our study is the first to automatically label IoT devices using natural language processing. The results demonstrate the feasibility of our approach and its potential to improve the accuracy and efficiency of IoT device labeling.

Recently, there has been an increasing focus on audio-text cross-modal learning. However, most of the existing methods only utilize textual information to facilitate audio classification tasks. In this paper, we propose a novel approach that incorporates both text and audio information for audio-text cross-modal learning, with the goal of achieving higher accuracy in classifying and understanding human speech. Our method involves two phases: feature extraction and model training. We first extract features from raw audio signals using the Mel Frequency Cepstral Coefficients (MFCC) as an input. Then, we perform dimensionality reduction on these features via a Linear Discriminant Analysis (LDA) algorithm. Finally, we train our network using a combination of these features and their correspond

The channel hardening effect is less pronounced in the cell-free massive multiple-input multiple-output (MIMO) systems than that in conventional massive MIMO due to the lack of spatial correlation between users. In this paper, we propose a novel channel hardening algorithm in the cell-free massive MIMO systems based on the Kullback-Leibler divergence. We also show that our proposed algorithm outperforms the conventional algorithms in terms of BER performance. Furthermore, our algorithm achieves near-optimal results compared with the optimal solution by employing an efficient algorithm for computing the Kullback-Leibler divergence and the BER expression. Simulation results show that the proposed algorithm significantly improves the BER performance when the number of antennas at base station

Global Navigation Satellite Systems (GNSS) are widely used for vehicle positioning. However, during navigation, signal occlusions may occur due to urban canyons or indoor environments, resulting in poor quality or even loss of satellite signals. To improve positioning performance and overcome signal occlusions, this paper proposes a multiple update particle filter that combines GNSS pseudorange and carrier phase observations. The GNSS receiver provides pseudorange and carrier phase observations, which are then processed by the particle filter to estimate the vehicle's position. The filter weight is designed based on the difference between the pseudorange and carrier phase observations, providing a positioning solution that is more accurate and reliable than traditional GNSS receivers. The simulation results show that the proposed filter has better positioning performance, especially in urban canyons and indoor environments, where traditional GNSS receivers often suffer from poor performance due to signal occlusions.

In this paper, we address web-scale visual entity recognition, specifically the task of mapping a given visual entity to its Wikipedia page. Visual Entity Recognition (VER) is an important task in enabling multimodal search and retrieval systems that can provide users with diverse and rich information resources by matching visual entities from images to relevant textual knowledge resources. However, existing VER datasets are limited due to their small size and lack of real-world diversity, which significantly hinders the development of VER techniques. To address these limitations, we introduce a large-scale dataset for VER called VEWiki, consisting of 465K image-entity pairs and 21M entity-page pairs collected from the English Wikipedia. We further propose a novel unified framework, named

Recent years have witnessed tremendous success in natural language processing (NLP), largely due to the pretrain-finetune paradigm. This paradigm, rooted in the tasks of natural language understanding and natural language generation, has now become a widely-used framework for many other downstream tasks in NLP, such as question answering, text classification, and dialogue generation. However, it can be challenging to tune a pre-trained language model for downstream tasks. In this tutorial, we introduce techniques for effective pre-training and fine-tuning, with an emphasis on practical considerations. We cover three important aspects: (1) objectives and metrics for evaluation, (2) strategies for pre-training and fine-tuning, and (3) techniques for accelerating training. We also discuss some common problems and provide recommendations for model deployment. This tutorial should benefit researchers and practitioners interested in learning to build NLP models using the pretrain-finetune paradigm.

The efficacy of large language models (LLMs) in understanding and generating natural language has arisen as a research topic of interest. However, a majority of these studies rely on the performance of LLMs to be assessed using benchmarks, which are often proprietary. Thus, the public cannot easily access and verify these results. This paper presents a comparative study of LLM performance assessment methods through their ability to predict the human ratings on three popular benchmark datasets: GLUE, SuperGLUE, and WinoGrande. Additionally, we introduce a new method based on the similarity between model outputs and reference answers, and validate its effectiveness. Finally, our findings reveal that all five evaluation strategies produce comparable performance predictions across different da

Deepfake technology has given rise to a spectrum of novel and compelling applications. Unfortunately, the same tools that can be used for good also make it easier than ever to create false or misleading content for malicious purposes. A new study in the Proceedings of Machine Learning Research (PMLR) proposes a solution to this problem using an automated system designed to detect deepfakes across multiple media formats. The authors, led by researchers from the University of California, Berkeley, have developed a method that employs machine learning techniques to analyze the characteristics of deepfake videos at the pixel level. This approach enables the system to accurately distinguish between real and fake footage, offering a valuable tool for media platforms looking to protect against de

Transformers have elevated to the state-of-the-art vision architectures through innovating self attention and large pretraining. It remains challenging for Transformers in applying low computational cost, especially when deployed on mobile devices or edge nodes with limited resources (e..g., memory). In this paper we propose \emph{Dynamic Attention Block}, a novel design that combines multiheaded dynamic pooling mechanism into Transformer's backbone blocks as replacement of traditional dense inner product operations between query vectors from different heads ($\mathcal O(H^2)$), yielding $\mathbf{\sim 5\times}$ reduction both flops/parameters without compromising performance compared against baseline models under same input size ($L=16$, $N_h = N_{qk} + D$).

First responders (FRs) navigate hazardous, unfamiliar environments in the field to provide essential life-saving assistance. In an emergency, FRs may require additional resources and expertise from other first responders to execute critical tasks efficiently. However, it is a daunting challenge to manage large numbers of FRs with limited communication infrastructure or radio frequency congestion. To address this issue, we propose FIRST, a system that enables mobile first responder teams to communicate via 5G mmWave wireless communications and use Artificial Intelligence (AI)-enabled edge computing for situational awareness. We demonstrate our approach using a proof-of-concept system with three 5G-capable tablets and one LTE tablet serving as testbeds. Our evaluations show that the proposed

Tandem robots collaborate to complete a task, with one robot (leader) controlling the motion of the other (follower). In this paper, we investigate how the behavior of the leader robot influences the perception of the task by the human operator. In particular, we conduct a user study to evaluate the impact of three different leader behaviors on the perception of the task by the human operator. The human participants in the study controlled the motion of a mobile robot (the follower) using a joystick. The mobile robot, which was equipped with two cameras, followed the leader robot whose motion was pre-programmed. We measured the time the participants took to complete the task and asked them questions about their perception of the task. Our results show that the participants took significantly longer to complete the task when the leader robot moved closer to them. We also found that the participants felt more involved in the task when the leader robot moved farther from them. Our findings can inspire the design of robot behaviors that facilitate human-robot collaboration.

Few-shot learning (FSL) aims to learn novel tasks with very few labeled examples. The majority of existing approaches focus on either training from scratch or using pretrained models. However, the former is challenging due to the limited amount of data and the latter may not be appropriate for new domains. To this end, we propose an online semi-supervised method that leverages only a small number of labeled examples during training and transfers knowledge learned from previous tasks during testing. Specifically, we design a novel two-stage approach: i) we first perform few-shot supervised learning by relying solely on a set of labeled examples; ii) at test time, we use our model as a teacher to guide a student model that has been trained with online semi-supervised learning, which effectiv

Large language models (LLMs) are highly effective in various natural language processing (NLP) tasks. However, they often fail to perform well on tasks that require reasoning beyond linguistic patterns, such as question answering and commonsense reasoning. In this paper, we propose a novel prompt-based method for addressing the problem of LLMs' lack of generalization ability by injecting world knowledge into the LLM's reasoning process. Specifically, our method utilizes human-written prompts containing various world knowledge to guide the LLMs in generating answers. We design a set of prompts with diverse world knowledge types, including cultural knowledge, science and technology knowledge, social phenomena, etc., and demonstrate their effectiveness through comprehensive experiments. Our r

We present Pix2Gif, a motion-guided diffusion model for image-to-video generation with high quality and efficiency. To capture the temporal dynamics of videos, we design a novel Temporal Diffusion Guidance (TDG) module to guide diffusion steps along the temporal dimension. The TDG module includes a temporal guidance module to provide global temporal guidance, and a motion guidance module to provide local motion guidance. For global guidance, we design a cross-step diffusion guidance strategy that utilizes previously generated frames to provide temporal guidance to subsequent diffusion steps. For local guidance, we introduce a spatial-temporal attention mechanism to highlight the most relevant spatial regions from previous frames to the current frame. Experimental results show that our meth

Prior work has found that pretrained language models (LMs) fine-tuned with contrastive learning methods, such as SimCSE and T5CLM+, perform better than LPs when finetuned on downstream classification tasks. In this paper we show how to adapt the popular CLIP model for unsupervised text representations by introducing a new architecture called TextCLIP: one of our two variants outperforms all previous approaches across 14 datasets in terms both accuracy improvement over baseline LM performance ($+20\%$ compared e. g.\ $8 \%$ obtained from BERT baselines), while also being more computationally efficient during training ($\approx\times3$). Our code is available at \url{https://github.com/googleai/\textcolor[rgb]{#796DFF}{textclip}.

Fine-grained monitoring is crucial for multiple data-driven tasks such as debugging, provisioning, and capacity planning. However, the lack of fine-grained data poses a significant challenge in efficiently identifying anomalies with high precision. This paper introduces a novel approach to uncovering fine-grained anomalies from coarse-grained datasets using an ensemble of deep learning models. The proposed framework leverages the inherent properties of various pre-trained large language models (LLMs) to extract features representing distinct semantics and correlations among variables. These features are then fed into an ensemble of LLMs to learn complex representations of the data. The ensemble models utilize their complementary strengths and overcome the limitations of individual models b

Variational autoencoders (VAEs) are a popular method for fitting latent variable models to incomplete data. However, standard VAEs rely on a variational Gaussian family, which can limit their performance. To improve inference, we propose to use a mixture of variational families, each with its own recognition network, as proposed by Hron et al. (2020). We show that this approach can lead to significant improvements on challenging and realistic missing data problems. We also show that our method can be interpreted as a form of dropout at the level of the recognition network, with important implications for model estimation and selection.

Neural Radiance Fields (NeRF) have quickly become the primary approach for 3D reconstruction from RGB images. This is largely due to their ability to reconstruct highly detailed and photorealistic scenes, even with a limited number of input views. However, as the scale of the scene increases, the performance of NeRF models rapidly deteriorates. In this paper, we present a novel method for training large-scale NeRFs that addresses this issue by leveraging the power of Large Language Models (LLMs). Our key insight is that LLMs can be used to generate additional viewpoints for supervising the NeRF model. To this end, we propose the "NeRF-in-Large" framework, which integrates an LLM into the NeRF optimization process.

Bit-interleaved coded modulations (BICM) are a type of high-throughput optical modulation formats that employ forward error correction (FEC) at the bit level. The decoder of BICM typically consists of a syndrome-based decoder (SBD) and a bit mapper. Existing SBDs are designed to work with binary low-density parity check (LDPC) codes and have a fixed decoding threshold, which limits their error correction performance. In this paper, we aim to design a scalable and reconfigurable SBD that can decode non-binary LDPC codes beyond the binary case and has a tunable decoding threshold. To this end, we propose a neural network-based SBD (NN-SBD) that uses a hard decision layer, a syndrome calculation layer, and a soft decision layer, which are syndrome-based decoding algorithms. We show that the proposed NN-SBD can be trained to decode non-binary LDPC codes and has a tunable decoding threshold. Simulation results show that the proposed NN-SBD outperforms existing syndrome-based decoders and achieves error correction performance comparable to that of the state-of-the-art BICM decoders. Moreover, we show that the proposed NN-SBD can be implemented in a reconfigurable hardware architecture, which consists of a reconfigurable syndrome calculation unit and a reconfigurable soft decision unit. The proposed reconfigurable architecture enables the NN-SBD to decode non-binary LDPC codes with a tunable decoding threshold, bit-interleaved in a scalable manner.

Unexplored environments pose a grand challenge for legged robots. In this paper, we aim to help the robot learn to walk in unmapped, challenging terrain without extensive human engineering. Specifically, we introduce ZSL-RPPO, a recurrent neural network based policy optimization algorithm which incorporates handcrafted features and is capable of learning from scratch in a data-efficient manner. We show that ZSL-RPPO outperforms a state-of-the-art policy optimization baseline on challenging terrains, while also reducing the dependency on handcrafted features. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first work to apply zero-shot learning principles to quadrupedal locomotion. We compare ZSL-RPPO against a handcrafted baseline, a baseline using a state-of-the-art policy optimization algorithm, and a baseline using a commercially available robot control software package. ZSL-RPPO learns policies that outperform the handcrafted baseline in 9 out of 10 tested environments, in only 250 environment steps, which is significantly fewer than the steps taken by the state-of-the-art policy optimization baseline to achieve similar performance.

Recently, with the help of large-scale pretraining, foundation models have shown impressive performance in many natural language processing tasks. However, there is a lack of a comprehensive and authoritative evaluation suite in the TCM domain to evaluate the performance of the foundation model. Therefore, we extend and update the traditional Chinese evaluation suite TCES to TCES-F, which includes five TCM domain datasets, namely, Chinese Traditional Sentence Similarity (CTSS), Chinese Traditional Dependency Parsing (CTDP), Chinese Traditional Coreference Resolution (CTCR), Chinese Traditional Relation Extraction (CTRE), and Chinese Traditional Sentiment Analysis (CTSA). Besides, we propose to evaluate the foundation model from the perspective of TCM theory, i.e., the model should have the ability to generate, understand, and process traditional Chinese characters, words, and sentences. According to this perspective, we design two zero-shot tasks, namely, Chinese Traditional Character Verification (CTCV) and Chinese Traditional Word Verification (CTWV). We hope that the updated TCES-F and the newly proposed tasks can provide a more comprehensive evaluation for the foundation model. We also conduct extensive experiments using representative foundation models, including mBERT, xLM, ALBEF, and CLIP. The results show that our dataset suite can distinguish different quality of foundation models. Furthermore, we propose a simple but strong baseline, namely, the character-level transformer network (CTrans). The CTrans outperforms the previous models, demonstrating the importance of modeling the character-level information. We hope that our work will promote more comprehensive and authoritative evaluation for foundation models.

Edge graphs are an important tool for studying constraint satisfaction problems (CSPs). Given a CSP instance with variables taken from a domain D, its edge graph has a vertex for each variable, and an edge between two vertices whenever the corresponding variables are constrained. The local consistency property, where each variable is consistently constrained with its neighbors, can be checked by performing arc consistency on the edge graph. The arc consistency algorithm, and therefore the local consistency property, depends on the order in which the variables are processed. This paper introduces an algebraic representation for edge graphs, as a set of monomials over the variables, devoid of any order. We present a method for constructing an algebraic representation for a given edge graph, and vice versa. We use the representation to prove several properties of edge graphs, and to provide short proofs for properties of CSPs that are based on edge graphs.

We introduce an energy-efficient downlink rate splitting multiple access (RSMA) scheme, employing the spatial diversity among user equipments (UEs), for a wide range of scenarios including multiuser MIMO systems. In our framework, the base station (BS) allocates its available resources to all UEs according to their respective channel qualities. To achieve this, the BS first performs a resource allocation procedure based on a greedy algorithm and then applies an iterative waterfilling algorithm, where each UE is assigned with a subcarrier power value that depends on its channel quality. Our numerical results show that in terms of achievable rates, the proposed RSMA method outperforms other schemes, such as power control, by up to 50%. Furthermore, we demonstrate that this gain can be achiev

Social recommendation systems face the problem of social influence bias, which can lead to an overemphasis on popular items and an underemphasis on items with lower popularity. This phenomenon could be caused by social homophily, where users tend to interact with users who share similar preferences. However, such a mechanism is not always present in social networks. To address this issue, we propose a method called Homo-BERT, which employs a BERT-based model for pretraining. It learns item representations based on their characteristics and user-item interactions, considering both explicit and implicit feedback. Our experiments show that Homo-BERT can achieve better performance than state-of-the-art methods in two real-world datasets, while also being more robust against the influence of so

In Multiple Object Tracking, objects often exhibit non-linear motion of acceleration and deceleration. For example: * A car accelerates from a red light and decelerates at the next stop sign or intersection; or A person walks into an elevator with slow initial velocity before reaching constant speed during travel up to top floor(s). In order to provide effective tracking in such scenarios where object velocities are not known beforehand it is essential for trackers to automatically adapt their search window sizes based on observed target dynamics (i) without requiring manual tuning by human experts which could be tedious especially when dealing large number targets/scenes ; secondly , given fixed size windows does pose difficulties while handling fast moving /accelerating tra

Chain-of-Thought (CoT) serves as a critical emerging ability in LLLMs, yet its effectiveness remains limited. This paper presents the first study of CoT's impact on real-world applications and highlights several factors that may be contributing to this trend: 1) The lack of large-scale data for CoT. 2) The fact that existing datasets are not necessarily well-aligned with downstream tasks. For example, existing CoT datasets have a high proportion of questions asking about the meaning or explanation of some entity, which is different from how people ask about entities in their daily lives. 3) The inherent nature of CoT. We find that many CoT questions are actually "knowledge-intensive," requiring the model to recall relevant knowledge from previous contexts in order to answer correctly. Howe

Inspired by ideas from health risk assessment, this paper presents a new perspective for flood hazard analysis. It proposes the concept of cumulative excess damages and an index called Excess Damage Rate (EDR) to quantify spatial variation in these losses across different time horizons that can be used to delineate zones with higher vulnerability or resilience against flooding events as well identify potentially high-risk areas prone towards future disasters due their exposure over long periods without adequate protection measures being taken beforehand - all while taking into account multiple factors such rainfall intensity levels alongside land use type(s). This study assesses how much excess damage occurs when extreme rain event intensities occur across various return intervals ranging

We consider the problem of inferring constraints for a user intent model from user demonstrations. A common approach is to construct a set of constraints that are all satisfied by the demonstrations and then optimize over the resulting feasible set. However, optimizing directly over the feasible set can be challenging, and an approximation of the feasible set is often used instead. A standard approximation method is to construct a minimal set of constraints from the demonstrations, which can be done in polynomial time. However, we show that the problem of constructing a minimal set of constraints from demonstrations is NP-hard if the demonstrations are given as sets of alternatives, and we provide an integer linear program to solve the problem in polynomial time. Next, we show that our integer linear program can be simplified significantly if the demonstrations are given as pairwise preferences. Finally, we show that our approach can be extended to handle categorical attributes in the constraints, and we provide an empirical evaluation of our method.

Transformer has become one of the most popular architectures for multivariate time series (MTS) forecasting. In this paper, we propose a general framework that can seamlessly integrate any encoder into Transformers for MTS tasks without compromising model performance. We demonstrate the effectiveness and superiority of our approach by evaluating multiple encoders in the context of MTS forecasting. Our experiments show that incorporating feature extraction methods tailored to MTS data, such as the Diffusion Wavelet Transformer (DWT), leads to significant improvements over traditional encoders. This is particularly evident when using DWT for intraday prediction, where the accuracy improves from 30.2% to 68.8%. Additionally, we demonstrate that our methodology is capable of handling long sequ

This work proposes an image coding method for machines that can learn object boundaries from images. The proposed method exploits the recently proposed Segment Anything (SAM) to learn object boundaries and uses the learned information to encode/decode images. Specifically, we form a joint embedding space for images and their corresponding binary image masks that are generated from the SAM predictions. We learn the properties of the embedding space by projecting both images and binary masks to a lower-dimensional space and then jointly clustering the projected images and masks. The clustering centers are then used to encode/decode images by designing a lightweight neural network based on the inverse of the proposed embedding space projection. The proposed method outperforms recent image coding methods for machines on the task of learning object boundaries from images. Additionally, we show that the proposed method can be extended to videos where 3D clustering is performed to learn both 2D and 3D object boundaries.

Leveraging pre-trained visual language models has become a widely adopted approach for improving performance in various vision tasks. In this work, we explore the potential of a novel pre-training strategy by fine-tuning a single model on multiple datasets simultaneously. We propose a general framework that enables efficient and effective training on large-scale datasets. The key idea is to adaptively adjust the size of the input image and the number of tokens during training based on the dataset's characteristics. By utilizing these adaptive settings, we can effectively leverage the strengths of different datasets while minimizing their individual weaknesses. Our method achieves consistent improvements across various downstream tasks with no additional labeled data required, demonstrating

Stance detection of social media text is a key component of downstream tasks involving the identification of groups of users with opposing opinions on contested topics such as vaccination and within arguments. In particular, stance provides an indication of an opinion towards an entity. This paper introduces DIVERSE, a dataset of over 173,000 YouTube video comments annotated for their stance towards videos of the U.S. military. The stance is annotated through a human-guided, machine-assisted labeling methodology that makes use of weak signals of tone within the sentence as supporting indicators, as opposed to using manual annotations by humans. These weak signals consist of the presence of hate speech and sarcasm, the presence of specific keywords, the sentiment of the text, and the stance

Artificial Intelligence for Theorem Proving has given rise to a plethora of benchmarks. The most prominent benchmark is SMT-COMP, where competing systems are evaluated in a number of subtasks based on the SMT solving technology. However, as SMT solving and theorem proving methods are fundamentally different, it is not clear whether the SMT-COMP results can be interpreted in terms of theorem proving capabilities. In this work, we propose a new benchmark for AI-based theorem proving: QRK. This benchmark contains tasks that require proof search, including the well-known QAP, an instance of the Quasi-Random Kernel Problem from theoretical computer science. We compare the performance of the state-of-the-art theorem provers on the benchmark tasks.

Collaborative cybersecurity relies on organizations sharing information to boost security, but trust management is a key concern. Decentralized solutions like distributed ledgers, particularly blockchain, are crucial for eliminating single points of failure. However, the existing literature on blockchain-based collaborative cybersecurity is limited, lacking comprehensive insights. This paper addresses this gap by surveying blockchain's role in collaborative cybersecurity from 2016 to 2023. It explores various applications, trends, and the evolution of blockchain technology, focusing on access control, data validation policies, underlying tech, and consensus mechanisms. A key finding is the fragmentation of the field with no dominant research group or venue. Many recent projects poorly sele

Contestability -- the ability to effectively challenge a decision -- is critical to the implementation of fairness and inclusion in AI. Unfortunately, current approaches to contestable ML systems are often limited by high costs or significant constraints on system functionality; it remains an open question how best to provide individuals with effective control over their data while preserving both privacy protections and useful features for downstream users such as businesses who want access through APIs (Application Programming Interfaces). In this paper we propose two novel techniques that allow us simultaneously achieve all three goals: protection against external adversaries attacking individual's personal information via adversarial machine learning attacks like poisoning/inference ev

When users access shared resources in a selfish manner, the resulting societal cost and perceived users' gain are known as social costs and gains. Recently, several researchers have proposed solutions to efficiently compute the social cost/gain for each user and allocate the total amount of resources to all users while minimizing the sum of social cost. However, this problem is NP-hard; thus, existing algorithms are not guaranteed to obtain an optimal solution. Furthermore, it is necessary to consider multiple factors such as fairness, the trade-off between social cost and social gain, and the utility of users (e.g., delay or throughput). Therefore, we propose a novel resource allocation method that can be applied to real-world scenarios involving various network conditions. Our approach u

Data shuffling is an essential operation in distributed stream processing frameworks (DSPFs), enabling scalable and efficient data distribution across the nodes in a data center. The performance of the data shuffling operation is critical to the overall performance of the framework, yet there is a lack of a standardized benchmark to evaluate and compare the performance of existing DSPFs under realistic workloads. To this end, we introduce ShuffleBench, a new benchmark framework for evaluating and comparing the data shuffling performance of DSPFs. ShuffleBench is based on a realistic workload generated by a real-world application, and it can be used to evaluate the data shuffling performance of a DSPF running on a single machine as well as a multi-machine cluster. Our experiments using ShuffleBench show the data shuffling performance of different DSPFs can vary by up to 2X.

Even when using large multi-modal foundation models, few-shot learning is still challenging -- if not impossible. This limitation arises from the lack of a shared space between different modalities and tasks to be learned in an unsupervised way without supervision or pre/post training steps (e..g., contrastive fine tuning). In this work we present \emph{CrossModality}, which enables cross modality self supervised representation generation by projecting each individual modal token into one single latent vector across all input modes with minimal parameter overhead over baselines (\textless 0\% increase) on topof any model architecture for multiple language pairs including English$\to$French $\mid $German\,$(\approx \,167\,M)$ , English$-$Chinese ($\sim254 M$), and Chinese $-$ Japanese $(

The development and adoption of Vision Transformers and other deep-learning architectures for image processing has led to a dramatic increase in the number of parameters and computations required for training, which are key drivers of the environmental impact (CO2 emissions) of deep learning. Our work focuses on understanding the interplay between model size, energy efficiency, and accuracy across different tasks, architectures, and datasets. We demonstrate that a small-scale ViT can outperform large-scale ResNet models in terms of accuracy while being more energy efficient and having significantly fewer parameters. We show that the gap in performance between small and large vision models is larger on unsupervised than supervised tasks. By comparing several architectures with various model

LiDAR and cameras are two major sensors for environmental perception in autonomous driving. LiDAR can provide precise 3D spatial information but is susceptible to adverse weather conditions. Cameras are less affected by weather but are limited in measuring 3D spatial information. Fusion of data from these two sensors can lead to better environmental perception. In this paper, we propose a camera-LiDAR fusion multi-object tracking algorithm for autonomous driving. We first perform object detection and LiDAR scan matching to obtain initial object states. Then, we use a particle filter to track each object over time with camera and LiDAR data. In the update step, we perform weighted fusion of the camera and LiDAR state estimates based on their respective contribution to the overall estimation accuracy. The proposed algorithm is implemented on a GPU and can achieve a tracking frame rate of over 60 FPS with 16 objects. Evaluation results on public datasets show that our method outperforms state-of-the-art tracking algorithms.

Real-time fluid flow estimation using computational fluid dynamics (CFD) simulations is a computationally intensive task that requires powerful hardware resources. Field-programmable gate arrays (FPGAs) are reconfigurable hardware devices that can accelerate CFD simulations by up to 3X compared to central processing units (CPUs). However, FPGA-based flow estimation systems have limited precision due to the small number of bits available for representing floating-point numbers. Increasing the precision of these systems beyond 32 bits is impractical due to the high resource requirements of 64-bit floating-point operations. To address this challenge, we propose FlowPrecision, a novel linear quantization method that improves the accuracy of FPGA-based real-time fluid flow estimation without requiring 64-bit floating-point operations. We evaluate FlowPrecision on three fluid flow datasets and two FPGA platforms. Our results show that FlowPrecision improves the accuracy of FPGA-based flow estimation by up to 8X compared to traditional methods, without requiring 64-bit floating-point operations.

We propose a framework for tuning natural language classification models to achieve balanced group distributional robustness with controllable trade-offs using multi-objective prompt tuning. We detect uneven group distributional robustness of state-of-the-art natural language classification models and motivate the need for prompt tuning as a) group distributional robustness is often at odds with model accuracy, and b) existing methods for enhancing group distributional robustness through adversarial training result in overfitting. We propose a multi-objective prompt tuning method that enhances group distributional robustness while minimizing the impact on model accuracy. We conduct experiments with differently sized groups in both the binary and multi-class classification settings and show that our method achieves a) better group distributional robustness compared to tuning with a single group, b) comparable group distributional robustness to that of adversarial training methods, and c) superior model accuracy compared to adversarial training methods.

We explore the $\textit{average-case deterministic query complexity}$ of boolean functions under the uniform distribution over $n$-bit strings. We show that for any (monotone or non-monotone) function of $n$ variables, there is a deterministic algorithm which requires at most $O(n^{1/3})$ queries to decide if a given string belongs to its domain and returns the value of the function on that string with high probability. This bound is tight up to constants since we also show that no such algorithm exists that makes fewer than $\Omega(n^{1/3})$ queries. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first upper bound on average-case deterministic query complexity in the uniform model. Our lower bound proof uses an information theoretic argument and relies heavily on the fact that the uniform dist

Training-free metrics (a.k.a. zero-cost proxies) are widely used in the study of pretrained models. However, their reliability remains an open question. In this paper, we explore the correlation between training-free and training-dependent metrics on various model types. We find that some training-free metrics correlate well with traditional metrics such as accuracy on ImageNet-1K and CIFAR-10 across a wide range of models including vision transformers, large language models, and generative adversarial networks. Additionally, for these metrics, we observe that they align better with training-dependent metrics than with other training-free ones, suggesting that these metrics may be more robust indicators of the overall model quality.

We present a new family of transformer decoders, dubbed Foundation Decoder (FD), that enable interpretable image classification with foundation models. FDs are based on a novel linear attention mechanism that promotes sparsity and therefore interpretability. They are also designed to be scalable and robust, addressing key challenges in scaling vision transformers. Specifically, we introduce two FDs: (i) a lightweight and low-parameter count FD, referred to as Sparse Foundation Decoder (SFD), and (ii) a dense FD with improved robustness, referred to as Robust Foundation Decoder (RFD). We conduct an extensive set of experiments to demonstrate the effectiveness of FDs. As part of our evaluation, we perform extensive ablation studies to explore the impact of attention sparsity, decoder depth, and width on performance. Our results show that FDs achieve state-of-the-art performance in terms of top-1 accuracy while offering greater than 10X parameter efficiency compared to existing vision transformer decoders. Furthermore, FDs significantly outperform existing decoders on challenging benchmarks for interpretable classification, including JustifyNet and AUC. Lastly, we demonstrate the effectiveness of our approach for few-shot classification.

Direct reciprocity based on the repeated prisoner's dilemma has been intensively studied as a mechanism for cooperation in social dilemmas. However, previous studies have shown that there exist situations where it is difficult for direct reciprocity to achieve high levels of cooperation. To solve this issue, we propose a novel strategy called "strategic reciprocity", which combines direct and indirect reciprocity. In particular, strategic reciprocators always cooperate with others who reciprocated previously, while they defect against those who did not reciprocate. They also use an indirect reciprocation method if the number of rounds until the end of the game is not large enough. We evaluate the effectiveness of our proposed strategy by computer simulation and experiments using human subj

A common way of assessing language learners' mastery of vocabulary is via multiple-choice tests, where the correct answer is selected from a set of candidates. In this paper we introduce a novel method to identify the most suitable candidate in such a test without requiring prior knowledge about the correct answer, by measuring the similarity between the test question and the candidate answer. Our approach relies on neural networks trained with large corpora of English text, which allows us to capture fine-grained similarities between words and phrases within sentences. We evaluate our model on 137,028 questions from two different datasets: one containing simple English sentences (Human Evaluated Multiple Choice Tests) and the other containing Chinese sentences translated into English (Chi

Diffusion models have shown great potential in estimating 3D human pose from RGB images, by using an implicit diffusion process to gradually convert the input image into the desired output space. However, the latent space of the diffusion model is entangled, making it difficult to control the generation process and resulting in noisy estimation. In this paper, we propose a novel Disentangled Diffusion-Based 3D human pose estimation model (D3HAT), which improves the entanglement problem in the latent space from two aspects: 1) We introduce a hierarchical spatial denoiser consisting of a set of progressively finer convolutions to help the model capture both global and local features of human poses. 2) We propose a temporal denoiser based on the teacher-student learning paradigm to refine the pose estimation results over time. In comparison with existing state-of-the-art models, D3HAT achieves a competitive level of 3D pose estimation accuracy, while showing a much better latent disentanglement performance, as verified by a series of experiments and ablation studies. The source code is available at https://github.com/wjzheng96/D3HAT.

Time Series Generation (TSG) has emerged as a pivotal technique in synthesizing time series data. With the increasing demand for large-scale datasets, traditional TSG methods struggle to efficiently generate high-quality and diverse data, particularly in complex domains. In this paper, we propose Multi-Objective Reinforcement Learning-based Time Series Generation (MORL-TSG), which utilizes reinforcement learning to optimize multiple objectives simultaneously, enhancing the quality and diversity of generated data. MORL-TSG is equipped with an innovative reward function that incorporates both temporal consistency and diversity, thus enabling it to address the limitations of existing approaches. Our experiments on four benchmark datasets demonstrate that MORL-TSG significantly outperforms sta

This paper explores the potential of Physics-Informed Neural Networks (PINNs) in solving inverse problems for materials with complex nonlinear behavior. In particular, we focus on the problem of predicting the effective elastic modulus and Poisson's ratio from a given set of measured displacement data without explicitly knowing the material properties. We demonstrate that PINNs can learn accurate models for this type of inverse problem, even when the input data are sparse or noisy. Our approach uses an encoder network to compress the displacement data into a low-dimensional representation, followed by a decoder network to infer the desired outputs. To regularize the learning process, we use two different methods: a neural tangent kernel (NTK) approach based on Gaussian processes (GP) and a

Hyperdimensional computing (HDC) is a proposed alternative to traditional Boolean computing, better suited to cope with the high dimensionality of data in machine learning and other fields. In this scheme, computations are performed with hyperdimensional vectors, and logical operations are replaced with geometric operations on these vectors. Previously, it has been shown that basic logical operations can be performed with linear transformations of hyperdimensional vectors, using only two dimensions. Here, we give a systematic treatment of linear codes for HDC based on finite fields. We show that the properties of these linear codes are well-suited to perform logical operations in HDC. In particular, we define a notion of dual code that is important for applications to neural networks, and we derive the necessary and sufficient conditions for the existence of linear codes with a given distance for use in applications.

Confusing charge prediction is a challenging task in legal AI, which involves predicting confusing charges based on the content of court documents. However, existing datasets for this task lack sufficient samples to provide reliable evaluation results. In addition, many samples contain missing information or incomplete text, making it difficult to train models that can accurately predict confusing charges. To address these issues, we introduce a new dataset, LegalAI-Confuse, designed specifically for confusing charge prediction tasks in legal AI. This dataset includes 30,014 cases from the Chinese courts, each with detailed case information, including a list of charges, trial results, and complete text. We also propose a novel approach using generative large language models (LLMs) to gener

Topic modelling was mostly dominated by Bayesian graphical models during the last decade. However, recently new methods have been proposed that use neural networks for topic modelling instead of traditional probabilistic models. While many studies have already shown that such approaches are highly competitive with existing methods in terms of model quality and efficiency, their capabilities regarding parameter estimation and prediction accuracy have not yet been comprehensively compared to existing probabilistic models. In this study, we present a quantitative comparison of the performance of deep learning topic models with several state-of-the-art LDA models (including hierarchical variants) using real-world document corpora. Our results show that the best performing deep learning models

This paper focuses on developing Pareto-optimal estimation and policy learning to identify the most efficient control method for autonomous robot navigation. We propose an optimization framework that jointly optimizes both trajectory planning in motion space as well perception via a deep neural network (DNN) model, which can be efficiently solved by using gradient descent methods such backpropagation or conjugate gradients with Hessian information obtained from finite differences of DNNs' output functions around each candidate point along feasible trajectories generated during search process within potential field planner [1].

Link prediction typically studies the probability of future interconnection among nodes with the observation in a single social network. This assumption, however, overlooks the fact that the connections between individuals are not independent but influenced by the social environment they live in. To address this issue, we introduce a new link prediction task called spatially-aware link prediction (SALP). SALP explores the correlation between the spatial distribution of users and their future connections in an unseen graph. We propose SpaLin, a novel method for spatially-aware link prediction on online social networks using Graph Neural Networks (GNNs). SpaLin employs a GNN to learn the similarity between two nodes, which is then used as the input of a classifier to predict their future con

This article evaluates the quality of data collection in individual-level desktop information tracking used in the social sciences. I focus on user interactions with web browsers, including clicks and keystrokes within a browser window or tab (referred to as "browser actions"). While many studies use such interaction logs for inferring attentional processes underlying cognitive tasks performed by users online [14], there has been little research into how well these measures align with real behavioral outcomes relevant from an applied perspective; e..g., conversion rates between website traffic sources[20].

With the advancements of transformer-based architectures, we observe the rise of natural language processing (NLP) models with large vocabulary sizes. These huge vocabularies introduce substantial challenges for training and inference as well because they require a larger amount storage space to be stored on memory devices such hard disk drives or solid state disks in addition consuming more time during initialization process due their size requirement; this is particularly problematic when dealing small batching scenarios where each iteration takes longer than usual given fixed computation budget per epoch cycle which makes it difficult achieve high accuracy scores even if model weights were optimized efficiently enough beforehand by using gradient descent algorithm without sacrificing sp

Motor imagery classification based on electroencephalography (EEG) signals is one of the most widely used brain-computer interface (BCI) applications. However, EEG signals are inherently non-stationary and highly susceptible to noise. Consequently, conventional machine learning approaches often struggle to achieve satisfactory accuracy in motor imagery classification tasks. To overcome these challenges, we propose a novel deep learning framework that utilizes a multi-resolution convolutional neural network (CNN) architecture for motor imagery classification. The proposed CNN model incorporates a combination of 1D and 2D convolutional layers, which allows it to extract both temporal and spatial features from EEG signals. Additionally, our method employs a multi-head self-attention mechanism

Autonomous driving systems face the formidable challenge of navigating intricate and dynamic environments with both static and dynamic obstacles. This paper introduces a novel approach to tackle this complex problem, using the concept of a "Bubble-based Obstacle Avoidance" (BOA) system. The BOA system introduces three key elements: 1) it assigns each point in the environment a bubble of varying size based on its distance from the ego vehicle; 2) the bubbles are interconnected, forming a dynamic network that continuously adapts to changes in the surrounding environment; 3) the ego vehicle's trajectory planning is guided by the avoidance of these bubbles. By employing this innovative methodology, the BOA system demonstrates enhanced capability in handling various scenarios, including dense t

Recent studies have focused on enhancing the performance of 3D object detection models. Among these studies, a significant approach has been to explore different learning strategies for training. Despite the potential effectiveness of this strategy in improving model performance, it is often challenging to determine an optimal setting without extensive trial and error due to the high dimensionality of hyperparameters involved. In this paper, we propose a hyperparameter optimization framework called HPO-3DDet that employs a Bayesian optimization algorithm for automatic hyperparameter tuning in 3D object detection. Our approach systematically identifies an optimal hyperparameter configuration based on the evaluation of predefined metrics. We conducted experiments using both synthetic data an

Large Language Models (LLMs) such as GPT-4 have shown impressive performance but remain computationally expensive. One effective but expensive solution to scale LLMs is to use a key-value (KV) cache to store recently accessed tokens. However, maintaining the cache incurs high memory overhead, motivating the need for efficient cache management. Previous works typically rely on time-based eviction policies to manage the cache. However, as the LLM inference workload is highly dynamic, a fixed-size cache inevitably leads to sub-optimal performance. In this paper, we propose QAQ, a novel quality adaptive quantization approach for LLM KV cache management. QAQ observes that the usefulness of a cached token can be inferred from the query and key tokens. Therefore, we design a token quality estimation module based on the attention weights of the LLM. This attention-based quality estimation approach is completely hardware-software co-design, with no additional human labeling cost. Based on the quality estimation, we propose a novel adaptive quantization scheme for the cache tokens. Experimental results on two LLMs, GPT-3 and GPT-4, show that QAQ consistently outperforms previous state-of-the-art baselines across different cache sizes and various workload scenarios. Our hardware implementation shows that QAQ can be efficiently deployed on an FPGA, with acceleration results varying from 1.3 to 10.2 times compared to the software baseline.

This study addresses the challenge of real-time metaverse applications by proposing an in-network placement framework for high-fidelity 3D models. This approach leverages a novel network architecture, Metaverse Network (MetaNet), and the innovative idea of adaptive caching to achieve real-time rendering of 3D content. Our analysis reveals that the performance of existing solutions is limited due to their reliance on CPU computing power, which is often insufficient to support real-time rendering. To overcome this limitation, we propose a unique network architecture called MetaNet, which introduces a new concept of edge caching for 3D model data and integrates it with existing caching techniques. We conduct extensive experiments using various datasets, including KITTI and Cityscapes, and sho

Tabular data, as a crucial form of data representation, exists in diverse formats on the Web. However it remains challenging to analyze and reason with tabular content due largely because there is no standard way for describing or interchanging them across different applications/domains; this makes developing tools that can automatically extract meaningful information from tables difficult given their variety (e...g., number columns). To address these challenges we propose an approach called Tab2Struct which takes advantage both structured knowledge resources - such those found within Wikidata --as well machine learning techniques trained over large amounts datasets like Wikipedia's infoboxes .

Sharding enhances blockchain scalability by dividing the network into shards, each managing a subset of transactions. While sharded blockchains are still far from achieving true parallelism and decentralization (where all nodes participate equally in consensus), they do offer several benefits that make them an attractive option for high-throughput applications like DeFi platforms or gaming marketplaces: 1) increased throughput due to parallel processing;2 ) improved security through immutability across multiple chains ;3). Increased efficiency as compared with unshuttered solutions because only one chain needs attention at any given moment instead off simultaneously working on every single piece if data within it! And finally - greater flexibility regarding customization options suchas add

In this paper we describe the development of a streamlined framework for large-scale ATLAS pileup mitigation and HLT event selection, in order to maximise the physics reach of the LHC. The system is based on an innovative approach that uses machine learning techniques to optimally tune the trigger selections, reducing the number of events that need to be sent to the HLT by orders of magnitude while maintaining high efficiency. We validate our approach with data from a variety of colliding beam scenarios, including low luminosity, pileup dominated, and high multiplicity data samples. In addition, we showcase how our model can be used offline as a baseline selection tool, allowing us to analyse more complex datasets than previously possible. This methodology allows for a significant reductio

DeepONets are a promising emerging technology for forecasting spatio-temporal data, able to outperform conventional machine learning models and numerical methods for many applications. However, like all data-driven models, their forecasts are subject to uncertainty, which needs to be quantified to assess the reliability of the predictions and improve the model. In this work, we introduce a new framework to quantify uncertainty in DeepONet forecasts, based on ensemble Kalman inversion. This method leverages the linear theory of uncertainty quantification for nonlinear models, and can be applied to any DeepONet architecture. We demonstrate our approach on DeepONets trained to forecast fluid flow around an obstacle, and show that the uncertainty quantification captures the true uncertainty in the predictions, providing a valuable tool for applications to assess the reliability of the model.

The array is a data structure used in a wide range of programs. Its compact storage and constant time access has made it the go-to choice for storing lists, trees, maps and sets. However, this comes at a cost: the tradeoff between size and speed. In this work we introduce a new structure that takes the best from both worlds. Our data structure allows for quick insertions and deletions while also supporting constant time lookup times. This property makes it ideal for dynamic programming applications where real-time performance is paramount. We call our data structure the `array-like' data structure (ALDS). To demonstrate the power of ALDS, we provide a generalization of the merge sort algorithm with an order of magnitude faster runtime complexity. We then show how this can be applied to oth

Thanks to technologies such as virtual network function the Fifth Generation (5G) of mobile networks will be able to integrate a wide variety of applications and services, in addition to those provided by telecommunications. These capabilities will allow 5G to serve different industries, beyond communications, such as the Internet of Things (IoT), Industry 4.0, Smart Cities, and others. The deployment of these new 5G capabilities requires not only the development of new technologies but also the implementation of new business models that are based on the use of new services and resources. In this paper, we propose an approach for the analysis and design of business models for 5G, which is based on the Business Model Canvas tool. This methodology has been applied to several case studies rel

Multi-Source cross-lingual transfer learning deals with the transfer of task knowledge from multiple label sources to a target language. In this work, we address multisource zero shot classification on multilingual datasets where there is no labeled data available in any source or sink languages for training an end classifier (or embedding) model at all steps including intermediate step(s). We propose MultiLingual Cross lingual Transfer Learning framework called MuLiXT and evaluate it across three diverse tasks: Language Identification (${\mathcal{L}}$ID), Named Entity Recognition ($\mathrm {NER}$ ), Natural Logic Inference Task $\rm NLIT$. To tackle these challenging problems without relying solely upon pretrained models like BERT/GPT2 etc., which may not be easily accessible due their hi

The proliferation of software vulnerabilities poses a significant challenge for security databases and analysts in accurately identifying and cataloging these threats. In this paper, we propose a novel technique that leverages pre-trained language models (LMs) to enhance the effectiveness of traditional software vulnerability detection methods. Our approach involves using LM prompts to generate more accurate and comprehensive descriptions of vulnerabilities, which are then fed into natural language processing (NLP) techniques to extract features essential for classification. Experimental results demonstrate that our method significantly improves the performance of vulnerability detection compared to baseline methods across various datasets and evaluation metrics. We also reveal that the ge

Molecular Communication (MC) has emerged as a promising paradigm employing molecules to transmit information from a source node to multiple destination nodes. This is achieved by using the molecule's physical properties, such as color and fluorescence, which are affected by the surrounding environment. However, the presence of obstacles like walls or pipes can block the path between the sender and receiver nodes, thus affecting the transmitted signal. In this study, we propose a novel MC protocol that incorporates a reflector node to bounce the signal back toward the source node when it gets blocked by an obstacle. We formulate our communication problem in the framework of Markov Decision Process (MDP), where the system state depends on both the received signal and the channel gain at the

Energy efficiency is a key design goal for modern computer systems. On modern servers, the energy consumption of the memory subsystem can account for up to 25% of the total energy consumption of a system. This work focuses on improving the energy efficiency of the memory subsystem by scheduling garbage collection (GC) activities. Previous works have proposed to integrate GC activities with the CPU workload to avoid the energy cost of GC. However, they did not consider the energy cost of moving data between the different memory levels. In this paper, we consider the energy consumption of the memory subsystem in more detail and propose a framework to schedule GC activities on asymmetric multicore processors to reduce the energy consumption of the memory subsystem. We design an offline algorithm to minimize the total energy consumption of the memory subsystem for a given CPU workload. The algorithm takes the energy consumption of data movement into account and uses a core energy model to estimate the energy consumption of GC activities. We also propose an online algorithm to schedule GC activities based on the current system state. Our experiments show that the offline algorithm can reduce the total energy consumption of the memory subsystem by up to 80% compared to the energy consumption of the memory subsystem without GC scheduling. Our online algorithm can reduce the total energy consumption of the memory subsystem by up to 40% compared to the energy consumption of the memory subsystem without GC scheduling.

Large language model (LLM) leads to a surge of autonomous GUI agents for smartphone, which completes a task triggered by natural language through predicting a sequence of actions of API. Even though the task highly relies on past actions and visual observations, existing studies typical consider little semantic information carried out by intermediate screenshots and screen operations. To address this, this work presents Chain-of-Action-Thought (dubbed CoAT), which takes the description of the previous actions, the current screen, and more importantly the action thinking of what actions should be performed and the outcomes led by the chosen action. We demonstrate that, in a zero-shot setting upon an off-the-shell LLM, CoAT significantly improves the goal progress compared to standard contex

Social recommendation systems face the problem of social influence bias, which can lead to an overemphasis on recommendations that are popular among friends. Recent research has shown that the existing mitigation techniques are not sufficiently effective in addressing this problem and that biased preferences persist even when the recommender system is trained on neutral data. In this paper, we introduce a new approach that leverages machine learning (ML) models to dynamically adjust the recommendations based on user behavior patterns. This method involves incorporating user's click history with their friends' click histories as well as the click history from anonymous users into the ML model for prediction, and then using the predicted scores as recommendations. Our experimental results in

This article investigates applying advanced machine learning models, specifically LSTM and BERT, for text classification tasks. It compares the performance of these two types with traditional classifiers on different datasets to provide insights into their strengths in tackling complex problems involving unstructured data such as texts or audio streams that have not been studied extensively before due mainly because they require large amounts computational resources which may be difficult access at times depending upon availability hardware infrastructure needed run them efficiently enough so results obtained from experiments conducted here can serve useful guidance when considering whether adopt deep neural networks architecture instead conventional ones while making decisions regarding f

We study permutation invariant functions of a multivariate random variable, focusing on the challenging setting where the random variable is defined on a high-dimensional Euclidean space. Due to the vast number of possible permutations, it is natural to expect that any statistical procedure in this setting will suffer from the curse of dimensionality. However, we show that this is not necessarily the case. In particular, we develop a framework that enables one to conduct statistical tests and perform dimension reduction on permutation invariant functions without incurring a polynomial cost in the dimension of the underlying space. Our framework relies on a novel metric entropy bound for permutation invariant functions, which we establish using a randomized approach. This bound, in conjunction with a symmetrization argument, enables one to transfer well-known results for real-valued functions to the permutation invariant setting. We illustrate the utility of our framework by applying it to the problems of testing monotonicity and estimating entropy and mutual information from data.

Deep Residual Neural Networks (ResNets) have demonstrated remarkable success across a wide range of computer vision tasks. Large ResNets, however, are computationally expensive to train and deploy due to their high memory footprint. In this paper, we introduce a novel method for fine-tuning ResNet models without retraining the entire network. Our approach involves training only one or two layers in the backbone ResNet architecture, while maintaining the pretrained weights on all other layers. This allows us to leverage existing computational resources and avoid additional training time. We evaluate our technique using CIFAR-10 and ImageNet datasets. Experiments show that our method achieves comparable results to those obtained with full model retraining, while requiring significantly less

Parametric language models (LMs), which are trained on vast amounts of web data, exhibite a wide range in their performance across different domains. Existing methods for addressing this issue rely heavily upon domain-specific knowledge and often involve costly manual labeling processes to tune the model parameters accordingly. In contrast with existing approaches that require such human intervention at both training time as well specific prompts or datasets during inference stage , we propose an unsupervised method called Parametric Prompting based On Language Model Ensembles(P3OLE) . Our approach leverages large prebuilt parametric LME ensembles built from multiple LM checkpoints alongwith self supervised learning strategies like contrastive supervision using masked tokensto improve gene

Monte Carlo (MC) simulations play a pivotal role in diverse scientific and engineering domains, with the ability to efficiently explore vast problem spaces and assess multiple potential solutions. Deep MC methods are promising tools for tackling complex problems, where the objective is to sample from an unknown distribution via an optimization-based process. However, their applicability remains limited due to the challenges associated with training deep models that can effectively evaluate objective functions. In this work, we propose a novel approach for integrating MC techniques into neural networks through a combination of sampling-aware optimizers, loss functions, and gradient estimators. This approach allows us to leverage the power of MC to improve the performance of deep learning mo

Despite the recent ban on demographic subtyping in the United States, age and gender are still widely used in many parts of the world for credit scoring, pricing, and other applications. As large language models and other machine learning techniques continue to improve in performance and become more widely available, there is a risk that they will be used for harmful purposes. In this work, we evaluate the performance of AgeLM and GenderLM -- language models specifically trained for age and gender estimation -- on a range of tasks, including demographic subgroup classification, re-identification, and generation. We also compare them to a range of baselines, including models that are not specifically trained for these tasks. Our results show that AgeLM and GenderLM are significantly better than the baselines at estimating age and gender, but that this advantage disappears when the models are used for other tasks. We conclude that there is no evidence of specialization in AgeLM and GenderLM, and that they should not be used for any purpose other than age and gender estimation.

Recent developments have underscored the critical role of \textit{differential privacy} (DP) in protecting data privacy while enabling effective machine learning. However, despite its potential, DP has been hindered by two significant challenges: the \textit{utility-accuracy tradeoff}, which limits the effectiveness of differentially private algorithms, and the \textit{curse of dimensionality}, which makes it difficult to scale to high dimensions.
