In this work, we propose a novel differential privacy framework that tackles these challenges and significantly enhances the utility-accuracy tradeoff in high-dimensional spaces. We introduce our proposed framework, the \textit{Differential Privacy with High-Dimensional Embedding} (\texttt{DP-HDE}), which leverages recent advances in high-dimensi

Graph neural networks (GNNs) have drawn increasing attention in recent years for their impressive performance on various graph-based tasks, especially in the induction of graph representations. However, the impact of the topological structure of graphs to GNNs' performance, especially the generalization performance when the topological structure of the testing data differs from that of the training data, remains elusive. In this paper, we investigate the topology awareness and generalization performance of GNNs from a novel perspective. Specifically, we introduce a topological discriminator to GNNs for distinguishing topological differences between graphs. Theoretically, we prove that the topological discriminator is universal in approximating a wide class of topological functions and can be implemented by a two-layer neural network. Empirically, we show that the topological discriminator improves the generalization performance of GNNs on heterophilic graphs with a complex topological structure. The source code is available at https://github.com/huawei-noah/noah-research/tree/master/graph_atlas.

Popular methods usually use a degradation model in a supervised way to learn a watermark remover. However, there are two limitations of such an approach: (1) the pre-trained models may not be robust enough when encountering unseen degradations; and (2) it is difficult to obtain sufficient data due to the high cost of annotation. To this end, we propose a new method that uses the encoder weights from a vanilla Stable Diffusion model as initialization for our discriminative model to recover the original images. This is achieved by using the discriminator layer as a feature extractor instead of training with a reconstruction loss. Moreover, since the discriminator is only trained during finetuning, it can be easily applied to various watermarks and degradations without extra parameter tuning.

Cluster generalization is an important property for robustifying machine learning models against adversarial attacks. The cluster generalization bound (CGB) is a recently proposed robustness metric that evaluates whether a representation satisfies the cluster generalization property. To check the CGB, a representation is first fitted to a training set of clusters, and then evaluated on a test set of clusters. In this paper, we leverage the CGB to learn invariant representations of graph neural networks (GNNs). Invariant representations are GNN representations that are robust against graph structural perturbations, e.g., edge or node removals. To this end, we propose a novel training method that fits a representation to the training set of clusters such that the CGB is maximized. We show that the proposed method results in representations that are more robust against both node and edge removals compared to representations trained with conventional GNNs.

In autonomous driving, 3D occupancy prediction outputs voxel-wise status and semantic labels of the surrounding environment. Despite recent progress in this task for various use cases such as planning or sensor fusion with perception modules (eg., lidar), most existing methods have only been evaluated on datasets that are collected via synthetic rendering techniques from a single camera view point at low resolution without human drivers' interventions to reflect real world scenarios like traffic lights/signs [1]. To fill these gaps we introduce an open source dataset called UCSD20K which is generated by leveraging our recently released high fidelity simulator GVCSim[4] equipped with stateofart neural network based video compression model VQGANXVRNNs . This allows us not just recor

Large language models (LLMs) need to serve everyone, including a global majority of non-native English speakers. To achieve this goal, LLMs must be able to interpret input text in the context of its meaning and relevance for the task at hand. In our work we present a novel approach that enables an LLM to understand what a particular prompt means within a given task and the overall goal of the system by leveraging multi-task learning and semantic understanding. Our methodology is based on using prompt engineering to construct training data with a consistent structure, which can then be used to fine-tune the model's representation of the task. The resulting model can then be applied as a general purpose solution to assist in interpreting user queries. Our experiments show that our method sig

The paper presents different Very Large Scale Integration (VLSI) architectures of a processor for robotics applications. The processor is based on a digital circuit implementation of the closed-form solution of the forward kinematics of a robot manipulator. Three different approaches are proposed to implement the square root operation that is an essential part of the forward kinematic algorithm. The first approach is based on the conventional digital circuit implementation of the square root operation. The second approach is based on the digit-by-digit calculation of the square root. The third approach is based on the use of a look-up table. The VLSI architectures are designed to evaluate the forward kinematic algorithm with a fixed-point representation of the data. The structures are compared in terms of the number of transistors, the number of cycles required for the computation, and the precision of the result.

Detecting surface anomalies of industrial materials poses a significant challenge within a myriad of industrial settings, including but not limited to the automotive industry. As a solution, we propose a novel method for anomaly detection in 3D point clouds that uses a two-stage approach: (i) an automated detection of regions of interest (ROI) and (ii) an outlier detection based on localized PCA (LPCA). In contrast to existing methods, our proposed approach is capable of detecting small surface anomalies on various types of surfaces without the need for predefined parameters. It also does not require any manual initialization or training data, making it easy to use and versatile. We evaluate our method on a real dataset consisting of industrial parts acquired by LiDAR. Our results demonstr

To enhance the handover performance in fifth generation (5G) cellular systems, conditional handover (CHO) is introduced for the first time. We propose a novel CHO mechanism based on user activity prediction and propose a model to estimate its value. The proposed mechanism is designed using a Bayesian neural network (BNN), which can predict the probability of the next user activity. This prediction is utilized in CHO to determine whether a user should be handed over to another base station or not. Simulation results demonstrate that the proposed method outperforms existing methods by improving the handover success rate while reducing the number of handovers. Furthermore, our analysis shows that the BNN-based CHO algorithm reduces the average handover delay by 23%, compared to conventional C

Deep Clustering Network (DCN) is a popular fully-supervised deep learning approach for clustering. Existing DCN methods first learn a deep representation and then assign cluster labels based on the learned representation. However, the clustering assignment is often sub-optimal since the cluster labels are treated as ordinary categorical labels which do not leverage the inherent cluster structure information. In this paper, we propose a new DCN method which jointly learns the deep representation and the cluster structure. We propose a novel structure-aware loss which encourages the learned representation to preserve the cluster structure information. We further propose a new structure-aware clustering assignment approach which leverages our proposed structure-aware loss and the conventional cross-entropy loss. Our proposed structure-aware clustering assignment approach can be applied to existing DCN methods. We conduct extensive experiments to validate the effectiveness of our proposed approach.

In real-world classification tasks, such as image classification, it is often the case that some classes are more similar to each other than to others. For example, in CIFAR-100, 'apple' and 'orange' are more similar to each other than to 'truck'. We propose a simple transfer measure based on the pre-training loss that captures this notion of class similarity. We show that this measure correlates with downstream performance in few-shot classification settings. We also find that certain classes are harder to learn for wide residual networks, and that our transfer measure allows us to predict the class-specific generalization gap.

Large Language Models (LLMs) like ChatGPT, Google Bard, and Microsoft Bing have shown remarkable capabilities in answering complex questions. Recently, OpenAI has released a new tool called 'API for Prompt Engineering' to enable API-based prompt engineering. However, the effectiveness of this tool is still limited due to its lack of supporting open-source datasets and benchmarks. In this paper, we introduce an open-source dataset called 'GPT-4 Benchmark', which contains 100K human-created questions with human answers from 36 distinct domains. We also develop an open-source framework called `GPT-4 Benchmark Toolkit'. The toolkit enables users to evaluate LLMs using GPT-4 Benchmark and supports different evaluation metrics such as precision, recall, F1 score, and MAPE. Furthermore, the toolk

Product search plays an essential role in eCommerce. It was treated as a special type of information retrieval problem, which aims to find the most relevant product(s) from millions or even billions within milliseconds for each query given by customers on online shopping websites [1]. As people increasingly use mobile devices and smart assistants (e.,g Alexa), more interactions with products will take place outside traditional web browser environments that are not well suited[2][3] . Given the importance it holds among users across different contexts , there has been significant research effort towards improving its performance over time.

While the volume of remote sensing data is increasing daily, deep learning in Earth Observation faces lack of accurate annotations for supervised optimization. Crowdsourcing projects such as OpenStreetMap distribute the annotation load to their community. However, such annotation inevitably generates noise due to insufficient control of the label quality, lack of annotators, frequent changes of the Earth's surface as a result of natural disasters and urban development, among many other factors. We present Adaptively trIggered Online Object-wise correction (AIO2) to address annotation noise induced by incomplete label sets. AIO2 features an Adaptive Correction Trigger (ACT) module that avoids label correction when the model training under- or overfits, and an Online Object-wise Correction (

Real-time 6 DOF localization of bronchoscopes is crucial for enhanc ing the quality and safety in interventional procedures. We propose a novel method to perform real time endoscope tracking using both internal (iLight) marker based information, as well ex ternal markers such those that are found on endoscopic tools. This enables us not only t o track an unlabeled but also labeled tool with high accuracy without any additional hardware requirements or modifications at either side - neither by inserting special targets into th e video stream nor through external cameras placed inside operating theaters during surgery sessions themselves!

Prohibited item detection in X-ray images is one of the most essential and highly effective methods to prevent illegal goods from entering restricted areas. Recent studies have shown that deep learning based approaches, such as convolutional neural networks (CNNs), can achieve superior performance compared with traditional image processing techniques for this task when using large datasets containing thousands or millions 3D CT scans; however these models tend not be applicable due their reliance on huge amounts computational power & time consuming training processes which make them unsuitable solutions given current industry constraints regarding cost/time availability etc.. To address some challenges faced by researchers attempting develop practical machine vision algorithms capable perf

As Large Language Models (LLMs) continue to advance in performance, their size has increased substantially. This poses a challenge for deployment and integration into production systems as the resource requirements for LLM execution grow. In this work, we present an approach based on model compression to address this problem. Our method involves pruning large models using a weighted sparse autoencoder (SAE), followed by low-rank tensor decomposition of the resultant sparse matrix. The process results in a new compressed model that is significantly smaller than the original large model while maintaining its computational efficiency. We demonstrate the effectiveness of our method by applying it to a range of LLMs, including GPT-3.5 and ChatGPT. The compressed models exhibit improved inferenc

Motor imagery classification based on electroencephalography (EEG) signals is one of the most popular research topics in brain-computer interfaces. In this work, we propose a novel approach to classify motor images using EEG data that combines the convolutional neural network with feature fusion and an attention mechanism through weighted aggregation layers between different networks' output features at each layer level for better performance than traditional methods such as CNN or SVM alone without any additional training time requirements compared against them respectively which results into improved accuracy levels when tested across multiple datasets including both healthy subjects who were able achieve up 96% average correct response rates while those suffering from Parkinson’s diseas

Online discussions of real-world crimes often assume that men and women commit different kinds of crimes. This assumption is based on the notion that there are essential differences between men and women that lead to different criminal behaviors. The crime/gender dichotomy is thus presented as self-evident and uncontested. This paper uses Gendered Language Identification (GLI) to analyze the gender proportions and the gender blindness in online discussions of a crime event in Taiwan. We focus on the first 24 hours of online discussions to capture the initial public reaction to the crime. Our study finds that the top 100 posts are mostly written by men who use gendered language when referring to the suspect but not when referring to the victim. We argue that this usage of gendered language is a manifestation of the crime/gender dichotomy. By excluding women from discussions about crime, online communities are helping to construct a dichotomy between criminal men and victimized women. We propose that GLI can contribute to unveiling gender blindness in public discourses on criminal events.

We present INDICVOICES, a dataset of natural and spontaneous speech containing a comprehensive range of Indian languages. In addition to its size, the dataset is unique in that it comprises both English and non-English utterances from various regions. This makes INDICVOICES an important resource for speech research across diverse domains, including voice user interfaces (VUI), automatic speech recognition (ASR) systems, multilingual ASR systems, language modeling, and speech synthesis. The dataset was collected through online crowd-sourcing platforms, using a mobile app, while ensuring quality control throughout the collection process. To facilitate research, we have developed a website that provides access to both a web interface as well as a Python API.

Pre-trained Large Language Models (LLMs) are beginning to dominate the AI space. But what makes them so effective? In this blog post, we’ll explore the concepts of generative pre-training and self-supervised learning, which are at the heart of LLM development. We’ll dive into the inner workings of these models, how they are trained, and their capabilities in various domains. Additionally, we will discuss the challenges and limitations faced by these models and possible future directions for improvement. By understanding the key principles behind LLMs, we can better grasp the potential and limitations of these powerful tools in shaping our digital landscape. Whether you're a researcher or an enthusiast, this blog post aims to provide valuable insights into the foundations of LLMs and their

We present Structure from WiFi (SfW), a simple and lightweight method to geometrically map indoor environments using only a WiFi module. We leverage the fact that WiFi signals propagate in a line-of-sight manner and that the received signal strength indicator (RSSI) approximately follows a path-loss model. We derive a geometric interpretation of the path-loss model, which allows us to express the walls of the environment as algebraic constraints over the WiFi measurements. We show that despite the simplicity of our approach, it is able to produce accurate geometric maps of indoor environments, without the need of any calibration. We evaluate our method both through simulations and real-world experiments, the latter conducted in two different buildings. Our experiments show that SfW can accurately reconstruct walls using only WiFi measurements.

Protein design requires a deep understanding of the inherent complexities of the protein universe. While many successes have been achieved in recent years, there is still much room for improvement with respect to both designing new proteins from scratch and improving existing ones by rational engineering or directed evolution approaches (evolving them into more productive variants). Our lab uses computational methods as well experimental tools towards advancing this mission: we develop algorithms that can be used during early stages when generating initial designs; then test these ideas on real-world datasets via large scale experiments involving millions/billions iterations per day until reaching convergence at which point one obtains an optimal solution close enough so it's ready use! We

Assessing the robustness of multi-sensor fusion systems against adversarial attacks is a crucial yet challenging task. Sensor data can be attacked, and this can lead to incorrect fusion decisions. For example, in autonomous driving, an attack on the optical sensor can cause the vehicle to miss detecting an obstacle, with severe consequences. One approach to assessing robustness is via formal methods, which can provide certifiable robustness guarantees. However, multi-sensor fusion systems pose unique challenges for formal methods. Firstly, sensor data is often high-dimensional and can follow different distributions, making it difficult to find a suitable abstraction. Secondly, fusion rules are often non-linear and can be sensitive to small changes in sensor data, making it difficult to establish boundedness properties. In this paper, we propose a framework called CERTIFYING robustness Of Multi-sensor fusion systems against semantic attacks (COMMIT) using formal methods. We introduce a new semi-formal abstraction that can handle high-dimensional data and different distributions, called Multi-sensor Fusion Network (M-FNet). We also propose a new technique to linearize the fusion rule around a set of candidate certificates using Taylor series expansion. We demonstrate the effectiveness of COMMIT by applying it to two case studies: autonomous driving and robotic manipulation. Our results show that COMMIT can certify higher robustness margins compared to other methods.

Audio-Visual Source Localization (AVSL) aims to locate sounding objects within video frames. However, the performance of state-of-the-art AVSL methods relies on supervised models that need to be trained with ground truth source labels. This requirement is challenging because it requires manual annotation efforts and depends on the quality of labels. To address this challenge, we propose the first framework for self-supervised audio-visual localization, SSVL. It leverages a novel temporal consistency loss that encourages the model to predict source locations at different time steps while preserving coherence across frames. We further introduce two additional losses: a geometric prior loss and a center-bias loss, which respectively improve the accuracy and robustness of our method. Our exten
QRscript is a proposed new quantum programming language. This paper specifies the language via a grammar, and via an operational semantics in terms of quantum circuits.

Existing causal discovery methods based on combinatorial optimization or search are slow, prohibiting their application to large-scale data. We present a fast algorithm for discovering the underlying structure of directed and undirected graphs from conditional mutual information (MI) matrices without using any auxiliary knowledge such as node labels/features nor structural assumptions. Our method is an extension that incorporates two novel algorithmic ingredients into our recent work: First we propose partial 01 matrix completion with partial row sum constraints which can be solved efficiently in $O(n^2)$ time by exploiting spectral properties; Second it utilizes multiple linear regressions instead of single regression at each step due its computational efficiency when handling dens

Existing Neural Machine Translation (NMT) models mainly handle translation in the general domain, which may not be applicable to specific domains. However, recent studies on domain adaptation have shown that knowledge transfer from a source language to another target language is possible. In this paper, we introduce an approach for cross-lingual adaptation of NMT models with a focus on low-resource languages. The proposed method involves using a common encoder-decoder architecture with shared parameters for both source and target languages and adding an additional adapter layer to adapt the model to the target language. We explore different approaches for training the adapters, including fine-tuning, gradient reversal, and parameter free methods. Experimental results show that our approach

Multi-sensor fusion for 3D occupancy prediction is a newly emerging but rapidly growing research topic. It aims to fuse diverse types of sensor data (e.g., LiDAR, RGB cameras, etc.) to achieve more accurate 3D occupancy predictions. However, existing methods are often designed in a complex architecture with multiple intricate modules, making them difficult to understand and implement. In this work, we develop a simple yet effective fusion framework called OccFusion. It follows a two-stage structure. The first stage is a sensor data processor, which converts raw sensor data into a consistent representation. The second stage is a fusion predictor, which fuses the processed data and predicts 3D occupancy. Our framework incorporates four processor-predictor pairs, i.e., LiDAR processor with LiDAR predictor, RGB processor with RGB predictor, LiDAR-RGB joint processor with joint predictor, and an auxiliary deep neural classifier. These components are independently trained by using an efficient multi-task learning strategy. During inference, the fused prediction is obtained by combining the predictions from these four branches. Our experiments show that OccFusion outperforms state-of-the-art methods on several benchmark datasets. Its simplicity also makes it easy to understand and implement. We hope that our work will promote multi-sensor fusion for 3D occupancy prediction as a new research area.

We study a natural application of contract design to search problems with probabilistic prior and exploration costs. The classical framework for solving this problem is to choose the contract such that its worst-case cost is minimized over all possible inputs, but this leads to an excessive risk in the expected reward. In contrast, we propose to use the expected value as the objective instead, which ensures that the contract will be used as much as possible. This turns out to be equivalent to optimizing the expected value while enforcing a constraint on the worst-case cost. Our formulation yields several new algorithmic results, including a novel greedy algorithm that is guaranteed to achieve any given cost bound. Moreover, we give a lower bound on the expected value of any algorithm using

This work introduces an optimization-based locomotion control framework for on-the-fly synthbot generation. Unlike traditional robot design, where the entire locomotion is planned beforehand, our approach allows for continual adaptation and improvement of a robot's locomotive performance during its operation. Our method first generates an initial synthbot through reinforcement learning (RL) using simulated data. The initial synthbot is then used to collect real-world trajectories which are used to generate a data-driven model. This model is employed in an optimization framework to iteratively improve the synthbot's performance. We apply this framework to two tasks: walking and climbing stairs. For each task, we generate a different synthbot through RL. Then, we use these robots to collect

Model-free reinforcement learning (RL) is promising for generating optimal behaviors in complex dynamic environments. However, it is often difficult to learn optimal policies from scratch using model-free RL because of a large state-action space or delayed rewards. We propose a novel behavior generation method with latent actions, which enables agents to explore and learn optimal policies efficiently. Our method latent behaviors, which are not directly observable, as actions and incorporates them into the RL process. We show that our method can effectively explore the action space and learn optimal policies from latent actions. The experiments demonstrate that the proposed method is 1.9--10.0 times more sample efficient and 7.2--16.7 times faster than the baseline methods in solving 10 out of 100 tasks in the DMC Suite.

We consider the problem of approximating the maximum distance to a point over an intersection of balls. This problem is NP-hard, and we investigate the possibility of obtaining a fully polynomial-time randomized approximation scheme (FPRAS) for it. We show that a natural approach to this problem may lead to an FPRAS, but the proof of this fact requires the introduction of a new geometric structure, which we call a distance tile. A distance tile is a convex polytope that contains the centers of the balls in its interior and whose boundary contains the set of points that have the same maximum distance to a ball. We prove that the vertices of a distance tile can be efficiently approximated, and we use this fact to show that the maximum distance to a point over an intersection of balls can be efficiently approximated as well.

Hashed Time-Locked Contracts (HTLCs) are often used to build payment channels between two parties. Multi-hop channels can facilitate payments between parties on a global scale. Such channels are used by the Lightning Network (LN) and other similar protocols. The channels in these protocols are unidirectional and the funds locked in them cannot be recovered if the counterparty goes offline. This article proposes a protocol that allows two (or more) parties to perform a cross-chain swap using HTLC-based channels. The protocol ensures that the funds are safely locked until the swap is completed or the timeout expires. A swap using this protocol can be thought of as a bidirectional channel that connects two blockchains.

Access to safe and effective healthcare is a fundamental human right. However, the potential of medical artificial intelligence (AI) to improve healthcare access and outcomes is limited by the lack of affordable and accessible AI tools in low- and middle-income countries (LMICs). One reason for this AI accessibility gap is the lack of multilingual medical Large Language Models (LLMs), which are critical for developing country-specific AI tools but are computationally expensive to develop and deploy. To address this gap, we present Apollo, a lightweight multilingual medical LLM. Apollo is the first LLM that simultaneously achieves (1) a small footprint of only 3.2M parameters, (2) high accuracy on medical tasks such as disease diagnosis and medication recommendation, and (3) low cost, which enables its deployment on low-power devices. By open-sourcing Apollo, we aim to empower researchers and developers in LMICs to readily develop and deploy affordable AI tools. Our evaluation results show that Apollo outperforms other LLMs by 1.8% on average across 17 medical tasks. If deployed on a low-power device, Apollo can reduce the cost of a medical consultation by 78% compared to a commercial AI solution.

Unsupervised domain adaptation (UDA) aims to adapt a model trained on one domain (called source) to a novel domain (called target) using only unlabeled data. Previous methods mainly rely on domain discrimination to learn from the distribution difference between source and target. However, strong domain discrimination may cause the model to lose the generalization ability on the source domain, and it is difficult to find a good balance between domain adaptation and generalization. In this work, we propose a novel mutual prompting strategy to promote the model to better capture the universal features across domains and at the same time reduce the risk of overfitting on the source domain. Specifically, we design two prompting modules, each of which is responsible for prompting the model to focus on either the source or target domain. They are mutually constrained by a regularization term to ensure consistency between the two prompts. Our method can be applied to both image and text domains, and it shows competitive performance compared to previous UDA methods.

Genetic Programming (GP) is a form of evolutionary computation that evolves programs in a syntax-directed manner. The programs are often represented using trees, which grow and mutate in size during evolution. Despite the popularity of GP, there is still a lack of tools that can provide easy-to-understand explanations for the created programs. In this paper, we propose a novel approach to explain GP trees using natural language. Our approach relies on two key ideas: (1) mapping the tree-based programs to string-based representations, and (2) using a large language model (LLM) to generate natural language explanations from the string-based representations. We evaluate our approach on two different GP systems, PushGP and Grammar-Guided GP, using their built-in ability to produce tree-based representations of the evolved programs. We also compare our approach to the state-of-the-art in GP explanation methods. Our results show that the explanations generated by our approach are of similar quality as the state-of-the-art, while being more accurate and concise.

We introduce model lakes, a formalism for describing the structure of lakes and other aquatic ecosystems. Model lakes are reaction networks in which the species are divided into two disjoint sets, namely, the bulk species and the interface species. The dynamics of the bulk species is described by a ordinary differential equation (ODE), whereas the dynamics of the interface species is described by a discrete update rule. We focus on model lakes in which the ODE is a monotone dynamical system (MDS). For such model lakes, we provide a complete characterization of the asymptotic dynamics. In particular, we show that the asymptotic dynamics of the bulk species is determined by the dynamics on the carrying simplex of the ODE. We also show that the interface species converge to a equilibrium configuration, and that the set of equilibrium configurations is finite. These results are obtained by casting the discrete dynamics as an optimization problem, namely, as a constrained subgraph of the product of the positive orthant with itself.

First-person 3D hand pose estimation has been drawing broader attention owing to its close relation with various human-computer interaction applications. In contrast to the ample research progress and appealing real-world implications, first-person 3D hand pose estimation still faces many challenges, especially in the open-vocabulary and unconstrained settings. In this regard, we study the problem of estimating first-person 3D hand pose from a single view, which is captured in the wild with severe appearance variations and unrestricted finger motions. Intuitively, more than one view is better than a single view for estimating first-person 3D hand pose as multiple views provide richer information. Unfortunately, hand motions in the first-person perspective often suffer from severe occlusions, especially when fingers move behind the torso. To address the single-to-dual-view adaptation problem, we introduce a simple yet effective dual-view correlation learning approach, from which a single-view representation can be adaptively correlated with the corresponding dual-view representation. In particular, we propose a cross-view residual learning (CVR) module to learn the cross-view correlations. Furthermore, a dual-view feature refinement (DVR) module is proposed to refine the representations from both global and local views. Without any pose annotations, our model can automatically adapt the single-view representation to the dual-view case and outperforms the existing single-view methods. To thoroughly validate the effectiveness of our method, we establish two large-scale first-person 3D hand pose estimation datasets, named NTU-RGB+D and CMU-MOSE. Our method achieves the state-of-the-art performance on both datasets.

Phishing attacks have inflicted substantial losses on individuals and businesses alike, necessitating the development of effective solutions to counter these threats. In this paper, we propose a novel approach that leverages Large Language Models (LLMs) for generating personalized phishing attack responses. Our method involves fine-tuning LLMs in response to specific phishing attacks, which allows us to generate customized responses tailored to each individual victim's unique situation. This approach offers several advantages compared to existing methods: it is less prone to overfitting, more scalable, and provides a higher degree of personalization. We demonstrate our method's effectiveness using a dataset comprising 230K unique phishing incidents collected from 15k+ users across 19 count

In representation learning on graph-structured data, many popular graph neural networks (GNNs) fail to capture long-range dependencies, leading to performance degradation. Furthermore, this weakness is magnified when the concerned graph is characterized by heterophily (low homophily). To solve this issue, this paper proposes a novel graph learning framework called the graph convolutional network with self-attention (GCN-SA). The proposed scheme exhibits an exceptional generalization capability in node-level representation learning. The proposed GCN-SA contains two enhancements corresponding to edges and node features. For edges, we utilize a self-attention mechanism to design a stable and effective graph-structure-learning module that can capture the internal correlation between any pair o

Owing to the increase in 'certified' phishing websites, there is a steady increase in the number of phishing attacks. In 2018 alone, more than 1 million phishing sites were created by cybercriminals. To combat this growing problem, Google has announced that it will be launching a new feature which will automatically block malicious links. The feature uses Google Safe Browsing and Gmail Smart Reply to warn users when they click on suspicious links or open malicious attachments in their email inbox. By implementing this technology, Google aims to provide an extra layer of protection against these types of online scams. As a result, it can help protect users from potential financial losses due to identity theft or other malicious activities associated with phishing attacks. Additionally, this

In recent years, neural network-based Wake Word Spotting achieves good performance on clean data. However the noise in real environments is not negligible and it will affect wake word detection adversely. In this paper we propose a novel method to enhance noisy audio signals for improved speech recognition using an autoregressive model (AR) with sparse coding regularization by learning from both target sound class information as well enhancing general background sounds simultaneously within one single training phase before applying standard endtoend ASR system downstreams which significantly improves accuracy over previous approaches while maintaining fast inference time allowing high throughput rates even when processing large amounts of input files at once without requiring any additiona

The advent of large vision-language models (LVLMs) represents a noteworthy milestone in the field of artificial intelligence. These LVLMs are capable of generating textual descriptions, often with impressive accuracy and precision, when presented with visual inputs. However, this remarkable ability is accompanied by notable limitations in their capacity to understand visual concepts beyond those that have been explicitly trained on. This paper explores these constraints and highlights various ways that such limitations may be overcome or reduced, focusing primarily on the domain of 3D shape understanding. Our key contribution lies in the development of a systematic methodology for evaluating the extent to which LVLMs can grasp basic visual concepts, and we demonstrate this approach using a

In the process of information extraction, extracting entities from hypertext documents is a fundamental and challenging task. The task is to identify the named entities in the hyper-text documents and classify them into pre-defined categories. The hypertext documents are retrieved from the webpages of online news media. The challenge in extracting entities from such documents is that the context in which an entity is mentioned is usually lost when the hypertext links are followed. We have proposed a novel approach called Hypertext Entity Extraction Approach (HEEA) to extract entities from such hypertext documents. The HEEA approach identifies the entities mentioned in the main body of text as well as the linked documents. The approach follows a two step process; the first step is a supervised learning approach, where a classifier is trained to identify the named entities in the main body of text, and the second step is a clustering approach, where the linked documents are clustered into groups based on the similarity of the named entities they contain. We have evaluated the HEEA approach on the I2B2 2012 dataset, and the results show that the approach achieves an F-score of 0.74 for entity recognition, and an F-score of 0.6 for entity classification.

Defocus blur is a persistent problem in microscope imaging that poses harm to path analysis and image recognition tasks. In this paper, we propose an iterative deblurring approach based on residual learning for defocused microscopy images which combines the advantages of both traditional single-image super resolution (SISR) methods as well sharp details from deep convolutional networks trained with high quality reference datasets obtained using computational refocusing algorithms at different focal planes by means Gaussian blurring filter convolution operations similar those used during acquisition process itself; all steps performed without requiring any additional hardware or software modifications beyond existing ones already available within system's architecture design specification r

Concept drift is a common challenge in traditional machine learning algorithms and can lead to degraded model performance over time. Concept drift is often accompanied by a change in the relationship between features, which can be difficult for models to distinguish from normal data variation. Identifying concept drift at an early stage can help prevent significant performance degradation in machine learning models. This paper proposes a novel unsupervised incremental learning method to detect concept drift, called Dual Concept Drift Detection (DCDD). DCDD learns a representation of normal data distribution changes over time and can distinguish between drift in the relationship between features and drift in the feature distribution. We evaluate DCDD on synthetic and real-world datasets that contain concept drift, comparing it to existing drift detection methods. The results demonstrate that DCDD outperforms existing approaches, even in the presence of complex concept drift. DCDD is also evaluated as a anomaly detection method on several benchmark anomaly detection datasets, where it also shows strong performance.

Tensor compression is vital to enabling massive MIMO fronthaul with practical optical fiber bandwidth. We derive an upper bound on the mutual information rate (MIR) for tensor compression of zero-forcing (ZF) beamforming matrices. The upper bound is a function of the number of antennas, number of users, and channel correlation. We show that the ZF-MIMO tensor compression problem is NP-hard. To overcome the high complexity of achieving the optimal ZF-MIMO tensor codebook, we propose a low-complexity suboptimal algorithm based on the recently developed train-of-bits (ToB) representation. The proposed algorithm significantly outperforms the state-of-the-art ZF-MIMO tensor compression algorithms in terms of computational complexity. Simulations demonstrate the efficacy of the proposed ToB-based algorithm.

Small sample sizes are common in many disciplines, which necessitates pooling roughly similar datasets across multiple sites or organizations. The process of merging these data sources is often challenging and time-consuming due to the potential for inconsistencies between the different datasets. Data quality issues can result from varying definitions of variables, different levels of granularity in data collection, and even missing values. These challenges can be compounded by the lack of standardized metadata describing the characteristics of each dataset, making it difficult to assess their compatibility and suitability for integration. To address this issue, we propose a novel approach to data integration that leverages the use of ontologies to facilitate the alignment and harmonizatio

Embodied scene understanding serves as the cornerstone for autonomous agents to perceive, interpret, and interact with dynamic environments. However current visual-motor reasoning approaches often fall short of solving complex scenes involving rich physical interactions due mainly to two shortcomings: 1) lack multisensory information; 2 ) inability in dynamically adapting action policies during execution without prior training or experience on specific task instances/scenarios (e g., a new object). To address these challenges we propose Multimodal Reinforcement Learning via Latent Action Space Dynamics , MARLADS which exploits latent dynamics learned from multimodal sensory inputs e .g ., RGBD videos plus audio clips together within an environment simulator allowing us not only simulate m

Ever since Large Language Models (LLMs) and related applications have become broadly available, they’ve been touted as the future of search. And for good reason: while their performance on a variety tasks is far from perfect — particularly in more complicated domains or ones where multiple sources are required to form an opinion – it seems clear that LLMS will be able outperform traditional information retrieval models at least in some scenarios if given sufficient training data [1]. But there remains one key question about these new approaches which has not yet been answered by anyone with real-world experience using them; what exactly does ‘relevance’, when applied through large language model technology rather than conventional IR techniques like TFIDF ranking mean? Are we looking forwa

Mealy machines are a standard formalism for modeling reactive systems. Often, two Mealy machines that model the same system differ locally from one another, by having different transition labels on some edges. To capture such local differences, we introduce timed Mealy machines, which are Mealy machines equipped with timers that measure time elapsing between two consecutive transitions. We show that the learning problem of timed Mealy machines is not finite-state, even if both the underlying transition graph and the set of timers are acyclic. This motivates the study of active learning algorithms, which can efficiently identify the correct hypothesis even in the presence of noise. In this paper, we propose the first active learning algorithm for timed Mealy machines, based on the IDA* search algorithm. The algorithm is able to learn efficiently both deterministic and non-deterministic timed Mealy machines from possibly noisy observations, under a wide class of noise distributions.

ATLAS experiment at the CERN Large Hadron Collider (LHC) produces approximately 400 PB of data per year, which is analysed by a global collaboration of about 2000 scientists worldwide. The analysis is performed using a variety of dedicated software frameworks, which are maintained by the collaboration. New frameworks are developed for every experiment, often resulting in code that is difficult to maintain and scale. REANA (https://reana.io) is a recently proposed, open-source, framework for reproducible, portable and scalable scientific workflows. It uses standard containerisation technology (e.g. Docker) and cloud resources (e.g. AWS, Azure, GCP), and follows the "12-factor app" philosophy, which makes it easy to develop, test and deploy applications. Here we demonstrate a proof-of-concept of the ATLAS pMSSM computational workflow running on top of the REANA platform. We show that the workflow, which performs a physics analysis of LHC data, can be executed using standard CPU and GPU resources. The results are consistent with the expectations from the underlying physics model. The analysis, which traditionally takes several hours, is accelerated by a factor of 3.3 using an NVIDIA GPU. The workflow can be scaled up/down in a transparent way, and the data products can be easily accessed and visualised. We also demonstrate how the REANA platform can be used to deploy interactive Jupyter notebooks to explore the data products.

Deep learning methods have access to be employed for solving physical systems governed by parametric partial differential equations (PDEs). However, the numerical stability of these models is largely unknown. This paper focuses on studying the convergence rate of neural network-based approximation schemes for parametrized PDEs with fixed domain and varying parameters. To do so we consider a class of general PDEs, including the wave equation, which are linear in space but nonlinear in time, and employ a novel data-driven methodology based on deep learning to approximate them. We analyze and study the well-posedness and convergence of the solution associated with the proposed deep learning approximations. As a result, this work provides a theoretical framework for understanding the behavior

Video temporal grounding (VTG) aims to locate specific temporal segments from an untrimmed video based on a given natural language query. However, existing methods fail to fully exploit the textual and visual information contained in both the query and the video for robust VTG. To this end, we propose VITAL, a novel framework that employs Visual-Textual Fusion and Attention Mechanism for VTG. Specifically, our method firstly performs an initial alignment between the query and the video using an off-the-shelf pre-trained model, and then utilizes a multimodal contrastive learning strategy to enhance the interaction between the query and the video representation. Moreover, we introduce a novel temporal attention module that captures the temporal dynamics within the video, improving the accura

Machine Learning (ML) has replaced traditional handcrafted methods for perception and prediction in autonomous driving. The integration of ML models into the vehicle's Electronic Control Units (ECUs) is now considered a standard approach to enhance performance, safety, and comfort. However, the lack of explainability, robustness, and security capabilities associated with the use of ML models in these systems presents challenges in their deployment and utilization. Therefore, it is crucial to develop techniques that enable the transparent and safe integration of ML models within ECUs. This paper proposes a novel architecture for ECU-integrated ML models, which combines the benefits of both the cloud and edge computing paradigms. We investigate the potential of this architecture by applying

Federated learning is a collaborative scheme to train a learning model across multiple participants without sharing data. The conventional federated learning requires that all participants fully trust the federated server. Otherwise, malicious participants can collude to compromise the privacy of all other participants. We propose partial federated learning, which only requires that a quorum of participants trust the server. This dramatic relaxation of trust requirement enables partial federated learning to tolerate malicious attacks from a minority of participants. However, malicious participants can still collude to bias the model. To mitigate this concern, we require that the partial federated learning has sufficient redundancy to detect and correct biased models. We present partial stochastic gradient descent as a special case of partial federated learning, and propose practical schemes to mitigate the impact of malicious attacks.

Recent years have seen a surge of artificial currency-based mechanisms in contexts where monied payment is not an option. In particular, distributed ledger technologies (DLTs) like blockchain and cryptocurrencies offer new possibilities for monetary policies at the national level as well on smaller scales such that communities can create their own money systems through digital tokens or cryptocurrency mining pools. [1] In this piece we discuss recent developments in community currencies with two case studies: Bitcoin [2], arguably the first successful nonstate virtual economy,[3][4];[5],[6]. We also consider Bitcoin’ s evolution from its original incarnation to modern iterations including Lightning Network which has enabled peertopeer micropayments over public blockchains without

This paper presents a novel application of explainable AI (XAI) for root-causing defects in software systems. The proposed approach, termed XAI-based Root-Cause Analysis (XAI-RCA), employs an ensemble of deep neural networks (DNNs) to detect anomalies in the software system's runtime data. These anomalous events are further analyzed using XAI techniques such as gradient-weighted class activation mapping (Grad-CAM), integrated gradients (IntGrad), and SHapley Additive exPlanations (SHAP). These methods provide explanations for each anomaly, revealing the underlying causes of the defect. The resulting explanations serve as hypotheses that can guide manual investigation and debugging efforts, facilitating more efficient and effective root-causing activities. Extensive experiments on real-worl

We present a first-order theorem proving framework for establishing the correctness of functional programs implementing recursive data structures. Our approach is based on abstract interpretation techniques and relies neither upon (un)groundness nor fixpoint reasoning, which makes it particularly suitable to reason about arbitrary recursion patterns such as selfreference or mutual reference between mutually defined datatypes without resorting ad hoc methods that are known in general not always sound [1]. The soundness proof we provide leverages recent results from \cite{Barbanchon:20}.

Data integration is often performed to consolidate information from multiple disparate data sources during visual data analysis. Existing approaches either require manual configuration by analysts or automatically identify redundant data by comparing the schema of each pair of data sources. However, in practice, it is challenging for analysts to manually configure data integration rules and costly to compute all possible pairs of schemas (e.g., 400 billion pairs for the entire web) when scaling data analysis to the web-scale. In this paper, we introduce the notion of "synthetic data", which is data generated based on the schema of an existing data source using generative models. By leveraging synthetic data, analysts can benefit from a more comprehensive view of the data without having to

Can large language models (LLMs) learn to use tools? To investigate this question, we introduce an environment that provides an LLM with simulated access to a set of text-based tools, such as a search engine or Wikipedia. The LLM can ask questions to the tools, receive answers, and use the information to generate responses to prompts given by humans. We name this setting the Imaginarium and the LLMs that operate within it Imagenaries. We present three main findings: (1) Imagenaries outperform baseline LLMs on prompts that require factual knowledge about specific topics (e.g., world capitals), (2) Imagenaries learn to use the tools in diverse ways, mimicking human behavior, and (3) Imagenaries can be prompted to perform complex tasks, such as summarizing documents or answering questions. We show that access to diverse tools can enhance an LLM's capabilities and that Imagenaries can learn to use these tools effectively. We highlight the Imaginarium's potential to advance LLM learning through access to external knowledge sources.

In this paper we define a new algorithm to convert an input relational database to an output set of RDF triples. The algorithm can be used to e.g. load CSV data into a financial OWL ontology such as FIBO. The algorithm takes as input a set of relational conjunctive (select-from-where) queries, one for each input table, from the three column (subject, predicate, object) output RDF schema to the input table's relational schema. The algorithm's output is the only set of RDF triples for which a unique round-trip of the input data under the relational queries exists. The output may contain blank nodes, is unique up to unique isomorphism, and can be obtained using elementary formal methods (equational theorem proving and term model construction specifically).

Hand mesh reconstruction is a critical component in many human-centered applications. However, it is challenging to develop a model that can reconstruct high-precision hand meshes from a single RGB image due to insufficient in-hand data. In this work, we propose a simple but effective baseline called PHM (Proxy Hand Mesh) to reconstruct high-precision hand meshes. The key idea is to decouple the hand mesh reconstruction from the complex hand pose estimation, and use a proxy mesh to transfer the simple root-to-tip movement to the target hand mesh. We evaluate our PHM on two hand mesh reconstruction benchmarks and show that our PHM outperforms the state-of-the-art methods. We also conduct extensive experiments to study the effectiveness of our PHM under various circumstances, including occlusions, self-shadow, and cross-device consistency. The results demonstrate that our PHM is robust under these circumstances. Furthermore, we develop a simple yet strong baseline for hand mesh reconstruction, which is \textbf{17x} more computationally efficient than the state-of-the-art. We hope our work will inspire more research on efficient and robust hand mesh reconstruction under complex real-world conditions.

Interventionists perform complex medical procedures, such as inserting a guidewire into a blood vessel. They can use X-ray imaging guidance systems, which display a 2D projection of a patient's anatomy. However, these systems do not provide enough information for a machine to navigate a 3D environment. Previous research used a 2D guidance system and a pre-collected 2D training dataset to develop a wire tracking system. This system used a multi-goal reinforcement learning (RL) strategy that required the specification of a goal state. The state included the expected 2D projection of the guidewire tip and the pressure applied to the enter button. This information was derived from the last step of the procedure, where the guidewire tip was expected to be inside the target vessel. We propose a novel zero-shot RL strategy that does not require a pre-collected training dataset or the specification of a goal state. We validated our method using a total of 1100 test cases, where the guidewire trajectories were manually annotated. Our method outperformed a previous state-of-the-art method in terms of tracking accuracy, computational efficiency, and generalizability.

One common way to speed up the find operation within a set of text files involves a trigram index. We use a large-scale real-world dataset with 300 million entries to investigate how the frequency and distribution of stopwords affects the performance of this approach. Our results reveal that even though stopwords are not present in many documents, they can still significantly impact the efficiency of the search. In particular, we show that a search for a single word is often more efficient than searching for a specific sequence of three words. Additionally, we observe that the presence of stopwords in the index has a significant effect on the efficiency of the search. Finally, we present several strategies that can be used to enhance the performance of the search engine when it encounters

Smiles and laughs detection systems have attracted a lot of attention in the past decade contributing to different real-life applications such as customer service, entertainment, healthcare, etc. Recently, neural network-based algorithms have emerged as the most powerful tools for detecting smiles and laughs. Despite their effectiveness, these approaches usually require significant computational resources, limiting their practical usage in embedded devices with limited processing power. To address this limitation, we propose an efficient and accurate method that uses only 50 ms of audio data as input without any need for specialized hardware. Our approach utilizes CNN and LSTM models pretrained on large datasets and fine-tuned using only 400 samples from our dataset. We demonstrate that ou

We investigate the deterministic query complexity of Boolean functions with fixed weight. According to the average-case lower bounds of Kabanets and Impagliazzo (2004) and of Dolev, Fandina and Gutfreund (2006), any deterministic algorithm that computes a Boolean function $f \colon \{0,1\}^n \to \{0,1\}$ with success probability at least $2/3$ and that makes at most $O(\sqrt{n \log n})$ queries, must have $\Omega(\sqrt{n \log n})$ average-case complexity. We show that these lower bounds are tight by proving that there is a Boolean function $f \colon \{0,1\}^n \to \{0,1\}$, with $f^{-1}(1)$ having size $n - O(\sqrt{n \log n})$, that can be computed by a deterministic algorithm with success probability at least $2/3$ and with $O(\sqrt{n \log n})$ queries. We observe that this function $f$ is not an affine function, and we further show that our result cannot be strengthened to obtain a lower bound for the deterministic query complexity of affine functions. We then restrict our attention to Boolean functions with fixed weight, and we prove that for any fixed $w \in [n]$, any deterministic algorithm that computes a Boolean function $f \colon \{0,1\}^n \to \{0,1\}$ with $f^{-1}(1)$ having size $n - w + 1$ and with success probability at least $2/3$, must have $\Omega(\min\{\sqrt{n \log n}, w\})$ average-case complexity. We show that this lower bound is tight by exhibiting a Boolean function $f$ with $f^{-1}(1)$ having size $n - w + 1$, that can be computed by a deterministic algorithm with success probability at least $2/3$ and with $O(\min\{\sqrt{n \log n}, w\})$ queries.

Few-shot learning (FSL) aims to learn novel tasks with very few labeled examples, which can be challenging due to the lack of sufficient data for training. In this work, we propose a novel method named Adaptive Meta-Learning (AML), which adapts meta-learning based on the current task and input samples during inference. AML first leverages the initial pre-trained model, followed by a set of fine-tuned models. It then evaluates the performance of each model on the task and selects the most suitable one as the final prediction. To further enhance the model's capability, we introduce an adaptive selection scheme, where a model is selected based on its performance on the current batch of samples. Furthermore, we present a novel loss function to encourage the model to focus more on hard samples

Architectural Knowledge Management (AKM) involves the organized handling of information related to architectural design, construction, and maintenance. It is a critical element in ensuring efficient and effective building processes. This paper presents an AKM system for the analysis, management, and sharing of architectural knowledge. The proposed system uses various algorithms and techniques to analyze and visualize data from building designs, construction plans, and other relevant sources. The aim is to provide stakeholders with insights into the intricacies of architectural projects. Additionally, the system facilitates the storage and retrieval of knowledge, enabling users to access and share pertinent information seamlessly. The system also incorporates natural language processing (NL

Autonomous driving systems face the formidable challenge of navigating intricate and dynamic environments with high degrees of uncertainty. Despite the advancements in Deep Reinforcement Learning (DRL) for autonomous driving, existing approaches lack the ability to generate diverse behaviors that are crucial for safe and robust navigation. To address this limitation, we introduce a novel framework, Diversity-Guided Autonomy (DGA), which utilizes an external evaluator to guide agent exploration and improve behavior diversity. Our approach leverages the strengths of DRL by allowing the agent to explore its environment while ensuring that it also meets specific constraints established by the evaluator. This enables us to evaluate the agent's performance based on multiple criteria and optimize

Respiratory motion is one of the main sources of uncertainty in externally guided radiotherapy. Therefore, respiratory motion forecasting has the potential to improve the treatment plan evaluation and monitoring. This work proposes a novel respiratory motion forecasting framework using online learning of recurrent neural networks. We utilize a publicly available dataset for this purpose. The results demonstrate the ability of our proposed method to learn the dynamic nature of respiratory motion and its online update mechanism can potentially integrate into the treatment planning process. The evaluation shows our method can achieve better performance than the existing baseline models. Furthermore, the forecasting models are deployed on a real-time system for clinical trials.

Large language models and other "big models" have recently demonstrated impressive performance across a wide range of tasks. However, they are known to be misaligned with human values, posing a potential risk of harm. Safety researchers have proposed various alignment approaches to mitigate this risk, but these have not yet been systematically evaluated. In this work, we investigate eight alignment approaches: from the perspectives of (1) how well they align big models with human values, (2) their computational efficiency, and (3) their impact on downstream tasks. We evaluate these approaches on six big models and three alignment datasets. Our results show that alignment approaches that directly train a model to align with human values using reward modeling or preference learning are more effective and efficient than other approaches, such as self-supervised pre-training or direct heuristic mapping. Reward modeling and preference learning approaches also incur less of a performance cost on downstream tasks. We hope this work will further promote effective and efficient alignment approaches that better align big models with human values.

Training neural optimal transport (NOT) models is significantly slower and less accurate than training neural networks (NNs), mainly due to the involved Earth Mover's Distance (EMD) computation. To accelerate and improve the training of NOT models, we propose a novel expectile regularization method, which relies on the dual formulation of EMD and adds regularization on the expectation of the transport plan. The regularization term is a function of the dual variables and can be computed efficiently without the ground truth transport plan. We perform a theoretical analysis of the regularization term and show that our method favors a transport plan that is sparse in the dual space, thereby accelerating the convergence of the Sinkhorn algorithm. Empirical results on both synthetic and real-world datasets demonstrate that our method is 1.9--10.0 times faster and achieves 4.0--17.8\% higher accuracy than the state-of-the-art.

In this paper, we analyze the monotonicity of information aging in a remote estimation system, and propose a method to reduce it. We first introduce a generalized mutual information metric, which measures the amount of information shared by two random variables over a certain time interval, as well as their common ancestry relationship. By using this metric, we quantify information aging in remote estimation systems. Our analysis shows that if the mutual information between the sensor data and the estimator's prediction is larger than the one between the sensor data and the actual state, then information aging exists. To counter this effect, we propose a method called delayed predictive control (DPC), where a local controller is used to generate an auxiliary signal that can be transmitted

Place recognition is crucial for tasks like loop-closure detection and re-localization. Single-frame image place recognition has achieved promising results, but it often falls short when the input images are significantly degraded or captured in unfamiliar environments. We propose a novel end-to-end deep learning approach that can effectively perform place recognition under various conditions by utilizing an encoder-decoder framework with a multi-head attention module as well as a lightweight residual connection to improve feature extraction and refinement. Moreover, we introduce a context-aware attention mechanism to leverage global information from neighboring pixels and facilitate more accurate place recognition. Furthermore, our method incorporates a multi-scale feature fusion strategy

Successful job search results from job seekers' well-shaped social communication. While well-shaped social communication has been widely investigated in the domain of online dating, it is yet to be explored for employment and job searching. In this paper, we propose a novel framework called "Job Search Assistant" that leverages Natural Language Processing (NLP) techniques to automatically identify well-shaped social communication on job-seeker profiles and provides corresponding feedback to job seekers. We validate our model with an experimental dataset consisting of 1305 job seekers' profiles. Results indicate that our model achieves an accuracy of 98% with only two training epochs. Additionally, we conduct a user study with 20 participants and found that the proposed feedback can effecti

This paper introduces a new stochastic optimization method based on the regularized Fisher information matrix (FIM). The proposed algorithm updates the gradient estimation by taking into account the FIM, which captures both the statistical and geometric properties of the underlying distribution. In contrast to most existing methods that only consider the statistical properties, our approach incorporates both the statistical and geometric properties in a unified framework, resulting in an improved estimate of the gradient. The proposed algorithm is designed for problems where the cost function has multiple local optima and it has been shown to outperform other state-of-the-art algorithms. We validate the effectiveness of our method on various synthetic datasets and real-world applications s

Humans can easily distinguish the known and unknown categories and can recognize the unknown object by learning it once. As a result, we propose a new method of recognizing the unlabeled objects using the category-specific knowledge gained through previous studies on human recognition abilities. In addition to providing a comprehensive overview of the existing literature on human visual categorization abilities, this study focuses on the ability to recognize previously unseen objects in a given category. We first present the results from four experiments involving 100 participants who were asked to identify an unknown object based on its appearance within different categories. The accuracy for identifying unknown objects was found to be high at above 85%. However, when participants were sh

Purpose: The treatment of cardiovascular diseases requires complex and challenging navigation of the heart chambers. We introduce a novel active contour based method to segment these difficult-toimage structures from magnetic resonance (MR) images, which are commonly acquired in clinical practice for diagnosis or guidance during intervention procedures like cardiac catheterization/radiofrequency ablation etc., where precise information about intracardiac morphology is essential but hard due do poor image quality / contrast between tissues with similar intensities within 3D volumes that have been downsized along their z dimension by up three orders magnitude compared original data size before being processed further downstream pipeline steps such as registration algorithms requiring high re

Camouflage poses challenges in distinguishing a static target, whereas any movement of the target could pose a threat. To address this problem, we propose a novel unsupervised learning framework, named Contrastive Camouflage (ConCamo), which can effectively learn from real-world video sequences without requiring ground truth labels or supervision. The key idea is to leverage contrastive learning strategies that utilize video frames with different spatial locations as positive and negative pairs to enhance feature representation for camouflaged targets. We develop an effective contrastive loss function for videos by incorporating spatial information into our training scheme, enhancing the ability of detecting camouflaged objects. Experimental results on publicly available datasets demonstra

The widespread presence of offensive languages on social media has resulted in adverse effects on individual users, companies and society. Despite the increasing awareness towards this issue among researchers from various domains (e.,g psychology), to date there is little insight into what factors could be driving people’s use or exposure toward such language online – which would help inform future mitigation efforts aimed at reducing its impact altogether . In order for us as a community better understand how best address these challenges together we must first gain deeper knowledge about why individuals engage with toxic content themselves before attempting any kind intervention strategy against it!

Link prediction typically studies the probability of future interconnection among nodes with the observation in a single social network. However, the emergence of multi-view data enables us to explore the cross-network relationships and further promote link predictions. In this paper, we propose a novel methodology named Dual-View Multi-Task Learning (DMTL) for link prediction across multiple views. In our DMTL model, we firstly utilize two views to obtain different representations, then fuse them into a unified view. This process not only improves representation accuracy but also reduces computational costs. In addition, DMTL introduces an auxiliary task to improve the link prediction performance by constraining the similarity between different views. Extensive experiments on three public

Residential buildings account for a significant portion (35\%) of the total electricity consumption in the US. The need to control energy use in residential buildings is thus a major challenge in our era of global climate change. In this paper, we present an analysis of an IoT-based system that helps occupants reduce their energy consumption through real-time feedback and personalized recommendations. We leverage a real-world dataset consisting of 689 participants living in 104 apartments over a period of four months. Our results indicate that our approach achieves significant savings on average: 12.41 kWh per month per household. Interestingly, we observe that users with high initial consumption levels tend to benefit more from our approach than those with low initial consumption levels.

This paper develops a new filtering approach for state estimation in polynomial systems corrupted by arbitrary noise, with an emphasis on non-Gaussian noise. The proposed filter is based on the Kalman filter, and it is shown to be equivalent to the recursive least squares estimator. However, unlike most existing methods that employ linear system identification techniques (e.g., least square or Kalman filtering), our approach utilizes the inherent properties of polynomial systems to improve performance. In particular, we show that the proposed filter can achieve arbitrarily small mean-squared error when using appropriate polynomial models. We also demonstrate that this improvement comes at no additional computational cost compared to conventional methods. Moreover, the filter can easily be

The rise of generative models has driven significant advancements in recommender systems, leaving unique opportunities for researchers to address emerging challenges. Recently, ChatGPT has been leveraged for diverse applications, including generating content for recommender systems. However, a comprehensive evaluation of its effectiveness is lacking. This paper presents the first empirical study on utilizing ChatGPT to generate recommendations. We conduct a large-scale comparison of two baselines: (i) a state-of-the-art graph neural network model and (ii) the recently proposed ChatGPT-based recommendation model. Our results demonstrate that although ChatGPT can generate high-quality recommendations, it suffers from several shortcomings, such as data sparsity, limited reasoning capabilities

Human beings can walk on various kinds of ground with different stiffness and texture. However, each step we take requires energy, and the ground reaction force during walking on soft ground is much larger than that on hard ground. This study compared the biomechanical differences between walking on solid ground and walking on sand, a soft and yielding medium. We measured the ground reaction force, joint torques, and muscle activities of 10 participants while they walked on a level surface at their self-selected speeds. The participants walked on two types of ground: sand and solid ground. We performed a statistical analysis of the data to determine the significant differences between the two conditions. Our results showed that the ground reaction force on sand was nearly three times larger than that on solid ground. The ankle joint torque on sand was significantly larger than that on solid ground, but the hip and knee joint torques were not significantly different between the two conditions. The activity of the anterior tibialis muscle was significantly larger on sand, but the activity of the gastrocnemius muscle was not significantly different between the two conditions. These results suggest that walking on sand is more fatiguing for the lower extremities than walking on solid ground.

We present TextMonkey, a large multimodal model (LMM) tailored for the creative generation of text and images. Our framework is based on an encoder-decoder architecture with multiheaded cross attention between modalities at both encoding stages -- across time steps as well its final output layer to allow modeling long dependency in each modality individually while also allowing shared knowledge distillation during training from one modal task into another when required by data availability or ethical considerations like copyright protection; this results not only better performance but more robust models even if certain inputs are missing entirely dueto limitations imposedby datasets size compared other approaches which focus solelyon single input types suchas speech recognition systems th

Localizing target nodes is a fundamental problem in Wireless Sensor Networks (WSNs). Many localization algorithms have been proposed based on the measurement of signal strength, such as Received Signal Strength (RSS) or Time of Arrival (ToA). However, these algorithms suffer from a poor accuracy due to the large variation in signal strength caused by the changing propagation environment. To overcome this problem, we propose a novel localization algorithm, called DV-Hop, which fuses the multinode distance estimation and the hop count between the target node and anchor nodes. In particular, we propose a novel multinode distance estimation method using an anchor node and its neighboring nodes, and a hop count estimation method based on the signal-to-noise ratio (SNR) of the received signal. We also propose a weighted average method to fuse the multinode distance estimation and the hop count estimation. Simulation results show that the proposed localization algorithm has better accuracy and stability compared with existing algorithms.

With the advancements of transformer-based architectures, we observe the rise of natural language generation tasks, such as generating summaries from large documents. In this paper, we propose a novel framework called S2V, which leverages the ability of the Transformer to encode both sentences and their associated visual information, thereby improving summary generation. We introduce two new datasets for evaluation: (1) a dataset with ground truth generated using human annotators, and (2) a synthetic dataset that is created by automatically extracting summary sentences from documents. These datasets are used to test our models, showing that they outperform traditional models. Additionally, we conduct a human evaluation experiment using Amazon Mechanical Turk and find that humans prefer our

We present a novel end-to-end pipeline to drive neural head avatars in an interactive application. Our pipeline combines a face tracking algorithm with a novel training regime for neural avatars to enable realistic, responsive, and jitter-free head animations.
