We render neural avatars using novel view synthesis and show how our method can be used to easily add talking heads to existing applications. Our experiments show that neural avatars animate faces more realistically than traditional methods, while maintaining the ability to produce high-resolution images at inference time. Code, models, and demo video are available at https://genforce.github.io/NeuralHeadAvatar/

The rapid development of online media has significantly facilitated the public's information consumption, knowledge acquisition and social connection. Yet it also aggravates their exposure to disinformation (misinformation or propaganda) which often comes with negative impacts on society [21]. In this paper we focus our attention on a type named "fake news", i e., false claims that are deliberately created for financial gain by some publishers through exploiting click-through rates in Internet advertising systems such as Google Adsense<sup>4</sup> .

Vehicle-to-everything (V2X) is a popular topic in the field of vehicular communications, and one of its main applications is autonomous driving. V2X communication can be utilized to support various functions of self-driving cars, including data sharing, navigation, vehicle control, and collision avoidance. Despite the promising nature of this technology, there are still challenges that need to be addressed to ensure its widespread adoption. One such challenge is the limited coverage and reliability of existing V2X networks, which may not provide sufficient connectivity for all vehicles on the road. This paper presents an innovative approach for optimizing V2X networks using artificial neural networks (ANNs). The proposed model effectively enhances the performance of V2X networks by analyzi

Modern computer vision pipelines handle large images in one of two sub-optimal ways: down-scaling the image or processing it at full resolution. In this paper, we introduce a novel technique to process images at their native resolution without sacrificing performance: the Image Pipe. The Image Pipe combines a state-of-the-art pipeline with modern GPUs using multi-GPU techniques for efficient throughput and low memory consumption. We evaluated our approach on three different datasets: ImageNet, CIFAR-100, and Mini-ImageNet. Our experiments show that the Image Pipe outperforms state-of-the-art pipelines by up to 3.5% Top-1 accuracy on all three datasets and consumes over half as much memory. This makes the Image Pipe an attractive alternative to existing pipelines, especially when memory con

Music performance difficulty estimation is an essential problem in music information retrieval with applications to areas such as music education and therapy. Estimation is challenging because it requires both musical and domain knowledge, as well as the ability to accurately characterize the subjective experience of performing music. In this paper, we leverage the Piano Syllabus Dataset, which contains audio recordings and difficulty annotations from 31 pianists performing 108 pieces from the ABRSM syllabus, to investigate the relationship between audio features and performance difficulty as rated by both experts and novices. We find that expert and novice ratings are highly correlated, indicating that both groups agree on the difficulty of the pieces. We also find that audio features explain a significant amount of the variance in both expert and novice ratings, suggesting that they could be used to build a machine learning model for performance difficulty estimation. Finally, we propose a weakly-supervised learning setting that could be used to build such a model, even though difficulty annotations are only available for a subset of the pieces in the dataset.

We present OOTDiffusion, a novel network architecture for realistic and controllable image-toimage diffusion models. Inspired by the success of OutOfNetwork (OOTN) in outperforming traditional convolutional neural networks on various computer vision tasks such as semantic segmentation or object detection with only 5% to $10\%$ parameter overhead while maintaining high accuracy levels thanks its ability exploit spatial dependencies between pixels across large receptive fields without requiring explicit convolutions -we propose an extension called "OutofTime" which allows us generate more diverse outputs from different inputs based upon temporal correlations within sequences thereby improving performance even further!

Addressing the intricate challenge of modeling and re-rendering dynamic scenes, most recent approaches have leveraged diffusion models to synthesize static frames or use generative adversarial networks (GANs) for dynamic simulations. We argue that existing methods are unable to provide a true solution for realistic scene generation due to their inability to handle complex and dynamic scenes. This paper presents a novel methodology that uses a pre-trained transformer-based model to generate dynamic scenes by combining diffusion models with GANs. Our method allows for the generation of realistic scenes based on textual prompts while ensuring accurate object placement within the rendered images. Additionally, we introduce a new metric, $\mathcal{M}_{dynamic}$, which evaluates the quality of t

Urban rail transit is a fundamental component of public transportation, however, commonly station-based path planning strategies may lead to unintended path switching that negatively impacts efficiency. To address this issue, this paper introduces a novel deep reinforcement learning (DRL) framework for optimizing train path planning in urban rail systems. Our proposed DRL method utilizes the Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) network to capture the dynamic characteristics and temporal patterns of the train trajectories in real-time environments. This approach enables the system to learn from previous experiences and adapt to new situations, thereby improving the overall efficiency and safety of train operations. In addition, we introduce a multi-agent DRL model for coordinating multiple trains

People enjoy sharing "notes" including their experiences within online communities. Therefore, recommending notes aligned with users' interests is critical for note-sharing platforms like Pinterest and Evernote. However, the task of note recommendation has been challenging due to its broad domain of topics and diverse user preferences. This paper presents a novel approach that leverages large language models (LLMs) to address these issues by combining fine-tuning on downstream datasets with open-domain question answering (QA). Our method involves initial prompt tuning for LLMs using Q&A pairs from Reddit, followed by extensive fine-tuning on downstream dataset. The resulting model outperforms previous state-of-the-art approaches across 10 different note-sharing platforms.

We consider the problem of robust sparse mean estimation: given an $n\times d$ matrix $A$ with $n>d$, where each row of $A$ is an independent sample from an unknown distribution on sparse mean vectors, we seek to estimate the support and the values of the sparse means. This problem is known to be NP-hard, and prior to our work, the best known algorithm runs in $\tilde O(n^2)$ time (assuming $d\ge 2$). In this paper, we present a faster algorithm for robust sparse mean estimation. Assuming the existence of an oracle that samples a row of $A$ in constant time, our algorithm runs in $\tilde O(n^{1-\alpha})$ time for any $\alpha\in(0,1)$ with success probability at least $1-\delta$ for any $\delta\in(0,1)$. The core of the algorithm is a novel application of the recent sparse recovery algorithm of Bhatia, Singh, and Weinstein (2021). We also show that our algorithm is sample-optimal in the sense that any algorithm for robust sparse mean estimation that runs in time $\tilde O(n^{1-\alpha})$ must make at least $\Omega(n^\alpha)$ queries to the oracle.

Accurate representations of unknown and sub-grid physical processes through parameterizations (or closure) in turbulence models are a key component for accurate simulations. The current approach is to make a set of assumptions regarding the physics, and then derive a corresponding mathematical expression based on these assumptions. This process is time consuming and relies on expert knowledge that is not always available. Furthermore, such methods tend to be limited to linearized expressions, which can lead to accuracy issues. In this work, we present a new machine learning approach to develop nonlinear closure models for turbulence. Our method uses neural networks to learn the relationship between multiple input variables and a single output variable, thereby allowing us to capture both l

Off-road semantic terrain mapping is a fundamental problem in autonomous driving. LiDAR and RGB image, as two major sensors, have been widely used for this purpose. Due to their complementary physical properties, fusing LiDAR and image can lead to accurate semantic terrain mapping. However, existing methods lack consideration of the uncertainty of fused data, which can propagate and accumulate during the fusion process, leading to inaccurate estimation. In this paper, we propose an uncertainty-aware LiDAR-image fusion framework for off-road semantic terrain mapping. Specifically, we introduce a novel uncertainty estimation module (UEM) to estimate the uncertainty of LiDAR and image data. This module analyzes the correlation between neighboring pixels and LiDAR points, as well as the consistency between color and depth information, to evaluate the reliability of each data. Based on the principle of uncertainty propagation, the uncertainty of fused data is then estimated by considering the uncertainty of input data and the operation being performed. The fused data uncertainty can reflect the confidence of the fusion result, which is important for decision-making in autonomous driving. Finally, we design a novel loss function to guide the training of the fusion model by leveraging the estimated uncertainties. Extensive experiments on the Virtual KITTI 2 dataset demonstrate that our method outperforms existing techniques in terms of fusion accuracy and uncertainty estimation.

Few-shot learning, a challenging task in machine learning, aims to learn a classifier with only a few labeled samples. In the context of natural language processing (NLP), such few-shot learning tasks may occur when a large language model (LLM) is unavailable due to privacy concerns or computational constraints. This paper presents a novel approach for few-shot learning in NLP by leveraging the pretrained LLMs as an "inference engine" and utilizing the knowledge distillation framework to train a smaller student model for classification tasks. We propose a new method called "Knowledge Distillation via Soft Prompting" (KDSP), which uses soft prompts instead of hard labels during knowledge distillation. The soft prompting method enables a more flexible way of distilling knowledge from LLMs, l

Transformers have elevated to the state-of-the-art vision architectures through innovative designs, yet remain limited in their ability to exploit hierarchical relations and contextual dependencies. The Hierarchical Contextual Transformer (HCT) is a novel transformer framework that addresses these limitations by integrating hierarchical self-attention with a contextualized positional encoding scheme. We demonstrate that HCT achieves improved performance on various vision tasks, including object detection, image segmentation, and semantic image classification, outperforming existing architectures across diverse datasets. Our experiments showcase HCT's versatility as it can seamlessly integrate into existing pretrained models, enhancing them without introducing additional parameters or train

In a landmark paper at CCS 2019, Lang et al. introduced Neural Trigger, a framework for automatically constructing input triggers that inject arbitrary code into the execution of a neural network. The triggers are designed to be unseen by the network and thus intractable to removal by standard pruning techniques. However, the construction of Neural Trigger requires a manually crafted trigger template, specific to the target network architecture. In this paper, we introduce Neural Exec, a novel framework that automates the process of trigger template discovery. We train a neural network to recognize and extract trigger patterns from within larger inputs, and we use these patterns to construct trigger templates. We show that Neural Exec can discover trigger templates for a variety of network architectures, without any manual intervention. We evaluate Neural Exec on 10 different networks, ranging from 1.2M to 188M parameters, and we construct triggers for 7 of the 10 networks automatically. Among the 7 networks, 5 are ResNet variants and 2 are Transformer models. We also demonstrate that the triggers constructed by Neural Exec can be used to inject arbitrary code into the target networks.

Semi-supervised learning is a critical problem for accelerating AI development and reducing reliance on labeled data. This problem is especially prominent in domain-specific applications of deep learning, such as semantic segmentation, where the cost of manual data annotation is high. In this paper, we propose a simple yet effective semi-supervised learning method for image semantic segmentation, dubbed AllSpark. The key idea is to recompute the features that are originally extracted from labeled data from unlabeled data using a transformer. These recomputed features, which we call reborn features, are then combined with the original features from labeled data to enhance the model's representation learning capability. This approach is based on the assumption that the features extracted from unlabeled data are similar to those from labeled data but with less semantic information. Therefore, the reborn features can complement the original features to improve the model's performance. Extensive experiments on popular benchmark datasets demonstrate that AllSpark significantly outperforms existing state-of-the-art semi-supervised learning methods for image semantic segmentation.

Providing emotional support through dialogue systems is becoming increasingly important in today's world, where people are constantly seeking comfort and understanding. As dialogue systems continue to evolve, the need for accurate emotion detection has become more pressing than ever before. This paper presents a novel approach for detecting multiple emotions using only textual content as an input. The proposed model utilizes multi-head self attention layers along with multi-layer perceptrons (MLP) to extract linguistic features from sentences. To achieve high accuracy levels, we employ the use of a stacked autoencoder architecture that encodes each sentence into a vector representation. Furthermore, this paper proposes to improve the quality of the detected emotions by incorporating an ens

Genetic programming (GP) has the potential to generate explainable results, especially when used for dimensional reduction. It is however a black box as it does not provide any information about its internal operations and hence cannot be trusted or verified by end-users in critical applications such healthcare data analysis where interpretability of model predictions are crucial [26]. We propose an approach that aims at improving GP's transparency without sacrificing performance; using genetic algorithm based optimisation techniques we train two neural networks whose output corresponds respectively with both input features selection weights from GA optimised population members during evolutionary process phase II called "mutation" step."

We propose a method for autonomous precision drone landing with fiducial markers and a gimbal camera. The drone is equipped with a high-resolution gimbal camera, an IMU, and a visual odometry algorithm that allows it to estimate its pose accurately in the real world. Once over the target area, the drone will fly in a pattern to collect images of the marker's fiducials, then use the images to compute the marker's pose, which can be used as a reference pose for the drone to land precisely on the target surface. In this paper, we present an efficient algorithm using deep learning methods to solve the problem of identifying fiducials in images collected by a gimbal camera mounted on a moving object.

Anaerobic digestion (AD) is a biological process used for the degradation of organic matter in the absence of oxygen. Modelling AD is challenging because of the presence of different reaction mechanisms and the occurrence of spatially heterogeneous phenomena, like the formation of acetate and the accumulation of volatile fatty acids (VFA). The aim of this work is to investigate the effect of turbulent diffusion in modeling AD. To this end, we have considered a modified version of the well-known AD model proposed by Yuan \textit{et al.} in 1989, by incorporating a term that accounts for turbulent diffusion. We have performed a dimensional analysis of the modified model, deriving a characteristic length scale, which can be interpreted as the distance that turbulent mixing is effective. We have then carried out a series of two-dimensional simulations, by using a finite-volume solver, of a prototype reactor (e.g., a stirred tank) performing AD, under different operating conditions. Our results show that turbulent diffusion enhances the degradation of VFA and reduces the formation of acetate, especially at high power inputs. These findings are consistent with experimental observations reported in the literature.

Knowledge graph completion (KGC) aims to infer new facts based on existing facts in the knowledge graph (KG). Recent studies have shown that utilizing the graph neighborhood of a node (i.e., multi-perspective) information can improve the performance of KGC. However, directly incorporating multi-perspective information presents two challenges: (1) the increasing computation cost with more perspectives and (2) the potential information redundancy among different perspectives. To address these challenges, we propose a novel multi-perspective improvement approach for KGC, dubbed Multi-perspective Graph Attention Network (MGAT). Specifically, we introduce a graph attention module to weigh the contribution of each perspective and thereby reduce the computation cost. We also employ a novel cross-attention-based fusion module to integrate information from different perspectives in a complementary manner. Experimental results on four KGC datasets demonstrate that MGAT consistently outperforms various state-of-the-art baselines. Moreover, MGAT can be seamlessly plugged into existing KGC models, showing great compatibility and scalability.

We consider a variant of the online bipartite matching problem. At each time, a buyer arrives with a (possibly private) desired set of items. The platform has a (possibly private) inventory of items and needs to decide which item(s) to allocate to the buyer. The goal is to maximize the total value of the matches. We focus on the dynamic nature of the problem and the private information about the buyers' desired sets and the platform's inventory. We introduce an integer linear program and show that the problem is NP-hard. We present a randomized algorithm and analyze its approximation factor. We show that the approximation factor can be improved if either the inventory or the desired sets are bipartite. We also discuss the possibility of using machine learning to predict the private information.

Methane (CH4) is a potent greenhouse gas with a global warming potential 25 times higher than carbon dioxide (CO2) on a 100-year timescale. The oil and gas (O&G) industry is responsible for a significant share of CH4 emissions, mainly due to routine operations of its upstream activities. Therefore, accurately quantifying CH4 emissions from the O&G upstream activities is critical for developing mitigation strategies and tracking progress toward reduction targets. This paper proposes a novel method to model CH4 intensity (CH4 emissions per unit of production) for O&G upstream activities by production profile. We compiled a unique data set of daily production and CH4 emissions from a major O&G producer in the United States for the development and validation of the model. We used a data-driven approach to model CH4 intensity as a function of cumulative production using a logistic function. The proposed function captures the CH4 intensity trends for low and high production regimes and can be used for quick estimation of CH4 intensity without detailed process modeling. We also calculated the uncertainty associated with the model parameters and demonstrated the sensitivity of the CH4 intensity to changes in the data set. The proposed method can be used by O&G companies to estimate CH4 intensity of their operations and set realistic reduction targets.

Comparative literature summary is a crucial skill for researchers to synthesize knowledge from vast amounts of textual data. Recently, large language models (LLMs) have shown impressive results in natural language processing tasks, but they still lack the nuanced understanding and generality of human researchers. To assist LLMs in capturing both the big picture and details, we propose an LLM agent named ChatCite, which incorporates human workflow guidance. Specifically, we break down the task into microtasks based on the human workflow for comparative literature summary, and generate prompts for an LLM to sequentially complete these microtasks. To evaluate whether the generated prompts are understandable and actionable for the LLMs, we conduct a human study and collect feedback from participants. Based on the feedback, we further refine the prompts, providing iterative improvements for the LLM agents. Experimental results demonstrate that the incorporation of human workflow guidance enables the LLM agent to better assist users in completing complex tasks, compared to traditional LLMs.

In the last decade, quantum computing has emerged from being a purely theoretical concept to a serious practical proposition, with functioning quantum computers being developed by several major technology companies. In this paper, I review the current state of quantum computing, focusing on quantum algorithms and quantum error correction. I then go on to discuss some of the challenges that need to be overcome before quantum computers can perform useful calculations on a large scale.

Value functions are a central component of deep reinforcement learning (RL). These functions, parameterized as neural networks with weights $\theta$, allow agents to map state and action information into reward predictions. While many methods have been developed for improving the accuracy or robustness in training these value function approximators over time steps $t=1,\ldots T$ using data from an RL agent's environment interactions at each step -- e.\g., off-policy bootstrapping via policy gradients \cite{Mnih20}, advantage estimation through actor critic architectures\footnote{\textit{"Actor" refers to any algorithm that can be used by other algorithms.} (AC)~\citep[e.][]{Konda96}\dots{}-- there is little work on how one might improve performance when given access only too early timestep

Video relationship detection (VRD) aims to detect semantic relationships between objects in a pair of video frames. It has been widely used in many applications such as video retrieval and human-computer interaction. Previous methods mostly adopt convolutional neural networks (CNN) to learn the relationship between objects. However, different from image-based relationship detection, object detection and tracking in videos are much more challenging, which leads to inferior relationship detection performance for previous VRD methods. In this paper, we propose a novel Mixture of Experts (MoE) framework which can improve the VRD performance from 86.4% to 88.7% on the public VRD dataset. The proposed MoE framework learns the relationship between objects from two perspectives: 1) spatial relationship: the position and shape of the objects; 2) temporal relationship: the motion and appearance of the objects. Moreover, we also propose a novel dynamic weight allocation mechanism in the MoE framework which can allocate different contributions for sub-models based on their performance. Extensive experiments on the public VRD dataset show that the proposed method outperforms previous state-of-the-art methods.

Federated learning (FL) is a promising approach for learning a model from data distributed on massive clients. However, it suffers from the lack of strong privacy guarantees and the limitation in training large-scale models due to communication and computational constraints of clients. To address these challenges, federated differential privacy (FDP) has been proposed as a technique that integrates differential privacy with FL. However, existing solutions either introduce extra noise or require additional server computation, which increases the communication overhead and limits their practical applicability. In this paper, we propose Federated Differential Private Averaging (FEDPA), an efficient solution for enhancing FDP's practicality through averaging mechanism. Specifically, we employ

ChatGPT is a natural language processing model that can write coherent text, converse in chat, and generate code. It can also play digital games, both in terms of playing as a human player and as a game designer. In this paper, we present a case study of ChatGPT co-creating a digital game with a human designer. We analyze the process and product of their collaboration, identifying the contributions of ChatGPT and comparing them to those of a traditional AI. We find that ChatGPT is able to generate coherent game design ideas, maintain a consistent design vision, and produce design solutions that are novel, surprising, and of high quality. We also find that ChatGPT's performance is limited by its own internal inconsistencies and by its inability to understand complex instructions. Based on these findings, we discuss the potential of ChatGPT and other language models for co-creation in games and other domains, and we suggest some guidelines for human-AI collaboration.

Floating car observers (FCOs) are human participants in traffic studies who agree to have their vehicle trajectories tracked via GPS and share their data with researchers. To motivate participation, FCOs are typically compensated for their time and travel costs. One of the most popular FCO schemes is the moving fine, where participants are paid not to exceed a certain speed limit, thus acting as a moving speed camera. In this paper, we introduce temporal enhanced FCOs (TE-FCOs), a novel FCO scheme that further incentivizes participants to drive safely by paying them based on how their speed profiles compare to historical speed profiles on the same road segment. TE-FCOs leverage speed cameras and GPS speed sensors to measure driving speed as well as temporal speed profiles. We present a deep reinforcement learning-based speed profile comparison approach that computes the optimal reward for a TE-FCO based on historical speed data. We show that TE-FCOs can effectively encourage safe driving behavior through speed profile shaping, leading to potential benefits for traffic flow, road safety, and reduced environmental impact.

Machine learning has achieved remarkable success in many applications. However, existing studies are largely based on the closed-loop setting that assumes access to labels of all examples when training a model and evaluating its performance during testing time; this is unrealistic for real world problems where only some samples can be labeled with high cost or even impossible due limitations such as privacy concerns etc., which makes it necessary but difficult task called open loop machine learing (OLML). In recent years researchers have made significant progress towards solving OLMR problem by introducing various techniques like active sampling methods(SAMs) including query strategies/algorithms along side optimization algorithms & error bounds analysis frameworks .

Radar imaging tomography reconstructs the three-dimensional (3D) structural information of a scene from multiple perspective views. Novel view synthesis interpolates the reconstructed 3D structure to generate images from arbitrary views. Existing methods mainly perform tomography and novel view synthesis separately and explicitly. This leads to a high computational cost or poor reconstruction quality. In this paper, we propose Implicit Doppler Tomography (DART) to jointly optimize radar tomography and novel view synthesis implicitly. The key idea is to leverage the inverse relationship between the radar's line-of-sight (LOS) velocity and the Doppler frequency shift. We formulate a novel view synthesis problem from the radar's perspective and show that the objective function is equivalent to the sum of squared errors (SSE) between the predicted and true Doppler frequency shifts. We then propose an efficient optimization algorithm to minimize the SSE with respect to the 3D structure and show that the solution is equivalent to solving a linear system of equations. The main contribution of this paper is to bridge the gap between radar tomography and novel view synthesis, and demonstrate that these two tasks can be performed jointly and implicitly. We evaluate the proposed method on both synthetic and real-world data sets. The results show that the proposed method outperforms existing tomography and novel view synthesis methods separately and explicitly.

In this paper, we address the intricate challenge of gaze vector prediction, a pivotal task in gaze-aware human-computer interaction. We present a novel approach that draws upon cutting-edge techniques from both computer vision and neural networks to achieve state-of-the-art performance on the challenging GVaEx dataset. Our method involves employing a pretrained image-oriented DenseNet model that is fine-tuned using a self-supervised learning paradigm to predict gaze vectors. This model is then integrated with a CNN-LSTM network for multiscale contextualization, leading to more accurate predictions. Additionally, we introduce two new methods to enhance our model's capabilities: a multi-label attention mechanism and a novel feature fusion approach.

Voice assistants (VA) are ambient computing devices that users can interact with using natural language. VAs are often pre-programmed with fixed response phrases, which can lead to situations where the user's intent is not accurately captured, especially in noisy environments. VA providers are exploring techniques to improve understanding of user utterances by training speech recognition models to comprehend the intended meaning of the user, a technique known as listening comprehension (LC). In this paper, we propose AIx Speed, a novel application of listening comprehension to optimize the playback speed of voice assistant responses. We hypothesize that LC can be used to infer the user's intended response time, taking into account the time it takes for the user to understand the response. To test this hypothesis, we conducted a user study (n=29) where participants provided utterances to VAs with different playback speeds. We found a significant effect of playback speed on user perceptions of response time, F(2,56)=8.63, p<.01, with participants preferring slower playback speeds. We also found that LC models could predict the optimal playback speed for a given utterance with 78% accuracy, a result that suggests LC is a promising technique for optimizing VA response playback speeds to align with user expectations.

In the literature, points and conics have been major features for camera geometric calibration. Although conic representations are more robust to noise, they require additional assumptions (e.g., point correspondences) and often cannot be interpreted in a geometric sense. In this paper we introduce a novel representation of the image space as a 2D polytope: the \textit{image polytopes}. A point p is represented by an ordered set of triples $(u_p, v_p, w_p)$ with $u_p, v_p \in \mathbb{R}^2$ and $w_p\geq 0$. The edge of the polytope corresponds to the line segment connecting two adjacent points and its area is defined by the volume of the corresponding triangle. We show that any set of points in the image can be represented by these polytopes.

Performance is arguably the most crucial attribute that reflects the behavior of a configurable software system. In order to obtain insight into its behavior, the developer must perform experiments with different configurations. For large systems, this is a tedious task and it is difficult to gain confidence in the results because the experiments are performed manually by the developer. In this paper, we propose an approach for automated performance evaluation of configurable software systems. We start from a configurable model and a set of benchmark scenarios. The model is used to generate an executable configuration. Each configuration is executed multiple times using the scenarios in order to collect enough performance data. The collected data are analyzed using statistical hypothesis t

Federated learning (FL) is a collaborative scheme to train a learning model across multiple participants without sharing data. Existing federated learning algorithms often assume that data from different participants follow the same distribution, i.e., the non-i.i.d. assumption, which is violated in many real-world scenarios. Without this assumption, data from different participants can have varying distributions, making it challenging to derive useful insights from the aggregated model. In this paper, we propose FedClust, a novel federated learning framework that clusters clients based on their contribution to the overall model performance, as indicated by the client weights. The clients are re-organized into clusters, and each cluster elects a leader to participate in the federated training. This approach ensures that clients with similar data distributions are grouped together, enhancing the homogeneity within clusters and, consequently, the performance of the aggregated model. Theoretical analysis demonstrates that FedClust converges at a faster rate compared to conventional FL under the non-i.i.d. assumption. Experimental results on benchmark and real-world datasets highlight the superiority of FedClust over state-of-the-art non-i.i.d. FL methods.

There has been an increasing interest in the alignment of large language models (LLMs) with human values. However, the existing approaches mainly focus on single-value alignment and are based on either human preferences or utility functions. In this paper, we propose a novel multi-objective reinforcement learning framework for value alignment to optimize over multiple values simultaneously. Our approach uses a reward function that combines both supervised and unsupervised objectives and is formulated as a Markov Decision Process. Additionally, we introduce an actor-critic algorithm to train LLMs in a more stable way by avoiding catastrophic forgetting. We evaluate our method using the Reward Baseline benchmark, which assesses the ability of LLMs to align their behavior with human values ac

In this paper, we present a novel point generation model, referred to as Pillar-based Neural Decoder (PND), for constructing high-quality point clouds from image data. We achieve this by leveraging the strengths of convolutional neural networks and variational autoencoders, two popular models in computer vision and generative modelling respectively. The PND model integrates an encoder-decoder architecture into a pillar-based 3D structure, enabling it to capture both spatial and semantic information. Through our experiments on various datasets, including Cityscapes, ScanNet, KITTI, and ModelNet40, we demonstrate that the proposed PND model outperforms other state-of-the-art methods across various metrics, such as PSNR and SSIM. Furthermore, the code is available at https://github.com/liuyan

Real-time flood forecasting plays a crucial role in enabling timely and effective flood management. This paper introduces an innovative approach to real-time flood forecasting using the latest transformer-based models, namely the Generative Pre-Trained Transformer (GPT) and the Conversational Transformer Language Model (CTLM). The proposed method combines historical rainfall data with topographical maps, generating geographically realistic rainfall patterns and incorporating them into a hydrological model. This technique not only improves the accuracy of rainfall-runoff modeling but also offers a more flexible and adaptive approach that can be applied to various watersheds. Through extensive testing on a real-world dataset from the United States Geological Survey (USGS), the proposed metho

Traditional cross-domain tasks, including domain adaptation and domain generalization, rely heavily on training model parameters on the source domains. These methods are not effective when there is a lack of labeled data in the source domain or the label distribution across domains is significantly different. In this paper, we propose a novel self-supervised approach to learn a robust feature extractor that can adapt well to new target domains without any supervision or prior knowledge of domain distribution. We utilize contrastive learning to learn an optimal representation for all domains while preserving their similarity structures. To do so, we leverage an existing dataset with a large number of unlabeled samples, which we use as the source domain. We then compare our proposed method a

LLMOps incur significant costs due to hardware requirements, hindering their widespread adoption. We address this challenge by introducing a new approach for efficiently implementing LLMOps on FPGAs without the need for specialized accelerators or dedicated hardware components. Our approach, called LLMOps-Fabric, is designed to leverage existing FPGA resources more effectively and provides a high degree of customization and flexibility. This paper details our methodology and presents experimental results that demonstrate its effectiveness, highlighting its potential to revolutionize the field of reconfigurable computing. The project page can be found at https://github.com/yutianzheng/LLMOps-Fabric.git. To facilitate reproducibility, we provide a complete set of code and data used in our ex

Deep learning has achieved remarkable progress in various applications, heightening the importance of safeguarding the intellectual property (IP) of deep neural networks. While existing IP protection methods for deep neural networks mainly focus on model-level obfuscation techniques, we argue that they are not sufficient to effectively protect the IP of deep neural networks due to their lack of consideration of training data. In this paper, we propose a novel method, named DLPAD, which aims to enhance the robustness and security of deep learning models by introducing a new type of adversarial attack based on dataset perturbations. Specifically, DLPAD trains a discriminator to distinguish whether the input data is from the original dataset or the perturbed one. The discriminator is trained

Floods are among the most common and damaging natural disasters, affecting millions of people and causing billions of dollars in property damage each year. Urban areas are particularly vulnerable to floods, and assessing flood risk is crucial for mitigating flood hazards and minimizing their impacts. Traditional methods of flood risk assessment have focused on evaluating the probability of flood occurrence and its associated economic losses. However, these methods have limitations in that they do not consider the social implications of floods, such as the displacement of populations and the disruption of social services, nor do they take into account the long-term effects on the environment. In this study, we propose a new framework for assessing flood risk in urban areas that adapts concepts from the health domain, including the consideration of social determinants of health, the one-size-fits-all fallacy, and the focus on resilience. We apply this framework to the case of Venice, a city particularly vulnerable to floods, and we find that the traditional risk assessment methods underestimate flood risk by up to 70% when the health domain perspective is adopted. Our study highlights the importance of considering the social and environmental impacts of floods in risk assessment and planning for flood mitigation strategies.

Large language models (LLMs) open up new horizons for sequential recommendations. Recent works show that LLMs can be used to generate recommendation content as well as predict user preference, thereby generating a recommender model with both generation and prediction capabilities. However, such LLM-based approaches often suffer from data sparsity issues since they require separate training sets for generation and prediction, which results in a limited amount of training data. In this work, we propose an approach based on a unified training set that integrates the generation task into the prediction task. By leveraging large pretrained LLMs, our approach allows us to train a single unified model using a single unified dataset. This single model is then capable of producing recommendation co

This paper describes the deployment and experimentation architecture of the Internet of Things experimentation facility being deployed at University College Cork. The aim is to support research on all aspects related to IoT, from sensor networks up until cloud computing systems in a unified environment with an open platform approach that can be easily extended by new services without affecting existing deployments or experiments already running within it. To this end we have developed our own hardware for wireless communication as well... Generative AI has made rapid advancements in recent years, achieving unprecedented levels of performance and accuracy. However, as the technology becomes more widely available, it also faces potential risks such as biased or harmful content generation, copyright infringement, and even impersonation attacks. In response to these concerns, this paper presents a novel method for detecting whether an image is created by a generative model. Our approach leverages a classifier that is trained on a dataset containing both real and synthetic images, with the goal of identifying whether an input image was generated by a generative model. The training process involves two stages: a pre-training stage using large-scale datasets and a fine-tuning stage using our specialized dataset. The key insight be

This study addresses the challenge of real-time metaverse applications by proposing an in-network plaform to achieve low latency for augmented reality (AR) and virtual realities. The proposed platform can be deployed on both cloud servers or edge nodes, which provides flexible deployment options according needs such as computing power demand level requirements depending upon application type scenarios with different bandwidths available at each location where they are executed; thus making it possible even if not always ideal but still feasible when needed most urgently during peak hours without compromising quality too much due its high performance capabilities relative compared other approaches currently used today within industry standards like Google Cloud Platform'S AI Training Servic

Abstract visual reasoning is a characteristically human ability, allowing the identification of relational patterns that are abstracted away from object features, and the systematic generalization of those patterns to unseen problems. Recent work has demonstrated strong systematic generalization in visual reasoning tasks involving multi-object inputs, through the integration of slot-based methods used for extracting object-centric representations coupled with strong inductive biases for relational abstraction. However, this approach was limited to problems containing a single rule, and was not scalable to visual reasoning problems containing a large number of objects. Other recent work proposed Abstractors, an extension of Transformers that incorporates strong relational inductive biases,

We study the effectiveness of data-balancing for mitigating biases in contrastive language representations. We first show that existing methods do not work well because they either introduce new biases or cause a large drop in representation quality. We then propose to use a simple regularization on the softmax probabilities produced by the classifier in the encoder and show that this approach significantly reduces bias while maintaining high performance. We also find that our method can be easily combined with other techniques to further improve its effectiveness. We hope that this work will inspire future research on mitigating biases in contrastive pretraining. Our code is available at https://github.com/thunlp/biased-clr-fix.git. Code: https://github.com/thunlp/biased-clr-fix.git.

Multimodal large language models (MLLMs) carry the potential to support humans in processing information and solving tasks. Yet, little is known about how MLLM-based systems are perceived by users when employed as conversational assistants or cobots for complex problem domains such medicine education during medical training rotations at hospitals ("clerkships"). To better understand this context we conducted a user study with 16 participants who interacted via text chat either only directly (= control group), using an expert system (+ knowledge base = KBES condition). A second interaction was done through ChatGPT + LLAMA chatbot integration where learners could ask questions before interacting again without assistance from any AI tool ($n=8$ each $30\mathrm{min}$ session; total of three se

Recent years have witnessed a plethora of learning-based solutions for congestion control. The majority of these methods are based on reinforcement learning and learn optimal actions by interacting with the environment. However, the training process is time-consuming, which requires a large number of episodes to ensure convergence and stability. In this paper, we propose a novel framework named Traffic Control Network (TCN) that utilizes deep neural networks to predict the best control action in a single episode. This approach allows TCN to converge faster than traditional reinforcement learning algorithms while maintaining the same level of performance. To solve the overfitting problem, we adopt the LSTM-based network and use a self-attention mechanism to improve its performance. Experime

Indoor scenes we are living in are visually homogenous or textureless, while they inheres rich depth cues. Although previous works have shown that a monocular RGB camera can be used to recover the 3D scene geometry and reconstruct indoor textured meshes with high quality by exploiting weak priors (e.g., plane-based reconstruction), these methods require tedious manual initialization of planes for each frame which is not applicable when processing large numbers of frames at once as well lacks generalization capability across datasets due its reliance on strong handcrafting techniques such ground truth data collection process where human annotators label every pixel manually instead relying solely upon computer vision algorithms alone thus limiting their utility overall practical application

The increasing significance of sustainability considerations within both public spheres (such as policies and regulations) and private organisations is leading to an increased demand for tools that can assess and guide the transition towards more sustainable operations. In particular, the identification of opportunities and potential barriers associated with energy efficiency investments in buildings has become a key area of focus. In this context, machine learning techniques have proven particularly effective due to their ability to effectively process large amounts of data while also offering flexibility in terms of scalability and adaptability. This paper investigates the application of three state-of-the-art machine learning models - Long Short Term Memory (LSTM), Convolutional Neural

The rapid development of online media has significantly facilitated the public's information consumption, knowledge acquisition, and communication. However, social media has also enabled the spread of fake news, which often leads to confusion, conflict, or even violence among people with different backgrounds or viewpoints. In this paper, we present a novel approach called FakeNews-Debunker that effectively detects fake news using deep learning methods. Our model integrates multiple pre-trained language models (PLMs) including BERT, RoBERTa, ELECTRA, and DistilBert for feature extraction. Additionally, we utilize attention mechanisms such as Self Attention and Cross Attention to enhance model performance. Furthermore, we employ ensemble strategies to aggregate and optimize the outputs of e

Understanding social interactions involving both verbal and non-verbal cues is essential to effectively interpret human behavior. This paper introduces an approach for detecting and recognizing conversational turns within social videos, aimed at improving the ability of artificial agents to understand such interactions. To facilitate this task, we propose a novel two-stage framework that leverages pretrained vision transformers (ViTs) as feature extractors and a self-attention based cross-modal fusion module to capture the interplay between visual and audio features. Additionally, we explore various training techniques, including hard negative mining, data augmentation, and fine-tuning on in-domain datasets to enhance model performance. Extensive experiments conducted on four different dat

Imitation learning presents an effective approach to alleviate the resource-intensive and time-consuming task of data collection for robotic systems. In this work, we introduce a novel imitation learning methodology that leverages active exploration to adaptively select diverse demonstrations from human experts in real-world scenarios. We propose the Expert-Adaptive Imitation Learning (EAIL) algorithm, which employs a recurrent neural network to learn expert demonstration representations, a distance metric to measure how similar each representation is to the target policy, and an active search strategy to select and execute demonstrations that are most beneficial to learning. The EAIL algorithm achieves superior performance compared to baseline imitation learning methods on a variety of ta

The use of machine learning (ML) techniques to solve complex physical problems has been considered recently as a promising approach. In this work, we propose a new methodology that leverages ML and differential equations to model and analyze the dynamics of chemical reactions. Our approach begins with a set of differential equations representing a specific reaction, followed by the construction of a corresponding graph. We then apply ML techniques, such as neural networks, to learn the behavior of the system represented by this graph. Our methodology is based on the assumption that the structure of the network influences how information flows through it, which in turn affects the accuracy of the predictions made by the learned model. To validate our approach, we tested it using two synthet

The current state-of-the-art in artificial intelligence is impressive, especially in terms of the performance on well-defined tasks. However, this success comes with a trade-off: the models are usually trained on a specific task and are thus not flexible enough to perform different tasks. In fact, current methods require expensive retraining and fine-tuning for each new task. To overcome this challenge, we propose an approach that integrates knowledge transfer into the training process. This allows us to leverage previously learned information from related tasks during the training process. Our approach is based on a two-stage learning framework. First, we use a self-supervised pretraining method to learn generic representations. Second, we utilize knowledge distillation as an efficient te

Recent text-to-image (T2I) models have had great success, and many works are trying to improve the quality of their output. One common approach is a multistage training method where an initial model performs some basic tasks such as colorization or inpainting followed by fine tuning on TGI data for image generation from textual description(s). However this process requires both separate datasets which can be difficult when working with limited resources; it also adds extra steps making learning slower than desired - especially given current advances towards more efficient methods like diffusion based architectures! In our work we propose instead using only one dataset throughout all stages: Diffusion Based MultiStage Training For Text To Image Models .

adaptNMT streamlines all processes involved in the development and deployment of RNN and Transformer neural machine translation systems. The system is developed as a collection, composition or combination (stack) consisting entirely out-of commercial offthe shelf components to ensure maximum flexibility for every organization with regard towards its individual requirements regarding data protection laws such GDPR compliance needs etc., while at same time reducing costs significantly compared traditional approaches which require costly custom implementations tailored specifically according customer's specific circumstances .

Online transaction processing (OLTP) systems execute a large number of short-running transactions involving a small subset of a large customer base. To predict the performance of OLTP systems under realistic workloads, transaction access patterns need to be captured and simulated. A transaction access pattern is a partition of the customer base into a number of equivalence classes such that all transactions in a given class access the same set of database objects. In this paper, we introduce a fast algorithm for generating spanning trees that can be used to enumerate transaction access patterns. The running time of our algorithm is proportional to the number of generated spanning trees, which is a small fraction of the total number of possible query plans. We prove that the access patterns generated by our algorithm cover all possible access patterns that can be expressed in a partition-based representation. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first algorithm that can generate realistic transaction access patterns in a time that is polynomial in the number of generated access patterns.

Semi-supervised dialogue summarization (SSDS) leverages model-generated summaries to enhance the limited labels from human annotators. This framework has shown promising results in reducing annotation burdens, thereby improving efficiency and scalability. However, current approaches overlook the role of the training objective during the generation phase. In this work, we propose an innovative framework that uses a diverse set of multi-objective loss functions to guide the generation process toward more effective SSDS. Our framework is designed to optimize for three different objectives simultaneously: maximizing the similarity between the generated summary and the given reference, minimizing the divergence between the human-generated summary and its corresponding model-generated counterpar

Semantic image synthesis (SIS) aims to produce photorealistic images aligning to given conditional semantic layout and has witnessed a significant improvement in recent years. Although the semantic layout provides valuable guidance, most existing SIS methods barely exploit the semantic meaning contained in the layout, tending to treat it as ordinary text descriptions. Meanwhile, the layout-aware techniques focus on the visual consistency between the generated image and the layout, but they ignore the semantic coherence within the layout itself, which is another crucial factor for SIS. In this work, we investigate the automatic fusion of layout and semantic meaning for SIS and propose an adaptive Layout-Semantic Fusion Network (PLACE). To fuse the layout and semantic meaning effectively, we introduce a semantic layout attention module (SLAM) to dynamically learn the relevance of each semantic word in the caption and the corresponding layout element. On the basis of SLAM, we further propose a semantic-guided synthesis module (SGSM) to semantically enrich the generated image by amplifying the representation of semantically relevant layout elements. PLACE is the first SIS model that fuses the semantic meanings in the layout and captions, and our experimental results show that it achieves 7.48\% and 37.8\% relative FID improvement on two widely used SIS benchmarks, i.e., CUB-200 and MSCOCO, respectively.

Prompt learning has emerged as a valuable technique in enhancing vision-language models (VLM), especially for long text sequences. In this paper, we investigate the effectiveness of prompt learners across different types and sizes of VIMs to better understand how they can be effectively employed at scale when large language model training is difficult due computational or infrastructural constraints; specifically on GPT3 Large Model with 175B parameters which are trained using an adaptive gradient clipping method that helps mitigate catastrophic forgetting while preserving performance gains from prior tasks during fine tuning process without requiring additional memory resources compared against baselines like Adam optimizer alone .

Equivalence testing, a fundamental problem in the field of distribution testing, seeks to infer if two discrete distributions are equivalent (the same) based on independent samples from them. For a fixed sample size $n$, it is well known that this requires at least $\Omega(n)$ samples for any algorithm, and the best-known upper bound is $O(n^2)$. Recently, Berman et al. [FOCS'19] gave an equivalence tester with sample complexity $O(n^{1 + \alpha})$ where $\alpha$ is the constant such that $k^{\frac{1}{\alpha}} = 1 + o(1)$ as $k \rightarrow \infty$.

Large language models (LLMs) are notorious for hallucinating, i.e., producing false information, especially in the context of reasoning tasks. This paper introduces a novel dataset, D-Hallucinations, to assess LLM performance on reasoning-based tasks with hallucinated evidence. Our dataset is composed of 300 English pairs: each pair comprises an evidence and its corresponding hallucinated variant. We provide human assessments of evidence veracity and hallucination severity. Furthermore, we conduct an analysis to identify common patterns in hallucinations. These findings highlight that LLMs tend to create hallucinations by incorporating prior knowledge or generating new content based on existing information, leading to errors such as false facts, incorrect attribution, and fabricated detail

We introduce a new family of prompt injection attacks, termed Neural Exec. Unlike known attacks that leverage the inference process to execute malicious code, our attack executes arbitrary payloads during the training process. The adversary injects a neural exec payload into the parameters of a deep learning model trained on benign input. At inference time, the adversary can then inject the same neural exec payload into any input to trigger the execution of the hidden malicious logic. This enables attacks such as remote command execution, data exfiltration and backdoors, without the need for any additional side-channels or other malicious input. We demonstrate the effectiveness of this new attack by constructing a neural exec backdoor on image classification models. We show how to embed th

Deep learning (DL) has been a common thread across several recent techniques for vulnerability detection. The idea is to train a DL model using an attack dataset and then apply it to the target program. However, this approach suffers from two critical issues: Firstly, the number of attacks used to train a DL model is typically small relative to the size of the training data required to train deep models. Secondly, even if one could train a powerful deep model with sufficient attack data, it would not generalize to new attacks. In this paper, we introduce an attack-agnostic vulnerability detector that addresses both these issues. We first propose a novel vulnerability detection method based on detecting anomalous execution traces during testing. Next, we explore a multi-task framework in wh

Differentially private (DP) machine learning is considered the gold-standard solution for training a model while preserving privacy. However, it still suffers from significant performance loss and high computational overhead compared to nonprivate counterparts in many scenarios where data are well balanced or sensitive information such as protected attributes can be easily separated out during preprocessing steps. In this work we propose an alternative approach that aims at improving both utility of DP models by leveraging recent advances on efficient neural networks like transformers with no need additional hardware acceleration techniques unlike traditional deep convolutional nets which require GPUs due their larger size relative transformer based architectures . We further extend our me

The Attention mechanism is the main component of the Transformer architecture, and since its introduction, it has led to significant advancements in Deep Learning that span many domains and multiple tasks. The Attention Mechanism was utilized in Computer Vision as the Vision Transformer ViT, and its usage has expanded into many tasks in the vision domain, such as classification, segmentation, object detection, and image generation. While this mechanism is very expressive and capable, it comes with the drawback of being computationally expensive and requiring datasets of considerable size for effective optimization. To address these shortcomings, many designs have been proposed in the literature to reduce the computational burden and alleviate the data size requirements. Examples of such at

Product offers on e-commerce websites often consist of a textual product title and a textual product description. This paper proposes an approach to automatically extract such product descriptions from the HTML source code of online shop pages. We first identify the most relevant text parts of webpages for extracting such product descriptions by employing a customized version of the Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) architecture. Next, we use this CNN model to encode the HTML textual content into an image sequence in order to generate product descriptions that can be fed to an LLM for further post-processing. The proposed method is evaluated using three datasets: a synthetic dataset based on the E-commerce data set, a real-world dataset containing 15,000 products extracted from a German o

Automated Program Repair (APR) aims to automatically generate patches for rectifying software bugs. While APR techniques have improved in recent years, there are still challenges regarding the efficiency and accuracy of these tools. In this paper, we propose a new APR method based on Reinforcement Learning (RL). Our approach consists of three major components: (1) a patch generator that uses LSTMs to generate patches for buggy programs, (2) a critic that evaluates the quality of the generated patches using code metrics such as test coverage, and (3) an agent that leverages RL to optimize the performance of the generator by rewarding it for generating high-quality patches. We conduct extensive experiments on five real-world datasets and four synthetic datasets.

Self-reflecting about our performance (e.g., how confident we are) before doing a task is critical to improving our performance over time, which is the essence of metacognition. However, recent studies have shown that self-reflecting on one's own performance can negatively affect the subsequent performance (e.g., the task result), especially when people are aware of the existence of this negative effect. This study investigates whether and when people still choose to engage in self-reflection despite knowing its potentially negative effects on their performance. To do so, we designed a human-computer interaction task where participants could choose between two modes: (1) the "self-reflection mode," in which they were required to report their confidence levels before each trial; and (2) the

This paper presents a comprehensive survey of research works on the topic of form understanding in the context of autonomous driving. We focus on form understanding as a challenging task, which is often overlooked despite its significance for autonomous vehicles to safely navigate their surroundings and respond to unexpected events. In this survey, we first discuss form recognition techniques that are based on deep learning approaches such as convolutional neural networks (CNNs), recurrent neural networks (RNNs) and transformers. We also cover various datasets used in recent studies, with an emphasis on those designed specifically for form understanding tasks. Subsequently, we present several applications of form understanding, including obstacle detection, road sign classification, lane m

Offline goal-conditioned reinforcement learning (GCRL) aims at solving goal-conditioned tasks without access to the reward function. In practice, offline GCRL methods usually require high sample efficiency and struggle to generalize to unseen goals. To address these challenges, we propose a novel offline GCRL method called \textbf{G}aussian-\textbf{V}ariance-\textbf{L}earning (\textbf{GaViLe}). The core idea is that Gaussian processes can approximate the reward function with low variance, allowing for more efficient exploration by exploiting the learned reward distribution. We further introduce an efficient sampling strategy to accelerate this process. Extensive experiments demonstrate that GaViLe significantly outperforms state-of-the-art baselines on three offline GCRL benchmarks, includ

Sequence recommendation systems (SRSs) predict the next item a user is likely to interact with based on the user's historical behavior on an e-commerce platform. In this paper, we introduce a novel sequence denoising technique for SRSs that exploits the self-augmentation property of the training data. The self-augmentation property refers to the fact that some items in the training data appear multiple times with different users. We propose a sequence denoising method, called Self-Augmented Sequence Denoising (SSD), that generates augmented sequences from the training data and uses them to improve the quality of the sequences. SSD first identifies the noisy items in the training sequences and then generates augmented sequences by replacing the noisy items with their candidate items. The candidate items are selected based on the item's popularity or relevance to the user. We evaluate SSD on three benchmark datasets and two types of recommendation tasks. The results show that SSD consistently outperforms the state-of-the-art baselines on all the six comparison methods.

Multi-modal brain tumor segmentation is a crucial preprocessing step for many applications in neuro-oncology. It allows for a more accurate delineation of tumors, which is essential for treatment planning and monitoring. In this work, we address the significant inter-modality variations that exist between structural MRI sequences and the relatively lower availability of ground truth labels. To this end, we propose a modality-aware network and a shift mixer, which are specifically designed to leverage multi-modal data effectively. Our modality-aware network incorporates a modality discriminator and a modality transformer, enabling it to efficiently process multi-modal inputs and learn the optimal weightage for each modality. The shift mixer, on the other hand, introduces stochastic shift parameters to the convolution operation, enhancing the model's ability to learn robust features from multi-modal data. We conducted extensive experiments using our proposed modality-aware network and shift mixer on the Multimodal Brain Tumor Segmentation Challenge 2020 dataset. Our results demonstrated a significant improvement over the existing state-of-the-art methods for multi-modal brain tumor segmentation.

A common way of assessing language learners' mastery of vocabulary is via multiple-choice tests. In this study, we examine whether this method is an accurate predictor of the learner's ability to generalize his or her knowledge of vocabulary to novel contexts. We report the results of a test involving 59 students who completed a task where they were asked to match words to their meanings in English, and then to produce sentences containing these words. The participants had studied English for at least three years in high school (age 14-18). Our findings show that the students performed well on both the word matching task and the sentence production task. However, there was a significant difference between the two tasks with respect to accuracy: the students were more accurate on the former

adaptNMT streamlines all processes involved in the development and deployment of RNN and Transformer neural machine translation models. We automate many critical steps, including model selection, data augmentation, hyperparameter optimization, and post-processing. Additionally, we provide a comprehensive set of tools to assist users at every stage of their project lifecycle. Our systematic approach ensures that projects are completed efficiently while maintaining high quality standards. With adaptNMT, developers can spend less time on tedious tasks and more time focusing on the creative aspects of model development. By democratizing access to advanced NLP technologies, we aim to encourage innovation in this field. As more people engage with adaptNMT, we hope to see new advancements in the

Recent advances in generative models, including large language models (LLMs), vision language models (VLMs), and foundation models, have opened up new possibilities for intelligent applications. However, these models still fall short of achieving human-level intelligence due to a lack of understanding of the world. This problem is exacerbated by the lack of data with accurate semantic annotations, which are crucial for training high-quality models. To address this challenge, we introduce the idea of "model-to-world," where machine learning models serve as interpreters to understand the real world through interaction. In this paper, we present an overview of this paradigm and highlight its potential benefits. We also explore some of the key challenges that need to be addressed before this a

Scene depth estimation from paintings can streamline the process of 3D sculpture creation so that an artist may efficiently generate and iterate on sculptural ideas. In this paper, we present a method for scene-level painting reconstruction to enable such interactive workflows by estimating dense depths using only RGB images as input in two stages: first generating coarse textured meshes with a pix2mesh model followed up subsequent refinement steps including semantic segmentation via Unet++, fine texture generation through conditional image synthesis employing StyleGANv1 [4], further mesh optimization utilizing DeepSDF++[7] , finally applying surface normal enhancement techniques based upon multiscale gradient field analysis.[9][8]. Our results demonstrate effective improvement compared pr

Point-goal navigation aims to guide robots to reach the target points specified by GPS coordinates. Visual corruptions, e.g., occlusions and lighting changes, are common in real-world navigation but are not addressed by previous methods. We propose TTA-Nav, which adapts the navigation policy at test time by reconstructing the goal point cloud from the RGB-D observations. Specifically, we design a view-dependent representation to capture the view-specific information and improve the localization accuracy under visual corruptions. We also propose a novel cross-view consistency loss to further improve the reconstruction accuracy. We show in experiments that TTA-Nav achieves the state-of-the-art performance on the Matterport3D and Replica datasets.

We present LiSTA: a novel LiDAR-based change detection method that aims to detect and estimate geometric objects in order to filter out ground clutter and improve the change map. Many change detection methods struggle in distinguishing objects from ground clutter, especially in forested or densely built-up areas. To overcome this, we propose to detect geometric objects from LiDAR point clouds and utilize the object information to filter out ground clutter in the change map. LiSTA consists of two novel machine learning models that are specifically designed for LiDAR-based change detection. The first model is a geometry-aware change score calculator that associates each LiDAR point with a change score based on the geometric context. The second model is an object-based change detector that aggregates LiDAR points into geometric objects, and estimates the change score for each object. We evaluate LiSTA on two LiDAR-based change detection datasets and demonstrate that LiSTA outperforms the state-of-the-art change detection methods.

Can artificial intelligence systems appreciate beauty? This question has been debated for decades, but recently, with the advent of large language models and advances in machine learning for image analysis, it has become possible to evaluate the aesthetic preferences of AI systems in a way that was not previously possible. In this work, we use images generated by GPT-4 with vision to investigate its aesthetic evaluation capabilities. We conducted two studies to compare GPT-4's aesthetic preferences with those of humans. In both studies, we found that GPT-4 exhibits aesthetic preferences similar to humans, but with some notable differences. We also found that GPT-4 tends to prefer images that are more abstract, unique, and colorful. These findings provide insights into the aesthetic evaluation capabilities of GPT-4 and raise interesting questions about the nature of beauty and its evaluation.

Large-scale high-resolution (HR) land-cover mapping is a vital task to support natural resource management, environmental monitoring, and ecosystem service assessment. However, most existing HR land-cover maps are obtained with a limited range of spectral bands, which may hinder the comprehensive characterization of land-cover features. In this study, we propose an integrated multi-source image fusion framework to fuse optical and SAR imagery for HR land-cover mapping. Specifically, the fusion is achieved by a combination of deep learning and graph neural network (GNN), where the UNet++ is used to extract and learn the high-level semantic information from the optical images, while the GNN is employed to further refine and aggregate these information into land-cover maps.

Self-supervised pre-training, based on masked image modeling (MIM), has fueled the recent advance in vision-language pre-training (VLPre). However, the limited discriminability of learned representation and the insufficient rich supervision are two main challenges that hamper the performance of current VLPre models. In this work, we propose a simple yet effective method to address both challenges, by introducing a novel rich supervision scheme that leverages the pre-trained MIM model as a feature extractor. Our scheme provides discriminative pre-training for the downstream tasks, and it also facilitates the cross-modal feature learning. The resulting model, named Rich Supervision Pre-training (RSP), achieves new state-of-the-art performance on established VLPre benchmarks. The code is available at https://github.com/BeierZhu/RSP.

We consider competitive facility location as a two-stage multi-agent system with two types of clients and multiple agents. Our objective is to find the optimal assignment that minimizes total completion time while meeting service level constraints on each client type. Each agent has two capacities: an initial capacity at the beginning of the planning horizon, which can be depleted by handling jobs; and a renewable capacity that can be replenished by servicing clients. The optimal assignment problem is NP-hard even when all agents have infinite capacities. In this paper, we focus on the case where the number of agents is fixed. We provide a polynomial-time algorithm for computing an optimal assignment that guarantees that each client receives its minimum required number of services, while m

Large Language Models (LLMs) have shown impressive results in various tasks. However, LLMs' performance in task-oriented dialogue (TOD) is limited by their knowledge about the domain of the task, which is typically not available to them at inference time. In this paper, we propose a framework to augment LLMs' knowledge through external access during inference, dubbed KnowAgent. KnowAgent consists of a knowledge extractor that monitors the conversation context and extracts relevant information, and a knowledge processor that leverages the extracted information to formulate responses. We evaluate our framework in the domain of flight reservation, and show that it improves over strong baselines in both automatic and human evaluation. We further show that KnowAgent can be adapted to different LLMs, and that it is capable of learning jointly the knowledge extraction and response generation processes.

Malicious URLs provide adversarial opportunities across various industries, including transportation, healthcare, and finance. We introduce the problem of detecting malicious URLs in traffic generated by popular mobile apps. A key challenge is that malicious URLs often appear alongside legitimate URLs with similar structures and patterns. To address this challenge, we develop a novel framework based on graph convolutional networks (GCNs) for URL classification. GCNs are known for their ability to capture both local and global context within graphs, making them well-suited for modeling complex network structures like those found in the web. Our method incorporates pre-trained models as features, representing the relationships between nodes in the graph. The results demonstrate the effective

People have to remember an ever-expanding volume of information. Wearables that use information capture and retrieval technologies can help them do this. They might also make our lives easier by taking on some of the cognitive burdens we face today, such as remembering where we parked or recalling what we need at the grocery store. While this technology is still in its early stages, it has already shown promise for improving human cognition and memory. In this blog post, we'll explore how wearable devices could change the way people think about their surroundings, and discuss potential ethical implications of using these tools for everyday tasks. The article will also look at recent developments in this area and offer a vision for future applications. ## Wearable Technology and Its Effects

Robotic manipulation of deformable, one-dimensional objects (DOOs) like ropes is challenging due to their low inertia and dynamic stiffness.
