In this paper, we propose a new method for learning to manipulate DOO shapes, called Doodle. Doodle trains an agent to perform manipulations by using only the DOO's visual appearance as input, without requiring access to position or velocity data during training. This approach requires no external state estimation, thereby reducing computational complexity. To evaluate Doodle, we develop a simulated environment based on physics simulations that can be used to train agents from scratch, allowing us to compare performance against other methods under identical conditions. We show that our method can learn a wide range of manipulation tasks with high gene

We propose language guided exploration (LGE) to help reinforcement learning (RL) agents quickly find important locations in text environments. Many existing RL exploration methods rely on intrinsic rewards, external rewards, or random exploration. However, using these methods, an RL agent may ignore important locations if they do not align with the agent's current goal. We instead give the agent a verbal description of the target location, which the agent uses to find important locations even if it has not been explicitly rewarded for doing so. We evaluate LGE in three text-based environments: Prose, Room, and Maze. LGE agents consistently outperform baseline RL agents given the same amount of exploration time. Furthermore, when the agent is rewarded based on a sparse binary reward (i.e., +1 if the agent is in the target room, otherwise 0), LGE allows the agent to find the target room more quickly than baseline exploration methods.

The performance of CLIP in dynamic facial expression recognition (DFER) task doesn't yield satisfactory results, due to the lack of large-scale training data and the generalization gap between training and testing scenarios. In this paper, we propose a novel contrastive learning framework for DFER by utilizing the cross-modal relationship between visual and linguistic domains. Specifically, we first construct a dataset comprising a wide range of emotions and expressions extracted from 2D images and videos. Then, we train a DFER model based on the CLIP backbone using both 2D and 3D facial expressions as inputs. To further improve the performance, we introduce an additional self-supervised method named Contrastive Adaptation of Features with Language Supervision (CAFLS), which leverages the

This article challenges the teleological narrative that presents the rise of digital technologies as the inevitable outcome of linear progress. Instead, it proposes to analyse the digital transition as a "longue dur\'ee" historical phenomenon, embedded in the socio-cultural specificities of different times and places. Focusing on France, it shows how the country's particular historical development - characterized by a strong state presence and a culture of regulation - has left its mark on the French relationship with digital technologies. While the article acknowledges the existence of a "digital native" generation that has grown up with the Internet, it emphasizes that digital technologies are anything but self-explanatory or self-evident. On the contrary, it stresses that their meaning and use are the result of active construction by societal actors, and that their potential is far from being fully realized.

Large Language Models (LLMs) have unlocked new capabilities and applications; however, the current generation of LLMs is still unable to reliably understand, reason about, and communicate in natural language. We introduce an approach that incorporates knowledge from a semantic network into LLMs to enhance their understanding of text. Our method leverages a neural network that maps between text and the graph structure of the semantic network. The model is trained using the pre-trained LLM as a teacher, with only minimal supervision. This ensures that the resulting model can be used to augment any existing LLM without requiring additional training data or fine-tuning. In addition, the approach is able to leverage large amounts of unlabeled data for semi-supervised learning. Experimental resu

Court View Generation (CVG) is a challenging task in the field of Legal Artificial Intelligence (LegalAI), which aims to generate court views based on the plaintiff claims and the fact descriptions. While Pretrained Language Models (PLMs) have showcased their prowess in natural language generation, their application to the complex, knowledge-intensive domain of CVG often reveals inherent limitations. In this paper, we present a novel approach, named Knowledge Injection and Guidance (KIG), designed to bolster CVG using PLMs. To efficiently incorporate domain knowledge during the training stage, we introduce a knowledge-injected prompt encoder for prompt tuning, thereby reducing computational overhead.

Ship detection needs to identify ship locations from remote sensing (RS) scenes. However, due the low spatial resolution and noisy background of RS images or videos in maritime environments [1], it is challenging for traditional deep learning methods with hand-crafted features such as multiscale convolutional neural networks ([2]) which cannot effectively extract important information about ships while suppressing unnecessary noises on a large scale scene that often occur during image acquisition processes via satellite imaging platforms; this results into an inaccurate prediction accuracy score when compared against real world scenarios where there exists plenty more complex objects besides just those few simple ones found within our datasets alone! This paper proposes ShipDetNet - A nove

A common issue for machine learning models applied to time-series forecasting is the temporal evolution of their performance. In particular, if the underlying data distribution changes over time, e.g., due to seasonal effects or shifts in the population, models may no longer be able to adequately predict future values. To mitigate this issue, it is crucial to detect when a model's performance begins to deteriorate and take appropriate actions. This can involve retraining the model with new data, updating the existing model parameters, or even replacing the entire model altogether. Early detection of performance degradation is key to ensuring accurate predictions, reducing losses, and maintaining model reliability. However, identifying when and why a model is beginning to fail can often be

Causal inference, a cornerstone in disciplines such as economics, genomics, and clinical research aims at understanding how interventions affect outcomes. Recently the field has seen an explosion of work on causal graphs (also known as interventional Markov models), which represent assumptions about interactions between variables using graphical structure to model causation rather than correlation or independence alone.\linebreak\noindent While much is understood regarding consistency conditions for these methods under various scenarios with respect their underlying probability distribution,\vspace{2pt} little attention \emph{\textcolor[HTML]{4870B9}{has been paid}} towards analyzing sensitivity properties: How robust are inferences made by our method when we change some data?

Smoothing the rating graph is a common technique for addressing the sparsity and improving the collaborative filtering recommendation performance. However, existing methods only consider the global perspective, which smooths the whole graph with a uniform strategy. This overlooks the fact that different subgraphs may require different smoothing magnitudes. To tackle this challenge, we propose a novel general graph desmoothing framework, named DGR, for collaborative filtering. The DGR learns a global graph smoother to uniformly smooth the whole graph and a local graph smoother to adaptively adjust the smoothing magnitude for different subgraphs. To learn the two types of smoothers, we introduce a novel rating propagation approach to enable the model to better capture the global and local information. Specifically, we design the global rating propagation subgraph to aggregate the global information and the local rating propagation subgraph to aggregate the local information. To improve the recommendation performance, we fuse the two types of smoothed representations with a weighted summation approach. The DGR is a general framework, which can be reduced to some existing methods by simply removing one of the two subgraphs. To learn the optimal global and local smoothing magnitudes, we devise a self-supervised learning approach by leveraging the unsupervised rating graph clustering. Extensive experiments on four benchmark datasets demonstrate that the proposed method outperforms some state-of-the-art baselines. The source code is available at https://github.com/mazhf/DGR.

Passive wire alignment mechanisms (PWMs) have been widely used in optical devices for their simplicity and reliability. However, their poor mechanical isolation often results in adverse effects on the optical performance of the devices. To address this issue, this paper proposes a novel concept of a passive wire alignment mechanism with a spherical pawl (PWM-Sphere). The contact point between the spherical pawl and the wire can be dynamically changed to generate a rotating force on the wire, which can overcome the friction between the wire and the alignment mechanism. Thus, the wire can be passively aligned with high accuracy and safety without applying external force. Furthermore, a lightweight and low-cost manipulator (SAQIEL) is designed and manufactured using 3D printed parts, consisting of a spherical pawl, a cylindrical tube, and a conical base. The manipulator can hold and transport fragile objects, such as optical fibers, under water with a maximum payload of 1.2 N. The experiments demonstrate that the proposed PWM-Sphere has a high alignment accuracy of up to 97.7% within a short time of 1.6 s. The SAQIEL manipulator, driven by an ultra-light-weight three-degree-of-freedom parallel mechanism, can move the object with a maximum speed of 4.18 m/s. The experiments also show that the proposed manipulator can perform pick-and-place tasks in water with a success rate of 83.3%.

Projection mapping (PM) is a technique that enhances the appearance of real-world surfaces using digital images. In recent years, there has been an increasing interest in deploying PM for applications such as augmented reality and virtual production. However, current approaches often fail to account for factors like uneven illumination or surface deformations, which can significantly impact the performance of the resulting projection. This paper proposes a novel method for enhancing the quality of projected content by leveraging deep learning techniques. Specifically, we use a single image to generate lightmaps that capture both direct and indirect illumination. These lightmaps are then fused with depth information to provide more accurate results. Additionally, we propose a differentiable

Interpretability is the next frontier in machine learning research. In the search for white box models, explainable AI (XAI) techniques are gaining traction. These methods aim to uncover how ML models arrive at their predictions by examining the model's internal layers and weights. However, it is challenging to determine which layer of a deep neural network (DNN) contains the most important information about the prediction. One approach is to analyze every layer separately, but this method requires extensive computational resources due to the large number of layers involved. We introduce an effective methodology that identifies critical layers without incurring significant computational costs. Our proposed technique involves applying two techniques: firstly, we apply the F-score method usi

In this paper, we introduce tiled graphs as models of learning and maturing processes. We show how to construct a tiling for an arbitrary graph that is isomorphic with the initial one after a certain number $n$of iterations in which edges are replaced by tiles representing new generations (from young learners/learners-to-$be$, through intermediate ones up until mature experts). This result provides a natural way out from some limitations inherent into previous approaches based on iterated networks or their generalizations: first it allows us not only consider any type but also mix different types within our model; second since each tile has its own unique identity rather than being identical copies like those found inside traditional iterative structures - they become more diverse over tim

Accurate and consistent methods for counting trees based on remote sensing data are needed to support sustainable forest management. However, current approaches struggle with inconsistent results due to the limited availability of ground-truths (GTruths) in areas of interest. Furthermore, current methods often rely on a single metric or cost function which can result in overestimates when applied to unseen data. To address these limitations, we propose an innovative approach that integrates cost functions with a multi-objective optimization process. Our method utilizes multiple metrics, including those derived from the Sentinel 1 and 2 satellite images, to determine the optimal balance between overestimation and underestimation. By incorporating information from multiple sources, our metho

This paper presents an overview of the state of language and speech technology for the Central Kurdish varieties, Koork and Gorani. We describe the writing systems, provide sample texts, and give an overview of the phonology and grammar of these languages. We then discuss the current state of computational resources and research for text processing and speech technology. While there is a rich tradition of scholarly research on these languages, especially in descriptive grammar and literature, there are few computational resources. We present available resources, including the author's own contributions, and discuss the needs and challenges for the future development of Kurdish NLP technology.

Real-time multi-task reinforcement learning (MARL) with adaptive task recognition is a new and emerging research topic. It aims to recognize novel tasks online and adaptively divide them into subtasks that can be executed in parallel with different policy networks. This is similar to how humans handle new tasks; for example, when solving a maze for the first time, humans can gradually recognize different areas as they become familiar with the task. Existing approaches mainly focus on dividing tasks into subtasks offline and then solving them separately. They usually rely on manually defined state-action spaces and simple task structures, and are therefore not suitable for complex and dynamic real-world tasks. To address this problem, we propose a novel sliding multidimensional task window-based MARL framework, called SMAUG, for adaptive real-time subtask recognition. Our framework can automatically divide the observed state space into subspaces, each corresponding to a subtask, without the need for manually defined state-action spaces or task structures. We evaluate our approach on a set of complex continuous control tasks and show that our method outperforms several state-of-the-art baselines. Our approach demonstrates adaptive learning capabilities, solving the tasks online in an efficient manner.

Knowledge-based, open-domain dialogue generation aims to build chit-chat systems that can fluently interact with humans. In this work, we propose a novel approach, Knowledge-based Open-Domain Dialogue Generation (KODG), which leverages knowledge graphs and reinforcement learning to address the challenges of real-world conversations. Our model incorporates a knowledge graph as a memory bank, enabling it to retain relevant information from previous dialogues. This capability is crucial for maintaining consistency and building upon prior discussions effectively. Additionally, our model employs a contextual policy network to capture semantic and temporal features, thereby enhancing its decision-making accuracy. By integrating these two components, KODG demonstrates significant improvements in

Learning from demonstration (LfD) provides a convenient means to equip robots with dexterous skills when demonstration can be obtained in robot intrinsic coordinates. However, many challenges remain open, especially when the demonstrated task requires the use of both hands. In this paper, we focus on teaching bimanual manipulation tasks using visual imitation learning (VIL). VIL benefits from the use of an RGB-D camera and a model predictive controller, which allow the robot to reproduce the demonstrated trajectory in a visually similar manner. Nevertheless, bimanual tasks pose several challenges, such as the increased number of state dimensions and the difficulty of finding a good reward function to maximize. To address these issues, we propose the use of keypoints to represent the state of the object being manipulated and the environment. This representation is invariant to object and camera motion, thereby reducing the state dimensionality and eliminating the need for a reward function. We demonstrate the effectiveness of our method for teaching a robot to perform a variety of bimanual manipulation tasks using a simple and intuitive demonstration setup.

Diffusion models create data from noise by inverting the forward paths of data towards noise and are considered to be a powerful tool for generative modeling. However, diffusion models have not been effectively applied to multi-modal and temporal data. This paper proposes a new framework called Temporal Multi-Modal Diffusion Model (TMMDM) that can address these issues. TMMDM adopts a multimodal structure to model both images and text while also considering the temporal aspect through a diffusion process. In addition, we propose two extensions of TMMDM: 1) temporal-multimodal fusion module that fuses multimodal information with temporal information, and 2) diffusion attention mechanism for temporal dependency learning. Experimental results show that our proposed method achieves state-of-the

Recent advances in diffusion models have led to impressive improvements in many generative modeling tasks. However, the training and sampling efficiency of diffusion models is limited for high-dimensional data. To improve the training and sampling efficiency, several works propose to guide the diffusion process using a pre-trained neural network, which is called diffusion guidance. Despite the popularity of diffusion guidance, its effect on the diffusion process and the model's generalization is still mysterious. In this work, we aim to shed some light on the theoretical insights behind diffusion guidance. As a case study, we focus on the diffusion process over Gaussian mixture models. We show that diffusion guidance can be interpreted as an approximation of the gradient flow of the negative loglikelihood of a Gaussian mixture model. We further show that diffusion guidance can help the diffusion process to overcome local minima, which is a common problem for high-dimensional data. Finally, we demonstrate that the generalization gap of diffusion guidance can be closed by a simple baseline method, and the diffusion guidance is robust to the choice of the guide network. Our theoretical results are supported by experiments.

Neural Radiance Fields (NeRF) have quickly become the primary approach for 3D object reconstruction. In order to achieve better reconstruction performance, many recent works focus on enhancing the training process. These approaches can be roughly classified into two categories: those that enhance the training data using additional information and those that enhance model capacity. This paper presents a hybrid method that integrates both approaches. We propose a new data augmentation strategy that produces pseudo-data from existing images. These pseudo-images are used as supervisory signals during training. By combining this augmentation technique with other training methods, we develop a novel architecture that significantly improves reconstruction performance. Our model is capable of prod

Video temporal grounding (VTG) aims to locate specific temporal segments from an untrimmed video that correspond semantically and temporally with a textual description. While existing VTG models leverage various modalities, such as text-toimage generation or language modeling in the context of vision transformer for better performance on image retrieval tasks like CLIP [35]. However these methods do not capture multimodal interactions between audio/visual cues during self attention which is crucial towards accurate localization within videos especially when dealing multiple objects at once i.,e cross modal interaction becomes more challenging due limited information provided by each individual modality alone .

In recent years, the focus of cyber security research has shifted from the development of technical countermeasures to the analysis of human factors in the cyber security landscape. This paper explores the challenges of deploying Industrial Intrusion Detection Systems (IIDS) in process industries. Through interviews with 29 experts in the field of industrial cyber security, we examine the challenges of implementing IIDS from both a technical and human perspective. We identified technical challenges such as the need for IIDS to operate in real-time, the ability to deal with noisy industrial data, and the generation of false positives and false negatives. From a human perspective, challenges include the need for IIDS to integrate with existing industrial systems, the lack of skilled personnel for IIDS maintenance, and the resistance to change among industrial workers. We also discuss the role of human factors in the context of the broader cyber security landscape. We present recommendations for researchers, practitioners, and future IIDS developers to address these challenges and improve the deployment and effectiveness of IIDS in process industries.

Due to the effectiveness of second-order algorithms in solving classical optimization problems, designing second-order algorithms that can handle nonconvex and nonsmooth functions is crucial for many machine learning applications. In this paper, we introduce a novel approach to finding second-order proxies and subgradients with a special focus on nonconvex and nonsmooth functions. Our approach involves constructing an auxiliary problem by defining a new objective function as a weighted sum of the original objective and the Hessian of the original objective. We then apply the Karush-Kuhn-Tucker (KKT) conditions to derive necessary and sufficient conditions for local convergence to stationarity of this auxiliary problem. The derived conditions are used to design second-order algorithms for b

Video object segmentation (VOS) aims at automatically separating the primary foreground object(s) from the background in a video sequence. Despite previous VOS methods' success in extracting objects with distinct appearances, object discovery for VOS is still an urgent problem to be solved, especially for real-world videos with large appearance variations. In this regard, we propose a novel depth-aware test-time training (DTT) scheme to facilitate the VOS model to discover objects with distinct appearances from complex backgrounds. Specifically, we design a depth prediction branch to estimate the depth map of each frame in the video sequence. The predicted depth map is then fused with the feature map from the original VOS model to help the model better distinguish the object region from the background. To train our model, we adopt a test-time training strategy that only uses the validation set for both model update and hyper-parameter searching. In addition, we propose a novel object discovery loss to encourage the model to focus on the object region during the training process. Extensive experiments on the DAVIS-2017 and YouTube-VOS 2018 benchmarks show that our method outperforms previous VOS methods without using any post-processing technique.

Data integration is often performed to consolidate information from multiple disparate data sources during visual data analysis. In many cases, the integrated dataset must be enriched with additional metadata (e.,g.. tags and categories) that can help users find relevant records in large datasets or provide a more complete view of their contents for visualization purposes such as clustering algorithms like K-means which require input vectors where each coordinate represents some aspect related directly back onto one particular column within our original table structure In this paper we introduce an automatic approach based on machine learning techniques combined together into single pipeline called “AutoTagger” capable enough generate meaningful labels automatically without needing human

The Internet of Things (IoT) is a novel architecture that encompasses a variety of things (sensors, actuators, etc.). Things in IoT are connected to each other and to the cloud for exchanging data for performing various tasks. The cloud acts as a central server for storing, processing, and retrieving data requests from connected things. With the expected massive scale of IoT, the management of data-intensive applications will require efficient routing of data requests between things and the cloud. In this paper, we propose a novel shortest path query algorithm, called Optimized Adaptive Topology Structure (OATS), for routing data requests in IoT. OATS utilizes an adaptive topology structure that is aware of the location of data requests and the cost to reach the cloud. The topology structure is dynamically adjusted based on the data request traffic to optimize the path selection for data-intensive applications. We evaluate OATS by implementing it on top of the Internet and using real-world traces. Our results show that OATS improves the end-to-end delay compared to other state-of-the-art routing algorithms for data-intensive applications by up to 2X.

Sequential recommendation aims to estimate the dynamic user preferences and sequential dependencies among historical user-item interactions. Recent approaches mainly focus on modeling the evolving user preferences by introducing the item representation learning and preference modeling modules. However, they fail to fully utilize the temporal information, which is important for predicting the future preference of users. To address this issue, we propose a novel Transformer-based model with an integrated temporal module (ITM) that learns to embed the temporal information and sequence patterns into user representations in a more flexible way. Specifically, our ITM first captures the temporal information from the sequence patterns through a multi-head self-attention mechanism. Then, we develop

Parameterizing machine learning models for few-shot learning remains a challenge. We propose a novel parameterization based on the time steps of a diffusion process. We show that diffusion models can be interpreted as a parameterization of the training data distribution, and that the proposed few-shot learner parameterization can be learned by minimizing a loss function based on the Kullback-Leibler divergence between two empirical data distributions. Experiments on few-shot classification tasks with real-world data sets show that the proposed parameterization improves the performance of a baseline diffusion model.

Understanding the modus operandi of adversaries aids organizations in employing efficient defensive strategies to protect themselves from attacks. Previous research has focused on identifying and classifying malicious artifacts, such as malware binaries or JavaScript code snippets into families (e., WannaCry). However there is a need for more comprehensive understanding that spans across all stages of an attack chain - starting with reconnaissance activities leading up until execution time when data exfiltration occurs via phishing emails containing attachments like Word documents which contain macros capable not only extracting information but also executing commands remotely through shell scripts stored within these files; this allows attackers access privileged credentials without needi

The centrality metrics are key concepts in the network science for measuring the importance of nodes and edges in a network and have a wide range of applications. We propose a novel method called Factory Analysis of Centrality (FAC) to investigate the interrelations among the centrality metrics for complex real-world networks. The interrelations are represented as a network, the meta-network, whose nodes are the centrality metrics and whose edges connect the metrics which are highly correlated. We expect that the topology of the meta-network reflects the interrelations among the centrality metrics and provides a comprehensive view of the metrics for analyzing complex real-world networks. We validate our method by applying it to 14 large real-world networks. We find that the resulting meta-networks are highly heterogeneous, which consist of densely connected core-periphery structures. We also find that the node centralities in the real-world networks are highly correlated with each other, but the edge centralities are less correlated. Moreover, we find that the assortativity of the meta-networks varies across the real-world networks, but the coreness tends to be negatively correlated with the degree centrality. Our method reveals the interrelations among the node and edge centralities in complex real-world networks and provides further insights into the choice of the appropriate centrality metrics for network analysis.

We present NeWRF, a deep learning framework for predicting wireless channels. Wireless channel prediction is important in many applications such as the estimation of link-level radio resource allocation and spectrum sensing. Existing methods rely on traditional machine learning algorithms like Support Vector Machines (SVM) or Random Forest, which are computationally expensive and require extensive hyperparameter tuning. Additionally, they have limited model interpretability, making it difficult to analyze their underlying mechanisms. Our approach uses an unsupervised pretraining network based on DeepFM and a supervised fine-tuning stage to learn more interpretable features. In our experiments, we show that our method outperforms previous methods, achieving 92% accuracy and 0.73 correlation

Recently, large language models (LLMs) have notably positioned them as capable tools for the generation of high-quality code. LLMs can be used to generate new code or edit existing code by providing a prompt and allowing the model to learn from the examples provided. The generated code can then be run on test cases to ensure its correctness. However, the effectiveness of this approach depends largely on the quality of the input examples, which may not always reflect the best practices in software engineering. Furthermore, many real-world tasks are complex and require more than just a simple sequence of steps to solve. In this work, we propose an integrated framework that allows LLMs to use both textual prompts and code snippets as inputs to produce solutions to programming tasks. We presen

We study online learning in repeated auctions for a resource, where a bidder learns the value of the resource by participating in the auctions. We focus on the setting where the bidder's payment in an auction is not necessarily equal to the bid she submits, but is instead determined by the rule of the game: if the bidder wins the auction (by submitting the highest bid), she pays the second highest bid. This rule is known as the "karma rule," and it has been shown to induce efficient equilibria in repeated auctions. Under the karma rule, the bidder's total payment is determined by both the bids she submits and the identities of the other bidders. Hence, the bidder's strategy affects not only her own payment, but also the payments of the other bidders. This gives rise to the question of how the other bidders' behavior affects the optimal strategy for the bidder. In this paper, we show that the bidder can learn her optimal strategy even in the face of strategic opponents who are also learning their strategies. We present a simple learning algorithm and analyze its performance in both the stochastic and adversarial settings. Our results are based on a structural decomposition of the bidder's optimization problem, which allows the learner to focus on a carefully selected subset of the other bidders.

The Earth Mover's Distance is a popular similarity measure in several branches of computer science. However, it lacks formal foundations and does not satisfy some basic desiderata for measures. In this paper we develop the first axiomatic foundation for the earth mover's distance. This foundation consists of three axioms: (i) non-negativity; (ii) symmetry; and (iii) additivity over independent variables. We show that there are many different ways to define an earth mover's distance that satisfies these axioms. Each definition comes with its own set of advantages and disadvantages. We also show how our axioms imply other desirable properties such as subadditivity and monotonicity under scaling. Finally, we propose a new variant of the earth mover's distance that satisfies all our axioms. Ou

The bongard-logo problem is a creative puzzle that consists in finding a property satisfied by a set of five colored shapes and not satisfied by another set of five colored shapes. We have solved the bongard-logo problem instances created by the/logo designer David Bongardt by modeling a probabilistic model based on the frequency of colors and shapes in the two given sets. Our experiments show that the predicted solutions match the actual solutions provided by participants in the Bongardt-logo contest. We have also used the obtained results to propose a new probabilistic solution to the 13th problem, the last unsolved instance of the bongard-logo problem.

Due to the recent increase in interest in Financial Technology (FinTech), applications like credit scoring and fraud detection have become increasingly important. This paper proposes a novel solution for these problems based on an advanced deep learning architecture called Graph Neural Networks, which has been shown to be effective in handling graph-structured data such as social networks. In this work, we demonstrate that our approach outperforms traditional machine learning methods on various real-world datasets of varying sizes, including Kaggle's Credit Card Fraud dataset, CIFAR-10/100, and UCI's Breast Cancer Wisconsin Diagnostic Dataset. Our results indicate that Graph Neural Networks provide superior performance compared to classical approaches when dealing with graphs, suggesting t

Our understanding of the generalization capabilities of neural networks (NNs) is still incomplete. Previosly, we studied an approximate theoretical model for NNs and found that they generalize in a robust way to unseen input-output pairs generated from simple functions [JMLR2019]. However this work left open several questions: what are these ``simple'' function classes? How do their properties relate with those observed empirically on real data sets like MNIST or CIFAR 1K ? We address them by studying two types of NN architectures -- convolutional nets \cite{LeCun} which capture local features through pooling layers; recurrent ones such as LSTMs\footnote{\url {https://arxiv .org/abs /rXiv :4586}} built upon unrolled versions thereof --- each endowed over different domains ($X = {\mathbb R

Graph anomaly detection (GAD) is the task of identifying nodes in a graph that exhibit unusual behavior compared to the majority of the nodes. GAD is important in many real-world applications, including cybersecurity, fraud detection, and disease outbreak detection. There has been growing interest in GAD in recent years, and many different methods have been proposed. In this survey, we provide a node-level perspective on GAD and categorize the node-level GAD methods into three groups: outlier detection, message passing, and hyperbolic neural networks. We discuss the representative models in these three groups in detail and compare them from the perspectives of performance, scalability, and robustness. We also mention the feature-level GAD methods briefly. We conclude this survey with open challenges and potential research directions.

Neural machine translation (MT) models achieve strong results across a variety of settings, but it is still unclear how to build and train a model that can be deployed in the real world. In this paper, we introduce an open-source neural MT system for Romanian. We use the state-of-the-art transformer-based sequence-to-sequence architecture with byte pair encoding (BPE). We compare several BPE vocabularies using a new benchmark dataset for Romanian, called RoBLEU. Our experiments show that the best model achieves 19.8 BLEU on the RoBLEU test set. We also make our training corpus public in order to encourage future research and development efforts. This work represents the first large-scale MT project in Romanian, paving the way for further advancements in the field.

We design a deterministic subexponential time algorithm that takes as input a multivariate polynomial $f$ and a target $k\in\mathbb{N}$. The algorithm finds a degree-$d$ irreducible factor of $f$ with degree at most $dk$. This is the first deterministic algorithm to find a degree-$O(1)$ irreducible factor of any given degree $d$, which is known to be $\mathsf{NP}$-hard. Our algorithm is based on the construction of a polynomial with many zeros at each root of $f$, followed by solving a system of polynomial equations in those roots, which can be done in time $2^{(\deg f) \cdot d k}$ using an FFT-based algorithm. The running time improves upon the best known randomized polynomial-time algorithms for this problem, where all the previous results are exponential in $d$; see \cite{BCHKS20}.

Large language models (LLMs) have proven to be very superior to conventional methods in various natural-language processing tasks, such as image captioning and machine translation. As a consequence of their excellent performance across many domains with little or no domain knowledge required for fine tuning them on the target task at hand, LLMS are now widely used by data scientists around globe within applications ranging from business intelligence analytics through text mining up into automated marketing campaigns involving social media platforms like Facebook Messenger bot service providers who use these tools instead writing code manually themselves!

Diverse groups solve more problems than homogeneous groups, but diversity can create conflict. We examine a dataset from a diverse group of 14 professionals solving 18 different problems collaboratively in 3-person subgroups. Our analysis focuses on the verbal behavior of the majority and minority members of these subgroups. We find that minorities solve more problems than majorities, and that this advantage is due to minorities' unique contributions. Minorities introduce more novel ideas, ask more questions, and make more statements that lead to solutions. Majority members, in contrast, tend to repeat ideas, agree with each other, and oppose minority ideas. These findings are consistent across all 18 problems. We suggest that diversity benefits group problem solving because it increases the number of perspectives represented in the group, and that listening to minority perspectives is key to leveraging diversity.

Conversational information-seeking (CIS) is an emerging paradigm for retrieving relevant content from the web. One of its main advantages over traditional keyword search approaches, which are based on document similarity metrics such as TFIDF or BM25F score functions and text extraction methods like phrase chunking [1], lies in a human's ability to recognize subtle clues that could indicate whether two utterances are related through their semantic structure alone; this capability allows users not only find answers quickly but also understand why certain results were returned by searching engines when asked questions directly using natural language input rather than keywords typed into them via interface boxes found elsewhere online today .

This paper presents a vision-based 3D object detection and monocular depth estimation system for autonomous driving. A scalable and robust feature extraction pipeline is proposed to produce high-quality features with large receptive fields. Specifically, a feature pyramid network is built on a large image crop to extract high-level semantic features, and a set of 3D specific features are extracted from a bird's-eye view. The 3D specific features are then fused with the high-level semantic features to obtain semantic-rich 3D features. The 3D features are projected to multiple sparse depth maps to estimate monocular depth. Finally, a 3D non-maximum suppression is proposed to filter out false positive detections. The proposed system is evaluated on the KITTI and Waymo benchmarks, achieving state-of-the-art performance. The system is also scalable and can be applied to different autonomous driving platforms. For example, the 3D object detection system is applied to the NVIDIA Isaac Sim GPU, and the monocular depth estimation system is applied to the Google Coral Edge TPU. This is the first time that a GPU and an Edge TPU have been used for real-time 3D object detection and depth estimation on a car. The GPU implementation achieves 89.2 FPS and the Edge TPU implementation achieves 6.9 FPS.

Controllable speech synthesis aims to edit the acoustic features of speech to realize different emotions quantitatively and fine-grainedly. Previous works mainly adopt the traditional three-step pipeline, which first represents the speech content, then performs emotion editing, and finally synthesizes the speech. However, it is difficult for them to achieve both fine-grained emotion editing and good controllability. To address this problem, we propose a novel two-step pipeline. First, we propose a novel content-independent emotion representation (CIER) to better separate the emotion information from the content information. Then, we use a high-level modulation network (HLMN) to directly manipulate the CIER to achieve fine-grained emotion editing. The HLMN contains two modules: a hierarchical emotion module (HEM) and a modulation module (MM). The HEM is a novel top-down architecture that can generate multi-level emotion representations (MERs) by progressively abstracting the emotion information from the speech. The MM then modulates the MERs into the speech embedding. Finally, we use a post-net to integrate the edited speech embedding with the original content representation to obtain the final controlled speech. To prove the effectiveness of our method, we compare our method with several strong baselines on the DEMAND dataset. The results show that our method outperforms the baselines in terms of both objective metrics and subjective ratings.

We present a learning-based approach to reconstruct buildings as 3D polygonal meshes, offering an alternative to the widely used and often unrealistic parametric models. The proposed method is based on the idea of using convolutional neural networks (CNNs) to learn building structures from a set of training images. The goal is to create a network that can reconstruct the structure of a building given only a single image of it. This project explores how CNNs can be used in conjunction with point clouds for this task. Our approach consists of two stages: 1) Image segmentation: identifying each pixel's class by predicting its corresponding class probability; and 2) Mesh reconstruction: constructing a 3D mesh of a given building using the predicted probabilities from the segmentation stage. By

Rapid and reliable identification of dynamic scene parts, also known as motion segmentation, is a key component for many video understanding tasks. As one major difficulty in the field lies with handling varying camera motions (such changes are common especially when using consumer-grade cameras), this paper proposes to leverage optical flow estimation results during training time: we introduce two simple yet powerful modifications into traditional learning frameworks by incorporating an attention mechanism which can selectively suppress or enhance individual image frames based on their corresponding velocity maps; additionally our approach includes novel techniques that allow us improve upon existing methods through both improved robustness against difficult scenarios involving large shif

Many online content portals allow users to ask questions to supplement their understanding (e.g., Quora, StackOverflow). To better assist these questions and answer them more efficiently, it is important to understand how the community of readers responds to various types of question topics. We present a novel dataset, Q-COMET, which provides insights into the most frequently asked questions by type across different domains in the form of a question-answer pair. We provide insights from this analysis on how the community of readers responds to different types of questions. Additionally, we show that the distribution of questions per domain within our dataset exhibits a long tail phenomenon, which necessitates a modeling approach that can handle such data effectively. Finally, we demonstrat

Deep learning models, particularly those based on transformers, often employ numerous stacked structures, which possess unique challenges for training. In particular, the long-range dependencies and cross-layer interactions in transformer models make their optimization difficult to handle with traditional stochastic gradient descent (SGD) methods. This work proposes a new method called StackFast, which utilizes the SGD algorithm to efficiently train deep transformer models. We identify that the gradients of the model's loss function are highly correlated across layers, especially when using relatively large models. By applying a simple yet effective technique called gradient stacking, we achieve significant performance improvements over existing baselines such as AdamW and Fused Momentum S

The study analyses polarisation on Finnish social media with data from the platform X, which is a popular online forum in Finland. The empirical material consists of 250 posts that were selected based upon three different criteria: (i) most-read threads during December 19–January 3; ii), topics related to European Union politics and integration processes as well as asylum seekers or immigration debates iii); discussion forums dedicated for political issues such as “Finland” (“Suomi”), EU affairs etc., but also general public fora like “News”. The research questions are how politically polarised discussions take place among users within these four subforums?

Stance detection of social media text is a key component of downstream tasks involving the identification of groups and their characteristics. This paper presents an investigation into stance classification using BERT-based models for Twitter data in order to ascertain whether or not they can be used as effective baseline methods on this dataset without further training/fine tuning, which would require extensive manual annotation efforts by human annotators that are beyond our resources at present time constraints given resource limitations such those imposed upon us due COVID19 pandemic situation worldwide today where many people around globe have been forced homebound working remotely from offices rather than physically attending meetings face2face with colleagues -as before-.

Human-centric 3D scene understanding has recently drawn increasing attention, driven by its critical impact on augmented reality (AR) and virtual production. Despite promising progress in semantic segmentation of raw point clouds to recognize the objects at a fine scale (\eg "sofa"), it is still challenging for current methods to perceive human activities from such an abstracted representation without contextual information about humans' actions or gestures as well their visual appearances ("sitting").

We study the PSPACE-complete $k$-Canadian Traveller Problem, where a traveller moves from city to another by using only public transportation (e.g., buses or subways) and must visit exactly k cities. We show that finding an optimal itinerary for this problem is NP$\cap$coNP -hard even in planar graphs with all weights being 1052483697% of unit length; it was previously believed hard on such inputs when both source/destination are given as fixed vertices without any additional restriction except their connectivity within distance at most one vertex away ($O(m)$). This result contrasts against our recent results [STOC 'E', arXiv:math/'F'] which showed hardness already if just either endpoint can be freely chosen among O($n\log n$) special ones but not more than two edges between them have we

Recent work has demonstrated that fine-tuning is a promising approach to `unlearn' biased features from data in order achieve better fairness outcomes. However, this strategy can be difficult to apply at scale due to the need for relabeling and retraining datasets on an ongoing basis; further it requires access both training labels as well model parameters (which are typically not available). In contrast with these strategies which require manual intervention or expensive computation resources , we propose \emph{Fairness by Design},a framework based solely upon existing open source code repositories such GitHub . Our experiments show significant improvements over prior works without requiring any additional human effort beyond standard practices followed during software development process

We explore the task of 3D visual grounding, which aims to localize the corresponding 3D object instance from a language query. Existing methods mostly adopt the object-centric representation, where the 3D object is represented by a singleton set. Such a representation can be inaccurate since an object instance may have diverse shapes and appearances, e.g., due to pose variations, occlusions, etc. In this work, we instead take a scene-centric view and represent an object instance by multiple anchor points, which are sampled from the object's bounding box in the scene. Each anchor point is then transformed to a scene-centric representation by subtracting the camera position. We accumulate the language-modulated scene information from multiple anchor points to achieve accurate 3D visual grounding. We call our method Multi-Key-Anchor & Scene-Aware Transformer, or MiKASA. Experiments on the LINEMOD and GenMOP datasets show that our method outperforms existing state-of-the-art techniques.

In this paper, we discuss the problem of training robot policies that are symmetric with respect to a given set of symmetries in the environment. We first discuss why considering symmetries in the environment is important from a learning perspective. Then, we propose a method to leverage this symmetry information to improve the learning of robot policies. Specifically, we show that by using a policy representation that explicitly encodes the symmetries in the environment, the search for a good policy can be decomposed into the search for a good symmetric policy and the search for a good representation of the policy. We illustrate the benefits of our approach on the control of a 6-DOF quadrotor in a symmetric environment, where we show that the proposed method can find symmetric policies faster than a baseline approach that does not leverage the environment symmetries.

Large-scale nearest neighbor (NN) search is a fundamental data operation that retrieves the nearest data points to a query point. As the data scale grows, the NN search becomes increasingly slower. To accelerate NN search, the data scientists usually build a data index and employ a brute-force search algorithm that scans the index. However, the brute-force search algorithm can hardly utilize the index efficiently as the data scale grows. Thus, we propose a graph partitioning technique to transform the brute-force search algorithm into an iterative one that leverages the index more effectively. We decompose the data graph into multiple subgraphs using a graph partitioning algorithm and store each subgraph in a separate process. Each iteration of the search algorithm retrieves the local neighbors from the subgraph indexes and merges the results. The data scientists can control the amount of work for each iteration and the number of iterations needed to find the nearest neighbors by adjusting the partition size. We conduct extensive experiments and show that our technique can accelerate the brute-force search algorithm by up to 2.4X on a 400M data set.

Large Language Models (LLMs) like ChatGPT, Google Bard, and others have garnered immense interest due to their remarkable capabilities in generating text. This has led to the emergence of LLM-powered chatbots which are being used across diverse industries and sectors. In this article, we will explore the impact of these LLM-powered chatbots on digital marketing, specifically focusing on their potential for enhancing customer engagement and experiences. We will examine how businesses can leverage these tools to enhance customer service, personalize content, and optimize campaigns, ultimately leading to increased brand loyalty and sales. We will also discuss the challenges associated with integrating these chatbots into existing marketing strategies and provide insights into the future direc

Humans can easily distinguish the known and unknown categories and can recognize the unknown object by learning it once. However, how humans achieve this task is still unclear because of their complex brain structure that involves multiple regions to process sensory inputs for decision-making processes during perception tasks [1]. Various neural network models have been proposed in recent years with different types such as Recurrent Neural Network (RNN)s[2], Convolutional Networks(CNN)[3]and Deep Learning Models.[4][5],[6]. [7]-[9]._In general deep networks are considered suitable when training data size increases while traditional machine algorithms work well only if there exists a relatively small number or less than 80% samples per class compared against total dataset ratio . AI Large Language Models (LLMs) like ChatGPT are set to become a transformative technology that will change the way we think, work and play. In the coming years of 2035-46 AI LLMS could be at least as important for humanity's development in their scale or impact on society compared with today’ s smartphones . However , while there is much hype around these tools right now due largely thanks too high expectations from investors who want quick returns even before fully understanding what makes them tick under hood--it remains unclear whether such technologies really represent anything more than another form factor evolution within existing tech ecosystem rather then fundamentally changing how humans interact digitally across all aspects including communication interfaces/platforms etc...

3D asset generation is getting massive amounts of attention, inspired by the recent success of text-to-image models. Deep learning approaches have shown promising results in generating 3D assets from textual descriptions or images; however, they still face challenges such as limited diversity and lack of control. To tackle these problems, we propose a novel framework that combines generative adversarial networks (GANs) with an autoregressive language model (ALM), named ALGM, which can generate diverse 3D assets with more control than existing methods. Our framework employs a novel architecture, consisting of a GAN for generating structural features and an ALM for generating semantic features. The generated semantic feature vectors are then used to guide the structural GAN.

Federated Learning (FL) is a machine learning (ML) approach that enables multiple decentralized users to jointly train an ML model without sharing their raw data. This way, the user privacy can be preserved while improving individual models and avoiding overfitting in centralized settings where all training samples are stored at one central server [1]. In FL scenarioes there exists two types of participants: federate agents(FAs), who conduct computations on local devices or servers; global coordinators/aggregators which aggregate results from FMs into updated weights for next round by aggregating partial updates performed locally during previous rounds until convergence has been reached - this process usually involves sending out requests periodically as well receiving back responses conta

Masked image modeling (MIM), which predicts randomly masked patches from unmasked ones, is a critical component of large-scale foundation models. Despite its importance, we find that the existing MIM training methods suffer from serious limitations in terms of efficiency and model quality. In this paper, we present DualMIM, a novel self-supervised MIM method with a dual-view design. Specifically, we introduce an image reconstruction branch for masked images to enhance the prediction accuracy on masked patches and thus further improve the overall performance. To mitigate the efficiency bottleneck caused by the dual-branch framework, we propose an efficient inference scheme to compute the reconstruction loss only once. Experimental results show that DualMIM outperforms state-of-the-art MIM m

The digital age introduced the Digital Ecological Niche (DEN), revolutionizing human interactions. DENs are social media platforms, which have altered the way people communicate with one another and their environment in a virtual world; they offer users opportunities to interact within an online community through content creation as well creating relationships between themselves by sharing personal information such photos or videos on public profiles that can be viewed publicly without restriction from third parties outside of your account' privacy settings only allowing access if you choose otherwise - making it easier than ever before! There has been much discussion around whether this new technology will help improve our lives for better purposes like education & health care services wh

Advanced life forms, sustained by the synergistic interaction of neural cognitive mechanisms, continual learning and social adaptation, are likely to emerge on Earth-like planets with similar geological, chemical, and astrobiological characteristics. The planet's evolutionary pathway may be influenced by its location in the habitable zone, resulting in distinct biogeochemical conditions that shape the emergence and development of intelligence. By studying the interplay between habitability, evolutionary trajectories, and the emergence of intelligent life, we can gain valuable insights into the potential distribution of advanced civilizations across the universe. This paper presents a comprehensive exploration of this topic, highlighting the role of planetary dynamics in fostering life and

In the highly competitive area of product marketing, the visibility of brand logos on packaging is a key factor in establishing consumer recognition and preference. The current research examines the impact of logo placement on product choice in retail environments. We investigated consumers' preferences for 3D or flat-on logos in three different contexts: at shelf level (eye level), at waist level, and at ceiling height. Consumers were also asked to rate their likelihood of choosing the products based on the logo design. Our findings suggest that the location of the logo does not significantly influence consumer choices, but the type of logo does. Flat-on logos are preferred by consumers due to their perceived higher quality and attractiveness compared to 3D logos. This preference is parti

Conditional independence is a key assumption in causal inference and feature engineering. Yet, in the presence of hidden confounders, standard methods can fail to learn conditional independence, leading to suboptimal performance. Assuming access to samples from the joint distribution of the variables, we propose OTClean, a data cleaning method to identify and remove hidden confounders that cause violations of conditional independence. OTClean constructs a optimal transport problem to match marginal distributions, and uses gradient-based optimization to transport data from the joint distribution to a "cleaned" joint distribution with reduced hidden confounding. We show that OTClean can effectively remove hidden confounders in synthetic data experiments, improving the performance of standard methods that assume conditional independence.

Offline goal-conditioned reinforcement learning (GCRL) aims at solving goal-directed tasks with limited data and no access to the reward function. In this paper, we propose a novel GCLR method that exploits an offloading policy network $\pi$ for improving sample efficiency of offline RL without requiring any additional information or samples from either $D_{0}$ ($i.\ e.$, prior dataset), oracle rewards ($\mathcal{R}^*$, if available in some form). The key idea is simple: instead letting our value model estimate action values directly on top all observed state actions sampled across multiple datasets $(e., D_T)$ , it also uses those provided by its sibling actor/offloader's recommendations when these are deemed more desirable than current ones based upon their estimated advantages using Q($

Diffusion models currently achieve state-of-the-art performance for many tasks in image synthesis. However, generating animated images with diffusion models is still an open problem. In this work, we propose the novel framework Pix2Gif which is able to generate animated GIFs directly from pixel inputs. Our method builds upon the recent advances in conditional diffusion models and incorporates two key ideas: (i) a motion-guided conditioning scheme to effectively communicate the temporal dependencies within the animated sequence, and (ii) a diffusion model with an explicit pixel-level attention mechanism to leverage the spatial dependencies. Our experimental results show that our proposed Pix2Gif surpasses all existing methods in terms of visual quality, while being more than 10 times faster than the previous approach.

The study analyses polarisation on Finnish social media with data from the platform X, which is a popular discussion forum. Based on this analysis and previous research into online forums, we suggest guidelines for moderation of X. We also discuss how the proposed moderating strategy could be used in other contexts. The guidelines are based on three principles: (1) the role of moderators should be to maintain order and ensure that everyone can participate freely; (2) moderators must act carefully and avoid interfering unnecessarily with the debate or taking sides; (3) there are situations where it is acceptable to delete posts if they violate the rules of debate. Our analysis shows that X has a high level of polarisation, with most users expressing strong opinions and showing little tolera

Owing to the increase in 'certified' phishing websites, there is a steady increase in demand for anti-phishing tools. However, the existing solutions are either too complex or lack the necessary features. To address this problem, we propose a new approach based on machine learning that can detect phishing URLs with high accuracy. We present an in-depth analysis of the datasets used in current solutions and identify their limitations. Additionally, we provide a detailed explanation of our approach, including data collection methods and feature selection techniques. Our experiments show that our proposed method has a higher detection rate compared to existing approaches while requiring less training time. This paper provides valuable insights into the potential of using machine learning for

We study the algorithmic problem of sparse mean estimation in the presence of adversarial outliers. Specifically, given $n$ points in $\mathbb{R}^d$, we aim to estimate their mean vector while minimizing the number of estimated data points that lie outside a prespecified distance from the true mean (i.e., the ``outlier'' region). This problem arises naturally in applications such as robotics and computer vision where it is important to accurately identify and reject samples that deviate significantly from the overall distribution, due to factors like sensor noise or environmental variations. In this paper, we provide the first constant-factor approximation algorithms for this problem when the adversary has access to only two noisy samples from the true mean. Our techniques are based on rec

Feature selection aims to identify the most pattern-discriminative feature subset. In prior literature, filter (e.g., backward elimination) and embedded (e.g., Lasso) methods have hyperparameters (e.g., top-K, score thresholding) and tie to specific models, thus, hard to generalize; wrapper methods search a feature subset in a huge discrete space and is computationally costly. To transform the way of feature selection, we regard a selected feature subset as a selection decision token sequence and reformulate feature selection as a deep sequential generative learning task that distills feature knowledge and generates decision sequences. Our method includes three steps: (1) We develop a deep variational transformer model over a joint of sequential reconstruction, variational, and performance

Motion segmentation from monocular video is a crucial task in many computer vision applications. It is typically achieved through the detection of motion magnification, usually by subtracting the current frame from its neighboring frames. However, choosing the optimal neighborhood size for motion segmentation is not a straightforward task and greatly affects the performance of the segmentation algorithm. In this work, we propose a unified model selection technique for spectral clustering based motion segmentation that utilizes information from all frames in a video sequence. Our proposed technique segments motion from a fixed number of selected frames, then uses a recently developed spectral clustering algorithm to obtain motion segments. This step is followed by a novel model selection procedure that chooses the optimal segmentation parameters, including the optimal neighborhood size, by evaluating the compactness and the separation of the obtained segments. Finally, the non-motion segments are removed, and the motion segments are refined by performing low-rank approximation on each segment. Our experiments show that the proposed method produces state-of-the-art results on the DMVR and MVS motion segmentation datasets.

Feature selection prepares the AI-readiness of data by eliminating redundant features. Prior research has demonstrated that feature selection improves model performance, but it can overlook important features, and most existing methods are designed for classification tasks. This paper introduces a new method to improve feature selection, called adaptive multi-level feature selection (AMFS). AMFS adopts an adaptive learning strategy to optimize the feature selection process in a two-step manner: 1) selecting the number of features based on their relevance; 2) optimizing which features to be selected from the initial set of features. Unlike conventional methods that use fixed thresholds or simple heuristics to select features, AMFS utilizes a deep neural network (DNN), which is capable of ef

Semantic segmentation of high-resolution remote sensing imagery (HRSI) suffers from two challenges: the inference time is prohibitively slow due to the large input size, and the training process requires a tremendous amount of annotation data. To alleviate these issues, we introduce SemiSegNet, a novel semi-supervised learning framework for HRSI semantic segmentation. We propose an information-aware self-training strategy that utilizes unlabeled samples with sufficient confidence to generate pseudo labels. This approach improves model generalization and reduces the need for manual annotations during training. Additionally, we introduce a lightweight encoder-decoder architecture with an attention mechanism to handle the large input size without sacrificing performance. Our experiments demon

We present SynCode a novel framework for efficient and general syntactical decoding of code with pre-trained language models (PLMs). Our method first uses a PLM to generate code from a prompt containing only the function name, input data types, and output data type. Then, we use a code graph matching algorithm to find the correct code snippet from the generated code. Finally, we propose an adaptive optimization approach that can be used as a plug-and-play module in other code generation systems. To evaluate our system, we conduct experiments on 10 datasets spanning 8 different programming languages, 24 programming tasks, and 38 code generation models. On all three aspects of performance, i.e., accuracy, efficiency, and flexibility, our proposed methods outperform existing state-of-the-art

The advent of Large Language Models (LLMs) has advanced the benchmark in various Natural Language Processing (NLP) tasks. Inspired by such models, we propose a new methodology for training and evaluating NLP systems, called Counterfactual-aware Neural Networks (CN2). Unlike prior methods, our approach does not require explicit generation of counterfactuals or knowledge of the model's parameters. Instead, it exploits the fact that the model is trained to minimize an objective function that depends on its predictions: changing these predictions changes the objective function and can be used to assess the model's confidence level. We show that CN2 allows us to generate counterfactual examples automatically and to evaluate their effectiveness at improving the performance of LLMs.

Many previous studies have proposed various warm-start methods for hyperparameter optimization (HPO), assuming that the underlying optimization algorithm has a good chance of finding the optimal hyperparameters if it is provided with a good starting point. However, this assumption does not necessarily hold, and in this paper, we show that a simple random search (RS) can outperform many previous warm-start methods in HPO. To this end, we first empirically examine several properties of the warm-start methods, and observe that they share a common property, i.e., the probability of finding the optimal hyperparameters decreases exponentially with the number of evaluations. Based on this property, we propose a simple encoder-based warm-start method, called Exponential Encoding (EE), which can effectively improve the performance of the underlying optimization algorithm. Unlike many previous studies that require additional hyperparameters, we show that EE can be applied to any optimization algorithm without additional hyperparameters. We conduct extensive experiments using 40 benchmark machine learning datasets that compare 12 state-of-the-art HPO methods, including both surrogate-based and random search-based methods. Our results show that EE consistently outperforms all the other 11 state-of-the-art HPO methods in terms of the optimization performance and the computational efficiency.

LLMOps incur significant costs due to hardware requirements, hindering their widespread adoption. Existing frameworks for offloading LLM computation are primarily designed for edge devices, making them less efficient on traditional CPUs and GPUs. This paper introduces LLOps: a unified framework for offloading the computation of LLMs onto accelerators. Our evaluation shows that LLM execution with LLOps can achieve up to 10x speedups compared to CPU and GPU baselines, while maintaining comparable accuracy. Additionally, we evaluate our system on a real-world scenario where a mobile robot is required to continuously process sensor input using an LLM. The results show that LLOps enables such a robot to perform continuous processing at an average speed of 47.2 Hz, demonstrating its effectivenes

Mobility-on-demand (MOD) services have the potential to significantly improve the efficiency of urban transportation and reduce emissions. However, their adoption will be limited if users do not perceive them as convenient or affordable enough. In this paper, we investigate how to design MOD services that satisfy user needs and preferences, while also being economically feasible for operators. We model MOD services by combining demand forecasts with dynamic vehicle routing. For both the demand and routing models, we identify parameters that are critical for the service design and deployment. Using a case study in Milan, we use these results to illustrate how to design an optimized MOD system and show how it compares to existing public transport systems. Our results highlight some critical

Tools are essential for large language models (LLMs) to acquire up-to-date knowledge. Existing methods require a large amount of human effort, and do not guarantee that the LLM is up-to-date with respect to new events or topics. In this paper, we present UpDated, an automated method for updating LLMs by leveraging both open web resources and closed-source software repositories. It automatically extracts software version information from web pages using a pre-trained LLM and a custom model, and updates the LLMs based on the extracted versions. Evaluation results show that UpDated can efficiently update the LLMs in a fully automated manner without requiring any human intervention. When applied to PaLM2, it improves the performance of downstream tasks such as question answering and code gener

Hallucinations pose a significant challenge to the reliability of large language models (LLMs). In this paper, we propose a novel method for detecting hallucination in LLM-generated text. Our approach leverages the capability of LLMs themselves by harnessing their ability to generate explanations for their own predictions. The key insight is that LLM-generated explanations exhibit unique characteristics when they are hallucinating compared to when they are not. To exploit this, we introduce an Explanation Augmented Model (ExAM), which incorporates an LLM's explanation generation capabilities into its decision process. ExAM first generates explanations for its prediction and then assesses the plausibility of these explanations using another LLM, leading to a more reliable final decision. We

Deep learning (DL) has been a common thread across several recent techniques for vulnerability detection. The aim of this study is to evaluate the effectiveness and performance of DL-based methods in detecting software vulnerabilities. We compare two popular DL-based methods, Convolutional Neural Networks (CNN) and Recurrent Neural Networks (RNN), by evaluating them on five public datasets that contain binary vulnerable or non-vulnerable files. Our results show that CNNs outperform RNNs with an average accuracy of 97% when classifying vulnerable files. Additionally, we demonstrate how the inclusion of various features can improve accuracy in detecting vulnerabilities, including size, MD5, SHA256, CRC32, and MD4. Our findings highlight the potential of deep learning as an effective approach

The curvature of the loss landscape is believed to play an important role in determining generalization properties of neural networks. In this work, we focus on the local curvature, i.e., the curvature around a certain point on the loss landscape, which is determined by the particular choice of parameters. We show that for artless initial conditions, i.e., initial conditions that do not exploit any symmetries of the loss landscape, the local curvature at the initial parameters is sufficient to predict generalization. In particular, we prove that for overparameterized neural networks and certain architectures, the local curvature at artless initial conditions is lower bounded by a positive constant, implying that generalization is guaranteed. Moreover, we show that for these models, bad minima have vanishing local curvature at artless initial conditions, which provides a possible explanation for why they are hard to find. Our results rely only on curvature properties of the loss landscape and do not rely on the specific choice of data distribution, implying that our findings can be applied to a wide range of problems.

Federated learning (FL) is a popular distributed learning paradigm. Existing industrial and academic FL deployments typically assume that the training data is homogeneous across all participants. As a result, the majority of FL algorithms are vulnerable to data heterogeneity in practice, which undermines their performance and stability. However, the root causes of data heterogeneity and its impact on FL are not well understood, especially in mixed-cloud settings where the effect is most severe. To address these limitations, we introduce a framework called HeteroSwitch, which characterizes and mitigates system-induced data heterogeneity in FL. HeteroSwitch consists of two components: 1) a data heterogeneity detector that identifies the source and degree of data heterogeneity, and 2) a data heterogeneity tamer that applies dynamic data processing pipelines to homogeneous and heterogeneous participants. Compared to existing methods, HeteroSwitch is more effective at addressing data heterogeneity from cloud-to-cloud, device-to-device, and participant-to-participant variations. Moreover, HeteroSwitch is robust against adversarial attacks and privacy-aware.

A common subgraph of two graphs $G_1$ and $G_2$ is a graph with vertex set the intersection between that in each, whose edges are those shared by both. This paper considers the problem to efficiently find all these subgraphs for any given pair $(V( G), E (H))$, where $\mathcal{D}=\{(W_{i},E _{j})\}$ denotes their edge lists such as adjacency matrices or sets obtained from an input file using standard programming language functions like numpy's loadtxt(). The most recent work on this subject proposes fast algorithms based upon dynamic trees but requires pre-processing time exponential wrt $| \bigcup W _ i |$.

Autonomous driving systems (ADS) aim to provide safe and comfortable transportation services. However, like any other complex software systems, ADS can fail, which could result in severe consequences. Fail-operational control (FOC) is a control strategy that aims to keep the vehicle driving safely when an ADS failure occurs. To apply FOC in practice, we need to monitor the state of the ADS, detect failures, and adjust the driving strategy accordingly. Bayesian learning (BL) is a powerful tool for inference under uncertainty, and it has been used in many engineering applications. Incremental Bayesian learning (IBL) is an online variant of BL that updates the posterior distribution as new data become available. In this paper, we propose a framework called Incremental Bayesian Fail-Operational Learning (IBFOL) that applies IBL to FOC. We use a Kalman filter (KF) to estimate the state of the vehicle and apply IBL on top of the KF to estimate the probability of failure of each ADS component.
