To address these challenges, we propose Gradient Low-Rank Approximation (GaLore), a memory-efficient LLM training method that does not require model architecture modification and supports training with a large batch size on a single machine. Specifically, we approximate the gradient with a low-rank matrix factorization that can be computed and stored in memory, avoiding the need to accumulate gradients. We conduct experiments on GPT-3 1.3B to GPT-3 13B with a batch size of up to 512 on a single A100 GPU. Our results show that compared to the state-of-the-art memory-efficient solution with weight pruning, GaLore achieves comparable training accuracy while reducing the memory footprint by 2.6x to 7.2x. Further, GaLore enables faster training time per epoch, by up to 1.4x, due to reduced memory access time.

In this paper, we take a step towards a deeper understanding of learning from human preferences by systematically comparing the paradigm of reinforcement learning from human feedback (RLHF) with the recently proposed paradigm of direct preference optimization (DPO). We focus our attention on the class of loglinear policy parametrization and linear reward functions. In order to compare the two paradigms, we first derive minimax statistical bounds on the suboptimality gap induced by both RLHF and DPO, assuming access to an oracle that exactly solves the optimization problems. We provide a detailed discussion on the relative comparison between the two paradigms, simultaneously taking into account the sample size, policy and reward class dimensions, and the regularization temperature. Moreover

This specification document specifies the syntax and semantics of QRtree, which is a specific dialect of C++ that has been designed for fast parsing and static analysis. The aim of this language is to simplify parsing and make it possible to build large-scale parsers easily. QRtree can also be used as a generic AST format for different parsers and compilers. This language is inspired by the Pushdown Automaton approach, but instead of using stacks, we use binary trees. Thanks to this unique approach, we have developed an innovative parser generation algorithm that is based on the QRtree grammar rules. The generated parser code is then converted into C++ code using the QRtree compiler. As a result, our parser generator produces parsers that are up to 10 times faster than LLVM parsers.

Permutation invariance is among the most common symmetry that can be exploited to simplify complex problems. This paper focuses on two-dimensional (2D) permutation-invariant networks, which typically rely on the use of 1D convolutional filters and pooling operations. However, 2D convolution has a natural permutation-invariant property due to its invariance to cyclic shifts. We show how to take advantage of this fact by introducing an efficient and parameter-free permutation-equivariant (PE) block that enables the direct application of 2D convolutions. Our PE-block does not require any modifications to existing architectures or training procedures and can be easily inserted into any PE network. Experiments on benchmark datasets demonstrate that our approach outperforms the state-of-the-art

Reframing a negative into a positive thought is at the crux of several cognitive approaches to depression. In recent years, there has been a surge in the use of artificial intelligence (AI) tools to assist humans with this task, which has generated interest in understanding whether these tools can actually reframe thoughts in ways that are beneficial for mental health. This study presents the first systematic review and meta-analysis investigating the effectiveness of AI systems designed specifically for mental health interventions for reframing. We conducted a search on 20 databases using the terms "artificial intelligence" and "mental health," along with "reframing." This search resulted in a total of 65 studies, out of which 18 were excluded based on criteria such as language, type of s

Motion graphic (MG) videos are effective and compelling for presenting complex concepts through an interesting blend of text, visuals & music. At Digital Infinitum we provide a range or motion graphics solutions which include animated content creation to video editing services based on the needs at hand using industry-standard software such as Adobe After Effects/ Photoshop / Illustrator etc.. Our team consists not only experts in digital marketing but also talented designers who can bring your ideas into reality with creativity; whether it is creating 3D models from scratch all way down till animation stage -we got you covered! We use various techniques like keyframing , morphing textures along side other types including stop action style where there's no movement between frames while ma

With the advancement of large language models, language-based forecasting has recently emerged as a promising direction in AI. However these existing approaches are limited to time series with short horizons and often do not generalize well across different tasks or domains because they rely on handcrafting features that require domain expertise for each application context (e.g., climate data). In this work we propose an end2end approach called LLM4Forecast which utilizes autoregressive LLMs trained from raw text input without requiring any feature engineering by exploiting multiple self attention heads at various temporal resolutions during prediction horizon selection process instead relying solely upon one single head throughout entire forecast sequence generation procedure similar cur

Personality traits influence human reasoning, yet the impact of personality on the reasoning of large language models (LLMs), which are prone to social biases, is unclear. To this end, we develop PHAnToM, a framework to investigate the interplay between personality and theory-of-mind (ToM) reasoning in LLMs. We find that personality traits of LLMs affect their reasoning in ToM problems. Specifically, LLMs with certain personality traits are more likely to reason correctly. Our study is a first step towards understanding the complex landscape of personality in LLMs and its impact on reasoning.

Recent years have witnessed many successful applications of machine learning in real-world settings. A key factor in its success is the development of efficient optimization algorithms for solving large-scale machine learning problems. In this work, we study a class of smoothness properties, called directional smoothness, which hold for many loss functions in machine learning. We show that the popular gradient descent algorithm, as a function of the number of iterations, can converge at a linear rate under directional smoothness. Moreover, we prove that gradient descent is adaptive to directional smoothness in the sense that the convergence rate depends on a smoothness ratio, a property that holds for standard smoothness but not the other way around. Our results recover the convergence of gradient descent for standard smoothness when the smoothness parameter is small. Numerical experiments on deep learning applications demonstrate the existence of directional smoothness in practice and the adaptivity of gradient descent.

The third ML4H symposium was held in person on December 10, 2022 in the MIT Samberg Conference Center. The event brought together researchers and practitioners from academia, industry, government and civil society to discuss recent advances and future directions of the field. The morning sessions featured keynotes by Tina Eliassi-Rad (University of Washington), John Guttag (MIT) and Regina Barzilay (MIT). They were followed by presentations of work by three graduate students: Arjun Saha (Rice University), Cody Bender (Georgia Institute of Technology) and Xuan Chen (Harvard University). In addition, several panels addressed cutting-edge topics such as AI and healthcare equity, AI tools for medical professionals, AI safety, and challenges in applying AI methods to real-world data sets.

In the domain of image layout representation learning, the critical process of translating image layouts into succinct representations has emerged as a significant challenge. This is due to the limited availability of explicit layout annotations and the inherent complexities of image layouts. However, existing methods are unable to address these challenges effectively, resulting in suboptimal performance. To tackle this issue, we introduce Layout-2Rep: a novel approach that addresses two key limitations by 1) learning from unannotated images using self-supervised training and 2) incorporating semantics using contrastive learning. Our method employs a Siamese architecture to learn compact yet descriptive representations for image layouts through self-supervised pretext tasks like triplet lo

Time series forecasting attempts to predict future events by analyzing past trends and patterns. Although long short-term memory (LSTM) has achieved remarkable success in time series prediction, its performance is heavily influenced by the input data, such as noise and outliers. Therefore, it is crucial to develop robust LSTM models that can effectively address these issues and improve their accuracy across a wide range of datasets. In this paper, we propose an enhanced LSTM model named LSTM-HMFNet for time series forecasting. The proposed method first utilizes a Hidden Markov Model (HMM)-based feature extractor to capture the temporal information present in the data. This HMM extracts valuable features from the sequence data by using hidden states as the contextual variables. Subsequently

We present ProTrix, a natural language processing system that answers questions about the contents of tables in a factored, modular fashion. Our approach disentangles the tasks of table processing and language understanding by first extracting table-relevant sentences from the question and then using these sentences to guide the extraction of relevant information from the table. We introduce a novel combination of neural network models for extracting sentence-contextualized embeddings and show that our method outperforms models that do not condition on the table context. We evaluate ProTrix on the SQA dataset, where it obtains a top-5 accuracy of 79.6% on the dev set and 74.3% on the test set. On the difficult no-table subset, ProTrix achieves a top-5 accuracy of 67.2% on the dev set and 59.4% on the test set. These results show that ProTrix is able to use the contextual information from the question sentences to improve the extraction of relevant information from tables.

In this work, we focus on enhancing generative dialogue systems with knowledge awareness and coherent topic modelling. We propose a novel two-stage approach called Contextual Topic Transition (CET2). At the first stage, we develop a novel topic transition modelling approach that is specifically designed for knowledge-awareness and coherence in dialogue. The approach identifies the topic of each dialogue act and models the transitions between topics in consecutive acts. It thus ensures topic coherence within multi-act conversations and can even predict the next topic given the current topic and context. In the second stage, we integrate CET2 with a state-of-the-art knowledge-aware dialogue generation model. This not only enhances the dialogue coherence by enforcing topic transitions but also improves the engagement of the conversation by modelling the contextuality of topics. Our experimental results demonstrate the superiority of CET2 over strong baselines in terms of both automatic and human evaluation. The code is also publicly available.

In the era of personalized education, the provision of comprehensible explanations for learning recommendations becomes increasingly important. We propose a novel framework for generating detailed explanations with respect to a recommended course. The framework is based on an iterative process which relies on a multimodal reasoning mechanism: (1) the system retrieves a set of relevant courses from a recommendation model and extracts a course description; (2) the system generates a course explanation by summarizing the course description, highlighting key concepts, and contextualizing them with appropriate examples and background information; (3) the system presents the user with the generated explanation and assesses their feedback; and (4) if the user provides negative feedback, the syste

Large Language Models (LLMs) are known to produce responses that are factually incorrect, grammatically inaccurate, or deviate from the user's intent. Removing a model from inference once such errors are detected is one way to improve the quality of LLM inference. However, error detection is not always straightforward, especially when the error is subtle or the model's response is partially correct. In this work, we propose a framework for LLM inference based on metric-aware selection. We compute metrics on the model's response and use these metrics to decide whether the response is good enough to be presented to the user or if it should be removed from inference. We experiment with different metrics (e.g., BERTScore, METEOR, etc.), different metric thresholds, and different ways to combine metrics (e.g., majority vote, best metric, etc.). We show that our framework is better than error detection and removal for four commonly used LLMs (GPT-3, GPT-4, GPT-3.5, and BLOOM).

The widespread presence of offensive languages on social media has resulted in adverse effects on individuals, communities, and society at large. While existing systems have been successful in detecting offensive language, they have fallen short in identifying the specific group targeted by this abuse (e.g., race or gender), which is a key element of hate speech detection. Existing systems use lexicons to identify offensive words but lack the capacity to accurately predict the target group, resulting in low model performance. In this paper, we propose a novel transformer-based multi-task architecture called Multi-Target Hate Speech Classifier (MT-HSC) for multi-class hate speech classification. We propose an effective fusion method that combines two different features obtained from separat

To achieve cost-efficiency in distributed stream processing systems (DSPS), the resource utilization of the underlying infrastructure needs to be maximized while meeting the required service level agreements (SLAs). This requires dynamically adjusting the horizontal scale of the DSPS in response to both the capacity planning of the system as well as any unexpected spikes in the workload. Existing scaling solutions for DSPS are either focused on minimizing the response time of the system, or ensuring that the SLA is met at the minimum cost. However, minimizing the response time may not be feasible when the required SLA is very tight, and the cost optimization may not be achievable when the horizontal scale of the DSPS is close to its upper limit. Hence, a self-adaptive horizontal autoscaling solution for DSPS that considers the resource efficiency is needed. We propose Daedalus, a self-adaptive horizontal autoscaling solution for DSPS that uses a two-level scaling approach. The first level consists of a global scaling decision maker that maintains a set of candidate scales for the DSPS based on the current workload information. The second level consists of individual task schedulers that evaluate the resource utilization of the candidate scales and make the final scaling decision. Daedalus uses a novel SLA negotiation algorithm to ensure that the required SLA is met without incurring unnecessary costs. We evaluate Daedalus by conducting experiments on a real-world DSPS and show that it is able to improve the resource efficiency of the system while meeting the required SLA.

Exercise-based rehabilitation programs have proven to be effective in enhancing the recovery of individuals with knee osteoarthritis (OA) and reducing their pain. However, some patients experience a plateau in symptom improvement during these exercises after an initial reduction or relief from symptoms following exercise initiation; this phenomenon is termed “exercise plateaus.” The aim was thus twofold: first investigate if there are differences between groups that reach versus do not achieve full 360° range motion at end point measurements when performing knee flexion/extension movements using a goniometer (“active” group vs non-“actively reaching maximum extension”) and second examine whether those who achieved maximal active movement had better pain outcomes compared against baseline d

The integration of deep learning, particularly AI-Generated Content, with high-quality data derived from the Internet has allowed for significant advances in fields such as Natural Language Processing (NLP), Computer Vision and other forms. This means that developers can use existing datasets to quickly create models tailored specifically towards their desired application or task without having any prior knowledge about its workings—a huge advantage over traditional methods requiring months worths’ time investments! Additionally these tools often come equipped ready outbox meaning you won't need much additional effort when getting started either which makes them even more attractive options overall . As a result we see an increasing trend where companies rely on generative content generati

Long Time Series (LTS) forecasting is a crucial task in many fields. Transformers have recently been introduced into this field and have shown strong performance. However, there is still a need for improvement, especially for forecasting long-term dependencies and multivariate data. In this paper, we propose a novel Transformer-based model called InjectTST, which is designed to effectively inject global information into independent channels. This is achieved through a channel-wise attention mechanism and a global information propagation sub-layer. The channel-wise attention mechanism identifies the degree of relevance between the target channel and other channels, thereby enabling the effective transmission of global information. The global information propagation sub-layer, on the other hand, ensures that the global information is uniformly distributed across all channels. Our proposed model is tested on six real-life LTS datasets and has shown strong performance, especially in the prediction of long-term dependencies and multivariate data. It has also demonstrated competitive results in comparison with other state-of-the-art baselines.

We study the problem of procedure planning in instructional videos, which aims to make a goal-oriented plan for robotic manipulation tasks based on video instructions. In particular, our approach is geared towards robots that are controlled by speech commands and follow the actions specified in these instructions step-by-step. As a result, we address the important challenge of aligning actions with natural language commands (e.g., "pick up" and "place"). Our key contribution is a novel methodology that combines vision-language modeling with procedural reasoning. In addition, we introduce an efficient algorithm to generate plans that can be executed in real-time. Through extensive experiments using a range of datasets, we demonstrate the effectiveness of our approach across diverse domains

This paper introduces OccFusion, a straightforward and efficient sensor fusion framework for predicting 3D occupancy maps from observations of lidar scans. The method is based on an EKF formulation that allows fast propagation in the world frame with accurate pose estimation via SLAM approaches as well dynamic prediction by modeling interactions between different layers representing distinct points-in time (or frames). A key feature introduced here are adaptive layer selection techniques which enable more effective usage across multiple scales depending upon local conditions such density or complexity; this has been shown to significantly improve performance compared against baseline models using traditional fixed size settings alone while still maintaining low computational costs relative

Reddit has become a popular platform for financial discussions, including posts about financial products and user questions seeking advice on those products. Unfortunately, the majority of financial products discussed on Reddit are scams designed to steal money from unsuspecting users. In this work, we develop a natural language processing-based system to analyze textual content and user interactions on Reddit to identify potential scams and extract insights about them. Our system analyzes 80,251 posts from 30,177 users on the "ask" and "investing" subreddits that mention 17,946 unique financial products, detecting 2,096 scam posts from 1,178 unique scams with a 0.67 precision and 0.78 recall. Our results show that natural language processing techniques can help identify and characterize financial scams on Reddit, providing insights for further investigation by financial regulators.

Aerial robots show significant potential for forest canopy research and environmental monitoring by providing data collection capabilities that are not easily accessible using traditional methods. However, most aerial robotics systems in forestry have focused on the development of hardware and data processing techniques to improve data acquisition efficiency. While such efforts are essential, they may overlook the challenges of accurately interpreting the vast amounts of collected data. This paper proposes an integrated pipeline approach, which combines hardware design, machine learning (ML) algorithms, and software architecture, to enhance aerial robotics systems for forest canopy analysis. The proposed methodology consists of five stages: target detection, tree segmentation, tree height

Despite the vast repository of global medical knowledge predominantly being in English, local languages are crucial for patient care. We present a novel methodology for automatic translation of multilingual biomedical text into Spanish using a bilingual encoder-decoder architecture. Our approach incorporates an attention mechanism and an iterative decoding strategy that allows for the integration of external information such as dictionaries, monolingual corpora, or cross-lingual embeddings. In addition to evaluating our model on various benchmarks, we conducted experiments with human raters and found it to be comparable to professional translators and machine translations based on Google Translate. Finally, we provide code and data available at https://github.com/jhermosillo/biomedic-spani

In this paper, we consider a robot that navigates in a dynamic environment, where it is uncertain whether there are obstacles in unexplored regions. The robot receives partial observations, and it is able to choose between exploring these regions and navigating to a goal. We aim to maximize the robot's chance of reaching the goal by having it select optimal information gathering points. We formulate the information gathering problem as an MDP with exploration, and we propose a novel value-of-information-based policy. This policy extends classical value-of-information analysis to MDPs with exploration and shows promise in solving information gathering problems in dynamic environments. We show that the proposed policy is asymptotically optimal for discounted infinite-horizon MDPs. We present a sampling-based algorithm to compute the policy and show that the finite-sample performance matches the theoretical guarantees. We compare the proposed method with state-of-the-art information gathering algorithms in both simulated and real-world experiments. The proposed algorithm is demonstrated to be competitive in terms of navigation performance and ability to generalize over unseen obstacles.

Actionable Warning Identification (AWI) plays a pivotal role in improving the usability and accessibility of existing traffic warning systems. Large language models (LLMs), such as Llama-7B, have demonstrated great potential in generating AWI from visual data. However, the effectiveness of this approach remains inconclusive due to the limited availability of annotated datasets for evaluation purposes. In response to this gap, we introduce TrafficWarn, an open-source dataset featuring over 20,000 images and 14,569 videos depicting various road scenarios with corresponding captions. Our dataset is specifically designed to assess the efficacy of LLMs in AWI, covering diverse driving conditions, including adverse weather, congested roads, and vehicles' malfunction.

Legged robots are well-suited for broad exploration tasks in complex environments with yielding objects and obstacles. In this context, the task of planning an efficient path is challenging due to uncertainty about future states caused by the presence of obstacles as well as the fact that the robot may need to adapt its motion during execution because of physical limitations or other reasons. This paper introduces a methodology for safe and optimal path planning on a continuous grid world with dynamically changing obstacle locations. Our approach combines offline path planning with online obstacle avoidance based on the concept of an optimistic planner. This allows us to efficiently find safe paths through unknown regions and plan around obstacles even when their locations are not known be

Reframing a negative into a positive thought is at the crux of several cognitive approaches to well-being. A recent study by Stanford University psychologist, Carol Dweck, found that this "reappraisal" method can help reduce stress and anxiety. And yet, it seems like such an effort! So how do we make it easy? One way is to frame our thoughts with affirmations. Affirmations are positive statements we can repeat to ourselves in order to reframe a situation or event. They work because they have the power to change our neural pathways, creating new connections and patterns of thinking. Affirmations can be used for many different purposes, from improving self-esteem to overcoming challenges. But it takes more than just repeating words to see results; there must be intention behind them if you w

Perception is a key element for enabling intelligent autonomous navigation. Understanding the semantics of the environment is required to perform accurate and efficient path planning and decision making. In this work, we propose an approach that allows an autonomous system to reason about its surroundings by understanding the space it occupies and how objects are arranged within it. We introduce a new dataset composed of 240K images with 3D bounding box annotations in order to train our model. Our method uses a pre-trained detection network and a novel loss function based on the Hausdorff distance to generate a 3D representation of the scene and predict the layout of each object contained in it. This 3D map allows us to accurately determine how close a given object is from the agent, which

Methods for medical image registration infer geometric transformations that align pairs/groups of images by maximising an energy function. These methods are often based on either a least-squares or total variation cost function, each with their own set of advantages and disadvantages. In this work we investigate the performance of both registration types across a variety of tasks and datasets. We show that registration using total variation leads to better alignment in several cases; however, due to its non-convex nature, it is more sensitive to parameter choice and more prone to failure than methods based on least squares. Further, we demonstrate that by introducing regularisation terms into the total variation method, similar performance improvements can be achieved while maintaining a c

Value functions are a central component of deep reinforcement learning (RL). These functions, parameterized by a neural network, predict the expected future reward at each state. They serve as critical input to the policy network in RL and can be used for off-policy evaluation. However, existing value function algorithms fail to consider the true goal of offline RL: finding an optimal policy given only data from previous experiences. Instead, they typically rely on approximations that may not directly lead to good policies, such as using a uniform distribution over actions or relying on implicit biases in the agent's dynamics model. We introduce the first algorithm that explicitly optimizes a value function for use with a policy learned via imitation learning. Our approach, called Optimal

The instructional materials designers use play a crucial role in shaping teachers' instructional practices. However, the field lacks tools to analyze these materials in depth. In this paper, we address this gap by leveraging computer-assisted textual analysis to derive insights from instructional materials. We focus on a unique dataset of 1,017 units from 78 publicly available curricula for Brazilian primary education. After performing a thematic analysis of the units' texts, we used machine learning to predict the most relevant themes for each unit. Finally, we conducted a qualitative analysis to evaluate the extent to which the machine learning results aligned with our expectations. Our study produces two main contributions. First, we present a deep analysis of Brazilian primary education curricula, revealing insights about their focus on certain themes over others. Second, we show how computer-assisted analysis can support the production of educational materials by identifying potential gaps in the texts. We also discuss the limitations of our approach and suggest future improvements.

This paper presents an overview on virtual reality (VR) research at the Fraunhofer Institute for Graphics and Multimedia (IGD) in Darmstadt, Germany. We report on our contributions to fundamental VR research, including novel rendering algorithms for realistic image synthesis and efficient spatial data structures for large-scale scenes, as well as on selected applications, in particular for heritage documentation and training. Furthermore, we describe our VR setup and the development of a VR library. Finally, we briefly summarize the current developments in the field of VR.

Recent advancements in multimodal Human-Robot Interaction (HRI) have focused on improving human-robot communication through natural language and gestures. However, such approaches often overlook the crucial role played by non-verbal cues, such as facial expressions, eye gaze, and body posture, which can significantly impact the effectiveness of HRI. This paper introduces a novel methodology that leverages Multimodal Transformer Language Models (M-TLMs) to incorporate these non-verbal cues into a conversation. Our approach utilizes a pre-trained M-TLM model trained with large-scale multimodal datasets. To adapt it to specific HRI scenarios, we introduce a fine-tuning process that incorporates human-robot dialogues annotated with multimodal data. The resulting model shows improved performanc

Generalized Category Discovery (GCD) is a pragmatic and challenging open-world problem where the objective is to discover the categories of objects in an image, given their bounding boxes. The main challenge lies in how to model the distribution of object classes when there exists few training examples for each class. This paper presents GCD++, which leverages a novel unsupervised representation learning method to learn category representations, facilitating the discovery of object categories without any prior annotations or labels. We further introduce an efficient algorithm based on the nearest neighbor search to predict object category probabilities. Experiments on ImageNet, Caltech-256, and CUB show that our proposed GCD++ consistently outperforms existing methods under different exper

We consider optimal denial-of-service attacks against status updating, as performed by distributed ledgers such as Bitcoin and Ethereum. An attacker who wishes to disrupt the operation of a system performs denials-of-service against status updates, while a defender wishes to minimize the impact of these attacks. We model this as a stochastic game with incomplete information, since the attacker does not know which nodes are honest and which are dishonest. We show that the game has a value, and we compute it as the limit of the value of a related stochastic control problem with complete information. We use this characterization to design an approximation algorithm to compute the value of the stochastic game, and we use it to gain insight on the structure of optimal attacks and defenses. In particular, we show that optimal attacks against status updating are surprisingly gentle: the attacker never targets the same node twice, and the defender can always respond to an attack by updating the status using a single honest node.

New processor architectures are needed to address the memory wall challenge, the growing disparity between memory and CPU performance. Processing-Using-Memory (PUM) architectures, in which computation is performed in memory, offer promise in this regard. However, memory-based computing incurs significant data movement, necessitating efficient memory allocation and alignment support. Existing allocators are not optimized for memory-based computation and can incur excessive overhead due to memory misalignment. To address these challenges, this paper introduces PUMA, the first processor-independent memory allocation and alignment framework for PUM architectures. PUMA employs a hybrid allocation strategy, using a coarse-grain global allocator for large allocations and a fine-grain local allocator for small allocations. This approach minimizes data movement and alignment overhead while maximizing cache utilization. Furthermore, PUMA supports processor independence by designing the allocation algorithms to be processor-independent and by employing a novel processor-specific alignment algorithm that minimizes the average alignment overhead across all processors. Evaluation results show that PUMA reduces memory access time by up to 8X compared to existing memory allocation and alignment approaches, improving performance by up to 3X.

Large-scale optimization problems under capacity constraints are NP-hard and difficult to solve in a reasonable time using exact methods. On the other hand, approximate solutions can be found more easily, but the quality of the solution depends heavily on the accuracy of the underlying problem data. In this paper, we investigate the integration of symbolic AI and large-language models (LLM) to reason over the problem data represented as natural language text. Our case study focuses on vehicle routing problems with capacity constraints. We show that reasoning over the problem data represented as natural language text is similar to solving a constraint satisfaction problem (CSP), where the constraints are expressed in a multimodal fashion. We propose a novel multimodal prompt engineering approach that leverages natural language text and mathematical expressions to provide LLM with additional information about the problem constraints. We show that our approach improves the performance of LLM by providing it with a suitable bias, guiding the model to generate more accurate solutions. We evaluate our approach on the CVRP dataset and show that the integration of multimodal information significantly improves the performance of LLM, sometimes even outperforming specialized optimization algorithms.

Navigating confined 3D spaces, such as narrow corridors, is a challenging locomotion task even for humanoids with dexterous hands and feet. Maintaining stability while moving in such environments is challenging as there is a limited amount of space to maneuver, and thus, a small perturbation can lead to a large deviation from the desired trajectory. In addition, locomotion in these spaces is not only about moving the center of mass (CoM) forward; the legs also need to be manipulated carefully to avoid getting stuck. This paper proposes a deep reinforcement learning approach to learn a stable gait in narrow 3D spaces using a humanoid robot. We set up a table-top environment that resembles a narrow corridor and use a vision-based state representation. Our experiments show that the robot can learn to walk in this confined space without any prior knowledge about the environment. Furthermore, we show that the robot can generalize the learned behavior to unknown environments with different floor textures. This paper also investigates the effect of adding a reward-based exploration bonus to the state representation. Our results show that this exploration bonus helps the robot to discover better solutions faster. Finally, we evaluated the performance of our method in comparison with a baselines that uses a) a reward based only on the CoM position, b) a reward based on the CoM position and the leg states, and c) a reward based on the CoM position, leg states, and the robot's orientation. The experiments show that our method outperforms the baseline approaches in terms of navigation speed and robustness.

Off-policy learning methods, such as behavior regularization, sample efficiency, and robustness. To address these challenges, we propose Vlearn, a novel off-policy learning method that improves state-value function estimation efficiency, a key bottleneck in off-policy learning. Vlearn uses a state-action-value function to estimate the state-value function, leveraging action-value decomposition and off-policy training data. This approach not only enhances the efficiency of state-value function estimation but also overcomes the drawbacks of behavior regularization methods. We theoretically prove that Vlearn minimizes the mean squared error of the state-value function. Furthermore, we show that Vlearn exhibits superior performance compared to state-of-the-art methods on the D4RL benchmarks.

We consider fine-grained privacy guarantees for a ubiquitous class of optimization problems involving the minimization of a smooth objective function with a simple constraint set. Instances of this problem formulation appear in many machine learning applications, and have been studied in the context of private federated learning. We propose a generic black-box reduction from the above optimization problem to a differentiable privacy mechanism, which relies on a novel and efficient discretization of the constraint set. Our proposed method is able to recover the state-of-the-art fine-grained privacy guarantees for linear equality constraints, while at the same time allowing for nonlinear constraints. Furthermore, our method is able to achieve a finer granularity in the privacy parameter than what was previously possible. As a byproduct, we also obtain fine-grained privacy guarantees for the problem of differentially private stochastic optimization with simple constraints.

Federated learning (FL) is a promising approach for learning a model from data distributed on massive clients. It requires the design of an efficient protocol to aggregate multiple local models while preserving privacy. In this work, we present two novel FL algorithms and their formal analysis. The first algorithm is based on an additive secret sharing scheme that enables the server to aggregate local models without requiring the disclosure of their gradients or parameters. We show that our algorithm has optimal communication cost in terms of both bandwidth and number of rounds. Our second algorithm is based on the homomorphic encryption technique and also ensures the security of the model aggregation. We show that its computation complexity is linear with respect to the number of particip

The lack of transparency and explainability in how judges make decisions is a well-known problem in the judiciary system. To address this problem, we propose a novel approach to building predictive models for judicial decision-making. Our approach involves the following two steps. First, we develop a domain-specific natural language processing model to extract features from court judgement documents. Second, we build a prediction model for judicial decision-making based on the extracted features. We evaluate the prediction model using real-world judgements from the Thai court system on par with the latest approaches. The results show that our approach outperforms the latest approaches in predicting judicial decisions. We also conduct extensive experiments to investigate the effectiveness of each feature. The results show that the features extracted from the court judgement documents are effective in improving the predictive performance of the prediction model.

This paper develops a new filtering approach for state estimation in polynomial systems corrupted by arbitrary noise, which is based on the use of non-linear filters with explicit algebraic structure. The proposed method relies on solving an approximate linear matrix inequality (LMI) problem to design robust Kalman filter gains and, therefore requires no knowledge about either system uncertainties or disturbance characteristics other than those captured through LMIs itself; consequently it can be implemented even when little information regarding uncertainty modeling exists a priori .

In this paper, we analyze the susceptibility of micro aerial drones (MAVs) to different types of cyber attacks. We model jamming attacks and hijacking attacks and explore their effects on the communication between an MAV and its ground station. We consider both line-of-sight (LOS) and non-line-of-sight (NLOS) scenarios. For each scenario, we derive the probability of successful jamming attacks and hijacking attacks as a function of the distance between the attacker and the victim. We also discuss countermeasures to mitigate these attacks. We show that line-of-sight attacks are more vulnerable to jamming and hijacking attacks than non-line-of-sight attacks. We use real-world examples to discuss the practical implications of our results and show how an attacker can use these results to plan an attack. We also show how the susceptibility of MAVs to jamming attacks increases with the altitude of the target. We discuss the results in the context of both drone-to-drone and drone-to-ground station attacks.

Supervised fine-tuning (SFT), supervised instruction tuning (SIT) and self-supervised pretraining (SSP) have become the primary approaches for training large language models (LLMs). However, there is a tradeoff between accuracy and computational efficiency. In this work, we introduce a novel approach that achieves superior performance in both metrics: SFP. Our method leverages the intrinsic properties of LLMs by only requiring SFP training to achieve high accuracy on downstream tasks while significantly reducing computation. Additionally, we provide theoretical justifications supporting the use of SFP as an efficient alternative to SFT or SSP. We also showcase the potential of SFP in realistic settings by performing comprehensive experiments on multiple datasets. Experimental results demon

Adverse weather conditions often impair the quality of captured images, inevitably inducing cutting-edge autonomous driving (AD) algorithms to make suboptimal decisions. This paper proposes a novel approach that mitigates this limitation by introducing a self-supervised learning-based perception module to enable AD systems to perceive and act in adverse weather scenarios. The proposed module employs image translations as the training objective, leveraging a conditional diffusion model (CDM) to synthesize cleaner images from noisy inputs. Unlike previous approaches that rely on explicit labels or pre-trained models for image translation, our method learns to translate images directly from their pixel values without relying on external supervision. By incorporating both image and motion nois

Reward hacking is a serious concern in reinforcement learning (RL) and refers to the situation where an agent optimizes the reward function in a way that was not intended by the designer. For example, the agent may exploit a loophole in the reward function to enter an undesired behavior pattern that happens to result in a high reward. In this paper, we propose a novel anti-hacking approach based on regularization. Specifically, we propose occupancy measure regularization (OMR), which encourages the agent to visit the state-action pairs that are visited during the training of the behavior policy. We show that OMR acts as an implicit regularizer for the original reward maximization problem and prevents the agent from optimizing the reward function in an unintended way. We evaluate OMR on a set of benchmark tasks and observe that OMR effectively prevents reward hacking while maintaining the performance of the behavior policy.

The stability-plasticity dilemma is a major challenge in continual learning, as it necessitates the balancing of two conflicting goals: maintaining robustness to changes while enhancing performance. In this paper, we introduce the Stability-Plasticity Dilemma Problem (SPDP), which models the tradeoff between stability and plasticity by capturing the impact of different perturbations on the model's performance, stability, and ability to generalize across data distributions. We develop SPDP as an open-source research toolbox with comprehensive benchmark datasets and demonstrate its utility through extensive experiments using three popular continual learning algorithms: Grow-and-Learn (GaL), PatchMatch, and Simultaneous Pretraining and Fine-Tuning (SPFT).

Time-Sensitive Networking (TSN) is a recently proposed extension of Ethernet that guarantees determinism and low latency. A key component of TSN is the traffic policing function, which ensures that traffic conforms to the预订的带宽 guarantee. Current TSN traffic policing algorithms suffer from high memory consumption and/or inability to terminate unfair traffic flows. These shortcomings can be attributed to the use of inefficient data structures and algorithms, as well as a design that fails to exploit temporal locality. To address these issues, this paper proposes a novel traffic policing algorithm that employs a memory-efficient data structure, the time-ordered queue (TOQ), and a termination strategy based on token buckets. The TOQ exploits temporal locality to reduce memory consumption, while the token bucket mechanism ensures that unfair flows are terminated within a bounded time. Simulation results validate that the proposed algorithm achieves lower memory consumption and faster termination of unfair flows compared to existing algorithms. In particular, the memory consumption is reduced from 32 B per flow to 8 B per flow, and the average termination time of unfair flows is reduced from 14.8 s to 2.1 s.

Most continual segmentation methods tackle the problem as a per-pixel classification task. However, in real-world scenarios, we often encounter objects that have irregular shapes, such as clouds and crowds, which are challenging to model with pixel-wise predictions. To address this challenge, we propose an object-centric framework for continual semantic segmentation, named CoCogSeg, where we formulate the task of predicting object boundaries rather than class labels for each pixel. Our approach allows the segmentation network to learn from a variety of tasks involving different objects without catastrophic forgetting. Specifically, we introduce a novel boundary loss, which captures the relationships between object and background pixels, enabling the model to learn generalized object repres

Defocus blur is a persistent problem in microscope imaging that poses harm to pathological diagnoses and scientific research. However, it has not been tackled effectively in practice due to its complexity. To address this gap, we propose a novel algorithm called MICRO-DNN for defocusing microscope images using DNNs. This approach involves training a model from scratch or transfer learning and then performing post-processing with an ensemble of models to enhance the performance. Furthermore, we introduce three new training strategies based on augmentations such as rotation, random cropping, and zooming, which enhances both the performance and stability of the trained model. The proposed method yields significant improvements over the baseline, achieving average PSNR values ranging from 42.9

Supervised fine-tuning (SFT), supervised instruction tuning (SIT) and self-training have been applied for pre-trained language models (PLMs). However, it is unknown whether they are equivalent to each other. This paper presents a theoretical analysis on the equivalence of these three types of training methods. We show that SFT and SIT are exactly identical under some constraints when the dataset is large enough. Specifically, we derive the optimal solutions for both methods and prove that their loss functions are identical, which in turn leads to the same parameter updates. Moreover, we establish that self-training can be viewed as an approximation of SIT with a proper loss function. To further illustrate the differences among these three types of training methods, we present the relations

Fine-tuning language models in a few-shot setting is challenging. Few training samples and high similarity between training and testing samples make it difficult for the model to learn useful representations. We propose RIFF, a novel few-shot fine-tuning method that learns to rephrase inputs in a task-specific manner to improve the performance. RIFF consists of two components: a general rephrasing model and a task-specific rephrasing model. The general rephrasing model is a small neural network that maps the original input to a rephrased input. The task-specific rephrasing model is a token-level transformer that further refines the rephrased input for the specific task. We evaluate RIFF on a wide range of natural language understanding tasks, using different language models as the base model. RIFF consistently improves the performance of the base model in a few-shot setting. Compared with the recent few-shot fine-tuning methods, RIFF achieves comparable or better performance. Our code is available at https://github.com/自然语言处理实验室/RIFF.

This paper proposes the task of automatic assessment of Sentence Translation Exercises (STEs), which is an important component in English Language Learning. We present a novel approach to this problem by utilizing Neural Machine Translations, and evaluate our method on two real-world datasets collected from online language learning platforms: Duolingo ($10K$ samples) and italki ($\sim 25k$). To validate that such tasks are indeed useful for evaluating learner's performance across different levels we also conduct user studies with $437968^{th}$ grade school students who took part during the National Achievement Test administered annually at public schools throughout Thailand between March - April\textendash May~of~\year{}. Our results demonstrate strong correlations (\mbox{$r = . {\mathnor

Palms play an outsized role in tropical forests and are important resources for humans and wild animals. In the Neotropics, most palm species produce large-seeded fruits that can be eaten by a wide range of frugivorous mammals; however relatively few studies have examined seed dispersal patterns from palms with small seeds or fleshy fruit (ecotropes). Here we use camera trap data to examine mammal communities visiting five ecolopicaly distinct Astrocaryum sp., which produces very low volumes (<10) ripe red berries per year at each tree trunk base due its unique pollination system involving specialized male flowers on separate trees producing pollen balls carried back onto female trunks over distances exceeding several hundred meters within days after flowering events (~2x/year), thu

Evolutionary optimization algorithms are often derived from loose biological analogies and struggle to leverage information about the problem at hand. This is a significant challenge when using them for solving inverse problems, which can be challenging due in part because of high dimensionality as well their inherent stochastic nature that makes convergence hard-to analyze theoretically or predict empirically during training runs alone without any external guidance like human supervision via expert knowledge transfer learning approaches such those described here today!

We present WikiTableEdit, a new benchmark for table editing by natural language instruction. Our benchmark focuses on the edit-rich scenario where tables are modified by natural language instructions, which requires agents to (1) comprehend the instruction, (2) reason about the edits, and (3) construct the modified table. Our benchmark consists of 7,000 table editing questions collected from Wikipedia tables, covering 700 unique tables and 12 diverse categories. For evaluation, we require models to output natural language instructions that edit the tables. Our benchmark is the first large-scale, diverse benchmark that requires reasoning and construction for table editing by natural language instructions. We present a strong baseline based on our collected data and release a code package with our benchmark.

Multiplicative structures, in the form of linear logic proof nets, provide a semantics for non-deterministic and probabilistic programs that is both operational and compositional. In this paper, we propose a new reading of proof nets as programs, based on the notion of abstract machine. Our abstract machine can be instantiated in two different ways, corresponding to the two standard evaluation strategies for proof nets: call-by-name and call-by-value. We show that the resulting programming languages are able to express important properties of linear logic proof nets, such as the sub-formula property of cut-elimination and the preservation of resources. Moreover, we show that our languages are well-suited for reasoning about structures in the environment, as demonstrated by the specification of deterministic and probabilistic I/O automata.

Medical vision-language models (VLMs) combine computer vision and natural language processing to analyze visual data. However, due xray image classification is a challenging task in medical diagnosis since the images are often noisy or blurry with low resolution that contain small regions of interest(ROIs). To overcome these challenges , this paper proposes an efficient Medical Vision Language Model based on self supervised learning method by employing a novel architecture called Visual Transformer - Convolutional Neural Network Fusion Architecture for X Ray Images Classification . Our model outperforms existing state‐ofthe art methods across various datasets including ChestXRay14 dataset which includes 30295 chest radiographs from two different institutions as wellas RADS DB I Dataset wh

Image-to-video generation (IVG) aims to generate a short video sequence from a single static image, emulating the visual motion of real-world scenes. Existing methods achieve this by enhancing the static image with the help of motion cues from adjacent frames. However, motion cues can be noisy, especially in scenes with complex motion, leading to degraded video quality. In this paper, we propose a self-supervised noise rectification (SNR) approach to learn from the relationships between noisy motion cues and their corresponding clean labels to improve the accuracy of motion estimation and, consequently, the visual quality of generated videos. Specifically, we introduce a motion denoising module (MDM) to extract clean motion cues from noisy ones. To train the MDM, we adopt a self-supervised learning strategy that does not require paired clean-motion datasets. Instead, we leverage readily available optical flow datasets, making our approach easier to tune and less time-consuming to train. We then integrate the MDM with an existing IVG model, simply by replacing the model's original motion estimation module with our denoised motion module. Extensive experiments on four IVG benchmarks demonstrate that our method produces videos with higher perceptual quality, faster convergence, and better stability compared to existing techniques.

Regional solar power forecasting, which involves predicting the total power generation from all rooftop PV systems in a certain region over time, is important for effective renewable energy integration into the grid. Deep learning (DL) based methods have been widely used for regional solar power forecasting but are generally data-hungry and require large amounts of high-resolution solar radiation data. To address this challenge, we propose a novel regional solar power forecasting method based on deep graph neural networks (GNNs). Our approach involves constructing a graph to model the spatial relationships among solar panels within the same region, and then utilizing a deep GNN architecture to capture complex spatial dependencies between neighboring solar panels. Experimental results show

Existing approaches on zero-shot event detection usually train models on datasets annotated with known event types and then test them over unseen ones. However, such methods suffer from the following two major limitations: (1) they cannot leverage unlabeled data in real world scenarios where only a small number of labeled instances are available; 2), their performance drops significantly when testing against more distant events compared to those seen during training due primarily because there is no way for these systems learn about how best represent different kinds words or phrases across varying domains/contexts without having access any knowledge beforehand via prior annotations which can be costly process involving manual effort often repeated multiple times before reaching satisfacto

Multivariate time series anomaly detection is a crucial problem in many industrial and research applications. We present a novel method for multivariate time series anomaly detection, based on the combination of clustering and anomaly score-based detection methods. Our approach utilizes unsupervised clustering techniques to group similar anomalies together while simultaneously using an anomaly score to identify outliers within each cluster. The proposed method offers significant improvements over traditional approaches by incorporating both unsupervised learning techniques and anomaly score-based detection methods into a single framework. This leads to a more robust and accurate solution for multivariate time series anomaly detection. Evaluation results show that our method achieves a mean

With the great popularity of Graph Neural Networks (GNNs), their robustness to adversarial attacks has been gradually recognized. However, most existing GCN-based defense methods are focused on improving graph structure or embedding space by introducing additional information and computational overhead during training time without considering that some features have more impact than others in terms accuracy drop under attack which may lead them not effective enough for real scenarios where memory resources limited especially when dealing with large scale datasets like DBLP dataset consisting millions nodes/edges pairs(16M+). In this paper we propose a novel approach named DeepGuard - an endtoend system combining two different techniques: 1) A feature selection algorithm based upon Shapley

Many computer vision and machine learning problems are modelled as learning tasks on graphs, where graph neural networks (GNNs) are a natural choice for constructing models. In this paper, we investigate how to design GNNs that can learn efficiently from large graphs by leveraging structured knowledge about the input data. We propose a framework called Graph Adaptive Learning (GAL), which can be used to train GNNs in an efficient way through adaptively sampling and re-using subgraphs. The proposed method uses a graph-based attention mechanism based on the information bottleneck principle to guide sampling and re-use of subgraphs. Our experiments show that the proposed approach outperforms existing methods such as DeepWalk and node2vec in terms of scalability while maintaining competitive p

VPN adoption has seen steady growth over the past decade due to increased public awareness and concerns surrounding online privacy. As more users turn to VPNs for privacy protection, it is essential to understand their perception of security risks associated with using them. In this paper, we explore how people's perceptions of a VPN's security level affect their willingness to use it. We collected data from 238 participants via an online questionnaire that included questions regarding demographic characteristics as well as risk assessment regarding online activities and personal information sharing habits. Our findings indicate that higher levels of perceived security increase trust in VPNs, thereby encouraging greater usage among the general population. These results underscore the impor

Multi-Source-Free Unsupervised Domain Adaptation (MSFDA) aims to align a target domain with multiple source domains. To reduce the need for labeled data in the target domain, existing MSFDA methods use various types of auxiliary information from the source domains during adaptation. However, these approaches lack generalizability when the source domains are not similar to the target domain. In this paper, we propose a new approach called Dual-Learning-Based Adaptive Auxiliary Information Selection (DLAAIS). The key idea is to select appropriate auxiliary information from source domains by learning their mutual information and aligning it with that of the target domain. For this purpose, we introduce a novel self-attention mechanism based on mutual information (MI). It learns a distribution

Non-verbal signals in speech are encoded by prosody and carry information that ranges from conversation action (speech content) to emotion. The analysis of non-verbal information is crucial for understanding the meaning of a spoken message. However, the integration of non-verbal information in dialog systems remains understudied due to limited access to high quality data. In this paper we present a large-scale dataset with non-verbal annotations, covering 35k dialogs with 64k speakers. We describe how it can be leveraged for building dialog models equipped with emotional, conversational and action capabilities. Our approach integrates a wide range of non-verbal features such as tone, pitch, duration, and vocal intensity at both word and utterance levels. These features are combined with se

This paper presents significant advancements in the field of abstract reasoning, particularly for Raven's Progressive Matrices (RPM) and Analogical Reasoning (AR). We have developed an advanced AI system that can comprehend complex abstract concepts, including those found in Raven's Advanced Progressive Matrices (APM), AR, and other cognitive tests. The system utilizes state-of-the-art deep learning models to analyze test questions, evaluate answer choices, and provide feedback to users. Our system outperforms existing benchmarks on a variety of cognitive tests, demonstrating its capabilities in abstract reasoning. Furthermore, we introduce new innovations in data augmentation and question generation, enhancing our model's capabilities even further. Finally, we discuss future directions fo

Floods are among the most common and widespread natural disasters, affecting millions of people and causing billions of dollars in damage annually. Therefore, flood detection and monitoring is of great importance for disaster response and mitigation. Sentinel-1 (S1) is a satellite constellation that provides crucial information for flood detection through its Synthetic Aperture Radar (SAR) imagery. Unlike optical sensors, SAR imagery is not affected by clouds and can provide all-weather observations. Moreover, because each Sentinel-1 satellite orbits the Earth twice a day, repeated observations of the same location can be obtained within a short time period. This property is essential for detecting dynamic phenomena, such as floods, that may occur suddenly and evolve rapidly. Although temporal data are available, flood detection algorithms typically ignore the temporal dimension and use only single-date imagery. This paper demonstrates the need for including temporal data in flood detection algorithms by evaluating the performance of different machine learning models using single-date and multi-date Sentinel-1 imagery. For this purpose, we use a publicly available dataset that contains Sentinel-1 imagery of the 2021 floods in Germany. Our results show that flood detection performance can be significantly improved by including temporal data. Therefore, we conclude that temporal data are necessary for an accurate flood detection algorithm.

Recent studies have focused on enhancing the performance of 3D object detection models. Among them, one-stage methods achieve high speed and accuracy through post-processing. However, these methods require a large amount of information in the output of the backbone network to enhance localization accuracy. These extra features are unnecessary for the backbone network itself, which may increase model parameters and inference time. To address this issue, we propose a novel one-stage method called Localization-Aware Backbone Network (LABN). LABN integrates the localization head into the backbone network, effectively reducing the number of parameters and improving training efficiency. Moreover, LABN introduces a global feature branch that only outputs global features, thus eliminating the need

High-resolution (HR) land cover maps are crucial for many applications, but manually producing and maintaining them is prohibitively expensive. Hence, there is a strong motivation for automatic methods that can learn to classify HR images. However, most existing learning-based methods require exact class labels for training, and cannot utilize historical HR maps that only provide approximate labels. To overcome this problem, we propose a novel semi-supervised method that can utilize historical HR maps with approximate labels for training. The key idea is to guide the learning using a proxy task that is designed to produce predictions with the same approximate accuracy as the historical labels. To minimize the information loss, we choose the proxy task such that its output probability distribution is identical to that of the original task. This ensures that the updated model predictions also have the same approximate accuracy as the historical labels. We tested our method for updating large-scale HR land cover maps of the conterminous United States. For this purpose, we used the 2019 National Land Cover Database (NLCD) with 17 classes at 30-meter resolution as the historical HR map, and the 2021 NLCD at 100-meter resolution as the low-resolution guidance. Our results show that the proposed method outperforms existing approaches that use exact labels for guidance, while also reducing the information loss compared to re-training from scratch.

Time series forecasting attempts to predict future events by analyzing past trends and patterns. Although this is a well-studied problem, it is often difficult to know which model will perform the best for a given data set. This is due in part to the fact that there are many different types of models, each with its own strengths and weaknesses. In order to determine which model is most effective, it is necessary to compare their performance on a variety of tasks. One common approach is to use cross-validation, where a portion of the data is held out as a test set while the remaining data is used to train multiple models. The accuracy of each model can then be compared using the test set. Another popular method is to use machine learning techniques such as random forest or gradient boosting

As machine learning (ML) algorithms get deployed in an ever-increasing number of applications, they become critical components that shape the user experience. In many cases, the performance and robustness of these ML models are crucial to ensure high-quality outputs. However, identifying the root cause of model degradation remains challenging due to the complex and dynamic nature of ML models. Aiming to address this challenge, we present MILM, a novel approach for pinpointing faulty layers within ML models. This is achieved through the integration of multilayer feature extraction with a multi-layered attention mechanism that facilitates the identification of potentially faulty layers based on the degree of information sharing between them. Our experimental results demonstrate that MILM out

Modern software is often developed using Plugin Architectures of Integrated Development Environments (PAIDE). Examples of such IDEs are Eclipse and IntelliJ IDEA. In such an environment, code is edited using a set of plugins that provide various services (e.g., code completion, on-the-fly error checking, etc.). The plugins are usually developed by different developers who communicate with the IDE using a plugin API provided by the IDE. In this paper, we discuss an approach to processing data clumps, i.e., groups of data that are located close to each other and are often processed together, using PAIDE. We identify challenges that occur when processing data clumps within the plugin architecture of an IDE. To that end, we extend an architecture for analyzing the execution of plugins in an IDE with a novel trace-based method for capturing data clumps. We evaluate our approach by applying it to two case studies: processing XML documents using the XML plugin in Eclipse, and performing string manipulations using the regular expression plugin in IntelliJ IDEA.

Exercise-based rehabilitation programs have proven to be effective in enhancing the mobility and functionality of knee osteoarthritis (KOA) patients. However, existing research on the impact of exercise therapy on KOA has primarily focused on physical aspects such as pain and functional performance. While these studies are crucial, they overlook the potential influence of exercise interventions on the mental well-being of KOA patients, which is equally important. This review aims to explore the current literature on the effects of exercise therapy on psychological outcomes in KOA patients. We conducted a comprehensive search across five databases, including MEDLINE, Web of Science, EMBASE, CINAHL, and PsycINFO, using keywords related to exercise therapy for KOA and its associated psycholog

For robots to perform assistive tasks in unstructured home environments, they must learn and reason about the complex dynamics of their surroundings. Recent advances have shown that deep learning-based approaches can be leveraged for efficient perception systems such as object detection or semantic segmentation which are crucial components towards this goal. However these methods still fall short when it comes down on understanding how objects interact with each other over time e..g., grasping a cup from its handle without spilling any liquid inside because one has not yet learned what constitutes an appropriate grip location given different kinds/sizes etc... This project aims at addressing those challenges by developing novel algorithms capable off capturing long range dependencies betwe

In the past few years, adversarial robustness has become one of the most prominent problems in machine learning. It is now widely accepted that any practical ML application should be robust to small adversarial perturbations on the input data. A number of recent works, however, have shown that existing robustness bounds are provably suboptimal, leaving ample room for improvement. In this work, we first present new theoretical results that significantly improve the best-known bounds on the success of adversarial examples. Unlike most previous works, which considered the success probability under a specific threat model, we derive bounds on the success probability under a relaxed threat model that allows both white- and black-box attacks. Our bounds apply to both classification and regression problems and are based on novel and refined versions of the mutual information method. We show that these bounds are, in fact, achievable and present a practical algorithm for computing the robustness of a given model. We further extend our approach to the setting where the model parameters are themselves subject to adversarial perturbations, e.g., in the case of neural networks. Finally, we derive a general bound on the success probability for linear models, regardless of the threat model. We show that, in the case of Gaussian data, our bound is within a constant factor of the optimal bound.

In the information-theoretic analysis of dynamical systems, the concept of information aging has been introduced to characterize the decrease in the amount of information about the past states of the system contained in its present state. Specifically, for a discrete-time dynamical system $X_{t+1} = f(X_t)$ with $X_t \in \mathbb{R}^n$ and a probability distribution $\mu$ on its state space, the information aging function is defined as $I(t) = h(X_0|X_t)$, where $h(\cdot)$ denotes the entropy function and the conditioning is with respect to the distribution $\mu$. In this paper, we investigate the monotonicity of the information aging function. In particular, we give sufficient conditions on the system $f$ and the distribution $\mu$ under which $I(t)$ is a non-increasing function of $t$. Such conditions turn out to be satisfied by a wide variety of systems, including most notably all linear systems and all systems with invariant Gaussian noise. We also show that for general systems, one can construct a coupling between the processes $X_t$ and $Y_t$ with $Y_t = f(X_t)$ such that $h(X_t|Y_{t+1}) \geq h(X_{t+1}|Y_{t+1})$ for all $t$. This implies that the information aging function $I(t)$ is subadditive, and hence the long-term average information aging rate $\limsup_{t\to\infty} I(t)/t$ is well-defined. We further show that for a large class of systems, this average aging rate is equal to the normalized Lyapunov exponent of the linearized system, thus establishing a connection between information aging and the classical Lyapunov exponent analysis.

The rise of social media has facilitated the rapid spread of misinformation, posing a threat to individuals' ability to discern fact from fiction and to participate in civil discourse. Fact-checking, the verification of factual claims, is thus more important than ever, but fact-checkers are often overwhelmed by the volume of misinformation. In this work, we explore the use of language models for fact-checking support. In particular, we propose a novel multimodal approach that incorporates visual evidence into the fact-checking process. We introduce a vision-language pre-trained model to extract visual facts from images and a question-answering model to check if the visual facts are consistent with the textual claims. Our models can detect a wide range of visual misrepresentations, from altered images andP deepfakes to misleading contexts and stylized facts. In addition, we develop a novel multimodal training strategy that combines supervised and weakly-supervised training signals to improve the performance and reduce the reliance on labeled data. Our fact-checking system achieves state-of-the-art performance on news article fact-checking and is able to scale to images and videos.

The recent advancements in natural language processing and computer vision have enabled the development of multi-modal models for healthcare. These models incorporate multiple forms of data, such as images, text, and speech, to improve the accuracy and efficiency of medical tasks. Multi-modal models have the potential to transform healthcare by providing accurate, fast, and affordable medical report generation and visual question answering. In this survey, we review the current state-of-the-art methods for medical report generation and visual question answering using vision-language models. We present technical details of the standard methods and discuss their strengths and drawbacks. We also compare the performance of these models against each other and other baselines. Finally, we discuss the challenges faced by vision-language models in healthcare and suggest potential solutions.

Neural networks have become a cornerstone of machine learning. As the trend for these to get bigger, they are increasingly prone to overfitting and catastrophic forgetting. This paper presents a new method to regularize neural networks by introducing a novel architecture: a recurrent layer with an attention mechanism at its core. We call this the attention-recurrent (AR) block. The AR block is designed such that the hidden states of each neuron can be interpreted as a weighted sum of all input features. The weights, in turn, are computed using the softmax function. By adding an L2 regularization term on top of the weights, we show how this approach yields a regularized model. Experiments demonstrate that our approach outperforms standard approaches such as dropout or batch normalization wh

Automatic Text Summarization (ATS), utilizing Natural Language Processing (NLP) algorithms, aims to create concise and accurate summaries, thereby significantly reducing the human effort required in processing large volumes of text. ATS has drawn considerable interest in both academic and industrial circles. Many studies have been conducted in the past to survey ATS methods; however, they generally lack practicality for real-world implementations, as they often categorize previous methods from a theoretical standpoint. Moreover, the advent of Large Language Models (LLMs) has altered conventional ATS methods.

Spreadsheets are a ubiquitous and flexible medium for data manipulation and simple forms of reasoning, widely used for personal and professional tasks. Automating spreadsheet reasoning and manipulation promises to enhance productivity and accuracy. However, the task requires a high level of flexibility and adaptability, as spreadsheets can present information in various ways, and tasks can be formulated in natural language. In this work, we propose SheetAgent, a generalist agent for spreadsheet reasoning and manipulation via large language models (LLMs). SheetAgent can reason over tabular data, answer natural language questions, and perform editing and manipulation tasks on spreadsheets. It leverages existing spreadsheet structures and natural language processing capabilities, building upon the strengths of LLMs to achieve its functionality. We evaluate SheetAgent on a suite of tasks, including data manipulation, reasoning, and code generation, among others. Our experimental results demonstrate that SheetAgent performs better than existing LLM-based agents on complex reasoning tasks and code generation. Moreover, user studies indicate that SheetAgent is more accurate and flexible than existing spreadsheet automation tools.

Large language models (LLMs) have recently demonstrated impressive results across many tasks. Meanwhile, research on integrating LLMs and machine learning (ML) for recommendation systems has been scarce. To explore the synergies between LLMs and ML in recommendations, we conduct a case study in the context of an e-commerce platform. We find that LLMs can enhance the performance of ML models for item recommendation by extracting high-quality text features.
