Our method achieves an accuracy comparable to prior ap

Indoor scenes we are living in are visually homogenous or textureless, while they inherently contain information for localizing a person's identity. In this paper, we propose a novel framework to enhance the performance of indoor person re-identification (ReID) by exploiting visual homogeneity and textureless cues from RGB images. Firstly, we extract homogeneous regions through an efficient clustering method based on the histograms of oriented gradients (HOG). The HOG features extracted from homogeneous regions represent the visual texture information and are used as supplementary inputs to the model. Secondly, we propose a new loss function that combines the contrastive learning with a triplet loss to learn discriminative features for ReID.

This paper introduces CN-RMA, a novel approach for 3D indoor object placement based on realistic and accurate camera navigation. We first present an overview of the problem domain with relevant definitions in Section II followed by our proposed method description which consists mainly three steps: (i) building efficient point cloud data structures to represent interior spaces; (ii); extracting useful features from these representations that are suitable as input into machine learning algorithms such e g neural networks or support vector machines , etc. ; finally using this information together along side other factors like furniture size constraints when placing objects within rooms .

APIs have become the prominent technology of choice for achieving inter-service communications. The growth of API deployments has driven the urgency in addressing the lack of automated testing frameworks for APIs. In this paper, we leverage mutation testing, a white-box testing approach, to generate test cases for RESTful APIs. We introduce ApiFuzz, a framework that performs mutation testing on RESTful APIs to generate mutation test cases. To our knowledge, this is the first effort that applies mutation testing to generate test cases for RESTful APIs. We evaluate ApiFuzz by testing 94 open-source RESTful APIs. ApiFuzz generated 4,095 test cases, and 744 of those test cases (18.1%) led to a different set of responses from the APIs, including 414 (10.1%) that led to errors. Error types include 116 500-series errors (28.7%), 74 400-series errors (17.7%), 59 300-series errors (14.3%), 34 200-series errors (8.3%), and 17 100-series errors (4.2%). We also found that 72.1% of the APIs have at least one vulnerable operation, and 38.3% of the APIs have at least one operation that returns an error. We hope that our efforts will bring more attention to the testing and validation of RESTful APIs.

Collaborative writing is a common activity in human society, and recently there has been a growing trend of using AI as a writing partner. However, existing studies of AI-assisted writing are mostly focused on assisting with writing tasks, such as grammar checking and word suggestion, rather than creative writing such as story generation. In this paper, we explore the possibility of creating a new genre of interactive literature that evolves through human-AI collaboration, where an AI assistant generates a story outline and the human writer fills in the details. We present a writing assistant system called SARD that uses a Transformer-based language model to generate story outlines and provides them to human writers for further elaboration. We evaluate the utility of SARD through a writing experiment and show that the stories written by participants with the help of SARD are rated higher in quality than those written without the assistant. We also conduct a user study to investigate the collaborative process between humans and AI assistants in story generation. Our results show that participants felt that the stories generated by the human-AI collaboration were of higher quality than those generated by humans alone, and that the AI assistant was able to provide useful support for human writers.

Neural machine translation (NMT) has progressed rapidly in the past few years, promising more accurate and flexible translations than traditional statistical methods. However one problem remains: it is difficult to capture long-term dependency information due its recursive structure or hierarchical architecture of encoder–decoder model . In this paper we propose a novel attention mechanism for NMT using an extended convolution layer as attentions instead conventional dot product based method between each word from source side sequence vector representation with all words on target language level up until current time step outputted by recurrent neural network(RNN).

This work investigates the problem of LiDAR 3D object detection using anchor-based methods. Although anchor-based methods have been widely used in LiDAR object detection, the existing works typically treat the center point of the anchor and the LiDAR point cloud separately. This leads to a sub-optimal use of the anchor point information. To address this issue, this work proposes a novel point assisted sample selection approach that leverages the anchor point information more effectively. Specifically, we propose a point assisted proposal generator that selects the proposal center from the anchor points based on the point cloud features. This approach not only helps to better utilize the anchor information but also reduces the computational cost. Furthermore, a point assisted sample selector is proposed that utilizes the selected proposal center to guide the sample selection. This ensures that the samples are efficiently selected around the object manifold. The proposed method is tested on the KITTI and NuScenes datasets. The results demonstrate that the proposed approach outperforms the state-of-the-art anchor-based methods by a significant margin.

The proliferation of wireless-enabled applications with divergent quality of service (QoS) requirements necessitates tailored QoS provisioning. With the growing complexity of wireless infrastructures, application-specific QoS perceived by a user equipment (UE) is jointly determined by its association with the supporting base station in heterogeneous networks (HetNets) and the amount of resource allocated to it. However, conventional application-agnostic objective-based user association and resource allocation often ignore the differences among applications' specific requirements for resources, inevitably preventing tailored QoS provisioning. Hence, in this paper, the problem of joint user association and resource allocation with application-specific objectives is investigated for achieving

For a long time, mathematicians have dreamed of creating a mathematical engine that could help them in their research. In recent years, there has been much progress in using machine learning for proving theorems. However, most machine learning applications in mathematics are limited to classifying mathematical objects or predicting numerical values. In this paper, we propose a new framework for a machine learning-based theorem prover. Instead of only classifying mathematical objects, we aim to understand the underlying concepts and connect them to other fields of mathematics. We do so by using information-theoretic concepts, such as information flow and causal relationships, to evaluate the importance of mathematical concepts for proving theorems. We then use a machine learning model to learn the relationship between mathematical concepts and theorems. Our experiments show that we can predict theorems from mathematical concepts, and our framework can be used to create a theorem prover that understands the underlying concepts of mathematics. We believe that our work is a first step towards an AI Mathematician.

Embodied Instruction Following (EIF) is a crucial task in embodied learning and intelligence, where an agent follows the instruction of another while maintaining its own identity. Previous approaches to this problem have either focused on optimizing one-step actions or relied solely upon visual information for decision making which are not suitable when navigating complex environments with multiple goals like rooms/corridors etc., as these methods do well only if they know their exact goal location beforehand but fail otherwise due lack knowledge about other potential destinations within environment space itself - especially those areas that might be hard reach without proper planning ahead time frame allocation constraints given limited computational resources available at any point durin

Optical flow estimation has been crucial for many computer vision tasks. Recently, scene flow, which models the 3D motion of pixels in a video sequence, has attracted much attention. Unlike optical flow, scene flow estimation requires solving a sparse linear system of equations, which is much more difficult. Existing methods solve this problem by using a flow network, which iteratively updates the flow field. However, it is difficult for the flow network to converge to the true flow field, due to the inherent nature of the optimization problem. To address this problem, we propose a novel scene flow estimation method, called ICFNet, which learns the true flow field directly, rather than iteratively updating it. To do so, we introduce a new idea, called instance-conditioned parameterization (ICP), which parameterizes the scene flow field of each individual pixel using its neighboring pixels. Each ICP has a unique parameter vector, which is conditioned on the instance (i.e., a group of neighboring pixels). We further propose a new loss function, called instance-conditioned loss (ICL), which helps the ICPs to learn the true flow field more effectively. ICL pulls the ICPs that belong to the same instance toward each other, while pushing the ICPs that belong to different instances away from each other. Extensive experiments show that ICFNet outperforms the state-of-the-art methods on two scene flow benchmarks.

We consider the fundamental problem of decomposing a large-scale approximate nearest neighbor search (ANNS) dataset into smaller subsets, such that the quality of each ANNS query is not degraded by more than some predefined threshold. We first provide an initial proof of concept in which we reduce this problem to an instance of the $k$-means clustering problem. However, we observe that the performance of existing methods for solving this problem is severely limited when faced with very large datasets and low thresholds. To address these challenges, we propose a novel approach, named Flexible Subsetting via Pairwise Clustering (FSPC), that iteratively partitions the data using two-dimensional pairwise similarity matrices. The key insight behind our method is that it can be implemented effic

This paper presents a new and improved DareFightingICE platform, a fighting game platform with the goal of increasing engagement in learning computational thinking (CT) skills. The original version has been used by over 10 million students worldwide since its launch on Juneteenth last year to teach CT concepts such as looping constructs through gameplay mechanics that utilize repetition for efficient movement across virtual environments filled with enemies known simply "ICED." This research report discusses how we have taken lessons learned from our previous project iteration - specifically regarding user interface design choices-to create an enhanced edition tailored towards older children aged seven years old plus who are already familiarized within digital spaces but still lack knowledg

As the scale of modern recommendation systems continues to grow, understanding the impact of scale on system performance becomes increasingly important. This paper takes a significant step towards this goal by proposing a scaling law for large-scale recommendation. We derive a general scaling relation between the three key factors of large-scale recommendation: computation, communication, and data. This scaling relation suggests that the performance of large-scale recommendation improves with scale if the three factors grow at similar rates. To verify this scaling law, we conduct a large-scale experiment on 778,074 historical orders from 357,287 customers in a Chinese online supermarket. We find that the scaling law holds across different levels of scale, providing valuable insights for practitioners in the industry. Based on the scaling relation, we develop Wukong, a framework for scaling recommendation systems. Wukong helps practitioners understand the scaling properties of their current systems and design future systems that can handle increased scale.

We introduce Cluster Edge Modification problems with constraints on the size of the clusters and study their complexity. The problems are motivated by the observation that in many real-life graphs, a large proportion of the nodes are densely connected and belong to the same cluster. In this paper, we present polynomial-time algorithms for both single-edge modification problems (Cluster Edge Swap, Cluster Edge Addition) under such constraint. For multi-edge modifications, we show that the problem of removing or adding multiple edges is NP-complete, while there are polynomial-time algorithms for swapping multiple edges. Our algorithmic approach is based on a novel graph transformation technique, which can be applied to other graph optimization problems as well. Furthermore, we also provide a

Complex networks are ubiquitous in the real world, and identifying critical nodes in these networks is of great practical importance. For example, in power grids, identifying critical nodes can help prevent blackouts; in transportation networks, it can help reduce traffic congestion; and in biological networks, it can help understand disease mechanisms. Therefore, many studies have been conducted to identify critical nodes in complex networks. However, most existing methods rely on domain knowledge and struggle to keep up with the massive amount of data generated in real-world applications. Hence, in this paper, we propose a novel method called LLaM-CR to identify critical nodes in complex networks using Large Language Models (LLMs). Specifically, we fine-tune a LLM with a large amount of text data to encode nodes in the network as text. Then, we use the cosine similarity between node-text and critical node-text to identify critical nodes in the network. Extensive experiments on four real-world datasets demonstrate that our method outperforms state-of-the-art methods by a significant margin. Therefore, our method is promising for identifying critical nodes in complex networks.

In this paper, we perform joint communication and sensing (JCAS) measurements in an industrial environment using a distributed multiple-input multiple-output (MIMO) setup. We employ two MIMO transceivers, one at the base station (BS) and one at the access point (AP), to enhance the communication performance and the sensing accuracy. We investigate the communication and sensing performance as a function of time in a realistic industrial environment. We also study the effect of adding a third transceiver at a different location to the network. We compare the performance of the distributed MIMO setup to the performance of a conventional system without sensing capability. We show that the distributed MIMO setup improves both the communication and sensing performance compared to the conventional system. We also derive upper and lower bounds for the sensing accuracy. We show that the lower bound can be approached by designing the sensing waveform transmitted by the BS. We discuss the implications of our results for future JCAS systems.

This paper presents significant advancements in the field of abstract reasoning, particularly for Raven's Progressive Matrices (RPM), which is one among many standardized intelligence tests. We describe a novel two-stage neural network approach based on convolutional and recurrent networks to solve RPM problems: first stage learns 2D image representations with deep learning techniques from scratch; second state uses LSTM model as an intelligent agent that mimics human cognitive process by deriving logical rules through interactions between learned images features via feedback loop mechanism over time steps until reaching final conclusion or giving up when no more progress can be made after exhausting all possible combinations within limited number attempts according given test conditions i

We present the first algorithm for query learning of a general class of Mealy machines with timers (MMPTs). MMPTs are composed of sequential and concurrent components, where each component has a finite number of inputs, outputs, states, and transitions. The input/output behavior is determined by state transition rules associated with each transition. In addition to state transition rules, a MMPT may have one or more timed-transitions whereby each input/output pair is associated with a timer. An MMPT accepts a string in its language iff there exists an accepting computation that starts with initial state and halts with final state while producing this string in response to the given input sequence. Query learning of MMPTs is a special case of the learning from examples problem where we are

We consider the problem of off-policy reinforcement learning with linear function approximation and tabular dynamic programming. In this setting, we perform a simple finite-time analysis of the temporal-difference (TD) learning algorithm with the mean-squared error (MSE) objective function. We establish that, under a wide range of stepsize parameters, TD learning with linear function approximation converges to a neighborhood of the optimal solution at a linear rate. We also show that the convergence result holds for a class of non-linear function approximators that satisfy the Poincar\'e inequality. Finally, we demonstrate that our convergence analysis applies to the case where the TD updates are performed in an episodic Markov decision process (MDP) with linear function approximation. Our theoretical results are supported by empirical studies of TD learning with linear function approximation on the Cartpole environment.

We present a survey of techniques that we refer to as policy space response oracles (PSROs). PSROs provide a way to respond to policy queries in a manner that is useful for learning policies in a variety of applications. We present a general framework for understanding PSROs, and then provide a detailed review of existing PSROs. We conclude with a discussion of open questions and future directions.

Large language models (LLMs) need to serve everyone, including a global majority of non-native English speakers. In this work, we analyze the performance of LLMs on non-English languages and explore what is needed for them to succeed. We find that LLMs are generally less capable at understanding long sentences in other languages than in English, even when translated back into English. They also struggle with rare words and have difficulty generating natural sounding sentences. We conduct an extensive evaluation across 41 languages and present a variety of methods to improve their performance. Our findings will guide future research and product development efforts to ensure LLMs can be useful in the full spectrum of world languages. Code and data are available at https://github.com/google-r

The creation of instruction data and evaluation benchmarks for serving Large language models often involves enormous human effort. In this work, we propose a novel framework to automatically generate training datasets for instructing large language models by leveraging the alignment between prompt engineering and code generation. Specifically, we first formulate the instruction data generation task as a code generation problem, where we treat a given prompt as an input code snippet and the generated instruction as the corresponding output code. Then, we design a prompt-code generator based on CodeGenGPT and train it with a large number of predefined prompts and their corresponding instructions. Finally, we utilize the trained model to generate various types of instructions by providing dif

Registration of point clouds collected from a pair of distant vehicles provides a comprehensive and accurate 3D representation for road environment. In this paper, we propose an end-toend framework to jointly solve the registration problem across multiple scales in both local patches (i) within one frame at different layers as well as global frames by considering two adjacent neighboring video sequences via graph convolution network with multiscale feature fusion strategy(ii). Furthermore , our method can obtain robust results under challenging scenarios where large ego motion occurs or when dynamic objects are present . Our experimental evaluation on real world datasets demonstrates that compared against baseline methods suchas ICPand SFMbasedregistrationmethodsourmodeloutperforms themin

Human trajectory data produced by daily mobile devices has proven its usefulness in various substantial fields such as transport and urban planning. However, the lack of standardization across different sources prevents researchers from performing cross-platform analysis on large amounts or datasets without manually processing them first. In this paper we present a dataset integration framework that aims to provide an automated solution for integrating multiple types human mobility trajectories into one single common format using Python programming language with its related packages (GeoPandas) which is open source software available online at GitHub repository under MIT license terms .

Mechanistic understanding of organic reactions can facilitate reaction development, impurity prediction, and in silico screening. However, the lack of a universal framework for predicting reaction mechanisms from chemical structures hampers these efforts. To address this issue, we developed a deep learning model, ReactionNet, that leverages the physics-based mechanism prediction network (MPNet) to directly predict reaction mechanisms from chemical structures. MPNet uses 159 million molecules to learn how molecular structures are transformed into reaction mechanisms. This large training set enables ReactionNet to achieve a new state-of-the-art performance on the CASP14 reaction mechanism prediction task, achieving an F1 score of 0.687, which is a 2% absolute improvement compared with the pr

Human trajectory data produced by daily mobile devices has proven its usefulness in various substantial fields such as transportation and emergency services. However, the lack of ground truth, which is a common issue with human trajectory datasets, makes it difficult to assess the quality of trajectories and their associated uncertainty. To address this, we propose a novel framework called Geometry-Aware Uncertainty Assessment for Trajectory Data (GATE). Our approach leverages geometric information and geographical constraints to capture the inherent structure of trajectory data, allowing us to evaluate trajectory uncertainty in a more comprehensive and accurate manner. GATE takes into account both spatial and temporal factors to enhance the assessment process, resulting in a more robust a

Three-dimensional Digital Subtraction Angiography (3D-DSA) is a well established technique for the visualization of coronary arteries. However, the conventional 3D-DSA reconstruction workflow can be time consuming and prone to errors. We present an automated 3D-DSA pipeline that reconstructs coronary artery images from multi-contrast DSA data without user input. The pipeline uses a novel deep neural network architecture to automatically segment vessel walls in angiographic images using a single pre-trained model. Our method then leverages this vessel wall mask to generate a three-dimensional volume representation of the vessel wall. Finally, we use the volume representation as a deformable template to warp the acquired projections onto the desired projection plane for image reconstruction.

The development of autonomous driving (AD) has recently entered a new stage, from perception and mapping to high-level decision-making and control. Learning-based planning, as a key enabling technology, has the potential to unlock greater AD capabilities. However, the lack of a comprehensive and standardized set of realistic scenarios with high-fidelity simulations and real-world data makes it difficult to develop and benchmark learning-based planning algorithms. To address this problem, we introduce the nuPlan benchmark, a comprehensive and standardized benchmark for learning-based planning in AD. The benchmark provides rich annotations and evaluation tools, and covers essential aspects such as controllability, realizability, and deployability. Our benchmark focuses on challenging urban driving scenarios, including but not limited to, dynamic and congested traffic, diverse pedestrian behaviors, and various vehicle types. These scenarios are represented by more than 100,000 real-world samples and 1,000 hand-crafted expert solutions. To showcase the utility of the benchmark, we present three state-of-the-art learning-based planning algorithms and perform an extensive set of experiments. Our experimental results demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed algorithms and their ability to outperform traditional methods. The nuPlan benchmark and its evaluation toolkit are publicly available, and we encourage the artificial intelligence (AI) and autonomous driving communities to develop more effective and efficient learning-based planning algorithms. By providing a comprehensive and standardized platform, the nuPlan benchmark aims to accelerate the development of next-generation AD systems and drive research towards more realistic and capable AI-driven planning solutions.

We extend the theory of locally checkable labeling problems (LCLs) from the classical LOCAL model to the non-uniform random access model. We first establish a dichotomy for LCLs in the uniform case: either each vertex has a constant number of labels, or every vertex has a polynomial number of labels but there are infinitely many distinct types of vertices. To do this we construct two different classes of LCLs and show that no other class exists. We then study the question of whether an LCL can be solved in the non-uniform random access model by a local algorithm whose running time depends only on the number of labels of the input instance. It turns out that the answer is positive, assuming the Unique Games Conjecture, if and only if the graph contains a path $P$ of length at least 2 such t

Smartphones are integral to modern life, yet research highlights the cognitive drawbacks of prolonged mobile device usage. Recent advances in ambient intelligence have paved the way for more natural and seamless interaction with smartphone apps, making them less obtrusive to users. However, current efforts largely focus on improving the user interface design rather than considering other aspects of the mobile app experience that may also contribute to cognitive overload. This paper presents a novel approach for addressing this issue: enhancing ambient intelligence through multimodal and context-aware interactions designed to reduce the cognitive load associated with using smartphone apps.

Learning to generate textures for a novel 3D mesh given a collection of 3D meshes and their corresponding texture maps is challenging as it requires the model to disentangle the latent code of texture from that of shape. To tackle this problem, we propose a novel approach based on cross-view contrastive learning. Our method utilizes a dual-encoder architecture to learn the latent representation of shapes and textures, respectively. Additionally, we employ the contrastive loss between different views of the same object in both shape and texture spaces. This leads to improved discrimination among different views of the same object while also improving generalization to unseen objects. Furthermore, we introduce an adversarial loss to enhance the realism of generated textures by encouraging th

Universal Domain Adaptation (UniDA) targets knowledge transfer in the presence of both covariate and label shift, which is a challenging task in real-world scenarios. UniDA leverages a domain discriminator to distinguish the source and target domains and adapts the source model to the target through minimizing the distance between the latent representations extracted from the source and target data. However, existing methods typically ignore the correlation among feature dimensions within each modality, resulting in suboptimal performance. In this paper, we introduce a novel method called Feature Correlation UniDA (FC-UniDA), which explicitly models inter-dimensional correlation for better feature reconstruction. Specifically, FC-UniDA first decomposes the features into multiple linear lay

Citing comprehensively and appropriately has become a challenging task with the explosive growth of knowledge. In response, existing approaches have focused on leveraging existing citations as reference or enhancing the quality of generated ones via generative models. However, these methods have ignored a crucial factor: the difficulty of generating citations that are grammatically correct and semantically relevant. This paper introduces Citation Assistant, an assistive system for generating comprehensive citations within scientific articles. It is designed to alleviate writers' difficulties in generating appropriate citations by providing real-time feedback, thereby improving the overall writing process. Our study evaluates the effectiveness of our approach using a dataset comprising 500

This paper introduces an approach for the classification of rare, prominent, and novel malware families. The approach utilizes static and dynamic analysis features extracted from a sample collection that includes a significant number of rare families, as well as prominent and novel families. The static analysis features are computed using a commercial static analyzer, while the dynamic analysis features are extracted from virtualized execution traces. The classification model is trained using a deep neural network that takes as input a concatenation of the static and dynamic analysis features. We evaluate our approach against a set of baseline models on two malware datasets. The results show that our approach outperforms the baselines on both datasets. Our approach achieves the highest F-score for the rare class on the first dataset, while it achieves the highest F-score for the prominent class on the second dataset. Additionally, we show that the addition of dynamic analysis features improves the classification results for all approaches.

In the standard model of fair allocation of resources to agents, every agent has some utility for every resource, and the goal is to assign resources to agents so that the agents' welfare is maximized. Motivated by job scheduling, interest in this problem dates back to the work of Deuermeyer et al. [SIAM J. on Algebraic Discrete Methods'82]. Recent works consider the compatibility between resources and assign only mutually compatible resources to an agent. We study a fair allocation problem in which we are given a set of agents, a set of resources, a utility function for every agent over a set of resources, and a {\it conflict graph} on the set of resources (where an edge denotes incompatibility).

Recent advances in natural language processing with the Transformer architecture have inspired many vision transformer architectures. However, implementing Transformers in computer vision tasks often requires large memory and computational resources, which limits their application in real-world scenarios. To address this challenge, we propose a family of vision transformer architectures, named Vision-RWKV. This novel architecture incorporates rotatable weight sharing among multiple attention heads, a technique drawn from the design of Transformer-XL, into the vision transformer framework. This modification significantly reduces the memory footprint and computational cost, making it more practical for real-world deployment. We validate our approach through experiments on the ImageNet-1K dataset, demonstrating that Vision-RWKV exhibits competitive performance compared to existing state-of-the-art vision transformer models. Furthermore, we show that Vision-RWKV can be easily scaled up for better performance without compromising resource efficiency. On the Pathology Breast Cancer Classification dataset, our architecture achieves a 6.5% higher accuracy than the previous state-of-the-art model with 78% less FLOPs and 94% less parameter. For the Cifar100 dataset, Vision-RWKV also shows comparable performance with significant resource savings. We hope that our work will inspire further research into resource-efficient Transformer designs for visual perception.

This paper presents an efficient method for updating particles in a particle filter (PF) to address the position estimation problem when dealing with sharp-peaked likelihood functions derived from multiple observations. Sharp-peaked likelihood functions commonly arise from millimeter-accurate distance observations of carrier phases in the global navigation satellite system (GNSS). However, when such likelihood functions are used for particle weight updates, the absence of particles within the peaks leads to all particle weights becoming zero. To overcome this problem, in this study, a straightforward and effective approach is introduced for updating particles when dealing with sharp-peaked likelihood functions obtained from multiple observations. The proposed method, termed as the multiple

The effectiveness of clopidogrel, a widely used antiplatelet medication, varie The effectiveness of clopidogrel, a widely used antiplatelet medication, varies among patients. It has been suggested that the interindividual variability in clopodigrel efficacy is due to genetic polymorphisms affecting drug metabolism and/or platelet response to clopidogrel. We sought to determine whether such genetic polymorphisms are associated with different responses to clopidogrel in patients who have undergone percutaneous coronary intervention (PCI).We performed a retrospective cohort study involving 450 consecutive patients treated with PCI at our institution between March 2013 and February 2016.

Synthetic control charts are an essential tool for detecting anomalous behavior in industrial processes. Charting the control limits based on a statistical model for the process data is a common approach for synthesizing control charts. The statistical models are fitted to the process data, and the control limits are computed as functions of the estimated parameters. The models are re-estimated at regular intervals, and the control limits are adjusted accordingly. A foundation time series model (FTSM) is a recently proposed robust approach for modeling and forecasting time series data. The key feature of FTSM is that it separates the modeling of the time series data into two distinct steps: (1) a structural model is fitted to the data, and (2) the structural model is used to forecast the data. The structural model is based on a finite basis of functions, and the coefficients are estimated by minimizing a robust loss function. In this paper, we investigate the possibility of using FTSM for synthesizing control charts. We compare the performance of the FTSM-based charts with the classical approach of modeling the control limits as functions of the parameters of a statistical model for the process data. The FTSM approach is shown to be more robust and reliable than the classical approach in the presence of outliers.

Angiography is the gold standard for detecting and characterizing vascular anomalies. Dynamic contrast-enhanced (DCE) angiography further enables the non-invasive assessment of tissue perfusion. Image reconstruction of DCE-angiography is a challenging task, as it requires deconvolution of the contrast agent concentration (CAC) from a single pixel time series, which typically exhibits nonlinear and non-stationary dynamics. Previous works have proposed physics-based methods, which model the dynamics of the CAC using compartmental models. However, such approaches are limited to modeling the dynamics of the CAC in a qualitative manner and are unable to reconstruct the CAC from image data. In this work, we propose a physics-informed learning framework for the reconstruction of CAC dynamics from DCE-angiographic images. We formulate a Bayesian inverse problem, where the objective is to reconstruct a spatio-temporal map of the CAC concentration from a single pixel time series. We design a physics-informed neural network (PINN) to learn the dynamics of the CAC, which incorporates prior knowledge about the physics of the system. We validate the reconstruction algorithm using both simulated and real-world DCE-angiography data. Our results demonstrate that the proposed PINN-based reconstruction framework produces accurate and precise reconstructions of the CAC dynamics. Compared to previous methods, PINN-based reconstruction offers a more physically plausible reconstruction of the CAC, while maintaining a low computational cost. In conclusion, we have developed a new framework for the reconstruction of CAC dynamics from DCE-angiographic images using physics-informed learning. This method offers accurate, precise, and physically plausible reconstruction, making it a valuable tool for the evaluation of vascular anomalies.

Fractional Differential Equations (FDEs) are essential tools for modelling complex phenomena that occur in nature. We present a survey of the application and recent advances regarding FDs, focusing on three aspects: first we address some basic concepts related to fractional derivatives; second our attention is drawn towards their applications in science & engineering fields as well solutions via traditional methods such Adomian Decomposition Method or more advanced approaches involving numerical simulations using finite difference approximations based upon Hermite-Taylor series expansions with various orders up through fourth order inclusive depending entirely according how fast convergence occurs within each particular case study scenario being considered at hand which will be disc

A topical challenge for algorithms in general and for automatic image categorization and generation in particular is presented by the large-scale availability of heterogeneous data. For example, a typical Internet search engine has to handle images with different sizes on many topics at once which requires efficient representations that can be generalized across domains while maintaining high accuracy within individual subspaces defined by each topic or domain [1]. In this article we present an approach based upon multiresolution local binary patterns (MLBP) as well suited representation scheme due its robustness towards noise introduced during acquisition process but also because it provides additional information about shape structure along multiple scales via multiresoulution ana

Animals learn to adapt agility of their movements to their capabilities and the environment they operate in. In contrast, humans must not only be agile themselves but also adaptively adjust their toolsets, i.e., robotic arms. Learning how to use tools is a challenging task for both animals and humans. We propose an interactive system that allows human users to teach a robotic arm how to perform simple manipulation tasks using a set of tools designed to simulate the movement capabilities of different animal species. Our proposed system can enable people to understand how different animals move through learning by imitation from various species' robots. Our experimental results show that participants successfully performed the manipulations with the robotic arm after receiving training. More

Even when using large multi-modal foundation models, few-shot learning is still challenging -- if not impossible -- for complex tasks. In this paper, we propose a novel method to efficiently fine-tune such large models with fewer examples and better performance in the few-shot setting. We first design a simple yet effective method called Few-Shot Pseudo Labeling (FSPL) that can produce high quality pseudo labels with much less human annotation cost. Then we combine it with several other methods such as meta-learning, curriculum learning, and self-supervised learning to improve its effectiveness. Our experiments show that our proposed method can effectively reduce the number of labeled samples required for training while maintaining or even improving model performance compared to existing m

Graph Convolutional Networks (GCNs) have become pivotal in recommendation systems for their ability to incorporate graph structure. However, existing GCN-based methods often overlook the heterogeneity of user attributes and item characteristics, resulting in suboptimal performance in real-world scenarios. To address this issue, we propose a novel framework named Attribute-aware Heterogeneous Graph Embedding (AHGE), designed to capture attribute information and leverage it effectively within a heterogeneous graph context. AHGE incorporates attribute knowledge into node embeddings through a novel attribute aggregation module, which ensures that nodes with similar attribute features are mapped to similar embeddings. The proposed method is evaluated on several benchmark datasets using differen

Modern machine learning (ML) applications, particularly those involving AI-driven networking, often require the deployment of multiple ML models to perform end-to-end tasks. Such compound AI systems (CAS) can offer improved performance if the ML models are coordinated and work as a team. However, coordinating multiple ML models to work together efficiently is challenging, as it requires taking into account diverse constraints, such as ensuring that the models use non-overlapping data, maintaining data privacy, and limiting communication. In this paper, we introduce ALTO, a novel orchestration framework for CAS that uses a multi-agent system (MAS) to coordinate the ML models. We show that ALTO can improve the performance of CAS compared to a baseline approach of deploying all models on a single node, while also reducing the resource requirements compared to a state-of-the-art orchestration framework.

Recent advances of locomotion controllers utilizing deep reinforcement learning (RL) have revolutionized the field, enabling robots to tackle complex physical tasks. We observe that these models share a common issue: poor sample efficiency. The reason is that in many cases, the training process does not make use of important prior information about the system dynamics and cost functions, thereby limiting the model's ability to learn quickly and efficiently. In this paper, we propose a novel approach for efficient RL that takes advantage of prior knowledge of the physics underlying the robotics systems and the task at hand. Our method, termed "Prior-Aware Reinforcement Learning" (PA-RL), addresses the lack of prior knowledge by first learning a physics model from demonstrations or other sou

Popular methods usually use a degradation model in a supervised way to learn a watermarking system. In this paper, we propose an unsupervised method that does not require any labeled training data but rather uses two unlabeled sets of images: clean and degraded ones obtained by applying different degradations on the same set (i) automatically from publicly available datasets or provided as input; it learns how each kind is affected individually during image processing steps so later when given unknown samples they can be correctly identified within one group only instead having difficulty distinguishing between them all together due their similarities being lost along with information regarding what caused such changes happening initially through noise addition etc..

Accurate detection and diagnosis of abnormal behaviors such as network attacks from multivariate time series are crucial for the security, resilience against cyber-attacks. However it is non trivial to detect anomalies in real life complex systems due their high dimensionality (high number or features), large volume size data sets (>10^6) with missing observations over long periods on multidimensional spaces which creates a challenge when using traditional ML approaches because they have difficulties capturing patterns that emerge within this context without sacrificing accuracy . To address these issues we propose an efficient anomaly scoring method based upon Autoencoder architecture combined together into one single model called "AE_SCORER", allowing users not only identify outliers but

Image data augmentation (IDDA) is a powerful tool for improving the performance of image-based machine learning models. However, we find that many IDDA methods introduce class-specific bias (CSB) into the augmented data, where the bias varies across classes. This CSB can hurt the performance of the model and is not addressed by existing methods. To address this CSB, we propose a novel data-centric approach that selects the augmentation function based on the statistical difference between classes. Specifically, we introduce a new IDDA method, called class-specific data augmentation (CSDA), which performs different augmentations for different classes based on their unique properties. To show the effectiveness of CSDA, we conduct extensive experiments on large-scale classification datasets, including CIFAR-10, CIFAR-100, ImageNet-1K, and others. The results demonstrate that CSDA consistently improves the performance of the model and reduces the CSB. For example, CSDA can boost the top-1 accuracy of ResNet-20 on CIFAR-100 from 91.7% to 92.5% and reduce the CSB from 0.48 to 0.31. As a data-centric approach, CSDA can be combined with existing model-centric approaches to further improve the performance.

Zero-shot image captioning (IC) without well-paired image-text data can be a challenging task. However, in recent years, generative pretraining of large language models has achieved remarkable progress in various downstream tasks with the help of massive unlabeled data. Thus, in this work, we investigate the effectiveness of pretraining zero-shot IC by employing text-image generative models pretrained on unlabeled data. To facilitate our study, we first evaluate existing zero-shot IC benchmarks and reveal that they contain significant gaps from realistic scenarios. Motivated by these findings, we present SCOPE, an open-source dataset collection that offers more realistic test conditions. We also propose a pretraining method for zero-shot IC based on the text-image generation capabilities o

In this paper, we analyze the monotonicity of information aging in a remote estimation system, where a sensor-equipped moving object collects data and sends it to an estimator. We assume that there is a communication link between the sensor and the estimator; however, the transmitted data may be corrupted by packet loss or channel noise. The sensor provides estimates on the basis of its local observations, while the estimator uses the received measurements to compute the true state. Our objective is to characterize the error dynamics as well as the convergence speed for the resulting state estimate. To do so, we introduce a notion of information aging: the amount of distortion induced by packet loss and/or channel noise. Specifically, we define the entropy production rate and propose two m

This work addresses the task of long-term person re-identification. Typically, person re-identification methods aim to identify people within a single camera sequence over a short period of time (e.g., tens or hundreds of frames). In contrast, we focus on the task of tracking and identifying people across multiple non-overlapping camera sequences with large temporal gaps between them (e.g., weeks or months). To address this challenging problem, we first propose a new benchmark that consists of real-world multi-camera person re-identification data from public events. We then show that state-of-the-art person re-identification methods perform poorly in this domain due to two main reasons: (1) they cannot generalize well between different datasets due to the lack of diversity in the training

Charts, figures, and text derived from data play an important role in decision making, from data exploration to policy formulation. However, the majority of visualizations are based on tabular data that is not conducive to human understanding. While recent advancements have enabled the generation of more comprehensive visuals by leveraging large language models (LLMs), these methods face challenges when interpreting complex tabular data. In this work, we propose a novel framework called Tabular-LLM for Visualization (TaLiVi) to improve LLMs' ability to synthesize tabular visualization.

OpenVPN is often promoted as the gold standard of open source VPN software. We show that this praise is unwarranted from a privacy perspective, as OpenVPN is open to VPN fingerprinting. We introduce a simple, lightweight attack that can uniquely identify millions of OpenVPN connections per second, even when using unique, one-time connection identifiers. Our attack exploits the fact that OpenVPN's default behavior is to include identifying information in the TCP stream, which can be observed passively. We also show that active attacks, where the adversary participates in the TCP handshake, can extract additional identifying information from the TCP options. TCP options are a well-studied attack surface, and our results add further evidence of their potential for fingerprinting.

The body movements accompanying speech aid speakers in expressing their ideas. Co-speech gestures can clarify the intended meaning of an utterance, while co-speech facial expressions can convey the speaker's attitude towards it. Recent research has shown that co-speech gestures and facial expressions have a strong impact on how people perceive others and themselves: Speakers are seen as more convincing when they use gestures, and listeners are perceived as more intelligent and likable when they display positive facial expressions. However, little is known about how these two sources of nonverbal information interact to influence the perception of speakers. In two experiments, we manipulated the presence of gestures and facial expressions in audiovisual and audio-only clips of the same spee

We study the PSPACE-complete $k$-Canadian Traveller Problem, where we are given a directed graph $G$, a positive integer $k$, and a vertex $v_0$ of $G$. The goal is to determine whether there is a sequence of $k+1$ vertices $(v_0, \ldots, v_{k})$ such that every vertex of $G$ is visited at most once. Our algorithm uses two new techniques: (i) an efficient data structure for maintaining a set of subsets of $V(G)$ with bounded size and (ii) a novel reduction technique which allows us to reduce the problem instance from an arbitrary graph $G$ to a tree $T$. We also study the computational complexity of variants of the $k$-Canadian Traveller Problem, where each vertex of $G$ has a nonnegative weight associated with it.

This paper considers a fully digital, matched-filter precoded rate splitting multiple access (MRSA) transceiver design for wireless networks. The conventional MRSA scheme uses two separate analog precoders, one at the transmitter and one at the receiver, to implement the precoding. This results in a complex analog-to-digital conversion at the receiver and also limits the achievable power savings. The proposed design eliminates the receiver-side analog precoding and instead uses a simpler, fully digital precoding scheme. The digital precoder is designed by solving a convex optimization problem that minimizes the sum of the maximum mean squared errors (MSEs) over the users, which is then followed by a energy efficiency maximization step. The proposed design is a significant improvement over the conventional MRSA scheme in terms of both performance and energy efficiency. Simulation results show that the proposed design improves the sum MSE performance by up to 10 dB and also reduces the maximum individual MSE by up to 20 dB compared to the conventional MRSA scheme. In addition, the fully digital precoding scheme reduces the required analog-to-digital conversion resolution by 2 bits or more, which further contributes to the overall energy savings.

One important desideratum of lifelong learning aims to discover novel classes from unlabelled data. However, most existing methods in this area typically require the training set and test sets both contain instances belonging all known/novel categories or at least one instance per class which is not practical for real-world scenarios where a large number (or infinite) unknown types may exist but only limited samples are available during testing time; also these approaches cannot effectively handle unseen domains with different distributions as well due their reliance on prior information about domain boundaries provided by users' input labels rather than automatically learned through interactions between model parameters via interaction networks like those commonly used nowadays within dee

Deep learning methods for perception are the cornerstone of many robotic systems. Despite their potential for performance improvement, they can be difficult to deploy due in part because deep neural networks (DNNs) have a large number parameters and high computational complexity that makes them challenging to execute on embedded devices with limited resources such as small form-factor robots or edge computing platforms like smart cameras equipped with processors running at lower clock speeds than standard GPUs found today' s servers .

Wind power forecasting is a challenging task, and missing values in the measurements exacerbate the problem. Moreover, the missing values are not completely random; they are more likely to occur when the wind speed is high. Ignoring the mechanism that generates the missing values can lead to a poor calibration of the forecasting model, because the model parameters will be poorly identified. To address this issue, we propose a generative model for wind power forecasting that incorporates the mechanism that generates the missing values. The proposed model is an extension of a state-space model for wind power forecasting, where the state equation is an autoregressive model of order 1 (AR(1)). The observation equation is a logistic function that models the probability of missing values, which depends on the wind speed. We implement maximum a posteriori (MAP) estimation to estimate the model parameters, and we use Hamiltonian Monte Carlo (HMC) to sample from the posterior distribution. We apply the proposed method to real wind speed data from the National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration (NOAA) and we compare the results with a standard state-space model.

The mutual reinforcement effect (MRE) is a fundamental phenomenon in human decision-making, where the decision to adopt an option is influenced by considering the potential decisions of others. We present an approach to modeling MRE based on the idea that individuals in a group share information about their options, and this information sharing influences their decisions. We capture the information flow between individuals using a directed information graph, and we capture the decision-making process using submodularity. We prove that the MRE emerges as a special case of our framework, by showing that there exists a one-to-one correspondence between the set of MRE outcomes and a specific subset of information flow graphs. We demonstrate that information flow graphs provide a more general and nuanced perspective on MRE, as compared to previous models based on the notion of a reference group. In particular, we show that information flow graphs can capture both positive and negative reinforcement, and can represent various degrees of reinforcement.

Image-to-video generation aims to produce a sequence of realistic frames that transition from a source image to a target image. This paper introduces AtomVideo, a novel image-to-video generation framework that incorporates both atom-level and molecular-level transformations. AtomoVideo first utilizes a set of pre-trained atom-level transformers to convert the source and target images into atom-level representations. These representations are then transformed into molecular-level representations using a molecular-level transformer, which generates intermediate frames by interpolating the atom positions and bond lengths between the source and target molecules. The final video is produced by rendering the molecular-level representations into images using a graphics card. AtomoVideo is the first image-to-video generation framework that performs atom-level transformations, enabling it to generate videos with molecular-level details and a sense of realism. Compared to existing methods, AtomoVideo achieves superior visual quality, as demonstrated by human preference tests and objective evaluation metrics. The source code is available at https://github.com/zjunlp/AtomoVideo.

Industry 5.0 is a concept that describes the next phase of industrial revolution, characterised by the integration of artificial intelligence (AI) and other emerging technologies with manufacturing processes and supply chains. The term Industry 5.0 was first introduced by the European Union in 2020, and since then it has been adopted by other regions, including Australia, where research funding bodies have invested in initiatives to promote AI adoption in manufacturing. However, before Industry 5.0 can be realised, several challenges must be overcome, including ensuring the trustworthiness of AI systems. To determine whether the trustworthiness of AI systems is being considered in Industry 5.0 projects, this paper conducts a systematic review of the literature on AI in Industry 5.0. The review focuses on identifying and analysing articles that discuss AI applications in Industry 5.0 and assesses the extent to which trustworthiness is addressed in these applications. The review also aims to identify the key enablers and challenges that facilitate or hinder the adoption of trustworthy AI in Industry 5.0. This paper provides a comprehensive overview of the current state of research on trustworthy AI in Industry 5.0 and offers insights into the key areas that need to be addressed to promote the adoption of trustworthy AI in future Industry 5.0 projects.

One common way to speed up the find operation within a set of text files involves a trigram index. The idea is that you scan through each file, extracting the trigrams and recording them in a hash table. A query is then performed by checking if any of these trigrams appear as prefixes in a query string. However, there is a more efficient method that I learned from a recent paper: using a trie structure instead of a hash table. This has some advantages over the hash table approach, including improved performance and the ability to handle duplicates without additional processing. In this post, I'll provide an explanation of how to use a trie for this purpose, along with some code samples. ## Background on Tries A trie (also known as a prefix tree) is a tree data structure that stores string

Knowledge distillation, the technique of transferring knowledge from large, complex models to smaller ones, has proven effective in various computer vision tasks. However, current methods are not able to generate diverse representations of a given input image across different scales and orientations. In this work we introduce scale-oriented knowledge distillation (SOKD), a novel method that addresses this issue by introducing an additional loss term focused on generating consistent representations across scale and orientation in the output. To evaluate our approach, we conducted experiments using the Flickr30k Entities dataset and compared SOKD with standard knowledge distillation approaches. Our results demonstrate that SOKD significantly improves the consistency of generated representati

The healthcare industry has been revolutionized by the convergence of Artificial Intelligence of Medical Things (AIoMT) and 5G. These technologies are changing the way patients receive care, from diagnosis to treatment, through remote monitoring and personalized medicine. In this blog post, we will explore the impact of AIoMT and 5G on healthcare and their potential to transform the industry in the coming years. We'll discuss the challenges and opportunities presented by these technologies and what the future of healthcare looks like with them. Whether you're a patient, healthcare provider, or an industry professional, understanding how AIoMT and 5G are reshaping the healthcare landscape is crucial for navigating the ever-changing world of healthcare technology. So, let's delve deeper into

The exponential growth of scientific literature requires effective management and extraction of valuable insights. While existing scientific information retrieval systems can provide satisfactory performance for keyword search, they fall short when users want to find relevant research articles in a specific field without knowing the exact keywords or have knowledge about recent developments on their topic area (i.e., cold start problem). To tackle this issue effectively at scale with limited resources from researchers' side while maintaining high accuracy levels that would otherwise be difficult due primarily because there is no ground truth dataset available today which makes it hard enough as well - we propose an end-toend approach based upon deep learning techniques such AS BERT/GPT2 mo

In this paper, we present FCDS, a novel approach for fusing constituency and dependency syntax into relation triplet extraction from full-text documents. Relation triplet extraction is a crucial but challenging task in information extraction and question answering. It aims to extract semantic relations among entities in the text, represented as triplets of subject, relation, and object. In this task, semantic role labeling (SRL) plays a crucial part in identifying the relation between entities. Previous approaches mainly focus on extracting semantic roles based on either constituency or dependency syntax, but not both. To leverage the strength of both syntactic representations, we propose a novel fully supervised learning approach called FCDS, which fuses the information from both representations. We first design a neural network-based model, named Semantic Role Fusion Network (SRFNet), to learn the representation of semantic roles. Then, we propose a novel document-level relation triplet extraction model, named TRIPNet, to extract relation triplets from the entire document. Our experimental results on the CoNLL 2014 dataset, one of the largest relation triplet extraction datasets, show that our approach outperforms the state-of-the-art methods by a significant margin.

In this paper, channel estimation problem for extremely large-scale multi-input multi-output (XL-MIMO) systems is investigated. We propose a novel non-orthogonal multiple access (NOMA)-based method to estimate the individual channels between user and base station (BS). Specifically, we design a low complexity two-stage algorithm that exploits NOMA technique to enhance the robustness of channel estimations. Our proposed algorithm first estimates the phase information of all users' pilot signals based on the assumption that their received powers are approximately equal. Then it recovers the phase of each user's data symbols based on the estimated phase of its pilot signal and the average power of all users' data signals. Theoretical analysis shows that our algorithm attains an overall perfor

Deep neural networks have been widely used in autonomous driving. However, existing works lack a principled approach to integrate the model training and deployment pipeline with the autonomous driving system. In this paper, we propose ActiveAD, a novel active learning framework for end-to-end autonomous driving that jointly optimizes the model and the control policy. ActiveAD uses a planning-oriented loss that incorporates the driving policy and vehicle dynamics into the model training process. This loss function encourages the model to produce outputs that are feasible and desirable for autonomous driving. ActiveAD also employs a sampling-based approach to select the most informative samples for model retraining. We evaluate ActiveAD on a challenging autonomous driving dataset that features diverse traffic scenarios in an urban environment. ActiveAD achieves state-of-the-art performance in terms of training a deep neural network for autonomous driving. Moreover, ActiveAD's integration of model training and deployment with autonomous driving demonstrates the importance of considering the driving policy and vehicle dynamics in deep neural network training for autonomous driving systems.

Molecular images are commonly used in drug discovery, structural biology, and chemical engineering. However, the analysis and processing of molecular images are time-consuming and challenging as they require a large amount of computational resources. Recently, deep learning models have shown high accuracy and efficiency in image recognition tasks, making them well-suited for analyzing molecular images. We propose MolNexTR, a generalized deep learning model for molecular image recognition. MolNexTR is based on a convolutional neural network architecture, which has been proven to be effective for image recognition tasks. It consists of several convolutional layers, followed by max pooling layers, and then fully connected layers. To train our model, we use the MolDTC dataset, which is a large-scale, diverse dataset of molecular images. We show that MolNexTR outperforms other state-of-the-art models on the MolDTC dataset. Furthermore, we demonstrate the transferability of MolNexTR by evaluating its performance on three other molecular image datasets.

In this study, a human evaluation is carried out on how hyperparameter settings impact the quality of Transformer-based models. The results show that for different datasets, the relationship between hyperparameters and model performance is not necessarily consistent. It has been found that some hyperparameters have a significant impact on the performance, while others do not. For example, it was observed that larger learning rate often leads to better performance in some cases, but smaller batch size does not always lead to better performance. These findings suggest that a more systematic approach to tune hyperparameters may be necessary in order to obtain optimal model performance. This study also discusses the limitations of existing hyperparameter optimization methods and provides sugge

The massive generation of multimodal fake news exhibits substantial distribution discrepancies, prompting the demand for a generalized evaluation benchmark. However, current evaluation methods are either limited to specific types or rely on manually annotated datasets. To address these challenges, we introduce FakeGPT-2, a large-scale generative model that integrates text and image modalities and produces synthetic multimodal fake news. To evaluate its quality, we propose an automatic classification method to assess the similarity between generated and real samples based on their mutual information (MI) distributions in the feature space. Experimental results show that our proposed MI-based method achieves superior performance with only 1/3 of the data required by the existing state-of-the

Current approaches to personality-aware dialogue often rely on pre-defined personality profiles. However, such profiles are not always readily available, and even when they are, they can be inaccurate or outdated. In this paper, we introduce IDA, a novel framework that learns personality-aware representations in a purely data-driven manner, without the need for pre-defined profiles. IDA identifies relevant information about the user's personality directly from dialogue history. To achieve this, we propose a new approach to modeling personality-aware dialogue: we construct a separate representation for each dialogue participant, which captures both the user's personality and the context of the current dialogue. These individual representations are then combined into a joint representation using a novel in-dialogue collaborative filtering approach. We evaluate IDA on two publicly available datasets for personality-aware dialogue, and demonstrate that IDA outperforms state-of-the-art methods, while also being more flexible and scalable. Our code is publicly available at https://github.com/IndigoPurple/IDA.

Large language models (LLMs) such as ChatGPT are increasingly proficent at generating text that is difficult to distinguish from human-written text. We propose an adversarial attack that exploits the inherent inability of LLMs to generate content outside their training distribution. Specifically, we introduce a two-stage process: first, we prompt the victim model with a malicious input and then use the output as a seed for another generation, aiming to elicit an untrustworthy response. Our results show that the proposed method effectively prompts LLM outputs to include toxic words, while also maintaining plausibility and avoiding detection by existing detectors. For example, our approach can fool 73% of users into believing the generated text is harmless when it contains toxic content. The

Sensemaking on a large collection of documents (corpus) is a challenging task often performed by domain experts. In this work, we introduce a novel framework called Vision-Oral-Sensemaker that leverages the complementary nature of visual and oral cues for document sensemaking. We develop three datasets to evaluate our approach: i) a visual dataset comprising 295k images from the Flickr30K Entities with labels and descriptions; ii) an audio dataset consisting of 487 audio clips collected from YouTube, each containing information about a specific image; and iii) a textual dataset featuring 15k Wikipedia pages describing 650k entities. Our method employs deep learning models trained on each modality to generate representations that are then combined using a Transformer encoder to produce a si

Identifying errors in parallel MPI programs is a challenging task. Despite the growing number of verification tools, existing techniques are still limited by a lack of efficiency. In this paper, we propose PARSE, an automated parallel error detection tool using symbolic execution for MPI-2 programs. We employ novel techniques to speed up symbolic execution and generate precise error reports, which can be used as the input for traditional error detectors. Our evaluations on a large benchmark suite show that our approach can identify most errors with high precision and completeness, surpassing state-of-the-art tools, while being orders of magnitude faster than existing approaches. The source code, data sets, and generated reports are available at https://github.com/hkust-sse/PARSE.git. Code,

We consider a robust motion planning problem for a robot that needs to visit a number of locations in a given order while minimizing travel time. The robot's speed is constant, known, and subject to change due to terrain variations. Each location is associated with a Normal distribution that represents the time the robot needs to navigate from one location to another. The parameters of these Normal distributions are unknown to the robot, but the robot has a prior belief about the mean navigation time and the correlation structure among navigation times. The robot needs to find a visitation order for the locations that minimizes the expected travel time while ensuring a certain level of robustness with respect to the uncertainty on navigation times. We formulate the problem as a Markov Information Tracking (MIT) problem, for which we provide a polynomial time optimal solution. We then extend the problem to include a time-varying robustness requirement, and provide an approximate solution based on our previous results. We also discuss how our approach can be used to implement a robust version of the Traveling Salesman Problem.

Label noise is a common issue in graph learning that affects the performance of the model. Previous works have focused on the abundance of label noise, assuming that there is a majority class. These methods, however, may not perform well when the majority class is not evident. In this work, we propose a new topology-based method to mitigate label noise. We first present a new topological measure, called density, that we use as a proxy for majority class in our problem. We then use density to guide our sample selection and construct a noise-free dataset to train a model. We show that our method performs well on synthetic and real-world graphs with varying levels of label noise. We also show that our method is complementary to existing methods that rely on majority class.

Human trajectory simulation is a powerful tool for urban planning and management. While many trajectory simulation models have been proposed recently to replicate human mobility patterns, they suffer from two major limitations: 1) Most models lack the ability to generate trajectories for large-scale populations. As a result, their deployment in urban planning applications is limited. 2) These models often rely on complex parameter tuning or the use of sophisticated neural networks to capture seasonality and other temporal effects. To address these issues, we propose a novel Cross-city Transformer, namely COLA, for human trajectory simulation. The proposed model is able to generate realistic trajectories for large-scale populations across multiple cities. It achieves this by adopting a city-agnostic approach, which allows for the learning of transferable representations that can be applied to various cities. Additionally, the model incorporates a trajectory transformer with auto-regressive properties, enabling the generation of long-term trajectories without the need for complex parameter tuning or sophisticated neural networks. We validate the proposed COLA Transformer on real-world mobility data from multiple cities. Our results demonstrate that COLA outperforms state-of-the-art baselines in terms of trajectory prediction accuracy, while also showing its superior scalability and transferability. As a testament to its effectiveness, we have successfully deployed COLA as a web-based application, providing a user-friendly interface for urban planners and managers to simulate human trajectories in their cities.

Recent breakthroughs in Large Language Models (LLMs) have revolutionized natural language understanding and generation. In the field of natural language processing, LLMs have shown tremendous potential in various tasks such as text summarization, question answering, code completion, etc., but their success has also led to some concerns regarding their reliability and interpretability. For example, they can sometimes produce incorrect or irrelevant answers to questions, fail to understand contextual information, lack transparency about their reasoning process, and generate harmful content. While existing methods have been proposed for improving the performance of LLMs, they focus more on training and optimizing existing LLMs rather than developing new ones from scratch. To tackle this issue

Routine visual inspections of concrete structures are imperative for upholding the safety and functionality of these vital structures. However, traditional inspection methods often rely on manual and time-consuming processes, making them prone to human error and inefficiencies. To address this challenge, a novel solution has emerged in the form of a self-driving inspection vehicle designed specifically for the monitoring of concrete structures. This paper presents an innovative methodology that employs a robotic platform capable of autonomously navigating through complex terrains with minimal human intervention. Equipped with advanced sensors and imaging technology, the vehicle is engineered to accurately identify potential issues within concrete structures, such as cracks, erosion, and de

Advanced language-processing and machine-learning techniques promise massive efficiency improvements in the previously widely manual field of image annotation. Researchers at Facebook's AI Research (FAIR) team have recently developed a new neural network model, called "MIST," that can automatically annotate millions or billions images by using existing annotated data from other sources as training samples for learning tasks such classification with deep convolutional networks through supervised fine tuning processes on large unlabeled datasets via self teaching method without requiring any additional human supervision during testing stage unlike traditional approaches where most information comes directly into system after completion; this allows them to achieve 94% accuracy across all cat

Federated Learning (FL) is a practical approach to train deep learning models collaboratively across user-owned devices while protecting privacy. The FL training process typically consists of two major steps: the parameter aggregation step and the model update step, where each client uploads its own parameters to a central server for aggregation and then receives back updated parameters. As more clients join in FL, the communication overhead increases significantly with the number of clients. To address this problem, we propose a novel algorithm called Efficient FedProx, which achieves significant performance improvements compared to previous work by minimizing both the number of rounds required to reach convergence and the total communication cost. Specifically, we first introduce an adap

Sourced from various sensors and organized chronologically, Multivariate Time-Series (MTS) data is widely used to characterize human activities for applications such as activity recognition and anomaly detection. For example, a smart home MTS dataset may include sensor readings that capture the occurrence of opening/closing doors or windows, switching on/off lights, playing music, etc., which can be used to detect abnormalities or characterize daily routines. Existing state-of-the-art approaches either leverage complex data processing techniques to preprocess each time series individually or use domain knowledge to design feature representations of the entire sequence. However, both strategies ignore the fact that MTS often contain multiple interconnected time series with common semantics

Few-shot anomaly detection (FSAD) is essential in industrial manufacturing. However, most existing methods heavily rely on handcrafted features or pretrained models for feature extraction and do not consider the inherent uncertainty of real-world data. To address these challenges, we propose a novel framework named PDVNet that utilizes uncertainty estimation and dynamic modeling to enhance the robustness and accuracy of few-shot anomaly detection in industrial manufacturing. Our method first employs an ensemble of pretrained neural networks to extract contextual features from raw signals with high uncertainty.
