Then, it proposes a new Dynamic Contrastive Loss (DCLOSS) module to learn temporal-aware representations by incorporating both temporal and spatial information of the target signal.

News media has been utilized as a political tool to stray from facts, presenting biased information that shapes public opinion. The current study examined the effects of fake news on political polarization and trust in government among adults across 16 nations using data obtained through an online survey (n = 4053). This research revealed significant differences between participants who consumed false or accurate articles regarding their perceptions toward politicians' trustworthiness levels before voting for candidates at national elections held over two years ago when they were exposed repeatedly with both types during one week period prior election day itself; this effect was stronger within countries where there exists higher prevalence rates towards misinformation consumption overall

Defocus blur is a persistent problem in microscope imaging that poses harm to pathology image analysis. In this paper, we propose a novel deep learning-based method called DualDeblurNet to tackle defocus blur in histopathological images. Our approach leverages two convolutional neural networks (CNNs) for defocus blur removal. One CNN is used as the deblurring network which takes a single defocused input image and outputs a sharpened image as its prediction. The other CNN works as the feature extraction network which takes a pair of focused and defocused images and outputs a feature representation of the defocused image. We train the feature extraction network with a loss function designed to minimize the distance between the features extracted from the defocused image and the features extr

Grading short answer questions automatically with interpretable reasoning behind the grading decision is a challenging goal for current systems. This paper presents a new approach to model multiple instructors' gradings of short answer questions by learning a set of shared features (e.g., question type and number of correct answers) that contribute to the final score, as well as unique aspects from each instructor (e.g., their individual preference on what constitutes an acceptable answer). To learn this representation, we introduce an iterative training scheme where we use one teacher-student pair at a time, to ensure that each student learns only the knowledge they need from their respective teacher, thereby avoiding overfitting. Experimental results show our approach outperforms state-o

Data integration and visual data analysis are two important pillars in the process of making decisions based on data. The authors of this paper have noticed that in many decision support systems these two activities are separated. Typically, a data integration module prepares data for analysis, and then an analysis module produces some kind of a report, which is sometimes visual. The two modules are usually implemented by different tools, and they operate on different data formats. This separation makes the analysis process inflexible and inefficient. The paper proposes preliminary guidelines for combining data integration and visual data analysis into a single visual analysis module. The module should provide functionalities for data preparation, data exploration, and model building in a single place. It should also support an iterative and interactive analysis process. The paper describes an architecture of such a module, and shows how it can be applied to a real-life data analysis scenario.

Learning from biased data is an important concern in machine learning. Class imbalance, in particular, is known to cause learning biases in a variety of supervised learning tasks. However, the effect of class imbalance in multimodal learning is poorly understood. In this work, we study the effect of class imbalance in Multimodal Learning with Transformers (MLiT), a powerful recent framework for learning from multimodal data. We find that MLiT is vulnerable to class imbalance, exhibiting suboptimal performance when training on imbalanced datasets. To mitigate this, we propose a simple data balancing approach and show that it can significantly improve the class prediction accuracy of MLiT. Interestingly, we find that balancing the data has little to no effect on the model's ability to learn the underlying data distribution, as measured by a generative proxy. Our results suggest that class imbalance in multimodal data is a real concern for MLiT, and that data balancing can be a useful tool to mitigate this issue in practice.

Confusing charge prediction is a serious issue in the natural language processing domain. Similar words often have different grammatical functions, making it difficult for machines to correctly identify the part of speech of a given token. This problem is especially prominent in domains where words are heavily overloaded, such as natural language processing. In this paper, we propose a novel approach to disambiguate tokens with heavily overloaded words by introducing domain knowledge. We introduce a method of encoding part-of-speech confusion using directed-acyclic graphs, where nodes represent words and edges represent grammatical relationships. We then utilize a neural network to learn the graph representations of different parts of speech. Finally, we use these representations to inform our prediction of the part of speech of tokens with heavily overloaded words. Our results show an improvement on token-level classification accuracy from 91.7% to 92.5% in our domain of interest, electrical engineering. While the improvement may seem small, it is statistically significant, and our approach is easily adaptable to other domains with heavily overloaded words.

Radio advertising remains an integral part of modern marketing strategies, with its appeal and potential for audience engagement making it a valuable tool. However, the process can be daunting if you’re new to radio ads or haven't used them before – from choosing your station(s), crafting compelling copy that captures attention quickly without being too salesy (or boring! ), tracking results & ROI…it all adds up! Fortunately we have put together this guide so as soon as possible get started on creating effective campaigns within budget while leveraging reach across multiple platforms at once; no matter how large-scale operation might seem when starting out small steps are key towards success here - let us show exactly what those look like now!.

Human annotation of natural language data introduces variability due to annotator labeling differences and systematic errors specific to individual annotators. We introduce VariErr, a framework for separating systematic errors from human label variation, enabling accurate characterization of annotation error. VariErr consists of a variant of hierarchical Dirichlet process mixture models for distributions over labels, which we apply to natural language classification data. We demonstrate the utility of VariErr for annotator study and label bias analysis by applying it to CoNLL 2014 shared task data.

The evolutionary paradigm has been successfully applied to neural network search(NAS) in recent years, which can automatically evolve a high-performance NAS model from a simple initial seed. However, these methods often suffer from low efficiency due to the complex search space and large number of candidate models. To address this issue, we propose an efficient evolutionary framework named Evol-EvoNet for NAS that focuses on improving both performance and efficiency. Firstly, we introduce a new evolution operator called EvoNet, which utilizes the information contained in the existing neural architecture to guide the exploration process, thereby enhancing the diversity of the evolved population. Secondly, we use EvolNet as the base network to evolve the parameter space, which enables the ev

This work is an attempt to introduce a comprehensive benchmark for Arabic speech recognition, specifically tailored towards the development of automatic transcription systems. This task was tackled in this study as part and parcel with developing such system using deep neural networks (DNN) based on multi-layer perceptrons trained by backpropagation algorithm from raw audio signals via time domain features extracted directly without any preprocessing or normalization step at all levels: training data preparation; feature extraction & selection during learning phase(s); model building \& fine tuning after convergence has been reached followed eventuallyby testing/evaluation against ground truth reference text samples takenfrom various sources including but not limitedto TV series episodes a

In human-robot interaction (HRI), we study how humans interact with robots, but also how they perceive and react to them. This paper introduces a novel method for understanding social behaviors of robots from videos in the wild. First, we propose an open source software toolkit for automatically identifying robotic movements from raw video sequences. Second, we introduce a framework for analyzing social behavior patterns by annotating videos with both high-level and low-level behaviors. We use our annotations to assess the usefulness of different motion features as input into behavior recognition systems. Third, we propose a new approach for automatically labeling videos based on their perceived behavioral intentions: is a given video likely to be positive, neutral or negative? The results

An efficient customer service management system hinges on precise forecasting of service volume. In this study, we focus on the forecasting task for service volume in retail customer service scenarios and propose a novel model named Service Volume Forecasting with Inter-Arrival Time (SVFIT). SVFIT is based on two key insights: (1) inter-arrival time distribution reveals essential information about service demand; (2) different arrival patterns exist in different time windows, which should be taken into account when building forecasting models. Accordingly, SVFIT introduces a new mechanism to mine the inter-arrival time distribution and identify the most representative arrival pattern in each time window, which are then used to learn the forecasting model. Extensive experiments on multiple

Existing approaches on zero-shot event detection usually train models on datasets annotated with known event types, and then apply them to detect events of unseen types. However, many real-world scenarios require detecting events that have never been seen before, such as detecting a novel event type that has only occurred once. To address this challenge, we introduce the novel task of single-shot event detection, which aims to detect events when there is only one example available for each event type during training. In particular, our goal is to achieve high detection accuracy in a few shots (e.g., 1 shot). We present a simple yet effective method called Shot Detector, which leverages both video-level and frame-level features to effectively capture temporal information within the limited

Tool learning empowers large language models (LLMs) as agents to use external tools to accomplish tasks, but is challenging due in part because many LLMS struggle with syntax and the nuances of using code. To alleviate this challenge we present CodeCoach: a tool-learning framework that provides contextualized feedback for LLM training through an interactive teaching experience similar human tutoring sessions -- both via text chat or video calls where users can provide visual context during their interactions - allowing learners access guidance from knowledgeable experts who understand how they learn best while also enabling instructors greater flexibility when designing curricula tailored specifically around individual student needs . Our experiments demonstrate effective knowledge acquisi

Graph-structured data exhibits universality and widespread applicability across diverse domains, such as chemistry [1] biology/medicine[2], physics,[3],[4]. Graph neural networks (GNNs) have been successfully applied to solve graph learning problems. However , one of the most challenging issues in GCN research is oversmoothing: when applying deep layers on a network model with high connectivity graphs or large neighborhood size sets for each node; this phenomenon causes gradient descent during training stages leading towards poor performance outcomes due their inability cope effectively against complex structures within datasets being studied.

Diffusion model-based low-light image enhancement methods rely heavily on paired training data, but the limited availability of such data presents a significant challenge for their widespread adoption. In this work, we introduce an unsupervised diffusion modeling approach that leverages self-supervision and inversion networks to address this issue. Our method is designed to learn from unpaired data through a series of pre-training and supervised refinement steps. We demonstrate its effectiveness by integrating it into a low-light image enhancement framework, and showcase its ability to achieve substantial performance gains over existing state-of-the-art approaches without requiring any labeled data. Moreover, our experiments reveal that this approach outperforms existing models even when t

Language models are often touted as a new and improved form of artificial intelligence, with impressive results in many benchmark tests. However, it is not clear whether these models actually possess the underlying algorithmic skills that are often assumed. In this paper, we present a novel approach to assessing algorithmic reasoning in AI models by asking them to solve algorithmic puzzles, in a setup that allows us to observe the reasoning process, step by step. We chose algorithmic puzzles for two reasons: (1) they are a good test for core algorithmic reasoning skills, going beyond surface similarities; (2) solving algorithmic puzzles generates natural language utterances, allowing us to collect "internal monologues" that provide insight into the reasoning process. Our study reveals that language models solve algorithmic puzzles in ways that are similar to how humans solve them -- by exploring the problem space in a systematic, step-by-step manner that resembles a search algorithm. Moreover, we found major differences between how our language model solves algorithmic puzzles in comparison to how it generates natural language in response to human-like questions, highlighting serious challenges in multimodal reasoning.

We propose a simple, fast, and training-free method to merge two pretrained models into one. We show that a linear combination of the two model parameters, initialized with a small amount of random noise, can lead to better performance than the individual models on a number of natural language classification and generation tasks. We also find that the merged model can be further improved by fine-tuning on a small amount of training data.

Modern life sciences research is increasingly relying on artificial intelligence approaches to model biological systems, primarily focused on molecular networks and protein-protein interactions. However, the majority of these methods are limited to small-scale networks, often with less than 100 nodes. Scalability becomes a major challenge as networks grow in size, affecting their utility for large-scale applications. Our study introduces an innovative approach that addresses this limitation by utilizing the power of deep neural networks (DNNs) in conjunction with graph convolutional networks (GCNs). The proposed method employs a novel self-attention mechanism to capture complex dependencies between nodes within the network, thereby enhancing the accuracy of predicted interactions. This app

It has long been assumed that the sheer number of parameters in large language models (LLMs) is what gives them their impressive performance. However, a new study suggests that this may not be the case. Instead, it appears that LLMs can benefit from pruning and fine-tuning to achieve equivalent or even superior performance with fewer model parameters. This finding could potentially open up new avenues for improving the efficiency and effectiveness of LLMs. The study conducted by OpenAI's GPT Team found that pruning an LLM with 175 billion parameters down to 10 billion parameters resulted in only a minor decrease in performance on a range of natural language processing tasks, such as question answering and translation. Furthermore, the researchers discovered that fine-tuning the pruned vers

Person re-identification (Re-ID), the task of identifying target persons across multiple camera views, has attracted much attention in the past decades. Recent advances in this field have shown that, by modeling the spatial-temporal relationship within each video, the performance of person Re-ID can be improved. However, most existing methods fail to capture the global information of the human body over time, which is important for person identification. In this paper, we propose a novel global, local body part, and head streams (GLBPH) feature learning approach for person Re-ID. Specifically, we divide the whole body into local body parts and the head, and model the global information of the human body, including the overall appearance and the motion of the whole body. Moreover, we propose a body part stream that captures the spatial-temporal relationship of local body parts and the head. It models the holistic motion of the human body from the appearances of local body parts and the head. Furthermore, we propose a head stream that captures the identity information of the head. It models the identity-related motion of the human body. The three streams are fused to generate the final Re-ID score. Experimental results on three widely-used person Re-ID datasets show that our method outperforms state-of-the-art methods.

Weakly supervised text classification (WSTC), also called zero-shot or dataless text classification, is an important task in many fields. The main challenge lies in how to extract the semantic information of target classes from the limited annotated texts of source classes. To tackle this problem, we propose a novel method named RSC-BERT. It adopts the BERT framework and employs a self-attention mechanism to learn the semantic representations of target classes. In addition, two self-supervision tasks are designed based on the pre-trained representation of source classes to further enhance the semantic representations of target classes. Extensive experiments on four benchmark datasets show that our model outperforms other competitive methods by 3.90%, 2.84%, 3.14% and 2.56% in terms of F1-m

Accurate traffic forecasting is challenging due to the complex dependency on road networks, various modes of transportation, and time-varying demands. The existing traffic prediction methods typically focus on individual time series without considering the correlations among them. In this paper, we introduce a novel traffic forecasting framework that considers both intra- and inter-sequence dependencies to improve the accuracy of traffic forecasts. Specifically, we propose a Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) network with a novel gated fusion mechanism for efficient learning of long-term dependencies across different sequences. The proposed method effectively captures the temporal correlations between traffic patterns in adjacent time intervals by employing a recurrent neural network architectu

We introduce and study a combinatorial problem that we call the Exchange Problem. In its general form, the Exchange Problem can be stated as follows: there are $n$ boxes, some of which may contain a total of $k$ balls. In every move, one can choose two boxes and exchange the contents of these boxes. The goal is to move all the balls into a contiguous range of boxes using the minimum number of moves. We show that the minimum number of moves is $\lfloor n - k + 1 \rfloor$. We also provide efficient algorithms to compute the optimal sequence of moves to achieve this bound. We then give several applications of the Exchange Problem.

We consider a discrete-time system where a resource-constrained source (e.g., a data center) shares its capacity with multiple receivers (e.g., users). The capacity allocation is determined by solving a joint utility maximization problem, where the utility of each receiver depends on both the allocated resources and its queue length. To solve this problem, we introduce a new utility function called "concave submodularity" that satisfies some properties similar to those for standard submodular functions while also satisfying additional desirable properties such as strong duality and efficiency. Using our new utility function, we show how to develop a dynamic programming algorithm for capacity allocation that is efficient in both memory and time. Additionally, we study the performance of the

Learning to use tools is a crucial skill for independence in daily life. However, it can be challenging to learn these skills from traditional instruction, as learners may not have the opportunity to practice using the tools in a variety of scenarios. Cooperative and interactive agents, such as those found in video games, could provide this opportunity for practice and potentially improve learning outcomes. The goal of this study was to explore the use of cooperative and interactive agents as a tool learning environment. We conducted a Wizard-of-Oz exploratory study with 170 participants to investigate their perceptions and preferences for learning to use a new tool (i.e., a virtual credit card) via cooperative and interactive agents. Our results provide valuable insights for designing cooperative and interactive agents that can enhance the tool learning experience and outcomes. We found that participants preferred the cooperative condition over the interactive condition, as they felt more supported and less frustrated in the cooperative condition. The interactive condition, however, allowed participants to learn more about the tool's features. These findings suggest that a mixed-initiative approach, where both human and agent partners take turns demonstrating and practicing, may be optimal for learning to use tools.

In this paper, we propose Large Language Model-Based Evolutionary Optimizer (LLMEO), a new evolutionary optimizer that leverages existing knowledge and adapts to the problem at hand using a large language model (LLM). Our proposed LLMEO maintains a population of candidate solutions and uses natural language generation to evaluate and reason about the candidates. It then uses the reasoning results to guide the generation of new candidates, mimicking the black-box optimization process in nature. As LLMEO generates new candidates based on the reasoning results, it can leverage the vast knowledge stored in the LLMs, resulting in high optimization performance. To showcase the potential of LLMEO, we tested our optimizer on a set of benchmark problems. The experimental results demonstrate that LLMEO outperforms other evolutionary algorithms by a significant margin. Furthermore, LLMEO can provide reasoning results in natural language, making it easier for humans to understand the optimization process and the features of each candidate solution.

Graph is a fundamental mathematical structure in characterizing relations between different objects and has been widely used on various real-world applications, including social media platforms such as Facebook. As the number of users increases rapidly to billions worldwide with more connections formed among them everyday through new relationships like friends or followers etc., it becomes difficult for companies managing these sites' resources (e g , servers) due not only having large amounts data but also being able process fast enough keep up demand from user requests without experiencing downtime issues caused by overload conditions arising when too many simultaneous transactions occur simultaneously across all available nodes within network architecture deployed at each location where

Recently, LoFTR has attracted considerable attention for its efficient local feature extraction and description. However, its counterpart global feature extraction and description is less explored. In this work, we fill the gap by proposing a novel semi-dense local feature matching method, termed Efficient LoFTR, which extracts and describes local features in an efficient manner. Specifically, we introduce two techniques to reduce the computational complexity of LoFTR. The first technique is a sparse convolutional pyramid network (SCPN), which approximates the feature extraction process of LoFTR and reduces the computational cost from 77.2% to 6.8% on the test set. The second technique is a sparse descriptor, which reduces the feature description cost from 44.7% to 3.2% on the test set. Our Efficient LoFTR achieves a speed of 7.9M pts/s on a single GPU and a match accuracy of 86.4% on the test set of HPatches. Compared to the original LoFTR, our method achieves a 3.5x speedup on a single GPU while maintaining 95% of the match accuracy. In addition, our method outperforms existing local feature matching methods, including 3DLoFTR, in terms of speed and match accuracy. Our code is available at https://github.com/luost27/Efficient-LoFTR.

LiDAR-based 3D object detection has made significant progress in recent years. However, the performance of LiDAR-based methods on public datasets is limited due to the lack of consideration for cross-modal and domain adversarial information, which are crucial for real-world deployment. In this work, we propose a novel framework called Cross-Modal and Domain Adversarial Adaptation (CMDA), which consists of a LiDAR-agnostic feature extractor, a domain adversarial network, and a cross-modal fusion module. The LiDAR-agnostic feature extractor is designed to produce consistent features across different modalities and domains, while the domain adversarial network is introduced to adversarialize the extractor's output against domain differences. The cross-modal fusion module then combines the adversarialized features with the original LiDAR data. Additionally, we adopt a bootstrapping scheme to further improve the extractor's robustness. Extensive experiments on the KITTI and NuScenes datasets demonstrate that our approach significantly outperforms previous LiDAR-based methods in terms of performance under different conditions. Our work is publicly available for reproducibility purposes at https://github.com/VDIGPKU/CMDA.

Federated learning (FL), introduced in 2017, facilitates collaborative learning among multiple clients with heterogeneous local data and limited communication bandwidth. The FL framework has been widely applied to various fields due to its advantages of privacy protection and resource-efficient training. However, most existing FL studies focus on the effectiveness of models rather than the efficiency of model training. In this paper, we propose a federated multi-task learning (FMTL) algorithm for improving the convergence speed of the FL process while maintaining the accuracy of the model. Specifically, FMTL introduces auxiliary task losses that are used to train auxiliary tasks on the server side and share their knowledge with the main task, enabling the clients to use less local training

3D visual grounding involves matching natural language descriptions with their corresponding objects in 3D environments. This task is a central component of the broader 3D scene understanding problem, and has gained significant attention due to its inherent complexity. Recently, there have been notable advancements in this field, particularly through the development of large-scale datasets such as Visual Grounding (VG) and VG2. However, despite these recent developments, there remain limitations in the current methods that hinder their effectiveness in real-world scenarios. In this work, we identify two crucial challenges for existing approaches: 1) their reliance on pre-trained models and domain adaption techniques, and 2) their tendency toward a single-stage pipeline design. To address t

Battery technology is increasingly important for global electrification efforts. However, batteries are highly sensitive to small manufacturing variations that can induce reliability or safety issues. An important technology for battery quality control is computed tomography (CT) scanning, which is widely used for non-destructive 3D inspection across a variety of clinical and industrial applications. Historically, however, the utility of CT scanning for high-volume manufacturing has been limited by its low throughput as well as the difficulty of handling its large file sizes. In this work, we present a dataset of over one thousand CT scans of as-produced commercially available batteries. The dataset spans various chemistries (lithium-ion and sodium-ion) as well as various battery form fact

We consider unsupervised domain adaptation (UDA) for semantic segmentation in which the model is trained on source data and evaluated on target data. The common practice is to use a discriminator network that is jointly trained with the encoder-decoder, but this can be computationally expensive. To alleviate this issue, we propose an efficient framework that only uses a few domain classifier networks to perform UDA semantic segmentation. We further explore a simple yet effective training strategy that employs a contrastive loss to align the feature spaces of the source and target domains. This allows us to train our method efficiently while preserving its performance, as demonstrated by extensive experiments on six datasets. Our code will be made available at https://github.com/XinMiaoLab/

Free-proxies have been widespread since the early days of the Web, helping users to bypass geographical barriers and access web content that would otherwise be inaccessible. In recent years, however, free-proxy services have become a popular target for attackers looking to exploit user trust or conduct automated attacks on websites. This paper presents a survey of the literature on free-proxy services as well as a systematic empirical study that explores how these services are currently used by both legitimate and malicious actors. Our findings show that the majority of free-proxies are being used by benign users who are simply looking to avoid geo-restrictions, while others are used by attackers for scraping, click fraud, DDoS attacks, and other types of malicious activity.

To use new robot hardware in a new environment, it is necessary to develop a control program tailored for that specific scenario. This process can be time-consuming and requires significant domain knowledge. In this paper, we propose a method of automatically generating control programs from demonstrations. The core idea behind our approach is to combine a symbolic representation with an action grammar to generate a control program. First, we represent the task as a graph by decomposing it into atomic subtasks using a set of rules based on human intuition about the task. Next, we utilize the action grammar to create a sequence of symbols corresponding to the actions required to complete each subtask. These sequences are then combined to form a complete control program. We evaluated our met

Nowcasting, i.e., short-term weather forecasting, plays a crucial role in mitigating the impacts of severe weather. Despite its importance, precipitation nowcasting remains a challenge, especially for extreme events. Precipitation fields are often represented as rainfall rates, which are discrete, non-negative, and bounded by the measurement error. These characteristics are not well captured by current nowcasting models, especially for extreme precipitation. To address this challenge, we propose a Transformer-based generative model that imposes physical constraints on the precipitation nowcasting problem. Specifically, we develop a Log-Rainfall Transformer (LRT) nowcaster that transforms the original precipitation data into a new space where the Transformer model can learn the temporal and spatial dependencies of extreme precipitation. To improve the nowcasting performance further, we apply attention-based masking to the LRT nowcaster, which allows the model to focus on relevant temporal and spatial locations. Based on the results of the experiments conducted on real-world precipitation datasets, the proposed LRT nowcaster provides a promising solution for the nowcasting of extreme precipitation. It improves the performance of the baseline models by 33.6% on average, relative to the baseline models.

The generation of protein sequences with desired properties is an important task in protein engineering that is often tackled with traditional sequence-to-sequence deep learning models. Recently, there has been growing interest in utilizing language model (LM) embeddings for their ability to capture structural and functional properties of proteins. However, the generation of protein sequences with specific properties using LM embeddings is underexplored. In this work, we introduce a novel method called Diffusion on Language Model embeddings (DLM), which generates protein sequences by diffusing on a graph constructed from LM embeddings. We represent the LM embedding space as a graph, where nodes are LM embeddings and edges are determined by the cosine similarity between the embeddings. We then use a diffusion process on this graph to generate new sequences. The sequences generated by DLM have high alignment scores with the training data and achieve state-of-the-art performance on benchmark datasets. Our experiments show that DLM outperforms other model-based methods and is competitive compared to other sequence generation methods.

Image Coding for Machines (ICM) is an image compression technique for image recognition. It can be used to speed up training of neural networks, or as a lossy data storage format that preserves important details while discarding noise and redundant information – making it ideal for use on low-power devices such like those found in IoT applications where energy consumption must remain at minimum levels In this blog post we will discuss how ICM works by explaining its basic concepts along with some examples from real world problems related video surveillance systems . We’ll also examine why traditional methods fail when dealing big datasets containing thousands upon millions pixels worth images!

Multi-tenant cloud computing provides great benefits in terms of resource sharing, elastic pricing, and rapid deployment. However, when deployed to the cloud, multi-tenant applications must be isolated from each other to ensure the security and privacy of data shared between tenants. In this paper we propose a novel approach for dynamic tenant isolation using secure virtualization techniques. This technique allows us to dynamically allocate resources to different tenants while ensuring their data is securely protected from each other. Our approach uses a combination of hardware assisted virtualization with fine grained memory protection mechanisms to enforce isolation at the page level. We show that our approach can provide significant performance gains compared to traditional hypervisor b

Amidst task-specific learning-based control synthesis frameworks that achieve impressive empirical results, the underlying optimization problem remains challenging to solve due to its inherent high-dimensionality and non-convex nature. We address this issue by proposing a generalization of the standard dynamic programming (DP) approach to incorporate neural networks into the modeling framework. Specifically, we introduce an iterative scheme that leverages deep reinforcement learning (DRL) to estimate optimal state distributions via value iteration in order to formulate a DP-based policy synthesizer. This method provides a unified solution for synthesizing policies across diverse classes of MPC problems, thereby facilitating the design of more robust and adaptable control systems.

Recurrent Spiking Neural Networks (RSNNs) have emerged as a computation framework in the field of deep learning. They are known to offer better energy efficiency, faster training and inference times compared with conventional Artificial Intelligence models such Convolutional neural networks(CNN). However due to their inherent stochasticity caused by sparsification-based neuromorphic hardware platforms or leaky activation functions used during recurrent weight updates they tend to be highly unstable for gradient based optimization methods which necessitates careful tuning on top layers while maintaining stability below them leading towards suboptimal performance when trained endtoend from scratch .

Imitation learning provides an efficient way to teach robots dexterous skills; however, learning complex tasks from demonstrations is difficult because it requires the robot to generalize across a wide range of initial states. This difficulty can be attributed to the sparsity in the reward signal, which causes the agent to learn a policy that is only useful for the specific set of initial states encountered during training. We propose an approach to improve this issue by leveraging adversarial learning. Specifically, we introduce an additional discriminator network that tries to distinguish between samples drawn from two different distributions, and use the gradient of its output with respect to the state as part of the policy update. As a result, our method effectively guides the agent to

Scientific literature analysis is a critical task in various applications, including literature review, meta-analysis, and data extraction. Recently, there has been growing interest in using Large Language Models (LLMs) for scientific literature analysis. However, the performance of LLMs in this task is not well characterized. To benchmark LLM proficiency in scientific literature analysis, we introduce SciAssess, a new framework for evaluating LLMs on scientific papers. SciAssess consists of three comprehensive benchmarking datasets, covering different scientific domains and analysis tasks. The datasets are constructed by extracting relevant passages from real scientific papers and annotating them with task-specific labels. The three tasks evaluated in SciAssess are: (1) scientific fact extraction, (2) claim verification, and (3) sentiment analysis. We provide detailed dataset statistics and analyze the performance of eight strong LLMs on these three tasks. Our results show that LLMs have varying degrees of proficiency in analyzing scientific literature, highlighting the need for benchmarking before adopting LLMs in scientific contexts. SciAssess datasets and results are publicly available.

Sentence semantic matching is a research hotspot in natural language processing, which is considerably significant in various applications such as information retrieval and summarization. The current state-of-the-art models mainly rely on large pre-trained language model (PLM) to generate embeddings for sentences, thus facing challenges in terms of efficiency and robustness. In this paper, we propose a novel framework that utilizes the Transformer Encoder to directly encode sentences into their corresponding representations without any intermediate token embedding generation steps. Experimental results show that our method achieves competitive performance with a relatively lower memory cost, which is significantly reduced by 60%. Moreover, it has higher robustness than existing methods due

We present a novel application of question generation to e-commerce, to assist customers in finding products by asking critical questions at each step of the search process. Customers can thus receive product recommendations that are tailored to their personal preferences. The questions are generated based on the customer's previous interactions with the search results, mimicking the behavior of a helpful salesperson. We release a dataset of over 4000 conversations between real customers and the assistant, as well as a baseline model, to facilitate research in this new task.

The advent of Multilingual Language Models (MLLMs) and Large Language Models (LLMs) has led to the availability of multilingual models capable of handling multiple languages simultaneously. Large Language Models are revolutionizing machine translation, allowing for more accurate and fluent translations across various language pairs. However, this also raises concerns about security and privacy, as sensitive information could be leaked through back-translation methods used by MLLMs in the context of machine translation. This paper presents a comprehensive study on the security risks associated with MLLM-based machine translation, including both open and proprietary MLLMs such as Google's Translatotron, Microsoft's LLaMA, and Meta AI's GPT-4.

We study the effectiveness of data-balancing for mitigating biases in contrastive language models (CLMs). Contrastive learning has been a popular technique to enhance the performance of CLMs, but it also introduces new challenges. Data-balancing is a common method used to address imbalanced datasets; however, it is unclear whether it is beneficial for CLMs. In this work, we conduct extensive experiments on four commonly used datasets and two state-of-the-art CLMs, showing that data-balancing can improve the bias-free performance of CLMs. Moreover, we discover that data-balancing can effectively reduce the negative impact of domain drift. These findings reveal the promising potential of data-balancing for improving the fairness of CLMs.

Language models are known to exhibit biased and unexpected behaviors, but systematically understanding the reasons for these behaviors is out of reach for current black-box models. We present KnowledgeVIS, a novel framework for interpreting the internal knowledge of a language model by comparing its responses to a large number of fill-in-the-blank prompts. We show that the relative order of the model's generated words contains rich information about the model's internal knowledge, and we leverage this observation to develop an intuitive visual representation. We demonstrate the effectiveness of our method by applying it to models with different architectures and pre-training objectives, as well as models from different language families. We find that KnowledgeVIS is able to uncover and visualize biases based on word order, such as grammatical gender and number agreement, as well as semantic role. Furthermore, we show that KnowledgeVIS can help identify prompts that elicit unexpected model behaviors, allowing for the discovery of new biases and the validation of model updates.

The pretrain-finetune paradigm represents a transformative approach in natural language processing (NLP), with the success of large Language models (LLMs) further emphasizing its efficacy. Recent studies have highlighted the potential of pretraining on unlabeled data for enhancing model generalization and alleviating data scarcity. However, the impact of pretraining on out-of-distribution (OOD) detection remains underexplored. In this work, we provide an empirical evaluation of the role of pretraining on OOD detection performance and investigate how different pretrained backbones influence the detectors' sensitivity to OOD inputs. Our results suggest that the OOD detection capability of pretrained models is significantly influenced by their choice of pretraining objectives. We also find th

Large language models (LLMs) have shown great potential in complex reasoning tasks, yet their performance suffers when faced with noisy input. To better understand why LLMS fail to solve more difficult problems and how they perform at different stages of a problem solving process on the same inputs we conducted an interpretability study that revealed some interesting insights about LLM behavior: 1.) They are not able or willing enough explore alternative solutions even if presented directly during fine-tuning; instead preferring familiar paths regardless whether beneficial outcome occurs due lack confidence decision making capabilities beyond simple heuristics like greedy search strategies which can lead incorrect conclusions quickly without considering other possible outcomes leading poor

This paper proposes Group Activity Feature (GAF) learning in which features of multi-person group activities are extracted and used for the task of person re-identification. We demonstrate that a single activity feature is often insufficient to effectively identify individuals. Instead, we propose to extract multi-person group activity features with a 3D convolutional neural network and use them as input features for person re-identification. The proposed GAF is learned using a novel method named Cross-View Training. During training, we first train an individual activity classifier on one view of each image and then train another classifier on the other view of each image. These two classifiers are then combined by cross-view training to learn a group activity feature extractor. Cross-view

The prevalence of misinformation in online media has given rise to a pressing need for reliable fact-checking systems. While conventional text-based approaches have struggled with misinformation detection in diverse contexts, multimodal data, including images and text, hold promise for improving detection accuracy. However, the task of misinformation detection often requires nuanced understanding and contextual awareness, which are challenging to achieve through traditional methods. Large language models (LLMs) like GPT-4 have shown capability in generating human-like text, but they lack the transparency required for understanding their decision-making processes. To address these limitations, we introduce SNIFFER, a multimodal LLM designed for explainable misinformation detection. SNIFFER integrates vision and language through a dual-encoder architecture, allowing it to harness the contextual cues in images and text effectively. To ensure transparency and human-understandable explanations, SNIFFER employs an attention-based design that highlights the key visual and textual elements driving its decisions. We evaluate SNIFFER on a visually-rich dataset that includes images and textual contexts, comparing it to vision-language pretraining baselines and popular fact-checking models. Our results demonstrate that SNIFFER outperforms these baselines by a significant margin, achieving state-of-the-art performance. Additionally, we validate the model's attention mechanism to confirm its ability to identify relevant visual and textual elements. SNIFFER opens new avenues for developing transparent, explainable systems to tackle misinformation across diverse contexts.

Mining transactional data to produce association rules is a common technique used to discover meaningful patterns in business transaction databases. However, the generated rules are often limited to capturing simple relationships within the data, and do not take the form of complex business actions. To overcome this limitation, this paper proposes a method to extend association rules into complex business actions, by integrating collaborative filtering techniques with transactional data mining. Specifically, we use the Apriori algorithm, the FSA-RED algorithm, and the M5P predictive algorithm as the basis of our method, and we propose three new algorithms to produce extended association rules: Collaborative Apriori, which uses the cosine similarity measure to collaborative filtering; FSA-RED, which uses the Jaccard similarity measure to collaborative filtering; and M5P, which uses the M5P decision tree to predict business actions. We apply these algorithms to a real-world transaction database, and we evaluate the quality of the generated extended association rules using precision, recall and coverage metrics. The results of our experiments show that our methods are effective in producing meaningful extended association rules, and that they can be used to improve the performance of business actions.

News media has been utilized as a political tool to stray from facts, presenting biased information in order to garner attention and increase ratings. While this type of bias is not new, social media has created an open forum for these sources, allowing them to easily reach their target audiences. The impact of this political media coverage on the public’s beliefs about climate change and the potential for political persuasion through this medium were explored using data collected in an online survey experiment (N = 348). Participants read one of three articles regarding President Trump’s withdrawal from the Paris Agreement and then answered questions regarding their opinions on climate change, attitudes toward the government, and perceptions of the news source. Findings indicated that par

Photonic computing has emerged as a promising solution for accelerating computation-intensive artificial intelligence (AI) applications due to its high energy efficiency and low latency. The field of photonics, however, is challenged by the development of complex systems that can accommodate diverse AI algorithms and their associated memory requirements. To address this challenge, we propose an innovative approach based on the use of holographic storage in photonic architectures. Our work introduces a novel design methodology for scalable all-optical photonic neural networks (PNNs), where each layer of the network is represented by a single photorefractive crystal. By leveraging the spatial modulation capabilities of these crystals, we are able to achieve high data density within the PNNs,

Context: In the first part of his 2012 presentation "Inventing on Princi- ple," David Kelley (CEO and founder, IDEO) describes how he developed a new approach to design through an experiment with two teams. One team was instructed not merely make improvements in existing products but rather invent something completely different--a challenge that is difficult for most designers because it requires them think beyond their current skills or knowledge base without falling back into familiar habits . The other group followed standard procedures by making incremental changes within specific constraints set forth beforehand(e.,g. ; size restrictions). Results from this study showed significant differences between both groups when tested against customer satisfaction surveys regarding product perf

With the advancement of language models (LMs), their exposure to private data is increasingly inevitable, and their deployment (especially for smaller ones) on personal devices, such as PCs and smartphones, has become a prevailing trend. In contexts laden with user information, enabling models to both safeguard user privacy and execute commands efficiently emerges as an essential research imperative. In this paper, we propose CoGenesis, a collaborative generation framework integrating large (hosted on cloud infrastructure) and small models (deployed on local devices) to address privacy concerns logically. Initially, we design a pipeline to create personalized writing instruction datasets enriched with extensive context details as the testbed of this research issue. Subsequently, we introdu

Federated learning (FL) provides an efficient paradigm to jointly train a global model leveraging data from distributed clients. As local training data comes from different distributions, FL is vulnerable to membership inference attacks (MIAs), in which an adversary aims to infer the training data distribution of a client from the local model. However, existing MIAs assume the adversary knows the global model, which is not practical in real-world FL scenarios. In this work, we study the MIA problem under practical assumptions where the adversary has access to the local models of all clients but does not know the global model. We show that the adversary can collate all local models to infer the training data distribution of each client, and further mount a successful MIA to the global model. To mitigate the MIA, we propose a robust FL framework that incorporates a novel robust aggregation mechanism based on the principle of maximum entropy. We show that the robust FL framework can mitigate the MIA from both theoretical and experimental perspectives.

Open-Source Software (OSS) has become a widely adopted phenomenon, and its repositories host a vast amount of documentation in the form of README files. Despite their prevalence, we lack a comprehensive understanding of what motivates practitioners to read and act upon that documentation. In this paper, we aim to plug this gap by investigating what practitioners consider when adopting OSS, and whether they even notice the documentation. We present a framework of adoption drivers derived from the literature, and test its applicability in the context of OSS. We do so through a survey of 251 OSS practitioners, followed by in-depth interviews with 17 of them. Our results show that the drivers we identified are indeed relevant in the context of OSS, but our interviews reveal that practitioners consider additional factors not covered by our framework. We learn that documentation matters, but in ways that go beyond what is captured by existing models of technical adoption.

The competition complexity of an auction setting is the number of additional bidders needed such that the second-price (SP) mechanism no longer achieves revenue close to optimal. This paper characterizes all settings where SP has tight $n$-competitiveness, i..e., it requires exactly $\Omega(1/H_k)$ extra players in any set with a unique winner and at most k types overall for some constant positive integer \emph{and} every potential opponent type can be achieved by adding fewer than 2^m opponents when there are m distinct possible prices; this captures cases known as ``close'' or "not far" from having strong competitiveness bounds [Lu et al.'46].

Text-to-image generative models can generate high-quality humans, but realism is lost when the face moves. In this paper, we introduce a method to enhance the photorealistic quality of generated videos by leveraging the video frame's context and the facial expression information in the face mask. Firstly, we utilize a 3DMM model to capture the detailed facial expression information from the input face mask. Secondly, we extract the spatial-temporal features from the video frames, which contain richer facial information. Finally, we feed the extracted facial expression information and temporal features into a conditional generator to synthesize realistic videos with expressions. We evaluated our method on several challenging human datasets and achieved comparable results with existing metho

Link prediction typically studies the probability of future interconnection among nodes with the observation in a single social network. This paper presents an approach to link recommendation, which is different from traditional methods and can be implemented on multiple networks simultaneously: it considers both existing links between users that belong at least two communities (i) directly connected by friendship or membership ties; also all indirect connections through intermediate members who share commonalities across these groups - thus creating potential for collaboration amongst individuals within one community while still maintaining autonomy over others outside thereof .

Autistic adults often experience stigma and discrimination at work, leading them to seek social support from co-workers. However previous research has shown that autistic people may not always receive the help they need because of a mismatch between their expectations for how colleagues should act (eliciting 'social demand') relative versus actual behaviour observed in practice ('actual provision'). We examined this issue by surveying 150 British autism charity employees with Asperger's syndrome/high functioning ASD about their experiences seeking workplace assistance.

Cross-domain recommendation (CDR) aims to enhance recommendation accuracy in a target domain with the help of source domains. Despite its promising potential, existing CDR methods often suffer from sub-optimal results due to inadequate exploration and exploitation of the domain information. In this paper, we propose a novel framework named CoRe to enable effective knowledge transfer across different domains for recommendation. To explore diverse source domain knowledge, we construct an adaptive memory bank consisting of multiple sub-banks that store feature embeddings from various sources and selectively retrieve them for model updating according to their effectiveness. Furthermore, a novel multi-modal cross-attention mechanism is introduced to facilitate model adaptation by leveraging bot

Deep clustering has exhibited remarkable performance; however, the overconfidence problem, i.e., the tendency to assign samples to clusters that are not supported by the data distribution, remains a significant challenge. This paper proposes an innovative approach for dealing with this issue: adaptive clustering. We introduce a novel model named ADAPT, which dynamically adjusts the hyperparameters and structure of deep clustering models based on their performance in detecting overconfident clusters. The key idea behind our method is to use Bayesian inference to evaluate the reliability of each cluster assignment using the posterior probability. If the confidence of the cluster assignment is high, we retain it and update the model accordingly. On the other hand, if the confidence is low, we

Large language models (LLMs) reflect societal norms and biases, especially about underrepresented groups. To understand the extent of this bias and to mitigate it, we propose a novel method for analyzing LLM outputs using multidimensional vector embeddings. Our approach is inspired by psychological research on stereotype activation that demonstrates how humans associate multiple traits with different social groups. We train models that embed words into a set of trait dimensions, based on a dataset of human judgments of word associations with 60 traits. We analyze 10,000 outputs from GPT-3.5 generated in response to prompts mentioning various gender and racial groups. The multidimensional representations allow us to compute quantitative measures such as similarity between groups along a giv

Unmanned Ground Vehicles (UGVs) are increasingly being considered for various tasks in hospitals, warehouses, and other indoor environments. However, ensuring the safety of humans and UGVs in these scenarios is paramount. One of the major challenges in ensuring safety is the presence of dynamic and complex environments with moving obstacles. Furthermore, the majority of learning-based models operate in a data-rich environment and are not suitable for safety-critical applications. In this paper, we propose a novel data-driven framework that computes safe trajectories for UGVs in complex environments by fusing spatial-temporal maps with motion forecasts of dynamic obstacles. A unique aspect of our proposed framework is its ability to quantify the level of discrepancy between the expected motion of dynamic obstacles and its actual motion. This discrepancy is then used to generate a set of safe trajectories that consider potential collisions and movement restrictions. Finally, the UGV trajectory is optimized using a multi-objective cost function that minimizes trajectory length, time, and risk. We demonstrate the efficacy of our method through extensive experiments in both simulation and real-world settings. Results show that our proposed framework produces high-quality trajectories that are close to the optimal solution while ensuring safety.

Regional solar power forecasting, which involves predicting the total power generation from all rooft op and ground-mounted photovoltaic (PV) systems in a specific region at a time scale of 1 hour to several days ahead. The regional PV output is highly dependent on weather conditions such as cloud cover that can be difficult or expensive for traditional meteorological sensors alone; thus it requires more complex modeling approaches than single site forecasts do This project aims at developing novel AI methods using satellite imagery data with high temporal resolution (~30 min), multispectral bands(Vis/NIR wavelengths up tp ~2 μm )and large spatial coverage(~5 km^2). These techniques will aid us understand climate change related effects like forest fire detection , land degradation monitori

Open-vocabulary semantic segmentation (OVS) aims to segment images into regions of semantic interest, guided by natural language queries. This task is fundamentally cross-modal, requiring alignment between the visual and the linguistic modalities. Previous methods imposed rigid one-to-one correspondences between words and visual regions, overlooking the possibility of many-to-one or one-to-many relationships. To address this limitation, we introduce a novel multi-grained cross-modal alignment approach that learns flexible correspondences through the introduction of virtual tokens. These tokens, being neither words nor regions, act as intermediaries in the alignment process, enabling the model to establish appropriate connections between the linguistic and visual inputs. Our method, dubbed ModalVLT, enhances the model's capability to comprehend complex linguistic instructions and discern relevant visual details, thereby improving the alignment between the two modalities. We evaluate our approach on standard OVS datasets and demonstrate its superiority over previous methods, achieving state-of-the-art performance. Our experiments also reveal that the virtual tokens play a crucial role in facilitating cross-modal alignment, highlighting the effectiveness of our approach.

Traditional sequential recommendation methods assume that users' sequence data is clean enough to learn accurate sequence representations. However, in reality, this assumption is often violated due to data noise and the inherent complexities of user behavior patterns. To address these challenges, we propose a novel graph learning method for sequential recommendation (GLSR) that leverages a graph neural network (GNN) to extract useful information from the noisy user-item interaction sequences. We introduce a new graph construction strategy based on the frequency of items in the sequences, which ensures that frequent items are assigned higher weights during graph construction. This approach enhances the effectiveness of GNN by enabling it to focus on high-frequency items and better capture t

The Koopman operator is a linear operator that encodes the dynamics of a nonlinear system in a Hilbert space of functions. We show that the Koopman operator can be leveraged to improve policy learning in reinforcement learning (RL). Specifically, we propose a method that constructs a Koopman-invariant subspace of functions whose dynamics are amenable to RL policy optimization. To this end, we introduce a regularized Markov decision process (RMDP) whose transition kernel is given by the restriction of the Koopman operator to the aforementioned subspace. We show that the value function for this RMDP is a Koopman-eigenfunction and that iteration in the RMDP induces policy improvement in the original Markov decision process (MDP). We further show that if the subspace is spanned by eigenfunctions of the Koopman operator, then the dynamics in the RMDP are linear, thereby facilitating policy learning. In particular, we propose a linear policy representation and gradient-based policy optimization algorithm for the RMDP, the latter inducing nonlinear policy updates in the original MDP. We show that our proposed method, on three distinct control problems, leads to policies that perform better than the baseline RL algorithm, especially in the case of partial observation.

Query performance (e.g., execution time) prediction is a critical component of modern DBMSes to ensure high-quality service level agreements are met. However, existing work on this topic has focused primarily on online query systems, where the goal is to predict the actual time taken by an already-executing query to complete its execution. In contrast, this paper focuses on offline query systems, where the goal is to predict the expected completion time of future queries that have not yet been submitted. This distinction leads to significant differences in how we should analyze and model query performance, as well as in the types of queries we can consider. We propose a general approach to modeling offline query system behavior, using stochastic Markov chains to capture both the query arri

Humans perceive the world through multiple senses, enabling them to create a comprehensive representation of their surroundings. However, modern artificial intelligence (AI) systems are typically constrained to one sense at a time. This raises the question: could we achieve more efficient and accurate AI models by leveraging cross-sensory information? To explore this possibility, we propose a novel model called Multisense Transformer, which utilizes information from diverse senses to enhance scene understanding. Our method incorporates both vision and audio data, using them as complementary modalities to improve object detection accuracy and performance. We demonstrate that our approach outperforms state-of-the-art methods in object detection tasks while maintaining efficiency and computat

We present UniDoorManip, a unified approach to learning a universal door manipulation policy that performs well in both simulated and real-world environments. Our approach addresses the challenges of training a door manipulation policy in a large-scale and diverse set of environments where doors have varying physical properties, such as different friction coefficients and mass distributions. These variations in door properties are reflected in the wide range of optimal opening torques required to open doors in the benchmark suite. To address these challenges, we introduce a single-stage policy representation that decouples the control logic from the door model. This representation allows learning a single policy that is capable of opening doors in various environments without the need for environment-specific policy adaptation. We evaluate our approach on a diverse set of 1000 simulated doors and two real-world doors. Our results demonstrate a consistently high success rate across all environments. Please refer to our project page for code and additional results: https://sailor-z.github.io/UniDoorManip/

High dynamic range (HDR) imaging captures scenes with a wider dynamic range than what can be displayed on a standard monitor. To obtain an HDR image, multiple exposures of a scene are taken and then fused into a single image using a multi-exposure HDR reconstruction algorithm. However, this process introduces ghosting artifacts, which are not present in any of the input images. Ghosts are typically located at the boundaries of moving objects or static objects with high contrast edges. In this paper, we propose a deep learning based method for ghost suppression in multi-exposure HDR imaging. We leverage the single-image HDR reconstruction property of deep neural networks to reconstruct a high-quality single-image HDR representation of the input multi-exposure images. We then use a dual-domain convolutional neural network (CNN) that operates on both the spatial image domain and the frequency image domain to suppress ghosts in the reconstructed single-image HDR representation. To preserve details, we use a residual learning strategy to learn the residual information between the original multi-exposure images and the reconstructed single-image HDR representation. We then combine the processed single-image HDR representation and the residual information to obtain the final high-quality ghost-suppressed and detail-preserved multi-exposure HDR image. Based on the experiments conducted on the challenging HDR+ dataset, our method outperforms other state-of-the-art algorithms in both the鬼影抑制和细节保留方面。Dual-domain CNN在频域中使用了特殊的Normalization层来加速收敛并抑制鬼影，Residual learning strategy有助于细节的保留。 our implementation is available at https://github.com/Duanlab123/GhostNet。

Data augmentation (DA) has been widely used to provide additional training data without extra human annotation automatically. Recently, with the help of large language models (LLMs), new possibilities for DA have emerged, showing great potential in various tasks such as text classification, question answering, and code generation. In this paper, we provide a comprehensive review of LLM-based data augmentation from the perspectives of data, learning paradigms, and challenges. We summarize different categories of LLM-based data augmentation methods and analyze their advantages and disadvantages. We also propose two new frameworks, called LLM-Assisted DA (LAD) and LLM-Enhanced DA (LED), to better present the future directions for LLM-based data augmentation.

Real-time computational speed and a high degree of precision are requirements for computer-assisted interventions in medical robotics. In this work, we present an innovative methodology to implement the motion planning algorithm for robotic surgery with the highest possible performance on commodity hardware. We show how a combination of parallelization and customized implementations of commonly used algorithms can lead to significant improvements in both latency and throughput. Our approach utilizes OpenCL as a programming framework for creating efficient kernels that target GPUs, taking advantage of their unique architectures and capabilities. Additionally, we introduce a new algorithm that allows us to perform motion planning while considering the constraints of the robot's kinematics. T

The increasing significance of sustainability considerations within both public spheres (such as policies and regimes) and private contexts (such as marketplaces) has made a significant impact on the development of corporate social responsibility. However, this has been largely at the expense of stakeholder engagement. This article proposes an alternative approach to understanding CSR, which is based upon the theory of deliberative democracy (TD), whereby CSR is viewed as a process for deliberating with all stakeholders. This article focuses on how TD can be used to explain CSR and its relevance in today's world. We then use TD as a framework to examine a number of important questions about the nature and implications of CSR. Finally, we argue that the deliberative view of CSR offers a pro

Many recent state-of-the-art results in language tasks were achieved using compound systems that combine pretrained models. These models are often trained on different tasks and languages, which can be beneficial for generalizing across domains. However, this also means that they are trained on a diverse range of data types, including textual and non-textual content. This poses the question of whether the compound system benefits from the various data modalities or if it is purely the result of the combination of multiple pretrained models. We address this by training an ensemble of pretrained models on text only (Text) and on a combination of text and vision data (Vision). We then compare these ensembles to two baselines: one that uses individual models, Text and Vision, and one that comb

The Covid-19 pandemic has posed a heavy burden on the global healthcare system and caused huge economic loss.mask-wearing is one of the most effective measures to prevent the spread of Covid-19. However, some people still do not believe in the effectiveness of masks or even spread anti-mask beliefs. In this paper, we quantify the influence of media on people's mask-wearing beliefs using a nationally representative survey in the United States. We find that people are exposed to diverse sources of information about masks, and news media play a particularly important role in shaping people's beliefs about masks. Specifically, cable news channels have the largest influence on people's beliefs, followed by social media and then traditional media such as newspapers and magazines. The influence of media can be explained by the extent to which media sources promote mask-wearing. Sources that do not promote masks tend to be conservative cable news channels, while sources that promote masks tend to be scientific journals and pro-science media outlets.

Dynamic Facial Expression Recognition (DFER) aims to identify the emotion conveyed by a dynamic facial expression, a crucial component of non-verbal communication. Despite significant progress in this field, the research community has overlooked the emotional contagion effect, which refers to the influence of an observer's emotions on their perception of the emotion portrayed by a dynamic facial expression. This effect is bidirectional, as an observer's perception can also influence their own emotional state. In this paper, we introduce a novel task for DFER, namely Affective Alignment, which requires models to align their predictions with the observer's emotions. We provide a theoretical analysis of why and how affective alignment is beneficial for DFER. Building upon our theoretical insights, we develop a pioneering deep learning model called A$^{3}$lign-DFER, which incorporates CLIP, a state-of-the-art model for image-text alignment. A$^{3}$lign-DFER represents an innovative approach to DFER by considering the emotional contagion effect and aligning the model's predictions with both the input expression and the observer's emotions. Our experimental results demonstrate the superiority of A$^{3}$lign-DFER over other models, highlighting the significance of affective alignment for DFER.

Virtual memory (VM) is a critical, yet complex, component of modern computers. It enables programmers to allocate and deallocate memory dynamically at runtime, using simple and flexible programming abstractions. However, this convenience comes at a cost: managing virtual memory efficiently is challenging, and VM-related bugs can be hard to find. To help debug and benchmark virtual memory managers (VMMs), we present Virtuoso, an open-source, comprehensive and modular simulation framework. Virtuoso allows the flexible combination of pre-built modules, which represent key components of a VMM, such as the page table, the memory allocator, and the fault handler. Users can easily extend Virtuoso by writing custom modules in a programming language of their choice. This way, Virtuoso can model state-of-the-art VMMs, from simple ones used in embedded systems, to complex ones used in cloud computing. We evaluate Virtuoso by using it to replicate a set of well-known benchmark results. We also use Virtuoso to identify and fix a bug in the Linux VMM that affects its energy efficiency.

Recently, there has been an increasing focus on audio-text cross-modal learning. However, existing methods face significant limitations in their ability to generate realistic and high-quality audio data for text inputs, especially when dealing with long text prompts that include complex concepts and a wide range of topics. This paper presents VANCET, a novel generative model that utilizes large language models (LLMs) and generative adversarial networks (GANs) to overcome these challenges. Our approach incorporates a powerful LLM as a discriminator, enabling it to provide feedback and enhance the quality of generated audio samples through iterative refinement. The GAN component leverages LLM capabilities to create audio data by generating acoustic features from text prompts and then synthes

The restricted isometry property (RIP) is essential for the linear map to guarantee the successful recovery of a signal from a set of noisy observations. When the signal dimension $N$ is larger than the number of measurements, i.e., $M<N$, it is well-known that $\ell_2$-$\ell_1$ minimization cannot recover the sparse signals without any additional information (measurement matrix), which has been proved by Candes and Tao in 2005. In this paper, we will show that the RIP does not hold for the $\ell_2$-$\ell_1$ optimization when $M < N$.

The rapid development of information technology, especially the Internet, has facilitated users with a quick and easy way to access data. This phenomenon is also applied in education where students are able acquire knowledge by using search engines such as Google or YouTube rather than attending traditional lectures at school/university (iCampus 2016). In fact, this kind online learning experience can be regarded more attractive for learners since it offers flexibility based on individual needs (eLearning Industry n d.). However, these advantages have been accompanied some drawbacks that may lead negative consequences if not controlled properly: first one being lack motivation due low interaction between student-teacher pairs; second problem arises when they do learn something but cannot a

We present an overview of the 01.AI foundation models, a family of open-source, modular foundation models for natural language processing tasks. Yi is the Chinese language counterpart of the 01.AI foundation models. It is a state-of-the-art, open foundation model family for Chinese natural language processing tasks. Yi consists of a pre-trained language model, named YiLM, and a meta-learning model, named YiMeta. YiLM is a multi-task language model with strong performance in both masked language modeling and transfer learning. It is built on top of the open pre-trained model GPT-2. YiMeta is a meta-learning model that can rapidly adapt to new tasks and datasets. It consists of a task-agnostic backbone network and several task-specific head networks. The backbone network is trained on a large-scale pre-training dataset, while the head networks are trained by solving a few tasks using meta-gradients. YiMeta can be used to quickly adapt YiLM to new tasks. It can also be used to solve tasks that require high resource. In particular, it can be used to solve tasks on under-resourced languages by transferring the knowledge from related languages.

Vision-Language Transformers (VLTs) have shown great success recently, but are limited in their ability to handle visual data of different sizes. Existing solutions often rely on a fixed resolution for both the vision and language inputs or use expensive downsampling techniques that reduce model efficiency by discarding valuable information from larger images/videos during training time steps when only small amounts can be processed at once due limits imposed upon them as well; this leads us away towards solving our original problem altogether! To address these limitations we propose an adaptive attention mechanism called AdaptiveViLTM which uses two novel methods: 1.)

PL/SQL is the most widely used procedural language for Oracle Database. Maintaining PL/SQL code is a challenging task, especially for large code bases. One of the main reasons for the difficulty of maintaining PL/SQL code is the presence of inconsistencies, which include logical inconsistencies (e.g., a condition that can never be true) and structural inconsistencies (e.g., a cursor that is declared but never used). In this paper, we present I3DE, an IDE that aids PL/SQL developers in detecting and resolving inconsistencies in their code.
