I3DE computes three kinds of inconsistencies: (1) local inconsistencies, (2) structural inconsistencies, and (3) potential performance inconsistencies. It also provides three kinds of assistance: (1) automatic repair, (2) refactoring suggestions, and (3) performance improvement suggestions. I3DE is integrated with the Oracle Developer IDE. We evaluated I3DE on three PL/SQL code bases of different sizes (i.e., 2.4K, 12.2K, and 31.3K lines of code). Our results show that I3DE can effectively detect inconsistencies in PL/SQL code. In particular, it detected 94 inconsistencies (i.e., 73 local, 17 structural, and 4 potential performance inconsistencies) from 179 PL/SQL procedures in the 31.3K lines of code. Moreover, I3DE can automatically repair 84.5% of the detected inconsistencies.

In human-robot interaction (HRI), we study how humans interact with robots, but the robot is often a simple agent. A recent trend in HAI has been to use generative adversarial networks for creating more complex agents that can simulate interactions and explore different types of behavior without needing an actual physical platform or other computational resources needed by traditional simulators like Gazebo . This approach allows researchers access new areas within robotics research because it enables them not only test ideas quickly at scale -- which would otherwise be prohibitively expensive given their size -but also helps reduce costs associated w/building hardware prototypes while speeding up development times significantly compared against existing methods available today thanks larg

In his 2018 paper, Herlihy introduced an atomic protocol for multi-party register synchronization. The protocol is based on a new type of read/write registers that are guaranteed to be closed under prefixes (i.e., if the register is in state p and you read p', then it must have been in state p' at some point). A natural question arises as to whether we can implement such registers in a distributed setting. We show how to implement this register abstraction using any shared memory object with an operation that returns the value of a location, but only when a value has been written. We also provide a lower bound that shows that our construction is optimal. Our proof relies on two basic tools: a lockless write-all algorithm due to Chaudhuri et al. and a lockless write-only algorithm due to He

Many concepts for future generations of wireless communication systems use coherent processing of signals from many distributed antennas. Coherent combining and beamforming require synchronization among the elements in a multi-antenna array, which is often achieved by means that involve phase synchronizers (PSs) to lock local oscillator frequencies at each antenna element onto common carrier or reference frequency [1]. However such PS designs are not immune against timing errors caused by clock jitter between collocated receivers with shared antennae arrays; this problem has been recognized in literature since 20 years ago but remains unsolved today due mainly because there lacks an effective solution framework capable enough handle all kinds potential misalignment scenarios encountered du

First responders, such as police officers and paramedics, often work in challenging environments with limited visibility and visibility-obstructing gear, which can impede their ability to navigate and interact with technology. See-through head-mounted displays (SHDs) are a type of augmented reality (AR) hardware that can project information directly into a user's field of view, potentially unobtrusively supporting first responders in the field. However, SHDs come with their own set of challenges, including limited form factors, potentially hazardous optical components, and immature software development kits. In this paper, we leverage user-centered design principles to explore the design space of SHDs for first responders. We conducted a mixed-methods study with 17 first responders and 11 members of the public. Quantitative data were analyzed using mixed-model ANOVA, and qualitative data were analyzed using thematic analysis. Our results provide insights for designers of AR SHDs to support first responders, including the importance of a hands-free design, the need for a robust content creation pipeline, and the potential of a social media-like sharing feature.

We study payment scheduling for a model of debt with dates, the interval debt model. In this model, debt is denoted as a function with domain the set of calendar dates. Operations allowed on the debt are payment and roll-over. A payment is a function that maps a date to a non-negative real number which represents the amount of debt paid on that date. A roll-over is an operation that shifts the debt by a certain number of years. In this paper we focus on the problem of computing a payment schedule for a given debt, that is, a sequence of payments which reduces the debt to zero. We compute payment schedules with minimal last payment, maximal first payment, and minimal number of payments. The computational complexity of these problems is analyzed. We also study the problem of computing an optimal payment schedule with respect to a lexicographic order on payments. This problem is NP-hard in general, but becomes polynomial-time solvable when the debt is periodic.

The number of older adults using voice assistants (VAs) has been increasing, yet little is known about their specific usage patterns and challenges. To address this gap, we conducted a month-long in-home study with 10 older adults (7 females, 3 males; mean age = 77.8 years) who used VAs frequently. We employed a mixed-methods approach, including daily usage logs, weekly semistructured interviews, and a final in-depth interview, to understand the participants' experiences with and perceptions of VAs. Our study provides nuanced insights into the benefits and challenges of VAs from the users' perspective. Quantitatively, we found that participants used VAs most frequently for information seeking and entertainment, followed by communication and task assistance. Qualitatively, we learned that participants appreciated VAs for their convenience, accessibility, and social aspects but were limited by the VAs' capabilities, particularly in understanding complex requests and providing social companionship. We also discuss practical implications for designing VAs that better serve older adults and other demographics.

In the past year, numerous companies have incorporated Generative AI (GenAI) capabilities into their products. These offerings include OpenAI's ChatGPT-4, Google's Bard, and Microsoft's Bing chatbot. GenAI technology has revolutionized many industries, including IT, healthcare, education, marketing, and others. It has significantly enhanced communication with users by simplifying tasks, providing insights, and improving accuracy. However, integrating GenAI technology also presents challenges, such as cost, security, data privacy concerns, and unpredictability of its performance. This report examines how GenAI has influenced different sectors and what issues arise from implementing it in various settings. The report is divided into three sections: an overview of GenAI technologies, a survey

Boolean Satisfiability problems are vital components in Electronic Design Automation, particularly within the Logic Synthesis and Timing Verification domains. However, a large number of these problem instances remain unsolved after decades of research into their complexity. We propose that this is due to an imbalance between the size of the statespace (the collection of all possible assignments for a boolean function) and its search space (the set of all possible paths through the statespace). Our approach is based on two core principles: 1) reduce the number of assignments to be explored by finding compact descriptions of those assignments; and 2) explore the search space most efficiently by finding patterns in the input (in our case boolean functions). We first describe the problem of mi

Traffic prediction constitutes a pivotal facet within the purview of Intelligent Transportation Systems (ITSs) for real-time traffic management and control. To this end, deep learning based models have gained widespread recognition due to their enhanced performance in handling large datasets with various complexities. However, conventional deep learning approaches suffer from two major challenges: 1) the lack of interpretability; and 2) the need for manual tuning and hyperparameter optimization processes. In this paper, we propose an interpretable deep learning model that provides a thorough explanation regarding the key drivers behind its predictions, namely, the Average Reciprocal Rank (ARR) metric, which indicates the average rank of relevant variables, thereby offering greater transpar

This work explores a closure task in comics, a medium where visual and textual elements are integrated. Our approach is inspired by the idea of generating image captions as if they were written by an AI that has been trained on human-authored comic scripts. To achieve this goal, we introduce a novel dataset containing 10,000 comics with corresponding dialogue transcriptions, where each page of the comic includes one or two panels, which contain the characters' speech balloons (SBs). We use our dataset to train a neural language model (LM) that produces natural caption-like texts for comics. Next, we fine-tune a pretrained LM to generate captions for the comics in our dataset using only the captions from our training set. These generated captions are evaluated against human annotations.

Scientists studying deep ocean microbial ecosystems use limited numbers of sediment samples collected by research vessels to characterize large-scale patterns in the distribution and activity of microorganisms. The small sample size and biased sampling locations, however, limit our ability to identify global ecological patterns in these systems and to understand how microbial communities are structured by environmental factors. Here we use a machine learning approach, called a support vector machine, to predict the location from which each sample was collected based on its physicochemical properties. We used these predictions to test whether the microbial community composition is organized along environmental gradients inferred from the original data set. Our results show that the microbia

In multicriteria decision-making, a user seeks a set of non-dominated solutions in the form $S = \arg\min_{x} f(X)$, where $\mathbf{f}: X^n_d -> R^{m}$ is an objective function. The traditional method to solve this problem uses local search algorithms which rely on gradient information and thus require differentiability assumptions as well approximation methods that do not exploit any structure inherent within each component's domain (e.~g., if one can only compute approximate gradients). In contrast with these approaches we propose GAMS: Global Approximate Minimum Search for efficient finding near optimal solution sets without requiring derivatives or approximating functions separately at every step during optimization process by utilizing locality constraints between objectives defined o

Recent LiDAR-based 3D Object Detection (3DOD) methods have demonstrated promising results on challenging datasets. However, the accuracy of these methods may be significantly affected by the positional errors caused by noisy GNSS data. In this paper, we propose a novel method to improve the object detection accuracy of 3DOD methods with the use of the SLAM system. The proposed method consists of three steps: firstly, a preprocessing step is designed to eliminate the impact of noise caused by GNSS inaccuracy; secondly, a localization and mapping step is developed for the SLAM system; finally, an optimization step is introduced to adjust the poses of detections using the accurate localization from the SLAM system. This approach can effectively reduce the error of the 3D pose of objects in bo

The equivalence of random features models (RFMs) and neural networks (NNs) has been exploited to obtain implicit regularisation in deep learning. However, this equivalence only holds for a specific choice of random features, which is often not used in practice. In this work, we study the performance of "neural redshift", which can be applied to any RFM and improves generalisation over random features models. Neural redshift is based on the observation that random networks are not random functions, and that the mapping from the random features to the outputs of the network is what we want to learn. We formulate an optimisation problem that learns both the random features and the network weights, and show that neural redshift improves generalisation performance over random features models in multiple tabular and image datasets.

Euler diagrams are used to visualize relationships between sets. In a rectangular Euler diagram, the sets are represented as axis-aligned rectangles. Two rectangles are adjacent if the corresponding sets have a non-empty intersection. In a x-monotone rectangular Euler diagram, any two adjacent rectangles share a common vertical side. In this paper, we study the realizability problem of rectangular Euler diagrams, where the input is a graph with two vertex sets, one for the rectangles and one for the labels. The question is whether there is a rectangular Euler diagram in which the rectangles correspond to the vertices of the first set and in which two rectangles are adjacent if and only if their corresponding vertices are adjacent in the input graph. We show that the realizability problem is NP-hard for general graphs and we give an characterization for the realizability of x-monotone diagrams. Based on this characterization, we show that the problem is in NP and we give a quadratic time algorithm for the construction of a x-monotone rectangular Euler diagram, if one exists.

Large language models (LLMs) such as GPT-4 have recently demonstrated impressive results across a wide range of tasks. LLMs are still limited, however, in that they frequently fail at complex reasoning, their reasoning processes are opaque, they are prone to 'hallucinate' facts, and there are concerns about their underlying biases. In this paper, we introduce In-Memory Learning (IML), a framework that makes LLMs more capable and controllable by integrating them with external memory to store and reason over factual knowledge. IML is inspired by how humans learn and use knowledge, and can be instantiated in different ways depending on the nature of the external memory. We show that IML can improve LLMs on a wide range of tasks, often by small but consistent margins. Our experiments also demonstrate that IML is more robust in generating factually consistent responses, and allows users to retrieve the reasoning steps of the LLM. While LLMs are often portrayed as 'knowledgeable', our results suggest that they may actually be 'knowledge-averse', and that integrating them with external memory is necessary for them to use knowledge effectively.

Recently, promptable segmentation models, such as the Segment Anything Model (SAM), have demonstrated superior performance compared to traditional classification-based methods. However, their efficiency is limited by the computational overhead associated with the generation of training samples and the inference time required for processing large images. To address these issues, we propose an efficient and effective segmentation framework that combines prompt learning and a knowledge distillation methodology. Our approach leverages a pretrained model, SAM, to generate diverse data instances. A teacher model, trained on small-scale data, is then used to distil knowledge from the SAM's pretraining sample dataset using a contrastive loss function. The knowledge distilled is then applied in a p

The past years have witnessed impressive developments in multimodal machine translation, thanks to large-scale annotated datasets and powerful neural models. However, the evaluation methodologies used in multimodal translation are not without flaws. In this work, we show that the standard practices in multimodal translation evaluation can be misleading, and that evaluating multimodal models on text-only datasets can provide a more accurate picture of their performance and uncover issues in their design. We illustrate this point in three ways. First, we show that multimodal models can achieve better performance on text datasets than on multimodal datasets, despite using only a subset of the available data. Second, we demonstrate that a simple multimodal model can outperform a complex one on several benchmarks if it is trained on a large enough text dataset. Finally, we identify a bug in the implementation of a popular multimodal translation model that affected its performance on the established benchmarks. After correcting the bug, we found that the performance of the model on the text datasets increased, while its performance on the multimodal datasets remained stable. Our results highlight the importance of thorough and fair evaluation, and of using text datasets when assessing the performance of multimodal models.

Current state-of-the-art (SOTA) 3D object detection methods often rely on multiple training epochs to achieve state-of-the-art performance. This is partly due to the complex nature of 3D object detection, which necessitates a multi-stage architecture and a large number of parameters. However, this leads to high computational costs that are challenging for resource-constrained systems like edge devices. In this work, we present a novel approach based on transformer encoders that allows for efficient training with only one epoch. Our method uses a single network with pretrained weights to perform 3D object detection, eliminating the need for additional networks or layers. Additionally, our method introduces adaptive scheduling and weight decay regularization to further reduce computational c

Recent LiDAR-based 3D Object Detection (3DOD) methods have achieved impressive performance in diverse scenes with complex structures. However, it remains challenging to extract meaningful representation from raw point clouds and effectively fuse multiscale features for accurate detection due the large difference between points' distribution density across different scales of objects as well their inherent nature that are sparsely distributed around a target object compared other surrounding clutter or background noise signals which makes them difficult distinguish within small regions without significant amount overlap among nearby neighboring clusters .

In this paper, we investigate on enhancing the adversarial robustness of deep neural networks via a novel budget control method for generating adversarial examples used in adversarial training (AT). The idea is to allocate a larger perturbation budget to samples that are more vulnerable to adversarial attacks, based on their distance to the decision boundary. We first show that using a fixed perturbation budget for all samples during AT is equivalent to performing minimax optimization, and that maximizing the entropy of the perturbation budget allocation is equivalent to performing maxmin optimization. Both optimizations are aimed at enhancing the adversarial robustness of the model. However, existing AT methods using a fixed perturbation budget fail to achieve maxmin optimization. To address this limitation, we propose a vulnerability-aware perturbation budget allocation method, which samples more vulnerable examples with a larger budget for perturbation. Experimental results on the CIFAR10 and ImageNet datasets demonstrate that our method effectively improves the adversarial robustness of deep neural networks compared to existing AT methods.

Optical character recognition (OCR) has been a transforming technology for the workflow of document data. As a result, research on document analysis has flourished, thanks to the availability of machine-readable text. Form understanding, in particular, has benefited greatly from this research, with applications in knowledge extraction, document management, and workflow automation. Transformers and language models are at the forefront of current research in form understanding. In this paper, we review the recent progress of Transformers and language models in form understanding. We provide an overview of the technology, including form representation learning, and then delve into Transformer-based models for form understanding. We present major findings and conclusions, and suggest future research directions in this field.

We present a learning-based approach to reconstruct buildings as 3D polygonal meshes from single images. Our method takes advantage of both the abundance and richness in architectural information contained within historical maps, which are frequently available for many cities around world over long periods spanning centuries (e. g., London: AD 1746–2059), but also allows us learn representations that can generalize well across time by leveraging modern large scale image datasets such egs Flickr8k/Commons or Street View Images with their corresponding ground truth building footprints provided through OpenStreetMap API services [see Figure below]. The key idea behind our proposal is based on two premises:(i) To enable accurate estimation during training phase we require fine grained annotati

Large Language Models (LLMs) such as GPT-4 have recently demonstrated impressive results across a wide range of tasks in natural language processing and other fields. LLMs are likely to become a major platform for many AI applications, much like how the widespread adoption of deep learning led to the current AI boom. We argue that LLMs are best viewed as cognitive models, i.e., models of human cognition. Like other cognitive models, LLMs should be studied using the tools of cognitive science, namely, experiments that test specific cognitive hypotheses. We outline three such experiments: (1) a reading comprehension task that tests the hypothesis that LLMs have a reading comprehension mechanism that is similar to humans, (2) a word embedding task that tests the hypothesis that LLMs have a word meaning mechanism that is similar to humans, and (3) a question-answering task that tests the hypothesis that LLMs have a mechanism for answering questions that is similar to humans. These experiments show that LLMs are similar to humans in these three cognitive tasks. We argue that LLMs will eventually lead to a revolution in the field of AI, much like how large-scale deep learning led to the AI boom. In the longer term, we expect that LLMs will lead to the development of general artificial intelligence (AGI), which is capable of performing any cognitive task that a human can perform.

Given time series data, how can we answer questions like "what will happen in the future?" and "what happened in the past?"? These are key challenges of causal reasoning and prediction. The goal of this survey is to present a unifying framework for time series forecasting that is grounded in causality theory and methods, thus enabling researchers and practitioners to build principled, robust, and interpretable models. We begin by introducing basic concepts from time series analysis, probability theory, and causality, and we then discuss how they intersect with each other. We identify three central challenges: (1) causal discovery from time series data; (2) causal modeling of time series; and (3) causal forecasting of time series. For each challenge, we provide an overview of the state-of-t

This study explores the challenge of sentence-level AI-generated text detection within human-AI collaboration, where both parties contribute to a text. The task is crucial for ensuring human accountability and protecting users from potential harm, but existing approaches struggle to recognize the subtle signs of machine writing in collaborative texts. To address this issue, we introduce the novel notion of "textual traces" as markers left by AI writing in collaboration. We propose a new dataset, CCTD, consisting of 10K real collaborative texts from GitHub pull requests with human annotations, enabling the detection of AI-generated sentences. Our extensive experiments on this dataset demonstrate that existing approaches fall short in detecting textual traces, even when they are highly relia

The learning to defer (L2D) framework allows autonomous systems to be safe and robust in the presence of uncertainty by prioritizing the information gain that would result from taking an action. In this paper, we introduce a new approach for learning to defer in a partially observable environment by using the Bellman operator of the system's true dynamics as our reward function, which can be estimated from trajectory data. Our method is shown to achieve superior performance compared to previous approaches on three different gridworld tasks, while being more sample efficient than the L2D-baseline. We also show that our approach generalizes better to unseen environments. Finally, we analyze the theoretical properties of our method in terms of stability and convergence speed, demonstrating it

We present SpaceHopper, a three-legged, small-scale robot designed for future space exploration. The design of the whole robotic system is based on simple hardware and low power consumption to make it suitable for long term missions in remote areas with no human intervention needed during operation or maintenance tasks like servicing satellites while they are still operational before reaching their endoflife phase (EOL). Spacehopper has been developed by iRobot Corporation as an experimental platform that can be used within simulated environments such those found aboard spacecraft where there might not always exist sufficient resources available onsite due either lack availability from outside sources; thus making use only what remains left behind once launched into orbit around Earth's Mo

Recently, it has been recognized that large language models demonstrate high performance on various intellectual tasks. However, due to the significant computational resources required for training and inferencing, their application in practical scenarios is limited. In this paper, we propose a novel approach called "Teacher-free Language Model Distillation" (TFLMD), which leverages the existing pretrained LLMs as teachers to guide the distilled models to learn specific skills. Our experiments show that TFLMD achieves competitive performance compared to the state-of-the-art approaches while significantly reducing the model size. Additionally, we present a lightweight version of the TFLMD algorithm named TFLMD-light, which further reduces the model size by 50%. Finally, we provide an open s

We study a natural application of contract design to search problems with probabilistic prior and exploration costs. Our model is inspired by the real-life situation where customers often have imperfect information about their preferences, hence they need some time before making decisions or committing themselves fully; this leads us into studying nonbinding contracts in order for both parties (the service provider as well... This paper proposes an efficient method based on two novel concepts: weighted random walks over graphs embedded within networks defined according to certain attributes ("weighted graph embedding") followed by "weight aggregation" at each network node using various weighting methods such that it can be used directly without any other preprocessing steps like feature ex

In this paper, channel estimation problem for extremely large-scale multi-input multi-output (XL-MIMO) systems is studied. XL-MIMO system consists of a large number of transmit antennas and a single receive antenna. The channel state information (CSI) of the received signal is estimated using a pilot-assisted method in XL-MIMO systems. However, due to the large number of transmit antennas, the CSI estimation in XL-MIMO systems has a large complexity compared to the conventional MIMO systems. To reduce the computational complexity, a low-complexity CSI estimation algorithm based on a successive interference cancellation (SIC)-based subspace learning method is proposed. In addition, the performance of the proposed algorithm is evaluated by numerical simulations, which shows that the proposed

Diffusion model-based low-light image enhancement methods rely heavily on paired training data, which are usually hard to obtain. To alleviate this issue, in this work, we propose a novel unpaired low-light image enhancement framework named UNPAIR4LL (UNPAIRed for Low-Light Image Enhancement). Specifically, our approach consists of two main components: (1) an image translation network based on CycleGAN that maps from the source domain to the target domain; and (2) a diffusion model-based refinement module that improves the quality of the translated images by learning from paired data. The proposed method is trained via a two-stage strategy. In the first stage, only the translation network is optimized with an adversarial loss function, while the second stage involves optimization of both t

Measles is a highly contagious disease, and its outbreak has posed a serious threat to public health in many countries, especially for those with a weak immune system. Agent-based models (ABMs) are effective tools for simulating an outbreak as they can mimic the disease spread at a fine spatial scale. However, ABMs are often limited by their computationally expensive nature, which makes them difficult to run in real-world scenarios. To address this issue, we propose an AI-enabled ABM framework that utilizes machine learning (ML) to replace the computationally expensive components in the traditional ABM. In this work, we focus on developing an AI-enabled ABM for measles outbreak simulation in New Zealand. We split the ABM into two parts: a high-resolution part that runs at a fine spatial scale and a low-resolution part that runs at a coarse spatial scale. We then use a deep reinforcement learning (DRL) agent to decide which part the ABM should use at any given time. We show that the proposed AI-enabled ABM can achieve similar performance as the traditional ABM while significantly reducing the computation time. We also show that the proposed framework can be used to develop an interactive web-based interface that allows users to manipulate various parameters in the ABM and instantly see the impact of their changes. This work demonstrates that AI technology can be used to improve the efficiency and applicability of ABMs, making them more useful tools for real-world outbreak response strategies.

When reviewing scientific papers, experts sometimes miss relevant prior work, due to limitations in their knowledge or the vast number of papers published each year. This can result in missed opportunities for authors, who cite relevant papers in their response to reviews. In this paper, we introduce a new task of recommending missed citations in scientific papers, annotated by human experts. We provide a dataset comprising 7,000 papers in computer science, annotated as having zero, one or multiple missed citations. We also present multiple baselines for the task, including a neural model trained to detect self-citations. Our results show that experts miss around 3.5 citations per paper on average, and that our dataset contains a challenging mixture of easy and hard cases for automatic systems. We show that state-of-the-art models can only slightly outperform simpler baselines on this task.

Real-time whole-body teleoperation allows an operator to control a humanoid robot using intuitive gestures. This intuitive control is typically achieved by mapping a subset of the operator's full range of motion (ROM) to the robot's ROM. Existing approaches often rely on manually crafted heuristics or imitation learning to determine the appropriate mapping. However, these methods can be limited in their ability to explore the full space of possible mappings and may suffer from suboptimal control. By contrast, end-to-end deep reinforcement learning (DRL) approaches have the potential to learn optimal control policies that map the operator's full ROM to the robot's full ROM. However, training DRL policies for whole-body teleoperation is significantly more challenging than for upper-body teleoperation due to increased state and action dimensions, as well as decreased environmental fidelity. To address these challenges, we propose a novel DRL training method that incorporates state-of-the-art lower-body models and a human-like reward function. We evaluate our method through extensive quantitative and qualitative experiments, including ablation studies and user studies. Our results demonstrate that our proposed method outperforms existing approaches, achieving higher success rates and closer alignment with human operators.

Medical image segmentation is a crucial task in computer-aided diagnosis and therapy monitoring. Prompt-based medical image segmentation can improve the situation where there is a shortage of annotated data by leveraging large pretrained models. However, previous works only segment the typical lesions, e.g., tumors, and are limited to using images from a single modality. In this work, we propose a promptable medical image segmentation framework using Swin Segmentation Model (SAM), which can segment both typical and atypical lesions prompted by textual queries. We evaluate our method using images from the Multimodal Brain Tumor Segmentation Challenge 2020 dataset and the Pancreas Segmentation Challenge 2021 dataset. The experimental results demonstrate that our method outperforms previous prompt-based medical image segmentation methods.

Large Language Models (LLMs) are known to perform well on structured reasoning tasks. However, their performance on multi-lingual structured reasoning tasks is known to be poor. In this work, we try to understand the role of LLM's multilingual capabilities in multilingual structured reasoning. Based on our understanding, we design a simple coding convention that involves just two characters, namely, ``|'' and ``-''. We show that this coding convention can help LLMs to better structure their thoughts, leading to improved performance on multi-lingual structured reasoning tasks. We evaluate the effect of our coding convention on LLMs' performance on a multi-lingual version of the Natural Language Inference (NLI) task, where we provide LLMs with translated premises and conclusions. Our results show that our coding convention improves LLMs' performance on multi-lingual NLI. As a side-effect, we also observe that our convention improves LLMs' performance on monolingual NLI.

Self-supervised large language models have demonstrated the ability to perform Machine Translation (MT) via contrastive learning, without using any parallel data. This is achieved by creating a synthetic dataset from monolingual text and then training an encoder that can distinguish between real translations of sentences in this corpus versus artificial ones created with translation techniques such as backtranslation or noisy channel model generation [1]. The main challenge here lies within finding effective ways for sampling both positive pairs — i. e., genuine sentence alignments—and negative counterparts - which would be artificially generated instances meant not only increase performance but also reduce computational cost .

With growing application of machine learning (ML) technologies in healthcare, there have been calls for the need to establish robust and transparent ML-based systems. Despite some progress made towards this goal such as algorithmic transparency through interpretability approaches or model validation via robustness measures against adversarial attacks etc., we still lack a principled way that could holistically address both aspects simultaneously from end users’ perspectives: "How well does an algorithm perform on unseen data?" "What is its behaviour when confronted with malicious/untrustworthy inputs?". In order tackle these challenges headon ,we propose two novel frameworks - one focused solely upon verification techniques used by experts during evaluation phases while another aims at pro

The array is a data structure used in a wide range of programs. Its compact storage and constant time access make it one the most useful structures for representing lists, sequences or groups as arrays are easily indexed using subscripts (e., $A[i]$ ).

Implementation

Dynamic Arrays

In C/C++, an array can be declared by specifying a name followed immediately with brackets containing a number that specifies the size: int A [10]; /* int-type values at indices from zero to nine */ This declaration creates space on your computer's memory system sufficient enough to hold ten integers; however you can only access those elements through subscripting notation like so An error occurs when attempting this operation because we have exceeded our maximum index value!

Natural language and images are commonly used as goal representations in goal-conditioned imitation learning (IL). However, they may not always be suitable for complex tasks requiring a sequence of subgoals. To address this challenge, we propose Goal-Conditioned Multi-Step IL (GCMSIL), which utilizes multi-step plans as goal representations to enhance generalization. Our approach introduces an initial plan generation step, followed by a fine-tuning stage on the generated plans. This strategy allows us to leverage plan generators trained on diverse data sources, such as instruction datasets or large language models, without the need to explicitly fine-tune them for each task. Experiments conducted on two challenging robot manipulation tasks demonstrate that GCMSIL outperforms existing metho

Software product line techniques encourage the reuse and adaptation of software components for creating customized products or software variants. The use case-based approach to develop a family is an ideal candidate because it allows early requirements analysis, which helps in reducing development costs by improving quality through better integration with customers’ needs at design time rather than after implementation when changes are more expensive [1]. This paper presents a systematic literature review (SLR) on how current research addresses variability modeling using Use Cases as well as their benefits regarding different kinds of models that can be created from them: scenarios/storyboards; feature diagrams /feature trees(FDTs); domainspecific language specifications based upon FDs). I

The Bellman equation and its continuous form, the Hamilton-Jacobi-Bellman (HJB) equation, are fundamental to reinforcement learning (RL). However, we observe that existing RL methods often do not effectively exploit these equations. In this paper, we propose a novel approach to RL which directly leverages the Bellman equation by decomposing the value function into an optimal policy function and an optimality measure. By doing so, our method enables gradient-based optimization of both functions in parallel, thereby circumventing the typical obstacles faced during policy search, such as saddle points, mode collapse, and oscillations. Experiments on various benchmark environments demonstrate significant performance improvements over state-of-the-art algorithms. Our code is available at https:

Smiles and laughs detection systems have attracted a lot of attention in the past decade contribuing to the development of social robotics. These systems detect smiles and laughter in order to assess the user's affective state. The main problem with these systems is that they are trained on data collected in controlled environments, thus limiting their application in unconstrained scenarios. In this paper we present SMiLE, an automatic system for detecting smiles and laughter in uncontrolled scenarios. Our experiments demonstrate that the proposed methodology outperforms current state-of-the-art methods in terms of accuracy and robustness to occlusions. We also present a new dataset containing 62620 videos of smiling and laughing faces from YouTube, which can be used to train and test mode

Time-Sensitive Networking (TSN) is an emerging real-time Ethernet standard that addresses a key shortcoming of existing network technologies. While Ethernet has been widely used in the industrial automation field, its inherent non-deterministic nature poses challenges in ensuring timing requirements for control applications. This limitation can result in unpredictable delays and interruptions, leading to suboptimal or even unsafe operating conditions. TSN aims to overcome these limitations by introducing new features such as precise time synchronization, enhanced quality of service, and deterministic delivery mechanisms. The result is a more robust and reliable communication infrastructure capable of meeting the stringent demands of mission-critical industrial environments. As TSN continue

With the advancements of transformer-based architectures, we observe the rise of natural language processing (NLP) models with billions of parameters. However, these models are typically trained on massive amounts of data and require significant computational resources during training. As a result, they are often difficult to access for most researchers and developers, particularly in low-resource settings such as those in developing countries. To address this challenge, we introduce Giant Transformer Language Model (GTLM), an open-source repository containing pretrained large-scale language models and their corresponding fine-tuned versions for various downstream tasks. The models in GTLM cover a wide range of sizes, from 100 million parameters up to 13 billion parameters, catering to div

Visual understanding and segmentation of materials and their states is fundamental for understanding the physical world. The infinite variety in appearances, textures or shapes across classes makes this a challenging task that requires learning data-driven representations from images to classify objects into material categories such as wood types [1] , concrete mix ratios[2], paints colors/brands,[3],[4]. This project proposes an endToEnd deep Learning model trained on large amounts labeled image datasets . We propose three main contributions: (i) A novel dataset with real life photographs taken by professional photographers; This has been achieved through collaboration between researchers at ETH Zurich (Switzerland), Carnegie Mellon University Pittsburgh Campus USA & Geor

Accurate solar power forecasting is important for power system operations and planning. This paper proposes a hierarchical temporal convolutional neural network (HTCNN) to forecast day-ahead solar power generation at the regional level. The HTCNN utilizes temporal patterns, both within and across days, by employing two levels of temporal convolutional layers. The model is applied to 2016-2019 hourly solar power generation data from 177 photovoltaic power plants in California. We evaluate the addition of weather data and find that the HTCNN performs best when incorporating weather data from the day prior to the forecast day. The HTCNN improves upon existing methods, reducing the mean absolute error by 2.4-3.1% on average, relative to a baseline model that uses only historical solar power generation data.

Human Activity Recognition (HAR) is one of the fundamental building blocks of human assistive systems. A major challenge in HAR lies in developing robust, real-time methods that can operate under high noise and dynamic conditions with minimal user intervention or setup requirements. In this paper we investigate how multimodal sensor fusion techniques may be employed to enhance activity recognition performance by addressing some key challenges involved such as dealing effectively with uncertainty across modalities at different levels while maintaining temporal synchronicity among all sensors; ensuring robustness against potential sources for error during data acquisition/processing steps like drift errors due motion artifacts from accelerometer readings on mobile devices etc. ; improving ac

Federated learning, as a promising distributed learning paradigm, enables collaborative training of a shared model among multiple parties without exposing their private data. However, this comes with the challenge of achieving robustness in the presence of malicious attacks such as model poisoning and backdoor attacks that can compromise the integrity of the learned model. Although existing research has focused on developing robust algorithms for federated learning, most of these solutions require additional assumptions (e.g., model-specific or dataset-specific) that may not be satisfied in practical scenarios, limiting their effectiveness. In this paper, we provide a unified framework to comprehensively analyze the impact of model poisoning and backdoor attacks on the robustness of federa

The challenge of spatial resource allocation is pervasive across various domains such as transportation, industry, and agriculture. In particular in the field of precision irrigation for crop growth management under water scarcity conditions that often occur due to climate change impacts on rainfall patterns or increased population density which result in more demand than supply capacity from existing infrastructure networks like canals/pipes etc., this problem becomes even harder because it involves both temporal (time) constraints related with when each unit needs watering along side physical location restrictions where some areas might be inaccessible by certain types machinery while others may not have enough room available given current levels usage rates if one were trying optimize e

Treatment effect estimation (TEE) is the task of determining the impact of various treatments on a target population, e.g., the efficacy of a drug in reducing the severity of an illness. The main challenge lies in data scarcity and the resulting difficulty to draw generalizable conclusions from limited observations. While recent advances have shown promising results for TEE using small-scale datasets, we observe that these methods often rely heavily on expert knowledge or require specific assumptions about the underlying distributions of treatment effects. This paper introduces Diffusion-based TEE (D-TEE), a novel approach that leverages diffusion models to learn generalizable features from the observed data, enabling more accurate and robust prediction across diverse scenarios.

Personalized cross-domain recommendation, which aims to leverage the user behavior data from one domain to predict the unknown preferences in another domain, has attracted increasing research attention. In this paper, we propose a novel federated learning based framework, namely FedHCDR, to address the personalized cross-domain recommendation problem. Specifically, we first design a user behavior graph (UBG) to capture the user preferences in the local domain, and then introduce a cross-domain recommendation graph (CDRG) to model the cross-domain recommendation relationships. To combine the information from the local UBG and the global CDRG, we propose a hypergraph signal decoupling method to separate the user preference signal and the cross-domain recommendation signal. In particular, a key idea is to use the user behavior data from the local domain to guide the separation. Experimental results on two benchmark datasets demonstrate that FedHCDR outperforms several state-of-the-art baselines.

Machine learning (ML) is vulnerable to inference (e.g., membership inference, property inference, etc.) attacks. Most of the existing defense methods target training phase and fail in detecting attackers during inference stage. To this end, we propose a novel framework called DETECTOR that detects ML models under inference-time attacks. Specifically, it consists of two main modules: a graph neural network (GNN) module and an ensemble model. The GNN module learns the global and local features from the input data. Then, it produces a feature map representing the global and local distribution of the input data. On the other hand, the ensemble model utilizes a set of different classifiers to learn the input data's representation using both the GNN feature map and the original input data. Final

Time-Sensitive Networking (TSN) is an emerging real-time Ethernet technology that enables high-performance communication and synchronization for industrial applications. The key challenges include network latency, packet loss, and jitter. This paper proposes a TSN-based protocol named GPS (Globally Packet Segmentation), aimed at mitigating these issues. The protocol is designed with a segmented frame structure, where each segment is transmitted in a separate time slot and encapsulates multiple packets within the same time slot. This mechanism ensures low-latency delivery of data by reducing the number of frames needed to transfer packets across the network, thereby minimizing the impact of jitter and packet loss. In addition, the proposed protocol introduces an automatic flow control mecha

Each LLM serving request goes through two phases. The first is prefill which processes the entire input and generates a context string for the second phase, postfill. In this work, we show that the quality of the prefilled output impacts the performance of downstream systems. We also observe that LLMs tend to generate spurious text in certain parts of the input when performing prefill. While some parts of the prefill output are useful for postfill, others are not. We identify the types of unhelpful text generated during prefill and present a method called Prefill Pruning that removes these texts from the prefilled output. Our experiments on 79 datasets show that pruned prefill outperforms original prefill by an average margin of 5% across different tasks. Further analysis reveals that the

Popular methods usually use a degradation model in a supervised way to learn a watermarking function. This approach has been widely studied and is effective in removing the watermarks while preserving the images. However, it fails when there are few watermarked images for training. In this paper, we propose a novel unsupervised adversarial watermark removal method by utilizing an unsupervised contrastive learning (UCL) framework. The key idea of our proposed method is that we can train a network with a large number of unlabeled images via UCL to find similar image patches. By comparing these patches with the given watermarked images, we can obtain some guidance information about their similarity. We further use this guidance information to remove watermarks from the watermarked images. Our

The growing interest in Large Language Models (LLMs) for specialized applications has revealed the need for reliable evaluation methods. However, existing LLM benchmarks suffer from limited coverage of diverse domains and a lack of clear guidance on their use. In response to this challenge, we introduce ChatGPT-Landscape: a comprehensive dataset containing 298 unique tasks that capture a broad range of application areas. This novel resource is designed to complement existing benchmarks by providing a more nuanced assessment of LLM performance across varying task types. To ensure high quality, all tasks were carefully curated by subject matter experts to maintain their relevance and practicality. Moreover, our dataset is publicly available as an open-source repository with code to facilitat

Changes in demand, various hydrological inputs, and environmental stressors are among the issues that can influence agricultural water consumption. A thorough understanding of the interplay between these factors and their effect on water use is essential for making informed decisions about sustainable water management practices. This study applies a systematic review methodology to examine the literature on factors influencing crop irrigation water usage. The analysis revealed a substantial number of studies conducted globally, with a particular focus on South Asia, Central America, and North America, highlighting a significant level of research activity. The identified papers explored various aspects of irrigation water usage, including water-use efficiency, crop yield, and water footprin

Ship detection needs to identify ship locations from remote sensing (RS) scenes. However, due to the large size of RS images and the complex background of scenes, detecting ships in RS scenes is a challenging task. In this paper, we propose a novel approach called DSSNet for accurate ship detection in RS scenes. The main contributions are: 1) We introduce an efficient feature extraction network to extract dense features by combining multi-scale pooling layers with attention mechanism. 2) Based on the dense features extracted, we design a two-stage framework that consists of a localization head and a segmentation head. To reduce computational complexity, we utilize the localization head to predict the bounding box while simultaneously training the segmentation head to generate pixelwise mas

Matrix completion is an important area of research in recommender systems. Recent methods view a rating matrix as low-rank and sparse, which leads to fast algorithms for computing the missing entries using nuclear norm regularization (NNR). In contrast with NNR approaches that use global ranking information only at initialization time or through warm starts from previous iterations during training , we propose local rank approximation via randomized sampling over small neighborhoods centered around each unobserved entry prior its update step .

Product offers on e-commerce websites often consist of a textual product title and a textual product description. Given the importance of these two text fields, it is critical to measure their quality before they are published. In this paper, we present an unsupervised method to assess the quality of product titles and descriptions for e-commerce websites using a novel combination of word embeddings and the latent Dirichlet allocation (LDA) model. We train the LDA model to generate synthetic text from different topics, such as products that have positive and negative feedback. We use the generated text to train a classifier that predicts if a given product title or description belongs to one of the synthetic categories created by the LDA model. Our experiments show that our approach can su

With the proposal of the Segment Anything Model (SAM), fine-tuning SAM on a single annotated image has been shown to achieve remarkable accuracy. However, as the training dataset increases, the improvement in performance begins to decline. To address this issue, we introduce an approach to train SAM using multiple datasets with different image characteristics and semantic segmentation tasks. Our proposed method effectively addresses the challenge of achieving consistent high-quality semantic segmentation results across diverse datasets and tasks without requiring significant model re-training. In contrast to traditional methods that rely on extensive retraining, our approach achieves outstanding results by simply re-using pre-trained models and adding minimal fine-tuning steps. This paper studies how large language models (LLMs) can act as effective, high-level agents in complex, multiagent environments. We introduce a novel method for teaching an LLM to control multiple characters within an interactive narrative environment. Our agent learns from data by solving problems in the environment with reward and punishment feedback. The agent's policy is learned through self-play with a curriculum that guides it towards increasingly complex tasks, including those involving more than one character. We show that our approach allows us to learn an LLM capable of effectively controlling two characters in complex narrative games and environments. The LLM not only exhibits human-like behavior but also surpasses human performance on some tasks. Additionally, we demonstrate ho

The quadratic complexity of the attention mechanism represents one of the biggest hurdles for processing long sequences using Transformer models. In order to overcome this limitation, several approaches have been proposed that scale the attention mechanism linearly with respect to sequence length. These methods either directly reduce the number of parameters in the self-attention module (e.g., by limiting the receptive field) or extend the model architecture with additional components, such as masked multihead self-attention. However, existing techniques are often limited to specific application scenarios and require a significant computational effort during training. In this paper we introduce a novel methodology based on a factorized representation of self-attention that can be used to o

Humans can easily distinguish the known and unknown categories and can recognize the unknown object by learning it once. However, when it comes to machine learning models, there is a huge gap between human performance and machine performance. Existing models still struggle with the task of open-set recognition (OSR), where they are asked to differentiate objects from an unseen class or unknown category while also recognizing known classes. To bridge this gap in OSR, we propose an innovative approach that employs multiple contrastive learning methods to enhance the representation ability of feature extraction networks for OSR tasks. Additionally, we introduce a novel framework called OpenContrast that incorporates these methods, enabling our model to perform well on the OSR benchmark datase

Graph is a fundamental mathematical structure in characterizing relations between different objects and has been widely used on various tasks, such as social recommendation. Inspired by the recent success of deep learning in graph-based recommendation systems (GRSs), we propose GNNRank, a novel ranking method for recommending items that are most likely to be clicked/purchased, using only the structural information of an undirected graph, i.e., without any explicit item features or user behavior data. Our proposed model consists of three modules: a Graph Embedding module, a Graph Neural Network module, and a Ranking Module.

Nano-machines circulating inside the human body, collecting data on tissue conditions and transmitting it to a central processing unit, is a promising approach for early disease diagnosis. In this paper we introduce a novel biocompatible nanorobotics platform based on magnetic shape memory polymers (MSMPs) that can operate in liquids without any external power supply or control units. We use an MSMP actuator as a biomagnetic probe that is able to change its geometry upon exposure to a magnetic field. The actuators are integrated into a thin elastic membrane with predefined shapes that can be transformed by magnetizing the probe. Using this method, a single actuator can be used to perform several tasks: it can sense pressure; it can measure temperature; it can act as a pump; and it can func

We consider the problem of node-private graph statistics under continual observation. Specifically, for a large graph with $n$ nodes, we wish to compute a statistic $f(t)$ of the graph at an arbitrary time $t$ using an algorithm that observes $o(n)$ nodes. At each time step, an adversary with omniscient knowledge wishes to guess the statistic. We give a general framework called time-aware projections that achieve privacy against the adversary for a large class of graph statistics. Our framework is based on a novel combination of node and edge projections with a novel time-aware random walk. This walk incorporates information about both the current time and the identity of the node into the random walk to project the graph structure at time $t$ to a small space. We give a clean characterization of the bias and variance of our estimator, and show that our algorithm is private against an omniscient adversary. Finally, we show that our framework can be applied to a wide range of graph statistics, including counts of triangles, $k$-plexes, and $k$-shortest paths.

Semi-supervised audio-visual source localization aims to identify the source of an audio-visual query among a set of candidates using labeled source signals and an unlabeled mixture signal. This task is typically tackled by leveraging the teacher model, which is often pre-trained in an unsupervised manner and then fine-tuned on the labeled sources. However, the bias of the teacher model becomes a non-negligible factor that hinders the performance of semi-supervised source localization when the unlabeled data is not clean. In this paper, we propose a novel dual mean-teacher framework to tackle this problem. Specifically, we introduce the cross-view contrastive learning strategy to enforce the consistency between the predictions of the teacher model and the hard-labeled sources in both the audio and visual domains. This strategy helps to reduce the bias of the teacher model from the perspective of view consistency. Furthermore, we adopt the self-supervised temporal attention network to constrain the context information and reduce the bias of the teacher model from the perspective of temporal consistency. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first work to introduce the view consistency constraint for the teacher model. Extensive experiments on the audio-visual DiSo dataset show that our method outperforms the state-of-the-art methods by a large margin.

Reinforcement learning (RL) has achieved phenomenal success in various domains. However, its data-driven nature leads to overfitting and poor generalization on unseen environments. In contrast, meta-learning methods have been proven effective in alleviating the overfitting issue by leveraging knowledge learned from previous experiences. Although recent works demonstrate the potential of meta-learning for RL tasks, most of them focus on tabular settings, which are computationally expensive due to their high dimensionality and lack of structure in state representation. To address this challenge, we propose a novel Meta-Reinforcement Learning framework with Graph Neural Networks, dubbed mGNN. mGNN is designed specifically for the continuous control setting, which utilizes Graph Neural Network

Recently, learning open-vocabulary semantic segmentation from text supervision has achieved great success. However, most existing methods use pre-trained language models (PLMs) as the decoder to obtain class names, ignoring the potential for PLMs as an encoder to extract visual features and align them with text features. In this paper, we propose the VLBERT model, which incorporates a vision transformer (ViT) into BERT as an encoder, allowing it to capture visual features. The ViT uses the same architecture as DeiT, while the self-attention mechanism is modified by replacing the query matrix with the linear projection of image patches. We also introduce a novel text generation method that employs PLM for class name generation. Experimental results show that our proposed method achieves sta

Deep learning models, particularly those based on transformers, often employ numerous stacked structures, which possess large model sizes and high computational costs. This paper presents a novel strategy to reduce the number of layers in deep networks by extracting layer-specific information from multiple datasets with various tasks or domains using multitask training methods while maintaining accuracy comparable to that obtained when employing full stacks during inference time for each task individually (i. e., one shot). Our experimental results show how our approach can be applied effectively across different settings such as language modelling/text classification(LMTC), machine translation et al.. For example: given two LMs pretrained independently upon English Wikipedia data;

Zero-shot Video Object Segmentation (ZSVOS) aims at segmenting the primary object of each video frame. By leveraging temporal consistency, ZSVOS has achieved promising performance in this field. However, most previous works only focus on the classification and mask head with limited capacity to handle more complex tasks such as multi-object detection or instance segmentation. In order to further improve the accuracy, we propose a Temporal Consistency Transformer Network (TCTN). TCTN is composed of three parts: object feature extraction, temporal information fusion, and segmentation. Based on the ResNet101 backbone network, we introduce a novel Temporal Convolution Module (TCM) to extract features from videos while preserving temporal information. Then, we use a Bi-directional Temporal Fusi

optimizing molecules via instruction is a emerging paradigm, which aspires to leverage natural language as a flexible interface for scientists to fulfill their needs in molecular design. Previous works craft molecules solely based on textual instructions, overlooking the pivotal role of substructures. Omitting substructures can lead to synthesized molecules that differ significantly from the intended molecular design, as numerous molecules share the same textual description but vary in their substructural compositions. To emphasize the significance of substructures, we introduce a novel framework called 3DSculptor, which integrates the instruction-based molecular optimization with substructure-awareness. 3DSculptor adopts a two-step strategy, where the first step entails the generation of molecular graphs that adhere to the given instructions, followed by the employment of a substructure prediction model to ensure the presence of critical substructures. This integration of instruction-based generation and substructure prediction amplifies the substructure-aware capabilities of our framework, thereby enhancing the accuracy of molecule optimization. We evaluate the effectiveness of 3DSculptor on a variety of molecular optimization tasks, demonstrating its superiority over recent related work. Moreover, we delve into the capability of 3DSculptor to optimize molecules with instructions containing numerical values, a feature that enables scientists to precisely control molecular properties.

Instead of making behavioral decisions directly from the exponentially expanding joint observational-action space, agents can exploit a hierarchical structure to organize their behavior. This enables them to act faster and more efficiently in complex environments where there are many possible actions that lead an agent towards its goals but only some few which bring it close enough for optimal goal achievement within reasonable time steps until termination or death by starvation/exhaustion etc., e g . The paper presents two novel algorithms based on reinforcement learning (RL) with deep neural networks as function approximators: Hierarchical Action Selection via Policy Gradient Ascent ("HASPGA") using vanilla policy gradient ascent without any extra reward shaping; HASPA+E(r)" ) employing

Deep neural networks are notoriously miscalibrated, i.e., their outputs do not reliably indicate the probability of a correct prediction. The lack of calibration can lead to poor model performance in many practical applications, such as classification and regression. In this paper, we present a novel framework that addresses the problem of calibration for deep learning models. Our method involves training an auxiliary network on the original model's output distributions. This auxiliary network is used to provide an improved estimate of the true probabilities, which can then be used to recalibrate the original model's predictions. We apply our method to several benchmark datasets, demonstrating that it outperforms traditional approaches to calibration, resulting in significant improvements

Vision-Language Models (VLMs) such as GPT-4V have recently achieved state-of the art results in many vision and language tasks. However, training these models can be extremely costly due to their large size of up 10 trillion parameters per model version which require huge amounts data for pretraining or fine tuning on downstream task datasets with high computational costs associated therewith during inference time when deployed at scale across multiple instances within a cloud infrastructure setup like Azure Machine Learning Platform(AML). This blog post will explore some potential solutions that could reduce both cost aspects without sacrificing performance accuracy by exploring novel techniques inspired from recent research papers focusing specifically around optimization methods applied

Reliable confidence estimation is a challenging yet fundamental requirement in many risk-sensitive applications. However, the current literature often neglects the potential impact of data distribution shift on confidence estimation. In this work, we introduce the idea of data distribution shift to reliably estimate the confidence level associated with an unseen target dataset, and propose an efficient framework, called ReLiable Confidence Estimation (ReLi), for achieving this goal. Our main insight is that a reliable confidence estimate can be derived by leveraging both the training set and the target test set as surrogate samples to obtain an estimate of the mean, variance, and skewness of the true confidence distribution of the target test set. In particular, for the case of the mean, w

We establish the first genuine QBF proof size lower bounds for random 3-CNFs. More precisely, we show that any depth-4 Frege system needs at least exponential many lines to prove unsatisfiability of random 3-CNFs with high probability. We also show that the same lower bound holds for a wide range of other proof systems, including AC-Proofs, IR-Calculus, IRM-Calculus, Long-Distance Q-Resolution, and Merge Resolution. Our hardness result is obtained by taking a starting graph (which is an expanded random 3-CNF) and repeatedly applying deterministic restrictions until the graph is no longer susceptible to the proof system in question. Each restriction corresponds to the application of a propositional rule, and the order in which the rules are applied is non-deterministic. This technique allows us to bypass the propositional analog of the Alon-Jaeger-Tarsi Theorem that is used in most QBF proof size lower bounds. We believe that our result can be extended to other proof systems, and that a more general theorem along the lines of the propositional Alon-Jaeger-Tarsi Theorem would imply exponential lower bounds for all (polynomially axiomatizable) QBF proof systems.

Few-shot knowledge graph completion (FKGC) aims to query the unseen facts of a KG by leveraging only a few labeled samples. Existing methods often focus on building classifiers that estimate label probabilities, while ignoring the importance of identifying candidate relations for each fact. In this paper, we propose an effective and efficient method named \textbf{R}elation \textbf{B}iased \textbf{S}ampler (RBS) to select the most likely relation for each fact and then use it to generate pseudo labels. Specifically, RBS first employs a \textbf{G}raph \textbf{N}eighborhood \textbf{E}mbedding (GNE) module to encode all triples into the same semantic space, where the semantics of entities are implicitly preserved.

Recently, safe reinforcement learning (RL) with the guarantee of stability has attracted increasing attention. In this paper, we propose a novel sampling-based safe RL algorithm for nonlinear dynamical systems. In contrast to the existing methods that require the Lyapunov function to be given explicitly, we develop a data-driven approach to construct a library of Lyapunov certificates. In particular, we use safety constraints to guide the data collection, and a neural network to approximate the Lyapunov function. We prove that the proposed method can find a safe policy if there exists a Lyapunov function that satisfies the safety constraints. We further show that the proposed sampling-based approach is able to learn the optimal policy if the safety constraints are perfect. Numerical results on both a benchmark problem and a real-world application show that the proposed method can find a safe policy efficiently.

The emergence of LLMs has ignited a fresh surge of breakthroughs in NLP applications, particularly in domains such as question-answering systems and text generation. As the need for longer context grows, a significant bottleneck in model deployment emerges due to the linear expansion of the Key-Value (KV) cache with the context length. Existing methods primarily rely on various hypotheses, such as sorting the KV cache based on attention scores for replacement or eviction, to compress the KV cache and improve model throughput. However, heuristics used by these strategies may wrongly evict essential KV cache, which can significantly degrade model performance. In this paper, we propose QAQ, a Quality Adaptive Quantization scheme for the KV cache.

Medical large language models (LLMs) have gained popularity recently due to their significant practical utility in medical settings. However, current LLMs are not trained on medical text and thus may exhibit biases against various demographic groups that appear within the medical field. In this study, we analyze the bias against medical specialties using LLM-generated prompts. We find that LLMs, such as ChatGPT and GPT-4, tend to prefer male-dominated specialties, which is consistent with gender disparities in the real world. This research underscores the importance of addressing potential biases and ensuring ethical considerations when implementing AI technologies in the healthcare industry. Our code is available at https://github.com/DandanLiu123/Medical_LLM_specialty.git.

A fundamental component in the theoretical school choice literature is the problem a student faces in deciding which schools to attend. The model typically assumes that each student receives an unbiased estimate of her ability, and she then selects her favorite school based on these estimates. However, recent empirical evidence suggests that students are often given biased estimations of their abilities. In this paper we extend the theoretical framework by considering the case when a school uses a biased estimate of a student's ability for admission purposes. This assumption allows us to develop a simple model, where the school has an incentive to misreport the accuracy of its estimate of each student's ability. We use this model to study how the distributional shift from unbiased to biase

Automatically estimating the performance difficulty of a music piece represents a key process in music education to create adaptive learning environments. In this paper, we study how well machine-learning models can predict the difficulty of musical pieces from audio data alone. We evaluate a series of models using two publicly available datasets and present our analysis of their strengths and weaknesses. Our results show that current state-of-the-art approaches are still unable to deliver accurate predictions due to the lack of relevant labels in the datasets. However, when we augment the training data by adding new labels generated by crowdsourcing, these models can provide good estimates for some pieces, but not others. These findings suggest that current methods may be insufficient on

Large Language Models (LLMs) have unlocked new capabilities and applications; however, they also introduce a variety of risks. LLMS are trained on large amounts data from the internet which is often biased or potentially harmful in nature—the content generated by an LLM can perpetuate this bias when used to create text-based products such as chatbots for customer support systems without addressing it first through careful evaluation practices like toxicity detection techniques before deployment into production environments where people rely upon their output results more than ever today!

Traditional approaches in mental health research apply General Linear Models (GLM) to describe the longitudal patterns of brain development. The goal is often twofold: 1.) To determine whether a specific model explains variance better than an alternative, or if there are differences between groups; and/or 2).To identify individual predictors that contribute significantly to this pattern across timepoints along with their respective effects sizes using t-tests from GLMs within R’s lme4 package [6]. However these methods do not account for measurement error which can lead one towards overestimating correlations as well increasing Type I errors when comparing models against each other via likelihood ratio tests due lack power under certain conditions including nonnormality data distributions

Robot navigation under visual corruption presents a formidable challenge. To address this, we propose a novel approach that combines the strengths of RGB-D cameras and LiDAR scanners. Specifically, we leverage the complementary nature of these sensors to enhance the robustness of our system. Our method first uses a 3D model to generate visual information from a LiDAR scan. This is then fused with RGB images using a convolutional neural network (CNN), enabling improved localization accuracy even in harsh conditions such as low lighting or occlusion. Our framework utilizes a simple backbone CNN for its fast execution time, while offering a high degree of generalization across environments. We demonstrate the effectiveness of our approach on various real-world datasets and benchmarks, achievi

The rise of online health communities has provided healthcare professionals and researchers with valuable resources to understand public discourse on various health topics. Online discussions allow the tracking of emerging opinions and concerns about medicines, diseases, and healthcare practices. However, the analysis of online health discourse presents challenges, including the informal nature of online communication, the heterogeneity of topics discussed, and the need for up-to-date information extraction. Large language models (LLMs) have shown high performance in natural language processing tasks and hold promise for extracting insights from online health discussions. We evaluated LLMs for their suitability to extract emerging opinions on medicines and diseases from online discussions. We selected five LLMs for their diversity in architecture and training data: GPT-3.5, GPT-4, GPT-4 Turbo, GPT-4 Turbo Instruct, and GPT-4 Code. We used a two-step approach to prompt engineering: (1) We crafted prompts to extract opinions on medicines and diseases from Twitter data annotated as containing either an opinion about a medicine or a disease. (2) We evaluated the LLMs' performance in generating similar opinions from raw, unannotated Twitter data. We measured the LLMs' performance based on their ability to extract relevant information from the data, as determined by human evaluators. Our results show that LLMs can accurately extract existing opinions from annotated data and generate similar opinions from raw data. GPT-4 Turbo Instruct and GPT-4 Turbo achieved the highest performance scores. We expect that LLMs will continue to improve in their ability to process natural language and perform tasks such as opinion extraction. As LLMs evolve, they will likely become valuable tools for tracking emerging opinions and concerns in online health discourse.
