We propose a new class of loss functions called Input Convex Loss Network (ICLN) for decision focused learning. ICLN is designed to directly optimize the performance on the target task by leveraging convexity with respect to the input. We show that ICLN can lead to performance improvements on tabular and image datasets. The loss function can be minimized using standard convex optimization algorithms. We propose an approximation of the loss function to facilitate differentiation and show that the approximate ICLN has the ability to generalize.

Neighborhood attention reduces the cost of self attention by restricting each token's attention span to a subset of its neighbors, but it introduces several challenges: (i) the lack of explicit modeling of long-range dependencies; (ii) the inability to capture nonlocal interactions; and (iii) the difficulty of incorporating prior knowledge. To address these issues, we propose an efficient method that explicitly models long-range dependencies and effectively captures the locality while ensuring consistency with global information. Specifically, our approach first constructs a graph representation that integrates neighborhood relationships and prior knowledge, then employs graph convolutions to extract global features from local views, followed by a fusion operation that fuses local and glob

Recently, 3D hand reconstruction has gained more attention in human-computer cooperation research. In this paper we introduce a novel method for real time dense depth and shape estimation of the hands using RGB input images without any special hardware or sensor attachment to the user's body parts such as face mask sensors etc.. The proposed system generates high resolution point clouds by incorporating multiple deep learning models which include Unet+CNN model (for fast background removal), GAN based color correction network(GANOC) that corrects colors between source image domain with target reference video frame sequence dataset generated from our own self annotated videos captured under different illumination conditions .

The quality of machine translation is often limited by the vocabulary coverage of the models used. In this paper, we propose a novel bidirectional vocabulary evaluation method using relations (BiVert) that can effectively improve the vocabulary coverage and thus the quality of machine translation. The idea is to leverage cross-lingual relations to infer missing translations and to use translated relations to improve the selection of sense disambiguation. We show that BiVert, when applied to large multilingual models, can significantly improve their performance on low-resource languages. As an application, we use BiVert to develop a new English-Portuguese machine translation system that outperforms the state-of-the-art system based on the same model and data.

Emoji have become ubiquitous in written communication, on the Web and beyond. They are used in text messages between friends but also by journalists to convey emotion or inform readers of an article's mood (e. g., a smiley face is often placed at end-of articles with happy endings). In this work we explore how emojis can be exploited for sentiment analysis purposes: given as input either textual data from Twitter tweets containing both words/phrases expressing emotions like anger ("angry", "mad"), happiness / sadness etcetera) plus corresponding emoji occurrences within these same documents - our goal here will focus primarily upon developing models which leverage such information effectively enough so that they perform better than standard approaches relying solelyupon word features alone

This paper explores the cutting-edge Large Language Model with analytical reasoning on sports. Our key contribution lies in developing a novel sports domain corpus, including 36,214 documents and an additional 53,078 articles from different sources. To evaluate its effectiveness, we introduce four benchmarks for the analytical reasoning tasks: (1) question-answering; (2) text classification; (3) commonsense reasoning; and (4) reading comprehension, covering various aspects of sports knowledge. Extensive experiments are conducted to evaluate the LLM's performance on these benchmarks, demonstrating its impressive capabilities. The code is available at https://github.com/HanChaoZhao/SportsAnalysisLLM.git.

Deep reinforcement learning (DRL) has achieved impressive sample efficiency in practice, but the fundamental scalability issue still remains open. Training a value function using regression on a large state-action space is inefficient and often leads to poor performance. In contrast, classification algorithms are much more sample efficient when dealing with high-dimensional inputs, but they have not been widely used in DRL because of the lack of a suitable probabilistic framework. In this paper, we propose a novel two-head value learning algorithm that can train a value function via classification and achieve scalability in DRL. We break down the value learning problem into two simpler heads, each dealing with a different type of value decomposition. The first head learns a state-value function by classification, which is then combined with the action-value function learned by regression to form the complete value function. Our proposed algorithm, termed Classification-then-Regression DQL (C2R-DQL), outperforms regression-based methods on multiple DRL benchmarks, particularly on large-scale problems. Furthermore, C2R-DQL enables us to discover a set of meaningful value decompositions that conform to the Bellman optimality equation, providing new insights for deep value learning.

Common law courts need to refer to similar precedents' judgments to inform their current decisions. The judges of the common-law countries have relied on these precedent cases for centuries as a source of guidance and inspiration in developing new laws. A well settled principle is that if previous rulings are followed, it will lead people to know what to expect from future proceedings; therefore they can plan accordingly without fear or uncertainty about how things might turn out differently depending upon whether one chooses an alternative course rather than adhering strictly within bounds set by tradition alone (which may not always be optimal). This article aims at analysing two main aspects: first -the use/nonuse criteria used when deciding which case should serve best interests accord

Since humans can listen to audio and watch videos at faster speeds than actually observed, we often listen to a few seconds of an audio clip or view only the first few frames of video. We propose the use of fast-forwarding as a tool for understanding audio and video by demonstrating that it allows us to efficiently extract important information from auditory and visual stimuli. To do this, we explore how the perception of sounds and images changes when they are heard/seen in faster than actual speed. In particular, we find that we are able to identify important information in both sound and vision when presented at faster than real-time speeds, while being less accurate when identifying irrelevant details. This suggests that fast-forwarding may be useful for improving human performance on

Object-centric LiDAR odometry and localisation has seen much success in recent years, thanks to the natural object correspondence between consecutive scans. However, the majority of object-centric methods rely on the use of a sliding window to compare a small portion of the latest scan to previous scans. While effective, the sliding window approach limits the ability to utilise the global context of the latest scan, as only a local region is compared at any one time. In this paper, we propose That's My Point (TMYPoint), a compact object-centric LiDAR localisation method that utilises global point clouds to estimate the pose of objects within. TMYPoint employs a unique global correspondence generation approach, where each point in the global point cloud is connected to its n-nearest neighbours, forming a graph representation. The global graph is then transformed into a compact representation, significantly reducing the memory footprint while preserving the global context. TMYPoint leverages object proposals to efficiently extract and match features between the compact global representation and the latest scan, providing accurate pose estimation without the need for a sliding window. Through extensive experiments, we demonstrate the effectiveness of TMYPoint on both synthetic and real-world data, showing significant improvements over state-of-the-art methods in terms of accuracy and efficiency. Our code is available at https://github.com/antao97/TMYPoint.

Virtual Private Networks (VPNs) are a crucial Privacy-Enhancing Technology (PET), used to secure Internet traffic and protect the privacy of users. As these services have become widespread, they have also been targeted by malicious actors who seek to exploit them for their own gain. This paper provides an overview of the key trends in VPN abuse, including new techniques employed by attackers to evade detection, as well as emerging challenges in detecting and mitigating such attacks. Additionally, we discuss potential future research directions aimed at improving the security and resilience of VPNs against malicious threats. Finally, we provide recommendations for network administrators and operators on how to enhance the security of their VPN systems against these threats.

The stochastic block model (SBM) is a generalization of the Erd\H{o}s-R\'{e}\i nyi random graph to models with community structure. The S2G library implements algorithms for estimating both vertex and edge communities in large sparse networks, using only local information on each node; this allows one or more nodes at any time during computation without sacrificing accuracy compared against full knowledge methods such as spectral clustering that require all data upfront before running their algorithm(and therefore cannot be used when processing individual users' personal records).

We propose Masked Capsule Autoencoders (MCAE), the first CVAEs that employ capsule networks, and show how to apply MCAes in an end-toend supervised learning framework. Our experiments on MNIST reveal substantial improvements over stateofart autoencoder baselines with no additional training data or parameter tuning required: 98% validation set accuracy versus baseline of $64\%$ for vanilla VAES; a reduction from $\sim70$\% failure rate down into single digits when using masked reconstruction loss instead.\\\\ The code is available at \url{https://github.com/hda2513aulerazazridiyanahmadzihan/.}

Introduction: Cystoscopy is the key diagnostic tool for bladder cancer (BC). Unfortunately, the procedure is associated with several limitations, the most prominent being the subjective evaluation of biopsies, which is time-consuming and prone to inter- and intra-observer variability. Methods: To address this important unmet need, we propose a hybrid convolutional neural network (CNN)-transformer model for automated classification of BC from cystoscopic images. Results: The proposed model outperforms existing methods for BC detection and classification, achieving a mean accuracy of 96.6% for BC detection and 91.7% for classification of the four major subtypes of BC. Conclusion: Our study demonstrates that the proposed hybrid CNN-transformer model is a promising tool for automated detection and classification of BC from cystoscopic images, with potential to improve the accuracy and reduce the variability of current gold standard procedures.

With the rapid development of Large Language Models (LLMs), there is an increasing need to evaluate them in a comprehensive and unbiased way. While existing technical metrics can evaluate specific aspects of LLMs, they often fail to capture the overall quality of the LLMs or are tailored to specific tasks. Human preference evaluation is a natural fit to complement these technical metrics, as it can provide an aggregate score for an LLM in a cost-effective manner. However, organizing human preference evaluations is often impractical due to the significant time and resource investments required. To address these practical challenges, we introduce Chatbot Arena, an open platform for organizing human preference evaluations of LLMs. The platform streamlines the process of creating evaluation experiments, collecting data, and analyzing results. We demonstrate the utility of Chatbot Arena by hosting two human preference experiments comparing LLMs. The first experiment compares five state-of-the-art LLMs on three different datasets, and the second experiment compares two LLMs on a single dataset. Our experimental results show that Chatbot Arena can help researchers easily organize human preference experiments and that the resulting data can capture the strengths and weaknesses of the LLMs in a comprehensive way. Our platform, code, and data are publicly available.

Non-verbal signals in speech are encoded by prosody and carry information that ranges from conversation action (e.g., turn taking) to emotion expression, which is typically captured via the intonational or pitch contour of a word sequence [1]. Although there has been considerable progress on understanding how non verbal cues contribute towards conveying affective meaning through tone variation across languages such as Mandarin Chinese[2], little research exists investigating whether this phenomenon holds true for tonal language varieties like English with its own distinctive intonation patterns. [3] Thus far however few studies have examined differences between different accents within single variety contexts; here we aim address these knowledge gaps focusing specifically upon British Stan

We study a new highly-practical problem setting that enables and encourages the development of new unsupervised domain adaptation (UDA) algorithms: universal domain adaptation (UDA), in which a single learner is asked to adapt to a "universal" target domain that includes many subdomains. In this setting, we propose LEAD, a novel source-free UDA algorithm that learns a decomposition of the universal target domain into its subdomains, and then adapts to each subdomain using only unlabeled samples. The key enablers of LEAD are (a) a novel subdomain discovery algorithm that identifies subdomains using only unlabeled target data, and (b) a new decomposition-based adaptation algorithm that combines the subdomain classifiers to make full use of the unlabeled data. Compared to existing source-free UDA methods, LEAD achieves significantly higher performance, in line with or exceeding the performance of supervised UDA algorithms. Furthermore, LEADs performance is competitive even when used with a strong source model, matching the performance of source-free UDA algorithms. Our code is publicly available at https://github.com/RAIVNLab/LEAD.

We investigate the problem of multimodal machine translation, where the translation is guided by a caption. Our starting point is a strong monomodal baseline: a Transformer-based model that achieves state-of-the-art performance on English-Vietnamese translation. We show that the model can be improved by multimodal pretraining, where the caption is used to regularize the hidden representations of the Transformer. We also find that the model benefits from seeing more training data when the caption is provided. Our experiments show that the gains are largest for difficult cases, i.e., when the source text is short or contains proper names.

Action Quality Assessment (AQA) evaluates diverse skills but models struggle with non-standard actions. We propose a method to incorporate user-defined Actions into the AQA process, called Action Guided AQA (AGAQA). To this end, we collect and analyze annotated videos of various activities performed by human subjects. Our results show that the quality assessment model trained on the collected dataset outperforms its counterpart trained on standard datasets. We further show that, using the same action definitions, the model generalizes well across datasets, domains, and evaluation metrics, demonstrating its versatility and robustness. The source code will be available at: https://github.com/zulkarnain123/Action-Guided-AQA.git.git.

The rapidly increasing capabilities of large language models (LLMs) raise an urgent need to align them with human values. One approach is ethical pretraining: fine-tuning LLMS on a corpus designed to instill specific desirable properties, such as social justice or fairness and diversity in text generation tasks like autocomplete systems [1]. However this process can be challenging for users who lack expertise about ethics; they may find it difficult to understand the underlying principles behind these datasets which might lead into unintentional biases being introduced during training due their incomplete knowledge base. [2] To address that challenge we propose EPEA - Ethical Pretrained Embeddings by Augmenting Ethics Awareness within Existing Pretrained Models via Adaptive Learning Rate C

Development of multimodal interactive systems is hindered by the lack of rich, multimodal data. To address this issue and to facilitate research on cross-modal learning in human computer interaction (HCI), we collected a novel dataset that contains multiple modalities across different sensor types: video frames captured using RGB cameras; depth maps extracted from stereo camera pair images or LiDAR point clouds with associated pose information for each frame as well its corresponding audio stream sampled at 16 kHz sample rate per channel .

Deep learning has great potential to transform optimization, but its application to constrained optimization problems is still developing. In this paper, we propose deep augmented Lagrangian methods (DALM) to solve general constrained optimization problems. DALM combines the Lagrangian dual function and the augmented Lagrangian method to derive a nonconvex penalty-based objective function, which consists of a differentiable function and a nondifferentiable penalty term. We show that the penalty term acts as a constraint qualification condition and the differentiable function has the saddle point property. These properties enable us to optimize the penalty-based objective function using recent advances in deep learning. We show that DALM outperforms the state-of-the-art methods on a variety of benchmark problems.

This paper investigates mechanism design for decision-aware collaboration via federated learning (FL) platforms. Specifically, we focus on a setting where multiple participants are simultaneously collecting and processing data in order to learn an accurate model that will be used by the platform operator or other parties outside of this FL environment without revealing their individual datasets' content during training time nor posttraining inference stage; however due limited resources available at each participant node level compared against those provided through centralized infrastructure models like Google Cloud Platform(GCP).

We present Neural Riemannian Distance Fields (NRDF), a novel representation for learning articulated pose priors. NRDF learns a latent space that encodes several important properties relevant to articulated shapes, such as rest pose, pose variance, and shape correspondence. NRDF relies on Riemannian distance fields (RDFs) to measure the similarity between any two vertices of an articulated model in a shape- and pose-aware manner. We demonstrate that NRDFs learn accurate and informative pose priors that improve results in several tasks, including shape deformation, pose estimation, and shape completion. Particularly, on the AMASS dataset, NRDFs yield state-of-the-art results in terms of reconstruction accuracy when used with a standard volumetric rendering model.

Multiple Object Tracking (MOT) is a critical area within computer vision, with a broad spectrum of applications including intelligent traffic management systems [1], autonomous driving technology[2] and sports analytics. Despite the extensive research in MOT algorithms over the last few decades,[3],[4][5]-[8]. The performance remains far from satisfactory for real-world scenarios due to large occlusion events. [9]- This can be attributed to two challenges; First ,the identification process involves matching an object's appearance across multiple frames which requires accurate detection as well tracking techniques that are robust against occlusions .

Modern computer vision pipelines handle large images in one of two sub-optimal ways: down-sampling to smaller sizes that do not preserve resolution, or using costly and latency-prone methods for full-resolution processing. Our work introduces a new framework that can perform both 1x1 convolutions and 2D Transformers at full resolution while being both efficient and scalable on modern hardware. This is achieved by rewriting the convolution to be a local summation over a sparse set of points, which we refer to as a "sparsely connected graph". To further reduce computational complexity, we leverage recent developments in graph neural networks to design an efficient algorithm that computes an approximate solution to this problem. As a result, our method allows us to compute arbitrary convoluti

There are large amounts of transactional data which showed consumer shopping cart at a store that sells various goods. Large language models (LLMs) have been utilized to process this kind of data, but the lack of labeled data for each item has caused inaccurate results. In this study, we propose a novel approach called Item-Driven Fine-Tuning (IDFT). IDFT is an approach that can fine-tune LLMs by providing high-quality item labels in the form of text. The method combines reinforcement learning with the pretrained model and allows the LLMs to learn from the generated item labels. To provide item labels, we propose the item-driven prompt (IDP), which is simple and easy to generate.

We study the complexity of Non-Gaussian Component Analysis (NGCA) in the Statistical Query model, a generalization of the PAC model that allows for efficient computations. Previous works have shown that NGCA is tractable under the PAC and the agnostic PAC models, but only for Gaussian distributions. In this work we show that NGCA is tractable under the statistical query model when the distribution is non-Gaussian. For that purpose, we develop new tools for the analysis of learning problems under this model and derive tight bounds for the sample complexity of NGCA. Our results can be seen as an extension of the well known KKL Theorem to the case of non-Gaussian distributions. Furthermore, our approach applies to any linear dimension reduction problem where the goal is to find the closest su

While deep learning has celebrated many successes, its results often hinge on the meticulous selection of hyperparameters. Although a wide variety of methods exist to tune these parameters, they are either computationally expensive or do not provide any guarantees on finding the optimal solution. The latter is particularly problematic for constrained problems (e.g., budgeted hyperparameter search), which are common in practice. In this work, we propose Hypergraph Primal-Dual Adaptive Search (HyPADAS) as an alternative to existing methods that does not rely on heuristic rules or gradient estimation to optimize constrained hyperparameter search problems. It employs dual decomposition and adaptive search to perform both exploration and exploitation, while providing theoretical guarantees on c

Object slip perception is essential for mobile manipulation robots to perform manipulation tasks reliably and accurately. However, existing approaches are either data-inefficient or require a large number of human demonstrations, which limits their practical applications. To address this issue, we propose a novel approach named CMPD, based on contrastive learning. In particular, our method can effectively capture the fine-grained differences between objects with similar appearance through unsupervised pre-training on abundant object slip datasets. Subsequently, these knowledge is utilized to fine-tune the model with real object slip datasets in an efficient manner. Experimental results demonstrate that our method outperforms state-of-the-art methods across multiple datasets. The code will

Language provides a way to break down complex concepts into digestible pieces. Recent works in robotics have started exploiting language to simplify planning and reasoning. However, the inherent complexity of these methods limits their ability to generalize to new problems or environments. We address this challenge by introducing a simple but effective approach that uses language-based instruction to guide the learning of visual representations from images. Our method, called ImageGPT, leverages a diffusion model as a visual representation learner and prompts it with natural language instructions extracted from human demonstrations. The resulting visual representations are then used to plan for novel tasks using a symbolic planner. Our experiments show that ImageGPT can achieve state-of-th

Few-shot learning (FSL) aims to learn novel tasks with very few labeled samples. Recently, contrastive self training(CST), which first trains a classifier on the source domain and then generates pseudo labels from it for unsupervised data augmentation in target domains has shown impressive performance improvements over prior FLS approaches that rely only upon supervised fine tuning of classifiers initialized by pretrained models or trained as generative adversarial networks.. However CST suffers severely when applied directly across datasets because its underlying assumption about label consistency between different sources is often violated due cross dataset differences suchas image stylesor semantic meanings associatedwith classes .

In production rendering systems, caustics are typically rendered via photon mapping and gathering, a technique that can yield highly accurate results but with very high computational cost. This paper introduces two novel methods for accelerating the generation of caustic photons by utilizing existing precomputed radiance maps to estimate both incoming illumination directions as well indirect contributions from specular reflections in nearby surfaces (in addition). We demonstrate how these techniques produce dramatic speedups while maintaining visual fidelity compared against traditional direct-lighting only approaches on complex scenes involving sharp edges or glossy materials such glass spheres immersed within water droplets falling onto reflective metal plates; this enables real time int

We present a simple framework for complex data clustering. The proposed method CDC (Cluster by Distance and Density) is based on the identification of density peaks and the use of a new distance measure, namely MDistance. The novelty of our approach is twofold. First, we propose to identify the clusters directly from the data by detecting the density peaks using a simplified version of the DBSCAN algorithm. Second, we introduce a new distance measure, namely MDistance, that takes into account both the Euclidean distance between data points and the density of the surrounding area. We show that the proposed method provides results comparable to the state-of-the-art on benchmark datasets.

The paper presents a heritage monitoring system, developed as a case study for the Italian municipalities of Volterra and Atari. The system, based on drone acquisitions, aims at assisting the monitoring of cultural heritage sites, through the creation of 3D models of the areas of interest. In addition, the system allows the integration of various types of data, creating a sort of digital twin of the heritage site. The monitoring system is composed of a drone equipped with a RGB-D camera and a LiDAR sensor, able to acquire images and point clouds of the heritage site. The images are then processed to obtain a textured mesh, while the point clouds are used to compute the variation of some selected features over time. In this work, we present the results obtained from the application of the monitoring system to the archaeological site of Volterra and to the industrial complex of Atari. While the former is a case study of an intangible heritage, the latter is a case study of a tangible heritage. Despite the different nature of the two sites, the monitoring system allows to obtain detailed models of both areas, useful to perform a close-to-the-ground analysis of the state of conservation of the heritage.

Sustainability has become one of the most important issues on the global agenda. The United Nations' Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs) provide a comprehensive sustainability framework that has been widely adopted by businesses. However, achieving sustainability goes beyond merely reporting on the SDGs; it requires a systematic approach to embed sustainability into the DNA of an organization. Many frameworks and approaches have been developed to assist organizations in their sustainability journeys. This paper aims to provide a comprehensive review of the current sustainability assessment frameworks and approaches. We analyzed 37 frameworks and approaches, including the latest GRI Standards, UN Global Compact, B Corp, and SASB standards, to identify the common elements and shortcomings. We present a holistic view of the current sustainability assessment frameworks and approaches, highlighting their relationships and differences. We found that while the frameworks and approaches have different focuses and structures, they share common elements, such as governance, strategy, and performance. The review provides valuable insights for practitioners, researchers, and standard-setting bodies working on sustainability reporting and disclosure.

Conventional control, such as model-based control, is commonly utilized in autonomous driving to guarantee safety and efficiency. However, it is challenging to adapt to different situations because it relies on a single control model that does not take into account the uncertainty of the environment. To address this issue, we propose an online adaptive feedback control method for autonomous vehicles. Our approach leverages reinforcement learning (RL) to learn optimal control policies based on observations of the vehicle's state. We apply RL to a multi-agent system comprising three agents: the vehicle, a human driver, and other traffic participants. The agent with more observations is prioritized when making decisions. Additionally, we adopt an adaptive feedback control strategy, which invo

We design a deterministic subexponential time algorithm that takes as input a multivariate polynomial $f \in \mathbb{F}_p[x_1,...,x_n]$ and solves the following problem. Given an integer $b$, find all pairs $(i,j)$ for which there exists an $m$-tuple $(y_1,...,y_m) \in \mathbb{F}_p^m$ such that $|f(y_1,...,y_m)| < p^{1/2}$. In other words, we determine the minimal number of variables in any nontrivial zero-dimensional solution set to $f$. Our algorithms are based on a novel application of the well known technique of $p$-adic integrals to polynomial factorization over finite fields.

Combining images from multi-modalities is beneficial to explore various information in computer vision, such as depth estimation and semantic segmentation. Recently deep learning has been demonstrated powerful ability of integrating the complementary modalities for better performance by utilizing cross modality features extracted at different scales or hierarchical levels across multiple layers via fusion module(s). However there are still some limitations that need further improvement: 1) The existing works mainly focus on fusing high level feature maps ignoring low frequency details which may lead poor results when applied into real world scenes with large scale variation; 2 ) Most methods only consider global consistency without considering local geometric structure constraint between

This paper focuses on developing Pareto-optimal estimation and policy learning to identify the most effective treatment policy for a given set of patients. The goal is to simultaneously determine the optimal treatment policy and identify the patient subpopulations that benefit from different treatments, without relying on any assumptions about treatment effects. We propose an optimization framework based on the Sinkhorn algorithm that generates Pareto-optimal solutions. We demonstrate the effectiveness of our method through simulated clinical trials, where we achieve higher welfare than existing methods while identifying more relevant subpopulations. Our work paves the way for a more comprehensive understanding of personalized medicine and provides a foundation for the development of futur

We study the inventory placement problem of splitting $Q$ units of a single item across warehouses when unit-demand customers arrive at each warehouse. The goal is to minimize the total expected cost over time, which is the sum of the setup costs incurred by opening or closing warehouses and the unit holding cost incurred due to unsold items. Our focus is on the setting with fixed capacity constraints: we assume that each warehouse has an upper bound on its inventory level. In addition, we consider a continuous version of this problem where each customer's demand follows an arbitrary distribution. To solve these problems, we first show that the corresponding LP relaxation can be solved in polynomial time via dynamic programming. Then, we apply a randomized rounding algorithm based on a wei

Theoretical linguists have suggested that some languages (e.g., Chinese and Japanese) are "cooler" than other languages based on the observation that the intended meaning of phrases in these languages depends more on their contexts. As a result, many expressions in these languages are shortened, and their meaning is inferred from the context. In this paper, we focus on the omission of the plurality and definiteness markers in Chinese noun phrases (NPs) to investigate the predictability of their intended meaning given the contexts. To this end, we built a corpus of Chinese NPs, each of which is accompanied by its corresponding context, and by labels indicating its singularity/plurality and definiteness/indefiniteness. We carried out corpus assessments and analyses. The results suggest that

Leveraging pre-trained visual language models has become a widely adopted approach for improving performance in vision and language tasks, including zero-shot classification. However, these models are often trained on large scale benchmarks that involve a wide range of object categories. In this work, we study the impact of category distribution in the training set and show that the performance of visual language models significantly degrades when they are evaluated on new distributions of categories not seen during training. To address this challenge, we propose a novel method called Categorical Augmentation (CatAug), which generates diverse synthetic images of objects from under-represented categories using 3D graphics. Our experimental results demonstrate that CatAug can improve accurac

Graph-level contrastive learning, aiming to learn the representations for each graph by contrasting two different views of it. However current methods often fail in large networks due that: (1) existing pooling strategies have difficulty aggregating global information from graphs with a high number nodes; and also cannot capture node features effectively;(2 )the representation space is not fully utilized when using only one negative sample during training because there are other candidate samples which could be useful as well.(3)(4). This paper proposes GCLN++(graph contrast learner network), an effective approach based on both local feature aggregation strategy via multiview attention mechanisms augmented selfattention block,(5))and efficient positive sampling method through multiple view

Modern deep learning (DL) models necessitate the employment of scaling and compression techniques for effective deployment on resource-limited edge devices. This paper presents a novel approach to address this challenge: Compressible Edge Deep Learning with Quantization and Pruning (CEDLQP). CEDLQP employs a hybrid quantization strategy, combining 16-bit fixed-point quantization for weights and activations and 8-bit fixed-point quantization for biases. It also integrates weight pruning through magnitude thresholding, followed by iterative retraining. The combination of these techniques enables the fine-tuning of the quantized model using limited training data without significant accuracy loss, while effectively reducing the model size and computational complexity. Experimental results reve

In the logic programming paradigm, a program is defined by a set of methods, each of which maps one input term into an output term. In a recent paper in IJCAI'21 we introduced a generalization of this model, called "Generalized Logic Programs", where a method may map multiple inputs to one or several outputs. The key idea is to extend the notion of substitution to replace terms with sets of terms. In this work we introduce and investigate a new kind of Generalized Logic Programs where the output of a method is not only a set of terms but also a set of relations between the output terms. This allows us to express the fact that two or more terms are related (e.g. they belong to the same list), while at the same time defining a mapping from all these terms to other terms. We show how this mod

Large Language Models (LLMs) have shown impressive performance in recent years. However, there are concerns that LLMs may hallucinate incorrect information, leading to degraded performance and trustworthiness. In this work, we propose a new benchmarking framework to evaluate and compare the hallucination tendencies of different LLMs. Specifically, we generate unanswerable math word problems that contain hallucinated facts and use them to perturb LLM-generated answers. We then propose a novel metric called Hallucination Score (HS) to quantify the hallucination tendency of an LLM. HS is calculated based on the overlap between the LLM-generated answer and the perturbed answers. We evaluate 15 popular LLMs by computing their HS on our benchmark. The results show that LLMs can be hallucinating in different degrees and tendencies. We also demonstrate the correlation between the pre-training objectives of LLMs and their hallucination tendencies.

Vision-language models (VLMs) are known to have a bias towards generating prompts, i.e., VLMs often outperform other models when prompted with task-specific language. However, VLMs are often less performant than other models on unsupervised benchmarks, because they are trained on supervised tasks with labeled data. In this work, we introduce PromptKD, a novel unsupervised prompt distillation method for VLMs that does not require labeled data. Specifically, PromptKD leverages the VLM's own predictions as pseudo-labels to supervise the learning of prompts. To this end, we propose a self-supervised prediction game where the VLM plays the role of a teacher model, and a small prompt model is trained as a student. During training, the prompt model is encouraged to generate prompts that help the student VLM to produce outputs similar to those of the teacher VLM. To ensure that the prompt model captures the essential characteristics of the teacher's predictions, we propose using a teacher forcing approach where the student uses the teacher's prompt to generate its own prediction. Experiments on standard VLM benchmarks show that PromptKD consistently outperforms other unsupervised baselines and achieves performance close to the supervised baselines. Our code is publicly available at https://github.com/RAIVNLab/PromptKD.

The execution and runtime performance of model-based analysis tools for realistic large-scale ABMs is a key challenge. We present the design, implementation (in Julia), evaluation using synthetic models as well sensitivity to stochasticity in population dynamics data from real world studies on human mobility patterns across 123 countries during COVID lockdowns We propose an approach that can efficiently process massive graphs with dynamic updates by maintaining multiple versions representing different snapshots over time while also providing fine granularity support through queries spanning specific intervals within those periods covered between these points when changes occurred most frequently - this makes it possible even without needing complete historical records which otherwise may

Caustics rendering is a challenging problem in computer graphics, which aims to simulate the realistic light transport phenomenon in optical media. In this paper, we propose a novel online photon guiding technique using 3D Gaussians to render caustics. Our method captures the spatial variation of the optical density of the medium in the forward path and the backward path of light transport. In the forward path, we propose to approximate the optical density field with 3D Gaussians centered at the intersection points of the light rays and the medium boundary. In the backward path, we accumulate the 3D Gaussians sampled along the traced photon path to estimate the optical density field. We then solve the photon guiding problem by minimizing the difference between the accumulated 3D Gaussians and the 3D Gaussians representing the optical density field. Our experiments show that the proposed method can produce high-quality caustics rendering results with fine structures and details. The proposed method is an extension of our previous work [Fang et al., 2021] with more comprehensive evaluation and comparison.

Are malicious repositories hiding under the educational label in GitHub? Recent studies have identified collections of GitHub repositories that are malicious, i.e., they contain code snippets designed to compromise systems or steal data from users. However, existing studies focus on identifying single malicious repositories instead of identifying whole collections of them. This paper presents a novel approach to detecting such malicious collections, based on analyzing the repository metadata and code patterns found within each collection. Our approach first constructs a large-scale database containing both educational and non-educational repositories. Then, it identifies suspicious repositories by analyzing their metadata and code patterns. Finally, our approach generates a collection of s

Multimodal electronic health records (mEHR) integrate structured data, such as diagnosis and procedure codes, with unstructured text, such as free-form notes written by clinicians. The unstructured data contains rich information that is difficult to extract using traditional methods, but is crucial for tasks such as medication outcome prediction or patient similarity search. One challenge in processing mEHR is the dynamic nature of patient data, which is recorded as events in a time series. Events can be temporally correlated, and modeling this correlation can help preserve the context of the events. In this paper, we propose a novel mEHR tokenization method that incorporates temporal information through a dynamic embedding mechanism. We use a bi-directional Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) network to encode the temporal correlation of each event, and cross-attention to learn the correlation between events from different modalities. Our model successfully captures the temporal dynamics of events in mEHR and achieves state-of-the-art performance on benchmark tasks.

Human comprehension of a video stream is naturally broad: in a few instants, we are able to identify the scene contents and recognize events from their unfolding. We argue that this ability relies on our capacity for spatio-temporal abstraction - i.e., detecting patterns across space or time (or both) within individual frames as well learning how these emerge over multiple ones by means of temporal generalization [1]. This work focuses specifically upon disentangling spatial aspects into features invariant/equivariant with respect towards object locations via an instance segmentation module coupled at inference stage onto frame stacks captured through camera motion trajectory trajectories; it also considers analogous abstractions based off image sequences generated using 3D graphics softwa

Public bus transit systems provide critical transportation services for large sections of modern communities. On-time performance (OTP) is a crucial metric that indicates the reliability and effectiveness of public transit systems in serving passengers' commuting needs. However, due to the high complexity of various factors affecting OTP, such as traffic congestion, driver behavior, and operational constraints, it remains challenging for managers to accurately assess the current state of OTP and predict future trends. This paper proposes an Artificial Intelligence-based solution aimed at providing real-time monitoring and forecasting of OTP, with the goal of improving service efficiency and quality. Our methodology involves data collection and processing, followed by model selection throug

Recommender systems are vulnerable to injective attacks, which inject limited fake users into the platforms. This paper proposes an attack methodology that exploits a specific feature of recommender systems: their tendency to recommend items similar to those previously recommended to a user. We propose a new injective attack, in which the injected fake users share similar preferences with each other and exhibit similar behaviors, such as viewing and clicking on a particular type of item. The attack leverages this similarity to increase its effectiveness and reduce detection rates. Our experiments demonstrate significant improvements compared to previous approaches and highlight the importance of understanding the behavioral similarities among users for effective recommendation attacks. As

The rise of social discussion platforms, like Reddit, has changed the way people discuss and decide on financial matters. However, little is known about the impact these platforms are having on financial behavior. Using a comprehensive dataset of financial discussions on Reddit and surveys of Reddit users, we find evidence that participation in financial discussions on Reddit influences users' financial decisions. Our results show that exposure to discussions about credit cards increases the likelihood that users will apply for a credit card, while exposure to discussions about investing increases users' knowledge about investing and their intention to invest. The effects are strongest among users who are susceptible to social influence. Moreover, users are able to find and participate in discussions that are relevant to their own financial situation, suggesting that these platforms can fulfill users' information needs and help them make sense of financial products. We find no evidence of harm from this behavior, and our results are consistent with the idea that social discussion platforms can be a net positive for society by helping users make informed decisions.

Recent advances in text-guided image compression have shown great potential to enhance the perceptual quality of compressed images while maintaining high compression ratios. This has paved the way for enhanced real-world applications, including autonomous driving systems and smart homes. However, the challenges posed by these complex real-world environments demand a higher level of performance from compression models, necessitating advanced architectures designed specifically for such applications. In this work, we introduce a novel approach that uses an encoder-decoder framework with Transformer blocks to process compressed features, effectively reducing data size. Additionally, we incorporate a fine-tuning strategy that utilizes additional model parameters trained on pre-trained data. Ou

Reaching consensus is key to multi-agent coordination. To accomplish a cooperative task, the agents need to converge on a common understanding of the task and their respective roles in achieving it. In this paper, we investigate the role of language in establishing consensus among autonomous agents. We propose a novel approach called Linguistic Consensus that leverages natural language processing (NLP) techniques to facilitate consensus building. Our method involves identifying salient words within an input text and then utilizing them as prompts to generate new texts using large language models (LLMs). By introducing these new texts into the conversation, the agents can refine their understanding of the problem and adjust their behavior accordingly. The linguistic perspective allows us to

Recent studies of the applications of conversational AI tools, such as chatbots powered by Large Language Models (LLMs), have revealed their potential to provide support for mental health interventions. However, these tools may also pose risks to patients' safety and privacy. In this paper, we first present a framework that encompasses both technical and non-technical safeguards related to LLM-powered chatbots in mental health contexts. We then conduct a systematic literature review on the existing approaches for developing safe and secure LLM-powered chatbots for mental health use cases. Our study reveals an urgent need for integrating both technical and non-technical safeguards within the development lifecycle of LLM-based chatbots in mental health contexts.

The vast accumulation of neuroscience knowledge presents a challenge for researchers to timely and accuratly identify the most important findings that can be generalized across different studies. In this work, we propose an unsupervised learning approach based on multi-layer graph representation model (MLGR) with multitask attention mechanism in orderto capture both common patterns shared by multiple tasks as well asa more specific task pattern learned within each individual data set from various domains suchas neuroimaging or behavioral experiments . Our proposed method enables us effectively discover meaningful similarities between diverse datasets without requiring any manual annotation process thus making it easierfor users understand their results quickly while also providing valuable

Integrating robotics into human-centric environments such as homes, necessitates advanced manipulation capabilities. One of the most fundamental and challenging tasks in this context is grasping objects with a robotic hand to pick them up or handle their surfaces for various purposes (e. g., cleaning). This task typically requires precise knowledge about object geometry which can be obtained through learning from data collected by physically interacting via touch sensing using tactile sensors on robot fingers/hands [1].

Our work addresses the challenges older adults face with commercial Voice Assistants (VAs) and how we can improve their experience. We are interested in understanding whether VAs provide a beneficial cognitive support for memory, as well as if they promote independence by helping people to access information stored on digital devices without needing help from others or resorting more frequently to written notes/lists which may be less accessible than voice-based input interfaces due old age related impairments such slow processing speed(s).

The capacity to generalize beyond the range of training data is a pivotal challenge, often synonymous with the concept of "out-of-distribution" (OOD) generalization. Despite significant advances in theoretical understanding and empirical performance, current state-of-the-art methods are restricted by their reliance on complex architectures that necessitate extensive computational resources for training. In this paper, we propose a simple yet effective method to alleviate these challenges through a novel approach of adversarial representation learning (ARL). Our ARL framework comprises two neural networks: a generative model and an adaptive discriminator. While the former generates samples from the latent space, the latter is specifically designed to detect samples that deviate from the dis

Actionable Warning Identification (AWI) plays a pivotal role in improving the usabililty of safety critical systems. AWI is an interactive system that prompts users with warnings to indicate potentially dangerous situations before they occur, thereby allowing them time and space for recovery actions or contingency planning activities such as rerouting flights via alternate airports due weather conditions at destination airport which could cause delays if left unchecked by flight crew members during pre-flight checks while onboard aircraft itself; these types include: 1.) Flight Control Warnings - When there are problems related specifically towards controlling speed/altitude etc., then appropriate messages will be displayed on cockpit display screens so pilots can make adjustments accordin

Root cause analysis (RCA) is a critical problem in various domains, including cybersecurity, healthcare, and engineering. The goal is to identify the underlying causes of observed symptoms or events. Traditional RCA methods are often limited in their ability to handle complex, real-world scenarios. Recently, there has been growing interest in using Large Language Models (LLMs) for RCA due to their potential to understand and generate natural language, reason over large datasets, and perform complex computations. In this paper, we investigate the effectiveness of LLMs for RCA by fine-tuning a state-of-the-art LLM, namely Red Hat's AI Assistant, to act as an agent that assists users in performing RCA. We evaluate the LLM-based agent in a user study and compare it to a baseline expert system. Our results show that the LLM-based agent performs similarly to the expert system in terms of task completion time and user satisfaction. However, the LLM-based agent provides more accurate results, takes less time to complete the task, and users find it more helpful.

The rise of deep learning has revolutionized many areas of computer science, including computer vision, natural language processing, and reinforcement learning. At the same time, deep learning models are computationally intensive and require extensive hyperparameter tuning to perform optimally on a given task. Unfortunately, the process of tuning deep learning models is often a black box, requiring extensive domain knowledge and manual trial and error. To tune deep learning models, researchers and engineers use a variety of heuristic methods, such as grid search and random search, as well as more recent advancements like reinforcement learning-based methods and Bayesian optimization. However, despite the popularity of these methods, there is a lack of a comprehensive survey of the current state-of-the-art in deep learning configuration performance. To fill this gap, our paper provides a systematic survey of the current methods for deep learning configuration performance. We present a taxonomy of the different methods and analyze the advantages and disadvantages of each method. We also discuss the relationship between deep learning configuration performance and related fields, such as reinforcement learning and meta-learning. Finally, we provide recommendations for future research in this field.

Managing complex Cyber-Physical Energy Systems (CPES) requires solving various optimization problems with multiple objectives. This is challenging due to the nonlinearities and interactions between components in these systems, such as power networks, electric vehicles, solar panels, wind turbines, and energy storage facilities. The emerging field of metaheuristics has shown promising results for CPES management by combining search algorithms with evolutionary approaches. However, current metaheuristic techniques struggle to adequately manage multi-objective optimization in CPES due to their limited capability to handle complex and nonlinear problems. In this paper, we introduce a new technique called "Meta-Evolutionary Evaluation" that leverages an evolutionary algorithm for evaluating sol

In few-shot learning, selecting a few informative examples for model training can significantly improve the learning efficiency. However, it is often challenging to identify the optimal examples due to the potential class imbalance and high similarity within classes. Previous works typically address this problem by designing models to learn example representations. In contrast, this paper focuses on the metric learning paradigm, where the metrics are directly learned from examples. Specifically, we propose a novel metric learning approach for few-shot example selection, which aims to maximize the inter-class distance and minimize the intra-class distance simultaneously. To our knowledge, this is the first work to apply metric learning principles to few-shot learning. We evaluate the proposed method on few-shot classification and meta-learning tasks and show that it consistently outperforms the current state-of-the-art example selection methods. Furthermore, we pre-train our model using a self-supervised clustering method, which further improves the few-shot learning performance. The source code is available at \url{https://github.com/luost26/metric-learning-for-few-shot-example-selection}.

Few-shot knowledge graph completion (FKGC) aims to query the unseen facts from the given few-shot knowledge graphs. To address this problem, we propose a novel framework called KG4Few that can effectively learn and generalize across different few-shot tasks with only one training set. Firstly, we introduce an effective representation learning mechanism, namely, \textbf{L}earning-\textbf{N}ormalized \textbf{C}ross-\textbf{E}ntropy (LoNeCe), which not only incorporates the instance features but also utilizes entity features for further improving the final embedding quality of the target entities. Additionally, we introduce a new task selection strategy named \textbf{T}ask-aware \textbf{S}elector (\textbf{TASTER}) based on the idea that we should select those tasks with high relevance to the

In recent years, palmprints have been widely used for individual verification. The rich privateness and anti-spoofing properties of palmprints make it an appropriate biometric modality in many applications. However, the traditional palmprint recognition methods are unable to deal with real-world challenges, such as large intra-class variations caused by age, gender, and posture changes, etc., thus limiting their practical applications. To address these issues, this study proposes a novel deep learning method based on generative adversarial networks (GANs) to synthesize realistic palm images from small datasets. Specifically, we propose a two-stage GAN framework: the first stage utilizes a conditional generative model to generate palm images with diverse identities while maintaining high fi

It is especially challenging to achieve real-time human motion tracking on a standalone VR Head Mounted Display (HMD). The main reason is that the HMD's limited processing power and memory limit the use of machine learning models. We propose an efficient, accurate, and scalable real-time framework for HMD-based motion tracking using only two cameras and no external sensors. Our framework leverages 3D pose estimation from videos, which facilitates fast and reliable tracking. We also introduce a novel loss function to train our network with minimal computation requirements. Through extensive experiments, we demonstrate the effectiveness of our method in real-world scenarios. The proposed framework enables seamless interaction between the user and virtual objects without compromising performa

(p,q)-clique enumeration on a bipartite graph is critical for calculating clustering coefficients. We study the problem of determining whether there exists an edge between two nodes in a bipartite graph such that it forms a $(p,q)$-clique. We propose an efficient algorithm to solve this problem in $O(n^4\log n)$ time. Our algorithm improves the previous best known $O(n^3)$ algorithm by Nishimura et al.~\cite{NISHI201859}. The new algorithm significantly reduces the number of comparisons and improves the running time from $O(n^4)$ to $O(n^4 \log n)$. As a special case, we also obtain an efficient algorithm for solving the $(p,p+q)$-clique enumeration problem in $O(n^3\log n)$ time.

Chinese landscape painting has a unique and artistic style, and its drawing technique is highly abstract in both brushwork and color. This study proposes an efficient method for automatic landscape drawing based on the characteristics of Chinese landscape painting. The proposed method first segments the image into four regions corresponding to four basic colors (i.e., green, yellow, blue, and black), where each region is then processed through a single-channel convolutional neural network with a 2D kernel of size $3\times 3$. The results show that the proposed method can effectively extract features from images and generate realistic landscape drawings. In addition, the proposed method can be applied to other styles of painting, demonstrating its general applicability. Our code and dataset

Deep clustering has exhibited remarkable performance; however, the overconfidence problem, i.e., too many samples are assigned to a single cluster and few points remain unclustered (i), hinders its further application in real-world tasks with large amounts of data clusters such as image retrieval systems where all images should be clustered into thousands or tens thousand categories for fast searching [1]. The stateoftheart methods usually adopt heuristic strategies that directly adjust hyperparameters without theoretical guarantees which could lead to inferior results when faced by complex scenarios containing diverse sample distributions(ii).

Second-order methods can converge much faster than first-order methods by incorporating second-order information. However, the majority of these methods rely on quadratic loss functions and suffer from high computational costs due to expensive Hessian matrix inversions or approximations. To reduce the cost, we propose an efficient algorithm for minimizing general nonlinear convex functions with second-order information. Our approach is based on a block-coordinate descent method that updates each variable independently, thereby avoiding the need for explicit Hessian computations. We show that the proposed algorithm converges linearly under the condition of Lipschitz continuity of the objective function and its gradient. Numerical experiments demonstrate the efficacy of our algorithm in solv

We study two-stage facility location games with $n$ atomic clients and $m$ facilities. In this setting, there are $n$ clients that are served by $m$ facilities. Each client $i$ has an initial location $x_i$ and a destination location $y_i$, and the facilities move from their initial locations $X = \{x_1, \dots, x_n\}$ to their final locations $Y = \{y_1, \dots, y_n\}$. Each facility $j$ has a cost $c_j$ of moving from its initial location $x_j$ to its final location $y_j$, and each client $i$ has a cost $d_i(j)$ of being served by a facility $j$. The goal of each client $i$ is to minimize its total cost of $\ell_1$-distance plus service cost, while the goal of each facility $j$ is to minimize its total service cost. We seek an equilibrium in which no client can reduce its total cost by moving to a different facility. We show that the problem admits a pure Nash equilibrium, and we present an algorithm to compute a pure Nash equilibrium with a polynomial number of iterations. We also show that the problem admits a randomized pure Nash equilibrium with a polynomial number of iterations.

Deep learning has great potential to change the landscape of software testing, with applications ranging from bug detection to vulnerability detection. Although many deep learning techniques have been applied to vulnerability detection, there is still a need to improve the state-of-the-art techniques in terms of accuracy and efficiency. In this paper, we propose several techniques to improve deep learning-based vulnerability detection. We propose a data augmentation technique that generates synthetic vulnerabilities by applying mutation operators to existing vulnerabilities. We use a multi-stage training approach that fine-tunes a pre-trained model on increasingly larger subsets of the training data. We also propose to reduce the dimensionality of the input features by selecting a subset of the most relevant features using a genetic algorithm. Our experiments show that our proposed techniques are effective in improving the accuracy and efficiency of deep learning-based vulnerability detection. Our data augmentation technique improves the F1 score by 16.8% on average, while our multi-stage training approach improves the F1 score by 13.2% on average. Our feature selection technique improves the F1 score by 12.7% on average.

Binary neural networks utilize 1-bit quantized weights and activations to reduce both the model size and inference latency, which are the two primary factors of computational cost. However, the bitwise operations in binary neural networks introduce non-linearities that are not present in their floating point counterparts. As a result, current techniques such as weight-sharing, quantization-aware training, and pruning often fail to effectively preserve model accuracy when applied to binary neural networks. In this paper, we propose a novel method, named Sparse Bitweight Networks (SBNets), for designing sparse binary networks with efficient memory access patterns. To do so, we first revisit existing methods and find that they typically follow a brute-force approach to identifying a subset of

Automatic Text Summarization (ATS), utilizing Natural Language Processing (NLP) techniques, has emerged as a critical tool in efficiently communicating information to audiences. However, ATS often relies on subjective human annotations of summarized texts, which can be time-consuming and prone to bias. To address this challenge, we propose an unsupervised approach that leverages the inherent semantic structure of natural language. Our method utilizes a multi-layered network architecture, which learns from sentence embeddings to generate informative and concise text summaries. We evaluate our model using three datasets: CNN/DM, XSum, and CQR, showcasing its effectiveness across varying lengths of input texts. Additionally, we perform a human evaluation of the generated summaries to assess t

The recent development of quantum computing, which makes use of entanglement, superposition, and other properties of quantum systems to perform computations, has the potential to revolutionize many fields. However, one of the main challenges in achieving this is the need for reliable error correction techniques to protect against errors introduced by noise in the system. Currently, most of the proposed quantum error correction (QEC) methods require a large amount of data or resources to implement efficiently. In this work, we introduce a novel QEC method based on a single-qubit error model, which requires only two qubits per encoded logical qubit. Our approach relies on the concept of parity-check matrices and uses classical optimization algorithms to solve the decoding problem. We show th

synthesizing realistic hand appearances is a fundamental problem in computer vision with a wide range of applications, from helping visually impaired people identify objects to enabling two-way communication with sign language. Despite significant progress in synthesizing realistic human faces, generating realistic hands is still challenging, especially when using text-to-image models that take a verbal description as input. This paper aims to fill this gap by focusing on the task of text-to-image generation with realistic hand appearances. We introduce a new model called HanDiffuser, which is a conditional diffusion model that generates realistic hands by combining a verbal description with a latent representation of the hand. To train our model, we create a new dataset called HanBank, which consists of 5,000 images of hands annotated with 17 types of verbal descriptions. To evaluate the performance of our model, we conduct two user studies, one for overall preference and one for realism. We also compare the performance of HanDiffuser with other models, including Stable Diffusion, ControlNet, and Pegasus. Our results show that HanDiffuser is preferred over other models for its more realistic hand appearances and finer details. Our work is publicly available at https://hanbank.github.io.

Recent text-to-image (T2I) models have had great success, and many benchmarks have been proposed to evaluate their performance and safety. However, they only consider explicit prompts while neglecting implicit prompts (hint at a target without explicitly mentioning it). These prompts may get rid of safety constraints and pose potential threats to the applications of these models. This position paper highlights the current state of T2I models toward implicit prompts. We present a benchmark named ImplicitBench and conduct an investigation on the performance and impacts of implicit prompts with popular T2I models. Specifically, we design and collect more than 2,000 implicit prompts of three aspects: General Symbols, Celebrity Privacy, and Not-Safe-For-Work (NSFW) Issues, and evaluate six well

Binary Neural Network (BNN) is a kind of hardware-friendly neural network that performs well on resource-constrained devices. However, existing BNNs suffer from low accuracy due to information loss in the binarization process. To improve the accuracy of BNNs, this paper proposes a novel A&B BNN that performs addition and bit-operation only. First, we design a new binary multiplier that performs addition and bit-operation only, and prove that the proposed binary multiplier can approximate the function of the conventional binary multiplier arbitrarily well. Then, we propose a bit-operation-based activation function, which is implemented by the newly designed binary multiplier. Finally, we prove that the proposed A&B BNN can approximate the function of the conventional BNN arbitrarily well. Experimental results on the CIFAR10 and ImageNet datasets demonstrate that the proposed A&B BNN achieves higher accuracy than the conventional BNN.

Large Language Models (LLMs) have demonstrated remarkable reasoning abilities, prompting interest in their application as black-box optimizers. This paper asserts that LLMs possess the capability for zero-shot optimization across diverse scenarios, including multi-objective and high-dimensional problems. We introduce a novel population-based method for numerical optimization using LLMs called Language-Model-Based Evolutionary Optimizer (LEO). Our hypothesis is supported through numerical examples, spanning benchmark and industrial engineering problems such as supersonic nozzle shape optimization, heat transfer, and windfarm layout optimization. We compare our method to several gradient-based and gradient-free optimization approaches. While LLMs yield comparable results to state-of-the-art

This paper describes the deployment and experimentation architecture of the Internet of Things experimentation facility being deployed at Santander city. The facility is implemented within the SmartSantander project, one of the projects of the Future Internet Research and Experimentation initiative of the European Commission and represents a unique in the world city-scale experimental research facility. Additionally, this facility supports typical applications and services of a smart city. Tangible results are expected to influence the definition and specification of Future Internet architecture design from viewpoints of Internet of Things and Internet of Services. The facility comprises a large number of Internet of Things devices deployed in several urban scenarios which will be federate

Face verification (FV) aims to verify whether two input images are of the same person or not. Although FV is a well-studied problem and achieved great success in the past decades, the lack of interpretability limits its application in real-world scenarios. In this paper, we propose an explainable FV framework by leveraging gradient backpropagation (GBP) and face feature guidance. Specifically, we first design a margin-based loss for enforcing stronger identity discrimination and thus improving the verifiability; then we employ face feature guidance to help GBP better locate the critical local visual clues for identity discrimination; finally, we use the guided GBP to explain the FV process by visualizing the critical visual clues. Extensive experiments on two widely-used FV datasets, i.e., IJB-B and YTF, demonstrate that our method achieves competitive FV performance and satisfactory explainability. Especially, the ablation study on the feature guidance module reveals that our method can focus on different critical visual clues for different testing images, showing the adaptivity of our method.

Automated theorem provers and formal proof assistants are general reasoning systems that are in theory capable of solving all problems of a given type. The vast majority of these tools is based on the resolution paradigm, which is known to be sound but incomplete. This has been a major challenge in applying automation technology to proofs. Recent developments have shown that, under certain circumstances, it can become feasible for modern automated theorem provers to produce fully automated proofs. In this paper we survey the current state of the art with respect to automating formal proof checking using decision procedures for first-order logic (FOL). We provide a detailed overview of both the mathematical foundations and practical algorithms used for FOL decision proving.

Miniaturised robotic systems are increasingly used for research and development on multi-robot teams, thanks to their low cost and ease of fabrication. However, they are often limited in terms of the payload they can carry, which severely affects the versatility of the teams they form. In addition, the lack of a standardised solution for actuation and communication prevents the widespread adoption of these platforms. In this paper, we present HeROS, a modular, reconfigurable and scalable heterogeneous robotic system platform. HeROS incorporates electric motors and wireless communication modules in each of its nodes, allowing for a compact design while maintaining the freedom of choosing the payload. The platform is provided with a user-friendly interface for quick setup and configuration, and its modular design allows for the creation of teams of robots with different specifications. We show the feasibility of our approach by building a prototype consisting of a team of three robots with different payloads. We demonstrate the potential of HeROS for research and development on heterogeneous robotic systems through two case studies: multi-robot reinforcement learning and multi-robot exploration.

Vision-Language Models (VLMs) such as GPT-4V have recently demonstrated a strong ability to perform tasks in both the visual and language domains. However, they struggle with performing these tasks across multiple modalities, resulting in a significant performance gap between monomodal and multimodal tasks. In this paper, we propose a simple yet effective technique to bridge this gap: Multimodal Prompting via Adaptation (MPA). MPA adapts textual prompts to multimodal ones using a prompt adaptation method, without retraining or fine-tuning the pretrained VLM. MPA is composed of two modules that can be applied to any VLMs: an adapter module to convert monomodal prompts into multimodal ones, and an optimizer to train the adapter module.

Masked image modeling (MIM), which predicts randomly masked patches from unmasked ones, has become an emerging paradigm for pretraining visual representations. However the existing MIM models are usually trained on large-scale datasets such as ImageNet and require expensive computing resources to obtain good results in downstream tasks like object detection or classification while providing limited finegrained information about small objects due their coarse resolution training images with a fixed size of 256×193 pixels across all channels/classes regardless what type they represent i..e., whether human faces versus cats vs cars etcetera).

Graph clustering, an important unsupervised problem, has been shown to be more resistant to overfitting than image classification in the literature. We propose a graph-based neural network called GCNM for graph clustering with the goal of providing better generalization and outperforming existing baselines. The GCNM model is composed of two parts: 1) A novel self-regularized message passing module that allows the model to learn local neighborhood information as well as the global graph structure simultaneously; 2) A novel contrastive loss function that enables the model to generate positive cluster assignments while rejecting negative ones. This approach significantly improves the overall performance of our proposed model on benchmark datasets and achieves superior results compared to stat

In the rapidly evolving field of Large Language Models (LLMs), there is a growing need for efficient and robust tools to evaluate their performance. This paper presents a novel LLM evaluation tool, termed Evaluation Tool for LLM (ET4LLM). Our tool offers two key features that distinguish it from existing solutions: a comprehensive suite of benchmarks and a highly customizable interface that allows users to adjust settings according to their specific needs. The benchmarks available through our platform cover several domains, including language modeling tasks such as grammar and style checking, question answering, text summarization, and more. Additionally, we have incorporated several parameters that enable fine-tuning of each evaluation according to specific user requirements, ensuring acc

This work explores an innovative algorithm designed to enhance the mobility of underactuated bipedal robots across challenging terrains, especially when navigating through spaces with constrained opportunities for foot support, like steps or stairs. By combining ankle torque with a refined angular momentum-based linear inverted pendulum model (ALIP), our method allows variability in the robot's center of mass height. We employ a dual-strategy controller that merges virtual constraints for precise motion regulation across essential degrees of freedom with an ALIP-centric model predictive control (MPC) framework, aimed at enforcing gait stability. The effectiveness of our feedback design is demonstrated through its application on the Cassie bipedal robot, which features 20 degrees of freedom

The stability-plasticity dilemma is a major challenge in continual learning, as the agent must be able to adapt to new tasks while retaining previously acquired knowledge. In this work, we propose SPLIT, an algorithm for Continual Learning with Stability and Plasticity that addresses this issue by leveraging self-supervised methods.
