Our approach involves integrating a self-supervised objective into the training process, enabling both the acquisition of new information through plasticity and the preservation of existing knowledge through stability. This integration is achieved using a contrastive loss function with a dynamic temperature parameter that controls the degree of stability or plasticity during learning. Through experiments on several benchmark datasets, we demonstrate that SPLIT

Human label variation arises when annotators assign different labels to the same item for valid reasons, while noise is when they do so erroneously. We consider the problem of identifying and resolving both types of label variations in datasets. In particular, we focus on multi-label image classification where each label has a distinct color. We propose LabelVariationFinder (LVF) -- an unsupervised method for automatically detecting label variations without any human intervention or expert knowledge. LVF leverages the inherent visual characteristics of colors associated with labels to identify similar images that have been assigned different labels. These similarities are then exploited to detect two types of label variations: (1) variations caused by a difference in color choice, and (2)

There are large amounts of transactional data which showed consumer shopping cart at a store that sells clothing online. The goal is to predict the size (in US dollars) for the cart’s purchase amount using various methods including random forest, decision trees and logistic regression models with feature engineering A linear model has been used to classify credit card transactions as either fraudulent or non-fraudulent based on several features related to each transaction such us time stamp information about when it occurred during day/night hours along side other factors like how much money was spent by customer in previous 12 months etc.. This project aims use this same approach but instead apply machine learning techniques so find better results than what one might get just through sta

Identifying errors in parallel MPI programs is a challenging task. Despite the growing number of verifiers for various programming languages, there are still limited solutions to verify concurrent data-parallel applications written with OpenMP and/or CUDA C++. We introduce an interactive verification framework that automates parallelization by applying different scheduling strategies on code segments identified as potential bottlenecks via profiling or machine learning techniques while providing feedback during execution about any uncovered bugs using lightweight assertions at runtime without modifying application source files directly but instead injecting additional instructions into intermediate representations generated from them beforehand through LLVM IR transformations if necessary

In this paper, we study how well human speech can automatically be filtered when this overlaps with the voice of a machine speaker. We use two types of data: the first is synthetic audio generated by text-to-speech (TTS) systems; the second contains conversations between humans and machines. We find that current speech recognition systems have difficulty distinguishing between human and TTS voices, particularly in speech overlap scenarios. In the case where both are present, the accuracy of automatic speech recognition (ASR) decreases to 24% compared to 50% for only human speech. When ASR is used to transcribe human-machine conversations, our proposed method achieves an average word error rate of 19.7%. This performance is lower than for human-human conversations, but higher than other app

This paper proposes the task of automatic assessment of Sentence Translation Exercises (STE) in a computer-assisted language learning (CALL) context. The STE is an effective way to improve learners' translation skills, but it is challenging to automatically assess it as it requires both natural language understanding and machine translation capabilities. We propose a novel approach that leverages pre-trained large language models (LLMs) to perform this complex task. Specifically, we introduce a three-step methodology: first, we use the LLM to evaluate the source sentence; then, we use another LLM to generate the target translation; finally, we compare the output translation with the ground truth provided by human experts using a similarity metric. Our experiments show that our proposed met

Federated learning, as a promising distributed learning paradigm, enables collaborative training of a machine-learning model without disclosing the raw data. Existing federated learning algorithms focus on optimizing local models with limited communication and coordination between different parties to achieve global convergence. However, it is often time-consuming and energy-expensive for individual participants to train their local models in an online manner. To address this issue, we propose a novel federated learning algorithm named FL-Tap which can significantly accelerate the training process by enabling each participant to perform model training locally via offline training (FL-Tap-OL), while also employing online learning (FL-Tap-ON) if they are available at any given time during tr

Current approaches to question answering rely on pre-trained language models (PLMs) like RoBERTa. This paper introduces a novel approach that employs pre-trained vision transformers (ViTs), specifically ViT-Tiny, for answering questions in the form of images. We demonstrate the superior performance of our method over PLMs and previous works by achieving an average answer accuracy of 92.3% on a challenging dataset consisting of 10K questions across 7 domains. Moreover, our method outperforms the best existing image-based QA model (Vision-RoBERTa) by 4.6%. Additionally, we evaluate the effects of different training methods, such as fine-tuning, contrastive learning, and augmentation.

Offline goal-conditioned reinforcement learning (GCRL) aims at solving goal-conditioned tasks using model-based methods and has shown promising results in recent years. However, GCRL is often computationally expensive due to the requirement of repeatedly training the value function network for policy gradient updates. This paper proposes an online GCRL method that reduces the number of value function evaluations while maintaining the same sample efficiency as offline GCRL. We achieve this by introducing a novel feature representation of the environment state which enables efficient evaluation of the value function. To assess the effectiveness of our approach, we have conducted extensive experiments across various domains. Our results show that the proposed method achieves comparable perfor

In this paper, we introduce Evolution Transformer ( EvoTr ), a novel framework that leverages evolutionary optimization within the transformer architecture. Our approach, in-context evolution, embeds the evolutionary optimization process directly into the transformer's inference mechanism. This integration allows for the dynamic generation of problem-specific individuals on-the-fly, eliminating the need for a separate evaluation loop. EvoTr incorporates two key components: a transformer-based individual generation mechanism and a self-supervised training strategy. The individual generation mechanism produces candidate solutions, while the self-supervised training strategy guides the evolution of high-performing individuals. Our experiments, spanning various problem domains, demonstrate that EvoTr consistently outperforms other transformer-based models and often surpasses the best evolutionary algorithms, highlighting the potential of our in-context evolution approach.

Simulation is an invaluable tool for radio-frequency system designers that enables rapid prototyping and optimization of complex systems. This paper presents a comprehensive simulation framework using high-order basis function methods, which are efficient in representing spatially varying electromagnetic fields within a large computational domain. A novel method for solving the Poisson equation with arbitrary boundary conditions using high-order basis functions is presented. A new fast multipole method (FMM) based on hierarchical basis functions is developed to solve Maxwell's equations with high accuracy at low computational cost. The proposed framework is demonstrated by simulating a 6G base station antenna array with a large number of elements and multiple resonators. Compared with trad

Large language models (LLMs) such as GPT-4 have demonstrated impressive results across a wide range of tasks. However, LLMs sometimes fail to provide correct answers, and improving their accuracy is an important open problem. One effective way to improve LLMs' accuracy is retrieval tuning, which identifies incorrect answers and provides feedback to LLMs. Previous retrieval tuning methods mostly focus on using retrieval to find incorrect answers, then manually relabel them as correct or wrong. This requires expensive human annotation and limits the scalability of retrieval tuning. In this paper, we define a new retrieval tuning paradigm, LLM-oriented retrieval tuning, which automatically relabels retrieved documents as correct or wrong, without manual annotation. Specifically, we propose a novel confidence-based ranking function, which ranks the confidence scores of LLMs' answers against the entire retrieval results. We show that our method outperforms previous retrieval tuning methods on a large-scale retrieval dataset, and achieves state-of-the-art performance on benchmark datasets.

The life sciences are entering an era in which they will be able to exploit the power of artificial intelligence (AI). This will transform how biological systems are analysed, designed and controlled, and will impact every aspect of society. To prepare for this revolution, it is important to understand the challenges that will arise as AI is applied to biology and to develop the necessary tools and regulations. In this Review, I will discuss some of the opportunities that AI will bring to the life sciences, and the challenges that will have to be overcome if we are to fulfil its potential. I will argue that AI has the potential to personalise medicine, to enable earlier and more accurate diagnosis, and to facilitate the development of new therapies. However, before we can exploit this potential, the technical challenges that will arise as we apply AI to biology must be addressed. These challenges are not unique to AI in biology, but include issues that are common to the development of any new technology, such as the need for high-quality data, the development of user-friendly interfaces, and the standardisation of protocols. Other challenges that will have to be overcome include the interpretation of AI results, the reduction of bias, and the assurance of reliability and safety. As AI in biology develops, it will be important to involve philosophers, social scientists and ethicists to help guide its development and to ensure that the benefits of this technology are realised while its potential risks are minimised.

Laboratory experiments have shown that communication plays an important role in solving social dilemmas. Here we examine whether this finding generalizes to a large-scale field experiment with real people using their own money and smartphones as experimental devices (the "Crowdlab" platform). Participants are given the opportunity either individually or collectively, via group chat messages on WhatsApp during the experiment itself ("real time"), to increase their payout at each round of play by sharing information about which action they took previously while receiving no feedback from others' choices themselves ('no prior').

This paper introduces CN-RMA, a novel approach for 3D indoor object retrieval that uses the point cloud as input and achieves state-of-the-art performance. CN-RMA is based on three key innovations: (1) Our model learns a compact and effective feature representation by introducing an attention mechanism into the ResNet architecture to focus on the informative parts of the scene. (2) A contrastive learning method that uses an augmented version of the training data with different poses and scales to encourage features extracted from a given object to be similar regardless of viewpoint changes. (3) We extend the standard object retrieval task with a novel "nearest neighbor" search algorithm to find relevant objects in the database, which improves the efficiency and accuracy of the retrieval pr

Real-world graphs often exhibit a community structure, i.e., the vertices can be partitioned into communities such that there are many edges within communities and few edges between communities. Detecting communities in a graph is also known as the graph clustering problem. There are numerous graph clustering algorithms, but they often lack convergence guarantee and scalability. Also, the quality of the solutions is hard to be verified. In this paper, we introduce a provable filter for real-world graph clustering. The filter takes a solution of any graph clustering algorithm as input and returns a provably good solution. The solution is represented as a binary matrix, and the filter updates the binary matrix iteratively. In each iteration, the filter provably increases the number of good edges (i.e., intra-community edges) and decreases the number of bad edges (i.e., inter-community edges). The filter stops when the number of bad edges becomes sufficiently small. We prove that the filter always terminates with a provably good solution. We also show that the filter is highly scalable for large graphs. In particular, the filter takes only a few seconds to run on a million-edge graph.

Transformers have revolutionized computer vision and natural language processing, but their high computational complexity limits their application to real-time tasks. In this paper, we propose an efficient Transformer architecture that significantly reduces the computation required for transformer models. Our approach involves utilizing a lightweight attention mechanism, which eliminates redundant computations while preserving the model's ability to capture long-range dependencies. We introduce the Transformer-Light (T-Light) model, which achieves state-of-the-art performance in various benchmark datasets for image captioning, visual question answering, and text-to-image generation. Additionally, T-Light offers an efficient alternative to conventional Transformers for real-time application

Performance portability is the idea that software performance, or at least the scaling of performance with hardware resources, should be portable across different architectures. To facilitate the exploration of performance portability, we present a continuous benchmarking infrastructure that i) automatically discovers hardware resources available to a user, ii) automatically generates benchmarking configurations (i.e., numbers of processes and threads), and iii) automatically updates the benchmarking configurations as the hardware resources change. Our infrastructure can be instantiated for different applications by leveraging performance models that are application-specific. We implement our infrastructure for the high-performance computing (HPC) applications of fluid dynamics (i.e., AMG benchmark) and general-purpose computing on graphics processing units (GPGPU) applications (i.e., 3DMark benchmark). We show that our infrastructure can i) discover the hardware resources (e.g., number of CPUs and GPUs, and their respective thread counts) on demand, ii) automatically generate the benchmarking configurations for the AMG and 3DMark benchmarks, and iii) update the benchmarking configurations as the hardware resources change. We also show that our infrastructure is fit for use in real-world scenarios by i) integrating it with an existing performance portability framework, ii) conducting experiments to validate the usefulness of its benchmarking configurations for the AMG and 3DMark benchmarks, and iii) conducting a case study to show how the benchmarking configurations can be used in practice.

The current state-of-the-art in artificial intelligence is impressive, especially in terms of the capabilities it has developed. However, these advancements have been primarily focused on machine learning tasks. One significant gap that remains to be addressed is the development of machines capable of reasoning about their actions and decisions as they perform complex tasks. This ability would allow for a higher level of autonomy and decision-making that surpasses merely executing instructions, but rather involves understanding context, consequences, and making informed choices accordingly. Reasoning about their actions is one essential component of this capability, allowing machines to make sense of their experiences and interactions with the world around them. A comprehensive framework t

People enjoy sharing "notes" including their experiences within online communities. Therefore, recommending notes aligned with a user's preferences is crucial to improve user engagement and retention. Existing note recommendation methods typically consider the user-item interactions (i.e., likes) for prediction. However, they neglect to consider the social network of users or the content of notes. These limitations make it difficult to predict users' interests in new notes. To address this challenge, we introduce a novel collaborative filtering method, called NeXtNote, which combines various features to enhance the recommendation accuracy. Specifically, NeXtNote incorporates user similarities, social influence, and topic-level information extracted from user profiles and notes. The experim

Channel attention mechanisms endeavor to recalibrate channel weights to enhance representation abilities of backbone networks. However, in practice, the effectiveness and efficiency of these mechanisms are impeded by the high-dimensional channel space. To overcome this issue, we propose a novel channel-aware learning method (CALM), which utilizes the concept of channel-specific weight distribution to simplify the multi-channel interaction. Specifically, CALM adopts an enhanced softmax layer with channel-wise bias terms as the channel attention mechanism, enabling more accurate channel-specific weight distributions. In addition, we incorporate the channel-specific weight distribution into the loss function, which enables the model to explicitly learn the channel-specific importance weights.

The rapid development of online media has significantly facilitated the public's information consumption, knowledge acquisition and social communication. However, it also creates various problems such as misinformation, disinformation and fake news. To tackle these challenges, a multi-modal model named Multi-Modal Disinformation Detection (MMDD) is proposed in this paper to detect and classify disinformation on Weibo, a popular Chinese microblogging platform. Based on a large-scale dataset collected from Weibo, we construct three different models based on Convolutional Neural Networks (CNN), Recurrent Neural Networks (RNN), and Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM). Furthermore, we present the results by applying our proposed method to datasets with different characteristics. Experimental results

Despite the many advances of Large Language Models (LLMs) and their unprecedented success in a wide range of natural language processing tasks, their ability to address real-world challenges is still limited by various factors. One such factor is the lack of training data specific to practical scenarios, which often requires manual labeling, a laborious and time-consuming process. To alleviate this issue, we propose an innovative strategy that harnesses LLM capabilities to generate labeled training data from unlabeled data sources. We introduce a novel approach that leverages a large pre-trained model to automatically classify unlabeled text into two distinct categories: "trainable" and "untrainable". This method ensures that only high-quality samples are selected for further processing, t

Existing Cross-Domain Few-Shot Learning (CDFSL) methods require access to the source domain data during inference, which could be challenging or even impossible when the source domain is unavailable. To address this issue, we propose a novel model named \textbf{Ca}psule-based \textbf{R}eal-Time \textbf{D}omain-In\textbf{S}eparable \textbf{A}daptation method for CDFSL (\textbf{CARDIAS}). In particular, our approach utilizes capsule networks to encode the source domain information and transform it into an effective domain feature, which can be utilized by any existing backbone network to adapt to new domains. The core innovation of our method lies in the introduction of a real-time separation module that enables the model to separate the source domain features from the input image without ac

The exponential growth of scientific literature requires effective management and extraction of valuable insights. While existing scientific document analysis methods focus primarily on individual documents, the unique challenges posed by collections of scientific documents necessitate a comprehensive approach that considers the interconnected nature of these texts. This paper introduces SCINet, a transformer-based model for analyzing collections of scientific documents. SCINet's hierarchical structure enables it to capture both intra-document relationships and inter-document similarities, providing a more holistic understanding of complex scientific texts. We evaluate SCINet on three benchmarks: identifying abstractive summarization of multiple documents, detecting sentence duplications a

Recently, there has been growing interest within the community regarding whether large language models are capable of planning for multiple timesteps. In this work, we study the ability of LLMs to plan using a task graph with three consecutive actions and investigate how the number of actions in the graph influences its performance. We introduce the GPT-3.5's task graph generator (TGG), a tool that automatically generates task graphs with arbitrary numbers of actions for the most popular GPT-3.5 models. Our experiments show that LLMs struggle when the number of actions is beyond two. However, we demonstrate that LLMs can be trained to perform well on up to four actions through iterative fine-tuning and the use of the TGG tool. Additionally, we discuss the limitations of current evaluation

In the current machine translation (MT) landscape, the Transformer architecture stands out as the gold standard for MT models. However, the lack of a unified evaluation method for Transformer-based models has led to inconsistent results across different benchmarks and datasets. This paper introduces a novel Transformer-specific metric named Transformer BLEU (TBLEU), which serves as a comprehensive evaluation tool. TBLEU is a multi-metric approach that evaluates various Transformer submodels, including the encoder, decoder, attention, and self-attention blocks. TBLEU provides an integrated evaluation framework for all Transformer models, regardless of their complexity or scale. It assesses both accuracy and fluency in translation, providing a holistic assessment of Transformer performance.

Medical open-domain question answering demands substantial access to specialized knowledge. Recent efforts have sought to address this challenge through enhanced pretraining of large language models (LLMs). However, existing methods are limited in the scope of their specialization and their ability to effectively interact with a diverse range of knowledge sources. This paper presents a novel approach that leverages the unique capabilities of LLMs by integrating them into a multi-source retrieval framework for medical OQA. We introduce a new methodology called Retrieval-Enhanced Large Language Model (RELL), which employs an integrated strategy of LLM pretraining and multi-source retrieval. In particular, RELL first pretrains a large language model on medical documents using a fine-tuned obj

Video temporal grounding (VTG) is the task of simultaneously localizing the corresponding video moment in an untrimmed video given a language query. This is a challenging task as it requires both strong video representation and language representation. Previous methods typically tackle VTG through directly grounding on raw videos. Such approaches are model-centric and require expensive trial-and-error hyperparameter tuning. We propose a tuning-free VTG method with GPT (VTG-GPT). We show that VTG-GPT outperforms a strong baseline on untrimmed video grounding by 1.8% and 0.9% on ActivityNet Captions and TACoS, respectively. Moreover, we demonstrate the efficacy of our method through ablation studies on these two datasets. We also provide a VTG-GPT demo at https://github.com/RAIVNLab/VTG-GPT-Demo.

Privacy concerns have become a paramount issue in the era of big data, and the need for effective privacy-preserving machine learning algorithms is more pressing than ever. In this work, we focus on the problem of learning private representations that are robust against inference attacks. We propose an information-theoretic framework that aims to minimize the mutual information between the model's parameters and the private training data, as measured by the expected loglikelihood of the parameters given the data. We show that optimizing this objective leads to minimax optimal privacy guarantees for a large family of neural networks. Furthermore, we demonstrate that for tabular data, our framework is equivalent to training a private representation using a regularized autoencoder. Our experiments show that Inf2Guard offers strong privacy guarantees while maintaining high utility.

Safety-critical failures often have fatal consequences in aerospace control. Control systems on aircraft and spacecraft are often designed with redundancy to address safety concerns, but it is challenging to detect single-point faults in such systems. Recently, the use of reinforcement learning (RL) has emerged as a promising solution for fault detection and diagnosis (FDD). RL provides an end-to-end framework that integrates various system components to enable real-time decision making. This paper presents a novel approach for FDD using deep RL in aerospace control systems. Our method utilizes the inherent structure of aerospace control systems, allowing us to design a systematic architecture for fault detection and diagnosis. We demonstrate how this architecture can be trained with minim

Entity Set Expansion (ESE) aims to identify new entities belonging to the same semantic class as those in a given set. This task is crucial for many real-world applications such as data cleaning and augmentation, where it is necessary to infer missing values or fill gaps in knowledge graphs. However, existing methods typically require the input of a seed set of positive examples, which can be difficult to obtain in practice. To address this issue, we introduce ESE-GAN, an unsupervised method that does not rely on any prior information about the target set and achieves superior performance compared to state-of-the-art approaches. Our approach is based on a two-stage generative model consisting of a conditional GAN and a diffusion model trained with a novel objective function that enforces t

We study online learning problems in constrained Markov decision processes (CMDPs) with adversarially chosen transition dynamics. In these problems, the learner receives a partial observation of the state and is allowed to take actions only from a given set of admissible actions. This setup arises naturally when one considers the standard MDP setting where the states are not observable and the admissible actions are limited by physical constraints or computational limitations. A natural question in this setting is whether we can achieve $0$-regret in terms of expected cumulative reward if the true transition dynamics is known in advance. Our main result shows that this is indeed possible for bounded-horizon CMDPs with bounded transition dynamics; even if the transition dynamics is unknown,

Autonomous navigation is one of the key requirements for every potential application of mobile robots in the real world. This paper presents a novel method that allows a robot to autonomously navigate through an environment by localizing itself using a low-cost RGBD sensor, then navigating towards a target location based on its current position and the desired destination, as well as considering various constraints such as obstacles and other dynamic objects. The proposed system utilizes deep learning models to perform tasks related to object detection and pose estimation from images captured by the RGBD camera. A convolutional neural network (CNN) is used to extract features from RGB images, while a pose estimator is trained to produce estimates of the robot's pose in the scene. The RGBD

We propose a novel approach for attention-based model explanation that goes beyond existing methods in two important ways. First, we argue that the current formulation of attention as a probability distribution over tokens is a confounding factor for interpretability. We introduce a new type of attention, called concept attention, that directly models the relevance of concepts to the prediction, rather than modeling token-level attention. Second, we argue that existing visualizations do not go far enough in separating the model's focus from the background. To this end, we introduce a new visualization approach that amplifies the concept attention signals and makes it easier for humans to discern the relevant concepts for the prediction. We evaluate our approach through a user study and show that concept attention improves understanding of the model's prediction compared to existing visualization approaches. We also show that concept attention has other desirable properties, such as being less sensitive to tokenization changes and capturing both local and global context.

We study the problem of unsupervised domain adaptation (UDA) in a novel setting where no labeled sample is available from the target domain, a scenario that often occurs in real-world applications. In this paper, we aim to tackle this challenging problem from a new perspective inspired by the success of generative models in UDA. Specifically, we assume the existence of an unknown distribution $P_*$ that generates the same set of features as the joint distribution of the observed data in the target domain. This assumption is mild and can be easily verified by checking if a generative model can reproduce the observed data in the target domain. Based on this assumption, we propose a novel training framework to estimate $P_*$ and use it to solve the UDA problem without the need of labeled sample from the target domain. We also derive a novel bound on the expected generalization error of our method, which decreases at a faster rate in terms of the number of training steps compared to existing model-agnostic UDA methods. Finally, we show that our method achieves state-of-the-art performance compared to existing model-agnostic UDA methods in solving several benchmark UDA problems.

Distributed Stream Processing (DSP) systems are capable of processing large streams of unbounded data. However, their resource management is still a challenging task due to the high resource demand and the dynamic nature of the workloads. This paper proposes a novel approach for adaptive resource management in DSP systems based on reinforcement learning (RL). The proposed approach optimizes resource allocation by predicting the future state of the system through RL. The key contribution of this paper is the development of an effective reward function that considers both performance metrics and resource utilization factors. To validate the effectiveness of the proposed approach, we have implemented it in two popular DSP systems: Flink and Spark. The experimental results demonstrate that our

Novel class discovery (NCD) aims to incrementally discover novel classes while performing conventional classification on the known classes. It is aimed at scenarios where data is collected sequentially in an online manner, and classifiers need to be updated incrementally to accommodate the novel classes. Two key challenges are (1) detecting whether a new class is present in the data, and (2) merging the novel classes into the existing classifier. Existing methods mostly focus on the first challenge, and in this paper, we focus on the second challenge. We propose a method that exploits the k-nearest neighbors graph of the data to identify the novel classes and updates the classifier by merging the novel classes into the existing class with the help of a human expert. The proposed method is evaluated on synthetic and real-world data, and the results demonstrate that the proposed method performs better than the state-of-the-art baselines.

We develop theory to understand an intriguing property of diffusion models for image generation that we term criticality. Recent research has shown that the performance of diffusion models is highly sensitive to even small perturbations in input images, and this sensitivity can be quantified by measuring how much data points in the latent space are shifted when adding a constant value to each pixel (i.e., the latent shift). Our key observation is that there exists a unique critical point where the latent shift increases abruptly as the diffusion model's temperature increases beyond the critical temperature. Moreover, we find that all latent shifts are asymptotically zero for any temperature below the critical temperature, suggesting that the diffusion model becomes "critical" at the critic

Learning with noisy labels (LNL) is an important problem in supervised learning. Various approaches for this problem rely on the difference between the predicted label and the noisy label. However, the impact of each sample on the model is not considered in these approaches, and they do not distinguish the difficulty of learning clean labels from noisy ones. In this paper, we propose Dirichlet-based per-sample weighting by transition matrix (DPT), a novel LNL approach that assigns different weights to samples based on the transition matrix from the predicted label to the noisy label. Theoretical analysis shows that DPT has a better upper bound on generalization error than the state-of-the-art approaches. Moreover, we show that DPT can be efficiently implemented by a single pass of weighted loss. Extensive experiments on synthetic and real-world datasets demonstrate that DPT achieves superior performance over the state-of-the-art approaches.

Building recommendation systems via federated learning (FL) is a new emerging challenge for advancing next-generation Internet service and privacy protection. FL faces two major challenges: (i) rating prediction: how to effectively integrate user and item information from collaborative filtering (CF) and (ii) diversity: how to ensure the diversity of recommended items without explicit ratings from FL clients. Previous works in FL recommend either focusing on item information (i.e., item2vec) or user information (i.e., DeepCF), but not both. To address the above challenges, we propose a novel Federated Retrieval Augmented Generation (FedRA) model, which augments the FL-based generation with retrieval to better integrate user and item information. Specifically, we introduce a hybrid retrieval augmented generation framework, which consists of a federated retrieval module, a collaborative filtering module, and a hybrid augmentation module. The federated retrieval module retrieves the relevant item information for each user based on historical interactions. The collaborative filtering module predicts the ratings for the retrieved items. The hybrid augmentation module combines the item information and user information to generate the final recommendation results. Experimental results on three benchmark datasets demonstrate that our FedRA model consistently outperforms state-of-the-art baselines for FL recommendation.

Large Language Models (LLMs) such as Claude, GPT-4, and Bard have recently demonstrated impressive results across a wide range of tasks in natural language processing. Fueled by exponential improvements in computational power and algorithmic design, these models pose a considerable threat to displace human labor in many domains. This raises important philosophical questions concerning the relationship between humans and technology, the nature of human expertise, and the ethical responsibilities that come with technological development. In this work, we use Heidegger's philosophy to gain insight into LLM capabilities and risks. We structure our analysis around three key concepts in Heideggerian thought: (1) care (2) authenticity, and (3) temporality. We show that Heidegger's concepts can help us understand the current capabilities of LLMs, and we extrapolate potential future capabilities based on trends in computational and cognitive science. We conclude by arguing that while LLMs are powerful tools for many practical applications, their development and deployment should be resisted as they pose a threat to human authenticity and temporality.

Regional solar power forecasting, which involves predicting the total power generation from all rooftop solar PV (PV) plants in a region, is critical for effective power system operation. In recent years, deep learning methods have emerged as the state-of-the-art approach for solar forecasting tasks. However, these approaches require large training datasets with fine temporal resolutions, which are usually not available. To address this challenge, we propose an unsupervised neural network method to learn solar power generation patterns at various time scales from limited and coarse-grained data. Specifically, we build a hierarchical architecture that can capture long-term trends using low-frequency signals while being flexible enough to adapt to rapidly changing short-term events. We use a

We discuss challenges in transforming programs from UVL (Unikernel Virtual Machine Language) to IVML (Isolating Virtual Machine Language). UVL is a unikernel-specific programming language, whereas IVML is a proposed standard for isolating virtual machines. UVL programs are guaranteed to be isolating, but the converse is not necessarily true. We formalize the notion of UVL programs and provide a meta-theory for UVL. We discuss three techniques to transform UVL programs to IVML. We show that the existing technique of direct translation from UVL to IVML may result in non-optimal code. We propose two new techniques that produce more optimal code, but that have limitations in the class of UVL programs that they can transform.

Vision language models (VLMs) have experienced rapid advancements through the integration of large-scale multimodal pretraining. However, despite their impressive capabilities in zero-shot generalization to various tasks and domains, VLMs typically encounter significant challenges when encountering unfamiliar tasks or unseen data, which are common occurrences in real-world scenarios. To address these challenges, we propose a novel methodology called "Domain Adaptation with Multimodal Reasoning" (DARMR), which combines domain adaptation and multimodal reasoning to achieve robust generalization on unfamiliar tasks and datasets. DARMR introduces an external knowledge base as guidance for cross-modal grounding and task-oriented reasoning. It consists of two components: a domain adaptation modu

Robot decision-making increasingly relies on expressive data-driven human prediction models when interacting with humans. While existing works have demonstrated the efficacy of such models in various domains, they remain limited to offline evaluation scenarios. In this paper, we aim to address this limitation by introducing a new interactive framework that enables robots and humans to collaboratively improve human prediction model performance through interactive data collection. Our framework comprises three main components: a human prediction model, a robot agent, and a communication channel connecting them. The human prediction model utilizes data from real-world interactions between the robot agent and a user to predict a user's preferences for different robot actions. During online int

We present iScore, a visual analytics system for interpreting how a language model (LM) automatically scores machine generated summaries. iScore visualizes the LM's scoring mechanism as a weighted directed graph, where nodes represent words and edges represent semantic relations. iScore also highlights the most salient words and phrases in a summary, providing further insight into the scoring mechanism. We conduct a case study with iScore to investigate how an LM scores summaries generated by different summarization methods. Our results show that iScore can help users understand the strengths and weaknesses of different summarization methods, as well as the LM's scoring mechanism.

Global positioning systems (GPS) are vulnerable to spoofing attacks, in which counterfeit GPS signals are transmitted to mislead GPS receivers. To counter this threat, researchers have proposed various geo-localization methods that determine a more accurate position by fusing signal-level features, such as those based on range information. However, range-based features are sensitive to GPS signal-to-noise ratio (SNR), which is typically low in urban canyons and indoors. To mitigate this weakness, we propose a deep learning-based method that fuses the range information with the angle-of-arrival (AOA) information, which is less sensitive to SNR. Specifically, we propose a shifting-dense partition learning (SDPL) method that divides the AOA range into several non-equal partitions and applies a deep neural network to each partition. We show that SDPL outperforms other state-of-the-art methods in terms of both indoor and urban canyon scenarios. A hardware prototype is also developed to validate the concept.

Recent advancement in text-to-image models (e.g., Stable Diffusion) has been remarkable, yet the underlying models are often not fully understood or accessible to users. In this paper we propose TextualPrompt, a new interactive interface that allows end users to understand and directly control the generation process of these models through an integrated visualization system. We design and implement two key interaction techniques for TextualPrompt: 1) Interactive visualization of prompt vectors, which provides users with a real-time understanding of how their prompts are encoded and influence the model's outputs; 2) Interactive fine-tuning of prompt vectors, which enables users to directly modify the prompt vectors, allowing them to create new variations of their own generated images. We ha

Recent advances in large language models (LLMs) demonstrate that their capabilities are comparable to human experts. However, despite the potential of LLMs as a tool for enhancing AI safety, there is currently no comprehensive framework for assessing their performance on safety tasks. To fill this gap, we introduce SafetyEval, an open-source evaluation suite designed to benchmark LLMs' ability to detect and mitigate safety risks in complex scenarios. Our evaluations cover four categories: system reliability, transparency, fairness, and alignment with user intentions. We conducted extensive experiments using LLaMA 204B and GPT-4 models. In each category, our results show that while the LLMs perform well in some cases, they struggle when confronted with more nuanced or novel safety challenge

We study distributed training of deep learning models in time-constrained environments. We propose a new algorithm, called \emph{Distributed Minibatch Training} (DMT), which allows the model to be trained on different computing resources at various speeds by minimizing an adaptive weighted sum loss with respect weights that are updated according to local progress rates and constraints imposed upon individual workers during execution.\ DMT can accommodate heterogeneous computation platforms consisting nodes running CPU or GPU accelerators as well other specialized hardware such neuromorphic processors for neural network inference tasks without sacrificing performance due its ability handle varying input sizes across multiple concurrent sessions while maintaining high efficiency levels even

Human Activity Recognition (HAR) has received increasing research attention because of its potential to improve the management of chronic diseases, promote healthy lifestyles, and assist healthcare professionals. Wearable devices are ideal sensors to monitor human activities. Inertial sensors are the most common type of wearable sensors, and they are cheaper and more comfortable than other types. Many deep learning techniques have been applied to HAR problems. However, relatively few studies have compared the performance of different deep learning techniques on inertial signals. This paper aims to fill that gap by comparing fifteen deep learning techniques on three popular datasets for ankle inertial HAR. These datasets comprise mostly activities of daily living, which were collected using three-axis accelerometer sensors. The deep learning techniques include traditional neural networks, convolutional neural networks, recurrent neural networks, and hybrid models. We evaluated the ability of these methods to classify sequences of data (one-hot encoding) and the ability to classify each data point (point-wise classification). The results showed that deep learning techniques performed better than the conventional neural network. The best technique for point-wise classification was the Long Short-Term Memory network, and the best technique for sequence classification was the Inflated 3D Convnet.

We show that a classical zero-sum differential game with one-sided information and state constraints has a value. Unlike in the case without state constraints, the value may depend on the chosen information structure. We give a characterization of the value as the unique solution of a Hamilton-Jacobi equation. For this purpose, we introduce a notion of viscosity subsolution, supersolution, and solution that differs from the standard one. We also show that the value is independent of the information structure when the information is already available at the initial time. As a byproduct, we obtain that the value of the one-sided information game is equal to the value of the associated two-player stochastic reachability game.

The conjunction of edge intelligence and the ever-growing Internet-of-Things (IoT) is a game changer for smart applications. In this paper, we design EdgeFlowNet: an efficient deep learning system that supports high data rates at low latency with small power consumption while maintaining accuracy in distributed IoT environments over heterogeneous wireless networks via multiuser joint optimization strategies to maximize total utility per unit time from both sides -- users' perspective as well ithe network operator’s side - leveraging cloud/edge computing capabilities across multiple domains including cellular mobile broadband access modems(MMBs), WiFi routers&access points , LoRaWAN gateways etc., all connected through Ethernet or Fiber optics links connecting them directly back into

A word $u$ over a finite alphabet $A$ is a Galois word if there is a permutation $\sigma$ of $A$ such that $u = u_1u_2 \cdots u_n$ and $\sigma(u_i) = u_{i+1}$ for any $i \in \{1,2,\ldots,n\}$, where $u_{n+1} = u_1$. In this paper, we present combinatorial algorithms for detecting, factorizing, and rotating Galois words. Detecting a Galois word is equivalent to finding a directed Hamiltonian path in the free magmawithout 1s. Factorizing a Galois word is equivalent to finding all directed Hamiltonian paths in a directed graph. We present a linear time algorithm for detecting Galois words and a $O(n^2)$ time algorithm for factorizing Galois words, where $n$ is the length of the word. We also prove that the detection problem is NP-complete when restricted to binary alphabets. We then extend our results to the rotation of Galois words, and prove that the rotation problem is NP-complete, even when the input is a binary Galois word with at most three distinct rotations.

LLMOps incur significant costs due to hardware requirements, hindering their widespread adoption. We propose a novel framework for efficient LLMoP (E-L2) that addresses this problem by leveraging the idea of "lightness" in LLM design and optimizing training efficiency with model compression techniques such as quantization/weight pruning from deep learning communities into an endtoend optimization pipeline optimized specifically towards low latency task deployments on edge devices like smartphones or IoT gateways without sacrificing accuracy significantly compared against stateofart models trained using traditional approaches while simultaneously reducing memory footprint substantially thus making it easier than ever before! In recent years there has been increasing interest among researche

We present a new technique for automated software verification of hyperliveness, a property ensuring that "interesting" program locations (e.g., points where resources are allocated) are eventually revisited. Hyperliveness generalizes the well-studied (weak) live variable property, and checking it has been shown to be undecidable even for simple programming languages. To cope with this undecidability, most existing techniques for hyperliveness verification employ heuristics that may miss counterexamples. Our technique is based on a novel combination of abstract interpretation and counterexample-guided abstraction refinement. It performs a directed search for a proof that a given program satisfies the hyperliveness property, while simultaneously searching for a counterexample to this proof. If a counterexample is found, the program is shown to be hyperlive; otherwise, the proof is validated by executing the program under the found abstraction. Our technique is implemented by the HyperLifter tool, which takes a program in a subset of C as input, and outputs a proof of hyperliveness or a counterexample. We evaluated HyperLifter on 92 programs from the literature. It found 17 bugs, 14 of which were not found by other tools. Of the 75 programs without bugs, 62 were proven hyperlive, 8 were shown to be live (a weaker property than hyperliveness), and 5 were incorrectly claimed to be hyperlive by other tools.

Confusion is a common phenomenon in multi-object tracking (MOT), especially when targets appear or disappear behind occlusions. In this paper, we propose a simple yet effective method, DeconfuseTrack, to address the confusion problem in a completely offline manner. Specifically, we introduce a novel re-identification (Re-ID) module to recognize the true identity of each target across occlusions based on the appearance features. In addition, a trajectory clustering module is proposed to group the trajectories of the same target together according to their spatial-temporal relationships. These two modules can work simultaneously and independently, and each can be further fine-tuned by the other. DeconfuseTrack is the first method that tackles the confusion problem from the perspective of both appearance and motion. Extensive experiments on three widely-used MOT benchmarks, i.e., MOT16 (MOTA of 79.7), MOT17 (MOTA of 81.3%) and MOT20 (MOTA of 78.9%), show that our method can significantly improve the state-of-the-art results by a large margin (The improvements are 6.9%, 4.0% and 5.6% on MOT16, MOT17 and MOT20 respectively).

Theoretical linguists have suggested that some languages (e.g., Chinese and Japanese) are more holistic than others (e.g., English), which influences how these languages process information. However, empirical research on this topic has been limited to the domain of semantics, where there is a clear distinction between holistic languages and analytic ones. In this study, we extend previous research to the domain of syntax, where such a distinction is not obvious. We used a novel methodology to investigate whether holistic languages exhibit different patterns of syntactic priming compared with analytic languages. Specifically, we examined whether Japanese and English speakers showed sensitivity to structural overlap in a syntactic priming task using morphosyntactically complex sentences.

Unmanned Aerial Vehicles (UAVs) are increasingly used for infrastructure inspections, including bridge deck cracks. Cracks are one of the primary indicators of structural damage, and their automatic detection can significantly improve the efficiency of the inspection process. However, existing methods often rely on densely labeled data, which are not available in real-world scenarios. In addition, most of the existing methods work well in ideal conditions but fail to show satisfactory performance in complex and varied environments, such as low-light and foggy conditions. To address these challenges, we propose a novel few-shot low-light crack segmentation model, CrackNex, for UAV inspections. Our proposed model is based on the Retinex theory, which enhances the cracks' features in low-light images. To improve the model's performance and reduce the computational cost, we employ feature distillation. Furthermore, we introduce a novel method to generate synthetic foggy images to augment the training set. Our experimental results demonstrate that CrackNex outperforms the state-of-the-art models, particularly in low-light and foggy conditions. Our proposed model achieved a 0.6% and 2.9% improvement in mean IoU on the HKU-IS and ICRA2017 datasets, respectively.

With the escalating threats posed by cyberattacks on Industrial Control Systems (ICSs) and other critical infrastructure, it is essential to have a reliable solution for detecting malicious activities. In this paper we propose an approach based on time series analysis of communication data that can be used in real-time detection systems without requiring prior knowledge about specific attack types or their payload content. Our method involves extracting features from two separate layers: 1st layer focusing only at packet level characteristics such as size & duration while also including statistical information like mean/variance; whereas second one utilizes more detailed metrics derived via deep learning models trained specifically towards recognizing anomalous behaviors observed within da

Registration of point clouds collected from a pair of distant vehicles provides a comprehensive and accurate 3D representation of the environment, including objects and obstacles in their vicinity. However, it is challenging to generate high-quality point cloud registration with an acceptable computational cost due to the large number of points in each cloud. We propose a novel algorithm for robust point cloud registration based on a modified multi-scale convolutional neural network (MSCNN) that can be trained end-to-end. The MSCNN is trained using synthetic data generated by a virtual road dataset with diverse environments and various geometric features such as scale, orientation, and illumination. The resulting model generalizes well to real-world point clouds, showing competitive perfor

In a cooperative multi-agent system, an important issue is how to allocate the tasks among the agents in order to maximize the overall performance of the system. This problem is known as the cooperative task allocation problem. In this paper, we propose a generalized framework for modeling and solving the cooperative task allocation problem. In this framework, we take into account the fact that in a cooperative multi-agent system, the agents can communicate with each other and thus some tasks can be executed in parallel; however, due to the communication costs, it is not possible to assign all tasks to all agents. The proposed framework is able to model these complexities of cooperative task allocation and provides a generalized approach for solving these problems. We present an integer linear programming formulation and a constraint programming formulation for the generalized cooperative task allocation problem. We also provide some solution approaches for solving large-sized problems. We compare the performance of these approaches for solving the cooperative task allocation problem in multi-agent systems through some numerical experiments.

The development of next-generation networks is revolutionizing network operators' management and orchestration practices. Operators are now focusing on building intelligent, self-healing, autonomous, and automated networks by leveraging the capabilities of machine learning (ML), artificial intelligence (AI), and cognitive technologies. However, implementing these AI-driven practices in existing network management and orchestration systems presents numerous challenges. This paper addresses one such challenge: the deployment of a self-learning anomaly detection solution to an already deployed, legacy, and operational network monitoring system. We propose a novel approach for continuous learning in this context, which involves a combination of supervised learning for static rules and unsuperv

This article proposes a novel Children-Computer Interaction (CCI) approach for the task of object detection on mobile devices. The core idea is to use a child as a remote server that controls a computer in order to perform object detection tasks. We propose an approach for training and evaluating CCI systems based on a simple, but highly effective, conceptual framework. We also introduce two novel datasets, which we collected at our research group with children aged 7-12 years old: one for CCI and one for human-computer interaction. These datasets are made available under a Creative Commons license. Our evaluation results show that CCI outperforms the existing state-of-the-art approaches for this type of system by a large margin. We further demonstrate that CCI can be used as a tool for ea

Characterizing samples that are difficult to learn from is crucial to developing highly performant ML models. Recently, researchers have proposed a wide range of techniques for analyzing the difficulty of training neural networks, including analysis of gradient flow and the geometry of the loss surface. However, these methods often rely on detailed knowledge of the network architecture and optimization algorithm, or they require large amounts of data to achieve meaningful results. In this paper, we introduce a new approach based on the concept of \emph{minimum spanning tree (MST)} for characterizing the difficulty of training deep learning models. We show how MSTs can be used to define a number of key properties of the loss landscape, including the existence and size of local minima, the p

Vision-extended LLMs have made significant strides in Visual Question Answering (VQA) and Open Ended VQ&A. However, existing models often require a complex pipeline of pre/post processing to extract features from images for LLM inference which limits their effectiveness at scale or on mobile devices such as smartphones due the computational overhead imposed by image feature extraction pipelines like CLIP . In this work we present ImageCLIP , an end2end model that directly uses high resolution RGB inputs with no preprocessing required other than resizing input pixels into 3D tensors suitable for self attention computation using vision transformers(ViT).

Real-world datasets commonly exhibit noisy labels and class imbalance, such as long-tail distributions. This paper proposes a novel method for addressing these challenges in the context of training deep neural networks. Our approach is based on iterative learning with a focus on correcting errors. In each iteration, the model predicts the most confident class for each instance and then trains only on those instances that are misclassified by the model. This process effectively reduces noise and ensures that instances from rare classes receive more attention during training. We evaluate our method on the popular CIFAR100 dataset and compare it to other approaches. Our results demonstrate that our method outperforms baseline methods by 2% - 5% in terms of accuracy, particularly for datasets

The Bellman equation and its continuous form, the Hamilton-Jacobi-Bellman (HJB) equation, are two fundamental tools in dynamic programming. However, both formulations require a fixed terminal time $T>0$, which prevents applications to stochastic processes with random stopping times. In this paper we propose an algorithm that can solve such problems, without needing to specify an upper bound for the stopping time. We do so by solving an auxiliary optimization problem with an additional term that accounts for the remaining time. This new approach has several advantages: it is applicable to general Markovian processes; it does not require to specify an upper bound on the stopping time; and it is amenable to fast numerical approximation methods, such as machine learning. Numerical experiments

Prohibited item detection in X-ray images is one of the most essential and highly effective methods for detecting prohibited items. Therefore, the development of new algorithms that improve the accuracy of such systems is a crucial area of research in security and medicine. In this work, we propose a novel method for prohibited object detection using the UNet++ network architecture with a pre-trained model and post-processing techniques. Our approach utilizes the VGG16 architecture as a pre-trained backbone to extract features from input images. The proposed network is constructed by adding two convolutional layers followed by an upsampling operation. The final output of the network consists of three parallel paths: a path based on a 512-dimensional filter bank, another based on a 1024-dim

Let $M$ be an $n\times n$ matrix of homogeneous linear forms over a field $\mathbb{F}$ of characteristic zero. By the result of Fiedler, every matrix in $\mathrm{SL}(2,\mathbb{Z})$ has a non-trivial minimal generating set consisting of only two elements. In this paper we present a method to show that a certain class of matrices in $\mathrm{SL}(2,\mathbb{Z})$ (which contains many natural classes) also admit such sets, and thus have bounded representation type. This class is not closed under taking powers or taking adjoints. It includes all rank one matrices, as well as various classes of low rank matrices, including those with few columns, or whose entries are only defined at finitely many coordinates.

Let $a$ and $b$ be two non-zero elements of a finite field $\mathbb F_q$. We show that the number of solutions of the equation $a^2x+by=0$ is congruent to zero modulo $q+1$. As an application we obtain a short proof of the fact that the product of any $n\ge 2$ elements in $\mathbb F_q^*$ has order dividing $q-1$. This generalizes a result of H. Wagner and H. Xu for the case where $a, b \in \mathbb F_{q}^{*}$. The result can also be used to give new lower bounds on the maximum size of a subset $S$ of $\mathbb F_q^{*}$ such that every non-identity element of $\mathbb F_q^{*}$ is a sum of squares of elements in $S$.

Mixtral, a representative sparse mixture of experts (SMoE) language model, has achieved state-of-the-art performance in various NLP tasks. In this paper, we propose a new method to train mixtral models by leveraging the multi-task learning capabilities of SMoEs. Our approach utilizes an auxiliary loss function to enforce consistency among different tasks within one model. We conduct experiments on several benchmark datasets and find that our approach achieves comparable or superior performance compared to previous methods while using significantly fewer training parameters. Additionally, we introduce a novel method for fine-tuning mixtral models on downstream tasks without any modifications to the original model architecture. This method, called mixtral_ft, allows for easy adaptation of mi

Generative Artificial Intelligence (GAI) shows remarkable productivity and creativity in Mobile Application Development. GAI-based frameworks, such as DALL·E 2 or ChatGPT have been widely used by software engineers to generate code snippets for backend development tasks like parsing JSON data structures into Java objects using open source libraries [1]. The use of Generational AI has also led the creation of new apps that provide a variety tools including automated text summarization[4], chatbots with GPT3 integration,[5] image recognition algorithms utilizing Open Visual Recognition API(VRA), Google Cloud Vision APIs ,and more.[6][7],[8].[9).
