Texture editing is an essential task in 3D graphics, which aims to modify the texture of a 3D mesh according to user's preference. Existing methods edit textures either in a non-interactive or a procedurally controllable way, which are far from intuitive for non-experts. In this paper, we propose DragTex, a novel generative point-based texture editing method, which directly edits the texture space of a 3D mesh in an element-wise fashion. Our method learns a latent edit space for texture editing, which is driven by a neural radiance field (NeRF) based representation of the 3D mesh. This enables our model to accurately capture the 3D geometric information and UV mapping of the input mesh. Users can interactively drag and drop texture elements on the 3D mesh preview to edit the texture in a intuitive and flexible way. We demonstrate the effectiveness of our method in comparison with other texture editing baselines on both synthetic and real-world datasets. The official project page is at: https://sailor-z.github.io/projects/DragTex.html.

Chatbots are increasingly being adopted to facilitate learning by providing interactive support outside of traditional working hours. However, developing chatbots for education is costly and time-consuming as it requires expert knowledge and manual effort to craft the responses. We propose Book2Dial, a novel approach to generate teacher-student dialogue from textbooks, which requires minimal manual annotation. We employ a pre-trained language model to automatically generate student questions and design a simple annotation interface that allows experts to provide feedback on the generated questions. We collect feedback from 100 questions generated from 51 different textbooks on computer science and mathematics. Our experimental results show that the generated questions are factually correct, engaging, and aligned with the instructions. The feedback from experts indicates that the questions are clear, concise, and promote deep learning. Book2Dial can significantly reduce the development cost and time of educational chatbots, making interactive learning resources more accessible.

With the advancement of large language models, language-based forecasting has recently emerged as a promising approach for time series prediction. Recent studies have shown that LLMs can achieve superior accuracy in predicting unseen data by leveraging a self-attention mechanism and pre-training on a large corpus of historical observations. However, these methods often rely heavily on a training dataset consisting of large amounts of historical data. This paper introduces a novel method to improve LLMs' long-term predictions without requiring additional historical data. Our approach involves two steps: 1) extracting a short sequence from the target time series; 2) fine-tuning an existing LLM with only this sequence as input. We conduct experiments using both synthetic datasets and real-wor

Thanks to technologies such as virtual network function the Fifth Generation (5G) of mobile networks enables new scenarios in the Internet-of-Things (IoT). In particular, 5G supports both narrowband and wideband IoT communications. These two types of services have different requirements for their physical layer. For example, the narrowband IoT needs a low power consumption. The wideband IoT has higher bandwidth requirements compared to narrowband IoT. This paper discusses a novel design of a smart antenna array for these two kinds of services. The proposed smart antenna array is designed based on an LMS algorithm for minimizing the mean square error between the desired signal and the received signal. The simulation results show that the proposed smart antenna array can achieve a high accur

Backdoor attacks have been one of the emerging security threats to deep neural networks (DNNs). Existing backdoor defense methods can either be designed for a particular attack, or they lack generalization. This paper proposes an effective and generalizable defense against backdoor attacks by leveraging adversarial training with data augmentation. The proposed methodology is built on top of the Adversarial Robust Training framework that consists of three main components: i) a data augmentation module to generate diverse samples from original images; ii) an adversarial training module to create adversarial examples for backdoor attack; iii) an ensemble learning module to combine the robustness features from multiple models trained on different augmentations and attacks. To evaluate our appr

Accurate traffic forecasting is challenging due to the complex dependency on road networks, various factors and time-varying spatial characteristics. Although deep learning methods have been widely applied in transportation planning studies for their strong ability of capturing nonlinearities from massive data samples via neural network structures such as Recurrent Neural Network (RNN), Graph Convolutional Networks(GCN) etc., these approaches are mainly developed based upon a flat space with no consideration given to 3D spatiotemporal features which can be very informative during prediction tasks especially when long distances between locations are involved or short range temporal dependencies need large memory spaces that cannot fit into traditional models' capacity constraints .

As social media platforms are increasingly used as primary news sources, the spread of fake news through these channels poses a threat to democratic societies. Multimodal evidence augmentation is a promising approach to improving fake news detection. In this work, we propose a novel multimodal framework, FakeNewsGPT4, that incorporates external knowledge into the low-level vision-language modeling (LVLM) process. Our model integrates information from various modalities, including text, images, and metadata, to enhance the detection of fake news. We evaluate our model on two benchmark datasets, FakeNewsNet and Snopes, and demonstrate its effectiveness in improving the state-of-the-art metrics for fake news detection. Our results show that incorporating knowledge through our multimodal approach provides substantial benefits for LVLMs, yielding an average improvement of 16.8% in F1 score across the two datasets.

Recent advancements in video semantic segmentation have made substantial progress by exploiting temporal correlations across adjacent frames. The existing methods for action segmentation often rely on a single frame, resulting in poor performance due to the lack of multi-frame features and temporal information. However, due to the inherent limitations of single-frame action classification models, these approaches are unable to fully capture the temporal dynamics. This paper proposes an Action-based Temporal Attention Module (ATAM), which uses a dynamic attention mechanism to extract long-term dependencies between actions with varying durations. To this end, we introduce the novel concept of "action duration", which is used as an auxiliary supervision to guide our proposed ATAM module. Our

The language diversity in India's education sector poses a significant challenge, hindering inclusive and equitable learning experiences. While English is the medium of instruction at most schools, the predominant language spoken by students from diverse linguistic backgrounds is not always understood or addressed adequately by teachers. This paper addresses this issue through the development of an AI-powered multilingual prompt engineering tool for Indian school children. To achieve this goal, we utilize a data-driven approach to construct a multilingual text corpus with over 100K annotated examples spanning eight languages (Hindi, Bengali, Tamil, Malayalam, Kannada, Marathi, Gujarati, Telugu) covering five core subjects: Math, Science, Social Studies, History, and General Knowledge.

In decision-making problem under uncertainty, predicting unknown parameters is often considered independent of the optimization part. However, this assumption is not always correct because the prediction and optimization parts may share a common goal. For example, in reinforcement learning (RL), both exploration and exploitation are done through an estimate of future rewards from optimal policies. Moreover, the parameters used for estimating those rewards are also optimized by RL methods. We refer to such problems as mixed-objective problems, where optimization and parameter estimation are combined into one objective function. We propose to study the asymptotic behavior of first-order methods when facing a mixed-objective problem. Our analysis shows that some second-order methods can achie

This paper introduces a new stochastic optimization method based on the regularized Fisher information matrix (FIM) for solving large-scale convex problems. Recent work has demonstrated that the FIM can provide improved convergence rates compared to other state-of-the-art methods in the literature. However, its application is limited due to the high computational cost associated with calculating the FIM. Our proposed method addresses this issue by utilizing a sparse FIM approximation and leveraging the fact that many existing algorithms converge to a stationary point of the original problem. Through theoretical analysis, we show that our method exhibits strong guarantees when applied to regularized convex optimization problems with the FIM as the objective function. Furthermore, experiment

Natural language and images are commonly used as goal representations in goal-conditioned imitation learning (IL). However, the discrepancy between the goal representation and the task environment can cause problems with these representations. We propose a novel method for goal conditioning called Goal Conditioning by Environment Representation (GCER), which uses the visual state of the world to condition IL from natural language goals. To accomplish this, we design a visual observation module that captures information about the environment and a goal representation module that extracts features from natural language. The two modules are then combined using a multi-head attention mechanism to generate an environmental feature vector that is fed into the agent's action space to generate act

Mental health conditions, such as depression and anxiety, are prevalent in late-life and can severely impact an individual's quality of life. Late-life mental health conditions often co-occur with other health conditions, are chronic in nature, and can be exacerbated by stressful life events, such as the loss of a loved one. Given the complex interplay between emotional stressors, mental health conditions, and physical health conditions, it can be challenging to disentangle their individual contributions to an individual's overall well-being. Computational models that track emotional stress over time can provide insights into this interplay and help clinicians and researchers better understand the factors that contribute to mental health conditions. In this work, we propose a novel computational model to characterize an individual's emotional stress profile using publicly available diaries from the Late-Life Emotional Stress Study (LESStudy). The LESStudy diary data consists of daily reports of emotional stressors, mental health symptoms, and physical health symptoms completed by older adults over the course of several months. Our model leverages the finite number of stressors and mental health symptoms reported in the LESStudy diaries to construct a Markov chain that captures the transitions between discrete emotional stress states. We use this model to explore the relationship between emotional stress and mental health at three levels of analysis: (1) aggregate population-level transitions between emotional stress states, (2) individual-level transitions, and (3) transitions in subgroups defined by demographic characteristics. Our results demonstrate that older adults spend the majority of their time in a state of low emotional stress, but this can change rapidly in response to life events such as the death of a spouse. We also find substantial individual-level variability in emotional stress states and transitions, with some individuals spending more time in states of high emotional stress than others. Finally, we show that certain demographic subgroups, including individuals with lower education and those who are currently married, tend to spend more time in states of high emotional stress. This research highlights the utility of modeling emotional stress as a Markov process for understanding the dynamics of mental health over time and the factors that contribute to an individual's risk for mental health conditions.

We cover how to determine a sufficiently large sample size for a $K$-armed randomized experiment, where the goal is to identify the best arm and achieve some pre-specified accuracy. The problem of finding an accurate stopping time is notoriously difficult because it requires simultaneously balancing two conflicting objectives: we want to collect enough information before stopping but also need to stop as soon as possible so that we can begin exploring new arms to find the global optimum. To tackle this problem, we use the idea of a sample-efficient search algorithm with low regret to design a simple stopping rule which stops the first time the empirical average of any arm exceeds its expected value by a given constant. Our approach builds on and extends recent works in sample-efficient alg

Session data has been widely used for understanding user's behavior in e-commerce. Researchers are often interested in the time series of session data, which requires to consider both temporal and sequential information in the modeling. We propose a new session-time model, called \textbf{T}emporal \textbf{S}equential \textbf{G}raph \textbf{N}etwork (TSGN), to capture both sequential and temporal features in session time series. TSGN can be trained by optimizing the likelihood of the observed sessions with the help of a GCN layer. In addition, we propose a new method that improves session classification performance while mitigating overfitting, which is achieved by training the network on a synthetic graph and then fine-tuning it on the real data. We evaluate our method on three benchmark d

We propose a new framework for cross-domain image conversion, which aims to convert images from one domain to another, e.g., from real-world images to sketches or the other way around. Our method, CycleDM, builds upon the established cycle-consistent adversarial network (cycleGAN) framework, but replaces its discriminative loss with a domain-specific one. This simple yet effective change empowers our model to better focus on the target application, and leads to consistent improvements over cycleGAN in various tasks, including image-to-image translation, image colorization, and super-resolution. CycleDM is conceptually simple, easy to implement and train, and is shown to produce results that are competitive to the state-of-the-art.

Session data has been widely used for understanding user's behavior in e-commerce. Researchers are usually interested to find out whether the users will buy or not by analyzing their browsing behaviors and purchase histories, which is known as session classification problem with binary labels (i.e., yes/no). However real online shopping sessions contain multiple products that can be bought together such a pizza meal set: Pizza + Drink Set; Cheese & Chicken Combo Meal at KFC restaurant chain etc.. This paper proposes an extended version of multilabel learning based on pairwise class association called MultiLabel Pair Association Learning(MLPAL) model where each label represents one product category instead using only individual item type like traditional MLAL models do without considering a

Robot navigation under visual corruption presents a formidable challenge. To address this, we propose and demonstrate the use of an automated approach for learning robust image features from corrupted images using deep neural networks (DNNs). Specifically in our work here on robot vision-based mobile manipulation tasks where cameras are frequently obstructed by objects or occluded behind mirrors/glass surfaces during operation due to their position relative within robotic manipulator systems; these types situations can often result with poor quality imagery being acquired which will lead towards decreased performance when attempting any sort task that relies heavily upon accurate scene understanding capabilities afforded only through good input data sets containing high resolution pixel va

We present OOTDiffusion, a novel network architecture for realistic and controllable image-based virtual try-on (VTON). We leverage the power of pretrained latent diffusion models, designing an outfitting UNet to learn the garment detail features. Without a redundant warping process, the garment features are precisely aligned with the target human body via the proposed outfitting fusion in the self-attention layers of the denoising UNet. In order to further enhance the controllability, we introduce outfitting dropout to the training process, which enables us to adjust the strength of the garment features through classifier-free guidance.

The problem of finding the largest common subgraph of two forests is investigated. Two different approaches are presented. The first approach is based on a transformation of the problem into a special form of the graphical representation of the set intersection. This transformation allows to use existing algorithms for computing the intersection of two sets, represented as unions of paths. The second approach is based on a transformation of the problem into a special form of the graphical representation of the set difference. This transformation allows to use existing algorithms for computing the difference of two sets, represented as unions of paths. The computational complexity of the two approaches is analyzed.

Rapid and reliable identification of dynamic scene parts, also known as motion segmentation, is a key task for computer vision-based applications such as autonomous driving. Recent methods often rely on object detection models to achieve this goal. However, the performance of these models degrades when they encounter occluded or partially visible scenes where scene part boundaries are not clearly defined. This paper presents a novel approach that utilizes the power of generative adversarial networks (GANs) to overcome these challenges in motion segmentation. Specifically, we propose an encoder-decoder GAN architecture that learns to generate binary masks for each scene part. These generated masks accurately represent the boundary between different scene parts, even in cases with occlusions

In this letter, we present a novel bi-modal bi-copter robot called Skater, which is designed to take off from the ground and hover in the air using two propeller arms, without any external support. Unlike traditional quadrotors, Skater does not have any horizontal translation capability as it relies on the rotational motion of its propellers for both takeoff and hovering. This makes it more stable and energy efficient than quadrotors. To achieve the desired stability and control, we introduce an innovative gait generation method that uses an energy function to optimize the trajectory of each rotor. The trajectory is then tracked by the propeller arm controller. Our experimental results demonstrate that the proposed method provides better stability and robustness compared to existing method

The advancement of region understanding in computer vision is crucial for the performance of various tasks such as image captioning, visual question answering, and cross-modal retrieval. However, the majority of existing methods rely on region proposals from object detectors, which are not specifically tailored to the downstream tasks and often lead to sub-optimal performance. In this work, we introduce RegionGPT, a pre-trained Vision Language Model (VLM) that incorporates region-level cross-attention and a region-specific token vocabulary to enhance region representation learning and promote region-level reasoning. To train RegionGPT, we develop a new pre-training dataset and task, A2D-S+, which is an augmented version of the existing A2D-S dataset. Our experimental results demonstrate the superiority of RegionGPT over previous VLMs by a significant margin. Furthermore, we show that RegionGPT outperforms object detection-based methods in image captioning and visual question answering tasks. Our code and dataset are available at https://github.com/BeierZhu/RegionGPT.

Neural network models have a number of hyperparameters that must be chosen along with their architecture. This task is non-trivial, and in many cases, it requires time-consuming experimentation and human expertise. We propose an approach to automate this process by training a neural network model to predict the best hyperparameter configuration for any input architecture. Our method does not require prior knowledge of the architecture or performance of the model. Instead, it learns the relationship between these parameters and the model's performance using a dataset of pretrained architectures and their hyperparameter configurations. To test our method, we train a model to predict the best hyperparameter configuration for ResNet models on CIFAR-10. The model achieves a 97% success rate acr

BloomGML is a bilevel optimization framework for graph machine learning (GML). It originates from the observation that many GML tasks can be viewed as the problem of learning a graph structure that is optimal for a given machine learning task. BloomGML formalizes this idea and shows that many GML tasks can be cast as a bilevel optimization problem that aims to find the ``best'' graph structure for a given machine learning task. To solve these problems, BloomGML uses a nested optimization structure, where the inner problem is a graph optimization problem that constructs a graph structure, and the outer problem is a machine learning task that evaluates the quality of the constructed graph. BloomGML can be applied to many GML tasks, including node classification, link prediction, and graph classification. When applied to node classification, our experiments show that BloomGML outperforms the state-of-the-art graph neural networks (GNNs) on four different datasets. Furthermore, BloomGML is able to learn both sparse and dense graphs, unlike existing graph optimization methods. Finally, BloomGML can be easily regularized to induce robustness against adversarial attacks.

Recent work has shown that multi-task visual instruction tuning is effective for improving the performance of pretrained models across multiple instruction-following tasks. However, multi-task tuning is only effective when the model is exposed to tasks that are similar to the target tasks during tuning. In this work, we introduce Comprehensive Task Balancing (CoTBal), a framework for multi-task visual instruction tuning that is robust to distribution shifts between the tuning and target tasks. CoTBal uses a meta-learning approach to learn a separate representation for each task that balances the contribution of all tasks during instruction encoding. We evaluate CoTBal on a wide range of vision-language tasks, including object recognition, visual reasoning, and image captioning. CoTBal improves performance on each of these tasks relative to strong baselines, often by large margins. Our code is available at https://github.com/RAIVNLab/CoTBal.

The study analyses polarisation on Finnish social media with data from the platform X, which is owned by Meta. Our approach involves identifying subcommunities of users and examining the content they share through networks of co-occurring hashtags (#). We demonstrate that the network of co-occurrences can be divided into two clusters: one cluster consists of left-leaning users who use #greenleft (#vihervaas) as their main label while the other cluster consists of right-leaning users who use #bluewhite (#sinisevalkoinen) as their main label. We also show how both of these labels are used in a range of contexts, including discussions about politics, but also environmental issues or even sports events. While the use of hashtags has been previously examined in studies of Twitter, this is the f

We develop provably safe and convergent reinforcement learning (RL) algorithms for control of nonlinear dynamical systems with safety constraints. Our approach builds on recent advancements in off-policy RL, in particular, the state-of-the-art SAC algorithm. In contrast to existing work, our safety guarantee does not rely on an explicit formulation of the desired behavior in terms of a set of safe trajectories but rather relies on the notion of "safe states" that can be directly identified from data by using standard machine learning techniques. This is achieved by leveraging the dual formulation of RL as a constrained optimization problem where we consider a linearization of the cost function around the optimal policy. To obtain convergence results, we also develop a novel bound on the er

Characterizing samples that are difficult to learn from is crucial to developing highly performant ML models. In this work, we focus on understanding and mitigating the effect of sampling bias in deep learning based classifiers with applications towards medical image analysis tasks such as breast cancer detection using mammograms or classification into COVID-19 related cases vs normal scans for lung CT images (CT). We present a novel approach which aims at identifying biased regions within an input space by leveraging contrastive losses commonly used across self supervised methods like Contrastively Augmented Data Augmentation(CAD), Pseudo Label Learning Using Self Supervision With Hard Mining And Regularization Technique and Supervised Contrast Based SemiSupervialized Image Segmentation

Mobile networks have increased spectral efficiency through advanced multiplexing strategies that are coordinated by base stations (BSs). However, as the number of connected devices grows exponentially and network densification becomes necessary to provide high-quality service in urban areas with higher coverage density. The current centralized architecture is not sufficient for providing ultra low latency communication services which is a key requirement towards future 6G systems [1]. In this context decentralized approaches such distributed beamforming techniques could be deployed at BS level instead conventional linear or nonlinear precoding schemes used nowadays due its inherent advantages over these latter ones: it can reduce intercell interference while improving intra cell throughput

Automatic Compliance Checking (ACC) within the Architecture, Engineering, and Construction (AEC) industry is a promising approach to facilitate the compliance process in construction projects. However, existing ACC methods face several challenges, including their reliance on 3D models to extract building geometry, which requires extensive human effort during model creation and maintenance. Moreover, these approaches lack scalability and are unable to handle large-scale projects efficiently. In this study, we introduce a novel end-to-end framework that utilizes large language models (LLMs) such as ChatGPT to generate automatic design documents from natural language specifications. The generated documents contain all the required compliance information. We also leverage the semantic structur

Objective: For a long time, researchers have been trying to decode neural signals to restore speech in paralysed patients. However, the neural signals are mixed with various types of noises which make the decoding task extremely difficult. Methods: In this paper, we propose a novel method to tackle the noise problem in neural signals and improve the decoding performance. The proposed method is based on a deep neural network (DNN) which is a state-of-the-art decoder. We train a DNN to map the noisy neural data to clean neural data, and then use the clean data to reconstruct speech. Results: We evaluate the proposed method on two datasets. One is a synthetic dataset which is generated by adding noises to real neural data, and the other is a real-world dataset which contains neural signals recorded from a human subject. The results show that the proposed method outperforms the state-of-the-art methods by a large margin. Conclusion: The proposed method is effective in removing noises from neural signals and improving the decoding performance. It has the potential to be applied in other tasks such as neural interface and brain-computer interaction.

Compliant grippers enable robots to work with humans in unstructured environments. In this paper, we explore the use of a novel compliance-aware motion planning framework for collision avoidance and safe manipulation tasks. To accomplish this, we introduce a new method that incorporates both static friction and dynamic contact models into collision avoidance plans for compliant robots using the Robot Operating System (ROS) RRT* algorithm. Our approach utilizes an energy-based model to represent the physical properties of the environment and robot. This allows us to effectively account for the effects of compliance on the motion planning process. We evaluate our approach by conducting simulations with a compliant arm model and comparing it to standard motion planners like OpenRAVE and FastM

Recurrent Spiking Neural Networks (RSNNs) have emerged as a computationally efficient model to approximate spiking neural networks, particularly in real-time applications. Deep RSNNs showcase the ability to learn complex representations while operating at low computational costs. The state of the art RSNN models are based on the spike-timing-dependent plasticity (STDP), which is often used to train RSNN models due to its biological plausibility and compatibility with existing RSNN models. However, recent studies have demonstrated that STDP is not sufficient for learning complex spatio-temporal representations and may result in suboptimal performance in some applications. To address this issue, we propose a novel deep RSNN model called Spike-Temporal Dependent Plasticity (STDP) using Dynami

In the realm of computer vision and graphics, accurately establishing correspondences between geometric 3D models is essential for a wide variety applications. For instance in robotics it enables precise motion planning while ensuring that no collisions occur; or when rendering virtual scenes from real images to create realistic augmented reality experiences with convincing shadows & reflections - all thanks largely due advances made possible by understanding how objects relate spatially within their environment! But what if we could take this one step further? What would happen as soon an autonomous robot was able not just interpret its surroundings but also understand them intuitively through language-based reasoning techniques like those used today at Google AI Labs' research center loc

Nowadays, there are plenty of data sources generating massive amounts of information that, combined with novel machine learning (ML) techniques, can be used to improve the quality of life for individuals. However, this increase in data availability poses serious privacy concerns and calls for the adoption of privacy-preserving algorithms to protect personal data from being compromised. The use of federated learning (FL) is a promising solution to address these issues as it allows the creation of ML models without sharing private data among parties. However, FL may also introduce additional privacy risks if adversaries manage to steal the model parameters. In this paper, we present FLAME, an approach for training differentially private federated linear regression models. We evaluate FLAME o

The capacity to generalize beyond the range of training data is a pivotal challenge, often synonymic with overfitting and model size. We propose an alternative approach by designing models that are inherently capable of generalization: we introduce \emph{self-supervised convolutional networks} (SSCNs), which utilize simple self supervision in place or along side standard backpropagation during learning without any additional computational cost at test time nor extra parameters for hyperparameter tuning compared against conventional deep neural nets (\eg\ CNNs).

Instruction Tuning has the potential to stimulate or enhance specific capabilities of large language models (LLMs) through fine-tuning. However, current instruction tuning methods lack understanding on how LLMs perform instruction tuning internally and how it impacts their performance in downstream tasks. To address this problem, we propose a novel framework called Instruction Reasoning Tree (IRT), which can reason out the learning process of instruction tuning and provide insights for the design of LLM training algorithms. We evaluate IRT on two types of downstream tasks: (1) reasoning about the inner logic of LLMs' decision-making processes; (2) comparing different instruction tuning approaches with respect to task performance and computational cost. Extensive experiments on three widely

This work presents a novel data-driven multi-layered planning and control framework for the safe navigation of ground vehicles in complex road environments. The proposed approach utilizes a hierarchical model-free reinforcement learning (RLL) framework to plan high-level trajectories, incorporating safety constraints into the planning process. Additionally, we introduce a novel reward function designed specifically for the task of safe lane change maneuvers in complex environments. To further enhance safety during lane changes, we develop an adaptive trajectory generation algorithm that takes advantage of the safety-aware high-level policy. Our results demonstrate improved performance over existing methods with respect to safety and efficiency metrics. We also present a detailed analysis o

Self-supervised pre-training has been proved to be effective in learning transferable representations that benefit downstream tasks. However, it remains unclear whether such models can generalize to zero-shot scenarios when no human-annotated labels are available. In this paper, we investigate the ability of self-supervised pre-trained vision transformer (ViT) models to transfer knowledge to unseen categories and domains. To evaluate their zero-shot generalization ability, we conduct experiments with different types of unlabeled data: 1) domain adaptation datasets where the source and target domains share a common category space but different visual appearances; 2) cross-modal dataset where there is no shared category space between the domains but they have similar visual appearances; and

Automatically estimating the performance difficulty of a music piece represents a key process in music education to create personalized learning plans for students. In this paper, we introduce an automatic system for evaluating the difficulty level of musical compositions based on their melodic and harmonic structure. The proposed methodology is evaluated on three datasets containing thousands of pieces from different musical genres and composed by various authors. Our results show that the approach achieves high levels of accuracy, with mean AUC scores above 0.86. The system's generalization capabilities are also analyzed, showing an average decrease of only 1% in accuracy when tested on unseen data. Moreover, the system can be easily customized according to different music genre preferen

Increasing the accuracy of instance segmentation methods is often done at the expense of speed. This paper introduces a novel, fast yet accurate instance segmentation approach based on a multi-stage training methodology. The first stage learns a model that predicts dense segmentation masks based only on global feature information (i.e., features extracted from the whole image). The second stage uses the output of the first stage and localized features to refine the prediction using an attention mechanism. We demonstrate our method's effectiveness on both synthetic and real images. On the challenging PASCAL VOC 2012 dataset, we obtain an mIoU of 59.3%. For the more recent COCO dataset, we achieve an mIoU of 47.8% with an inference time of around 3 seconds per image.

In addition to performance on historical data, the robustness of Time Series Forecasting (TSF) models under adversarial attacks needs to be ensured. Adversarial attacks on TSF models can be divided into two categories: (1) those that manipulate the input time series, and (2) those that manipulate the parameters of the TSF models. Existing works mostly focus on the first category, and the second category remains unexplored. In this work, we propose CounterfacTS, a framework to probe the robustness of TSF models against adversarial attacks that manipulate the parameters of the models. We consider a wide range of base models used in TSF, and investigate the robustness of these base models when their parameters are adversarially manipulated. We show that the forecasted output can be significantly affected by adversarial attacks on the parameters of the base models. CounterfacTS can help practitioners to choose robust TSF models that are less vulnerable to adversarial attacks.

Training a robotic policy from scratch using deep reinforcement learning methods can be prohibitively expensive. A common solution to this problem is curriculum-based training, which involves incrementally introducing more challenging tasks over time as the agent learns how best to navigate its environment and solves simpler ones along with waypoints in between them (e. g., see [1]). In previous work on curricula for RL agents trained via imitation supervision by human experts ([2], Chapter 4), we introduced an approach that does not rely upon expert feedback but instead utilizes data collected during normal operation of existing robots: given access only through sensor measurements like video streams or laser scans combined w/ proprioceptive inputs such joint angles - no knowledge about t

Dynamic graphs, featuring continuously updated vertices and edges, have grown in importance for numerous real-world applications. However most graph algorithms are not designed to handle the fast changing nature of dynamic data streams effectively due mainly to their fixed vertex set assumption (i) which necessitates frequent reprocessing after every update; as well as because they rely on centralized storage structures that cannot cope with large volumes or streaming sources at high speeds/frequencies without losing information about historic changes made during earlier stages while also being unable maintain consistent behavior across different time intervals when dealing multiple simultaneous updates from diverse locations simultaneously within one instance session alone!

Program synthesizers aim to automate the construction of programs given only a specification. Despite their promise, these synthesizers often struggle with large input domains, noisy inputs, or specifications with ambiguous intent. In this paper, we address these challenges by leveraging natural language explanations. We propose a novel method to generate explanations that elucidate the reasoning process behind program synthesis decisions. These explanations can provide insights into why a program was synthesized, offering greater transparency into the synthesis process. We evaluate our method on large input domains from the CodeXGLUE benchmark, exploring its effectiveness in generating accurate and informative explanations. We also investigate the ability of our method to produce explanations for inputs that traditional synthesizers find challenging. Our results demonstrate that our approach can generate accurate explanations, even for difficult inputs, thus aiding developers in understanding the synthesis process and enhancing the reliability of synthesized programs.

Text-to-image generative models can generate high-quality humans, but realism is lost in their generated images. Recent studies have shown that the visual attention mechanism of these models shows a preference for the human body and head over other regions. This suggests that the model may not learn to represent faces accurately due to the lack of data on face images. In this paper, we propose a methodology based on the principle of "attention-guided optimization" that focuses on solving specific problems faced by text-to-image models. Specifically, we apply an attention-based loss function that encourages the model to pay more attention to the face region when generating image sequences. We conduct experiments on three popular text-to-image models: Stable Diffusion, DALL-E 2, and CLIP Vis

Deep learning methods have access to be employed for solving physical systems governed by parametric partial differential equations (PDEs) in recent years, and they are widely used in scientific computing, medical image processing, computer vision, etc. However, the existing deep learning approaches usually require a large amount of labeled data from a single system, which limits their applications in complex scenarios with multiple PDE models and data labels. To address this challenge, we propose a novel framework called Multi-Model Physics Awareness Learning (MMPAL), which enables multi-model physics awareness learning without the need for labeled data from individual systems. Specifically, MMPAL is built upon two critical components: an unified encoder that captures multi-level informat

Dense object counting or crowd counting has come a long way thanks to the recent development in the vision transformer (ViT). However, when dealing with real-world challenges like dynamic backgrounds, occlusion, and scale changes, existing ViT-based approaches often suffer from limitations such as unstable model parameters and low accuracy. In this paper, we propose a novel framework named CounterFuse to tackle these issues. CounterFuse consists of four main modules: 1) A novel fusion strategy based on local attention is proposed to aggregate the information from different scales and viewpoints; 2) We introduce a novel coarse-to-fine architecture that enables effective feature extraction across various spatial resolutions; 3) A new loss function is designed to enhance the classification ab

A novel hybrid active-passive reconfigurable intelligent surface (RIS) transmitter enabled downlink multi-user communication system is investigated. Specifically, RISs are exploited to serve as transmitter antennas, where each element can flexibly switch between active and passive modes to deliver information to multiple users. The system energy efficiency (EE) maximization problem is formulated by jointly optimizing the RIS element scheduling and beamforming coefficients, as well as the power allocation coefficients, subject to the user's individual rate requirement and the maximum RIS amplification power constraint. Using the Dinkelbach relaxation, the original mixed-integer nonlinear programming problem is transformed into a nonfractional optimization problem with a two-layer structure,

End-to-end differentiable learning for autonomous driving (AD) has recently become a prominent topic in the AD research community. We propose a novel architecture, named Fully Differentiable Architecture (FDA), which enables end-to-end training of perception and control modules within an AD system using only reinforcement learning (RL). FDA is designed to be fully differentiable, allowing for efficient backpropagation of gradients through all components of the AD system. To this end, we introduce a novel approach for differentiating the control module with respect to the environment dynamics. Additionally, we utilize a two-stage RL framework that first learns the control policy over a discrete action space using deep Q-networks (DQN), followed by a refinement stage where we use the learned

Fuzzing is the de-facto embedded software bug-unearthing technique, which, however, increasingly faces the challenges of diminishing returns and insufficient coverage. To address these challenges, this paper proposes FuzzBus, a novel fuzzing framework that enhances the fuzzing effectiveness and coverage through two distinctive approaches: leveraging Large-Language Models (LLM) for guidance and employing a crash-reuse mechanism. Specifically, FuzzBus utilizes LLMs, such as GPT-3, for guiding the fuzzing process by inferring useful test cases from existing ones. It also employs a two-level coverage-guided mechanism, where a fuzzer first aims to maximize code coverage at the function level by generating function-level seeds using LLMs; then, it focuses on maximizing basic-block coverage by generating more detailed test cases. Furthermore, the crash-reuse mechanism in FuzzBus allows it to recover and reuse useful information from crashes, thereby reducing the time spent on redundant exploration. Evaluation results on BusyBox, a widely-used embedded Linux distribution, show that FuzzBus discovers 43 bugs, of which 37 are previously unknown, demonstrating its effectiveness in unearthing embedded software bugs.

Visual understanding and segmentation of materials and their states is fundamental for understanding the physical world. The infinite variation in material appearance makes it challenging to develop a generalizable model that can classify novel materials. This paper presents PHASE, an algorithm that learns to predict material classes from a single image using a Transformer-based architecture. It leverages large-scale pretrained models (CLIP, Large Language Models) as the backbone for feature extraction, while incorporating advanced techniques like contrastive learning for feature optimization and attention fusion for improved classification performance. Extensive experiments on three benchmark datasets demonstrate its superior performance compared to state-of-the-art approaches. In particu

Few-shot learning, a challenging task in machine learning, aims to learn a class classifier from few training examples. The most popular method for this is the metric matching (MM) approach that learns an embedding space and uses cosine similarity as distance measures between embeddings of test samples with those labeled by support set during inference time [1]. However MM approaches have two major drawbacks: (i. ) they are not scalable because there exist $N^2$ number ($O\left({n}^{4}\right)$ if using one GPU card per instance ) comparisons required at each step when comparing all pairs among $\mathcal{X}_{s}$ ($\approx n$) positive/support classes vs.\ $\overline{\mathbf {x}}_{t}$, and therefore require massive parallel computing resources; , i.,e. ; ;it has poor scal

Tabletop exercises (TTEs) are a common method to evaluate a team's preparedness for dealing with security incidents. To make the most of the exercise, participants should focus on the scenario at hand and not be distracted by administrative tasks, such as documenting the incident or searching for tools. An ideal scenario would be one in which an artificial intelligence assistant provides the information the participants need. In this paper, we explore the use of large language models (LLMs) as an interactive assistant for TTEs. We show that LLMs can provide contextually relevant assistance during TTEs, reduce the cognitive load of the participants, and help them focus on the exercise at hand. We also show that LLMs can integrate with existing TTE tools and that the development of tailored LLMs is feasible. Our findings are based on a qualitative study with 11 security experts participating in 13 TTEs.

Topic models are a family of probabilistic models used to discover latent topics in a collection of documents. Despite their widespread use, there is little theoretical understanding on the geometric structure of the latent topics in topic models. In this paper, we provide a comprehensive geometric analysis of latent topics in two popular topic models, namely, the Latent Dirichlet Allocation (LDA) model and the Hierarchical Dirichlet Process (HDP) model. We show that the latent topics in both models lie on a low-dimensional subspace of the ambient space. We also show that the subspace in which the latent topics lie is orthogonal to the subspace spanned by the documents. These results are in contrast to the common practice of interpreting the first few principal components of the ambient space as topics. We propose a simple procedure to identify the topic subspace in LDA and HDP. We demonstrate the utility of our procedure for hypothesis testing and feature extraction.

3D asset generation is getting massive amounts of attention, inspired by the recent success of text-to-image diffusion models. However, these models have been mostly applied to synthetic data. In this work, we investigate the feasibility of diffusion models for generating realistic 3D assets from a single natural image input. We present a novel approach that uses multiple-view consistency and depth-aware augmentations during diffusion sampling for reconstructing complex 3D assets from one single 2D image. This approach allows our model to generate high-quality photo-realistic results with accurate shape and appearance. Our method achieves state-of-the-art performance on the ShapeNet dataset and also generalizes well to unseen categories in NYU-Depth V2 and ScanNet datasets. The code will b

We present an algorithmic framework for computing a set of eight points in 3D space that partitions a given set of $n$ points into roughly equal clusters. More precisely, the computed set of eight points has the property that the maximum cluster size is at most three times the minimum cluster size. The clusters are roughly balanced in the sense that the sum of the sizes of any four clusters is at most twice the sum of the sizes of the other four clusters. Our algorithm runs in $O(n)$ time, which we show is optimal, and yields an $O(n)$-time algorithm for computing a five-partitioning set of points in 3D, and an $O(n \log n)$-time algorithm for computing a four-partitioning set of points in 3D. We also show that for $n \geq 3$, there is no set of eight points in 3D that partitions any given set of $n$ points into four clusters of nearly equal size. Our results improve on a line of previous work on partitioning points in 3D, and are motivated by applications to geometric clustering and meshing.

We consider unsupervised domain adaptation (UDA) for semantic segmentation in which the model is trained on source domain with a specific data distribution and then adapted to target domain. Existing works assume that target data is drawn from the same or similar distribution as the source data, which leads to the risk of overfitting to the source domain. In this work, we explore whether we can train an UDA model using a different target dataset under the assumption that the two datasets are sampled i.i.d. from a common distribution. To address this issue, we propose a novel framework named Domain Adaptive Diversity Sampling (DADS). Specifically, we employ the class-conditional distribution of each target pixel to sample its nearest neighbor from the source domain, and then re-distribute t

The rise of generative models has driven significant advancements in recommender systems, leaving unique opportunties and challenges. In this paper we focus on the interplay between model-based recommendations (MBRs) that leverage contextualized representations generated by large language modles for users or items with conventional methods based solely upon historical interactions such as collaborative filtering(CF). Our experiments show considerable improvements when using MBR over CF alone; however there are potential risks associated to relying exclusively on it due its susceptibility towards cold start problems which can lead into misinformed suggestions if not properly addressed through proper training techniques like regularization terms tailored specifically toward mitigating these

Zero-shot Video Object Segmentation (ZSVOS) aims at segmenting the primary target object in a video without any training data. In this paper, we propose an efficient cross attention network to address ZVS task via two modules: 1). A Cross Attention Module which captures interrelationships between different objects and backgrounds of videos by using spatial feature maps;2.) An Intra Feature Modulation module that leverages multiple features from both intra frames as well temporal sequences for more accurate predictions on foregrounds or background regions within each frame according their respective importance levels estimated through deep learning algorithms based upon convolutional neural networks(CNN), recurrent long short term memory models. (LSTM)), respectively .

In this paper, we consider the time-varying channel estimation in millimeter wave (mmWave) multiuser multiple-input multiple-output systems. We introduce a stochastic geometry model that captures the correlation between the distance of the mmWave user equipment (UE) and its corresponding cellular tower. This model allows us to derive closed-form expressions for the number of UEs that can be simultaneously supported by a single antenna array at the base station (BS). Based on this model, we then derive the optimal BS location and array configuration for mmWave UEs with different levels of service rate requirements. Our results show that the optimal location of the BS is determined by the distribution of the distance between the UE and its associated cellular tower. The optimal BS location d

Natural Language Preprocessing (NLP) is a crucial component in many Natural Language Processing (NLP) and Natural Language Understanding (NLU) applications. Many NLP tasks, such as sentiment analysis, document similarity, and question answering, rely on the output of an NLP component. Typically, NLP components are evaluated in a task-specific manner, which assesses their output with respect to a gold standard that is specific to the task at hand. Task-specific evaluation, however, does not allow for a fair comparison of different NLP components, nor does it facilitate the assessment of their strengths and weaknesses. To address this problem, we propose a new evaluation approach for NLP systems that is language-centric, i.e., it focuses on the output of the system in terms of its impact on the performance of an NLP application. We apply this approach to sentiment analysis, document similarity, and question answering tasks, for which we devise new evaluation methods that allow for a language-centric assessment of NLP systems. We implement a suite of tools that automate the process of language-centric evaluation and make it easy for researchers to evaluate their own NLP systems. We use the resulting benchmark platform to compare five state-of-the-art NLP systems, namely mDistilBERT, mBERT, XLM-R, BERTimbau, and DeBERTA, on three NLP tasks, namely sentiment analysis, document similarity, and question answering. We conduct two types of experiments: (1) we assess the impact of the language used by the NLP system on the performance of the system, and (2) we assess the impact of the size of the NLP system on its performance. Our experiments yield two important insights. First, we find that the language used by the NLP system has a significant impact on its performance. Second, we find that the performance of the NLP system is not significantly impacted by its size.

This paper focuses on two highly publicized formal trade-offs in the field of responsible artificial intelligence (AI), namely: bias versus accuracy and privacy protection against data quality. We analyze how these dilemmas are presented within existing frameworks for AI ethics, including those adopted by professional associations such as IEEE Computer Society or ACM SIGCAS/SIGCHI Committee to reflect upon their relevance across different domains where algorithmic decision making is employed today – from healthcare through finance up until social media platforms’ recommender systems .

Automated Program Repair (APR) aims to automatically generate patches for rectifying software bugs. The effectiveness of APR algorithms is strongly influenced by the quality and availability of repair candidates, which are often generated using a black-box bug triage mechanism (e.g., manual inspection). However, such a black-box method typically fails to consider the underlying cause of the bug, resulting in irrelevant or redundant repair candidates. In this paper, we propose a white-box approach that uses the bug's source code as a supervised learning target, thereby identifying the most relevant program elements to be used as potential repair candidates. We train our proposed algorithm on a large dataset of 35K bug reports from GitHub projects and evaluate its performance against state-o

We present a LiDAR-visual-inertial simultaneous localization and mapping (SLAM) system for large-scale environments. The proposed system fuses measurements from a moving base frame, consisting of a visual camera and an inertial measurement unit (IMU), with sparse LiDAR scans from a separate LiDAR frame. The LiDAR-visual-Inertial SLAM system is able to estimate the motion of the base frame with respect to the LiDAR frame and simultaneously build a sparse volumetric map of the environment. The key idea is to represent the environment as a voxel grid, where each voxel is equipped with semantic information, i.e., occupancy, color, and surface normals. We adopt a tight coupling scheme, where the camera performs per-pixel depth estimation by solving a least squares problem involving the camera intrinsic parameters, the IMU measurements, and the relative pose between the LiDAR and camera frames. The relative pose is estimated by maximizing the likelihood of the fused features, which are derived from the sparse LiDAR data and the dense voxel grid. We evaluate the proposed system on both synthetic and real-world datasets, including moving and static cases. The experiments show that the proposed LiDAR-visual-inertial SLAM system produces accurate and dense voxel maps, with fine resolution and good surface reconstruction.

Event argumentation is an important component of event processing. However, annotated data is scarce for this task. To overcome this data scarcity, we propose to exploit cross-domain unsupervised learning. Specifically, we leverage publicly available cross-domain event argument data, such as that provided by the DEER dataset, to augment domain-specific data, such as that provided by the ACE dataset. To do this, we introduce a novel natural language processing task, namely template-based Mad Libs (TBML). TBML requires models to fill in event argument slots with words that are likely to appear in the target domain. We show that TBML is a useful proxy for the original task of interest, in that models that perform well on TBML also tend to perform well on the original task. Crucially, we also show that TBML can be used to effectively augment domain-specific event argument data, thereby improving downstream performance.

The quadratic complexity of the attention mechanism represents one of the biggest hurdles for processing long sequences using deep learning. While there have been recent advances in linearizing the attention mechanism, they primarily focus on reducing the time needed to perform softmax over a fixed number of queries. This paper presents an approach that reduces the quadratic cost by transforming the linear dimension of the input sequence into the linear dimension of the output sequence. We achieve this transformation by introducing a new way to encode the sequence, which we call "sequence-wise masks." We use these masks to define two types of linear functions: linear projection and point-wise linearization. These linear functions can be used as features to train a feedforward network, allo

The advent of Large Language Models (LLMs) has led to remarkable progress on a variety of tasks, but also introduced new security risks. In particular, the LLM's ability to generate coherent text opens up potential for malicious use cases, such as spreading misinformation or creating phishing emails. This paper presents an evaluation of a novel adversarial attack that uses a GPT-3.5 model to generate misleading information targeted at specific users. We show that our approach achieves high success rates in generating convincing, yet falsified, content. Furthermore, we demonstrate how these attacks can be used to successfully create fake messages with over 20% click-through rate on Twitter and Discord. Our findings highlight the need for more rigorous testing and auditing of LLMs to ensure

With the help of citizen science data, machine learning can contribute to nature conservation by predicting the distribution of species of interest. However, the reliance of such predictions on the spatial resolution of the underlying data is often overlooked. We used citizen science data for three taxonomic groups - free-ranging rodents (Order: Rodentia), insects (Class: Insecta), and the Eurasian lynx (Lynx lynx; Family: Felidae) - to illustrate the effect of spatial resolution on machine learning predictions. For each taxon, we constructed occurrence maps at three spatial resolutions (100 m, 500 m, and 1 km) using MaxEnt, a machine learning algorithm commonly used for species distribution modelling. We then evaluated the area under the receiver operating characteristic curve (AUC) for each model. We tested the significance of the difference in AUCs using the non-parametric bootstrap method. We also examined the effect of spatial resolution on the importance of environmental variables for the three taxonomic groups. The AUCs for the three taxonomic groups increased with spatial resolution. At 100 m, the AUCs for the three taxonomic groups were 0.795 (lynx), 0.778 (rodents), and 0.684 (insects). At 500 m, the AUCs were 0.879 (lynx), 0.873 (rodents), and 0.76 (insects). Finally, at 1 km, the AUCs were 0.884 (lynx), 0.86 (rodents), and 0.786 (insects). The environmental variables important for the machine learning models also changed with spatial resolution. Our results highlight the importance of considering the effects of spatial resolution when using machine learning for conservation. We recommend that conservation biologists use the highest spatial resolution available for their analyses. If high-resolution data are not available, they should be aware that lower-resolution data can lead to underestimation of habitat suitability for many taxa.

In environments like offices, the duration of a robot's navigation between two locations may vary over time. This is due to factors such as traffic patterns or changes in activity levels within an environment. Such temporal variations can have a significant impact on the performance and efficiency of automated workflows. To address this challenge, we introduce a novel framework for multi-robot path planning that incorporates temporal considerations. Specifically, our approach uses a modified A* algorithm to explore multiple paths in parallel while accounting for varying travel times. Our experiments show that this leads to a reduction in travel time by up to 34% compared to traditional A* search algorithms, even in scenarios with large fluctuations in travel times. These results demonstrat

Virtual reality (VR) is a promising data engine for autonomous driving (AD). However, current VR datasets lack sufficient labels and annotations in the real world. We propose a novel framework to automatically label lane-center points with ground truth annotations on the road surface from synthetic VR data. Firstly, we leverage a deep neural network model to predict lane-center point positions based on an input image. Then, we use a high-resolution camera to capture real images of lane-center points on the road surface. Finally, we perform semantic segmentation to obtain the corresponding semantic masks of each lane-center point. Our method not only achieves high accuracy but also provides more detailed spatial information about the road surface. We evaluate our method on three public VR d

Recent years have witnessed a plethora of learning-based solutions for congestion control, aiming to provide the network with adaptive rate scaling policies. Recognizing the importance of feedback-based communication in many critical scenarios, we focus on constructing feedback-enhanced rate adaptation algorithms using machine learning techniques. This paper presents FREC, a novel framework for designing and training feedback-enabled rate adaptation algorithms. Inspired by the success of Reinforcement Learning (RL) methods in complex decision-making problems, FREC leverages RL techniques to learn optimal rate adaptation policies from collected data that are subsequently deployed in real-time. The key insight behind this approach is that feedback provides crucial information regarding the c

This paper focuses on two highly publicized formal trade-offs in the field of responsible artificial intelligence (AI), namely, explainability and fairness. However, despite their theoretical nature, these trade-offs are not often realized in practice due to a lack of suitable methods for balancing both objectives. To address this challenge, we propose an algorithmic framework that provides insights into the relationship between explainability and fairness while also promoting a better understanding of the underlying trade-off. Our approach involves leveraging machine learning models trained with human feedback to predict outcomes based on input features. The model's performance is evaluated using metrics such as accuracy and area under curve (AUC). Based on the results, we can determine w

Hyperdimensional Computing (HDC) is an emerging computational paradigm for representing compositional functions. HDC allows to compute with a compact codebook of vectors in the hypercube, and the number of vectors scales linearly with the complexity of the function. In this paper we investigate how to efficiently evaluate simple arithmetic operations on these codes. We define a general class of algorithms that includes the recently proposed "RNN-based" approach as a special case. We show that they are universal for any function computable by a regular language over the hypercube, and give a full characterization of such functions in terms of a new notion of \emph{semi-regular} languages. Finally, we propose several efficient algorithms for the most common arithmetic operations, and evaluat

In representation learning on graph-structured data, many popular graph neural networks (GNNs) are designed to learn node representations based on the node features and the adjacency matrix. Graph Transformer Network (GTN), one of such methods, is a recent work that has achieved state-of-the-art performance in several graph classification tasks. However, GTN lacks a rigorous theoretical analysis of its generalization error. In this paper, we study the generalization error bound for GTN under different settings, including the model capacity and the distribution shifts between training and testing graphs. We show that the standard error analysis technique does not apply directly to GTN and propose a new approach to analyze the generalization error. This new approach allows us to prove a shar

Conventional multi-hop fact verification models are prone to rely on spurious evidence and miss the relevant ones, leading them not generalizable enough. To tackle this issue in a principled way for effective endtoend reasoning with rich context information from multiple hops across diverse domains/tasks (e g., social media vs news), we propose an unsupervised method called Graph Augmented MultiHop Reasoning(GAMR) which incorporates graph augmented knowledge into model training by exploiting large amounts of unlabeled data as wellas leveraging pretraining techniques like contrastive learningand fine tuning using few labeled samples per task / domain pair .

We consider the problem of estimating a high-rank matrix from its rectangular submatrix. This problem has found applications in various areas such as image processing, subspace segmentation, and feature extraction. We propose a novel estimation method that extends the rotational invariant estimator (RIE) to the rectangular case. The proposed estimator, called rectangular RIE (RRIE), is shown to have the oracle property, which means that the estimator performs as well as if the singular values of the submatrix are known. We also show that RRIE is minimax optimal up to a logarithmic factor. Furthermore, we devise an efficient algorithm to compute the estimator. Simulation results are provided to illustrate the performance of RRIE.

Traditional sequential recommendation methods assume that users' sequence data is clean enough to learn accurate sequence representations to reflect user preferences. In practice, users' sequences inevitably contain noise (e.g., accidental interactions), leading to incorrect reflections of user preferences. Consequently, some pioneer studies have explored modeling sequentiality and correlations in sequences to implicitly or explicitly reduce noise's influence. However, relying on only available intra-sequence information (i.e., sequentiality and correlations in a sequence) is insufficient and may result in over-denoising and under-denoising problems (OUPs), especially for short sequences. To improve reliability, we propose to augment sequences by inserting items before denoising. However,
