In the last decades, there has been a deceleration in the rates of poverty reduction worldwide. We aim to investigate the role that technology can play in reducing poverty. By doing so, we also hope to shed light on the effects of technological innovations and their impacts on society at large. To this end, we first present a brief overview of various historical periods' economic evolution, with special emphasis placed on recent developments such as the rise of artificial intelligence (AI) and other disruptive technologies like smartphones or blockchain. Then, we will discuss how these new technologies have affected the distribution of wealth in different countries across time. Finally, we conclude by offering some recommendations for future research on this topic. Throughout our work, we

Policy gradient (PG) methods are among the most popular policy-based reinforcement learning algorithms. To guarantee the stability of PG algorithms, many existing works impose a strong convexity assumption on the policy parameterization. In this paper, we develop a new stability analysis framework for PG methods when applied to stochastic differential equations (SDEs), without the strong convexity assumption. In contrast to the existing stability analysis framework, our new framework relies on a novel stability notion, called consistency with the perturbation process. This new stability notion allows us to analyze the stability of PG algorithms applied to SDEs with a general class of policy parameterizations, and furthermore, our stability results do not require the satisfaction of the strong convexity assumption. In addition, based on the developed stability analysis framework, we propose a new PG algorithm, called consistency policy gradient (CPG), which stabilizes the policy update by leveraging the consistency with the perturbation process. The stability of CPG is guaranteed without the strong convexity assumption on the policy parameterization. In numerical examples, we show that CPG often performs better than the existing PG algorithms that require the strong convexity assumption.

Palmprint biometric, being one of the most reliable biometric modalities, is widely used in various high-security scenarios. However, the potential leakage of palmprint biometric information poses a threat to an individual's privacy. To address this security and privacy issue, we propose a novel dual-level cancelable framework that enables both cancelable palmprint verification and hack-proof data storage. At the device side, a hardware-software co-design approach is taken to implement a cancelable template storage scheme based on a programmable logic array. This scheme introduces an additional layer of security by embedding a user-specific key in the hardware level, which is used to encrypt the biometric template. The encrypted template is then stored in the database. When authentication is required, the template is recalled and decrypted using the user-specific key. The proposed framework is evaluated in terms of its security, privacy, and usability. A user study is also conducted to evaluate the palmprint enrollment and verification performance. The results demonstrate that the proposed framework achieves a high acceptance rate and low processing time. It also passes the FIPS 201 security and privacy evaluation criteria.

Graph structure learning, a key component in numerous network-structured data applications, is aimed at inferring the underlying graph structure of a given graph-structured data. The learned graph structure is then used for various downstream tasks such as node classification and node clustering. In the existing works, due to the limitation of the adjacent matrix representation, the graph structure learning models fail to capture the global graph structure information and the node embedding models fail to utilize the learned graph structure information to enhance the node representation. To address these problems, we propose a novel Self-Attention Empowered Graph Convolutional Network (SA-GCN) model to learn the graph structure and generate the node embedding simultaneously. Specifically, the self-attention mechanism is applied to the modified adjacent matrix to capture the global graph structure information, and then the graph structure information is leveraged to enhance the node embedding. The experimental results on various benchmark datasets demonstrate that our model outperforms the state-of-the-art methods. The source code is available at https://github.com/GKNL/SA-GCN.

Differentially private (DP) machine learning is considered the gold-standard solution for training a model without compromising privacy. However, in practical settings, DP mechanisms often require high accuracy or sample complexity to ensure privacy guarantees. To address this challenge, we propose the first differentially private mechanism that can achieve optimal sample complexity and provably high accuracy while still providing strong privacy guarantees. Our mechanism, which we call "Differential Privacy via Repeated Sampling" (DPRS), combines techniques from repeated sampling with differential privacy. We prove that our method achieves optimal sample complexity under a relaxed notion of accuracy. This allows us to provide tighter bounds on the accuracy of our method than existing ones.

This study investigates the application of Nash equilibrium strategies in optimizing forest harvesting decisions. The objective is to maximize profit while considering a set of constraints related to harvesting, transportation, and market limitations. An optimization model based on the linear programming (LP) approach has been developed to address this problem. The proposed model incorporates the selection of harvest blocks and their corresponding harvest rates within each block, as well as the determination of the number of trucks needed for transportation from each selected block to specific markets. To tackle the computational challenges associated with large-scale problems, a genetic algorithm (GA) is implemented for solving the model. The GA is designed to efficiently search through t

Managing complex Cyber-Physical Energy Systems (CPES) requires solving various optimization problems with different objectives. In this paper, we propose a multiobjective model for managing CPESS by taking into account the energy efficiency and cost of generating power in renewable sources as well as load shedding costs due to insufficient generated electricity when needed at peak hours is not available from these resources alone or cannot be stored until later use because there are no batteries capable enough yet today on market scale deployment level which would allow us do so economically feasible way .

On-device learning has emerged as a prevailing trend that avoids the slow response of remote servers. Deep learning (DL) models are often too large to be deployed on resource-constrained devices, and they require intensive computation for inference at the edge. Consequently, DL models must undergo optimization in order to run efficiently on these devices. However, this process is not straightforward due to the constraints imposed by hardware capabilities. In this work, we present a novel approach called Adaptive Optimization (AO), which enables efficient on-device execution of DL models with limited computational resources. Our approach can adaptively optimize different layers of the model separately based on their respective resource requirements, resulting in significant performance impr

We propose a method to teach multiple large language models (LLM) to collaborate by interleavely executing tasks in parallel. We achieve this through the use of token-level coordination, which allows LMs with different capabilities and task focus areas access each other's outputs at any point during execution without relying on explicit supervision or training mechanisms such as reinforcement learning based methods that require human interaction for feedback loops between LLMS running concurrently over time intervals called 'rounds'. Through our experiments using GPT3 we show how effective these techniques can be when applied towards solving complex problems involving several subtasks simultaneously while maintaining efficiency gains compared against single model versions due their ability

Deep Residual Neural Networks (ResNets) have demonstrated remarkable success across a wide range of computer vision tasks. In recent years, the introduction of ResNet-like architectures has significantly advanced the field of 3D object detection and segmentation, but their effectiveness in 2D object detection remains unclear. To address this gap, we conduct an in-depth investigation into the potential advantages offered by ResNets for 2D object detection. Our findings reveal that while ResNets demonstrate superior performance compared to traditional architectures on standard benchmarks such as PASCAL VOC and COCO, they struggle to outperform conventional networks when applied to more challenging datasets like MS COCO Hard. To better understand this behavior, we analyze the impact of ResNet

Advanced machine learning algorithms are increasingly utilized to provide data-based prediction and decision-making support for complex problems. In this context, the ability of these models to reason about their predictions is crucial. Reasoning models have been shown to enhance performance in domains ranging from healthcare to robotics, but they face limitations when it comes to handling complex tasks involving multiple modalities. This study presents a novel reasoning framework based on an innovative multi-modal attention mechanism that leverages both visual and textual information. By integrating features from various modalities, the proposed model delivers accurate results while maintaining computational efficiency. The proposed approach is evaluated using the challenging task of dete

The emergence of LLMs has ignited a fresh surge of breakthroughs in NLU. To fully unleash their potential, we believe it is important to understand the underlying properties and limitations that determine how well they can perform on various tasks (e. g., commonsense reasoning). One key property for this purpose—and one which remains understudied with respect LLM performance —is semantic compositionality: The ability or inability by an agent/model $M$ to combine different pieces $(A_1,..., A_{|S|, B})$ into new information ($C$) where $| S|$ denotes set size; i e.,, when given inputs such as "A dog runs after two squirrels" followed immediately afterwards without intervening context ("Now there are three dogs running"), does M correctly infer "$D = D \cup \{ C\} $" ? In other words,, wha

Data-driven description in control systems is a recently proposed formalism that allows one to describe the behavior of a system in an abstract way, focusing on the data the system produces. It uses the concept of a data space, which is a set of data vectors that the system can produce, and a data dictionary, which is a set of rules that describe how the data vectors are related to each other. In this paper, we propose a canonical form for data-driven description. This canonical form is a special representation of the data dictionary that makes the structure of the description more apparent. It also has the advantage that certain operations on the description, such as computing the set of data vectors that can be produced by the system, become simpler.

In camera calibration, the non-linear distortion of the image plane is usually modeled as a function of the radial distance from the image center. The distortion function is then estimated by a polynomial, the coefficients of which are obtained by minimizing the reprojection error. However, minimizing the reprojection error may not be a good optimization criterion when the distortion is large. In this paper, we propose a new optimization criterion, called the unbiased estimator (UE), which is a function of the expected value of the squared reprojected error. We show that the UE is a better criterion than the reprojection error for calibrating a wide-angle lens. We also show that the UE is related to the mean squared error (MSE) and the generalized mean squared error (GMSE) criteria. We provide numerical examples that illustrate the theoretical results.

The breadth, scale, and temporal granularity of modern electronic health records (EHR) present a unique opportunity for longitudinal studies. In this work, we explore the use of EHR data to study chronic disease using deep learning techniques. Specifically, we developed a framework that leverages the richness of longitudinal EHR data to extract features from time-series data related to diagnoses and treatments for patients with various chronic diseases. Our method combines traditional machine learning approaches such as clustering and regression analysis with more advanced techniques like graph convolutional networks (GCN), to provide insights into the progression of chronic conditions over time. We apply our model to two real-world datasets: one from Kaiser Permanente Northern California

We consider the problem of offline runtime verification of distributed systems specified as interaction nets. Our verification method checks whether a blackbox distributed system, which is under test, interacts with its environment in accordance with a given interaction-based specification. The interaction-based specification is given as a tuple (G, L), where G is a graph with labeled edges that specifies the nominal behavior of the system, and L is a set of lifelines that specify the temporal behavior of the system. In this paper, we extend an existing seed-based verification method with lifeline removal. The seed-based verification method works offline and uses randomization to explore the state space of the system. With our extension, the seeds are enriched with information on how to remove lifelines from the system. To this end, we introduce a new notion of seeds, called interaction seeds, which are equipped with information on which edges of the interaction graph G are relevant for the remaining lifetime of the seed. Interaction seeds allow us to precisely control the removal of lifelines during the exploration of the state space of the system. We show that our method is more efficient than the original seed-based verification method. In particular, we show that our method is able to exploit parallelism inherent in the specification (G, L) and, thus, can significantly reduce the time required for verification.

Recent advancements in large language models (LLMs) have underscored their importance in the evolution of artificial intelligence. However, despite extensive pretraining on multilingual datasets, available open-sourced LLMs exhibit limited effectiveness in processing Vietnamese. The challenge is exacerbated by the absence of systematic benchmark datasets and metrics tailored for Vietnamese LLM evaluation. To mitigate these issues, we have finetuned LLMs specifically for Vietnamese and developed a comprehensive evaluation framework encompassing 10 common tasks and 31 metrics. Our evaluation results reveal that the fine-tuned LLMs exhibit enhanced comprehension and generative capabilities in Vietnamese. Moreover, our analysis indicates that models with more parameters can introduce more bias

Knowledge graph (KG) completion aims to infer missing facts in a KG. Most existing models complete KGs by predicting missing (subject, object) pairs given a predicate. However, the temporal aspect, i.e., the time when an (subject, object) fact holds, is often ignored in such tasks. In this paper, we propose a novel task of temporal KG completion, which aims to predict both the missing (subject, object) pairs and their corresponding timestamps. To address this task, we propose a novel time-sensitive embedding method, namely Hypercomplex Embedding with Time (HET), which represents both the static and temporal information of entities and time-sensitive relations in a unified manner. HET learns the joint distribution of missing (subject, object) pairs and their timestamps by adopting a tripartite graph structure. It also imposes a sparsity constraint on the learned embeddings to improve the interpretability. We design a tailored sampling strategy to facilitate the learning of time-sensitive relations. Experimental results on three benchmark datasets demonstrate that our method outperforms state-of-the-art baselines. The source code will be publicly released at https://github.com/wjzheng96/HET.

Semi-supervised source localization aims to localize the sound source in a room environment by using a small amount of labeled data and a large amount of unlabeled data. Previous works mainly use the pseudo-labeling method to deal with the semi-supervised task. However, the distribution of the features between the labeled and unlabeled data is usually quite different, which makes the model trained by pseudo-labeling inaccurate. To alleviate this problem, we propose a cross pseudo-labeling method, which selects the pseudo-labels from the intersection of the prediction results of the labeled and unlabeled data. Furthermore, we also propose a joint embedding model to make the features of the labeled and unlabeled data consistent, thus improving the accuracy of the pseudo-labels. Experiments show that the proposed method performs much better than the previous state-of-the-art methods.

Recently, promptable segmentation models, such as the Segment Anything Model (SAM) and its variants, have achieved remarkable success in semantic segmentation. However, these models face challenges in adapting to low-resource domains, especially when fine-tuning is not feasible due to limited data. To address this, we propose a novel framework called Self-Prompted Learning (SPL). SPL involves two components: (1) self-prompting based on pretrained text encoders, which leverages prior knowledge to generate diverse yet relevant prompts for unseen images; and (2) learning from prompted predictions by utilizing a consistency loss, which enhances the model's capacity to accurately detect the boundaries of objects. Experiments on four datasets demonstrate that SPL achieves consistent performance

Adversarial robustness is a crucial but often overlooked property of vision-language models (VLMs). In this work, we investigate a simple and effective method to improve the adversarial robustness of pre-trained VLMs. Specifically, we propose the Prompt Word Boost (PWB) method, which selects one prompt word from each pre-defined prompt template as the representation of the template. We show that PWB can be naturally integrated with existing robustness enhancement methods for VLMs, and further improves their performance. Our experiments on the MMLU, ImageNet, and JFT datasets show that PWB significantly improves the adversarial robustness of pre-trained VLMs under various attack and defense mechanisms. Moreover, we find that the robustness of PWB-VLMs generalizes well to unseen prompt templates.

A novel hybrid active-passive reconfigurable intelligent surface (RIS) transmitter, which is composed of RIS with hybrid passive and fully or partially controllable elements as well its associated antenna configuration for multiuser massive multiple input single output scenario. The proposed system consists of three layers: the first layer contains a 2D planar array formed by two rows $\times$ columns rectangular patch antennas; second one includes $N=4$, each containing four identical circularly polarized parasitic dipoles acting like an inductive reactance element to form virtual monopole at centre position above ground plane in order generate different phase shift from $-360^{\circ}$ to $+189^\text{o}$.

Real-time flood forecasting plays a crucial role in enabling timely and effective response to floods. However, real time water level data are not always available for use as inputs due various technical issues (e..g., communication failure). Physical Unclonable Functions (PUFs) have been shown to be a highly promising source of randomness in hardware security applications. This work explores the use and performance characteristics associated with 2-dimensional PUFs, also known as image based or matrix type structures for generating random data within various cryptographic scenarios such those related key generation operations involved during symmetric encryption schemes like AES/DES etc., asymmetric cryptosystems including RSA algorithm along side elliptic curve cryptography implementations amongst others where high entropy is required throughout each stage from initialization phase onwards until final output delivery occurs after processing all inputs received priorly by user' s request input message(data). The goal here then

High dynamic range (HDR) imaging involves capturing a series of frames of the same scene at different exposures, which can be used to reconstruct HDR images. However, current approaches still suffer from significant ghosting artifacts. In this paper, we propose an image-guided deep neural network (DNN)-based method that utilizes an image as input and produces a mask for each frame in order to generate high dynamic range (HDR) images without ghosting artefacts. Our approach employs a DNN-based image-to-mask model, which estimates the image-dependent light field and generates a binary mask that delineates regions with various exposure levels. This mask is then applied to each frame in the video sequence, resulting in a set of cleaned up frames free from ghosting artefacts.

Despite the significant achievements of existing prompting methods such as in-context learning and chain-of-thought models, they still have some limitations. In this paper, we propose a new approach called Prompt-Augmented Model (PAM), which combines the advantages of existing methods to achieve better performance. The PAM model consists of two parts: the main model and the augmentation model. The main model is based on the Chain-of-Thought (CoT) method, which is pre-trained on large language models such as GPT-4. The augmentation model is a fine-tuning model that utilizes prompts to enhance the capability of the main model. We introduce two types of prompts: direct prompts and indirect prompts. Direct prompts are used to directly provide information for the main model while indirect promp

Information retrieval (IR) is a pivotal component in various applications. Recent advances in large language models (LLMs) have prompted the integration of LLMs into IR systems, providing new opportunities for enhancing IR effectiveness. However, existing works tend to focus on utilizing single or limited combinations of LLMs as retrievers with fixed pre-trained LLMs serving as the knowledge source, limiting their potential for improving IR performance. This paper presents the first comprehensive overview of LLM-based retriever techniques that integrate multiple LLMs and/or incorporate diverse knowledge sources to enhance information retrieval. Our findings reveal a diverse landscape of LLM-based retriever methods and highlight the emergence of novel approaches tailored towards specific IR

The White House Executive Order on Artificial Intelligence highlights the risks of large language models like ChatGPT. A new study shows that humans are able to outperform AI algorithms in a game called 'Guess The Number.' This could have important implications for how we understand and use artificial intelligence technology going forward. AI-generated content is becoming more prominent, but it's also leading many users down an ethical rabbit hole with unintended consequences when using these tools without understanding their capabilities or limitations first hand by themselves through trial & error experiences before they can be trusted as reliable sources because there isn’t enough data available yet; this makes them dangerous places where mistakes happen quickly if not caught early duri

Efficient deployment of neural networks (NN) requires the co-optimization of accuracy and latency. We introduce an innovative framework that optimizes NN latencies using a novel training methodology. Our approach leverages recent advancements in computational graph theory to identify critical operations within NN architectures, and then utilizes this information to reduce memory accesses during training. This results in more efficient use of memory resources while maintaining accurate network performance. To demonstrate its effectiveness, we conduct extensive experiments on the MNIST dataset, achieving significant improvements in both inference speed and test error rate when compared to state-of-the-art methods. Moreover, our framework provides robustness against data noise and generalizes

Simulation is an invaluable tool for radio-frequency system designers that enables rapid prototyping and optimization of complex wireless systems. However, the time-consuming nature of simulation results evaluation limits its use to specific scenarios, often preventing engineers from fully exploring the potential of their designs. To address this challenge, we propose a novel approach to automating the evaluation of simulation results by extracting features from the simulation traces with LLMs. This allows us to evaluate the performance of different configurations without requiring extensive manual analysis or re-simulations. Our framework is flexible enough to accommodate multiple types of LLMs and can be easily customized based on the needs of individual users. In our experiments, we com

Older people are increasingly using smartphones to browse the web, yet they are considered a vulnerable group to cybercrime. This is in part due to the ubiquitousness of complex security features that were designed with a young, tech-savvy user in mind. In this paper, we adopt a socio-technical approach to examine the tensions between security and inclusivity through the lens of older people's experiences and needs. We do this by designing and evaluating a age-inclusive secure browsing prototype, which we tested through a between-subjects experiment (n=75). Our results indicate that security features that are invisible or require too much effort from users can undermine their inclusivity. Conversely, security features that are visible and easy to use can improve inclusivity. Based on these findings, we share design guidelines for creating age-inclusive secure browsing tools. We also discuss the role of service providers in adopting these guidelines to create more inclusive ecosystems.

In lung radiotherapy, infrared cameras can record the location of reflective objects on the patient’s surface. The object positions are used to generate a deformation field between the reference and treatment planning computed tomography (CT) scans. This deformation field is then applied to the planning target volume, which is used for dose calculation, thus making it possible to correct respiratory motion effects without respiratory gating or tracking. We present a new method that uses 4DCT information to improve the accuracy of registration and deformation field estimation. A deep learning model was trained to predict the displacement vector fields between pairs of 4DCT images, with one image containing an infrared camera. In this way, the proposed method leverages the information contai

We present Splat-Nav, a navigation pipeline that consists of a real-time safe planning algorithm and a fast motion planner. Our approach is based on the idea of planning with splats, i.e., we use a sparse set of waypoints to guide the robot's motion, instead of a dense set of trajectories as in classical motion planning algorithms. This allows us to solve the motion planning problem in two steps: (1) generating a feasible splat and (2) solving the trajectory optimization problem for each splat point. The first step involves finding a feasible splat using a simple heuristic. Since finding a feasible splat can be costly, our algorithm iteratively improves the splat by optimizing its position. To do so, it uses a modified version of the TSP formulation of motion planning, which enforces admis

Knowledge distillation, the technique of transferring knowledge from large, complex models to smaller ones, has been studied for decades. However, these studies have mainly focused on static networks and a limited range of tasks, such as classification, ignoring the potential benefits of dynamic neural networks (DNNs) in this field. To bridge this gap, we propose a novel framework, named Dynamic Neural Network Distillation (DyNND), which introduces the concept of task-based dynamic neural networks into the field of knowledge distillation. In DyNND, we design two novel training strategies: (1) Task-aware Training Strategy (TTS), which involves two-phase training: firstly, we train the student network with a single task, then fine-tune it with multiple tasks; (2) Multi-task-aware Teacher Mod

Advanced Audio-Visual Speech Recognition (AVSR) systems have been observed to be sensitive to missing video frames, performing even worse than single-modality models. While applying the dropout technique to the video modality enhances robustness to missing frames, it simultaneously results in a performance loss when dealing with complete data input. In this paper, we investigate this contrasting phenomenon from the perspective of modality bias and reveal that an excessive modality bias on the audio caused by dropout is the underlying reason. Moreover, we present the Modality Bias Hypothesis (MBH) to systematically describe the relationship between modality bias and robustness against missing modality in multimodal systems.

Matrix completion is an important area of research in recommender systems. Recent methods view a rating matrix as a low-rank matrix, and reconstruct its missing entries by leveraging the low-rank property. However, these methods have not considered the influence of the sparsity of user ratings on recommendation performance. In this paper, we propose a novel matrix completion method that considers the sparse nature of user ratings for recommendation tasks. We first introduce the sparse representation of users' historical rating patterns as well as items, which enables the use of the sparse regularization term to address the sparse nature of user ratings. Then, we apply the sparse reconstruction algorithm based on alternating direction method of multipliers (ADMM) to solve our proposed model

In this paper, we analyze the monotonicity of information aging in a remote estimation system, where an observer (agent) collects measurements from multiple sources and sends them to another agent for inference. We formulate three types of monotone policies: state-dependent age policy $\pi_{A}$, measurement dependence degree $d$, which is used as control parameters by agents during data collection; observation time sequence $\{T_k\}$, called observation schedule or simply scheduling strategy with fixed length window size parameter $(N)$; finally source selection rule ($\xi$), that defines how many sensors are active at any given instant within each group/cluster while taking into account their relative distance between themselves versus other groups' members without regard toward

The purpose of this feasibility study was to examine the potential impact of reading digital interactive e-books on children's early literacy development. The researchers tested the efficacy of a digital interactive book (DIB) in improving letter recognition and phoneme segmentation skills among 42 prekindergarten students. The results indicate that, compared with traditional paper books, DIBs may have positive effects on early literacy skills, particularly for the most advanced readers. This study provides valuable insights into the potential benefits of DIBs and their role in facilitating young learners' transition from print to digital media. It underscores the importance of incorporating interactive elements such as sound effects, animations, and interactivity features in educational m

Product search plays an essential role in eCommerce. It was treated as a special type of information retrieval, which is different from text-based search engines due to the unique characteristics of product data. The objective of this study is to analyze various features used for product search and compare their effectiveness in improving product search performance on three product datasets. Our results suggest that some features are more effective than others in improving product search performance. Furthermore, we found that some features have negative impacts on search performance when they are used alone. This finding suggests that not all features can be combined effectively. In addition, our results show that the effects of individual features vary across different product categories

This paper introduces a novel neuro-symbolic architecture for relation classification (RC) that combides the strength of symbolic reasoning with neural network capabilities. Specifically, our model leverages two encoders to represent symbols and text respectively as well structured semantic graphs via graph convolutional networks(GCNs). The corresponding encoder representations are then integrated into an iterative propagation module which can adaptively capture complex correlations among different nodes in both symbol/text embeddings within each encoding step until reaching convergence .

Inspired by ideas from health risk assessment, this paper presents a new perspective for flood risk evaluation and management. We aim to develop an innovative methodology that integrates the potential of deep learning (DL) and graph neural networks (GNNs), leveraging these technologies as tools in data analysis, modeling, and decision-making processes. Specifically, we investigate how to incorporate DL and GNNs into flood risk assessments, enhancing their capabilities with advanced analytical models, while also considering social implications and environmental impacts. Furthermore, our methodology aims to enhance collaboration between multiple stakeholders, including government agencies, local communities, and private organizations. Overall, our goal is to advance understanding of flood ri

Sub-\SI{50}{\gram} nano-drones are gaining momentum in the commercial drone market. The majority of these drones have a low-cost single-board computer (SBC) as their central processing unit (CPU), which can be programmed to operate autonomously or remotely controlled by a human operator. A common issue with these SBCs is that they lack proper security and privacy protection for the data stored on them, thereby compromising their integrity and confidentiality. To address this gap, we develop a novel software framework called \textit{NanoDroneSecure}, consisting of three main components: a secure firmware bootloader, a root-of-trust mechanism using a hardware-based attestation module, and an encrypted storage module. Our proposed framework ensures that all the data stored on the microcontrol

We introduce a novel methodology for identifying adversarial attacks on deepfake detectors using eXplainable Artificial Intelligence (xAI) tools. We leverage the concept of explainability as an attack vector and develop a series of experiments to demonstrate its effectiveness in evaluating the robustness of deepfakes detectors against adversarial attacks. Our experimental setup includes both textual and image-based attack methods, encompassing diverse scenarios such as low-probability and high-probability attacks. Notably, we find that the performance of deepfake detectors is significantly impacted by these types of attacks, underscoring their potential vulnerabilities in this domain. The results of our study provide valuable insights into the susceptibility of deepfake detection systems t

This work explores a closure task in comics, a medium where visual and textual elements are arranged in panels. Our model relies on a novel pre-trained language model that predicts the next panel given previous ones, including the previous visual content of the panel. We propose a two-stage learning approach that jointly trains our model using weak supervision obtained from annotated comic strips. In the first stage, we train a Transformer-based LM to extract visual representations by utilizing captions as weak supervisory signals. The second stage then adopts these learned visual features to improve the model's ability to generate panels in response to context. Experimental results show that our model outperforms the state-of-the-art methods in both automatic evaluation and human evaluati

The literature review is an indispensable step in the research process. It provides the benefit of contextualizing the topic of interest and enhancing the comprehension of existing work in the field. However, the current tools available for literature reviews are often limited to text-based search engines. This paper presents an innovative solution that leverages AI technology to enhance the efficiency of literature reviews by providing a more personalized experience tailored to individual preferences, interests, and needs. Our approach, which we refer to as "AI-Powered Literature Review Assistant," consists of several modules. Firstly, it collects user information through online questionnaires or interviews to determine their academic background, interests, and research areas. Secondly, i

We introduce and study a class of geometric codes called maximally extendable sheaf codes. These are linear codes constructed from coherent analytic sheaves on certain algebraic varieties, primarily projective spaces and their blow-ups. The key property is that they admit a monomial basis with respect to some finite affine cover. This property implies that the dual of such a code is also a sheaf code, constructed from the dual sheaf, and allows one to use Gr\"obner basis theory to compute the minimum distance of the code. These codes share many properties with toric codes, and in particular, like toric codes, they can be classified in terms of the geometry of the associated polytopes.

Support vector machine (SVM) has achieved many successes in machine learning, especially for a small number of features. However it is not suitable for high-dimensional data such as text documents since the sample size grows exponentially with respect to feature dimension and thus leads SVM into overfitting easily on training sets which causes bad generalization performance when applied onto test datasets [1]. To mitigate this problem we propose an approximate method that uses random projection techniques by projecting original large dimensional input space down towards low dimensions where most information about class labels can be preserved without losing too much accuracy compared against its full version counterpart algorithm while still maintaining fast computation times thanks largel

We propose a new technique for estimating mutual information between two random variables, $X$ and $Y$, with support on $\mathbb{R}^d$: we fit a normalizing flow to $p(X,Y)$, and use the resulting density function to estimate mutual information. Normalizing flows are invertible neural networks which can approximate complex density functions, and have recently been used for efficient generation of high-dimensional samples, as an alternative to Markov chain Monte Carlo methods. We show that mutual information can be conveniently expressed in terms of the log-likelihood ratio, and propose an estimator which is a function of the parameters of the normalizing flow. We minimize the expected value of the squared error between the true log-likelihood ratio and its estimate, using gradient descent, and show that the estimator converges to a minimum. We then fix the parameters of the normalizing flow at the minimum, and show that the resulting estimate of mutual information is consistent. Consistency is established by showing that the estimate converges to the true value as the number of training samples grows, at a rate of $O(n^{-1/2})$. We also derive a bound on the expected value of the squared error, which is of order $O(n^{-1})$ for a one hidden layer normalizing flow with $O(d)$ parameters.

Semi-supervised dialogue summarization (SSDS) leverages model-generated summaries to facilitate human annotation of high-quality training data. However, most SSDS methods still rely on manually crafted response selection prompts and heuristic rules for data augmentation, resulting in suboptimal summaries with low consistency across different data points. To alleviate the need for manual intervention, this paper introduces a novel knowledge-enhanced dialogue summarizer, which is guided by human experts' opinions during the training phase. Specifically, our method employs a knowledge-aware dialogue summarizer (KADS), which incorporates human expert knowledge as auxiliary information to improve the quality of generated summaries. Furthermore, we propose a new training strategy, named Self-Dis

Computing largest obstacle-free regions (LORs) in 2-D and 3-D spaces, i.e., the largest regions that can be inflated from a given region without colliding with obstacles, is a fundamental problem in robotics. It is NP-hard in general and even harder for convex obstacles. We present a fast, simple, and robust region inflation algorithm for computing approximately optimal LORs for convex regions with general (possibly non-convex) obstacles. Our algorithm iteratively inflates a region by a growing circle (in 2-D) or a growing sphere (in 3-D) until the region collides with an obstacle. It then deflates the region by a deflation circle (in 2-D) or a deflation sphere (in 3-D) until no collision is detected. The deflation step is new and crucial to the performance of our algorithm. In contrast to other state-of-the-art algorithms, our algorithm is simple enough to be easily implemented and robustified. We provide theoretical analysis of our algorithm and demonstrate its practical performance.

Game theory is a widely accepted framework for analyzing strategic interactions. However, in many applications, such as economics and engineering, the game is not given a priori, but rather it is learned from observations of players' behavior. This paper reviews the emerging literature that analyzes games in a learning framework. We focus on sequential games with complete information and review the main approaches that have been used to learn the game payoffs and the equilibria from data. In addition, we discuss the complexity issues and the statistical properties of the learning algorithms. Finally, we present some applications and we discuss future directions for empirical game-theoretic analysis.

Existing research often posits spurious features as "easier" to learn than core aspects of the data distribution. In this work, we demonstrate that certain spurious features can be more difficult to learn in the presence of noise. We showcase this behavior on three image datasets: CelebA, Fashion-MNIST, and CIFAR10. Our experiments indicate that while core class features can be learned under noisy conditions, certain spurious features become harder to learn. This observation is particularly significant when both core and spurious features are present simultaneously, with spurious features becoming harder to learn than the core feature. The phenomenon occurs across a range of models and training strategies, suggesting that it may be a fundamental property of neural networks. Overall, our fi

Changes in demand, various hydrological inputs, and environmental stressors are among the issues that must be addressed by water resource management. The development of robust forecasting models to predict future conditions can assist in managing these problems effectively. This study proposes a novel method for forecasting water level fluctuations using artificial neural networks (ANNs) with adaptive learning rates. Our approach combines gradient-based optimization methods with ANNs, allowing them to adapt their learning rate based on the loss function's recent history. We apply our method to four data sets from China's Three Gorges Dam: monthly, seasonal, annual, and 10-year time scales. Results show that our method outperforms traditional fixed-rate training strategies in terms of gener

Large Language Models (LLMs) such as GPT-4 have recently demonstrated impressive results across a wide range of tasks. There is increasing interest in understanding the internal representations that underlie their capabilities. A common tool for probing representations is linear classification, often implemented as a linear separator in the embedding space. We find that LLMs do not naturally exhibit linear separability in their embedding space, but can be easily modified to obtain linear separability with a few training steps. We further find that the modified models produce suboptimal results, suggesting that linear classifiers are not ideal for probing LLM representations. We recommend using non-linear probes instead, as they correlate better with model performance.

Visual entailment (VE) is a multimodal reasoning task consisting of image-sentence pairs. In this work, we propose a novel method based on GPT-4 to solve the VE problem with a unique hybrid paradigm that fuses vision and text in an innovative manner. Our model, dubbed "GV-GPT", incorporates a vision module based on GV-Vision (Han et al., 2023) to extract visual features from images and a text module equipped with GPT-4 to generate plausible sentence generation. Through careful design and integration, our approach facilitates the simultaneous utilization of both modalities for solving the VE challenge, leading to improved performance.

Utilizing multi-view inputs to synthesize novel-view images, Neural Radiance Field (NeRF) achieves state of the art results on reconstruction and rendering tasks. However it is not efficient for real time applications as its high memory requirement prevents large scale deployment in low resource environments such smartphones or IoT devices with limited hardware resources like small internal storage capacity (< 10 MB). To address this issue we propose a new compression method that significantly reduces NeRFs data footprint by compressing both parameters ($W$) - which are usually stored using float32 precision due their non linearity -- into integer values via quantization without sacrificing accuracy; while also reducing parameter size from $5\times$ larger than usual weights through optimi

The growing prevalence of hate speech online is a cause for concern, as it can lead to real-world harm and undermine the stability of our societies. To tackle this issue, there is a pressing need to develop effective detection systems that can identify hate speech in its various forms, including code-mixed Hinglish, which is a blend of the Hindi language and English, often used in online communities. However, annotated datasets for code-mixed Hinglish hate speech detection are scarce and costly to acquire, which poses a challenge for researchers and practitioners in this field. In this work, we investigate the feasibility of using weakly annotated data for code-mixed Hinglish hate speech detection. We propose a novel transfer learning approach that leverages large language models (LLMs), such as BLOOM, GPT-3, and ALBEF. Our method involves fine-tuning a LLM with weakly annotated data and then using it to generate pseudo-annotations for the original dataset. These pseudo-annotations are based on the LLM's predictions and are used to further train the model in a weakly supervised manner. We evaluate our approach on two code-mixed Hinglish datasets and two English datasets as a baseline. Our results demonstrate that the use of weakly annotated data can improve the performance of hate speech detection in code-mixed Hinglish. Specifically, we achieve an F1 score of 0.61 on the CMH dataset and 0.79 on the HSH dataset, which outperforms the current state-of-the-art models. We also show that our method is transferable to English datasets, although the performance is lower than for the code-mixed datasets.

Training high-quality deep learning models is a challenging task due to computational and technical requirements. A typical large-scale model requires billions of parameters, and each parameter needs to be updated by gradient descent during training. To scale up the training of these large-scale models, distributed parallel training is used as a scalable alternative to single GPU training. However, the current state-of-the-art in distributed training for deep learning is still far from optimal. We observe that in distributed training, each process only has access to a subset of data samples, which may lead to inconsistencies across processes. We propose a novel approach that leverages this inconsistency to develop a more efficient and scalable training algorithm. Our key insight is that th

Considering generating samples with high rewards, we focus on optimizing deep neural networks parameterized stochastically. We first propose a method to estimate the gradient of the loss function. Then we derive an explicit lower bound for the optimal batch size of each epoch and show that it is not necessarily equal to the batch size used in training. Further, we introduce an algorithm to compute this optimal batch size, named adaptive mini-batch optimization (AMO), which can reduce the variance while preserving the mean of the gradient estimates. Through extensive experiments on different datasets, we demonstrate that our proposed AMO outperforms state-of-the-art methods. Code is available at https://github.com/KaixingLi0219/AMO.git. Project page: https://kaixingli0219.github.io/AMO/.

Collaborative cybersecurity relies on organizations sharing information to boost security, but trust management is challenging because the parties are untrustworthy. Existing solutions either require a centralized entity or make assumptions about an entity's intentions that may be difficult to justify in practice. To address these limitations, we introduce Trapeze, a novel approach for collaborative cybersecurity based on a distributed mechanism called the "Trusted-to-trap" protocol. This protocol enables entities to securely share and use information with each other while maintaining their anonymity. The key insight behind Trapeze is that each entity can choose to act as a trapdoor for another entity, allowing them to contribute to the overall security of the network without disclosing se

Automatic Text Summarization (ATS), utilizing Natural Language Processing (NLP) techniques to extract an abstract of a text, has been the subject for numerous research studies. This paper explores several approaches in extracting summaries from texts and evaluates their efficiency using 180 scientific articles belonging mainly to four sub-domains: physics; computer science/engineering sciences); humanities & social Sciences(HSS)/medicine ;and economics /management . The results show that while many methods perform well with some domains ,they are not as efficient when dealing simultaneouslywith diverse content types suchas those encounteredin mixed domain corpora like our test set comprising all these subjects together or single ones focusing on just one theme out ofthe three mentioned abo

Denoising diffusion probabilistic models (DDPMs) are becoming the leading paradigm for generative modeling. However, most existing DDPS-based methods rely on expensive training and inference procedures in order to generate realistic high resolution images or videos at a reasonable speed by using large datasets such as ImageNet/LSVRC2017 dataset of 8643K image pairs which take several days up to weeks with an efficient model architecture like Swin Transformer based Diffusion Model [Huang et al., NeurIPS' 'J]. In this paper we propose Fast Video DENOIsing DiffusIoN method(FVDIN), that reduces both computational complexity while maintaining good performance compared against stateofart approaches including SwinTransformer baselines . Our FVIN achieves real time processing speeds without sacrif

The advent of large vision-language models (LVLMs) represents a noteworthy paradigm shift in natural language processing. Our goal is to build a system that enables us to directly query LVLMs for information. This paper introduces the concept of "Asking Large Vision-Language Models" (ALVM). To this end, we introduce a method called ALVM which consists of three steps: (1) constructing prompts and queries; (2) fine-tuning LVLMs with our customized pretraining tasks; and (3) evaluating the performance of LVLMs on downstream tasks. We also release a dataset called LLAMD (Large Language Model Accessed by Multi-document Retrieval Dataset), which contains 160,885 human-written questions that are answered using the LVLMs' responses and 49,700 relevant documents.

Given the large-scale multi-modal training of recent vision-based models and their generalization capabilities, we have witnessed a significant improvement in performance on challenging computer graphics tasks. However most previous work has focused mainly on image classification or segmentation which are limited to two input modalities: images (visual) only from real world scenes as well some text labels for task guidance; this is not sufficient when dealing with more complex 3D data such that found within virtual reality environments like gaming worlds where there may be many types/sources including audio recordings along side video feeds etc., all leading towards an even greater challenge than before - how do you train AI systems?

This paper presents Text-Neural Image Encoder (TNIE), a novel image compression method that incorporates textual descriptions to enhance compression efficiency. TNIE uses a neural encoder to convert images and textual descriptions into a shared latent space, where the distance between latent representations corresponds to the perceptual difference between the original images. This ensures that images with similar content but different appearances, as described by the text, are encoded into closely located latent representations. TNIE also employs a pixel-level encoder that generates a pixel-level representation of the image, independent of the text. The pixel-level encoder is trained to minimize the difference between downsampled original and reconstructed images, with the reconstruction guided by both the latent representation and the text embedding. This dual-encoding approach allows TNIE to achieve high pixel-level fidelity, as measured by objective metrics, while simultaneously ensuring perceptual fidelity, evaluated subjectively. We compare TNIE against recent image compression methods in terms of objective and subjective qualities. The results demonstrate that TNIE outperforms competing methods in both pixel-level and perceptual fidelity, achieving state-of-the-art performance. Code is available at https://github.com/ildoonet/TNIE.

The ongoing expansion of Neural Machine Translation (NMT) to low-resource languages is hampered by the lack of pre-trained models and annotated data. While automatic evaluation metrics and model explanations are helpful, they do not provide sufficient insight into the translation process. In this paper, we propose a framework to enhance NMT for low-resource languages through corpus development, human evaluation, and explainable AI architectures. We develop a parallel corpus for Serbo-Croatian to English translation and expand an existing monolingual corpus for English-Serbo-Croatian machine translation. We then compare the quality of machine translations of Serbo-Croatian to English produced by different NMT models using human evaluators and automatic evaluation metrics. Finally, we investigate the feasibility of explainable AI approaches for Serbo-Croatian to English NMT. We find that our neural models produce translations of higher quality than the statistical baseline. Human evaluators are unable to distinguish between the quality of translations produced by different neural models and prefer the translations of the explainable AI models due to their better linguistic quality.

Human trajectory data produced by daily mobile devices has proven its usefulness in various substantial fields such as healthcare and smart city management. However, current methods of representing human movements often lack the essential characteristics of a proper representation, i.e., they do not capture adequately the dynamic nature of human motion and are therefore limited in their utility for applications that require such knowledge. This paper introduces Trajectory2D, a novel methodology that integrates an accurate 3D representation of human movement with the use of a 2D projection layer, providing a comprehensive analysis of this approach for future research in this field. The results of our experiments show that Trajectory2D outperforms existing methods in terms of accuracy and ef

Self-supervised pre-training has been proved to be effective in learning transferable representations that benefit downstream tasks. However, current self-supervised models mainly focus on the global representation of a single image while neglecting the local representation, resulting in a limited capacity for fine-grained reasoning and segmentation tasks. In this paper, we present a new framework for self-supervised learning, termed Local Pretext Tasks (LPT), which is designed to learn local representations from images. LPT incorporates four self-supervised tasks: local contrastive learning (LocalCL), local semantic segmentation (LocalSeg), local relative position learning (LocalRPL), and local multi-scale visual context modeling (LocalVC). These tasks are specifically designed to promote

Location awareness is a critical issue in wireless sensor network applications. For more accurate location estimation, the Angle of Arrival (AoA) and Angle of Departure (AoD) are widely used in most indoor localization methods. AoAs are typically estimated using the time-of-arrival (TOA) measurements, which can be affected by the propagation path loss, fading, and multipath effect. As such, they may not provide reliable information about the locations of the sources. In this paper, we present an efficient AoD estimation method based on the Time Difference of Arrival (TDOA). Our method is based on the assumption that if there are $N$ sources with known locations and the source $i$, located at $\mathbf{R}_i$, sends a signal to the receiver $j$.

Recent progress in generative compression technology has significantly improved the perceptual quality of compressed data and enhanced downstream applications. However, this progress has been limited by the high computational cost associated with these models. In particular, large-scale generators such as GauGAN are computationally intensive due to their complex architectures and high resolutions, which make them unsuitable for resource-constrained devices. To address this limitation, we propose a novel algorithm, referred to as C-CNN, that leverages convolutional neural networks (CNN) to compress images while maintaining their perceptual quality. The proposed method is based on a hybrid architecture that combines a CNN encoder with an autoencoder decoder, effectively reducing the computat

Abrupt maneuvers by surrounding vehicles (SVs) can typically lead to safety concerns and affect the driving comfort of automated vehicles (AVs). In this paper, we consider the problem of planning a trajectory that is safe for an AV and comfortable for its passenger. Toward this end, we propose an integrated approach that combines the optimization of the ego's trajectory with a model predictive control (MPC) strategy to handle the vehicle dynamics. To ensure safety, we employ a robust MPC method with state constraints that considers the worst-case scenarios of SVs. For comfort, we design a novel cost function based on the passenger's subjective preferences regarding comfort measures such as jerk or acceleration rate in the longitudinal direction. We formulate the integrated optimal control

World models are representations of the real world that can be used to plan actions by simulating the effects of those actions in the model. They are often associated with reinforcement learning agents, which use the models to master memory tasks, such as delayed reward problems. In this paper, we review the literature on world models and their application to memory tasks. We focus on tasks that require the agent to remember information over long periods of time and solve problems by retrieving the information from memory. We show that world models can be used to solve memory tasks in a sample-efficient manner and discuss the relationship between world models and other approaches for solving memory tasks, such as those based on neural networks.

Adolescent peer relationships, essential for their development, are increasingly mediated by digital technologies. Yet little is known about how adolescents perceive the social benefits and risks of these virtual interactions in comparison to those offline or face-toface (F2f). This study examined differences between Facebook friends with whom young people spent time F $ 1$ both online $(N=65)$ versus offine only $(\mathrm{n}=74)$. A mixed methods approach was used combining a quantitative survey questionnaire followed up several weeks laterby qualitative semistructured interviewswith selected respondents from each group .

Edge Computing (EC) has gained significant traction in recent years, promising enhanced efficiency and latency for IoT applications. However, the current approach of deploying a large number of EC servers at the edge poses challenges such as increased power consumption and infrastructure costs. To address these issues, this paper proposes an innovative solution called the Distributed Multi-Server Caching System (DMCS), which leverages data placement optimization to achieve efficient and cost-effective edge computing. In DMCS, multiple EC servers are deployed to serve heterogeneous users' requests, with each server responsible for specific groups of users based on their QoS requirements. Through a comprehensive analysis of user traffic patterns and cache capacity, we propose a novel multi-s

3D patient body modeling is critical to the success of automated patient positioning for smart surgery. However, this task is challenging due to the limited data size and high intra-patient variability. In this paper, we propose a novel deep learning framework, namely, Adaptive-MAS (AMAS), to effectively adapt patient-specific models from limited available anatomical images to the target patient's 3D pose. Specifically, AMAS first uses a pretrained UNet model to generate the coarse body mesh and then utilizes two learnable morphable models to refine the body shape and pose. To enhance the generalization ability of our model, we further introduce the adaptive training strategy by generating diverse synthetic training samples using the same source data. Extensive experiments on two public da

The growing dependence on Large Language Models (LLMs) for finishing user instructions necessitates a more thorough understanding of their reasoning patterns. We propose the first systematic study of instruction-following capabilities, revealing that LLMs struggle with following instructions involving specific subtasks or multiple steps. These limitations are highlighted by the introduction of the Instruction-Following Rationality Assessment Tool (IFRAT), which captures LLM's ability to comprehend instructions and successfully complete them. Our empirical evaluation demonstrates that LLMs exhibit limited knowledge of the sequence in which different subtasks must be performed, with some models even requiring explicit instruction verification before starting each subtask. This paper introduc

This work explores a closure task in comics, a medium where visual and textual elements are closely intertwined. We propose to solve this problem using the transformer model with two-stage training strategy (PreT) as our baseline approach for comparison purposes: first we pretrain it on image captioning dataset before fine tuning its final weights at text only level via Masked Language Modeling objective; then finally reusing these new parameters during inference time while taking into account both words' positions within panel sequences together along side their respective images/visuals features extracted from source material itself -- yielding better accuracy rates than prior approaches thus far reported literature!

Adverse weather conditions often impair the quality of captured images, inevitably inducing cutting-edge autonomous driving systems (ADSs) to erroneously perceive surrounding objects. This study proposes a novel approach for weather-conditioned image denoising based on a novel training scheme. Specifically, we propose an adversarial training method by utilizing a customized adversary that generates denoised images with a different noise level. The proposed methodology is evaluated using various publicly available datasets and benchmarks, demonstrating superior performance compared to current state-of-the-art approaches. Moreover, our model shows robustness under diverse scenarios with varying degrees of noise levels and weather conditions. Through ablation studies, we highlight the efficac

Registration of point clouds collected from a pair of distant vehicles provides a comprehensive and accurate 3D view of the environment. However, existing methods either require multiple ground control points (GCPs) or suffer from low registration accuracy and poor generalizability. In this work, we address the challenge of registering large-scale point clouds by developing an effective learning method that can overcome the aforementioned limitations. Specifically, our approach comprises two stages: (1) We introduce a novel Multi-view Feature Fusion (MFF) module to learn robust global features for point cloud registration by leveraging multi-view information in a single image. (2) To effectively utilize this learned feature for large-scale point cloud registration, we propose a Dynamic Att

Counterfactual explanations (CEs) enhance the interpretability of machine learning models by explaining predictions in terms that are more intuitive and understandable. However, most existing CE approaches focus on individual examples rather than providing a global view for multiple instances or groups to facilitate explanation-based decision making at an organization level. In this paper we propose Group CounterFactuAl Explanations(GCFAs), which aims towards generating group CEs via counterpositive training with batch normalization regularization techniques across large data sets containing sensitive features like gender/ethnicity as well other nonsensitive ones such age range etc., thereby enabling organizations develop better policies based upon their collective understanding around pot

Training for multi-agent reinforcement learning(MARL) is a time-consuming process that requires thousands of episodes to achieve the optimal performance. However, due to the lack of sufficient training data, many MART models are stuck in local optima, resulting in poor generalization ability. This paper proposes a novel offline meta-learning algorithm with imitation learning (OLMIL), which utilizes the experience of previous agents to train new ones and enhance their generalization capabilities. The proposed algorithm employs a teacher model trained using imitation learning, and it can be applied without modifying the reward function or the reward distribution. We design an optimization strategy for the OLMIL algorithm to balance exploration and exploitation during training. Our experiment

In production rendering systems, caustics are typically rendered via photon mapping and gathering, a process that can be time-consuming. This paper introduces a novel approach to caustic rendering by using data from previously rendered image regions for the current scene. We use this "caustic history" to construct a lookup table (LUT) based on previous caustic images. The LUT contains samples of known caustic values, which can then be used to quickly estimate future caustic values. By leveraging our technique, we demonstrate an 85% reduction in caustic rendering time over traditional methods. Additionally, we introduce a novel image-based light transport method for caustics, which allows us to achieve a substantial improvement in quality compared to existing techniques. Finally, we compare

We present a solution to the Remote Pairwise Comparison (RPM) and the Bongard-logo problems by using a novel distribution mechanism for concepts: the Concept Distribution Agent (CDA). The CDA is a trainable component that learns to distribute concepts among a set of targets, minimizing a comparison cost. The comparison cost is based on the Earth Mover's Distance (EMD) between concept distributions, as learned by the CDA. We show that the CDA can solve the RPM and Bongard-logo problems, by distributing the appropriate concepts on the basis of training samples. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first time that the RPM problem is solved by a purely distribution-based method.

Most e-commerce platforms employ a hierarchical product classification system to organize their catalogues. This not only helps in presenting a structured view of the products to the customers but also aids in backend operations such as search and recommendation. Nevertheless, populating and maintaining such classification systems is a tedious task, often requiring significant human supervision. To address this challenge, many researchers have proposed automatic approaches for product categorization. However, most existing techniques are limited to a single level of categorization, or they require manually labeled data at each level. In this paper, we propose a novel multi-level product category prediction model that can simultaneously predict categories at multiple levels of an e-commerce hierarchy, from the root node all the way to the leaf nodes. We evaluate our model on two real-world datasets from Amazon and Flipkart, and we find that it outperforms existing techniques, requiring only a fraction of the training data.

Autonomous off-road navigation requires an accurate semantic understanding of the environment, often converted into a high resolution map. While maps can be created by manual efforts and ground truth data collection in small scales or with expensive sensors like lidar for larger areas (e..g., urban settings), this process is not feasible for large scale deployments that require consistent updates as new infrastructure evolves over time due to seasonal changes such winter snowfall accumulation patterns on roadsides/sidewalks etc.) To address these challenges we propose using satellite imagery combined wtih publicly available open source datasets(suchas OSM)to generate autonomous vehicle ready roadmaps automatically without any human intervention whatsoever! Our approach leverages deep learn

The array is a data structure used in a wide range of programs. Its compact storage and constant time access to any element make it highly efficient for processing large amounts of data. In this article, we will look at the most common algorithms that use arrays: - Selection Sort; - Insertion Sort; - Merge Sort; - Quick Sort; - Binary Search; - Heap Sort; - Counting Sort; - Radix Sort. ## 1. Selection Sort Selection sort works as follows: - the smallest element in the unsorted part of the array is selected; - it is swapped with the first element in the unsorted area; - then, the next smallest element in the unsorted portion is chosen, swapping it with the second element in the unsorted area; - repeating this process until all elements are sorted.

Text documents, including programs, typically have human-readable semantic structure. Historically, programmatically extracting this structure has required the use of a domain-specific language (DSL), which can be cumbersome and inelegant to write. In this paper we introduce SemiStruct, a framework for semi-structured text editing that enables users to define their own structured representations for any type of text document, then edit the document based on those structures using a novel visualization. SemiStruct is implemented as a VS Code extension and supports both custom user structures and built-in support for HTML and JSON. We present examples of how SemiStruct may be used by software developers and web designers to improve their productivity and creativity when working with complex

The proliferation of software vulnerabilities poses a significant challenge for security databases and analyst communities. Software vulnerability discovery methods have advanced significantly in recent years, including the use automated code analysis tools to detect bugs early during development cycles or by extracting knowledge from existing database entries through machine learning algorithms [1]. While these approaches are useful as an extensional means (i. e., based on known instances), they lack explanatory power since it is not clear why certain features lead towards bug-proneness while others do so less frequently; this leaves little room understanding how programmers might improve their coding practices without relying entirely upon trial & error experimentation with new technique

In reasoning tasks, even a minor error can cascade into inaccurate results, leading to erroneous conclusions. For example, when a model answers a question with the wrong answer, the remaining steps of the reasoning process may lead to further incorrect predictions. The inherent sequential nature of the reasoning task makes it challenging for the model to correct itself after making an early mistake. To address this issue, we introduce the Correct-Yourself (CY) technique, which enables models to self-correct by retracing their reasoning steps and identifying where they went wrong. We show that, by applying CY at each step during the reasoning process, our method significantly improves accuracy on several benchmarks, including CommonSenseQA, Winogrande, and ReCCoR. Specifically, our approach

Federated learning (FL) is a privacy-preserving distributed management framework based on collaborative model training of distributed devices in edge networks. However, recent studies have shown that FL is vulnerable to adversarial examples (AEs), leading to a significant drop in its performance. Meanwhile, the non-independent and identically distributed (non-IID) challenge of data distribution between edge devices can further degrade the performance of models. Consequently, both AEs and non-IID pose challenges to deploying robust learning models at the edge. In this work, we adopt the adversarial training (AT) framework to improve the robustness of FL models against adversarial example (AE) attacks, which can be termed as federated adversarial training (FAT).

We present a novel projection mapping system that replaces room lights with heterogeneous projectors to produce vivid large-scale images on arbitrary surfaces. Our system projects multiple colors at sub-pixel resolution and uses environmental lighting as the primary light source. We develop a new projection mapping technique that utilizes the human visual system's (HVS) perception of brightness, saturation, and spatial frequency. Our method can produce accurate images using non-uniform brightness projectors that are 1.4 to 2.2 times dimmer than conventional projection mapping with uniform brightness projectors. We also show that using heterogeneous projectors enables us to control the image's saturation and spatial frequency, which are typically difficult to control in projection mapping. Finally, we demonstrate our system's effectiveness through physical experiments and compare it to conventional projection mapping.

Graph neural networks (GNNs) have become increasingly popular in modeling graph-structured data. However, training GNN models requires collecting a large amount of labeled graphs from the real world which is costly and time consuming; as such it would be more efficient to leverage unlabeled datasets for semi/unsupervised learning tasks instead by transferring knowledge across different domains or modalities via domain adaptation techniques like GAN inversion . In this work we propose an approach called D2GAN that enables unsupervised representation transfer between two types: 1.) heterogeneous representations where features come directly off raw input samples without any preprocessing steps taken beforehand e..g., images vs text files etc.

Generative models have shown strong generation ability while efficient likelihood estimation is less explored. Energy-based generative model (EBGM) has the potential to directly solve this issue, but it needs a sophisticated sampler for better performance and suffers from difficulty in tuning hyperparameters such as temperature $\tau$. In practice we usually use annealed Gibbs sampling which requires $O(\log \frac{1}{\epsilon})$ steps or more complex samplers like Langevin dynamics with high computational cost especially when dealing large data sets of millions points per dataset instance even if using GPU acceleration methods available today due limited memory constraints on modern GPUs . To address these challenges ,we present an alternative approach based upon optimization algorithms in

While electronic games have evolved into a mature and diverse form of entertainment media, they still lack the level of interactivity and intelligence found in traditional narrative media such as movies and books. An important ingredient to achieve more engaging and interactive games is artificial intelligence, which has the potential to enable complex behaviors in non-player characters (NPCs) and thus improve the overall gameplay experience. In this paper, we explore the use of deep reinforcement learning (DRL) to control NPCs in Red Dead Redemption II (RDR2), a popular open-world action game. Specifically, we investigate the effectiveness of DRL-based control in three different scenarios, including horseback riding, gunfighting, and general exploration. As control via DRL requires an environment that can interface with the game and evaluate the actions of the NPCs, we also present a novel multimodal environment for RDR2 that can render game frames, process natural language instructions, and perform evaluations. Our experimental results demonstrate that our DRL-based control method is effective in all three scenarios, outperforming existing methods where applicable. In addition, our experimental analysis and user study reveal that DRL-based control can bring more realism and intelligence to NPCs, improve the gaming experience, and thus has the potential to be widely used in future game development. As the first study to successfully apply DRL for general computer control in a complex open-world game, our work opens the door for more sophisticated agent-based NPCs in the future.

Abstract reasoning is one of the essential cognitive skills that humans possess, enabling them to solve problems and make decisions based on limited information. Artificial intelligence (AI) systems, however, struggle with abstract reasoning, as they often require explicit instructions and large amounts of data to perform well.
