We show that our method outperforms a recent planner based on graph search and Monte Carlo simulation for the same problem formulation, while also being more computationally efficient. We further show that our method is able to explore stepping stones effectively, by deploying a legged robot in a confined space with obstacles.

Machine Translation is a mature technology for many high-resource language pairs. However in the context of low-resource languages, there is a paucity of parallel data datasets available for developing translation models. Furthermore, the development of datasets for low-resource languages often focuses on simply creating the largest possible dataset for generic translation. The benefits and development of smaller in-domain datasets can easily be overlooked. To assess the merits of using in-domain data, a dataset for the specific domain of health was developed for the low-resource English to Irish language pair. Our study outlines the process used in developing the corpus and empirically demonstrates the benefits of using an in-domain dataset for the health domain.

Dynamic graphs, featuring continuously updated vertices and edges, have grown in importance for numerous real-world applications. We investigate the problem of identifying the most influential nodes in dynamic networks by leveraging a single snapshot of the graph. Conventional methods utilize static node features to compute node influence scores, which may lead to suboptimal performance when the graph dynamics are significant. To overcome this limitation, we propose to learn a sequence of vertex representations from successive snapshots of the network, using an end-to-end differentiable model. We further introduce a novel influence score update strategy that combines both node feature information and sequence-based representations, enhancing its accuracy. Experimental results on three data

Typologically diverse benchmarks are increasingly created to track the progress achieved in multilingual NLP. While these benchmarks have become an integral part of the research ecosystem, they remain challenging for languages with limited data availability and resource-intensive processing requirements. In this work, we propose a new type of multilingual benchmark - `zero-shot' - that uses pretrained models to enable rapid adaptation from large-scale datasets without requiring any additional training data. We provide extensive empirical evidence indicating that zero-shot benchmarks can lead to significant improvements over traditional benchmarks across different language pairs and tasks. Moreover, we show that the effectiveness of zero-shot benchmarks is not tied to the size of the pretra

Robot-assisted feeding can greatly enhance the lives of those with mobility limitations. This paper describes a novel robotic system for feeding patients in hospitals and nursing homes, which uses an active suction cup to grasp objects by exploiting surface tension effects on liquid menisci at contact points between object surfaces [1]. The robot is equipped as well with force sensing capabilities that allow it not only to detect when food has been picked up but also to assess its texture characteristics (e., hardness) using data from tactile sensors placed underneath each pair or three fingers during grasping tasks involving different types/sizes thereof respectively; this provides additional information about how easily someone could swallow their meal without choking once theyve begun e

The recent developments in neural fields have brought phenomenal capabilities to the field of shape generation, but they still lack a solid understanding of geometry. In this work we introduce an efficient learning framework for generating shapes with precise geometric semantics: (i) exact boundary representation and arbitrary-order implicit surface; (ii)~geometric properties such as tangency at sharp corners or planar regions on smooth surfaces by enforcing smoothness constraints up until 4th order derivatives along all directions simultaneously during training phase without sacrificing speed nor accuracy compared stateofart methods which only focus single direction optimization instead multidirectional ones like ours does .

Active learning seeks to reduce the amount of data required to fit the parameters of a model, thus forming an important class of techniques in modern machine learning. However, existing methods typically rely on the strong assumption that the underlying data is fully labeled. In many real-world scenarios, such as healthcare, obtaining labels is costly and often only a partial labeling is available. In this work, we develop an active learning framework when only partial labels are given. Our approach, termed SUPClust, identifies unlabeled data points that are either boundary points or have many neighboring boundary points as highly informative and thus prioritizes them for labeling. We validate our method across several synthetic and real-world datasets, demonstrating that SUPClust outperforms existing techniques under various conditions. Notably, SUPClust exhibits robust performance across different model architectures, making it a versatile solution for active learning in partially labeled datasets.

The advent of the post-human era has blurred the boundary between the body and artifacts. It is now possible to design a machine that can replicate various human functions, such as perception or movement with high precision; however designing machines capable in emulating complex behaviors requires a much deeper understanding on how humans generate these actions through coordination among different organs like skin sensors (tactile receptors), muscles etc., which are currently not well understood by neuroscience researchers due mainly because most studies focus only one aspect at time instead looking comprehensively across multiple levels simultaneously In this dissertation I aim address some key questions related specifically but more generally about our approach towards creating intelli

This paper explores the cutting-edge Large Language Model with analytical reasoning on sports. Our work aims to understand the intricate interplay between statistics, analytics, and human intuition in sports through the lens of LLMs' analytical reasoning capabilities. With the introduction of state-of-the-art LLMs such as ChatGPT and Bing Chat, the field of sports analytics has experienced significant advancements. The ability of LLMs to provide quick and accurate answers to complex queries has revolutionized sports analytics, enabling real-time analysis and strategic planning. However, the potential for misinterpretation due to biases or inaccuracies requires careful consideration. In this study, we explore the effectiveness of LLMs in understanding sports analytics and their strengths an

Reinforcement learning (RL) and causal modelling naturally complement each other. The goal of RL is to learn an optimal policy, whereas the aim in causality research has been developing methods for estimating treatment effects or counterfactual quantities from observational data that suffer bias due confounding factors which are often hard-toobserve/unobserved variables within a given dataset [1]. In this talk I will discuss how we can combine these two fields by leveraging techniques developed during my PhD such as causal inference with latent confounders using potential outcomes models[2], generalised cross validation via deep neural networks when dealing large datasets containing millions observations,[3] along some new ideas on integrating Bayesian optimisation into reinforcement learn

Eco-driving, i.e., the practice of driving in an environmentally friendly manner, can help reduce traffic congestion and improve air quality. Cooperative eco-driving, in particular, involves a group of drivers who collectively plan their trips in an eco-friendly way while considering the departures and destinations of other drivers. We develop a deep reinforcement learning approach for cooperative eco-driving that goes beyond the state-of-the-art in three key ways. First, we introduce a novel reward function that more accurately reflects the benefits of cooperative eco-driving. Second, we propose a multi-agent multi-residual task learning approach that enables agents to learn cooperative policies even when they have incomplete information about the other agents. Third, we design an attention-based mechanism that allows agents to learn the importance of other agents in making cooperative decisions. We conduct experiments using three real-world road networks of different sizes and densities. The results show that our approach significantly outperforms several state-of-the-art baselines in terms of environmental benefits, convergence speed, and robustness. Our approach is able to reduce the total amount of fuel consumption by up to 30% compared to the best baseline.

The need to reduce the dimensionality of analog signals, such as those acquired by radio telescopes, is a common requirement in many signal processing applications. Such signals are often high dimensional, contain only a few frequency components, and are observed with additive white Gaussian noise. In this paper, we propose a deep-learned compression (DLC) framework that exploits these characteristics to perform signal classification. Specifically, we design a compression operator using a deep neural network (DNN) that maps the signal to a lower-dimensional space, where the modified discrete Fourier transform (DFT) of the compressed signal has a sparse representation. We then use another DNN to classify the signal based on its compressed modified DFT. We show that the proposed DLC framework outperforms other state-of-the-art methods for signal classification in terms of accuracy, especially in the presence of noise.

Three-dimensional point cloud anomaly detection that aims to detect anomaly data points from a training set of point clouds has gained increasing research attention. The main challenge is the limited availability of ground truths for benchmarking, which makes it challenging to evaluate the performance of methods and compare them with each other. To address this issue, we introduce two publicly available datasets: PCA-AD and PointCloud-Anomaly-Benchmark (PCAB), where we provide both synthetic and real 3D point cloud datasets annotated by domain experts. In addition, we propose a novel method based on a multi-scale anomaly detector called PCDADET. Our method shows state-of-the-art performance in three datasets across different categories of anomalies. This dataset and our proposed method can

This paper presents an extensive empirical study on the integration of dimensionality reduction techniques with advanced unsupervised clustering. We examine the performance of seven well-known dimensionality reduction methods under a wide range of configurations, including different input data types and sampling schemes. Our findings shed light on the potential pitfalls in combining these methods with clustering algorithms, underscoring the importance of considering them as complementary rather than competitive tools. The findings are validated using diverse datasets across various domains, further substantiating our observations. In addition to providing insight into the best practices for integrating dimensionality reduction with clustering, this paper offers valuable guidance for resear

Code understanding and generation have fast become some of the most popular applications of language models (LMs). However, current LLM architectures struggle to generate code that is both valid/correct and relevant. Validness can be understood as correctness with respect a predefined set rules such those given by compilers or linting tools while relevance refers generally refering back to user's intentions in context which may involve reasoning about semantics beyond syntax alone - this requires taking into account additional information sources like documentation comments describing what each function does etc., if available for example cases where there isn’t enough evidence present within source files themselves but still need accurate results nonetheless!

Recently, model merging techniques have surfaced as a solution to combine multiple single-talent models into one powerful multi-talented model. We study the impact of merging on the training process and find that merging does not always lead to a more efficient learning procedure. Specifically, we show that merging can result in an increased number of iterations compared to simply training each model individually. This finding holds true even when merging is performed early during training. Moreover, we observe that merging has a significant impact on the model's convergence behavior: while merging might improve performance, it may also introduce instabilities or non-monotonicity in loss curves, making it challenging for optimization algorithms to converge optimally. Our empirical results

In this paper, we study how well human speech can automatically be filtered when this overlaps with the background noise. We focus on a scenario in which only one speaker is present and the background noise is stationary. This type of scenario is relevant for applications such as automated speech recognition, where the background noise may change, but the presence of one or more speakers remains constant. We conduct an empirical study to investigate whether humans are able to effectively separate speech from background noise if they have access to both modalities. We found that people are capable of doing so, but their ability to do so depends heavily on the amount of overlap between speech and background noise. As a consequence, when there is large overlap (>40%), they tend to rely mainly

Modern consumer devices must execute multimedia applications that exhibit high resource utilization. In order to efficiently execute these workloads, system designers have adopted the model of scheduling multiple tasks per core with limited context switching capabilities. We show that this model is unsatisfactory in terms of performance and energy consumption for low-latency workloads. To address these issues, we present a hardware accelerator capable of executing single-threaded media applications at significantly higher throughput than traditional cores. Additionally, our design can achieve higher throughput with lower energy use compared to state-of-the-art CPUs. Finally, we evaluate our design using an FPGA implementation showing a 14x increase in performance while consuming only 20% m

Large-scale high-resolution (HR) land-cover mapping is a vital task to support environmental monitoring and urban planning. In the past, supervised learning has dominated this field. However, due to the lack of ground truth data or labeling cost for training in certain regions, unsupervised learning methods are often employed as an alternative solution. Recently, deep learning with Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) has shown promising results in unsupervised learning tasks. To further improve accuracy, various self-learning strategies such as multi-scale feature fusion, multi-modality integration, and self-training have been proposed. Nonetheless, the current state-of-the-art methods still face challenges such as low generalizability across different domains, poor interpretability, and

The capabilities of large language models (LLMs) have been progressing at a breathttaking pace, and so far the trend has been very positive. In this talk I'll explore what happens when we stop optimizing LLMS for text-only inputs: How do they perform on nontext data? What can LLM scaling tell us about their performance across modalities like images or audio as well?-I will present some initial results that show how our approach to multimodal AI is changing in response.-This research was done with my collaborator Yaroslav≫ Bulatov from FacebookAI Research In recent years there’s much excitement around Generative Adversarial Networks (“GAN”) which can be used both towards art generation but also image manipulation by inserting objects into an existing picture - think replacing people faces e

Graph neural networks (GNNs) have become increasingly popular in modeling graph-structured data. However, these models often suffer from the curse of dimensionality due to their complex parameterization and computation costs. The purpose of this paper is to propose a novel framework for graph learning called Layerwise Factorization (LF) that can reduce the complexity of GNNs by up to $2^{15}$ without compromising the quality of model predictions. This technique introduces an innovative idea known as "factorization," which allows us to break down the problem into simpler tasks. By leveraging this approach, we are able to efficiently solve problems with large graphs while avoiding the need for more expensive computations. Our method outperforms the state-of-the-art methods on several benchma

Programming assistants have reshaped the experience of programming into one where programmers spend less time debugging and more on writing code. In this talk, we will discuss how a programmer can leverage AI to help write their own tests with examples from our ongoing work in creating automated test generation tools for Python web applications using Pytest-OpenAPI (https://github/pytestspecs). We will also explore some challenges that arise when trying out new technologies like OpenAI’S GPT 3 model or GitHub Copilot as well opportunities ahead if these approaches are adopted widely by developers around world! Finally ,we'll touch upon what needs done next so people donot face similar difficulties again down line - making sure everyone has access too much needed knowledge without having ba

Recent advances in Neural Architecture Search (NAS) have led to the development of efficient deep neural networks. However, existing NAS methods still require a considerable amount of time and resources to find new architectures. In addition, the process of training a large number of candidate models on expensive hardware is becoming a bottleneck in NAS research. To address these challenges, this paper proposes SWAP-NAS, a novel ultra-fast NAS method that employs precomputed Sample-Wise Activation Patterns (SWAPs) to evaluate candidate models without the need for training. SWAPs are binary feature maps that indicate the activation status of each neuron for a given input sample. They can be precomputed and stored for each candidate model, allowing for instantaneous architecture evaluation. Our experimental results demonstrate that SWAP-NAS can achieve a similar performance to traditional NAS methods that rely on training. Furthermore, SWAP-NAS enables the discovery of architectures with better performance and efficiency, as it reduces the search space by eliminating the need for training. The proposed method shows a 10x to 100x speedup compared to conventional NAS approaches, making it feasible to explore larger search spaces and potentially discover more efficient architectures.

Small-scale robots hold great potential for targeted cargo delivery in minimally-inv asive environments, but their physical size and limited computational resources make them susceptible to unpredictable environmental conditions. Large-scale robots are more capable of handling these challenges, but they are not suitable for applications in confined spaces due to their sheer size. To address this issue, we propose a novel approach that leverages the capabilities of small-scale robots while also enabling seamless integration with large-scale robots. Our approach involves dividing the cargo delivery task into multiple subtasks, each of which can be performed by a different robot. By aggregating the results from these subtasks, our system ensures successful completion of the entire task while

We consider a competitive facility location problem where two firms, leader and follower, sequentially open facilities to maximize their market shares. Customers are strategic and choose the facility that maximizes their utility, which is the sum of the service quality and the travel time to the facility. We assume that the service quality is firm-specific and the travel time is distance-specific. Customers are distributed according to a random utility model, and the leader opens its facilities before the follower. We show that the follower has a competitive advantage and can attract more customers than the leader, regardless of the leader's facilities locations. We prove that the follower can even achieve a positive market share when its service quality is arbitrarily worse than the leader's. We develop an integer programming formulation for the problem and provide bounds and heuristics to solve it. We also characterize a class of random utility functions for which the problem can be solved efficiently.

Increasing the accuracy of instance segmentation methods is often done at the expense of speed. In this work, we introduce a novel approach to address this trade-off by leveraging a combination of 2D and 3D data sources. Specifically, we propose a hybrid architecture that fuses the strengths of 2D convolutional networks and point cloud processing. Our method integrates 3D data sources into a pre-trained 2D network, thereby enhancing its capacity to capture 3D spatial information while maintaining the efficiency of 2D feature extraction. This fusion enables our model to achieve superior performance in object detection tasks compared to state-of-the-art methods. Furthermore, we demonstrate that the proposed hybrid architecture can be seamlessly integrated into existing frameworks for instanc

Conventional multi-hop fact verification models are prone to rely on spurious connections that may lack sufficient evidence. Therefore, in this paper we introduce a novel approach for improving the robustness of fact checking under knowledge graph (KG)-based settings by leveraging graph embeddings and graph convolutional networks. Specifically, we develop a two-stage framework where the first stage involves generating a set of representative graphs through graph autoencoders trained with multiple views of KG embeddings. In the second stage, these representative graphs are used as inputs to a GNN based fact verifier to improve its robustness. We evaluate our approach across three popular datasets: FB15k-237, Freebase Factoids, and WikiFacts, and showcase superior performance compared to the

Phishing attacks have inflicted substantial losses on individuals and businesses alike, necessitating the development of robust and efficient automated phishing detection approaches. Reference-based phishing detectors (RBPDs), which compare the logos on a target webpage to a known set of logos, have emerged as the state-of-the-art approach. However, a major limitation of existing RBPDs is that they rely on a manually constructed brand knowledge base, making it infeasible to scale to a large number of brands, which results in false negative errors due to the insufficient brand coverage of the knowledge base. To address this issue, we propose an automated knowledge collection pipeline, using which we collect and release a large-scale multimodal brand knowledge base, KnowPhish, containing 20k

In this paper, we study nonparametric estimation of instrumental variable (IV) regress ion parameters when the outcome is not observed for a subset of treated observations. We develop asymp totic normality and convergence results under mild assumptions on sample size that allow us t o exploit sparse sampling efficiently in finite samples through bootstrap methods or Bayesian inference using Markov chain Monte Carlo samplers; these procedures are shown numerically by simulations with synthetic data sets as well-performing alternatives compared wit h classical estimators based only on fully sampled units where feasible but also ableto handle incompletely measured cases via imputation techniques like kNN regression treesor predictive mean matching imputations from Gaussian process priors over

X-Y-Z (XL-MIMO) arrays are a promising technology for future wireless networks, as they enable a massive number of access points (APs) to establish high-rate wireless links with multiple users in a spatially non-stationary fashion. However, this spatial non-stationary channel imposes significant challenges on channel estimation, especially when the number of APs is very large. To address this challenge, we propose a novel three-layer generalized approximate message passing (GAMP) method, which can efficiently estimate the spatially non-stationary channel coefficients between each pair of APs and users. To this end, we first derive a novel GAMP algorithm to estimate the channel coefficients from each AP to all users, where the channel estimation problem is treated as a low-rank matrix completion task. Then, we develop a two-layer GAMP algorithm to jointly estimate the channel coefficients between all APs and users, where the channel estimation problem is viewed as a non-convex feasibility problem. Finally, we propose a one-layer GAMP algorithm to estimate the spatially non-stationary channel coefficients between all APs and users, where the channel estimation problem is cast as a non-linear least squares problem. Simulation results demonstrate that the proposed three-layer GAMP algorithm can achieve a faster convergence rate and a better steady-state accuracy than the state-of-the-art benchmarks.

The body movements accompanying speech aid speakers in expressing their ideas. Co-speech gestures are also crucial for comprehending spoken language, especially when the linguistic content is ambiguous or difficult to understand from acoustic cues alone (e.,g.,, visual information can help listeners interpret an ironic message). Previous studies have shown that listeners exploit co‑gesture as well during listening: specifically by using it to detect and track a speaker’s intentions at early stages of comprehension – even before they have fully understood what was said orally [1]. In this study we asked whether gesture could be used similarly during reading - i. e ., as people read text containing words with multiple meanings/interpretations (“polysemic” terms), do readers use concu

Large language models (LLMs) like GPT-4 have demonstrated impressive results across many tasks, including mathematical reasoning. However, LLMs still struggle with complex mathematical problem-solving, often failing to find counterexamples or missing subtle errors in student work. In this paper, we propose instruction tuning with scale-invariant prompts (Scale-Invariant Instruction Tuning, SIT) to improve LLMs' performance on mathematical reasoning. We introduce MathScale, a new LLM that improves upon GPT-4's mathematical reasoning capabilities by generating scale-invariant prompts from mathematical problems and instructions. We evaluate MathScale alongside GPT-4 and several other baselines on MMLU, a mathematical reasoning dataset, and find that MathScale outperforms all baselines consistently. We show that scaling instruction tuning is a promising direction for improving LLMs' mathematical reasoning abilities.

Pessimistic Q-learning (PQL) has been demonstrated to be effective in solving Markov decision processes with adversarial perturbations. To handle adversarial perturbations, PQL maintains an optimistic Q-value for each state-action pair and an additional pessimistic Q-value for each state. In this paper, we find that the pessimistic Q-value can be further improved by leveraging the belief over the adversarial perturbations. To this end, we propose belief-enriched PQL (B-PQL), which computes the pessimistic Q-value as the expected Q-value under the worst-case belief. We show that B-PQL is able to learn optimal policies with linear function approximation under adversarial state perturbations in a tabular environment. In particular, we prove that B-PQL converges to the optimal policy with a sublinear regret bound. Finally, we demonstrate the performance of B-PQL on several adversarial robustness tasks using linear function approximation. B-PQL achieves superior performance compared with existing methods.

The increasing interest in Large Language Models (LLMs) within the telecommunications sector has led to an increase in their usage. This paper provides a comprehensive overview of LLM adoption, highlighting its impact on industry. We reviewed various aspects of LLMs including the current state-of-the-art, application domains, and potential areas for improvement. Additionally, we investigated the future direction of this technology by exploring how it will evolve. Our analysis concludes that while there are significant opportunities for the use of LLMs, further research is needed to ensure responsible and ethical deployment. Moreover, increased collaboration between different stakeholders across industries is crucial to maximize the benefits of LLMs. Overall, our study aims to provide insig

We present a simple, unsupervised method for distant point cloud registration. Our method extends correspondences progressively by adding points whose addition cannot be avoided by a volumetric hashing based correspondence method. Our method does not require the knowledge of the camera motion or the 3D structures of the scenes. Experiments show that our method works well in the case of static scenes as well as in the case of moving scenes with structure. Our method is able to recover the camera motion from densely sampled point clouds without the need of differentiable hashing. We compare the computational efficiency and the registration accuracy of our method with state-of-the-art techniques.

In recent advancements in Conversational Large Language Models (LLMs), a new trend of pre-training models on large datasets has emerged. While these methods have shown promising results, they also pose significant challenges for fine tuning and deployment due to the need to process vast amounts data at inference time during each dialogue turn with an LLM server backend running continuously 24/7 behind every chatbot instance deployed worldwide today by commercial companies such as Microsoft or Google .

The focus of the software industry on sustainability is increasing, and so is the demand for sustainable software developers. However, only a handful of studies empirically investigate how to develop sustainable software and none, to our knowledge, have explored whether large language models (LLMs) can assist in generating sustainable code. To this end, we develop a novel prompt engineering approach that LLMs can use to generate sustainable code snippets. We fine-tune state-of-the-art LLMs, namely, GPT-4, GPT-4 Turbo, GPT-3.5, and GPT-3, on a dataset of sustainable code snippets. We then compare the performance of these LLMs against a baseline LLM (i.e., GPT-3) on a coding benchmark that consists of 72 coding problems. We find that LLMs prompt engineered with our approach generate more sustainable code snippets than the baseline LLM. Our results also show that our approach is transferable to LLMs with different architectures and pre-training stages. As an important step towards sustainable software development, our study shows that LLMs can be used to generate sustainable code snippets.

Visual imitation learning has achieved impressive progress in learning unimanual manipulation tasks from a small number of demonstrations. However, when facing multiple objects with complex interactions, it often fails to learn effective policies due to the lack of rich demonstrations and high-dimensional observations. To address this challenge, we propose a novel multi-modal demonstration-based imitation learning framework, called MultiDIL, which adopts both language and visual information to facilitate the learning process. Specifically, our approach first utilizes an object detector and a semantic parser to extract linguistic features from visual-language demonstrations, then leverages a hierarchical policy network to learn a sequence of object-level control policies. In addition, a cro

Tabular data, as a crucial form of data representation, exists in diverse formats on the Web. In order to perform useful analysis and utilization of these data sources, the integration of tabular data and its transformation into other forms of data becomes necessary. However, most existing approaches are not able to automatically transform tabular data into a usable format. In this paper, we propose an automatic transformation approach for tabular data that is capable of extracting and transforming tabular data into three forms: structured (SQL), semi-structured (JSON), and unstructured (text) formats. To achieve this objective, our method leverages text preprocessing techniques such as named entity recognition and part-of-speech tagging to identify the entities contained within the data.

Efficient deployment of neural networks (NN) requires the co-optimization of accuracy and latency, which is challenging due to the interplay between NN architectures and underlying hardware. We propose a novel framework called FlexFlow that enables efficient acceleration of complex NNs on various heterogeneous hardware platforms. It automatically decomposes an NN into multiple subnetworks that can be efficiently executed on diverse accelerators. FlexFlow is based on graph partitioning, which has been previously used in compiler-based optimization for parallel execution on homogenous processors. However, we leverage advanced graph-theoretic methods from the field of network science to adapt this technique for heterogeneous systems. Our approach achieves superior performance across a wide ra

Decoding language from brain dynamics is an important open direction in the realm of brain-computer interfaces. In this paper, we propose a novel method for decoding language from EEG data using deep learning models. Our approach leverages a generative model to learn the latent space of linguistic features extracted from EEG signals and a discriminative model to decode the language with high accuracy. We validate our method on a publicly available dataset consisting of 120 EEG recordings of 40 participants performing various tasks. The results demonstrate that our method outperforms the state-of-the-art methods by achieving up to 97.5% accuracy in identifying spoken words. This research paves the way towards developing advanced brain-computer interfaces for natural language processing appl

Virtual Private Networks (VPNs) are a crucial Privacy-Enhancing Technology. They have been used since the early 90s, and they became popular in the last decade as more people began to use them for personal purposes or their companies started using cloud computing solutions instead of on premises data centers which require less infrastructure costs but also expose sensitive information through public networks such that it could be accessed by anyone with an internet connection if not protected properly via encryption algorithms like TLS/SSL certificates among others [1].

Large Language Models (LLMs) have shown impressive results on a wide range of tasks. However, analytical reasoning, a key component of human intelligence, seems to be a weak point of LLMs. To benchmark analytical reasoning abilities, we propose a new task setting that requires models to find the missing links in a chain of reasoning provided by humans. We call this task Analogical Reasoning Chain Completion (ARCC). To show the weakness of LLMs in this task, we first conduct a human study on a set of ARCC problems, and show that humans can solve the problems by finding the missing links in the reasoning chains. We then fine-tune a LLM with ARCC problems, hoping that it can learn to reason analytically. Unfortunately, our LLM performs worse than the baseline models in solving ARCC problems. We also find that LLMs are vulnerable to prompt engineering in this task.

Malicious URLs provide adversarial opportunities across various industries, including transportation, healthcare, and public safety. Malicious URL detection is crucial to mitigate these threats; however, many existing solutions are based on blacklisting and whitelisting methods, which are ineffective due to the ever-increasing number of malicious URLs. Moreover, these traditional approaches often require extensive manual efforts to maintain the lists, resulting in significant costs and delays. To address these challenges, we propose an anomaly detection approach for detecting malicious URLs using a transformer-based self-supervised model trained on domain-specific text corpora. Our model leverages the strengths of pretrained language models (PLMs) to identify anomalous patterns within URLs

The United Nations is currently negotiating a new treaty to conserve and sustainably use the biodiversity of the ocean beyond national jurisdiction (the BBNJ treaty). Meanwhile, marine policy experts are under pressure to quickly disseminate their knowledge to a wide audience of non-experts, including policymakers, stakeholders, and the general public. To help with this task, the UN Educational, Scientific and Cultural Organization (UNESCO) developed a question-answering chatbot named BBNJ-AI. The goal of this paper is to analyze the potential of AI language models to improve equity in marine policymaking. We do so by examining the BBNJ-AI chatbot, interviewing the people behind it, and testing the chatbot with real users. Our results show that AI language models have the potential to improve equity in marine policymaking by providing easy access to information for people who are excluded from traditional channels of information dissemination. However, we also warn that such models can perpetuate existing power dynamics if they are not carefully designed and implemented. We recommend that future AI tools for marine policy be co-designed with end-users to ensure that they are useful, equitable, and sustainable.

Adversarial attacks pose a serious threat to deepfake detectors, but the lack of explainability in these models makes it difficult to understand how and why they fail. To address this problem, we propose a novel framework that uses Shapley Value Sampling (SVS) to explain the decisions of a deepfake detector and detect adversarial attacks. We apply our framework to two types of adversarial attacks: gradient-based and gradient-free based. Experiments show that our method is effective in detecting adversarial attacks from the explanations of deepfake detectors. In addition, our results show that gradient-based attacks are easier to detect than gradient-free based attacks.

Lexicon-based constrained decoding approaches aim to control the meaning or style of the generated text. However, such methods often rely on large pre-trained language models (PLMs) and are limited by their inability to generate novel content. This paper presents a lexicon-based style-controlled generation method based on transformer-language models (TLMs), which can be used with any TLM, including those that do not have access to external corpora or dictionaries. The proposed approach integrates a lexicon into the generation process as a post-processing step, resulting in improved generation quality without sacrificing performance. Our experiments show that the proposed method achieves comparable results to state-of-the-art methods while being more efficient and versatile. The code is ava

In the standard model of fair allocation of resources to agents, every agent has some utility for every resource. The goal is to allocate resources among agents in a way that maximizes total agent utility. This paper shows that, when there are many resources and each resource can be used by many agents, the standard model cannot guarantee that any feasible allocation will have at least one agent who is strictly better off than in the standard model, or even that such an agent exists. We introduce and study a variant of the standard model where an agent's utility for a resource is allowed to depend on how much they like using it (and perhaps also on how much other agents like using it). An allocation is feasible if no agent wants more than their share, so we call this model "the strong vers

Medical open-domain question answering demands substantial access to specialized knowledge. Recent efforts have sought for a better integration of external domain resources into the model, in particular by constructing and utilizing large graphs that capture complex relationships between entities within each medical subfield (e. g., anatomy or physiology). However they are usually restricted at one single topic level without considering broader context across different topics due mainly from data scarcity concerns; this may lead some questions outside their scope when asked about multiple related ones on topically diverse subjects like drug interactions/side effects versus symptoms alone - which could be detrimental given how important accurate answers can often turn out during treatment p

Linear dynamical systems, linear logic, and category theory are three fields that, although developed independently, are strongly interconnected. In this work, we explore the interplay between linear dynamical systems and linear logic by interpreting linear logical formulas as specifications for linear dynamical systems. We focus on open linear systems, i.e., linear systems with inputs and outputs that communicate with their environment. Our main result is the proof that the two notions of open linear system and of bipartite process are equivalent. The first notion is a combination of the notions of open system and of linear system, where an open system is a system with interfaces, i.e., a system that can interact with its environment through input and output wires. A linear system is a system whose internal dynamics is described by a linear map. The composition of open linear systems is given by a tensor product that satisfies the Eilenberg-Moore condition, so that we can construct free open linear systems over a given set of generators. The second notion, that of bipartite process, is a generalization of the notion of coherent process, introduced by Gay and the third author of this paper, to the setting where inputs and outputs are present. Bipartite processes enjoy a cut elimination property, as do coherent processes, and this property is used to prove the main result of the paper.

The challenge of spatial resource allocation is pervasive across various domains such as transportation, industry, and agriculture. To meet this challenge, we propose the Spatial Resource Allocation Planner (SPARP), a fully autonomous algorithm that can learn to plan high-quality spatial resource allocation strategies. SPARP operates by training an agent on a dataset of plans for which the cost of using resources has been annotated. We find that, with minimal human supervision, our approach learns to improve upon existing methods in terms of both efficiency and robustness under uncertainty. Our results demonstrate that the SPARP framework enables planning agents to acquire generalizable skills through self-supervised learning, allowing them to adapt to new scenarios without retraining. The prevailing grasp prediction methods predominantly rely on offline learning, overlooking the dynamic nature of human grasping. In this paper we propose an online approach to learn a data-driven model for real time hand pose estimation and manipulation force feedback in robotic hands with embedded tactile sensors (iHand). We explore three types neural networks models: recurrent convolutional long short term memory network(RC_LSTM), deep residual LSTM netwwork w/o temporal pooling layer using raw image input as well RC\_DNN that utilizes both visual information from RGB images along side proprioceptive sensor values at each joint angle level extracted by depth maps generated via point cloud processing techniques based off 3D structured light scanning technology; these approaches are compa

Hardware development relies on simulations, particularly cycle-accurate RTL (Register Transfer Level) simulation. The high cost of cycle-accurate simulators often necessitates the use of low-cost models to speed up simulations. While these low-cost models can provide a fast and accurate estimate of the behavior of the design under test, they are still prone to errors that may lead to erroneous results. To address this challenge, we propose an approach for the automatic detection of erroneous behavior in low-cost RTL simulation models. Our approach is based on a statistical analysis of simulation runs by leveraging the power consumption and clock cycles of the simulated circuit as quality indicators. We have implemented our approach in an automated tool called FEALERT. To evaluate its effec

An expander decomposition of a graph $G=(V,E)$ is a partition of $V$ into clusters $C_1,\ldots,C_k$ such that (i) each cluster $C_i$ contains a constant number of vertices, (ii) each edge $ij \in E$ is "satisfied" by a constant number of clusters, and (iii) the set of satisfied clusters for any vertex $i \in V$ forms a connected subgraph of $G$. The goal is to find a decomposition where the number of unsatisfied edges is at most $\epsilon$ times the number of edges of $G$. Formerly, it was proven that a $(\log n)^{O(\epsilon)}$-time algorithm exists for this task. We present a new, simpler proof of this fact, based on a randomized approach. The previous proofs were based on a potential function approach, which led to a more complicated analysis. Our new proof allows for a more careful analysis, which yields an algorithm running in time $n^{O(\epsilon)}$, up to logarithmic factors. This improvement is mainly based on the simple observation that the algorithm of Dinur, Karger, and Peled runs in fact in polynomial time, and does not rely on the non-optimal decomposition of a small neighborhood. We also show that a variant of this problem, where one asks for a decomposition where each cluster contains a bounded number of unsatisfied edges, can be solved in polynomial time.

To use new robot hardware in a new environment, it is necessary to develop a control program tailored to that specific robot in that environment. Considering the reusability of software among robots is crucial to minimize the effort involved in this process and maximize software reuse across different robots in different environments. This paper proposes a method to remedy this process by considering hardware-level reusability, using Learning-from-observation (LfO) paradigm with a pre-designed skill-agent library. The LfO framework represents the required actions in hardware-independent representations, referred to as task models, from observing human demonstrations, capturing the necessary parameters for the interaction between the environment and the robot. When executing the desired act

Stance detection of social media text is a key component of downstream tasks involving the identification of groups with similar stances, such as in political polarization. Existing methods typically rely on handcrafted features or pre-trained language models. However, these approaches are limited by their reliance on specific datasets and often fail to generalize well across different domains. To overcome this limitation, we propose a novel stance detection method that leverages large language models (LLMs) for zero-shot cross-domain transfer. Our approach involves training an LLM with a large amount of data from one domain and then fine-tuning it to identify stances in another domain without any labeled data from the target domain. We evaluate our approach using two popular LLMs and a va

Traditional sequential recommendation methods assume that users' sequence data is clean enough to learn accurate sequence representations, which may not be the case in real-world applications. However, learning from noisy sequence data often leads to worse performance for downstream tasks due to unreliable modeling of user preferences and a lack of robustness to the noise. In this paper, we propose SequenceRefiner, an effective method to improve the reliability of sequence models by refining the corrupted sequence data. Our approach leverages the self-attention mechanism and incorporates the refinement task into the pre-training objective. By iteratively updating the representation with the refined sequence data, our method enhances the quality of sequence models without relying on extra a

Motivated by the success of gamification in non-game contexts, we designed a plugin for a popular IDE (NetBeans) that turns the act of submitting code into a game. The plugin rewards frequent and timely submissions, as well as improvements in code quality, with points and leaderboard rankings. It also provides hints to help users improve their code. We evaluated the plugin in two ways. First, we performed a within-subject study with 21 participants, most of whom were professional software developers. We measured the effects of gamification on code quality, submission frequency, and participants' attitudes towards the IDE, code quality, and gamification. Second, we conducted a user study with 17 participants, all of whom were new to the IDE. We measured the effects of the plugin on the same variables, as well as on participants' ability to solve programming tasks. Our results show that the plugin increased participants' submission frequency by 1.5X-2X and improved their attitudes towards the IDE and code quality. However, it did not improve participants' performance on programming tasks, nor did it change their perceptions of the tasks.

In recent years, operator learning, particularly the DeepONet, has received much attention for efficiently learning operators that capture complex nonlinear relationships among sensor data. Recently, a novel and efficient autoencoder-based approach to learn linear operators from raw data was proposed by Li et al., which shows strong generalization ability in various real-world applications. However, there are three major challenges in this method: 1) The model is sensitive to initialization; 2) It needs to train an extra encoder network to learn a low-dimensional encoding representation of raw data; 3) Since the output of the decoder is only used as a target function, it may not be able to well represent the actual target functions. To address these challenges, we propose a novel autoencod

Federated learning (FL) is a collaborative scheme to train a learning model across multiple participants without sharing data. In FL, it is natural for each participant to weigh in on the final model, but the existing weighting schemes are model-agnostic and purely based on the contribution of each participant to the overall performance, which does not necessarily align with the individual interests. In this work, we first expose a potential pitfall of using Shapley values for contribution-based weighting in FL, where the Shapley value of a participant can be manipulated by another participant with a small cost. Then, we propose a mechanism for decision-aware collaborative FL, where the participants not only take their contribution into account but also consider the impact on their individual interests. In particular, we consider two types of decision-aware mechanisms: one is based on the Nash social welfare, which assumes that the participants are risk-neutral; the other is based on the core, which does not make such an assumption. We show that the two mechanisms can be implemented by a polynomial-time algorithm and a fully polynomial-time randomized approximation scheme (FPRAS), respectively. We also provide experimental results to demonstrate the performance of the two mechanisms.

We consider the problem of online learning where the sequence of actions played by the learner must adhere to a stochastic policy $\pi$. In this work, we study how to design an adversary that plays against a learner who optimizes for any given policy. This adversarial setting is particularly challenging because the learner can only learn from each action in hindsight while the adversary has access to all the information at once. We show that there are adversaries which guarantee to make the learner regret the decisions she makes under any policy and that there exist policies such that even the best-performing learner has bounded regret with probability $1-\delta$ (regret bound $\mathcal{O}(\sqrt{\log(T)\log(\frac{1}{\delta}))}$).

Bipedal robots are inherently underactuated and therefore challenging to control, especially when walking in complex environments. Controlling an underactuated bipedal robot requires careful state estimation and force control, as walking in a stationary environment is already a challenging task for such robots. Walking in non-stationary environments, such as among moving obstacles, is even more challenging. This paper demonstrates a robust walking algorithm for underactuated bipedal robots in non-stationary environments. The algorithm uses a whole-body control approach that is able to produce the necessary torques to move the robot's center of mass (CoM) forward while respecting the robot's physical limitations. The CoM trajectory is planned using a hybrid approach that combines a fast heuristic search algorithm with a trajectory optimizer. The optimizer uses a simplified model to quickly find a feasible trajectory, which is then refined using the full model. The walking algorithm is demonstrated in simulation and on physical robots. The simulations show that the robot can walk stably in environments with moving obstacles, where other state-of-the-art algorithms would crash. The experiments show that the robot can walk for at least 10 minutes without falling, using a simple controller that only considers the CoM trajectory. This is the first time that a underactuated bipedal robot walks stably in a challenging environment for such a long time.

Recent advancements in multimodal Human-Robot Interaction (HRI) datasets have highlighted the fusion of speech and gesture, expanding robots' capabilities to absorb explicit and implicit HRI insights. However, existing speech-gesture HRI datasets often focus on elementary tasks, like object pointing and pushing, revealing limitations in scaling to intricate domains and prioritizing human command data over robot behavior records. To bridge these gaps, we introduce NatSGD, a multimodal HRI dataset encompassing human commands through speech and gestures that are natural, synchronized with robot behavior demonstrations. NatSGD serves as a foundational resource at the intersection of machine learning and HRI research, and we demonstrate its effectiveness in training robots to understand tasks t

Existing approaches on zero-shot event detection usually train models on datasets annotated with known event types, which leads to overfitting and performance degradation when the model is applied to new events. In this paper, we propose a novel approach that leverages an external knowledge base to generate training examples for event types not present in the original dataset. We demonstrate that our approach outperforms existing methods on various datasets. Our code is available at https://github.com/hailuo1234567890/ZeroShotEventDetection.git. The source code will be released within 3 weeks after the acceptance of the paper. The model can be accessed at https://zenodo.org/record/7376418#. This project was supported by National Key R&D Program (No.

Data similarity assumptions have traditionally been relied upon to understand the convergence behaviors of federated learning (FL). However, these traditional methods may not be able capture all relevant aspects in real-world settings. In this work we study a new data feature for understanding FL: data structure and its impact on training dynamics when combining across clients from different domains/sectors with varying underlying structures compared against one another during distributed parameter averaging steps -- which is an important component within many popular frameworks such as Federated Averaging or FedAvg .

Topic models are a popular tool for clustering and analyzing textual data. They allow texts to be grouped into meaningful clusters, revealing underlying topics and relationships between them. However, many datasets include multiple types of data that do not share the same semantics (e.g., documents and images). When working with these mixed modalities, it is important to find a way to leverage information from both modalities to improve the quality of topic modeling. In this work, we propose an approach called Mixed Modal Mixup, which combines two kinds of mixup: within-modality and cross-modal mixups. Within-modality mixups allow us to use the high-order semantic structure in the training data to improve the performance of the topic modeling while avoiding the need to retrain existing enc

Vietnamese is the native language of over 85 million people and is spoken by communities in Vietnam, Cambodia, and elsewhere. Despite the language's significance, there are no off-the-shelf large language models (LLMs) that can natively process Vietnamese. In this work, we fill in this gap by (i) porting six state-of-the-art LLMs to Vietnamese, (ii) introducing a unified pipeline to evaluate LLMs across multiple tasks and languages, and (iii) conducting a comprehensive evaluation of the resulting Vietnamese LLMs. We cover five major tasks, namely, text classification, text generation, question answering, machine translation, and dialogue generation, using three LLMs, namely, GPT-3, GPT-4, and T5. Our study provides valuable insights for the development of LLMs for under-resourced languages.

Recent advances in Neural Radiance Fields (NeRFs) treat the problem of novel view synthesis as a purely per-pixel prediction task, which ignores the 3D scene understanding power of deep neural networks and the perceptual qualities of light transport. In this work, we propose Semantic Neural Radiance Fields (SNeRFs) as a general framework for novel view synthesis which is built on the premise that understanding 3D scenes semantically is crucial for generating realistic images. SNeRFs leverage recent developments in scene representation learning to achieve this semantic understanding, and integrate the power of 3D scene understanding and image synthesis into a unified model. Our model learns a 3D semantic representation of the scene, which can be rendered from any view. This representation can be visualized as a volume of semantic information, similar to a bird's-eye view of the scene. We evaluate our method on large-scale outdoor scenes and show that our method outperforms recent view synthesis techniques and that the resulting images are of comparable quality to the state-of-the-art. Our code is publicly available at https://github.com/ErlerPhilipp/GSNeRF.

Large Language Models (LLMs) like GPT-4 have recently demonstrated impressive results across a wide range of tasks. LLMs are still limited, however, in that they frequently fail at complex reasoning, their reasoning processes are opaque, they are prone to 'hallucinate' facts, and there are concerns about their underlying biases. One way to mitigate these limitations is to evaluate the LLM-generated content against a set of criteria, using natural language as the query. In this paper we propose a methodology to do so, by extracting criteria from the instructions given to humans for creating the content, and from the desired properties of the content. We call this methodology Content Evaluation Protocol (CEP). We demonstrate the applicability of CEP to educational content by applying it to 17,000 historical date pairs annotated as fact or non-fact. We also show that the LLMs can be prompted to generate better content, by fine-tuning them with CEP-based feedback.

Action Quality Assessment (AQA) evaluates diverse skills but models struggle with non-linearity, uncertainties, and the presence of missing data. A novel framework called SAMQA is presented to address these challenges by capturing the full spectrum of action quality metrics using a single network architecture. This approach uses Multi-Head Attention (MHA) to model the non-linearity in the dataset and incorporate the concept of uncertainty through dropout layers. Additionally, the proposed method handles missing data using an input fusion strategy that combines the raw values from different sources while ensuring compatibility with downstream tasks. The experimental results demonstrate significant improvement over existing methods on AI2-THor Dataset with an overall accuracy increase of 9%.

Current approaches to question answering rely on pre-trained language models (PLMs) like RoBERTa and BERT. However, PLM training is costly in terms of time complexity: a single epoch requires $O(n^2)$ operations, where n denotes the sequence length [1]. This leads us towards exploring efficient alternatives for scaling up such methods without sacrificing performance or quality; one promising option could be using smaller sequences as input instead which would reduce computational demands considerably while still yielding accurate results .

Large Language Models (LLMs) are susceptible to catastrophic forgetting when transitioning from one task to another. Recent proposals to mitigate this issue have focused on either preventing forgetting through continual learning techniques or unlearning the previously learned tasks. However, unlearning methods have received less attention in the LLM domain. In this work, we aim to understand the sources of catastrophic forgetting in LLMs and develop a guardrail baseline for unlearning. We first perform a comparative analysis of different LLMs to identify the most forgetful model. We then investigate the impact of two factors, prompt engineering and data ordering, on the forgetting behavior of the LLMs. Finally, we propose a simple guardrail baseline for unlearning based on our observations. Our experimental results demonstrate that the guardrail baseline can help mitigate catastrophic forgetting for LLMs.

Visual entailment (VE) systems infer from a pair of images whether one image entails (logically follows) the other. To test such systems, we propose a novel data augmentation method, dubbed object-aligned joint erasing (OAJE). OAJE augments the testing data by simultaneously erasing object regions from both images in a pair proportionally to their sizes. We use OAJE to test seven existing VE systems and observe that they are not robust to object-level modifications. This finding is surprising because these VE systems are trained on heavily augmented data. We further investigate the effect of training data size and find that larger datasets produce more robust VE systems. Based on our results, we recommend that (1) object-level robustness should be a desired property in VE systems, (2) current VE systems lack object-level robustness, and (3) future VE systems should be tested using data augmentation techniques like OAJE.

Point masses connected by springs, or mass-spring systems, are widely used in computer animation to model the deformation of elastic materials. The dynamics associated with these models can be characterized using a simple linear oscillator (LSO). This study presents an analysis for two different types: damped and undampened LOs whose equations include nonlinear damping effects that arise from contact forces between multiple bodies interacting within one system; this is achieved through numerical simulations performed on MATLAB/Simulink software platform utilizing multibody dynamic simulation toolkits developed specifically towards such applications .

We study the problem of automatically synthesizing timing constraints for systems modeled as timed automata. The synthesized constraints should be satisfied by all (unknown) implementations of the automaton. We show that this problem is undecidable, even when the automaton is deterministic and has only one clock. To cope with this undecidability result, we propose a learning-based approach that takes as input a timed automaton with unknown biases and timing constraints, and returns a new timed automaton with the same unknowns, but with learned constraints. The learning algorithm works even if the original automaton is non-deterministic. We implemented our algorithm in a tool based on the open-source platforms TChecker and Relu. We show that our tool produces automata with fewer constraints than those produced by other state-of-the-art tools.

Image editing software gives users the ability to freely edit and manipulate images. However, existing image editing methods that allow free-form editing, such as content-aware filling, are based on pixel-level operations. These operations, however, are sensitive to boundary conditions, leading to unstable results when editing near object boundaries. In this work, we introduce StableDrag, a novel stable dragging method for point-based image editing that is inspired by vector field visualization techniques. Our method is able to produce stable edits, even when object boundaries are poorly defined. We achieve this by designing the editing operation such that the edited image is the solution of an energy functional, the minimum of which is stable and can be found using gradient descent. Our energy functional has two terms: the first term is a data fidelity term that ensures the edited image is close to the original image; the second term is a smoothness term that ensures the edited image is stable. We demonstrate the effectiveness of StableDrag for performing free-form editing tasks such as image inpainting and object removal.

Link prediction is the task of inferring missing links between nodes in a graph, and is performed by GNNs that are calibrated to the task. A common calibration strategy is to fine-tune a GNN's output layer on a validation set of positive and negative links. However, the existence of a missing link between two nodes provides no information about the likelihood of a link existing between them, making it unclear what the target output distribution should be. We show that link prediction can be viewed as a density estimation problem, and that the above strategy corresponds to maximum likelihood estimation (MLE) of the link existence probabilities. MLE has been shown to overfit on sparse datasets, and we show that link prediction datasets are sparse and that overfitting occurs. We propose to calibrate GNNs for link prediction by MLE of the normalized adjacency matrix, which corresponds to maximizing the geometric mean of the link existence probabilities. We show that this calibration strategy reduces the link prediction error on four datasets.

The evolutionary paradigm has been successfully applied to neural network search(NAS) in recent years, and the majority of NAS methods have been developed based on it. However, a number of issues with this strategy are not fully addressed by current methods: 1) most existing methods only apply evolutionary algorithms on the architecture space but ignore parameters' optimization; 2) most methods focus on the single-objective optimization but neglect multi-objective optimization; 3) most methods use the same evolutionary operators for both architecture search and parameter optimization, which makes them hard to adapt to various problems; 4) most methods do not support transfer learning between different tasks and networks, making it difficult to efficiently reuse past knowledge. To address t

Large language models (LLMs) have shown remarkable performance improvements in recent years. However, serving LLMs efficiently at inference time remains a challenge. Attention mechanisms are a key bottleneck in LLM serving, as they require a high computational cost for large model scales and long sequence lengths. In this paper, we propose Attention on Top Principal Keys (ATP), a novel approach to enabling fast LLM serving via attention. The idea is to reduce the attention computation cost by leveraging only a subset of the entire key and value vectors, namely the top principal keys that are most relevant to the query. We theoretically prove that ATP attention is equivalent to the original attention mechanism when the number of principal keys is the same as the number of attention heads. This equivalence guarantee provides a theoretical foundation for using fewer principal keys in ATP attention. We conduct extensive experiments on two LLMs, namely BLOOM and GPT-3, and show that ATP attention can reduce the computation cost by up to 8X while maintaining high accuracy.

Understanding the reasoning capabilities of Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) is crucial for effectively utilizing their potential in solving complex tasks. The ability to infer the intentions and goals behind an input, also known as inference-based reasoning, is a critical capability for MLLMs to understand human behavior, motivations, and intents. In this study, we focus on analyzing the inference-based reasoning abilities of two highly regarded Llama models, GPT-4 and InstructGPT, using two benchmark datasets: the AI2 Reasoning benchmark and the Few-Shot Intent Classification benchmark. We found that while both models exhibit good performance on simple inference questions with clear instructions, they struggle with more challenging scenarios where the instructions are ambiguous o

Transformers have elevated to the state-of-the-art vision architectures through innovations in model design and training. However, they are still limited by their inherent challenges, including the large memory footprint and computational complexity of self-attention mechanisms, which makes them impractical for resource-constrained edge devices. To this end, we propose a lightweight architecture that leverages local attention and depthwise separable convolutions (DSConv) for feature extraction to reduce the computational burden of transformer models while maintaining high performance on low-resource tasks. Our architecture achieves competitive results on challenging datasets such as CIFAR100 and ImageNet 22k, demonstrating its potential in practical applications requiring low-resource scen

This paper focuses on developing Pareto-optimal estimation and policy learning to identify the most beneficial subset of intervention candidates. We introduce a novel framework for jointly optimizing over multiple objectives, including cost efficiency (measured in terms of the total cost) and performance gain (i.e., how much better the chosen sub-population performs compared to all other candidates). To this end, we employ an efficient multi-objective evolutionary algorithm that is customized for optimizing the expected cost and gain associated with each candidate. Our experimental results show that our proposed approach outperforms several baselines, demonstrating its potential as a practical decision support tool in drug repurposing. Furthermore, our findings underscore the importance of

Recently, promptable segmentation models, such as the Segment Anything Model (SAM), have demonstrated robust zero-shot generalization capabilities on static images. These promptable models exhibit denoising abilities for imprecise prompt inputs, such as imprecise bounding boxes. In this paper, we explore the potential of applying SAM to track and segment objects in videos where we recognize the tracking task as a prompt denoising task. Specifically, we iteratively propagate the bounding box of each object's mask in the preceding frame as the prompt for the next frame. Furthermore, to enhance SAM's denoising capability against position and size variations, we propose a multi-prompt strategy where we provide multiple jittered and scaled box prompts for each object and preserve the mask predi

Due to the effectiveness of second-order algorithms in solving classical optimization problems, designing second- or high order methods for stochastic convex minimization has received a considerable attention. In this paper we propose two novel approaches that allow us efficiently combine first and higher orders information with random observations at each iteration step; namely adaptive regularization approach (ARA) which uses Tikhonov regularizations on both sides as well an augmented Lagrangian framework based algorithm called Augmented Primal Dual Approach(APDA). These techniques can be applied either directly within gradient descent schemes such $\text{GD}+\lambda\nabla^2$ ,$\mathrm {SVRG}+ \Lambda/\sqrt t$, etc.,or by using them inside primal dual frameworks like ADMM/Proximal Splitt

Medical vision-language models (VLMs) combine computer vision and natural language processing to analyze visual information. In this paper, we propose a novel approach to enhance the accuracy of VLMs by incorporating self-supervised learning with additional pretraining. Specifically, we introduce a method that utilizes an external dataset to help train the model on unlabeled data, thereby improving its performance in downstream tasks. Our proposed model achieves state-of-the-art results on medical image classification benchmarks such as MIMIC-CXR. Furthermore, we demonstrate its effectiveness in enhancing the ability of VLMs to interpret and comprehend complex medical images. We hope our work will encourage further research into self-supervised learning for VLMs and inspire new directions

Combining images from multi-modalities is beneficial to explore various information in computer vision, but existing methods are mainly limited by the lack of semantic understanding. To address this limitation, we propose a new task called Image-Text Masked Diffusion (ITMD) for image generation based on diffusion model and introduce a novel conceptual mask, named Mask Text-Image (MTI), which can be regarded as a novel type of text prompt in text-to-image generation tasks. Experimental results show that ITMD achieves superior performance compared with state-of-the-art multimodal image generation models on three public datasets: Visual Genome, COCO, and CLEVR. Moreover, our proposed model also outperforms a number of existing single modality image generation methods on all three datasets. Co

IoT smart home devices provide comfort and convenience to homeowners; however, they can pose security and privacy risks if not properly secured. IoT devices are often connected to a local area network (LAN) through a router, which acts as a gateway to the internet. Homeowners can configure the LAN settings and manage connected devices through the router's web-based interface. Malicious actors may attempt to exploit vulnerabilities in these devices to gain unauthorized access to the network. Therefore, it is important to monitor network traffic for signs of malicious activity, especially from devices that are managed through the LAN. In this paper, we propose a self-adaptive traffic anomaly detection system for smart home environments. We employ a reference traffic profiling technique to establish normal traffic patterns for each device and detect anomalies based on deviations from these profiles. To evaluate our approach, we generate reference traffic for 25 smart home devices by collecting baseline data during idle periods. We then introduce simulated anomalies and evaluate the detection system's performance in terms of its ability to accurately identify anomalous traffic. Our preliminary results show that the proposed system can effectively detect anomalous traffic patterns with low false positive and false negative rates.

Large Language Models (LLMs) have achieved impressive results in recent years, but they often fail at complex task completion. Multi-agent systems (MAS) can enhance LLM capabilities, as agents can divide the task among themselves and leverage their individual strengths. However, traditional MAS architectures focus on independent agents that do not exploit the capabilities of LLMs. In this paper, we introduce LLM-centric agents, a novel MAS architecture that centers around the LLM, allowing it to distribute the workload among the agents and act as a coordination mechanism. We perform a theoretical analysis of LLM-centric agents in instruction following, revealing a trade-off between using more components (agents) and less (neurons). This trade-off is governed by the LLM's component-wise efficiency, measured as the ratio between the component's contribution to the instruction's semantics and its computational cost. We use this insight to design a multi-stage training process that encourages the LLM to connect its most efficient components for instruction following. Our experimental results show that LLM-centric agents can improve the LLM's instruction following performance by up to 80%, highlighting the potential of this architecture for enhancing LLM capabilities.

Theory of mind (ToM) is the ability to attribute mental states to oneself and others. ToM is crucial for social cognition and is thought to be grounded in the ability to simulate the sensory-motor experiences of others. ToM deficit is observed in several clinical conditions, most notably in autism spectrum disorder (ASD). However, standard ToM tests are typically designed for human participants, and their direct application to animals is not feasible. We propose a novel paradigm, ToM-CG, based on the notion of common ground (CG), to benchmark ToM in animals. The paradigm involves two agents, a human and a robot, who perform a joint task. The robot can either share the same goal as the human (CG+) or a different goal (CG-). We tested ToM-CG with two groups of participants: adults with (n=14) and without (n=13) ASD. Participants controlled a avatar and interacted with a robot that moved either in a predictable or an unpredictable manner. We measured several ToM-related and social cognition measures. With a few exceptions, results replicated previous findings in a virtual reality (VR) environment. More importantly, we demonstrated the utility of our paradigm by showing that ToM-CG scores are correlated with ToM-VR scores, that is, participants who scored higher in the ToM-CG paradigm also scored higher in the ToM-VR paradigm. Notably, we found that the ToM-CG paradigm differentiated participants with and without ASD, thus providing a viable animal model to benchmark ToM.

Context: Edge graphs are graphs whose edges are labelled with identifiers, and nodes can have multiple incident edges. Edge graphs appear naturally in a variety of applications, including computational biology, network science, and natural language processing, where they are used to represent relationships between entities or concepts. In this paper, we introduce the problem of finding a subgraph isomorphism on edge graphs that maximizes a predefined objective function. This problem arises in various domains where it is essential to identify the most significant set of common structures between two edge graphs. We propose an efficient algorithm for computing this optimal solution, based on the dynamic programming technique and a novel scoring scheme. Our approach leverages a unique propert

Diffusion-weighted Magnetic Resonance Imaging (dMRI) is a noninvasive technique that can be used to identify the presence of brain lesions. In this study, we propose an unsupervised method for segmenting white matter regions in dMRI images using Convolutional Neural Networks and Sparse Autoencoders.
