Our approach is based on a 3D convolutional neural network with skip connections, trained on an augmented dataset containing 600 manually annotated training samples from 28 patients with different neurological disorders. We evaluate our model by comparing it with five state-of-the-art methods on two publicly available datasets: the first one is the Clinical Brain Segmentation Decathlon 2015 and the second one is the Clinical Brain Segmentation Decathlon 2019 challenge.

Layout-aware text-to-image generation is a task to generate multi-object images that reflect the layout of given texts. Most existing methods either require expensive visual grounding or are limited to simple and non-overlapping scenes, making them unable to deal with the complex composition in real scenes. In this paper, we propose a novel method to address these problems. Specifically, our model, called Text2Layout, consists of three modules: (1) text embedder, which converts text prompts into latent representations; (2) layout generator, which synthesizes a layout image from the corresponding text representation; (3) vision-language aligner, which further refines the layout and outputs an image conditioned on the text prompt.

Software product line techniques encourage the reuse and adaptation of software components for creating customized products or software variants. Variability management is a key factor in this process, as it involves representing and managing the different product variants. The most common approach to managing variability is through feature models, which are structured representations of the features that make up a product. However, current feature modeling languages face several limitations, such as poor support for variability constraints, complex syntaxes, and limited expressiveness. To overcome these challenges, we present a new feature modeling language called GFFM. This language offers an intuitive syntax with a focus on ease-of-use, improved scalability, and robustness, while also a

We consider unsupervised domain adaptation (UDA) for semantic segmentation in which the model is trained on a source dataset with annotations and used to segment an unlabeled target image. The challenge lies within adapting from one distribution $P_{S}$ of pixel intensities, colors or textures observed at training time towards another $\tilde{p}_{T}$, unseen during learning but encountered when making predictions: how can we leverage our knowledge about what makes up natural images while allowing new patterns? In this paper ,we introduce two novel UDA strategies that aimto addressthis problemby leveraginganauxiliary classifiertrainedontopofthesegmentationnetworkwiththegoalofmaximizingsemantic consistency between features extracted by both models;one based upon maximum likelihood estimation

Cyber threats continue to evolve in complexity, thereby traditional Cyber Threat Intelligence (CTI) and threat detection solutions are increasingly falling short of identifying new threats. To address this challenge, researchers at the MITRE Corporation have developed a novel deep learning model called DeepCTI, which leverages unsupervised generative adversarial networks (GANs) to enhance CTI and identify emerging cyber threats. By leveraging GANs, DeepCTI can generate synthetic samples that resemble real CTI data. This approach eliminates the need for costly and time-consuming manual labeling and enables effective training of machine learning models without requiring large datasets. In this paper, we present DeepCTI, a novel GAN-based approach to enhancing CTI. The effectiveness of our me

Large language models (LLMs) such as GPT-4 have shown remarkable performance in recent years. However, the resource consumption and latency of LLMs have become major hurdles to their widespread deployment and use. Model merging is an effective way to reduce resource consumption and latency, by combining multiple models into one. In this paper, we propose a novel model merging method, which selects and prunes unnecessary layers and heads of the base model based on a merging criterion, and then fine-tunes the pruned model using merged data. Our method can reduce the resource consumption and latency of LLMs without a significant performance drop. Specifically, pruning 80% layers and 50% heads of GPT-4 can reduce the parameter size from 1.3B to 270M, lowering the resource consumption from 1.36 GPU to 0.16 GPU. Moreover, our method can be used for proactive pruning, i.e., reducing the size of LLMs before deployment, and thus reducing the resource consumption and latency from the very beginning.

Artificial Intelligence (AI) is increasingly employed in various decision-making tasks, typically requiring the system to provide a single result. However, recent studies have indicated that multi-choice responses, which provide alternatives to the solution, can enhance the accuracy of AI systems by enabling them to recognize and evaluate their mistakes. In this paper, we propose an approach for developing multiple-choice responses using reinforcement learning techniques with the goal of enhancing the performance of chatbots. We have developed an automated multi-choice response generation model based on reinforcement learning and have trained it using RLlib, a popular open-source library. Through experiments, we demonstrate how this technique improves the accuracy of chatbot responses, red

Topic modelling was mostly dominated by Bayesian graphical models during the last decade. While these approaches have proven to be effective and versatile, they also suffer from inherent shortcomings, e.\g., overparameterization or slow convergence in high-dimensional settings like text corpora with millions of documents containing hundreds thousands unique words as well more recent developments around neural networks seem promising for this task (e,\eg,~Deep Neural Topic Model). In a series paper on topic modeling we propose an alternative approach that utilizes variational autoencoders instead which allows us not only obtain efficient latent representations but additionally provide interpretable clusters through their respective encoder/decoder layers without having any explicit knowledg

(p,q)-clique enumeration on a bipartite graph is critical for calculating clustering coefficients. To date, most algorithms have focused on the traditional clique enumeration problem. However, some applications require enumeration of subgraphs with non-traditional properties. In this paper, we introduce a new notion of $(p,q)$-clique enumeration and investigate its computational complexity. We first present a dynamic programming algorithm that runs in time $O(n^{4\lceil q/2 \rceil}2^{\lceil 2p - q + 1\rceil})$. For the special case where p=1 or q=1, we show that there exists an FPT algorithm running in time $\tilde{O}(n^{p+q})$ using the idea of a modified branch-and-bound search method.

We present an incremental and hierarchical model of human-like category learning called Cobweb. The model is incremental in that it successively learns a hierarchy of increasingly fine-grained subcategories. The model is hierarchical in that it learns relationships between categories in the form of a directed graph. The graph structure captures both the hierarchical relationships between the subcategories as well as the associations between subcategories at different levels of the hierarchy. The model is human-like in that it replicates key cognitive and neural characteristics of category learning in humans. Specifically, we show that Cobweb replicates the learning trajectories of human participants as they learn categories in a hierarchical concept learning task. We also show that the model produces neural activation patterns that are similar to those observed in human neuroimaging data. Finally, we demonstrate that Cobweb can be used to make sense of real-world data by applying it to learn the structure of the U.S. political spectrum.

Models of computations over the integers are equivalent from a computability and complexity theory point of view, but some computational models may be more suitable for certain applications. The algebraic structure underlying these different approaches is usually ignored in discussions about their equivalence or superiority to others; however it can prove useful when comparing them against each other with regard not only as algorithms themselves (or equivalently programs) which implement such structures). We study this question by examining two well-known examples: finite field arithmetic operations versus integer addition modulo 2<sup>64</sup> .

Despite the success of the carefully-annotated benchmarks, the effectiveness of existing graph neural networks (GNNs) is limited by their rigid structures and lack robustness to noise. In this work we introduce a novel GCN architecture that allows for an automatic selection between multiple candidate kernels at each layer based on its input features while still preserving efficient message passing in both time complexity as well messages per node computation cost compared with previous approaches like Graph Convolutional Network Architectures [1]. Our approach significantly improves over stateofart methods across diverse benchmark datasets including those where there are no groundtruth labels available or when training data has missing nodes/edges dueto incomplete information about connect

Time Series Generation (TSG) has emerged as a pivotal technique in synthesizing synthetic time series data for applications such as forecasting, anomaly detection and model training. Current TSG methods typically generate sequences of discrete values which are not interpretable or can be misleading when used with continuous measurements from real-world sensors that exhibit high variance over short periods; this is especially true under noisy conditions where small fluctuations may lead to large differences between generated samples due their dependence on initial seed inputs making it difficult predict future states accurately during inference phase leading potential problems down line like misinterpretation false alarms etc., thus motivating us create novel generative framework called Spa

Novel view synthesis for dynamic scenes is a challenging problem, due to the appearance of motion blur. Existing methods try to address this by modeling the motion blur effect, usually by integrating information from multiple frames. However, integrating information from too many frames leads to an increase in computation cost, while integrating information from too few frames results in a lack of motion smoothness. To solve this dilemma, we propose direction-aware representation (DaRe), which represents the scene information in a direction-aware manner. In this way, we can obtain sharp and smooth results with only information from two frames. Specifically, we design direction embedding to encode the motion direction and speed of each pixel. Then, we use a masked aggregation approach to combine the current and the previous frame based on the direction embedding. To further improve the quality of the synthesized views, we propose a motion compensation attention module to explicitly learn the motion compensation weights for neighboring pixels. Experiments on DynamicNMR and BOP show that our method outperforms state-of-the-art methods in terms of both objective metrics and user study. The implementation is available at https://github.com/ErlerPhilipp/DaReNeRF.

Camouflage poses challenges in distinguishing a static target, whereas any movement of the object or its surroundings can reveal it. We have investigated how camouflage is broken when an animal moves, and found that animals are not always aware they are moving until after their body parts have moved past them (a 'kinesthetic blind spot'). This results in delayed responses to avoid obstacles while walking through dense foliage: our experiments suggest these delays could be as long 20 seconds for some species - potentially leading prey items into danger zones before being spotted by predators!

Despite the success of the carefully-annotated benchmarks, the effectiveness of existing graph neural networks (GNNs) is still far from satisfactory. One reason is that the annotated data in these benchmarks are not comprehensive enough to cover all aspects of real-world graphs. The other reason is that the existing GNNs cannot handle high-order graph structures well. To address these issues, we propose a new benchmark that contains 10 large-scale datasets with diverse types of graphs, including social graphs, knowledge graphs, biological graphs, and transportation graphs. Moreover, we introduce a powerful and general method called HODM for learning on high-order graphs based on the diffusion operation. Experiments show that our HODM achieves state-of-the-art performance on various tasks.

Tools are essential for large language models (LLMs) to acquire up-to-date knowledge, but they also introduce the risk of overfitting. In this work we investigate a novel method that allows LLMs to update their knowledge without needing access to the underlying tool. Our approach involves training an LLM on unstructured data which is then used to generate textual instructions for tools. These instructions can be used by other LLMs to retrieve information from different sources and build new knowledge without requiring any additional tool-specific expertise. We demonstrate the effectiveness of our approach through two experiments: first, we show how it enables a model to learn about the world even when the toolset is not available; second, we show that models can use our method to learn mor

We study the computational complexity of several polynomial-time-solvable graph problems parameterized by vertex connectivity. Our main result is that every monotone threshold function can be computed on an $f$-connected graph in time $O(n^{1-\delta})$ for some constant $\delta>0$ (for a class of functions that includes parity and majority). The best previous bound was exponential, while our current approach requires logarithmic space. We also obtain several other results, such as: if $k\geq 2$, then computing $k$-core or $k$-clique can be done in time $n^{\mathcal{O}(k)}$. This improves upon the known upper bounds which are linear when $k=2$.

Large Language Models (LLMs) have demonstrated exceptional performance in biochemical tasks, such as drug design. However, the accuracy of LLMs in predicting protein-protein interactions (PPIs) is limited by their inability to comprehend the underlying biological concepts and relationships. In this paper, we introduce a novel methodology for utilizing LLMs within an integrated framework that extends beyond simple question answering and enables them to engage in active learning through iterative prompt-based fine-tuning. Our approach involves prompt engineering and active learning based on PPI prediction errors, which enhances LLMs' proficiency at PPI prediction. We use the recently released Biomolecule-AI Benchmark to evaluate our method. The results demonstrate significant improvement ove

This study conducts a thorough examination of malware detection using machine learning techniques, focusing on the impact of different data preprocessing methods and feature selection strategies. Experimentation was conducted on four datasets comprising 2190 samples: WEB-DAVOS, MAD-Labs, PE-Worm, and PE-Flaws. The results indicate that the feature selection technique significantly affects the accuracy of the model, with the best performance achieved by Random Forest Classifier (RFC) and Gradient Boosting Classifier (GBC). The experimental findings also reveal the importance of data preprocessing in malware classification, highlighting the crucial role played by data preprocessing and feature selection. Furthermore, this investigation underscores the vital significance of evaluating the gen

Used car pricing is a challenging regression task that involves many nonlinear and interacting features, such as the make, model, year of manufacture, and mileage. Pricing models need to account for these complexities to avoid losing money on sales or missing out on potential buyers. Tree-based models and neural networks are commonly used for their ability to learn complex relationships between features. However, they lack transparency, making it difficult to understand the impact of individual features on the final price. In this paper, we propose Probabilistic Tabular Regression (ProbSAINT), a transparent regression model that quantifies the impact of each feature on the car price. ProbSAINT is a tabular model that incorporates probabilistic principles, assigning a probability distribution to each prediction. This distribution reflects the uncertainty of the prediction, helping to avoid overconfidence. The model is trained using a Bayesian approach, considering a prior distribution for the model parameters and computing the posterior distribution given the training data. We evaluate ProbSAINT against a neural network and a decision tree on a publicly available used car dataset. ProbSAINT achieves competitive performance in terms of mean squared error and shows the ability to provide more accurate confidence intervals for predictions. The feature importance scores computed by the model are also more interpretable than those of the neural network.

Facial Action Unit (AU) detection is a crucial but challenging task in facial expression recognition. The inherent small inter-class discrepancy within facial AUs makes it difficult for a network to learn them in a discriminative manner. Meanwhile, different distributions of facial AUs exist among individuals, which leads to a need for person-independent representations. In this paper, we propose a novel person-independent contrastive learning method, termed \textit{Facial AU-independent Contrastive Learning} (FAC), to learn person-independent representations for facial AU detection. Specifically, we propose a new facial landmark-based method, termed \textit{Facial Centralization}, to reduce the impact of facial orientation variations. Furthermore, we adopt a two-branch network structure, consisting of a common path and individual paths. This design enables the network to learn person-independent features from the common path while accommodating individual differences through the individual paths. To evaluate the performance of our proposed method, we conduct extensive experiments using three facial AU detection datasets, namely FER+, FER, and EMOTIC. Encouraging results demonstrate that our approach outperforms existing person-independent methods for facial AU detection, highlighting the effectiveness of our proposed FAC method.

This work explores an innovative algorithm designed to enhance the mobility of underactuated robotic systems, which could be applied to a wide range applications. A novel approach based on adaptive impedance control and neural network-based learning is proposed for this purpose in order provide self adaptability with respect both external disturbances as well internal friction changes due wearover time during operation; these features allow robots equippedwith such capabilities achieve optimal performance despite their lackof actuators or joints - making them ideal candidatesfor tasks where precision movements are required but actuation power limited . The design process began by selecting suitable candidate structures from among those available through existing literature before conductin

Feature selection plays a crucial role in various machine learning tasks. In this work, we propose a novel knockoff-guided feature selection framework through a single pre-trained reinforced agent. We leverage the power of reinforcement learning to develop an agent that can evaluate the quality of features and select the most relevant ones. In particular, we design a knockoff-guided reward function to guide the agent in selecting the important features. We also propose using a pre-trained agent to reduce the sample complexity and computational cost of the feature selection process. Furthermore, we employ a novel feature grouping mechanism to enhance the interpretability of the selected features. We compare our method to several state-of-the-art feature selection methods and show that our method consistently outperforms these baselines in terms of selection accuracy, model accuracy, and interpretability.

Prompt learning has emerged as a valuable technique in enhancing vision-language models (VLMs) by providing fine-tuning instructions to guide their training. However, prompt engineering remains an open-ended challenge due to the lack of reliable guidance and high cost of expert designers' involvement. To address this issue, we propose P2L, which stands for "Prompt-to-Label", introduces a new paradigm of prompt engineering that is guided by label-conditioned prompts. Specifically, we employ a semi-supervised objective, namely "label consistency loss" across two different stages: firstly, the model is finetuned with a fixed prompt based on a small set of labeled examples; secondly, another prompt is designed by leveraging the model parameters learned from the previous stage.

Training a robotic policy from scratch using deep reinforcement learning methods can be prohibitively expensive. Prior work has proposed to learn the task-relevant dynamics of a simulated robot from raw high-dimensional state and action data collected in simulation, then use these learned dynamics for imitation learning (IL) or inverse reinforcement learning (IRL). However, current approaches often rely on a fixed number of discrete latent factors to describe the dynamics, which leads to poor performance when the true dynamics are continuous. In this paper we propose to directly learn continuous dynamics of a simulated robot using an autoencoder, and show that these learned dynamics can be used effectively for IL/IRL with a small amount of expert demonstrations. We demonstrate the effectiv

We present Spectrum AUC Difference (SAUCD), a novel 3D shape analysis framework aligned with human perception. SAUCD compares two shapes by first transforming them into the Fourier frequency domain, then computing the Area Under the Receiver Operating Characteristic Curve (AUC) of each frequency domain entry, and finally taking the difference of the two shapes' AUCs. Our human study results show that SAUCD better aligns with human perception than existing shape comparison methods. Further, we show that SAUCD can be used for unsupervised 3D shape clustering, without the need for ground truth labels. Our code is available at https://github.com/3DVillagers/saucd.

The field of robotics faces inherent challenges in manipulating deformable objects, particularly in medical and industrial settings. To address these limitations, a novel framework known as Soft-Coded Robot (SCR) has emerged, which utilizes both soft actuators and neural networks for control. This paper delves into the research topic of SCRs, exploring their recent advancements, various applications, and potential future directions. Through comprehensive literature review and analysis, we identify four key pillars that characterize this burgeoning field: learning from demonstrations, model-based control, motion planning, and robustness in unstructured environments. We also discuss open research opportunities and provide insights on how to overcome common obstacles faced by SCRs, such as li

The rapidly increasing capabilities of large language models (LLMs) raise an urgent need to align them with human values. The development of LLMs, however, is largely driven by metrics that are not necessarily aligned with the goal of promoting human values. In this paper, we propose a new evaluation metric called Value-Aligned Measurement of Language Model Efficiency (VAMOLE), which aims to evaluate the alignment between model performance and human values. VAMOLE integrates two components: (i) a measure of model efficiency based on the predictive loss in language generation tasks; and (ii) a value alignment score derived from a preference-based decision-making method. We compare different models using VAMOLE, finding that LLAMA performs better than other models when evaluating values such

Can machines reason? This fundamental question in artificial intelligence (AI) has been debated for decades, with some arguing that AI reasoning is just around the corner and others claiming that it is impossible. In this paper, we analyze the current state-of-the-art in machine reasoning and ask: "Given the latest advances in AI, is intelligent reasoning possible?" To answer this question, we adopt a historical perspective and compare the current state-of-the-art with past predictions. We find that while progress has been faster than anticipated, current machines are still far from intelligent reasoning. Our analysis suggests that before we can reasonably expect machines to engage in intelligent reasoning, we must first develop algorithms that can reason about visual concepts at a high level of abstraction.

Imagine generating a city's electricity demand pattern based on weather, the presence of an electric vehicle charger at a home, and how often that resident charges their vehicle. This is what our machine learning model does. In this project, we use the historical data from Pecan Street (https://pecanstreetinc.org/), a smart grid research facility in Austin, Texas, to train a long short-term memory network (LSTM) to predict residential load time series. We use several features including outside temperature, wind speed, solar radiation, and occupancy status to make predictions. The model can generate a one-day ahead prediction with high accuracy - 97% correlation with actual load. This enables us to forecast future energy demands by feeding in weather forecasts for the coming day and night.

The isometric mapping method employs the shortest path algorithm to estimate the Euclidean distance between two 3D shapes. In this work, we introduce a novel geometric approach that utilizes the angle of intersection (AoI) and point-to-point distance (P2P) to accurately measure the shape similarity between two 3D objects. Our proposed approach introduces a unified AoI and P2P measurement function, which is an improvement over existing methods. We have demonstrated our method's efficiency in estimating AoI and P2P values for different shapes, particularly on the IQM dataset, where it achieved an accuracy of 94%. Moreover, our method exhibits superior performance compared to the closest object approach in estimating AoI and P2P values. Our approach has potential applications in various field

With growing popularity, deep learning (DL) models are becoming larger-scale, and only the most advanced hardware can meet their demands. To this end we propose a novel design flow for DL accelerator architectures that is based on an innovative mix of high level synthesis tools with machine intelligence techniques such as genetic algorithms to optimize power efficiency while keeping model accuracy intact across different target devices from commodity FPGAs up through custom ASICs using 16nm technology node at maximum frequency clock speed achieved during testing stage before deployment onto real world applications like self driving cars or autonomous robots etc., which require fast responses times in order not get stuck when making decisions about what action should be taken next given cur

On-demand controlled drug delivery is essential for the treatment of a wide range of chronic disea‐ses. The development and application in medicine, engineering or biotechnology require systems that are able to deliver drugs with high accuracy on demand but without any external intervention such as pumps/actuators [1–3]. In this context we present an intelligent autonomous system based upon microfluidics principles which can be operated by simple physical stimuli (e. g., light) from outside its environment ([4], Figure 2). Such smart biochips have been demonstrated using different techniques including chemical sensors,[5] optical tweezers[6],[7](laser trapping), magnetic actuation methods like electromagnets.[8][9].

Optical Transport Networks (OTNs) based on wavelength-division multiplexing (WDM) technology have been commercially deployed for more than two decades. To provide flexible and reliable optical transport services, OTN-over-WDM networks require robust wavelength assignment and protection strategies, which ensure that the traffic demand between any two OTN nodes can be satisfied with a pre-specified level of service, even in the presence of network faults. Traffic grooming is a key enabling technology for robust OTN-over-WDM networks, as it allows the aggregation of traffic flows onto suitable numbers of wavelengths, with minimal additional bandwidth overhead. In this paper, we extend our previous work on deterministic traffic grooming in OTN-over-WDM networks to the probabilistic setting, where link failures occur according to an exogenous failure process, and propose a generalized robust traffic grooming problem. We prove that the minimum expected additional bandwidth overhead required to achieve a given level of service protection in a probabilistic setting is equal to the maximum additional bandwidth overhead required to block a set of mutually exclusive traffic flows in the deterministic setting. Based on this result, we propose a polynomial-time algorithm for computing optimal robust traffic groomings in OTN-over-WDM networks, and investigate the impact of fiber nonuniformity on the minimum expected additional bandwidth overhead.

In this work, we propose a generative approach to news recommendation that aims to overcome the overspecialization problem in the long tail of news categories. A neural topic model learns to generate text for unseen topics, while a neural news recommender simultaneously learns to predict the relevance of seen and unseen topics based on user history. We introduce a novel aspect of news recommendation, termed topic discovery, that evaluates a model's ability to recommend relevant topics to users. We compare our generative approach to a state-of-the-art news recommendation system and show that it outperforms the state-of-the-art on both seen and unseen topics. We also show that topic discovery performance improves with more training data and longer user history.

Poor sleep health is an increasingly concerning public healthcare crisis, especially when coupled with a sedentary lifestyle. The current study examined the relationship between self-reported daily sitting time and objective measures of total duration (hours) spent in bed as well how this differs across age groups using data from 2017/8 National Health Survey Wave C3 sample consisting primarily Aboriginal people living on remote Australian communities or urban centres (>65% Indigenous population). A secondary aim was to examine whether sleeping late into early morning hours had greater impact on estimated risk for poor metabolic outcomes than waking up earlier compared to those who wakeup at usual times but have shorter overall durations (<4hrs )inbed during nighttime rest periods .

Cross-domain recommendation (CDR) aims to enhance recommendation accuracy in a target domain with the help of auxiliary knowledge from other domains. This paper introduces a novel graph-based CDR method that employs an auxiliary graph to model the co-purchasing relationships between items and users across different domains. By leveraging the graph's inherent hierarchical structure, we introduce two types of auxiliary nodes -- the item node and user node -- which allow us to accurately identify the correlations between item and user behaviors. Specifically, we employ two types of adjacency matrixes for constructing the graph -- one is constructed using only user-item interactions within each domain, while the other considers user-user interactions among all domains. We also propose a new we

DST systems aim to track the state of a dialogue by automatically summarizing the history of the dialogue. However, existing methods are deficient in that they either lack attention to the internal state transition process of the DST system or lack consideration of the grammatical coherence of the generated state descriptions. To address these problems, we introduce a novel Chain of Thought explanation approach for DST, termed CoCoDST, which enforces coherent state transitions and simultaneously produces more accurate and grammatically correct state descriptions. Specifically, our approach follows the design principles of chain-of-thought prompting, where the model is prompted to explicitly verbalize the intermediate reasoning steps. For DST, these steps can be naturally organized as a sequence of state transitions. We show that forcing the model to explicitly verbalize the state transitions induces grammatically correct state descriptions that better capture the true dialogue state. Our proposed CoCoDST approach achieves state-of-the-art performance on the benchmark datasets of both DST and CoCoDST. The code is available at https://github.com/yuanpeng16/CoCoDST.

The problem of matrix completion is to recover a low-rank matrix from a subset of its entries. This problem has been extensively studied in recent years, and various efficient algorithms have been proposed. However, existing methods typically suffer from high time complexity when the matrix size is large. Meanwhile, though column subset selection has been applied to reduce the time complexity of matrix completion, the corresponding algorithm suffers from a high space complexity. To address these challenges, we propose a novel algorithm that combines convex optimization and column subset selection for matrix completion. The proposed algorithm not only has a much lower time complexity but also requires less space. We prove that the proposed algorithm can recover any $n\times n$ matrix with a high probability, provided that the subset of entries satisfies a certain geometric condition and the algorithm selects at least $O(n\log n)$ columns. Furthermore, we show that the proposed algorithm outperforms existing methods in numerical experiments.

The IoT market is diverse and characterized by a multitude of vendors that support different device types, communication protocols, application environments, etc. This makes it difficult for system integrators to find suitable solutions for their customers, as they have no access to information about the products offered by other suppliers or cannot evaluate the suitability of these products on their own due to technical limitations. As a result, integrators often resort to custom development solutions which are expensive and time-consuming. To overcome this problem, an open marketplace for IoT devices and services has been developed within the framework of the EU project "IoT4EU".

Autistic adults often experience stigma and discrimination at work, leading them to seek social communication support from coworkers, friends, and family despite emotional risks. Large language models (LLMs) are increasingly considered an alternative. In this work, we investigate the phenomenon of LLM use by autistic adults at work and explore opportunities and risks of LLMs as a source of social communication advice. We asked 11 autistic participants to present questions about their own workplace-related social difficulties to (1) a GPT-4-based chatbot and (2) a disguised human confederate. Our evaluation shows that participants strongly preferred LLM over confederate interactions. However, a coach specializing in supporting autistic job-seekers raised concerns that the LLM was dispensing

Visual understanding and segmentation of materials and their states is fundamental for understanding the physical world. The infinite variety of shapes, textures, colors, and states of the material objects presents a formidable challenge to autonomous agents. In this work, we introduce a novel dataset, MaterialNet that contains 1.5 million images and 160K annotations for material and material state classification. We propose an end-to-end deep neural network architecture that leverages the self-supervised pretext task and can be trained in an unsupervised fashion using our dataset. Our approach significantly outperforms other existing methods in both unsupervised and supervised settings. Finally, we explore the use of our model for material-aware image generation. We show that our method g

With the escalating threats posed by cyberattacks on Industrial Control Systems (ICS), it is crucial to implement a robust security solution. This comprehensive guide aims to address the growing concerns surrounding ICS cybersecurity, offering insights into the challenges and strategies for mitigating these risks. We will examine the unique characteristics of ICS networks, the evolution of cyber threats targeting them, and how security measures can be effectively deployed. Furthermore, we delve into various aspects of ICS cybersecurity, including network segmentation, secure remote access, threat detection, incident response plans, and continuous monitoring. In addition, we explore the potential of emerging technologies such as Artificial Intelligence (AI) and Machine Learning (ML) in enha

Session data understanding (SDU) is a crucial task in various industrial applications. It aims to predict the intent of the current session based on historical session data and external knowledge. Current approaches focus on leveraging the sequence information within sessions, which is insufficient for capturing the user's complete intent. In this paper, we propose a novel contrastive pre-training method, termed Session Contrastive Pre-training (SCP), to enhance SDU. The proposed method SCP introduces two innovative techniques, namely session continuation and negative session sampling, to generate contrastive samples. These techniques enable the model to better distinguish relevant and irrelevant sessions, improving the quality of the pre-training process. We evaluate our method on three benchmark datasets, using both pre-trained and fine-tuned models. Experimental results demonstrate the superiority of our method over existing state-of-the-art baselines, achieving an improvement of up to 9.6% in terms of mean recall at 20 (MR@20).

We present TextMonkey, a large multimodal model (LMM) tailored for monkey-based studies. TextMonkey is built upon the foundation of ChatGLM-6B and Llama-2-70B, with an additional 30B parameters dedicated to monkey-related knowledge and text-to-speech generation. Our model demonstrates remarkable performance in a wide range of tasks, including human-monkey communication, primate psychology, behavior analysis, and experimental design. The TextMonkey can be used as a virtual monkey, offering insights into monkey cognition and social dynamics. Additionally, it serves as a valuable tool for researchers conducting experiments involving monkeys. The model can provide a detailed report based on the user's input, enabling researchers to analyze the results more efficiently. Furthermore, it can simu

This paper questions the marginalisation of non-verbal information in current computational models of spontaneous speech. We argue that the neglect of non-verbal information is due to the lack of appropriate analytical tools. To demonstrate the importance of non-verbal information, we present a new framework for its analysis, based on the concept of event. We show that the temporal organisation of non-verbal events constitutes a layer of meaning, which is integral to the complete understanding of the speaker's message. We demonstrate the feasibility of our approach by analysing transcripts of 90 spontaneous conversations, focusing on the non-verbal events that co-occur with disfluent verbal expressions. We present the results of a qualitative analysis of these co-occurrences, showing that non-verbal events often provide contextual information, which helps the listener to interpret the disfluent verbal expression. We also show that non-verbal events can be exploited to build predictive models of verbal disfluency.

Recent work has embodied LLMs as agents, allowing them to access tools, perform actions, and make decisions. While these agents can generate code, they struggle when faced with complex tasks requiring knowledge of the world and its dynamics. In this work, we propose a new approach that allows an agent to plan by interacting directly with a large language model, providing it with a richer understanding of the world. To enable planning in LLMs, we create a new task-solving interface that enables agents to ask for predictions about the effects of actions on different parts of the world. We evaluate our framework on a range of real-world tasks involving navigation, object manipulation, and tool use and find that our approach outperforms existing alternatives. Finally, we show that our method i

Distance metric learning (DML) is a powerful tool for anomaly detection (AD). With a good distance metric, we can measure the abnormality of an observation by its distance to the normal cluster in the feature space. Most existing DML algorithms are supervised methods that need a lot of normal samples, which is not available in the real world. To reduce the reliance on normal samples, we propose an unsupervised DML algorithm for anomaly detection over multivariate time series (UMTSD). Specifically, we first propose a new triplet loss to learn the distance metric from multi-triplet samples, where each triplet consists of one normal and two abnormal samples. We next propose a new mechanism to dynamically adjust the abnormality threshold for each class of UMTSD. This threshold is based on the distribution of the learned distance metric. The proposed UMTSD method outperforms the existing unsupervised baseline and achieves a competitive performance compared to the supervised DML algorithms.

We propose SnapNTell, a retrieval augmented multimodal framework to answer questions about real-world objects in the visual domain. Our approach conditions a pre-trained multimodal language model on an external database of multimodal snapshots of real-world objects that provides context about the object, its attributes, and associated questions. We show that SnapNTell outperforms retrieval-free multimodal models and achieves state-of-the-art performance on Visual Genome and ObjectQ.

This paper presents an efficient method for updating particles in a particle filter (PF) to address the positioning of robots. The proposed approach utilizes a Gaussian process latent variable model (GPLVM) to estimate the robot's state by incorporating the robot's motion and sensor noise into the GPLVM. To further improve the accuracy, we introduce a novel concept called "influence region" to limit the estimation range for each sample point. As a result, the overall estimation error is significantly reduced compared to conventional methods, resulting in more accurate positioning. Additionally, this method effectively resolves the issue of particle degeneration, which is a common challenge in PF-based positioning systems. Moreover, the influence region is adaptive to the environment, allow

Large Language Models (LLM) like GPT-4 have recently demonstrated impressive results on various tasks. There are concerns that LLMs may pose a threat to the programming community, especially to coders who cannot afford expensive LLMs and to the integrity of code. For example, could GPT-4 have authored the solution to a programming contest problem? To address these concerns, we need to investigate the characteristics of GPT-4 generated code and compare them to human authored code. In this paper, we conduct a case study on CodeChef, a popular online programming contest platform. We download the source code of the top 50 solutions of popular CodeChef problems solved by humans and GPT-4 and classify them as human or GPT-4 generated using a survey-based study. We analyze the survey results and present statistical evidence that GPT-4 generated code can be distinguished from human authored code based on its syntactic and semantic characteristics. We also present a framework to calculate an upper bound on the perplexity of a given programming problem that helps coders choose between GPT-4 generated and human authored solutions.

The human-centered artificial intelligence (HCAI) design approach, the user-centered design methodology adopted in artificial intelligence (AI), focuses on creating AI that is both useful and acceptable to people. HCAI aims to ensure that AI technology aligns with user needs and values, particularly when it involves sensitive data or has the potential for significant impacts on people's lives. This paper reviews existing research on the HCAI design approach, highlighting its significance and challenges. We identify key areas for future research, including a deeper understanding of user experiences, the integration of technical and ethical considerations, and the creation of open source tools and resources. Our review contributes to the ongoing development of HCAI as an evolving discipline

This paper describes the deployment and experimentation architecture of the Internet of Things experimentation facility being deployed at UCLan, UK. The facility is a research infrastructure that will be available to students, academics, industry partners, and public bodies for conducting IoT research activities in an industrial environment. It consists of sensors embedded in different objects (such as streetlights or benches) and nodes, which act as gateway devices, and are connected to a local network through Wi-Fi or cellular connections. In this paper we present the hardware components employed in the facility, their technical specifications, the operational system used to control them, the tools used for monitoring and analysing the collected data, and the interfaces for communication

Streaming live 360-degree video content to mobile devices is challenging, as it requires predicting the viewport that the user will interact with and delivering only that portion of the video to save bandwidth and improve the user experience. Under-predicting the viewport can result in missed interactions, while over-predicting it leads to unnecessary bandwidth usage. Existing research in this area has focused on desktop viewers, and their mobile counterparts have used a simple heuristic approach. In this paper, we propose a machine learning-based approach to predict the viewport for live 360-degree videos viewed on mobile devices. We extract features from live 360-degree video streams and train a machine learning model to predict the viewport based on these features. Through an extensive set of experiments, we show that our approach improves on the state-of-the-art desktop and mobile baselines in terms of both performance and resource utilization. Our work is a step towards providing personalized viewing experiences on mobile devices.

Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) have garnered significant attention for their success in capturing the rich topological structure of data. However, GCN models are limited to small-sized datasets due large computational costs and memory requirements when processing graphs with high connectivity or larger sizes at inference time; this is particularly challenging given that graph size increases exponentially as more edges/nodes added respectively! Fortunately recent research has provided innovative solutions including efficient architectures like Chebyshev Spectral Graph Convolutional Layers which can be combined into a single layer achieving near optimal results without sacrificing accuracy significantly while reducing computation by up 50%. Additionally novel algorithms such A* Search Algorith

Multimodal Large Language Model (MLLMs) leverages Large Language Models (LLMs) and multimodal data to improve performance in various downstream tasks, such as visual question answering. In particular, visual question answering (VQA), a fundamental task of Vision-Language Interpretation (VLI), plays an important role in the development of LLM-based systems for diverse applications. However, existing ML models for VQA often face challenges with limited training datasets that contain relatively few images or questions, especially for high-quality images with complex semantics, resulting in suboptimal performance. To address this issue, we propose a novel ML approach, called VQAL, which utilizes a multi-task learning framework to train a single model on both VQA and Image Captioning (IC).

Smartphones are integral to modern life, yet research highlights the cognitive drawbacks of using them. These include reduced attention span and impaired memory for everyday information (e-mail addresses; phone numbers). Here we investigate a potential new explanation: that smartphones disrupt our ability efficiently process incoming messages as they arrive in real time on screen ("message bursts"). We tested this by measuring message burst processing speed across 130 young adults with varying smartphone usage habits while having participants process fake text or email notifications containing numerical sequences presented at different rates per second during silent meditation sessions lasting up from one minute over four days via an iOS app called Insight Timer Pro Meditation App [https:/

Distance-based time series anomaly detection methods are prevalent due to their relative non-parametric nature. However, such methods lack a temporal component which is critical in identifying anomalies that occur over multiple points in time and may span days or weeks. To address this gap, we propose a novel distance-based approach for detecting multi-dimensional anomalies in time series data using cross-series correlations. We demonstrate that our method can effectively identify anomalous patterns that span long periods of time, significantly outperforming traditional approaches. We show that the proposed approach can be applied across a range of time series datasets with various characteristics, demonstrating its versatility and effectiveness. The code for our experiments is available a

Large-scale geospatial data visualization is an essential tool for many applications, allowing users to derive meaning from vast amounts of spatially referenced data. One common challenge is the presence of temporal data, which can be difficult to visualize effectively. A useful technique is to identify salient time steps within the data, i.e., the steps that contain the most information or are the most different from other steps. These steps can then be examined in more detail. However, the computation of salient time steps is usually a computationally expensive task, which becomes intractable as the size of the dataset grows. In this paper, we present SalienTime, a novel approach that enables interactive selection of salient time steps from large-scale geospatial datasets. SalienTime leverages a spatial hashing technique to efficiently compute a set of candidate salient time steps that covers the entire dataset. It then presents these candidates to the user, who can interactively evaluate and refine the selection based on spatial-temporal interests. We demonstrate the effectiveness of SalienTime for selecting salient time steps in two common geospatial data visualization applications: exploring spatio-temporal heatmaps and trajectory visualization. Through quantitative and qualitative evaluations, we show that SalienTime is an effective tool for users to interactively explore large-scale geospatial data.

Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback (\textbf{RLHF}) has emerged as a promising approach to help agents learn in challenging, realistic settings with scarce data and human supervision. However,\linebreak \textendash the task of generating effective feedback remains an open challenge.\vspace{-0mm} \\ In this work we propose $A^2$, which incorporates two novel components into RL training: (1) A new model-based agent that utilizes off policy learning techniques such as Generalized Advantage Estimation\citeyear{\model_agent};\\(3)~An interactive curriculum tailored for each individual learner by employing reinforcement sampling based on past interactions.\\$^{4}$~\emph{$^*$\footnote[5]{Equal contribution.}}

This paper presents a novel framework for the cognitive profiling of typefaces, founded on the hypothesis that legibility is rooted in cognitive processes. The framework was developed through a three-step research process, beginning with a literature review of cognitive science literature on reading and legibility (Step 1). This was followed by a survey of 144 participants, designed to map typeface characteristics to cognitive processes (Step 2). Finally, we conducted a follow-up survey with 87 participants to test the robustness of the findings and explore individual differences (Step 3). Our results provide insights into the cognitive processes involved in reading and suggest that typefaces can be designed to better support these processes, potentially improving legibility and readability. We present the findings in the context of current theories of reading and propose several applications for designers, educators, and researchers.

Consider a binary statistical hypothesis testing problem, where $n$ independent and identically distributed random variables $X_i$, $1\le i \le n$, are observed. The goal is to test whether these observations originate from the null or alternative hypotheses, based on a given testing criterion. In this paper, we propose an efficient method for solving this task that leverages the capabilities of large language models (LLMs). Our approach involves pre-training a LLM in order to build the capability of generating the decision rule directly without requiring any further post-processing of the output. We evaluate our model on 72 synthetic datasets with various parameter settings including different sample sizes ($n$) and dimensionalities ($d$), as well as different distributions under each hyp

Large language models (LLMs) such as ChatGPT are increasingly proficent at generating text, but they can also be used for malicious purposes. To mitigate the risk of harmful use cases and potential misuse by bad actors in general society or within organizations themselves—for example: using LLMS to generate fake news articles with false information about competitors' products; creating misleading content intended specifically towards vulnerable populations like children via social media platforms where users often do not know who is behind an account until after interacting directly through direct messaging functionality available on many popular sites today - we need effective solutions that address both technical challenges associated when trying apply traditional security methods across

Lexicon-based constrained decoding approaches aim to control the meaning or style of the generated text by exploiting linguistic knowledge. However, these methods are often limited to discrete constraints due to the lack of a continuous representation for semantic information. In this paper, we propose a novel approach that integrates an existing encoder-decoder framework with a continuous-valued latent space. Our model is equipped with two separate decoders: one for generating sentences and another for predicting a set of real-valued latent variables corresponding to a given constraint. To ensure consistency between generated text and latent representations, we introduce a novel loss function that minimizes the difference between predicted and true latent variables during training. We eva

We propose a multi-agent system that enables groups of agents to collaborate and work autonomously to complete complex tasks. However, the problem of communication overhead among these agents remains unresolved, as each agent must transmit its decisions to other agents in order to achieve cooperative performance. This paper introduces an adaptive communication model for multi-agent systems called ACOM (Adaptive Communication Model). The proposed scheme dynamically adjusts the transmission frequency and payload size based on the current state of the environment and the decision of the agents. Furthermore, we provide an analytical approach for estimating the transmission cost when using ACOM under certain conditions. Finally, we conduct extensive experiments with various environments and age

Suppose we are given a text $T [1..n]$, a straight-line program with $m$ instructions, and two queries: given an instruction $i$, compute the smallest index of a character that appears in $i$ before the last line number in $i$ (if any), and given a character $c$, find all lines containing it. The standard solution is to preprocess $T$ for suffix tree and LCP arrays, then construct a balanced search tree for each instruction and a balanced tree for each character. This takes $O(mn \log n)$ time and $O(mn + \sigma)$ space. We show how to solve this problem faster and using less space. Our main result is a data structure on the string $T$ that uses $O(n + m)$ space and supports both queries in $O(\log^2 n)$ time.

Facial Action Units (AU) is a vital concept in the realm of affective computing, as it has been shown to be an effective tool for recognizing emotional states. In this paper we present our experiments on AU recognition using deep neural networks with multiple channels and gated recurrent units that aim at combining information from both facial videos or images along time sequences while preserving spatial features extracted by convolutional layers within each frame individually; all these combined produce high accuracy rates when compared against existing methods which only consider single frames separately without taking advantage off their temporal nature during training phases . We have developed new method based upon fusion between two different types: one utilizing face detection algo

This paper addresses the challenges of aligning large language models (LLMs) with human values and preferences. We introduce a novel framework for evaluating and optimizing LLM alignment with human preferences using subjective and objective criteria, including diversity, consistency, comprehensiveness, and accuracy. Our experiments show that existing methods struggle to meet these criteria at scale, and that the optimal configurations vary by domain, task, and dataset. We propose an adaptive framework for fine-tuning LLMs, which can be applied both before and after optimization, leading to a significant improvement in overall performance, as measured by our evaluation metrics. Our results indicate that adaptive fine-tuning combined with a careful selection of hyperparameters is key to achi

Context: Roblox Studio lets millions of creators build interactive experiences by programming in a variant of Lua. A new feature called "Scripts" allows users to program in JavaScript and Python, while still running within the same runtime sandbox as existing Roblox games. We set out to understand how these new languages have changed the development experience for creators, from the beginning stages of prototyping through to production. Method: We interviewed 12 creators, ranging from novices with less than two years of experience to veterans with over six years of experience. We also observed these creators using Scripts to create games, allowing us to identify their goals, pain points, and strategies for solving problems. Results: Scripts allow creators to prototype new ideas faster than

We propose Definition-Enriched Experts (DEEP-ICL), a novel framework for improving language model in-context learning (ICL) through definition-based knowledge distillation. Unlike existing distillation approaches that focus on directly transferring model parameters, DEEP-ICL enrichs the ICL process with domain knowledge represented in definition graphs. This enhancement empowers the language model to better understand and generate responses that are contextually appropriate and semantically meaningful. We evaluate our method on a benchmark of 100 natural language inference datasets and achieve an impressive 6.7% improvement in accuracy. Additionally, we confirm that DEEP-ICL enhances the performance of ICL when deployed in real-world applications, without the need for retraining. Our code is publicly available at https://github.com/asyml/deep-icl.

Autonomous Driving Systems (ADSs) aim to provide safe, comfortable, and efficient transportation. However, current ADSs often fail to adapt to users' dynamic preferences, such as varying comfort tolerances and destination changes, leading to sub-optimal driving styles and missed opportunities for energy savings. To address this limitation, this paper introduces a novel user-driven adaptation approach to dynamically tailor ADSs to users' changing preferences. The approach leverages a driving behavior model that captures a driver's comfort preference and updates it in real-time based on the driver's latest feedback. The latest comfort preference is then used to re-optimize the ADS's driving strategy, ensuring alignment with the user's current needs. The effectiveness of the proposed approach is validated through a two-pronged case study involving a real-world dataset and a simulation platform. The results demonstrate that the proposed method can effectively adapt to users' dynamic preferences, resulting in improved driving comfort and fuel efficiency. The method also exhibits robustness against inaccurate preference predictions.

Neural machine translation (MT) models achieve strong results across a variety of settings, but it is still challenging to model unseen words and phrases that appear infrequently in the training data. To address this problem we introduce an encoder-decoder architecture called \textsc{Coda} which combines two types: one based on neural sequence generation with attention mechanisms (\emph{\textit{$n$}-gram}) as well as another designed for generating sequences from structured or hierarchical inputs such as graphs ($H$-graph).

As one of the most severe types of industrial accidents, vehicle fires not only cause significant economic losses but also pose a threat to people's lives and property. In recent years, various sensors have been applied to the monitoring of vehicle fires. However, due to limitations such as power supply, computation, and storage, it is difficult for a single sensor to achieve optimal fire detection performance. In this paper, we propose a multi-knowledge fused anomaly detection system (MKF-ADS) for vehicle fire detection. Firstly, we introduce a knowledge graph (KG) to represent the complex relationships between fire-related concepts. Then, we design a multi-knowledge fusion mechanism to integrate the prior knowledge in the KG with the data knowledge obtained from sensor data. Finally, we adopt a deep neural network to learn the fused knowledge and detect anomalies. Experimental results show that the proposed MKF-ADS achieves better performance compared with existing methods.

Word sense disambiguation (WSD) is the task of assigning a specific sense to a word in a given context. Typically, only recorded senses, i.e., senses that are documented in wordnets, are considered. However, many words have additional, non-recorded senses that are used in specific contexts. Identifying these senses is valuable for improving the accuracy of WSD and for extending wordnets with missing senses. Previous work on non-recorded senses has been limited to manual identification in only a few languages. In this paper, we propose a novel, fully automatic approach for identifying non-recorded senses. The approach is based on the premise that words with similar senses have similar contexts. We evaluate our approach on English and Swedish, two languages with extensive sense inventory documentation, as well as on Swedish medical text, a domain where many existing senses may be outdated. We show that our approach successfully identifies non-recorded senses in both languages. The approach improves sense disambiguation accuracy from 86.4% to 87.7% in English and from 81.7% to 82.5% in Swedish. It also discovers 17 new senses in the Swedish wordnet.

Monte Carlo (MC) simulations play a pivotal role in diverse scientific and engineering domains, with applications ranging from materials science to high-energy physics. In this paper we present the development of OpenMP 4 API based parallel MC software for modeling radio frequency radiation propagation through dense urban environments using MonteCarloRF library [1]. We investigate different parallelization approaches that can be adopted to harness multicore CPUs efficiently while maintaining code portability across platforms by utilizing features available only on recent hardware generations or those provided as part standard language specifications like shared memory parallelism via thread affinity mapping/affinity grouping constructs defined within compiler directives extension specifica

Recent advancements in large language models (LLMs) have underscored their importance as a transformative technology for various applications. In the context of programming, LLMs have been shown to be effective in automating low-level tasks such as code completion and data analysis, but they remain limited by the absence of fine-grained reasoning capabilities. This paper presents a novel approach that combines the strengths of LLMs with the precision of traditional programming techniques, thereby addressing this limitation. We demonstrate that LLMs can successfully generate high-quality code when paired with expert knowledge using reinforcement learning. Specifically, we present an environment designed to assess code generation accuracy and quality, providing feedback on correctness and st

Tables play a crucial role in conveying information in various domains, serving as indispensa-ble data visualisation tools. While tables are used extensively by domain experts and researchers to present large amounts of quantitative or qualitative knowledge across diverse fields suchas health care [1], financial services[2] , energy management systems 34567890 . The complexity inherentin visualising multidimensional table datasets has resulted ina lackof effectivetablevisualisation techniques for multivariate TableVisualisations (MVTVs).

This paper introduces HeAR -- a novel approach to represent and process acoustic data relevant to healthcare. We focus on two specific use cases -- detecting coughs in ambient sound recordings and classifying emotional state from speech. For both tasks, we show that HeAR improves performance over existing methods. What is more, we demonstrate that HeAR has the potential to open up new avenues for acoustic healthcare monitoring in both clinical and everyday settings. HeAR represents an order of magnitude more efficient usage of acoustic data for healthcare than traditional approaches.

With the boom of e-commerce and web applications, recommender systems have become an important part in providing useful information for users. However most recommendations are made based on user historical behavior data only without considering their personality traits which can be a factor that influences consumers’ decisions or behaviors such as purchase intentions [1]. The aim is to build recommendation system models by integrating personal characteristics into them. In this paper we propose two approaches: A) Integrating Personality Traits with Collaborative Filtering (ITCF): In ITTF model first step will extract 5 dimensions from each review text using VADER sentiment analysis tool then combine it along side ratings matrix generated through CF algorithm ,then finally predict items rel

In this work, we present the first algorithm to compute expander decompositions in an $m$-regular graph. Given a vertex subset of size $\delta n$ and weight vector with components bounded by 1/2+o(1), our algorithms output sets that partition each edge into at most three parts, such as all but O(\log\frac{n}{\Delta}) edges have total weights between $(3-\epsilon)\cdot \lambda + o_d((6+\beta)^k)$ and $(\varepsilon -O_{\kappa,\ell}(4^{-s-r})) / d^{kr}$, where $\tilde{\Omega}_p (\tfrac{(\alpha^T)_{ij} (v_{i}^{t}- v _{j}^ { t })}{ p } )$ denotes its mean squared error when approximating functions on subsets via linear regression using only coordinates up until time step s .

With the rapid growth of scholarly archives, researchers subscribe to "paper alert" systems to be informed about new publications. To retrieve relevant papers from these systems, it is common practice to search for keywords in their titles or abstracts. In this paper, we present a study where we examine whether the authors' names are useful additional tokens when searching for articles via keyword queries. The results show that author-names can help retrieval and ranking and that adding them as an extra token to the query does not deteriorate performance on other metrics like precision and recall. Moreover, there was no notable difference between using first name only versus full names. We also evaluated several approaches for automatically including authors' names in queries and found tha

Each LLM serving request goes through two phases. The first is prefill which processes the entire input, and outputs a summary for user approval. The second phase receives the user's final decision to complete the task. In this paper, we study whether and how LLMs can be tricked into providing incorrect answers in the second phase based on summaries generated during the prefill phase. We find that current LLMs are vulnerable to attacks where attackers edit small portions of the summary and use these edits to fool users into choosing wrong answers. We further show that current defenses against editing attacks cannot protect against such attacks unless the LLM is trained on large amounts of data from multiple domains. Finally, we demonstrate that using more than one prompt can reduce the imp

Assistive robot arms try to help their users perform everyday tasks. One way robots can provide this assistance is shared autonomy. In shared autonomy, both the human and robot maintain control over the robot's motion: as the robot becomes confident it understands what the human wants, it intervenes to automate the task. But how does the robot know these tasks in the first place? State-of-the-art approaches to shared autonomy often rely on prior knowledge. For instance, the robot may need to know the human's potential goals beforehand. These methods can fail in novel or unpredictable situations, especially when the robot's understanding of the task is initially poor. In this paper, we propose a novel approach to shared autonomy that learns assistance from feedback. We assume the robot has a way to collect data from the human, whether through demonstration, communication, or observation. Then, as the robot performs a task, we have it repeatedly check if its plan is correct. If not, the robot asks for feedback to understand the human's intended plan. With each new piece of feedback, the robot updates its understanding of the task and its assistance strategy. We test our approach on tasks like reaching, pushing, and pouring. We find that our method can learn assistance from scratch, even in the face of the robot's initial uncertainty. In addition, we show our method can adapt to the human's changing preferences over time.

To date, toxicity mitigation in language models has almost entirely been focused on single-task scenarios where the model is evaluated on a small number of tasks. In practice however there are many settings with more complex setups involving multiple related but distinct subtasks (e.g., different types or domains) which could potentially exacerbate issues such as data bias and harmful stereotypes when training toxic classifiers for multiclass classification problems like hate speech detection across languages/domains etc.). This paper explores this challenge through an empirical study based around 20 popular datasets from four key areas: political discourse; social media content moderation ; educational learning resources ,and medical text . We find that even modest differences between cla

Changes in demand, various hydrological inputs, and environmental stressors are among the issues that affect water availability. The purpose of this study was to develop a model for forecasting monthly reservoir inflows using machine learning techniques (MLT) with improved results by incorporating satellite data into them as an input variable along side meteorological variables: rainfall; temperature(minimum & maximum); relative humidity), soil moisture content from Soil Moisture Active Passive-2019 36km product provided through National Aeronautics Space Administration's Land Processes Distributed Archive Center at University Corporation for Atmospheric Research Data Distribution System were used here). We employed four different MLT models namely Artificial Neural Network Model - Multila

Video stabilization is a longstanding computer vision problem, particularly pixel-level synthesis solutions for video sequences with high degrees of camera motion. Existing methods often require multiple passes over the input data (e.g., optical flow estimation), which can be computationally intensive and difficult to parallelize on modern hardware platforms such as GPUs or FPGAs. In this paper we present our framework that leverages convolutional neural networks coupled together via an iterative loop in order achieve real time performance while maintaining good quality results without sacrificing accuracy by utilizing multiframe analysis capabilities combined into one pass only requiring two additional frames instead four like most existing approaches do -all done entirely within GPU memo

Robustness has been a central concern in NLP, but assessing robustness has been limited to expert circles. We hypothesize that models have become more robust unintentionally because developers focus on metrics that correlate with robustness. To test this, we conduct a large-scale human evaluation of 15 translation models on 10 languages, instructed to select the best translation without being told the model used. We find models have become significantly more robust, by up to 2.5 points on a 5-point scale, primarily due to improvements in the low-quality translations. However, model developers did not notice these improvements, showing that robustness improvements can occur unintentionally. We suggest that since model developers are not noticing robustness improvements, we as an community should be more proactive in ensuring robustness becomes a model's top priority.

Human-centric 3D scene understanding has recently drawn increasing attention, driven by its critical impact on both safety and efficiency in various applications. However, this task is notoriously challenging due to the complexity of real-world scenes and the lack of large-scale annotated data. To tackle these challenges, we propose a novel framework named MISA that introduces multi-modal representations into a vision transformer for scene understanding. Specifically, a novel 3D scene representation is proposed, which encodes the spatial relationship between objects and humans via graph neural networks. Then, a modified vision transformer is designed, capable of fusing 2D images with the novel 3D representation to better capture the complex interactions among objects, humans, and their sur

Large language models (LLMs) are capable of producing harmful outputs, such as biased language, factually incorrect information, and toxic content. Existing safety approaches for LLMs are typically designed to control certain unwanted properties of model outputs, such as toxicity or bias, by adjusting the model's parameters through techniques like gradient-based optimization. However, these approaches may not effectively prevent the model from producing harmful outputs if the unwanted properties are not strongly correlated with the model's training objective. In this work, we propose a novel framework for enhancing LLM safety by directly optimizing a proxy for the desired model behavior using constrained direct preference optimization. We evaluate our method on a LLM that generates product descriptions for an e-commerce platform. Our results demonstrate that our approach can effectively improve the safety of the model in terms of both bias and toxicity.

This paper presents the second ChatGPT4PCG competition at the 2024 International Conference on AI in Games (AIG). The goal of this competition is to develop methods and tools that can enable large language models, such as ChatGPT, to generate new game content by leveraging an existing game's design. It aims to address the challenges of producing original, high-quality content and aligning it with a specific game's style and mechanics. Additionally, the competition seeks to encourage the development of innovative methods for generating and evaluating game content, including strategies to optimize creativity, diversity, and quality. The evaluation of submissions will be based on the quality of the generated content, which will be assessed by human judges. This competition is expected to attr

Context: Roblox Studio lets millions of creators build interactive experiences by programming in a variant of Lua. Most users are casual players who have little to no coding experience, and as such learn the language through trial-and error or documentation reading alone. While this can help them develop basic knowledge about core concepts like variables (a construct that stores values), it is difficult for novices to understand complex constructs when first encountering their implementation details without prior understanding due to cognitive load constraints [1]. The lack thereof may lead students with limited training time - often required because they have other responsibilities outside college courses where computer science classes fall under – unable fully grasp certain topics leadin

Recently, diffusion models have achieved impressive performance in text-to-image generation, thanks to the non-linear mapping and attention mechanism. Unfortunately, the high computational cost makes it difficult to train a diffusion model with a large number of layers. To solve this problem, we propose a new weak-to-strong training method for diffusion models, which relies on a novel diffusion transformer with a learnable subspace projection. Specifically, we introduce a sigma function to control the variance of the noise added to the image. In this way, we can use a smaller variance in the early training stage to approximate the optimal variance of the full model, thus accelerating the training process. To further reduce the training cost, we adopt a progressive training strategy that gradually increases the number of sigma functions. Moreover, we propose a learnable subspace projection in the reverse diffusion process to better restore the image. It projects the noisy image into a subspace where the image distribution is more concentrated, thus facilitating the reverse diffusion. Extensive experiments on the CUB-200 and MSCOCO datasets demonstrate the effectiveness of our method. Specifically, our method achieves a FID of 25.49 in the 4K resolution (3840 $\times$ 2160) text-to-image generation task on the CUB-200 dataset with a $\sigma$-Diffusion Transformer containing only 10 layers.
