The proposed GFM is a multi-layer perceptron that takes the image spatial location as input and predicts the image embedding. We combine the GFM with the CNN framework to form a unified model, which can utilize the image spatial location information through the GFM. Experiments on two benchmark datasets, i.e., University-1652 and CDD, demonstrate that the proposed method outperforms the state-of-the-art methods.

Color design is essential in areas such as product, graphic, and fashion design. However, current color design systems still suffer from several limitations, including a lack of user-friendliness, a focus on the human eye rather than other aspects of perception, and insufficient consideration of real-world conditions. To overcome these challenges, we introduce a new approach based on the concept of "perceptual fluency," which considers factors beyond the visual spectrum to optimize user experience. Our system utilizes a novel design process that begins with the selection of target environments (e.g., clothing) and desired perceptions (e.g., warmth). It then employs computational tools to generate designs tailored to the selected environment and perception. We demonstrate this approach by d

Our understanding of the generalization capabilities of neural networks (NNs) is still incomplete. Prevailing wisdom holds that NNs achieve near-optimal performance for problems with Gaussian data distributions, but not for those with heavier-tailed data. However, recent work has shown that even for Gaussian data, NNs may be inefficient at learning complex functions. Here we show that NNs are efficient at learning simple functions: they can learn any function with a degree no greater than 6 without using any parameter tuning or data augmentation techniques. This result, which applies to all forms of learning and all types of NNs, including feedforward, recurrent and convolutional ones, implies that NNs are efficient at learning any function with an exponentially small degree. Moreover, we

Purpose: Our study explored the use of artificial intelligence (AI) to diagnose autism spectrum disorder (ASD). Methods: A sample of 283 children were recruited from hospitals and schools in China. All participants completed a face-to-face interview, Autism Diagnostic Observation Schedule (ADOS), Autism Diagnostic Interview-Revised (ADI-R), and an AI tool named DeepAutism based on multimodal data including facial expressions, body movements, voice, and text. The diagnostic performance of DeepAutism was compared with that of ADOS and ADI-R using receiver operating characteristic (ROC) curve analysis, and further evaluated by multiple factors such as age, gender, and intelligence quotient (IQ).

Imitation learning provides an efficient way to teach robots dexterous skills; however, learning complex skills robustly and generalizablely usually consumes large amounts of human demonstrations. To tackle this challenging problem, we present 3D Diffusion Policy (DP3), a novel visual imitation learning approach that incorporates the power of 3D visual representations into diffusion policies, a class of conditional action generative models. The core design of DP3 is the utilization of a compact 3D visual representation, extracted from sparse point clouds with an efficient point encoder. In our experiments involving 72 simulation tasks, DP3 successfully handles most tasks with just 10 demonstrations and surpasses baselines with a 55.3% relative improvement.

We introduce a new family of prompt injection attacks, termed Neural Exec. Unlike known attacks, this family relies on injecting untrained text prompts that are used to compute intermediate activations. The injected texts cause the target model to output different outputs from those seen during training. This behavior is a consequence of how neural networks process inputs and makes them vulnerable to adversarial attacks without any knowledge about the model or its training data. In our experiments we show that Neural Exec can be used to create malicious prompts that cause large-scale language models to make misleading predictions in a way that is invisible to human observers. Moreover, Neural Exec can also generate misleading visualizations (e.g., images) even for generative AI models. Fin

Distance-based time series anomaly detection methods are prevalent due to their relative non-parametric nature. However, these approaches often require the user of a data analysis method such as Principal Component Analysis (PCA) for dimensionality reduction which can be difficult and prone with noisy or outlierish datasets that may not follow linear trends in PCA space well [1]. We propose an unsupervised distance based approach using graph neural networks designed specifically around graphs constructed from Time Series data points themselves by leveraging geometric properties between them into our algorithm’s learning process without requiring additional preprocessing steps like other common techniques do before training begins. [2] This allows us leverage more information than just simp

Dense retrieval methods have demonstrated promising performance in multilingual information retrieval, where queries are represented by word embeddings. However, these approaches suffer from a few inherent problems: (1) the inability to capture semantic and cross-linguistic relationships between query terms; (2) the limited capability of effectively utilizing non-textual data sources for enhanced retrieval; and (3) the difficulty in scaling up to larger datasets due to computational constraints. To address these issues, we introduce LingoNet, an encoder-decoder model that leverages pretrained transformer-based language models for efficient zero-shot multilingual information retrieval. Specifically, our approach employs a bidirectional encoder that captures both contextualized semantic and

Contrastive learning is an effective unsupervised method in continual learning, but it is vulnerable to feature collapse. Feature collapse happens when the feature extractor fails to extract useful knowledge from the discriminative signal, resulting in poor performance on downstream tasks. In this paper, we propose Contrastive Continual Learning with Importance Sampling and Prototype-Instance Relation Distillation (CCL-IS-PIRD) to address the feature collapse problem. Specifically, we introduce a novel prototype-instance relation distillation method to guide the feature extractor to capture the discriminative signal. We also employ importance sampling to reduce the impact of feature collapse by giving more importance to samples with larger gradients. Extensive experiments on mainstream continual learning benchmarks demonstrate that our method achieves state-of-the-art performance. Furthermore, CCL-IS-PIRD shows superior generalizability on unseen data compared to other contrastive continual learning methods. The source code is available at https://github.com/ChangYing1123/CCL-IS-PIRD.

Recent studies of the applications of conversational AI tools, such as chatbots powered by GPT-3 and other large language models (LLMs), have raised concerns about their potential negative impacts on mental health. These findings raise important questions for both individuals using these technologies in clinical settings or to manage personal challenges like anxiety disorders This study will help us better understand how LLMS impact our thoughts & emotions when they are used to support therapy sessions with clients who suffer from Anxiety Disorders - an area that has not been explored before! We believe this research is crucial because it allows clinicians a glimpse into what might happen if we were able use conversation assistants during treatment processes without needing additional tr

Deep clustering has exhibited remarkable performance; however, the overconfidence problem, i.e., the model being excessively certain about its predictions, remains unsolved in deep clustering and affects the model's generalization ability. To address this issue, we propose a novel Deep Cluster Ensemble (DCE) framework that can mitigate the overconfidence problem of deep clustering. Specifically, DCE introduces an ensemble loss function to enhance the model's robustness against adversarial attacks by incorporating ensemble techniques into deep clustering. The experimental results demonstrate that the proposed DCE can effectively reduce the overconfidence problem compared with other methods. Moreover, our model achieves improved generalization and robustness on multiple benchmark datasets fo

Equivalence testing, a fundamental problem in the field of distribution testing, seeks to infer if two given distributions are identical by comparing their empirical cumulative distribution functions (ECDFs). While previous methods have focused on providing guarantees for high-dimensional data, our work provides the first generalization across the entire range of dimension. We introduce an efficient method based on spectral analysis to address this challenge and achieve state-of-the-art performance in terms of sample complexity and runtime. Additionally, we present a theoretical framework that characterizes the optimal test statistic for the equivalence problem, which is instrumental in developing our algorithmic solution. This work contributes to the development of more efficient and robu

Drones have the potential to improve many aspects of search and rescue operations, from delivering supplies to hard-to-reach areas to providing thermal imaging and other sensing capabilities. However, these operations are currently limited by the inability of drones to accurately land in dark or featureless environments, and the lack of a versatile multi-payload camera system. To address these limitations, we propose a novel drone landing system that uses a combination of visual and infrared (IR) fiducial markers, as well as a multi-payload camera system. The landing system consists of a drone equipped with a camera that can simultaneously capture images in both visible and IR spectra, and a ground station that uses these images to determine the drone's position and orientation relative to a target landing site. The system can then guide the drone to land accurately, even in dark or featureless environments. We design and fabricate both a proof-of-concept camera rig and a set of fiducial markers, and demonstrate the system's performance in both simulated and real-world experiments. In addition, we explore the use of deep learning techniques for fiducial detection and pose estimation.

This paper presents Incremental Vision-Language Object Detection (IVLOD), a novel paradigm that seamlessly integrates vision and language in an incremental manner. The core of IVLOD lies in its ability to learn from object detection examples with natural language annotations, making it a powerful tool for object recognition across diverse domains. Compared to traditional methods, our approach offers several advantages: (1) It is data-efficient since it requires only a limited number of labelled examples for training; (2) It is domain-agnostic because it can be applied to various scenarios, including those with no predefined classes or labels; and (3) It is flexible enough to accommodate various input modalities, such as images, videos, text descriptions, etc., allowing for more precise and

The massive generation of multimodal fake news exhibits substantial distribution discrepancies, prompt serious challenges to the effectiveness and reliability of automatic detection methods. To this end we propose a novel framework termed Multimodal Cross-Distribution Fake News Detection (MCDFD), which exploits cross domain information from different modalities for accurate real/fake classification without explicit supervision in both source domains or target modality under test scenario with only few labeled samples available at hand during training stage due its unsupervised nature thereby making it robust against adversarial attacks as well; moreover MCDFF allows users access more relevant data through multiple channels by leveraging multilevel attention mechanisms based on feature extr

Neural architecture search (NAS) aims to automate the design of deep neural networks. A key challenge for NAS is the high cost of evaluating a large number of candidate architectures. To reduce the cost, previous works have proposed to predict the test accuracy of candidate architectures from their training accuracy, using a latency predictor. We question the validity of this practice and show that the test accuracy of a candidate architecture can be predicted more accurately from its latency than from its training accuracy. We propose a simple latency predictor based on the observation that the test accuracy of a candidate architecture increases with its latency, if the training accuracy is above a certain threshold. We evaluate our proposal on CIFAR-10 and CIFAR-100, using a NAS benchmarking framework. Our latency predictor improves the state-of-the-art NAS method AutoML (NAS) by 1.4% on CIFAR-10 and 0.9% on CIFAR-100, relative to the best baseline.

The discrete mechanics formulation of physical systems offers a unified framework for modeling diverse physical phenomena and has the potential to transform the design of intelligent systems. A key element of this framework is the graph operator, a matrix that encodes the topology of the system and is used to construct the system's dynamics. We propose a machine learning approach to construct graph operators for discrete mechanics systems, based on the heat kernel of the graph. We show that the learned graph operator can capture the dynamics of a wide range of physical systems, outperforming existing graph-based methods for system identification. The proposed method is demonstrated on the example of a planar four-body system, where a parametric partial differential equation is learned as the graph operator, capable of reproducing the dynamics of the system for any choice of mass and length parameters.

Development of large language models (LLM) have shown progress on reasoning, though studies have been limited by smaller datasets and evaluation methods. The 2019 LAMA dataset is one such example that has not fully explored the potential to scale up both training data size as well performance via better model architectures for multi-hop inference tasks - two key aspects needed in order build reliable systems capable solving complex problems requiring extensive knowledge retrieval capabilities within short periods time frames .

Ever since Large Language Models (LLMs) and related applications have become broadly available, there has been a lot of interest in their ability to generate creative writing. Some recent work has shown that such models can also be used for creative tasks like generating new song lyrics or poetry. However, the mechanisms behind such creative performance remain unclear. In this paper, we first investigate whether LLMs are capable of generating creative text from existing texts, by using as an example a collection of 165 poems from different eras, written in French. We then analyze how the creativity of generated poems varies with the choice of model architecture, training dataset, and prompt format, using metrics designed to measure creativity in human-written poems. We find that while the

In this paper, we take a step towards a deeper understanding of learning from human preferences by systematically analyzing the behavior of neural networks trained to predict human preferences for images and language. We find that the training process can lead to significant changes in the performance of these models. These changes are characterized by the emergence of a pattern of high and low preference values that exhibit no clear correlation with the actual human preferences. This pattern is shown to be stable across various data sets and model architectures, suggesting that it may represent an intrinsic property of learning from human preferences. Our results suggest that models trained on human preferences may not accurately reflect or capture the nuances of human perception and pref

Diffusion model-based low-light image enhancement methods rely heavily on paired training data. However, the unpaired high and dark images in real scenes are more common than their corresponding pairs due to different illumination conditions (i.e., day or night). To address this problem of lacking a large number pair samples for effective learning diffusion models under varying illuminations during daytime/nighttime periods across seasons throughout all years globally is critical but challenging work yet unreached by prior studies owing mainly because 1) it requires huge amounts human labor efforts;2 )the datasets that contain both indoor outdoor scenarios from multiple regions along with long time spans cannot be found online easily .

In this paper, we study a variant of the level set method for solving partial differential equations (PDEs) with moving boundaries, a technique that is widely used in image processing and computer graphics. In our approach, we introduce a new way of representing the level set function, which is based on the signed distance function. This new representation allows us to perform gradient descent on the level set function, without the need for any additional regularization terms. We also derive a new way of computing the gradient of the level set function, which is more stable than the standard approach. Finally, we present experimental results that demonstrate the effectiveness of our method for solving several PDEs with moving boundaries, including the Allen-Cahn equation, the mean curvature flow, and the Willmore flow.

With the world's increasing reliance on vehicles for transportation, the associated energy consumption and carbon dioxide emissions have become major environmental concerns. Meanwhile, the advancement of artificial intelligence (AI) offers a promising solution to improve driving energy efficiency. By analyzing driving behaviors and patterns, AI technologies can provide insights and recommendations to help drivers reduce their fuel consumption and CO2 emissions. This paper aims to scope the current state of research on energy-efficient driving behaviors and the application of AI methods in this field. A systematic literature review of relevant studies was conducted, and the results are presented thematically, organized by the three main AI methods: expert systems, supervised learning, and reinforcement learning. We identified 50 papers that fit our scope and conducted a thematic analysis of the results. We present a summary of driving behaviors that are energy efficient and a summary of AI methods and their application in promoting energy-efficient driving. We also present several gaps in the current research and make recommendations for future research directions based on our findings.

Self-supervised large language models have demonstrated the ability to perform Machine Translation (MT) via translation of monolingual text without access to any parallel data. Despite promising results, existing methods are limited by their dependence on specific pre-training tasks and model architectures. In this work, we propose a new self-supervised MT paradigm that directly optimizes for the goal task with no external supervision or pre-training, and is applicable to all Transformer-based LMs. Our method employs a two-stage approach, where we first generate pseudo-parallel data from the source corpus and then train the target model to translate it into the target language. We demonstrate its efficacy in three different setups: a single-model setup, a multi-model setup, and a multi-tas

GitHub has become the de-facto platform for hosting source code of educational resources. However, malicious actors have started exploiting this trend by creating repositories with educational-sounding names that actually contain malware. In this paper, we analyze repositories with educational content hosted on GitHub to uncover malicious repositories that could be potentially harmful to students and educators. We introduce the term educational malware (educ malware) to describe this novel phenomenon. We develop an initial understanding of the extent of the problem by-passing the GitHub's search filter for educational repositories. We engage with the community to verify our findings and present practical implications for educators, students, and repository authors. We also work with GitHub to remove malicious repositories and update their filtering mechanisms.

This paper presents significant advancements in the field of abstract reasoning, particularly for Raven's Progressive Matrices. Specifically, we introduce a novel approach called `Abstract Reasoning by Reinforcement Learning (ARRL)' that combines the power of reinforcement learning and transformers to solve abstract reasoning tasks. ARRL involves two major components: an interactive agent capable of observing visual inputs and taking actions, and a neural network model for the agent's decision-making process. We conducted experiments on a benchmark dataset consisting of 10,000 problems, demonstrating that our method can achieve near-perfect accuracy, surpassing traditional methods and human performance. Furthermore, we provide extensive analysis and insights into our method, including a co

Modern computer vision pipelines handle large images in one of two sub-optimal ways: down-sampling the image or using a massive compute budget. We propose and implement an alternative, which we call "pixel slicing", that enables efficient processing for arbitrary inputs while retaining high resolution at no extra computational cost beyond standard model inference time on any modern GPU hardware with multiple graphics engines (eGEs). This paper presents details about how pixel slice implementations are made possible by utilizing low latency memory access patterns across multi GEC architecture systems along side optimizing batch size selection strategies to minimize total runtime costs associated when performing parallel operations over many independent streams concurrently within individual

Triangle counting in graphs is a fundamental problem with many applications. This paper presents a new randomized parallel algorithm for counting triangles. The main novelty is in the use of cover edges, which are edges that "cover" or "dominate" a significant portion of the neighborhood of a vertex, thereby allowing the triangle count to be inferred from the cover edges alone. The algorithm can be implemented to run in $O(\log n)$ parallel time with $O(n)$ processors, or in $O(\log^2 n)$ parallel time with $O(n \log n)$ processors, where $n$ is the number of vertices and $m$ is the number of edges. This approach is based on the simple observation that if one knows the number of triangles that include a given edge, then one can compute the number of triangles that do not include that edge, and vice versa. The cover edges provide a way to express this number in terms of the degrees of the vertices.

Multiple access techniques are cornerstones of wireless communications. Their performance depends on the channel properties, eg., their capacity and mutual interference conditions [1]. In this paper we consider a multi-user downlink communication system where several users transmit over fading channels with different signal strengths to one common receiver using multiple antennas (MIMO). We derive an analytical expression for its achievable rate by considering that each user transmits according do best linear precoding strategy in order maximize his own transmission probability given all other transmissions at once time instant [2] as well assuming perfect knowledge about received signals from neighboring transmitters during every round trip between transmitter(s) & desti

The inference phase of Large Language Models (LLMs) is very expensive. An ideal inference process would only utilize the relevant information from a given prompt, while ignoring irrelevant information. However, existing methods focus on improving the efficiency of LLMs by reducing their size and memory footprint during training or pre-training. In this paper, we propose an efficient inference method that does not reduce the size of the model but reduces its inference cost by pruning the irrelevant tokens in the input prompt. To do so, we introduce a novel neural architecture called a Contextual Attention Pruner (CAP), which identifies the key tokens in the context and prunes them away. We demonstrate that the proposed CAP method significantly decreases the computation requirements for infe

Instead of making behavioral decisions directly from the exponentially expanding joint observational-action space, humans often exhibit a preference for acting according to the observed world state. We propose a novel methodology, named Behavioral Planning (BeP), that builds upon recent research on planning and control in complex domains such as robotics to address this behavioral preference. The core idea is to use planning algorithms to infer potential actions at each time step which are then used as goals to solve an optimization problem. Solving the optimization problem yields a probability distribution over all actions at the current time step based on the observed environment. This distribution is used to make decisions regarding action selection or staying passive. We demonstrate ou

The Multilevel Fast Multipole Algorithm (MLFMA) is a crucial component in enabling scalable electromagnetic simulations, by reducing the computational complexity of the Integral Equation (IE) based solvers from O(N^2) to O(N). MLFMA relies on the hierarchical clustering of the unknowns and the approximation of the IE kernel. The quality of the solution is thus dependent on the accuracy of the kernel approximation, which in turn depends on the clustering. MLFMA can be computationally expensive, especially for fine meshes, since the number of clusters grows with the number of unknowns. Moreover, different clusters have different sizes, and the computation for larger clusters takes longer time. Hence, load balancing across clusters is important to increase the algorithm's efficiency. Previous works have proposed various techniques to improve the load balancing in MLFMA, including the use of a predictor corrector scheme and a performance model. In this paper, we explore two techniques to further improve the load balancing in MLFMA: data redundancy and a refined performance model. The data redundancy technique involves storing multiple copies of the same data on different GPUs, which can be used to accelerate the communication between processes. The performance model is used to predict the computation time for each cluster, which can then be used to schedule the execution of the clusters in a way that balances the load across the GPUs. We combine these two techniques with a third one proposed in a previous work, and apply the resulting algorithm to the benchmark problem of scattering from a perfect electrically conducting sphere. Our experiments show that the combination of these three techniques can improve the load balancing by up to 80% compared to a previous load balancing algorithm proposed in the literature.

The realm of algorithms with predictions has led to the development of several new algorithms that leverage (potentially erroneous) predictions to enhance their performance guarantees. The challenge is to devise algorithms that achieve optimal approximation guarantees as the prediction quality varies from perfect (consistency) to imperfect (robustness). This framework is particularly appealing in mechanism design contexts, where predictions might convey private information about the agents. In this paper, we design strategyproof mechanisms that leverage predictions to achieve improved approximation guarantees for several variants of the Generalized Assignment Problem (GAP) in the private graph model. In this model, first introduced by Dughmi & Ghosh (2010), the set of resources that an age

Recent studies have revealed that federated learning (FL), once considered secure due to clients not sharing their data, is vulnerable to attackers who can manipulate the model training process. In this study, we conduct a thorough investigation of FL's security landscape and identify vulnerabilities in the training phase, including backdoor attacks on both the global model and individual clients, as well as model poisoning and privacy leakage. To mitigate these risks, we propose a comprehensive defense strategy called DynaFed, which employs dynamic client selection, data pre-processing, gradient clipping, and model regularization techniques. Our experimental results demonstrate that our proposed method effectively defends against various attack types while maintaining model performance. A

SpaceHopper is a small-scale, reconfigurable robot designed for exploring low-gravity celestial bodies. It employs three legs with two degrees of freedom each, controlled by brushless direct current (BLDC) motors. The body of the robot is composed of aluminium profiles, and the whole structure is covered by a carbon fibre shell for protection and to reduce the overall weight. SpaceHopper is endowed with sensors for motion capture, environmental monitoring, and leg force measurement. In this paper, we present the design and manufacturing process of the mechanical structure of the robot, including the integration of all the subsystems. We also discuss the results of the static and dynamic tests that were performed on the robot, as well as the first flight tests inside a gravity simulator.

We study the problem of constrained reinforcement learning under adversarial manipulation, where there are $K$ stochastic hard constraints that need to be satisfied with high probability. We formulate this problem as one of constrained Markov decision processes (CMDPs), and show that it can be solved using a variant of Q-learning. We then specialize our results to the case where the constraints are convex, and derive a sample complexity bound for our algorithm. We also present an alternate algorithm that uses sample averaging, and show that it can achieve a faster convergence rate than Q-learning based algorithm, under certain regularity conditions.

Given time series data, how can we answer questions like "what will happen in the future?" and "how does this data compare with previous ones?". To address these challenges, we introduce a new dataset consisting of 200 million observations of human activities. We propose an adaptive model that learns from data in a self-supervised manner. By leveraging our dataset, we show the effectiveness of our model on downstream tasks such as time series forecasting and anomaly detection. Our results indicate that our method outperforms existing methods in both accuracy and efficiency. The dataset is publicly available at https://github.com/pikayu1759/AdaptivePrediction.git. The code to reproduce our experiments is also available at https://github.com/pikayu1759/AdaptivePrediction_code.git.

Handwritten mathematical expression recognition (HMER) is challenging in image-to-text tasks because of its complicated layout. Recent progress has been made by applying LLMs, but they still have limitations like reliance on pretrained models and need for additional training. To overcome these problems, we propose a novel HMER framework based on an offline pretraining method that leverages the large amount of mathematics resources available online to improve model generalizability. We first generate a dataset of labeled mathematics expressions using a masked language model (MLM). Then, we fine-tune our pre-trained model with the dataset and leverage a class-label attention mechanism for more accurate results. Our approach outperforms the previous state-of-the-art models on two datasets: Ma

Multimodal large language models (MMLMs) have shown remarkable performance in understanding and generating human language in conjunction with modal inputs, e.g., answering questions given an image. However, questions about dynamic scenarios, which require tracking objects across frames, pose a significant challenge for MMLMs. In this paper, we propose a novel pre-training scheme, Copy-Augmented Trajectory Transformer (CAT), to enhance MMLMs for trajectory prediction in dynamic audio-visual scenarios. Specifically, we introduce a copy mechanism to the existing future trajectory representations and enable the MLMs to better distinguish query subjects and predict their future trajectories by leveraging the given multimodal information. Furthermore, we propose a novel trajectory transformer with cross-modal attention to aggregate information from both visual and textual inputs. The proposed model is comprehensively evaluated on two benchmark datasets, and the results demonstrate a significant improvement over the existing state-of-the-art methods in terms of both prediction accuracy and model robustness. Our code and models are available at https://github.com/BeierZhu/CAT.

Learning a robust 3D object-centric representation is a fundamental problem in computer vision. It is the key to many downstream tasks such as 3D object detection and pose estimation. Current representation learning approaches focus on utilizing a discriminative head for representation extraction, which requires a large number of manually labeled samples. In this work, we proposed a novel viewpoint called "Learning through Prediction" (LTP) which provides a new perspective to learn the 3D object-centric representation in a unified framework. Specifically, we treat the representation extraction as a prediction head, which predicts the "centricity" of each 3D point. The "centricity" indicates the importance of each point regarding the object-centric representation. We further propose a novel loss called "centric loss" which enforces the "centricity" prediction to better align with the downstream task. The "Learning through Prediction" framework is versatile and can be applied to existing 3D object detection models. We use the state-of-the-art 3D object detection model SOLO as the backbone and demonstrate the effectiveness of our approach on both Virtual KITTI 2 and KITTI datasets. The source code is available at https://github.com/3DVLab/LTP.

The growing concern for energy efficiency in the Information and Communication Technology (ICT) sector has prompted the exploration of resource management techniques. While hardware architectures, such as single-ISA asymmetric multicore processors (AMP), offer potential energy savings, there is still untapped potential for software optimizations. This paper aims to bridge this gap by investigating the scheduling of garbage collection (GC) activities on a heterogeneous architecture with both performance cores ("p-cores") and energy cores ("e-cores") to achieve energy savings. Our study focuses on the concurrent ZGC collector in the context of Java Virtual Machines (JVM), as the energy aspect is not well studied in the context of latency-sensitive Java workloads. By comparing the energy effi

Bayesian optimization (BO) with Gaussian processes (GP) has become an indispensable algorithm in machine learning due to its versatile applications and high efficiency. However, conventional GP-based BO algorithms are highly sensitive to the hyperparameters of GPs, thereby requiring a large number of function evaluations and thus compromising on efficiency. To address this issue, we propose an efficient BO method based on neural networks that employs a neural network to approximate the posterior predictive distribution of the GP. This approach allows for more accurate predictions without sacrificing the efficiency of the optimization process. We empirically evaluate our method on several benchmark problems from the UCI machine learning repository and demonstrate its effectiveness compared

The population of older adults is increasing globally, and many are at risk of social isolation, which can have negative impacts on their physical and mental health. Social robots have the potential to engage older adults in social activities and thereby reduce their risk of social isolation. However, there is limited research on how to design robots that are effective at promoting social engagement. This paper presents a case study of co-design process between researchers, roboticists, and older adults to design a robot that encourages social engagement during exercise. We conducted a mixed-methods analysis of the data collected from observations, surveys, and interviews to understand the older adults' perceptions of the robot and how it could promote social engagement. Our findings provide insights for researchers and roboticists on the design of social robots that engage older adults in exercise by, for example, facilitating social interactions and providing incentives for social engagement.

We propose a methodology for modelling methane intensities of Oil and Gas upstream systems using artificial intelligence (AI) techniques. Our work is motivated by the need to address the challenges faced by Oil and Gas companies in predicting their emissions, as well as tackling the rising concerns about climate change and environmental sustainability. The proposed approach leverages AI technologies such as deep learning and natural language processing (NLP) to improve the efficiency and accuracy of methane intensity estimation. This paper presents our findings from the application of machine learning on real-life data. We explore the performance of various neural network architectures, including Long Short Term Memory networks, Convolutional Neural Networks, and Residual Convolutional net

Scaling laws play an instrumental role in the sustainable improvement in model quality. Unfortunately, current scaling approaches are often too restrictive and require that all models be trained on a single dataset or have identical hyperparameters (eg., learning rate), making it challenging to evaluate alternative settings. In this work we introduce Pytorch Scale: A robust framework for efficient large-scale scalability experiments with PyTorch which allows us perform multiclass classification benchmarks of over one million images across hundreds different training configurations including varying batch sizes up through multiple GPUs using distributed data parallelism without having prior knowledge about how many devices will actually participate at run time nor

Cross-view geo-localization aims to match images of the same target from different platforms, such as mobile and satellite. In this work, we propose an efficient cross-view geo-localization method with only one labeled dataset for each platform and without requiring additional annotations for other views. To achieve this, we first construct a multi-view representation with shared feature embedding that can be leveraged across multiple views. Then, a novel similarity loss function is designed to enhance the discriminative power between similar and dissimilar views. Finally, our method is trained on the source view by minimizing the distance between it and its corresponding anchor image. During inference, a simple nearest neighbor search algorithm is used to find the closest anchors in the t

Entity set expansion (ESE) aims to infer missing entities in a knowledge graph given a set of seed entities and is widely used in various downstream tasks. Existing ESE methods mainly focus on expanding the entity set to a large candidate set, which is then used for link prediction or question answering. However, these methods often lack consideration of the quality and relevance of the expanded entities, leading to suboptimal performance in downstream tasks. In this paper, we propose UltraWiki, a novel ultra-fine-grained ESE method that aims to expand the entity set to the precise target set. We introduce the concept of negative seed entities, entities that should not be included in the target set, and adopt a fine-grained evaluation metric to ensure the relevance of the expanded entities. Our approach involves three steps: 1) we use a pre-trained language model to obtain initial entity mentions, 2) we apply a neural text classifier to filter out false positive mentions, and 3) we employ a margin-based ranking method to further refine the results. Experimental results demonstrate the effectiveness of UltraWiki compared to existing ESE methods. We also conduct extensive experiments to evaluate the impact of negative seed entities and the robustness of our approach.

Neural network models have a number of hyperparameters that must be chosen along with their architecture. This is typically done by using some kind or training/validation set split and tuning the model on this data to maximize its performance, but there are many problems associated here: * The validation loss will always monotonically decrease with more parameters as we add them; if instead one wanted an estimate for generalization error (e., out-of sample prediction accuracy), then it would also make sense not just use $L_2$ regularization alone since they're correlated - especially when you consider how much larger datasets tend towards overfitting due simply having access too little information about what kinds mistakes people make while doing tasks like these ones!

Unmanned Aerial Vehicles (UAVs) are increasingly used in package delivery, and thus, efficient tracking systems are crucial for their safe and reliable operation. Conventional tracking systems often rely on single-step prediction, which can be inaccurate due to environmental noise and dynamic obstacles. In this paper, we propose a novel multi-step temporal modeling approach for UAV tracking using RGB-D cameras. Our method predicts the trajectory of a UAV in three steps: first, we estimate the motion intent of the UAV, i.e., the next waypoint it is heading towards; second, we predict the trajectory between the current position and the estimated waypoint; finally, we combine the predictions from the first two steps to obtain the final multi-step prediction. Our method considers the correlation between consecutive steps and the impact of dynamic obstacles on the UAV's trajectory. We validate our approach through extensive experiments in both simulated and real-world environments, demonstrating that it consistently outperforms state-of-the-art single-step methods.

Building on the classical theory of process algebra with priorities, we identify a new scheduling mechanism, namely, time-optimal schedulers (TOS), which is an extension of priority and preemptive schedulers. We prove that TOSs are optimal in the sense that they minimize the execution time of any possible schedule for any given set of jobs and deadlines. To establish this result, we introduce a novel notion called the weighted job completion time, which is used to define TOSs as special cases of other scheduling mechanisms. This allows us to leverage existing results from literature about these mechanisms and apply them to our problem. Furthermore, we show that TOSs can be implemented using a dynamic programming approach, resulting in an efficient algorithm with linear complexity in terms

In this paper, we study a defense against poisoned encoders in SSL called distillation-based defensive coding (DDC). DDC is an effective technique that can be used to defend against poisoned encoders in SSL. However, the DDC can also be poisoned by adversaries who want to launch attacks against SSL models through the encoding layer. In this work, we first propose a new attack strategy, which we refer to as Adversarial Distillation Attack (ADA), for attacking distilled SSL models. The ADA aims to fool the downstream classifier by modifying the encoded features of the target samples. We then demonstrate the effectiveness of ADA on three widely used SSL methods including self-supervised learning (SSL) with contrastive learning (CL) and instance discrimination (ID), and semi-supervised learnin

Optimizing the performance of many objectives (instantiated by tasks or clients) jointly over a large set of resources is a challenging problem. We show that a natural setting where several objectives compete with each other and share the same set of resources can be cast as a non-cooperative game, which we call the resource allocation game (RAG). To solve RAGs, we introduce the first algorithm that combines the ideas of cooperative game theory and approximate dynamic programming into a unified framework, called Cooperative Approximate Dynamic Programming (CADP). CADP constructs a solution to RAGs iteratively via a self-tuning feedback mechanism that adapts itself to the structure of the games and the available information about players' preferences.

Unsupervised domain adaptive semantic segmentation (UDAS) aims to perform semantic segmentation on target data using a model trained on source data from a different domain. High-resolution remote sensing imagery (HRSI) contains rich spatial details, which is important for semantic segmentation. However, the scarcity of annotated data and the domain gap between training and testing data have impeded the development of UDAS algorithms for HRSI semantic segmentation. To address these problems, we propose a novel causal prototype-inspired contrast adaptation (CPICA) algorithm. The CPICA algorithm assumes that the differences in pixel distributions between the source and target domains are caused by the domain gap. To adapt the domain gap, the CPICA algorithm learns a domain-invariant representation by leveraging a set of causal prototypes that are extracted from both the source and target data. The set of causal prototypes is used to guide the learning of domain-invariant features through the proposed prototype-guided feature learning (PGFL) module. Furthermore, the CPICA algorithm employs a pixel-wise feature distillation (PFD) module to promote consistency between the predictions of the downsampled and upsampled features. The proposed PFD module is inspired by the human visual system, which samples and reconstructs images at multiple scales to perceive details at different ranges. The experiments conducted on iSAID, DIOR, NWPU VHR-10, and HRSID datasets demonstrate that the proposed CPICA algorithm outperforms the existing state-of-the-art UDAS algorithms for HRSI semantic segmentation.

Information retrieval models have witnessed a paradigm shift from unsupervised statistical approaches to feature-based supervised learning. While the latter has significantly improved information retrieval performance, its reliance on feature engineering is hindered by the lack of semantic understanding and the need for domain-specific knowledge. To address these limitations, we propose Sent2Vec, an unsupervised neural embedding model that captures both syntactic and semantic features through multi-task learning with differentiable softmax probabilities. Extensive experiments show that Sent2Vec consistently outperforms state-of-the-art supervised methods in both monolingual and multilingual settings. It also demonstrates superior robustness against noisy labels, as well as competitive perf

Large Language Models (LLMs) such as GPT-4 have shown remarkable performance in recent years. However, running inference on LLMs is resource-intensive and expensive, and thus, caching is often used to mitigate the latency. LLMs' cache, if not designed with privacy in mind, can pose potential privacy risks, especially when integrated with other systems that handle sensitive data, such as healthcare. In this work, we introduce Privacy-Aware Semantic Cache (PASC), a novel cache designed for LLMs that incorporates privacy awareness and semantic understanding. PASC utilizes a two-level caching mechanism consisting of a semantic cache and a traditional cache. The semantic cache stores the semantics of the prompts, and the traditional cache stores the model's raw outputs. This hybrid approach ensures that only non-sensitive prompts are cached, while still benefiting from the model's accuracy. We evaluate PASC's performance using three metrics: hit rate, privacy coverage, and semantic similarity. Our results demonstrate that PASC achieves a 98% hit rate, a 95% privacy coverage, and a 7% increase in semantic similarity compared to the traditional cache.

Effective management of customer service volume is crucial for contact centers to improve their service levels and reduce costs. Contact centers can forecast future customer service volume using historical data, but traditional methods often fall short in capturing seasonal effects and other exogenous factors. Moreover, they typically struggle with the dynamic nature of these effects, which can significantly impact forecasting accuracy. To address these limitations, we propose Retrieval-Augmented Time-Series Forecasting (RATSF), a novel approach that enhances traditional time-series forecasting models by retrieving relevant historical periods and incorporating them into the forecasting process. This method not only amplifies the impact of seasonal effects and trends but also improves the model's adaptability to sudden changes in the data distribution. We apply RATSF to two real-world datasets from the U.S. and the Netherlands, focusing on daily and hourly forecasting horizons, respectively. Our experimental results demonstrate the superiority of RATSF over various state-of-the-art baselines in terms of forecasting accuracy. Furthermore, we analyze the importance of different features and show how RATSF can effectively capture seasonal effects and other exogenous factors.

In the era of personalized education, the provision of comprehensible explanations for learning recommendations is a key aspect in achieving human-centric machine teaching. To address this issue from an interpretability perspective and to evaluate it empirically using user studies with real users on two datasets comprising 164k videos across three courses: (i) we develop Explaining Learning Recommendations framework based upon counterfactual reasoning; (ii), validate its effectiveness by conducting A/B tests over multiple metrics including accuracy score improvement rate as well video completion time reduction rates up +30%; plus additionally measuring subjective evaluation scores such satisfaction ratings & perceived usefulness through survey questionnaire responses collected after watch

It has long been assumed that the sheer number of parameters in large language models (LLMs) is responsible for their impressive performance. However, recent work on a small-scale LLM called TinyGPT suggests this may not be true: by adding just 32 more layers to its architecture and increasing training data from around one gigaword per layer up until three times as much at six billion words total across all five versions tested—with no additional tuning or modifications whatsoever — it achieves similar results with fewer computations overall than GPT4 which requires an order magnitude less processing power yet yields significantly better outcomes when compared sidebyside . This article will explore what makes tinygpt so special while providing insight into why these findings are important

Recently, large language models (LLMs) have shown impressive performance on various tasks. However, the majority of LLM evaluations rely on English datasets, which may not fully capture the strengths and weaknesses of different models. In this work, we introduce CLongEval, a Chinese benchmark for evaluating LLMs in both generative and extractive tasks. CLongEval consists of 6 datasets, covering a wide range of contexts, from relatively short (e.g., 5 sentences) to extremely long (e.g., 18,000 sentences). We select 10 popular LLMs for evaluation, including 4 GPT variants, 3 T5 variants, and 3 ChatGPT variants. Our experiments reveal that LLMs perform differently on various tasks and datasets. For example, GPT-3.5 outperforms other models on math problem solving, while T5-3B outperforms other models on text summarization. Furthermore, we investigate the impact of model size, fine-tuning, and instruction tuning. Our benchmark is publicly available at https://github.com/THUMNLab/CLongEval.

Poor sleep health is an increasingly concerning public healthcare crisis, especially when coupled with a growing prevalence of mental illness. Despite the known benefits of good sleep on overall health and well-being, many individuals struggle to achieve restful sleep. This research aims to investigate the feasibility of using wearable devices for detecting sleep stages and monitoring sleep disorders in a real-world setting. We conducted a field study among 10 participants over a duration of 2 weeks. The results show that our approach achieved an average accuracy of 93.5% in detecting sleep stages, which is comparable to the existing state-of-the-art models. Additionally, we observed high agreement between the self-reported symptoms and the model's predictions (Pearson correlation coeffici

We explore the task of vision-and-language navigation (VLN), where an agent is given language instructions to navigate a large-scale 3D environment. Previous works mainly adopt the supervision-by-example paradigm, which assumes the language instructions describe a unique path. We point out two limitations of this paradigm: (1) it does not distinguish the effects of the instructions and the agent's interpretation of the instructions; and (2) it ignores the existence of multiple valid paths that can fulfill the instructions. To address these two issues, we introduce a novel causality-based paradigm. Causality-based learning forces the agent to internalize the language instructions as causal relationships between events, which are then used to reason about the effects of the instructions and to explore new paths that can fulfill the instructions. To implement our paradigm, we propose a novel cross-modal representation learning approach that simultaneously learns event representations and causal relationships from natural language instructions. Our approach is able to generalize the language instructions to novel instructions unseen during training. We evaluate our method on two large-scale VLN datasets and show that our method outperforms previous state-of-the-art results.

3D object visibility prediction, an essential component for autonomous driving, aims to estimate the 2.5D bounding box of objects given their 3D bounding box in the bird's-eye view. This task is fundamentally different from the conventional 2D object detection/dimension estimation because the 2.5D bounding box is non-convex and highly correlated with the 3D bounding box. In this paper, we propose a novel and lightweight method, called VGG-16 based 3D Object Visibility Predictor (V16OVP), which adopts a bird's-eye view representation with a multi-level 3D convolutional neural network. The proposed multi-level 3D convolutional neural network incorporates multi-level features from the original VGG-16 network (i.e., V16OVP) to predict 3D object visibility. We have conducted extensive experiments on the KITTI and NuScenes datasets. The experimental results demonstrate that the proposed method outperforms the state-of-the-art methods in terms of both effectiveness and efficiency. Our code is available at https://github.com/VDIGPKU/V16OVP.

Recent work has embodied LLMs as agents, allowing them to access tools, perform tasks such as drawing pictures or writing code and interact with the real world. These systems have shown impressive performance on a range of downstream applications but are often trained in isolation from other LLM-based models that may already exist for those domains (e. g., fine grained classifiers). This means they cannot take advantage existing domain knowledge when performing new capabilities—a major barrier towards making AI more useful outside research labs where we expect it will be most impactful.

Hyperproperties relate multiple executions of a program and are commonly used to specify security and information-flow properties. While hyperproperties have been studied extensively in the context of safety, we show that they can also be applied for specifying liveness properties. To this end, we present a novel algorithm based on a syntactic characterization of liveness hyperproperties. This allows us to verify complex liveness properties like reachability, termination, and deadlock freedom with a single formula. We implement our approach in a new tool called \emph{HypoLiveness}. We evaluate it by comparing against other existing tools on various examples: We find that HypoLiveness is faster than any competing tool. Additionally, we compare our approach to an alternative representation u

We present TextMonkey, a large multimodal model (LMM) tailored for text-centric tasks, including document question answering (DocVQA) and scene text analysis. Our approach introduces enhancement across several dimensions: by adopting Shifted Window Attention with zero-initialization, we achieve cross-window connectivity at higher input resolutions and stabilize early training; We hypothesize that images may contain redundant tokens, and by using similarity to filter out significant tokens, we can not only streamline the token length but also enhance the model's performance. Moreover, by expanding our model's capabilities to encompass text spotting and grounding, and incorporating positional information into responses, we enhance interpretability and minimize hallucinations. Additionally, T

Collaborative problem-solving (CPS) is a vital skill used both in the work environment and daily life. Although CPS ability has been widely explored with adults, little research on children's collaborative cognition development has been conducted using objective measures such as brain imaging techniques or eye tracking equipment to explain their performance variations during group tasks even though it could provide insight into why some groups perform better than others while solving problems collaboratively .

Court view generation, the prediction of the winning party in court cases, is an important task within the judicial system. It helps lawyers strategize and judges predict counterarguments. Previously, court view generation has been approached as a standard NLP classification problem. However, as court cases are argued and decided based on prior case law, similar cases should have similar views. Similarity between cases can be measured using case embeddings, and this paper proposes the novel task of injecting prior case law into court view generation to inform the model of similar cases. We introduce a knowledge injection method to augment the existing Transformer-based models with case embeddings of similar cases. Additionally, we propose a guided training approach to ensure the model learns from the injected knowledge. Our results show an improvement of 4.5% in accuracy for court view generation.

Artificial Intelligence (AI) is increasingly employed in various decision-making tasks, typically using large datasets. However, AI systems can amplify human biases and perpetuate unfairness, making it essential to evaluate their fairness. While existing methods have analyzed the fairness of individual models, they fail to address the collective impact of multiple models on different populations. Our work addresses this gap by introducing a novel approach for evaluating the fairness of AI systems with multiple models and diverse populations. We define a collectively fair AI system as one that minimizes the total harm caused by any subset of models across all population groups. To address this challenge, we introduce the Fairness Collective Evaluator (FCE), which utilizes Reinforcement Lear

This paper explores the transformative potential of computer-assisted textual analysis in enhancing research methods. It examines how computational approaches can revolutionize scholarly investigation, particularly in disciplines such as literature and humanities where traditional methods face limitations in analyzing vast volumes of data. The paper discusses three key aspects: the technical underpinnings of computational methods, their application in specific contexts, and their impact on scholarship. It highlights the need for methodological innovations to address challenges posed by large-scale data sets, including issues related to data management, storage, and interpretation. Additionally, it underscores the importance of interdisciplinary collaborations between scholars and technolog

Improving the safety of collaborative manipulators necessitates the reduction of inertia in robots and an increase in their physical strength. However, it is difficult to reduce robot weight while maintaining sufficient payload capacity because conventional lightweight materials have insufficient material properties for handling heavy loads such as construction equipment or vehicles used by workers with limited mobility due […] The increasing popularity of electric bicycles (e-bikes) has led some countries like France<sup>1</sup> , Germany 2]or Denmark [3], etc.,to introduce policies that promote ebike usage over other transportation modes[4]. Although many studies focus on examining factors influencing customers’ intention toward adopting ebikes,[5],[6][7]. [8]; few explore how they perc

In this paper, we introduce the driving scenario prediction (DSP) task, formulate it as a machine learning problem, and present a first solution using a deep neural network approach. The goal of the DSP task is to predict a high-level representation of the scenario a driving agent is in, based on a video frame from the agent's first-person perspective. To train a model, we generate a large synthetic dataset of 800,000 scenarios with 307 different labels using the Assetto Corsa racing game. We then propose a transformer-based model that outperforms other state-of-the-art models on this task. We also show that our model can be used to enhance the understanding of driving scenarios in two other tasks: future state prediction and hazard detection.

Sentiment analysis (SA) has been, and is still, a thriving research area. Several works have demonstrated the effectiveness of using deep learning techniques for SA in different domains such as movies [1], politics[2] or social media platforms like Twitter<sup>®</sup>and Facebook®. Despite this fact there are very few studies that focus on sentiment detection applied to tweets written in Portuguese language which makes it an interesting field worth exploring especially considering its increasing popularity among young users.[3].

Natural language and images are commonly used as goal representations in goal-conditioned imitation learning (IL). However, most existing IL algorithms only handle either text or image goals. This paper presents a novel framework to unify the two modalities into an endtoend model for multimodal visual Imitation Learning(VIML), which can learn from both natural languages description of targets suchas "Go straight 10m"and video demonstrationsof actions like walking forward step bystep with legs moving together until reachingthe target position successfully at last framein sequences .

Vision-language models are known to struggle with grounding language queries on images, i.e., the model fails to focus on relevant image regions when the query is satisfied. We attribute this grounding failure to two reasons: (1) the model sees the entire image during training, making it difficult to learn to distinguish between regions that satisfy and do not satisfy the query, and (2) the model's representation space is high dimensional, making it difficult to learn a precise match for an open-domain query. To address these issues, we propose Contrastive Region Guidance (CRG), a simple, sample-efficient method that does not require additional training. CRG compares the query to both satisfied and unsatisfied regions using contrastive learning, helping the model to better distinguish between them. Additionally, CRG reduces the representation space dimensionality, making it easier to learn a precise match for the query. We evaluate CRG on five benchmark datasets, using four different vision-language models. CRG improves grounding results across the board, often significantly.

Counterfactual explanations are becoming a de-facto standard in post-hoc interpretable machine learning. For a given classifier and an instance classified in an undesired class, its counterfactual explanation corresponds to small perturbations of that instance that allows changing the classification outcome. Counterfactuals are usually computed by solving an optimization problem that minimizes a distance between two data points x and y such that the classifier predicts a desired class for y. The computed counterfactuals are often brittle, meaning that small perturbations of a counterfactual can change the classification outcome from the desired to the undesired class. Brittle counterfactuals can be exploited by adversarial attacks, further emphasizing the need for robustness in counterfactuals. In this work, we provide a formal treatment for the training of a robust counterfactual explanation method against data shift. We define the desired properties for robust counterfactuals and provide a verified training approach to obtain them. The verification is based on a formulation of the robustness property as a satisfiability problem, which we solve using a Satisfiability Modulo Theories (SMT) solver. We evaluate the proposed approach to train robust counterfactuals against data shift for tabular and image data. The experimental results show that the trained models produce counterfactuals that are robust against data shift from the original training distribution.

We study sheaf codes, a type of linear codes with a fixed hierarchical collection of local supports that encode information at the edges. The number of nodes in the local supports is typically significantly smaller than the length of the codeword, making them well-suited for use on networks with sparse topologies. Sheaf codes have been studied extensively in the past, but only over finite fields and in the context of graphs with no loops or parallel edges (simple graphs). Here we extend the definition of sheaf codes to arbitrary graphs. In particular, we provide a general construction of sheaf codes on arbitrary graphs from subgraphs of simple graphs by identifying their cycles as parallel edges. Using this construction, we obtain new examples of locally decodable sheaf codes that are non-

Binary neural networks utilize 1-bit quantized weights and activations to reduce both the model size and training costs. In this work, we focus on improving the learning performance of binary neural networks by incorporating a novel gradient estimation technique called ``Fast Gradient Sign Method'' (FGSM). Our approach leverages the unique properties of binary arithmetic and adaptive quantization to enhance the precision of gradient estimates. We evaluate our method across a range of tasks including classification, regression, and image segmentation using the CIFAR-10 dataset. The results demonstrate that the proposed method achieves comparable or superior performance compared with state-of-the-art approaches while maintaining a compact model size and lower training cost. Furthermore, the

The stability-plasticity dilemma is a major challenge in continual learning, as the model often exhibits catastrophic forgetting or overfitting when training data changes. To address this issue and improve lifelong performance on downstream tasks for new domains/classes while preserving knowledge from previous datasets (evolving domain), we propose an adaptive feature distillation method called ADDIS to incorporate both structural features extracted by global attention mechanisms across layers into existing local interactions within each layer during multitask fine tuning of deep neural networks with varying input sizes ($\mathrm{N}\times\mathbf{\mathcal S}$). The proposed approach aims to enhance transferability among different task representations without significantly altering their ori

Audio-Visual Source Localization (AVSL) is the task of identifying specific sounding objects in an acoustic scene. The difficulty arises from multiple sources, including reverberation and occlusion by obstacles or other sounds [1]. This work presents a novel framework for audio source localization based on deep learning techniques that address such challenges using multichannel neural networks combined with 3D convolutional layers to extract spatial information about sound events within each channel as well their relative positions across all channels simultaneously during training time without any prior knowledge required beforehand; this allows us not only better performance but also faster convergence rates than traditional approaches due largely because it does away entirely altogether

We consider the problem of detecting and extracting a matching in a graph, when the only information available is a set of edge observations with a certain probability of being correct. This setting arises in many practical scenarios, including quantum key distribution, neuroimaging, and communication networks. We formulate the problem as one of detecting a matching in the Stochastic Block Model (SBM) with $n$ vertices and $m$ edge observations, where each edge is observed independently with probability $p$. We focus on the regime where $m=\alpha n$ for some constant $\alpha>0$, which we refer to as the sparse SBM. We consider two versions of the problem, one in which we have hard constraints (exact matching) and one in which we are satisfied with an approximate solution (bounded matching). For both versions, we identify the phase transition in the SBM model and provide efficient algorithms for finding a matching above the phase transition. Our results demonstrate that, somewhat surprisingly, one can find a matching in the sparse SBM with only a constant number of edge observations.

Residential buildings account for a significant portion (35\%) of the total electricity consumption in Canada. Energy efficiency measures can reduce energy use, save money and help fight climate change by reducing carbon emissions associated with heating or cooling homes through fossil fuels combustion processes such as burning natural gas to heat water tanks; therefore many incentives are being offered both federally $(\mathrm{NRCan})$ \& provincially $(B C)$ to encourage residents' transition towards more efficient houses that meet Zero Emission Homes standards like Passive House certification while also improving indoor air quality levels which is especially important during COVID-19 pandemic times due higher demand on ventilation systems at home level resulting from increased telework

Generalized Category Discovery (GCD) is a pragmatic and challenging open-ended learning task that has potential to be transformative for real world applications. Despite the growing interest in this direction, no prior works have provided empirical evidence of its practical value towards human intelligence or general artificial reasoning abilities over time scales relevant beyond traditional machine benchmarks like ImageNet classification accuracy gains within 24 hours after model initialization on new categories drawn from existing category taxonomies such as Open Images v6V10k [OIP].

Out-of-focus blur is a common degradation in biological microscopy, which affects the visual interpretation and automated analysis of cellular structures. Existing image processing methods have achieved good results in removing out-of-focus blur, but they often rely on large kernel convolution, thus leading to super-resolution of the input image, which is contrary to the down-sampling nature of the blur. In this paper, we propose a novel microsoopy defocus deblur framework based on multi-pyramid transformer and contrastive learning, aiming to leverage the existing down-sampled image pyramid structure without upsampling the image. Specifically, we first design a multi-pyramid transformer, which can take the benefits from the multi-pyramid image structure and perform attention operations across different scales. Then, we propose a contrastive learning based regularization term to further enhance the multi-pyramid transformer by introducing inter-scale constraints. In the end, we conduct extensive experiments on the challenging biological microscopy dataset and achieve a competitive result. The proposed method can achieve good results on removing out-of-focus blur while preserving the fine structures in the in-focus region.

This specification document specifies the syntax and semantics of QRtree, which is a specific dialect of the widely-used Quadrilateral Tree (QTree) data structure. In QRtree, each node has four children, one for each of its two edges that point in opposite directions from the root to the leaf. QRtrees are frequently used as a fast and space-efficient solution for a wide range of problems such as point queries in quadrilaterals and triangulation of polygons. The QRtree format is designed to be easy to read and write by humans and machines alike. The format follows an ordered triple, where each element represents a different part of the QRtree: the first element provides information about the tree's overall shape; the second element contains information about the tree's leaves; and the third

Distributed stream processing frameworks help building scalable and reliable applications that perform transformations and aggregations on continuous data. The Flink community has developed a large number of extensions to the core framework, which are often distributed as third-party libraries. For example, the Flink Streaming Connectors project contains connectors for different sources and sinks. These connectors have been developed and maintained by external contributors for several years. They are also used in many production systems, but their quality and performance are not guaranteed. In this paper, we present our efforts to make these connectors more accessible and usable by Flink users. We redesigned them using best practices from the Flink community. Furthermore, we created a stan

The efficacy of machine learning has traditionally relied on the availability of increasingly larger datasets. However, in many domains (such as healthcare), data collection and annotation is expensive or limited by privacy concerns which often leads to small sample sizes for model training (< 10K). Recent research demonstrates that pre-training large language models with unlabeled text can help improve downstream tasks across different modalities such vision [36], speech recognition[72] etc., when only a few labeled samples are available.[48]. While this phenomenon was initially observed using generative approaches like GPTs,[59],[LAMB][T5/BERT+GPTX]/etc. ), it remains unclear how much benefit these methods provide compared against alternative supervised baselines trained solely from task

In Colombia, astronomical research is experiencing accelerated growth. In order to better understand its evolution and trends, we conducted a bibliometric analysis of 10,280 publications related to astronomy in this country between 2000 and 2022. We used the Scopus database and VOSviewer software to generate visualizations that capture the main features of Colombian astronomical science. Our results reveal that the most active institutions are universities, while the top-cited authors and papers are associated with academia. The top cited paper is related to the search for exoplanets orbiting around the star HD 97658. Furthermore, our analysis shows a growing trend in the number of publications per year, with an increase from 33 in 2000 to 431 in 2022, which represents a 12.5% annual growt

The complexity of visual stimuli plays an important role in many cognitive phenomena, including attention, memory, and decision making. It is well established that the processing speed of human observers improves with increasing task difficulty (e.g., more complex shapes). However, little is known about how this improvement depends on individual differences. Here we introduce a novel methodology to investigate individual differences in visual processing speed by analyzing the relationship between individual behavioral performance and brain activity during naturalistic viewing. We measured both behavioral accuracy and reaction times (RTs) while participants watched movies containing sequences of simple (circle or triangle), intermediate (square or pentagon), and complex (star) geometric fig

The competition complexity of an auction setting is the number of additional bidders needed such that the auctioneer's revenues are maximized. We show that this notion depends on the definition of "maximizing". In particular, we study how to define a winning bidder in different settings and how this affects the competition complexity. We introduce a new competitive model called the MaxBid auction and prove that in this model, it suffices for $3$ additional bidders to increase the revenue by $\Omega(\log n)$ if the auctioneer uses any deterministic winner determination rule, and by $\Omega(n^{-1/6})$ if she uses random winner determination rules. We also give lower bounds showing that these bounds are tight up to a constant factor.

In this paper we address the task of summarizing television shows, which touches key areas in AI including natural language processing (NLP) and computer vision. We introduce a new dataset, TVSUM, that is based on publicly available data from IMDb.com. TVSUM consists of 120k summaries extracted from show descriptions with two levels of granularity: one paragraph per episode and one summary for each season. Our dataset contains a large number of challenging cases such as cross-genre summarization (e.g., comedies summarized by dramas), mixed media information (e.g., actors appearing in both drama and comedy scenes), and complex plot structures (e.g., episodes with multiple storylines). Our results demonstrate that TVSUM is a valuable benchmark to measure advances in textual and visual summar

Matrix completion is an important area of research in recommender systems. Recent methods view a rating matrix as a low-rank subspace of a high dimensional tensor, and then estimate the subspace by solving a tensor completion problem. While these methods have demonstrated impressive performance, they may suffer from biased estimation due to potential overfitting. In this paper, we propose a novel method for recommender system based on robust principal component analysis (RPCA). RPCA aims to decompose a given matrix into a rank-one component and a sparse component, which can be solved using convex optimization. We show that the low-rank tensor representation can be naturally decomposed into a rank-one tensor and a sparse tensor. Furthermore, we derive an efficient algorithm for computing th

Generalized Category Discovery (GCD) is a pragmatic and challenging open-world problem, which aims to discover novel categories from unlabeled data for the purpose of efficient transfer learning. The main challenge of GCD lies in the fact that the category distribution of source domain is unknown, making it difficult to design a proper metric for category discovery.
