To address this issue, we propose to utilize a well-trained classification model to approximate category distributions of source domain using the classifier confidence score, and then define a metric based on the difference between this approximation and the confidence scores of target samples. We further consider the impact of target domain data quantity on GCD and propose a novel generalized category discovery framework, name

Learning a universal manipulation policy encompassing doors with diverse categories, geometries and mechanisms is challenging due to the inherent difficulty in data collection. In this paper, we propose a new approach, which utilizes an existing door-opening task-oriented (TO) agent as a teacher to assist a reinforcement learning (RL)-based agent in learning manipulations for other classes of doors. Specifically, our method involves two main modules: 1) a teacher-student pipeline that employs a TO agent to facilitate RL learning by serving as a teacher and providing rewards based on human demonstrations, and 2) a curriculum learning module to gradually adjust the difficulty level of the training process. We evaluate our method on several standardized datasets, including Door2Door, UT-RoboS

Industry is at the forefront of adopting new technologies, and the process followed by the adoption has a significant impact on the economy and society. In this work, we focus on analysing the current paradigm in which industry evolves, making it more sustainable and Trustworthy. In Industry 5.0, Artificial Intelligence (AI), among other technology enablers, is used to build services from a sustainable, human-centric and resilient perspective. It is crucial to understand those aspects that can bring AI to industry, respecting Trustworthy principles by collecting information to define how it is incorporated in the early stages, its impact, and the trends observed in the field.

SQL queries with integrity constraints (ICs) are used to extract data from relational databases. Sometimes, two SQL queries may behave equivalently on a database if we only consider the ICs. For example, consider a database of students with fields roll_no, name, and dept. If a query selects all students from the database and another query selects all students from the dept table, then these two queries are equivalent under the IC that the dept table contains all students. In this paper, we address the problem of verifying equivalence of two SQL queries q1 and q2, given a set ICs. We call this problem Equivalence Verification (EV). We show that EV is undecidable, even when the ICs are simple constraints over a single relation. However, we observe that many existing equivalence verification methods for SQL queries without ICs fail on simple ICs. Hence, we propose VeriEQL, a technique that can verify equivalence of complex SQL queries with simple ICs. We empirically show that VeriEQL can verify equivalence on ICs that existing methods cannot handle.

Bootstrapping rare object detection in high-resolution satellite imagery is challenging because of the class imbalance between common and rare objects. Due to limited training samples, it is difficult to learn object detectors that can generalize over classes and that are not biased towards common objects. At the same time, evaluating object detectors on rare objects is difficult because ground truth annotations are needed for training and these are only available in limited amounts. We propose a novel bootstrapping approach that relies on the detection of object proxies, i.e., objects from a common class that are similar to the rare objects. The idea is to use the few available ground truth annotations of rare objects to learn object proxies which can then be used to generate synthetic ground truth annotations for training an object detector. We show that our method is effective for the detection of oil spills and plastic waste in high-resolution satellite imagery.

We present a new approach to the multi-objective multi-agent path planning problem, where each agent has a discrete set of target locations it wishes to visit. Our approach computes a single joint trajectory for all agents that visits each agent's target location at least as often as the optimal single-agent trajectory for that target. The joint trajectory is computed by solving a linear program that minimizes the sum of the maximum travel costs of the individual agents, where travel cost is the sum of the distance traveled and the total waiting time experienced by the agent. We show that this objective reflects the intuition that the "fair" way to transport many objects is to minimize the maximum workload experienced by any one of them. Our algorithm is able to compute the optimal solution for instances with up to 20 agents and 30 targets in a few seconds. We also show that for randomly generated instances, the solution found by our algorithm has nearly optimal quality. Finally, we present an approximation algorithm for the more general problem where each agent has a flexible travel speed that depends on the number of agents assigned to its target.

Many concepts for future generations of wireless communication systems use coherent processing of signals from many distributed antennas. In contrast to the traditional approach, where individual antenna elements are combined in a linear fashion, these concepts rely on advanced nonlinear processing techniques, such as analog and digital signal processing with neural networks. One of the key requirements of these advanced processing techniques is that the input signals must be synchronized so that they arrive at the same time at the different antenna elements. As a consequence, the synchronization of these antenna arrays becomes a critical task, which can significantly impact their performance. This article describes the state-of-the-art in synchronizing phased arrays and provides an overvi

The execution of smart contracts on Ethereum, a public blockchain system, incurs a fee, also known as “gas,” to compensate for the computational resources consumed by nodes. Smart contract developers need to determine how much gas should be set aside to ensure that their contracts are executed properly and efficiently. Unfortunately, current methods for determining this require extensive computational efforts and may not always provide accurate estimates. In this paper, we propose an efficient method called GasPredictor to predict the gas consumption of smart contracts using reinforcement learning. Our approach leverages the Markov Decision Process (MDP) framework and trains an agent with experiences derived from executing real-world transactions. Experimental results show that our approac

The Canadian Traveller Problem (CTP) is a two-player zero-sum game defined on a graph. One player, called the Traveller, tries to find a specific vertex, called the target, while the other player, called the Hider, tries to prevent the Traveller from finding it. Both players have perfect information about the opponent's moves. The goal of the Traveller is to minimize the number of vertex inspections until the target is found, while the Hider's goal is to maximize this number. The worst-case number of inspections needed to find the target is called the CTP-value of the graph. The CTP-value of a graph is known to be NP-hard to compute, even on planar graphs. We study the CTP-value on outerplanar graphs, which are planar graphs where the vertices can be arranged in convex order around the boundary of the outer face. We show that the CTP-value is computable in polynomial time on outerplanar graphs. We also show that the CTP-value is at most 2 on outerplanar graphs, and we characterize the outerplanar graphs that attain this bound.

Let $\mathcal{P}$ be a simple polygon with $m$ vertices and let $P$ be a set of $n$ points inside $\mathcal{P}$. We prove that there exists, for any $\varepsilon>0$, a set $\mathcal{C} \subset P$ of size $O(1/\varepsilon^2)$ such that the following holds: for any query point $q$ inside the polygon $\mathcal{P}$, the geodesic distance from $q$ to its furthest neighbor in $\mathcal{C}$ is at least $1-\varepsilon$ times the geodesic distance to its further neighbor in $P$. Thus the set $\mathcal{C}$ can be used for answering $\varepsilon$-approximate furthest-neighbor queries with a data structure whose storage requirement is independent of the size of $P$.

Spatiotemporal (ST) learning has become a crucial technique to enable smart cities and intelligent transportation systems. We aim at constructing effective ST models that can capture the inherent spatio-temporal patterns in urban data for the purpose of predictive analytics. However, most existing ST learning methods are limited by three challenges: 1) inadequate modeling of temporal dependencies between different spatial regions; 2) unsatisfactory generalization capabilities when handling sparse/noisy data; and 3) poor scalability when dealing with massive datasets. In this paper, we present DynST-Net, an efficient and adaptable framework for spatiotemporal prediction tasks. Our key insight is leveraging dynamic time warping (DTW) as a preprocessing step before the main neural network.

Dense object counting or crowd counting has come a long way thanks to the recent development in the vision and deep learning research community. However, most of these methods are evaluated on synthetic datasets that lack the complexity of real-world scenarios. The purpose of this paper is to evaluate object counting models in challenging real-world scenarios where the data distribution might be different from the training set. We propose a novel approach to assess the robustness of such models by creating three new datasets containing images collected from the internet. We create these datasets using the same pipeline as existing benchmarks but with images from the web rather than synthesized ones. Our results show that the state-of-the-art models perform significantly worse in our real-w

Large language models (LLMs) are highly effective in various natural language processing (NLP) tasks, but they tend to suffer from knowledge gaps. These issues can be addressed by fine-tuning LLMs with additional datasets, wherein the model's performance is improved through training on a specific task while preserving its generalization capability on new tasks. However, existing works often require extensive manual effort and rely solely on the dataset's data labels for tuning the model. To overcome these challenges, we propose a novel approach that leverages the domain-specific reasoning capabilities of LLMs to automatically select relevant datasets for finetuning. Our approach involves a two-stage process: 1) We first obtain the key concepts related to our target task by analyzing the pr

In the realm of computer security, the importance of efficient and reliable user authentication methods has become increasingly evident. The current landscape is dominated by password-based authentication systems, which are vulnerable to a wide range of attacks. This paper introduces a novel approach called PIN-Free Authentication (PFA) that eliminates the need for traditional passwords altogether. PFA leverages a combination of user behavioural biometrics and smartphone sensor data to verify identity without relying on static credentials such as passwords. We evaluate the performance of PFA against various adversarial attacks in three scenarios: i) online registration with existing datasets, ii) offline registration using new dataset, and iii) verification during login. Our results show t

The recent explosion in popularity of large language models (LLMs) has inspired learning engineers to use them as an alternative to traditional search engines. Despite their success, LLMs still suffer from several limitations, especially in the context of information retrieval. In this paper, we propose a novel approach that leverages LLMs and search engine technologies together for improved performance in retrieving information from large datasets. Our method combines the strengths of LLMs -- which can generate high-quality snippets based on user queries -- with the precision and efficiency of search engine technology. We introduce a training framework that enables LLMs to learn how to generate useful snippets for specific datasets by interacting with search engine queries, resulting in e

Microscale robots and machines have great potential to transform medicine through minimally invasive technologies for targeted drug delivery and disease diagnosis. Biological surfaces, while soft and compliant, are often slippery, presenting a major challenge for the development of locomotion strategies for microscale robots. Here, we present a millirobot that walks on slippery biological surfaces for targeted cargo delivery. The robot is actuated by three permanent magnets that generate a unidirectional pulling force on a fourth magnet attached to a cargo holder. The locomotion strategy is based on the principle of metachronal wave propulsion, where the phase difference in the pulling forces generates a net propulsive force for movement. We characterize the robot's locomotion performance on different surfaces using a custom-built optical tracking system. We also present a simple model that captures the qualitative behavior of the robot on both smooth and patterned surfaces. Our work highlights the importance of adhesion in locomotion on slippery surfaces and provides design principles for magnetic millirobots.

One of the main challenges in the area of Neuro-Symbolic AI is to perform symbolic manipulations over knowledge that involves neural information. In this talk, I will present a new method for representing and reasoning about uncertainty within neurally learned functions by exploiting Bayesian Inference as well Probabilistic Graphical Models (PGMs). Our approach aims at allowing us not only compute probabilistic posterior distributions from our models but also reason with them through standard PGM inference techniques such like belief propagation or variational methods which are highly efficient when dealing large scale problems where conventional sampling approaches fail due their high computational cost .

First responders (FRs) navigate hazardous, unfamiliar environments in the field and may need to locate a victim or provide care. They often rely on their training and experience to identify locations in the environment that pose risks and benefits. The ability of FRs to navigate such environments can be improved with the use of technology like augmented reality (AR). Augmenting the real world with virtual information can help them better understand the environment by providing information about the location and identifying risks and rewards. This paper presents the findings of an experiment that explores how the addition of AR information affects FRs' navigation performance. We find that AR improves FRs' ability to detect features that are useful for navigation and reduces errors in route

With the requirements of Intelligent Transport Systems (ITSs) for extensive connectivity of Electronic Vehicles (EVs), wireless communication technologies have been widely applied. Conventional wireless communications are characterized by a high number of handovers, which significantly impact EV battery life and network stability. To overcome this issue, EV-to-everything (E2X) communication technology has emerged as an innovative solution, enabling direct communication between EVs and infrastructure through dedicated frequency bands. However, the low-cost nature of the existing solutions results in limited coverage, which is detrimental to EVs' safety and convenience. In this paper, we present our research on developing a low-cost LTE-based E2X technology that addresses the limitations ass

The thesis investigates the utilization of memristive and memcapacitive crossbar arrays to store and process information. In contrast to conventional digital computers, such computing architectures require no external power source for operation. Instead, they rely on their intrinsic memory and electrical properties, which can be altered by application-specific voltage pulses. This property enables them to operate in a self-sustaining manner, eliminating the need for a constant energy supply. Our work explores various applications of these devices, including analog computing, logic operations, and learning circuits. By leveraging the inherent characteristics of these devices, we aim to develop novel solutions that enhance efficiency and scalability, while also reducing energy consumption. F

Out-of-distribution (OOD) generalization in the graph domain is challenging due to the inherent complexities associated with graphs. We introduce a novel method, Graph Contrastive Unsupervised Domain Adaptation (GraphConUDA), which utilizes contrastive learning to enhance the robustness of graph classification models against OOD samples. Our approach involves training on the source data and then fine-tuning the model on a target dataset through unsupervised domain adaptation. The key component of our method is the use of GraphConv2, an advanced variant of GraphConv that employs a new type of graph convolutional layer that can be trained using both positive and negative pairs within the same batch. This allows us to construct a strong discriminator for OOD detection that effectively disting

In this paper, we consider a sensor-based multi-robot search and coverage problem in unstructured environments where two robots are tasked with jointly exploring and covering a large, connected space while maintaining a pre-specified minimum distance from each other to ensure safe operation and avoid collisions. One robot is equipped with a omni-directional camera and the other robot is equipped with a laser range finder. We develop a novel potential field-based approach that encourages the robots to explore jointly while maintaining the desired minimum distance. In particular, we design a multi-robot coverage algorithm that uses a coverage map representation to drive robot exploration and a separation map representation to ensure safe operation. We prove that the proposed algorithm guarantees coverage of the environment and maintains the desired minimum distance between the robots. We perform simulations to evaluate the performance of the proposed approach in both cluttered and uncluttered environments. We also perform real-world experiments in an unstructured environment to demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed method.

Neural networks, while powerful, often lack interpretability. Physics-Informed Neural Networks (PINNs) are a popular approach for solving partial differential equations (PDEs), but they do not provide the ability to understand and explain model outputs in terms of their physical processes. In this study, we present an interpretable method to identify underlying parameters within PINN models using Bayesian inference, which can be used to identify hidden structures such as those that may exist in the data. This allows us to use a combination of physics-based priors on physical parameters and Bayesian inference to estimate the posterior distribution of these parameters, providing a more complete understanding of the physical processes being represented by the neural network. Additionally, we

In this paper, we consider a Micro Aerial Vehicle (MAV) system teleoperated by an operator. The operator sees a virtual representation of the MAV through a Head-Mounted Display (HMD). The MAV is equipped with cameras that provide visual information to the operator through the HMD. We propose a model for the time delay between the MAV and its virtual representation in the HMD. Using this model, we analyze a control architecture where the operator's input consists of a reference velocity command and the MAV follows a feedback law based on the state error and the desired angular rate of the system. The control objective is to minimize the tracking error. We derive sufficient conditions on the tracking performance in terms of the time delays. Numerical simulations are provided to illustrate ou

Neural Signed Distance Fields (NSDFs) are a promising representation for indoor scenes, as they are able to encode both geometry and semantics. Following recent works on Neural Radiance Fields (NeRFs), several NSDFs have been proposed to go beyond the capabilities of traditional SDFs. However, unlike NeRFs, NSDFs are not invariant to scene structure, nor are they designed to efficiently leverage potential structure information. In this work, we propose AiSDF, a structure-aware NSDF that encodes both geometry and scene structure. To this end, we introduce a novel structure-aware decoder that promotes depth estimation and facilitates training by using a proxy shape. The proxy shape is a structure-preserving representation that is consistently aligned with the NSDF's geometry. This alignment is achieved through a shape transformer, which updates the position of the proxy shape by leveraging both the SDF volume and an auxiliary shape grid. The shape grid is a structured representation that encodes the presence of structural primitives and their relationships. We evaluate AiSDF on indoor scenes from ShapeNet and FFHQ, and show that it consistently outperforms recent NSDFs on tasks such as view-consistent geometry, generalization, and structure-aware interpolation.

Deformable image registration (DIR) is a critical task in medical image analysis. Recent advances in machine learning for DIR have shown that neural networks can learn optimal deformation fields when paired with robust regularizers. Selecting the right regularizer, however, can be challenging, as it often requires extensive grid search. To simplify this process, we propose HyperPredict, a novel instance-specific regularizer that uses a precomputed library of hyperparameters to efficiently estimate the best regularizer for any given training sample. HyperPredict leverages the observation that different regions of the parameter space are more difficult to optimize than others, and thus should be approached with more caution. This is formalized through the concept of hyperparameter uncertainty, which we define and show to be empirically correlated with the curvature of the loss landscape. Our method can be applied to any differentiable regularizer and learning-based optimization framework, and we demonstrate its benefits for two common DIR applications: whole-heart function estimation and image-to-image alignment.

Point-based image editing has attracted remarkable attention since the emergence of DragGAN. Recently, DragGAN++ was proposed to further improve the performance on editing tasks with point-to-point correspondence. However, this method requires additional parameters and introduces some redundancy, which can be avoided by simply incorporating a new point-wise alignment strategy into DragGAN++. In this work, we propose a simple yet effective method called DualDragGAN++ for point-based image editing, where the input point cloud is first transformed into a 2D grid based on the 3D coordinates of the points. Then, the proposed dual point-wise alignment scheme is employed to learn the spatial relationship between the points in the source and target images, which enables the network to generate a m

Effective weed control plays a crucial role in optimizing crop yield and enhancing sustainability. In recent years, the adoption of agricultural robots has revolutionized traditional farming practices, offering efficient and precise weed management solutions. However, existing research on agricultural robotic systems primarily focuses on individual applications such as planting or harvesting crops. Consequently, there is a lack of comprehensive studies addressing the integration of multiple functionalities into one system for achieving improved agriculture operations. This paper presents an innovative approach that integrates various functionalities to enhance the efficiency of agricultural robotic systems. The proposed system incorporates precision agricultural technology to optimize cult

Recent years have witnessed a plethora of learning-based solutions for congestion control (CC) that demonstrate better performance over traditional TCP schemes. However, they fail to provide consistently good convergence properties, including {\em fairness}, {\em fast convergence} and {\em stability}, due to the mismatch between their objective functions and these properties. Despite being intuitive, integrating these properties into existing learning-based CC is challenging, because: 1) their training environments are designed for the performance optimization of single flow but incapable of cooperative multi-flow optimization, and 2) there is no directly measurable metric to represent these properties into the training objective function. We present Astraea, a new learning-based congestio

Training high-quality deep learning models is a challenging task due to computational and technical requirements. A common approach is to use an offline-trained model, which has been trained on large datasets but requires expensive computation during training. To reduce the computational cost of fine-tuning a model, we propose a novel method that combines self-supervised pretraining with online fine-tuning. Our approach uses a novel multi-task loss function that encourages the model to learn both discriminative features and efficient feature representations. The resulting model achieves state-of-the-art performance in terms of accuracy, efficiency, and generalization. We demonstrate its effectiveness across various tasks including image classification, segmentation, object detection, and i

As companies increasingly adopt machine learning technologies, there is a growing need to facilitate collaborative human-AI workflows. Explanation is key to facilitating collaboration between humans and AI systems. We propose an explanation method for Bayesian optimization that calculates the Shapley value of each evaluated point. We conduct two user studies to evaluate the utility of this explanation method for facilitating human-AI collaboration. In both studies, participants collaborated with an AI system to optimize a synthetic function. The results of both studies show that the explanation method we propose facilitates human-AI collaboration. We expect that our explanation method will enable humans to better understand the results of the optimization process and thus facilitate their collaboration with AI systems.

3D object detection based on LiDAR point cloud and prior anchor boxes is a critical technology for autonomous driving. However, the traditional paradigm of one-stage methods suffers from the limited semantic information of point clouds, while two-stage methods are challenged by inaccurate bounding box proposals. To address these issues, we propose a novel Multi-Stage Anchor Box Generation Network (MSABGN) that leverages an accurate anchor box generation module to facilitate high-quality bounding box proposals for subsequent classification and regression. In addition, MSABGN adopts a multi-stage cascade framework, which introduces a series of network stages with progressive feature extractors. The feature extractor of each stage uses the output of the previous stage as its input. Furthermor

Conventional control, such as model-based control, is commonly utilized in autonomous driving applications. However it has limitations due to high computational complexity and performance degradation caused by uncertainty or disturbances [4]. Reinforcement learning (RL) based methods are promising alternatives because they can adapt the controller parameters directly from data without prior knowledge about system dynamics while also considering environmental constraints like safety requirements during training process[5] . This paper focuses on deep reinforcement learn ing approach with a focus towards understanding how different design choices affect overall behavior of vehicle trajectory planning algorithms when applied for lane change maneuvers at intersections where traffic conditions

This paper announces the first Law Large Language Model (LLM), SaulLM-7B, and its application to predict legal concepts from natural language. LLMs are transformer-based language models trained on large corpora of natural language. They have recently achieved impressive results in various domains, including natural language understanding and generation. However, before this work, LLMs had not been applied to legal texts. Here, we present SaulLM-7B, a LLM trained on a unique dataset handcrafted from the digital text of German court decisions. We fine-tune six different models from the Hugging Face universe on this dataset. Then, we perform an initial exploration of the capabilities of LLMs for legal text analysis by asking the models questions about legal concepts. Our experiments show that LLMs can correctly answer simple factual questions about legal concepts and that our SaulLM-7B model outperforms the other LLMs in our comparison. We make our code publicly available.

Fourier Neural Operators (FNO) have shown strong performance in solving partial differential equations (PDE), by leveraging convolutional neural networks (CNN) in the Fourier domain. To solve a PDE, FNO takes the role of a black box operator, which is applied repeatedly to intermediate results. We observe that the updates in FNO correspond to one step of the backward Euler method for solving ordinary differential equations (ODE). This inspires us to generalize FNO as a Denoising Operator Transformer (DOT), where the operator is decoupled from the data, and the unknown function is proactively estimated by a neural network. With DOT, we develop a new pre-training method for FNO, which significantly improves the existing FNO pre-training methods. With the help of the pre-training method, FNO can be readily applied to large-scale PDE solving tasks, where high-resolution data is available. We demonstrate the effectiveness of our method for solving two typical PDEs, the Navier-Stokes equation and the Helmholtz equation.

Meta-evaluation has been proposed as a promising means to overcome the data annotation bottleneck in NLP. The idea is to use a model (the meta-evaluator) to score the outputs of another model (the system under evaluation), without needing ground truth reference. In this work, we revisit the problem of meta-evaluation for Grammatical Error Correction (GEC), where the system under evaluation produces a single corrected version of each erroneous input sentence. We propose a strong baseline for the task, based on the notion of confidence intervals. We then show that a meta-evaluator can learn to exploit the syntactic structure of error types, thus allowing it to outperform the confidence interval baseline. We also show that, contrary to what was suggested in previous work, the meta-evaluation framework can be successfully applied to ranking-based metrics (which are commonly used in GEC). Our results finally suggest that meta-evaluation is a promising tool to extend automatically the coverage of GEC systems.

Recent advances of locomotion controllers utilizing deep reinforcement learning (RL) have successfully shown the capabilities to learn complex locomotion skills. However, existing works are often limited to a specific scenario and can not easily be generalized to other scenarios. In this paper, we propose an effective multi-task reinforcement learning method with the help of imitation learning to simultaneously learn multiple locomotion skills in different scenarios. The key is to introduce a self-generated dataset as guidance for the model to explore diverse scenarios. We use a multi-objective reward design to encourage the agent to explore both novel and successful experiences. To evaluate our proposed method, we train 16 models on three types of terrains to learn four locomotion tasks i

Distributed Stream Processing (DSP) focuses on the near real-time processing of large data streams. It is an emerging and challenging paradigm for next generation big data systems to handle continuous streams with high velocity, variety, and veracity. The fundamental task in DSP is the continuous query (CQ), which queries over data streams. To efficiently process CQs, there have been various indexes proposed, such as temporal indexes and spatial indexes. However, existing indexes do not consider the correlation between multiple attributes in a stream, resulting in suboptimal performance when evaluating CQs that involve multiple attribute values. In this paper, we propose a novel multi-attribute index (MAI) to address this issue. Specifically, MAI introduces two key concepts: value window a

Palms play an outsized role in tropical forests and are important resources for humans and wild animals. Yet little is known about the role of palms in tropical forests, and how their role changes with environmental conditions. To address this question, we collected data on palm species composition, abundance, and regeneration in 170 plots from 53 sites across the Neotropics, a biodiversity hotspot containing most of the world's palm diversity. We then used these data to analyze patterns of palm composition and abundance across sites, and to model how palm abundance is affected by environmental variables at sites. We found that palms were relatively rare, comprising only 16% of all individuals in our dataset. However, when considering individual families, they made up between 58% (Arecacea

Bladder cancer ranks within the top 10 most diagnosed cancers worldwide and is one of those with increasing incidence rates. Despite recent progress in therapies, bladder tumor recurrence remains a major challenge for patients since no reliable biomarkers have been identified to predict disease progression. In this study we aimed at identifying novel protein signatures that could be used as non-invasive diagnostic markers or indicators of therapy response by analyzing urinary exosome proteome profiles obtained from healthy donors (n = 4) versus advanced stage IV urothelial carcinoma individuals before treatment initiation using label free quantitative mass spectrometry analysis followed up on subsequent time points after chemotherapy administration through liquid chromatography -tandem qua

In this work, we define the problem of finding an optimal query plan as finding spanning trees with minimum cost in a weighted directed acyclic graph. We show that this problem is NP-hard and can be solved by a polynomial time approximation algorithm with a bounded factor. Based on this result, we derive two algorithms for finding optimal query plans. The first one is based on a branch-and-bound strategy, while the second is an exact algorithm relying on dynamic programming techniques. For both approaches, the computational complexity has been determined as well as their performance when compared to a state-of-the-art heuristic for determining query plans. Furthermore, the performance of the exact approaches was evaluated on the standard data sets of the TPC-H benchmark, showing that they

Small-scale robots hold great potential for targeted cargo delivery in minimally-inv asive surgical scenarios. However, such robots are often limited by their size and power consumption constraints, making them ill-suited for operating in complex environments or over long distances. To address this challenge, we propose a novel miniature robotic arm with a modular structure that enables its attachment to a variety of carriers, including drones. This design allows the robot to travel over long distances while also providing a range of motion suitable for tasks like endoscopic surgery. Our approach involves leveraging machine learning algorithms to optimize the control parameters for precise manipulation tasks, enhancing the robotic arm's performance and versatility. By combining the advanta

Spreadsheet manipulation is widely existing in most daily works and significantly improves working efficiency. Large-scale spreadsheets are often involved in various business scenarios, such as financial forecasting, budget planning, or even medical diagnosis. The problem is that they can be difficult to manage and edit manually due to the large volume of data. This paper proposes a multi-task learning framework for spreadsheet automation and recommends a novel dataset to improve the performance of automatic spreadsheet generation. Specifically, we first develop a novel spreadsheet generation network based on the pretrained language model, including two components: a spreadsheet representation module (SRM) and a spreadsheet generation module (SGM). Then, we propose a multi-task learning fr

Despite remarkable progress, existing multimodal large language models (MLLMs) are still inferior to humans in terms of generalization and robustness. In this work, we aim to address these challenges by proposing a novel framework called Rational Multimodal Language Models (Rational MLLMs), which infuses rational reasoning into the MLLM paradigm. At the core of our approach lies a novel rational reasoning module that can effectively capture and analyze the rationality of LLMs' outputs. Our method is flexible and can be seamlessly integrated with various existing MLLMs. The experimental results demonstrate that our proposed framework outperforms state-of-the-art baselines on diverse multimodal tasks while also improving generalization capabilities and reducing negative biases. Code is avail

Machine learning (ML) systems are put to use in a wide variety of settings, each with its own purpose and requirements. ML systems are thus engineered to optimize specific trade-offs, such as accuracy vs. interpretability, or training time vs. performance. Yet, the engineering of these trade-offs is itself subject to social and political constraints, which vary across contexts. For example, ML systems that classify job candidates are subject to labor laws, company policies, and social expectations about hiring practices. These sociotechnical constraints shape the parameters of the trade-offs that ML systems can instantiate, yet they are often invisible to the algorithms that learn these trade-offs. To bring these constraints to the surface, we propose a framework to analyze and design ML trade-offs in a given context, based on the notion of a sociotechnical regime. A regime is a stable configuration of technology and social practices that instantiate particular trade-offs. We illustrate our framework by applying it to the case of ML systems that classify job candidates. We identify three distinct regimes that classify job candidates based on their name, location, and skills, respectively. We show that these regimes are instantiated by different ML trade-offs, which are in turn shaped by socio-technical constraints. We use our framework to design a method to intentionally discipline deliberation about trade-offs, by making the sociotechnical constraints that shape them visible. We show that discipline can help align ML trade-offs with societal values, by facilitating informed debate and by documenting the reasons why certain trade-offs are made.

The rapid advancement of Internet of Things (IoT) necessitates the development of optimized edge computing and communication systems that can handle an increasingly complex range of devices, data types, and applications. This paper proposes a novel architecture for IoT that prioritizes energy efficiency by leveraging distributed edge processing and a multi-channel transmission approach. Our design integrates distributed intelligence into the network to facilitate efficient decision making within the system. We utilize machine learning algorithms for effective management of channel resources, enabling more efficient resource allocation and reduced latency for critical tasks. The proposed approach offers significant benefits in terms of energy savings and improved performance across various

This paper delves into the contrasting roles of data within academic and industrial spheres, highlighting a disparity in accessibility. Contrastive learning is an integral component of AI-based research, particularly in computer vision (CV), where it plays a significant role. However, this technique remains unavailable to many in academia due to costly datasets and lack of access. To address this issue, we introduce a new dataset, called 1000Vision, which contains 1000 videos captured by over 500 camera models at 34 locations across different weather conditions. We have collected images from multiple camera vendors, including Nikon, Sony, Panasonic, and Canon, with resolutions ranging from VGA to 8K. Our work emphasizes the importance of providing free public datasets that are representati

Semi-supervised hyperspectral image clustering aims to cluster similar pixels into one class while separating different classes, which is beneficial for discovering the unknown number of clusters and reducing the dependence on ground truth labels. Existing methods often rely on spectral features for clustering, which lack robustness against the variations in the spatial-temporal domain. To address this limitation, we propose a spatial-temporal graph contrastive clustering method, termed Superpixel Graph Contrastive Clustering (SGCC), for hyperspectral image clustering. Specifically, we first design a superpixel graph construction method to integrate the spatial-temporal information with the spectral information for capturing the holistic features of hyperspectral images. Then, we propose a graph contrastive clustering method to promote the within-cluster homogeneity and inter-cluster heterogeneity with an iterative optimization strategy. Furthermore, we introduce semantic-invariant augmentations to enhance the clustering performance by augmenting the holistic features with domain-invariant information. The proposed SGCC is evaluated with extensive experiments on three public datasets, demonstrating that SGCC outperforms the state-of-the-art methods.

The restricted isometry property (RIP) is a fundamental concept in compressed sensing. For a matrix $A \in \mathbb{C}^{m \times n}$, the $k$-RIP constant $\delta_k(A)$ is defined as the smallest number such that $\delta_k(A)\|x\|_2 \leq \|Ax\|_2 \leq \|x\|_2$ holds for all $k$-sparse vectors $x \in \mathbb{C}^n$. In this paper, we study the RIP constant of rank-one measurements with random unit-modulus vectors. More precisely, we prove that there exist universal constants $c_1$ and $c_2$ such that for any $m \times n$ matrix $A$ with $m \geq c_1 n \log^2(m)$, we have $\delta_{\lfloor c_2 m \log(m)\rfloor}(A) \leq 2 - \delta$ with probability at least $1 - n^{-\Omega(\log(m))}$.

Measuring the impact of a publication in a fair way is a significant challenge in bibliometrics. In recent years, the application of a popularity-based approach has been proposed as an alternative to measure the impact by counting citations. However, the use of popularity metrics is controversial since it is based on unsubstantiated assumptions and requires more effort than counting citation numbers. We propose a novel approach that can be considered as a complementary tool for measuring the impact: the use of citation rates, which are obtained from the co-cited publications of each article. The proposed methodology is applied to a sample of 7851 articles published in the Journal of Biological Chemistry (JBC) between 2014 and 2022. Our findings suggest that using citation rates can achieve

Deep neural networks are notoriously miscalibrated, i.e., their outputs do not follow the calibrated sigmoid function of probability that a classifier is trained for under most standard loss functions (Cross-Entropy or Hinge Loss). This mismatch between probabilities and confidence scores can lead to poor classification performance in many applications such as computer vision tasks like image segmentation with deep learning models on autonomous driving platforms where real time decisions need high accuracy without costly retraining cycles due constraints imposed by hardware limitations .

Deep learning models have emerged as the cornerstone of medical image segmentation, but their efficacy is often hampered by an insufficient volume and quality of annotated training data. In this work, we propose a novel generative adversarial network (GAN) architecture for synthetic image generation that facilitates large-scale data augmentation. Our approach leverages a high-resolution encoder-decoder architecture, capable of generating realistic images with fine details. We employ a multi-scale attention mechanism to enhance the model's ability to capture spatial context, enabling it to seamlessly integrate into existing pipelines without retraining pretrained weights. To further improve robustness, we incorporate knowledge distillation techniques, enabling the synthetic images to retain

Recommender systems are vulnerable to injective attacks, which inject limited fake users into the platforms to manipulate the system. Existing studies mainly focus on attacking recommendation quality metrics in cold-start scenarios where few or no training data are available. However, as more and more user data is collected, the quality metrics become less important, but other factors such as revenue loss from fake users also come into play. In this paper, we propose a new attack method called Revenue-aware Injective Attack (RIA), which aims to maximize the overall profit of the adversary by controlling not only the number of fake users but also their behaviors. Specifically, our RIA can learn and optimize the actions of the fake users based on the current state of the system, leading to h

Residential buildings account for a significant portion (35\%) of the total electricity consumption. In this paper, we aim to develop an energy management system (EMS) that can achieve optimal performance while satisfying various operational constraints in the residential environment. A real-world dataset containing 19 months of smart meter data is used to train and evaluate an EMS model based on reinforcement learning (RL). The evaluation of the RL approach demonstrates its effectiveness in optimizing the energy consumption of residential buildings while satisfying various operational constraints. Additionally, the results demonstrate the robustness of the proposed method by adapting to unseen environments with varying weather conditions and operational constraints. This work contributes

Recent advancements in drone technology have shown that commercial off-the-shelf Micro Aerial Vehicles (MAVs) can be used to conduct surveillance tasks. A key challenge facing the development of an autonomous aerial reconnaissance system is how best a single vehicle should manoeuvre so as it may remain undetected by ground targets while collecting information on those same objects and their surroundings, i. e., its mission objective(O). This paper investigates novel approaches for determining optimal MAV navigation strategies with respect O using reinforcement learning techniques based upon Markov decision processes within simulated environments involving dynamic terrain features such wind gusts or obstacles present during flight operations over long distances across varying terrains types

Causal discovery is the process of inferring causal relationships between variables in a system, and is often formalized through the causal graph representation. Existing methods for causal graph learning from observational data are largely model- or algorithm-centered, focusing on efficiently scoring and searching the space of all possible causal graphs. However, the evaluation and selection of these models is typically performed through heuristic score functions, due to the lack of ground truth causal graphs in real-world scenarios. In this work, we introduce a novel human-centered approach for bootstrapping causal graph discovery. We present a case study with human participants inferring the causal relationships between variables in a simulated environment. We find that participants are able to accurately infer causal relationships, and that their inferences align with those of existing model- and algorithm-centered approaches. We propose a novel ground truth scoring function for causal graphs, based on the average node degree. We find that graphs with higher average node degree are preferred by participants, all else being equal. We use our degree-based scoring function to develop a human-centered algorithm for causal graph discovery, and show that it produces graphs with high average node degree. Our work highlights the importance of human inference in causal discovery, and demonstrates the potential of human-centered approaches for advancing causal discovery research.

In his 2018 paper, Herlihy introduced an atomic protocol for multi-party computation (MPC) with one round of communication in the case where all parties have access to a shared input $X$. The protocol can be adapted to the distributed setting by requiring each party to hold a copy of $X$, and is known as the herlihy protocol. We consider generalizing this protocol to allow for multiple rounds of communication among any subset of parties, which we refer to as \emph{herlihy's protocol}. We find that the herlihy protocol does not admit a non-trivial extension to allow for arbitrary number of communication rounds in the distributed setting. However, we show that the problem can be reduced to solving a linear system over the field $\mathbb{F}_q$ (where $q$ is the prime order of the underlying g

The transferability of deep neural networks (DNNs) has made significant progress in image and language domains. However, the transfer of DNNs in tabular data remains largely unexplored. In this work, we propose a novel approach to improve the generalization performance of DNNs on unseen datasets through multi-task learning with adversarial attacks (MTLAA). We first introduce the Multi-Task Learning Advantage (MTLA) for tabular data, which trains multiple models to predict different target labels simultaneously and uses the prediction as supervision during training. To better utilize MTLA, we propose an Adversarial Attack Strategy (AAS), which generates new samples for each classifier to enhance its ability to distinguish between different classes. Finally, we combine MTLA and AAS into a fr

We consider the approximate furthest-neighbor (AFN) query in a simple polygon $P$ in the plane, where the distance between two points is measured by the Euclidean distance. Given a point $q$ in $P$, we want to find a point $p$ in $P$ such that the Euclidean distance between $p$ and $q$ is maximized, up to a factor of $(1+\varepsilon)$. We assume that $P$ is represented by its vertex sequence in a counter-clockwise order. We present a randomized data structure that consists of a set of $O(\frac{\log n}{\varepsilon})$ vertices of $P$, called a coreset, from which an approximate solution can be obtained. The construction of the coreset runs in $O(n)$ expected time, and the size of the coreset is independent of the number of vertices of $P$. We also present a deterministic data structure that consists of a set of $\frac{O(\log n)}{\varepsilon}$ vertices of $P$, called a sample set, from which an approximate solution can be obtained. The construction of the sample set runs in $O(n)$ time, and the size of the sample set is independent of the number of vertices of $P$. We then use the coreset and the sample set to obtain a randomized data structure for the AFN query. The data structure runs in $O(\log n)$ expected time, and it is the first data structure whose query time is sublinear in $n$. We also show that our data structure for the AFN query can be extended to the AFN query with polygonal obstacles.

The Transformer model is the state-of-the-art in Machine Translation. However, it requires a lot of computation power and training time to reach good results for new language pairs or domains as well (e g., news vs tweets). In this work we present an adaptation strategy that allows us not only reducing 30% on inference costs but also speeding up by around two times its initialization process during fine tuning - without affecting performance negatively! This makes our approach suitable when you need quick updates with high quality translations every day at scale In recent years there has been increasing interest from researchers into whether deep learning can be used effectively within medical imaging applications such As Radiology .

Vehicle Routing Problem (VRP) is a classic combinatorial optimization problem that aims to find the optimal route for a fleet of vehicles to serve a set of customers. With the rapid development of AI and the increasing popularity of deep learning-based optimization models, explainability in VRP is gaining more attention. VRP explainability can help customers better understand the rationale behind the optimized routes provided by the system, thereby improving customer trust and acceptance of AI recommendations. In this paper, we propose an explanation framework called RouteExplainer for VRP. Our framework can explain the generated routes from the perspective of both the overall fleet and individual vehicles. We evaluate our explanation framework using real-world delivery data from a leading e-commerce company in China. The results show that our framework can generate reasonable explanations for VRP.

Reinforcement learning (RL) has achieved phenomenal success in various domains. However, its data-hungry nature often makes it difficult to apply RL directly when the available dataset is limited or noisy and/or an expert demonstrator cannot be found easily for a particular task like teaching humans how they can use their mobile phones more efficiently with less effort than before by leveraging on deep reinforcement algorithms such as Q Learning which requires fewer samples due primarily because of faster convergence rates compared other methods while also achieving superior performance over those alternatives at times too!

Graph clustering, an important unsupervised problem, has been shown to be more resistant to adversarial attacks compared with classification and regression. However existing defense approaches against graph-clustering attackers are either ineffective or computationally expensive in the real world due their oversimplified assumptions of node features (e. g., binary vectors). To address this challenge we propose a robust framework for detecting poisoning nodes that is effective under realistic settings where feature information may not always exist but can still provide useful hints regarding whether there exists some potential malicious activity on graphs such as fraud detection tasks which require extensive knowledge about customers' behaviors before making decisions based off those observ

Exercise-based rehabilitation programs have proven to be effective in enhancing the quality of life (QoL) and physical functioning of patients with chronic low back pain (CLBP). The efficacy of exercise therapy is influenced by several factors, including the exercises performed and the frequency at which they are completed. However, there is a paucity of research investigating the effectiveness of different exercise regimens on QoL and physical functioning among individuals with CLBP. This study aimed to investigate the effects of two distinct exercise protocols on these outcomes. A systematic review was conducted according to the Preferred Reporting Items for Systematic Reviews and Meta-Analyses guidelines. The databases searched included PubMed, Embase, Cochrane Library, Web of Science,

Trusted I/O (TIO) is an appealing solution to improve I/O performance and reduce latency in cloud datacenters. However, the effectiveness of TIO has been questioned due to its coarse-grained nature and potential impacts on system scalability. In this paper, we explore a fine-grained version of TIO called Fine-Grained Trusted I/O (FGTIO). FGTIO relies on trusted hardware to perform fine-grained trust verifications at a much smaller granularity than traditional TIO implementations. This allows FGTIO to accurately identify and isolate malicious guests from benign ones. We design and implement two prototype versions of FGTIO: a software implementation using Intel Software Guard Extensions (SGX), and a hardware implementation leveraging Trusted Execution Environment (TEE).

In the early stages of development, software systems are often debugged by inserting print statements into the code, which is commonly known as printf debugging. However, printf debugging is error-prone as it relies on developers to manually construct consistent and accurate print formats. In this paper, we automate the construction of print formats by framing debug protocols, which are essentially type-checked formats. We implement our approach in a tool named LiveRec, which also suggests probe functions that can be inserted into the code being debugged. Probe functions are similar to printf statements, but they are first-class citizens in the programming language and can be statically typed. We show that LiveRec can help developers locate and fix bugs more efficiently than traditional printf debugging. In addition, we show that the type information that LiveRec collects can improve the suggestions of probe functions.

Information retrieval (IR) is a pivotal component in various applications. Recent advances have seen the emergence of large language models (LLMs) as new IR systems, with their ability to understand and process complex queries. However, due to their high computational costs, LLMs are often deployed on a small subset of devices, leading to an inherent gap between the training and deployment environments. In this work, we introduce S2I-GPT, a novel approach for training GPT models using synthetic data derived from real-world datasets. Specifically, our method involves two key stages: 1) generation of text-related pseudo labels based on raw documents; 2) distillation of these labels into semantic labels through a graph-based similarity matching process. This results in a large set of labeled

This work is an attempt to introduce a comprehensive benchmark for Arabic speech recognition, specifically tailored for the purpose of evaluating large language models (LLMs). The benchmark comprises three components: A set of 100 hours of data from various dialects and domains; a unified evaluation framework that allows for accurate quantification and comparison across different LLMs on diverse tasks in one standardized environment; and a suite of automatic metrics designed to provide detailed insights into the performance of LLMs on spoken language understanding. Large language model (LLM) has demonstrated its ability to generate human-like speech, but its applicability for realistic speech processing remains limited due to a lack of comprehensive benchmarks for natural language understa

The isometric mapping method employs the shortest path algorithm to estimate the Euclidean distance between two nodes in a network. The performance of this approach is sensitive with respect not only on parameters associated with road geometry (i.e., curvature and width) but also traffic flow characteristics as well, e-g.-travel time variation due congestion or other factors such speed limit variations across links etcetera

Image retrieval enables an efficient search through vast amounts of satellite imagery and returns similar images to a given query. It is the first step in many downstream tasks, such as land cover classification or object detection. In this paper, we present an overview of state-of-the-art approaches for retrieving satellite imagery. We provide a comprehensive survey of these methods and discuss their advantages and disadvantages. Furthermore, we introduce the Image Retrieval Challenge (IRC) dataset, which contains pairs of similar images from Sentinel-2, Landsat 8, and PlanetScope. The dataset allows for benchmarking image retrieval algorithms and serves as a valuable tool for researchers and practitioners working in the field of remote sensing. Finally, we evaluate several state-of-the-a

AI approaches are progressing besting humans at game-related tasks (e.g. chatting). However, the deployment of these models still faces significant challenges in terms of security and privacy issues. In this paper, we present a new attack paradigm based on the exploitation of the memory leakage vulnerabilities of the AI models. We demonstrate that a single instance of a leaked memory can be sufficient to recover the entire trained model, including all its parameters. This opens up the possibility for a novel type of attack called Leak-Attack which exploits the memory leakage vulnerability to gain access to sensitive data stored within an AI model. Our experiments show that our approach can successfully recover models from various AI applications, including speech recognition, image classif

In this paper we define a new algorithm to convert an input relational database to an output set of rules, with the following properties: (1) each rule is expressed in a subset of a well-known logic programming language; (2) each rule has at most two variables in its head and no more than one variable in each of its body literals; (3) each rule is minimally non-deterministic; (4) all rules are in the normal form proposed by Kowalski for the Datalog language. We show that if the input database contains functional dependencies then the algorithm always produces a correct and minimal set of rules. We also show that the algorithm can be implemented efficiently, using only simple operations on binary relations. The results are presented as an answer to the challenge issued by Minker in [9].

Image watermark removal is an important preprocessing step for image denoising, especially for the protection of personal privacy on social media. However, existing methods often rely on the contrast between the watermark and the background image, which is not significant in real-world scenarios, especially for grayscale images. To address this issue, we propose a perceptive self-supervised learning network (PSLNet) that learns the perceptual features of the watermark and background image to remove the watermark more effectively. Specifically, we design a style transfer loss that encourages the feature distribution of the watermark to match that of the background image. We also employ a self-supervised learning strategy to enhance the model's ability to perceive the watermark. Furthermore, we introduce a new dataset, called MWR, consisting of 1000 images with watermarks in the wild, to facilitate the training of PSLNet. Experimental results demonstrate that the proposed PSLNet outperforms state-of-the-art methods in terms of PSNR, SSIM, and visual perception.

Generative AI has made rapid advancements in recent years, achieving unprecedented levels of performance across diverse domains. However, the reliance on large datasets and the significant computational resources required for training remain major obstacles to its widespread adoption. To address these issues, we propose a novel approach called Large-Scale Few-Shot Learning (LSFSL), which utilizes few-shot learning techniques to effectively leverage limited data without sacrificing model performance. Our proposed methodology comprises two main components: (1) a novel prompt language learning process that enhances the robustness of few-shot learning; and (2) an adaptive pretraining strategy that facilitates efficient cross-modal transfer between the prompt language and the target domain. Thi

The demise of Moore's Law and Dennard Scaling has revived interest in specialized computer architectures and accelerators. Verification and testing of this hardware depend heavily upon cycle-accurate simulation of register-transfer-level (RTL) designs. The fastest software RTL simulators can simulate designs at 1--1000 kHz, i.e., more than three orders of magnitude slower than hardware. Improved simulators can increase designers' productivity by speeding design iterations and permitting more exhaustive exploration. One possibility is parallel simulation, but RTL designs pose challenges for parallelization because they are not naturally decomposed into independent processes. We propose Parendi, a parallel RTL simulator that can exploit multiprocessor systems by ordering and partitioning concurrent processes in a way that minimizes data dependencies. We evaluate Parendi by benchmarking three RTL designs running on four processors. Our results show that Parendi can achieve speedups of up to 22X over a sequential RTL simulator, approaching the lower bound of 16X set by the Amdahl's Law.

Feature selection aims to identify the most pattern-discriminative feature subset. In prior work, we proposed a new framework for feature selection that leverages an encoder-decoder architecture and gradient-based optimization. This approach utilizes a decoder to approximate the distribution of features conditioned on the label and uses a contrastive loss to optimize the decoder. However, this model suffers from two main limitations: (1) it does not support continuous labels; and (2) its optimization objective does not fully leverage the information provided by the encoder output. To address these issues, we introduce a novel extension of our previous framework. Specifically, we employ a multi-layer perceptron as a discriminator to obtain more accurate estimates of the class conditional fe

Graph neural networks (GNNs) have emerged as a series of competent graph learning methods for real-world applications. However, different from the ideal conditions assumed in theory, real-world graphs often suffer from various defects, e.g., class imbalance, noisy edges, privacy concerns, or out-of-distribution (OOD) samples, which severely affect the performance of GNNs. To tackle these challenges, researchers have proposed a large number of GNNs with innovative architectures or training techniques. Meanwhile, there are also some general methods that can be applied to different scenarios. In this paper, we conduct a comprehensive survey of the most recent advances in robust GNNs against the five real-world challenges. Specifically, we propose a unified framework called GROUSE to encompass the architectures and training techniques of robust GNNs. GROUSE provides a systematic perspective to understand the GNNs developed in an ad-hoc manner for real-world applications. Furthermore, we propose some promising directions based on the survey results.

With the advancement of language models (LMs), their exposure to private data is increasingly concerning. This study aims to develop an open-source tool that empowers researchers and developers to assess and mitigate potential privacy risks in LMs by analyzing the dataset used for training these models. To achieve this, we present PPML, a Privacy-Preserving Model Learner toolkit based on TensorFlow. PPML allows users to perform security analyses at multiple levels, including static analysis, vulnerability detection, and dynamic analysis. Our approach uses a combination of techniques such as secure computation, homomorphic encryption, and differential privacy to preserve sensitive information during training and testing. We evaluate our approach's effectiveness using benchmark datasets from

Real-world systems are often formulated as constrained optimization problems. Techniques to incorporate constraints in the search phase of Reinforcement Learning (RL) algorithms have been proposed, but existing approaches suffer from two key issues: they require a priori information about the problem structure and they do not support the online addition or removal of constraints during training. To address these limitations, we propose an approach to integrate constraints into RL algorithms based on the use of linear programming. We demonstrate our method using the example of a discrete-time robotic system with unknown state space and constraints defined by reward functions and safety conditions expressed in terms of joint angles. We compare the performance of the proposed algorithm to the

We propose a novel framework for filtering image-text data by leveraging fine-tuned MultImodal Retrieval Transformer (MIRT) and BERT. We show that our proposed method is able to achieve state of the art results on multiple datasets in both retrieval task as well text classification tasks, which demonstrates its general applicability across different domains with small adjustments only needed when targeting specific use cases or new applications altogether This project presents an application based upon Microsoft's Speech API technology using C# programming language where it recognizes speech commands from user voice input via microphone connected device then controls various devices/appliances like TV volume control & smart home appliances such Smart Bulb color changer etc., without requi

Despite the success of the carefully-annotated benchmarks, the effectiveness of existing graph neural networks (GNN) has yet to be demonstrated in the practical applications. We hypothesize that GNNs can be improved by incorporating domain-specific features into the training process, thereby enhancing their performance. In this paper, we develop a novel approach for constructing feature-enhanced GNNs (FE-GNNs) based on the concept of contrastive learning. Our method utilizes the training data and domain knowledge to enhance GNNs with features, leading to a more robust model. We also propose an effective technique for generating feature graphs to represent real-world domains during training, resulting in higher performance than baseline methods. Extensive experiments on two real-world datas

The recent development of quantum computing, which makes use of entanglement, superposition, and other quantum effects to solve computational problems in a way that is not possible with classical computers alone, has led to the emergence of Quantum Machine Learning (QML). QML involves the training of quantum algorithms on data sets, with the aim of optimizing performance and improving the accuracy of predictions. To do this, it is necessary to develop new methods for data preparation, feature extraction, and training. Additionally, QML requires specialized hardware and software tools designed specifically for quantum computing. The goal of this paper is to provide a comprehensive overview of the state-of-the-art in QML, covering all aspects from data preparation to model selection and depl

Multi-robot systems have increasingly become instrumental in tackling search and coverage problems. However, to achieve this goal, it is crucial for multi-robot systems to collaborate seamlessly and communicate efficiently. In particular, this paper considers the problem of assigning tasks to robots while accounting for communication constraints. We propose a novel framework that utilizes a novel task assignment algorithm that ensures the feasibility of the system. The proposed algorithm can handle both static and dynamic environments, where robots may encounter obstacles or other agents during their journey. Moreover, we show how our approach can be adapted to incorporate additional objectives such as minimizing robot travel times or optimally assigning coverage areas among multiple group

The use of deep learning methods in scientific computing represents a potential paradigm shift in engineering problem solving, and it has been shown to outperform traditional approaches. This paper presents the first comprehensive review of deep learning for scientific computing, highlighting its applications across various domains such as computational chemistry, computational physics, and computational biology. The paper begins with an overview of the field, exploring the diverse applications of deep learning for scientific computing, including classification, regression, and dimensionality reduction tasks. It then delves into specific algorithms, discussing their strengths and limitations, before turning to future directions for research in this exciting area. Throughout the text, pract

This study explores the benefits of integrating the novel clustered federated learning (CFL) algorithm into graph neural networks (GNNs), enabling efficient collaboration across nodes. We demonstrate how CFL-enhanced GNNs can address various real-world problems, including the clustering of users and movies in a movie recommendation system and the identification of fraudulent credit card transactions. Our experimental results show that the CFL-enhanced GNN achieves state-of-the-art performance on both tasks while maintaining high privacy protection and energy efficiency. Additionally, we analyze the effectiveness of CFL-enhanced GNN in different scenarios, revealing its versatility in diverse applications. Our findings highlight the potential for hybrid algorithms to achieve superior accura

Human brain and behavior provide a rich venue that can inspire novel control and learning methods for robotics, but they also pose significant challenges. In this work we study the problem of continuous and sparse reward learning in the context of human demonstrations. In particular, we focus on the setting where human demonstrations are sparsely labeled with rewards, and we aim to learn an optimal policy from such observations. We first study the theoretical properties of the problem. Then, we propose a new algorithm based on a novel formulation that enables us to learn an optimal policy using only data collected by the robot itself and without requiring any prior information about the problem. We provide empirical results showing that our approach outperforms existing techniques on a var

The ability to translate natural language queries into SQL is a crucial skill for database management systems. Recently, this capability has been claimed for large language models (LLMs), prompting us to thoroughly evaluate this claim across a spectrum of LLMs. We propose a comprehensive framework to benchmark the Text-to-SQL capability of LLMs, encompassing three dimensions: (1) the generation capability, i.e., can an LLM generate correct SQL queries from natural language questions; (2) the understanding capability, i.e., can an LLM comprehend the meaning of natural language questions, as reflected by the generation of correct SQL queries; and (3) the improvement with training data, i.e., how does an LLM's Text-to-SQL capability evolve with the size and domain of training data. We systematically vary the training data size and source (i.e., Reddit, Stackexchange, and a combination of both) for four LLMs (i.e., GPT-3, GPT-4, GPT-3.5, and GPT-2). We find that (1) the LLMs generally perform well on simple SQL queries but struggle with complex queries; (2) the LLMs frequently produce SQL queries with syntax errors; and (3) increasing the training data size improves the generation accuracy but at the cost of increased syntax errors.

Automatic short answer grading (ASAG) has gained increasing attention for its potential to alleviate the burden of grading in large-scale, high-stakes exams. However, the development of ASAG systems has been hampered by the lack of explainability in the decision-making process of such systems, making it difficult to identify and address the root causes of grading errors. In this paper, we propose an explainable neuro-symbolic pipeline for multi-domain ASAG. Our proposed pipeline first employs a pre-trained language model (PLM) as a selector to choose the most relevant paragraph for the given question from the student response. Then, a domain-specific lexical semantic network (DLSN) is used to extract lexical-semantic features from the selected paragraph. Finally, a neural network (NN) is applied to predict the score of the response based on the lexical-semantic features. To demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed explainable pipeline, we conducted extensive experiments on two multi-domain ASAG datasets and one multi-modal ASAG dataset. Experimental results show that the proposed explainable pipeline outperforms previous PLM-based models by a significant margin. Moreover, we can easily obtain the explanation for each prediction made by the proposed explainable pipeline, which can be used for error analysis and model improvement.

Machine learning (ML) has made significant progress in recent decades, largely thanks to the rapid development of deep learning technologies. However, the current ML paradigm is far from being able to deal with open-world data, where the number of classes is not fixed and can grow over time, and where novel classes appear alongside new data. In fact, open-world conditions are the rule rather than the exception in real-world applications, and yet the majority of ML techniques have been developed under the assumption of a fixed number of classes and do not generalize well to open-world data. To overcome this limitation, there is a growing effort to develop open-world ML techniques, which have the potential to revolutionize how we do ML in the future. In this survey, we review the state-of-the-art in open-world ML, including open-world supervised learning, open-world unsupervised learning, and open-world reinforcement learning. We provide new perspectives for the future development of open-world ML techniques, with a particular focus on the open-world reinforcement learning problem.

First responders (FRs) navigate hazardous, unfamiliar environments in the field. They often lack access to a shared understanding of their surroundings, leading them towards suboptimal decision-making and operational efficiency during missions like search & rescue operations or disaster response scenarios such as floods/wildfires etc., which can have dire consequences on both human life safety outcomes but also economic costs incurred by these types activities when conducted poorly due too little information available about conditions beforehand via technology platforms that could help streamline processes involving FR teams before deployment begins altogether
