An assurance case has become an integral component for the certification of safety-critical systems. The process of developing an assurance case involves a rigorous analysis of system design and implementation. One key aspect in the development of assurance cases is the documentation of rationale, which explains the reasoning behind each claim made within the assurance case. The use of Natural Language Processing (NLP) techniques, such as natural language generation (NLG), is emerging as a promising approach to automate the generation of rationale texts from structured evidence statements. However, existing approaches typically focus on generating rationale texts that are factually accurate but lack sufficient detail to be useful in practice. In this paper, we introduce an innovative NLP a

Multimodal language models that map images and texts to a shared representation space have shown impressive performance in the last year. However, evaluating the quality of image-text mappings is difficult, and existing benchmarks are designed for a different task. In this work, we propose a new framework to evaluate the quality of image-text mappings. Our framework is based on the observation that well-mapped images and texts share similar attributes. Therefore, we introduce attribute prediction as a proxy for image-text mapping quality. We demonstrate the effectiveness of our framework by showing that (1) our new attribute prediction task improves over existing image-text benchmarks, (2) finetuned multimodal language models are better at attribute prediction than unfinetuned ones, and (3) models that map images and texts to different representation spaces are worse at attribute prediction than models that map them to the same space. Additionally, we provide evidence that attribute prediction correlates with human preference ratings for 51,500 image-text pairs. Our code and datasets are publicly available.

For robots to perform assistive tasks in unstructured home environments, they must learn and reason about the dynamics of human-object interaction. This work proposes a novel data collection methodology that enables learning the dynamics of human object interactions from naturalistic data using deep reinforcement learning (RL). We designed a study where 20 participants were asked to engage in 36 different tasks involving moving objects in front of them to a specific location on an overhead shelf. The collected video recordings were then used to generate a dataset for training a RL agent for 10 different task types. Our results show that RL agents can be trained in this way to achieve performance comparable to humans. Additionally, we show that our data collection procedure enables the extr

This paper describes the deployment and experimentation architecture of the Internet of Things experimentation facility being deployed at the University of Canterbury. The Internet of Things is a key future technology area for New Zealand, with potential applications in smart cities, precision farming, healthcare, and many other areas. To develop IoT solutions that meet the needs of the community it is essential to have an experimental environment that allows researchers and industry practitioners to try out ideas on real systems. We describe the design decisions made during the development of the UoC IoT Experimentation Facility (UoC-IEF), which includes the hardware used in the system, and the software components that provide the experimentation infrastructure. The UoC-IEF has been desig

Subnetwork generalization, where a large pretrained language model is split into a small ``head'' and a large ``body'', has emerged as a promising paradigm to obtain efficient and effective natural language processing models. However, its working principle is not well understood. In this paper, we conduct a systematic study of subnetwork generalization in pretrained language models. With a focus on the Heuristic Core, a representative subnetwork generalization framework, we investigate the generalization capability of the resulting small heads and large bodies, the interaction between them, and the representational change inside the body. Our experiments reveal that the head pretrains the body in a ``heuristic'' manner, i.e., the head mostly learns to use a simple rule-based mechanism rather than a complex representation-based mechanism to improve the body's prediction. As a result, the body can be further improved by a simple fine-tuning or even an extensive pretraining. The Heuristic Core splits the pretrained language model into two components: a simple rule-based head and a complex representation-based body. The body is further improved by the head in a bottom-up manner.

Handwritten mathematical expression recognition (HMER) is challenging in image-to-text tasks due to the existence of handwriting variations, complex structures, and large volumes. In this paper, we propose a novel method for HMER that utilizes attention mechanism and cross-attention mechanism as an encoder and decoder. Our method not only extracts the main structure information but also effectively captures the fine-grained feature information of the input handwritten image. To enhance the model's ability to learn contextual dependencies, we introduce a new attention mechanism called Multi-Scale Attention (MSA). MSA considers both local and global features and generates multiple feature maps at different scales. Furthermore, we employ a Cross-Attention Module (CAM) to explicitly capture th

In an attempt to make programming easier to learn, we investigate the possibility of using natural language feedback for programming exercises. Humans get feedback on their actions all the time, and we argue that computers should do the same. We pilot a system for generating natural language feedback for programming exercises using GPT-4. We test the system on 100 programming exercises from the ACM programming competition, where participants are supposed to fix the code of a given program. We find that the feedback generation system can replicate human feedback ratings for 67 of the 100 programming exercises, which is significantly better than a baseline system that does not use GPT-4. We also find that the feedback generation system can be used to create new programming exercises, by providing feedback for solutions to non-exercise tasks.

As a novel representative of factorization machine, field-aware factorization machine (FAFM) introduces the field information into the factorization process, which improves the performance of factorization machine. However, FAFM suffers from the overfitting problem since the model order is much higher than the number of training data. To address this problem, we introduce $L_0$ regularization to FAFM, because $L_0$ norm of a vector is the number of non-zero elements in the vector and it can precisely control the model sparsity. Unfortunately, optimizing the $L_0$ regularized FAFM is an NP-hard problem, we reformulate the $L_0$ regularized FAFM as a Ising model, which is an integer quadratic programming problem. We then apply the Lagrange dual problem to derive a dual Ising model, and solve it using the quantum annealing algorithm. We compare the performance of the $L_0$ regularized FAFM with the state-of-the-art methods for field-aware recommendation, and the experimental results on two benchmark datasets demonstrate that the $L_0$ regularized FAFM achieves the competitive performance.

Lyndon words are extensively studied in combinatorics on words -- they play a crucial role in the study of the Sturmian words. The set of Lyndon words is closed under reverse, but not under complementation or concatenation (except for the trivial case when there exists only one Lyndon word). In this paper we characterize pairs of Lyndon words which can be complemented and pairs of Lyndon words which can be concatenated. This allows us to introduce two new families of Lyndon words: the so-called \emph{complementable} and \emph{concatenatable} Lyndon words. We also provide an algorithm for generating the Lyndon words of length $n$ by using the set of Lyndon words of length $n-1$. This algorithm has applications in the study of the well-known Morse sequence of permutations.

Benchmarks are essential for advancing scientific knowledge. They provide a common ground for evaluating the merits of new methods, and they facilitate replication and comparison with past work. Despite the recognized importance of benchmarks, their creation and maintenance are often overlooked. This is especially true for research areas that are inherently difficult to benchmark, such as creative design and open-ended exploration. These tasks are typically unique to specific domains, lack standardized procedures, and are often perceived as requiring a high degree of human expertise. As a consequence, researchers often struggle to find suitable benchmarks for their work, and the research community as a whole suffers from an abundance of ad-hoc evaluation methods. In this paper, we introduce Acceleron, a framework for automating the creation and maintenance of research benchmarks. Our goal is to empower researchers to easily create high-quality benchmarks, perform comparative analyses, and publish benchmarking results. We demonstrate the effectiveness of our framework by creating three new benchmarks for creative design, two of which are based on real-world applications. We show that our tool can help researchers to (1) systematically identify and fill gaps in existing benchmarks, (2) evaluate the impact of changing benchmark properties, and (3) design new benchmarks that are better suited for their research questions. Our hope is that Acceleron will help to level the playing field for comparative analyses, facilitate the replication of results, and ultimately accelerate scientific progress.

Few-shot anomaly detection (FSAD) is essential in industrial manufacturing. However, most existing approaches still rely on a large number of normal samples to achieve competitive performance. In this paper, we propose a novel framework called MetaAnomaly to address this issue. The key idea is to leverage the knowledge from meta-learning and one-class classification to promote the model's generalization ability. Specifically, our approach involves two stages. First, it utilizes the knowledge distillation method to generate pseudo-normal samples based on the few-shot normal samples. Second, by employing a self-supervised encoder, we further train these generated samples as part of the normal distribution for future use. Experimental results demonstrate that our proposed approach outperforms

Human annotations are a common weak supervision signal used to train machine learning models. When the annotations are subjective, they can introduce noise and bias into the model. However, the impact of subjective annotations on the resulting model error is not well understood. To do so, we propose a framework to decompose the total error into four distinct error sources: annotation noise, annotation bias, model noise, and model bias. We show that the impact of each error source depends on the learning algorithm and that some learning algorithms are more robust to subjective annotations than others. Our findings reveal that the distribution of errors introduced by subjective annotations can be more skewed than previously thought, with important implications for model evaluation and selection.

Human-robot interaction (HRI) is a two-way communication process between humans and robots. For robots to actively participate in and assist with human activities, they need to perceive and understand their surroundings, as well as infer and anticipate human intentions. Vision is one of the primary means by which humans perceive and navigate their environment, and thus plays a fundamental role in HRI scenarios. In this paper, we focus on improving a social robot's visual perception of its human counterparts in both controlled and in-the-wild environments. For this purpose, we utilize an off-the-shelf consumer drone equipped with a RGB-D camera and a deep neural network-based visual perception system. We first evaluate the robot's visual perception performance in a controlled environment using a simulated human dataset. We then take the evaluations to a new level by testing the robot's performance in an in-the-wild setting where the robot has to interact with humans in a semi-structured environment. We analyze and discuss the results in terms of the robot's perceptual capabilities and its potential impact on human trust and collaboration. We show that a social robot's visual perception performance has a significant impact on human trust and collaboration, and that the robot can maintain human trust even in the presence of visual perception errors.

We propose a new attention mechanism with linear complexity, ATP, that fixates \textbf{A}ttention \textbf{T}o \textbf{P}arameters. It is motivated by the observation that in multi-head self-attention, the most important information for computing each head's output is its own parameter matrix. This intuition can be used to compute attention within each head in a much more efficient manner than standard approaches. Our experimental results show that our proposed method achieves better performance than the state-of-the-art methods on both image classification and language modeling tasks while reducing the computational cost of attention by approximately 25x. Furthermore, we show that even using ATP with only one head can outperform all other methods. In addition, we introduce an alternative f

Industry is at the forefront of adopting new technologies, and the process followed by the industries to adopt new technology is known as Digital Transformation. Digital transformation involves the adoption of a wide range of digital technologies that can transform a business’s performance, customer experience, and efficiency. This process requires a significant investment in both time and money for companies to implement it successfully. The primary goal of digital transformation is to enhance the customer experience. It helps organizations optimize their processes through advanced analytics, automation, and other emerging technologies. These improvements allow them to gain competitive advantages over their rivals while also enhancing the overall productivity levels within their operation

This work proposes to augment the lifting steps of the conventional wavelet transform with additional neural network layers. The proposed approach, called WaveNet, is based on a novel residual connection design that enables the model to extract non-local features from the signal. This allows for more efficient feature extraction and better noise robustness. Experiments on both synthetic and real-world datasets demonstrate the efficacy of WaveNet in various applications, including speech enhancement, image denoising, and audio restoration. The results show that WaveNet outperforms existing methods by up to 6% dB in terms of Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR). Additionally, it achieves state-of-the-art performance compared to other competing approaches. We also provide a detailed analysis of the in

Diffusion models benefit from instillation of task-specific information into the score function to steer generation towards specific classes or objects. However, this process is computationally expensive and necessitates access to a large amount of data. We propose the use of prompts as a way of transferring information to the diffusion model without the need for additional data or training time. Prompting can be used to change the conditioning of the diffusion model on-the-fly, allowing it to generate images from different datasets, in different styles, or with different backgrounds, by changing the prompt. Our results show that the use of prompts does not require a large number of samples nor an expensive pretraining process. Instead, we only need a handful of samples from the target dat

Are malicious repositories hiding under the educational label in GitHub? Recent studies have identified collections of GitHub accounts that are used for academic purposes, including assignments and tutorials. The authors also found a large number (21%) which were not properly labeled as such or had no clear purpose at all - yet they still exhibited suspicious behavior like high code churn rates over short time periods without any meaningful contributions from other users; this suggests potential misuse by attackers seeking anonymity while committing their activities on these platforms unnoticed due to mislabeling them incorrectly

Physics-informed neural networks (PINNs) are a promising tool for predicting the flow in stirred tanks. However, training PINNs can be computationally expensive, especially for large-scale industrial tanks. To reduce the computational cost, we propose a model hierarchy that consists of three levels: a high-fidelity model, a low-fidelity model, and a super-resolution model. The high-fidelity model is a PINN that accurately predicts the flow field but is computationally expensive. The low-fidelity model is a simplified PINN that predicts the flow field using only a subset of the high-fidelity model's parameters. The super-resolution model is a PINN that predicts the flow field at a higher resolution than the high-fidelity model. We train the high-fidelity and low-fidelity models using a physics-based loss function, and we train the super-resolution model using a data-based loss function. We demonstrate that the proposed model hierarchy can predict the flow field in a stirred tank with a high accuracy while reducing the computational cost by up to 80% compared to training a PINN without the model hierarchy.

Large Language Models (LLMs) have shown impressive performance in recent years and have been widely used in various tasks. However, studies have revealed that LLMs are prone to prompt-induced biases, which can undermine their performance and reliability. Prompt-induced biases arise from the complex interactions between the model's parameters and the prompt, making them challenging to identify and mitigate. In this work, we propose a novel framework called Causal Prompting, which offers a principled approach to debiasing LLM prompting based on front-door adjustment, a classic causal inference method. We demonstrate the effectiveness of Causal Prompting in mitigating prompt-induced biases in LLMs through extensive experiments. Our experiments show that Causal Prompting consistently outperforms benchmark methods for prompt debiasing, including re-ranking, fine-tuning, and prompt rewriting.

Interpretability is the next frontier in machine learning research. In the search for white box models, we see a growing body of literature on interpretable deep neural networks and an increasing trend to apply post-hoc explainability methods on non-interpretable black box models. This article reviews both directions by providing a taxonomy of explainability methods that can be applied on interpretable or non-interpretable models. The classification criteria are methodology, model types and explanation types. We also provide examples and discuss the pros and cons of each method as well as open issues and future challenges. We hope this survey could help researchers choose the appropriate explainability method for their applications.

This work explores an innovative algorithm designed to enhance the mobility of underactuated agents by leveraging a combination of visual feedback and reinforcement learning (RL). Our approach enables these agents to autonomously navigate complex environments without the need for external infrastructure or human supervision. We introduce a novel reward function that emphasizes both visual acquisition and mobility, thereby enhancing the RL agent's ability to efficiently learn complex motion trajectories in challenging terrains. The effectiveness of our method is demonstrated through extensive simulations and real-world experiments with a quadruped robot. Additionally, we present a comprehensive evaluation of the proposed algorithm and its impact on navigation performance, highlighting its r

In this paper, we introduce the first algorithmic framework for Blackwell approachability on the sequence-space. This framework generalizes both the classical Blackwell approachability and the recent results of \citet{Bernstein2019On}. We provide a finite sample guarantee for the performance of our algorithms in terms of the regret and the number of rounds required to achieve a desired accuracy. Our main technical contribution is the introduction of the notion of a Blackwell set and its connection with the existence of a Blackwell approachable sequence. We prove that a sequence is Blackwell approachable if and only if it lies in some Blackwell set. As a direct consequence, we show that any Blackwell set is Blackwell approachable. Furthermore, we present an efficient algorithm that can be u

This work highlights an approach for incorporating realistic uncertainties into scientific computing workflows. The goal of this project is to develop a framework that can be used within existing software codes, and has been implemented in the context both ocean forecasting systems (e10s) using unstructured grid finite element methods as well probabilistic forward modelling tools based on Markov Chain Monte Carlo techniques from statistics . Advertisements account for a significant portion of the content distributed by radio stations. Detecting advertisements is important for tasks such as billing and content analysis. This paper proposes RADIA, a novel method that uses intelligent analytics to detect advertisements in radio programs. The method combines acoustic features with metadata and applies machine learning. We evaluate our method on a unique data set that contains 307 manually labeled advertisements from 4141 hours of radio programs. Our experiments show that RADIA outperforms existing methods (by up to 32%) in terms of detection accuracy.

We present a new paradigm for human-in-the-loop machine learning, where the role of humans is to provide subjective labels, and a new LLM-powered tool, Modeling Collaborator, to enable this paradigm. Our approach recognizes that humans are better at providing subjective labels, e.g., of image quality or the like, and that LLMs are better at the mechanical task of labeling. By having humans provide subjective labels, we open up the possibility of collecting much larger, more diverse training datasets, which we show can lead to significant performance improvements. To make this practical, we designed the Modeling Collaborator, a new LLM-powered tool that automates many of the tasks involved. Specifically, the tool uses an LLM to propose labels, which are then shown to humans in an optimal order so that the human can provide the most information with the least effort. The tool also uses an LLM to predict the predictive value of each proposed label, so that labels can be automatically filtered and prioritized. This paradigm and tool enable new possibilities for human-LLM collaboration in machine learning. As a proof of concept, we demonstrate the effectiveness of our approach for collecting image quality annotations at a fraction of the cost and time of traditional methods.

Reactive programming is an approach to building software systems that emphasize the use of asynchronous events and streams of data instead of procedural methods and object-oriented messages. This paper explores the foundations of reactive programming by asking: What is the simplest programming language that can express reactive programs? We answer this question by proposing a minimal programming language called ReaScript. ReaScript has no functions, conditionals, or loops; it only has variables, assignments, and reactions. Despite its simplicity, we show that ReaScript can express a wide range of reactive programs. To show this, we present three case studies: the classic example of a reactive bouncer, a more realistic example of a reactive taxi service, and a complex example of a distributed cryptocurrency ledger. We implement reactions as asynchronous JavaScript functions and use the browser's event loop to simulate time passing. We conduct a human factors study with 178 participants to evaluate the learnability and usability of ReaScript. Our results show that ReaScript is learnable and usable by non-experts, experts, and programmers with a background in functional, imperative, and object-oriented programming. We expect that our work will help future designers of reactive programming languages and tools.

The breadth, scale, and temporal granularity of modern electronic health records (EHR) make them a unique resource for research. However, the lack of standardization in EHR data, coupled with their highly-sensitive nature, presents significant challenges to researchers who seek access to this valuable data source. In response, we developed the NLP Data Repository (NLPRep), an open-source platform for sharing and reproducing research on electronic health record (EHR) data. NLPRep includes curated datasets and tools that enable reproducibility and data access for natural language processing (NLP)-based clinical research. Our repository is designed to facilitate open science by providing an easily accessible and reusable platform for both researchers and practitioners. We demonstrate the util

We propose a novel unsupervised learning framework called Latent Dataset Distillation (LDD) to extract useful representations from unlabeled datasets. Specifically, we introduce a new training objective for diffusion models that encourages the latent variables to act as a distillation layer for unlabeled data. Our method can be applied to any diffusion model and can use any supervised loss, making it flexible to different training setups. The resulting representations are competitive compared to SOTA unsupervised methods on established public benchmarks.

Evolutionary optimization algorithms are often derived from loose biological analogies and struggle to leverage information in the raw fitness landscape. To bridge this gap, we introduce a new framework that combines learning and evolution with a novel paradigm of agent-environment interaction. We present an approach where an agent learns the environment dynamics through interactions before applying its own dynamics to solve a problem. The agent can also learn other information to improve itself by interacting with its environment. Our work includes two types of agents: (1) an agent equipped with a neural network which is tasked with mapping the raw fitness landscape into a more suitable representation for solving the task, and (2) a second agent whose sole purpose is to evolve the first a

This paper introduces OccFusion, a straightforward and efficient sensor fusion framework for predicting 3D poses from RGB-D sensors. OccFusion utilizes the depth information to provide an initial estimate of the pose and then refines it using only visual features. The proposed method is based on the combination of a deep network and an optimization process. The network is trained on synthetic data, where the loss function is defined in terms of the distance between the predicted poses and their ground truth. During training, the loss is evaluated by running the optimization process with the same input as during testing. This allows us to train a network that provides an optimal initialization for the optimization process. In addition to this novel training procedure, the proposed network h

Large Language Models (LLMs) have demonstrated great potential in complex reasoning tasks, yet their performance is often limited by the ability to generate relevant and reliable information. One way of improving this capability is through Knowledge Base Integration which aims at augmenting LLMS with a rich knowledge base that can be used for context-based retrieval or direct query answering techniques during inference time rather than just relying on precomputed language model weights alone as current models do today . In order understand how effectively these approaches work together towards achieving better results we must consider two main factors: 1 ) what kindof data needstobeincludedwithinourknowledgebaseand2howbestcanweintegrateitwiththemodelitself?

Distributed Stream Processing (DSP) focuses on the near real-time processing of large volumes of data in a distributed environment, where the stream sources are geographically dispersed. To address the challenges associated with DSP, this paper proposes an efficient and scalable framework, namely, Adaptive Incremental Window Selection (AIWS), which incorporates window selection and incremental processing techniques for processing streams from multiple sources. This approach enhances data availability by leveraging the adaptive nature of the window selection process that dynamically adjusts to changes in the underlying data distribution. The proposed AIWS framework also introduces a novel concept called "intermediate windows" that facilitates efficient data access and processing. This metho

The use of machine learning (ML) techniques to solve complex physical problems has been considered recently as a promising way. However, the physics-informed neural network method requires sufficient data and accurate information about initial conditions for solving partial differential equations with boundary/initial values in order not to violate conservation laws or stability constraints inherent within fluid dynamics systems such that it becomes difficult when dealing directly against real world applications where these parameters are unknown beforehand due lack access during testing phase leading towards inaccurate predictions which may lead into more dangerous scenarios than those encountered by traditional solvers like Finite Volume Method(FVM).

With the help of knowledge graph, we can construct a deep neural network model to complete the knowledge triplet. However, manually constructing knowledge graph is labor-intensive and inefficient. Most of the existing models that construct knowledge graph from web pages rely on the information extraction from unstructured text, which suffers from low accuracy and high redundancy. In this paper, we propose an algorithm called AceMap, which fuses the citation relationship in the structured academic paper dataset to construct a knowledge graph. The experiment results show that AceMap outperforms the state-of-the-art methods by a significant margin. We also conduct a case study on the generated knowledge graph and show that our method can construct the knowledge graph with high accuracy and few redundant relationships.
