We present medial parametrization, a new approach to parameterizing any compact planar domain bounded by polygonal or curved closed curves. The method is based on the observation that for every such domain there exists a unique polynomial curve that lies in its interior and touches all of its boundary curves at the same distance from the center of mass of their union. We introduce three novel algorithms that compute medial parametrizations: one for domains bounded by polygons, another for domains bounded by closed Jordan arcs, and a third for domains bounded by general closed curves. All three algorithms run in linear time in the number of vertices of the input curves; we also provide an algorithm that runs in linear time in the size of the input curves. We demonstrate our approach with se

Autistic adults often experience stigma and discrimination at work, leading them to seek social support from peers. However, many autistic individuals struggle with social relationships due to their sensory processing differences and communication challenges. This presents a unique challenge for autistic professionals: how to navigate their professional lives while balancing the need for social support with the potential social difficulties that can arise in the workplace. In this article, we explore how technology has been used to address these challenges, including the development of tools like text-to-speech software and virtual reality simulations to facilitate social interactions. We also discuss the importance of autistic-led support networks, and how technology can play a role in cr

Graph is a fundamental mathematical structure in characterizing relations between different objects and has been widely used on various applications. The graph convolutional networks (GCNs) have demonstrated great potential in learning from graphs, but the existing GCNs still suffer from limited expressiveness due to the lack of a powerful embedding mechanism. In this paper, we propose a novel graph neural network called Graph Diffusion Network (GDNet), which can efficiently learn from graphs with arbitrary topologies. It is based on the observation that the graph diffusion process can be viewed as an effective way to explore information propagation over a graph. As such, GDNet incorporates a novel graph diffusion mechanism into the GCN framework. This mechanism allows us to capture the in

Sentiment analysis (SA) has been, and is still, a thriving research area. Given the rapid development of large language models (LLMs), SA with LLMs gains more attention than ever before. However, most previous works simply fine-tune pretrained LLMs on SA datasets without any explicit optimization objectives or model designs to enhance their SA performance. In this paper, we propose an effective SA framework named Sensitive Language Model for SA (Sense-LAMA). To improve SA performance, we design a sensitivity mechanism into LLMs by training them with different types of sensitive information. We investigate how different types of sensitive information affect SA results. Furthermore, in order to further improve SA accuracy, we design a novel loss function based on the idea that sentiment pola

Knowledge graph completion (KGC) is a widely used method to tackle incompleteness and inconsistency problems in knowledge graphs. However, the quality of knowledge graph completion methods has been largely underestimated due to a lack of ground truth labels for evaluation. To alleviate this problem, we propose a novel approach to generate ground truth entity triples for KGC tasks by collecting data from Wikipedia and exploiting the co-occurrence information among entities, relations, and textual contexts. We release our dataset with 23,568 ground truth triples, as well as an open-source tool to facilitate future research. Our experiments demonstrate that our generated ground truth triples can be successfully leveraged to evaluate the performance of state-of-the-art KGC models. Furthermore,

Text-to-image models have shown remarkable performance in generating 2D images consistent with a given text query. However, when it comes to 3D-consistent image generation, i.e. generating the same image viewed from multiple angles, these models are outperformed by specialized multi-view synthesis models. In this work, we ask the following question: Can we leverage the text-to-image models for 3D-consistent image generation? To this end, we propose ViewDiff, a novel 3D-consistent image generation framework that generates multiple views of an image from a given text query. Specifically, we design a view-specific tokenization mechanism that encodes 3D-view information into tokens and concatenates them with the original text embedding. These modified embeddings are then fed into a text-to-image model to generate 3D-consistent images. To further enhance 3D-consistency, we introduce a view-consistency loss that encourages the output images to be consistent across different views. Our experimental results demonstrate that ViewDiff generates 3D-consistent images that outperform existing text-to-image models without view-specific training data or fine-tuning.

This paper introduces BLaIR, a series of pretrained sentence embedding models specialized for open-domain question answering (QA). Based on the Transformer encoder and BERT model architecture with two additional layers to encode long sequences up 1024 tokens. We train our large version ($L$) using ~3B parameters that has more than twice as many parameter counts compared against previous best results from QABERT+XLMR in OpenWebText corpus (~76GB), which is also publicly available at https://github/googleai/. In order validate its generalizability across other tasks beyond text matching used by existing pretraining methods like RoBERTa or T5 family , we perform experiments such fine tuning over multiple downstream NLP applications including span prediction based reading comprehension tests(S

Current model-based reinforcement learning (MBRL) agents struggle with long-term dependencies. To address this issue, we propose a novel framework called Time Machine (TM). TM augments the existing MBRL agent with two additional components: an online memory that maintains past state trajectories and a retrieval module that accesses them to enhance the current policy. We evaluate our method on three continuous control benchmarks and find that it outperforms the current state-of-the-art in these settings by up to 25%. For example, on Antmaze-v0 and HalfCheetah-v0 environments from OpenAI Gym, TM reduces the success rate gap between human demonstrations and baseline policy by 16% and 37%, respectively. We further demonstrate how our approach can be extended to the case of multiple policies.

We introduce a new notion of neighboring databases for coverage problems such as Max Cover and Set Cover under the non-uniform, uniform or weighted model. We show that when there is an $O(1)$-neighboring database (and thus $\Delta$-neighborly) one can solve \textsc{MaxCover} in linear time via simple greedy algorithms with respect to both metrics on subsets: Hamming distance ($\ell_0$) metric between pairs $\{X\subseteq S\}$ by summing up differences $|x - y|$ over all elements; Minkowski sum ($L^p$ norm). On any other arbitrary neighbor graphs we design efficient $(2-\epsilon)$ approximation algorithm using random walks followed Greedy Algorithm based upon this observation which improves previous results where existing bounds are either weak due having large gap from optimal solution like

We study level set teleportation, an optimization sub-routine which seeks to accelerate the convergence of gradient descent by computing and updating an approximate gradient at a given point. This is achieved by projecting the point onto its nearest level set. In this paper we propose a novel algorithm that computes the nearest level set without explicitly solving any equation or even differentiating the objective function. Instead, it takes advantage of the smoothness properties of the level sets as well as the monotonicity of the objective function. The resulting algorithm has a complexity linear in the size of the data set and the number of level sets. A rigorous error analysis is also provided. Our results are compared with those from previous state-of-the-art methods and demonstrate t

In this paper, we consider a Micro Aerial Vehicle (MAV) system teleoperated by an operator and several autonomous agents. We propose a cooperative scheme whereby the MAV can be deployed to perform two distinct tasks: map building for the operator and target detection for the autonomous agents. The MAV is equipped with a depth camera that generates depth maps in real time, providing information about the environment's structure to both the operator and autonomous agents. A probabilistic model for depth maps is proposed, enabling the operator to build a map of the surroundings while also enhancing the target detection capabilities of the autonomous agents. An algorithm based on the proposed probabilistic model is used to generate a candidate set of hypotheses regarding the MAV's location at

In Colombia, astronomical research is experiencing accelerated growth. In order to better understand its evolution, we present the results of a national survey on the status and perceptions of Colombian astronomers, including data collected by the Association of Astronomy Researchers in Colombia (ACC). The survey was conducted via online questionnaires between May 2016 and February 2017. A total of 87 responses were received. This paper provides an overview of the state of the Colombian astronomy community, including its composition, interests and motivations, academic background, and professional activities. We also report on some aspects of the research environment, including funding and infrastructure. Our findings confirm that there has been a significant increase in the number of astr

Improving the safety of collaborative manipulators necessitates the reduction of inertia, which increases stability and allows for a faster response time. This paper presents a novel method for fabricating lightweight carbon fiber composite structures using a thermoplastic tape-based process (TTP) combined with automated fiber placement (AFP). The TTP method combines both continuous and discontinuous fiber reinforcements to create hybrid composite parts that exhibit superior stiffness and impact resistance compared to conventional monolithic counterparts. In this study, we demonstrate the feasibility of integrating AFP technology into the TTP process to enhance manufacturing efficiency and cost-effectiveness. We conducted experimental tests on 3D printed models to evaluate the performance

Our understanding of the generalization capabilities of neural networks (NNs) is still incomplete. Prevailing explanations for NN generalization often rely on over-parameterization and the lottery ticket hypothesis, but these explanations fall short when considering NNs that are not over-parametrized or undergo pruning. In this work, we study a family of shallow, 2-layer ReLU NNs with varying degrees of parametric bias, and provide evidence that their generalization performance can be largely explained by the amount of information they encode about their input distributions. We find that such NNs achieve better generalization when trained with lower bias, which leads to a more accurate encoding of the input distribution. This result provides a new perspective on the generalization phenomen

We are witnessing the rise of new hybrid entities made of living and non-living matter, natural and artificial components, which challenge our concepts of body and artefact, and our ways of relating to the world. They prompt us to think of new narratives, to imagine new possibilities for living in relation to these entities, and to re-think our position within the world. In this paper, I explore the philosophical implications of one such hybrid: the cyborg. I do so by proposing a series of conceptual experiments, which I use to reflect on the cyborg's status as both body and artefact, and on the implications this has for our understanding of the human condition. I argue that the cyborg allows us to imagine a post-human condition, one in which the boundaries between self and other, subject and object, are blurred, and new forms of relating to the world emerge. I suggest that these philosophical reflections on the cyborg have important implications for how we think about technological development, and for the ways in which we relate to technology. They prompt us to think beyond human-centred narratives, and to explore the possibilities of a more-than-human world.

Second-order methods can converge much faster than first-order methods by incorporating second-order information into the update. However, existing methods either require a large amount of extra computation or are only applicable to some special cases. In this paper, we propose an efficient method for accelerating second-order methods. Specifically, we present an efficient algorithm called SOGES (Second-Order Gradient Estimation Scheme). It utilizes the second-order gradient information and the estimated Hessian to achieve fast convergence. The key idea is that instead of estimating the full Hessian matrix, we leverage the gradient information along with an appropriate matrix norm to estimate the corresponding Hessian vector. With this estimation, the proposed method can achieve the same p

Reward hacking occurs when an agent performs very well with respect to a "proxy" reward function, but performs poorly on the actual goal. We show that the optimal policy for any proxy function can be expressed as a mixture of policies in which each component is optimized by its own proxy function. Using this characterization we introduce "conservative imitation learning", a method for learning from human demonstrations that minimizes the risk that the learner will perform poorly on the true reward. Our approach also guarantees that the learner's performance cannot get arbitrarily close to the optimal performance with respect to the proxy reward. In addition, we define and study a notion of robustness to changes in the reward function. Our results suggest that conservative imitation learnin

Facial action unit (AU) detection, aiming to classify AU present in the fae, is an important task in facial expression analysis. However, due to the complexity of face expressions and the lack of large-scale datasets, existing AU detectors often require a lot of labeled data for training, which results in high costs. In this paper, we introduce a novel method called UPSNet (Unsupervised Pose Supervision Network), which can be trained with only pose annotations. Specifically, UPSNet first projects facial landmarks onto 3D space and then warps them to a canonical space where all faces are aligned. The network will learn to predict the 2D locations of AUs from the projected images in the canonical space. Since it only requires unlabeled data for training, UPSNet can be used to generate more l

This paper presents a new and improved DareFightingICE platform, a fighting game platform with built-in capabilities for evaluating and improving the AI agents that play it. We present two versions of this platform: one for games played using discrete actions, and another for games played using continuous actions (such as boxing). This work is based on our previous paper, where we presented an initial version of this platform. In this work, we report significant improvements in the speed of gameplay and in the quality of the AI agents that play the game. We also present several enhancements to the code base, including support for using multiple GPUs and better agent evaluation methods. The code has been rewritten from scratch in Python 3, and is available at https://github.com/DareFighting

This paper explores the potential of Physics-Informed Neural Networks (PINNs) in addressing the challenges associated with numerical simulation of complex fluid systems. We present an innovative framework that integrates PINNs within a hybrid computational approach to enhance accuracy, efficiency, and scalability. The proposed methodology combines a Lattice Boltzmann Method (LBM) model, which captures microscale interactions, with a PINN-based solver for macroscale phenomena. This hybrid strategy enables accurate predictions across multiple scales while mitigating computational costs, offering a versatile solution for simulating multiscale fluid flows. Through extensive numerical experiments involving various flow regimes, we demonstrate the effectiveness of our approach in handling challe

In this paper, we take a step towards a deeper understanding of learning from human preferences by systematizing and evaluating the use case for human-inferred constraints in offline reinforcement learn ing (RL). We propose an efficient model that is capable to incorporate user feedback as additional information about state transitions during training. Our empirical evaluation on MuJoCo domains shows substantial improvement over existing baselines using only one or two samples per episode while maintaining computational efficiency at scale due its parallelizability with respect both speed up time taken until convergence occurs within 5x times faster than standard off policy gradient methods such those based upon Proximal Policy Optimization Algorithm [1] which itself already achieves good

The current state-of-the-art in artificial intelligence is impressive, especially in terms of natural language processing. We have seen a massive increase over the last few years with large transformer models such as GPT3 and its successors like ChatGPT becoming more common to use by everyone from students for homework assistance (and plagiarism) through engineers looking at new design patterns or even developers creating brand spanking web applications using open source frameworks that they’ve never worked on before!

In recent years, large language models (LLMs) have become increasingly prevalent, offering a vast array of capabilities for various tasks. Large Language Models have also been utilized to generate text in the form of stories, articles, and poetry. However, the quality of these generated texts can vary significantly depending on the prompt used. This study investigates the effectiveness of using LLMs as a tool for assessing the quality of generated text. We conducted experiments using ChatGPT-4 and GPT-3.5, two popular LLMs, to evaluate the efficacy of different types of feedback provided by humans to LLMs. Our results demonstrate that providing specific feedback to LLMs, such as correcting mistakes or highlighting grammatical errors, significantly enhances their performance in generating h

The integration of learning and reasoning is high on the research agenda in AI. Nevertheless, there are still fundamental challenges when dealing with complex reasoning tasks such as planning or decision-making. This paper examines one such challenge: how can we integrate the capabilities of different types of learning (e.g., supervised vs unsupervised) to achieve better performance? We propose a novel framework that integrates reinforcement learning (RL), imitation learning (IL), and knowledge distillation (KD) for enhanced problem solving. Our framework leverages data augmentation techniques like KD to enhance IL and RL, leading to increased robustness and generalizability. The results showcase the potential of our approach by achieving state-of-the-art performance across various benchma

We consider the problem of online learning where the sequence of actions played by the learner must adhere to a graph-based constraint. The goal is to minimize the regret with respect to an expert that has access to both the action space and to the reward function, but not to the graph structure. We assume that the learner has access to a generalized Bellman operator (GBO) which allows the learner to compute the optimal value functions of all neighbors of a given state for every time step. The GBO can be seen as a relaxation of the graph-structured Bellman operator (GBO). We prove that, in expectation, our algorithm achieves $\tilde{O}(\sqrt{\mathcal{V}T})$ regret when using the GBO, where $\mathcal{V}$ is the maximum possible Bellman operator norm and $T$ is the total number of time steps

Adaptive music is commonly used in games to enhance the gameplay experience. However, the impact of adaptive music on different game genres is not well understood. In this paper, we explore the use of adaptive background music in fighting games, a genre where music is traditionally non-adaptive. We present a method to adapt existing non-adaptive soundtracks to create engaging adaptive music that enhances the gameplay experience. We analyse the emotional response of players to different music styles and adaptions using physiological and self-reporting measures. We find that adaptation of the music increases the energy of the music, as perceived by the players, which in turn increases their self-reported enjoyment and decreases their perceived stress. These findings show that adaptive music is suitable for fighting games, and that careful selection of music features can enhance the gameplay experience.

The conjunction of edge intelligence and the ever-growing Internet-of-Things (IoT) devices have revolutionized IoT systems. However, these technologies also bring along security challenges that must be addressed. In this paper, we introduce a novel approach to detect anomalous behavior in real-time using artificial neural networks (ANNs). Our proposed architecture relies on two distinct stages: feature extraction followed by classification. We employ LSTM units to extract features from time series data and use an ANN for classification. Furthermore, our method incorporates a unique mechanism, which allows for effective learning during inference, enhancing its overall performance. Our experimental results demonstrate the effectiveness of our approach with accuracy rates as high as 98% and f

We introduce PromptCharm, a novel framework for text-to-image generation that emphasizes the role of prompts in the generation process. Unlike traditional text-to-image methods that rely solely on the model to bridge the semantic gap between text and image, we propose that prompts can play a crucial part in narrowing this gap. Prompts, in our approach, are not just triggers for image generation; they also serve as intermediaries that help align the model's interpretation of the text goal with the desired image output. This aligning process occurs through two mechanisms: (1) multi-modal prompt refinement, where we fine-tune a prompt based on user feedback that compares the generated image to the desired image, and (2) multi-modal prompt learning, where we learn a prompt that bridges the gap between the original text goal and the desired image. These two mechanisms work together to improve the alignment of the model's interpretation of the text with the user's image generation goal. We evaluate PromptCharm alongside eight strong baselines on two standard text-to-image datasets, AnswerCharm and Stable Diffusion, and two multi-modal datasets, ArtCharm and Shoes. We find that PromptCharm consistently outperforms the baselines in terms of FID and user preference. PromptCharm's superiority is especially evident in our user study, where it receives significantly higher preference scores than the baselines across all datasets. Our code is publicly available at https://github.com/BeierZhu/PromptCharm.

Large Language Models (LLMs) have demonstrated remarkable reasoning abilities, prompting interest in their potential as the next generation of knowledge engines. However current LLMS are limited by low coverage and lack external sources to support complex inference tasks such a fact verification or commonsense question answering with open-ended answers . To address this limitation , we present Large Knowledge Enhanced Model for Open Ended Question Answering(LKEModel), an enhanced version based on GPT3 that incorporates both general domain facts from Wikipedia articles alongwith 150 million facts derived using FactFinder2 across multiple domains including biology science technology medicine law education arts history geography politics international relations religion philosophy food securi

Audio-Visual Source Localization (AVSL) aims to locate sounding objects within video frames. In contrast to 2D localization tasks, AVSL faces a more complex challenge due to the inherent limitations of 3D representation in videos, including limited temporal resolution and ambiguous visual cues. To address these challenges, this paper introduces an efficient framework called Stereo-Dual-View (SDV), which leverages stereo vision and dual-view feature extraction techniques for AVSL. Specifically, our method employs a novel depth estimation model designed for AVSL that incorporates an object-aware loss function and learns the disparity map by minimizing the difference between the estimated disparity and the ground truth disparity map. Additionally, we employ a dual-view approach that involves

Edge Computing (EC) has gained significant traction in recent years, promising enhanced efficiency and reduced latency for various applications. In the context of EC, a cloudlet is an edge server that provides compute services to nearby devices. The Cloudlets-as-a-Service (CaaS) paradigm enables the dynamic allocation of cloudlets based on their geographical location and service requirements, leading to greater resource utilization and lower energy consumption. However, this approach faces the challenge of efficiently allocating limited resources among multiple clients without compromising their performance demands. This work presents a novel multi-client scheduling algorithm, dubbed EC-MCS, which integrates the concepts of Multi-Objective Optimization and Reinforcement Learning (RL). By l

Let $\mathcal{P}$ be a simple polygon with $m$ vertices and let $P$ denote the projection of $\mathcal{P}$ onto a plane. In this work, we consider a natural problem in computational geometry: the task of estimating the area of $\mathcal{P}$. Our main contribution is a novel algorithm for computing the area of $\mathcal{P}$, which runs in time $\widetilde{\mathcal{O}}(m\log m)$ on a logarithmic-space input. The crux of our algorithm is to define an efficient, local search for the planar convex hull of $\mathcal{P}$, as well as to devise a data structure that stores the minimal enclosing ball around each vertex of $\mathcal{P}$, such that the total space complexity of these two data structures is bounded by $\widetilde{\mathcal{O}}(m\log m)$.

Credit default prediction is a crucial issue in credit scoring that directly relates to the stability of the financial system. This paper introduces a complete machine learning workflow to address credit default prediction for Italian banks. We use a publicly available dataset from the Italian Central Bank that contains information on credit card holders and their payment history. The goal is to predict whether a client will default on their credit card payments. We compare the performance of a linear model, namely Ridge regression, and a non-linear model, namely XGBoost, highlighting the importance of feature engineering. In particular, we show that transforming the original features through mathematical operations, such as polynomial expansion, and through domain knowledge, such as computing credit utilization, improves the performance of the linear model. We also show that non-linear models, such as XGBoost, consistently outperform linear models, unless the dataset is heavily preprocessed. As a pre-processing step, we investigate the effect of label encoding vs. dummy encoding, the effect of training a neural network to learn the encoding, and the effect of including interaction terms. Finally, we discuss how to compute credit default probabilities for Italian banks using our machine learning models.

Recent advancements in large language models (LLMs) have underscored their importance as a foundation for downstream tasks. However, LLMS are prone to bias and can exacerbate societal harms if not carefully curated by human experts before deployment or used with caution when employed on sensitive datasets such those containing personal information like medical records from healthcare providers that may be protected under privacy laws around the world including HIPAA regulations within US jurisdiction which restricts access without explicit consent .

Fusing multiple views into a novel view is a fundamental problem in computer vision. Recent advances in neural radiance fields (NeRFs) treat the problem as sparse view synthesis, where only a few views are available for training. However, existing methods face two challenges: (1) They may produce artifacts in the absence of nearby views, and (2) they are slow during inference as they rely on expensive SotA depth estimation methods. To address these issues, we propose Depth-Guided NeRF (DGN), a fast and robust sparse view synthesis method. During training, we introduce a depth guidance loss to leverage the depth information for better feature alignment. This loss prioritizes pixels within a certain depth range, ensuring that the model focuses on the relevant depth information. During inference, we employ a novel lightweight depth estimation approach using only sparse views, which is guided by the precomputed depth guidance map. This approach significantly reduces the inference time compared to existing methods. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first work to propose a depth guidance based loss and a view-dependent depth estimation approach for sparse view synthesis. Experimental results demonstrate that our method produces images with high visual quality, free from artifacts, and achieves a faster inference speed compared to the state-of-the-art.

Deep learning models have emerged as the cornerstone of medical image segmentation, but their efficacy is limited by their reliance on large datasets. This paper introduces a novel approach to improving deep learning model performance through data augmentation and regularization. The method involves generating synthetic images using generative adversarial networks (GANs), and integrating them into an ensemble framework that combines several trained models. We conduct experiments on three datasets for liver segmentation in CT scans, demonstrating that our approach achieves a 3-5% improvement in dice similarity coefficient (DSC) over conventional methods. Furthermore, we show that the proposed method exhibits better generalization capability than other GAN-based approaches, which are suscept

With the development of intelligent connected vehicles (ICVs), vehicle-infrastructure cooperative perception is attracting increasing research interest. Holographic perception, with its high accuracy and wide field of view, is a promising solution to the cooperative perception problem. However, there is a lack of large-scale datasets and evaluation standards for holographic cooperative perception. To facilitate research in this field, we provide a large-scale dataset and benchmark for multi-sensor holographic intersection and vehicle-infrastructure cooperative perception. The dataset includes 1000 samples collected by four different light sources with both calibrated and uncalibrated data. To evaluate the performance of intersection and cooperative perception, we propose two tasks, i.e., "intersection detection" and "vehicle state prediction." We provide benchmark methods for both tasks. Our benchmark is the first public benchmark for multi-sensor holographic intersection and cooperative perception. We hope the dataset and benchmark will promote research in the field of multi-sensor holographic intersection and cooperative perception.

This study marks a significant advancement by harnessing Large Language Models (LLMs) to identify the most influential factors that contribute to the success of digital health interventions. We demonstrate how LLMs can extract and synthesize key findings from academic literature in order to provide actionable insights for healthcare professionals, researchers, and policymakers. Our analysis reveals 17 major factors that are crucial for successful implementation of digital health interventions. These factors include organizational factors such as leadership support, stakeholder involvement, and data privacy; financial factors such as funding models and cost-effectiveness; technological factors such as system usability and scalability; and patient engagement factors such as perceived usefuln

Instead of making behavioral decisions directly from the exponentially expanding joint observational-action space, we propose to formulate a Markov Decision Process (MDP) for robotic manipulation with two types of variables: discrete and continuous. The discrete variables represent the state of the environment and the robot's actions, while the continuous variables represent the joint positions and velocities of the robot. We then learn an approximate MDP model by fitting a neural network to observed trajectories using inverse reinforcement learning (IRL). This MDP model is trained to capture the underlying reward structure in the observed data without requiring any knowledge about the reward function. Finally, we introduce a novel planning algorithm that uses the learned MDP model to gene

The field of robotics faces inherent challenges in manipulating deformable objects, particularly in the presence of external forces. This paper addresses this challenge by presenting a novel solution that harnesses both the robot's force and vision sensors to achieve reliable deformable object handling. Specifically, we introduce a novel methodology for accurately estimating contact forces during grasping operations. Our approach utilizes a low-cost RGB camera to capture images of the object, which are then processed using deep learning algorithms to estimate the contact points between the object and the gripper. These contact points serve as input for an iterative algorithm that refines the force estimation based on robot sensor readings. The result is a highly accurate estimation of the

Motion graphic (MG) videos are effective and compelling for presenting complex concepts through dynamic visualization. Despite their potential, the existing MG video creation process remains tedious and time-consuming due to the need for manual editing of multiple layers, including objects, texts, and effects. This paper presents a novel approach to automate the creation of MG videos based on natural language input. Our method involves training a generative language model to generate code instructions for constructing an MG video's timeline structure and then using a text-to-code system to convert these instructions into Python code. The generated code is then executed to create the final video output. We present two experimental models for our proposed task: CodeGen (an autoencoding model

Multiple Object Tracking (MOT) is a critical area within computer vision, with a broad spectrum of applications across domains. The development of MOT models has been hampered by the scarcity and heterogeneity of real-world datasets, which have led to a lack of generalizability in existing models. To address this challenge, we introduce the VAST dataset for Video Action Semantic tracking (VAST), a novel large-scale and challenging benchmark designed specifically for MOT. This dataset comprises 3750 annotated videos totaling 146 hours, containing diverse scenes, objects, and actions from both indoor and outdoor settings. We also present an extensive evaluation of several state-of-the-art models trained on this benchmark, including LDPC, CK-track, Fusenet, DTAMO, CADC, HACS, SAMOT, and BLSTM

In the rapidly advancing realm of visual generation, diffusion models have revolutionized the landscape, marking a significant milestone in the journey toward more advanced and sophisticated AI systems. With their intrinsic capacity to generate realistic images from text prompts and latent representations, these models hold immense potential for various applications ranging from image synthesis to medical imaging. However, the computational cost associated with training these models presents challenges that hinder their widespread deployment, particularly in resource-constrained environments. To address this concern, this paper introduces an innovative approach to train diffusion models on a single GPU with limited memory capacity, enabling the efficient utilization of GPU resources. Our m

In production rendering systems, caustics are typically rendered via photon mapping and gathering, a process that involves sampling the light transport equation. However, this process is expensive, especially in high-dimensional scenes with many caustic sources. To address this issue, we propose a novel deep learning approach for fast caustic rendering of complex scenes using an encoder-decoder architecture trained on synthetic data. Our method exploits the fact that caustics often follow simple, repeating patterns, which allows us to use a recurrent convolutional decoder to generate the final image directly from the encoded feature map. Our approach significantly improves over previous work by leveraging hierarchical encoding and decoding. It produces caustic images with significantly few

Measuring the impact of a publication in a fair way is a significant challenge in bibliometrics, as it must not introduce biases between fields and should enable comparison of the impact of publications from different years. In this paper, we propose a Bayesian approach to tackle this problem, motivated by empirical data demonstrating heterogeneity in citation distributions. The approach uses the a priori distribution of citations in each field to estimate the expected a posteriori distribution in that field. This distribution is then employed to normalize the citations received by a publication in that field. Our main contribution is the Bayesian Impact Score, a measure of the impact of a publication. This score is increasing and concave with the number of citations received and decreasin

Few-Shot Class-Incremental Learning (FSCIL) has gained considerable attention due to the growing demand for adaptive models that can quickly adjust to emerging data. As a subfield of incremental learning, FSCIL focuses on addressing the limitations of conventional classifiers in scenarios where training data is insufficient or rapidly changing. Existing solutions typically rely on memorization of previous training examples or retraining from scratch, which can be resource intensive and time consuming. Recently, some methods have been proposed that employ few-shot fine-tuning with extra data to boost their performance. However, these approaches only consider the additional data as a supplement to the original fine-tuned model, which may lead to overfitting and deterioration in generalizatio

Court View Generation (CVG) is a challenging task in the field of Legal Artificial Intelligence, especially for Chinese civil law. Recent studies on CVG have focused on improving the accuracy and consistency of predictions by leveraging large-scale data, while neglecting the quality and readability of generated texts. This paper aims to address these issues by proposing a novel framework, named SMART-CVG, which combines multiple strategies to enhance the overall performance of CVG systems. The proposed framework includes three main components: (i) a strategy to improve prediction accuracy through an ensemble model; (ii) a method to mitigate potential bias in the predicted outcomes; and (iii) an approach to enhance readability via machine reading comprehension techniques. This paper proposes Group Activity Feature (GAF) learning in which features of multi-person groups are learned from group activity data. Specifically, the proposed GGA framework consists three modules: 1.) A feature extractor to extract salient body parts and their appearance information; 2.). An attention module for extracting useful local context around each part using the previously extracted saliency map as a weight mask matrix on all input channels . The contextual representations then combine with global representation by concatenation operation ;3). Finally an SVM classifier is trained based upon these combined contextsual/global inputs generated at stage two via cross validation method over training datasets before being applied onto unseen test set(s), where we observed accuracy

State-of-the-art natural language processing models are vulnerable to simple grammatical errors, often producing wrong results or crashing. We propose a new multilingual code generation dataset and model to mitigate this issue. Deviating from previous work, we do not ask models to directly generate corrected code. Instead, we introduce an intermediate representation (IR) - a structured object that separates the meaning of the code (what the code does) from its syntax (how the code is written). We show that by generating IRs, models learn to identify and fix grammatical errors, without any explicit correction training. Our IR-based approach improves robustness by 7.5x over strong baselines on CodeXGLUE, a multilingual code completion dataset, and by 15.8x over a baseline of generating corrected code directly from erroneous code. Our code is publicly available at https://github.com/reddy-lab-code-research/IRCoder/.

Simulation is pivotal in evaluating the performance of autonomous driving systems due to the advantages it offers over real-world testing. However, most existing simulators are either generic and require extensive engineering effort from domain experts or specialized for a particular vehicle model with limited scalability across various scenarios / sensors/ vehicles etc., making them unsuitable for rapid evaluation purposes by researchers who need quick access without much setup time constraints (e .g ., those working on advanced algorithms).

Human mobility forecasting is an essential problem for many applications, and increasingly attracts the attention of the research community. In this paper, we focus on the fundamental problem of forecasting a person's location at a specific time, given their historical location data. Our key insight is that the problem of human mobility forecasting can be viewed as a natural language processing (NLP) problem, where a person's location can be represented as a sequence of natural language prompts. Based on this insight, we propose a novel prompt mining algorithm that can automatically extract meaningful prompts from a sequence of locations. We then use the obtained prompts to construct a language model for forecasting a person's future locations. Experimental results on real-world location datasets show that our prompt mining algorithm can extract useful information from raw location data, and our language model can effectively utilize the information to make accurate mobility predictions. In particular, the prompt mining algorithm improves the state-of-the-art location prediction algorithm by 1.7% in terms of prediction accuracy.

As the Internet of Things evolves into a more interconnected and autonomous network of devices, the role of Artificial Intelligence in embedded systems design is expected to become increasingly prominent. However, the deployment of AI in such devices is not without challenges; limited resources in embedded systems coupled with the black-box nature of many AI models can make it difficult to identify the root cause of a given prediction. This can in turn make it challenging to meet the reliability and explainability requirements placed on AI-powered systems. To address these challenges, this paper proposes a multi-pronged approach that leverages explainable AI and fault injection to enhance the reliability and explainability of redundant Non-Volatile Memory (NVM) write prediction in embedded systems. A case study of a static redundant NVM write prediction is used to demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed approach. Results show that the proposed approach can effectively improve the reliability and explainability of AI-powered embedded systems.

In this paper, we present our solution for the Machine Learning for Physical Simulations Challenge, where participants were asked to design an airfoil with a zero lift drag coefficient using physics-based simulations and machine learning. Our team won the challenge, and this paper describes the methods we used. We designed the airfoil by optimizing the geometry using gradient descent, where the objective function is the lift drag coefficient, and the gradients are computed using physics-based simulations. For the physics-based simulations, we used computational fluid dynamics (CFD) simulations for drag force calculation and a neural network for lift force calculation. For the neural network, we used a physics-informed loss function to enforce the satisfaction of the Navier-Stokes equation. To train the neural network, we used a small subset of the CFD data, and for the gradient computation, we used the adjoint method. In addition, we regularized the optimization by using information from previous iterations, which acts as a substitute for the missing second derivative information. The final design has a lift drag coefficient of 0.0048, which is the lowest among all the teams.

With the requirements of Intelligent Transport Systems (ITSs) for extensive connectivity of Electronically Tag Reading System, Electronic Toll Collection systems and other applications including Automatic Vehicle Identification/Tracking system in mind as well as to provide a better solution with regards ease-of installation, security against tampering or unauthorized access etc., we have developed an electronic toll collection based on RFID technology.

Part 1 (this page you're reading now; covers simple statistical power analysis based on confidence intervals.) • Next article → : part II - sample sizing via hypothesis tests ($z$ score); also explains why $\alpha = .05$, commonly used by practitioners today is not optimal choice of threshold). A key challenge when analyzing data from online experiments or other field studies that involve randomization such an AB Test ("split testing") concerns estimating treatment effects given imperfect compliance rates due noncompliance issues like "ad blocking" which can

Realism in image synthesis is often achieved by matching a distribution of synthetic images to a distribution of real-world images. However, it can be difficult to precisely specify the real-world distribution, and harder still to ensure that a synthetic distribution matches it. To help address this problem, we propose a novel framework for learning and evaluating the realism of an image synthesis model, based on the Earth Mover's Distance (EMD) between its output and a set of real-world images. We show that EMD is a better correlate of human perception than either FID or SSIM, and that our EMD-based metric is more effective than these established metrics at distinguishing real-world images from various synthetic distributions. Finally, we use EMD to help us better understand the strengths and weaknesses of several recent image synthesis models.

Many important query processing methods proactively use semijoins or semijoin-like filters to reduce the cost of querying large, noisy datasets. These techniques are often based on an assumption that we know how many “good” rows (i.e., true positives) there should be in a result set and filter out only those with less than this amount before running further expensive postprocessing steps such as computing join results for all candidates found by applying semi joins alone . The problem is complicated because it involves two types: 1 ) when using traditional database systems where one has access not just raw data but also metadata describing structure; here finding good estimates becomes harder due lack constraints like cardinality bounds imposed upon column values within tables themselves ;

Analyzing people's opinions and sentiments about certain topics is of great importance in many real-life applications. Although there has been much research on sentiment analysis for major languages, such as English and Chinese, research on Arabic sentiment analysis is still limited. In this paper, we extend and improve a previously introduced Arabic sentiment analysis system (ASAS) through the addition of a topic extraction component. In particular, we employ a binarization approach for topic extraction, which allows us to leverage existing sentiment lexicons. Our experiments show that the new system (ASAS2) performs better than the original ASAS system. We also perform a user study to evaluate the sentiment analysis performance of ASAS2. The results of the user study show that ASAS2 is preferred over the original ASAS system. We make our code publicly available.

When users access shared resources in a selfish manner, the resulting societal cost and perceived users' experience are compromised. We investigate how to incentivize user cooperation for efficient resource sharing using digital currency as an incentive mechanism by modeling it under two aspects: 1) The economic aspect of fairness between individual peers (i-fairness); i-) whether participants can recover their expected monetary benefits; ii-), if yes with what probability they do so? This is formulated via Nash equilibrium analysis where we propose different allocation schemes based on game theory principles such that any participant who joins later will not be able gain more than those existing players already have gained. These strategies also ensure stability among all parties involved

Agriculture is one of the most important sectors in the Indian economy, and India is the largest producer of several agricultural products in the world. However, crop yield is affected by many factors, and these factors are not easy to quantify. Therefore, accurate prediction of crop growth is challenging, but necessary for decision-making. Data assimilation techniques are well developed for weather forecasting and are starting to be applied to crop growth modelling. The goal of this paper is to develop and evaluate a data assimilation algorithm for crop growth models. Specifically, we develop a data assimilation algorithm based on the ensemble Kalman filter (EnKF) to update the states of a crop growth model, using observations of crop phenology. The crop growth model used in this study is a modified version of the APSIM-Wheat model, which includes phenological stages and yield. The model parameters are estimated using the Levenberg-Marquardt algorithm. The data assimilation algorithm developed is a modification of the traditional EnKF algorithm, which includes a non-linear land surface model (LSM) and a long short-term memory (LSTM) neural network. The modified EnKF algorithm is tested on both synthetic and real-world datasets. The results show that the algorithm can accurately estimate the states of the crop growth model, even when the model parameters are not well known. The algorithm also has the advantage of being able to include expert knowledge, which is important when the model parameters are not well constrained by the observations.

We present a two-stage text-to-3D generation system, namely 3DTopoNet, that generates high-quality 3D human models from text. Our method is based on the observation that different types of descriptions correspond to different levels of detail in 3D models. Inspired by this observation, we propose a two-stage pipeline. Firstly, we generate a topological structure which represents a rough shape of a 3D model from a given description via a natural language processing approach. Secondly, we use an image synthesis framework to render the topology into a fine 3D model. In the first stage, we utilize the encoder-decoder architecture to extract information from descriptions and generate topological structures. In the second stage, we leverage diffusion models for image synthesis to obtain high-qua

Inspired by the adorable Pikachu, the bony fish that can generate and withstand strong electric fields, we designed a soft underwater gripper for capturing electrically active prey. Unlike traditional rigid devices, soft robotics offers the compliance needed to safely interact with unknown, electrically active environments. However, soft robots are limited in their ability to generate the high forces needed to grasp electrically active prey. To overcome this challenge, we propose an aerial-deployed soft underwater gripper that combines a soft body with a rigid internal skeleton and external frame. This unique structure allows the gripper to be lightweight and compliant during deployment, yet strong and rigid when capturing prey. To test our prototype, we conducted drop tests from a drone into an electrically active tank filled with underwater structures of varying stiffness and conductivity. Our results show that the aerial-deployed soft gripper can successfully capture rigid objects, as well as deformable, electrically active prey without damaging them. This study demonstrates the potential of soft robots for safe, compliant interactions with unknown, electrically active environments.

Evolutionary algorithms, such as Differential Evolution, excel in solving real-parameter optimization problems with a large number of variables. However their performance deteriorates when the problem exhibits local optima or an irregular landscape: most methods get stuck close to one optimum and have difficulties finding others nearby (multimodal search), while they are also easily misled by spurious peaks that occur away from good solutions but do not actually correspond well on any reasonable metric for comparing candidate points' quality ("spurious peak avoidance"). These shortcomings motivate us here to study how information about neighboring regions can be used to improve both multimodal exploration and avoid being fooled by deceptive landscapes; we propose two new schemes which use

Federated learning (FL) is a collaborative scheme to fine-tune a global model without sharing local data. FL is data efficient, communication efficient, and privacy-preserving. Despite its many advantages, FL is model- and data-dependent. In particular, FL assumes that the local data is of high quality, and FL's performance is hurt if the local data is noisy. Unfortunately, real-world data is often noisy, and this noise hurts the performance of the global model. In this work, we introduce a simple yet effective method to enhance data quality in FL, called FL Data Cleaning (FLDC). FLDC identifies and removes noisy data from the local clients before the local updates are aggregated into the global model. We evaluate FLDC on a text classification task using the Stackoverflow dataset and a question-answering task using the TREC dataset. FLDC improves the data quality of both synthetic and real-world datasets. In particular, FLDC with the GPT-3 model improves the performance of the global model by 4.4% compared to the baseline.

The development and adoption of Vision Transformers and other deep-learning architectures for image recognition have revolutionized the field, achieving state-of-the-art performance. Large language models such as GPT3 are similarly disruptive for text generation. In this work, we present a novel framework that combines both vision and language models to perform multimodal task completion with no or limited human supervision. Our approach leverages self-supervised pretraining on large multi-modal datasets, fine-tuning to downstream tasks, and prompting to enhance efficiency, generalization, and robustness. We evaluate our approach across several tasks spanning image classification, object detection, segmentation, and question answering, demonstrating its effectiveness in diverse domains. Mo

This paper presents a novel multi-modal Multi-Object Tracking (MOT) algorithm for self-driving cars, which combines the strengths of Lidar and camera data to achieve robustness in challenging environments. The key challenge is that the two modalities are inherently different, with Lidars providing high accuracy but limited range and cameras offering wide coverage at the expense of lower precision. To address this problem, we introduce an innovative method for combining these two modalities into one unified representation. We then present a multi-scale tracking framework that leverages this representation to achieve robust performance across diverse scenarios. Additionally, our proposed method outperforms the current state-of-the-art approaches in terms of both accuracy and generalization c

National Security Letters (NSLs) are similar to administrative subpoenas and can be issued by the FBI without court approval. The letters demand information from third parties, such as internet providers, about the identity of a targeted person or group. In addition to obtaining metadata, NSLs enable government agencies to seize electronic communication records that include emails, phone calls, text messages, and online chat logs. The FBI issues these letters under Section 215 of the Patriot Act, which is part of the Foreign Intelligence Surveillance Act (FISA). NSLs have been used extensively since their creation in 1986 but only became publicly known in 2003 when former FBI agent Coleen Rowley revealed their existence. Since then, there has been ongoing debate over their effectiveness an

Object detection (OD) is one of the most researched and widely used computer vision tasks. Real-time performance, accuracy, and model size are the three most important metrics for OD. In this paper, we propose a series of lightweight OD models based on YOLOv7 integrated with ShuffleNetv2 and Vision Transformer (ViT). We first integrate ShuffleNetv2 with YOLOv7 as the backbone, neck, and head of the model, which are respectively responsible for feature extraction, feature aggregation, and feature prediction. We then replace the feature extraction module with ViT, which outperforms ShuffleNetv2 in terms of accuracy and model size. To evaluate the performance of our models, we conduct extensive experiments on the COCO, VOC 2007, and ADE 20K datasets. As expected, our models significantly outperform YOLOv7 in terms of accuracy and model size. In particular, our model ShuffleYOLOv7-ViT-14 achieves 88.7% accuracy on the COCO dataset with a 58.7M parameter size, which outperforms YOLOv7 (87.3%, 61.9M). ShuffleYOLOv7-ViT-14 also outperforms other existing ViT-based OD models, including DeiT-B-OD (87.5%, 77.2M), PLEX (87.9%, 108.9M), and YOLOv7-ViT (88.3%, 86.7M).

Field Programmable Gate Arrays (FPGAs) are being increasingly used in cloud settings, bringing the benefits of reconfigurable hardware to a wider audience. However, this also opens up the possibility of malicious attacks on the reconfigurable resources of cloud FPGAs. To deter such attacks, it is essential to continuously monitor the configurations being loaded onto cloud FPGAs and detect any unauthorized changes. Previous works have proposed various techniques for monitoring configuration traffic and detecting potential attacks. However, these approaches suffer from either a very high false positive rate, a very high false negative rate, or compatibility issues with the FPGA configuration protocols. To address these challenges, we propose MaliGNNoma, a novel GNN-based classifier that utilizes a traffic profiling approach to learn the characteristics of malicious circuit configurations. To train our model, we generate profiling data from legitimate and malicious circuit configurations using a grey-box FPGA simulator. Our experiments show that MaliGNNoma has a much lower false positive and false negative rate compared to previous works (by up to 10X). Furthermore, we show that MaliGNNoma can achieve high detection accuracy for both VPR and Stratix-10 FPGA devices. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first work to propose a GNN-based approach for FPGA circuit classification.

Multivariate time series anomaly detection is a crucial problem in many industrial and research applications. Most existing methods, however, are limited to detecting anomalies caused by abrupt changes or shifts in data distributions. In real-world scenarios, such as manufacturing processes or medical monitoring systems, anomalous events can also be characterized by continuous drifts or evolutions over time. Therefore, it is important to develop an effective approach for detecting such evolving anomalies from multivariate time series. To address this challenge, we propose the novel method of Evolving Anomaly Detection (EAD), which employs an autoencoder-based generative model to capture the intrinsic dynamics of the underlying system. Specifically, our method utilizes a multi-step forward

Estimating influence or identifying influential nodes in a network is a fundamental problem with a wide range of applications including e-commerce, social media, and epidemiology. Influence estimation is typically carried out in a greedy manner by maximizing a submodular score. While submodularity guarantees a constant factor approximation to the optimal solution, the greedy algorithm has a linear complexity with the number of nodes. Hence for a large-scale network, greedy methods can be computationally expensive. To address this challenge, we propose a diffusion framework that is scalable, flexible, and provably accurate for influence estimation. The framework is based on a continuous-time diffusion process on the node-edge graph of the network. We show that the diffusion framework is flexible to different types of noise and can be scaled up using parallel computation. Moreover, we show that the diffusion process converges to the optimal solution at a fast rate, which is independent of the network size. We conduct experiments on four real-world networks to evaluate the performance of our method for influence estimation. The results demonstrate a consistent good performance of our method in comparison with state-of-the-art baselines.

Adversarial Training (AT) effectively improves the robustness of Deep Neural Networks (DNNs). However, it is expensive to generate effective adversarial samples in real-world applications. Recently proposed Fast Adversary Attackers such as Projected Gradient Descent and Signum have been shown more efficient than AT for generating robust DNN models with limited computation resources but their effectiveness has not yet been explored extensively on large scale datasets or long duration tasks like autonomous driving where high quality data generation can be extremely difficult especially under time constraints during training sessions due its inherent dependency upon input conditions that change over short periods without prior warning making them unreliable sources when dealing complex scenar

Low precision training can significantly reduce the computational overhead of training deep neural networks (DNNs). Though low precision inference offers a promising means to accelerate DNN inference, the impact of low-precision inference on model performance remains underexplored. Recent studies show that low-precision training can improve the robustness and generalizability of models through weight sparsity or quantization. However, these studies are limited in scope, focusing primarily on CIFAR10, ImageNet, or other benchmark datasets. In this paper, we investigate the effectiveness of low-precision inference on model performance across various downstream vision tasks. Our extensive experiments across six popular image classification benchmarks reveal a mixed picture: although low-preci

Viewport prediction is the crucial task for adaptive 360-degree video streaming, which predicts the viewpoints of users to reduce the computational burden and network bandwidth. To address this problem, we propose a novel self-supervised method, named as S2ViP. The method consists of three key components: (1) a novel feature extraction module based on self-attention with residual connection (SARC), (2) a dual-head attention mechanism, and (3) an uncertainty-aware loss function. (1) Our proposed SARC module integrates local and global information in videos by utilizing both spatial and channel attention to extract more discriminative features. (2) We introduce a dual-head attention mechanism that combines two multi-head attention mechanisms for better performance. In addition, we design the

Routing Protocol for Low Power and Lossy Networks (RPL) is the de facto standard protocol in Internet of Things (IoT) networks, which enables these networks to build a routing tree. However, there are many issues related to RPL, such as the lack of support for dynamic topology changes, and the vulnerability of the routing tree to attacks. In this paper, we propose an innovative extension to the RPL routing mechanism called Dynamic RPL (D-RPL), which supports dynamic network topology changes and increases security by incorporating a new node selection criterion based on the concept of redundancy. We also present a novel approach to select routes that increase the robustness of the network against attacks. The main idea behind our proposed scheme is to exploit the information collected from

Abrupt maneuvers by surrounding vehicles (SVs) can typically lead to safety concerns and affect the stability of a vehicle. To address these issues, this study proposes an adaptive longitudinal control system for autonomous heavy-duty trucks in mixed traffic conditions with aggressive SVs; more specifically: i). when following closely behind slower moving or stationary objects ii.) while overtaking slow/stationary object iii.). The proposed controller is based on model predictive trajectory planning that optimizes future acceleration values using linear quadratic regulator optimization subject to constraints related to lateral offset from reference lane boundary during followings mode as well rear collision avoidance constraint at overtake scenario iv. ), followed v.))by feedback correctio

Inside-mouth bite transfer, a crucial skill for oral surgery, is challenging to learn and perform. This paper presents a robot-assisted training system for inside-mouth bite transfer, which uses a head-mounted display to provide visual guidance and haptic feedback. The system tracks the user's mouth movements to align the virtual model with the user's own anatomy. It also models the physical interactions between the robot and the user to adjust the guidance strategy based on the force feedback from the robot. In this way, the system helps the user to feel and learn the target bite motion by observing the visual guidance and the physical interaction force simultaneously. We evaluated the effectiveness of the proposed system by having the participants practice bite transfer on a virtual apple. The results show that the participants were able to learn the target bite motion more quickly using our system than without any guidance.

Advanced language-processing and machine-learning techniques promise massive efficiency improvements in the previously widely manual field of research paper curation. However, these methods are rarely available as standalone tools for use by researchers who lack training in software development. Our contribution is a novel tool that can automate the process of curating a research paper, i.e., extracting important information from it using deep learning models. The tool, called Auto-Curation, is implemented as an online Python notebook, which makes it accessible to users without programming skills. It uses pretrained models to perform tasks such as sentence extraction, named entity recognition, citation detection, and abstract generation. This allows users to automatically generate summarie

Micro-Electro-Mechanical Systems (MEMS) gyroscopes are cheap and widely used sensors for measuring angular velocity. However, they suffer from drift over time which increases with their operating temperature. To compensate for this drift, gyroscope calibration is necessary. Traditional calibration methods are based on accelerating the gyroscope to a known angular velocity which is achieved using expensive motion control systems. In this paper, we propose a deep learning based calibration method that does not require any motion control system. We exploit the fact that the drift of MEMS gyroscopes is frequency dependent and model it using a first order polynomial. The proposed network, TinyGC-Net, is an extremely small convolutional neural network that takes as input the measurements of the gyroscope at different frequencies and outputs the calibration coefficients. We train our network using a dataset of 512 MEMS gyroscopes from two different manufacturers. To evaluate our method, we test it using a left-out dataset of 128 gyroscopes from the same two manufacturers. We also compare the performance of our method with the state-of-the-art. From the results, we observe that TinyGC-Net outperforms all the state-of-the-art methods. It achieves a mean squared error (MSE) of 0.0078 at a sampling rate of 500 Hz which is an improvement of 7.9% over the best performing state-of-the-art method. Furthermore, TinyGC-Net is an extremely small network that consists of only 477 parameters which is approximately 10 times smaller than the next smallest network. Due to its small size, TinyGC-Net can be implemented on resource-constrained devices and can potentially be used in embedded systems.

Training a robotic policy from scratch using deep reinforcement learning methods can be prohibitively expensive due to the need for large amounts of data. To address this, previous work has proposed using imitation learning (IL) to generate high-quality demonstrations and then train policies on them. However, these approaches often fail in scenarios where the IL policies struggle with challenging tasks or environments, leading to poor performance. In this paper, we propose an approach that leverages the strengths of both imitation learning and reinforcement learning by incorporating a "learned IL" framework into the training process. This allows the agent to learn more efficient IL policies that are able to handle difficult tasks in new environments. We demonstrate the effectiveness of our

This paper considers a movable antenna (MA)-aided secure multiple-input multiple-output (MIMO) system with $K$ single-antenna eavesdroppers, where the legitimate user is equipped with one transmit antenna and the base station (BS) is equipped with $N_T$ receive antennas. In order to guarantee confidential information transmission, we propose an optimal power allocation (OPA) scheme, where the transmit beamforming matrix at the BS is derived by minimizing the total transmit power of the BS subject to the reliable secrecy rate constraints of all legitimate users under the joint effect of channel estimation error and noise. Furthermore, we derive the asymptotic secure diversity order as the number of transmit antennas tends to infinity.

We present RISeg, a deep learning solution to the robot interactive object segmentation (RIOS) problem. RIOS is a fundamental problem in robot manipulation that aims to identify the object a robot is interacting with, given a single RGB-D image. Solving RIOS is challenging as robots often lack contextual information about the environment and object appearances can be highly varied. Previous methods have focused on using CNNs to extract features from RGB-D images, with little consideration for the robot's motion. However, robots often follow similar motions when interacting with objects, and these motions can provide valuable cues for segmentation. In this work, we leverage skeleton-based features (SBF) to exploit the repetitive nature of robot motions and improve the segmentation performance. SBF are frame-invariant, i.e., they remain constant regardless of the camera pose, which is ideal for describing periodic motions. We show that SBF can effectively improve the segmentation performance for both synthetic and real datasets. RISeg outperforms existing methods on the RIOSSyn dataset and is able to generalize object categories seen in the wild. Our code and datasets are available at https://github.com/luost26/RISeg.

We describe the first algorithms that satisfy the standard notion of node-differential privacy in the context of Diffusion models for graph generation. In particular, we prove that our approach satisfies a variant of the notion of differential privacy known as node-differential privacy. This notion was introduced by McMahan et al. (2019) and has been used to design differentially private stochastic gradient descent methods for training deep neural networks. Our results are based on ideas from the theory of differential privacy, and they show that the use of diffusion models can lead to significant improvements in terms of performance over traditional approaches such as Markov Chain Monte Carlo methods. More generally, these results highlight the power of probabilistic modeling techniques f

Data annotation interfaces predominantly leverage ground truth labels to guide annotators toward accurate responses. With advancements in large language models, it is now possible to predict the likely response given an input, yet this potential has been overlooked for data annotation. In this work, we introduce a new methodology based on probabilistic machine learning and demonstrate its effectiveness with two evaluation scenarios: (i) when the correct label is missing or unavailable, and (ii) when multiple plausible labels are possible. We apply our method on three datasets that involve various types of annotation tasks. Our results show that prediction-based annotations outperform ground-truth annotations by up to 23% in terms of accuracy, while also increasing annotator efficiency by u

While machine learning (ML) models have shown great promise for predicting the products of organic reactions, a common limitation of current methodologies is that they often fail to reproduce the reactant-to-product transformation mechanism. Instead, they may predict alternative, less favorable mechanisms with minor product distribution changes. To address this limitation, we propose a novel ML framework that can accurately reproduce the correct reaction mechanism, using a large-scale dataset of mechanistic experiments. We demonstrate the utility of our framework for both experimental and computational reactions, showing that models can learn the difference between mechanisms with and without a given reaction step. Our results also suggest that ML models may be used to efficiently screen large libraries of reactions, mechanisms, and catalysts.

Semi-supervised multi-organ segmentation (MSeg) aims to perform organ segmentation on medical images using a minimal number of manual annotations. This task is challenging because (1) organs have varying shapes and appearances across patients, (2) there is a lack of discriminative features due to similar intensities among neighboring pixels, and (3) the presence of complex anatomical structures results in ambiguous labels. In this paper, we propose a novel Dual Contrastive Learning Network (Dcl-Net) to address these challenges. Specifically, we introduce a dual-branch framework that simultaneously performs instance discrimination and cluster refinement. Instance discrimination is achieved through a proxy-based contrastive learning approach, where each cluster generated by a spectral normalization classifier is represented by a proxy point. These proxy points are then used to guide the clustering of unlabeled data, effectively resolving the ambiguity arising from similar intensities among neighboring pixels. Furthermore, we employ a novel inter-organ distance map to facilitate the clustering of organ regions with varying shapes and appearances. We validate our method on the Pancreas, BraTS18, and MS-COCO datasets, and demonstrate its superiority over existing methods in terms of both effectiveness and efficiency. Our approach achieves state-of-the-art performance on the Pancreas and BraTS18 datasets, with significant improvements over existing methods. Additionally, the Dcl-Net exhibits robust generalizability across different platforms and demonstrates a 10.2% higher mean IoU and a 3.1% faster inference speed on the BraTS18 dataset compared to the previous best model.
We introduce quadratic algebraic structures living on graphs, in close analogy with how quadratic forms live on vector spaces. We study their properties and provide examples.

Neural machine translation (MT) models achieve strong results across a variety of settings, but it is not clear to what extent the improvements are attributable to their training data or their models. Recent work has proposed two approaches: using a different MT model on the same dataset and fine-tuning an existing model with new data. However, these approaches do not offer guarantees that the improvements are due to better data, and they fail for complex tasks like MT for low-resource languages. In this work, we propose a rigorous approach based on the classical notion of "goodness" in statistical learning theory. Our approach provides strong guarantees by leveraging the theory's standard assumptions about MT datasets and MT models. We demonstrate its effectiveness empirically on several

Textured human mesh reconstruction aims to reconstruct a semantically meaningful mesh representation with texture information from a single image. This is in contrast to semantic image segmentation that aims to segment semantically meaningful regions in an image. Although these two tasks are complementary, most methods focus on one task, and there are few methods that consider both tasks simultaneously. In this paper, we propose a novel method that reconstructs a semantic mesh representation with textures by integrating semantic image segmentation and 3D human mesh reconstruction. Our method follows the pipeline of existing works with some key differences. First, we generate an intermediate representation in the form of a mesh with semantic information by using a mask generated from a semantic image segmentation model. Second, we propose a novel loss function to enforce consistency between the generated mesh and the original input image in terms of both geometry and semantics. We conduct experiments on Textual Human Shape (THS) and Pixel to Pixel Human Shape (P2PHS) datasets. Our method outperforms existing methods in terms of both geometry and semantics.

Cooperative multi-agent reinforcement learning (MARL) is a powerful tool for training agents to perform complex joint tasks. However, it can be inefficient when the agents have diverse preferences or operate in partially observable environments. In this paper, we propose a new cooperative MARL framework with goal imagination, which enables the agents to quickly reach an agreement on the optimal course of action by predicting the goals of their partners. The key idea is to use a policy representation that consists of two components: a partner policy, which specifies the action to take given the current observation, and a goal policy, which specifies the desired next observation given the current observation. This way, each agent can infer the goal of its partners and compute the best response accordingly.
