In particular, we propose a consensus objective that encourages the partner policy to follow the majority decision while ensuring that the goal policy remains optimal. We show that our method can significantly reduce the training time in various cooperative tasks. In particular, without any additional reward function, our method can solve the tasks faster than self-play.

We introduce a two-step method for the matrix recovery problem. Our approach combines the theoretical founda­tions of convex optimization, information theory and random coding to achieve optimal performance under various constraints on storage complexity (the number $n$ or size $\ell$) as well entries in each column ($m$). This includes solving arbitrary rank-$k$'s matrix completion problems with near linear time/space requirements $(O(nm^2 \log n))$ which is an improvement over prior results by several orders magnitude when compared against standard methods such like SVD ($\Omega(\min\{mn,\sqrt{km}d\}))$.

This study investigates the generation of synthetic disinformation by OpenAI's Large Language Models (LLMs) on Twitter. Our findings indicate that LLMs can generate content with a high degree of authenticity, including retweets and replies, potentially undermining social media platforms' efforts to combat misinformation. We conduct an extensive evaluation of ChatGPT, GPT-4, and GPT-3.5's ability to craft plausible disinformation campaigns, assessing their capacity for factual knowledge acquisition and content diversity in comparison to human-written tweets. While GPT-4 exhibits exceptional performance in generating diverse tweets with varying levels of persuasiveness, it demonstrates a notable gap in factual knowledge acquisition compared to humans. Furthermore, we assess the robustness of

With the growth of online media, there is an increasing need for tools that help users to track the online information related to their research interests. Information tracking systems can help users to stay updated with the latest research developments, find relevant information sources, and identify key researchers and research institutions. Existing tracking systems, such as Google Scholar, Microsoft Academic, and ResearchGate, index and rank publications based on a global popularity score, which is typically based on the number of citations. However, using citations as a sole metric has several drawbacks, including overlooking the impact of the cited work on the citing work, not differentiating between self-citations and citations from unrelated fields, and favoring studies with more authors and longer titles. To address these drawbacks, we introduce a new tracking system, AcademicTracker, which computes a citation-based similarity score between each pair of citing and cited publications, and uses the resulting similarity network to propagate the quality of cited publications to citing publications. In contrast to existing tracking systems, AcademicTracker is designed to be more sensitive to the content of the publications and less sensitive to the popularity of the citing publications. This way, AcademicTracker can better capture the true impact of cited publications on citing publications, and mitigate the influence of noise caused by non-experts citing popular papers. We evaluate the coverage, recall, and precision of AcademicTracker, and compare it to Google Scholar, Microsoft Academic, and ResearchGate.

Abstract visual reasoning is a characteristically human ability, allowing the identification of relational patterns that are not immediately obvious in images. As artificial intelligence advances, it becomes increasingly important to equip machines with this skill to navigate complex real-world environments and improve decision-making. Despite numerous efforts, the field has made limited progress due to challenges posed by its inherent complexity. To address these obstacles, we introduce a new benchmark for abstract visual reasoning tasks: AiR (Abstract Image Reasoning). The dataset consists of 150k questions on 480k images sourced from the Internet, covering diverse domains such as arts and landscapes. Each question includes multiple visual inputs, requiring machines to reason across them

Recently, learning open-vocabulary semantic segmentation from text supervision has achieved promising results. However, these methods are sensitive to the choice of pretrained language models (PLMs). In this work, we investigate how different PLMs influence the performance of our method. Specifically, we compare six PLMs and their variants on three datasets, namely MNIST, STL10, and COCO2014. Our findings demonstrate that using larger PLMs for learning semantic segmentation with text information can significantly improve the performance. Additionally, we found that the model trained by Chain of Thought (CoT) can obtain better performance than other PLMs when fine-tuning with the text. These findings suggest that selecting an appropriate PLM is crucial in achieving effective text-based sema

Federated learning, as a promising distributed learning paradigm, enables collaborative training of a global model across multiple clients while keeping the data on each client private. The federate-andthenshare (FATS) protocol is an important baseline for evaluating novel methods in this domain and has been widely used to compare with other approaches by many works; however it suffers from severe communication overhead when dealing with large datasets due mostly because all participants need complete copies during every round even though only partial information may be required at most timesteps per iteration depending upon what kind/amount local updates are made available beforehand before sharing them publicly via their respective servers so that everyone else can learn too without havi

The rise of machine learning in recent years has brought benefits to various research fields such as wide fire detection, object recognition, and classification. These benefits are not limited to the scientific community but also extend to industrial applications, offering businesses an opportunity to gain valuable insights from their data. However, despite the significant advancements in machine learning, there is still a lack of understanding about how these algorithms work. This article aims to provide a comprehensive overview of machine learning, emphasizing its capabilities and limitations, with a particular focus on the financial industry. We will explore the potential of machine learning to transform financial markets by enabling real-time analysis, personalized recommendations, and

Graph neural network (GNN) based recommender systems have become one of the mainstream trends in recent years due to their superior performance and the ability to handle complex and dynamic data. Recently, a novel approach called GNN-based attention mechanism is introduced by combining the strengths of graph neural networks and attention mechanisms to enhance recommendation performance. However, existing methods mainly focus on the attention over nodes without considering the interaction between nodes and edges. To address this issue, we propose an effective method named GINN-Augmented to improve the performance of GNN-based recommendation models. We conduct extensive experiments using real datasets from four different application scenarios and evaluate our proposed method through several

This article evaluates the quality of data collection in individual-level desktop information tracking used in the social sciences. We focus on a specific methodology, namely the use of keyboards and mice to record keystrokes and mouse movements for the purpose of investigating computer usage patterns in the laboratory or at home. Our study includes an empirical investigation of the accuracy of this data collection method by comparing it with a systematic coding of the same behavioral sessions. The results suggest that this method is more accurate than previously thought, although there are still some limitations, particularly when high levels of multitasking are involved. We also discuss other issues related to this type of data collection and propose an alternative method which involves

Ageism is a pervasive yet often overlooked design problem. Prejudices and stereotypes about older people's abilities and needs can lead to problematic design choices that alienate older users. One underexplored approach to mitigate ageism is intergenerational design, which involves bringing together users of different ages to co-design products that meet the needs of all users. Through a collaborative escape room design activity, we brought together 12 older adults (65+) and 12 younger adults (18-35) to co-design a virtual reality (VR) experience that would be enjoyable and accessible to both generations. Our study examines how intergenerational collaboration can inform the design of inclusive VR experiences and mitigate ageist stereotypes. We found that the escape room activity was a fun and engaging way to prompt participants to think creatively about VR design and that participants of different ages were able to effectively collaborate and generate innovative ideas together. We share design insights and implications for using VR as a design approach to mitigate ageism and promote intergenerational collaboration.

As a common atmospheric effect, haze seriously affects the visual perception and pose adverse effects on human health. To mitigate the adverse effects of haze, many previous works have studied image dehazing techniques from the perspective of computer vision, focusing on improving the performance of specific tasks such as object detection and image classification. However, these methods often fail to consider the actual usage scenario and subjective visual effects, leading to sub-optimal visual dehazing effects. To address these problems, we propose a novel detection-friendly image dehazing network, named FriendNet. From the perspective of medical image processing, we establish a new guided filtering-based loss function to better preserve image details and structures. Inspired by the boundary supervision used in medical image segmentation tasks, we propose a new hybrid boundary supervision scheme to effectively improve the dehazing effect on the boundary area. To verify the effectiveness of FriendNet, we adopt the classic MS-COCO evaluation protocol, using the detection results of the original images and the dehazed images to calculate the average precision (AP) and the mean intersection of union (mIoU). Experimental results demonstrate that our method significantly improves the AP and mIoU of the detection results under hazy conditions.

Deepfake technology has given rise to a spectrum of novel and compelling applications. Unfortunately, the potential for malicious use remains an ongoing concern in recent years, especially with the prevalence of Large Language Models (LLMs). In this paper, we introduce MimicGPT, which can be trained as a malicious deepfaker using LLM-generated prompts that target specific victims, thus enabling the generation of personalized deepfakes. We provide empirical evidence demonstrating the effectiveness of our proposed methodology, showcasing how it can generate deepfaked videos containing synthetic human faces, while also highlighting its potential for creating impersonation attacks through voice cloning. Additionally, we evaluate the robustness of LLMs against adversarial prompts generated by M

Intent classification models are often used to predict the intended emotion of a utterance, given its text. However, studies show that models frequently fail at distinguishing negated intentions from implicit intentions. To investigate the reason for this model behaviour, we perform a qualitative analysis of how negations and implicatures are encoded in BERT-based intent models. Thereby, we make use of BERT's self-explanatory capabilities and present a novel method to manually analyse token-level explanations. Our findings reveal that negations and implicatures are hardly encoded in the BERT-based models we investigate, which we attribute to the models' training objectives. We introduce a simple change to the training of generative intent models that allows for a significant improvement in distinguishing negations from implicatures.

Federated learning (FL) is a collaborative scheme to train a global model across multiple participants without sharing local data. Existing industrial frameworks adopt simple averaging for aggregating local updates and thus are vulnerable to Byzantine attacks. Although some recent works consider more robust aggregation rules, they may sacrifice the accuracy under benign conditions to gain robustness. To address this challenge, we propose FLGuard, a novel robust federated learning framework that leverages ensemble and contrastive learning techniques. The key idea is to ensemble multiple contrastive models, each of which is trained to distinguish the local update of the global model. The contrastive models are trained in an adversarial manner such that the ensemble rule becomes robust against Byzantine attacks. Theoretically, we present a bound on the prediction error of our method under adversarial attacks and show that FLGuard learns a robust prediction rule even under Byzantine attacks. Empirically, we conduct extensive experiments on synthetic and real-world data sets that demonstrate the superior robustness of FLGuard over state-of-the-art FL methods.

Audio-Visual Source Localization (AVSL) is the task of identifying specific sounding objects, such as speakers or microphones, from a set of possible sources. The conventional approach to AVSL relies on manually selecting and processing audio clips captured by multiple microphones (MIMO). However, this process can be time-consuming and impractical for large data sets, particularly in real-world scenarios involving numerous targets and sensors. In this work, we present an efficient and scalable solution to the AVSL problem based on Deep Learning (DL). Our method leverages pretrained models such as Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs), Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM), and Transformers to achieve accurate source localization without the need for manual feature extraction. We conducted extensive

Current scene flow methods broadly fail to describe motion on small objects, and current scene flow evaluation protocols have only focused on large-scale scenes. Large scale scenes are in the majority of real-world scenarios. We propose a new scene flow evaluation metric called SFE for Small Scale Evaluation. It is designed specifically for small-scale object motions. The SFE consists of two parts: a scene flow dataset, and an evaluation protocol with four test cases, each covering different aspects of small-scale scene flows. To facilitate the development of SFE, we curate the world's first publicly available scene flow benchmark for small-scale scenes - SFMOS (Scene Flow MOTionS). We provide extensive results from 17 state-of-the-art scene flow algorithms to evaluate their performances o

Recent advances in text-guided image compression have shown great potential to enhance the perceptual quality of compressed images. However, these methods rely on pre-trained models and a relatively large amount of data for training. This paper presents Text2ImageNet (Text2ImgNet), a novel approach that leverages the power of language model (LM) finetuning to achieve state-of-the-art performance with only 20K samples from ImageNet. By combining our Text2ImagNet with VQGAN, we achieve a new state-of-the-art in FID performance at 4 bpp under Kodak24, achieving a FID score of 13.93, an improvement of 42% over the previous best result reported by Wang et al. The results demonstrate the effectiveness of our method in improving the perceptual quality of compressed images without relying on exter

Despite the success in specific tasks and scenarios, existing foundation agents, empowered by large models (e.g., GPT-3), still exhibit severe performance drop when deployed to various real world environments that are different from their training ones [12]. In this work we investigate how knowledge distillation can help alleviate such domain gap between model pretraining data ("source") vs deployment target/task "target". We analyze two main challenges arising during distilling a source agent into an effective task solver: (i) misalignment of actions taken across domains due to action space mismatch or limited context information; i.. Misalignments occur even if both sources have similar input representations but vastly dissimilar output distributions . To address these issues ,we

Free-proxies have been widespread since the early days of the Web, helping users to access content that may be blocked by their ISPs. In recent years, these proxies have grown in popularity with the rise of online privacy concerns and geo-restrictions. However, many free proxies are not secure, leading to user data leakage and exposing sensitive information online. This paper presents a study aimed at identifying the most frequently used free proxy servers worldwide. We conducted an analysis on over 10 million IP addresses from a free proxy server website. We found that the top 5 free proxy providers account for more than 62% of all free proxy traffic, and that the majority of them were hosted outside of the United States. Additionally, we identified a significant number of malicious proxi

Recent works have revealed the vulnerabilities of pre-trained language models to backdoor attacks, in which an attacker injects poisoned samples into the training process with the goal of manipulating the model's prediction. To mitigate this threat, a common practice is to fine-tune a lightweight detector using manually labeled poisoned samples. However, this requires the attacker to reveal the existence of the backdoor and the poisoned samples, which is not always feasible. In this work, we investigate the effectiveness of distillation in mitigating backdoor attacks in pre-trained encoders. Specifically, we propose a novel distillation-based method that does not require the attacker's collaboration and can be applied to backdoors with arbitrary activation functions. We experimentally validate our method on several backdoor attacks, using both black-box and white-box settings. Our results show that distillation can effectively mitigate backdoor attacks and improve the detection rate of detectors.

Optical character recognition (OCR) aims to recognize characters in images and is widely used in many fields. However, it is challenging to extract accurate features from complex and noisy natural scenes. In this paper, we propose a novel Location-Guided Transformer (LOCR) to improve the performance of scene text recognition. Specifically, we introduce a location encoding mechanism to the Transformer model, which can effectively mine the location information of text elements in the image. Furthermore, we propose a novel auxiliary loss to guide the model to learn the location features more effectively. Without any additional data annotation, our model achieves state-of-the-art performance on ICDAR17 and ICDAR15.

Human comprehension of a video stream is naturally broad: in a few instants, we are able to identify the main activities and the actors involved. It is however not trivial to understand the interactions between agents within such scenarios. We propose a novel approach for detecting and localizing human-human interaction (HHI) events in videos, where a pair of interacting humans can be identified by analyzing their joint actions. To this end, we introduce a new dataset with 234 HHIs annotated in 1000 RGB sequences. This dataset was created using the same annotation methodology as our existing human activity detection dataset (HAD). For testing purposes, we also introduce a new dataset consisting of 500 HR videos with human-robot interaction events (HRIs), which were previously collected by

The fusion models, which effectively combine information from different sources, are widely used in solving multimodal tasks. In this paper, we aim to explore the potential of using pretrained fusion models as a plug-and-play module for improving the performance of existing models on downstream tasks. We propose a new paradigm called Fusion Plug & Play (FPP) that enables us to adapt and improve various task-specific models with pretrained fusion models via an easy plug-and-play mechanism. First, we introduce three main techniques: 1) feature fusion, which involves fusing multiple features from diverse modalities; 2) modality adaptation, which enhances the utilization of features across modalities; and 3) prompting, which allows us to easily tailor the pretrained fusion model to specific ta

Human knowledge is composed of interconnected concepts. Understanding how these concepts are organized in the human brain can help us shed light on fundamental aspects of human cognition. Neuroimaging data provide a window into the neural representations of knowledge, but interpreting this data is challenging. Here, we leverage large language models (LLMs) to extract semantic information from neuroimaging data. Specifically, we use LLMs to compute meaning composition scores for words in the human brain, based on BOLD signal data from over 1500 participants. We find that the composition scores are reflected in the BOLD signal, above and beyond the information captured by word embedding models. The scores are also positively correlated with the degree to which a word can be used in a given semantic context, as indicated by its word embedding norm. Finally, we show that the composition scores are consistently measured across participants, brain regions, and LLMs. Our results provide a framework for interpreting semantic information in neuroimaging data, and for understanding how knowledge is organized in the human brain.

Energy-based models (EBMs) are attracting increasing attention for their flexibility and controllability in generating realistic data. However, the inference of EBMs is usually intractable, and the existing approximation methods are still inefficient. In this paper, we propose a simple yet effective inference method for EBMs, named diffusion process-based inference (DPI), which can improve the EBM's adversarial performance by directly operating on the EBM's energy function. We show that DPI can be viewed as a special case of the more general stochastic gradient descent (SGD) algorithm with a non-Euclidean metric. This viewpoint enables us to prove that DPI has a provable convergence guarantee and an optimal learning rate, which are important theoretical properties for any gradient-based algorithm. Extensive experiments on various tasks demonstrate that our DPI method is superior to the state-of-the-art techniques.

Multi-label classification aims to recognize multiple topics from documents. The label space is usually large and the documents are short, making it difficult to capture label correlations and document-label attention. Existing methods often use the co-occurrence of labels in the training set to simulate the label correlation matrix, which is independent of the current document being classified and lacks personalization. In this paper, we propose a knowledge-enhanced attention network, KeNet, to capture dynamic label correlations and document-label attention. We design the knowledge-enhanced label attention module to integrate the latest knowledge with the traditional co-occurrence-based label attention. The module first transforms the label space into a knowledge space using a pre-trained language model (e.g., BERT). We then calculate the attention between the labels and the document words in an iterative and dynamic manner, where the attention distribution is guided by both the word representation space and the knowledge space. In addition, we propose a label co-attention module to further enhance the label correlation representation. Experimental results on two benchmark datasets, 20 Newsgroups and Stackexchange, demonstrate that our approach achieves better performance than existing state-of-the-art methods.

Neural networks have become a cornerstone of machine learning. As the trend for these to get larger has continued, the need for efficient algorithms and hardware has grown. We propose a novel architecture, the Dynamic Convolution Network (DCN), which is composed of two main components: a dynamic convolutional layer that allows filtering only on active neurons, and an adaptive convolution layer that provides the capability to vary the number of filters at runtime. This combination makes it possible to perform convolutions with high memory efficiency and flexibility. A theoretical analysis shows that DCNs can be implemented efficiently in software or hardware, such as FPGAs and ASICs. The experimental results demonstrate up to 10x speedups compared to traditional CNNs without compromising ac

Log density policy gradient (LogDPG) methods have shown impressive empirical success in reinforcement learning. However, their convergence properties and the impact of function approximation remain elusive. In this paper, we establish finite-time convergence analyses of a class of LogDPG algorithms with and without function approximation. In particular, we prove that the function-free LogDPG algorithm converges to a stationary solution at a rate of $\tilde{O}(1/T)$ up to a function approximation error. In addition, we show that the function-free LogDPG algorithm with simple averaging satisfies the \emph{persistence of excitation} condition, and hence the function-free LogDPG algorithm enjoys a faster convergence rate of $\tilde{O}(1/T^2)$ up to a function approximation error. Finally, we demonstrate that the function-free LogDPG algorithm with function approximation also converges to a stationary solution at a rate of $\tilde{O}(1/T)$, up to a function approximation error, provided that the policy parameterized by the function approximation has a sufficiently large support.

In his 2018 paper, Herlihy introduced an atomic protocol for multi-party commit with a single round of communication. We show that the optimal number rounds is $n$ (where n denotes party size) and prove lower bounds on message complexity as well: if all parties are required to send at least one bit then $\Omega(N)$ bits must be exchanged; otherwise we obtain similar results using broadcast channels instead or without any assumption about their availability ($O(\log^3 N / \varepsilon$) messages). Our proof relies heavily upon techniques from distributed computing theory such like randomized consensus algorithms but also employs recent advances in graph connectivity analysis which help us derive sharper upper limits regarding how quickly certain graphs can traverse through random walks while

This study conducts a thorough examination of malware detection using machine learning techniques, focusing on the performance and effectiveness of various classifiers. The study employs 10 datasets that encompass distinct categories of malware threats, including spyware, worms, Trojan horses, and viruses. Utilizing these datasets, we evaluate the performance of machine learning algorithms in identifying potential malware. Through extensive experiments, we examine the efficacy of various classifiers, such as support vector machines (SVM), random forest (RF), k-nearest neighbors (KNN), decision trees (DT), and others. Additionally, we explore the impact of feature selection techniques on classification accuracy. This work contributes to advancing the field of malware detection by providing

Current multimodal models leveraging contrastive learning often face limitations in developing fine-grained representations that effectively encode semantics from multiple modalities. In this paper, we propose a novel framework for enhancing representation learning by incorporating a dual contrastive loss function, enabling the model to more effectively extract and integrate diverse semantic information from various modalities. By introducing a new contrastive loss function based on the InfoNCE principle, our approach is designed to enhance the learning of visual features while simultaneously improving the discriminative capability of textual features. Additionally, we introduce a novel method of augmenting the input data through a contextual masking approach. This augmentation process is

Diffusion models create data from noise by inverting the forward paths of data towards noise and can be used for image generation. The inverse path is obtained by using reverse-mode automatic differentiation (RMD). However, existing RMDs are not well-suited to training diffusion models due to their high computational cost. To overcome this challenge, we propose a new approximate RMD for diffusion models that is efficient enough to train on large datasets with modern hardware. Our approach combines backpropagation with approximate dynamic programming, resulting in an algorithm whose memory usage scales linearly with the number of layers instead of exponentially. We show that our method achieves state-of-the-art performance, demonstrating its efficacy in training diffusion models. This work

We introduce MagicClay, a novel approach for intuitive mesh editing using generative neural fields. Our key insight is to represent the deformation field as a neural network, trained to produce plausible mesh deformations. We can then use gradient descent to optimize the deformation field, deforming the mesh in a way that preserves its overall structure while responding to the artist's intent. We demonstrate that MagicClay produces high-quality deformations on complex meshes, even under large deformations. Our method is also significantly more stable and produces higher quality results than other neural mesh editing approaches. Finally, we show that MagicClay can be used to create detailed artistic styles, such as the stylization of faces.

Web tables contain a large amount of valuable knowledge and have inspired tabular language models aimed at tackling downstream tasks. However, most existing works are not designed to capture the hierarchical structure of tabular data, which is widely applicable in various domains like financial, healthcare, and education. In this paper, we propose TRAMPO (Transformer for Analyzing Multi-level Pivot and Ordinal Data), a novel transformer architecture that captures and exploits the underlying hierarchical relations among multiple levels of pivot variables and ordinal features in web tables. Our proposed model leverages an attention-based self-supervised pretraining approach for learning hierarchical representations from unlabeled tabular data and can be easily fine-tuned on downstream tasks.

Neural networks, while powerful, often lack interpretability. Physics-Informed Neural Networks (PINNs) provide a potential solution to this problem by leveraging the ability of neural nets in approximating complex functions and their inherent interpretation via physics constraints into one framework which offers both high accuracy as well performance on tasks that are difficult for other approaches like deep learning algorithms alone or traditional numerical methods such solving partial differential equations through Finite Element Method(FEM).

Time series (TS) generation is a key component in many applications, yet is often overlooked in favor of classification and regression tasks. A major challenge in TS generation is capturing long-term dependencies, which are crucial in healthcare applications, for example, where a patient's recent vital signs may be indicative of a larger trend such as worsening health. In this work, we propose a novel framework based on recent advances in score-based diffusion models to generate realistic TS data, which can be tuned to desired characteristics using interpretable controls. Unlike traditional diffusion models, our approach can produce high-fidelity results even with relatively short training sequences, thanks to a novel scoring mechanism and careful attention to signal dynamics. We validate our method on several real-world TS datasets, showing that our model outperforms other state-of-the-art baselines in terms of generation quality and training stability. We further demonstrate the utility of our model for downstream tasks such as forecasting and anomaly detection. Finally, we present an interpretable control interface that allows users to manipulate the generated TS data and see the effects in real-time, making our model a valuable tool for domain experts.

We present Ivie, a tool that generates explanations for just-generated code. Current approaches focus on either extracting explanations from existing code or using opaque neural models. This limits their applicability when the code is generated by an opaque model, e.g., a large Transformer. We instead take a model-agnostic approach and directly explain the generated code by highlighting relevant tokens. To do so, we define an anchor as a token in the generated code that is covered by a beam search decoder. We then calculate the importance of each token with respect to the anchor using Shapley values. Finally, we present the explanations in an user-friendly manner that shows the developer the intended meaning of the anchor and its surroundings. We evaluate Ivie on code generated by a Transformer-based model for various programming tasks. We also compare it with an existing code explanation tool and show that it generates more accurate explanations, especially for longer sequences.

Feature selection prepares the AI-readiness of data by eliminating redundant features. Prior to training your model, it is important to filter out irrelevant and noisy variables in a dataset that may affect performance negatively or lead toward overfitting during learning phase [1]. This article provides an overview on feature importance methods for classification problems using Python library scikit learn with examples from our recently released paper “Towards Explainability: A Novel Approach To Feature Importance Using Local Shapley Values”. The approach presented here leverages local explanations based upon shap values which are known as one effective method among others such like Gini impurity index (GINI) , mutual information gain ratio(MI), correlation coefficients etc., however requ

Language models are prone to fail in real-world applications. Uncovered corner cases, unexpected inputs, or simple model errors can lead to wrong decisions with high stakes. We propose a framework to make language models more reliable, adaptable, and attributable by leveraging retrieval. Our framework, called ReAR, introduces a novel training paradigm that combines supervised training of a small number of manually labeled examples with unsupervised training on a large corpus of naturally labeled examples. We also propose a novel adapter-based model architecture that is designed to be flexible and scalable, allowing for easy integration of new languages and retrieval strategies. Finally, we develop a method to attribute model predictions to specific retrieval results, providing insights into the decision-making process of the model and enabling the identification of relevant context. We evaluate ReAR on a custom dataset and demonstrate the superiority of our framework over strong baselines in terms of reliability, adaptability, and attributability. Our experiments further show that ReAR scales well to new languages and retrieval strategies, making it a versatile solution for real-world applications of language models.

The field of Contextual Optimization (CO) integrates machine learning and optimization to solve decision problems under uncertainty. In this paper, we consider the problem of sequential decision making in continuous-state, discrete-action environments in which a learner must make decisions while receiving noisy observations of the environment state and a reward signal. We focus on the case where the agent's goal is to minimize the cumulative regret with respect to an arbitrary unknown reference policy. To address this problem, we develop an online algorithm based on a combination of dual averaging and stochastic gradient descent. This algorithm enjoys a linear regret bound that is independent of the horizon length and the size of the action set. To validate our approach, we conduct extensi

We propose a new procedure for adaptively pooling image datasets that have multiple sources of covariate shift and class imbalance. Our procedure improves on existing techniques that pool datasets with class imbalance (e.g., domain adaptation, multi-task learning) by (1) simultaneously accounting for both covariate shift and class imbalance across multiple datasets, and (2) adaptively combining information from datasets with similar characteristics. We show that our method learns more robust features when the datasets have multiple sources of covariate shift and class imbalance, leading to improved performance on downstream tasks. We evaluate our method on several benchmark and large real-world datasets.

This paper proposes a regularized DeepIV model with model selection for causal effect estimation from observational data. Regularization is a common technique for stabilizing causal effect estimates, but it often requires careful tuning. Unregularized DeepIV model often suffers from underfitting, while the regularized counterpart may suffer from overfitting, especially in the presence of complex confounding. To address this challenge, we propose a novel regularization strategy for DeepIV that incorporates a structured penalty on the treatment effect. This penalty helps to reduce the complexity of the model and, as a result, improves the generalizability of the treatment effect estimate. To select the optimal level of regularization, we employ a model selection criterion, Generalized Information Criterion (GIC), which balances the goodness of fit and the complexity of the model. Through comprehensive numerical experiments, we demonstrate that the regularized DeepIV model outperforms the unregularized version and that the proposed method can effectively capture the causal effect even in the presence of complex confounding.

How political beliefs change in accordance with media exposure is a complicated matter. Some studies have demonstrated that people exposed to biased, partisan media are more likely to hold polarized opinions, while others suggest that people can become more tolerant of dissenting views if they are regularly exposed to opposing viewpoints. In this paper, we examine the impact of exposure to misleading content on political views using data from three experiments where participants were shown misinformation about climate change and COVID-19 vaccines. We find that those who consume misleading information tend to be less tolerant of contradictory views. Our results provide novel insights into how individuals form their political views, highlighting the importance of factual information in foste

The effectiveness of machine learning (ML) algorithms is deeply intertwined with the quality and diversity of their training datasets. This motivates the emergence of domain adaptation (DA) as an important subfield, which aims to improve ML performance by transferring knowledge from labeled source domains to unlabeled target domains. However, existing DA approaches often suffer from two limitations: (1) they are unable to leverage the available labeled data in both domains; and (2) they struggle to capture heterogeneous information in both domains, leading to suboptimal results. To address these issues, we propose to incorporate the latent features of images into the model architecture, thereby enabling them to better capture semantic relationships between domains.

Micro Aerial Vehicles (MAVs) are prone to collisions in complex environments due to their small size and limited sensors. Human operators can teleoperate MAVs to navigate safely, but it is difficult for them to have a comprehensive view of the surroundings due to line-of-sight restrictions. In this paper, we develop a novel teleoperation framework with visual-inertial Simultaneous Localization and Mapping (SLAM) and control barrier functions to help operators avoid obstacles. We propose a novel barrier-based control scheme to maintain safe distances from obstacles, where the front, back, left, and right directions are defined with respect to the operator's frame. This ensures that the operator is always aware of the robot's position relative to the obstacles. We also propose a novel visual-inertial SLAM algorithm to estimate the camera pose and generate a 360-degree visual map of the environment. The map is updated in real-time as the operator moves the vehicle, and the robot's pose is estimated using an extended Kalman filter. We prove that the proposed control scheme is globally asymptotically stable and demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed framework in avoiding static and dynamic obstacles in simulated and real-world experiments.

Estimating depth in historical paintings is important for many applications, including art authentication, provenance determination, and condition assessment. Oriental landscape paintings, created using traditional techniques and materials, are particularly challenging to process using existing methods because of their distinctive artistic styles. In this paper, we propose a novel scene depth estimation method for traditional Oriental landscape paintings. First, we manually label a dataset consisting of 2000 depth-annotated regions on 200 landscape paintings from the Ming and Qing dynasties. Then, we propose a region-based depth estimation model using the labeled dataset. Finally, we evaluate the proposed model on the test set and show that it outperforms existing methods, achieving a mean absolute error of 0.077 and a correlation coefficient of 0.77. We also demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed method in a real-world application for estimating the depth of cracks in ancient paintings.

Title generation for programming question and answer websites is a crucial but challenging task. It not only attracts more attention but also helps users find relevant questions more easily. Bi-modal models that combine text and visual information are more capable of capturing the essence of the questions and generating accurate titles. However, training such a bi-modal model requires a large amount of paired visual-textual data, which is difficult to obtain. In this paper, we propose a novel prompt learning based method to generate programming question titles, dubbed PromptGen. We design prompts with reasoning paths for the model to follow and introduce a question-image-relation graph to provide implicit pairing information. In contrast to traditional methods, our approach does not require visual-textual pairing during training. The generated titles are concise and fluent, and our model outperforms several strong baselines in terms of both automatic and human evaluation.

Monte Carlo (MC) simulations play a pivotal role in diverse scientific and engineering domains, with applications ranging from medical research to climate modeling. As these simulations become more complex, the need for enhanced performance has intensified. In this article, we present our efforts to optimize MC simulation throughput on the Intel Xeon E5-2697v3 processor. We introduce an innovative approach, known as the "Cycle-Driven" technique, which focuses on minimizing instruction latencies by maximizing the number of instructions executed per cycle. To achieve this goal, we employ two distinct strategies: (i) dynamic voltage and frequency scaling (DVFS), and (ii) fine-grained instruction pipelining.

This work proposes to augment the lifting steps of the conventional wavelet transform with additional neural network layers. The proposed approach is named as Neural Wavelet Transform (NWT). We show that NWT can be interpreted as a specific case of the recently introduced Residual Lifting Networks (RLNs) and it provides an alternative for the latter when a lower level of expressiveness is required. In addition, we demonstrate that NWT outperforms state-of-the-art CNN-based models on a number of vision tasks involving image compression, classification, segmentation, and inpainting. To our knowledge, this paper presents the first attempts to design CNNs based on the classical lifting scheme. It also brings new evidence supporting the usefulness of the lifting concept for modern learning arch

We present LiSTA (LiDAR Spatio-Temporal Analysis), a system to automatically extract and analyze spatiotemporal features from 3D point clouds. Our method extracts a set of spatio-temporal features from LiDAR data that can be used to train machine learning models for traffic analysis. The system includes a novel approach to cluster the extracted features, which we apply to segment the LiDAR into road segments with clear boundaries. Furthermore, our clustering algorithm allows us to create an atlas of the traffic dynamics in each area, by computing a series of spatiotemporal statistics, including average speed, congestion level, and others. Finally, we train and evaluate classification models using the resulting features, obtaining state-of-the-art results on standard datasets. All code is o

Educational chatbots are a promising tool for assisting student learning. However, the effectiveness of educational chatbot-based interventions has yet to be extensively researched and validated in real world contexts within higher education settings with diverse learners at scale. This article presents findings from an intervention study that involved implementing three different types (i) instructional; ii), support iii); motivational -chatbot interactions across 10 weeks as part students' regular course work on introductory programming courses involving over two hundred university undergraduates who were enrolled either full time or online mode during COVID pandemic restrictions period when many schools had shifted towards remote instruction modes due changing global conditions around h

This work explores an innovative algorithm designed to enhance the mobility of underactuated robots in challenging environments. By utilizing a multi-scale approach, this novel technique effectively integrates both local and global information within a single optimization framework. The algorithm's primary objective is to minimize a cost function that incorporates a distance metric between the robot's current state and its goal position. It achieves this by leveraging a biologically inspired mechanism known as the Hebbian rule, which facilitates the adjustment of synaptic weights in neural networks. Additionally, this paper presents a novel method for enhancing the computational efficiency of the algorithm through dimensionality reduction techniques, specifically employing principal compon

Inspired by ideas from health risk assessment, this paper presents a new perspective for flood hazard mapping. Our approach is motivated by the fact that floods are multidimensional and can be characterized along multiple dimensions, such as depth, velocity, duration, and debris load. For each dimension, we develop a novel metric called the "exposure-consequence curve" (ECC), which captures the relationship between exposure and consequence (e.g., economic losses or human casualties). By aggregating these ECCs across all relevant dimensions, our method generates a comprehensive picture of flood hazards.

Mobility-on-demand (MOD) services have the potential to significantly improve the efficiency of urban transport. The emergence and rapid development in MOD service providers such as Uber, Lyft or Didi Chuxing indicates that this emerging mobility model is likely gaining popularity among users across different cities around globe with varying degrees [1]. The dynamic characteristics inherent within these popular on demand transportation systems present significant challenges for traffic management agencies who are responsible managing vehicle access into various road networks during peak periods using a combination techniques including signal timing optimization models which require continuous reconfiguration throughout each day depending upon changing conditions at any given time period(s)

We are united in how emotions are central to shaping our experiences; and yet, individuals differ in their propensity for emotional experiences. These differences are partly genetically influenced. Here, we used an experience-sampling approach (N = 252) to measure individual differences in daily affect and gene expression. In a first study, we demonstrated that both individual differences in daily positive and negative affect were correlated with gene expression in the blood. In a second study, we replicated this effect and showed that these relationships were specific to particular genes that have been implicated in the regulation of emotional processes or mood disorders, as well as to genes that have not been previously associated with emotion. Importantly, these effects were independent

We present NeWRF, a deep learning framework for predicting wireless channels. Wireless channel prediction is critical for accurate modeling of the propagation environment and has numerous applications in the telecommunications industry, including network planning, performance optimization, and spectral efficiency improvement. Traditional channel prediction methods rely on empirical models based on statistical data analysis or physical principles. However, these methods face limitations such as complex model construction, high computational cost, and lack of adaptability to rapidly evolving environments. In contrast, machine learning techniques offer a promising approach to address these challenges by utilizing large-scale datasets to learn the nonlinear relationships between environmental

In the realm of computer security, the importance of efficient and reliable user authentication methods has become increasing critical. As the internet becomes more pervasive in our daily lives, ensuring that only authorized users can access sensitive information has become an urgent need. One potential solution to this challenge is using a smartwatch as a two-factor authentication device, leveraging its capabilities for biometric data processing and wireless connectivity. While there are various existing methods for utilizing smartwatches in two-factor authentication scenarios, they often suffer from issues such as limited compatibility with different types of devices, lack of support for multiple authentication factors, and vulnerabilities to attacks on the Bluetooth connection between t

Emoji are universally recognized icons that enhance understanding and convey meaning, emotion, and intent in online communication. This paper presents Emojinize, a system that automatically enriches any given text with emoji translations. We introduce a novel approach to selecting the appropriate emoji, based on semantic analysis. Furthermore, we are the first to publish a dataset of more than 10,000 manually labeled examples, which we use to evaluate and improve our model. Our experiments show that Emojinize produces high-quality results, is easily integrable into existing workflows, and has the potential to enhance various NLP tasks.

The growing interest in Large Language Models (LLMs) for specialized applications has revealed the importance of fine-tuning these models to suit specific domains. However, due to limitations such as computational resources and accessibility, fine-tuning LLMs for niche domains remains a challenge. In this work, we propose FineTuneBots, an open-source tool that streamlines the process of fine-tuning LLM bots for customized tasks. Our approach combines the utilization of HuggingFace Hub repositories with customizable bot generation code, empowering users to adapt their LLM bots seamlessly. By leveraging HuggingFace's extensive repository infrastructure, FineTuneBots enables users to easily access pre-trained models tailored to various domains. This significantly reduces the burden on users f

This paper investigates mechanism design for decision-aware collaboration via federated learning (FL) platforms. We consider a setting where a service provider seeks to train a model across multiple client devices in a FL manner with the goal of maximizing social welfare. The clients are assumed to have private data, and they may also be heterogeneous in terms of their preferences over the trained models. As such, we study how to incentivize the clients to participate in training and provide their data while ensuring that the resulting model is socially optimal. To this end, we propose a general framework for designing mechanism design schemes that leverage the private data of each client as a resource. We show that the proposed framework can be instantiated to derive three different mecha

The main objective of the Multiple Kernel k-Means (MKKM) algorithm is to improve clustering results by using multiple kernels. However, it does not include an optimization process for choosing appropriate kernel weights that affect its performance and accuracy significantly; therefore this study introduces a novel MKM model based on particle swarm optimisation with adaptive inertia weight strategy called PSOIAWMKLM in order tackle these issues effectively while achieving improved classification outcomes compared against traditional approaches such as MKRGCNN or SVM+KSVM++ when applied across various datasets within diverse domains including image processing tasks involving medical images featuring high dimensionality characteristics requiring sophisticated feature extraction techniques due

Radio Frequency (RF) device fingerprinting has been recognized as a potential technology for enhancing the security of modern wireless communication systems. However, the conventional RF device fingerprinting scheme suffers from privacy concerns due to its reliance on signal measurements with known identifiers. To address this issue, we propose an Enhanced Privacy-Preserving RF Device Fingerprinting (EPPF) method that leverages the concept of pseudonyms and cryptographic techniques to enhance the level of privacy protection in RF device fingerprinting. Our proposed scheme consists of two main components: 1) Generation of unique pseudonyms using a one-time pad (OTP) encryption algorithm; and 2) Use of public-key cryptography to perform secure data transmission between the fingerprinting ser

We consider an online learning setting where the environment is an adversarial MDP with linear mixture structure. At each round, the learner chooses an action, and a transition kernel samples a next state from a linear mixture of $S$ states with an unknown parameter $\boldsymbol{w}$. The learner only observes a reward and the next state, and the goal is to maximize the cumulative reward over $T$ rounds. We develop an efficient algorithm that achieves a regret bound of $\tilde{O}(S^{1/2}K^{1/3}T^{2/3})$, where $K$ is the number of actions. The main technique is to reduce the problem to a sequence of adversarial bandit problems with $S$ actions and then apply the best-arm identification algorithm.

Accurate detection and diagnosis of abnormal behaviors such as network attacks from multivariate time series (MTS) are crucial for ensuring the stable and effective operation of industrial cyber-physical systems (CPS). However, existing researches pay little attention to the logical dependencies among system working states, and have difficulties in explaining the evolution mechanisms of abnormal signals. To reveal the spatio-temporal association relationships and evolution mechanisms of the working states of industrial CPS, this paper proposes a fine-grained adaptive anomaly diagnosis method (i.e. MAD-Transformer) to identify and diagnose anomalies in MTS. MAD-Transformer first constructs a temporal state matrix to characterize and estimate the change patterns of the system states in the t

We analyze the space complexity of Euclidean $k$-means and $k$-median clustering in fixed dimension Euclidean spaces. We show that any algorithm that achieves an approximation factor of $2t-1$ for $k$-means clustering uses $\Omega(n^{1-\frac{1}{t}})$ space, and that any algorithm that achieves an approximation factor of $t$ for $k$-median clustering uses $\Omega(n^{1-\frac{1}{t}})$ space. Our lower bounds hold even for fixed dimension Euclidean spaces. We also show that for every fixed $\epsilon>0$, there is a constant $c_\epsilon$ such that any algorithm that achieves an approximation factor of $2t-1$ for $k$-means clustering in $c_\epsilon n^{1-\epsilon}$ space must run in at least $n^{\Omega(\epsilon)}$ time. Our results improve upon the results of previous works which established similar lower bounds only for $k$-means clustering in the plane.

Hallucinations are a debilitating side-effect of psychosis, and a common experience for patients with schizophrenia. They can take many forms, but are most commonly auditory, consisting of voices that patients hear but which are not present in the environment. Hallucinations are highly distressing for patients, and can be challenging to treat. Early detection of hallucinations could facilitate early intervention, but methods of detecting hallucinations have limitations. For example, they may be based on symptoms that are not specific to hallucinations, or may require a gold-standard diagnosis, which is not always available. In this work, we propose an alternative approach to hallucination detection, based on the idea that hallucinations are false beliefs. We test our approach on a dataset of transcribed interviews with patients who have schizophrenia, some of whom are hallucinating and some of whom are not. Our results show that attributes of beliefs, as extracted by natural language processing tools, can indeed be used to distinguish between hallucinating and non-hallucinating patients, with relatively high accuracy. We suggest that our approach could be developed into a tool for identifying patients who are at risk of developing hallucinations, or for monitoring the effectiveness of interventions designed to reduce hallucinations.

We present a new benchmarking tool for wave propagation simulations: METAMAT 01. This first version focuses on the simulation of homogeneous materials in one-dimensional (1D) and three-dimensional (3D) spaces as well as two-dimensional (2D) configurations. The benchmarking tool is based on a semi-analytic solution that propagates a wave packet in a periodic medium with arbitrary dispersion relation, which is described by a generalized Airy equation. The solution is valid for any spatial dimension and does not involve any approximation, thus allowing for a direct assessment of the accuracy of numerical methods. We consider three types of absorption modeled by the absorption coefficient, the quality factor, and the loss tangent. The benchmarking tool is provided as ready-to-run C++ and Python codes with a common set of test cases. The tests validate the implementation of the benchmarking tool and can be used to validate the implementation of wave propagation simulations in the context of various projects. The benchmarking tool is hosted on GitHub (https://github.com/ErlerPhilipp/METAMAT) and is distributed under the permissive BSD license.

We present a novel method for efficiently producing semi-dense matches across images. Previous detector based matching methods suffer from the inability to scale in terms of both input resolution and number of keypoints, which is particularly problematic when dealing with large amounts of data such as videos or image collections containing thousands/millions instances each frame having many hundreds (or more) interest points per scene point that need corresponding counterparts within other scenes on all frames at high resolutions - this requires an efficient algorithm capable not only finding optimal matchings but also doing so quickly enough given constraints imposed by computational resources available today while still maintaining reasonable accuracy levels necessary due complexity inhe

Code-mixed data, the combination of human language and machine code, is an important challenge of natural language processing because its characteristics completely vary from the traditional structures of standard languages. This paper describes our effort to build a new code-mixed dataset, MaCmS, from Magahi, a language spoken in northern India, and its Hindi translation. We present multiple experiments on this dataset for sentiment analysis, as it is one of the first attempts to introduce a code-mixed dataset for a resource-poor language. We also propose a new hybrid approach for sentiment analysis of code-mixed data by combining part-of-speech tagging and deep learning. Our experiments show that MaCmS is a difficult dataset for sentiment analysis because of its complex structure. We hope that our efforts will encourage other researchers to work on code-mixed data from resource-poor languages.

Current studies on human locomotion focus mainly on solid ground walking conditions. In this paper, we present an integrated framework for understanding the impact of slipping motion during soft ground walking. To this end, we introduce a novel dataset of 2D-3D ground truth trajectories that provides rich information about individual subjects' behavior and walking dynamics in both solid ground and slippery environments. Using this dataset, we develop a model to predict slips in soft ground walking from 3D sensor measurements. This model is based on a physics-based computational model that can capture the dynamic behavior of human walking. We conduct extensive experiments to evaluate our method and show its effectiveness in predicting slips using only sensor data and without the need for hu

Constraint Acquisition (CA) is the problem of inferring, for a given domain, the constraints that hold among a set of variables. It has been formulated as a machine learning problem by interpreting the constraints as logical rules and the variables as features in a suitable space. In this paper, we propose a new approach to CA, which relies on the intuition that the more an input sample is constrained, the less the corresponding output label is meaningful. We validate this intuition by proving that, for a sufficiently large network, the average gradient of the loss function is zero on constrained inputs. Based on this property, we design a tailored loss function that CA networks should minimize to acquire constraints. We show that our loss function is a proper objective for CA, as it leads to a reduction of the Generalization Error. We also show that our loss function is advantageous for the training of CA networks, as it results in a better coverage of the constraint space. Finally, we show that our loss function is effective for both synthetic and real-world datasets.

The pharmaceutical industry is on the cusp of a computational revolution, thanks to the potential of deep learning in drug discovery and development. However, many drug development steps can take up to four years and cost over a billion dollars, making the process both time-consuming and expensive. Deep learning has the power to accelerate this process by predicting the efficacy and safety of candidate drugs, as well as identifying potential interactions between drugs. This review aims to provide a comprehensive overview of the current state of deep learning in drug discovery and development, focusing on deep learning-based models that utilize publicly available data. We cover various drug development stages, from target identification and validation to lead discovery and optimization. Additionally, we discuss the challenges that deep learning models face in drug discovery and development, and propose potential solutions. With its ability to analyze complex biological data and identify patterns, deep learning has the potential to transform the pharmaceutical industry and revolutionize the way we discover and develop drugs.

Scene depth estimation from paintings can streamline the process of 3D sculpture creation so that artists no longer need to manually draw out every individual line. However, existing works on painting-based scene understanding are limited by their inability to accurately estimate scene geometry and object contours. In this paper, we propose a novel pipeline for achieving accurate 3D reconstruction from paintings. Our method leverages cutting edge techniques such as UNet-like architecture, image pyramids, and multi-scale feature aggregation. Furthermore, our work incorporates self-supervised learning strategies for enhancing the model's capabilities. Additionally, we implement a robust training mechanism based on pseudo-ground truth data derived from an advanced pretrained segmentation mode

Animals learn to adapt agility of their movements to their capabilities and the environment they operate in. Humanoid robots have the potential to learn similar capabilities through imitation from humans, as well as by training with virtual agents. However, the existing learning frameworks for humanoid robotics are not yet mature enough to train full-body skills such as balancing, walking, or reaching. To close this gap, we propose a framework that allows for data-efficient learning of diverse full-body movements through imitation and reinforcement learning. We apply our method to a wide range of tasks and environments ranging from single legged hopping on a cylinder to quadrupedal locomotion over uneven terrain. This approach combines several existing techniques to improve efficiency and

We present ZSL-RPPO, an improved zero-shot learning architecture that overcomes the limitations of current approaches. The existing methods are primarily designed to address the issues related to the lack of diversity in the training data and the absence of semantic knowledge for target classes. However, these methods fail to consider the problem of semantic gaps between seen and unseen categories. Our approach focuses on addressing this challenge by incorporating a novel methodology called 'semantic gap learning'. This approach is implemented through a novel module known as 'Semantic Gap Representation Module (SGRM)', which enhances the model's ability to learn from the data available for unseen classes during training. To evaluate our method, we conduct experiments on four publicly avail

Semantic segmentation of high-resolution remote sensing imagery (HRSI) suffers from the problem of data scarcity in many applications. The few available labeled training images are often expensive to obtain and may not be representative of the distribution of unlabeled test examples, leading to domain gap. To address this challenge, we propose a novel pretext task for HRSI semantic segmentation: a pixel-wise contrastive learning approach that leverages the visual similarity between neighboring pixels across multiple resolutions. In contrast to existing approaches which rely on single-scale predictions or global features as representations, our method uses multi-scale features extracted from HRSI as input to the contrastive loss.

HTTP/3 is the latest version of the Hypertext Transfer Protocol and the successor of HTTP/2. It introduces several new features, including multiplexing over a single TCP connection, which aims to improve the Quality of Experience (QoE) for users. However, multiplexing increases the complexity of the protocol, and servers often struggle to handle concurrent streams efficiently, leading to suboptimal QoE. To address this issue, we propose using an HTTP/3-specific adaptation layer based on Explicit Priority Streaming (EPS), a multiplexed transport protocol designed for interactive sessions. We evaluate the benefits of this approach by implementing an EPS client and server and assessing the QoE improvements experimentally. Our results show that the HTTP/3+EPS combination provides a better QoE than HTTP/3 alone, mainly due to reduced perceptual jitter.

The field of 3D detailed human mesh reconstruction has made significant progress in recent years. However, most existing methods focus on the upper body and lack a comprehensive solution for the entire body. This paper introduces Fusion-3D, an end-to-end pipeline that can reconstruct human meshes from a single RGB image with full-body coverage. Our approach leverages the powerful fusion capabilities of diffusion models to seamlessly integrate various components into a coherent and unified framework. These components include 2D pose estimation, foreground masking, depth prediction, surface normal generation, and mesh generation. Each component is carefully trained using the appropriate datasets and techniques, ensuring their optimal performance within our system. To enhance realism, we prop

Image retrieval enables an efficient search through vast amounts of satellite imagery and returns similar images to a query image. In this paper, we present a novel framework for large-scale image retrieval that leverages the advantages of both Convolutional Neural Networks (CNN) and Transformer architectures. Our method combines convolutional features with Transformer attention mechanisms within a single network, allowing it to capture spatial relationships as well as contextual information across different regions in the feature space. By incorporating two different types of attention modules into our model, we are able to improve the accuracy of image retrieval by reducing noise and enhancing the discriminative power of visual features. Furthermore, by utilizing self-supervised learning

We study Empirical Group Distributional Robust Optimization (EGDRO), a natural distributional robust optimization (DRO) counterpart of Empirical Risk Minimization (ERM). In EGDRO, we aim to find a model that performs well not only on the empirical distribution of the training data but also on an unknown distribution that is "close" to the empirical distribution in a distributional sense. The "closeness" is measured by the Wasserstein distance in our work. Due to the complexity of the distributional constraint, previous works either use a non-convex relaxation or a first-order approximation to tackle EGDRO. As a consequence, it is not clear how to efficiently compute the EGDRO estimator, neither is it clear how to use the EGDRO framework to perform model selection. We develop a novel convex relaxation for EGDRO, which leads to an efficient algorithm for computing the EGDRO estimator. We also show that the proposed algorithm can be used for model selection. We test the proposed method on image classification and structured prediction tasks. Our results show that the proposed method outperforms the non-convex relaxation based method and the first-order approximation based method in terms of both efficiency and effectiveness.

AI Large Language Models (LLMs) like ChatGPT are set to transform the legal landscape, but there is a dearth of research on how they perform in different areas of law. We conducted an empirical study to assess the performance of LLMs across various subfields of U.S. criminal law, including criminal procedure, evidence, and substantive offenses. Our findings show that while these models excel in specific areas of criminal law, such as criminal procedure, they struggle in other fields, particularly those with technical or nuanced terminology, suggesting room for improvement in their capabilities. These results highlight the need for continued research into the performance of LLMs in specialized domains and underscore the importance of integrating diverse perspectives and expertise to ensure

Growing concerns about safety and alignment of AI systems highlight the importance of embedding moral capabilities in these models. In this paper, we present an approach to enhance AI systems with ethical decision-making abilities. The core principle underlying our approach is the concept of "ethical self-awareness" which refers to the ability for the AI system to reflect on its own actions and outcomes from a moral perspective. We introduce a novel architecture that embeds ethical self-awareness into an AI system by incorporating a moral module within the model's feedback loop. This module utilizes the outcomes of the AI system's actions as inputs to generate moral insights, enabling the AI system to become aware of the potential consequences of its decisions. Additionally, we incorporate

Information retrieval models have witnessed a paradigm shift from unsupervised statistical approaches to feature-rich deep learning methods in the recent decade. These advances have led, among others things such as better ranking and relevance understanding of queries at scale by search engines like Google or Bing; however they come with their own challenges for privacy preservation: (a) these systems are usually built on top layers that extract features via neural networks trained over large datasets containing sensitive information about users' interests/activities which may be inferred even when encrypted endtoend encryption is used - leading us towards an arms race between security researchers who try mitigating this threat while attackers keep finding new ways around it through advers

As many as 8 in 10 adults share intimate content such as nude or semi-nude photos of themselves with their romantic partners. Most people (73%) delete the content after sharing it, and only 29% of people report that they regret sharing this kind of content. Of those who do not delete intimate content, 45% keep it on their phone, and 64% store it on cloud services like iCloud. These findings come from a new study by Pew Research Center, which polled a nationally representative sample of U.S. adults about the type and extent of sexually explicit content they create and share digitally — including images and videos of themselves — as well as their attitudes toward doing so. The survey included questions about how adults find or encounter these types of content online, as well as their experie

Accurate traffic forecasting is challenging due to the complex dependency on road networks, various demand patterns, and dynamic traveler behavior. This paper proposes a new framework that incorporates deep generative models to learn spatial-temporal dependencies in large-scale urban traffic data. The proposed method leverages the generative nature of Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) for spatio-temporal modeling and the efficient sampling of trajectories using diffusion models. We demonstrate the effectiveness of our approach by applying it to real-world urban traffic data, where it achieves competitive results compared to state-of-the-art methods, with the added benefit of being able to generate samples from the learned distribution of future traffic flows. Our code is available at https://gi

Machine learning has achieved remarkable success in many applications. However, existing studies are largely based on the closed-world assumption, which is too idealistic for real-world environments where data may contain outliers or be noisy. In this work, we propose a robust learning framework to tackle these challenges with the open-world assumption. Specifically, we introduce a novel concept of "robustness" that considers the performance of machine learning models when exposed to unseen, outlier, or noisy data. We also formulate an optimization problem to maximize model robustness while minimizing the loss function of known samples. Finally, we demonstrate the effectiveness of our method through experiments on both synthetic and real-world datasets. The results show that our method can

In the past year, numerous companies have incorporated Generative AI (GenAI) capabilities into their services and products. However with this new technology comes a large amount of risk to be considered by both service providers as well end users in order for it not only work efficiently but also safely without any unintended consequences such us biased output or malicious attacks via deep fakes videos/images generated from real-world data sets which could lead towards serious security breaches within digital platforms where these types algorithms are being used today especially those relying heavily on visual content processing like surveillance cameras & drones etc... This talk will discuss how we can leverage existing research around machine learning safety methods combined together thr

Creating 3D textured meshes using generative artificial intelligence has garnered significant attention recently, but the resulting output often lacks visual realism. However, current works focus on 2D images while neglecting their depth information. To address this issue, we propose a novel method that leverages 3D shape priors to generate high-fidelity 3D textures from synthetic 3D models with minimal input noise. Our approach utilizes an encoder-decoder framework that combines self-supervised learning and diffusion modeling techniques to effectively learn and encode both shape and texture information into latent codes for subsequent generation. We evaluate our proposed method on the ScanObjectNN dataset, demonstrating its superior performance in terms of both quality and speed when comp

Legged robots have become capable of performing highly dynamic maneuvers in the past few years. However, despite their ability to perform complex tasks, they still struggle with the problem of stability while standing on one leg or during locomotion. This is especially true when dealing with uneven terrain and rough surfaces. We present an approach that leverages the potential of neural networks to predict the contact force exerted by the robot's foot on the ground, thus enabling a safe control policy. The proposed neural network architecture is trained using supervised learning based on real-world data collected from experiments. We evaluate our method on the ANYmal quadrupedal platform, where we demonstrate its robustness and effectiveness in challenging scenarios, such as maintaining ba

Higher-order patterns reveal sequential multistep state transitions, which are usually superior to single steps for a complex task. However the neural mechanisms underlying such higher order dynamics remain elusive because of their complexity and small proportion in brain data series; they can be captured only with large sample sizes but at high cost or low fidelity by electrophysiological measurements like EEG/MEG recordings [1]. Here we propose that functional connectivity (FC), as quantified using spectral coherence between two time courses across frequency bands (δ,\mspace width=0px {}$$\mu$$<$, $\gamma$) has potential advantages over traditional approaches due its simplicity yet robust estimation from relatively short duration datasets eve

Feature space adaptation (FSA) is a key technique for few-shot learning, which adapts the feature extractor trained on the source domain to the target domain. Specifically, a discriminative FSA method finds an optimal projection that minimizes the classification loss between source and target. Although it has shown great potential in few-shot learning, the existing discriminative FSA methods suffer from two limitations.
