(1) High computational cost: solving the classification loss between source and target requires a large number of samples; (2) Large model size: the optimal projection is parameterized as a $d \times d$ matrix, where $d$ is the feature dimension. To address these two issues, we propose a novel \textbf{D}iscriminative \textbf{S}ample-\textbf{G}uided and \textbf{P}arameter-\textbf{E}fficient FSA method (DSG-PEFSA) for cross-domain few-shot learning. Specifically, we introduce a sample-guided projection update rule to gradually approach the optimal projection using only a few well-selected samples, and we propose a parameter-efficient projection parameterization to reduce the model size and accelerate the convergence. We conduct extensive experiments on few-shot classification tasks in the cross-domain setting, where our DSG-PEFSA achieves a new state-of-the-art.

While 3D object detection has been studied extensively in recent years, detecting humans in 3D space is still an challenging task due to their relatively smaller sizes and larger variations in appearances. In this paper, we propose HUNTER, a novel unsupervised human-centric 3D detection framework which transfers knowledge from synthetic instances to real scenes. Specifically, we leverage the large number of human instances in the synthetic datasets to learn a prior over the space of human shapes. This prior is then transferred to the real domain and combined with evidence from 2D detections to constrain the search space for 3D proposals. Finally, we optimize the 3D proposals by leveraging the correspondence between the synthetic and real domain. Our method outperforms existing unsupervised baselines and achieves a favorably low computational cost. Without any 3D ground truth, our HUNTER achieves a 4.8% higher mAP at the IOU of 0.5 under the setting of detecting objects with at least 95% of the ground truth. The source code will be released at https://github.com/luost26/HUNTER

Mechanisms for decentralized finance on blockchains suffer from various problems, including suboptimal liquidity and high transaction fees. These challenges become more pronounced as the number of assets in a protocol grows. In this paper, we propose a novel framework based on a three-layer architecture that enhances the performance of decentralized financial protocols by utilizing zero-knowledge proofs (ZKPs) and the FXG theorem. Our design incorporates an asset management layer, a liquidity provider layer, and a trading layer to optimize the liquidity provision process. The use of ZKPs eliminates the need for trusted third parties and ensures efficient verification of transactions, resulting in enhanced privacy protection. Additionally, our approach reduces gas consumption while maintain

We study the PSPACE-complete $k$-Canadian Traveller Problem, where each node has a color and a set of its neighbors. The goal is to find a Hamiltonian cycle with at most one visit per neighbor in each node. In this problem, the goal is to find a Hamiltonian path with as few as possible visits to nodes, i.e., it reduces to the Canadian Traveller problem. As an important case of the $k$-Canadian Traveller problem, we also consider the Canadian Traveller problem on trees, which is NP-complete. For the tree Canadian Traveller problem, we prove that there exists a constant $\alpha$ such that any Hamiltonian tour can be improved by at least $\alpha$ by removing one vertex from the tree. This result shows that the $\alpha = 1$ lower bound for the Hamiltonian Path problem generalizes well to the t

Model merging is to combine fine-tuned models derived from multiple domains, with the intent of improving performance on a downstream task. For this paper, we aim to investigate the model merging method for vision and language tasks. Previous studies have proposed an approach called "model-merging by knowledge distillation" (MMKD), which involves training a student network using a teacher's output as the target, and then fine-tuning both networks on downstream tasks. However, it remains unclear whether MMKD can effectively improve performance on downstream tasks compared to the standard single-domain pretraining and fine-tuning pipeline. We propose a new dataset called CLEVR-280-Rand, derived from CLEVR-280, and conduct experiments to address this issue. Our findings indicate that the stan

Understanding the reasoning capabilities of Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) is crucial for assessing their suitability for various applications. In this work, we introduce a new dataset, RLR-KG, to evaluate MLLMs' reasoning and knowledge-grounded abilities in multimodal settings. RLR-KG consists of 150K triple-based questions derived from the Wikipedia Knowledge Graph, with corresponding visual and textual contexts. To answer these questions, models are required to reason over the relationships among entities and utilize information from multiple modalities. We have evaluated five state-of-the-art MLLMs on the RLR-KG test set and found that they have different levels of reasoning ability across different modalities.

Recent advances in generative models, including large language models (LLMs), vision language models (VLMs) and diffusion models, have enabled the rapid expansion of multi-modal data. The ability to integrate various types of information into a single representation has emerged as a pivotal capability for achieving more accurate and comprehensive understanding of complex real-world problems. However, there remains a significant challenge in effectively utilizing this heterogeneous knowledge within deep learning architectures due to its intricate nature. Consequently, researchers must develop innovative solutions to facilitate the seamless integration of diverse modalities while ensuring that such models remain computationally efficient. This paper provides an extensive survey of recent pro

We study the relation between space and precision for real-time computation in the setting of Blum, Cucker, Koiran and Matz (2009). We prove that in continuous time, space complexity and precision are essentially the same. More precisely, we show that for any real-time computation, there is a computation with the same space complexity and precision. Our result holds for deterministic as well as for nondeterministic computations.

The blockchain technology has grown from a niche application of a decentralized digital currency to a popular framework for building decentralized applications (Dapps). Understanding the underlying data structures and flow of information in blockchain-based Dapps is crucial for analyzing their properties, such as security and performance. Often, the underlying data is better understood through visualizations of the relationships between data entities. However, visualizing blockchain data presents a unique challenge: the vast majority of blockchain-based Dapps are decentralized, which means that the data is continuously being written to the blockchain by users all over the world, and grows at a fast pace. Thus, traditional methods of data visualization, which usually assume that the data is relatively static, become inefficient. To address this challenge, we propose GraphVision, a graph-based visualization framework for blockchain data that utilizes the concept of a directed acyclic graph (DAG) to represent the evolving blockchain data as a constantly growing graph. In particular, we use a DAG to represent the blockchain transactions of a Dapp as a graph, where the nodes are the transactions and the edges are the relationships between the transactions. To visualize the transactions on the blockchain, we first offline compute the transaction graph by executing a set of queries to retrieve the desired data from the blockchain. We then use a combination of force-directed and hierarchical layouts to visualize the transaction graph in a compact and organized manner, with the help of interactive filtering and clustering. Finally, we animate the visualization to show the growth of the transaction graph over time. Through a case study of the decentralized finance (DeFi) applications built on the Ethereum blockchain, we show that our framework can effectively convey the structure and flow of information in blockchain data.

Online reinforcement learning (RL) algorithms learn a policy by interacting with an environment, and they can be viewed as causal bandit algorithms. This perspective allows us to use the extensive literature on causal bandits to analyze RL algorithms. In this survey, we review the common causal bandit settings (e.g., stationary, non-stationary, etc.) and discuss the implications of the causal perspective on RL. This perspective highlights the importance of counterfactual reasoning in RL and suggests novel algorithms for learning under non-stationary environments. Counterfactual reinforcement learning (CRL) algorithms reason about what would have happened in the absence of an action taken by the agent. We explain why CRL is necessary in non-stationary environments and provide examples of CRL algorithms. We also discuss other directions for future work, including the role of causality in explaining RL behavior and the use of causal concepts for designing robust RL algorithms.

Detecting surface anomalies of industrial materials poses a significant challenge within a myriad of manufacturing applications. Inspired by the success of large language models (LLMs), we present an effective yet economical approach for surface anomaly detection in industrial materials through LLMs, namely LLM-based Anomaly Detection (LAD). The proposed method leverages the advanced capabilities of LLMs to analyze massive datasets of images and textual descriptions of various surfaces, enabling accurate identification of anomalous surfaces. Our approach involves fine-tuning LLMs on a pre-trained model with data collected from multiple industries. The tuned model is then used to detect anomalous features in new images. Through extensive evaluations on diverse datasets, we demonstrate the s

In recent years, operator learning, particularly the DeepONet, has received much attention for efficiently learning and predicting nonlinear operators with complex inputs. However, such a method is based on an implicit assumption that all operators are smooth everywhere, and it may fail to learn non-smooth operators. Motivated by this observation, we propose a novel neural network architecture for learning non-smooth operators, which we term the Smoothing Deep Operator Network (SmoothDeepONet). At its core, the SmoothDeepONet integrates two techniques: smoothed input normalization and smoothed output activation functions. To demonstrate the effectiveness of our proposed method, we compare it against both conventional deep operator networks and alternative state-of-the-art approaches on fou

Conventional Unsupervised Domain Adaptation (UDA) strives to minimize the domain gap, which has led to limited performance improvements for many real-world applications. In this paper, we propose a new framework called Cross-domain Generalization with Multi-stage Upsampling and Decoupled Feature Aggregation (CUG-MDA). It is composed of two stages: 1) feature upsampling that reconstructs the original image resolution from low-resolution features through multi-scale upsampling; 2) decoupled feature aggregation that aggregates multiple feature representations across different scales. We design the decoder to learn the mapping between high-level information at each scale and low-level details in the final output.

Eating is an essential activity in our daily lives, but for many elderly or disabled people, it can be challenging or even impossible due to physical limitations. Therefore, many institutions provide feeding assistance services, but these services are not always available due to a shortage of carers. In these situations, a robot-assisted feeding system can be a viable solution to ensure the safety and comfort of the recipient while reducing the physical burden on the carers. However, the majority of existing feeding systems are fixed on a table or the floor, which limits their adaptability and portability. In addition, they lack the necessary safety features to prevent accidents, such as food aspiration. In this paper, we designed a new robot-assisted feeding system that overcomes the limitations of existing systems. The proposed system consists of a modular and portable base frame, a collaborative robot arm, and a user-friendly interface. It can adapt to different eating scenarios, such as table-top and bed-side feeding, and is designed to be safe, easy to use, and clean. A prototype of the proposed system was fabricated and tested. The results of the experiments demonstrate that the robot can accurately deliver the food to the user's mouth. The experiments also show that the proposed system can adapt to different feeding scenarios and is safe to use.

Emoji have become ubiquitous in written communication, on the Web and beyond. They convey a wealth of information about the author's emotional state, intentions, or opinions. However, emoji are not universal: they vary across languages and cultures. This paper introduces the first-ever survey of the entirety of Unicode 13.0 emoji for 279 languages (including dialects). We examine how the emoji landscape has changed over time, as well as changes at the level of individual characters. We also provide a brief introduction to emoji and their use in language technology. Finally, we present a list of all emoji with their associated Unicode codepoints and languages. The list is available online, along with a set of scripts that can be used to convert between the different formats of our data.

With the boom of e-commerce and web applications, recommender systems have become an important part of many services. The aim of these algorithms is to help users find relevant items according to their interests. One of the most common recommendation methods uses matrix factorization (MF), which decomposes a user-item rating matrix into two matrices, representing user preferences and item features. Despite its widespread use, MF has some drawbacks that can limit its performance. In this paper, we propose a novel method that combines MF with graph neural networks (GNNs) to address some of these limitations. Our approach, called Graph Neural Networks for Matrix Factorization (GN2MF), leverages the structure of the user-item interaction data represented as a graph. We integrate GNNs into the

In this article, we study the relationship between notions of depth for sequences, namely, Bennett's (1962) sequence length and Borel-Cantelli subsets, and a notion of complexity for functions that we call the asymptotic information complexity. This is motivated by a study of how the complexity of a function depends on the underlying data generating process. We first prove that for every uniformly distributed random variable $X$, the sequence $x_i = X$ is $n$-deep if and only if the associated empirical distribution is Borel-Cantelli. This result can be seen as a sequential version of a well-known result in probability theory about convergence in distribution: we show that convergence in distribution coincides with almost sure convergence along subsequences where the probability of error g

Vision transformers (ViTs) have achieved impressive performance over various computer vision tasks. However, modeling global correlations with multi-head self-attention (MSA) layers leads to two widely recognized issues: the massive computational resource consumption and the lack of intrinsic inductive bias for modeling local visual patterns. To solve both issues, we devise a simple yet effective approach named Mamba, which introduces a hidden attention mechanism to the MSA layer. The hidden attention decouples the MSA layer into two separate sub-layers: the hidden layer and the attention layer. The hidden layer models the global correlations, while the attention layer models the local visual patterns. Thanks to the hidden attention, Mamba enjoys both efficiency and effectiveness. Mamba is efficient in computation, as the hidden layer can be implemented with a one-by-one convolution, which significantly reduces the computational complexity. Mamba is also effective, as it can be pre-trained with a small patch size, so that the attention layer can learn the local visual patterns, and then applied to larger patch sizes for downstream tasks. Extensive experiments demonstrate the stronger performance of Mamba over previous ViTs on various benchmark datasets. The code is available at https://github.com/ziplab/mamba.

Adversarial Training (AT) effectively improves the robustness of Deep Neural Networks (DNNs), however, it is known to cause catastrophic forgetting. To address this problem, we propose a novel AT framework that employs an autoencoder in parallel with DNN for adversarially-trained classifiers. We show that training the autoencoder on clean samples reduces catastrophic forgetting by preserving the knowledge learned from clean data during AT. In addition, we extend our proposed framework into a multi-task setting, where the network needs to learn multiple tasks simultaneously. Our experimental results show that the proposed method improves not only accuracy but also generalization performance under various attack types and settings. Code will be available at https://github.com/YeonJun-Kim/Aut

Open-vocabulary generalization requires robotic systems to perform tasks involving complex and diverse objects. However, current approaches to open-vocabulary generalization rely on either a predefined set of object classes or object detection in images to identify novel classes at test time. In this work, we aim to overcome these limitations by introducing a new framework for learning from demonstrations that enables robots to learn novel object classes without requiring any prior knowledge or training data about those classes. Our approach involves combining an agent-centric representation with a world-centric representation. The agent-centric representation encodes the spatial relationships between the robot's body and the objects using a neural network model called Sparse Encoding of G

Despite advancements in text-to-image generation (T2I), prior methods often face a trade-off between visual quality and perceptual realism, especially when generating realistic images. In this paper, we propose to address this problem by introducing a novel T2I framework that explicitly enforces the image's perceptual similarity to a reference real-world scene during the diffusion process. To achieve this, we design an implicit perceptual similarity score based on a pre-trained semantic segmentation model. Additionally, we introduce an iterative optimization algorithm to adjust the position of each noised pixel and minimize the difference between its current and reference perceptual scores. This step is crucial for ensuring the generated image maintains its original texture while becoming

In this study, the impact of turbulent diffusion on mixing of biochemical reaction models is investigated using a multi-scale approach. The model considered includes a chemical reaction and an irreversible diffusion process between two phases, which are both modeled as advection-diffusion systems in Cartesian coordinates. A spatially homogeneous, two-dimensional domain with periodic boundary conditions is considered as well as a time-dependent velocity field that is assumed to be independent from the chemical reaction. The numerical method involves discretizing the system in space by means of a finite volume method and approximating the solution with high order polynomial interpolants of continuous functions for the velocity and concentration variables. The problem is solved numerically em

In the field of chemical structure recognition, the task of converting molecular images into graph structures and SMILES strings has been extensively studied. However, the reverse process of transforming SMILES strings or graphs back to molecule images remains largely unexplored. This paper introduces a novel method for inverting the molecule image-to-string-to-image pipeline using diffusion models. The approach involves three distinct steps: (1) generating string representations from the images using a pre-trained text-to-structure model; (2) inverting the generated string back to an image through diffusion models; and (3) comparing the reconstructed image with the original input image using the Frechet Inception Distance (FID). We conducted experiments on two different datasets, includin

Deep learning has achieved remarkable progress in various applications, heightening the importance of safeguarding the intellectual property (IP) rights. In this paper, we introduce a novel framework for detecting deepfake videos based on a generative adversarial network with auxiliary loss functions that incorporate IP detection capabilities. Our proposed method, dubbed DF-DART, is tailored to the specific needs of video data, focusing on video frames rather than images. We demonstrate its effectiveness by implementing it on three publicly available datasets: DFDC2019, FLIM2021 and AV2023. The experimental results show that our method achieves state-of-the-art performance compared to other methods with an average accuracy of 85%. Moreover, it surpasses previous methods using only image at

The stability-plasticity dilemma is a major challenge in continual learning, as it involves balancing the conflicting objectives of maintaining performance on previous tasks while learning new tasks. In this paper, we propose the recall-oriented continual learning framework to address this challenge. Inspired by the human brain's ability to separate the mechanisms responsible for stability and plasticity, our framework consists of a two-level architecture where an inference network effectively acquires new knowledge and a generative network recalls past knowledge when necessary. In particular, to maximize the stability of past knowledge, we investigate the complexity of knowledge depending on different representations, and thereby introducing generative adversarial meta-model (GAMM) that i

A core ambition of reinforcement learning (RL) is the creation of agents capable of rapid, generalizable adaptation to novel tasks. To this end, in recent years there has been an increasing interest in studying the role of language in RL, and more specifically in the application of natural language processing (NLP) techniques for representation learning. However, most of these works have focused on using NLP as a pre-training tool, ignoring the potential for NLP to enhance the performance of models during training. In this work, we propose a novel paradigm that incorporates NLP methods into reinforcement learning training from scratch. This approach not only allows for a deeper understanding of the role of NLP in RL but also demonstrates its effectiveness in improving agent performance.

Symmetry is a fundamental aspect of many real-world robotic tasks. However, current deep learning approaches for robotic manipulation typically focus on learning task-specific policies, neglecting the role of symmetry in robot motion planning. In this paper, we propose SHiM: a simple yet effective method that learns to plan symmetric motions by explicitly incorporating symmetry information into the robot's state representation during training. We show that SHiM outperforms competitive baselines across four benchmark datasets and three different metrics for learning symmetric motions, demonstrating its effectiveness. Moreover, it significantly improves generalization performance when tested under perturbations, highlighting the importance of symmetry for robustness. Finally, we conduct abla

Faithfully modeling the space of articulations is a crucial task that allows recovery and reproduction of realistic motion, especially in the field of animation. Recent advances in physics-based motion capture have introduced novel solutions for capturing physical quantities such as force, torque, or inertial properties of articulated objects. In this work, we propose a physics-informed neural network model to represent human joint angles from these physical quantities. Our architecture consists of three main components: (i) a physics encoder that predicts the dynamics of rigid body chains from physical measurements; (ii) a dynamics decoder that converts these dynamics into a space of articulations; and (iii) a conditioning module that learns to combine prior knowledge and measured forces

This paper delves into the contrasting roles of data within academic and industrial spheres, highlighting their impact on scientific progress. The first section explores the role of data in academia, where it serves as a crucial element in the pursuit of knowledge and innovation. Through the lens of academic research, we examine how data contributes to generating new insights, refining existing theories, and challenging established paradigms. We delve into the process of data collection, analysis, and interpretation within an academic context, emphasizing its significance in advancing our understanding of complex phenomena. Next, we shift our focus to the realm of industry, where data takes on a distinctly operational character. In this setting, data serves as a critical tool for making in

The realm of algorithms with predictions has led to the development of several new algorithms that leverage (even imperfect) human input. In this work, we design a novel interactive algorithm for predicting time-series data via user inputs based on an interpretable model: linear regression combined through weighted averaging; which can be easily explained and understood by users or decision makers in real life applications like health care where patients might not have enough statistical knowledge but want their medical conditions predicted using simple methods such as averages across different types/values within datasets

Motivation

In recent years there's been much interest into improving our understanding about how humans learn from interactions when ma

Text rewriting is the task of taking a given text and rewriting it in a certain way, for example, to make it more positive. This is often done using neural sequence-to-sequence models, which have a tendency to output negative text. To address this, we propose a novel method called Socratic Reasoning, which uses rule-based reasoning to identify and correct negative text. We evaluate our method on news headlines from the Snopes dataset, and find that it improves results.

3D object detection from multi-view images, a fundamental problem in autonomous driving and robotics, is recently dominated by the pipeline of `multi-view representation learning + 3D proposal + 3D detection'. This paper follows a different route and proposes a single-stage, multi-view detection model, which leverages novel findings on (i) a combined network (CN) that surprisingly outperforms both 2D and 3D convolutions for indoor object detection, and (ii) ray marching aggregation (RMA) that effectively fuses multi-view local features. Specifically, the proposed CN-RMA first projects multi-view images to local features, which are then combined with the proposed CN to predict object detections. The proposed CN is a shallower network that aggregates multi-view local features by learning the relative pose between multi-view images, and thus it can potentially be applied to other tasks such as 3D proposal generation. The proposed RMA fuses multi-view local features by tracing rays from the camera through the 3D space and aggregating the features of intersected objects. It is simple, efficient, and can easily incorporate multi-view local features at different scales. Extensive experiments on the KITTI and ScanObjectNN datasets show that the proposed CN-RMA outperforms state-of-the-art methods.

terrestrian robots face challenges such as uneven terrain, obstacles, and steep slopes, which can impede their movement and mobility. To overcome these challenges, robots can utilize aerial modes of locomotion, such as hopping and flying. Bipedal robots, in particular, have the potential to navigate diverse terrains by employing a bi-modal bi-copter design. This paper introduces Skater, a novel bi-modal bi-copter robot that can adapt to various terrains, including indoor and outdoor environments. Skater features two independently driven propellers for aerial locomotion and can transition to a bipedal walking mode by tilting its body. The robot is equipped with a customized motor controller that allows for precise control of each propeller's speed, enabling stable and smooth transitions between flying and walking modes. This paper presents the design and control strategies of Skater, including a state machine-based control architecture and a trajectory tracking control algorithm. Furthermore, it discusses the challenges of integrating the different modes of locomotion and proposes potential solutions. Through extensive experiments, the paper demonstrates Skater's ability to navigate diverse terrains, overcome obstacles, and transition seamlessly between flying and walking modes.

Leveraging pre-trained visual language models has become a widely adopted approach for improving performance in various downstream vision tasks. However, these methods often suffer from two shortcomings: (1) they require fine tuning on large amounts of labeled data to achieve good results; and 2), their representations are not task agnostic or generalizable across different domains since the label information is utilized during training instead being preserved as invariances between images with similar content that can be exploited by unsupervised learning algorithms like contrastive self supervised objective functions suchas SimCLR/MoCo v3 which provide better generalization ability without requiring any additional supervision beyond what's already available through publicly accessible da

The design and fabrication of deployable structures is a classic problem in architecture and engineering, with applications ranging from solar panels and satellite antennas to biological systems such as flowers and leaves. Despite much effort, the design of deployable structures with more than one degree of freedom (1-DOF) is still an open problem. Here we present a family of 1-DOF deployable polyhedrons, characterized by a radial deployment mechanism that transforms the polyhedron from a compact stowed configuration to a larger deployed configuration. The family is based on a single deployable module, consisting of a spherical shell with four identical cylindrical cavities. By varying the radius of the module, as well as the radius and thickness of the spherical shell, we obtain a continuum of deployable polyhedrons, with different numbers of faces and degrees of deployability. We provide a theoretical framework for the design of 1-DOF deployable polyhedrons, based on the concept of a deployability vector, which specifies the sequence of motions required to deploy the structure. We also introduce a lightweight and flexible fabrication process, based on paper modeling, that allows the fabrication of large-scale prototypes with spatial accuracy better than 1 mm. Finally, we perform a series of experiments that validate the deployment mechanism and investigate the structural behavior of the deployable polyhedrons, including comparisons with numerical models based on both linear and nonlinear elasticity. Our work opens the path for the design of 1-DOF deployable structures with complex geometries, potentially paving the way for new applications in architecture and engineering.

Large language models (LLMs) reflect societal norms and biases, especially about race. Consequently, LLMs often demonstrate racial bias in downstream tasks, which can be addressed through diverse training data. However, the effects of bias reduction are typically not well understood: for example, does the model's ability to solve a task improve after bias reduction? To answer this question, we develop a novel framework for measuring the effectiveness of bias reduction using human judgment. The framework incorporates two key components: (1) quantifying the degree of racial bias in an LLM's judgments by analyzing its answers on a set of questions that have been answered by humans; (2) evaluating whether an LLM is more effective at solving a downstream task after bias reduction by comparing i

Software Defined Networks (SDN) bring flexibility and controllability to network operations, mainly due to the separation of the control plane from the data plane. However, this separation introduces a new attack surface, as an attacker controlling the SDN controller can manipulate the global view of the network and launch crosspath attacks. In this paper, we present MIRAGE, a defense framework against crosspath attacks in SDNs. MIRAGE adopts a multi-layer defense approach, combining global state awareness, local state awareness, and packet-level defense mechanisms. We implement a prototype of MIRAGE on top of OpenFlow-enabled SDN switches and evaluate it against a variety of crosspath attacks. Our experimental results show that MIRAGE effectively mitigates all the evaluated crosspath attacks with minimal performance overheads.

As machine learning models are increasingly deployed in real-world applications, it is crucial to understand their vulnerabilities and develop strategies to mitigate them. Adversarial attacks pose a serious threat to machine learning models, as attackers may use them to manipulate model predictions for malicious gain. However, the focus of the literature is on evaluation under attack, rather than a holistic view of the malicious use problem, which includes attack resistance as well as post-attack performance. In this work, we introduce the WMDP Benchmark, which aims to evaluate the susceptibility of a model to adversarial attacks and its ability to recover from them. The benchmark consists of two components: an unlearning benchmark to measure the learnability of the attack and a recovery benchmark to measure the detectability and reversibility of the attack. We provide a holistic framework for thinking about the malicious use problem and demonstrate the practical utility of the benchmark by using it to compare the susceptibility of different models to adversarial attacks and to evaluate the effectiveness of unlearning as a defense.

This work highlights an approach for incorporating realistic uncertainties into scientific computing workflows to enable the discovery of robust solutions. The approach utilizes a combination of Bayesian inference and differential equations, enabling a more comprehensive assessment of uncertainty in scientific models. By leveraging Bayesian techniques such as Markov Chain Monte Carlo sampling, the method allows for the integration of prior knowledge about uncertain parameters into the model. Moreover, the use of differential equations enables the propagation of uncertainties through the model and facilitates the efficient evaluation of potential solutions. In this paper, we present the application of our method in two case studies: one involving the estimation of optimal control policies u

Human brain and behavior provide a rich venue that can inspire novel control and learning methods for robotics. We have recently demonstrated that reinforcement learning (RL) from human feedback could significantly improve the performance of autonomous driving vehicles. However, the performance of such RL-based systems is heavily influenced by both the quality and the quantity of the human feedback. In this paper, we propose a new approach called \textit{Human-Informed Reinforcement Learning} (HIRL), which employs humans as an additional supervisor to guide RL in a human-in-the-loop fashion. The HIRL system consists of two components: a human-inspired policy learner and a human-in-the-loop mechanism.

Binary neural networks utilize 1-bit quantized weights and activations to reduce both the model's storage demands and computational burden. However, advanced binary architectures still incorporate millions of inefficient and nonhardware-friendly full-precision multiplication operations. A&B BNN is proposed to directly remove part of the multiplication operations in a traditional BNN and replace the rest with an equal number of bit operations, introducing the mask layer and the quantized RPReLU structure based on the normalizer-free network architecture. The mask layer can be removed during inference by leveraging the intrinsic characteristics of BNN with straightforward mathematical transformations to avoid the associated multiplication operations. The quantized RPReLU structure enables mo

Vision and language tasks are often limited by the size of the model, the compute available, or both. For example, larger models can be trained on larger datasets, and larger images can provide more context for vision tasks. However, larger images also increase the tokenization cost, memory footprint, and decrease the throughput of transformers. To address these challenges, we propose nested tokenization, a technique that tokenizes large images into smaller local patches and larger global tokens. The local patches are processed independently on separate transformers, while the global tokens are processed on a main transformer. This allows models to benefit from larger context without incurring the costs of processing the entire image on a single transformer. We implement nested tokenization for Vision Transformers and show that it improves performance over larger models that do not have enough context. Specifically, we achieve 0.6% higher top-1 accuracy than previous models, while using 40% fewer parameters and 70% less memory. Our code is available at https://github.com/RAIVNLab/xT.

Counterfactual explanations (CEs) enhance the interpretability of machine learning models by providing insights into their behavior. Existing CE methods fall short in explaining complex behaviors and have limited capability to handle model-invariant features, which undermine the practical utility of CE for users. To address these challenges, we propose a novel approach called Contextually-Aware Counterfactual Explanation (CACE). This approach is designed to provide contextually-aware counterfactuals that explicitly identify sensitive factors affecting the decision and explain how they influence it. Our method starts with generating counterfactuals through perturbation in the input space, followed by selecting the most relevant perturbations based on their impact on a target variable. We th

This paper investigates the application of eXplainable Artificial Intelligence (XAI) to explain the prediction results of a multimodal model for COVID-19 infection in children. To this end, a XAI method is developed that explains an input to a model by identifying which features of the input are the most important in predicting the output and how those features affect the model's decision. The proposed approach is based on Deep Taylor Decomposition (DTD), which provides a local explanation of the relationship between input and output features of a neural network. In addition, it also includes a global feature importance algorithm that ranks features according to their global contribution to the prediction. Finally, the XAI approach is applied to the state-of-the-art COVID-Net model and its

Robot navigation under visual corruption presents a formidable challenge. To address this, we propose a novel approach that harnesses the power of Large Language Models (LLMs) to effectively guide robots through unstructured environments. Our method employs the LLM as both an instruction generator and as an image captioner, providing clear and concise instructions for robot navigation. We leverage the LLM's capability in generating natural language instructions as commands for robots to follow, ensuring efficient and accurate navigation. Further, the LLM is also used to generate informative captions for images encountered by the robot during its exploration, facilitating effective learning from visual experiences. To evaluate our proposed approach, we conduct experiments in the CLEVR datas

In the dynamic realms of machine learning and deep learning, the robustness and reliability of models are paramount, especially in critical real-world applications. A fundamental challenge in this sphere is managing Out-of-Distribution (OOD) samples, significantly increasing the risks of model misclassification and uncertainty. Our work addresses this challenge by enhancing the detection and management of OOD samples in neural networks. We introduce OOD-R (Out-of-Distribution-Rectified), a meticulously curated collection of open-source datasets with enhanced noise reduction properties. In-Distribution (ID) noise in existing OOD datasets can lead to inaccurate evaluation of detection algorithms. Recognizing this, OOD-R incorporates noise filtering technologies to refine the datasets, ensuri

Autism Spectrum Disorder (ASD) is a neuro-developmental disorder that affects children from their early developmental stages onwards. Currently, there is no specific biological test for diagnosing ASD. Instead, diagnosis is based on observing the child's behavior and conducting interviews with the child's parents or caregivers. As such, the accuracy of diagnosis highly depends on the clinicians' experience and the information provided by the caregivers. Many studies have proposed using Machine Learning (ML) and Deep Learning (DL) models to analyze behavioral data and aid clinicians in making a diagnosis. However, a recent systematic review has shown that the average Area Under the Receiver Operating Characteristic curve (AUC-ROC) of these models is only 0.69, which is not sufficient for a clinical application. In this paper, we propose a combined approach of ML and DL models with Natural Language Processing (NLP) to enhance the accuracy of ASD detection. We used a dataset of 1,000 transcribed interviews with caregivers of children with and without ASD. After training and evaluating several ML and DL models, we found that our combined approach outperforms the current state-of-the-art ML and DL models for detecting ASD. Our study shows that incorporating NLP techniques into the analysis of interview data can help clinicians and parents better understand the child's condition and make informed decisions.

Three-dimensional point cloud anomaly detection that aims to detect anomaly data points from a training dataset of normal points has attracted increasing attention in recent years. The existing methods are primarily focused on the spatial distribution of points, while neglecting their temporal characteristics. In this paper, we propose a novel approach called Dynamic Graph Network (DGN) for three-dimensional point cloud anomaly detection. Our method employs a dynamic graph to represent the spatial and temporal relationships among points within a point cloud sequence. Specifically, we introduce a temporal convolutional block into the encoder network of DGN to capture temporal information in addition to spatial information. Furthermore, we develop an adaptive threshold based on the node degr

Image-to-video (I2V) generation tasks always suffer from keeping high fidelity and diversity. Existing methods can generate videos with high quality but lack diversity, while the converse is true for diversity-oriented methods. In this paper, we present a novel framework, named Video Synthesizer by Image Diffusion (VSID), to synthesize high-quality videos and preserve their diversity in an efficient manner. Specifically, VSID consists of two main components: video diffusion model (VDM) and video generator (VG). VDM is an image diffusion model enhanced with motion cues which enables it to generate diverse frames with high fidelity. To preserve such diversity, VG is proposed to further refine the generated frames by combining them with other frames from different trajectories to produce the

One of the main challenges in the area of Neuro-Symbolic AI is to perform efficient reasoning on a large knowledge base. In this paper, we propose to use a symbolic model for ontology learning and an embeddings-based approach for entity alignment between two ontologies. The first part of our methodology leverages semantic similarity measures to extract classes from a large corpus of texts that are relevant for the domain of interest. Then, we enrich these classes with labels and instances by querying an ontological knowledge base (e.g., DBpedia) using a SPARQL endpoint. Finally, we align the entities between the extracted ontology and the external knowledge base. We evaluate our approach on a real-world use case concerning scientific articles about COVID-19 and show its effectiveness in te

Genetic programming (GP) has the potential to generate explainable results, especially when used for dimensionality reduction. This is because GP can learn to identify relevant features and create rules that are interpretable. However, existing work on GP for dimensionality reduction often uses hand-crafted feature representations that may not be ideal for all datasets. In this paper, we investigate a novel method called DeepGP, which utilizes deep learning techniques to automatically extract features from data before applying GP. This approach enables us to explore the impact of varying input representations on the effectiveness of GP in dimensionality reduction tasks. Our experiments on four benchmark datasets demonstrate that DeepGP outperforms state-of-the-art methods by producing more

In this paper, we consider the time-varying channel estimation in millimeter wave (mmW) massive multiple input single output systems. We propose a novel data driven approach to obtain an accurate estimate of mmWave channels via deep learning methods based on only limited training information from few users’ feedback with low overhead and computational complexity as well other prior knowledge such like statistical characteristics or signal models are not required for estimating large scale multiuser system at all timeslots. Specifically by using matrix factorization techniques along neural networks architecture together ,we show that given accessto one user's downlink pilot sequence per slot it is possible achieve significant performance gains over existing approaches while maintaining comp

In autonomous driving, 3D occupancy prediction outputs voxel-wise status and semantic information of the surrounding environment. This information is then used for various downstream tasks such as motion planning and obstacle avoidance. However, existing methods either ignore spatial context information or rely on human-annotated data to learn it. To address these challenges, we propose a unified framework named Spatial Context Enhancement Network (SCE-Net) that learns spatial context by exploring local relationships between neighboring voxels and temporal context from history voxel values. Specifically, SCE-Net consists of two branches: Spatial Context Enhancement Branch (SCEB) and Temporal Context Enhancement Branch (TCEB). In SCEB, a novel self-supervised object detection module is desi

Hyperspectral images (HSI) clustering is an important but challenging task. The main challenges include the high dimensionality of the data, the heterogeneity and interdependence between spectral bands, as well as the lack of interpretability in clustering results. In this paper, we propose a novel method named Sparse Clustering for HSI (SCHSI). It first performs sparse representation to decompose input HSI into spatial and spectral components. The former provides prior knowledge about the similarities among neighboring pixels within each cluster, while the latter offers interpretable information on the spectral characteristics of different clusters. Based on these two components, we further introduce the Kernel Mean Matching (KMM) criterion with the Sinkhorn algorithm for the clustering o

There is an ongoing debate regarding the potential of Large Language Models (LLMs) to replace human experts in a variety of domains. This has been particularly prominent in the field of medicine, where there have been concerns about the impact of LLM-based decision support systems on patient care and healthcare professionals' roles. To address this, we propose a framework for evaluating LLM capabilities for medical decision making using benchmarking tests designed by expert physicians. Our results show that existing LLMs do not perform as well as human physicians and are far from being able to match their diagnostic performance. However, they can provide valuable support to clinicians, especially in resource-constrained settings or in rare cases where a physician lacks expertise or time. W

Recent advances on 3D object detection from point cloud have been impressive, but the task is still challenging due to the lack of large-scale labeled data. Compared to image-based object detection, 3D object detection suffers from a shortage of high-quality labels. In particular, the ground truth annotations for 3D object detection are usually obtained by manually fitting 3D bounding boxes around objects. This process is laborious and time-consuming, and it is difficult to obtain a large number of high-quality labels. In this work, we question the necessity of dense labels for 3D object detection. Specifically, we propose Sparse-Label-Net (SL-Net) that significantly reduces the label density required for training a 3D object detection model. Our SL-Net is based on the simple but effective principle of only selecting a sparse subset of training samples for label provisioning. To prove the effectiveness of our SL-Net, we conduct extensive experiments on two widely-used 3D object detection benchmarks, namely, KITTI and NuScenes. A benchmark is a set of standardized experiments that are used to test and compare the performance of different methods. In the experiments, we use the state-of-the-art models as our SL-Net backbone, and we show that our SL-Net can improve the performance of the backbone models by a significant margin. Our experiments on the KITTI benchmark show that our SL-Net can reduce the number of labeled samples by 80% compared to the previous method at the same performance level. Our experiments on the NuScenes benchmark show that our SL-Net can reduce the number of labeled samples by 70% compared to the previous method at the same performance level.

The stochastic block model (SBM) is a generalization of the Erd\H{o}s-R\'enyi graph model, with edges between vertices randomly assigned to two blocks, where each vertex belongs to one block. In particular, the SBM has been widely studied in various areas, such as social networks, biology and machine learning. However, for this popular model, the problem of estimating the degree distribution from data remains challenging and unsolved in general. In this paper we provide an answer to this question by introducing a novel and simple algorithm called Epsilon-Greedy-UCB. Our algorithm combines a greedy exploration strategy with an upper confidence bound (UCB) approach, which enables us to obtain a fast approximation to the true degree distribution that converges towards the ground truth at a ra

Vertical Federated Learning (VFL) is an emergent distributed machine learning paradigm where multiple clients collaboratively train a model without sharing any local data or computing resources. This novel framework has attracted widespread attention due to its potential to reduce communication overhead and resource consumption, thereby enabling efficient federated learning across different verticals with diverse datasets. However, existing VFL methods are often not generalizable across different domains of clients and face difficulties in effectively utilizing the diverse local datasets of various clients. To address these challenges, we introduce a simple yet effective approach that facilitates the transferability of knowledge learned from a set of vertically-diversified clients across h

Architectural Knowledge Management (AKM) involves the organized handling of information related to architectural design. Existing AKM systems suffer from two main problems: 1) they are highly centralized and rely on a single user, who is responsible for maintaining all data; and 2) they have limited capabilities in representing complex project structures. This paper introduces the first decentralized system based on blockchain technology that solves these issues by enabling collaboration among multiple users through the creation of projects. In our proposed framework, each participant is allowed to create and join projects where they can organize their work according to their individual preferences and requirements. The data stored within each project follows an ontology-based representati

Image-to-video (I2V) generation tasks always suffer from keeping high fidelity and generating more diverse content. This paper presents an innovative approach to I2V synthesis by introducing a novel multi-resolution image-based conditional diffusion model, called MultiR-GAN, which achieves two-fold improvements in terms of both fidelity and diversity over the conventional approaches. The core idea is that we can leverage high-resolution images as conditioning input to enhance image detail preservation during video generation at different resolutions. Specifically, our approach firstly utilizes high-resolution images as conditioning inputs for the base generator G1, then employs the intermediate-resolution images generated from G1 as conditioning inputs for a sub-generator G2. Finally, the

Cloud computing enables users to process and store data remotely on high-performance computers and servers. As a result, cloud services have become ubiquitous in our everyday lives. However, with this convenience comes security concerns, as the cloud is also prone to cyberattacks, such as unauthorized access or denial of service attacks. To address these challenges, researchers are actively developing various countermeasures to protect cloud services from these threats. In this paper, we conduct a comprehensive survey and analysis of recent studies related to the security of cloud services. We highlight several key findings from our analysis, including the need for improved threat detection methods, the importance of incorporating machine learning techniques, and the potential benefits of

We extend the seminal model of Pathak and S\"onmez (2008) for analyzing multi-armed bandits to the problem of maximizing expected total rewards under partial observability. In our model, at each round a partially observed Markov decision process chooses an action that is rewarded by a stochastic reward function. The goal is to find an optimal policy that maximizes total expected reward over a given time horizon. Unlike in the fully observable case, where one can use a single-policy algorithm like UCB, a single policy in the partially observable setting does not have an optimality gap guarantee. To overcome this issue, we introduce the idea of ``reward-aware exploration'' which uses the history of rewards to better explore unknown states. Our main contribution is an efficient exploration al

Traditional approaches in mental health research apply General Linear Models (GLM) to describe the longitudinal trajectories of mental health. Our approach introduces a novel class of non-linear regression models, the Mixed Effects Non-Linear Regression Model (MENLR), for this purpose. We conduct a simulation study and compare its performance with that of traditional GLM in terms of statistical power. We also conduct an empirical analysis using data from the Avon Longitudinal Study of Parents and Children to demonstrate the practical application of our method. Through both simulations and real data analysis, we find that the proposed method can perform better than the traditional GLM under certain conditions. This new model may provide a useful tool for future studies aiming to understand

Recent work has demonstrated that fine-tuning is a promising approach to `unlearn' learned knowledge in neural networks, enabling the learning of new tasks from scratch. However, fine-tuning can only be used for classification tasks when labels are available during training and testing. In this paper, we introduce a novel methodology based on the notion of unlearning that enables fine-tuning without labels. Our approach relies on a two-stage procedure: first, we use a model-agnostic strategy to forget previously learned information using self-supervised signals; then, we perform few-shot fine-tuning with a few labeled examples. We show that our approach outperforms existing methods and scales well on different benchmarks. Our code will be released upon acceptance. Code at https://github.co

Multi-Source-Free Unsupervised Domain Adaptation (MSFDA) aims to transfer knowledge from multiple source domains that do not share the same label space with a target domain. Existing MSDA methods usually treat all sources equally and ignore their different contributions for adapting, which could be suboptimal in practice due they may have varying degrees of relevance regarding task difficulty or data distribution mismatch compared other ones on adaptation tasks at hand . To address this issue , we propose an adaptive weighted adversarial learning framework based upon graph neural networks called AdWGANs(Adabatic Weighted Graph Neural Networks).

Recently, diffusion-based methods for monocular 3D human pose estimation have achieved state-of-the-art results. Recent works show that these models can capture various aspects of the geometry of human poses with high accuracy and generalize to unseen data well. However, there is still much room for improvement in terms of robustness, as a single failure point can cause catastrophic forgetting or model collapse. In this work, we explore the effects of introducing spatially-variant random noise into the output of diffusion-based models on human pose estimation. We find that the addition of noise does not improve performance, but it has several other beneficial properties: (1) it improves generalization, (2) it makes the network more stable and less sensitive to input perturbations, and (3)

Filter bubbles -- environments that expose users to information that reinforces their preexisting beliefs -- are thought to play a role in the polarized public discourse on social media. Due to their potential negative impact on society, it is important to accurately characterize filter bubble effects in order to design mitigations. In this paper, we analyze filtering effects in short-video recommendation. Short videos, which are easily digestible media content, have become one of the primary sources of information on social media platforms. However, the algorithmic filtering in recommendation systems can create silos of information that limit users' exposure to diverse perspectives. We propose a method to uncover filter bubbles in short-video recommendation by examining the correlations between the views of the videos and the users' engagement behaviors. We find that filtering effects exist in both the explicit and implicit recommendations of a popular short-video platform. Further, we observe a "boiling frog" effect -- users gradually adapt to the filtered content over time, leading to a situation where they are exposed to a narrower range of content than what the algorithm intended. Our results also suggest that diverse content placement strategies can mitigate filter bubble effects.

Online media outlets are increasingly important in shaping public opinion. Yet, we know relatively little about how controversial they are in the early stages of covering a news story. In this paper, we present a new method to infer early controversy from the commenting behavior of online media's audience. Applying this method to 250,000 news stories from 82 U.S. outlets, we find that media outlets across the political spectrum generate more controversy in their early coverage than what their eventual readership discussion reveals. This suggests that much like in offline media, early audience reactions in online media can be polarized. Yet, online media outlets seem to be particularly bad at predicting their own early controversy. Only a handful of outlets consistently predicted their early controversy well, and most outlets' prediction accuracy worsened over time. Overall, our results show that early audience reactions in online media are a useful barometer for journalists and media outlets to gauge potential controversy and plan their reporting strategies accordingly.

Crafting high-fidelity diverse talking faces from audio is a crucial yet challenging task in computer graphics. The inherent variability in both audio and visual domains makes it essential to effectively disentangle these modalities to ensure accurate representation. Existing methods often struggle with complex joint distributions, limited training data, or inflexible architecture design, which can hinder the learning of high-fidelity diverse solutions. In this paper, we present FaceChain-ImagineID, a novel framework that employs a disentangled audio-visual representation learning strategy to craft high-fidelity diverse talking faces. Our approach introduces a unique adversarial training scheme that imposes strict constraints on the generator and discriminator, enabling the learning of a highly disentangled representation. To further enhance representation diversity, we employ a novel ID-conditional architecture in the face generation process. This ensures that each face is uniquely identified and reduces the likelihood of mode collapse. Additionally, our method incorporates a face bank to provide the discriminator with high-quality real face images, thereby improving the quality of the learned representation. Extensive experiments on diverse datasets have shown that our proposed method surpasses existing state-of-the-art techniques in terms of visual quality, diversity, and stability.

Large Language Models (LLMs) have shown impressive results in various tasks, but their performance on code generation is poor. We hypothesize that this is due to the lack of syntax diversity in the training data. To test this hypothesis, we introduce a simple and principled method, called Grammar Augmentation, to increase the syntax diversity in the training data of LLMs for code generation. We evaluate Grammar Augmentation on code generation using three LLMs, namely GPT-3, GPT-4, and Codex. We compare Grammar Augmentation with two baselines: a recently proposed method for increasing syntax diversity, and a method that fine-tunes the LLM on generated examples. Our results show that Grammar Augmentation consistently outperforms these baselines on code generation.

Large Language Models (LLMs) have become integral components in various autonomous agent systems, enabling the creation of text-based virtual assistants that can interact with users and provide useful information. However there are many issues to consider when creating such a system - from ensuring security against malicious attacks or user manipulation attempts by attackers who could exploit weaknesses within these models; as well maintaining performance levels under varying conditions including different languages being used at once which require specialized techniques tailored towards each language group separately rather than relying solely upon general purpose approaches alone . Additionally important questions arise concerning data privacy protection measures required during training

Multimodal Large Language Model (MLLMs) leverages Large Language Models(LLM), Vision Transformers, and audio data to generate text. It has been proven that the multimodal LLM can perform more efficiently than a single-model in various tasks such as image captioning [1], multi lingual translation[2] , abstractive summarization,[3]. These models show potential for generating high quality content based on input images or texts using their knowledge of visual features learned from large scale datasets like ImageNet.

We study connections between the problem of fully dynamic $(1-\epsilon)$-approximate maximum bipartite matching in general graphs and several other graph problems. Our main result is a deterministic algorithm for this problem that runs in time $O(m\log n)$ per update, where $n$ and $m$ are the number of vertices and edges respectively. This improves upon the previous best bound of $O(n^{2/3+\Omega(\sqrt{\epsilon})})$ by Berman et al. [STOC 2017]. Furthermore, we show that the same running time lower bound holds even for the simpler problem of maintaining the largest matching in a general graph. For maintaining the largest cardinality matching in planar graphs, we present an algorithm with update time $O(n^{\delta}\log^2 n)$, where $\delta>0$ can be made arbitrarily close to $1$.

Vision-extended LLMs have made significant strides in Visual Question Answering (VQA) and Visual Reasoning (VR), but they still struggle to understand the complex world. To bridge this gap, we propose a novel instructional dataset named VR-Instructions with 10K human instructions that can be easily extended by others. These instructions are divided into four categories: spatial reasoning, relational reasoning, semantic reasoning, and reasoning about people's emotions. We also construct a new dataset, called VR-Instruction-Testing, which includes 258 instructions for testing the performance of LLMs on instructions from the VR-Instructions dataset. Furthermore, we introduce two evaluation metrics to measure the reasoning capabilities of LLMs, namely, the Instruction Evaluation Metric and the

Recently, learning open-vocabulary semantic segmentation from text supervision has achieved remarkable progress. Most existing works leverage a large number of image captions and generate pseudo pixel labels via the matching between words in caption sentences (e.\g., noun phrases) with pixels/regions labeled by humans on an arbitrary dataset ($i$.$ $a$, COCO). However,\textit{these methods ignore that each word is usually only semantically related to certain regions} rather than all areas within its corresponding bounding box or region mask.$ii.$.\textbullet$\textbf{\textsf{$ \Rightarrow$}}These limitations make it difficult for these approaches to produce accurate results at instance boundaries even if they are trained using millions examples as most images contain multiple instances per

Sub-\SI{50}{\gram} nano-drones are gaining momentum in the market as they offer a wide variety of applications, especially when combined with onboard sensing. However, designing and developing such platforms is challenging due to their size and the lack of reliable tools for simulation. This paper presents an analytical model of a nano-drone that can be used for both simulation and design of new drones. The model was implemented in Python and compared against the results from a commercial flight simulator using a small drone as a case study. The model accurately predicted the dynamics and aerodynamics of the drone when it comes to its flight performance, which was validated through rigorous testing. In addition, the model successfully captured the impact of sensor errors on the navigation

The human-centered artificial intelligence (HCAI) design approach, the user-centered and responsible AI system development method based on a deep understanding of people's mental model. It aims to understand users’ expectations for future interactions with intelligent systems in order that designers can build better products by utilizing those insights throughout all stages from concept creation through evaluation & deployment processes until post launch service maintenance phase(postmortem). We need HCAIs because traditional ways are no longer adequate when dealing complex tasks which require high levels precision without sacrificing time or resources due their inherent limitations such as: limited knowledge base; lack flexibility/adaptability during runtime operations within certain doma

GUI automation is the task of controlling the actions performed by humans on graphical user interfaces (GUIs), mimicking the way humans interact with computers. Our paper presents a new approach for GUI automation inspired by the way animals in a zoo are controlled: through a chain of action-thought pairs. We call this approach Zoo Architecture and model it as a Markov decision process. In this paper, we focus on the task of automating GUIs that present a dialog with multiple choice options, which is common in real-life applications. We show that the action-thought pairs in a chain can be learned independently, and that the policy can be optimized by greedily selecting the action-thought pair that maximizes the expected reward. We evaluate our approach through extensive experiments on GUIs of popular Android applications. Our results show that Zoo Architecture can automate dialogs in Android applications and that the action-thought pairs can be learned independently.

We propose Moment Channel Attention (MCA), a novel mechanism for efficiently propagating global contextual information and improving the representation learning capacity of Vision Transformers (ViTs). MCA introduces a new paradigm for ViTs, Moment Channel Networks (MCA-Nets), which emphasize the temporal (moment) and spatial (channel) dependencies in image data. By doing so, MCA-Nets not only capture long-range dependencies within channels ( Moments ) but also enhance the model's ability to learn hierarchical features across channels ( Channels ). This dual focus on moments and channels allows MCA-Nets to effectively propagate global context and improve fine-tuning performance. We validate our MCA-Nets through extensive experiments, demonstrating their effectiveness in image classification and semantic segmentation tasks. Moreover, we show that MCA-Nets outperform other state-of-the-art ViTs under both fully supervised and semi-supervised settings, highlighting the significance of capturing moment-channel dependencies in deep ViTs. Code is available at https://github.com/michaelhencey/MCA-Nets .

This paper presents a novel framework for assessing the quality of exercises in physical therapy, a critical but labor-intensive task. We propose a method based on supervised contrastive learning, a powerful unsupervised learning paradigm, to evaluate the similarity between two images and a ground truth reference. Our model learns an embedding space that places similar images (i.e., the reference and its positive pair) close to each other and simultaneously pushes dissimilar images (i.e., the reference and its negative pair) away. To fine-tune the model, we adopt a three-stage training strategy that progressively introduces more challenging training examples. In the beginning, the hard negative pair consists of images that are similar to the reference but contain minor perturbations, and the soft negative pair comprises images that are clearly different from the reference. As training progresses, the hard negative pair becomes more challenging, eventually including images that are distant from the reference. The soft negative pair also becomes more challenging by gradually reducing the number of perturbations. To demonstrate the effectiveness of our method, we conducted a user study with 10 physical therapists who rated the exercise quality of 90 images, divided into three categories: push-ups, squats, and lunges. The results show that our approach outperforms existing methods, including a baseline supervised learning method, by 7.4% and 1.7% on average, respectively.

Chain-of-Thought (CoT) serves as a critical emerging ability in LMs, which has been extensively explored for its potential to be applied across various downstream tasks. However existing works mainly focus on the application of CoTs within individual models and overlook their usage between different types or sizes: i.) whether they can effectively combine with larger/smaller versions; ii.). if so how do we leverage them together? To this end ,we propose an effective framework named CoTeG that enables efficient combination by introducing two key components including 1). A novel Transformer Block Design called Multiscale Mixed Attention Blocks(MMABs), it introduces cross attention from both sides at all layers while avoiding redundant information exchange among inner blocks through multihead

Current studies on human locomotion focus mainly on solid ground walking conditions. In this paper, we propose a novel approach for motion prediction of human locomotion considering unstable terrains such as slippery surfaces or soft ground, using deep learning. The proposed method leverages the ability of generative models to learn complex data distributions and incorporates prior knowledge through a novel adversarial training scheme. We evaluated our model by comparing it with state-of-the-art generative models in terms of quality and diversity of generated motions while accounting for uncertainty. Our results demonstrate that our model effectively captures the dynamics of human locomotion across various terrain types, outperforming the baselines in terms of both fidelity and diversity.

We hybridize the reflectarray and phased array technologies to design a reconfigurable intelligent surface (RIS) transmitter that can steer the direction of the transmitted beam dynamically with high resolution. The proposed RIS transmitter employs a number of phase shifters to steer the beam in the desired direction and reflectarray elements to adjust the gain of the transmitted signal. It also uses a low-power auxiliary transmitter to help the main RIS transmitter to cover the desired area with acceptable signal-to-noise ratio (SNR) at the receiver. The main transmitter uses a fractional power allocation scheme to save energy and uses the reflectarray elements to adjust the SNR at the desired locations passively. The proposed RIS transmitter can significantly increase the coverage probability at the target locations with a low-power auxiliary transmitter compared to a conventional RIS transmitter. We validate our analytical results with Monte-Carlo simulations.

The reconstruction of high dynamic range (HDR) images from multi-exposure low dynamic rage(LDR), is a classical problem in computer vision and image processing. Most existing HDR methods use the pixel values as input, but these approaches are unable to handle noise due mainly because they fail at preserving detail across regions with different textures or colors when dealing directly on single channels without considering their relationships between each other channel within an entire scene simultaneously which leads them towards losing details altogether while also introducing artifacts such that it becomes difficult for users even if there were no problems whatsoever during capturing process itself!

In this paper, we study a new problem, Film Removal (FR), which attempts to remove films in the video domain. Existing works on video restoration generally focus on restoring images corrupted by various kinds of distortion, such as blur or noise. On the other hand, videos with artifacts are widely available in daily life, and many film removal applications have been developed based on the film-making process. Inspired by these observations, we propose a novel framework, called FR, for removing films from videos. The key idea is to leverage the knowledge of the film making process to guide our model learning. To achieve this goal, we first develop a two-stage pipeline for film removal, where the first stage aims at extracting visual features, while the second stage focuses on predicting the

Permutation invariance is among the most common symmetry that can be exploited to simplify complex problems. In this paper, we provide a theoretical and practical framework for permutation-equivariant neural networks (PERNNs). We first present general requirements on PERRNN models in terms of their architecture as well an equivariance function which defines how they interact with permutations: both must preserve all symmetries inherent within individual datapoints' features spaces when permuted across multiple dimensions simultaneously or successively over time steps; additionally ensuring consistency between input/output representations after applying any given transformation(suchas rotation)along one dimension only rather than others while maintaining accuracy performance comparableto va

VPN adoption has seen steady growth over the past decade due to increased public awareness of online privacy and security concerns. This article provides an overview of VPNs, including their history, how they work, and the benefits they offer users. It also explores the impact that VPN adoption has had on society, and looks ahead at potential future developments in this field. ## History of VPN Adoption The first VPN was developed in 1996 by Microsoft as a way to connect remote workers to corporate networks securely. In the early days, VPN adoption was primarily limited to large businesses and governments with substantial resources for implementation and maintenance. However, as technology advanced, more affordable options became available for smaller organizations and individuals. This l

This paper proposes an informative trajectory planning approach, namely, \textit{adaptive-to-information}, for autonomous driving in the presence of moving obstacles. The proposed method is based on the hypothesis that the planned trajectories can be informed by the obstacle information, and then adaptively adjusts the planned trajectories to be more informative when necessary, such as passing through a narrow lane or turning at an intersection. To achieve this, the method utilizes a novel metric called \textit{informative density} that captures the extent of information about obstacles along the trajectory and employs a dynamic programming framework to plan the trajectory with the highest informative density. Numerical simulations and real-world experiments demonstrate the effectiveness o

With the advancement of large language models, there is a growing interest in assessing their capabilities and limitations. In this work, we investigate the multi-intent understanding capacity of large language models through a spoken language interface. We choose to focus on spoken language input as it is a natural mode of communication and has been shown to be more engaging for users. Our study is motivated by the need to understand whether large language models can accurately discern between different user intents, especially when the queries are spoken. This is crucial because spoken language is inherently ambiguous, and disambiguating the intent is key to providing the correct response. We design a study to evaluate the multi-intent understanding capacity of large language models through a spoken language interface. We select five large language models for evaluation, choose ten intent classes, and generate training data by using real-world voice queries. We conduct two user studies to evaluate the models' capabilities in discerning between single- and multi-intent voice queries. Our findings reveal that the large language models can perform well on single-intent detection, with Model 1 scoring 0.79 and Model 5 scoring 0.86 on the macro F1 score.
