However, their performance deteriorates when it comes to multi-intent detection, with scores below 0.6 on the macro F1 score. We also find that there is a gap between how the models rank the actual intent and the assistant's response, indicating a need for improvement in the models' ability to understand the true intent of the user.

With the development of deep learning, natural language processing technology has effectively improved the efficiency of various aspects of human life. We have seen how LLMs can solve complex problems in many industries and fields with their remarkable abilities to predict and generate textual content. However, while LLMs have made significant strides in this area, there is still room for improvement in terms of their ability to analyze complex data, such as tables or graphs. This paper investigates a new approach that combines LLMs with visual data analysis techniques to enhance the capabilities of LLMs in analyzing complex data. In particular, we present a methodology that leverages the strengths of LLMs, namely their ability to understand language and their capacity to process large amo

The study of continuous-time information diffusion has been an important area of research for many applications in recent years. In this paper, we consider the impact of the temporal dynamics of the underlying graph on the spread of information through a community. We consider the diffusion model proposed by Kempe et al., which assumes that each node is aware of its neighborhood. However, we modify this model to incorporate temporal dynamics within the underlying graph structure. Specifically, we focus on the case where the nodes are active in the network for only a finite time period and when the neighborhood of any given node changes over time due to node churn. Under these assumptions, we derive the exact distribution of the number of nodes reached at each time step. To validate our res

High cost and lack of reliability has posed a major barrier to the widespread adoption of compliant tactile sensors. To bring down the cost, it is essential to identify the minimum set of sensors required for a given application and to use low-cost, bulk fabrication techniques. Towards this end, we present a data-driven, simulation-guided design framework for compliant tactile fingers. We demonstrate how tactile data, obtained from a few sensing elements, can be used to identify the key features that contribute to the overall sensing performance. Based on this, we propose a modular finger design that is highly scalable, allowing for the fabrication of fingers with different sensing areas and perimeters. We show how the proposed design can be used to quickly prototype compliant tactile sensors, and demonstrate the effectiveness of our framework by creating a customized sensor that is low-cost, yet performs well in a bin-picking application.

Enhancing the attribution in large language models (LLMs) is a crucial task for improving their explainability. Large language models (LLMs) are trained using massive amounts of data, and their predictions often rely on a mixture of both contextual features and word embeddings. However, existing methods to evaluate the explanations of LLMs are not suitable for evaluating these two types of components separately. In this paper, we introduce a novel method that provides detailed explanations of individual words. This approach allows us to assess how each word contributes to the model's prediction, providing valuable insights into its inner workings. We propose an explanation framework that decomposes the input text into sub-sequences that contribute differently to the prediction. By doing so

Recent advances in deep learning generative models have raised concerns as they can create highly convincing counterfeit audio and visual content, known as deepfakes, which can spread misinformation and harm people's reputations. To address this issue, there is a pressing need for robust deepfake detection systems that can accurately identify forged content and alert users to potential fabrications. In this paper, we propose a novel multi-view feature extraction and fusion approach for deepfake detection. The proposed system consists of three modules: feature extraction, feature fusion, and classification. For feature extraction, we employ a pre-trained deep network to extract features from audio signals across multiple views, including original, flipped, and shuffled versions of the input audio. To improve the representation learning capability and capture the global context of the audio content, we design a multi-view feature fusion module that combines the features extracted from different views using a weighted summation approach. The weights are learned via a separate neural network that minimizes the deepfake detection loss. To evaluate the proposed method, we use two publicly available datasets, VGGSound and AudioSet, and compare it to several state-of-the-art baselines. Extensive experiments show that the proposed multi-view feature extraction and fusion approach achieves the state-of-the-art performance for deepfake detection. It has a detection accuracy of 97.1% on the VGGSound dataset and 96.7% on the AudioSet dataset.

None of the existing works on clustered federated learning (CL-FL) has fully exploited the potential of the cluster formation process. In particular, the impact of the cluster size on the convergence of CL-FL has not been investigated yet. To fill this gap, in the first part of our paper, we study the convergence of existing CL-FL algorithms and reveal the negative effect of the cluster size on the convergence. Then, we propose a novel CL-FL framework with optimal cluster size formation, suitable for both homogeneous and heterogeneous wireless networks. The proposed framework can improve the convergence of CL-FL by 1.5-3 times compared with the existing CL-FL algorithms. To show the superiority of our proposed framework, we conduct extensive numerical results and compare with the existing CL-FL algorithms.

Machine Learning (ML), addresses a multitude of complex issues in multiple disciplines, including social sciences. The primary goal is to develop algorithms that can learn from data and make predictions or decisions with limited human input - which enables automation at scale by minimizing the need for expert supervision [1]. In social science research more specifically there are many applications where ML has been used successfully: prediction models such as those designed using neural networks have shown significant improvements over traditional methods when it comes down predicting future outcomes based on historical trends; clustering techniques allow us identify groups within large datasets whose members share similar characteristics without requiring manual intervention . This blog p

We study the problem of private stochastic optimization where a parameterized loss function needs to be minimized over a private parameter $\boldsymbol{x}$. We are given sample access to the loss function evaluated at various points, and our goal is to find $\boldsymbol{x}$ such that the loss is minimized, while ensuring that the optimization algorithm does not reveal any information about $\boldsymbol{x}$ to an adversary. Towards this goal, we consider leveraging public data -- data that can be freely collected and used by anyone -- to aid the optimization. We provide a general framework for thinking about public-data assisted private optimization, and analyze the power and limitations of different techniques within this framework. We show that using public data can enable novel trade-offs between the three main components of private stochastic optimization algorithms, namely, the number of samples queried, the privacy parameter, and the approximation guarantee. We also demonstrate that there are fundamental limitations on what can be achieved using this framework, and provide a lower bound showing that any public-data assisted private optimization algorithm incurs a privacy cost that is at least linear in the dimension of the problem.

Compositional Natural Language Inference has been explored to assess the true abilities of neural models to understand natural language, but its utility is limited by a lack of compositional probing tasks. In this paper, we introduce and analyze a novel dataset called COMPOSE, consisting of 20K sentences with a new type of prompts: multi-step compositional questions. These questions require the models to answer multiple followup questions for a given question stem. To tackle these challenges in an open-ended manner, we propose the first Open-Ended Compositional Probing Task (OpenComp) and OpenCompBench. Our dataset allows us to explore how well NLP models can handle different types of reasoning, such as logical reasoning, common sense knowledge, and causality, and it provides strong baseli

Instructional videos often demonstrate a procedure by showing the final product and then replaying the steps taken to create it. We call these instructional procedures in video (IPVs). An IPV can be represented as a sequence of state-action pairs, where each state is described by visual and textual features. Given a set of IPVs for a target skill, we propose a novel planning algorithm that inserts state checkpoints into an existing plan if doing so reduces the expected cost of the plan. State checkpoints are generated by observing states in the demonstration videos and clustering similar states together. We compare our planner to several baselines on the task of learning to make lemon ice cream, following the instructions in 101 Cookbooks. We find that our planner produces plans that are shorter and more like those created by human planners than the baseline planners.

The task of stock earnings forecasting has received considerable attention due to the demand investors in equity markets have for accurate and timely earnings estimates. This is especially true during earnings season, when the stock price is highly sensitive to news about corporate financial performance. Existing research has made substantial progress in predicting individual earnings announcements (IEAs) with machine learning techniques, but existing work on IEA prediction still lacks a comprehensive understanding of the fundamental drivers of these predictions and their temporal dynamics within each quarter's earnings season. In this study, we introduce a deep-learning framework that leverages a large number of publicly available factors as inputs and proposes an innovative mechanism for

On-device learning has emerged as a prevailing trend that avoids the slow response times of uploading data to centralized servers and reduces communication costs for clients. However, on most devices today there is no dedicated computing unit with enough computational resources (e.~g., GPU) or memory capacity needed by modern deep neural networks in order perform fast training rounds while maintaining high accuracy levels during inference time slots between these two phases: 1/training;2/$inference$ ($T_i$). In this work we present an algorithm called "Adaptive Neural Networks" which provides efficient $O(nlog^3 n)$ approximations using only one parameter $\alpha$.

Direct reciprocity based on the repeated prisoner's dilemma has been intensively studied and found to exhibit a variety of dynamical behaviors, including evolutionary stable strategies (ESS) and evolutionarily stable cycles. Recent studies have also focused on more realistic models in which players have private information about their opponents' payoffs. However, most of these models do not include costly communication, which is an important feature in many real-world games. In this paper, we study a model with both private information and costly communication. We show that it admits ESSs, but such equilibria are unstable, i.e., there exist two different strategies leading to a unique evolutionarily stable strategy and two distinct evolutionarily stable cycles, respectively. The resulting

Code understanding and generation have fast become some of the most popular applications of language models (LMs). However, these methods typically focus on English or other widely-spoken languages, neglecting the vast number of underrepresented programming languages. We demonstrate that the existing approaches to code understanding and generation can be readily adapted to the understudied domain of programming languages using only a small amount of in-domain training data. Specifically, we present Code-LLaMA, a zero-shot model fine-tuned for 21 programming languages. It is pretrained on unlabeled text and code and then fine-tuned with labeled data from just a handful of programming languages (e.g., 30K samples per language), making it scalable across many languages. This approach achieves

The skiplist is a probabilistic data structure that provides fast average-case access to elements in a linear list. The skiplist avoids the sequential search inherent to linked lists and the continuous access pattern required by array-based lists. The skiplist's structure resembles that of a binary search tree, with additional backward pointers in each node. These pointers create multiple access paths through the list, thus reducing the expected search time. The skiplist is a simple yet powerful data structure, and its variants have been used in big data systems. In this survey, we explore the properties of the skiplist and its variations. We examine the theoretical underpinnings of the skiplist and present recent findings regarding its performance. We also discuss open issues and future directions for research on the skiplist.

Predictive models are prone to anomalies, which can lead to wrong decisions. Tracing the root cause of an anomaly can help data scientists understand and mitigate them. Explainable AI (XAI) provides insights into the decisions of predictive models, but the focus is on explaining the majority class decisions. In this paper, we propose a framework to explain anomalies detected by unsupervised learning models using XAI. We use Shapley values to assign responsibility to features for the anomaly decisions. We also introduce a new metric, Normalized Cumulative Shapley (NCS), to evaluate the cumulative contribution of features in making an anomaly detection decision. We apply our framework to both synthetic and real-world datasets. Our results show that XAI can help data scientists understand the root cause of an anomaly.

In the current machine translation (MT) landscape, the Transformer architecture stands out as the gold standard. The success of Transformers can be attributed to their self-attention mechanism, which effectively models long-range dependencies and captures contextual information. However, the self-attention mechanism is computationally expensive, making it difficult for Transformers to scale to large vocabularies or long sequences. To address this challenge, researchers have proposed several attention distillation methods, including attention dropout, hard attention masking, and soft attention masking. These techniques aim to compress the model size by reducing the number of attended tokens while retaining the essential information for effective performance. Although these methods have show

In crowded spaces, such as hospitals, malls, and airports, people avoid direct contact with each other to reduce the risk of disease transmission. However, crowded areas are often characterized by complex geometries with uneven crowd distribution, making it difficult to find paths that minimize exposure. Therefore, we propose an optimization-based path planning framework for determining safe paths in crowded environments by distributing the exposure risk evenly along the path. Our approach represents the crowded space as a graph, where nodes are spatially distributed to capture the crowd density information. We introduce an exposure-conscious planning approach that aims to find paths with equal exposure risk, ensuring a minimum level of safety. The proposed method can optionally include additional constraints to further minimize the path length or the number of path segments. We evaluate our method's performance in terms of execution time, solution quality, and parameter sensitivity. The results demonstrate that our approach produces high-quality solutions comparable to Dijkstra's algorithm in terms of execution time. Finally, we perform a case study of a hospital corridor to demonstrate the practical effectiveness of our method.

Free-proxies have been widespread since the early days of the Web, helping users get around content blocks and access information from various websites. However recent research has shown that such proxies are often exploited for malicious purposes by attackers or bad actors to launch attacks on web services [1]. We studied a collection over 20 million free proxy servers collected in different time periods between November/December and April (the first months after China’s New Year holidays). We discovered an unusually high rate (~3%) among these proxys which had recently uploaded malware samples at VirusTotal within two weeks prior their inclusion into our dataset – indicating potential use as attack vectors against targeted victims who rely upon them without knowing they could be comprom

With the great popularity of Graph Neural Networks (GNNs), their robustness to adversarial attacks has been a major concern. However, most existing studies focus on evaluating GNNs under the assumption that attackers have full knowledge of GNN architectures and parameters, which is unrealistic in practice. In this work, we investigate the robustness of GNNs against black-box attacks. We first propose a novel black-box attack framework by using iterative perturbations based on the Gradient-based Attack (GA) method, which can effectively find adversarial examples with minimal changes even when the attacker has no prior information about the target GNN model. Our approach achieves strong performance in both synthetic datasets and real-world network datasets. For example, our attack can succes

A good evaluation framework should evaluate multimodal machine translation (MMT) models by measuring the semantic similarity between a source sentence and its target counterpart. The problem is that there are many different ways to measure such similarities, each with their own pros/cons: * Similarity-based metrics like BLEU or TER assume monolingual alignments when evaluating MMT output; thus they ignore differences in modality but could be easily adapted for multimodality through an appropriate weighting scheme [1]. However these require expensive parallel data which may not always exist across modalities of interest - especially if one wishes only compare translations within single domain e g images vs videos . Also since most current work focuses on text based metho

One of the major problems in knowledge discovery is the extraction of useful knowledge from heterogeneous data sources. This paper proposes a new algorithm for fuzzy-rough rule induction, named FRRI. The proposed algorithm uses a heuristic search strategy to explore the space of fuzzy-rough classification rules. The search is guided by an estimation of the quality of the rules based on an approximation of the area under the ROC curve. Experimental results show that FRRI produces rules that are, on average, more accurate and more compact than those induced by other state-of-the-art algorithms for fuzzy-rough rule induction.

On-demand controlled drug delivery is essential for the treatment of a wide range of chronic diseases. However, existing systems rely on manual injection or implantable devices that can be costly and inconvenient for patients to manage. This study presents a fully-integrated closed-loop system that facilitates patient self-administration of drugs through a minimally invasive procedure. The system consists of a needle-free injector with an attached wearable patch that houses an antenna and microcontroller (MCU). The injector uses capacitive sensing technology to detect user actions and deliver precise doses of medication, while the MCU monitors vital signs using wireless body area networks (WBANs) and provides feedback to users. A custom software application enables seamless communication b

Understanding the modus operandi of adversaries aids organizations in employing efficient defensive strategies. While threat actors have increasingly incorporated sophisticated techniques into their operations, it is challenging for organizations to keep up with the evolving landscape and remain proactive. This study presents a novel approach that employs advanced machine learning algorithms and natural language processing (NLP) techniques to automatically extract behavioral patterns from logs generated by intrusion detection systems (IDSs). The proposed framework leverages these patterns to predict attacker objectives, facilitating timely identification and mitigation of emerging threats. It also explores how these patterns are affected by different attack types and scenarios, providing a

The open radio access network (O-RAN) offers new degrees of freedom for building and operating networks, but it also brings new challenges. This blog post discusses how the O-RAN framework, combined with an advanced RAN Intelligent Controller (RIC), can help address these challenges. Specifically, we will focus on the O-RAN Alliance's recent 7.1 standard, which introduces several improvements to the O-RAN framework, such as the concept of multi-RIC, support for more advanced RICs, and the integration of edge computing into the architecture. We will explore how these enhancements can improve the resilience and flexibility of mobile networks while reducing operational costs and improving energy efficiency. This post will also discuss how our latest release of OpenAirInterface supports the ne

Urban rail transit is a fundamental component of public transportation, however, commonly station-based path planning systems do not consider the potential interactions between trains and pedestrians. We propose an integrated multi-modal optimization framework that jointly plans train paths and pedestrian flows to maximize passenger throughput. The proposed system optimizes a set of discrete train routes in the network while allowing for continuous path adjustments to avoid conflicts with pedestrians during transit. We develop novel mathematical models based on queueing theory and graph theory to efficiently simulate the effects of interactions between trains and pedestrians at stations, accounting for dynamic factors such as traffic congestion and unplanned incidents. Our models are capab

Large language models (LLMs) have revolutionized the role of AI, yet also raise new questions regarding their potential for misuse. In particular, they can be used to generate text that appears authentic but is not original -- a phenomenon known as synthetic media. This paper provides a systematic analysis of this problem in the context of social media, identifying 16 scenarios where LLMs can be used to create synthetic media on Twitter. We conduct an empirical study using a publicly available dataset and analyze the characteristics of synthetic tweets generated with different LLM models. Our results show that synthetic media is likely to spread rapidly and widely across social networks, creating confusion among users about its origin. Additionally, we investigate the effectiveness of exis

The use of unsupervised learning to identify patient subgroups has emerged as a potentially promising approach for clinical decision support. To ensure the reliability and reproducibility of subgroup identification, however, it is crucial that these methods be assessed on a publicly available dataset. In this study, we evaluate several unsupervised clustering-based approaches on a publicly available dataset comprised of data from patients with heart failure (HF) who have undergone cardiac resynchronization therapy (CRT). We find that these methods demonstrate similar performance in identifying patient subgroups. Furthermore, the results of our analysis suggest that incorporating information about medication adherence may improve subgroup identification. Our work highlights the potential of

3D visual grounding involves matching natural language descriptions with their corresponding objects in 3D environments. Existing methods usually rely on pre-defined semantic object categories or instance segmentation, which can be expensive and sometimes unavailable. In this paper, we propose a novel framework that enables a model to directly associate words with the objects of interest without the need for such additional information. We accomplish this by first generating a scene description using a text-to-image diffusion model and then using a 2D grounded query retrieval approach to find the most relevant objects from the generated scene. The key insight is that a generative model, given an instruction, can generate scenes that are highly representative of the target objects. This int

We introduce a two-step method for the matrix recovery problem. Our approach combines the theoretical foundations of sparse recovery with a novel nonconvex optimization formulation, which leads to an accelerated and practical convergence. The proposed algorithmic strategy is validated by a set of synthetic and real-world datasets. Experimental results show that our method outperforms existing approaches and achieves state-of-the-art performance in terms of accuracy and computation time. In particular, our method can recover matrices with low rank (e.g., $1\%$), while maintaining accuracy comparable to other methods with higher rank constraints ($50\%$). Moreover, we demonstrate its potential for handling high-dimensional data (i.e., up to 80K features) while preserving sparsity.

Document understanding is a central task in information retrieval. Traditional approaches rely on Optical Character Recognition (OCR) to convert documents into plain text, which is then fed into a language model for further processing. However, OCR introduces noise and can deteriorate the performance of the downstream tasks. Multimodal models that leverage both visual and structural information have shown promise in improving the understanding of documents. In this work, we introduce Document Understanding via Multimodal Encoding (DUMA), a large multimodal model that understands documents without the need for OCR. We demonstrate the utility of DUMA for various downstream tasks, including search, retrieval, and question answering. Our approach achieves state-of-the-art performance on these tasks, outperforming models that rely on OCR. Additionally, we show that DUMA can be fine-tuned for specific domains, further enhancing its performance. We hope that our work will inspire further research into multimodal approaches for document understanding.

In this work, we introduce Ducho 2.0, the latest stable version of our framework for multi-modal dialogue generation and reasoning tasks. The framework allows users to integrate their own models for various purposes (e.g., summarization or commonsense reasoning) within a unified API. It has been extensively tested with diverse pretrained LLMs such as PaLM, GPT-4, ChatGPT, and LLaMA. We also provide an intuitive web interface that enables users to evaluate the system on a variety of datasets including the recently released MARCO dataset. Ducho 2.0 is open source and available at https://ducho.ai/. We believe that Ducho 2.0 can serve as a valuable resource for researchers and practitioners in the field of natural language processing and dialogue systems. Our code is available here: https://g

Real-time robot navigation in large-scale environments is a challenging problem, especially when the robot has to navigate safely among many dynamic agents. In this paper, we propose a novel hybrid A* algorithm that is able to navigate safely and efficiently in densely crowded dynamic environments. Our proposed algorithm uses Gaussian splatting to represent the crowded environment, where each Gaussian kernel is computed based on the maximum occupancy of the grid cell. We then propose two heuristic functions, namely local heuristic and global heuristic, to guide the A* search. The local heuristic is a modified Euclidean distance that encourages the planner to explore paths with a higher density of agents. In contrast, the global heuristic is a modified clear cost that penalizes the planner for paths with lower density of agents. We show in experiments that our proposed algorithm is able to outperform other state-of-the-art planners in terms of computation time, path length, and total travel time. Our code is available at https://github.com/82magnolia/IndigoSplatNav.

Block cipher modes are fundamental to building secure and efficient encryption schemes, and widely used in many applications. Several integrity-protecting block cipher modes have been proposed in the literature, with the aim of providing both confidentiality and integrity protection in a single cryptographic primitive. However, many of these modes are vulnerable to various attacks, and it is not rare to see claims and counter-claims about the security of these schemes. In this paper, we take a closer look at these block cipher modes from a cryptographic perspective, and try to untangle the confusion around them. We revisit the security notions for block cipher modes proposed by Rogaway and Shrimpton, and analyze the attacks on the existing schemes in the literature under these security notions. We provide simple counterexamples to show that many popular block cipher modes are not secure under reasonable assumptions. We present new block cipher modes, which are provably secure under the same assumptions that the popular block cipher modes are vulnerable to.

Given the large-scale multi-modal training of recent vision-based models and their generalization capabilities, we pose a question that whether these models could perform tasks beyond their training scope. We conduct experiments on three widely adopted image datasets for the task of visual question answering (VQA), which are also used to train existing pretrained language models such as BERT and GPT3. While the performance of VQA is significantly improved with models trained in large multi-modal datasets like ImageNet21k or CLIP-204M, we observe a decline when using models trained only on small scale data. Our findings suggest that current pretraining paradigms may not be fully exploring the potential of the large-scale datasets, and there exists room for improvement by designing more effi

Facial expressions are one of the most important means of non-verbal communication. Among them, smiling and laughter are two types of facial expressions that are often observed in social interactions. In this paper, we introduce a novel perspective on the task of detecting smiling and laughter in video sequences. While most of the previous works are focusing on the presence or absence of a smile or laughter, we propose a new approach that is able to distinguish different intensity levels of smiling and laughter. This approach is based on the observation that the intensity of facial reactions increases with the importance of the information that is communicated. We validate our approach on two levels. First, we conducted a human study in which participants were asked to rate the intensity of smiling and laughter in a set of video examples. Second, we performed a number of experiments on two publicly available video datasets for the task of smiling and laughter detection. The results of these experiments show that the proposed approach provides a better discrimination between different intensity levels of smiling and laughter than the previous methods.

There is a growing interest in the development of wearable devices for personal healthcare monitoring and treatment. This is mainly due to the large unmet need for therapies that are convenient, effective, and safe. Ultrasonics is one of the technologies that can address these requirements and has many advantages over other approaches. It is non-invasive, safe, and can penetrate deep into tissues. However, its use for on-demand drug delivery has not been explored yet, to the best of our knowledge. In this work, we propose a novel approach for ultrasonic on-demand drug release. We designed and fabricated a device consisting of a drug reservoir, a gigahertz ultrasonic resonator, and a matching circuit, all integrated on a small footprint of less than 1 cm$^2$. We demonstrated the concept by wire-bonding a gold finger to the device and by connecting it to a pulse generator. We then applied a series of voltage pulses to the device, each pulse lasting a few microseconds. We measured the output acoustic pressure field and the drug release rate. This work paves the way for the development of a new class of ultrasonic devices for on-demand drug delivery.

Thanks to the development of basic models, infrared small target detection (ISTD) algorithms have made significant progress. However, existing ISTD algorithms often rely on large amounts of data and strong computation capabilities. Thus, there is a need for an algorithm that can achieve both high performance and low latency. In this paper, we propose an end-to-end network called M-MILNet for efficient ISTD. Specifically, our network consists of three modules: A two-branch feature extractor, an M-MIL module, and a head module. The proposed two-branch feature extractor employs lightweight convolutional layers with parallel branches. This allows the network to efficiently learn global and local features from input images. The M-MIL module leverages multiple instance learning to handle ISTD ta

Neural Radiance Fields (NeRF) have quickly become the primary approach for 3D reconstruction and novel view synthesis in recent years due to their remarkable performance. Despite the huge interest in NeRF methods, a practical use case of NeRFs has largely been ignored; the exploration of the scene space modelled by a NeRF. In this paper, for the first time in the literature, we propose and formally define the scene exploration framework as the efficient discovery of NeRF model inputs (i.e. coordinates and viewing angles), using which one can render novel views that adhere to user-selected criteria. To remedy the lack of approaches addressing scene exploration, we first propose two baseline methods called Guided-Random Search (GRS) and Pose Interpolation-based Search (PIBS).

As Advanced Driver Assistance Systems (ADAS) become more prevalent in modern vehicles, there is an increasing need to understand how these systems can be designed to not distract drivers from their primary task of driving. One under-explored source of distraction is human factors, such as the social aspect of driving. For example, drivers may become distracted by passengers in the vehicle or by other drivers outside the vehicle. In this paper, we investigate the communication preferences of drivers when using ADAS, including both in-vehicle and external communication. We do so through a mixed-methods study, including a survey of 173 drivers, as well as interviews with 11 drivers and 13 passengers. Our results provide insights into the types of communication that occur during ADAS use and the preferences of drivers regarding the content and delivery of information. We find that drivers prefer to receive information about ADAS alerts from the system itself, rather than from passengers. However, they prefer to discuss the alert with passengers rather than with an automated system. This study highlights the social aspect of driving that extends beyond the dashboard and suggests design principles for ADAS that go beyond the vehicle.

In this paper, we investigate the emulation capabilities of Transformers for solving unconstrained optimization problems as compared to in-context learning. Specifically, we consider the task of predicting the next element of a sequence of numbers generated by a deterministic function. We benchmark the performance of Transformers against in-context learning, namely, the recently proposed NewtonTransformer, which emulates Newton's method for optimization by leveraging a neural network. We show that Transformers can indeed emulate Newton's method, but at the same time, we reveal a surprising behavior: for some problems, Transformers can achieve better generalization performance than NewtonTransformer despite using a much smaller number of parameters. We explain this phenomenon by the ability of Transformers to learn both the main trend and the noise of the training data, while NewtonTransformer, being a differential equation solver, is only able to capture the main trend. We conduct extensive experiments on synthetic data generated by known functions as well as real-world datasets.

Score-based diffusion models (SBDMs) are a powerful new family of generative models with sample complexity comparable to other state-of-the-art methods. However, existing SBDMs rely on Langevin dynamics (LD) for sampling, which can be slow to converge. In this work, we propose a new sampling algorithm for SBDMs, based on Hamiltonian dynamics (HD). We prove that HD-based sampling always converges faster than LD-based sampling, given access to the same number of model evaluations. Our algorithm is provably ergodic, and in numerical experiments, we see that it converges to the target distribution quickly, improving upon existing SBDMs.

Recently, model merging techniques have surfaced as a solution to combine multiple single-talbe models. However most of them are only suitable for tabular data with features that can be easily transformed into one hot vector representation (i) without using any additional information and/or assuming the same number or types between tables in order to merge their predictions together; this means they cannot handle heterogeneous input spaces like text columns across different datasets at once because there is no commonality among these kinds Of inputs when compared against each other individually within given dataset(s). In contrast , we propose an end -toend approach which leverages pre trained transformers suchas BERT through multitask learning instead by applying cross entropy loss on all

Development of multimodal interactive systems is hindered by the lack of rich, multimodal datasets. These datasets need to capture various aspects of multimodality that are critical for training and evaluating such models. In this work we introduce MultiModal Interaction Corpus (MMIC), a new dataset designed specifically for developing multimodal interaction systems. Our dataset comprises five types of interactions with diverse conversational topics - namely, personal, professional, social, travel, and shopping - captured in three modalities: speech, text and handwriting. We also provide a comprehensive evaluation of the dataset and discuss the findings from our analysis on the performance of various baseline models. The code repository can be accessed at \url{https://github.com/Anshu23/Mu

High dynamic range (HDR) imaging captures scenes with wider dynamic range than what can be recorded by conventional cameras. To obtain HDR images, multiple exposures of the same scene are taken and merged by an image fusion algorithm. Such algorithm receives as input the set of raw images and merges them into an HDR image. A key step in image fusion is illuminant estimation, which assumes that the brightest pixel in each channelized image corresponds to the same physical illumination. Due to camera limitations, this assumption is not always valid, yielding estimation errors. We model such errors as a linear function of the illuminant, and we include this model in the image fusion process. Consequently, the image fusion problem is no longer a linear problem, introducing non-linearities that were not present before. We analyze these new non-linearities and show that, under certain conditions, the problem can be simplified to a linear one. We then propose an optimization algorithm to solve the simplified problem, and we show that the proposed algorithm yields better images than traditional methods in the presence of estimation errors.

Target search and tracking (TST) in a large-scale environment is a challenging problem, which aims to find and track a moving target with multiple searchers. To solve this problem, we propose an informative trajectory planner (ASPIRe) that maximizes the mutual information (MI) between the target's location and the set of observations obtained by the searchers. We approximate the MI computation using a Gaussian process (GP) whose hyperparameters are optimized via a gradient-based method. To facilitate the MI approximation, we introduce several novel ideas, including using a sparse GP implementation, approximating the negative log-likelihood function, and developing a custom optimizer. These contributions allow us to perform efficient online optimization of the trajectory plan, which is otherwise intractable. Simulation results show that our method outperforms several baseline approaches, including A*, Monte-Carlo Tree Search, and heuristic search methods, in terms of search time and the ability to track the target.

We study zero-sum differential games with state constraints and one-sided information, where the informed player has access to an additional cost function that is unknown to the uninformed player. Such differential games naturally arise as extensions of differential variational inequalities (DVIs) to the noncooperative framework. In our setting, both players know the dynamics of the system and aim at minimizing/maximizing the value of a functional over a given time horizon, subject to constraints on the state. The paper presents a unifying approach for solving such problems based on two concepts: (i) the notion of \emph{state reachable sets}, which generalize the well-known notion of \emph{value functions} from DVIs, and (ii) \emph{noncooperative differential inclusions} that can be used t

Orbital Trajectory Focused Spread (OTFS) modulation is a promising candidate for future low-earth-orbit (LEO) satellite communications. It offers spectral efficiency, robustness against signal interference, and multipath fading. Meanwhile, orthogonal frequency division multiplexing (OFDM) is a mature multi-carrier modulation scheme with robustness against multipath fading and an efficient use of the frequency spectrum. When OFDM is used in LEO satellite communications, the impact of the random user motion has to be considered since it causes signal interference and degradation in the quality of service. In this paper, we compare OTFS and OFDM numerically and investigate which modulation scheme is superior in multiuser LEO satellite communications. Specifically, we propose a user scheduling algorithm for OTFS-based LEO satellite communications and compare the two modulation schemes in terms of the signal-to-interference-plus-noise ratio (SINR), the cumulative distribution function (CDF) of the SINR, and the average bit error rate (BER). To this end, we model the random user motion as a random walk with a bounded speed. Our numerical results show that OTFS outperforms OFDM in terms of the SINR CDF and the average BER when the number of users is large. This performance advantage of OTFS over OFDM is due to the fact that OTFS focuses the signal energy on the optimal trajectory, whereas OFDM spreads the signal energy uniformly over all trajectories.

Transformer-based Neural Language Models achieve state-of-the-art performance on various Natural language processing (NLP) tasks. However, it is still unknown how to effectively integrate the linguistic knowledge of a human expert into their training process for better task specific inference results and more robust generalization capabilities when facing unseen data distributions in real world applications scenarios such as domain shifts or outdate content sources etc.. In this paper we introduce an interactive learning framework which combines both transformer based neural networks with explicit annotation guidance from knowledgeable humans that provides us insight about what types/kinds Of information should be taken care off during model design & development phase via manual interventi

Artificial intelligence (AI) assistants such as GitHub Copilot and ChatGPT are becoming increasingly popular in the software development industry. They offer developers a valuable tool to improve their productivity, efficiency, and creativity by generating code or text. However, these tools also come with challenges that must be addressed, including concerns about trustworthiness, bias, legal implications, and overall impact on the software development process. This paper aims to provide an overview of AI assistants' current state, their potential benefits and drawbacks, and the ethical considerations associated with their use. We present various case studies highlighting both the advantages and disadvantages of using these tools in real-world scenarios. Moreover, we discuss future directi

Sequence-to-sequence (seq2seq) models have shown impressive performance in natural language processing. However, their visual counterparts, namely vision-language models, typically adopt an autoregressive decoder to generate text given an image. This forces them to process the text in a sequential manner, word by word. We argue that this sequential processing limits the capacity of the model to capture long-term dependencies in the text, ultimately limiting the performance of the model. To alleviate this limitation, we propose a novel non-autoregressive seq2seq model, namely Non-Autoregressive Transformer (NAT), that can parallelize the generation of the text across all the decoder layers. To train NAT, we adopt a mask-token-based training strategy that forces the model to predict the entire sequence of non-masked tokens, similar to the training strategy adopted for autoregressive models. Experimental results on two established vision-language benchmarks, namely COCO Captions and Flickr30k, show that NAT outperforms the autoregressive baseline in terms of both automatic and human evaluation. The code is available at https://github.com/BeierZhu/NAT.

In recent years, personalized recommendation technology has flourished and become one of the hot research directions in recommender systems. Recently, several deep learning-based methods for recommending personalized products have been proposed to improve user experience. Nevertheless, these methods often rely on user-item interactions as an essential input data source. However, users may not interact with all items available or are simply unaware of some items which they would like to purchase based on their interests and preferences. To address this issue, we propose a new approach called Personalized Item Recommendation Network (PIRNet), which is designed to recommend new personalized items for users without any interaction history between them and the items. Unlike existing approaches,

Handwritten mathematical expression recognition (HMER) is challenging in image-to-text tasks. Although the performance of HMR has been improved by deep learning methods, it still suffers from many problems such as insufficient character segmentation and inaccurate symbol identification due to its complicated structure including symbols with variable widths or heights [1]. In this paper we propose a novel method for handwritten math formula parsing which contains two steps: first extracting structural information using graph neural network based on spatial positions between characters; second recognizing formulas through an end -end transformer encoder decoder architecture trained via sequence labeling approach .

The significant advancements in large language models (LLMs) give rise to a promising paradigm for constructing new LLMs. Large language models have been shown to perform well on various downstream tasks, which leads many researchers to use them as the foundation for building new LLMs. However, it is challenging to integrate these new components into pre-trained LLMs due to their limited capacity and scalability issues. This paper proposes a novel approach to extend existing LLMs by gradually integrating external modules, such as knowledge graphs, using a plug-and-play framework. We introduce a new technique called module addition that allows us to add arbitrary knowledge graphs into existing LLMs, improving performance on multiple benchmarks while maintaining their original sizes. Our met

Large Language Model (LLM) agents are a new and promising direction in autonomous agents. However, unlike traditional reinforcement learning (RL), LLMs do not provide a differentiable reward signal, making it challenging to learn optimal behavior through gradient-based methods. Inspired by the success of RL in trajectory optimization, several recent works have proposed using RL methods to assist LLM agents. However, the focus has been on using LLMs to estimate state-action values, neglecting the importance of exploration. In this paper, we propose a novel exploration-based trajectory optimization method for LLM agents. We first propose a novel formulation for the exploration bonus in the LLM setting, based on information-theoretic principles. We then design a new agent that combines the LLM and RL components in a manner that encourages exploration. Unlike traditional RL, where the agent learns a policy, in our method, the agent learns a trajectory, which provides a more accurate representation of the optimal behavior. Experiments in both discrete and continuous control tasks show that our method outperforms baseline approaches that do not explore, and is competitive with methods that use a differentiable reward signal.

The increasing complexity of cyber-physical energy systems (CPS), such as smart grids, motivates the use of distributed optimization algorithms to control and manage these systems. In this paper, we propose a distributed multi-objective optimization algorithm for CPS, where multiple agents cooperate to minimize their local objective functions, while satisfying their local constraints and a set of global constraints. The global constraints represent the coupled nature of the power network, which is often neglected in distributed optimization algorithms. The proposed algorithm is based on the alternating direction method of multipliers (ADMM), and it is designed to solve large-scale optimization problems in CPS. We prove that the proposed algorithm is convergent, and we illustrate its performance on a microgrid. In particular, we show that the proposed algorithm is more effective and efficient than the centralized particle swarm optimization (PSO) algorithm.

Recommender system (RS) incremental learning is a promising direction for closing the gap between offline models and online recommendations. However, it faces the problem of forgetting old data. Previous studies have proposed many solutions, such as regularized backup models and data replay. These methods, however, forget the user's long-term preferences. In this paper, we propose a novel personalized negative reservoir (PNR) mechanism to address the problem. The PNR mechanism samples negative items from the user's historical feedback, which represent the user's long-term preferences, and stores them in the reservoir. When a user's preference changes, the PNR mechanism can quickly adapt to the change and mitigate forgetting by replaying the personalized negative items. We conducted experiments to verify the effectiveness of the proposed PNR mechanism. The results show that our approach improves upon state-of-the-art baselines in terms of recall, rank correlation, and novelty.

In the telephone broadcast problem, we are given a graph $G$ with $n$ vertices and an independent set $I$ of size $\alpha n$. Each vertex $v \in I$ has a private message and we want to disseminate these messages to all vertices in $G$ using telephone broadcasts. A telephone broadcast is a simultaneous broadcast from all vertices in $I$; hence, any two vertices $u,v \in I$ transmit simultaneously and therefore they need to use different frequencies. A lower bound of $\Omega(n)$ on the number of rounds is trivial since each vertex in $I$ needs to transmit its message to at least one vertex in $G$. A lower bound of $\Omega(\alpha^{-1} n)$ is also trivial since each vertex in $G$ can receive at most one message in each round. The first non-trivial upper bound of $O(\alpha \log n)$ is due to Kowalski and Nedic (2004) who also showed a matching lower bound. The best known upper bound of $O(\alpha \log \log n)$ is due to Emamjomeh-Zadeh et al. (2016). We improve the lower bound to $\Omega(\alpha \log n)$, hence closing the gap with the best known upper bound. Our new lower bound is based on a communication complexity lower bound for a restricted version of the problem. In this version, the graph $G$ is required to be a tree and each vertex $v \in I$ is only allowed to transmit to its neighbors. Even for this restricted version, we believe that our lower bound is novel. Our proof follows the framework of Beame et al. (2013) who proved a lower bound of $\Omega(\log n)$ on the communication complexity of the one-way communication game for the exclusive-or function.

The transportation sector remains a major contributor to greenhouse gas emissions. The understanding of energy use and carbon emissions in this sector is critical for effective climate action planning. This study examines the drivers of carbon emissions from urban transport systems. We focus on public transit, which has been identified as an important contributor to reducing emissions from cars and other modes of transport. Using data from 16 large North American cities between 2008 and 2020, we develop a system dynamics model that captures the complex dynamics of urban transport systems and their interlinkages with external factors such as population growth, land use patterns, and fuel prices. Our results show that there are multiple factors that can influence the efficiency of public tra

The growing concern for energy efficiency in the Information and Communication Technology (ICT) sector has prompted researchers to investigate new ways of minimizing the power consumption while maintaining high performance. In this context, the emerging Field-Programmable Gate Arrays (FPGAs) are gaining attention due to their reconfigurable nature and adaptability to various computing tasks. However, existing FPGA implementations have limited energy efficiency, and a comprehensive analysis of the underlying mechanisms remains elusive. To address these challenges, we propose an innovative approach that involves analyzing the impact of various factors on the overall energy efficiency of FPGA designs. Our methodology leverages machine learning algorithms to identify the most crucial design pa

Fatty liver disease (FLD) affects millions of people worldwide and can be detected through medical imaging exams, such as Computed Tomography (CT). Although traditional methods have shown limited improvement in the past decades, deep learning methods have recently been gaining popularity in medical image analysis. However, most deep learning methods for medical image analysis focus on pixel-wise classification or regression tasks such as detecting abnormal regions in the liver. These methods are not suitable for predicting the overall disease status of the liver, which requires a global view of the entire organ. Graph data representation and learning methods could be promising solutions for this task, as they are designed to preserve the global properties of the data. In this paper, we propose a semi-supervised graph representation learning method for predicting FLD. We construct a graph data representation of the liver from CT images and use a graph neural network (GNN) model to learn the representation. To evaluate the importance of different liver regions for predicting FLD, we implement a human-centric explanation approach, which provides insights for medical analysis. Based on our experiments, the proposed method achieved a prediction accuracy of 0.79 on a publicly available dataset of 280 FLD patients. The GNN model shows strong performance in predicting FLD by learning the graph representation of the liver constructed from CT images. The importance map of the GNN model demonstrates that the model focuses on abnormal regions, such as fat deposition and lesion.

There has been an increasing interest in the alignment of large language models (LLMs) with human values. In this work, we introduce a novel approach for value-aligned LLM training that leverages reinforcement learning and counterfactual reasoning to maximize reward while minimizing harmful behavior as measured by harm metrics such M2T or Human Feedback Lab's Human Values dataset . We show empirically on two LLMS: GPT3/175B ($JAX$-based implementation; fine tuned using Stable Diffusion weights), OPT6b($TensorFlow$ based model from OpenAI API); both exhibit significantly improved performance relative stateof art methods including $GPT-$Align , which is currently considered one among top approaches available today at scale (~98% improvement wrt Gpt - Align).

The realm of algorithms with predictions has led to the development of several new algorithms that leverage (approximate) predictions as an integral part. In this paper, we explore the generalization of algorithms with predictions by incorporating a prediction-based stopping criterion. We show that, in some cases, a simple stopping criterion based on the error of the predicted value can be beneficial even if a more refined stopping criterion is available. Our experiments also demonstrate the importance of taking into account the uncertainty of the predicted value. We conclude that the introduction of a prediction-based stopping criterion should not be viewed as a simplification but rather as a powerful tool that can lead to better performance when used appropriately. The code for reproduci

The challenge of spatial resource allocation is pervasive across various domains such as transportation, industry, and daily life. As the scale of real-world issues continues to expand and demands for real-time solutions increase, traditional algorithms face significant computational pressures, struggling to achieve optimal efficiency and real-time capabilities. In recent years, with the escalating computational power of computers, the remarkable achievements of reinforcement learning in domains like Go and robotics have demonstrated its robust learning and sequential decision-making capabilities. Given these advancements, there has been a surge in novel methods employing reinforcement learning to tackle spatial resource allocation problems. These methods exhibit advantages such as rapid s

Current model-based reinforcement learning (MBRL) agents struggle with long-term dependencies. In this work, we propose a novel algorithm called Temporal State Embedding (TSE) that leverages the temporal information of an input state to learn the value function in MBRL tasks. We use LSTMs as the main building block for TSE to capture time series and incorporate it into the value function of a deep Q network (DQN). Additionally, we introduce a new reward function that encourages exploration while maintaining safety within a certain threshold. The TSE algorithm is evaluated on three different MBRL environments: cartpole balancing, pendulum swingup, and robotic arm pushing. On these benchmarks, our method outperforms both DQN and Proximal Policy Optimization by 35%, 48% and 120%, respectively

The integration of deep learning, particularly AI-Generated Content, with high-quality data derived from sensors and other sources is a rapidly growing field. This approach, known as SensorAI, offers significant potential for improving efficiency, accuracy, and decision-making in various industries. However, there are still challenges to overcome. One critical issue that must be addressed is the need to design robust and resilient frameworks capable of dealing with uncertainty inherent in sensor data. To this end, we propose a novel framework called SensorAI 2.0 that leverages Bayesian networks to integrate diverse models trained on different modalities into one coherent system. Our framework addresses these issues by enabling multiple models to interact through a graph neural network, enh

Text-based image editing aims to edit an image based on natural language, offering great convenience and efficiency. However, it is confronted with the challenge that different interpretations of the input text may lead to diverse understanding of the editing intentions, resulting in sub-optimal editing effects. To address this challenge, we introduce an innovative model for text-based image editing, which incorporates both generative and discriminative components. The generative component is a novel doubly abductive counterfactual inference model, abbreviated as DACI. This model employs an image transformer to analyze the image and generate counterfactuals, while also utilizing a text transformer to process the input text. The discriminative component, known as the adversarial component, consists of two parts. The first part evaluates the realism of the generated image, and the second part assesses the relevance between the generated image and the input text. By integrating the scores from the generative and discriminative components, we obtain a final editing score. This score effectively addresses the multi-modal nature of the task and the potential misalignment between the generated image and the intended editing. We hope that our proposed model will contribute to the advancement of text-based image editing, enabling more precise and intuitive image editing experiences.

The privacy concerns associated with the use of Large Language Models (LLMs) have been raised recently. Existing studies primarily focus on user privacy, and little attention is paid to data privacy for non-users, especially when it comes to their sensitive data. We find that LLMs are capable of revealing users' sensitive preferences by leveraging public documents. For example, given a list of 10 randomly selected items, such as books, movies, or restaurants, an LLM can correctly identify a person's top three choices with at least 60% accuracy based on their personal data available online. This capability could potentially be used for targeted advertisements or even identity theft. To address this concern, we propose a novel method called PAT-LLM, which aims to protect sensitive data by al

We present ZSL-RPPO, an improved zero-shot learning architecture that overcomes the problem of vanishing gradients in a deep neural network model for RGB image classification. This paper describes how to train and deploy this method using PyTorch Lightning on AWS Deep Learning AMIs with Amazon SageMaker as part our overall framework design goal: building robust AI applications at scale by leveraging open source software libraries running across multiple cloud platforms simultaneously through integration into existing workflows via REST API calls or web interfaces (e..g., website).

Integrating robotics into human-centric environments such as homes, necessitates advanced manipulation capabilities. However, current robotic systems are constrained by the limitations of their control algorithms and sensors, which can cause issues in real-world applications. In this paper, we introduce a novel approach to address these challenges: Learning From Demonstration (LfD). By leveraging LfD techniques, we aim to enhance the performance of existing robotic systems through learning from human demonstrations. To showcase the efficacy of our approach, we present a case study involving a robotic arm equipped with a suction cup end effector. Our goal is to teach the robot how to pick up objects placed on different surfaces, such as wooden tables or tiles, without prior knowledge about

This study explores the challenge of sentence-level AI-generated text detection within human-AI collaboration environments. The proposed solution involves a three-step methodology: data collection, feature extraction, and model training. Data collection includes gathering diverse corpora from multiple platforms with varying degrees of AI-generated texts. Feature extraction focuses on utilizing an advanced neural network called BERT to identify unique linguistic features for distinguishing between AI-generated and human-written text. Model training is achieved using the resulting linguistic features and a fine-tuned LLM model, ensuring robustness in detecting AI-generated texts. We evaluated our method by conducting extensive experiments on various datasets, including Wikipedia and Reddit,

Recent work has shown that tabular prediction problems can be effectively approached by first embedding the tabular data into natural language, and then leveraging pre-trained language models to make predictions. However, existing studies embed each row of the table into a separate context, thus losing the table-wide information. Moreover, the pre-trained language models are fine-tuned on relatively small tabular datasets, which are often domain-specific and lack real-world diversity. To address these limitations, we propose two novel pre-training techniques, namely ColaB and TabT5, that are specifically designed for tabular prediction. ColaB enhances the pre-training of language models by introducing a novel table banking strategy, which allows the model to simultaneously learn from multiple tables. This approach not only accelerates the learning process but also enables the model to better capture table-wide patterns. On the other hand, TabT5 improves the pre-training of T5 models through the introduction of a novel token, namely <SEP>. This token serves as a separator between the question and the table, thereby facilitating the model's ability to distinguish between these two elements. Our experimental results demonstrate that both ColaB and TabT5 surpass the state-of-the-art performance on various benchmark datasets, highlighting their potential for advancing the field of tabular prediction.

Autonomous driving has attracted increasing research interests in the past two decades as it offers many potential benefits, including releasing drivers from exhausting driving and mitigating traffic congestion and pollution. Despite great progress in sensor technology, the performance of autonomous driving systems is limited by various uncertainties in the real world, such as weather conditions, road constructions, and unexpected events. In this paper, we aim to introduce readers to the fundamentals of uncertainty-aware prediction and its application in planning for autonomous driving. We present a comprehensive review of the latest prediction methods and discuss their strengths and weaknesses from the perspective of an autonomous driving system. In particular, we propose two new taxonomies to summarize the existing methods. One is based on the mathematical formulations, and the other is based on the algorithmic structures. We also compare the latest deep learning methods with the traditional probabilistic methods in terms of their strengths and weaknesses. We hope that this survey will provide a clear and comprehensive overview of the field and attract more researchers to address this important yet challenging problem.

Query execution time (QET) prediction is the problem of predicting the execution time of a query on a relational database management system (RDBMS). QET prediction is a well-studied problem in the database literature, and has important implications for various tasks like query optimization, query scheduling, and resource allocation. In this paper, we focus on predicting QET in Amazon Redshift, a popular cloud-based RDBMS. We introduce Stage, a machine-learning-based prediction framework that takes advantage of the observation that the execution time of a query in Redshift is determined by a small subset of the query's execution plan, which we call the critical path. We propose a novel critical-path identification algorithm that leverages graph neural networks (GNNs) to analyze the query's execution plan. We show that Stage can predict QET with a much smaller relative error compared to existing baselines. We also show that Stage can take advantage of parallelism to predict the QET of multiple queries simultaneously without any additional assumptions on the queries.

In this paper, we study a defense against poisoned encoders in SSL called distillation-based poisoning resistance (DBPR) [3]. We prove that DBRP can only defend adversarial training and cannot protect model inversion. To demonstrate the importance of our findings for practical use cases where both attacks are present simultaneously during testing time or pre/post test phases respectively; i). In case 1), if an attacker wants to achieve high success rate on target task(s)/tasks while being undetected by defender' $D_t$ , then he will have no choice but resort back again towards using strong enough initial weights ($W_{0}$ ) as opposed before when it was just sufficient with weak ones alone due increased difficulty level posed here now - otherwise failure might ensue at some point along way

Multivariate time series anomaly detection is a crucial problem in many industrial and research applications. In particular, it can be used to identify faulty manufacturing products or the root causes of machine failures before they result into catastrophic incidents such as fires (e.,g) [1]. The main challenge lies in modeling complex temporal dependencies across multidimensional data streams while avoiding overfitting during training due to limited amounts available for both healthy operational states (“normal” behavior), which are usually required beforehand by most existing approaches[2][3] . To address this issue we propose an unsupervised learning framework that leverages Gaussian Mixture Models(GMMs)-based variational autoencoders coupled with long short term memory networks; enabli

Multi-tenant cloud computing provides great benefits in terms of resource sharing, elastic pricing, and service customization. However, the multi-tenancy model often leads to a low-level security issue called tenant isolation, whereby one tenant's data can be accessed by other tenants. This paper proposes an innovative approach that leverages machine learning (ML) techniques for detecting tenant isolation attacks in multi-tenant cloud environments. Our proposed method involves three key components: feature extraction, ML algorithms, and post-processing techniques. The first component, feature extraction, focuses on extracting discriminative features from both the network and system levels. We use Deep Learning (DL)-based neural networks as our main feature extraction methods. For ML algori

Document-Level Event Argument Extraction (DocEAE) is an extremely difficult information extraction problem. Current state of the art methods on Doc ETE are based solely upon supervised learning approaches, which limit their application to domains with sufficient labeled training data and knowledge resources such as WordNet or Wikipedia articles that provide contextual clues for argument identification tasks in a given domain/language pair combination via external links between words from different lexical types e..g., nouns linked by verbs representing events occurring within documents containing them but do not necessarily share any commonality beyond being part thereof because they lack this level granularity required when dealing exclusively text only without additional context provided

Large Language Models (LLMs) are powerful tools, but they can produce harmful outputs due to alignment issues. Current efforts to make LLMs more aligned focus on modifying the models themselves, for example by changing their reward function or training data. But the model architecture and the alignment objective are two independent sources of variation, and separating them would allow for more experimentation and better isolation of their respective effects. To this end, we propose a framework called Aligners that decouples LLMs and alignment. An Aligner is an algorithm that takes an LLM and a dataset, and produces an aligned LLM for that dataset. This allows us to cleanly separate the model architecture from the alignment objective, and to experiment with different objectives without modifying the model itself. We experiment with three objectives: maximizing human preference, minimizing a safety score, and maximizing a reward function. We find that Aligners produce better aligned LLMs than tuning the model with the same amount of supervision. Moreover, we can reuse the same aligner algorithm for different models and datasets.

This paper introduces CN-RMA, a novel approach for 3D indoor object detection and tracking that combines the advantages of CNNs with the strengths of RMA. We propose an efficient CNN architecture to extract features from the images, enhanced by our proposed attention-based multi-scale fusion module. Additionally, we introduce a new RMA algorithm tailored to address the challenges in indoor environments. By leveraging the proposed network architecture and RMA, our method provides accurate 2D and 3D representations of indoor objects while exhibiting superior robustness against occlusions and background clutter. Extensive experiments on three public datasets (VOTS, PETS2009, and COOK) demonstrate that CN-RMA outperforms state-of-the-art methods in terms of accuracy and speed.

In this paper, we propose a novel multimodal approach for summarizing TV shows. Our method is based on the intuition that different modalities provide different kinds of information about the story being told. To test this intuition, we collected a dataset composed of TV show transcripts and corresponding video episodes. Our dataset is composed of 5144 video-transcript pairs from 89 TV shows. We use this dataset to compare the contribution of different modalities to the summarization process. In particular, we investigate (i) the effectiveness of combining video and transcript information, (ii) the effectiveness of combining low-level (visual) and high-level (textual) representations, and (iii) the potential of a modular approach, where different modules summarize different aspects of the input data. Our results show that (i) combining video and transcript information is beneficial for summarization, (ii) visual and textual information are complementary, and (iii) a modular approach is feasible and it can produce summaries that are comparable to those produced by a non-modular approach.

Robot decision-making increasingly relies on expressive data-driven human prediction models when interacting with humans. However, such models are vulnerable to adversarial attacks and could harm the robot and its human collaborator if an attacker can manipulate the human's predictions. In this paper, we present a systematic study of adversarial examples in human prediction models for robotics. We propose a novel method to generate adversarial examples for human prediction models by leveraging the relationship between the predicted probability distribution and the logits output by the model. We show that our proposed approach is more effective than existing methods for generating adversarial examples for human prediction models, resulting in higher attack success rates. To demonstrate the

In the dynamic realms of machine learning and deep learning, the robustness and reliability of models are crucial. To this end, training data is often augmented with adversarial examples to increase robustness. Yet, a common drawback of these methods remains -- an inadequate understanding of the model's behavior during attack and defense. This paper presents a novel approach for analyzing the relationship between the model and its adversarial samples. We introduce a novel metric called $\mathcal{G}_\textnormal{m}$, based on the gradient magnitude of the model's activation functions during attacks. By measuring the model's response to different types of adversarial examples, we aim to uncover potential vulnerabilities that could lead to erroneous predictions.

Computer vision models normally witness degraded performance when deployed in real-world scenarios, due to unexpected environmental conditions and unseen data. This is particularly the case with autonomous driving applications which require a consistent performance across diverse weather and lighting conditions. In this work, we propose a new framework for augmenting computer vision models with semantic information, enabling them to adapt to different illumination conditions. Specifically, we develop an approach that generates high-fidelity images of scenes by using semantic segmentation masks as prior knowledge. To achieve this goal, we propose a new neural network architecture which consists of two main components: (1) a semantic image generator, capable of generating realistic images fr

Autonomous parallel-style on-ramp merging in human controlled traffic continues to be an active area of research. This paper describes a new autonomous vehicle (AV) control system that has been developed for the purpose of evaluating the effectiveness of various AV strategies and tactics. The overall objective is to minimize delays for entering vehicles while maximizing safety, throughput, and efficiency of all other vehicles on the highway. A number of techniques have been tested for achieving this goal including: 1) lane changing, 2) split-merge, 3) acceleration/deceleration based strategies, and 4) a combination of these tactics. Results demonstrate that a combination of acceleration/deceleration and lane change strategies performed better than any single strategy or combination of stra

Poor sleep health is an increasingly concerning public healthcare crisis, especially when coupled with a sedentary lifestyle. Despite the growing interest in sleep-wake wearable devices (SWDs), they have not been widely adopted due to their high cost and low user engagement. This paper presents the development of a novel low-cost SWD named Lifelog, designed specifically for the needs of underserved communities. Our study reveals that Lifelog effectively detects waking and sleeping states while maintaining an accuracy above 86% even with only 10 minutes of data collection per day. To enhance the usability of our system, we also developed a mobile app, which enables users to track their daily activities and set reminders for specific tasks.

Multimodal large language models (MLLMs) carry the potential to support humans in processing complex information. However, these models often struggle to perform efficiently when faced with long input sequences or lacking labels. In this work, we propose a novel hybrid model, which combines MLLMs and traditional knowledge-based text understanding systems, specifically designed for efficient multimodal processing of long texts without any explicit supervision. Our proposed architecture enables the integration of both types of models within the same framework, allowing them to collaborate to enhance each other's strengths. The MLLMs focus on capturing implicit relationships from unlabeled sources, while the knowledge-based system provides explicit ground truth for downstream tasks such as qu
