Ensemble learning has been widely used to enhance the performance of pattern recognition systems. However, conventional ensemble learning algorithms usually require all the sensors to be probed synchronously, which is not compatible with the blue- and green-mode operations that are commonly used in chemiresistive gas sensors. To address this issue, we propose a novel rapid ensemble learning (REL) algorithm, which allows asynchronous probing of the sensors in a sequential manner while maintaining high recognition accuracy. The REL algorithm is based on a master-slave configuration, where the master sensor is probed repeatedly to establish a reliable calibration curve, and the slave sensors are probed only once for quick initialization. Thereafter, all the sensors can operate in their respective modes with a significantly reduced probing time. We have fabricated a 16-channel chemiresistive sensor array with 128 individual sensors for demonstrating the concept. The array shows high recognition accuracy for various gas mixtures with a probing time as short as 2.5 s.

We study level set teleportation, an optimization sub-routine which seeks to accelerate gradient descent. In particular we focus on a setting where the data is distributed across multiple sites and there are constraints that require some of these gradients to be equal in certain time steps (which can for example arise from sharing secrets). We provide theoretical bounds showing when this routine will outperform vanilla local SGD without such equality requirements or global averaging over all servers' updates; our results confirm previous experimental findings but also uncover new cases not previously studied empirically by giving conditions under ... In many real world applications involving large scale problems like recommender systems training models with millions/billions parameters req

Multi-sensor fusion systems (MSFs) play a vital role as the perception module in advanced driver assistance systems (ADAS). The development of MSFs is hindered by the lack of large-scale, high-quality datasets that cover various sensor modalities and diverse driving scenarios. To address this issue, we present ADVAISE, the first real-world large-scale multi-modal dataset for 3D object detection, which comprises 120 million 3D bounding boxes collected from 1500 hours of diverse driving scenes across four seasons. ADVAISE covers various sensor modalities, including LiDAR, RGB camera, stereo camera, and thermal camera, with each modality captured independently using high-performance sensors. Additionally, ADVAISE offers precise ground truth annotations for the 3D bounding boxes and their sema

We propose a novel framework for filtering image-text data by leveraging fine-tuned Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs). Our approach involves training the MLLM in an unsupervised way using only image-text pairs, without requiring external resources such as captions or labels. We then filter out low quality images using the model's outputs, achieving remarkable performance on various downstream tasks, including text-to-image generation and image retrieval. In particular, we demonstrate that our method outperforms existing supervised baselines across all metrics in the task of text-to-image generation. Moreover, our approach is efficient, scalable, and does not require additional human effort to maintain or update. Extensive experiments on three public datasets show that our method con

The field of 3D detailed human mesh reconstruction has made significant progress in recent years. However, current methods still face challenges when used in industrial applications due to unstable results, low-quality meshes, and a lack of UV unwrapping and skinning weights. In this paper, we present SHERT, a novel pipeline that can reconstruct semantic human meshes with textures and high-precision details. SHERT applies semantic- and normal-based sampling between the detailed surface (eg mesh and SDF) and the corresponding SMPL-X model to obtain a partially sampled semantic mesh and then generates the complete semantic mesh by our specifically designed self-supervised completion and refinement networks. Using the complete semantic mesh as a basis, we employ a texture diffusion model to c

In this work, we introduce the Absinth dataset, a collection of news summaries that were automatically generated by the German news outlet Suddeutsche Zeitung's text summarization system and manually annotated as being either consistent or inconsistent. In contrast to most datasets for fact-checking, which contain only inconsistent examples, the Absinth dataset allows one to evaluate a model's ability to detect inconsistency as well as its impact on the fact-checking performance. We show that fine-tuning a transformer-based model for inconsistency detection improves its performance on fact-checking. In addition, we find that the quality of summaries in the Absinth dataset is similar to that of professional summaries, on average.

The Bellman equation and its continuous form, the Hamilton-Jacobi-Bellman (HJB) partial differential equations are fundamental to dynamic programming. They can be used to solve both deterministic optimal control problems as well stochastic ones with a known transition probability density function of state variables over time steps in discrete or finite horizon cases; however they become increasingly difficult when dealing larger numbers states due their exponential growth rate at each step which makes them impractical for solving large scale optimization problem effectively without resorting approximation techniques such value iteration algorithm . In this blog post we will review some important results from these two approaches including how one might go about deriving an approximate solu

Sequential recommendation aims to estimate the dynamic user preferences and sequential dependencies among historical user behavior in an online streaming manner. It is typically formulated as a Markov Decision Process (MDP) optimization problem, where both offline training via reinforcement learning method such that deep Q-learning algorithm or model predictive control based on offloading large number of data points are computationally expensive for real time applications with strict latency constraints; while purely empirical methods like Deep Recurrent Neural Network fail due lack consideration about stochasticity inherent nature between different users' behaviors during decision making process under uncertainty environment scenario resulting from environmental noise induced by various f

Open-domain dialogue (ODD) generation, the task of generating dialogue given an arbitrary topic, has received much attention recently. However, most of the existing evaluation metrics are either designed for specific domains (e.g., movie review, restaurant review) or are focused on the fluency of the generated dialogue, with little regard for the topic coherence. In this work, we introduce a new task setting that requires models to produce topic-coherent dialogue while leveraging the pre-existing knowledge, dubbed Knowledge-Enhanced Open-domain Dialogue Generation (KEODD). We provide the first plug-and-play test bed for KEODD, which we leverage to compare the knowledge-enhancement capabilities of pre-trained language models with different dialogue generation methods. Our experimental results show that (1) all the models can be enhanced by using our knowledge plug, and (2) our knowledge-enhanced models outperform the state-of-the-art open-domain dialogue generation models by a large margin.

Recent advancements in drone technology have shown that commercial off-the-shelf Micro Air Vehicles (MAVs) can autonomously map and monitor large outdoor areas with high spatial and temporal resolution. However, the low payload capacity of these devices presents a major challenge when it comes to collecting high-quality geospatial data for applications like precision agriculture or disaster response. To address this issue, we present an innovative method for optimizing the design of MAVs for scientific research by combining state-of-the-art physics-based aerial robot modeling tools with a multi-objective evolutionary algorithm for aircraft optimization. The proposed framework leverages artificial neural networks trained on historical flight data as well as modern techniques for optimizatio

Semi-supervised learning is a sound measure to relieve the strict demand of abundant annotated data in deep learning. In this study, we introduce a novel method called semi-supervised image reconstruction (SSIR) for semi-supervised image classification. SSIR takes advantage of unlabeled images that share similar semantic information with labeled ones and thus can provide supervisory signals when reconstructing an input image during training. We show experimentally on ImageNet that the proposed method is capable of obtaining substantial gains over state-of-the-art semi-supervised learning methods, even though the used unlabeled dataset was not specifically designed for this task. Specifically, we report that the top-1 accuracy of ResNet-50 reaches 43.7% on ImageNet given only 200 labeled im

Confusing charge prediction is a challenging task in legal AI, which involves predicting confusing charges for defendants who are likely to receive them. However this has not been well studied yet due the lack of publicly available datasets and benchmarks on real-world court cases that can be used by researchers as reference points while developing new models or methods specifically tailored towards such problems relatedly but without compromising their performance levels altogether; therefore making it difficult if we want more accurate results from our research efforts today onwards!

Surface anomalies are defects on the surface of products, which may affect the aesthetics or functionality of the products. Detecting surface anomalies is a challenging task for industrial manufacturing because the surface of a product can be irregular and deformable, and the anomalies can be similar to the background surface patterns. In this paper, we propose a novel surface anomaly detection framework, called Contrastively Selected Embedding (CSE), which projects the anomalous regions to a more discriminative representation space. Specifically, we first design a Siamese network to learn the embedding that can capture the local structure information of the surface. Then, we propose a new selection module to selectively highlight the anomalous regions based on the embedding. Finally, we use the selected embedding to project the anomalous regions to a more discriminative representation space, and thus the surface anomalies can be easily detected with a simple thresholding operation. Compared to existing methods, CSE is more effective in detecting surface anomalies with irregular shapes and small sizes. Extensive experiments on two public datasets demonstrate that our method outperforms state-of-the-art methods by a significant margin.

Predictor-based methods have substantially enhanced Neural Architecture Search (NAS) optimization. However, existing works primarily focus on reducing the number of search spaces and optimizing hyperparameters without considering the training process. This paper presents PDQA, a novel predictive dynamic quantization architecture for NAS. First, we propose a 3D quantized-weight quantitative learning approach that considers both the weight and channel quantization. Second, to reduce the search space size in multi-stage NAS, we design a lightweight 1D binary-search strategy. Third, we develop a prediction-driven dynamic quantization architecture where the prediction model is trained with the proposed 3D quantization method and used to guide the weight quantization. Experimental results on mul

Privacy-relevant app reviews play a critical role in helping users make informed decisions and exerting post-installation control over the privacy practices of apps. We present a comprehensive analysis of privacy-relevant reviews of Android apps that were posted over a period of ten years, from 2012 to 2021. Our study is based on a dataset that contains 4,016,515 reviews from 77,147 apps. This dataset is orders of magnitude larger than the datasets used in prior studies on privacy-relevant reviews, which typically contained a few thousand reviews from a few dozen apps. We employed multiple machine learning models to analyze the text of the reviews, extract insights, and identify trends. Our findings reveal that privacy-relevant reviews are growing at a fast pace, that most of them are negative, that users are more concerned with advertising-related privacy issues, and that they tend to mention specific privacy features of apps rather than privacy risks. We also found that the average number of privacy-related reviews per app is a better predictor of an app's privacy score than the average number of reviews per se. Finally, we present a visualization of our findings, which can help users, app developers, and policymakers better understand the implications of privacy-relevant reviews.

Object slip is one of the common failures in mobile manipulation robots. Early detection of object slip can help robots to respond in a timely manner to maintain the safety of the surrounding environment. This paper proposes a novel multimodal anomaly detection method based on deep auto-encoder, which fuses the visual and tactile information of the manipulation process to detect object slip. The proposed method is implemented on a mobile manipulation platform to evaluate the performance of the method in a real-world scenario. Experimental results demonstrate that the proposed method can achieve better slip detection performance than the single-modal case, and the visual information is more effective for slip detection than the tactile information.

As many as 8 in 10 adults share intimate content such as nude or seminude selfies online. This has led to a growing need for tools that can detect and flag harmful content. Existing solutions often fail when applied to images of human faces due to their complexity, resulting in high false-positive rates. In this paper, we introduce an open-source dataset with over 50k annotated images of human faces from four different perspectives: frontal, profile, left side, and right side. The data was collected by a volunteer group on social media platforms through the creation of a challenge where participants had to upload selfie photos. We validate our dataset using existing face detection models (FaceNet, YOLOv2) and show that it is more accurate than existing datasets. Moreover, we present a nove

This paper proposes a novel multi-task learning framework for real-time autonomous driving, which simultaneously performs high-level task such as behavior prediction and low-level task such as motion prediction. The proposed framework focuses on the information exchange between the two tasks. Specifically, a task-adaptive attention generator is devised to adaptively aggregate the task-relevant information from the high-level task and the low-level task. Furthermore, a cross-task message propagation module is proposed to explicitly facilitate the information exchange between the high-level task and the low-level task. The proposed framework is applied to the real-time autonomous driving benchmark scenarios, including behavior prediction and motion prediction. Experimental results demonstrate that the proposed framework achieves the state-of-the-art performance on the real-time autonomous driving benchmark, indicating the effectiveness of the proposed framework.

Human label variation arises when annotators assign different labels to the same item for valid reasons, while annotation errors arise from mistakes that happen during human labeling. We propose a novel framework called Variational Label Assignment (VLA) which incorporates both of these processes into an intuitive probabilistic model and employs variational inference techniques with automatic hyperparameter learning algorithms in order tackle this problem efficiently on large datasets without requiring any prior knowledge about them or their annotations at all! Our approach is capable not only identifying individual instances where there was disagreement between two people but also detecting what type(s). Our method has been shown effective by comparing its performance against those obtain

Advanced life forms, sustained by the synergistic interaction of neural cognitive mechanisms, continually face an ever-increasing number of complex problems and challenges. Recent advances in artificial intelligence have allowed the development of systems that exhibit impressive performance capabilities. However, these systems are characterized by a lack of interpretability and transparency, raising significant concerns about their potential to replace human beings as decision makers in critical situations. This article explores the potential of combining human expertise with machine learning techniques in order to improve the accuracy and comprehensibility of predictive models. We introduce the concept of the "Human-AI Assistant," a new generation of hybrid intelligent systems designed to

We propose a novel multi-object tracking (MOT) algorithm, dubbed VastTrack, which achieves outstanding performance on large-scale benchmarks. The success of VastTrack is attributed to two innovative designs, namely 1) a transformer-based multi-object detection and tracking framework, and 2) a novel training strategy that simultaneously optimizes the tracking and detection algorithms. VastTrack is the first MOT algorithm that achieves over 80.0 MOTA (Multiple Object Tracking Accuracy) on the test set of MOT17 with 30 FPS inference speed on a single V100 GPU. It also achieves 79.7 MOTA on the test set of MOT16 with 48.9 FPS. Furthermore, VastTrack is the first transformer-based algorithm that achieves over 80.0 MOTA on MOT17 without using any benchmark data for finetuning. The source code is available at https://github.com/leonnnop/VastTrack.

Distributed Stream Processing (DSP) systems are capable of processing large streams of unbounded data. In the event that a DSP system is required to process an unbounded stream, it must be able to manage its resources in such a way that the system continues to operate without interruption. Resource management is crucial for ensuring the smooth operation of distributed systems and protecting their reliability. However, resource management for distributed stream processing systems has been overlooked, resulting in poor performance and reliability. We propose an adaptive, load-aware resource allocation strategy called ALARA for DSP systems. Our approach enables the system to dynamically adjust the number of workers based on the workload. Through extensive experiments conducted using real-worl

Deep Neural Network (DNN) accelerators based on systolic arrays have gained significant attention for their ability to efficiently accelerate matrix-intensive operations in DNNs. However, reliability concerns, such as the accumulation of errors due to the finite precision of intermediate results, pose challenges to their widespread adoption. To address these reliability concerns, it is essential to develop a framework that can accurately predict the error rates of systolic-array-based DNN accelerators under different operating conditions. Such a framework can help designers make informed decisions regarding the use of these accelerators in their designs. In this paper, we propose SAFFIRA, a framework to predict the reliability of systolic-array-based DNN accelerators. SAFFIRA models the error propagation in a systematic manner and can predict the end-to-end reliability of a DNN accelerator. We validate our framework by evaluating three commercial DNN accelerators: Google's TPU, Intel's Arria GX, and Xilinx's Alveo U280. We also validate our framework by assessing the reliability of DNN accelerators under various conditions, such as different operating frequencies and precision formats. Our results show that SAFFIRA can predict the reliability of DNN accelerators with a high degree of accuracy, making it a valuable tool for designers.

Machine learning has achieved remarkable success in many applications. However, existing studies are largely based on the closed-world assumption that all data points belong to the same class label. In realistic settings, this assumption is frequently violated as there may be a small number of unlabelled samples for which the correct label is unknown but can potentially still be inferred from other data. To address this challenge, we propose an improved approach called \textit{Open World Learning} (OWL) that leverages the idea of open-set classification within semi-supervised learning (SSL). OWL is a two-stage algorithm that first applies SSL to obtain a classifier using only labeled data and then employs a test set of unlabelled samples to refine the classifier by discriminating between k

Underwater soft grippers exhibit potential for applications such as monitoring, research, and object retrieval. However, their performance can be limited by environmental conditions, including temperature. A novel approach to address this limitation has been proposed through the integration of a heating element within the gripper. This work presents a systematic study examining the effectiveness of this approach in increasing the gripping force of an underwater soft gripper. The results show that the addition of a heating element can significantly improve grip strength and stability, with values ranging between 15% and 63%. Additionally, the impact of heating on gripper durability was also investigated. Overall, this study provides valuable insights into the potential benefits and limitati

Fine-tuning large language models (LLMs) on private datasets is prohibitively expensive as it requires many expensive GPU resources. Therefore, a number of recent works have proposed to compress LLMs to smaller sizes that can be fine-tuned on cheaper hardware. However, existing methods focus on compressing LLMs through weight pruning or low-rank approximation, which do not consider the privacy concerns of the training data and can actually worsen the privacy. In this work, we propose a novel privacy-preserving model compression framework for LLMs based on the flatness of the model parameters, which measures the average number of iterations required to reach a certain training objective. We show that flat LLMs have a higher likelihood of containing the solutions with privacy guarantees for a given number of training steps and fewer parameters. As such, we first develop an algorithm to efficiently approximate the flatness of a given LLM model, and then propose a flatness-aware compression algorithm that prioritizes the compression of flat LLMs. Compared to existing methods, our experiments show that the proposed algorithm can achieve better privacy-performance trade-offs for LLMs on various datasets.

Given two strings $S$ and $P$, the Mem Finding problem is to find the shortest substring of $S$ that contains $P$ as a subsequence. The parameterized complexity of this problem is well-understood: it is NP-hard to find a solution of size $k$ in time bounded by a polynomial in $k$, but it is solvable in time $O(k^r)$, for some constant $r$. We present a new algorithm for Mem Finding, which improves upon the running time of previous best algorithms for most values of $r$. In particular, we show that the problem can be solved in time $O(r + \bar{r} + g)$, where $\bar{r}$ is the recompression number of $P$ (the maximum number of times a given subsequence of $P$ occurs in $P$), and $g$ is the length of $P$. Our algorithm is based on a new technique of recompression of patterns, which might be of independent interest.

We propose a novel learning rate scheduler for heterogeneous decentralized learning. We observe that in such settings, the average model parameters often converge faster than the parameters of any individual worker. Based on this, we propose to accelerate the learning process by using the average model parameters as the starting point for individual workers. We design an averaging rate scheduler that determines the timing for this switch from the global model to the averaged model and show that the proposed switching strategy can improve the convergence rate over 1.5 times compared to the state-of-the-art. We also show that the proposed method can significantly improve the performance of small-batch sizes and highly heterogeneous datasets.

Large language models (LLMs) have proven to be very superior to conventional methods in various fields. Existing benchmark datasets for evaluating LLMs are mainly focused on the evaluation of model performance, but not much attention has been paid to the efficiency of the evaluation process itself. In this paper, we introduce a novel framework that incorporates data generation and inference into an unified evaluation system, aiming to effectively evaluate LLMs across different tasks. The core idea is to use data generated from the LLM as training samples for the evaluation system. This approach has two significant advantages: 1) It avoids the need for pre-training and fine-tuning of the evaluation system on large amounts of external data, making it more efficient; 2) It enables the evaluat

We investigate the empirical counterpart of group distributionally robust optimization (GDRO), which aims to design an algorithm that performs well in any distribution over the data. The GDRO literature mainly focuses on the performance guarantee for single-stage algorithms, i.e., how much does the error increase if we choose an adversarially selected distribution? In this paper, we study GDRO with multiple stages: first, the data is split into several batches; then, each batch goes through a single-stage GDRO procedure before the final decision is made. This setting arises naturally when splitting data into training and testing. We call it multi-stage GDRO (MSGDRO). For MSGDRO under the $\ell_1$-loss, we provide an upper bound on the regret of the optimal policy. Furthermore, we show that

Listing and counting triangles in graphs is a key algorithmic kernel for network analyses, with diverse applications including community detection. We present a general-purpose parallel algorithm that leverages the recent advances in graph streaming algorithms to solve this problem on a streaming model of computation. Our results apply to both streaming graphs where we have access only to a sequence of edge insertions/deletions as well as streaming graphs that also allow random queries. In particular, our streaming algorithm allows us to compute the number of triangles in a streaming graph of size $n$ in $\tilde{O}(m \log n + d)$ time, where $m$ is the total number of edges added to the graph, and $d$ is the maximum degree of any vertex.

With the growth of internet of things (IoT) devices, cyberattacks, such as denial-of-service (DoS), botnets, and ransomware, have become more frequent. Large language models (LLMs) can be used to detect IoT attacks by analyzing network traffic data and identifying suspicious patterns using natural language processing (NLP). In this study, we propose a model named CBM-GPT2-Llama, which leverages the capabilities of GPT-2 Llama and Chatbot Multimodal (CBM) to detect IoT attacks. Our approach utilizes NLP techniques to extract information from network traffic and then incorporates it into a sequence-to-sequence model with attention mechanisms for classification purposes.

Serverless computing is a promising paradigm for simplifying the deployment and scaling of applications, with Functions-as-a-Service (FaaS) platforms such as AWS Lambda, Azure Functions, and Google Cloud Functions serving millions of requests per day. While these platforms have become the de-facto standard for deploying serverless applications, they are limited by the underlying virtual machine (VM) or container-based architectures, which introduce unnecessary overhead for latency-sensitive workloads. Bare-metal serverless platforms without VMs or containers, on the other hand, are able to achieve much lower latency, but lack the ecosystem support and developer experience of FaaS platforms. We posit that by bypassing the kernel, both FaaS and bare-metal serverless platforms can achieve low latency while maintaining a serverless experience. To this end, we present Junctiond, a novel serverless runtime that extends existing FaaS platforms with kernel-bypass capabilities. We show that Junctiond can achieve the low latency of bare-metal serverless platforms while maintaining the benefits of FaaS platforms, with no changes required to existing serverless applications.

Sentence semantic matching is a research hotspot in natural language processing, which is considerably significant in practical applications such as automatic summarization and sentiment analysis. However, existing methods either rely on the complex pre-trained language model or are not suitable for sentence pairs with different lengths. In this paper, we propose an Efficient Transformer (EffiTrans) to capture semantics more effectively by introducing an effective mechanism of semantic aggregation across positions and layers. Specifically, our method employs a novel self-attention scheme that allows information from all tokens in each layer to be utilized for computing contextual representations. This approach helps reduce the computational complexity while enhancing the learning capabilit

Currently, infrared imaging technology enjoys widespread usage, with infrared thermal imaging cameras playing a crucial role. However, due to the complex and dynamic nature of objects in real-world scenes, it is challenging for conventional algorithms to accurately identify the targets of interest in these images. To address this issue, we propose a novel framework that leverages large language models (LLMs) and generative AI (AI), which enables enhanced target detection performance in infrared thermal images. This innovative approach integrates LLM's natural language capabilities with AI's image processing expertise, thereby enhancing the ability to recognize and locate objects of interest within complex environments. Our method incorporates an in-depth understanding of natural language c

Large Language Models (LLMs) have demonstrated exceptional performance in biochemical tasks, such as protein structure prediction and drug design. However, LLMs often lack the capability to understand complex concepts and perform tasks requiring specialized knowledge. This paper presents a new framework for enhancing large language models with domain-specific knowledge through knowledge transfer from expert-curated knowledge bases. We first demonstrate that this can be achieved by fine-tuning a pretrained large language model on domain-relevant questions and their corresponding answers extracted from the knowledge base. Then we use the same approach to train a LLM specialized in molecular biology tasks, including predicting protein structures and biological pathways. Finally, we apply our

In recent years, Cross-Domain Recommendation (CDR) has drawn significant attention, especially in the field of social networks. We propose a novel model called \textit{Dual-Adaptive Cross-Domain Graph Neural Networks} (\textbf{DACGNN}) for CDR. DACGNN is an encoder-decoder framework that utilizes graph neural network to capture the structural and semantic relationships between items in different domains simultaneously. Specifically, DACGNN incorporates two adaptive learning mechanisms: domain-adaptive and user-adaptive. The domain-adaptive mechanism is designed to learn domain-specific features while preserving the cross-domain compatibility, while the user-adaptive mechanism leverages the user preference information to enhance the personalized recommendation.

National Security Letters (NSLs) are similar to administrative subpoenas and can be issued by the FBI or other law enforcement agencies. However, unlike a traditional subpoena that requires an agent’s probable cause affidavit supporting issuance of search warrant for evidence within your control at the time it is served upon you; NSL allows investigators unlimited access during period covered under this order without requiring further authorization from court system once granted initial permission via judge appointed specifically on case file(or panel).

The Federal Bureau Investigation Act gives federal agents authority over information in their possession relating directly back towards individuals involved with criminal activ

Recent advances in deep neural networks for speaker separation have led to models that outperform traditional methods. However, they still suffer from limitations such as reliance on multi-channel input, inability to generalize across wide frequency bands, and poor performance in the presence of overlapping speakers. To address these limitations, we propose the CrossNet architecture, which incorporates four innovative designs: global encoding, cross-band mutual attention, narrow-band isolation, and positional encoding. These designs enable CrossNet to effectively separate speakers in both single- and multi-channel scenarios, improve generalization across wide frequency bands, and enhance performance in the presence of overlapping speakers. We evaluate CrossNet on a speaker separation dataset covering a wide range of realistic scenarios, including multi-channel input, overlapping speakers, and various reverberation conditions. The results demonstrate the superiority of CrossNet over existing state-of-the-art methods, with improvements in objective evaluation metrics ranging from 1.2 to 3.7 dB.

The researcher explained this phenomenon as follows (quoted from here): “We see it as being part human error – we made some mistakes in implementing our techniques.” The paper also suggests various solutions to overcome such problems with randomness but none is yet implemented at scale or even evaluated on real-world datasets for practical use cases today! It is especially challenging to achieve real-time human motion tracking on a standalone VR Head Mounted Display (HMD). This paper proposes an end-to-end deep learning framework, named MotionVision, which addresses the challenges in both vision and motion tracking. The proposed framework achieves high efficiency by processing low-resolution images, while maintaining high accuracy through the combination of image and optical flow data. Our approach does not require prior calibration or external sensors, thus facilitating deployment on standard off-the-shelf HMDs. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our model is capable of achieving real-time performance with a processing speed of 30 frames per second using a standard NVIDIA Jetson Xavier NX board.

Recent advancements in large-scale pre-trained text-to-image models have enabled the generation of photorealistic images from natural language instructions. In this work, we study a new paradigm for image creation: generating an image by instructing a model to follow a recipe that describes how to cook a dish. We introduce a dataset and benchmark for recipe-based image generation called Recipes2Images (R2I). R2I contains 5,197 recipes with images and corresponding instruction captions that describe both the ingredients and the preparation steps. To evaluate these models on this new task, we propose a novel metric called Image Consistency Score (ICS) which evaluates the quality of generated images based on their correspondence with the original recipe.

We present Pix2Gif, a motion-guided diffusion model for image-to-gif conversion. To achieve this goal, we design a novel 3D convolution layer with an explicit gif pose and motion guidance module to extract gif-specific features from the input images. Additionally, we introduce a novel self-supervised loss function that is designed to learn the mapping from the gif pose and motion guidance modules to the latent space of our diffusion model. Experimental results demonstrate that our proposed method outperforms existing methods in terms of visual quality, preserving key characteristics of gifs such as animations, pose control, and flicker-free frames. Furthermore, we showcase its ability to generate videos based on single input images, demonstrating its potential for applications beyond still

Topic modeling is a popular unsupervised learning algorithm for discovering latent topics in a corpus of textual data. Traditional topic modeling algorithms, which are based on a generative probabilistic framework, assume that the training data is a reliable representation of the topics. This assumption holds in ideal settings, but in practice, an adversary may manipulate the training data to hide or create topics. Membership inference attacks, where an adversary aims to deduce whether a particular document belongs to a sensitive topic, are a serious concern in topic modeling. In this work, we show that membership inference attacks are inherently possible in topic modeling, regardless of the model's architecture. We introduce a novel adversarial training strategy that uses topic-specific words to craft perturbations that are indistinguishable to humans but can be used to mount a membership inference attack. We show that topic modeling algorithms that are based on variational inference are particularly vulnerable to our attack. Our experimental results demonstrate that an adversary can perform a successful membership inference attack with as few as 10 perturbed documents. To mitigate these attacks, we propose a simple countermeasure based on data augmentation that increases the difficulty of mounting a successful attack. We also show that privacy and performance are not mutually exclusive, and that it is possible to train topic models in a way that preserves utility for honest users while making it difficult for an adversary to perform a successful membership inference attack.

Physical Unclonable Functions (PUFs) have been shown to be a highly secure and reliable source for on-chip unique identifiers, and thus can be used as a replacement of ROM memory. The idea is that the PUF response, which depends on a specific silicon fingerprint, contains an intrinsic signature of the device. However, it is essential to verify this uniqueness, i.e., that the signature is indeed unique. In this paper we propose a novel approach to address this problem by leveraging the inherent nature of PUF responses: randomness. We present a methodology based on a statistical test to evaluate the uniqueness of PUF responses, whereby the significance of the statistical difference between two PUF responses is computed using random sampling. Our proposal allows us to perform statistical test

Transformers have revolutionized computer vision and natural language processing, but their high computational complexity limits their adoption in resource-constrained settings. A key challenge is to accelerate the inference of large Transformers without sacrificing accuracy or increasing memory footprint beyond what current accelerators can handle efficiently (e..g., 4GB on a mobile device). To address this gap between Transformer performance at scale vs efficiency for constrained environments we propose \textit{Dynamic Multiscale}, an innovative method that dynamically adapts multiscaling across layers during execution by leveraging existing accelerator hardware optimizations like subword partitioning (\textsf{\emph {XLA}}), layer fusing with shared weights within groups ($\ell$-fusion)

Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback (\textbf{RLHF}) has emerged as a promising approach to fine-tune Large Language Models (LLMs) based on human demonstrations. However, existing RLHF approaches mainly focus on textual inputs and outputs, which are not sufficient for LLMs that can interact with the external world through various modalities such as text, images, and videos. In this paper, we propose \textbf{R}einforcement \textbf{L}earning \textbf{M}odal \textbf{I}nteraction framework (\textbf{RLMI}), a novel paradigm for LLMs that can interact with the physical world based on reinforcement learning. To realize RLMI, we first design an interactive environment that contains multiple objects that can be manipulated by an agent.

This paper is about 3D pose estimation on LiDAR scans with extremely minimal storage requirements, using a neural network trained on only 1-2 million points. We first introduce an approach for training the network in a more efficient way by pretraining it on a set of synthetically generated LiDAR scans. Then we demonstrate that this pretrained model can be used to estimate pose on real LiDAR scans without any further fine-tuning. Our experiments show that our method achieves state-of-the-art accuracy while requiring far less training data than previous methods. Moreover, we show how the same neural network could potentially also be used to perform semantic segmentation and reconstruction tasks on LiDAR scans. Our code will be made publicly available at https://github.com/davidxu2015/LiDAR-

Multi-hop fact verification (MHFV) aims to verify the truthfulness of a statement by searching for a path of hop-wise supported facts that connects the claim to a set of ground facts. While hop-wise support guarantees that each fact in the path is causally relevant to the claim, the overall path-level distribution can still be biased, e.g., due to omitted confounders in individual hops. In this work, we propose Causal Walk, a novel framework for debiasing MHFV by applying front-door adjustment to causal walks. We introduce a novel causal variant of the random walk algorithm to construct a set of causal walks from the claim to the ground facts. We then apply front-door adjustment to each walk to construct a debiased evidence set for verifying the claim. Causal Walk leverages the causal relevance guarantee of causal walks and the aggregation power of front-door adjustment to construct strong evidence sets, even in the presence of omitted confounders. Experimental results on real-world graph datasets show that Causal Walk produces more accurate MHFV compared to existing baselines.

Service Mesh is gaining recognition as a crucial component in modern application architectures, emphasizing the management of inter-service communications. However, existing Service Mesh solutions lack a comprehensive approach to securing inter-service communications, relying on traditional, node-to-node encryption. This approach neglects the security of service discovery and the integrity of service configuration, which are vital in cloud and edge environments. To address these security concerns, this paper introduces CARISMA, a novel Service Mesh architecture that integrates the principles of Secure Multi-party Computation (MPC) and introduces a trusted execution environment based on Hardware-Software Trustlets. The result is a secure, resilient, and private service mesh that can withstand attacks on the network and improve the security of service discovery and configuration. This paper presents the design and implementation of CARISMA, including its trusted execution environment, network architecture, and key management scheme. Furthermore, it evaluates the performance and security of CARISMA, demonstrating its effectiveness in real-world scenarios.

Large Language Models (LLMs) such as GPT-4 have recently demonstrated impressive results across a wide range of tasks. Unfortunately, they are prone to security vulnerabilities such as inputting poisoned prompts that can lead to harmful consequences. To mitigate this, context-awareness is crucial, where an LLM examines the context of a prompt to ensure it aligns with the user's original intent. However, existing context-aware approaches are limited to a narrow focus on either the prompt or the response, and they do not utilize available information from the other. In this paper, we propose a novel framework called CoGenesis for securing instruction following that considers the often-overlooked intermediate context between prompts and responses. CoGenesis collaborates a large language model (LLM) and a small language model (SLM) to verify the intermediate context, leveraging the strengths of both models to maximize security. We evaluate CoGenesis for its security, privacy, and usability properties, and we empirically demonstrate its effectiveness in mitigating prompt poisoning attacks across three tasks.

In this paper, we investigate the bi-static sensing performance in multi-carrier wireless systems. We consider an orthogonal frequency division multiplexing (OFDM) transmitter and a multi-antenna receiver, where the effect of the user equipment (UE) movement is taken into account. A sensing data transmission framework is proposed, where the OFDM pilot symbols are utilized for channel estimation and sensing target detection. The conventional zero-correlation zone (ZCZ) sequences and the recently proposed partial ZCZ sequences are implemented to study their sensing performance. Moreover, the impact of the number of OFDM subcarriers and the UE movement on the sensing performance is investigated. The results show that OFDM-based sensing systems can achieve centimeter-level sensing accuracy, which is suitable for indoor scenarios. Additionally, the computational complexity of the proposed algorithms is analyzed.

We present Human to Humanoid (H2O), a reinforcement learning (RL) framework for training robots with human demonstrations. H2O provides a simple and effective way of incorporating user instructions into robot RL, where the user can directly provide commands to the robot, similar to how they would control it themselves. The user's commands are then translated into low-level actions by an RL agent, which can be either pre-trained or trained from scratch. Our framework includes a variety of user commands such as pointing gestures, spoken language, and button presses that can be used in conjunction with each other. Additionally, our framework enables users to specify different levels of assistance, such as guiding the robot using full control or just providing high-level goals. Our evaluation

Exosuits are gaining popularity for their potential to help people with mobility impairments. However, integrating passive compliance into exosuits remains challenging. A key ingredient to achieve passive compliance is an anchoring system that can adapt to the body movements. The human body is composed of many compliant joints that store and release energy during movement. Hence, mimicking joint mechanics with an adaptive anchor can help reduce the energy cost of exosuits. This study presents a novel stretchable pneumatic anchor that is compliant, adjustable, and can withstand tensile forces up to 80 N. The anchor is composed of a stretchable tube with an inner bladder that is inflated with air to generate the tensile force. A rigid outer shell is attached to the tube to provide structural support and prevent the tube from bursting. The anchor can be adjusted to different lengths (110-170 % of the initial length) with a precision of 1 % of the initial length, which corresponds to a force resolution of 80 mN. A total of four anchors were fabricated and tested. The average mass of the anchors was 17.3 g, and the average inflation time was 27.1 s. The anchors were tested to failure (100 % elongation) in a tensile testing machine. The average maximum force before failure was 78.6 N. A preliminary human study was conducted to assess the anchors' comfort. The results of this study show that the proposed pneumatic anchors are comfortable, compliant, adjustable, and strong, making them suitable for exosuits.

The term "Edge Computing" refers to the provision of computing resources (e.g., servers, storage, software applications) locally, i.e., at or near the users' proximity. This computing paradigm is gaining increasing popularity, driven by the needs of the Internet of Things (IoT), smart vehicles, and other emerging applications that require low-latency and data privacy. Machine Learning (ML) is one of the major enabling technologies behind Edge Computing, thanks to its ability to bring intelligence to the edge devices and improve the autonomy of IoT systems. This paper provides a comprehensive survey of the current state-of-the-art in Edge ML. We present a novel taxonomy of the hardware and software solutions that enable ML at the edge, and identify the key research challenges in this field.

This work highlights an approach for incorporating realistic uncertainties into scientific computing workflows. The goal is to enable probabilistic reasoning over the whole workflow, from the initial formulation of the problem to its final solution. We introduce a systematic framework that leverages uncertainty quantification (UQ) and machine learning techniques to address this challenge. Our key innovation lies in integrating UQ tools within the workflow, thereby allowing for seamless propagation of model uncertainty throughout various stages. We provide several examples of the proposed framework's application, including optimization-based simulations of complex systems such as chemical reactions and fluid dynamics. By providing an efficient way to handle inherent uncertainties in scienti

In this paper, we introduce tiled graphs as models of learning and maturing processes. We show that tiled graphs can be used to model learning processes where the knowledge of an agent changes gradually over time. This is done by representing knowledge as a state of a process and by defining what we call tiles as abstractions or categories of states. These abstractions can represent different levels of generalization in the knowledge of an agent. We study how these abstractions are connected, both explicitly via edges and implicitly via transitions between tiles. We argue that these connections between abstractions form a suitable model for maturing, in which the learner progresses from more specific to more abstract representations of the world around it. Finally, we discuss the relation

The effectiveness of clopidogrel, a widely used antiplatelet medication, varies significantly among individuals, necessitating the development of precise predictive models to optimize patient care. In this study, we leverage federated learning strategies to address clopidogrel treatment failure detection. Our research harnesses the collaborative power of multiple healthcare institutions, allowing them to jointly train machine learning models while safeguarding sensitive patient data. Utilizing the UK Biobank dataset, which encompasses a vast and diverse population, we partitioned the data based on geographic centers and evaluated the performance of federated learning. Our results show that while centralized training achieves higher Area Under the Curve (AUC) values and faster convergence,

By 2030, the sixth generation (6G) of mobile networks is expected to support a wide range of new services, many of which will require an unprecedented level of quality of service (QoS), e.g., in terms of guaranteed minimum bit-rate or maximum tolerable delay. To this end, future 6G heterogeneous networks (HetNets) will have to overcome the significant resource allocation and user association challenges that arise from the massive network heterogeneity and the QoS diversity requirements of the supported applications. For this purpose, we propose a novel deep reinforcement learning-based approach for joint user association and resource allocation, which aims at provisioning tailored QoS guarantees while minimizing the network resources required for this purpose. In particular, we introduce a QoS provisioning game among the base stations (BSs) of a 6G HetNet, and we prove that there exists an optimal association policy that allows each BS to allocate its resources in an opportunistic yet fair manner. To find such a policy, we adopt a multi-agent deep reinforcement learning (DRL) approach, which enables the BSs to learn the optimal association and resource allocation strategy by jointly exploring the user-to-BS association and the allocation of the available network resources. Simulation results show that the proposed DRL-based algorithm provisioning tailored QoS guarantees outperforms classical user association methods, at the same time minimizing the network resources required for this purpose. In addition, the computational complexity analysis shows that the proposed algorithm can be efficiently implemented in 6G HetNets.

Automated Program Repair (APR) aims to automatically generate patches for rectifying software defects. In recent years, deep learning models have been widely used in APR tasks due their superior ability of feature extraction and representation power compared with traditional machine-learning algorithms. However there are still some challenges that hinder the application scope or development direction of current Deep Learning Models based Automated Patch Generation techniques: 1). The lack accurate patch repair semantics;2); it is difficult to balance between code fidelity requirements from developers while retaining high quality defect fixes3), these problems prevent us effectively solve existing real world bug fix scenarios .

Continual Learning (CL) aims to train a model incrementally on a sequence of tasks without forgetting previous knowledge. CL is facing two major challenges: (1) catastrophic forgetting, i.e., forgetting old knowledge due to new knowledge, and (2) the inability to learn efficiently in an open-ended manner. In this paper, we propose Interactive Continual Learning (ICL) which employs a two-level learning architecture to address the above challenges. ICL introduces a novel way of human-machine interaction in the CL setup, where the human agent can influence the machine's learning process and vice versa. The machine agent can reflect on its own learning progress and adjust the pace of learning for different tasks based on the available resources. The human agent can provide necessary assistance to the machine agent when it encounters difficult tasks and monitor the learning progress. We conduct a series of experiments with ICL and demonstrate that ICL can learn more tasks with better accuracy and faster speed compared to the baseline methods.

There is a growing academic interest as well as commercial exploitation of millimetre-wave scanning radar systems for surveillance and security applications. Millimetre-wave systems are able to resolve targets in the range from 0.5 m up to several kilometres, depending on the size and power of the transmitter. The main drawback of millimetre-wave systems is that they are susceptible to weather effects such as rain, snow and fog. In this paper we present a new approach to mitigate weather effects on radar signals using multiple receive antennas. We show that multiple receive antennas can be used to enhance the signal quality by taking advantage of the spatial diversity that they offer. This technique could be applied to any antenna array system but is particularly beneficial for millimetre-

Creating effective educational materials generally requires expensive and time-consuming studies of student learning outcomes. To address this challenge, we present a novel approach that utilizes reinforcement learning to develop adaptive educational materials tailored to individual students' learning needs. We formulate the problem as a Markov decision process with two states: student knowledge level and student engagement level. We use a value function that incorporates both state transitions and rewards associated with student performance on tasks, thereby balancing between exploration for improving student knowledge and exploitation for maximizing student motivation. Our method effectively learns from a relatively small number of human feedback examples (10-20) through its powerful com

Common law courts need to refer to similar precedents' judgments to inform their current decisions. While many legal researchers and practitioners have investigated the relationship between case law and AI, few have explored how these technologies can collaborate in building a comprehensive collection of past cases for future reference. To this end, we propose a novel framework, CaseLawNet, that combines AI with legal expertise to support judges in finding relevant information about prior court rulings. Our approach entails three interconnected components: (i) a large-scale dataset of cases annotated by judicial experts; (ii) a multimodal question-answering model that enables case retrieval based on questions asked by users; and (iii) a legal reasoning module, which integrates AI-generated

In common law systems, court judgments play a crucial role in shaping legal precedents and principles. As such, the ability to extract key arguments and decisions from court judgments is of great importance. However, the task is beset with challenges, notably the lack of annotated data for training machine learning models. In this work, we leverage publicly available court judgments from the United States and the United Kingdom to investigate the feasibility of summarizing court judgments in a low-resource setting. We introduce a novel approach that combines deep learning techniques with traditional text summarization methods. Our model takes as input a court judgment and generates as output a summary of the key arguments and decisions in the judgment. To evaluate the effectiveness of our approach, we employ a standard set of metrics for text summarization, namely ROUGE, METEOR, and BERTScore. We also conduct a human evaluation to assess the quality of the summaries generated by our model. Compared to the baseline models, our approach yields significant improvements on all the metrics.

Real-time collaboration with humans poses challenges due to the different behavior patterns of humans resulting from their unique abilities. This study aims to understand human interaction by analyzing collaborative interactions in a real physical environment, and how this understanding can be utilized for efficient robot navigation assistance as part o The goal is not only an increase but also a reduction – that’s the challenge facing many companies today when it comes to production processes: on one hand they want higher output volumes without compromising product quality; yet at same time ensuring safety standards remain met too!

Evaluating and enhancing the general capabilities of large language models (LLMs) has emerged as a critical task in the field of artificial intelligence. Recent research has revealed that LLMs are vulnerable to adversarial attacks, which exploit their weaknesses to manipulate predictions or even cause outright failure. This paper focuses on evaluating the robustness of LLMs against adversarial attacks using prompt engineering and prompt tuning techniques. To this end, we propose a comprehensive framework that leverages the power of prompt engineering and tuning for enhanced robustness. Our methodology involves utilizing different prompt templates along with adversarial training to achieve effective robustness. We conduct thorough experiments to demonstrate the efficacy of our approach acro

Perception is a key element for enabling intelligent autonomous navigation. Understanding the semantics of the environment and the context in which vehicles operate are essential to safely and efficiently navigate through complex environments, and this involves both vision-based perception tasks (e.g., detection, segmentation) and understanding semantic relationships between objects (i.e., classification). However, the current state of the art does not take advantage of the synergies that can be derived from combining these two tasks. We introduce a novel framework for perception, called Vision-Semantic Pipelines (VSPs), where we combine different modalities (visual, semantic) and data sources to leverage their complementary information. Specifically, we propose a novel semantic segmentati

Continual Segmentation is a popular yet challenging task in computer vision. In this task, the model is trained incrementally with non-repeating categories, where the presence of an object is initially unknown. Previous methods first segment the image into objects and background, and then identify the objects one by one. However, this leads to a difficult background class and segmentation boundaries. To address these problems, we propose a novel disentangled objectness learning and class recognition approach for continual segmentation, called DISC. The DISC method assumes that the image is composed of foreground objects and a complex background, and learns the objectness score by analyzing the relationship between the two. To distinguish objects from the background, we use a prototype-based class recognition approach, which compares the query object with the prototypes of foreground and background classes. The proposed method is evaluated on popular continual segmentation datasets, and shows competitive performance with other state-of-the-art methods. The source code is available at https://github.com/ContinualN/CIS_DISC.

(p,q)-clique enumeration on a bipartite graph is critical for calculating clustereffectiveness measures such as modularity. We present an $O\left((n+m) \cdot 2^{s}\right)$ time algorithm to enumerate all cliques in the intersection of two independent sets with size at most s and report experimental results that indicate good performance over randomly generated graphs where traditional algorithms fail dueto high connectivity between nodes within each set.\newline {\bf Keywords:} Clique Enumeration; Bi-Partite Graph;\qquad{\sc DBLP categories: F10543} We study random walks whose transition matrix satisfies certain assumptions similar to those found in the theory governing reversible Markov chains (e.,g,. total variation distance).

Identifying critical nodes in networks is a classical decision-making task, and many methods struggle to strike a balance between robustness and efficiency. In this paper, we address this problem by introducing two measures that capture the relative importance of nodes: the criticality degree and the structural sensitivity. The former measures how much influence each node exerts on the system's stability, while the latter quantifies how vulnerable a node is when it becomes disconnected from the network. We prove that these quantities are interrelated and provide a novel optimization framework for identifying important nodes. Our approach not only outperforms existing algorithms but also significantly reduces the computational cost. We test our method on synthetic and real-world networks fr

Highlighting particularly relevant regions of an image can improve the performance of vision-language models (VLM) on multimodality tasks, such as visual commonsense reasoning. We propose a simple yet effective method to inject salient information from text into images by applying self attention onto both modalities and masking out irrelevant parts in each modality based upon their alignments with its counterpart's focus region mask generated via contrastive learning between masked tokens across two VLMs jointly initialized for this task using pretrain + fine tune strategy . Through extensive experiments involving four datasets spanning different domains ranging from natural language description generation/retrieval all way up towards question answering & dialog systems , our approach con

In this paper, we introduce the first algorithmic framework for Blackwell approachability on the sequence-to-set domain. We prove that the problem of deciding if a given set is reachable from a given seed set in $O(n^{2})$ time using only polynomial size counters can be reduced to solving a $3$CNF formula with $O(n^4)$ variables and $O(n^5)$ clauses in the worst case. Moreover, we show how the same result holds when the number of variables is bounded by $\log n$, giving a generalization of previous algorithms for deterministic cases of $k$-counter problems to the randomized setting. We also analyze the complexity of Blackwell approachability for certain classes of finite state machines, including finite automata and context-free grammars. Our results have applications in various fields suc

Digital memcomputing machines (DMMs) solve combinatorial problems by finding fixed points of a dynamical system, which represents a network of memristive elements. Solving a given problem requires setting the initial configuration of the network connections, which is usually done sequentially by a CPU. This process can be slow for large networks, and it is not clear that parallelizing it would help, as the required number of parallel steps grows exponentially with the number of memristors. We show that, contrary to this intuition, the initial configuration can be set much faster than with a CPU, using only a small fraction of the memristors. This is done by exploiting the dynamical nature of memcomputing, and performing an analog preprocessing step that provides an approximate solution to the problem, from which the initial configuration is derived.

A topical challenge for algorithms in general and for automatic image categorization and generation in particular is presented by images with multiple, semantically diverse objects or scenes. For instance, an image may contain both a cat and a dog, which are difficult to differentiate visually. We propose to encode such images as a mixture of visual concepts by introducing a new model architecture. Our approach is based on the observation that visual concepts can be described by distinct distributions over object categories, or scene classes, and that these concept distributions interact in complex ways in multimodal images. Our new model architecture, referred to as Concept Mixture Model (CMM), learns a set of visual concepts from data and assigns a probability distribution over these con

The emergence of large language models (LLMs) has revolutionized numerous applications across industries. In the field of finance, LLMs have demonstrated exceptional capabilities in financial text summarization, enabling the quick extraction of essential information from lengthy documents. This paper delves into the realm of LLM-powered financial text summarization, exploring its potential and challenges. We first conduct a comprehensive literature review to understand the current state of the art in this domain. Our findings reveal that while LLMs exhibit remarkable ability in financial text summarization, there are still many challenges associated with their integration into real-world settings. For example, the cost-efficiency of large-scale LLMs is an ongoing concern as they require si

Confusing charge prediction is a challenging task in legal AI, which involves predicting confusing charges from legal texts. The existing methods for this task rely on pre-trained models and can only extract features from the input text. They do not take advantage of the rich contextual information that can be retrieved from the historical records of court cases. To address this limitation, we propose a novel approach based on an LLM that utilizes contextualized features extracted from court documents to enhance the accuracy of confusion charge prediction. Our method leverages the concept of attention mechanisms to focus on relevant evidence within each case, while also considering relationships between cases to improve prediction consistency. We demonstrate our approach's effectiveness on

We study the fine-grained complexity of the Earth Mover's Distance (EMD) problem under translation. Let $P$ and $Q$ be two sets of $n$ points in the plane, and let $\mathsf{OPT}$ be the EMD between $P$ and $Q$, which we assume to be an integer. The question is to decide whether $\mathsf{OPT}$ is at most $k$ for a given integer $k$. We show that this problem is NP-hard for any fixed $k \geq 1$. We further show that the problem is W[1]-hard when parameterized by $k$. Our main result is a new algorithm that runs in $\tilde O(n + n \log \log n - n \log n)$ time, which improves over a previous $\tilde O(n + n \log n)$-time algorithm. The improvement is achieved by observing that the problem admits a so-called submodular reformulation, which can be solved more efficiently using dynamic submodular maximization. We complement this result by showing that submodularity is in fact necessary, by proving that the EMD problem is not fixed-parameter tractable (FPT) when parameterized by $k$, unless $\mathrm{P} = \mathrm{NP}$. Finally, we show that the approximation factor of $k$ can be replaced by an approximation parameter $\gamma = \mathsf{OPT} / k$. Our algorithm then runs in $\tilde O(n + n \log \log n - n \log n + n \gamma^{-1})$ time and produces a solution that is correct up to an additive error of $\gamma$.

Existing off-policy reinforcement learning algorithms typically necessitate an explicit state-action-value (SAV) model for learning. In this paper, we propose a novel framework termed \textbf{O}ff-\textbf{P}olicy \textbf{R}einforcement \textbf{L}earning with \textbf{E}xplicit \textbf{S}tate \textbf{M}odel (\textbf{OPRELSTM}), which allows us to train a policy without explicitly estimating the SAV model. The proposed method, OPRELSTM, is based on a recurrent neural network (RNN), where the hidden states of the RNN are used as an implicit SAV model. We show that our algorithm can achieve comparable performance to existing off-policy methods by comparing the performance of the learned policies using state distributions similar to the behavior policy.

We propose DNNLasso, a method that extends the matrix-variate graphical model to incorporate node-level dependencies. Our formulation is based on a node-dependent precision matrix, whose elements are modeled as a function of node features. We learn the model parameters via an efficient optimization algorithm that scales to large problems. The resulting algorithm is convex, provably convergent, and exhibits competitive performance on real-world data sets. Our method is an important step towards learning graph structures from matrix-variate data.

libEnsemble is a Python-based toolkit for running dynamic ensembles, developed as part of the Computational Biophysics Group in Dr. Chris Smith's lab at Yale University [1]. The ensemble code has been under active development since 207 and is still being actively maintained by several collaborators across multiple institutions (e3bioproject@googlegroups). The libensemble core library consists of two main modules: one that handles data structures to efficiently represent multidimensional arrays along with metadata describing their properties; another module which provides methods useful when constructing these types from external files or converting them into formats suitable later use cases like visualization tools such matplotlib . This includes functions allowing users quickly extract in

adaptNMT is an open-source application that offers a streamlined approach to the development and deployment of neural machine translation (NMT) systems. The tool was initially designed for research purposes, but it has also found significant use in production settings. In this paper, we present a complete evaluation of adaptNMT's performance on different NMT tasks. Our benchmark includes 18 language pairs, with diverse characteristics in terms of domain, language, and size. We conduct extensive experiments to assess the tool's efficiency, flexibility, and scalability. The results show that adaptNMT consistently performs well across all tested scenarios, with the best translation quality achieved by models trained using the tool. This study provides valuable insights into adaptNMT's capabil

Large Language Models (LLMs) have recently been shown to possess a multitude of scientific and engineering knowledge, acquired through in-context learning. However, the scope and sufficiency of this knowledge is difficult to assess through conventional means. To systematically evaluate LLMs on molecular prediction tasks, we present a novel dataset termed Molecular Properties Benchmark (MPBench), comprising 1,000 molecules with diverse properties. Using MPBench, we demonstrate that LLMs can accurately predict molecular properties, often outperforming specialized models. Furthermore, we show that LLMs learn the molecular structures underlying the properties, as evidenced by their ability to generalize and transfer knowledge to new tasks. Our findings reveal that LLMs are competent in learning from molecules in an in-context manner, highlighting their potential in advancing scientific understanding and discovery.

Radar is a crucial sensor for maritime navigation, offering high reliability and range accuracy in adverse weather conditions. However, radar images are affected by motion blur due to the target motion during the integration time of the radar. Motion blur degrades the performance of radar odometry, which estimates the motion of the radar sensor with respect to the targets. To overcome this issue, we propose a novel radar descriptor, called LodeStar, which is specifically designed for motion-blurred radar images. The proposed descriptor is based on the polar grid representation of the radar image, where the radial distance is encoded in the hue channel, and the angular coordinate is encoded in the saturation channel. We evaluate the proposed radar descriptor on a maritime radar odometry dataset, where a moving marine vessel is tracked in sea clutter. The experimental results demonstrate that the LodeStar descriptor outperforms the state-of-the-art descriptors, while the computational complexity is lower than the baselines.

Debugging is a fundamental part of creating software. Without it, we would spend much more time waiting for things to break before we can address the issues. Debuggers have come a long way since the early 1970s when they were simple monitors. Today, debuggers are sophisticated pieces of software that help developers find and fix bugs more quickly. This paper explores the history of one particular debugger, the Visual Debugger, from its inception in 1986 up until today. We interview the creators of the Visual Debugger, as well as current and former students and staff at Babel, to understand how and why the Visual Debugger was created, how it has changed over time, and how it has affected their lives. We also discuss the future of the Visual Debugger and its impact on the field of software debugging.

Human motion is a fundamental aspect of many real-world phenomena, and thus, an important topic of study in many scientific disciplines. Higher-order derivatives of human movement, such as acceleration and jerk, are known to be closely related to human perception and behavior. As such, higher-order movement models are becoming increasingly popular among human motion analysts. However, these analysts typically lack the computational models and visualizations needed to effectively design and evaluate these higher-order movement models. To address this gap, we have developed HoLens, a novel visual analytics design that facilitates the modeling and visualization of higher-order human movement. HoLens incorporates three integrated components: (1) a data exploration tool for analyzing and selecting input data, (2) a movement model designer for creating higher-order movement models, and (3) a visualization tool for evaluating the designed movement models. To validate the effectiveness of HoLens, we conducted a user study with 17 motion analysts. The results of this user study demonstrate that HoLens effectively supports motion analysts in designing and evaluating higher-order movement models. In addition, we provide design insights for creating effective visual analytics tools and discuss the broader impacts of our research.

Deep learning methods have access to be employed for solving physical systems governed by parametric partial differential equations (PDEs) through differentiable physics. In this work, we propose a novel method based on deep reinforcement and imitation learners coupled with the adjoint sensitivity analysis of PDE solvers as well-posed inverse problems that can tackle uncertainty quantification tasks in high dimensions while providing continuous inference capabilities throughout parameter spaces at low computational costs compared against traditional Monte Carlo samplers or Bayesian inversion algorithms using Markov chain monte carlo sampling strategies such us gibbs sampler/Metropolis–Hastings algorithm etc.. Our approach is applicable across diverse domains including fluid dynamics simula

This paper introduces the novel task of multimodal puzzle solving, framed within the paradigm of multimodal retrieval. In this setting, we present a set of images and an instruction text, and the goal is to retrieve the correct solution from a given set of images. The retrieved image should be aligned with the instruction's context, as well as visually similar to the desired solution. To assess the performance of our models, we introduce a new evaluation metric for measuring similarity between solutions and retrieval results. We provide a detailed analysis of various approaches using both multimodal retrieval techniques and multi-modal vision-language alignment methods. Our results show that while these methods achieve significant improvements over single-modality baselines in terms of ret

Spatial resource allocation is a crucial optimization problem that arises in many real-world scenarios, including transportation, logistics, and infrastructure management. In general, the allocation of resources involves making decisions based on the current demand and the expected future demand. Recently, reinforcement learning (RL), a powerful data-driven optimization tool, has been gaining popularity for solving complex allocation problems. In this survey, we provide an overview of the applications of RL in spatial resource allocation. We present technical details of the underlying RL-based optimization models and discuss the computational complexity of these models. We then review the current state-of-the-art algorithms for solving these complex optimization models. Finally, we present some concluding remarks and discuss future directions for RL-based spatial resource allocation.

It is especially challenging to achieve real-time human motion tracking on a standalone VR Head Mounted Display (HMD) because of the high data rate and latency requirements. This paper introduces an efficient 3D camera pose estimation method for achieving both low computational complexity and fast processing speed, which are essential in obtaining accurate results within milliseconds from each frame captured by multiple cameras mounted at different positions around users' heads inside HMVs/VR devices while simultaneously avoiding any potential occlusions caused due their location near one another when compared against traditional single sensor approaches such as depth sensors like Kinect or stereo vision algorithms that require two separate units working together - making them much more co

Cedar is a new programming language that makes it easy to write expressive, efficient, safe, and analyzable authorization policies.
