It is a object-capability language, in which objects are modeled as types and capabilities as methods; it comes with a static type system that enforces type safety and method (or, capability) invocations; and it generates efficient machine code that is ready for execution. Cedar is expressive, in that it allows the fine-grained control needed for building authorization policies; it is fast, in that it incurs no runtime overheads and enjoys good data locality; it is safe, in that it enjoys the guarantees provided by a verified type system; and it is analyzable, in that it allows the static analysis tools to precisely reason about object properties.

The built environment is responsible for a substantial share of energy consumption and carbon emissions. Understanding and predicting how buildings are used is therefore crucial for developing strategies to make cities more sustainable. Building flexibility, the degree to which a building can change its energy demand and supply in response to external signals, plays a central role in this context. Yet, building flexibility is neither measured nor predicted in existing literature and policy frameworks, partly due to its complexity and the lack of data. This paper proposes a framework to quantify and predict building flexibility using machine learning methods. We use the example of residential buildings in Berlin, Germany, and identify three types of flexibility that can be derived from publicly available metered data: thermal, electrical, and overall flexibility. We find that buildings can be classified into three groups based on their flexibility profile, and that weather, occupancy, and building characteristics are the main drivers of flexibility. The results further show that machine learning models are better suited to predict flexibility than traditional statistical models. This framework can be used by city planners and building owners to identify flexible buildings in a cost- and time-efficient manner, and can contribute to the development of flexibility markets.

Deep learning models are often trained with more parameters than necessary, leading to opportunities for model compression and scaling. Model compression reduces the number of parameters in a model, typically by pruning or low-rank approximation, while model scaling increases the number of parameters. Recent work has shown that the performance of a compressed model can be further improved by fine-tuning after compression. However, existing methods often rely on static compression ratios, which may not be optimal for all tasks. To address this limitation, we propose Dynamic Configurable Exiting (DyCE), a method that allows for dynamic compression and scaling based on the task at hand. DyCE uses a binary mask to represent the connectivity between neurons, enabling both addition and removal of connections. We evaluate DyCE on a series of supervised learning tasks, including image classification, natural language processing, and time series analysis. Our results demonstrate that DyCE consistently outperforms existing methods, achieving optimal performance with either 1x or 2x fewer parameters than the baseline. Furthermore, DyCE shows strong robustness in out-of-distribution detection and adversarial robustness. We hope our work will inspire further research into dynamic model configuration.

With the great popularity of Graph Neural Networks (GNNs), their robustness to adversarial perturbations has become an increasingly important research topic. In this paper, we focus on GNNs in a fully connected graph and propose a novel attack method called "Adversarial Sampling". The core idea is to introduce a new node into the graph, then find a set of nodes that can be affected by this new node based on the structural similarity between the original graph and the modified graph. We first use the sampling strategy to generate a set of candidates, then utilize the structural similarity to select the final adversarial sampled nodes. Experiments show that our approach achieves state-of-the-art performance in attacking GNNs under various attack strategies, including Structural Adversarial A

Recent years have witnessed great success in handling graph-related tasks with Graph Neural Networks (GNN). In this work, we focus on the issue of model selection for GNN, aiming to address the problem that the performance is unstable and it is challenging to select an appropriate architecture. Recently, a new paradigm called Graph Convolutional Network (GCN) has been proposed, which can be viewed as a universal approximation method. However, it is not clear whether other models can be approximated by GCN, thus leading to the difficulty of choosing the optimal GNN architecture. To address these issues, we propose a simple yet effective strategy based on the idea of model selection and universal approximation theory. The proposed approach enables us to choose the most suitable GNN architect

Current scene flow methods broadly fail to describe motion on small objects, and current scene flow evaluation protocols do not test for this failure. In this paper we propose a new object-level scene flow method that provides reliable estimates of motion on small objects using only one stereo pair per frame, as well as a new object-level scene flow evaluation protocol. We benchmark the state of the art on our protocol and demonstrate that it is difficult to achieve high accuracy on both large and small objects simultaneously. Our object-level scene flow method, which performs well on our protocol, exploits optical flow computed from two stereo pairs to estimate the 3D shape of an object and then uses that shape to initialize a sparse particle filter, which tracks the object in subsequent

The rapid development of deep learning techniques, improved computational power, and the availability of vast training data has enabled new approaches for detecting abnormalities in medical imaging. However to develop accurate models that perform well across a broad spectrum of applications is challenging due largely because many such algorithms are trained on datasets from single institutions or hospitals (SDOH) with limited diversity leading them prone overfitting problems which can result inaccurate predictions when applied outside their original context .

The recent advancements in Generative AI have significantly advanced the field of text-to-image generation, enabling users to translate their imagination into visual forms. However, the lack of a comprehensive evaluation framework has hindered the development and adoption of these tools. To address this gap, we introduce GIQA, a large-scale open-source dataset of 102K images generated by 36 state-of-the-art text-to-image models. Our data includes 123K questions, 108K captions, and 40K human judgements, spanning various categories such as realism, novelty, coherence, quality, and diversity. We further present a comprehensive analysis of the generated images and provide a detailed comparison between different generations, highlighting the strengths and weaknesses of each model. Additionally,

Novels are complex emotional experiences for both readers and writers, but they are also challenging objects of scientific study due to their length, complexity, and subjectivity. Here we use computational methods to analyze the emotions portrayed in 5,000 literary novels from the English Open Access corpus, focusing on the dynamics of emotion distribution within and across novels. We find that novels tend to open with positive emotions and transition to more negative emotions as they progress; that negative emotions are more salient than positive emotions; and that certain pairs of emotions co-occur more frequently than others, providing support for previous claims that emotions function as narrative motivators. We also find that individual novels exhibit consistent emotion dynamics, that these dynamics are stable across different time periods, and that they can be used to create accurate species of novel, with certain emotion dynamics associated with certain species. Finally, we find that while our emotional annotations are consistent with human annotations, the emotions portrayed in novels have become less positive and more negative over time, with the largest changes occurring in the 19th century. This research demonstrates the value of computational methods for understanding the emotional dynamics of literary texts and for uncovering long-term trends in narrative representation.

We study the limits and capability of public-data assisted differentially private (PA-DP) algorithms, which are designed to protect privacy in federated learning by only using public data. We first introduce two PA-DP algorithms: a gradient descent algorithm, called PA-GD, and a stochastic subgradient algorithm, called PA-SSA. We prove that PA-GD converges at an $\epsilon$-rate, while PA-SSA only converges up to an $\epsilon$-order. The convergence rates depend on the local learning rate $\eta$ and the sampling size $m$. For both algorithms, we show that as long as $\eta \leq \frac{1}{2}$, the optimal sampling size is $m^* = O(\sqrt{\log(d)})$, where $d$ is the total number of devices.

In Multiple Object Tracking, objects often exhibit non-linear motion of acceleration and deceleration. These motions are rarely modeled in state-of-the-art trackers since they require expensive computation and introduce additional parameters. To address this issue, we propose a simple yet effective method called Acceleration Deceleration Motion (ADM) to model such non-linear motions by using a single parameter $D$ to capture the degree of acceleration or deceleration. ADM achieves this by exploiting two properties: (i) it is invariant to the direction of motion (ii) it is not affected by the magnitude of motion. The proposed ADM model is integrated into the state-of-the-art tracker SiamRPN+++, resulting in a new tracker called ADM-SiamRPN+++.

Dense Retrieval (DR) is now considered as a promising tool to enhance the memorability and salience of neural networks. However, in order for DR models with large parameter size like BERT-base or RoBERTa ($\approx 125M$) not exceeding memory limits on mobile devices during inference time ($<$30ms), it usually has been adopted that only top-$k$ queries are retrieved from their index before sending them through downstream classifiers such LMs - but this approach can lead towards significant loss when applied against small datasets containing less than $n\times k$-tuples per class where $\frac{T}{N} > \log_e(m)$; especially if these instances don't match exactly those found within training set!

Multi-tenant cloud computing provides great benefits in terms of resource sharing, elastic pricing, and on demand deployment. However it also introduces new challenges for data security because users share the same hardware with unknown cohabitants that are potentially malicious or compromised by adversaries outside their control domain (e..g., malware). This work proposes a novel approach to mitigate these risks using verifiable trusted execution environments running within commodity multicore processors via Intel SGX enclaves technology [1]. We propose an open source software stack called SecureCloud consisting solely from free/open components: 32bit Linux OS kernel version v4+ as host operating system; GNU C library glibc versions >=v2.

Research on scaled models has contributed significantly to the development of seismic design guidelines for RC structures. This paper presents a shaking table test on a scaled RC frame with intermediate ductility designed to fail in a specific mode to study the seismic behavior of ductile structures. The model is a 1/2.5 scaled version of a 4-bay by 3-bay 2.5D RC frame with a plan dimension of 8.3 m by 6.2 m. The frame is supported by four rigid legs, and the top is fully constrained in translation. The model is constructed with 3D printed parts and RC members made of fiber-reinforced polymer (FRP). The FRP members are cured ex-situ and have a higher strength and stiffness compared to conventional FRP. The frame is tested on a large-scale shaking table with input motion recorded from a real earthquake. A set of strain gauges, displacement sensors, and a video camera are used for data collection. The test is conducted in two phases. The first phase is a monotonic load test to determine the yield strength and the ultimate load capacity of the frame. The second phase is a cyclic load test to study the seismic behavior of the frame. In this paper, we present the test setup, testing procedure, and monitoring system. We also discuss the damage mechanism observed on the model frame during the test.

Metrics are the foundation for automatic evaluation in grammatical error correction (GEC), with their effectiveness depending on how well they correlate with human judgments. While several studies have demonstrated that automatic metrics for GEC do not perform as well as desired, little is known about the relationship between these metrics and human judgments at different levels of granularity. In this paper, we examine this relationship by looking into the correlation between automatic metrics and human judgments of individual sentences and corpus-level. We find that Pearson's correlation coefficient between human judgments and automatic metrics is always lower at sentence level than at corpus level, indicating a lack of consistency in scores provided by automatic metrics across sentences

Symmetry is a fundamental aspect of many real-world robotic tasks. However, current deep learning approaches do not leverage symmetry as part their representation or training and thus require large amounts data to learn the symmetries present in such settings from scratch. In this paper we propose an approach that utilizes equivariant representations combined with invariantly trained models for efficient task generalization over symmetric configurations without relying on prior knowledge about underlying structures within datasets .

Several tools have recently been proposed for assisting researchers during various stages of the research life-cycle. We refer to these as "research assistants". The aim of this paper is twofold: (1) identify a set that captures the diversity in recent efforts; and, more importantly,(2), provide guidance on how such assistant systems can be developed so they meet existing needs while also contributing novel insights into new forms of assistance needed by modern day scientists who are under increasing pressure from funding agencies not only publish papers but deliver results quickly too!

Identifying flood affected areas in remote sensing data is a critical problem in earth observation to analyze the impact of flooding and provide relevant support for disaster management. In this paper, we propose a novel algorithm, namely, Flood Detection with Generative Adversarial Network (FD-GAN), which combines the advantages of generative adversarial network (GAN) and convolutional neural networks (CNN). FD-GAN consists of two main components: an encoder module that extracts features from the input image; and a generator module that synthesizes fake images to discriminate between flooded and non-flooded regions based on the features extracted by the encoder.

Out-of-distribution (OOD) generalization in the graph domain is challenging due to limited OOD data and complex distributions. We propose a novel framework, named Graph-Adversarial Learning with Reasoning (GALR), for improving OOD generalization by incorporating reasoning into adversarial training. Specifically, we first devise a generative model based on graph neural networks to generate adversarial samples. To enhance their quality, we propose a new discriminator, which utilizes reasoning ability to differentiate between real samples and generated samples. Furthermore, we design an enhanced generator that can utilize both the generator's own outputs and the discriminator's reasoning results as auxiliary information. The proposed GALR method effectively improves OOD performance of graph c

High dynamic range (HDR) videos capture the full range of brightness in the real world with high resolution. Due to their high bit depth, HDR videos can store much more information than conventional videos, but they also require more storage and thus are less practical for real-time applications. To overcome this challenge, we propose HDRFlow, a novel real-time pipeline for HDR video reconstruction that can handle large motions. First, we develop a deep neural network (DNN) based estimator to predict the exposure compensation factor (ECF) using only the low-resolution (LR) input frame and its neighbors. This LR ECF estimation module (LRECM) is trained using a large dataset of LR frames and their corresponding ECFs. Then, we propose a motion compensation network (MCN) that takes the LR input frame and its neighbors as inputs and predicts the motion vectors (MVs) and the warped frames. Finally, we combine the LR ECF estimation and the MCN to develop a deep network for estimating ECFs of high-resolution (HR) frames. We also introduce a novel synthetic dataset, HDR100, consisting of 100 HDR videos that we use to evaluate and compare the performance of our method. Our experiments show that HDRFlow can reconstruct HR HDR videos in a real-time fashion, making it suitable for applications such as online streaming. Furthermore, HDRFlow can handle large motions, which is a challenging task for conventional methods.

Text-to-image diffusion models have witnessed a rapid development in recent years, accompanied by a proliferation of new applications and architectures. As the latest trend, diffusion models have drawn increasing attention for their impressive generation quality and controllable capabilities. However, the majority of existing surveys on text-to-image generation have focused on GAN-based methods, with few covering diffusion models. To fill this gap, we present a comprehensive survey of text-to-image diffusion models, particularly those with controllable capabilities. We begin by introducing the basic principles of diffusion models, including image denoising and reverse diffusion. We then elaborate on various diffusion-based methods for text-to-image generation, categorizing them into unconditional, supervised, and reinforcement learning-based approaches. We also discuss several key techniques and metrics for training and evaluating these models. Finally, we present an overview of the current challenges in this field and suggest potential directions for future research.

As robots are expected to become more and more involved in human environments, there is an increasing need to equip them with open-ended learning capabilities. However, robotics literature lacks a comprehensive taxonomy of the different forms open-ended learning can take. In this paper, we present a computational taxonomy of open-ended learning robots based on a systematic literature review of robot learning behaviours. We identified at least ten different learning behaviours, grouped into four categories: exploration, exploitation, self-learning, and social learning. We present a general description for each category, illustrating the typical characteristics of the learning robots that exhibit such behaviours. We also discuss the relationship between these categories and mainstream machine learning approaches. Finally, we propose a framework, based on the taxonomy, to help researchers design and evaluate open-ended learning systems.

Neural networks are vulnerable to adversarial attacks, and this vulnerability depends on the ambient dimension of the input space and the intrinsic dimension of the data manifold. We investigate the effect of the dimension gap on adversarial vulnerability using theoretical and empirical approaches. We propose a simple model to quantitatively analyze the relationship and demonstrate that larger ambient dimension relative to the intrinsic dimension makes the data manifold susceptible to small perturbations. We complement our theoretical analysis with experiments on synthetic and real datasets. Our results show that the empirical adversarial vulnerability increases with the ambient-intrinsic dimension gap.

The idea of computing with neurons dates back to the very beginning of the digital computer. John von Neumann proposed the concept of a neural-like computer in the 1950s, and since then, many have followed up on the idea. However, none of the proposals so far have managed to capture the full power of the biological brain, which is able to recognize patterns, learn new information, and adapt to changing circumstances. Here, we take a step toward capturing the full power of the biological brain by proposing a novel way to realize a neuron as a pattern recognition unit that is capable of unsupervised learning and continual learning. Specifically, we show that a single neuron can be viewed as a one-layer autoencoder, which has the ability to reconstruct itself and thereby learn and store patterns in its weights. We demonstrate the autoencoding property of a neuron using the formalism of probabilistic inference, and validate the learning capability of the neuron as a proof of principle. The proposed neuron model paves the way for a new class of neuromorphic computers that are capable of performing at the edge in a constantly changing environment.

We consider competitive facility location as a two-stage multi-agent system with two types of clients and an arbitrary number (k) facilities. In the first stage, agents compete for opening their own private k–1/2(n−m+b)/3+(x_0+\epsilon )^t/(a\cdot n^{s})public points belonging only to one client group in each point cluster; i.,e when there are multiple groups present within some region centered at this public center), we call it "centralized".

Neural networks have limited memory and often rely on external memory to improve their capabilities. However, accessing external memory incurs a significant performance overhead due to the involvement of the host CPU. This paper proposes Memoro, a novel approach that leverages large language models (LLMs) as an intermediate layer between the CPU and external memory, thereby reducing the frequency of CPU involvement and minimizing data access latency. We evaluate Memoro on two memory-augmented neural network architectures, namely Neural Turing Machine (NTM) and Stable Memory Network (SMN). Experimental results demonstrate that Memoro can improve the performance of both NTM and SMN by up to 1.7X and 2.2X, respectively, over a baseline approach that directly involves the CPU in memory accesses.

Large language models (LLMs) have shown impressive results in the past few years. However, recent graph-based benchmark results show that LLMs are poorly equipped to handle graph data. To address this limitation, we propose GraphInstruct, a novel pre-training method that enhances LLMs with graph understanding and reasoning capabilities. Specifically, GraphInstruct minimally modifies existing LLMs to include graph data processing capability. This allows the LLM to natively process graph data without requiring significant changes to the model architecture. We evaluate GraphInstruct on a variety of graph-based benchmarks and show that our method outperforms previous LLMs by a large margin. Our code is publicly available at https://github.com/THUMNLab/GraphInstruct.

This paper introduces Hierarchical Diffusion Policy (HDP), a hierarchical agent architecture for performing multi-stage decision making. The proposed approach leverages the potential of reinforcement learning to learn high level policies, while it combines them with hand coded strategies that can act as low levels in order address short term tasks efficiently and effectively by using domain knowledge instead relying solely on learned behavior from scratch via RL alone which would take longer time periods due its inherent exploration process required during training phase(s).

This paper presents improvements and evaluations on the MLCommons CloudMask benchmark. The latest version of the benchmark was released in May 2021 and consists of two tasks: cloud detection and cloud classification. In this work, we propose three improvements on the benchmark. The first improvement is a change in the cloud detection task. We update the task description and provide additional training samples to make the task more challenging. The second improvement is a new benchmarking framework that supports distributed training and allows us to scale the training process horizontally. The third improvement is a new set of baselines trained on larger models. We also provide a detailed analysis of the impact of the changes made to the benchmark. We re-evaluate all the existing methods on the updated benchmark and submit new results. Our experiments show that the CloudMask benchmark is robust to the changes made to the dataset and that our improvements are fair and reasonable. The updated benchmark is publicly available at https://github.com/mlcommons/benchmark/tree/master/cloudmask.

Boolean Satisfiability problems are vital components in Electronic Design Automation, particularly within the Logic Synthesis domain. This study investigs three different methods for solving such problems: (1) a novel Binary Decision Diagram based approach; (2) an evolutionary approach and (3) a combination of the two approaches. The proposed Binary Decision Diagrams (BDD) method is compared against previous results from existing research. We observe that the performance of our method compares favourably with previously published results when applied to specific instances of the problem. We also present a novel use of evolutionary algorithms in solving SAT problems by using them as heuristics to guide search in BDDs. Our approach achieves better performance than the binary decision diagram

Federated learning (FL) is a privacy-preserving distributed management framework based on collaborative training of multiple parties. It enables the data owners to protect their local sensitive information and achieve efficient global model optimization, while avoiding excessive reliance or exposure to third party servers for cloud computing resources during FL process in traditional centralized machine leaning methods [1]. The key steps include: (i). Data sampling; In this step all participants will choose an optimal sample set from existing dataset according certain requirements such as classification accuracy rate/execution time cost etc. ; then they send these selected samples along with corresponding labels into server side via Internet connection protocol like HTTP POST requ

Recently, because of the high-quality representations of contrastive learning methods, rehearsal sampling has been widely adopted for efficient speech recognition. However, it is known that the representation obtained in this case does not accurately reflect the speech characteristics. In this work, we propose a novel contrastive learning framework named Efficient Contrastive Learning (ECL) to improve the robustness and generalization capability of the learned representations by introducing the masking strategy into the contrastive loss function. To be specific, an important task in training an encoder is to learn how to identify similar and dissimilar instances in a mini-batch. ECL employs an adaptively adjustable mask based on the input instance's similarity with other instances in the s

Bilevel optimization refers to scenarios whereby the optimal solution of a lower-level energy function serves as an input in formulating an upper-level decision problem. The latter is often formulated using mixed integer programming (MIP), and its objective is to select the best inputs from among those that are feasible for the lower level. In this work, we consider the bilevel optimization framework with a single objective at both levels. We show how the dual variables of the MIP can be used as a proxy for the primal variables in the low-level optimization, thereby allowing us to leverage recent advances in mixed integer programming to solve our high-level problems efficiently. We also provide a tractable approximation scheme for the low-level problem which allows us to use a sampling str

One important desideratum of lifelong learning aims to discover novel classes from unlabelled data. In this paper, we study the generalisation performance of such a model as it learns new concepts. We show that even in the simple case where the model is trained on two tasks (i.e., one old task and one new task), the model often exhibits catastrophic forgetting for the old task, especially when the new task is large and the two tasks are dissimilar. This phenomenon has not been extensively studied before, despite its practical significance. Our main contribution is a theoretical analysis that shows how a model with similar-but-non-identical inputs can still perform well on both tasks by using a mixture of classifiers that each specialises in one task. Using this idea, we develop a new algor

Growing concerns about safety and alignment of AI systems highlight the importance of embedding moral capabilities in them. To address this need, we propose a multi-level approach to incorporating ethics into machine learning (ML) by augmenting ML tools with ethical considerations at every stage from data preparation through model evaluation as well... Machine Learning techniques are being increasingly used for security analysis on software artifacts like binaries or source code files containing sensitive information such that can be exploited via vulnerabilities present within those artefacts during runtime if not detected early enough prior deployment/execution phase(s). In recent years there has been an increasing focus towards developing automated approaches leveraging various Machine

Hardware development relies on simulations, particularly cycle-accurate RTL (Register Transfer Level) simulation. As the complexity of SoCs increases, so does the cost and time required to perform accurate RTL simulations. In this paper, we present a novel approach for accelerating cycle-accurate RTL simulations using graph convolutional networks (GCNs). Our approach leverages the temporal correlations within the execution trace and utilizes GCNs to predict the next instruction's effect on the control flow graph. This enables us to dynamically skip instructions, thereby reducing the overall simulation time while preserving accuracy. The evaluation on various benchmarks demonstrates that our approach is capable of accurately identifying the skipped instructions, resulting in a significant r

We present an analysis of 12 million instances of privacy-relevant reviews publicly visible on Amazon.com. Our goal is to understand how customers describe their experiences with products that collect and store private data, and how this information can be used by consumers to make informed choices in the marketplace. Using a combination of natural language processing techniques and manual coding we find that privacy concerns are discussed much more frequently than previously reported, and that they tend to be highly negative. This suggests that the majority of consumers who express their opinions about smart devices online do so in reaction to privacy issues. Furthermore, we find that these privacy concerns appear most prominently in the titles of reviews, suggesting that consumers are in

In this paper, we propose Neural Fractional Differential Equations (NFDE) as a novel representation for neural networks. NFDE expresses the state of a neural network as the solution of a fractional differential equation, thereby introducing fractional calculus into neural networks. NFDE has the capacity to enhance the solvability of complex problems, such as long-term dependencies in sequential data and anisotropic phenomena in spatial data, due to the memory and non-local properties of fractional calculus. As a fractional counterpart of Neural Ordinary Differential Equations (NODE), NFDE shares the advantages of NODE, such as flexible memory and smooth interpolation, while addressing their limitations, such as the inability to solve long-term dependencies and anisotropic phenomena. We demonstrate the effectiveness of NFDE on several benchmark problems, including long-term dependency estimation and image inpainting.

How political beliefs change in accordance with media exposure is a complicated matter. Some studies have argued that people's political beliefs are not influenced by the media, but instead by their personal values and background. However, other studies show that the exposure to different types of news media may lead to changes in political beliefs. The present study investigates this issue using data from the Pew Research Center (USA). We employ Bayesian factor analysis to identify 7 factors that explain changes in political beliefs. Our findings suggest that exposure to online news has the largest effect on attitudes towards social issues while TV news and social media have an influence on attitudes towards economic issues. Interestingly, the effects of TV and print media on social issue

Citing comprehensively and appropriately has become a challenging task with the explosive growth of research publications. Manually checking the references for each paper is time-consuming and error-prone. This work proposes an AI-powered citation verification system to automate this process. The core of our approach lies in utilizing large language models (LLMs) as a semantic search engine that allows us to compare citations against their corresponding research papers. In addition, we develop a novel methodology for LLM-based citation verification, which involves several crucial steps: data preprocessing, data augmentation, LLM fine-tuning, and citation verification. Our extensive experiments on various datasets demonstrate that our proposed approach achieves high accuracy levels in detec

Large Language Models (LLMs) have become integral components in various autonomous agent systems, including robots. However, the complex nature of LLM responses makes it challenging to effectively integrate them into robotic decision-making processes. In this paper, we introduce GUIDE: a novel and efficient method for integrating LLM outputs into robot decision making by leveraging symbolic representations. We demonstrate that GUIDE can be applied to various LLMs, including both open-source and commercial models, while minimizing the need for manual annotations or tuning. Our experiments show that GUIDE consistently outperforms state-of-the-art baselines across diverse benchmarks and scenarios. The code is available at https://github.com/gkang28/GUIDE.git. Video: https://youtu.be/1KQNH6D-p

When drafting question posts for Stack Overflow, developers may not accurately summarize the core problems in their questions. This can lead to confusion among readers and unanswered questions. We study how developers use natural language processing (NLP) techniques to craft effective titles and body text. Our survey of 1070 users who posted at least one question on Stack Overflow suggests that most (72%) use NLP to prepare their postings. However, less than half of these users use NLP tools to improve their content. To better understand user practices with respect to NLP preparation and application, we present a case study of a user who adopted different NLP approaches when drafting his first two posts. Through this analysis, we identify common NLP-related challenges faced by users when d

Context: Linked Data and, more recently, the W3C's Neighborhood Geometry Schema (NGS) have been proposed as possible solutions for sharing and connecting open data. Objective: We developed a connector to automatically publish NGSI-LD data from IoT devices into Open Data portals that expect data in the GeoJSON format. Method: The connector is based on a Mediator pattern that transforms data from one format (NGSI-LD) to another (GeoJSON) and uses the OAuth 2.0 protocol for secure API requests. Results: We published data from four IoT devices of the FIWARE ecosystem into the City of Barcelona's Open Data portal. Conclusions: Our experiments show that the connector successfully published data from IoT devices into the Open Data portal. The portal's users can now access this data without any additional configuration.

Complex networks are widely used to represent an abundance of real-world relations ranging from social networks, road networks, and brain connections. The task of community detection aims at segmenting a network into multiple communities based on the inherent structure present in these graphs. In this study, we investigate how a complex network's topology affects its community structure. We conduct a survey of community detection algorithms that are suitable for complex networks and identify their performance gaps for different topologies. Our evaluation shows that most state-of-the-art algorithms perform poorly on highly irregular topologies. To address this problem, we propose a novel method called BRAIN that leverages a Bayesian framework to infer the optimal number of clusters and thei

Evolutionary algorithms, such as Differential Evolution, excel in solving real-parameter optimization problems. However, they may struggle to handle discrete variables effectively due to their continuous nature and the lack of built-in mechanisms for handling discrete search spaces. In this paper, we propose a new evolutionary algorithm called Discrete DE (DDE) that specifically addresses the challenges associated with discrete optimizations. Our approach involves two main components: a binary representation scheme for the population members and an efficient mutation operator designed to promote the exploration of feasible solutions in the discrete search space. We test DDE on a diverse set of benchmark functions and compare its performance against other well-established algorithms. The re

Camouflage poses challenges in distinguishing a static target, whereas any movement of the target would make it stand out. However, in most real-world scenarios, the target undergoes a limited amount of motion. This paper investigates how to effectively exploit these small motions for target detection. We propose a novel learning-based approach that utilizes a motion-sensitive encoder and decoder with a discriminator to extract subtle motion features from video data and predict the location of the camouflaged target. Our model can leverage both spatial and temporal information, making it robust against variations in the motion patterns of targets. Extensive experiments show that our method can significantly improve the accuracy of target detection by leveraging subtle motions. Code is avai

Multi-Source cross-lingual transfer learning deals with the transfer of task knowledge from multiple labelled source languages to unlabelled target languages. Our previous work on multi-source cross-lingual transfer learning has focused on identifying the most important source languages for a target language, while ignoring the importance of the source languages' relationships (e.g., similarity and relatedness) in relation to each other and the target language. In this paper, we propose a novel approach that simultaneously learns the importance of the source languages for the target language, as well as their relationships to each other. We evaluate our proposed method using two datasets: a low-resource dataset for German-English translation and a high-resource dataset for English-German t

The increasing interest in Large Language Models (LLMs) within the telecommunications sector has led to the development of LLM-based tools for network monitoring. These tools aim to automate the identification and resolution of potential network issues. However, their performance may be affected by various factors, such as the quality of data used for training or the availability of relevant data. In this study, we examine the effectiveness of a state-of-the-art LLM-based tool (OpenAI's GPT-4) on a real-world telecommunications dataset. Our findings suggest that although the model exhibits promising results on certain tasks, it struggles with others. Specifically, we observe an error rate of 57% when identifying potential network issues, which is significantly higher than the 8% observed i

The growing interest in Large Language Models (LLMs) for specialized applications has revealed the need to incorporate domain knowledge into LLMS. However, fine-tuning existing language models on small amounts of data leads them towards memorization and overfitting which hinders their ability at generalization across domains/tasks or even questions that are slightly different from what they were trained with(which is referred as fewshot learning). To address this problem we propose a new method termed "Kinetics" based upon recently proposed multitask training methods like Contrastive Learning using Knowledge Graph Inductive Bias - CLIKGIB & Multistage Fine Tuning MFT . The Kinetics framework takes advantage not only by utilizing both these approaches but also through incorporating addition

The African continent lacks enough qualified teachers which hampers the provision of adequate learning support for learners. In South Africa, a country with over 50% of the population under the age of 24 years and a high unemployment rate, teachers are in short supply. This study explores how to address teacher shortages in South African schools by exploring alternative pathways into teaching through the lens of the social constructivist theory. A qualitative approach was adopted using interviews and focus groups as data collection methods. Data were analysed thematically using the ATLAS.ti software. Findings show that teacher candidates from alternative pathways into teaching have more practical experience than their counterparts who have gone through traditional university-based educatio

Real-time instance segmentation, a fundamental problem in computer vision, aims to achieve fast object detection and accurate object segmentation simultaneously. In this work, we propose CenterDisks, a fast and accurate real-time instance segmentation method. Motivated by the observation that the center points of objects can be approximately covered by a set of disks, we propose to utilize disks for instance segmentation. Specifically, we introduce a novel disk covering head to the two-stage Mask R-CNN framework, which can efficiently predict the instance-specific disk centers and radii. In the inference stage, we propose a lightweight post-processing step, namely disk non-max suppression, to further improve the segmentation accuracy. Without any bells and whistles, CenterDisks outperforms all existing real-time instance segmentation methods on MS-COCO 2017 Val, ADE20K Val, and Cityscapes Val. It achieves 82.7 box AP, 79.7 mask AP, and 75.9 disk AP at the inference speed of 100.3 FPS (disk AP is computed with a disk radius of 3.0).

Collaborative competitions have gained popularity in the scientific and technological fields. These competitions require a team of players to solve tasks, often with shared resources or knowledge, within certain time limits. However, due to their complexity, it can be challenging for participants to understand how they performed in comparison to other teams. This paper introduces an approach for scoring collaborative competitions that utilizes the game-theory concept of Nash Equilibrium (NE), which measures the optimal strategy for each player in a competition environment. We demonstrate the efficacy of our scoring method through a case study involving a real-world collaborative competition and compare its results against those obtained using two alternative methods. Our findings suggest t

Dialogue state tracking (DST) aims to record user queries and goals during a conversational session. The DST model can then be used for later tasks, such as generating answers or understanding the conversation. Many models are designed to learn from human-human interactions in an unsupervised manner, which is challenging since it requires large amounts of data. In this paper, we propose a novel approach called Pairwise Conversation Model (PCM). PCM uses a pair of users' utterances to predict whether they were both said by the same agent or not. We use this prediction to train a Transformer-based DST model on a dataset with only 150 dialogues. To our best knowledge, this is the first work that achieves DST using such small data. PCM outperforms baseline models on three metrics: accuracy, f1

Most existing news recommendation methods tackle this task by conducting semantic matching between candidate news and user representation to capture the contextual information of users. However, these approaches may fail in capturing implicit interests due their limited capability for handling long-tail content or noisy signals from sparse interactions such as clicks on irrelevant articles that do not reflect real interest changes over time windows (eg., 1 day). In order better handle cold start problem during initial training stage when no historical preference data available while maintaining high performance with sufficient amount labeled examples later acquired through traditional ways like click prediction models etc), we propose an approach called “Dual Loss Learning” which ut

Since the foundational work of William Labov on the social stratification of language (Labov, 1972), sociolinguists have consistently emphasized the importance of context. Social context is essential for understanding how language variation occurs, and it plays a crucial role in shaping communication dynamics across various linguistic communities. However, this paper suggests that social context does not necessarily encompass all dimensions of the human experience. We draw on theories from the natural sciences to argue that there are additional factors at play that should be considered when studying social variability in speech: namely, biology, neurology, and cognition. To do so, we explore the potential impacts of these underlying structures on language use through case studies in both e

Process mining has become a cornerstone of process analysis and improvement over the last few years. With this increase in popularity, there is an emerging need to incorporate machine learning into the field. In this paper we present the first dataset that captures the complexity of process mining tasks as well as introduces a framework for the evaluation of process mining algorithms using complex data. We collected human-annotated event logs from 20 real-world organizations across diverse industries. The collected datasets consist of 134 process models from which we created a benchmark for evaluating process mining algorithms. Through our experiments with a set of seven state-of-the-art process mining algorithms on this dataset, we show that the use of complex event log data enables bette

Chinese landscape painting has a unique and artistic style, and its drawing technique is highly abstract in both the brushwork and composition. The Chinese have always held mountains to be sacred objects; they are regarded as divine beings that protect humans from evil spirits or demons who live on earth but cannot enter into heaven above them without being blocked by their presence at each stage of ascension between world levels below ground level up until reaching an altitude where nothing else can exist except clouds - which means no more landmass so these mountain tops must represent heavens' doors before entering within it completely . In this course you will learn: 1) How draw basic shapes like hills/mountains with perspective lines (horizon), sky background color variation based off

Generative modeling of complex behaviors from labeled datasets has been a longstanding problem in decision making and reinforcement learning. The most common approach, behavior cloning (BC), consists of training an off-policy model using supervised regression on the labels as output targets to estimate future rewards for each state action pair observed during test time; this is typically followed by optimizing these estimates via policy gradient updates or other methods like Q/V function approximation + proximal policies that are known not be optimal under BC alone due their inability capture uncertainty arising form label noise associated with such models which can lead worse performance than simpler baseline approaches when applied directly without any additional tuning steps needed befo

Place recognition is crucial for tasks like loop-closure detection and re-localization. Single-view methods have achieved promising results, but are vulnerable to noise and outliers in features. In this paper, we propose a novel dual-view method to mitigate these issues by leveraging additional information provided by the second camera view. Our approach incorporates cross-view self-supervised learning to generate pseudo labels for unlabeled data. The method can be applied to both RGB and depth images. We propose two network architectures: one based on the PointNet++ architecture and another using the ResNet18 encoder. We also show that our approach can be extended to the task of semantic segmentation, with improved performance over the single-view method. Our results demonstrate the poten

People enjoy sharing "notes" including their experiences within online communities. Therefore, recommending notes aligned with the user's interests can help users find useful information in a timely manner and contribute to improving social interaction quality for community members as well [1]. In this study we investigate how note recommendation methods affect communication strategies among two types of people: (i) newcomers who have just joined an existing group discussion; or conversely those (ii), already engaged but not yet contributing any comments on the topic at hand (e., lurkers). Our research questions focus upon whether these different groups will adopt similar approaches when interacting through recommendations made by our system -- which may be influenced both positivel

Live programming provides feedback on run-time behavior by visualizing concrete values of expressions close to the source code. It enables developers to understand and improve their software more efficiently. However, live programming is hindered when expression values are large or complex, since the programmer has to read them from a textual representation. In this paper, we introduce a novel approach for visualizing data in live programs that maps complex data structures to 3D shapes. We describe how this mapping can be automatically derived from existing specifications such as type systems and object models. Our approach allows us to visualize values of any size and complexity with minimal effort. We implemented our approach as part of a live programming system called Pencil and demonst

Court View Generation (CVG) is a challenging task in the field of Legal ArtificiaL Intelligence (LAI). This work introduces Diverse-VQA, an innovative approach to CVG that employs a novel method based on Vision-Language Models (VLMs), specifically Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs). The proposed framework leverages the unique capabilities of MLLMs, particularly their multimodal nature and ability to generate coherent text from visual inputs. This approach is distinct from traditional methods that rely solely on textual data and require extensive preprocessing. Our model utilizes a comprehensive set of labeled images for training, resulting in an improved performance. Moreover, our approach incorporates an additional module designed to enhance the diversity of outputs generated by the

Small-scale robots hold great potential for targeted cargo delivery in minimally-inv asive surgery. However, current methods of controlling these devices are typically limited to direct physical manipulation by an external actor or indirect control through fluid flows and magnetic fields that can be imprecisely directed due the devices' small size (20–150μm). Herein we present our initial work towards developing autonomous motion planning algorithms specifically tailored toward 3D navigation with microrobots within constrained environments such as blood vessels using optical forces generated from laser beams focused on microspheres attached onto robot platforms via fiber optic cables connected externally at either end along their length(s) which allows us greater precision than other appro

Our work addresses the challenges older adults face with commercial Voice Assistants (VAs). We conducted a longitudinal study of 16 VA users in Singapore aged between 60 and 97 years. These individuals had varied levels of experience and comfort interacting with VAs, ranging from those who are not comfortable using VAs to long-term regular users. The results show that while the majority of participants preferred a human interlocutor over an AI assistant for non-technical questions, they still found the VAs useful. However, participants struggled with complex commands and understanding the system's replies. Many participants also expressed concerns about the privacy and security issues associated with using VAs. We also identified specific design considerations for improving the usability o

We study a natural application of contract design to search problems with probabilistic prior and exploration costs. The prior is over the distributions of a large class of stochastic games that are parametrized by an unknown vector $\theta \in {\mathbb{R}}^d$. We construct a contract which, given any number of random samples from the unknown distribution, outputs a fixed-length path in the search space that is guaranteed to be optimal in expectation. This result is obtained using a new algorithmic tool called contract-based sampling. Our contract uses the idea of sampling from the uniform distribution over the unit ball in ${\mathbb{R}}^d$ instead of from the prior itself. A key challenge in our analysis is to show that this simple approach suffices for learning the unknown parameter $\th

Open-set Intrusion Detection (OSID) aims to detect known attacks and reject unknown attacks (also called novelties) in a cost-effective way. OSID adopts the one-class classifier, which classifies data as normal or unknown, from the perspective of open-set learning. However, existing OSID methods face the problem that the performance of detecting known attacks is limited due to the data distribution shift between the training and testing phases. In this paper, we propose VAEMax, which fuses the one-class classifier and the attack generator to form a closed loop. The one-class classifier is used to distinguish normal data from unknown data, and the attack generator is employed to generate missing features caused by concept drift. VAEMax updates the one-class classifier with the generated data, and the two modules jointly learn the normal data distribution and the unknown data distribution. The experimental results on the NSL-KDD dataset demonstrate that VAEMax improves the detection accuracy by 13.1% compared with the baseline method.

The advent of the post-human era has blurred the boundary between the body and artifacts. This ambiguity has been further accentuated by recent developments in artificial intelligence, such as Large Language Models (LLMs), which have the capacity to produce human-like language. This raises questions about the distinction between physical and virtual entities, particularly in relation to law. A key challenge is that the legal system has not yet caught up with these technological advancements, which leaves significant gaps in its ability to effectively regulate this emerging area. In order to address this issue, we propose a framework for legally defining "virtual" entities based on their unique characteristics. Our proposed approach involves two main stages: (1) classifying "virtual" entiti

High dynamic range (HDR) imaging involves capturing a series of frames of the same scene at different exposure settings, and then combining them into one image to capture detail in both dark shadows as well bright highlights. While HDR has been around for more than 20 years now—since its invention by James Ferwerda back when he was still working on his Ph.D.—it’s only recently that it really came onto mainstream smartphones with Apple adding support for iPhone XS models last year along with HDR video recording from iPhone XR onwards [1]. To enable this feature though requires some kind sensor-based autoexposure control system which is able not just take multiple images but also combine these quickly enough so they can be displayed without noticeable motion blur or other artifacts caused th

Pandora's Box is a fundamental stochastic optimization problem, where the decision-maker must find a good alternative while minimizing the search cost of exploring the value of each alternative. In this problem, the decision-maker is usually assumed to have a budget constraint on the total number of alternatives that can be explored. We study Pandora's Box under a different, more general constraint: the budget on the expected value of the explored alternatives. This constraint arises in many practical settings, such as hiring a skilled worker or advertising a product, where the budget is determined by the total expected quality of the candidates or the expected customer utility. We show that Pandora's Box under expected value constraint is at least as hard as the classical problem with budget constraint on exploration. We mitigate this hardness by showing a simple, yet powerful, reduction from the general problem to the budget exploration problem. This reduction yields a randomized search strategy, called Pandora's Oracle, that finds a near-optimal solution by solving a sequence of budget exploration problems. Each of these problems can be solved by a polynomial-time algorithm, or even an FPTAS, by exploiting the special structure of the value functions. Pandora's Oracle is a randomized adaptive search strategy, but its expected number of iterations is bounded by a polynomial in the problem size. This bound can be further improved to a logarithmic level by a non-trivial application of the multiplicative weight method. In summary, we show that Pandora's Box under expected value constraint can be efficiently solved in a variety of settings, by leveraging the structure of the problem and the adaptivity of the search strategy.

This paper explores the transformative potential of computer-assisted textual analysis in enhancing research on literature for a wider audience. The focus is specifically upon literary criticism, and more broadly cultural studies - those fields that require deep knowledge about human communication but often lack systematic approaches to data processing techniques (such as text mining). In order address this gap between analytical skills required by researchers and practical application methods available today we introduce an open source software package called "textminer" which provides easy access into advanced algorithms without requiring any prior programming experience or specialized training courses/certificates etc..

The study of continuous-time information diffusion has been an important area of research for many applications in recent years. In this paper, we propose a new model to capture the dynamics underlying real world phenomena that are characterized by nonmonotonic changes and abrupt jumps with respect to time (eg., news spreading). The proposed approach is based on nonlinear differential equations which represent both linear growth along some intervals as well discontinuous evolution over others so it can be used effectively when modeling processes such like epidemics or rumor propagation within social networks where individuals may switch from one state into another at different moments due either natural causes(such disease)or external stimuli e eg advertising campaigns), wh

Diffusion models have achieved impressive performance in generating realistic 3D shapes. However, the generated shapes are often limited by an inherent 2D-to-3D lifting bias, which stems from the 2D-to-3D noise mapping in the latent space. Existing methods mainly solve this problem by designing more capable 3D diffusion models, yet are still limited in generating accurate 3D shapes. In this paper, we tackle this 2D-to-3D lifting bias in diffusion models from two perspectives: 1) enhancing the 2D-to-3D noise mapping and 2) introducing a 3D-specific regularization. For the 2D-to-3D noise mapping, we propose a 3D diffusion policy that imposes a 3D structure prior on the 2D noise map and guides the 2D-to-3D lifting. For the 3D-specific regularization, we propose a 3D consistency loss that encourages the 3D shape to be consistent with its own 2D view. Combined, our method enhances the capability of existing diffusion models to generate accurate 3D shapes. Extensive experiments on various shape categories demonstrate that our method successfully reduces the 2D-to-3D lifting bias and produces superior 3D shapes. Code, models, and shapes are available at https://genforce.github.io/3D-Diffusion-Policy/.

The performance of CLIP in dynamic facial expression recognition (DFER) task doesn't yield satisfactory results. To address this problem, we propose a novel DFER framework named Dynamic Face Expression Recognition with Adaptive Attention Fusion Module and Feature Reweighting module(DAFFR). The main contributions are twofold: First is the proposed adaptive attention fusion method to effectively combine multi-task features from different levels for better model generalization ability; Second one focuses on improving feature representation by introducing an effective reweigh strategy based upon gradient information flow analysis during training process which enables us achieve superior accuracy than baseline methods even when using fewer samples per class or lower resolution images compared o

Let $M$ be an $n\times n$ matrix of homogeneous linear forms over a field. We study the minimal polynomial $\mu_M(X)$ in one variable, which is irreducible by assumption and has degree at most $(2-\sqrt{2})^k$, where we denote with ${\rm trdeg}(K,\overline K)=d< \infty$. Moreover every element that generates this extension can represented as sums-of-$3$-products for all but finitely many variables; i.~e., it lies inside some sumset consisting of cubes formed from elements belonging to different spaces spanned by vectors lying on level sets corresponding either to principal or nonprincipal minors (or both) depending on whether they are even powers ($=0^t$) or odd ones ($\neq 1$).

The development and adoption of Vision Transformers and other deep-learning architectures for image analysis has spurred a rethinking in the way that images are encoded. While traditional methods encode pixels directly, modern approaches instead rely on self attention to model their relationships via large matrices or tensors with billions (or more) entries each time they process an input frame from video data [1]. These models produce representations which can be used as inputs into downstream tasks like object detection/tracking without any further processing needed by hand engineers - just plug them together! However it is worth noting there still exists some potential pitfalls when using these new techniques so we recommend reviewing our guide before getting started if you're intereste

Recommender systems are vulnerable to injective attacks, which inject limited fake users into the platforms. Existing defense methods rely on offline training with a certain number of real-user data and cannot defend against an online attack in time because they need more user visits for model learning or feature selection after being attacked by attackers’ injection strategy (egs., random ranking). However this can be avoided if we know how many injections have been performed so that only features used by those injected items would be removed from future recommendations without waiting too long before responding again once it has finished processing all requests coming through its network connection at any given moment during runtime execution cycle . In other words: If you knew exactly w

Modern consumer devices must execute multimedia applications that exhibit high resource utilization. In order to efficiently execute such code, it is imperative for the application developers and compilers targeting these platforms to have access at compile time or run-time information about how a given instruction set will behave on an underlying hardware platform in terms of power consumption as well performance parameters like execution cycles etc., which can be used by compiler optimizations [1].

Confidence estimation is a fundamental problem in machine learning. It refers to the task of predicting, alongside with a classification decision, a confidence score that reflects the reliability of the prediction. High-confidence predictions are only valuable if they are indeed correct. Thus, a reliable confidence estimate is crucial for decision makers to utilize the predictions. Despite the simplicity of its task, confidence estimation is challenging. It requires a careful calibration of the confidence scores output by the model, as raw scores are usually optimistic and need to be adjusted for reliable failure prediction. In this work, we revisit confidence estimation and provide a unifying overview of the existing methods. We categorize the techniques based on the type of the reference set used for calibration, which includes out-of-distribution data, validation data, or a surrogate model. We also propose a new method that relies on a self-supervised learning paradigm. Our experimental results show that the choice of the reference set has a significant impact on the performance of the confidence estimator. We compare the existing techniques and discuss the advantages of our proposed method.

Electroencephalography (EEG) representation learning is a preeminent yet under-discovered research topic. It aims to learn a general EEG representation from extensive amounts of unlabeled raw EEG data, without the need of manual feature engineering. This is in contrast to conventional EEG analysis which primarily relies on handcrafted features. Learning a general EEG representation has the potential to revolutionize how we analyze and utilize EEG data, opening up new possibilities for both research and clinical applications. However, the majority of existing methods focus on learning representations from other modalities, and there is a lack of research that leverages domain knowledge in the learning process. In this work, we propose a novel framework that incorporates prior knowledge into the representation learning process. We achieve this by employing a multi-task learning approach, where one task is EEG classification and the other is a pseudo-task that we design to introduce domain knowledge. To ensure the representation is useful for both tasks, we employ a contrastive learning strategy. Our knowledge-guided learning approach results in improved performance for both tasks. More importantly, we see a significant boost in the performance of the EEG classification task, which is the primary application we are interested in. Our code is available at https://github.com/davevatterott/ knowledge_guided_EEG_representation_learning

Rank correlation coefficients are widely used to evaluate the quality of rankings produced by neural rankers. However, rank correlation coefficients do not provide explanations for why a certain ranking is better than another one. In this paper, we propose a novel approach to evaluate the explainability of neural rankers. Our approach constructs explainability networks, which are graphs that capture the relationships between items and their rankings. We use these networks to compute explainability scores for individual items, which provide detailed explanations for their rankings. We also use the networks to compute the average explainability score of a neural ranker, which provides an overall evaluation of the ranker's explainability. We evaluate our approach on large-scale information retrieval datasets. Our results show that the explainability networks capture the intuition behind rank correlation coefficients. They also show that our approach produces explainability scores that are consistent with human intuition about the relative rankings of items. Finally, our results demonstrate that neural rankers can be evaluated in an explainable manner, which is important for applications where explanations are needed.

The $(k, z)$-Clustering problem in Euclidean space $\mathbb{R}^d$ is the task of partitioning a set of $n$ points into at most $k$ clusters such that the sum of the squared distances between each point and its cluster center is minimized. In this work, we study a variant of this problem where some of the points are allowed to be outliers that can be placed anywhere in the input space. We show that it is possible to approximate the optimal solution to within an additive factor of $O(1/z)$ by running the algorithm for the standard problem on a carefully chosen subset of size $n/z$, achieving an overall approximation ratio of $O(k/z)$. The algorithm runs in time $O((n+k) \log^3 n)$ and uses only $O(kn^{2/3} \log^4 n)$ space.

Effective action abstraction is crucial in tackling challenges associated with large action spaces in Imperfect Information Games (IIGs). We propose an approach based on reinforcement learning and action abstraction to tackle the action space problem in IIGs. Our method first uses a policy network to generate actions, then employs a value network to evaluate the corresponding game states. The value network is trained using both the generated state-action pairs and state-reward pairs obtained from interactions with the environment. This results in a policy network that can generate good actions while considering the rewards gained through those actions. We demonstrate the efficacy of our proposed approach through extensive experiments on two representative IIGs, namely Rock-Paper-Scissors a

The emergence of LLMs has ignited a fresh surge of breakthroughs in NLP. However, the current paradigm of pre-training followed by fine-tuning for each task faces two key challenges: (1) the lack of large-scale labeled data for specific tasks; and (2) the limited capacity of LLMs to adapt to domain knowledge, resulting in suboptimal performance on downstream tasks. To address these issues, we propose DomainSpec, a novel approach that introduces specialized domain knowledge into the language model through a two-stage training process. Firstly, we employ domain-specific datasets to train a lightweight domain-adapted encoder for LLM. Secondly, we apply this encoder to perform domain adaptation on the LLM's pretrained parameters. Our method effectively combines domain knowledge with LLMs and s

Imitation learning presents an effective approach to alleviate the resource-intensive and time-consuming process of building data-driven systems by leveraging human demonstrations. However, in real applications, these systems often encounter unseen situations that are not covered in demonstrations, leading to suboptimal behavior or even failure. Therefore, we propose a novel framework called Dual Guided Imitation Learning (DGIL), which is designed to explicitly address the challenge of handling unseen situations. Specifically, our approach employs both policy and environment knowledge to guide the imitation learning process. To obtain the environmental information, we introduce a dual guidance mechanism for imitating and predicting behaviors based on both the demonstrated state-action pair

We propose and explore a new split aggregation approach for meta-reinforcement learning (MRL). Our proposed method, named SplAgger, learns a split function to separate the task dimension into easy and hard tasks, and an aggregation function to combine the predictions from these two functions. The split function is learned by a ranking loss, and the aggregation function is a simple weighted sum. SplAgger is a general approach that can be applied to different MRL algorithms. We show that our method improves the performance of state-of-the-art MRL algorithms on the MAML-EMR benchmark, especially under fewer training tasks. Our code is available at https://github.com/EliSchwartz/SplAgger .

Video motion magnification is a technique to capture and amplify subtle motion in a video that is often invisible or not easily detected by the human eye. This can be useful in various applications such as surveillance, sports analysis, and medical imaging. While this task has received significant attention in the computer vision community, the majority of existing methods are designed for RGB videos and do not effectively handle videos with multiple channels, such as depth maps and optical flow. This study focuses on developing novel techniques tailored for handling multiple-channel video data, which we refer to as multichannel video magnification (MVM). We introduce two frameworks: Channel-Wise Feature Magnification (CWFMag) and Channel-Aware Spatial Attention Magnification (CSAMag). CWF

Tables play a crucial role in conveying information in various domains, serving as indispensable tools for organizing and analyzing data. However, the process of extracting useful information from tables is often hampered by the presence of noise, which can lead to misinterpretation or incorrect conclusions. This paper presents an innovative method for enhancing the clarity of tabular representations by employing artificial intelligence (AI) techniques to remove noise from tables. Our proposed approach involves three key stages: (1) noise detection, where we identify potential noise elements within each table; (2) noise removal, wherein the identified noise elements are eliminated through AI-based methods that leverage machine learning algorithms trained on noise patterns; and (3) table re

Recent studies have investigated utilizing Knowledge Graphs (KGs) to enhance the performance of recommender systems. We propose a novel framework that integrates KGs with Neural Collaborative Filtering and utilizes embeddings from both domains, which we call NeuRec-KGEmbed; it is based on two main observations: 1.) Embedding models are powerful methods for extracting latent features in KG data sets as well users' preferences over items can be seen through their interactions or relations within this context - thus making them an ideal candidate model type when applying neural collaborative filtering techniques such those employed by existing frameworks like LightGCN [2]. The proposed approach leverages these insights while also taking advantage off advanced graph embedding technologies deve

Out-of-distribution (OOD) generalization in the graph domain is challenging due to high data diversity and large model parameter spaces. Recently, contrastive learning has shown promising results on improving ODD generalizability for tabular datasets by exploiting latent representations learned from unlabeled samples through self or mutual supervisions between label space pairs of similar/dissimilar instances based upon their semantic similarities as measured via a distance metric called embedding similarity measure(ESM).

There is a well-documented need for multilingual natural language processing (NLP) resources, tools, and models, especially for low-resource languages. This need is especially acute in the domain of health, where insufficient resources can put patients' lives in danger. This paper introduces gaHealth, a manually annotated English-Irish bilingual corpus of health data. The corpus consists of 7,000 manually annotated parallel sentences extracted from patient notes, along with part-of-speech tagging, sentence-level sentiment analysis, and named entity recognition. This mixed-methods approach allows for both quantitative and qualitative analysis of the data. We use our new corpus to compare the performance of existing multilingual NLP models, to fine-tune state-of-the-art English health-language models for use in an Irish context, and to extract valuable insights for future research in multilingual health data analysis.

Mixtral, a representative sparse mixture of experts (SMoE) language model, has achieved state-of-the-art (SOTA) performance on various benchmarks with only 40 billion parameters. However, it faces challenges in training and deployment due to its large size and high cost. To address these issues, we propose MixtralLite, an efficient version of Mixtral that significantly reduces the training time and parameter size without compromising accuracy. Specifically, our method involves three key strategies: (i) a novel data preprocessing approach based on GPT3.5-turbo, which efficiently compresses large datasets; (ii) a lightweight sparsity pattern generation algorithm, which effectively selects expert weights for efficient training; and (iii) a novel pruning method tailored to SMoE models, which f

We investigate the optimization dynamics of gradient descent in a non-convex and high-dimensional setting, where each dimension can be considered as an individual local loss function. By leveraging tools from random matrix theory and stochastic calculus, we derive several fundamental properties of the iterates generated by gradient descent in this setting. In particular, we show that, in expectation, all the iterates can be seen as a sum of independent copies of a single Gaussian random variable. This allows us to establish explicit convergence rates for gradient descent on non-convex high-dimensional landscapes. Our results provide valuable insights into the behavior of gradient descent under these assumptions, shedding light on its underlying stochastic dynamics and facilitating a better

Hints are a common feature in AI-driven learning environments. However, the cognitive load of processing hints can vary greatly depending on their formulation. Some users may find certain hints confusing, while others may find them helpful. Yet, the majority of AI-driven learning environments provide generic hints, without considering the individual differences in users' cognitive abilities. In this paper, we investigate the effects of individual differences in users' cognitive abilities on their perceptions of hint helpfulness and their learning outcomes. In particular, we personalize the formulation of hints based on users' cognitive abilities, and evaluate the effects of this personalization approach on users' perceptions and learning. We used an experimental method with 3x2 factorial design to assign participants to conditions: (1) concrete or abstract hints, (2) with or without personalization, and (3) for a domain familiar or unfamiliar to the participants. Our results show that personalization of hints based on users' cognitive abilities can improve their perceptions of hint helpfulness, and this, in turn, can improve their learning outcomes. We found no significant differences in perceptions of hint helpfulness based on the domain familiarity. We expect that our results will encourage designers of AI-driven learning environments to consider the cognitive abilities of their users when designing the support features in their environments.
