In the domain of image layout representation learning, the critical process of translating image layouts into succint yet expressive representations is usually referred to as layout embedding. While considerable progress has been achieved in recent years by leveraging deep neural networks and large-scale datasets (euivalent), existing approaches are typically trained on a single modality data source with an exclusive focus solely directed towards either visual or textual information about images' content structure; this neglects complementary aspects that can be gained from both modalities together when developing effective models for understanding complex real world scenes suchas those foundin social media platforms like Instagram where users often post photos containing multiple o

Radio Frequency (RF) device fingerprinting has been recognized as a potential technology for enriching data sets with mobile devices' information. Although existing studies have demonstrated its effectiveness in detecting malicious devices, the problem of distinguishing benign devices from each other remains under-explored. This study aims to address this gap by introducing a novel approach that leverages semantic embeddings to extract high-level features from RF signatures and classify devices into unique groups based on their similarities. To ensure reliable performance, we evaluate the accuracy of our method using multiple datasets and investigate its robustness against various attacks. Our findings reveal that our proposed approach is effective at clustering similar devices, providing

Robotic manipulation of deformable, one-dimensional objects (DOOs) like ropes and belts is essential for a variety of applications. However, the nonlinear, contact-rich dynamics of these systems make them challenging to model and control. In this work, we present a deep reinforcement learning approach that enables an anthropomorphic robot arm to autonomously learn to manipulate DOOs, including the ability to tie knots, in simulation and on real hardware. We develop a novel reward function that encourages the robot to grasp DOOs, move them to desired locations, and tie knots by making use of the robot's state information. This reward function can be used with any state representation, allowing the method to scale to arbitrary DOO sizes. Our method requires no prior knowledge about the task,

Runtime Verification (RV) refers to a family of techniques in which system executions are observed and monitored by automated tools. RV is an important technique for verifying the correctness of cyber-physical systems as it provides a powerful means to express properties of interest, without requiring any prior knowledge about the implementation or structure of the system under verification. In this paper we describe our experience with applying runtime verification to the task of verifying security requirements in smart grid scenarios. We use the SALSA runtime verification framework and develop two case studies: one concerning the authentication of smart meters, and another about the integrity of a smart meter's measurements. This work shows that it is possible to use runtime verification

Recent growth in the popularity of large language models has led to their increased usage for summarizing text. In this work, we study the use of large language models as summarizers by using them to generate summaries for scientific papers, a task that requires a specific style of summarization due to the need for accurate and concise descriptions of complex concepts. To assess the quality of these summaries, we introduce the Scientific Summarizer Evaluation (SSE) dataset, consisting of 60K pairs of original research articles and human-generated summaries, annotated with four different evaluation metrics: BLEU, ROUGE, CIDEr, and METEOR. We perform experiments on two model types: an encoder-only model based on LLaMA3-7B, and an encoder-decoder model based on GPT-4.

With the growth of internet of things (IoT) devices, cyberattacks, such as botnets and distributed denial-of-service (DDoS), are on the rise. According to Akamai's 2018 DDoS attack trends report, there was a 37% increase in the number of attacks between 2016 and 2017. As IoT devices become more prevalent and interconnected, they will be increasingly vulnerable to these attacks. One way to mitigate this threat is through the use of blockchain technology. A blockchain is a decentralized database that stores information across multiple devices. It uses cryptography to secure transactions and ensure that data cannot be modified or deleted. Blockchain can provide several benefits for protecting IoT devices from cyberattacks. First, it can help to prevent DDoS attacks by using distributed ledger

Contrastive learning is a popular unsupervised approach to learning representations, but its efficacy in facilitating inference and planning remains elusive. To this end, we propose a novel theoretical framework that establishes a clear roadmap for understanding how contrastive representations can enable inference and planning. We first identify two key properties, i.e., discrimination and generalization, that any inference-friendly representation should possess. Then, we show that contrastive representations, by design, satisfy both properties under commonly used temperature parameter settings. Finally, we provide a unifying view of inference via interpolation, through which we demonstrate that discrimination and generalization enable faster convergence of the interpolation weight to the optimal value. Our theoretical results shed light on why and how contrastive representations can be a good choice for inference and planning. We complement our theoretical analysis with experiments. Issues addressed include the effect of temperature, the impact of batch sampling, and the convergence of the interpolation weight.

We propose a new paradigm for multimodal fusion, one that is based on the idea of context. We start with a simple premise: different modes of information often have different contexts. For example, in a retail store, the context of customers is quite different from that of products. This simple premise leads us to a new way of thinking about multimodal fusion. In particular, it suggests that we should first identify the context of each mode of information, and only then combine the modes in a way that is appropriate for the identified contexts. This context-based paradigm for multimodal fusion is general and applies to all modes of information, unifies past approaches, and suggests new ones. As a first step towards realizing this new paradigm, we propose a method for fusion of text and images based on distributional context. Our method improves on established baselines for multimodal fusion on two standard datasets.

Object spatial locality, the tendency of objects to be accessed spatially close to each other, is an important performance-related property in C++ programming. This paper presents a technique to control object spatial locality by means of a collective allocator abstraction. A collective allocator is an abstraction that represents the aggregate behavior of a set of allocators. We define and prove the properties of a collective allocator that is sufficient to guarantee object spatial locality at the level of abstraction. We present an implementation of the collective allocator abstraction in terms of a template class. We show that the use of the collective allocator incurs no runtime overhead for object allocation and deallocation. We demonstrate that the collective allocator can be used to control object spatial locality in a set of realistic benchmarks.

Large Language Models (LLMs) have demonstrated great potential in complex reasoning tasks, yet their performance often falls short of human-level intelligence. Deep learning techniques typically require large amounts of annotated data to train models with high accuracy and generalization. However, obtaining sufficient training datasets for highly specialized use cases is challenging due to the limited availability of labeled data. In this paper, we propose a novel approach based on self-supervised pretraining using large unlabeled corpora followed by fine-tuning with small labeled datasets. This approach leverages the ability of LLMs to generate large quantities of coherent text for self-supervision. The generated data is used to train an encoder-decoder model that is then optimized throug

Recent work has shown that masked image modeling (MIM) with Transformers excels in predicting the missing patches in masked images. However, the lack of a comprehensive theoretical understanding of how Transformers learn to model feature-position correlations in MIM hinders the scaling and application of Transformers to larger vision tasks. In this work, we provide the first theoretical analysis of how Transformers learn feature-position correlations in MIM. We demonstrate that Transformers can learn feature-position correlations if the position information is sufficiently informative for predicting the missing patches. We further show that self-attention modules can be viewed as implicit feature-position correlation models, where the learnable position embedding interacts with the visual features in a linear manner. Inspired by our theoretical findings, we propose a simple yet effective position-aware token sampling method that explicitly models the position information in a tokenized image. Extensive experiments on various MIM tasks demonstrate that our method improves the performance of existing Transformer-based models, such as ViT and Swin. We also show that our method achieves state-of-the-art performance on the challenging DINO dataset, a benchmark for evaluating the robustness of MIM methods. Our code is publicly available at https://github.com/ziplab/Transformer-MIM.

Wildfires are one of the most destructive natural disasters, and their behavior is extremely difficult to predict due to the complex interaction of fire, air, and terrain. Therefore, it is crucial to develop accurate simulation models for wildfire propagation. In this work, we propose FLAME Diffuser, a novel image synthesis approach for generating realistic wildfire images and their corresponding heatmaps. Our method is based on diffusion models, which have recently achieved impressive results in image synthesis. To ensure the realism of the synthesized images, we introduce a mask-guided diffusion training scheme. This scheme allows the model to focus on the fire area and learn the characteristics of wildfire images better. Furthermore, we propose a novel diffusion-based image inpainting approach to fill the masked out areas, which is inspired by the image synthesis results of FLAME Diffuser. Our experimental results demonstrate that FLAME Diffuser produces high-quality, realistic wildfire images and heatmaps. The proposed diffusion-based image inpainting approach shows superior performance compared to existing methods.

The increasing interest in Large Language Models (LLMs) within the telecommunications sector has raised a number of concerns, including potential security vulnerabilities. This article will highlight some common LLM-based attacks and provide insights into how telecom operators can mitigate them effectively to ensure secure networks that are safe from malicious actors who aim at exploiting flaws or weaknesses present across various stages throughout their infrastructure stack — protecting both customer data as well network integrity alike!
