Sharding enhances blockchain scalability by dividing the network into shards, each managing a subset of transactions. This approach improves throughput and transaction cost efficiency. However, it also introduces new challenges such as shard coordination (i.e., synchronizing transactions across shards) and security, especially when the number of shards grows. To address these issues, we propose Scalable, Secure, and Efficient Sharding (S3), which incorporates consensus protocols from distributed ledger technologies (DLT) with sharded storage in cloud computing systems. The proposed system consists of two main components: a leaderless PoW-based DAG chain for sharding consensus and shard registration; and a sharded data store for storing and accessing data on distributed servers. The system'

The existence of adversarial attacks on machine learning models imperceptible to a human is still quite a challenge. Conversely, the ability for humans to detect such attacks has been explored in various studies that have not yet reached conclusive results. In this paper we present a series of experiments investigating how humans recognize the presence of an attack when presented with natural images and the corresponding prediction by a trained classifier. We also look into the human ability to distinguish between the presence or absence of an attack given only the original image as input. Our findings indicate that human can successfully detect the presence of an attack if they are familiar with the specific attack strategy used, but their ability to identify the attack is limited if they

Privacy concerns loom large over the future of artificial intelligence. Differential privacy (DP) is a widely accepted standard for providing privacy guarantees in a variety of machine learning tasks. However, DP comes at the cost of reduced utility. Thus, an important goal in the field of private learning is to develop methods that can provide high utility while still maintaining strong privacy guarantees. In this work, we propose a novel differentially private image captioning model. Our model learns to generate image captions in an entirely different representation space, which we show can provide higher utility than traditional methods. We test our theory on the Flickr30k dataset and show that our method outperforms non-private baselines and prior differentially private baselines.

In this paper, we study nonparametric estimation of instrumental variable (IV) regressions with weak IVs. We consider the setting where a linear regression model is subject to endogeneity bias due to the presence of unobserved confounders in both treatment and outcome. The primary contributions are twofold: First, we propose a novel estimator that is built upon a new class of nonparametric IVs based on kernel density estimation. Our approach extends the existing literature by incorporating data-driven methods for constructing the nonparametric IVs, which can effectively utilize the information from both the treated and untreated samples to improve estimation efficiency. Second, we establish the consistency of our proposed estimator under very general conditions. Specifically, we assume onl

Tap accuracy is an essential metric for assessing the usability of a mobile app. Previous work has shown that tap accuracy is predictable by the visual properties of the target element and the finger movement properties of the user. However, these properties are not always available, measurable, or controllable, which limits the applicability of previous models. To explore alternative prediction approaches, we collected a new dataset (n=1,009) that includes tap accuracy scores and detailed HTML structures of the tapped elements. We found that tap accuracy is predictable (R² > 0.7) by the reverse-engineered structures alone, without the visual properties. The structures capture the intent behind the visual design and can be extracted automatically from any HTML page. Therefore, our model can be used to assess the tap accuracy of existing or hypothetical user interfaces before they are realized as visual designs.

Motor imagery classification based on electroencephalography (EEG) signals is one of the most promising approaches to enhance the efficacy and safety of neuroprosthetic devices. We introduce a novel hybrid framework, namely M-CNN-BERT-RNN-GNN, that effectively combines convolutional neural networks (CNNs), bidirectional recurrent neural networks (BRNNs), bidirectional long short term memory networks (BLSTMs), and graph neural networks (GNNs). This approach utilizes the CNNs' capacity for image feature extraction, BRNNs' capability for temporal sequence analysis, BLSTMs' efficiency in learning long-term dependencies, and GNNs' potential for modeling complex interdependencies among neuronal populations.

The transportation sector remains a major contributor to greenhouse gas emissions. The understanding of energy conversion efficiency and the development of high-energy density batteries are crucial for reducing carbon footprints in this sector. In this work, we propose a novel two-terminal battery model based on an electrochemical double-layer capacitor (EDLC) with nonlinear diffusion-reaction kinetics. This model is used to capture the effects of concentration polarization at the electrodes that occur due to ionic transport limitations. A simplified version of the model is also proposed for analytical tractability. Numerical simulations demonstrate that the proposed model can accurately reproduce the experimental results of a battery cell under different operating conditions. Furthermore,

The $(k, z)$-Clustering problem in Euclidean space $\mathbb{R}^d$ aims to partition $n$ points into $k$ clusters of size $z$, where each cluster is represented by a convex body. We present a novel and efficient algorithm for solving the $(2,1)$-clustering problem with linear programming relaxation. To achieve the optimal objective value, our algorithm first identifies all possible clusters using a branch-and-bound method. Then it computes a clustering solution that maximizes the linear programming objective, which leads to an approximate optimal solution. The performance of the proposed algorithm is compared against other algorithms for the $(2,1)$ clustering problem on synthetic data sets, showing superiority over existing methods. Finally, we also show that our algorithm can be applied t

Robotic locomotion in cluttered and unstructured environments is challenging, especially when dealing with unexpected interactions, such as foot-mud contact. This paper presents a novel, reduced-order model for predicting the resistive force generated when a robotic foot contacts mud. We utilize a biomechanical approach to modeling foot-mud interaction and employ a deep neural network to learn the relationship between the contact area and the resulting resistive force. To train the deep neural network, a data set was collected using a multi-fingered robot foot interacting with a wide range of mud consistency. After training, the deep neural network can predict the resistive force given the contact area between the foot and mud. We also show that the proposed deep neural network approach can be used to augment a whole-body dynamic model, improving the robot's ability to locomote in cluttered and unstructured environments. Finally, we demonstrate that the proposed reduced-order model is 12 times faster than a previous high-fidelity model.

Treatment failure with the antiplatelet drug clopidogrel is associated with adverse cardiovascular events. As treatment failure can occur in the first year of treatment, monitoring treatment response over time is important. Machine learning models that predict treatment failure from electronic health record (EHR) data could support clinical decision-making. In this study, we applied federated learning (FL) to develop prognostic models for detecting clopidogrel treatment failures. FL is an emerging collaborative machine learning paradigm that trains a global model across multiple sites without sharing local data. We applied FL to EHR data from 3,095 patients with coronary artery disease treated with clopidogrel at 11 hospitals in Australia. We compared the performance of our FL models with published models developed using data from a single site. Our FL models performed better than the single-site models (by 4.5% on average), with greater improvements for models predicting earlier treatment failures (by up to 11.7% compared with the best single-site model). Our study is the first to apply FL to detect clopidogrel treatment failures. By improving the accuracy of predictions, FL has the potential to improve patient outcomes.

Large pre-trained Vision-Language Models (VLMs) like CLIP have achieved state-of-the-art performance on various vision tasks. However, these models are only evaluated using small-scale benchmarks that do not necessarily reflect real-world scenarios. In this paper, we propose a large-scale dataset for evaluating the generalization ability of VLMs in vision tasks. We construct the dataset by selecting 10,000 images from 35 popular image datasets and assigning each image a unique title. The titles are designed to represent diverse concepts that are likely encountered in real-world visual scenarios. These include common nouns, adjectives, actions, and more abstract categories. Our dataset covers both image generation and classification tasks.

Leveraging pre-trained visual language models has become a widely adopted approach for improving performance in downstream visual question answering (VQA) applications. However, in the specialized field of medical VQA, the scarcity of available data poses a significant barrier to achieving reliable model generalization. Numerous methods have been proposed to enhance model generalization, addressing the issue from data-centric and model-centric perspectives. Data augmentation techniques are commonly employed to enrich the dataset, while various regularization approaches aim to prevent model overfitting, especially when training on limited data samples. In this paper, we introduce a method that incorporates gradient-guided parameter perturbations to the visual encoder of the multimodality mo

Architectural Knowledge Management (AKM) involves the organized handling of information related to architectural projects and processes. It aims at increasing efficiency, productivity as well sharing expertise within organizations by providing a system that supports both individuals in their daily work tasks but also enables teams with cross-functional knowledge transfer across disciplines throughout all phases from conception up until completion . The goal is achieved through integration between different types such systems – which include document management tools like file repositories or databases where data can be stored securely along side collaborative software platforms enabling users access via web interface browser extensions allowing them collaborate more effectively than ever b

Open Radio Access Network (RAN) deployments promise to bring openness and intelligence to the traditional closed and proprietary RAN technology. However, open RAN deployments face many challenges, including the need for new security approaches to protect the integrity and confidentiality of the open network functions (ONFs). Traditional trust models are no longer applicable in open RAN deployments, and new security approaches are needed. In this paper, we introduce Zero Trust Security (ZTS) as a suitable security approach for open RAN deployments. ZTS requires that all ONFs be authenticated and authorized before they are allowed to communicate with each other. ZTS also requires that all communications between ONFs be encrypted. We show that ZTS can be implemented in open RAN deployments using xApps. We develop a set of ZTS xApps for open RAN deployments and deploy them in a real-world open RAN testbed. We evaluate the performance of the ZTS xApps in our testbed and show that they can be used to secure communications between ONFs. We also discuss the challenges of deploying ZTS in open RAN deployments and propose some potential solutions.

Virtual memory is a cornerstone of modern computing systems.Introduced as one of the earliest instances of software-based hardware extensions, virtual memory has evolved in tandem with hardware and software innovations for over 50 years.Today's high-performance machines have large, complex address spaces that support a diverse set of workloads, and are often tightly coupled to specialized hardware. However, these advanced features come at the cost of increased complexity, making it difficult for developers to leverage them effectively. In this paper we introduce VirtVM: an open source framework that enables developers to quickly evaluate and optimize their applications for contemporary computer architectures. VirtVM uses simulated memory management to provide a fast development environment

Artificial intelligence (AI) assistants such as GitHub Copilot and ChatGPT are becoming more commonplace in the software development process. These tools have been praised for their ability to assist developers with tasks like code completion, debugging errors or generating documentation from source files – but they also raise questions around copyright infringement when it comes time review contracts involving AI-generated content! The use of generative artificial intelligences has become increasingly popular among individuals interested in creating art that is both unique yet still conveys a sense authenticity .

Policy gradient methods are a vital ingredient behind the success of modern reinforcement learning. Modern policy gradients leverage stochastic optimization to approximate intractable integrals over continuous action spaces, but this comes at high computational cost compared with simpler algorithms which do not perform such approximations (e.\ g., Q-learning). We present \textbf{R}obust and efficient\textquotesingle{}s~\textit{\bf(RE)}~policy evaluation for deterministic control problems in discrete state space via an analysis that combines ideas from robust statistics (\emph{$L_1$}\/--norm) as well general theory about function approximation under random inputs using Bernstein polynomials coupled into novel bounds on Bellman error propagation through value iteration steps; these result di

This paper presents a new and improved DareFightingICE platform, a fighting game platform with machine learning capabilities. This platform uses reinforcement learning to autonomously train agents for multi-agent systems, which is particularly useful in competitive environments such as fighting games. The DareFightingICE platform has been designed with open source software and an easy-to-use interface, making it accessible for both experienced developers and those who are new to AI. In addition, this platform includes advanced tools like LLMs (Large Language Models), and allows the user to generate their own AI fighters, which can be customized according to their needs and preferences.

The integration of learning and reasoning is high on the research agenda in AI. Nevertheless, there is only a little attention to use existing background knowledge for reasoning about partially observed scenes to answer questions about the scene. Yet, we as humans use such knowledge frequently to infer plausible answers to visual questions (by eliminating all inconsistent ones). Such knowledge often comes in the form of constraints about objects and it tends to be highly domain or environment-specific. We contribute a novel benchmark called CLEVR-POC for reasoning-intensive visual question answering (VQA) in partially observable environments under constraints. In CLEVR-POC, knowledge in the form of logical constraints needs to be leveraged to generate plausible answers to questions about a

We study the problem of unscrewing a bottle cap with two hands, a common but challenging everyday task. We wear a glove on one hand and attempt to unscrew the cap by pressing it with the gloved hand while holding the bottle with the other hand. We repeat this experiment with different bottle caps and record the torque applied to the cap and the resulting twist angle. We find that the maximum twist angle achievable with a given amount of applied torque depends on the coefficient of friction between the gloved hand and the bottle cap. We also find that the distribution of the applied torque among the fingers is important for high performance. These findings are consistent across all participants in the study. We use our data to create a simple model of the mechanics of unscrewing a bottle cap with two hands. Our model captures the dependence of the maximum twist angle on the applied torque and the coefficient of friction.

We describe the first algorithms that satisfy the standard notion of node-differential privacy in the setting where an adversary can corrupt any number of nodes, and provide the first non-trivial bounds on the accuracy they achieve. Our results show that the best achievable accuracy decreases as a function of the number of corrupted nodes, and is independent of the total number of nodes in the network. The algorithms we propose are adaptations of the local differential private mechanisms for the local model, which were recently introduced by Abadi et al. (2016), and Chaudhuri et al. (2018). Our methods apply to arbitrary query functions, but also to the case of aggregating randomized queries from an arbitrary algorithm that satisfies standard differential privacy.

Learning-based methods have improved locomotion skills of quadruped robots through deep reinforcement learning (DRL) by considering the entire locomotion process, including walking and jumping. In this work, we introduce a novel method, the multi-stage learning approach, to further improve performance by focusing on single tasks within these two sub-processes. We investigate how to optimize different stages during locomotion based on DRL to enhance specific abilities. To do so, we develop a robotic platform that can learn to perform various single locomotion behaviors with an emphasis on balance and stability. The proposed method consists of three main parts: 1) task-oriented reward design; 2) task-oriented network architecture design; and 3) task-oriented training.

Accurate data association is crucial in reducing confusion, such as ID switches and assignment errors. This paper proposes a deep learning based method to address the problem of data association for ground targets with multiple tracks. We first develop a novel approach named "Track-to-Target" to fuse track information and target observation data. Then we design an end-to-end trainable model that can learn track-to-target associations, and formulate it as a multi-label classification problem. Our proposed algorithm has been tested on a benchmark dataset (TUD-ADB) collected by the Traffic Lab at Technische Universitaet Darmstadt. The experimental results indicate that our approach outperforms the state-of-the-art algorithms. In particular, our method achieves a $38\%$ reduction in ID switche

One of the main challenges in the area of Neuro-Symbolic AI is to perform logical reasoning in the presence of both neural and symbolic data. This requires combining heterogeneous data sources such as knowledge graphs, neural model predictions, structured databases, crowd-sourced data, and many more. To allow for such reasoning, we generalise the standard rule-based language Datalog with existential rules (commonly referred to as tuple-generating dependencies) to the fuzzy setting, by allowing for arbitrary t-norms in the place of classical conjunctions in rule bodies. The resulting formalism allows us to perform reasoning about data associated with degrees of uncertainty while preserving computational complexity results and the applicability of reasoning techniques established for the sta

Embodied Instruction Following (EIF) is a crucial task in embodied learning, aiming to enable agents to understand and follow human instructions. It poses several challenges for language models due to the complexity of the semantics involved. This work explores the use of large language models (LLMs) with visual reasoning capabilities for EIF, focusing on the LLaVA framework. Our findings highlight the benefits of employing LLMs with advanced reasoning capabilities, specifically in the context of following instructions that require logical reasoning. However, we also reveal that LLMs struggle with tasks that require complex spatial reasoning. Additionally, our findings indicate that while LLMs can perform well on some instruction subsets, they may be prone to hallucinations when confronted

We describe the first algorithms that satisfy the standard notion of node-differential privacy in the setting with multiple rounds. The model assumes a user whose data is divided into $m$ independent blocks and each block may be viewed by an adversary for up to $\ell_i\le m-\frac{1}{2}$ times (for any index i), where we require \emph{$sum(\{\,\tilde{}\ln (\cdot)\}_{\; 0<x<\infty})=O(log^{3} n)$}. Our results include: - An algorithm which satisfies $(\epsilon,4)$-node differentialprivacy when viewing at most two distinct nodes per round over all time steps ($s$-nodes) or one unique node from every other pair ($\sqrt s/6 + o_{d}(n^{-5})$)-distinct sets across both queries).

Federated learning (FL) is a collaborative scheme to train a learning model across multiple participants without sharing data. As FL is gaining popularity for its potential to enable new applications that were not feasible before, ensuring the resilience of FL to heterogeneous scenarios is becoming critical. Heterogeneity in FL arises due to the diverse characteristics of the participants in FL, e.g., differing device capabilities, network conditions, and local data distributions. Such heterogeneity can degrade the performance of FL and even cause certain tasks to become non-convergent. To tackle this challenge, this paper proposes a novel heterogeneous-resilient FL framework, dubbed HeteroFL, which aims to improve the convergence and generalizability of FL in heterogeneous scenarios. To this end, HeteroFL introduces a master-slave architecture with a parameter-server-based communication scheme. HeteroFL also employs gradient aggregation to address the data heterogeneity. Further, HeteroFL utilizes a novel heterogeneous parameter-server (HPS) design to aggregate gradients from diverse participants. HPS employs domain-specific local optimization to improve convergence and employs gradient compression to mitigate the impact of non-convergent participants. Finally, HeteroFL uses a dynamic learning rate scheme to accelerate convergence. Theoretical analysis shows that the design of HPS in HeteroFL can improve the convergence of FL and reduce the impact of heterogeneous participants. Experimental evaluation demonstrates the superiority of HeteroFL over state-of-the-art FL algorithms in heterogeneous scenarios.

Weakly supervised text classification (WSTC), also called zero-shot or dataless text classification, is a popular approach to addressing the data scarcity and label complexity issue faced in traditional text classification. Recently, researchers have achieved significant progress using weak supervision from various knowledge sources, such as large pretrained language models and existing datasets. However, most of these approaches still face challenges with limited scalability due to their reliance on costly pretraining or complex feature engineering methods. This paper introduces a simple yet effective method for WSTC based on self-supervised learning (SSL). Unlike previous works that learn features directly from raw texts without additional context, our approach incorporates both local an

3D hand pose estimation has found broad application in areas such as gesture recognition and human-machine interaction. Deep neural networks have been widely used to solve this task, but the performance still needs to be improved, especially when there is a large amount of data for training. Therefore, we propose an efficient data augmentation method based on deep learning to improve the performance of 3D hand pose estimation. The core idea of our method is to generate synthetic data with different parameters and then use these data to train the model. The results show that our method can effectively increase the diversity of data, reduce overfitting, and improve the robustness of the model. We conduct extensive experiments on two public datasets, namely the SURREAL and NYU Hand Dataset. O

Faithfully modeling the space of articulations is a crucial task that allows recovery and prediction in robotics, machine learning, and human motion analysis. In this paper, we introduce ArticulationSpace, a novel method for efficient and accurate modeling of articulations from images or videos. Our approach utilizes a latent variable model (LVM) to capture the high-level structure of joints while capturing the low-level details through a conditional variational autoencoder (CVAE). This combination results in an expressive representation that enables accurate reconstruction and prediction, outperforming other state-of-the-art methods in a variety of tasks such as pose estimation, tracking, and generation. Additionally, our approach can be easily scaled for large datasets, making it well-su

We introduce Cluster Edge Modification problems with constraints on the size of the clusters and study their complexity. Our main result is that, for every k, there exists a problem in 2-CNF-XPSPACE with parameter (n+k) such that no polynomial time algorithm can solve it unless RP has subexponential-time randomized query access to n-bit strings (i.e., unless P=RP). In particular, we show that this holds for k=1, 2, and 3, whereas for k=4, the problem remains open. Furthermore, we show that the complexity of solving a similar problem with k bits in the cluster size constraints is coNP-hard for all k>0. Our results are obtained by combining results from algebraic complexity theory with results from approximation algorithms.

Growing concerns about safety and alignment of AI systems highlight the importance of embedding moral capabilities in these systems. However, the ability to reason ethically remains a significant challenge for AI systems. In this paper, we propose a novel framework called Moral-Guided Reinforcement Learning (MORL) that leverages reinforcement learning techniques to teach robots how to make moral decisions based on their own perceptions of human actions. Our approach relies on human demonstrations of moral actions, enabling the agent to infer morally correct behaviors from those demonstrated by humans, and then apply them during training and testing. We conduct experiments involving two different domains: a robotic manipulation task with 24 different scenarios involving various moral action

Decoding language from brain dynamics is an important open direction in the realm of brain-computer interfaces. Inspired by previous successes in decoding speech from non-invasive recordings, we developed a decoder that extracts linguistic information from magnetoencephalography (MEG) data acquired through invasively implanted electrodes. To this end, we first extracted features from the MEG signal using recurrent neural networks and then employed a convolutional neural network to decode linguistic information from these features. We applied the resulting decoder on two datasets: one with sentences from the International Affective Picture System (IAPS), and another with continuous speech recordings from the LENA database. For both tasks, our results show that our approach achieved similar

Therefore it has become more important for organizations and researchers alike that they should be able identify those malicious sites with much higher accuracy than ever before so as not only avoid any data theft but also save their reputation among customers by maintaining trust level at high levels which leads towards customer retention rate improvement over long run periods of time - this being one goal every company wants today! With PHISHY you can now detect potential phishers on your website or other online properties quickly without having access into code files; making sure everything stays safe from bad actors while improving user experience simultaneously through an a

Integrating topology optimization and additive manufacturing (AM) technology can facilitate innovative product development, streamlining the design process while enhancing production efficiency. However, existing works primarily focus on optimizing a single design parameter, such as stiffness or weight, which may not always align with the overall performance objectives of AM-enabled topology optimization. To address this gap, this paper presents an approach that optimizes multiple design parameters simultaneously for the purpose of improving AM-related functionality, such as heat transfer, surface quality, and mechanical properties. The proposed methodology employs multi-objective evolutionary algorithms to optimize various design parameters simultaneously in a comprehensive manner. This a

Scene depth estimation from paintings can streamline the process of 3D sculpture creation so that artists can concentrate on aesthetic aspects. While machine learning models are able to make predictions with high accuracy, they require a large amount of training data and often overfit to particular styles and genres. In this paper we introduce an algorithm for depth estimation from paintings that is style-agnostic and does not require training data. The approach is based on using the painting's structure as an input to a generative adversarial network (GAN) that predicts the scene depth map. This method allows us to leverage the vast amounts of publicly available paintings in the artistic style without having to train the model on them. We showcase our results in a video demonstrating our

Small sample sizes are common in many disciplines, which necessitates pooling roughly similar datasets across studies. Here we investigate the consequences of cross-study data aggregation for model selection when using Bayesian methods and a broad class (so called informative) priors on regression coefficients: if such models with informative prior distributions do not fit well to individual samples from an experiment or observation process then they will also not likely be useful after aggregating multiple smaller ones into one large dataset -- even though these might have been chosen as optimal among all available alternatives given their respective training sets alone! We show that both predictive performance metrics tend towards overestimating true uncertainty due primarily at l

Utilizing multi-view inputs to synthesize novel-view images, Neural Radiance Fields (NeRF) have emerged as a promising tool for enhancing visual fidelity. However, their representation abilities are limited by the lack of expressive power in their latent space. To address this issue, we introduce a new transformer architecture that integrates the power of attention mechanisms with the ability to perform multimodal reasoning. The proposed Transformer-based Neural Radiance Field (TNReF) achieves superior performance when compared to existing approaches on diverse tasks such as novel view synthesis and image denoising. We also demonstrate its versatility across various datasets and downstream tasks. Our code is publicly available at https://github.com/YangJianjun1234567890/TNReF .

Pretrained language models (PLMs) have shown remarkable few-shot learning capabilities when provided with a suitable prompt. Recent works showed that the performance of PLM can be improved by selecting and reweighting informative tokens in each input sample during training, which has been referred to as token selection methods or Prompt Tuning techniques since they are applied on top off existing prompts for fine tuning tasks without changing them significantly at all timesteps across different samples within an epoch while also reducing computational complexity compared traditional approaches like masking/filtering inputs entirely based only their positional indices . However , these improvements come along side challenges related stability & scalability namely needing more computation ti

Deep wide residual networks (DWRN) have shown impressive performance in many computer vision tasks. However, like other deep learning models, DWRN also suffers from poor generalization ability. In this paper, we propose a simple method to improve the generalization ability of DWRN on image classification tasks. We find that appropriately scaling the convolutional feature maps of DWRN before the average pooling layer can improve the generalization ability of DWRN without additional training data. We analyze the function of the scaling factor from the perspective of the activation map. The activation map can be viewed as the solution of an approximate differential equation, and the scaling factor can be viewed as the solution of a boundary value problem of the differential equation. We use the finite element method to solve the boundary value problem and obtain the scaling factor. We conduct extensive experiments on the CIFAR10 and ImageNet datasets. The results show that our method improves the generalization ability of DWRN.

This dissertation is a multifaceted contribution to the advancement of vision-based deep learning, including fundamental theoretical insights and practical techniques. The first part focuses on understanding the dynamics of neural networks at the training stage. We investigate how different activation functions affect gradient flow in an artificial neural network (ANN), providing new perspectives on ANN training from the viewpoints of gradient descent and optimization. Our investigation reveals that the commonly used Rectified Linear Unit (ReLU) function outperforms other activation functions when it comes to speeding up convergence during training. However, its use can lead to a phenomenon known as "catastrophic forgetting" where newly learned information gets lost over time, making it le

We study distributed training of deep learning models in time-constrained environments. We propose a new algorithm, called Temporal Accelerated Gradient Descent (TAGD), which improves upon the state-of-the-art by adapting the learning rate and batch size dynamically according to the remaining time for training. We show that the proposed method can significantly reduce the number of epochs needed to achieve a given accuracy without sacrificing accuracy, while still being computationally efficient. We also provide an analysis of the method under the assumption of small noise and bounded variance and prove that it is effective when applied to smooth loss functions. Finally, we validate our approach empirically on image classification tasks using the ResNet-18 model on the CIFAR-10 dataset.

Embodied scene understanding serves as the cornerstone for autonomous agents to perceive, interpret, and act in real-world environments. The ability to recognize objects is of paramount importance, yet conventional methods are limited by their reliance on handcrafted features or predefined object categories. In this study, we introduce a novel approach that utilizes a Transformer encoder coupled with an embedding model for learning object representation. Our method introduces a flexible architecture capable of adapting to various tasks without requiring extensive labeled data. This paper presents three core contributions: (1) A novel Transformer model for scene understanding that can be trained with a small amount of data; (2) An innovative representation scheme that unifies object recogni

Robotic manipulation in cluttered environments is a challenging problem, especially when grasping and manipulating small objects. Reachability, which is affected by the kinematics of the robot, is an important factor that determines whether an object can be grasped or not. In this paper, we propose a hierarchical diffusion policy (HD-POL) to address the grasping and manipulation problem in cluttered environments. Our approach is based on the diffusion policy (DP), a recently proposed reinforcement learning-based approach that has been shown to be effective for grasping in cluttered environments. To improve the DP, we propose the HD-POL, which encodes the kinematic information of the robot at both the task and sub-task levels. We show that the HD-POL is superior to the DP and that it is able to learn grasping policies in a kinematics-aware manner. We perform extensive sets of experiments in simulation and on physical robots to evaluate the performance of the HD-POL. Our experimental results show that the HD-POL is able to outperform the DP and other state-of-the-art baselines on both simulated and physical robots.

The effectiveness of machine learning (ML) algorithms is deeply intertwined with the quality and diversification of the training data. However, data quality issues can adversely affect ML model performance by introducing bias and inaccuracies into models trained on this data. This can occur due to various factors such as the distribution shift between the training and test datasets, label noise, class imbalance, etc. To address these challenges, we introduce a new paradigm for improving ML model performance: Data Augmentation through Translation (DATA). By leveraging large language models (LLMs), DATA generates new data points based on existing ones, thus expanding the available training set. Unlike other existing techniques that augment data through sampling or synthesizing, our approach

Version Control Systems, such as Git and Mercurial, manage the history of a project as a Direct Acyclic Graph (DAG). The nodes in this graph represent changes to files or directories. Edges between two DAG-nodes indicate that one node is an immediate successor of another; i. e., it was created from its predecessor by some kind change operation like add/delete file(s) etc… The main goal for VCSes today seems largely focused on making sure users can easily access their codebase’S historical data when needed without sacrificing performance too much along with other requirements related directly towards managing large volumes across multiple repositories simultaneously – which means we need good algorithms capable enough at handling complex tasks efficiently while still being relatively simpl

Simulation is an invaluable tool for radio-frequency system designers that enables rapid protyping, optimization and validation. However simulation can be a time consuming process even with modern computing hardware so it's important to use the most efficient techniques available in order not only improve performance but also gain insight into how well your design will perform when implemented on real world devices such as receivers or transmitters (RF).

This article explores some of these methods focusing mainly around 2D electromagnetic field solvers which are used extensively within RF applications including antenna designs where they allow us access detailed information about electric fields at different points throughout our structure without having actually bui

Treatment effect estimation (TEE) is the task of determining the impact of various treatements on an outcome. The goal of this paper is to show that TEE is a hard problem in general, even for a simple setting where we have access to samples from a single treatment and can estimate the counterfactual mean through a regression algorithm. We define three properties -- monotonicity, submodularity and stability -- which characterize classes of functions for which TEE is hard. We prove that our definitions are indeed hardness measures for TEE by showing that they imply PAC-hardness (i.e., that no efficient estimator can achieve low error). Additionally, we show that these hardness notions are tight by providing algorithms for TEE with provable guarantees. Finally, we consider the problem of esti

Topic modelling was mostly dominated by Bayesian graphical models during the last decade. However, neural topic models have been shown to be more efficient in some cases. This paper presents a novel framework for learning topic distributions on graphs from unlabelled data. We introduce two novel concepts: topic-aware node embeddings and topic-aware attention mechanism. Our experiments show that our model is able to learn topics of varying granularity and can generalise to new nodes that are not part of the training set. We conduct extensive experiments across various synthetic and real-world datasets, and evaluate our approach using standard benchmark metrics (BLEU and METEOR). The results demonstrate that our proposed method outperforms both baseline neural topic models and state-of-the-a

Technology has been successfully used to augment human cognitive capabilities for centuries, from the abacus to the smartphone. Despite its evident advantages, the widespread adoption of technology as a cognitive prosthesis has been limited. One of the main reasons for this limited adoption is the disruptive nature of technology, which often causes more distractions than assistance. In this paper, we introduce the concept of distraction-aware augmentation, which aims to use technology to diminish distractions in the user's real environment, thus making the user more productive. We designed a proof-of-concept application that identifies and masks distracting stimuli in the user's field of view based on the user's cognitive load. We evaluated the application in a between-subjects experiment (n=24) in which participants performed two cognitive tasks: reading comprehension and arithmetic. Our results show that the application successfully masked distractions, as indicated by a significant main effect of condition on self-reported distraction scores (F(2,45)=6.14, p=0.004). Contrary to our expectations, however, the application did not have the intended beneficial effect on cognitive performance. In fact, we found a significant main effect of condition on reading comprehension scores (F(2,45)=7.41, p=0.001), with the distraction-aware condition scoring lower than the other two conditions. We discuss the limitations of our study and suggest several avenues for future research.

Legged robots have become capable of performing highly dynamic maneuvers in the past few years. In this paper, we focus on the robot's ability to perform a complex and dynamic task: a jump from one surface to another. We present a novel approach that incorporates real-time dynamics simulation during motion planning, enabling the robot to accurately predict its landing trajectory and ensure a safe landing without the need for additional sensor calibration or external force feedback. Our method uses both visual inputs (for 3D reconstruction) and tactile information (from the ground sensors) to generate a detailed representation of the scene and predict the landing location with high accuracy. Additionally, our system is designed to be scalable, allowing us to extend it to handle more challen

The advent of Multilingual Language Models (MLLMs) and Large Language Models trained on multilingual data has made it possible to perform cross-lingual tasks like translation, question answering or text classification in low resource settings. However due the lack datasets with English annotations for most languages other than few popular ones such as German , Spanish etc., existing MLLM based models are not very effective when training is performed using only monolingual source language dataset(s). To overcome this issue we propose a novel method called Monolink which uses an additional link between input encoder output vector corresponding each word token from sentence pair having same index value during model initialization stage so that knowledge gained by learning task can be shared a

Multi-sensor fusion systems (MSFs) play a vital role as the perception module in the autonomous driving system. In MSF, fusion is performed by combining different sensor data and estimating an estimate of the object from the combined data. The primary objective of this paper is to develop the dynamic fused state (DFS) model for the multi-sensor fusion system. For a multi-sensor fusion system with multiple sensors, each sensor can provide its measurement to the DFS model. Then, according to the information provided by the measurements, the DFS model estimates an estimate of the object. To make the DFS model adaptive, it was designed using an adaptive neural network model. The estimated result of the proposed DFS model is compared with other approaches based on the mean absolute percentage e

Autonomous parallel-style on-ramp merging in human controlled traffic continues to be an area of interest for researchers. Despite the increasing efforts by researchers, there is still a lack of realistic data. This paper presents a novel dataset that is designed to simulate real-world on-ramp merging scenarios, while providing rich information about vehicle dynamics and interactions with other vehicles. The dataset is collected from a simulated environment using a high-fidelity vehicle model and a physics engine. The driving trajectory of each vehicle is captured during on-ramp merging scenarios. It can be used to train various AI agents to perform on-ramp merging tasks in different scenarios such as lane changing, multiple exiting lanes, and varying traffic density. The paper provides a

Data similarity assumptions have traditionally been relied upon to understand the convergence behaviors of federated learning methods. Unfortunately, this approach often demands fine-tuning step sizes based on the level of data similarity. When data similarity is low, these small step sizes result in an unacceptably slow convergence speed for federated methods. In this paper, we present a novel and unified framework for analyzing the convergence of federated learning algorithms without the need for data similarity conditions. Our analysis centers on an inequality that captures the influence of step sizes on algorithmic convergence performance. By applying our theorems to well-known federated algorithms, we derive precise expressions for three widely used step size schedules: fixed, diminis

Dynamic memory managers (DMMs) are responsible for allocating and deallocating memory during the execution of a program. DMMs have a significant impact on an application's performance, as they can cause memory overhead, cache misses, and irregular memory access patterns. Therefore, it is important to find an optimal DMM for a given application. This paper proposes a methodology to automatically optimize DMMs using grammatical evolution (GE), a type of genetic algorithm that evolves individuals expressed in a specified formal grammar. The individuals produced by the GE-based optimization algorithm are valid configurations of the DMM being optimized. The methodology proposed in this paper can be applied to any DMM that follows a certain structure, and it does not require any user intervention. We applied our methodology to optimize the Linux C++ standard library's memory manager, which we benchmarked in a previous paper (Fajardo et al., 2019). In this new paper, we report two important findings. First, we improved the GE-based optimization algorithm, so it can terminate earlier than the original version. This improvement is based on a mutation operator that GE can apply to individuals. Second, we conducted a more extensive set of experiments with different populationsizes and compared our results with those of other state-of-the-art optimization algorithms. Our results show that the GE-based optimization algorithm is competitive compared with other state-of-the-art optimization algorithms. In particular, the GE-based optimization algorithm is better than the sequential version of the generalized heuristic search algorithm (GHS) applied to the memory manager. The GE-based optimization algorithm is also better than the sequential version of the genetic algorithm (GA) applied to the memory manager.

Real-time collaboration with humans poses challenges due to the different behavior patterns of humans resulting from their unique preferences and prior experiences. To better support this collaboration, we introduce a novel framework that integrates deep reinforcement learning (DRL) into human-AI collaborative environments. Specifically, our approach leverages DRL agents as virtual partners who can observe human interactions and adapt their strategies accordingly. This integration enables these agents to learn and utilize knowledge gained from previous interactions, enhancing their ability to cooperate effectively in real-time environments. In this study, we focus on two key scenarios: 1) a simulated environment where DRL agents interact with humans and learn from them, and 2) a real-world

Wrist-worn devices are increasingly adopted by Chinese adolescents. In this paper, we explore how the adoption of smartwatches mediates adolescents' socialization within their complex socio-cultural context. Through a qualitative study in a Chinese high school (N=72), we find that the smartwatch is more than a time-telling device; it is a source of status and a medium for social interaction. We introduce three concepts to describe the socialization practice enabled by the smartwatch: (1) Guanxi, referring to the smartwatch-mediated extension of the traditional Chinese concept of interpersonal relationships; (2) Jiazu, referring to the smartwatch-mediated expansion of the circle of people one can interact with; and (3) Kuolie, referring to the smartwatch-mediated keeping of the other party in one's social circle. We show how these three concepts interplay in the adolescents' socialization practice. We also discuss the implications of our findings for theory and practice.

Continuous authentication is a promising paradigm for next-generation human-computer interfaces. It aims to identify users continuously and automatically, reducing the friction of traditional authentication methods (e.g., passwords, PINs). Many researchers propose using continuous biometrics (e.g., gait, keystroke dynamics) for this purpose. However, such biometric data is not always available, easy to acquire, or sufficiently unique to guarantee security. In this paper, we explore the use of mouse dynamics for continuous authentication. We first show that mouse movements are sufficiently unique to individuals and can be used for continuous authentication. Next, we investigate the viability of this approach in real-world scenarios by collecting mouse data from 10 participants interacting with a website. Finally, we compare the performance of our mouse-based system with that of a commercial continuous authentication solution. Our results show that mouse dynamics provide a promising alternative for continuous authentication, offering a good balance between performance, usability, and security.

In this paper, we propose a novel privacy-preserving framework with multi-modal data for cross-domain recommendation. Users' personalized data is the most valuable resource in the process of building a recommendation system, but it is also the main culprit behind privacy violations. Existing approaches, such as local differential privacy, adopt simple homomorphic encryption techniques to protect user privacy. However, they usually suffer from low utility, high computational complexity, or restrictions on the data format. To address these challenges, we introduce a multi-party computation framework that utilizes secure multi-party computation techniques, including homomorphic encryption and secure hash algorithms. This framework enables the aggregation of user behavior data from edge devices to a trusted third party for training a recommendation model. It also allows users to obtain recommendation results without exposing their personalized data. To accommodate the heterogeneous nature of cross-domain data, we employ a multi-modal data processing approach. Specifically, we use structured data containing user behavior records and generate personalized item embeddings from unstructured data, such as images and texts, using a pre-trained model. Experiments conducted on three benchmark datasets demonstrate that our framework achieves recommendation performance comparable to that of a non-private baseline while significantly outperforming existing privacy-preserving methods in terms of utility and computational efficiency.

Effective action abstraction is crucial in tackling challenges associated with large action spaces in Imperfect-Information Multiagent Reinforcement Learning (MIRIL) settings. Recent studies on this topic have focused primarily on the reward function, which has been used to abstract actions and achieve sample efficiency in MIRIL environments. However, recent work has demonstrated that state abstraction plays a significant role in improving performance. This paper introduces State-Based Action Abstraction (SBAA), a novel framework for state-based action abstraction in MIRIL scenarios. SBAA integrates existing techniques from RL and deep learning into an effective framework for state-based action abstraction. SBAA leverages the concept of latent features to represent abstracted states, enabl

In this paper, we investigate a fundamental problem in network design: given a set of $n$ points in the plane and a parameter $k$, connect these points using a tree network having at most $k$ leaves. The goal is to minimize the sum of the lengths of the edges in the network. This problem is known to be NP-hard, and the best known approximation factor is $2k-1$ by means of a connection to the well-known Steiner tree problem. We show that the problem is in fact NP-hard for any fixed $k \geq 2$, by proving that it is NP-hard to approximate the optimal value by a factor of $2k-\delta$ for any constant $\delta > 0$. We then revisit the case of two dimensions and show that the problem admits a polynomial time approximation scheme (PTAS) for $k=2$, and that it is NP-hard for $k \geq 3$. For the PTAS, we actually provide an alternative simpler proof that improves upon the approximation factor of $9/4 + \delta$ to $5/2 + \delta$ for $k=2$. Finally, we also provide some heuristics for the general $k$-leaves problem in the plane, and analyze their performance for specific values of $k$.

In this paper, we study a dynamic analogue of the Path Cover problem, which can be solved in polynomial time by the classical greedy algorithm. We consider a variant of the problem where a sequence of edges is presented one-by-one and, at each step, we need to decide whether or not to add the current edge to the path cover. The decision is based only on the subset of previously added edges. Our goal is to minimize the total number of newly added edges at all steps. This problem is a well-known example of an online problem with feedback, and it admits a simple 2-competitive online algorithm (called Greedy). However, this algorithm may select a large number of edges that are later deleted from the path cover, so its performance is not very impressive. To address this issue, we introduce an i

The integration of learning and reasoning is high on the research agenda in AI. Nevertheless, there are only a handful of methods that address this challenge. In this paper, we propose an approach that combines a neural network with a rule-based system. We show that our method can be used to improve the performance of neural networks when training data is limited or noisy. Our experimental results demonstrate that the proposed method can generalize better than existing approaches and is applicable to a wide range of problems. In particular, we have successfully applied it to a variety of real-world applications, including robotics, finance, and language processing. Finally, we provide insights into the working principles of the algorithm and discuss its limitations. We believe that the pro

Cross-view geo-localization aims to match images of the same target from different platforms with diverse camera viewpoints, e.g., aerial vs. ground, which has gained tremendous attention in recent years for its application in autonomous driving and augmented reality (AR). Most previous works on cross-view geo-localization focus on matching aerial views with ground views or vice versa, which, however, is still difficult and unsatisfactory when we have both aerial and ground views simultaneously. In this paper, we propose a multi-view fusion method that can effectively utilize multiple geo-localization results generated by each individual model. Our method first generates geo-localization results for the inputs of all views, then fuses these predictions by using a novel 3D-based localizatio

Chinese noun phrases (NP) can be either definite or indefinite, and can refer to either a plurality or a singleton. The distinction between definite and indefinite is grammatical, whereas the distinction between plurality and singleton is semantic. Previous work has mostly focused on the grammatical distinction, and has left the semantic distinction unexplored. In this paper, we first present evidence that the semantic distinction between plurality and singleton can be captured by a simple computational model, which suggests that the semantic distinction is in principle separable from the grammatical distinction. We then show that a minimal extension to the model can distinguish between definite and indefinite NPs, thus capturing both the grammatical and semantic distinctions. While our model provides a proof of concept, a more general computational model of definiteness and plurality in Chinese NPs would have far-reaching implications for various NLP tasks, such as semantic role labelling, information extraction and machine translation.

VPN adoption has seen steady growth over the past decade due to increased public awareness of online privacy and security. As a result, VPNs have become an integral part in our daily lives for personal use as well by businesses who rely on them when working remotely from home or other locations outside their usual office environment where they may be exposed more frequently than normal through cyber threats such DDoS attacks against websites hosted within cloud environments like AWS EC2 instances running Linux OS’es without any additional protection measures taken into account beforehand (eg., firewall rules).
