Amazon Virtual Private Cloud(VPC) provides users with access control features that allow organizations not only manage network traffic between resource

The Internet has become a primary source of information for many people. This paper explores public opinions on the U.S. military, as expressed on YouTube comments. The data were collected in 2019 and 2020 and consist of 1,000 videos with 500 pro-military and 500 anti-military comments each, making a total of 500,000 comments. To annotate the data, we employed a novel combination of automatic and manual methods that produced a benchmark dataset with 180,000 manually labeled comments. Our experiments with five state-of-the-art models for stance classification show that human annotators outperform machine annotators by 11.5% on average. We present Diverse, a pipeline for decoding Internet views on the U.S. military, and analyze the implications of our findings for future research on online discourse and military public relations.

In this paper, we study a dynamic analogue of the Path Cover problem, which can be solved in polynomial time. We consider an undirected graph with positive edge weights and an initial configuration where each node has a probability to move along its adjacent edges. The goal is to find a sequence of node movements that maximizes the total weight of visited nodes. To solve our problem efficiently, we develop a dynamic programming algorithm for computing the maximum weight visited set, and show how it can be used to produce a feasible solution for the Path Cover problem. This solution generalizes previous algorithms from [Ba92] and [Ku01]. We then consider the setting when the maximum weight visited set needs to have a given cardinality $k$. To obtain a combinatorial optimum, we use a strateg

Data similarity assumptions have traditionally been relied upon to understand the convergence behaviors of federated learning. However, these assumptions are not always met in practice. In this paper we aim to explore alternative explanations for convergence behaviors using a data-driven approach that utilizes only local models and local datasets. We propose a novel metric called \textit{local model consistency} (LMC), which quantifies how consistent different local models are given the same data distribution. This metric is based on the notion that two models trained with data from the same distribution should yield similar predictions. To further enhance its predictive power, we combine LMC with gradient alignment metrics, thereby providing an explanation for global convergence in terms

Transformer-based Neural Language Models achieve state-of-the-art performance on various natural language processing tasks. We demonstrate that, even when trained on a single task, they exhibit remarkable zero-shot generalization capabilities on unrelated ones. However, the extent of their ability to adapt to new tasks is not always guaranteed, as the model's performance can deteriorate significantly upon fine-tuning. In this work, we introduce an approach that enables models to be trained and fine-tuned on multiple tasks simultaneously. Our method leverages multi-task learning techniques to enhance model performance across different tasks while retaining their zero-shot generalizability. Experimental results demonstrate that our approach improves model performance on downstream tasks comp

Satellite imagery is a crucial input to a variety of deep learning models that are used for Earth observation and climate change research. However, these images can be altered by atmospheric effects, altering the color and texture information in a consistent manner across the entire image. These alterations can impact the ability of deep learning models to accurately process the data. This paper aims to quantify the impact of these alterations on deep learning models used for Earth observation by generating distorted images and measuring the resulting changes in model predictions. We find that distortion of both color and texture information generally results in decreased accuracy of classification models, with texture distortion having a larger impact than color distortion. Additionally, we find that certain models are more robust to these distortions than others, and that the impact of distortion can vary depending on the specific characteristics of the distorted images. Our results provide valuable information for researchers and practitioners working with satellite imagery and deep learning models, highlighting the need to account for atmospheric effects when available and the importance of using robust models where possible.

This study presents a systematic enumeration of spherical ($SO(3)$) type parallel robots. It is based on the notion of a duality between $SE(3)$-equivalent and $SO(3)$-equivariant robots. We show that for each robot with three rigid links in $SE(3)$, there exists an equivalent one with four rigid links, but no equivalent robot with two rigid links. Furthermore, we show that this equivalence relationship allows us to systematically enumerate all spherical robots with up to five links, resulting in 24 different types. The paper also includes a discussion of some of the properties of these robots, including their number of degrees of freedom (DOFs), singular configurations, as well as methods to compute the corresponding Jacobian matrices. Several examples are provided to illustrate our findi

Compliant grippers enable robots to work with humans in unstructured environments. In this paper, we present a novel class of compliant robotic fingertip devices that have the ability to adapt their compliance properties by varying an applied electric field across them using piezoelectric materials (PZT). This enables robot fingers equipped these fingertips can interact differently with different objects and perform various grasp tasks safely without requiring any additional actuation or sensing hardware at each joint location on top which makes it more efficient than traditional methods such as tendon-driven ones where multiple actuators are required for controlling only one degree freedom per link while our design requires just two PZTs but provides three degrees freedoms along its lengt

Recent advancement in text-to-image models (e.g., Stable Diffusers) has demonstrated impressive image generation capability and generated a massive amount of images that can be used for downstream tasks, such as computer vision or machine learning model pre training without the need to collect data from scratch anymore thanks largely due their open source nature which encourages collaboration between researchers around world who are interested developing these types technology further down road ahead To ensure our safety when using AI systems it is important we create ethical guidelines so they do not harm society by making decisions based on biased information derived through unsupervised methods like Generative Adversarial Networks(GAN). In this blog post you will learn about how GANs w

Graph clustering, an important unsupervised problem, has been shown to be more resistant to adversarial attacks than supervised learning tasks. However, recent studies have revealed that graph clustering can also be vulnerable to adversarial examples if the attacker has access to a specific model architecture and training dataset. To address this issue, we propose a novel method for generating robust adversarial perturbations in the graph setting. Our approach leverages a graph neural network (GNN) encoder to extract the graph representation and a decoder to reconstruct the original graph structure. The objective is to maximize the reconstruction loss while maintaining a low cosine similarity between the original and perturbed representations. Experimental results demonstrate that our meth

Colliding with clutter is a significant risk for autonomous robots. To mitigate this risk, robots must be able to adapt their flight trajectories in real-time as they perceive new obstacles. However, real-world clutter is often dynamic, and pre-computed maps are quickly becoming outdated. Thus, robots must continually learn and adapt to new clutter configurations while flying. In this paper, we introduce a novel framework that enables real-time learning and adaptation of flight trajectories in dynamic clutter. Our approach uses a hybrid state-space representation that combines the global context, represented as a graph, and the local perception, represented as a point cloud. This hybrid representation allows the use of powerful graph neural networks (GNNs) to process the global context and makes the local perception robust against noise. Our GNN-based planner reasons about agile movements in clutter by computing trajectories that steer clear of nearby obstacles while maintaining safe distances to distant obstacles. We show that our method can outperform traditional methods, such as Rapidly-expanding Random Trees (RRT), in terms of trajectory cost and computation time. We also show that our approach is safer in the event of map updates, as it can re-plan trajectories in real-time without global map reconstruction.

Generative models have shown strong generation ability while efficient likelihood estimation is less explored. Energy-based (EB) models are an emerging framework that provides both generation and inference abilities. Recently, some works showed the potential of EB models in unpaired supervision scenarios. However, these works only focus on image generation tasks, which lacks a comprehensive analysis of the characteristics and performance of different model architectures for diverse generation tasks. To address this issue, we propose a benchmark called GENERATE, which consists of 10 challenging datasets with various types of data, such as images, videos, text, music, and audio signals. We also construct 5 different task groups with different complexity levels, ranging from image classificat

Training-free metrics (a.k.a. zero-cost proxies) are widely used to rank and select pre-trained models for downstream tasks without training them on the target task data. However, these metrics rely on task-specific downstream datasets that are expensive to collect, making it difficult to train-free ranking models for a wide range of real-world applications. In this work, we introduce \textbf{S}elf-\textbf{A}ligned \textbf{D}atasets (\textbf{SAD}) to enable training-free ranking across diverse downstream tasks. Our key idea is to construct SADs by aligning the representations of source and target tasks with respect to the target's intrinsic semantics using the available pre-trained models. We show that the resulting SADs can be effectively utilized to accurately rank pre-trained models wit

In representation learning on graph-structured data, many popular graph neural networks (GNNs) employ the node sampling strategy to alleviate the computational overhead associated with the large-scale graph. However, sampling may introduce significant bias in the learned representations and thus hamper their generalization performance. To address this issue, we propose a novel sampling-free approach called Graph Diffusion Network (GDNet), which relies on diffusion processes for graph signal propagation. The proposed model is based on the observation that the stationary state of the diffusion process on graphs coincides with the eigenvectors of the normalized Laplacian matrix of the graph, which are known to preserve the spectral information of the graph. As a result, we train GDNet by opti

NeRFs have shown impressive results in novel view synthesis. However, existing techniques often rely on slow exploration of the entire four-dimensional shape+appearance space. Moreover, it is not clear how to leverage potential symmetries in the scene, which often exist in real-world environments. To address these challenges, we propose the novel view prediction problem and present a method that exploits symmetries to efficiently explore the NeRF scene space. Specifically, we introduce an auxiliary network that predicts whether a candidate view is symmetrical to the reference view. We show that this network can be trained in an unsupervised manner using a proxy loss. Then, we formulate a novel view synthesis pipeline that first performs a symmetric view proposal and subsequently renders the symmetrical pair using a NeRF. Our method can leverage existing NeRF implementations and, more importantly, can terminate expensive shape+appearance exploration as soon as a symmetric view is found. We show that our proposal method is 1.9--10.0x faster than existing techniques. Moreover, we demonstrate that our method can exploit symmetries in 3D scenes, whether they are synthetic or real-world scenes.

Citing comprehensively and appropriately has become a challenging task with the explosive growth of digital publications. Currently, there are no automatic approaches to assist authors in finding suitable references from an online database for their papers when citing others’ works or reviewing them during preparation steps such as outline creation (i. e., early-stage writing). In this paper we introduce CiteNet: A Deep Learning Framework that uses neural networks trained on citation graphs derived from 10 million scientific articles indexed by Google Scholar over eight years spanning multiple disciplines within Computer Science; resulting models achieve state–of –the art performance at recommending relevant sources based upon text snippets extracted directly without relying solely only au

Consider the Telephone Broadcast problem in which an input is a connected graph $G$ on $n \geq 2406185739(d+1)$ vertices, with maximum degree $\Delta$, and we want to find out if there exists any subset of at most size $(m-s)(i+\epsilon) + s/(\delta i)$, for some integers where: * The number ratio between minimum edge colorability ($k$)and clique bound ($\gamma$), such that no more than two colors are used among edges incident upon each vertex. $$\frac{min_x \{ f_{K_{\chi}} (f^{-1} x )\}}{max\{ {g}_{C_\eta}(y)\colon y\in [-\infty,\log n] \} }\leq K$$ If this condition holds true then it can be shown via reduction from Graph Coloring Problem by using linear programming duality theory[paper].

Rare object detection is a fundamental task in applied geospatial machine learning, however is often challengingly difficult due to the heterogeneity of rare objects in remote sensing imagery. In this work, we present a new dataset of 1000 synthetic aerial images, each containing a set of different rare objects. The dataset contains 56 different rare object categories and is well balanced across all classes. We also introduce an innovative framework for generating synthetic rare objects in remote sensing imagery from high-quality, hand-drawn sketches. Furthermore, we explore several approaches to evaluate deep learning models on this challenging task, including both traditional approaches, such as mean average precision (mAP), and novel approaches that focus more on individual rare objects

In this paper, we look at cross-domain few-shot classification which presents the challenging task of adapting a classifier trained on one domain (source) to another unseen but related target. We propose an effective and efficient model based approach that uses semantic information from source data for adaptation by leveraging precomputed embeddings in low dimensional spaces such as BERT or Word2Vec vectors across domains via cosine similarity metrics between classes/tokens within each dataset instance set while still maintaining high accuracy scores even when only provided with limited labeled samples per class label category during training stage itself prior its deployment into production environment settings where more accurate predictions are desired most urgently upon encountering ne

Unsupervised multilingual learning aims to learn a multilingual model from monolingual data, which has shown great potential in reducing the reliance on labeled data. However, existing methods face challenges such as limited linguistic information extraction and sub-optimal cross-lingual knowledge transfer. To address these challenges, we introduce a novel unsupervised multilingual dense retrieval method, dubbed MUL-TRE. This method follows a generative-pseudo-labelling paradigm, involving two key steps: 1) a multilingual retriever is trained to retrieve relevant documents for a given query, even if the query and documents are in different languages, by leveraging a bi-encoder model; 2) the retrieved documents are then used to pseudo-label the original data, which is subsequently employed to supervise the training of a multilingual reader. The reader and retriever are designed to form a unified whole, with the retriever capable of retrieving documents from 104 languages and the reader able to understand queries posed in 72 languages. Extensive experiments conducted on MS MARCO, NQ, and BEIR benchmarks demonstrate the competitive performance of our proposed method. Notably, MUL-TRE achieves substantial improvements over strong baselines, with gains of 4.4%, 3.3%, and 2.4% on MS MARCO, NQ, and BEIR, respectively. The code is available at https://github.com/BeierZhu/MUL-TRE.

Robotic manipulation of deformable, one-dimensional objects (DOOs) like ropes and cables is a challenging task due to the inherent instability caused by their high compliance. This paper addresses this problem through an approach that leverages both force sensing feedback from grasping contacts with two fingers as well As visual Servoing Control based on 3D Point Cloud Data for pose estimation in order To achieve robust motion control during contact interaction With compliant DOOs while avoiding collisions between them And other obstacles In real environments where there are many uncertainties such AS unpredictable movements Of humans Or machines nearby or unexpected changes/interruptions During operation processes etc . The proposed method has been tested successfully On several experimen

Despite recent advances in wearable technology, interfacing movement assistance devices with the human body remains challenging. Here we present a new approach that does not require invasive surgery or external sensors to monitor motion and control assistive systems: using miniature ultrasound transducers as both actuators for restoring lost muscle function through targeted vibration therapy (TVT) and non-invasive surface electromyography electrodes capable of recording electric signals generated by muscles during their activation via TVTs at specific frequencies (~10 kHz).

This study investigates the application of advanced machine learning models, specifically Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) networks and Gradient Booster models, for accurate energy consumption estimation within a Kubernetes cluster environment. It aims to enhance sustainable computing practices by providing precise predictions of energy usage across various computing nodes. Through meticulous analysis of model performance on both master and worker nodes, the research reveals the strengths and potential applications of these models in promoting energy efficiency. The LSTM model demonstrates remarkable predictive accuracy, particularly in capturing dynamic computing workloads over time, evidenced by low mean squared error (MSE) rates and the ability to closely track actual energy consumption tr

The Vehicle Routing Problem (VRP) is a widely studied combinatorial optimization problem in logistics and transportation. It involves finding the most efficient routes for delivering goods or services to multiple customers using a fleet of vehicles that have limited capacity, time windows, and other constraints. One of the significant challenges faced by researchers is the computational complexity of solving large-scale VRP instances. To address this challenge, researchers have developed various approximation algorithms with different levels of accuracy. However, evaluating the performance of these algorithms requires the use of real-world data sets with known solutions. This is where the benchmark data sets come into play. In this paper, we provide an overview of 15 publicly available ben

Animals learn to adapt agility of their movements to their capabilities and the environment they operate in. While this learning process has been explored extensively for humans, it is not well understood how animals perform such adaptations during daily life activities or why specific motor skills are developed over others early on as infants (e..g., crawling vs walking). In order understand these questions better we first propose a novel computational framework that leverages deep reinforcement-learning based control methods coupled with evolutionary optimization algorithms from biology - which allows us simulate real world environments more accurately than existing approaches by taking into account both physical constraints arising due interactions between objects/agents within simulate

Recently, it has been shown that the architecture of neural networks has a strong influence on their base capabilities, i.e., the performance achieved by the network before any fine-tuning. In this paper, we investigate the influence of the architecture of pre-trained language models. To this end, we compare a series of FFN-wider transformer models, i.e., transformer models with wider hidden layers, different attention heads, and layer norms. The pre-trained language models are evaluated on the GLUE benchmark before any fine-tuning. We show that wider hidden layers consistently improve the base capabilities of pre-trained language models. However, the impact of the other architectural choices is less clear-cut. Our experiments further show that the base capabilities of pre-trained language models are strongly correlated with their parameters. When controlling for the number of parameters, wider hidden layers no longer provide a consistent boost, and the best configuration depends on the specific task. Nonetheless, we show that a model with a hidden size of at least 3072 is required to outperform the baseline on the GLUE benchmark.

Recent advancements in large language models (LLMs) have underscored their importance for software engineering. However, LLMs' limited understanding of software fundamentals and the complexities of code, particularly when it comes to maintaining its quality, are significant challenges. The primary objective of this research is to demonstrate that LLMs can be trained with domain-specific knowledge and code examples to enhance their comprehension of software engineering concepts. We also aim to investigate whether LLMs can write high-quality comments for code and identify bugs in code. To address these objectives, we propose a new approach called CodePilot that uses code examples as training data and fine-tunes an existing LLM model. Our approach leverages the strengths of LLMs by utilizing

Since the foundational work of William Labov on the social stratification of language (Labov, 1969), the relationship between language and socioeconomic status has been a crucial topic in sociolinguistics. However, research on this topic is often limited to comparisons within individual languages or cultures, despite the fact that language contact is ubiquitous and multilingualism is widespread. In this paper, we conduct a large-scale cross-linguistic analysis using the publicly available Linguistic Variation Database (LVDB; Koplenig et al., 2018) to investigate the connection between socioeconomic status and language variation across 27 languages from 5 continents. The results indicate that there are significant differences in the association between socioeconomic status and linguistic va

Measuring the impact of a publication in a fair way is a significant challenge in bibliometrics. The current paradigm for measuring the impact of publications is based on citations, which are known to be biased towards English-language publications and more established disciplines. Recently, there has been growing interest in using alternative metrics (Altmetrics) as indicators of scholarly impact. Altmetrics use data from various sources, including social media platforms, to track the engagement and dissemination of scholarly work. However, the validity and reliability of these Altmetrics are unclear, and their utility in evaluating research impact remains understudied. This paper presents an overview of recent developments in Altmetrics research and discusses its limitations and future d

Recent literature has shown that it is possible to make profit from financial markets by using information contained in textual financial reports. This paper proposes a novel method to extract sentiment from textual financial statements and use it to predict future financial returns. The proposed method combines natural language processing techniques with financial time series analysis. Specifically, we use a pre-trained transformer-based model (FinBERT) to obtain a vectorized representation of textual financial data. We then combine these data with traditional numerical features for financial time series analysis, and apply a Bayesian neural network to make predictions. The results of out-of-sample predictions on three different datasets show that the proposed method performs better than traditional models that use numerical features only.

Accurate representations of unknown and sub-grid physical processes through parameterizations (or closure) in numerical models are a key challenge for the simulation, prediction or understanding climate dynamics. In recent years there has been an increased interest in applying machine learning methods to address this problem as they allow more flexible approaches than traditional physics based closures which can be constrained by prior knowledge on model structure but also have limitations when dealing with complex nonlinearities involved within dynamical systems such those found across many aspects climatology including feedback loops between different variables; however due its potential implications regarding bias towards certain types/scales features during training time periods it is

In this paper, we provide expressions for the secrecy outage probability (SOP) for sub-6 GHz and mmWave cellular systems employing hybrid precoding. For 5G/B38+ cells with bandwidths ranging from $10$ MHz to $\sim249\text{ } \mathrm {MHz}$, which represents most of today' s deployed networks , it is shown that SOP can be approximated by a function only dependent on SNR . This simplifies system design as one does not have consider channel parameters when calculating optimal levels of transmit power or number $\mathcal N_{\rm th}$th order transform matrix in practical designs).

Data-centric AI (DCAI) as an emerging paradigm is gaining recognition for its potential to reshape how we design and build AI systems. DCAI emphasizes that data is a first-class citizen in the AI development process and that we must design AI systems that are data-driven, data-centric, and data-trustworthy. This paper argues that the realization of DCAI's full potential requires a new approach to bridging the divide between academic ideals and industrial pragmatism. We propose model-based DCAI (MB-DCAI) as a novel paradigm to achieve this goal. MB-DCAI recognizes the critical role of models in DCAI and proposes a systematic approach to model selection, training, evaluation, and deployment. MB-DCAI also emphasizes the need to integrate domain knowledge, data, and models to build trustworthiness in AI systems. We demonstrate the practical utility of MB-DCAI by applying it to a real-world AI project. We also show that MB-DCAI can lead to better decision-making, higher efficiency, and increased trustworthiness in AI systems.

The ever-increasing sensor service, though opening a precious path and providing a deluge of data to be explored in the domain of healthcare, is also facing severe challenges. As there are a number of sources of data, it is crucial to efficiently utilize them to gain valuable insights for the community at large. In this context, we propose a novel framework called Fusion Sense to address these issues by harnessing the power of Large Language Models (LLMs) integrated with federated learning techniques. The proposed approach leverages the LLMs' language proficiency to generate high quality queries on distributed devices while concurrently performing federated learning for model optimization.

The efficacy of large language models (LLMs) in understanding and generating natural language has aroused widespread interest. Conventional LLM-based natural language understanding systems typically rely on pre-trained models such as GPT or BERT, which are typically trained with a limited amount of data. However, the ability to scale up these models is constrained by the limited size of their respective models. In this paper, we introduce the Natural Language Understanding-Transformer (NLU-Trans), an open-source software framework that utilizes Transformer architectures for natural language understanding tasks. Our approach leverages existing large language models (LLMs) and Transformers trained for various natural language understanding tasks, enabling us to adapt them for new application

This paper presents an efficient method for updating particles in a particle filter (PF) to address the positional uncertainty of tracking objects. The proposed approach leverages the Gaussian mixture model (GMM)-based particle filter and utilizes the Kalman filter (KF) to estimate the position of particles. Through the use of particle-based tracking, the proposed method can handle occlusions and large variations in object size with high precision. Additionally, it enables tracking multiple targets simultaneously by using a single PF. The method incorporates a two-step procedure to effectively update the positions of particles: first, the KF is used to estimate the particle positions and then, the GMM-based filter is applied to refine their locations. To further improve the efficiency of t

The field of 3D detailed human mesh reconstruction has made significant progress in recent years. However, most existing methods are designed for high-resolution scenarios and cannot be used on large-scale datasets due to the increased time and memory requirements. In this study, we present a novel approach for reconstructing dense meshes from low-resolution images, which significantly reduces the computation and memory demands while maintaining high accuracy. Our method uses a set of reference meshes generated by a coarse-to-fine strategy, and employs an iterative refinement scheme based on neural networks to generate a more precise mesh. We demonstrate that our method can effectively reconstruct accurate and visually pleasing meshes even with limited computing resources. This work will p

Large language model (LLM) leads to a surge of autonomous GUI agents for smartphone applications. However, they are far from being robust in real-world settings and lack the capability to adapt to various usage contexts. In this paper, we propose an innovative approach by leveraging LLMs to generate customized GUI designs for any given application scenario. We employ a generative design process that involves the generation of personalized designs based on user input data and subsequent optimization using LLM feedback. Our approach is unique as it not only considers the interaction with the application but also incorporates the user's preference and behavioral patterns. Through extensive experiments conducted on three mobile applications, we demonstrate the effectiveness of our method. Spec

Stochastic finite elements are a popular approach to model uncertainty in computational mechanics. In this paper, we investigate the use of Mat\'ern-type random fields and their finite element approximation for modelling uncertainty. We review the existing theory for Mat\'ern-type random fields and their implementation in a computational framework. We then investigate the impact of the uncertainty in the random field realizations on the computed quantities of interest. In particular, we look at uncertainty quantification for the case when the random field is used as a load in the mechanical problem. We also look at how the uncertainty in the random field affects the optimization of a mechanical component. Mat\'ern-type random fields provide a flexible model for random heterogeneous materials. We show that the correlation length of the random field has a strong influence on the uncertainty of the quantities of interest. This parameter should therefore be chosen carefully, for example, based on prior information. We also demonstrate that, when using a Mat\'ern-type random field, the optimal design may change significantly when the uncertainty in the random field realizations is taken into account.

A fundamental component in the theoretical school choice literature is the problem a student faces in deciding which schools to apply for. In practice, students often have to rank or score their preferences among available options (e..g., by using an ordinal scale). This paper studies how this ranking affects equilibrium outcomes of various mechanisms that are commonly employed and compares these effects across different types of rankings with increasing complexity: ordered choices versus scores on continuous scales from 0-1; simple ranks vs more complex ranked lists where multiple orderings can be specified within one list .

Recent work has shown that event definition following (EDF) can help improve the performance of event detection (ED) in videos. EDF aims to refine the event boundaries obtained from event cameras by leveraging the temporal consistency between the event stream and the video. However, existing EDF methods mainly focus on training a model using event data and struggle to improve from a pre-trained model. In this work, we study the zero-shot EDF problem, where a pre-trained video model is fine-tuned to utilize the event stream. We first analyze the failure cases of existing methods and identify two main challenges: (1) existing methods often rely on expensive re-training, which is not applicable to large-scale datasets, and (2) they lack consideration of the temporal alignment between the event stream and the video, which can be adversarial to the model. Thereby, we propose two simple yet effective solutions, namely self-supervised alignment (SA) and event-driven clipping (EC), to improve the performance of existing ED models without re-training. Specifically, SA aligns the event stream and the video by utilizing the predicted event boundaries from an ED model. EC further improves the alignment by clipping the video at the predicted event boundaries. Experimental results on publicly available datasets demonstrate that our method significantly outperforms existing EDF methods by a large margin.

Counterfactual explanations (CEs) enhance the interpretability of machine learning models by providing counterfactual conditions that can alter model predictions. However, recent works have shown that CEs are vulnerable to adversarial attacks designed to mislead humans. In this paper, we propose a new attack strategy called Adversarial Perturbation Attack (APA), which targets the counterfactual generation algorithm directly. APA is an efficient black-box attack that does not require access to the internal structure of the model. Experimental results show that our method significantly outperforms existing attacks in terms of effectiveness and stealthiness, suggesting that it poses a severe threat to the trustworthiness of CEs. Moreover, we conduct a human study that demonstrates that the ge

Poverty is one of the most pressing problems in the world, and there is a urgent need for effective policies to address it. Many would agree that discrimination is unfair and should be reduced, but it is not clear that doing so would help reduce poverty. Using an agent-based model, we explore the complex relationships between discrimination and poverty, and the potential of different policies to reduce poverty by acting on discrimination. Our model simulates a fictional country where people are discriminated against based on their ethnicity, and where employers can choose to hire more productive workers regardless of their ethnicity. We find that discrimination is bad for the economy as a whole, and that policies which reduce discrimination can lead to significant reductions in poverty. However, we also find that it is possible for a country to have low levels of discrimination and high levels of poverty, and that the relationship between discrimination and poverty is not monotonic. Our results suggest that reducing discrimination is good for poverty reduction, but that it is not the only factor that determines poverty levels.

With the proposal of the Segment Anything Model (SAM), fine-tuning SAM on ImageNet has achieved impressive results for image segmentation. We are interested in extending these powerful capabilities to video analysis, where videos are composed of a sequence of frames and each frame has its own semantics. As there is no ground truth annotation for the entire sequence, we instead propose to use SAM to generate a segmentation map that can be used as input to a downstream task. For example, we use the generated semantic map to train an action detection model that can detect actions in any frame of the video. In this paper, we conduct extensive experiments on several video datasets such as Human Activity Video Dataset (HAViD), UCF101, UCF101M, Kinetics400, MSRVTT, and Tacotron2.

Modern consumer devices must execute multimedia applications that exhibit high resource utilization. In order to efficiently execute these demanding applications, a processor architecture should have the ability to reconfigure its resources on-the-fly as dictated by the application demands. The performance of such an adaptive system is highly dependent upon how well it can predict and respond to its changing workloads. Traditional instruction-level profiling techniques are not effective in this domain due to their large overheads and limited sampling capability. Recent machine learning (ML)-based solutions have shown promising results for predicting execution time. However, they require access to a large dataset to train a model with sufficient accuracy. We propose an efficient online meth

Knowledge graph (KG) systems have been shown to be effective in capturing and presenting comprehensive knowledge on the web. However, their utility is limited without proper navigation and search methods. While state-of-the-art KG search methods have improved in recent years, they still struggle with ambiguity resolution and providing relevant results to users with diverse backgrounds and knowledge gaps. To address these problems, we propose iSummary, a novel personalized summary generation approach for KGs that uses natural language generation (NLG) and is powered by a deep reinforcement learning (DRL) based conversational agent. The agent interacts with users, understands their information needs, and generates personalized summaries that are tailored to the user's knowledge level and interests. We evaluate iSummary's effectiveness through a case study with the DBpedia KG. The results demonstrate the superiority of iSummary over state-of-the-art baselines in addressing user information needs, with an improvement of up to 32.6% in user satisfaction.

Legged robots are well-suited for broad exploration tasks in complex environments with yielding terrain. The legs provide the robot’s only contact points, and thus must be controlled to balance both stability demands (e.g., walking) and task objectives such as climbing or digging holes while avoiding obstacles like rocks on uneven ground surfaces [1]. While these challenges have been addressed extensively by recent research into legged locomotion control algorithms that use foot pressure data from multidimensional tactile sensors embedded within each leg of a robotic platform [2]], little work has explored how an onboard camera might also contribute toward improving overall performance during obstacle avoidance maneuvers using visual feedback alone without relying upon any other s

The advent of large vision-language models (LVLMs) represents a noteworthy milestone in the field, demonstrating their ability to perform robustly on various tasks. Despite this impressive feat and significant progress made by LVLs across diverse domains over recent years , there remains an urgent need for further research into how these powerful AI systems can be deployed responsibly when dealing with sensitive data or ethical concerns such as bias mitigation strategies . In particular during training stage where potential biases may emerge from dataset preparation process leading towards unequal outcomes due biased representations learned within networks' internal architectures resulting unfairness among different groups which must not happen at all costs! Hence we present here some key

Language models are capable of performing various tasks without explicit instruction, utilizing implicit planning skills. However, evaluating these skills in a systematic way is challenging. To address this challenge, we introduce the Procedural Warnings and Tips Dataset (PWT), a novel dataset designed to evaluate the procedural planning skills of language models. The dataset consists of warnings and tips generated by humans for 101 procedures from Wikipedia, covering domains such as cooking, cleaning, and transportation. These warnings and tips serve as implicit plans that describe the expected sequence of events and problem-solving steps in natural language. We present PARSADISE, a framework that leverages the PWT dataset to assess the planning skills of language models. PARSADISE utilizes causal event order validation, semantic similarity calculation, and hierarchical clustering to evaluate the logical consistency and relevance of the model's generated events. We demonstrate the effectiveness of PARSADISE by applying it to two case studies. The first case study involves fine-tuning a planner that generates warnings and tips for the repair domain. The second case study compares the planning skills of six state-of-the-art language models.

Laboratory experiments have shown that communication plays an important role in solving social dilemmas. Here we investigate a generalization of the prisoner's dilemma game to networks, where two players may communicate before deciding their strategies. Our model allows for the investigation of both deterministic and stochastic communication. We study this model on various types of networks with different characteristics. To quantify how communication affects the cooperation level, we use three metrics: the average payoff, the fraction of cooperators, and the average cost of defection. We find that communication has a significant positive impact on all three metrics, especially for larger populations. However, the effect depends on the network type. Communication is particularly effective

This paper presents a novel multi-modal Multi-Object Tracking (MOT) algorithm for self-driving cars. The key contributions are: 1) an efficient and effective MOT pipeline that addresses the challenges of multi-camera, multi-object tracking in real-time self-driving scenarios; 2) a new fusion module that fuses visual and thermal information to enhance the accuracy and robustness of object detection; 3) a multi-modality prediction model that utilizes both visual and thermal features to predict the future trajectory of the tracked objects. The proposed method achieves significant performance improvements over existing approaches, demonstrating its practical applicability in self-driving vehicles. Code is available at https://github.com/SunilDhanapalan/MM-MOT.git.

Purpose: Our study explored the use of artificial intelligence (AI) to diagnose autism spectrum disorder (ASD), leveraging the potential to improve early screening and intervention. In this paper, we present a comprehensive investigation into the efficacy of various AI techniques in ASD detection, focusing on multimodal AI approaches utilizing medical imaging data such as MRI scans. Methods: We conducted an extensive literature review to collect relevant studies published from 2016-2023. The methodological rigor of each study was evaluated using PRISMA guidelines for systematic reviews. Our analysis included both qualitative and quantitative assessments, aimed at providing insights into the state of current research efforts and identifying promising directions for future research.

Recent human-object interaction (HOI) detection approaches rely on high cost of manpower and time to generate large-scale training data. To address this issue, we propose a self-supervised learning approach that can effectively train models without the need for additional human annotations. The proposed approach involves a self-supervised pretext task in which objects are extracted from images with their corresponding textual descriptions. This is achieved through a novel multi-modal alignment network that leverages language-image alignment as well as object-context alignment. By incorporating these two types of alignments, the model learns to associate textual descriptions with the correct objects in images. Experimental results show that the proposed method achieves competitive performan

Large language models (LLMs) such as ChatGPT are increasingly proficient at writing essays, but their performance is limited by the narrowness of their knowledge. In contrast to human authors who can draw from a vast range of prior experience and expertise, LLMs must rely on the specific information they have been trained on. This paper introduces a novel method for augmenting the knowledge base of LLMs through co-authoring with humans. Our approach involves prompting a user to generate an initial draft using a chat interface, then editing this text in response to the model's generated corrections. We evaluate our approach via a user study involving 24 participants. The results show that users found our system easier to use than standard LLM editing interfaces, resulting in higher satisfac

Federated Learning (FL) thrives in training a global model with numerous clients by only sharing local model parameters. However, FL's performance suffers from the lack of data diversity and the non-IID distribution of the data. In this paper, we propose an efficient Multi-client Data Augmentation (MDA) method for FL to enhance the generalization ability and efficiency of FL systems. MDA introduces multiple clients in each communication round to improve the diversity of the augmented data and to reduce the server computational cost. We also present an online client selection strategy that can effectively select the representative clients. Experimental results on benchmark datasets demonstrate that MDA outperforms state-of-the-art methods in terms of both accuracy and efficiency. Additional

Large Language Models (LLMs) are composed of multiple layers of parameterized functions, and recent studies have shown that they can be fine-tuned to perform a wide range of tasks. However, removing layers from an LLM can significantly affect its performance, especially for tasks that require a deep understanding of the input text, such as question answering and code generation. Therefore, it is important to understand the contribution of each layer to the overall performance of the LLM. In this work, we propose a novel approach called Inference Layer Pruning (ILP) that can identify the necessary layers for inference in an LLM. ILP leverages soft pruning masks that can be learned through a proxy loss function to evaluate the contribution of each layer during inference. We evaluate ILP on two LLMs, namely GPT-2 and GPT-3, and show that we can remove up to 70% of the layers from each model, without a significant drop in performance. Our approach is 10 times more effective than recent layer pruning methods, and can help reduce the computational cost of LLMs.

Semi-supervised learning is a sound measure to relieve the strict demand of abundant annotated data. It has recently been widely applied in the field of natural language processing (NLP), including semantic segmentation. The core problem of semi-supervised learning lies in how to efficiently utilize unlabeled data while effectively leveraging limited labeled data to mitigate the performance gap between supervised and self-training methods. In this paper, we present an effective semi-supervised method named S2V for semantic image segmentation based on variational autoencoder (VAE). Specifically, we first apply VAE as an encoder to extract feature representations from both labeled and unlabeled images. Then, we develop a novel objective function that encourages the decoder to reconstruct the

The past few years have witnessed an arms-race between DNN-watermarking schemes and attacks. While attacks have become more and more effective, watermarking schemes have also become more sophisticated. However, all known DNN-watermarking schemes share a fundamental flaw: They all rely on the honest-verifier assumption. In this paper, we exploit this assumption to develop DeepEclipse, a framework to break DNN-watermarking schemes. DeepEclipse consists of two components: (i) a verifier that checks whether a given model is watermarked, and (ii) a fuzzer that generates a saliency map to locate the watermark. Our experiments show that DeepEclipse can break 11 out of 11 known DNN-watermarking schemes.

We introduce Cluster Edge Modification problems with constraints on the size of the clusters and study their complexity. The problem settings we consider are: a) Edge Deletion, b)... This paper studies $k$-connected subgraph detection in large graphs using local information only (e.\ g., vertex degrees). This is an important task for understanding how well connected different... A network design algorithm minimizes costs while maintaining connectivity properties to satisfy user requirements or service level agreements by creating physical connections between nodes within networks.... In this work I present research that has been carried out over the past five years at EURECOM as part my efforts towards developing more efficient algorithms under various optimization paradigms,...

We consider a learning-augmented online algorithm for a generalized newsstand problem introduced by Karp, Vazirani and Vazirani (1979). A newsstand seller has a finite set of $N$ newspapers and a finite time horizon of $T$ periods. In each period, the seller needs to decide the amount of each newspaper to order, given the prices of the newspapers and their reading deadlines. The objective is to minimize the sum of the transmission costs, incurred by the newspaper publisher, and the age of information, experienced by the customers. In the offline setting, the optimal algorithm is known to have a constant factor approximation for this objective. We aim to maintain a competitive ratio in an online setting where the publisher's prices and the customers' reading deadlines are not known a priori. We propose a deterministic online algorithm and show that its competitive ratio is bounded by a function of the age of information in the offline optimal solution. We also show that the competitive ratio can be further improved if the publisher's prices are either non-increasing or non-decreasing over time. For the general case, we propose a randomized online algorithm and show that its expected competitive ratio is bounded by twice the competitive ratio of the best deterministic online algorithm.

Circular programs are programs that can be executed in a cyclic manner, line by line, without any loss of information. They have been formalized by means of well-known fixed-point theorems, and their properties have been studied in relation to other program forms, notably to recursive programs. In this paper, we study the interplay between circularity and self-reference, by means of a simply-typed programming language that can be interpreted in two different ways: as programs that operate on finite data structures, and as specifications of infinite self-referential structures. We define what it means for a program to be circular in a purely syntactic way, without appealing to any form of evaluation. We then show that our notion of circular program is equivalent to the more traditional one, based on a semantic definition in terms of fixed points. We prove a generalization of Tait and Martin-L\"of's characterization of exact fixed points, and use it to show that self-referential structures can be circular. Finally, we show that our programming language is expressively complete with respect to the class of circular programs.

With the development of smart sensors and the Internet of Things, traffic data is easier to collect than ever before. However, the value of the data is limited without proper analysis. Traffic prediction, as one of the most important applications of Intelligent Transportation System, is facing new challenges: (1) Different from the previous homogeneous traffic data, the newly collected data is much more heterogeneous, containing values collected from sensors with different resolutions and accuracies; (2) The traffic data is much larger in volume, generated by more sensors and collected over longer periods of time; (3) The traffic data is much more complex, with structures ranging from simple speed data to complex multimodal data. To this end, we propose a traffic prediction framework called TPLLM, which leverages pretrained large language models (PLLMs) to enable zero-shot learning for traffic prediction. PLLMs are language models that are pretrained on a large dataset, e.g., natural language text. They can be fine-tuned on a downstream task, e.g., traffic prediction, by only a few labeled data. We evaluate TPLLM on three public traffic datasets, i.e., METR-LA, PRJ, and ETH. The results show that TPLLM outperforms state-of-the-art methods in terms of both effectiveness and efficiency.

Federated Learning (FL) is a machine learning (ML) approach that enables multiple decentralized clients to collaboratively train a shared model without sharing their raw data. However, the performance of FL can be significantly hindered by the presence of non-IID (non-independent and identically distributed) data distribution across clients. In this study, we propose a novel solution called Non-IID Data Reweighting (NIR), which addresses the problem of non-IID data in FL. Our method employs a reweighting strategy to redistribute the contribution of each client's data points based on their local accuracy. This technique effectively reduces the impact of heterogeneous data distribution among clients, thereby improving the overall performance of FL. Extensive experiments conducted on both syn

Advanced language-processing and machine-learning techniques promise massive efficiency improvements in the previously widely manual field of patent and technical knowledge management. This field presents large-scale and complex data with very precise contents and language representation of those contents. Particularly, patent texts can differ from mundane texts in various aspects, which entails significant opportunities and challenges. This paper presents a systematic overview of patent-related tasks and popular methodologies with a special focus on evolving and promising techniques. Language processing and particularly large language models as well as the recent boost of general generative methods promise to become game changers in the patent field. The patent literature and the fact-bas

Routing Protocol for Low Power and Lossy Networks (RPL) is the de facto routing protocol for low-power and lossy networks (LLNs), which is widely deployed in many applications, such as smart grids, building automation systems, and Industry 4.0. However, a recent attack, called RPL-DoS, has been discovered that exploits RPL's design flaw to compromise the security of LLN nodes and to disrupt its routing operations. Although several researchers have proposed solutions to detect this attack by leveraging network monitoring techniques, most of them rely on the use of centralized authorities, which may not be practical or scalable in large-scale deployments. In contrast, we propose a novel approach based on distributed and peer-to-peer mechanisms, which enables us to build a robust defense agai

Conversational systems often rely on embedding models for intent classification and intent clustering tasks. The advent of large language models (LLMs) has revolutionized the development of such systems, enabling more effective handling of complex queries that require deep contextual understanding. However, these LLM-based approaches lack interpretability and fail to provide explanations for their reasoning process. To address this issue, we introduce a novel approach for providing explainable responses in conversational systems using pretrained large language models. Our method employs three main steps: first, we extract relevant information from the input query by employing a sequence-to-sequence transformer model. Second, we generate an explanation using the extracted information as the

The field of biomedical research has witnessed a significant increase in the accumulation of vast amounts of data, particularly in the fields of genetics and proteomics. This surge in data generation has led to a growing need for automated solutions that can efficiently analyze and interpret complex biological datasets. To address this challenge, our team presents BIOME, a versatile deep learning framework designed for biomedical data analysis. Through a series of comprehensive experiments on three distinct biomedical datasets, we demonstrate the effectiveness of BIOME, highlighting its ability to accurately identify key features within various biomedical data types. Our results showcase the model's robustness in classifying different types of genes and proteins, providing valuable insight

Neural network models have a number of hyperparameters that must be chosen along with their architecture. This often involves the use of validation data, which is costly for both training and deployment. We propose using a model with an unstructured neural network architecture to estimate these parameters during training by solving a sequence of optimization problems. The estimated parameters are then used to train a structured architecture that can be deployed in production. Experiments show significant improvements over state-of-the-art methods across 18 diverse datasets from the UCI repository. Additionally, the proposed method has shown to be successful for training models on very large data sets (e.g., 270 million samples) in a single GPU day without any additional optimization or tra

Motion graphic (MG) videos are effective and compelling for presenting complex concepts through visualization. Motion graphics play an important role in education, instructional video, e-learning, business promotion, advertisement, and entertainment. However, it is challenging to generate MGs due to the time-consuming and tedious process of authoring MG assets. Recently, deep generative models have shown great potential in generating textures, 3D models, and images. Although these methods can be applied for MG generation, they still lack the capability of generating high-quality MGs with diverse content. In this paper, we propose a novel unified framework based on the diffusion model for MG generation. The proposed method enables the direct learning from unlabeled MG data as well as genera

With the advent of quantum technologies, we are on the verge of a new quantum era, which will fundamentally change how we process and manage data. Indeed, quantum mechanics provides novel ways to store and manipulate information, offering advantages over classical approaches in terms of speed and resource efficiency. Recognizing the potential of quantum data management (QDM), researchers have proposed various quantum database models and algorithms. However, the majority of them suffer from high complexity, limited scalability, or practicality issues, thus failing to provide real advantages over classical counterparts. In this paper, we propose a new QDM framework that overcomes the limitations of previous models. We introduce the quantum nearest neighbor problem, a fundamental task in QDM, and provide the first sublinear-time quantum algorithm for this problem. We also show that our algorithm is practical by implementing it on a real-world dataset. Our work opens the door to more efficient and practical QDM algorithms. In particular, we show how our quantum nearest neighbor algorithm can be applied to improve quantum recommendation systems and propose other potential applications of QDM.

Self-supervised learning has produced impressive results in multimedia domains of audio, vision and speech. However, these advances have largely been achieved using domain-specific datasets, which are not scalable to new modalities or realistic scenarios. In this paper, we propose a unified framework that can be adapted to new modalities by simply fine-tuning a few parameters, without requiring any additional data collection. We achieve this by introducing a multi-modal prompting network that enables the model to leverage information from multiple modalities during training. By contrast with existing methods, our approach is effective even when only one modality is available for training. Our extensive evaluation across three diverse datasets demonstrates the effectiveness and generalizabi

The audio-text data simulation pipeline presented in this paper aims to produce simulated data that emulates the process of capturing audio samples and transcribing them into text. The motivation behind this pipeline is to facilitate the development and evaluation of audio-based models, particularly those employing automatic speech recognition (ASR) and audio captioning. The pipeline commences with the selection of appropriate single-event sounds from a sound effects library, followed by the synthesis of audio samples through the concatenation of these sounds. To transcribe the generated audio samples into text, we employ both automatic and manual transcription methods. The resulting data consists of audio samples, their corresponding text transcriptions, and metadata. The metadata encompass information about the audio sources, the transcription methods applied, and various other details. This data simulation pipeline yields a dataset that can be utilized for the training and evaluation of ASR models, as demonstrated by the inclusion of a pre-trained example in the paper. The code and instructions for obtaining the data are available at https://github.com/mazaheri96/ATP.

Autonomous off-road navigation requires an accurate semantic understanding of the environment, often converted into a semantic map. The current state-of-the-art uses deep learning to extract features from raw images for use in mapping and localization. However, these approaches are computationally expensive, limiting their applicability on resource-constrained platforms such as micro aerial vehicles (MAVs). In this paper, we propose a novel approach that leverages lightweight segmentation models with limited capacity, but capable of handling low-resolution imagery. Our method achieves comparable performance to existing methods utilizing large-scale models, while also demonstrating superior real-time capabilities. Additionally, our results showcase how our method's performance can be furthe

With the advancement of large language models, language-based forecasting has recently emerged as a promising method for predicting time series. However, these methods are vulnerable to common-sense errors that arise due to unintended interactions between the model and its environment. In this paper, we demonstrate the occurrence of such an error in forecasting future COVID-19 cases using GPT-3.5 and LLaMA2. The error is triggered by a subtle change in the question prompt used to generate forecasts, which leads to the model making incorrect predictions about the number of cases. We then present an approach to mitigate this type of error, based on fine-tuning the model with synthetic data generated from controlled simulation experiments. Our results show that fine-tuned models perform bette

Low precision training can significantly reduce the computational overhead of training deep neural networks (DNNs). Though, it may degrade their accuracy. This study proposes a novel approach that utilizes multiple DNNs for low-precision training to mitigate this tradeoff and improve accuracy. The proposed method employs a customized loss function to train each DNN independently with different precisions. The results from the first layer of all DNNs are then combined using a weighted sum to obtain a final output. We show that our approach achieves up to 17% higher accuracy over conventional low-precision training methods while still retaining 95% of the computational savings. Experiments on six image classification datasets demonstrate the effectiveness of our approach in enhancing the acc

Event Causality Identification (ECI) refers to detect causal relations between events in texts. The existing studies on ECI are mainly based upon the supervised learning methods that require a large number of labeled data, which is costly and time-consuming for real applications since it requires manual annotation by domain experts with specialized knowledge about causality relationship detection tasks. In this paper we propose an unsupervised approach named Causal Transformer(CaT), aiming at solving both problems above via training from unlabeled text corpora only without any external supervision signals or prior annotations as input features extracted automatically through language model pretraining . Specifically , our proposed method consists two parts: 1 ) A contrastive framework call

Data annotation interfaces predominantly leverage ground truth labels to guide annotators toward accurate responses. With the development of advanced machine learning models, pre-trained models can be used as a source of knowledge and guidance for data labeling. In this paper, we present PreLabeler, a novel framework that leverages large language models (LLMs) as data assistants during manual annotation. Given an input task, PreLabeler generates context-specific guidance tailored to each step of the annotation process. The resulting instructions are then presented to human annotators alongside the ground truth labels. Our experiments on various datasets show that PreLabeler improves annotation efficiency by up to 46% while maintaining high accuracy. Furthermore, we observe that PreLabeler

Text data has become extremely valuable due to the emergence of machine learning algorithms that learn from it. The majority of this data is not labeled, which necessitates methods for inferring missing labels. Inferring missing labels in text presents challenges because it must be done at a document-level and involves multiple aspects like word meaning, sentence structure, and contextual information. However, existing approaches primarily focus on solving individual tasks within these broader categories. To address this gap, we introduce a framework for multi-task learning across a wide range of labeling tasks. Our framework is designed with modularity in mind and can easily accommodate new tasks without any changes. We experimentally evaluate our approach on several datasets with varying

The effectiveness of clopidogrel, a widely used antiplatelet medication, varies among patients. Clopidogrel is metabolized by the cytochrome P450 enzyme CYP2C19 and produces active metabolites via two distinct pathways: Thromboxane synthase (TXAS) inhibition and Adenosine diphosphate-ribose pyrophosphatase (ADPRT) inhibition. Some studies have found that 55% of the population lack the functional TXAS gene, whereas other studies estimate as high as 78%. These genetic differences may lead to disparities in how well clopidogrel works for different groups of people. This study aimed to determine if there is a difference in antiplatelet efficacy based on these variants.

Multimodal large language models (MMLLMs) are capable of processing natural language together with other modalities, such as images. When an MMLLM is used in a downstream application, the application's input may consist of a single modality, e.g., an image, or a mixture of multiple modalities, e.g., an image and text. Remarkably, recent work has shown that a single MMLLM can outperform multiple specialized models that are tailored to individual modalities. The generalization power of MMLLMs stems from their ability to adapt to the input mixture at inference time. However, existing MMLLMs adapt to the input mixture in a static fashion, using a fixed number of adaptation layers. This ignores the fact that some modalities, e.g., text, are more parameter efficient to represent than others, e.g., images. To address this challenge, we propose a novel MMLLM called FeastYourEyes, which adapts to the input mixture in a dynamic fashion, using a mixture-of-resolution adaptation mechanism. We show that FeastYourEyes outperforms existing MMLLMs on benchmark datasets, while also reducing the number of adaptation layers needed at inference time. We further show that FeastYourEyes can be seamlessly integrated with existing MMLLMs, without any modifications to the base models.

AI Large Language Models (LLMs) like ChatGPT are set to revolutionise the way we interact with technology and each other. Despite their incredible potential, some argue that LLMs' lack of 'consciousness' disqualifies them from being considered truly intelligent. This article explores the nature of consciousness and its relevance to artificial intelligence (AI), focusing on the unique characteristics of LLMs. It argues that these models do not exhibit true consciousness, but instead demonstrate a form of simulated consciousness, which is distinct from human consciousness. We delve into the intricacies of consciousness, the scientific understanding of it, and how it differs from simple algorithms or complex decision-making processes. Through a comprehensive analysis of current research and p

This paper introduces the novel task of multimodal puzzle solving, framed within the setting of a board game. We propose an open-source dataset called PuzzleSolving, consisting of 10,000 instances designed to test the reasoning abilities of AI agents across multiple modalities: vision, language, and audio. Our experiments demonstrate that current state-of-the-art large language models struggle with this task, highlighting a need for further research in multimodal reasoning. The dataset, code, and additional details can be found at https://www.multimodalsolvepuzzles.com/. We hope this work will inspire further research in multimodal reasoning and contribute to advancing the field of artificial intelligence.

We present HMD-Poser, a monocular camera system for real-time, scalable, and accurate human motion tracking. Scalability is a key prerequisite for the widespread deployment of motion tracking in challenging environments such as crowded spaces. However, traditional approaches often rely on dense observations, high computational cost, or both, which hinder their applicability in practice. In contrast, HMD-Poser leverages the inherent sparsity of human motion to significantly reduce the number of observations required for accurate tracking, thus greatly decreasing computational cost and latency. Our approach exploits two fundamental properties of human motion to achieve this: (1) the repetitive nature of movements results in many motion cycles being present in a relatively sparse set of observations, and (2) the sequential correlation in video frames can be used to predict future frames given a sparse number of observations. By combining these properties, we can track complex human motions in real-time at 60fps on a single GPU and deploy the system on-device for latency-critical applications. In addition, our method is scalable to large group tracking and unseen pose tracking scenarios. We evaluate our framework on multiple benchmark datasets, demonstrating competitive performance compared to state-of-the-art methods.

Physical Unclonable Functions (PUFs) have been shown to be a highly efficient means of authentication in the past decade. The majority of existing PUFs rely on the inherent physical variability of electronic circuits, which is not applicable to most emerging technologies such as 3D Integrated Circuit (3DIC). In this work, we propose a novel method for generating PUFs using a single-chip integrated circuit that can provide an effective mechanism for security protection. To generate a unique pattern on each chip, we use a programmable 3D IC to randomly position two types of transistors: MOSFET and NMOSFET. Different combinations of these transistor types result in different resistance values, thus providing distinct patterns. We utilize random logic with multiplexing techniques to generate u

The pursuit of accurate 3D hand pose estimation stands as a keystone for understanding human-computer interaction in various domains. Recent advancements have witnessed the emergence of 3D hand pose estimation models, harnessing large-scale datasets and pre-trained vision transformers to achieve remarkable performance. However, these models often exhibit overfitting issues due to their high complexity. To address this challenge, we propose HAND, a novel framework that leverages distillation from a simple teacher to enhance generalization capabilities in the student model. Specifically, our teacher model is equipped with a simplified version of the Student's architecture, effectively reducing computational overheads while retaining robustness against overfitting. By incorporating distillati

Given the large-scale multi-modal training of recent vision-based models and their generalization capabilities, we believe that these models have a great potential in addressing the long-standing problem of human activity recognition (HAR) from surveillance video. However, to our knowledge, there is no study that evaluates how vision-based models perform on HAR datasets. In this work, we evaluate the performance of 16 different vision-based models on 7 different HAR datasets. The results show that these models are indeed capable of learning complex representations of human activities, even if they were not specifically trained for HAR. Furthermore, we show that simple modifications such as fine-tuning can significantly improve the performance of these models. Finally, we provide insights i

Web tables contain a large amount of valuable knowledge and have inspired tabular language models aimed at tacking the task on extracting text in table-like format. However, it is not straightforward to evaluate how well these methods actually capture this kind of information with existing evaluation metrics for question answering tasks or even specific metric designed specifically towards web extraction such as WEMD which can only be used during training time when using precomputed datasets like WikiTableQuestions (WTQ). In order address both issues mentioned above we propose an automatic framework that enables fast generation diverse test sets from real world sources without any manual annotation effort required by human experts; while still being able provide meaningful insights regardi

There is an ongoing debate regarding the potential of Large Language Models (LLMs) in generating images. While LLMs have demonstrated remarkable proficiency in language generation, their capabilities with respect to visual imagery remain ambiguous. This paper investigates the feasibility of employing LLMs as image generators through a comprehensive evaluation that covers various aspects, including text-image alignment and semantic representation, generative diversity, and model performance. Our findings indicate significant limitations in terms of both content quality and diversity, highlighting the need for further research on improving the capabilities of LLMs in image generation. Additionally, we explore the potential of incorporating LLMs into existing image generation systems, providi

Lyndon words are extensively studied in combinatorics on words -- they play a crucial role, for example (but not exclusively), in the classification of finite-state automata and transducers. We consider two problems: first to characterize those Lyndon words that belong simultaneously to any given set $\mathcal{S}$ or family ${\cal S} $of sets; second we wish [...] We prove that all infinite selfcomplementary binary sequences have complexity at most linear times logarithmic with respectto their length when viewed as 1D cellular automaton rules under standard clocking conditions but no more than this bound can be obtained without additional assumptions about periodicity properties like being eventually periodic after an initial delay period.
