To address this issue, we propose an adaptive prompt tuning method that can continuously optimize prompts based on the LLM's actual performance rather than expert guesses. Our method employs a novel performance metric called Prompt Score, which evaluates the effectiveness of LLM outputs by comparing them with those obtained from expert models. The Prompt Score is then used as a proxy for loss functions in a reinforcement learning framework. We demonstrate the eff

In this paper, we investigate denial-of-service attacks against status updating. The target system is the one where a single entity sends out updates to a large number of entities in a short period. We propose two types of denial-of-service attacks, namely, update-spoofing and update-delaying attacks, as well as the corresponding countermeasures. For these attacks, we provide concrete attack scenarios with an evaluation of their effectiveness. As a result, we show that both types of attacks are effective at some extent. Furthermore, we demonstrate that the proposed countermeasures can reduce the effectiveness of the attacks, but they may also introduce new security problems and performance issues. Therefore, we conclude that it is challenging to achieve complete defense against status upda

This paper introduces OccFusion, a straightforward and efficient sensor fusion framework for predicting 3D object occupancy with only one camera. We make use of the 2D-to-3D projection matrix to map the point cloud into a coordinate system that is consistent across multiple cameras. The camera intrinsics are then transformed from one frame to the next through warping, ensuring that the same intrinsic parameters remain valid. Afterward, we apply occlusion reasoning to determine which parts of each object are visible in the current view. These two steps allow us to establish correspondence between points across different frames, which facilitates sensor fusion. Additionally, we propose a new loss function that encourages the fused feature representations to be more aligned with the ground tr

Recently, large vision-language models (VLMs) have been demonstrated to possess impressive performance in many downstream vision tasks. However, the improvements are often evaluated on specialized test sets that are tailored to the tasks, and the evaluations are often conducted without any comparison to the previous VLMs. In this work, we aim to provide a broad and fair assessment of the recent VLMs, by evaluating them on a large panel of 30+ downstream tasks, using two different benchmarks for each task, and reporting the results on four different VLMs, namely CLIP, VICReg, Barlow Twins, and PLEX. We first show that the performance of the VLMs varies greatly across different downstream tasks, and that none of the VLMs consistently outperforms the others. We then investigate the impact of the VLMs' size, depth, and pre-training tasks on their downstream performance. We find that larger VLMs tend to perform better on downstream tasks, but the depth of the VLMs has little impact on their performance. We also find that pre-training VLMs on more diverse datasets can lead to better downstream performance. Finally, we compare the computational efficiency of the VLMs by measuring their inference latency and memory consumption. We hope that our study provides a comprehensive and fair assessment of the recent VLMs, and helps the practitioners to better utilize the VLMs for their downstream vision tasks.

Several tools have recently been proposed for assisting researchers during various stages of the research life-cycle, from literature reviews to data analysis. These tools are typically standalone, and they often work in isolation from each other. As a result, researchers are forced to navigate through multiple platforms, resulting in increased effort and potentially leading to missed opportunities. In this paper, we present Rex: A platform that aims to provide researchers with an integrated set of tools for conducting their research efficiently and effectively. We discuss our approach towards integrating existing tools, as well as the challenges and opportunities presented by such an integration. Finally, we provide details on our current progress and future directions for Rex. Our goal i

Current model-based reinforcement learning (MBRL) agents struggle with long-term dependencies. These challenges are exacerbated when the state space is large and continuous, such as in robotics or autonomous driving domains where a single observation may contain information from thousands of pixels that must be processed quickly for real time control decisions to occur on an embedded device like those found onboard many modern robots today! In this paper we propose two novel techniques: first using an attention mechanism based off vanilla ResNet architecture; second employing self supervised contrastive training via latent feature alignment tasks which allows us learn robust representations without expensive data labels . Our results show significant improvements over current baselines acr

Support vector machine (SVM) has achieved many successes in machine learning, especially for a small number of examples. However, the high computational complexity and time consumption limit its application to large-scale data sets, which hinders it from further development. To overcome this challenge, neural network-based SVM was proposed to combine the advantages of both methods by replacing the nonlinear kernel with a simple linear function, reducing the complexity significantly but keeping the efficiency of the SVM model. This paper focuses on proposing an improved neural network-based SVM approach that combines the strengths of the SVM method with that of neural networks by employing various activation functions, such as rectified linear unit (ReLU), softmax, sigmoid, tanh, etc., to e

This paper addresses the challenges of aligning large language models (LLMs) with human values. We propose a framework that leverages recent advances in counterfactual reasoning to align LLMS by comparing their outputs against real-world data and correcting them when necessary, while preserving model performance on downstream tasks such as text classification or question answering . Our approach is evaluated across multiple datasets for different LLM architectures ranging from small (<1B parameters), midsize (~250M - ~376 M params. ), big (> 8 B ) , including GPTJ and GForCe to understand how well it can handle challenging alignment problems like hate speech detection using biased training sets versus neutral ones; we also demonstrate its ability at scaling up into larger sizes via an e

To enable large-scale and efficient deployment of artificial intelligence (AI), the combination of AI and hardware has become an important topic. This paper proposes a novel framework, called Hardware Augmented Reinforcement Learning with Memory Modules in Proximal Policy Optimization for Large Scale Systems(HARL) to address this issue by utilizing both offloading optimization algorithms based on proximal policy networks as well edge memory modules combined into reinforcement learning models using actor–critic architecture .

This study presents a systematic enumeration of spherical ($SO(3)$) type parallel roboballs. The system is defined by the geometric configurations and operation modes of individual robots, which are modeled as homogeneous transformations in $SE\left ( 2 \right )$, such that they can be assembled to form robot spheres with arbitrary radii under certain constraints on their rotations $\theta$ ($\in R^{1}$) or velocities ${\dot{\rho}} = {\rm d}\hat{R}/dt$. A complete set-theoretic classification scheme for these types based upon fundamental algebraic properties was developed; namely: i).

Providing emotional support through dialogue systems is becoming increasingly important in today's world, where technology-mediated communication has become the norm. However, traditional chatbots are often limited to a fixed set of predefined responses and fail to understand user emotions accurately. This study aims to address this gap by proposing an innovative methodology for creating emotionally intelligent chatbots that can empathize and respond with personalized messages tailored to individual users. Our proposed approach focuses on developing a dataset that captures the nuances of user emotions and their corresponding conversational patterns. We also introduce a novel strategy for generating customized responses based on contextual understanding and sentiment analysis. The experimen

We study the design of trust assignment mechanisms in the private graph model, where a seller has a graph of potential buyers, knows their valuations, and wishes to maximize its revenue by solving the trust assignment problem. In this problem, the seller needs to decide which buyers to trust and how many units of the good to sell to each trusted buyer. We consider two objectives for the seller: maximizing its revenue or its Bayesian expected revenue. In both cases, we show that the seller can use a simplified version of the problem, which we call the trusted assignment problem. In this problem, the seller knows in advance which buyers it will trust and needs to decide how many units to sell to each of them. We present a general mechanism for the private graph model that achieves a constant factor approximation for both objectives. This mechanism relies on a new robustness notion for Bayesian games, which we call prediction robustness. We show that the trusted assignment problem is prediction robust, and hence the mechanism can be applied to the private graph model. We move from the trusted assignment problem to the private graph model by presenting two new techniques. The first is a general technique that transforms any approximation algorithm for the trusted assignment problem into an approximation mechanism for the private graph model. The second technique is a postprocessing method that further improves the approximation factor by optimizing over the set of trust assignments. We move from the private graph model to the public graph model by presenting a reduction from the latter to the former. Via our results, we improve on the approximation factors for both models. For the private graph model, we improve the approximation factor for revenue maximization from 1/2 to 2/3 and the approximation factor for Bayesian expected revenue from 1/2 to 3/4. For the public graph model, we improve the approximation factor for revenue maximization from 1/3 to 2/5.

Viewport prediction is the crucial task for adaptive 360-degree video streaming. It has been investigated in various ways, and most works have focused on modeling user interaction behaviors to predict a potential viewport area or viewpoint position of future frames by exploiting deep learning models with human gaze data as input features [1]. However it is challenging to use this kind approach due that large amount labeled training dataset are required while users’ eye movement behavior varies from persontoperson even under similar scene conditions . In recent years , researchers started exploring alternative approaches which exploit lowlevel visual information suchas image saliency maps generated via computational methods[2]or using multiview images captured during head movements taken at

Self-supervised learning has produced impressive results in multimedia domains of audio, vision and speech. However its application to text remains largely unexplored due partly by the lack a suitable data augmentation strategy that can effectively transform textual information into an appropriate form for self supervision while preserving semantic meaningfulness at training time but also maintaining fidelity during inference when applied on unseen documents with potentially different vocabularies or structures from those seen before (e..g., short vs long sentences). In this paper we introduce \textit{TextAug}, which consists two components: A novel sentence compression module leveraging word alignments extracted via machine translation models; An augmented version based off cosine similar

Machine Translation is a mature technology for many high-resource language pairs. However in the context of low resource languages, especially when dealing with complex morphological phenomena such as agglutination, machine translation systems often fail to achieve good performance. One possible solution is to learn morphology-based linguistic features from the training data and integrate them into the neural network architecture. In this paper we present a novel approach based on a combination of a morphological parser and a recurrent neural network, which provides a better understanding of how these two components interact in order to improve machine translation results for a variety of low-resource language pairs. In particular, our method outperforms state-of-the art approaches for Bas

Recent advancements in large-scale pre-trained text-to-image models have sparked a surge of interest in generative AI, revolutionizing the field of digital art. However, existing studies have mainly focused on human evaluations to assess the aesthetics and quality of generated images. In this paper, we investigate how different text-to-image model types impact the quality of generated images from a technical perspective. We introduce a quantitative evaluation framework that leverages metrics for image similarity, structural integrity, color accuracy, and realism assessment to evaluate the quality of generated images across six popular text-to-image models: DALL-E 2, Stable Diffusion, Imagen, OpenAI CLIP-VQGAN, OpenAI GPT-3.5-turbo, and Hugging Face Lora.

This paper introduces Hierarchical Diffusion Policy (HDP), a hierarchical agent architecture that enables the simultaneous execution of multiple policies at different levels. We propose an LLM-based hierarchical agent with three layers, where each layer performs a specific task in a multi-level decision-making process. The LLM is responsible for generating policies and performing policy evaluation and selection, while the other two layers are dedicated to executing these policies. Our experimental results on the Atari game Ms. Pac-Man demonstrate that HDP achieves superior performance compared to single-layer agents when executing complex tasks. Additionally, we introduce the concept of "hierarchy bias" which can be used to better understand the tradeoffs between single-level vs. hierarchi

Transformer has become one of the most popular architectures for multivariate time series (MTS) modeling. However, current transformer models still suffer from two limitations: 1) They are unable to capture complex temporal interactions among time series, which often exist in real-world MTS data; and 2) They exhibit suboptimal memory efficiency due to the quadratic computational complexity between input and output dimensions. To address these challenges, this paper proposes a new framework that leverages graph neural networks to enhance the capability of capturing complex temporal interactions. We propose a new design called Graph Attention Fusion (GAF) that integrates with transformers to effectively learn both local and global dependencies in MTS data. Furthermore, we introduce an effici

In the rapidly evolving field of machine learning (ML), data augmentation (DA) has emerges as a powerful tool for enhancing model generalization and preventing overfitting. However, traditional DA methods often struggle to generate diverse, high-quality synthetic data in complex domains, leading to suboptimal results. To address these challenges, we propose DA-GPT, a novel framework that leverages large language models (LLMs) as generative tools for synthetic data generation. Specifically, LLM is used to automatically create prompts based on the training data, which are then used to generate synthetic data with similar characteristics. This approach not only ensures the diversity and quality of generated data but also greatly reduces the human effort required for data annotation. Our exten

Pre-trained language models have been proven to possess strong base capabilities, which not only excel in downstream tasks but also serve as a foundation for prompting. However, the complexity of prompting makes it difficult to quantify and understand the role of pre-training. This paper proposes Promptability, a novel metric that measures the inherent capability of a model in prompting. Specifically, we propose an efficient promptability estimation framework consisting of (i) a multi-task learning strategy for parameter-efficient training of the model, and (ii) a task-agnostic evaluation method based on a novel masked prompting paradigm. Empirical results show that Promptability can effectively measure the promptability of various pre-trained models, and is more correlated with prompt per

Grading short answer questions automatically with interpretable reasoning behind the grading decision is a challenging goal. The most prominent methods for automatic grading are based on machine learning and perform reasonably well. However, they cannot provide an explanation for their decisions. In this paper, we explore the question of whether a rule-based system can be designed to grade short answers automatically with explanations. We investigate two key factors that contribute to successful rule-based systems: 1) the design of the rules themselves (i.e., what makes a good rule), and 2) the ability of the rule-based system to generalize from the rules it has learned to new problems. Our results show that a simple rule-based system can achieve an accuracy comparable to state-of-the-art

Model merging is to combine fine-tuned models derived from multiple domains, with the intent of generating a more robust and generalized model. In this paper we present an approach for multidomain neural language modeling by leveraging recent advances in self supervised learning (SSL) through generative pre training methods such as GPT2/3 or BERT based approaches like RoBERTa; specifically our focus will be on how these techniques can improve accuracy when dealing cross domain data sets containing text that differs significantly across categories e.,g news vs science articles etc .

This paper explores the potential of Physics-Informed Neural Networks (PINNs) to solve two challenging problems in structural dynamics: estimating damping properties from measured vibrations and modeling dynamic responses using only data. This study presents a novel approach that combines PINN with an ensemble learning method, where multiple predictions are generated by varying model hyperparameters such as training time steps or input layer sizes within specified ranges for each prediction task; this allows us not just generate one but many possible solutions simultaneously which could potentially be more accurate than single solution obtained via traditional methods like FEM/FEA simulations alone due increased accuracy through averaging process across all individual models' outputs durin

Nano-machines circulating inside the human body, collecting data on tissue conditions, represent a vital part of next-generation medical diagnostic systems. However, for these devices to operate effectively, they need to relay not only their medical measurements but also their positions. This paper introduces a novel localization method for in-body nano-machines based on the magnetic field, leveraging the advantageous magnetic permeability of all human tissues. The entire proposed localization system is described, starting from 10x10 ${\mu}m^2$ magnetometers to be integrated into the nano-machines, to a set of external wires generating the magnetic field. Mathematical equations for the localization algorithm are also provided, assuming the nano-machines do not execute the computations them

Self-supervised learning has produced impressive results in multimedia domains of audio, vision and speech. In this paper, we present a comprehensive review of self-supervised learning methods for video tasks, focusing on the state-of-the-art techniques and their applications to various downstream tasks. We first introduce pretext tasks that have been widely used to train self-supervised models from unlabeled data. We then discuss different types of architectures used for training these pretext tasks. Finally, we present a review of downstream tasks where these pretrained models are fine-tuned, including action recognition, temporal segmentation, event detection and action localization. For each type of task, we discuss the corresponding evaluation metrics, and provide an overview of recen

An open stochastic system \`a la Willems is a system affected two qualitatively different types of disturbances: the internal ones, which depend on its own state only; and external inputs (e.g., exogenous noise). The goal in this paper is to construct an optimal controller that minimizes the long-term average cost function subject both $to$ internal disturbance/stochasticity within itself as well {\it {and}} with respect 2)external input from outside environment(s), i.~{w}. We start by introducing some basic concepts such s stability theory for partially observed Markovian systems via Lyapunov functions under certain constraints due their inherent randomness nature . Next we extend these results using advanced techniques like differential inclusions or stochastic control methods where app

An \emph{eight-partition} of a finite set of points (respectively, vectors) in the Euclidean plane is an eight-tuple of subsets that partitions the given set. The first eight-partition problem in the literature was introduced by Katchalsky and Lunzer in 1976. They showed that for every $n\geq 3$, there exist $n$ points on the plane such that no subset of three points forms a triangle and the set cannot be partitioned into four subsets with the following property: each pair of points in distinct sets does not form a straight line. This result has been generalized to other types of sets such as segments, polygons, and polyhedra in higher dimensions.

The internet has had a profound impact on human sexuality, facilitating new forms of both connection and exploitation. This paper explores the intersection of digital technology and image-based sexual abuse (IBSA), examining how online platforms can proactively protect users from non-consensual image sharing. We introduce the concept of a Consent Ledger, a decentralized digital record that maintains a history of a photo's consensual status, enabling recipients to verify consent status and origin. The ledger employs a novel, permissionless consensus protocol to add and update records, ensuring accessibility and control. We evaluate the technical feasibility of the Consent Ledger and its potential to empower individuals, deter abusers, and support legal action. This work aims to foster safer digital intimacy by enabling informed decisions and proactive protection.

Consider the Telephone Broadcast problem in which an input is a connected graph $G$ on $n$ vertices and a set of pairs $\{u_1,v_1\}, ...,\{u_q,v_q\}$ of vertices (the telephone calls). Each vertex has some non-negative cost. The task is to find a minimum-cost tree spanning all vertices such that for every pair $\{u_i,v_i\}$, one vertex among $u_i$ and $v_i$ lies on the path from the other vertex to the root. We show that this problem admits a $(2+\epsilon)$-approximation algorithm, where $\epsilon > 0$ can be any constant, improving upon the previously known factor of $3$.

We study zero-sum differential games with state constraints and one-sided information, where the informed players can make nonanticipative strategies while the uninformed player is required to follow a prescribed strategy. In this paper we show that, for any such game between a finite number of players with complete information, there exists a finite-horizon optimal policy for the informed players which is not necessarily nonanticipative. This implies that no finite-horizon strategy for the informed player can be made nonanticipative without knowledge of the full state space of the uninformed player; in particular, if the state space of the uninformed player is infinite, then there does not exist a finite-horizon nonanticipative strategy.

In this paper, we present a self-supervised learning framework for visual perception tasks, which is able to learn multiple tasks simultaneously with a shared visual representation. Our proposed framework learns a task-agnostic representation by predicting the execution of a diverse set of tasks on-board a nano-quadrotor. The quadrotor is equipped with a limited set of hardware resources, which severely limits the computation and storage capabilities on-board. To overcome these limitations, we propose a novel approach that leverages parallel processing and in-memory computing hardware architectures, and is able to perform gradient-based learning of neural networks directly on-board. Experiments show that our approach learns a shared representation from scratch, without any pre-training, and improves the performance of each individual task over time. Furthermore, we show that our framework is able to learn multiple tasks simultaneously, without any task-specific configuration, by predicting the execution of four different tasks: forward motion, obstacle avoidance, landing site detection, and object tracking. Finally, we evaluate the performance of our framework in simulated and real-world environments, and show that it consistently outperforms a baseline approach that learns separately for each task. Our code is publicly available at https://github.com/MISTLab/Self-Supervised-Learning.

The digital age introduced the Digital Ecological Niche (DEN), revolutionizing human interactions. Our study explores the concept of DEN, a virtual space that facilitates social connection and information exchange within the online realm. We analyze how DENs operate through the lenses of structure, agency, and ecology to enhance user engagement, knowledge acquisition, and resource allocation in various social media platforms like Twitter, Instagram, TikTok, and Reddit. By examining these elements, we delve into the unique features and functionalities of each platform, while also considering the role of users as active participants in shaping and navigating these ecosystems. The emergence of DENs has significantly impacted our social lives by offering alternative spaces for interaction and

We present TextMonkey, a large multimodal model (LMM) tailored for the task of generating text from an image and monkey videos. We introduce a novel approach to incorporate monkey videos as an additional modality, which can enhance text generation in both high-level visual concepts and low-level detail. Our LMM is trained on 80 billion tokens across 5.7 million images and 134K monkey videos with 22K monkeys. It is evaluated by comparing its performance against a state-of-the-art image-only baseline, Textual-Large Language Model (TLLM). Our results show that TextMonkey significantly outperforms TLLM on both text quality and content consistency metrics, achieving an absolute improvement of 2.6% on average across all metrics.

In this paper, we address web-scale visual entity recognition, specifically the task of mapping a given image to the most relevant Wikipedia page. We focus on images that have been collected from the wild, and thus may exhibit various degrees of quality, scale, viewpoint, etc. Our work contributes two novel approaches for handling such challenges: i) a method for detecting and correcting perspective distortions (e.g., caused by camera calibration errors), and ii) an algorithm for improving visual similarity in terms of semantic content. The latter approach is inspired by the notion of contextualized word embeddings, and is based on a neural language model that learns to predict image-related metadata. Through extensive experiments with real-world data sets, we show that both techniques sig

Academic documents are packed with texts, equations, tables, and figures, requiring comprehensive understanding. The current text-based retrieval models cannot provide a full picture of the academic document's content without considering these components. In this paper, we propose a novel framework named FLUENT to tackle the challenges in the multimodal academic document understanding. Specifically, we first utilize a multi-modal encoder to extract features from all relevant modalities. Then, we propose an effective semantic attention module to model the interactions among different modalities. Moreover, we introduce a cross-modal retrieval mechanism based on the contrastive loss to enhance the consistency between query and results. Finally, we design a novel contrastive ranking strategy t

This paper considers a movable antenna (MA)-aided secure multiple-input multiple-output system in the presence of an eavesdropper. The MA is installed at each legitimate user to provide auxiliary signals for encryption, and it can be moved along its linear path with time delay constraints such that no security breach occurs during movement or when moving back into position after transmission completion. Assuming perfect synchronization between transmit antennas across different links as well ... We consider two classes of continuous-time Markov processes: one class has independent increments on disjoint intervals; another requires only independence up until finite stopping times but allows dependent jumps thereafter.

The Internet of Things (IoT) has the potential to improve our lives in numerous ways, from smart homes and cities to connected vehicles and healthcare. At the same time, IoT also presents significant challenges, not least of which is security and privacy. One of the most fundamental security properties is trustworthiness, which is essential for reliable and secure IoT systems. Trustworthiness depends on the accuracy and reliability of the data used to make decisions, as well as the integrity of the systems that process and act on that data. Therefore, building trust in data is crucial for IoT systems. In this paper, we provide an overview of the challenges and potential solutions for building trust in data for IoT systems. We focus on three key areas: 1) trust in data, 2) trust in systems, and 3) trustworthiness assessment. We propose a framework for building trust in data for IoT systems, which we apply to two case studies: smart homes and connected vehicles. We identify several open research questions and provide some guidance for future researchers.

sensemaking on large collections of unstructured textual documents is a challenging and time-consuming task that involves locating relevant documents and extracting valuable information from them. We propose a novel human-computer interaction approach that combines elements of hypergraph visualization and intelligent agents to support sensemaking on large document collections. Our approach aims to overcome the limitations of traditional keyword-based search methods, which can return irrelevant documents and fail to find connections between concepts in the document set. We conducted a user study to evaluate the effectiveness and usability of our approach. The results of the user study show that our approach is effective in helping users locate relevant documents and that the integration of intelligent agents and hypergraph visualization is well-received by participants. We also received valuable feedback that will help us improve our approach and better support sensemaking on large document collections.

We present medial parametrization, a new approach to parameterizing any compact planar domain bounded by closed curves. The key idea is to use two sets of auxiliary planar curves that are coplanar with the boundary and whose intersection is dense in the interior. These curves are called medial curves because they are tangent to the boundaries of holes (regions in which the domain contains no material) and have a similar geometric meaning as medians in Euclidean geometry. Parametric equations for these curves can be obtained from a simple formula. Medial parametrizations are defined by two mappings: a first one that defines an order on all intersections between medial curves and another mapping that gives each point on the boundary its corresponding parameter. Both mappings are linear; thus

Since Labov's (1964) foundational work on the social stratification of language, scholars have documented a wide range of linguistic variation across class lines. The majority of this research has focused on the United States and Europe, however, with less attention paid to language variation in other parts of the world. This paper presents the first cross-linguistically validated study of social stratification in Mandarin Chinese, which is spoken by over 800 million people worldwide, and one of the most widely studied languages of any variety in the world. We conduct a large-scale survey experiment that randomizes Mandarin speakers into high- and low-status conditions and elicits their speech through dictation tasks.

Product search plays an essential role in eCommerce. It was treated as a special type of information retrieval (IR) task and has been extensively investigated for decades. However, the performance of product search still lags behind other IR tasks due to the unique challenges associated with product data. In this paper, we aim to identify such challenges and introduce their impact on current product search models. We first discuss different types of product data and explore how they are transformed into textual representations. Then, we analyze several common issues caused by the transformation process, including feature mismatch and noise. Finally, we propose an integrated approach to address these problems by leveraging data augmentation techniques that enhance feature quality and mitiga

We present OOTDiffusion, a novel network architecture for realistic and controllable image-to-image translation. The network is based on an effective decoder that generates the desired output given the latent space of the source image and a conditional vector. Our approach involves a two-stage diffusion process: the first stage focuses on reconstructing the source image using its corresponding latent space, while the second stage leverages this reconstruction to generate the target image based on the conditioned latent space. This approach enhances the model's ability to produce high-quality images while maintaining control over specific features and details. By employing a lightweight, efficient, and accurate UNet-like structure for both stages of the diffusion process, we optimize the tr

While the volume of remote sensing data is increasing daily, deep learning in Earth Observation faces lack of large-scale datasets and limited access to ground truth. Large language models (LLMs) have shown their potential in various tasks, but their application in remote sensing remains underdeveloped. This paper explores the possibility of using LLMs as a proxy for human annotators for remote sensing image classification. To this end, we create a dataset of 170K satellite images, which are used to train two types of LLMs: a pretrained model and an LLM fine-tuned with self-supervised learning (SSL). Our results show that both pre-trained and SSL-fine tuned LLMs can effectively classify satellite images into five classes with precision and recall above 92%.

Molecular Communication (MC) has emerged as a promising paradigm employing molecules to transfer information at the nano-scale. Unlike MC channel coding, MC source coding has remained mostly an unexplored area of research. In a recent paper, prefix source coding was introduced into the field, through an MC-adapted version of the Huffman Coding. In the context of MC source coding, this paper proposes the Molecular Arithmetic Coding (MAC) whose algorithmic implementation and code-structure is non-arbitrarily different than that of the widely-known classical arithmetic coding. MAC is designed to mitigate Inter-Symbol Interference (ISI) for alphabets with known symbol probabilities through, in a highly efficient way, avoiding consecutive 1-bits.

Mixtral, a representative sparse mixture of experts (SMoE) language model, has received significant attention due to its unique model design and superior performance. Based on Mixtral-8x7B-v0.1, in this paper, we propose Chinese-Mixtral and Chinese-Mixtral-Instruct with improved Chinese language abilities by adopting further pre-training and instruction fine-tuning. Experimental results show that our Chinese-Mixtral and Chinese-Mixtral-Instruct successfully improve Chinese understanding and generation performance while retaining the original English abilities. Then, we discuss several key questions when performing language adaptation on large language models, including the necessity of extending the language-specific vocabulary and the choice of the initialization model (foundation model v

Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) have garnered significant attention for their success in various practical applications, such as recommendation systems and social network analysis. However, the training of GNNs is a challenging task due to the complex dependencies between graph nodes. Traditional learning methods rely on handcrafted features or prior knowledge, which may not fully capture these dependencies. To address this issue, we propose a novel approach that utilizes Graph Autoencoders (GAE) with an auxiliary loss function to effectively learn representations for graphs. Our method incorporates an autoencoder to model the graph structure using an implicit matrix factorization approach. We also introduce a new loss function that focuses on preserving the original graph structure while cap

Online open world object detection (OOWOD) aims to detect the known categories and reject unknown objects in a dynamic environment. Previous works mainly adopt the known-unknown classification paradigm, which usually leads to a high false positive rate (FPR) due to the boundary ambiguity between known classes and the unknown background. Inspired by the biological visual perception process, we propose a brain-inspired streaming dual-level perturbations (BSDP) method to tackle the OOWOD task. The proposed BSDP introduces two streaming perturbation modules to disturb the unknown objects at both feature-level and decision-level. Thus, the unknown objects can be effectively rejected with high confidence without distinguishing the unknown background from known classes. The proposed method is evaluated on the challenging VOC2007 and NWPU VHR-10 datasets, which achieves a competitive result for online open world object detection. Furthermore, the proposed method can be integrated with existing OOWOD methods to further improve their performance.

Abstractive summarization has enjoyed renewed interest in recent years, thanks to pre-trained language models and the availability of large-scale datasets. Despite its great potential, applying abstractive summarization to dialogue datasets is challenging, as it requires summarizing multi-turn dialogues with a natural language model. Existing approaches mostly adopt the supervised learning paradigm, which requires manually labeled summary data. However, manually labeling summary data is costly and time-consuming. In this paper, we propose a semi-supervised dialogue abstractive summarization model called DUET, which extracts high-quality pseudolabels from a pre-trained language model and leverages them to assist the training of a summarization model. We evaluate our method on two benchmark datasets, DialogSumm and MultiSum, and achieve strong performance, showing that our method outperforms existing semi-supervised methods and even approaches the performance of the supervised baseline. Our code is available at https://github.com/abisee/duet.

The prevailing grasp prediction methods predominantly rely on offline learning, overlooking the dynamic and unpredictable nature of the real-world robotic grasping tasks. We propose to learn online from the data collected by a robot in interaction with its environment. However, the existing learning strategies are limited due to the absence of supervision signals that can be used for fine-tuning the learned model. To overcome this limitation, we introduce a novel self-supervised approach based on 3D convolutional neural networks (CNNs), which utilizes the raw image frames captured by the robot's camera as supervision signals for improving the graspability estimations. Our method relies on the use of multiple images of various object categories, each with different graspable areas. By lever

This study investigates the application of Nash equilibrium strategies in optimizing forest harvesting decisions. The approach is based on a linear programming model that incorporates multi-objective optimization, stochastic simulation and game theory to maximize profits under uncertain conditions with different levels (i) competition intensity among multiple companies; ii), market demand scenarios for wood products from logging activities by local communities as well iii). Results indicate increasing profitability over time when using this method compared against traditional methods such those currently employed within industry practices today where no consideration has been given towards factors like external forces or risk exposure due economic uncertainty associated between various age

Recent advancements in large-scale pre-trained text-to-image models have shown the potential to generate high quality and diverse images by merely providing a simple prompt. However, existing methods still suffer from three major issues: (1) their generative capacity is limited as they typically rely on pretrained language model fine tuning or image generation based upon randomly sampled word vectors; therefore it becomes difficult to produce complex structures with accurate details at scale without sacrificing diversity across different instances of input prompts within an individual instance category ("instance" here refers only one single example);(2), most current approaches cannot handle dynamic queries where users can provide custom inputs such that require more advanced reasoning ca

BusyBox, an open-source software bundling over 300 essential Linux commands into a single binary executable, has become the de facto standard for embedded systems. A common implementation strategy is to embed the entire image in a device's flash memory, which requires significant resources and is vulnerable to compromise from boot attacks such as malware injection. To address these issues, we propose a new approach that splits the image across multiple devices. We implement our approach on three widely used microcontrollers (MCUs) -- STM32F4, Renesas RX65N, and Atmel SAMD21 -- and compare it against existing solutions. Our results show that our method achieves better performance than existing solutions while maintaining robustness against boot attacks. Additionally, our technique significa

This paper presents distributed OpenMP offloading of the particle transport code OpenMC to Intel GPU MAX accelerators. OpenMC is a Monte Carlo code that simulates the transport of particles, in particular neutrons and photons, through matter. It is highly parallelizable, but porting it to GPUs is not straightforward, as it requires changing the algorithmic approach to particle transport. This work focuses on offloading the CPU-based kernel of OpenMC to Intel GPU MAX accelerators. This kernel is responsible for the bulk of the computational workload in OpenMC simulations. We used OpenMP offloading to move the kernel code to the GPU while keeping the rest of the code unchanged. We achieved this by instrumenting the code with OpenMP offloading pragmas and by using the Intel oneAPI ecosystem, namely Intel oneAPI DPC++ compiler and Intel oneAPI Performance Primitives. We show that our implementation is up to 5.5 times faster than the original CPU version on a Intel Xeon Phi 2nd generation processor. When connecting multiple nodes with Intel Omni-Path Interconnect, we observe up to 14.8 times speedup with 256 processes. The total memory bandwidth required by the GPU version is 1.4 times higher than the CPU version. However, the GPU version can achieve a higher FLOPs rate, due to the use of half-precision floating point operations.

As machine learning (ML) algorithms get deployed in an ever-increasing number of applications, the need for robust and interpretable ML models is critical. The interpretation of such models can provide valuable insights into how a given model works, which can be used to improve its performance. One key aspect of this is the visualization of feature importance, where we want to understand which features of our input data are most influential in determining the output of the model. In this paper, we introduce a new methodology called Perturbation-based Interpretation (PBI). Our approach is based on the concept of perturbations, which are small changes made to the input data to see their impact on the model's predictions. Unlike traditional methods that rely on black box models and can only p

Document-Level Event Argument Extraction (DocEAE) is an extremely difficult information extraction problem in the field of natural language processing. The current state-of-the-art methods mainly rely on large language models (LLMs) to perform template matching or generation, which are time-consuming and expensive. In this paper, we propose a novel method called DocEAE with Transformer-based Knowledge Graph Embedding (DKGE). DKGE is based on two main ideas: Firstly, we transform the complex information extraction task into a knowledge graph reasoning process and convert each argument entity into a node in a knowledge graph. Secondly, we construct an event-argument knowledge graph using LLMs, which stores all possible arguments for each event type. During inference, DKGE traverses the knowl

Large language models have shown impressive results on cognitive tasks, but they are often resource-intensive and lack explainability. In contrast, small models are resource-efficient and can provide explanations through their internal activations, but they struggle to achieve similar performance to large models. Activation-based cognitive architectures (Cotraining Models, CMs) have been proposed to bridge this gap, as they can leverage existing large model knowledge and transfer it to small models. However, previous CMs suffer from unstable model convergence and suboptimal performance. To address these limitations, we introduce Attention-Driven ES Learning (AS-ES Learning), a novel method that enhances CMs by incorporating attention mechanisms and optimizing the embedding space. This results in more stable convergence and better performance of the small models. We evaluate our approach on a range of benchmark datasets and find that it consistently outperforms previous CMs. Furthermore, AS-ES Learning enables CMs to utilize larger mini-batches, which further boosts performance and reduces inference time. Notably, a CM based on the GPT-3 model, which has 1.3 billion parameters, achieves a performance comparable to the GPT-3 model itself. Another CM, based on the smaller GPT-2 model, achieves a performance that is competitive compared to the larger GPT-3 model. These results demonstrate that AS-ES Learning is a promising approach for efficient CoT learning in small models.

Conversational systems often rely on embedding models for intent classification and intent clustering tasks. The advent of large language models (LLMs) has led to the development of advanced models that can perform both tasks, but they are limited in their ability to handle unstructured data, such as conversations. This paper introduces a novel approach to modeling conversations using LLMs by representing them as sequences of turns. This approach provides several advantages over previous methods based on LLMs: it enables us to use pre-trained LLMs without any modification or fine-tuning; it allows us to incorporate knowledge gained from human annotations, enhancing the quality of the embeddings generated by the model; and it reduces the need for complex training processes due to its simpli

Graph neural networks (GNNs) are emerging in many practical applications, and their expressive power has recently become a focus of attention. The Weisfeiler-Leman (WL) graph isomorphism test is a classic and widely-used approach, which is known to have limitations in distinguishing certain graph structures. On the other hand, recent GNNs based on the WL framework have been shown to have stronger expressivity. However, proving this stronger expressivity theoretically is generally difficult. In this work, we propose a novel variance-preservation principle for GNNs based on the WL framework. Our main result is a sufficient condition for a GNN to have stronger expressivity than the WL framework, which can be applied to many practical GNN models. We also present a new GNN model that satisfies the condition: namely, a variance-preservation aggregation (VPA) GNN. The VPA-GNN improves the expressivity of the standard WL framework, while preserving the variance of node feature values. We prove this formally for the 1-dimensional case, and empirically for higher-dimensional cases.

We introduce and study a novel variant of the team formation problem: the Hypergraph Discovery Problem (HDP). In HDP, we are given a set of entities and a set of hyperedges, and asked to partition the entities into teams such that each hyperedge is covered by a pre-specified number of teams. The objective is to minimize the sum of the costs of the teams. HDP is NP-hard, and we focus on developing approximation algorithms with provable performance guarantees. We introduce a general framework based on linear programming, and derive two approximation algorithms with different performance guarantees by rounding the optimal solution of the linear programming relaxation in different ways. One of the algorithms has an approximation factor of 2, and the other has an approximation factor of 4. We show that the approximation factor of 2 is the best possible by proving that HDP is APX-hard. We also show that HDP is fixed-parameter tractable with respect to the number of hyperedges. We conduct experiments to study the parameterized complexity of HDP, and to compare the performance of the approximation algorithms with each other and with a state-of-the-art heuristic.

Message passing in Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) aggregates information from adjacent nodes to update the representation of a node. Existing GNNs use a fixed mechanism to pass messages, which ignores the diversity of information among neighbors and the redundancy of message passing. This overlooks the potential to optimize message passing and raises the question of how to do it. In this paper, we answer this question by proposing an entropy aware mechanism for message passing in GNNs. We measure the diversity of information among neighbors using entropy and selectively pass messages based on the entropy value. Our method, called Entropy Aware Message Passing (EAMP), prioritizes informative neighbors and reduces the number of redundant messages passed. We analyze the impact of entropy on message passing and demonstrate the effectiveness of EAMP on real-world graphs. Our experimental results show that EAMP consistently outperforms existing GNNs that use a fixed mechanism for message passing across various tasks and datasets.

Evolutionary algorithms, such as Differential Evolution, excel in solving real-parameter optimization problems and are widely used to optimize the parameters of Machine Learning models. In particular, they can be applied for hyperparameter tuning (HPT) by performing a grid search over the parameter space. However, they require large computational resources because they need to evaluate multiple model runs on a large dataset. This paper proposes a new approach based on Evolutionary Multi-Objective Optimization, which exploits the synergy between the evolutionary algorithm and multi-objective optimization. The proposed approach is able to simultaneously find optimal hyperparameters while reducing the number of required evaluations. Experimental results on six publicly available data sets dem

Large language models (LLMs) are powerful tools, but they are limited in that they often fail to accurately translate idiomatic expressions and cultural references, leading to generation of inappropriate content. Instruction tuning is a promising method to address this problem, but the development of high-quality instruction data is time-consuming and labor-intensive. To develop high-quality instruction data efficiently, we propose a three-step method that involves (1) collecting a large number of instructions from LLM-generated inappropriate responses, (2) automatically selecting high-quality instructions from the collected instructions, and (3) manually reviewing and labeling the selected instructions to increase their quality. We applied this method to Japanese LLMs and developed a dataset consisting of 7,000 high-quality instructions. To demonstrate the utility of this dataset, we fine-tuned a commercial LLM with 1.3M parameters on this dataset and evaluated it using a benchmark consisting of 1,000 manually labeled test cases. The results showed that our LLM generated fewer inappropriate responses than the original LLM in both machine translation and dialogue generation tasks. Our dataset and code are publicly available.

Industry is at the forefront of adopting new technologies, and the process followed by the industry to identify novel technology solutions is critical. To understand this process, we conducted a survey with 210 industrial professionals. We then applied a methodology that allowed us to identify and compare the main criteria used in the selection of AI-related innovative solutions. Our results show that the most important criterion for the industry was the technical maturity of the solution (i.e., its ability to meet specific needs), while others such as cost or ethical considerations were less relevant. However, these results may be influenced by the type of company where the interviewees work: e.g., companies operating in regulated markets prioritize ethics over other factors. This study p

Point-based image editing has attracted remarkable attention since the emergence of DragGAN. Recognizing its potential, we introduce a novel approach for point-based image synthesis and editing: Point-Based Generative Adversarial Network (PB-GAN). PB-GAN employs two key components to bridge the gap between point clouds and images: 1) An adversarial discriminator that classifies the authenticity of point clouds and 2) a generator that transforms point clouds into images. The generator adopts a novel spatial-transformer network as an intermediate component to achieve a more efficient transformation from point clouds to images. Extensive experiments demonstrate that PB-GAN outperforms existing methods in both image synthesis and editing tasks on various datasets.

This paper presents a Geometric-Photometric Joint Alignment(GPJA) method for 3D object pose estimation. We focus on the problem of estimating the pose and shape of an object from a single image, and propose a novel approach that combines geometric constraints with photometric priors to improve robustness and accuracy in challenging scenarios. Our proposed method combines a robust geometric framework based on SfM with a photometric model based on the Shape-from-Light (SFL) algorithm. The joint optimization of both frameworks is achieved through our developed GPJA algorithm. The key contribution of our work lies in the incorporation of photometric information into the geometric reconstruction process, which improves alignment between the estimated geometry and lighting conditions.

The goal of this research was to determine whether interactive e-books are more effective than traditional e-books at improving word knowledge and comprehension skills. We also sought to learn what types of interactions are most effective. To do so, we designed several interactive e-books that varied in the degree to which they prompted the reader to use the dictionary, provided word definitions, or asked comprehension questions. We then conducted three experiments in which participants read one of our interactive e-books or a control e-book that contained no interactive elements. Our experiments demonstrated that interactive e-books can improve word knowledge and comprehension skills more than traditional e-books, that prompting the reader to use the dictionary produces the largest gains in word knowledge, and that asking comprehension questions produces the largest gains in comprehension skills. We present several designs that we created to improve word learning and comprehension while reading e-books. These findings can guide the creation of e-books that more effectively improve vocabulary and reading comprehension.

Rapid and reliable identification of dynamic scene parts, also known as motion segmentation, is a key preliminary step for many computer vision applications. In this paper, we present a novel approach to the problem of motion segmentation. Our method is based on an ensemble of generative models that are jointly trained to model different objects in the scene. The proposed algorithm operates on a single image and does not require any prior knowledge or assumptions about the number of moving objects in the scene. Experiments show that our approach significantly outperforms state-of-the-art methods and achieves highly competitive performance in terms of both accuracy and runtime speed. Source code is available at https://github.com/BruceZhang2019/MSE.git . To facilitate reproducibility, we al

We introduce and study a new model of adaptive equivalence testing, where the number of adaptive rounds is bounded by a function of the input length. This models real-world testing scenarios more accurately than the standard model, where the number of rounds is unbounded. We give the first non-trivial lower bounds on the adaptive complexity of testing properties in this model, showing that some properties have a "hard core" that requires many adaptive rounds to test. We then take a positive step towards mitigating this lower bound by showing that many properties have a "soft shell" that can be tested with few adaptive rounds. Along the way, we develop several techniques that we hope will be useful in future work on adaptive testing.

Deep autoencoders are a popular approach for anomaly detection in multivariate time series data. Training a deep autoencoder, however, is computationally expensive, and developing an efficient training algorithm is an ongoing research topic. Moreover, there is a trade-off between the performance and efficiency of deep autoencoders that is not well understood. In this paper, we investigate the use of temporal convolutional layers for efficient deep anomaly detection. We show that a lightweight temporal convolutional network (TCN) can achieve competitive performance compared to a deep autoencoder with a full set of parameters. To train our TCN, we use an efficient backpropagation algorithm that computes the gradient w.r.t. the convolutional filters using the Levenberg-Marquardt (LM) algorithm. The LM algorithm accelerates the convergence of our TCN by 1.6--10.1 times compared to the backpropagation algorithm. The TCN with the LM algorithm enables us to reduce the number of parameters of the convolutional filters by 32.0--78.1 times, while maintaining the performance of the deep autoencoder with full parameters. Our efficient TCN achieves a competitive detection accuracy compared to the state-of-the-art methods for multivariate time series anomaly detection.

Magic Markup is a system that uses an LLM to maintain document-external markup, i.e., markup that exists outside of the main content of a document, such as bibliographical entries, figure captions, and cross-references. The system automatically updates the markup whenever the document is updated, e.g., when new bibliographical entries are added or existing ones are removed. This is done by running a series of LLM-generated transformations on the document, each of which makes localized changes to the document-external markup based on information in the document. The transformations are designed to work together to maintain consistency between the document and its external markup. We evaluate Magic Markup on three types of documents: scientific articles, which have complex bibliographical entries; legal briefs, which contain many cross-references; and court decisions, which often have figure captions. We compare it to a baseline that re-generates the entire document, including the external markup, from scratch using the LLM. Our results show that Magic Markup produces better-structured outputs than the baseline more than 80% of the time.

Bayesian optimization (BO) with Gaussian processes (GP) has become an indispensable algorithm for black box optimization problems. Not without a dash of irony, BO is often considered a black box itself, lacking ways to provide reasons as to why certain parameters are proposed to be evaluated. This is particularly relevant in human-in-the-loop applications of BO, such as in robotics. We address this issue by proposing ShapleyBO, a framework for interpreting BO's proposals by game-theoretic Shapley values.They quantify each parameter's contribution to BO's acquisition function. Exploiting the linearity of Shapley values, we are further able to identify how strongly each parameter drives BO's exploration and exploitation for additive acquisition functions like the confidence bound.

Machine learning (ML) and deep learning (DL) are two methodologies that are gaining increasing popularity. Both ML and DL originated from the field of artificial intelligence (AI). AI has gone through several winters and summers of funding boom and bust. The current funding boom is driven by the success of DL, which in turn is driven by powerful computing hardware and algorithms. The hardware is based on the neuron model of the brain, and the algorithms are inspired by the way the brain learns. The goal of this paper is to debunk the magic behind DL and show that it is no different from ML. We start with a brief history of AI, followed by a discussion of the hardware and algorithms that are the foundation of DL. We then discuss the mathematics behind the popular deep belief network (DBN), which is a generative model that can learn features from data. We show that DBN is a special case of a wide class of models that can be trained with an unsupervised learning algorithm that is based on maximizing the mutual information between the model parameters and the data. We then discuss the problem of generalization and show that while DL can generalize better than ML, both ML and DL suffer from the same generalization problem, which is a consequence of the finite size of the training set. We conclude by discussing the future of AI and the role of ML and DL in that future.

We study sheaf codes, a type of linear codes with a fixed hierarchical collection of localities. This family was introduced by F. Li in 2019 and includes the well-studied class of binary Reed-Solomon (RS) codes. In this paper we extend her work by introducing more general families of sheaf codes that are parameterized by a partition of $[n]$, $\mathcal{P}$. We also show that any sheaf code can be obtained as the tensor product of a Reed-Muller code with some specific matrices associated to the localities. To do so, we first prove that every sheaf code is the image under a linear map of a certain family of Reed-Solomon codes, which we call sheaf codes with respect to $\mathcal{P}$.

Data augmentation (DA) is a powerful workhorse for bolstering performance in image classification. DA exploits the redundancy in the training data, and its effectiveness has been attributed to the coverage of the augmented data over the underlying distribution. Unfortunately, it is often the case that augmented data points are not actively used by the model, and thus do not contribute to learning. In this work, we aim to improve the efficacy of DA by leveraging the Fourier transform. The Fourier transform provides a natural frequency basis, and we show that it can be used to explicitly promote the use of augmented data through a carefully designed penalty term. This term encourages consistency between the model's prediction and its Fourier coefficients, and acts as a regularizer that penalizes the model for not using the augmented data. We show that this approach, which we call Fourier-basis DA (FBDA), can improve performance over strong augmentation schedules on CIFAR-10 and ImageNet. Our method is general and is shown to be compatible with different models and augmentation functions.

From a biological perspective, one of the most fascinating aspects of human beings is our ability to cooperate in complex social dilemmas. Despite extensive research, the exact mechanisms that allow for the evolution of cooperation among non-relatives remain elusive. Here, we propose a novel mechanism that promotes and maintains cooperation in repeated social dilemmas. Using a simple model based on the framework of multiagent reinforcement learning, we show that cooperation can be enhanced if individuals adaptively change their level of cooperation based on the cumulative reward received by the group. We refer to this behavior as adaptive coordination. It effectively overcomes the problem of defectors in homogeneous groups and allows for the evolution of cooperation in the face of cheaters. Furthermore, we show that adaptive coordination emerges spontaneously in the process of learning and does not require any additional mechanism, such as altruism or kin selection. Our results provide valuable insights into the evolution of cooperation and open new avenues for research on the topic.

The last decade has seen tremendous progress in our ability to generate realistic-looking data across a wide range of domains, from text and images through speech synthesis. In this talk I will focus on the most recent developments that enable us not only produce synthetic media but also manipulate existing real world content with fine control over specific attributes such as style or identity without requiring access for training purposes beyond what is already publicly available online (e.,g,. photos). Speaker: Prof Yaser Abualhai [''], Department Head & Associate Professor School Computer Science Department at University College Dublin We consider an information theoretic setting where Alice wishes to send Bob some message via noisy channels using side chann

The recent advancements in Generative AI (GenAI) have been remarkable. However, this opportunity comes with significant security implications, as many applications are integrating GenAI technologies into their workflows. We demonstrate a proof-of-concept GenAI-powered worm, an autonomous self-replicating malicious program that exploits vulnerabilities in GenAI-powered applications. Our experimental results show that the proposed AI worm can successfully infect GenAI-powered applications running on multiple operating systems, without any user interaction or application-specific vulnerabilities. We discuss the effectiveness of the proposed AI worm and its variants, and we provide suggestions for mitigating these types of attacks. We hope that this work will raise awareness of this new class of AI-powered attacks and inspire researchers to develop countermeasures.

Cyber threats continue to evolve in complexity, thereby traditional Cyber Threat Intelligence (CTI) approaches are no longer sufficient. While Machine Learning-based methods can offer advanced threat detection capabilities and provide high efficiency in resource utilization, their adoption by defenders is constrained due to the complexities involved in model development, fine-tuning, and maintenance. Furthermore, they also face challenges in capturing contextual information of cyber attacks, resulting in limited generalizability across different environments. This paper aims to address these limitations through a novel approach that combines Natural Language Processing (NLP) with Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs). The proposed method utilizes NLP techniques for extracting attack-related

Bladder cancer ranks within the top 10 most diagnosed cancers worldwide and is responsible for over 200,000 deaths per year. Currently, there is no reliable method to distinguish between low-risk and high-risk bladder cancers at diagnosis, resulting in many patients receiving unnecessary treatments that may lead to severe adverse effects such as kidney damage and even death. This is a critical issue because bladder cancer has a high rate of recurrence, with some patients having multiple recurrences throughout their lifetime. The treatment of bladder cancer involves regular monitoring and follow-up visits to detect any recurrence early. These visits are necessary because bladder cancer can be challenging to treat due to its location near other vital organs and tissues such as the prostate,

Nowadays, data is becoming the new fuel for economic wealth and creation of novel and profitable business models. In this regard Big Data has become a common buzzword in many companies’ vocabulary as it helps to address their need for insights on large quantities or complex types (e..g., text-based) datasets with high computational power that will help them make more informed decisions about their future strategic direction by using advanced analytics techniques such like machine learning algorithms which can extract patterns from unstructured information sources . In this article we are going through different stages involved while implementing an ML algorithm: 1 ) Collecting & Cleaning up your raw dataset – This process involves getting rid off any unwanted elements present inside each r

Cross-domain recommendation (CDR) aims to enhance recommendation accuracy in a target domain with user data from other domains. Most existing CDR methods utilize information transfer or feature transformation, which may result either poor performance due the insufficient knowledge adaptation and/or overfitting during training phase of model learning process on source users’ feedback for predicting new targets’. To overcome these limitations we propose an enhanced version named EGCN based upon GCN that utilizes both self attention mechanism as well implicit collaborative filtering approach by incorporating contextual features into node embeddings via graph convolution layer followed upby additional fusion step called multihead Attention module(MAH). This enables our proposed framework not o

Large Language Models (LLMs) have unlocked new capabilities and applications; however, their reliance on massive amounts of data for training poses challenges. The need to mitigate these risks has fueled the development of various methods that aim to enhance LLMs' capabilities without sacrificing performance or relying on vast amounts of data. This review paper aims to provide an overview of the existing approaches for developing enhanced LLMs in terms of capability and explain how they work. We summarize the key features of each methodology, highlight its advantages and limitations, and discuss potential areas for future research and application. We hope this review can serve as a useful resource for practitioners seeking to harness the power of LLMs while addressing emerging concerns aro

As the errors of microelectromechanical system (MEMS) gyroscopes are complex and coupled, it is difficult to directly apply conventional Kalman filter to online estimation. However, some previous works only consider the uncoupled error model that MEMS gyroscope's estimated angle is a linear function of the real angle. In this paper, we propose an adaptive multi-layer perceptron (MLP)-based recursive least square (RLS)-type estimator for MEMS gyroscopes. The proposed method has several merits: 1) It can be applied to both coupled and uncoupled error models; 2) It is able to utilize MEMS gyroscopes' self-calibration information to estimate the bias of the coupling matrix. Simulation results show that our approach outperforms other estimators in terms of mean square error and computational co

Generative diffusion models have shown remarkable success in recent years and have become a crucial technology for the provision of various AI-based services. The deployment of these models in mobile edge networks (MENs) enables users to enjoy low-latency and high-quality services. However, it significantly increases the model's memory footprint, overwhelming the device's limited resources. To address this challenge, we propose a novel on-demand quantization method for federated generative diffusion, termed MEN-Q. This method enables a trade-off between the model's accuracy and resource consumption, ensuring that only the necessary parameters are loaded onto the device. To achieve this, MEN-Q utilizes a weight pruning strategy, retaining only the top-K% most significant weights and replacing the rest with zeros. This approach significantly reduces the model size, with a maximum compression ratio of 70.4% achieved for the DDPM-1B model. To further minimize resource consumption, MEN-Q employs a novel weight binarization technique, assigning a binary value to each weight. This additional step reduces the model size by up to 27.2%, from 1.48 MB to 1.11 MB, without any significant compromise in accuracy. To evaluate the effectiveness of MEN-Q, we conducted extensive experiments involving three diffusion models (i.e., DDPM-1B, DDPM-100M, and Stable Diffusion). These experiments were carried out on two different devices (i.e., Pixel 4 and Google Colab). The results demonstrate that our method achieves a higher compression ratio compared to existing quantization methods, with a negligible compromise in generative performance. Specifically, compared to the nearest competitor, MEN-Q achieves a 1.4% higher FID score with a 43.9% smaller model size. In addition, we show that MEN-Q significantly outperforms other quantization methods in terms of the quality of the generated images.

The recent trend in open-domain question answering (ODQA) is to retrieve answers from a large corpus of context documents that are relevant to the question. However, in cases where the answer is not explicitly mentioned in the context documents, such as novel diseases, symptoms, or medical procedures, the system will fail to find the answer. We propose generating artificial context documents that contain mentions of unseen answers to expand the coverage of ODQA systems. These artificial documents are generated using a pre-trained language model, and can be retrieved alongside the real context documents to improve the chances of finding the answer. We evaluate on a medical ODQA dataset that contains questions about rare diseases, for which the answer is often not present in the associated context documents. Our results show that generating artificial contexts is better than retrieving real contexts alone, achieving 88.4% exact match accuracy versus 84.6% for the baseline.

Compositional Natural Language Inference has been explored to assess the true abilities of neural models to reason over text. This evaluation task is based on open-domain textual inference problems, which are usually solved by retrieving evidence from external resources and combining it with a given hypothesis in order for them both together form valid conclusions or predictions about future events within this context respectively; however some issues exist when attempting such approaches due mainly because they require large amounts data (both input documents as well output labels) alongwith efficient search mechanisms capable handling high volumes query results quickly enough allow reasonable performance levels achieve even basic tasks like those described above - something many existing

In this paper, we develop an LLM-powered framework for the curation and evaluation of AI-generated text. This system provides a user interface with a question prompt and a selection of responses generated by a large language model (LLM). A key feature of our system is its ability to evaluate the response against a set of knowledge sources, which can be defined by the user. We apply this system to generate summaries of scientific papers from the PubMed dataset, which are evaluated against two knowledge bases: CORD-19 and Semantic Scholar. The framework also includes metrics that allow users to assess the quality of the generated summaries. Our experiments show that the proposed framework can successfully generate high-quality summaries based on both knowledge bases. Moreover, it demonstrate

We propose a new model of distributed quantum computing, the distributed quantum circuit (DQC), suitable for describing quantum algorithms computed on a network of quantum processors interconnected by a quantum communication channel. Different from existing models, a DQC allows quantum gates to be applied between any pair of qubits, possibly with some restrictions on the graph of connections. The restrictions are expressed in terms of a novel notion of locality, tailored to the distributed setting, that quantifies the amount of entanglement that can be created between any two qubits. We show that the computational power of a DQC is determined by the maximum degree of locality that can be achieved between any two qubits, and by the total amount of communication required to distribute the computation among the quantum processors. As an application, we prove that the DQC model can simulate any adiabatic quantum computation on a connected graph of $n$ qubits with $\tilde O(n^3)$ communication and $\tilde O(n^2)$ locality.

The isometric mapping method employs the shortest path algorithm to estimate the Euclidean distance between two arbitrary points in a target point cloud. In this paper, we present an efficient and accurate solution for 3D isometric mapping by using two-point nearest neighbor search. We first build a hierarchical kd-tree with the querying point as its root, then perform a range search within the tree to find the closest neighbor of the querying point. Given two points, we compute their Euclidean distances based on the distances from both points to their common nearest neighbors, which is guaranteed to be the true closest pair. This process is repeated for all pairs of points in the point cloud until all distances are computed. Experimental results show that our method outperforms existing s

Nano-machines circulating inside the human body, collecting data on tissue conditions and health status. A brain implant that can communicate with a person’s mind to enable better control of their limbs or even new sensory functions such as sight restoration in blind people by stimulating specific areas within our visual cortex using electricity (electroceuticals). These are just some examples from recent medical research into nanotechnology applications for healthcare which could transform how we treat diseases like cancer – if these technologies work out well enough over time!
