We cover how to determine a sufficiently large sample size for a $K$-armed randomized experiment with success probability $p$ that meets an accuracy requirement. This is known as the asymptotic optimality problem. Our main result shows that if there are at most $K-1$ levels of noise, then it suffices to use $\frac{2}{1-\min\left\{\frac{1}{2}, \frac{p}{K+1}\right\}}N$ independent samples. Furthermore, we show that the optimum can be improved by using a fractional number of samples from the distribution of the noisy version of the target function and that this fraction increases in $K$. Our results also apply to estimation in the context of the Rademacher complexity.

We introduce Mars 2.0 for modeling, analysis, verification and code generation of Cyber-Physical Systems (CPS) with uncertainties in their parameters. In particular, we focus on the use of fuzzy logic to model uncertainty in CPSs' parameters by using fuzzy variables to represent the uncertain values as well as the different membership functions that are used to represent them. Our contributions include: (i) an extension of the Mars language to support fuzzy variables and fuzzy expressions; (ii) a semantics for Mars 2.0 that extends the existing semantics of Mars; (iii) tools for the specification and analysis of fuzzy programs; (iv) a proof of soundness of the approach; and (v) a case study involving the design of a fuzzy controller for a mobile robot.

Deep video semantic segmentation models typically employ convolutional neural networks (CNNs) to extract features from videos. However, existing models usually treat video features and image features separately, which leads to the loss of semantic information and a decrease in segmentation accuracy. To address this problem, we propose a deep common feature mining network that extracts common features from image frames and their corresponding video features. Specifically, we introduce a cross-modal feature extraction module that extracts common features from image frames and video features. This module is based on the observation that image frames and video features share some common semantic information, which can be extracted by designing attention maps to highlight the common regions between image frames and video features. Furthermore, we propose a feature refinement module that refines the common features extracted by the cross-modal feature extraction module. This module consists of two branches: one for extracting deep features from the common features, and the other for extracting shallow features from the common features. These deep and shallow features are then fused to obtain accurate semantic segmentation results. Extensive experiments on five popular benchmarks demonstrate that our method achieves state-of-the-art performance. Moreover, the proposed method is efficient to implement and can be deployed for practical applications.

This paper introduces the novel task of multimodal puzzle solving, framed within the context and architecture from visual question answering. The goal is to answer questions about a given jigsaw image by dynamically assembling its pieces using only text instructions as guidance; e..g., "please assemble this piece in position 1." We introduce JigsawQA-24k with over ~50K examples across four domains: objects/animals (97%), transportation means (~3%) , nature landscapes (<~), people's faces(<~) or human bodies( < ), each having upto eight distinct scenes for training data plus additional test sets that are unseen during model development time . To evaluate performance on our dataset we provide baselines trained separately per domain while also demonstrating an average accuracy score achieved

We present a novel method for efficiently producing semi-dense matches across images. Previous detector-based methods typically rely on dense features and keypoints to perform matching, which are computationally expensive in terms of both time and memory consumption. Our method instead focuses on the detection of image correspondences between input images using only a sparse set of feature vectors extracted from each image. The correspondence scores generated by the model are further refined through an iterative process that incorporates additional image data at each iteration. By employing this method, we can significantly reduce the computational cost associated with matching and still achieve comparable results to those obtained by conventional methods. In addition to its improved effic

Irish is an official language of Ireland alongside English, yet it is spoken by only about 1% of the population. Thus, Irish English bilingualism is not a common scenario in Ireland, and there are few resources for Irish natural language processing. In this work, we examine the output of an English--Irish Transformer-based neural machine translation model. We compare the output of the Transformer-based model to a strong baseline Transformer model that has been trained on the same data. We also examine the effect of training data size on model output quality. Human evaluators blind to the specific models used rate the output of both models. We find that the Transformer-based model outperforms the baseline Transformer model by a significant margin. We also find that increasing the size of the training corpus improves the quality of the outputted translations.

Objective: Depression is a common mental disorder, and like other mental disorders, it is often underdiagnosed and undertreated. Virtual reality (VR) has high potential to be used as a tool for detection and intervention of depression, but its effectiveness has not been systematically reviewed yet. Therefore, the objective of this review is to examine the evidence on VR-based tools for detection and intervention of depression. Method: Literature on VR-based tools for detection and intervention of depression was searched from the inception of each database until January 2021. The quality of the studies was assessed using the Mixed Methods Appraisal Tool. Results: After removing duplicates, 57 studies were included in the quality assessment. Most studies (n=47) were conducted with participants diagnosed with depression, and the remaining studies (n=10) were conducted with healthy participants. Most studies (n=50) used VR as a tool for intervention, and the remaining studies (n=7) used VR as a tool for detection. The quality assessment results showed that most studies were at high risk of bias. The majority of the studies (n=50) used VR headsets as the delivery platform, and the remaining studies (n=7) used desktop-based VR environments. Conclusion: VR has high potential to be used as an intervention tool for depression, but more research is needed to confirm its effectiveness. In addition, more research is needed to examine the acceptance and feasibility of VR-based detection tools for depression.

The advent of Large Language Models (LLMs) has led to remarkable progress on a wide range problems in natural language processing, including sequence generation and question answering. LLMS have been shown capable of generating content that can be used as inputs for downstream tasks such summarization or classification; however current approaches still struggle when the input contains errors generated by an LLM itself . In this paper we propose \textit{L4T}, which leverages both local knowledge from nearby positions within text chunks (\texttt{\textbf{$l$}ocal}) as well contextualized embeddings obtained via self-supervised learning(\textsf{$\mathbf{~s}elf$ -supervision}).

Reinforcement learning (RL) and causal modelling naturally complement each other. The goal of this work is to leverage causal models as a novel resource for RL, and vice versa. We first show that when the optimal policy in an RL problem can be learned using a finite-state automaton (FSA), then by leveraging the FSA, one can efficiently train a causal model to predict the optimal policy. This approach, however, requires the availability of training data of the form $(s,r,a,\pi)$, where $s$ is the state, $r$ is the reward, $a$ is the action, $\pi$ is the policy, and all elements are deterministic functions of the current state. When such data is available, we develop a practical algorithm that combines policy gradient methods with structural inference based on FSAs.

Online language learning and adaptation is a challenging task for static, reference-based language models. The lack of contextual information and the diversity of natural language pose difficulties in updating the model in an open-ended manner. In this paper, we propose a novel approach that captures amortized context through a memory of fixed capacity and size. This memory is used to adapt the language model parameters via a behavior cloning based learning objective. Our method, named MemLAM, outperforms several baselines in terms of perplexity on the LAMBADA dataset. We also show that MemLAM is capable of capturing both local and global context, as evaluated by human annotators.

In the realm of big data and digital healthcare, Electronic Health Records (EHR) are one type that can be considered as a treasure trove for clinical research. While EHRs have been used to analyze large-scale outcomes in population studies with impressive results since 2015<sup>[</sup><u>https://pubmedhealth</u>), their use is still limited due mainly to several challenges inherent within this technology: * The lack or absence altogether from certain patient records; (i).e., missing data points may lead us astray when trying understand what happened during an individual' s journey through life - especially if we don’t know exactly where they were at any given time point along its course!

Smartphones are integral to modern life, yet research highlights the cognitive drawbacks of excessive smartphone usage. Our study explores how multitasking with a smartphone impacts working memory and attention during concurrent task performance and how these effects vary across different levels of smartphone addiction. We conducted a series of experiments using the N-Back Task (NBT) and the Cognitive Assessment System (CAS) to assess participants' working memory and attention abilities. Results show that individuals with high smartphone addiction have lower working memory and attention capabilities than those with low smartphone addiction. Multitasking with a smartphone has significant negative effects on working memory and attention among all groups regardless of smartphone addiction lev

Creating 3D textured meshes using generative artificial intelligence has garnered significant attention recently. However, existing methods often struggle to generate high-quality textures for complex shapes with intricate details and sharp features. To address this challenge, we introduce the novel concept of "Texture Priority" in our proposed method called "Texture Prioritized Mesh Generation (TPMG)." TPMG leverages the idea that the importance of texture generation should be directly proportional to the complexity of the shape's surface geometry, thereby ensuring that textures are generated for areas requiring more detail. Our approach involves decomposing the input mesh into convex regions using a Delaunay triangulation algorithm. This decomposition enables us to prioritize the selecti

Building on the classical theory of process algebra with priorities, we identify a new scheduling mechanism, called a "fair round robin" (FRR), which is based on a simple counting process. The FRR offers a new methodology for the construction and analysis of process algebraic languages with fair scheduling, such as the Priority Calculus with Fair Scheduling. We show how this methodology can be applied to construct several variants of PCFS; these variants have different fairness properties and can serve as a basis for various application domains. We provide operational semantics and equivalence definitions for each variant and show that the variants are closed under bisimulation equivalence. As an example of the applicability of our framework, we demonstrate the equivalence of a process alg

The transferability of deep neural networks (DNNs) has made significant progress in image and language processing. However, due to the heterogeneity among tables, such DNN bonus is still far from being well exploited on tabular data prediction (e.g., regression or classification tasks). Condensing knowledge from diverse domains, language models (LMs) possess the capability to comprehend feature names from various tables, potentially serving as versatile learners in transferring knowledge across distinct tables and diverse prediction tasks, but their discrete text representation space is inherently incompatible with numerical feature values in tables. In this paper, we present TP-BERTa, a specifically pre-trained LM model for tabular data prediction. Concretely, a novel relative magnitude t

Bladder cancer ranks within the top 10 most diagnosed cancers worldwide and is one of the deadliest malignancies. It is usually detected at a late stage due to its asymptomatic nature, which reduces the probability of successful treatment. This work proposes a novel method for bladder cancer detection using histopathology images, employing the convolutional neural network (CNN). The proposed model involves training on an image dataset with various subtypes of bladder tumors. A total of 646 images were used, including 320 images of normal bladders and 326 images of non-normal bladders, obtained from various sources, such as medical records and public datasets. Additionally, we have employed transfer learning techniques by fine-tuning the pretrained VGG-19 CNN model to improve accuracy.

We present an analysis of 12 million instances of privacy-relevant reviews publicly visible on Twitter, with a focus on the topic of medical devices. We find that the majority of tweets (85%) are informative but contain no actionable safety or effectiveness information. However, we also discover that a minority of users (3-7% of all users) post misleading content about medical devices, which they frequently disguise as expert opinion and often link to online retailers. These users also tend to have a higher degree of influence within their networks. We further observe that the more influential users are overwhelmingly likely to be men. To help health authorities better identify problematic content in social media, we created an open source tool for classifying medical device posts into fou

The open radio access network (O-RAN) offers new degrees of freedom for building and operating 5G networks. With the introduction of O-RAN, operators have an opportunity to build and operate their networks with a high degree of flexibility and efficiency. However, operators face challenges in implementing and managing O-RAN systems because of its complexity and the need for specialized expertise. In this paper, we discuss the opportunities that O-RAN presents for operators, including increased operational efficiencies through virtualization and automation, reduced capital expenditures through disaggregated hardware, and improved agility through flexible deployment models. We also examine some of the challenges that operators may encounter in adopting and integrating O-RAN systems, such as

Deep learning models are increasingly deployed on resource-constrained edge/end devices. Such devices often perform model inference on sensitive data (e.g., biometric authentication). Consequently, an adversary with physical access to such devices can gather side-channel information and potentially extract the underlying model weights. However, existing side-channel attacks are inefficient and impractical, as they require a prohibitively large number of measurements. In this paper, we present a novel side-channel attack that is precise, efficient, and practical. We exploit the fact that device hardware characteristics (e.g., transistor threshold voltage) have a strong influence on the power consumption during model inference. Our attack correlates power consumption measurements with the model inference results to recover the model weights. We evaluate our attack on two datasets (CIFAR-10 and MNIST) and two models (VGG-16 and ResNet-20). To achieve a precision of 80%, our attack requires only 5,000 to 10,000 measurements, which can be performed in less than an hour.

Mobile networks have increased spectral efficiency through advanced multiplexing strategies that are coordinated by base stations (BSs). This coordination has enabled the use of higher-order modulation formats, such as 3D-MIMO and MIMO-OFDM. The performance of these schemes is often limited due to imperfect channel knowledge at the BSs. To address this problem, we propose a novel framework for jointly optimizing the beamformers in multi-user systems with imperfect CSI at the transmitter side. We develop an efficient algorithm based on alternating optimization, which can efficiently solve both binary and continuous variables problems. Simulation results show that the proposed method outperforms existing methods in terms of spectral efficiency and energy efficiency. Moreover, our method also

C is the most popular systems programming language. However, C lacks advanced features found in modern high-level programming languages, such as type inference and exception handling. Adding such features to C is possible, but it is not trivial. For example, a try-catch construct in C++ is implemented using nested functions and setjmp/longjmp. These low-level mechanisms are in conflict with high-level abstractions and make the code less readable. We propose Dr Wenowdis, a tool that automatically generates C extensions that provide dynamic language features such as late binding and exceptions. The extensions are specialized using type information, computed using a type inference algorithm. We evaluated Dr Wenowdis on 31,677 C programs from 64 GitHub repositories. Dr Wenowdis automatically generated 1,003,225 lines of C code, which is 33.9% smaller than the original setjmp/longjmp-based version. The generated code has 7.3% fewer variables, 14.7% fewer assignments, and 19.1% fewer jumps than the original code.

Effective weed control plays a crucial role in optimizing crop yield and enhancing farm productivity. However, traditional methods of weed control have been met with challenges such as environmental impact and labor-intensive practices. This article examines the potential of precision agriculture technologies like drone mapping and GIS to improve weed management, emphasizing their advantages over conventional methods. The integration of these cutting-edge technologies offers farmers an efficient and environmentally sustainable approach to tackling weeds. By incorporating advanced imaging techniques and geospatial analyses into weed control strategies, farmers can achieve more accurate and targeted weed identification, enabling them to implement precise herbicide applications. In addition t

Generative artificial intelligence (GenAI) has ushered in a new era for storytellers, bringing forth an unprecedented level of creativity and expression. This article explores the diverse landscape of GenAI-driven stories across various formats, from text to video, highlighting their potential to redefine the creative process and push narrative boundaries. We delve into the intriguing ways GenAI is employed, from its role as a writing companion and collaborator, to its ability to generate entire narratives through prompting. The article also discusses the challenges that arise with these tools, such as biases and ethical considerations. Furthermore, it analyzes the impact of these technologies on the literary world, examining how they can empower creators and democratize storytelling while

This dissertation is a multifaceted contribution to the advancement of vision-based autonomous robotic systems, with particular focus on the development and integration of new computer vision algorithms. The core contributions are threefold: First, we present a novel visual servoing algorithm for robots operating in dynamic environments, which enables the robot to track moving objects while avoiding collisions with other static or moving obstacles. Our approach combines an adaptive object tracking method and a collision avoidance technique based on image features and semantic segmentation. This method effectively addresses the challenges associated with traditional visual servoing approaches when applied to dynamic scenes. Second, we propose a solution for the problem of estimating human j

Hallucinations pose a significant challenge to the reliability of large language models (LLMs). Although LLMs can hallucinate with high confidence, their behavior is not always deterministic. We design and implement an algorithm for detecting hallucinations in LLM responses based on this non-determinism: we randomly shuffle inputs across model tokens until it produces different outputs; if two such runs produce inconsistent answers or are too dissimilar according our metric that accounts for length differences between them then they constitute evidence against correctness/hallucination respectively!

Successful job search results from job seekers' well-shaped social communication. While well-developed communication skills are essential, job seekers face challenges in presenting themselves as socially skilled in online applications. Job seekers must convey their interpersonal abilities to hiring managers and recruiters through resumes, cover letters, and other application materials. This paper presents a novel framework that employs OpenAI ChatGPT to generate effective communication skills descriptions for job seekers. Our proposed method is based on the idea of using advanced AI technologies such as LLM (Large Language Model) and NLP (Natural Language Processing) to analyze job seeker's profiles and produce a personalized resume with well-structured communication skill descriptions. Th

We propose a multi-agent system that enables groups of agents to collaborate and work autonomously to perform high level tasks such as transportation. The main novelty is the use, for coordination purposes only (and not in task planning), an external oracle which provides information about possible actions available to each agent at any given time instant based on its local perception; this way we avoid costly communications between all members during decision making processes while still allowing individual autonomy when deciding what action should be taken next within their own domain knowledge base(s). We show through simulations how these results can help improve performance compared with traditional approaches where communication occurs across multiple levels but without taking advant

To use new robot hardware in a new environment, it is necessary to develop a control program tailored for that hardware. This development process may be facilitated by an offline simulator that can simulate the new robot hardware and environment. However, such simulation models may have uncertainties due to various factors including: (1) the physical model of the robot's dynamics; (2) the model of the environments; (3) the model of human-robot interactions (HRI); and (4) the effectiveness of the control algorithms. The first three types of uncertainty can be modeled by adding noise to the simulated system dynamics. However, the fourth type of uncertainty arises from the imperfect control algorithms themselves. In this work, we consider the problem of learning a controller for a robot in a

The growing concern for energy efficiency in the Information and Communication Technology (ICT) sector has prompted the adoption of energy-efficient approaches to data center operation. However, limited knowledge of energy consumption patterns and the impact of energy conservation measures within data centers is hindering their implementation. In this study, we leverage the power consumption records of an enterprise data center in China to investigate its energy consumption characteristics. We analyzed and compared the monthly average electricity usage, peak hourly power demand, and average daily power consumption between different time periods in the past five years. Our findings indicate that the data center's power consumption was influenced by factors such as network traffic, server ut

Toru\'n Forest District is one of the oldest forest districts in Poland. Due to past management practices, the forest stand is very uneven-aged and consists mainly of overmature trees. Therefore, the forest management authority plans to harvest part of the stand to regenerate the forest. However, the private landowners whose plots are located within the forest district are against the idea. They fear that the proposed clear-cutting will harm the environment and reduce the future value of their land. In this paper, we model the conflict of interests between the forest management authority and the private landowners as a non-cooperative game. We show that there exists a unique Nash equilibrium which provides a compromise solution to the conflict. We have also conducted a sensitivity analysis to investigate the impact of changes in the parameters of the utility functions of the players on the equilibrium solution.

Federated learning (FL) enables collaborative training of shared models without sharing data, which is particularly attractive for vertical data partitions. However, existing FL frameworks often assume that the model parameters can be updated by a centralized server. This assumption is valid for horizontally partitioned data, where each client has the entire feature space, but is often violated in vertical settings, where each client only has a subset of the features. In this work, we propose a decoupled vertical FL framework that allows practical training on vertically partitioned data. The framework decouples the model update process from data aggregation and enables clients to update the shared model using local data without exposing local features. Theoretical analysis shows that the framework can achieve a convergence rate of $O(1/T)$ up to a function approximation error. Moreover, the function approximation error can be further reduced by a client-specific error compensation technique. We conduct extensive experiments on deep neural networks for image classification and natural language processing tasks, as well as for personalized medicine. The results demonstrate that the proposed framework achieves competitive performance compared to centralized training.

The fourth Machine Learning for Health (ML4H) Symposium, held in Granada, Spain, in June 2023, brought together researchers from diverse backgrounds to discuss the state of machine learning in health research. The symposium featured several research roundtables that provided a unique opportunity to engage in in-depth discussions on specific topics. This paper aims to capture the essence of these discussions by summarizing the talks and engaging with the audience through live-tweeting. We reflect on the current state of machine learning for health research, identify emerging trends, and discuss challenges that the community is likely to face in the near future. We highlight the importance of interdisciplinary collaboration, the need for high-quality annotated data, and the development of explainable and trustworthy machine learning methods as key areas that require our attention.

The breadth, scale, and temporal granularity of modern electronic health records (EHR) provide a rich data resource for research. However extracting the most useful information from EHRs can be challenging due to: 1.) noisy or incomplete clinical documentation; e.,g free-text notes that are often difficult to analyze with traditional algorithms such as natural language processing models trained on clean text datasets like PubMed abstracts),2,) complex relationships between different types/sourcesof structuredand unstructureddata(e.. , lab results versus physician note texts);3. ), limited understanding about howto use machine learning techniquesfor effective extractionfrom heterogeneous sourceswithin large healthcare systems.;4.);5.)) privacy concerns around sharing sensitive personal medi

Vision-extended LLMs have made significant strides in Visual Question Answering (VQA), yet their ability to reason about image concepts, rather than just the content, remains limited. Recent studies aim to enhance vision reasoning by adding a novel 'reasoning' module to existing models. However, these methods lack generalizability and suffer from overfitting. In this paper, we propose a new method that leverages prompt engineering, an effective text-based approach for enhancing pretrained LLMs. Our method, PE-Reasoner, uses a diverse set of prompt templates and employs multi-modal learning to improve visual reasoning abilities. We evaluate our model on benchmark datasets, including CLEVR and HellaSwag, and demonstrate its superior performance compared to previous works. Further, we showcas

In the literature, points and conics have been major features for camera geometric calibration. Although conics can be used to fit a circle or an ellipse, they cannot fit other shapes such as rectangles. In this paper, we introduce a new calibration approach that uses both points and curves as features. Specifically, we use a set of elliptical arcs (EAs) for calibration. Our proposed method has three main advantages: it is robust against outliers because it has multiple constraints, it is stable due to its high degree of freedom (DoF), and it can recover the extrinsic parameters without using the intrinsic parameter information. We evaluate our proposed approach on two datasets: one generated by a virtual camera model and the other captured by real cameras. The results demonstrate that our

Most continual segmentation methods tackle the problem as a per-pixel classification task. However, recent studies show that localization is crucial for accurate and efficient mask prediction in image segmentation tasks, which leads to higher accuracy, more robustness, and less memory consumption when leveraging attention mechanisms. In this paper, we propose the first method, named Local-Attention Segmentation (LAS), to learn localized representations for mask prediction with attention mechanisms. LAS can be viewed as a novel extension of the popular segmentation method, Multi-Scale Context Enhancement (MSCE). Firstly, a multi-scale convolutional encoder is designed to extract localized feature maps at different scales. Secondly, an attention module is adopted to capture discriminative in

We propose a novel approach to reasoning about computational complexity by means of a three-valued logic: apart from the usual true and false, our logic also features a third value, which we call simple. Intuitively, a statement is simple if it is true, and can be established by a self-evident, almost obvious argument. We illustrate our approach by means of a case study: we revisit the complexity of satisfiability for the fragment of CNF formulas where each variable occurs at most three times, and prove that this problem is not NP-hard. In order to do so, we first identify the simple instances of this problem, i.e., the instances that can be solved by a self-evident argument. We then show that all these simple instances can be solved in polynomial time, thus establishing that the entire problem can be solved in polynomial time. We argue that this approach is amenable to automation by a proof assistant, and that it can shed light on the P vs NP problem.

The need to efficiently store and transmit visual content, especially images, is a fundamental problem in signal processing. This work focuses on improving the compression performance of the industry standard JPEG 2000 image compression format. Towards this goal, we propose the JPEG 2000 Neural Network Assisted Lifting Steps (JPEG 2000 NAL), a novel lossy image compression method that enhances the wavelet-based compression scheme used in JPEG 2000. The proposed JPEG 2000 NAL introduces two key innovations: (i) a method to learn the optimal wavelet filter coefficients for the JPEG 2000 compression format directly from image data, and (ii) a novel lifting step design that incorporates learned filter coefficients into the lifting steps used in JPEG 2000. These innovations are designed to improve the compression performance of JPEG 2000, particularly for high-complexity images, without any additional entropy coding. We conduct extensive experiments to evaluate the performance of the proposed JPEG 2000 NAL method. The results demonstrate the effectiveness of our approach in enhancing compression performance for a variety of images. As an open-source implementation of our method, we provide a drop-in replacement for the wavelet transform in the industry standard JPEG 2000 reference software, making it easy for others to use and build upon our work.

Stock earnings forecasting is a crucial problem in the financial domain. Although many researchers have proposed various methods to analyze the relationship between stock earnings and news, few of them consider the mechanism by which news affects stock earnings. In this paper, we propose a novel explainable stock earnings forecasting model, FinReport, which aims to address the impact of news on stock earnings. Specifically, we introduce a news factor analyzing module based on the economic sense that news with similar topics and sentiments may affect stock earnings in a similar way. This module can be further interpreted as a weighted average of sentiments, where the weights are topic-specific. The proposed model is applied to a real-world dataset, and both experimental results and ablation studies demonstrate that our model outperforms several state-of-the-art baselines in terms of explainability and stock earnings forecasting performance. Our code is available at https://github.com/FinMSE/FinReport.

The robotics community is witnessing a significant shift towards cloud native technologies to leverage the benefits of robotic orchestration, scalability, and resilience. Kubernetes (K8s) has emerged as the de-facto orchestration platform, but its adoption in robotics presents new challenges, including the need for specialized K8s distributions, enhanced security features, and a user-friendly interface. To address these challenges, this paper introduces RoboKube, a cloud native robotics platform built on top of K8s. RoboKube aims to simplify the process of deploying and managing robot clusters while providing enhanced security through features such as role-based access control and cluster isolation. Additionally, RoboKube includes a robust set of monitoring and logging tools, as well as a visual dashboard. This paper presents the design and implementation of RoboKube, discusses its unique features, and evaluates its performance and security aspects. The results demonstrate that RoboKube can effectively support robotic applications in a cloud native manner, addressing the specific needs of the robotics community.

Enhancing the attribution in large language models (LLMs) is a crucial task to ensure LLMs are safe, ethical, and trustworthy. Previous work has demonstrated that various prompt-based methods can enhance attribution of LLMs on specific tasks. However, the potential of such methods for general attribution enhancement remains unknown. In this paper, we conduct an extensive empirical study by evaluating 28 different prompt-based methods on four common benchmark datasets. We also explore the effectiveness of these methods in different scenarios, including different model sizes and task types. Our results show that although many methods fail to achieve significant performance improvements, there is a small subset of methods that consistently perform well across all models and tasks. By combinin

This paper presents a Geometric-Photometric Joint Alignment(GPJA) method for automatic image registration. We propose to use the geometric information from SIFT descriptors and photometry features in RGB channels, which are commonly used independently of each other during traditional matching stage between images with similar appearance but different lighting conditions or viewpoints using feature detectors such as Harris Corner Detector (HCD), Scale Invariant Feature Transform [SIFTS], etc., respectively instead combining them together by exploiting their complementary properties through joint optimization process based on Expectation Maximization algorithm[EM].

Filter bubbles have been studied extensively within the context of online content platforms due to their potential effects on social polarization. The phenomenon occurs when algorithms filter out information that is not aligned with users' existing beliefs and interests, resulting in a lack diversity among recommended articles or videos which may lead individuals down rabbit holes into echo chambers where they receive only one perspective without any opportunity for cross-examination by opposing viewpoints . However , there remains much left unexplored about how these filters actually work outside traditional text based environments like news feeds -- especially audio streaming services such as Spotify whose popularity has skyrocketed over recent years but remain largely unstudied from an

Medical image segmentation is an essential task in computer-aided diagnosis and therapy. Deep learning methods for medical image segmentation mainly adopt the supervised learning strategy, which requires a large number of manually annotated samples. However, it is time-consuming and labor-intensive. In this paper, we propose a simple yet effective method to improve the training labels to reduce the supervision cost. Specifically, we first design a self-supervised learning branch to force the model to learn accurate training labels by predicting the class probabilities of the training set itself. Then, we propose a mixed supervision learning strategy that combines the predictions of the self-supervised branch and the traditional supervised learning branch. Our method can be applied to existing medical image segmentation networks without additional training data. Experiments on the Pancreas, BraTS18, and BraTS19 datasets show that our method achieves state-of-the-art performance.

Link prediction is a key problem for network-structured data, attracting considerable research efforts owing to its diverse applications. Nowadays, multi-relational data are collected and utilized to establish multiplex networks, which provide rich information for inferring missing links. Collective link prediction (CLP) is an emerging trend in link prediction, which considers a set of target links sharing the same predicate (i.e., relation). By exploiting the predicate-specific embedding space, CLP models induce a holistic view of the target links, thereby improving the prediction performance. Despite the progress in CLP for monorelational networks, the link prediction in multiplex networks is still challenging, as it is difficult to obtain the optimal embedding space for each predicate. In this paper, we propose a novel multiplex collective link prediction model, namely RCoCo, which establishes a cross-predicate embedding space for multiplex networks. Specifically, we design a multiplex margin-maximization module to enhance the separability among different predicates. Then, we introduce a Riemannian subspace to capture the intrinsic predicate-specific patterns. Furthermore, we employ a cross-attentional aggregation module to aggregate the information from the whole graph data. The experimental results on four benchmark datasets verify the effectiveness of the proposed RCoCo model. The source code is available at https://github.com/wjzheng96/RCoCo.

Modern data analysis tasks, such as analytics for fraud detection, often require sophisticated processing pipelines that consist of multiple data processing stages. Manually designing such data analysis applications is tedious, error-prone, and often infeasible due to the combinatorial explosion of possible pipeline configurations. AutoML libraries alleviate this situation by automatically searching for pipelines with better performance. However, the resulting pipelines are often difficult to understand and maintain, let alone debug when things go wrong. In this paper, we introduce DACO, a novel approach that automatically generates application code along with the data processing pipeline. Compared to autoML libraries, DACO not only achieves comparable or better performance, but also makes the pipelines easier to understand and thus debug. DACO achieves this comprehensiveness in data analysis applications through three techniques: (1) an application-driven search space that ensures the generated applications can be understood and maintained, (2) a code template that provides a comprehensible structure for the generated applications, and (3) an evaluation framework that enables fair and comprehensive performance evaluation. Our experiments show that DACO generates applications that achieve 1.4--15.4 times better performance than those from an autoML library, while maintaining code quality.

Nano-machines circulating inside the human body, collecting data on tissue conditions and disease processes, are being developed as a new approach to noninvasive, real-time diagnosis of diseases. These nano-machines are called nanobots. They have been in development for about 10 years at the University of Tokyo and the National Institute of Advanced Industrial Science and Technology (AIST). In September 2018, AIST announced that it had succeeded in manufacturing nanobots from a gel material that can be controlled by light using a method called two-photon polymerization. The gel is a special form of polymer, with the same chemical structure as plastic, but with its molecular chains arranged randomly and not forming a crystalline structure like plastic. The nanobots were made from this gel,

Multi-agent reinforcement learning (MARL) shows great promise in coordinating a group of agents to accomplish complex tasks. However, MARL training often suffers from slow convergence due to the exploration-exploitation dilemma and the vanishing gradient problem. To address these problems, we propose a simple yet effective method, Periodically Parameter Sharing (PPS), to accelerate the convergence of MARL. The key idea is to periodically share the parameters of the agents, so that the agents can explore the shared parameter space and take advantage of the collectively learned knowledge. Theoretical analysis shows that PPS can find a balance between exploration and exploitation, and numerical experiments show that PPS can accelerate the convergence of multi-agent reinforcement learning in both cooperative and competitive environments. As an important ingredient of our PPS-QMIX method, the periodically parameter sharing technique can be applied to existing MARL algorithms to improve their performance.

In this paper, we investigate denial-of-service attacks against status updating. The target system is a web site that provides an interface to allow users update their own statuses and view other people’s updated messages in real time (e..g., Facebook). We study the attack scenarios where malicious entities flood requests into victim systems so as not only crash them but also cause cascading failures of all dependent services which are called secondary victims through various forms such like DDoS or botnet swarms etc.).

Topic modeling seems to be almost synonymous with generating lists of top words to represent topics within a corpus, and indeed this is one powerful way that topic models can generate interpretable outputs. However in many cases it would also help if we could understand the relative importance or contribution of each word across multiple different contexts (or "topics"). This post presents a simple framework for comparing contributions between two related but distinct sources using Kullback-Leibler divergence - specifically I'll focus on how these measures might be used to explore differences between an authorial style guide document ("Style Guide") versus actual text from a novel by H G Wells entitled The Invisible Man which was written in around $1907$.

Spatiotemporal (ST) learning has become a crucial technique to enable smart cities and smart transportation. In this paper, we investigate the problem of ST learning with sparse labels in the presence of missing and unlabeled data. Specifically, we propose a novel approach that leverages both labeled and unlabeled samples in a multi-task setting. The proposed method is based on a novel objective function that incorporates regularized matrix completion for label recovery, and also includes a novel loss term that penalizes the difference between the recovered labels and the ground truth. Our extensive empirical evaluation demonstrates the effectiveness of our method across various datasets and benchmarks. Notably, the proposed method significantly improves the accuracy of ST prediction compa

Comparative reinforcement learning (CRL) learns a reward model by comparing demonstrations from an expert. In this work, we investigate the effect of expert policy quality on CRL algorithms. We find that high-quality expert policies can be hurt by noisy demonstrations, and that direct policy optimization (DPO) algorithms are more robust in such scenarios. Based on this, we introduce an algorithm that combines the reward model with the policy representation from DPO, to produce improved policies. We test our algorithm on simulated robot locomotion environments from the MuJoCo suite that have expert policies from human demonstrations. With access to expert demonstrations, our algorithm outperforms traditional CRL algorithms and achieves policies comparable to those of expert DPO algorithms. Without expert demonstrations, our algorithm still outperforms traditional CRL algorithms, but lags behind expert DPO algorithms. Our work suggests that combining reward model learning and direct policy optimization can lead to better policies than either approach alone, when expert demonstrations are available.

Software-defined networks (SDN) enable flexible and effective communication systems that are managed by centralized software controllers. However, this centralization introduces potential vulnerabilities, as an adversarial controller can monitor and manipulate the communication between different entities. In this paper, we discuss the design and implementation of a covert timing channel between two colluding entities that uses the SDN controller as a timing reference. The channel is established by having the sender transmit packets with carefully chosen inter-arrival times that convey a binary message. The receiver, which is in possession of the expected message, decodes the received packets' inter-arrival times and recovers the transmitted message. We show that the proposed channel is able to achieve a data rate of 1 bit/s with a hiddenness of 99.9%. Furthermore, we discuss the channel's limitations and potential improvements.

Disaggregated memory is promising for improving memory utilization in computer clusters in which memory demarcations are not required. However, existing disaggregated memory systems require a large number of host-memory interfaces to achieve high performance, thereby increasing the cost and complexity. We propose a novel system architecture that enables efficient use of memory resources without an increase in cost or complexity. This architecture enables efficient data movement between hosts and disaggregated memories while reducing the overheads associated with complex interconnection fabrics. We demonstrate that our architecture can provide 160% better performance than state-of-the-art approaches in disaggregated memory systems, with up to 45% lower energy consumption. Our results also s

Social media platforms (SMPs) play a prominent role in shaping public opinion on socio-political issues. Previous research has shown that exposure to politically biased news on SMPs can reinforce users' existing beliefs, strengthening their commitment to a particular political ideology. However, the impact of politically biased news on users' attitudes towards health-related issues, such as vaccines, remains unclear. To fill this gap in the literature, we investigate the effects of politically biased news on vaccine attitudes. We define politically biased news as media content produced by sources with a known political affiliation that presents information in a way that aligns with the source's political affiliation (e.g., pro-Trump or pro-Biden). We measure the exposure to politically biased news and vaccine attitudes among American users of Twitter and Facebook. We find that exposure to politically biased news decreases vaccine attitudes, especially among users who are susceptible to polarization (i.e., users with few like-minded peers). The effect is stronger among pro-Trump users, possibly because Trump-era media environment is more polarized and chaotic than the pre-2016 era.iasm users.iasm users.

Video stabilization (VS) aims to remove camera shake from hand-held videos and typically operates on a per-frame basis. Recently, deep learning based frame-level VS methods have shown superior performance by modeling the temporal smoothness of the video. However, they may suffer from motion blur or double-movement artifacts, especially when the camera motion is large or complex. In this paper, we propose a novel meta-learning based VS method to tackle large and complex camera motions. Specifically, we formulate VS as a meta-problem that aims to find a good initialization for the frame-level VS model, where the goodness is measured by the validation loss. Such an initialization can quickly adapt to new videos with large camera motions. Experiments show that the proposed method can produce better stabilized videos than its non-met learning counterpart and achieves state-of-the-art performance on some benchmarks.

While deepfakes are becoming increasingly harder to distinguish with the naked eye, forensic analysis can uncover the clues that expose the deception. In this paper, we introduce a multi-modal approach to deepfake detection that analyzes audio and video clues in addition to face and voice embeddings. We source audio from the VGGSound dataset and video from J-HMDB, and utilize pre-trained models to obtain embeddings for both. We compare deepfake detection performance of individual and multi-modal forensic models using face, voice, and multi-modal embeddings. We also investigate the effect of training a multi-modal model jointly versus training voice and face embeddings separately. Our results indicate that multi-modal models consistently outperform individual modalities for deepfake detection. Additionally, we find that jointly training the multi-modal model improves performance over training voice and face embeddings separately, though the difference is small. Our study identifies important audio-visual clues for deepfake detection and confirms the effectiveness of multi-modal approaches in uncovering deepfake deceptions.

Developing Large Language Models (LLMs) with robust long-context capabilities has been a crucial endeavor in the field of AI. Recently, researchers have made significant progress by leveraging multi-head attention mechanisms to enhance LLM's contextual reasoning capability. However, the current methods face challenges when handling multiple inputs simultaneously. This paper presents a novel approach that utilizes a single multi-head attention module to effectively attend to all inputs and outputs simultaneously. We evaluate our method using two popular benchmarks, RACE and MMLU, and demonstrate its superior performance compared to existing approaches. Additionally, we introduce a new task called Long-Context Reading Comprehension (LCRC), which evaluates LLMs' ability to understand complex

Data-driven models are vulnerable to concept drift, as their performance degrades when the properties of the data used for inference differ from those of the data used for training. To adapt to concept drift, models need to be continually learned along with the changes in the data distribution. However, continual learning over long periods is challenging because the models tend to forget the old knowledge represented in the earlier layers. In this paper, we propose a complementary spatiotemporal learning system (ComS2T) that consists of a spatial memory module (SMM) and a temporal memory module (TMM). The SMM stores the feature representations of the data samples at each time step. The TMM maintains the model parameters at each time step and updates the parameters based on the difference between the new and the old data samples. The two modules work complementarily to enable the model to adapt to the changes in the data distribution over time. Specifically, the SMM helps the TMM to focus on the relevant data features that have changed over time, and the TMM helps the SMM to update the feature representations of the data samples at each time step. The proposed ComS2T system is evaluated over a range of benchmark tasks, and the results demonstrate that it achieves superior performance compared to the existing state-of-the-art methods.

Pre-trained language models have been proven to possess strong base capabilities, which not only excel in downstream tasks but also enable fine tuning for new types of problems. However the underlying mechanism remains largely unclear and needs more investigation from a theoretical standpoint. In this paper we propose an information theoretic framework that analyzes BERT's inductive bias by decomposing its performance on any task into two components: (1) what it can learn based solely upon contextualized representations; (2 ) additional contributions due specifically tailored pre training objectives or auxiliary losses during finetuning .

The widespread presence of offensive languages on social media has resulted in adverse effects on the well-being of online users, especially minors. This study aims to develop an AI-powered system that detects abusive content and provides automated moderation solutions. Our approach leverages Machine Learning (ML) techniques such as Convolutional Neural Networks (CNN), Recurrent Neural Networks (RNN), and Large Language Models (LLM). We evaluate our model's efficacy by conducting extensive experiments across multiple datasets. Furthermore, we integrate our solution into various platforms to enhance their ability to combat cyberbullying and hate speech. Our results showcase the superiority of our proposed model compared to other available methods. The effectiveness of our model is substanti

Feature selection prepares the AI-readiness of data by eliminating redundant features. Prior work has shown that feature selection is critical in improving the performance and interpretability of deep learning models. However, existing methods rely on a static set of features for the entire training process, which does not consider the dynamic nature of data and may not fully leverage all features available for the task at hand. This paper introduces Adaptive Feature Selection (AFS), a novel approach for dynamically selecting the optimal feature subset during training. AFS incorporates an adaptive feedback mechanism to refine the feature selection strategy based on model performance over time. We evaluate AFS on 12 datasets from different domains and show that it outperforms the state-of-t

Large Language Models (LLMs) have shown remarkable performance in the past few years. However, recent works have revealed that LLMs suffer from hallucinating incorrect information. To better understand the hallucination problem, we propose HaluEval-Wild, a new framework to evaluate hallucinations of LLMs in the wild. Compared to existing works, HaluEval-Wild is more comprehensive and realistic, considering both the quantity and quality of hallucinations, and can be applied to LLMs in any task and setting. The framework consists of three systematic modules: a data generation module to create diverse datasets in different tasks and settings, a benchmarking module to quantitatively evaluate the quantity of hallucinations, and a qualitative analysis module to examine the types and causes of hallucinations. Based on the UNLMMs dataset, we demonstrate the effectiveness of our framework by applying it to the original GPT-3 model and several state-of-the-art LLMs. We find that despite much better performance, LLMs with more parameters do not hallucinate more than LLMs with fewer parameters. Our study reveals that LLMs in the wild still suffer from severe hallucinations, especially in realistic settings, and provides valuable insights for LLM researchers and users.

We study the best-of-both-worlds problem in a linear contextual bandit setting, where $S$ is the set of arms, $\mathcal{X}$ is the set of contexts, and the expected reward of arm $s \in S$ in context $x \in \mathcal{X}$ is given by a linear function $\langle \theta^*, x \rangle$ for an unknown parameter $\theta^*$. We introduce LC-Tsalis-INF, a novel best-of-both-worlds algorithm that relies on a novel application of the multi-armed bandit algorithm Tsalis-INF by Garivier & Kaufmann (2016). We prove that the regret of LC-Tsalis-INF is upper bounded by $\tilde{\mathcal{O}} \left( \frac{\Vert \theta^* \Vert}{\min_{\varepsilon} \Vert \mathbb{E}[\sqrt{\varepsilon X}] \Vert} \right)$, where $X$ is a random vector uniformly distributed on the set of contexts, and $\varepsilon$ is a parameter that controls the variance of the rewards. We also prove a lower bound of $\tilde{\Omega} \left( \frac{\Vert \theta^* \Vert}{\min_{\varepsilon} \Vert \mathbb{E}[\sqrt{\varepsilon X}] \Vert} \right)$ showing that LC-Tsalis-INF is minimax optimal.

In this study, a human evaluation is carried out on how hyperparameter settings impact the quality of Transformer-based models for image classification. The model architecture and dataset used in the experiment are inspired by CLIPV2 (Contrastive Image-Text Pretraining Version 2). This framework includes an encoder network that processes images and text input to generate embeddings, followed by a decoder network that predicts the image class labels based on these embeddings. The hyperparameters tested include the number of tokens in the input vocabulary, the learning rate schedule, and the optimization algorithm. We have also compared the results with LLaMA2, a large language model recently released by Microsoft. Our findings demonstrate that LLaMA2 offers better performance than CLIPV2 wh

In recent years, personalized recommendation technology has flourished and become one of the hot research directions. The matrix factorization model and the metric learning model which proposed successively have been widely studied and applied. The latter uses the Euclidean distance instead of the dot product used by the former to measure the latent space vector. While avoiding the shortcomings of the dot product, the assumption of Euclidean distance is neglected, resulting in limited recommendation quality of the model.

Spherical robots, characterized by their spherical shell structure, offer unique advantages in various applications, including search and rescue, space exploration, and entertainment. The inherent symmetry and curvature of these robots present both challenges and opportunities in terms of motion planning and control. Previous work on spherical robots has focused on developing motion planning algorithms that take into account the robots' physical constraints, such as limited joint ranges and friction. However, these algorithms typically assume that the robots are equipped with omnidirectional wheels, which allow them to move freely on a flat surface. In this paper, we consider a more general and complete variant of spherical robots that are equipped with non-omnidirectional wheels, such as uniball or castor wheels. We propose a novel motion planning framework that computes kinodynamically feasible and smooth trajectories for these robots on both flat and curved surfaces. Our framework extends the use of control barriers to ensure kinodynamic feasibility and smoothness of the generated trajectories. We have conducted extensive experiments to validate the effectiveness of our approach in real-world scenarios, including both simulated and physical environments. Our results demonstrate that the proposed framework produces high-quality solutions that are feasible for the robot to execute.

Human understanding of complex ecosystems is aided by visualizations that can represent the interconnections between ecosystem components. However, visualizations that depict these complex interconnections often lack spatial context, and those that depict spatial context rarely show more than two dimensions. With increasing interest in3D printing and other fabrication technologies, there is a growing need for digital designers and engineers to visualize and fabricate complex multidimensional geometries. Seabed ecosystems, which are three-dimensional by nature, provide a particularly interesting case study for such visualizations. Here, we present DeepSee, a novel visual analytics system that aims to improve the understanding of complex three-dimensional seabed ecosystems by generating multidimensional visualizations that preserve the spatial context of the organisms within their natural habitat. To achieve this, DeepSee uses deep learning techniques to analyze underwater images and automatically generate customized visualizations that summarize the distribution and abundance of different seabed ecosystems' components. Users can interact with these visualizations to explore the data from multiple dimensions while maintaining spatial context. DeepSee's visualizations can also be exported as physical models using 3D printing technology, thereby allowing users to physically interact with and explore the data. Through a collaborative and iterative design process with marine biologists, we demonstrate the effectiveness of DeepSee in engaging stakeholders in exploratory data analysis and facilitating their understanding of the data.

We present SpaceHopper, a three-legged, small-scale robot designed for future Mars exploration missions. We address the problem of robust and stable locomotion on uneven terrain with our novel legged control scheme called Hybrid Dynamic Motion Planning (HDMP). HDMP leverages the advantages of both kinematic control and hybrid dynamic motion planning, enabling the robot to effectively navigate various terrains while minimizing energy consumption. The controller employs a combination of open-loop control based on an optimization method, along with closed-loop feedback through a robust stabilization mechanism, achieving a balance between speed and efficiency. To ensure stability, we introduce a new feedback term that uses an exponential moving average of the error to prevent large oscillation

Machine learning models, while progressively advanced, rely heavily on the IID assumption, which is often violated in real-world applications. Recent works have revealed that a small fraction of outliers (i) can result into severe performance degradation and even trigger catastrophic failures; moreover they may also introduce additional challenges such as bias amplification or backdoor attack vulnerability [1]. Thus there exists an urgent need to develop robust machine learners against adversarial data contamination with low computational overhead for practical use cases like healthcare surveillance systems. [2] In this work we propose a novel outlier detection method called Local Outlier Factor ($LOF$) based Adaptive Ensemble Learning($LAEL$), where $LOF$ measures how isolated each instan

Reservoir computing (RC) offers a neuromorphic framework that is particularly effective for processing spatiotemporal data, due to its capability of processing high-dimensional inputs in an analog manner. In this study, we present RC2Spatial, which combines the RC framework with spatial convolutions (i.e., 2D convolutions). The proposed approach utilizes local nonlinearities and spatially modulated temporal delays to process input vectors with multiple dimensions simultaneously, resulting in enhanced performance compared to existing approaches. Our experiments demonstrate that RC2Spatial outperforms conventional RC and CNN architectures on both synthetic and real-world datasets, achieving state-of-the-art accuracy on the challenging MNIST dataset. Additionally, we showcase how RC2Spatial c

Multi-Label Text Classification (MLTC) is a fundamental task in the field of Natural Language Processings. It has many real world applications, eg., sentiment analysis and information retrieval system for legal documents classification that help to improve efficiency at work places as well reduce human errors by increasing speed with higher accuracy level than traditional methods like manual labelling or data set creation from scratch which requires more time & resources thus increases cost too much compared when using ML techniques instead! To solve this problem we have proposed an efficient multilabel text classifier model based on deep learning architecture called Convolutional Neural Network(CNN). The experimental results show significant improvement over existing approaches s

This paper discusses the effectiveness of leveraging Chatbot: Generative Pre-trained Transformer (ChatGPT) to enhance the performance of a traditional Natural Language Processing (NLP) model in the context of text classification. Specifically, this study investigates the impact of using ChatGPT as an external source of knowledge for the NLP model when dealing with limited training data. Through experimental analysis, we demonstrate that integrating ChatGPT into the pre-training phase of an NLP model can significantly improve its performance and generalization ability, particularly in cases where there is a lack of sufficient training data. The study also highlights the potential challenges associated with using ChatGPT in this context, including ethical concerns and the need for careful ca

Code quality assessment is an important issue in software engineering. It is helpful for code refactoring, automated program repair, and other tasks. There are many features that contribute to code quality, and these features are not equally important for all types of programs. For example, mathematical programs are expected to utilize numerical libraries to perform operations such as linear algebra, and programs written in imperative style are expected to contain loops and conditional statements. Features that are consistently associated with code quality are called code quality dimensions. In this paper, we introduce a new approach for unsupervised learning of code quality dimensions. We propose Neural Hidden Markov Model (Neural HMM), an extension of Hidden Markov Model (HMM) that can extract long-range temporal features using neural networks. We prove that Neural HMM can learn the harmonic analysis based representation of code quality, without the need for manual labeling. We evaluate our approach for the three tasks: prediction of program location, prediction of programming language, and classification of code snippets as good or bad quality. Neural HMM outperforms other state-of-the-art methods on these tasks.

Creating 3D textured meshes using generative artificial intelligence has garnered significant attention recently, with the emergence of approaches such as MeshFormer and Mesha. However previous work in this area have focused on generating synthetic images or simple polygonal objects without considering materials that are physically plausible for real-world applications (e.,g metals). In contrast to these prior works our approach focuses solely upon rendering photorealistic textures onto a set mesh which can be used by designers/engineers alike when creating digital prototypes early during product development cycles; thereby reducing unnecessary physical prototyping costs associated therewith while allowing them access greater flexibility through simulation based design exploration possibil

In reasoning tasks, even a minor error can cascade into inaccurate results, leading to poor quality outputs. This is especially prevalent in text-based problems where errors are more likely due the high degree of ambiguity inherent within natural language input and output data sets; thus making it difficult for current models trained solely on traditional metrics like accuracy or precision alone as they cannot accurately assess performance over time spans without considering other factors such how far off predictions were from expected outcomes (e..g., misclassifying 7 out10 times). To address this challenge headon , we propose an evaluation method that takes both correctness &amp distance between predicted values versus actual ones so users may gain insight about their model's overall eff

Session data has been widely used for understanding user's behavior in e-commerce. Researchers are actively exploring how to leverage session data to improve the performance of recommender systems, but most existing studies assume that session data is a single sequence of events, which is not applicable in reality. In this paper, we propose a novel framework called \textbf{S}equence \textbf{M}ultiple \textbf{E}vent \textbf{I}nference \textbf{F}ramework (\textbf{SMIEF}), which can be utilized to mine the semantic information hidden in multiple event sequences. SMIEF consists of two modules: (1) a graph representation learning module and (2) an attention mechanism. The graph representation learning module learns rich semantics from event sequences by leveraging graph convolution networks and

Real-world datasets commonly exhibit noisy labels and class imbalance, such as long-tail distributions. In the context of large language models (LLMs), these properties may result in suboptimal or even misleading predictions. To address this problem, we introduce a novel framework for modeling uncertainty in LLM predictions by leveraging self-distillation with noise-tolerant knowledge distillation (NTKD) as an unsupervised regularization mechanism. The core idea is to train an ensemble of student models using NTKD, which is designed to suppress noise effects by penalizing prediction errors that are more extreme than their training data distribution. Additionally, we incorporate hard negative mining into our approach to improve performance on unseen samples, where the majority of classes ha

One of the commonly used seismic force-resisting systems in structures is Reinforced Concrete Columns (RCC) which are subjected to various types of earthquakes. The performance of RCCs under seismic loading depends on their structural response and ductility. To study the structural behavior of RCCs, it is necessary to model the structural system accurately. The present paper deals with the detailed analysis of reinforced concrete columns using a numerical approach. This numerical approach was validated by comparing its results against those from analytical approaches. A three-dimensional finite element based modeling technique has been used to analyze the responses of an RCC column under different combinations of axial forces and bending moments. Moreover, the effects of various parameters

Autonomous open-ended learning (OEL) robots are able to cumulatively acquring new capabilities from their experiences in the environment. However, most OEL frameworks have been developed and tested in simulation, which differs significantly from real-world environments. Moreover, few studies have investigated how autonomous OEL can be achieved for an end-to-end robotics pipeline. In this paper, we present a fully autonomous OEL framework called GROCER (GROunded Cumulative Experiential Robotics) that trains an end-to-end robotics agent with both policy learning and world modeling. The proposed framework enables agents to learn how to interact with physical objects, solve complex tasks, and adapt to new tasks in a cumulative manner. We apply GROCER to a real-world robot kitchen to teach it h

Large Language Models (LLMs) have shown impressive results across various tasks. Nevertheless, it remains unclear whether they can perform zero-shot inference on activity recognition, a key task in understanding human behavior from video. In this work, we explore this very question for action recognition in the wild, where humans are not available to provide annotations. We present HARGPT, a novel framework that leverages LLMs as universal classifiers for human activity recognition. HARGPT harnesses the power of LLMs to analyze video frames, transforming them into text embeddings that can be readily classified by the LLM. We demonstrate the zero-shot capability of HARGPT by testing it on four action recognition datasets, two of which consist of videos with no available annotations. Our experiments reveal that HARGPT outperforms other models that rely on multi-modal learning by up to 4.4% across all datasets. This performance boost is statistically significant in three datasets. Moreover, we show that HARGPT can generalize across different LLMs, datasets, and frame extraction methods. We hope that our work will inspire further research on the potential of LLMs in video understanding.

In image classification, we propose a unified system that can handle frequent, few-shot and zero-shot learning simultaneously. Its core component is a novel prototype update rule, which generalizes the existing prototype update rules for few-shot learning. The generalized rule is based on the principle of maximizing the minimum distance between any two prototypes, which is a convex problem. For few-shot learning, we use the existing prototype selection methods. For the two new scenarios, i.e., frequent learning and zero-shot learning, we propose two novel prototype learning methods, which are based on the principle of maximizing the margin between the prototype of the majority class and those of the minority classes at both the feature and label spaces. The feature space prototype learning method is a non-convex problem, while the label space one is a convex problem. We evaluate the proposed system on a large number of classification tasks, which involves frequent, few-shot and zero-shot learning. The results demonstrate that the proposed system outperforms the existing methods for frequent learning. For few-shot learning, our method achieves competitive results compared to the state-of-the-art methods. For the two new scenarios, i.e., frequent learning and zero-shot learning, our method also achieves competitive results compared to the state-of-the-art methods.

Tropical forests are among the most biodiverse ecosystems on the planet, but human activities are putting them at risk. Palm trees are a crucial part of this ecosystem, providing habitat and food for many animals. However, deforestation and climate change are threatening their survival. In this work, we use a pre-trained convolutional neural network to classify images of leaves as belonging to healthy or stressed palms, obtaining a leaf-level classification. We then use a logistic regression model to relate leaf-level classification to tree-level classification. We also introduce a new metric called Average Healthy Leaf Percentage (AHLP) to better reflect the health of the tree. We evaluate our method on two datasets of palm leaves from the Ecuadorian Amazon and compare it to other methods, showing that our method outperforms them. We also show that our method can be extended to a fully probabilistic approach, providing not only a classification but also a measure of uncertainty, which is crucial for applications in the real world. Finally, we show that our method can be used to monitor the health of palm trees in tropical forests, providing valuable information for conservation efforts.

Storing data on remote servers has become ubiquitous. Cloud computing offers many advantages, including pay-per-use pricing, high availability, and fault tolerance. However, data privacy and security are major concerns when using cloud services. A cloud service provider (CSP) may snoop on a customer's data for security or surveillance purposes. To prevent this, a CSP may pretend to be a regular user and store a copy of the data on its own account. This way, the CSP can access the data easily when needed. To avoid this privacy violation, we propose an immersion-based coding scheme. The main idea is to encode the data items in such a way that a CSP cannot extract any information from a user's data without the user's collaboration. In this scheme, data items are encoded into a large matrix, and users are given a subset of the rows. To retrieve a data item, the users need to combine their rows in a specific way, which is only possible if they all participate. We present an information-theoretic analysis of the proposed scheme and prove that it achieves the desired privacy goal. We also show that the scheme is efficient in terms of communication and storage. Finally, we discuss its practicality and provide some implementation details.

Context: In the first part of his 2012 presentation "Inventing on Principle", Elon Musk presented a design for an advanced AI architecture. The architecture is based on a "modular" model, which is similar to that of human brains and allows the system to adapt itself to new situations. In this paper we describe a novel approach to implementing this modularity in neural networks. Our approach uses self-attention mechanisms to represent each module, which enables these modules to communicate with each other through attention weights. We demonstrate how such a network can be used to solve complex problems by training it using reinforcement learning (RL). This results in a highly effective and flexible AI system that can learn from experience, adapt to new environments, and perform well under v

A novel hybrid active-passive reconfigurable intelligent surface (RIS) transmitter based on the concept of energy harvesting is proposed. The passive RIS, which consists of an array of metasurfaces and a large antenna array at its output, serves as a beamformer for the active RIS. To reduce interference from non-targeted users in the downlink, a beam steering algorithm with a fast time response is used to control the phase shift of each element in the active RIS. This enables the system to adaptively modify the incident signal's directionality by controlling the relative phases among different elements of the active RIS. In addition, the proposed hybrid transmitter employs two power splitting units for energy harvesting to obtain additional energy from ambient sources such as light or radi

Model-free reinforcement learning (RL) methods have shown strong performance in various complex control tasks, often outperforming model-based methods. However, model-based methods have the potential to significantly outperform model-free methods in terms of sample efficiency and therefore have not been completely replaced by model-free RL approaches. In this paper, we propose a novel hybrid model-based RL approach that can learn control policies for agile motor skills without the need for a reinforcement signal. The proposed method learns a reward-free latent representation that is well-suited for controlling complex agile systems.
