We demonstrate that the proposed method can learn policies that significantly outperform the state-of-the-art model-free approaches in terms of sample efficiency. Furthermore, we show that the proposed method is able to learn robust policies from very limited expert demonstrations.

Fetal brain development is strongly correlated with cognitive development after birth. Therefore, many disorders of the central nervous system originate in the fetal period. However, in utero, the human brain is not visible with structural MRI due to the lack of myelination and the presence of cerebrospinal fluid. To overcome this limitation, we propose a method that reconstructs virtual tracts of the fetal brain from diffusion MRI data. By leveraging the anatomical constraints of the developing brain, our method can accurately track fetal brain tracts, even in the presence of cerebrospinal fluid. We evaluated our method using ex-vivo diffusion MRI data from 13 fetuses obtained from a single center. After tractography, we measured the fractional anisotropy and the mean diffusivity of the tracts to investigate the maturity of the fetal brain. We also compared the tractography results with those of a standard diffusion MRI analysis pipeline. Our method demonstrated a higher correlation with gestational age than the standard pipeline, suggesting that it is more robust for fetal brain tractography. Furthermore, we showed that the reconstructed tracts are consistent across subjects and that our method can be used to reliably analyze large populations of fetuses.

Coupled tensor decomposition (CTD) can extract joint features from multimodal data in a unified framework, which has been successfully applied to tasks like image classification and feature extraction. In this paper, we investigate the use of CTD for the task of multilingual text translation. We develop an encoder-decoder model based on CTD, which incorporates contextual information from both source and target languages into the decoding process. Our experiments show that our model achieves competitive performance compared to existing methods with more complex architectures, and outperforms them on datasets with low resource availability. This indicates that the simplicity of our approach is not detrimental to its performance. Additionally, we conduct extensive ablation studies to validate

Recently, it has been recognized that large language models demonstrate high performance on various intellectual tasks. However, their robustness and interpretability have not been thoroughly investigated. In this study, we propose a novel method to explain the decision-making process of large language models using adversarial examples. We demonstrate the effectiveness of our method in revealing the internal structure of large language models by identifying common vulnerabilities across multiple datasets. Furthermore, we utilize the identified vulnerabilities as a measure of interpretability. Finally, we discuss future directions for research in the field of large language model analysis and propose a new challenge for further investigation. Our code is available at https://github.com/Yuji

Machine learning models, while progressively advanced, rely heavily on the IID assumption, which is often unrealistic in real-world scenarios. This blog post aims to explore the practical implications of this assumption and discusses recent advances in ML research that aim to address these challenges. The key takeaway from this article is the importance of considering non-IID data when developing machine learning models. We will explore three different approaches: (1) data augmentation, (2) meta-learning, and (3) federated learning. Each approach has its advantages and limitations, but they all share a common goal of improving the robustness and generalizability of machine learning models by taking into account non-IID data distributions. In conclusion, we argue that ignoring non-IID data

We present SpaceHopper, a three-legged, small-scale robot designed for future space exploration. Deep reinforcement learning is used to train the robot on a variety of tasks such as object recognition and grasping. This work demonstrates how SpaceHopper can learn new skills using just a few examples from the environment. It also shows that by combining this learned knowledge with predefined rules, we are able to teach SpaceHopper how to act more efficiently in the face of uncertainty. This ability will be essential when SpaceHoppers begin their explorations deep within space. In addition, we discuss several challenges faced during the development process and provide recommendations for future research. The source code and instructions are available at https://github.com/matthewwildrick/Spa

Federated learning (FL) is a collaborative scheme to train a global model across multiple participants without sharing local data. FL's collaborative and distributed nature makes it vulnerable to various adversarial attacks. Unlike in traditional settings, where an attacker can be easily identified and isolated, in FL, the boundary between legitimate and adversarial participants is often unclear. Furthermore, existing detection methods are often specialized and lack the ability to recover from attacks. In this paper, we introduce FL-GUARD, a holistic framework for detecting and recovering from negative FL. FL-GUARD consists of two components: a monitoring component and a recovery component. The monitoring component watches over the global model and issues an alert if it detects abnormal behavior indicative of an attack. In contrast to existing monitoring approaches, which are specific to certain attack models, FL-GUARD's monitoring component is able to detect a wide range of attacks in an attack-agnostic manner. The recovery component then steps in to mitigate the effects of the detected attack. FL-GUARD is designed to be executed on the fly, with minimal overhead, ensuring that it does not impede the performance of the FL system. We evaluate FL-GUARD by deploying it on 1) three real-world datasets (CIFAR10, MNIST, and Tiny-ImageNet) and two benchmark FL datasets (FedCifar10 and FedTiny-ImageNet), and 2) two types of attack (Byzantine and Gradient-Inverter) on both a homogeneous network (all participants have the same capabilities) and a heterogeneous network (participants have different capabilities). Experimental results show that FL-GUARD can effectively detect various attacks and help the FL system recover from these attacks.

We consider the fundamental problem of decomposing a large-scale approximate nearest neighbor search (ANNS) query into smaller, more tractable queries. In particular, we focus on the case where the underlying metric space is the unit sphere in Euclidean spaces and the query is defined by a finite set of vectors, also called points. The goal is to determine whether there exist a pair of points $u$ and $v$, such that their dot product $\langle u, v\rangle$ satisfies an inequality $f(u,v)$ that holds for all pairs $(x, y)$, i.e., for all points $x \in {\mathbb S}^d$.

Semi-supervised semantic segmentation (SSSS) has been proposed to alleviate the data scarcity problem in pixelwise annotation tasks. However, it is difficult for current SSSS methods to efficiently exploit information from both labeled and unlabeled samples due their simple model structures with a single encoder or two encoders sharing one decoder network architecture that can not fully utilize diverse auxiliary supervisions provided by different types of image regions including foreground objects/background scenes as well boundaries between them during training process simultaneously .

Link prediction in knowledge graphs (KGs) plays a crucial role in expanding the scope of questions that can be answered using the existing triples in the KG. Enhanced with question-answering capabilities, LLMs can serve as a question-answering agent and predict missing links between entities in a KG. However, directly applying LLMs to predict links in KGs requires the model to understand the natural language queries and the entity relations in the KG, which is challenging for LLMs to grasp. To address this challenge, we propose a novel K-Link model that fuses the question-answering capability of LLMs and the temporal dependency modeling capability of neural time-series models to predict links in KGs. Specifically, we design a knowledge-link graph (K-Link) that represents both the question-answering knowledge and the temporal relations for LLMs to learn the representation of triples in the KG. We then propose a novel training strategy that alternately performs the question-answering and link-prediction tasks to enhance the LLM's understanding of the KG knowledge and its temporal dynamics. Experimental results on two benchmark KGs demonstrate that the proposed K-Link model outperforms the state-of-the-art methods for link prediction in KGs.

This paper presents DNAct, a language-conditioned multi-task policy framework that integrates data-driven approaches and reinforcement learning (RL) for the task of automated testing. The main challenge is to learn how to effectively reuse testing code in different contexts, while taking into account various factors such as the application being tested, its underlying technology stack, and the user's preferences. We present our novel approach based on RL that allows the test suite to be conditioned on the application being tested, thus making it more adaptable to the application under test (AUT). This approach can also leverage data collected from human testers who have previously interacted with the AUT. Experiments on real applications show that DNAct significantly outperforms baselines

Real-time processing is crucial in autonomous driving systems due to the imperative of instantaneous decision making. To meet these demands, conventional RNNs are often replaced by LSTM and GRU variants that offer faster training speed. However, such models' long-term memory capacity can be limited, posing a challenge for real-world applications requiring significant temporal context. In this paper, we propose a novel architecture called Memory-Augmented Recurrent Neural Network (MARNN) with two key innovations: 1) A new memory module based on Sparse Distributed Representation (SDR), and 2) An efficient recurrent unit design. Our MARNN surpasses existing state-of-the-art models in both long-term memory and inference efficiency, demonstrating superior performance on multiple benchmark datas

Interpretable computer vision models can produce transparent predictions, where the features of an image are compared with a set of training examples to make a prediction. We study how to achieve this in the case of neural networks with rectified linear units (ReLUs). A common approach is to use ReLU-aware loss functions that enforce sparsity when training. However, these methods typically do not generalize well to novel images that were not seen during training. To address this problem we propose using a weighted loss function, where the weights depend on the distance between the input and training samples. We show that such a loss function produces models with good predictive performance and interpretability across many datasets. Our method also has practical advantages over other approa

The capacity to generalize beyond the range of training data is a pivotal challenge, often synonymous with machine learning. In this work, we approach the problem from an information-theoretic perspective and present a generalization error bound that holds for arbitrary neural networks and nonlinear activations. We provide a detailed analysis of the underlying bottleneck in our generalization bounds: the mutual information between the hidden layer output and the target outputs. This analysis reveals that the mutual information is not only dependent on the network architecture but also on the data distribution. Safety-critical failures often have fatal consequences in aerospace control. Control systems on aircraft are therefore highly complex and very expensive to develop. In this project, we focus on the verification of safety-critical flight control laws, including their robustness to nonlinearities of the underlying aircraft dynamics, using high-fidelity simulations that can be executed efficiently on parallel computers. To achieve this goal, we employ formal verification techniques, which we extend with methods from model checking and approximate computing. Our approach is illustrated by means of an example of a pitch control law for an airplane. The results demonstrate that our methodology leads to significant efficiency gains while ensuring adequate levels of safety. This research contribut

Electronic Health Records (EHRs) are a valuable asset to facilitate clinical research and point of care applications; however, extracting meaningful information from EHRs using electronic medical data is challenging. Multi-Task Learning (MTL) is a promising deep learning approach for jointly learning several tasks, which is ideal for disease prediction using EHRs. MTL can not only improve the performance for each individual task but also has the potential to enable information sharing among tasks. However, existing techniques of MTL are inflexible and require manually specified task relationships, which is time-consuming and labor-intensive. To address this issue, we propose an automated MTL framework for joint disease prediction on EHRs using a latent task relationship learning approach. Our automated framework can automatically learn the relationships between tasks based on the similarities between clinical concepts. The advantages of our approach over existing techniques are two-fold: (1) the ability to automatically learn task relationships, and (2) the use of a novel constrained optimization method to ensure the interpretability of the learned task relationships. We evaluated our framework using real-world EHR data from the MIMIC-III database, and demonstrated the efficacy of our approach for multi-task disease prediction.
